heading: 2.3 Introduction
text: estimator may differ truth two reasons: systematically (targets wrong value average) randomly (bounces around average). Bias captures first; variance captures second. Together determine close estimator truth, measured mean squared error (MSE). Many important statistical choices – raw shrinkage estimators, parametric non-parametric methods, simple flexible models – bias/variance trade-offs.

heading: 2.4 Prerequisites
text: reader know expectation comfortable simulating repeated sampling R replicate().

heading: 2.5 Theory
text: estimator \(\hat{\theta}\) population parameter \(\theta\):Bias: \(\mathrm{Bias}(\hat{\theta}) = E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta\). unbiased estimator zero bias.Variance: \(\mathrm{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = E[(\hat{\theta} - E[\hat{\theta}])^2]\). square standard error.Mean squared error: \(\mathrm{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = E[(\hat{\theta} - \theta)^2]\).linked classical decomposition:\[\mathrm{MSE}(\hat{\theta}) = \mathrm{Bias}(\hat{\theta})^2 + \mathrm{Var}(\hat{\theta}).\]biased estimator small variance can lower MSE unbiased estimator large variance. principle behind shrinkage estimators (James-Stein, ridge regression, Bayesian posterior means informative priors): accept small bias exchange large variance reduction.Examples bias:sample variance divisor \(n\) biased; divisor \(n - 1\) unbiased. consistent.MLE variance normal model (\(\sigma^2 = \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 / n\)) biased downward factor \((n-1)/n\).sample standard deviation \(s = \sqrt{s^2}\) biased even though \(s^2\) unbiased, square root concave.

heading: 2.6 Assumptions
text: decomposition identity – always holds – empirical assessment via simulation requires one can simulate known truth. real data, bias variance directly observable, MSE---validation-set related proxies.

heading: 2.7 R Implementation
text: Two competing estimators population variance compared: MLE (divide \(n\)) unbiased estimator (divide \(n - 1\)).
code:
library(ggplot2)
set.seed(2026)

true_sigma2 <- 9
n <- 10
reps <- 20000

mle_var <- function(x) mean((x - mean(x))^2)
ub_var  <- function(x) var(x)

est_mle <- replicate(reps, mle_var(rnorm(n, sd = sqrt(true_sigma2))))
est_ub  <- replicate(reps, ub_var(rnorm(n, sd = sqrt(true_sigma2))))

bias_var <- function(est, truth) {
  c(bias     = mean(est) - truth,
    variance = var(est),
    mse      = mean((est - truth)^2))
}

round(bias_var(est_mle, true_sigma2), 3)
round(bias_var(est_ub,  true_sigma2), 3)

heading: 2.8 Output & Results
text: \(n = 10\), MLE biased (expected value 8.10 vs. truth 9.00) lower variance lower MSE. regime, biased estimator better point estimate MSE criterion. \(n\) grows, bias gap MSE vanish.
code:
bias variance      mse
   -0.90    14.68    15.49      # MLE, biased downward
    0.01    18.11    18.11      # unbiased estimator

heading: 2.9 Interpretation
text: reporting estimator, one state whether unbiased, whether alternative () smaller MSE. predictive models, bias/variance decomposition motivates cross-validation: flexible model low bias high variance, --sample MSE minimised intermediate complexity chosen CV.

heading: 2.10 Practical Tips
text: Unbiasedness mathematical convenience, goal. MSE criterion generally prefers biased estimators high-dimensional low-\(n\) settings.Bias reported “bias = \(E[\hat{\theta}] - \theta\)”; authors flip sign (especially MCMC diagnostics), read definitions carefully.regression, predictions single input can decomposed bias variance terms; decomposition motivates regularisation.Shrinkage estimators (ridge, Bayesian posterior mean, empirical Bayes) explicitly trade small bias larger variance reduction. visible MSE comparison.simulating bias/variance inform methodology choice, use many replicates (10 000+); small-reps estimates bias noisy.

heading: 2.13 Introduction
text: Asymptotic statistics constantly deals sequences “approximately” given size \(n \\infty\). Big-\(O\) little-\(o\) notation calculus handle deterministic sequences; stochastic counterparts – big-\(O_p\) little-\(o_p\) – handle random ones. symbols compress pages tedious bookkeeping letters essential reading modern statistics paper.

heading: 2.14 Prerequisites
text: reader understand convergence probability deterministic big-\(O\), little-\(o\) notation.

heading: 2.15 Theory
text: Big-\(O_p\) (stochastic boundedness). sequence \(Y_n\) \(O_p(r_n)\) every \(\varepsilon > 0\) exist \(M, N\) \[P(|Y_n / r_n| \leq M) \geq 1 - \varepsilon \quad \text{} n \geq N.\]Equivalently, \(Y_n / r_n\) stochastically bounded – grow without bound probability.Little-\(o_p\) (negligibility). sequence \(Y_n\) \(o_p(r_n)\) \(Y_n / r_n \xrightarrow{P} 0\).Standard asymptotic rates.\(\bar{X}_n - \mu = O_p(n^{-1/2})\) CLT.\(\hat{\sigma}_n^2 - \sigma^2 = O_p(n^{-1/2})\) similarly.\(\hat{\theta}_n\) consistent, \(\hat{\theta}_n - \theta = o_p(1)\).regression, \(\hat{\beta}_n - \beta = O_p(n^{-1/2})\) OLS standard conditions.Algebraic rules.\(O_p(1) + O_p(1) = O_p(1)\) (sum bounded remains bounded).\(o_p(1) + o_p(1) = o_p(1)\).\(O_p(a_n) \cdot O_p(b_n) = O_p(a_n b_n)\).\(O_p(1) \cdot o_p(1) = o_p(1)\) (bounded times negligible negligible).rules let us manipulate asymptotic expressions without repeatedly invoking convergence definitions.Delta-method expansion. \(\hat{\theta}_n - \theta = O_p(n^{-1/2})\) \(g\) smooth,\[g(\hat{\theta}_n) - g(\theta) = g'(\theta)(\hat{\theta}_n - \theta) + O_p(n^{-1}).\]leading term delta method keeps; \(O_p(n^{-1})\) remainder negligible compared \(O_p(n^{-1/2})\) leading term, asymptotic distribution determined first-order expansion alone.

heading: 2.16 Assumptions
text: Stochastic order symbols defined usual probability-space setup; concepts distributional, almost-sure, work even pointwise behaviour complicated.

heading: 2.17 R Implementation
text: check empirically \(\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_n - \mu)\) stochastically bounded: mean absolute value stabilise \(n\) grows.
code:
set.seed(2026)

n_vals <- round(10^seq(1, 4, by = 0.5))
reps <- 1000

sim <- sapply(n_vals, function(n) {
  dev <- replicate(reps, abs(mean(rnorm(n)) - 0))
  mean(dev * sqrt(n))
})

# If xbar - mu = O_p(n^-1/2), sqrt(n) * (xbar - mu) should be bounded
data.frame(n = n_vals, mean_abs_sqrt_n = sim)

heading: 2.18 Output & Results
text: product stabilises near \(\sqrt{2/\pi} \approx 0.798\), mean absolute value standard normal – exactly \(O_p(n^{-1/2})\) rate predicts.

heading: 2.19 Interpretation
text: paper writes “estimator \(\sqrt{n}\)-consistent” “error \(O_p(n^{-1/2})\)”, making formal statement rate estimator approaches truth. Different estimators can different rates (parametric vs. non-parametric, standard vs. super-efficient), rates directly affect sample-size planning.

heading: 2.20 Practical Tips
text: \(\sqrt{n}\)-consistent gold standard parametric estimators; non-parametric estimators often slower rates like \(n^{-1/3}\) \(n^{-2/5}\).bias-variance decompositions, knowing rate term tells dominates sample size.leading term Taylor expansion \(O_p(n^{-1/2})\) remainder \(O_p(n^{-1})\), expansion consistent delta method applies.confuse “rate” “variance” – rate magnitude random variable, specific distribution.complex estimators (profile likelihood, semi-parametric), establishing \(O_p\) rate often first technical step asymptotic derivation.

heading: 2.23 Introduction
text: Cauchy-Schwarz inequality single useful inequality applied mathematics. probability bounds covariance product standard deviations, giving well-known result correlation coefficient lies \([-1, 1]\). also drives Cramer-Rao lower bound, Jensen’s inequality Hilbert space, numerous regression identities.

heading: 2.24 Prerequisites
text: reader comfortable expectations, variances, covariances, elementary algebra.

heading: 2.25 Theory
text: Cauchy-Schwarz expectations. random variables \(X, Y\) finite second moments,\[|E[XY]| \leq \sqrt{E[X^2] \, E[Y^2]}.\]Equality holds \(Y = aX\) constant \(\) almost surely (.e., \(X, Y\) linearly dependent).Corollary covariances. Applied \(X - \mu_X\) \(Y - \mu_Y\),\[|\mathrm{Cov}(X, Y)| \leq \sigma_X \sigma_Y,\]equivalently, \(|\rho_{XY}| \leq 1\). Pearson correlation bounded \(-1\) \(+1\).Inner-product form. Hilbert space \(H\) inner product \(\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle\), every pair \(u, v \H\),\[|\langle u, v \rangle| \leq \|u\| \cdot \|v\|.\]expectations form arises special case \(\langle X, Y \rangle = E[XY]\).Applications.Cauchy-Schwarz bound Cramer-Rao lower bound relates variance estimator Fisher information.linear regression, \(R^2\) bounded 1 equals squared correlation; Cauchy-Schwarz guarantees .information theory, Cauchy-Schwarz bound correlations implies bound \(|(X; Y)| \leq \min(H(X), H(Y))\) mutual information.Cauchy-Schwarz provides one line Holder inequality generalisation: \(|E[XY]| \leq (E|X|^p)^{1/p} (E|Y|^q)^{1/q}\) \(1/p + 1/q = 1\).

heading: 2.26 Assumptions
text: Cauchy-Schwarz requires second moments \(X\) \(Y\) finite. \(E[X^2]\) infinite, inequality trivial (\(|E[XY]|\) may even defined).

heading: 2.27 R Implementation
text: generate data varying noise levels compute covariance, product SDs, Pearson correlation.
code:
set.seed(2026)
n <- 500
x <- rnorm(n)
noise_levels <- c(0, 0.5, 1, 2, 5)

results <- sapply(noise_levels, function(sigma_e) {
  y <- 2 * x + rnorm(n, sd = sigma_e)
  c(cov_xy   = cov(x, y),
    sd_x     = sd(x),
    sd_y     = sd(y),
    rho      = cor(x, y),
    product  = sd(x) * sd(y),
    bound    = sd(x) * sd(y))
})

t(results)

heading: 2.28 Output & Results
text: covariance bounded product SDs every row; ratio rho = covariance / (product SDs) lies \([-1, 1]\). \(\sigma_e = 0\), \(Y\) deterministic linear function \(X\) Cauchy-Schwarz holds equality.

heading: 2.29 Interpretation
text: Cauchy-Schwarz rarely invoked name applied papers, conclusions appear everywhere: correlations bounded 1, \(R^2\) bounded 1, regression slopes expressible terms correlations SDs. see bounds respected “free” software output, Cauchy-Schwarz ensuring hold algebraically.

heading: 2.30 Practical Tips
text: correlation value exceeds 1 output, bug – Cauchy-Schwarz forbids . Look : division wrong quantity, incorrect standard-error calculation, numerical instability.inequality becomes tight (equality) exactly variables linearly dependent; near-equality signals near-collinearity, matters multicollinearity diagnostics.discrete sums, \((\sum a_i b_i)^2 \leq (\sum a_i^2)(\sum b_i^2)\) Cauchy-Schwarz form used sum--squares decompositions R^2 calculation.Extensions like Holder inequality cover cases Cauchy-Schwarz quite enough (heavier-tailed variables); consider second moments fail.Hilbert space (function spaces, sequence spaces), inequality drives theory least-squares projection Fourier series.

heading: 2.33 Introduction
text: central limit theorem (CLT) single result makes classical statistical inference work. says draw samples size \(n\) population mean \(\mu\) finite variance \(\sigma^2\), distribution sample mean approaches normal distribution mean \(\mu\) variance \(\sigma^2/n\) \(n\) grows – true regardless shape original population. Almost every t-test, z-test, confidence interval mean rests theorem. Without , normal distribution privileged role; , normal attractor toward sample means pulled.

heading: 2.34 Prerequisites
text: reader comfortable notions random variable, mean variance, distinction population parameter sample statistic. Familiarity R’s vectorised operations helpful required.

heading: 2.35 Theory
text: Let \(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\) independent, identically distributed random variables finite mean \(\mu\) finite variance \(\sigma^2\). Define sample mean \(\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^n X_i\). CLT states \(n \\infty\),\[\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_n - \mu) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2),\]equivalently, standardised statistic \(Z_n = (\bar{X}_n - \mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt{n})\) converges distribution standard normal. practical terms, sampling distribution \(\bar{X}_n\) approximately \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2/n)\) \(n\) “large enough”. counts large enough depends skewness source: uniform distribution nearly normal \(n \approx 5\), right-skewed distribution like exponential needs \(n \approx 30\), heavy-tailed distribution like Cauchy (finite mean) never approximated CLT assumptions fail.

heading: 2.36 Assumptions
text: theorem requires three conditions:Observations independent .Observations identically distributed – come population.population finite variance.Independence can violated time-series data clustered sampling; identical distribution fails confounding; finite variance fails heavy-tailed generators like Cauchy power-law distributions tail exponent two.

heading: 2.37 R Implementation
text: clearest way see CLT simulation. draw many samples skewed population, compute mean , plot histogram means.source distribution exponential rate 1, mean 1 variance 1. \(n = 5\) histogram still visibly right-skewed; \(n = 30\) symmetric bell-shaped; \(n = 100\) indistinguishable theoretical normal curve.
code:
library(ggplot2)
set.seed(2026)

simulate_clt <- function(n, reps = 10000, rate = 1) {
  replicate(reps, mean(rexp(n, rate = rate)))
}

means_n5   <- simulate_clt(n = 5)
means_n30  <- simulate_clt(n = 30)
means_n100 <- simulate_clt(n = 100)

df <- data.frame(
  mean_value = c(means_n5, means_n30, means_n100),
  n = factor(rep(c(5, 30, 100), each = 10000),
             levels = c(5, 30, 100))
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = mean_value)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 bins = 60, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~ n, scales = "free",
             labeller = labeller(n = function(x) paste0("n = ", x))) +
  stat_function(fun = dnorm,
                args = list(mean = 1, sd = 1 / sqrt(as.numeric(levels(df$n)[1]))),
                colour = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(x = expression(bar(X)), y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

heading: 2.38 Output & Results
text: Running simulation produces three panels. empirical standard deviation sample means panel close \(1/\sqrt{n}\): 0.447 \(n = 5\), 0.183 \(n = 30\), 0.100 \(n = 100\). empirical mean close 1 every panel, CLT requires.

heading: 2.39 Interpretation

heading: 2.43 Introduction
text: characteristic function Fourier transform probability distribution. Unlike moment generating function, characteristic function always exists – even distributions without finite moments, like Cauchy. Characteristic functions provide cleanest proofs classical limit theorems (CLT, LLN) elegant derivations closure properties distributional families.

heading: 2.44 Prerequisites
text: reader know complex numbers, elementary calculus, concept expectation.

heading: 2.45 Theory
text: random variable \(X\), characteristic function \[\varphi_X(t) = E[e^{itX}] = E[\cos(tX)] + E[\sin(tX)], \qquad t \\mathbb{R}.\]\(|e^{itX}| = 1\), expectation always finite; characteristic function defined every real \(t\) every distribution.Properties.\(\varphi_X(0) = 1\) \(|\varphi_X(t)| \leq 1\) \(t\).\(\varphi_{aX + b}(t) = e^{itb} \varphi_X()\).independent \(X, Y\): \(\varphi_{X + Y}(t) = \varphi_X(t) \varphi_Y(t)\).Uniqueness: two random variables characteristic function distribution.Inversion: density (exists) can recovered \(\varphi\) inverse Fourier transform.Derivatives: \(E[|X|^k] < \infty\), \(\varphi_X^{(k)}(0) = ^k E[X^k]\).Continuity theorem (Levy). \(X_n \xrightarrow{d} X\) \(\varphi_{X_n}(t) \\varphi_X(t)\) every \(t\), provided \(\varphi_X\) continuous 0. theorem provides shortest proof CLT: show characteristic function standardised mean converges \(e^{-t^2/2}\), characteristic function standard normal.Examples.Normal \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\): \(\varphi(t) = \exp(\mu t - \sigma^2 t^2/2)\).Cauchy: \(\varphi(t) = e^{-|t|}\). (MGF exist; CF .)Poisson\((\lambda)\): \(\varphi(t) = \exp[\lambda(e^{} - 1)]\).Exponential\((\lambda)\): \(\varphi(t) = \lambda / (\lambda - )\).

heading: 2.46 Assumptions
text: Characteristic functions require moments distributional regularity – just \(X\) random variable probability space. generality chief virtue.

heading: 2.47 R Implementation
text: estimate characteristic function Cauchy distribution sample compare known form \(e^{-|t|}\).
code:
set.seed(2026)
n <- 1e5

# Empirical characteristic function of samples from a Cauchy
x <- rcauchy(n)
t_vals <- seq(-3, 3, length.out = 61)
cf_empirical <- sapply(t_vals,
                       function(t) mean(exp(1i * t * x)))

cf_theoretical <- exp(-abs(t_vals))

data.frame(t          = t_vals,
           theoretical = cf_theoretical,
           Re_empir    = Re(cf_empirical),
           Im_empir    = Im(cf_empirical))[seq(1, 61, by = 10), ]

heading: 2.48 Output & Results
text: empirical CF closely matches theoretical \(e^{-|t|}\); imaginary part near zero Cauchy distribution symmetric.

heading: 2.49 Interpretation
text: Characteristic functions rarely used directly applied practitioners appear everywhere theoretical proofs. canonical tool proving sequence distributions converges specific limit.

heading: 2.53 Introduction
text: Markov’s Chebyshev’s inequalities basic tail bounds probability theory. give universal (distribution-free) upper bounds probability random variable exceeds value, using first moment (Markov) first two moments (Chebyshev). rarely tightest bounds given problem, generality makes reliable first step every probabilistic argument.

heading: 2.54 Prerequisites
text: reader know expectation elementary probability manipulation.

heading: 2.55 Theory
text:
Markov’s inequality. non-negative random variable \(X\) \(t > 0\),\[P(X \geq t) \leq \frac{E[X]}{t}.\]Proof: \(E[X] \geq t \cdot P(X \geq t)\) \(X\) non-negative. Rearranging gives inequality. bound tight two-point distributions supported \(\{0, t\}\).Chebyshev’s inequality. random variable \(X\) finite variance \(\sigma^2\) mean \(\mu\),\[P(|X - \mu| \geq k\sigma) \leq \frac{1}{k^2}.\]Proof: apply Markov non-negative random variable \((X - \mu)^2\) threshold \(k^2 \sigma^2\).Consequences.Weak LLN Chebyshev. \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) iid variance \(\sigma^2\), \(\mathrm{Var}(\bar{X}_n) = \sigma^2/n\), Chebyshev,
\[P(|\bar{X}_n - \mu| \geq \varepsilon) \leq \frac{\sigma^2}{n \varepsilon^2} \0.\]
simplest proof WLLN.Weak LLN Chebyshev. \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) iid variance \(\sigma^2\), \(\mathrm{Var}(\bar{X}_n) = \sigma^2/n\), Chebyshev,
\[P(|\bar{X}_n - \mu| \geq \varepsilon) \leq \frac{\sigma^2}{n \varepsilon^2} \0.\]
simplest proof WLLN.Chernoff’s bound. Applying Markov \(e^{tX}\) gives \(P(X \geq ) \leq e^{-ta} E[e^{tX}]\), yielding Chernoff bound optimised \(t\). typically far tighter Markov Chebyshev light-tailed \(X\).Chernoff’s bound. Applying Markov \(e^{tX}\) gives \(P(X \geq ) \leq e^{-ta} E[e^{tX}]\), yielding Chernoff bound optimised \(t\). typically far tighter Markov Chebyshev light-tailed \(X\).Markov Chebyshev bounds generally loose: normally distributed \(X\), true \(P(|X - \mu| > 3\sigma) \approx 0.003\), Chebyshev guarantees \(\leq 1/9 \approx 0.111\). power comes requiring distributional assumptions.

heading: 2.56 Assumptions
text: Markov requires non-negativity \(X\) finite first moment. Chebyshev requires finite variance. Neither requires specific distributional form – beauty exactly generality.

heading: 2.57 R Implementation
text: compare empirical tail probability, Chebyshev bound \(1/k^2\), true normal tail probability several \(k\).
code:
set.seed(2026)

x <- rnorm(1e6, mean = 0, sd = 1)

k_values <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)

empirical  <- sapply(k_values, function(k) mean(abs(x) >= k))
chebyshev  <- 1 / k_values^2
normal_true <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(k_values))

comparison <- cbind(
  k        = k_values,
  empirical = empirical,
  chebyshev = chebyshev,
  normal_exact = normal_true
)
comparison

heading: 2.58 Output & Results
text: Chebyshev’s bound holds universally gross overstatement normal data, true tail probability 4 SDs one ten-thousandth, vs. Chebyshev’s guarantee one sixteenth.

heading: 2.59 Interpretation
text: Markov Chebyshev bounds shortest proofs probability theory. rarely give sharp answers, reliable first-pass sanity checks: “1/9 observations can 3 SDs mean, regardless distribution”. can surprisingly useful universal statement.

heading: 2.60 Practical Tips

Simple widely used; coverage depends close sampling distribution normal.Normal approximation: asymptotically normal estimators, \(\hat{\theta} \pm z_{1-\alpha/2} \widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\hat{\theta})\). heading: 2.66 Assumptions
text: construction assumptions. t-interval requires normal data large enough sample CLT. Wald intervals require asymptotic normality \(\hat{\theta}\) well-estimated SE. Bootstrap intervals require exchangeable observations inherit sampling design.

heading: 2.67 R Implementation
text: compute t-interval, Wald (normal-approximation) interval, two bootstrap intervals sample mean.
code:
library(boot)

set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(40, mean = 75, sd = 12)

t.test(x)$conf.int

n <- length(x)
xbar <- mean(x); s <- sd(x)
xbar + c(-1, 1) * qnorm(0.975) * s / sqrt(n)

boot_mean <- function(d, i) mean(d[i])
b <- boot(x, boot_mean, R = 5000)
boot.ci(b, type = c("perc", "bca"))

[1] 71.51 78.51   # Wald

heading: 2.73 Introduction
text: Convergence distribution (also called weak convergence) mode convergence underlies central limit theorem. Unlike convergence probability, says random variables get close, convergence distribution says probability distributions get close. weakest useful mode convergence classical probability theory one defines every “asymptotic distribution” quoted applied statistics.

heading: 2.74 Prerequisites
text: reader know CDF seen central limit theorem least .

heading: 2.75 Theory
text: sequence \(Y_n\) converges distribution \(Y\), written \(Y_n \xrightarrow{d} Y\), \[\lim_{n \\infty} F_{Y_n}(y) = F_Y(y)\]every continuity point \(y\) \(F_Y\). Equivalently, \(E[g(Y_n)] \E[g(Y)]\) every bounded continuous \(g\) (“portmanteau” characterisation).Key examples.central limit theorem: \(\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_n - \mu)/\sigma \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\).Binomial Poisson: \(\text{Binomial}(n, \lambda/n) \xrightarrow{d} \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\).Sample maxima uniform variables: \(n(1 - \max X_i / \theta) \xrightarrow{d} \text{Exponential}(1)\) \(X_i \sim \text{Uniform}(0, \theta)\).Relationships.Convergence probability implies convergence distribution.Convergence distribution constant implies convergence probability.Neither implies \(E[Y_n] \E[Y]\) unless additional integrability holds.Slutsky’s theorem: \(Y_n \xrightarrow{d} Y\) \(Z_n \xrightarrow{P} c\) (constant), \(Y_n + Z_n \xrightarrow{d} Y + c\) \(Y_n Z_n \xrightarrow{d} cY\). tool lets us substitute consistent estimators asymptotic formulas without changing limit.Delta method: \(\sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta} - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\) \(g\) differentiable \(\theta\) \(g'(\theta) \neq 0\), \[\sqrt{n}(g(\hat{\theta}) - g(\theta)) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, [g'(\theta)]^2 \sigma^2).\]

heading: 2.76 Assumptions
text: Convergence distribution defined sequence values metric space; classical case, real-valued, CDF every \(n\). CLT additionally requires iid observations finite variance.

heading: 2.77 R Implementation
text: standardise sample mean exponential draws; CLT, distribution approximate standard normal \(n\) grows.
code:
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 30

xbars <- replicate(5000, {
  x <- rexp(n, rate = 1)
  (mean(x) - 1) / (1 / sqrt(n))
})

df <- data.frame(z = xbars)
ggplot(df, aes(x = z)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 60,
                 fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  stat_function(fun = dnorm, colour = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(x = "Standardised sample mean",
       y = "Density",
       title = "CLT in action: exponential source, n = 30") +
  theme_minimal()

library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
mu <- 5

epsilon <- 0.1
n_vals <- round(10^seq(1, 5, by = 0.25))

prob_far <- sapply(n_vals, function(n) {
  mean(replicate(2000, abs(mean(rnorm(n, mu, 3)) - mu) > epsilon))
})

ggplot(data.frame(n = n_vals, p = prob_far),
       aes(x = n, y = p)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue") + geom_point() +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(x = "n (log scale)",
       y = expression(P(abs(bar(X)[n] - mu) > epsilon)),
       title = paste0("Empirical P(|xbar - mu| > ", epsilon, ")")) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 2.93 Introduction
text: Cramer-Rao lower bound (CRLB) theoretical limit: unbiased estimator parameter can smaller variance value determined Fisher information. Estimators attain bound called efficient; MLE reaches bound asymptotically regular models. CRLB lets us talk “using data well possible” formal, computable sense.

heading: 2.94 Prerequisites
text: reader understand Fisher information, notion unbiased estimator, elementary linear algebra multivariate version.

heading: 2.95 Theory
text: regular parametric family Fisher information \((\theta)\), unbiased estimator \(\hat{\theta}\) \(\theta\) satisfies\[\mathrm{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{n (\theta)}.\]scalar function \(g(\theta)\) estimated unbiased \(\hat{g}\),\[\mathrm{Var}(\hat{g}) \geq \frac{[g'(\theta)]^2}{n (\theta)}.\]multivariate case parameter \(\theta \\mathbb{R}^p\) unbiased estimator \(\hat{\theta}\),\[\mathrm{Cov}(\hat{\theta}) \succeq \frac{1}{n} (\theta)^{-1},\]meaning difference \(\mathrm{Cov}(\hat{\theta}) - \frac{1}{n} (\theta)^{-1}\) positive semi-definite.estimator attains CRLB efficient; variance equals inverse information. Maximum likelihood estimators general efficient finite samples asymptotically efficient: variance approaches CRLB \(n \\infty\).CRLB also used optimal design: choosing design (sample allocation, predictor values) maximise \((\theta)\), one minimises achievable variance unbiased estimator.

heading: 2.96 Assumptions
text: CRLB applies regular parametric families: smooth densities, identifiable parameters, finite information. non-regular problems (uniform unknown endpoint, step functions), different bounds apply MLE can outperform “CRLB” computed naively.

heading: 2.97 R Implementation
text: compare empirical variance two variance estimators (MLE unbiased \(n - 1\) estimator) CRLB \(2\sigma^4/n\) across range sample sizes.
code:
set.seed(2026)

n_values <- c(20, 50, 200, 1000)
true_sigma2 <- 4
reps <- 5000

crlb_sigma2 <- function(sigma2, n) 2 * sigma2^2 / n

compare <- function(n) {
  mle <- replicate(reps, {
    x <- rnorm(n, 0, sqrt(true_sigma2))
    mean((x - mean(x))^2)
  })
  ubv <- replicate(reps, var(rnorm(n, 0, sqrt(true_sigma2))))
  c(n = n,
    var_mle = var(mle), var_ubv = var(ubv),
    crlb    = crlb_sigma2(true_sigma2, n))
}

do.call(rbind, lapply(n_values, compare))

heading: 2.98 Output & Results
text: \(n\) grows, estimators approach CRLB; unbiased estimator’s variance always slightly larger MLE’s (slightly biased), converge bound.

heading: 2.99 Interpretation
text: reader methods paper claims estimator “efficient”, CRLB benchmark. Journals require showing attainment CRLB, simulation studies compare estimators, bound natural reference line. point well CRLB indicates room improvement; point near bound indicates gains possible without bias.

heading: 2.107 R Implementation
text: msm::deltamethod() function computes delta-method SE symbolically formula.
code:
library(msm)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
x <- rnorm(n, mean = 5, sd = 2)
theta_hat <- mean(x)
se_theta  <- sd(x) / sqrt(n)

g  <- function(t) t^2
gp <- function(t) 2 * t

g_hat <- g(theta_hat)
se_g  <- abs(gp(theta_hat)) * se_theta
c(estimate = g_hat, se = se_g)

deltamethod(~ x1^2, mean = theta_hat, cov = se_theta^2)

heading: 2.108 Output & Results
text: two approaches agree: SE \(\hat{\theta}^2\) 1.41, computed either hand deltamethod().

heading: 2.109 Interpretation
text: practice, regression outputs report coefficients scale SE available (log odds, log rate), researchers need effects original scale. Delta method standard tool translation. 95% CI odds ratio usually constructed \(\exp(\hat{\beta} \pm 1.96 \widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\hat{\beta}))\), delta method log scale combined back-transformation – often accurate applying delta method directly .

heading: 2.110 Practical Tips

heading: 2.113 Introduction
text: empirical distribution function (ECDF) simplest possible non-parametric estimator probability distribution: puts mass \(1/n\) observation returns resulting step-function CDF. Despite simplicity, ECDF consistent uniformly true CDF (Glivenko-Cantelli), tight universal error bound (Dvoretzky-Kiefer-Wolfowitz), underlies every non-parametric statistic built ranks percentiles.

heading: 2.114 Prerequisites
text: reader know CDF able sort vector R.

heading: 2.115 Theory
text: iid observations \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) CDF \(F\), ECDF \[\hat{F}_n(x) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^n \mathbb{1}\{X_i \leq x\}.\]Properties fixed \(x\):\(n \hat{F}_n(x) \sim \text{Binomial}(n, F(x))\), \(E[\hat{F}_n(x)] = F(x)\) \(\mathrm{Var}[\hat{F}_n(x)] = F(x)(1 - F(x))/n\).CLT, \(\sqrt{n}(\hat{F}_n(x) - F(x)) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, F(x)(1 - F(x)))\).Dvoretzky-Kiefer-Wolfowitz (DKW) inequality. every \(n\) every \(\varepsilon > 0\),\[P\!\left(\sup_x |\hat{F}_n(x) - F(x)| > \varepsilon\right) \leq 2 e^{-2 n \varepsilon^2}.\]uniform bound – controls supremum \(x\) – holds every \(F\). underwrites non-parametric confidence bands entire CDF, just pointwise.Sup-norm CI. Setting DKW bound equal \(\alpha\) solving \(\varepsilon\) gives \(1 - \alpha\) confidence band form \([\hat{F}_n(x) - \varepsilon, \hat{F}_n(x) + \varepsilon]\) \(x\) simultaneously.

heading: 2.116 Assumptions
text: ECDF defined distribution; estimator minimal assumptions – iid sampling (exchangeability). Dependent data complicate variance point estimator.

heading: 2.117 R Implementation
text: plot overlays ECDF, DKW 95% band, true CDF. \(n = 100\) \(\alpha = 0.05\), band width \(\varepsilon = \sqrt{\log(40)/200} \approx 0.136\) – constant across \(x\).
code:
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 100
alpha <- 0.05
x <- rnorm(n)

Fn <- ecdf(x)
eps <- sqrt(log(2 / alpha) / (2 * n))

grid <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 1001)
df <- data.frame(
  x      = grid,
  Fhat   = Fn(grid),
  lower  = pmax(0, Fn(grid) - eps),
  upper  = pmin(1, Fn(grid) + eps),
  Ftrue  = pnorm(grid)
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_step(aes(y = Fhat), colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower, ymax = upper), alpha = 0.2,
              fill = "steelblue") +
  geom_line(aes(y = Ftrue), colour = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(y = "CDF",
       title = sprintf("ECDF with 95%% DKW band (n = %d)", n)) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 2.118 Output & Results
text: ECDF traces true CDF closely; DKW band contains every \(x\), expected since DKW bound universal. Increasing \(n\) 1000 shrinks band \(\varepsilon \approx 0.043\).

heading: 2.119 Interpretation
text: ECDF omnibus, assumption-light summary data. Reporting plot ECDF DKW bands sensible way communicate distribution’s shape parametric form assumed. Quantile reporting, K-S tests, many non-parametric procedures ultimately derive ECDF.

heading: 2.120 Practical Tips
text: small samples (\(n < 30\)), DKW band wide; report pointwise Clopper-Pearson Wilson CIs instead need uncertainty specific quantiles.ecdf() function R returns function; apply vector \(x\) values get empirical probabilities.Smooth variants (kernel-based) exist, ECDF’s simplicity feature: non-parametric MLE sufficient statistic non-parametric model.DKW constant 2 tight (Massart 1990); older texts quote larger constants.ECDF foundation quantile plots, Q-Q plots, goodness--fit statistics like Kolmogorov-Smirnov Cramer-von Mises.

heading: 2.123 Introduction
text: estimator rule – function data – produces guess unknown parameter population. estimate value rule returns specific sample. sample mean estimator population mean; sample \(\{3.1, 2.7, 3.4\}\) returns estimate \(3.07\). Every inferential procedure begins estimator.

heading: 2.124 Prerequisites

heading: 2.125 Theory
text: Given iid observations \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) distribution \(F\) parameter \(\theta\), estimator function \(\hat{\theta} = T(X_1, \ldots, X_n)\). Common examples:Sample mean \(\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i\) population mean \(\mu\).Sample variance \(s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2\) \(\sigma^2\).Sample proportion \(\hat{p} = \bar{X}\) \(\pi\) \(X_i \\{0, 1\}\).Sample correlation \(\hat{\rho}\) \(\rho\).plug-principle motivates many estimators: estimate functional \(\theta = T(F)\) distribution, replace \(F\) empirical distribution \(\hat{F}_n\) compute \(T(\hat{F}_n)\). principle yields sample mean estimator population mean, sample quantile estimator population quantile, .Properties estimators judged:Unbiasedness: \(E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta\).Consistency: \(\hat{\theta} \\theta\) probability \(n \\infty\).Efficiency: small variance (ideally attaining Cramer-Rao lower bound).Robustness: stability deviations modelling assumptions.Sufficiency: using information \(\theta\) data.General-purpose construction methods – method moments, maximum likelihood, least squares, Bayesian posterior summaries – yield estimators characteristic properties model assume.

heading: 2.126 Assumptions

heading: 2.127 R Implementation

set.seed(2026)

mean(x)
var(x); sd(x)
median(x); quantile(x, probs = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75))

heading: 2.133 Introduction
text: Fisher information numerical summary much information data sample carries unknown parameter. likelihood sharply peaked, small changes parameter produce big changes data’s probability, data tell us lot parameter – information high. likelihood flat, data nearly indifferent parameter, information low. intuitive idea turns precise quantity drives asymptotic theory maximum likelihood.

heading: 2.134 Prerequisites
text: reader comfortable calculus, likelihood function, notion taking expectation.

heading: 2.135 Theory
text: parametric density \(f(x \mid \theta)\), score function gradient log-likelihood single observation:\[U(\theta; x) = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \log f(x \mid \theta).\]expected value true parameter zero; variance Fisher information:\[(\theta) = \mathrm{Var}[U(\theta; X)] = E\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial \log f}{\partial \theta}\right)^2\right].\]sufficient regularity, equals expected negative curvature log-likelihood:\[(\theta) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \log f}{\partial \theta^2}\right].\]\(n\) iid observations, \(I_n(\theta) = n (\theta)\) – information additive across independent observations. observed information replaces expectation empirical curvature MLE:\[\hat{\mathcal{}}(\hat{\theta}) = -\frac{\partial^2 \ell(\theta)}{\partial \theta \partial \theta^\top}\bigg|_{\theta = \hat{\theta}}.\]inverse asymptotic covariance MLE, inverse diagonal squared standard-error estimates reported every maximum-likelihood fit.Fisher information cornerstone Cramer-Rao lower bound, asymptotic efficiency MLE, optimal experimental design (design maximises determinant information matrix).

heading: 2.136 Assumptions
text: classical definition curvature-based identity require smoothness log-density \(\theta\) ability interchange differentiation integration. Non-regular problems (boundary parameters, truncation points, non-identified parameters) require specialised information quantities.

heading: 2.137 R Implementation
text: fit normal model ML, extract observed information Hessian, compute expected information closed form, recover standard errors inverting.
code:
set.seed(2026)

n <- 200
x <- rnorm(n, mean = 5, sd = 2)

nll <- function(par) {
  mu <- par[1]; sigma <- par[2]
  if (sigma <= 0) return(Inf)
  -sum(dnorm(x, mean = mu, sd = sigma, log = TRUE))
}

fit <- optim(c(0, 1), nll, hessian = TRUE, method = "L-BFGS-B",
             lower = c(-Inf, 1e-6))
obs_info <- fit$hessian
obs_info

exp_info <- matrix(c(n / fit$par[2]^2, 0,
                     0, 2 * n / fit$par[2]^2), 2, 2)
exp_info

se <- sqrt(diag(solve(obs_info)))
se

heading: 2.138 Output & Results
text: Observed expected information agree within simulation noise. inverse-information standard errors match R’s summary.lm() summary.glm() report parameters.

heading: 2.139 Interpretation
text: Information turns data precision. Reporting standard errors maximum-likelihood fit amounts reporting inverse-square-root diagonal information matrix. planning study, maximising information per unit cost (D-optimality, -optimality, -optimality optimal design) mathematical expression “get much precision possible resources available”.

heading: 2.143 Introduction
text: Glivenko-Cantelli theorem sometimes called “fundamental theorem statistics”. says empirical distribution function converges uniformly, almost surely, true CDF sample size grows. stronger guarantee pointwise convergence every \(x\): says worst-case error \(x\) vanishes. Every non-parametric inference built ECDF rests theorem.

heading: 2.144 Prerequisites
text: reader know CDF , ECDF , difference pointwise uniform convergence.

heading: 2.145 Theory
text: Let \(X_1, X_2, \ldots\) iid CDF \(F\), let \(\hat{F}_n\) denote ECDF computed first \(n\) observations. Glivenko-Cantelli theorem states \[\sup_{x \\mathbb{R}} |\hat{F}_n(x) - F(x)| \xrightarrow{.s.} 0 \quad \text{} n \\infty.\]Equivalently, supremum pointwise error – sup-norm error – goes zero almost surely.uniform convergence matters. strong LLN gives \(\hat{F}_n(x) \F(x)\) almost surely fixed \(x\). Borel-Cantelli-style argument needed upgrade uniform statement: \(n\) works every \(x\) simultaneously. non-parametric statistics involve integral supremum \(x\) (like Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic), uniform convergence exactly needed.Generalisations. Glivenko-Cantelli classes extend result richer families events \(\{\mathcal{}_i\}\) whose empirical probabilities converge true probabilities uniformly class. starting point modern empirical process theory Vapnik-Chervonenkis machinery used statistical learning.DKW inequality (finite-sample refinement) gives explicit rate: sup-norm error drops rate \(O_P(1/\sqrt{n})\).

heading: 2.146 Assumptions
text: classical theorem requires iid sampling. Extensions stationary ergodic sequences hold, though rate uniform convergence can slower.

heading: 2.147 R Implementation
text: \(n\), compute sup-norm error \(\sup_x |\hat{F}_n(x) - F(x)|\) fine grid average across replications.
code:
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
n_vals <- round(10^seq(1, 4, by = 0.25))

compute_sup <- function(n) {
  x <- rnorm(n)
  Fn <- ecdf(x)
  grid <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 2001)
  max(abs(Fn(grid) - pnorm(grid)))
}

reps <- 200
sup_errors <- sapply(n_vals, function(n) {
  mean(replicate(reps, compute_sup(n)))
})

ggplot(data.frame(n = n_vals, sup_err = sup_errors),
       aes(x = n, y = sup_err)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue") + geom_point() +
  scale_x_log10() + scale_y_log10() +
  labs(x = "n (log scale)",
       y = "Expected sup-norm error (log scale)",
       title = "Glivenko-Cantelli: uniform ECDF error as n grows") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 2.148 Output & Results
text: log-log plot, sup-norm error drops straight line slope approximately \(-1/2\), confirming \(1/\sqrt{n}\) rate predicted DKW. \(n = 10^4\), expected sup-norm error around 0.01; \(n = 100\), around 0.1.

heading: 2.149 Interpretation
text: reader methods paper: Glivenko-Cantelli rarely named, theorem invoked procedure “based ECDF” claimed consistent. K-S goodness--fit, bootstrap consistency, many non-parametric estimators rely theorem proof level.

heading: 2.150 Practical Tips
text: Glivenko-Cantelli pure convergence result; quantitative error bounds, use DKW’s finite-sample version.theorem holds real line; multivariate extension requires class events (rectangles, half-spaces) form Glivenko-Cantelli class, automatic low-dimensional CDFs harder complex function classes.Uniform convergence makes bootstrap validity proofs work; without , bootstrap distribution might close truth \(x\) .uniform rate \(1/\sqrt{n}\) optimal without additional smoothness; smooth CDF, kernel-based smoothings can beat ECDF rate pointwise.Empirical process theory, built Glivenko-Cantelli, right language thinking non-parametric semi-parametric asymptotics.

heading: 2.153 Introduction
text: Jensen’s inequality relates expectation function function expectation: convex function \(g\), \(E[g(X)] \geq g(E[X])\), equality \(X\) constant (\(g\) linear). inequality behind many routine biases applied statistics (bias sample SD sample variance, instance), underpins arithmetic-geometric mean inequality, justifies use log-likelihoods EM algorithms variational inference.

heading: 2.154 Prerequisites
text: reader know convex function (equivalently, concave function ) definition expectation.

heading: 2.155 Theory
text: function \(g: \mathbb{R} \\mathbb{R}\) convex \(, b\) \(\lambda \[0, 1]\),\[g(\lambda + (1 - \lambda) b) \leq \lambda g() + (1 - \lambda) g(b).\]Equivalently, line segment two points graph lies () graph . function strictly convex inequality strict \(\neq b\) \(\lambda \(0, 1)\).Jensen’s inequality. \(g\) convex \(X\) integrable,\[g(E[X]) \leq E[g(X)].\]\(g\) strictly convex \(X\) non-degenerate, inequality strict. concave \(g\), inequality flips: \(g(E[X]) \geq E[g(X)]\).Applications.Log concave: \(E[\log X] \leq \log E[X]\). Taking log average averaging log; difference KL divergence, central information theory.Square convex: \(E[X^2] \geq (E[X])^2\); variance non-negative.Reciprocal: \(E[1/X] \geq 1/E[X]\) positive \(X\). explains sample harmonic mean always \(\leq\) arithmetic mean.Exponential: \(E[e^X] \geq e^{E[X]}\). MGF \(t = 1\) exceeds \(e^{E[X]}\).maximum likelihood via EM, Jensen’s inequality provides lower bound log-likelihood E-step maximises; variational inference, underwrites evidence lower bound (ELBO).

heading: 2.156 Assumptions
text: \(X\) must integrable: \(E[|X|] < \infty\).\(g\) convex (concave). convex \(g\) restricted domain, \(X\) must take values domain almost surely.

heading: 2.157 R Implementation
text: lognormal data, gap \(\log E[X] - E[\log X]\) exactly \(\sigma^2/2\) \(\sigma\) lognormal scale. simulation confirms.
code:
set.seed(2026)
n <- 10000
x <- rlnorm(n, meanlog = 0, sdlog = 1)

mean_x <- mean(x)
log_of_mean <- log(mean_x)

mean_of_log <- mean(log(x))

c(log_of_mean = log_of_mean,
  mean_of_log = mean_of_log,
  gap         = log_of_mean - mean_of_log)

theoretical_gap <- log(exp(0.5)) - 0   # = 0.5
theoretical_gap

heading: 2.158 Output & Results
text: difference approximately 0.5 – exactly Jensen predicts distribution. gap never negative, inequality requires.
code:

     0.499       -0.002        0.501

[1] 0.5

heading: 2.159 Interpretation
text: Jensen’s inequality explains many estimators biased transformation. example, \(E[\sqrt{s^2}] < \sqrt{E[s^2]}\) square root concave; sample SD biased downward even though \(s^2\) unbiased. Reporting bias matters small-sample variance components estimation.

heading: 2.160 Practical Tips
text: Always check convexity applying Jensen: sign inequality flips concave \(g\).strictly convex \(g\) non-degenerate \(X\), Jensen’s inequality strict; zero gap indicates either \(X\) constant \(g\) effectively linear support \(X\).Jensen’s gap – difference \(E[g(X)] - g(E[X])\) – can sometimes bounded approximated (e.g., \(\frac{g''(\mu) \sigma^2}{2}\) first non-trivial order).geometric mean always \(\leq\) arithmetic mean; direct consequence Jensen applied \(\log\).machine learning, Jensen underlies EM algorithm, variational inference, contrastive divergence, “softmax” trick stable computation.

heading: 2.163 Introduction
text: law large numbers (LLN) formal statement intuition students bring statistics day one: averages settle collect data. precise statement comes two flavours – weak law strong law – consequences flow estimation theory, simulation methodology, machine learning.

heading: 2.164 Prerequisites
text: reader know expectation , sample mean , seen replicate() similar simulation pattern R.

heading: 2.165 Theory
text: Let \(X_1, X_2, \ldots\) iid random variables finite mean \(\mu = E[X_1]\). sample mean \(\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^n X_i\).Weak law (WLLN): every \(\varepsilon > 0\),\[\lim_{n \\infty} P(|\bar{X}_n - \mu| > \varepsilon) = 0.\]words, sample mean eventually within pre-specified distance population mean probability approaching 1. convergence probability: \(\bar{X}_n \xrightarrow{P} \mu\).Strong law (SLLN):\[P\!\left(\lim_{n \\infty} \bar{X}_n = \mu\right) = 1.\]sample mean converges population mean almost surely – almost every realisation infinite sequence, sample mean settles \(\mu\). strictly stronger weak law.laws depend existence \(\mu\). distributions without finite mean (Cauchy, Pareto tail index 1), sample mean converge ; remains wild regardless sample size.LLN generalises beyond means: continuous function sample mean consistent function \(\mu\) (continuous mapping theorem). underwrites plug-principle consistency practical estimators.

heading: 2.166 Assumptions
text: classical LLN requires iid observations finite mean. LLN non-iid sequences (stationary ergodic, martingale-difference, mixing) holds appropriate generalisations requires care.

heading: 2.167 R Implementation
text: Plotting running sample mean \(n\) shows converging \(\mu = 5\); different random seeds produce different paths, converging horizontal line.
code:
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
mu <- 5
sigma <- 3

n_vals <- seq(10, 10000, by = 50)

draws <- rnorm(max(n_vals), mean = mu, sd = sigma)
path <- data.frame(n = 1:length(draws),
                   running_mean = cumsum(draws) / seq_along(draws))

ggplot(path, aes(x = n, y = running_mean)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = mu, linetype = "dashed", colour = "red") +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(x = "n (log scale)",
       y = expression(bar(X)[n]),
       title = "LLN in action: running mean vs. population mean") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 2.168 Output & Results
text: plot shows initial ragged behaviour small \(n\), narrowing steadily \(n\) grows, running mean nested closer closer around true 5. Classical theoretical rates: typical deviation sample size \(n\) scales \(\sigma / \sqrt{n}\) (standard error).

heading: 2.169 Interpretation
text: LLN justifies every Monte Carlo estimate every use sample mean point estimate. paper reports “estimated averaging \(N = 10^5\) simulation replicates” implicitly invoking LLN: average converges population mean , CLT, well-characterised standard error.

heading: 2.170 Practical Tips
text: LLN guarantees convergence says nothing speed – CLT . practice, “large enough \(n\)” depends population’s tail behaviour.Check mean exists trusting LLN. single quiet assumption failure (sampling heavy-tailed population) turns routine simulation misleading result.LLN extends sample variances, proportions, correlations, general functionals – “mean transformed data” consistent.Running-mean plots useful diagnostics simulations: running mean still drifting end simulation, replicates needed.online learning streaming algorithms, LLN underlies convergence stochastic-gradient recursive estimation schemes.

heading: 2.173 Introduction
text: likelihood ratio test (LRT) compares two nested statistical models ratio maximised likelihoods. added parameters larger model help explain data, ratio departs one predictable amount; Wilks’ theorem gives amount asymptotic chi-squared distribution, turning ratio usable p-value. LRTs underlie model-comparison tools throughout GLMs, mixed models, survival analysis, beyond.

heading: 2.174 Prerequisites
text: reader understand maximum likelihood, concept nested models, degrees freedom.

heading: 2.175 Theory
text: Consider two nested parametric models: “null” model \(M_0\) parameter space \(\Theta_0\) larger model \(M_1\) parameter space \(\Theta_1 \supset \Theta_0\). Let \(\hat{\ell}_0 = \ell(\hat{\theta}_0)\) log-likelihood maximised \(\Theta_0\), \(\hat{\ell}_1\) \(\Theta_1\). likelihood ratio statistic \[\Lambda = 2(\hat{\ell}_1 - \hat{\ell}_0).\]Wilks’ theorem states , regularity, \(M_0\) true, \(n \\infty\)\[\Lambda \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_k,\]\(k = \dim \Theta_1 - \dim \Theta_0\) number free parameters added. p-value computed \(1 - F_{\chi^2_k}(\Lambda)\).LRTs generalise Wald score tests often accurate. invariant reparameterisation (unlike Wald tests) good small-sample behaviour regularity holds.Important cases:Testing scalar parameter equals specific value (\(k = 1\)).Testing several parameters equal zero simultaneously (add multiple predictors regression; \(k\) = number added).Testing whole submodel saturated model (goodness--fit).Profile likelihood ratios give confidence intervals: invert LRT candidate parameter value collect values LRT reject.

heading: 2.176 Assumptions
text: Standard Wilks’ theorem requires: () \(M_0\) interior parameter space \(M_1\), (ii) regularity (smooth likelihood, finite Fisher information), (iii) correct model specification \(M_1\). Boundary cases (testing variance = 0 mixed model) require mixture chi-squared null distribution.

heading: 2.177 R Implementation
text: lmtest::lrtest() computes LRT linear models (using variance estimator consistent likelihood); anova(..., test = "Chisq") GLMs.
code:
library(lmtest)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
x <- rnorm(n)
z <- rnorm(n)
y <- 2 + 1.5 * x + 0.8 * z + rnorm(n, sd = 2)

fit0 <- lm(y ~ x)
fit1 <- lm(y ~ x + z)

lrtest(fit0, fit1)

glm0 <- glm(y > 3 ~ x, family = binomial)
glm1 <- glm(y > 3 ~ x + z, family = binomial)
anova(glm0, glm1, test = "Chisq")

heading: 2.178 Output & Results
text: Adding z model increases log-likelihood 9.4, yielding likelihood-ratio statistic 18.85 1 df, highly significant p-value.
code:
Likelihood ratio test

Model 1: y ~ x
Model 2: y ~ x + z
  #Df  LogLik Df  Chisq Pr(>Chisq)
1   3 -429.44
2   4 -420.02  1  18.85 1.42e-05 ***

heading: 2.179 Interpretation
text: manuscript, LRT reported df p-value: “Adding region covariate improved fit significantly (likelihood-ratio \(\chi^2_3 = 12.4\), \(p = 0.006\)).” LRT answers whether extra parameters jointly non-zero population, given nested structure.

heading: 2.180 Practical Tips
text: LRTs require data likelihood models – missing-data differences, different link functions. Software happily compute nonsense careful.mixed models, LRTs random-effects variances test parameter boundary; use lmerTest, RLRsim, parametric bootstrap rather naive chi-squared.Wilks’ chi-squared asymptotic; small samples, consider parametric bootstrap likelihood ratios.LRT saturated model classical goodness--fit test categorical data (\(G\)-statistic); continuous data GLMs, residual deviance plays role.LRTs defined nested models. non-nested comparison, use information criteria (AIC, BIC) Vuong’s test.

heading: 2.183 Introduction
text: Maximum likelihood dominant estimation technique modern statistics. idea: given data parametric model, choose parameter values make observed data probable. intuition supports powerful general method well-understood consistency, efficiency, distributional properties, underpins almost every regression model statistician’s toolkit.

heading: 2.184 Prerequisites
text: reader know probability density , comfortable calculus level derivatives log transforms, able call optim() R.

heading: 2.185 Theory
text: iid data \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) density \(f(x \mid \theta)\), likelihood function \[L(\theta) = \prod_{=1}^n f(X_i \mid \theta),\]log-likelihood \(\ell(\theta) = \sum \log f(X_i \mid \theta)\). MLE \[\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_\theta \ell(\theta).\]\(\ell\) smooth, MLE satisfies score equation \(\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \theta} = 0\). score gradient log-likelihood; expectation true parameter zero.regularity conditions (smoothness, identifiability, interior optimum, finite Fisher information), MLE :Consistent: \(\hat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta_0\).Asymptotically normal: \(\sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta}_n - \theta_0) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, (\theta_0)^{-1})\).Asymptotically efficient: variance attains Cramer-Rao lower bound.Equivariant: one--one transformation \(g\), \(g(\hat{\theta}_{\text{MLE}})\) MLE \(g(\theta)\).Standard errors MLE come inverse observed Fisher information, \(\mathcal{}(\hat{\theta}) = -\partial^2 \ell / \partial \theta \partial \theta^\top\), evaluated \(\hat{\theta}\). Confidence intervals can based normal approximation , better, likelihood ratio, invariant reparameterisation.

heading: 2.186 Assumptions
text: asymptotic theory assumes well-specified model, finite Fisher information, enough smoothness Taylor expansion, parameter interior parameter space. Boundary parameters (variance zero, proportion 0 1) non-identifiable models break standard theory.

heading: 2.187 R Implementation
text: negative log-likelihood minimised optim(); standard errors come inverse Hessian.
code:
set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(200, mean = 5, sd = 2)

nll <- function(par) {
  mu    <- par[1]
  sigma <- par[2]
  if (sigma <= 0) return(Inf)
  -sum(dnorm(x, mean = mu, sd = sigma, log = TRUE))
}

fit <- optim(par = c(0, 1), fn = nll, hessian = TRUE,
             method = "L-BFGS-B", lower = c(-Inf, 1e-6))
mle <- fit$par
se  <- sqrt(diag(solve(fit$hessian)))

data.frame(parameter = c("mu", "sigma"),
           estimate = mle, se = se,
           ci_low = mle - 1.96 * se,
           ci_high = mle + 1.96 * se)

heading: 2.188 Output & Results
text: MLE recovers true parameters (\(\mu = 5\), \(\sigma = 2\)) uncertainty quantified observed-information-based standard errors.
code:
parameter estimate    se  ci_low ci_high
1        mu    5.093 0.143   4.812   5.374
2     sigma    2.022 0.101   1.823   2.221

heading: 2.189 Interpretation
text: manuscript, MLE typically reported “parameters estimated maximum likelihood (Fisher-scoring convergence 1e-8)”, point estimates, standard errors, either Wald profile-likelihood confidence intervals. Information criteria (AIC, BIC) functions maximised likelihood reported alongside.

heading: 2.190 Practical Tips
text: Always work log scale numerical stability; products densities underflow quickly.Start optimiser sensible initial values – method--moments estimates good default.Parameterise parameter space unbounded (log-variance instead variance, logit-probability instead probability); makes optim() easier avoids boundary issues.models many parameters, use nlm(), optim(..., method = "BFGS"), specialised packages (bbmle, maxLik, model-specific tools like glm()).MLE boundary, standard asymptotic inference fails; use bootstrap CIs specialised boundary-aware procedures instead.

heading: 2.193 Introduction
text: method moments (MoM) oldest systematic technique parameter estimation. replaces theoretical moments distribution sample counterparts solves resulting equations parameters. MoM estimators typically consistent easy derive, making useful standalone estimators starting values refined procedures like maximum likelihood.

heading: 2.194 Prerequisites
text: reader know moment distribution (\(E[X^k]\)) able compute simple summary statistics R.

heading: 2.195 Theory
text:
distribution parameterised \(\theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_p)\), \(k\)-th theoretical moment \(\mu_k(\theta) = E[X^k]\), \(k\)-th sample moment \(\hat{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i^k\). MoM sets\[\hat{\mu}_k = \mu_k(\theta), \qquad k = 1, \ldots, p,\]solves \(\theta\). method generalises central moments, fractional moments, functions moments; essential idea moments easy estimate, parameter expressible function moments can estimated plug-.Examples:Gamma\((\alpha, \beta)\) mean \(\alpha/\beta\) variance \(\alpha/\beta^2\):
\[\hat{\beta} = \bar{X}/s^2, \qquad \hat{\alpha} = \bar{X}^2 / s^2.\]Gamma\((\alpha, \beta)\) mean \(\alpha/\beta\) variance \(\alpha/\beta^2\):
\[\hat{\beta} = \bar{X}/s^2, \qquad \hat{\alpha} = \bar{X}^2 / s^2.\]Normal\((\mu, \sigma^2)\): first moment gives \(\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}\); second central moment gives \(\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2\).Normal\((\mu, \sigma^2)\): first moment gives \(\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}\); second central moment gives \(\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2\).Beta\((\alpha, \beta)\) mean \(\alpha/(\alpha + \beta)\) variance \(\alpha\beta / [(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)]\): solve two moment equations \(\hat{\alpha}, \hat{\beta}\).Beta\((\alpha, \beta)\) mean \(\alpha/(\alpha + \beta)\) variance \(\alpha\beta / [(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)]\): solve two moment equations \(\hat{\alpha}, \hat{\beta}\).MoM estimators consistent mild conditions (existence moments used continuity inverse mapping moments parameters). asymptotic distribution normal, variance obtainable via delta method. generally less efficient MLE, often much simpler compute.

heading: 2.196 Assumptions
text: necessary moments must exist; heavy-tailed distributions like Cauchy (finite mean), MoM fails. mapping moments parameters must solvable, requires enough moments identify parameters uniquely.

heading: 2.197 R Implementation
text: generate 500 draws Gamma(4, 0.5), compute first two sample moments, back MoM estimators.
code:
set.seed(2026)

x <- rgamma(500, shape = 4, rate = 0.5)

xbar  <- mean(x)
s2    <- var(x)

beta_hat  <- xbar / s2
alpha_hat <- xbar^2 / s2

c(alpha_hat = alpha_hat, beta_hat = beta_hat,
  true_alpha = 4, true_rate = 0.5)

heading: 2.198 Output & Results
text: estimators close truth. Across many simulated datasets, bias goes zero variances shrink rate \(1/n\).
code:
alpha_hat  beta_hat true_alpha  true_rate
  3.91         0.48       4.00       0.50

heading: 2.199 Interpretation
text: MoM estimators useful closed-form expression desirable reporting, MLE requires numerical optimisation might fail specific initial values. many textbook distributions, MoM MLE coincide; others, MoM reasonable starting value MLE search.

heading: 2.200 Practical Tips

heading: 2.203 Introduction
text: Moments descending sequence expectations \(E[X], E[X^2], E[X^3], \ldots\) summarise distribution’s location, spread, skewness, kurtosis, finer shape features. moment generating function (MGF) packages moments single function; exists, MGF characterises distribution uniquely. MGFs give elegant proofs CLT, distribution sums independent variables, closure properties many distributional families.

heading: 2.204 Prerequisites
text: reader know expectation comfortable basic calculus.

heading: 2.205 Theory
text: \(k\)-th moment \(X\) \(\mu_k = E[X^k]\) (provided integral converges). central moments \(\mu^{(c)}_k = E[(X - \mu)^k]\); \(\mu^{(c)}_2\) variance, \(\mu^{(c)}_3\) feeds skewness, \(\mu^{(c)}_4\) feeds kurtosis.moment generating function \[M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}], \qquad \text{} t \text{ expectation finite}.\]\(M_X\) finite neighbourhood zero, () moments \(X\) exist, (ii) \(k\)-th moment equals \(k\)-th derivative \(M_X\) zero, \(M_X^{(k)}(0) = \mu_k\), (iii) \(M_X\) determines distribution uniquely.Properties.independent \(X, Y\): \(M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t) M_Y(t)\). MGFs turn sums products.affine transformations: \(M_{aX + b}(t) = e^{bt} M_X()\).MGF standard normal \(M(t) = e^{t^2/2}\). normal \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\): \(M(t) = e^{\mu t + \sigma^2 t^2/2}\).Limitations. MGF may exist \(t\) – Cauchy’s MGF infinite everywhere except \(t = 0\). distributions, characteristic function \(\varphi_X(t) = E[e^{itX}]\) preferred: always exists shares useful properties MGF.MGFs prove CLT elegantly: MGF standardised mean converges \(e^{t^2/2}\), MGF standard normal, uniqueness MGFs gives convergence distribution.

heading: 2.206 Assumptions
text: Existence moments order \(k\) requires \(E[|X|^k] < \infty\). Existence MGF neighbourhood zero much stronger condition (light-tailed distributions ).

heading: 2.207 R Implementation
text: compare empirical theoretical moments MGF values gamma distribution.
code:
set.seed(2026)
n <- 1e6

# Moments of a sample from a gamma distribution
x <- rgamma(n, shape = 3, rate = 2)
moments <- sapply(1:4, function(k) mean(x^k))
names(moments) <- paste0("m_", 1:4)
moments

# Theoretical moments: E[X^k] = Gamma(shape + k)/Gamma(shape) * rate^-k
shape <- 3; rate <- 2
theoretical <- sapply(1:4, function(k) {
  gamma(shape + k) / gamma(shape) * rate^(-k)
})
names(theoretical) <- paste0("m_", 1:4)
theoretical

# MGF of gamma at a few t: M(t) = (1 - t/rate)^-shape for t < rate
t_vals <- c(0.1, 0.5, 1.0)
mgf_theoretical <- (1 - t_vals / rate)^(-shape)
mgf_empirical   <- sapply(t_vals, function(t) mean(exp(t * x)))
cbind(t = t_vals, theoretical = mgf_theoretical,
      empirical = mgf_empirical)

heading: 2.208 Output & Results
text: Empirical theoretical values agree three decimal places one million observations; smaller samples agreement poorer higher moments far tails.

heading: 2.209 Interpretation
text: applied statistics, MGFs appear often proof tools rather quantities compute. Many closure properties (normal family closed convolution; gamma family closure fixed rate parameter) memorised via MGFs.

heading: 2.210 Practical Tips
text: heavy-tailed distributions, higher moments may exist; always check moments computing finite.Characteristic functions always exist; use MGFs don’t.applied work, first four moments (mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis) typically suffice; higher moments noisy rarely informative.MGF sum independent variables product individual MGFs – fact makes many distributional derivations tractable.confuse moments cumulants: cumulant generating function (log MGF) additive behaviour sums often preferred theoretical work.

heading: 2.213 Introduction
text: Given sample \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\), order statistics sorted values \(X_{(1)} \leq X_{(2)} \leq \ldots \leq X_{(n)}\). raw material median, quantiles, range, IQR, many non-parametric robust methods. distributional theory provides exact confidence intervals quantiles limiting distributions extremes – tools analytical approaches mean reach.

heading: 2.214 Prerequisites
text: reader know CDF PDF , comfortable sort() quantile() R.

heading: 2.215 Theory
text: iid \(X_i\) CDF \(F\), CDF \(k\)-th order statistic \[F_{X_{(k)}}(x) = \sum_{j=k}^n \binom{n}{j} F(x)^j [1 - F(x)]^{n-j}.\]\(F\) density \(f\), density \(X_{(k)}\) \[f_{X_{(k)}}(x) = \frac{n!}{(k-1)!(n-k)!} F(x)^{k-1} [1 - F(x)]^{n-k} f(x).\]joint density \(X_{()}\) \(X_{(j)}\) \(< j\) similar combinatorial form.Uniform case. \(U_i \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)\), \(U_{(k)} \sim \text{Beta}(k, n - k + 1)\) mean \(k/(n+1)\). plotting positions Q-Q plots use \((k - 0.5)/n\) \(k/(n+1)\) rather \(k/n\).Sample quantiles. sample size \(n\), sample \(p\)-quantile usually defined \(X_{(\lceil np \rceil)}\) interpolation adjacent order statistics; R’s quantile() offers nine definitions via type argument. sample median \(X_{((n+1)/2)}\) odd \(n\).Extremes. distributions \(X_{(1)}\) \(X_{(n)}\) independent interest. large \(n\), appropriately normalised extremes limiting distributions (Gumbel, Frechet, Weibull) given extreme-value theory.Confidence intervals quantiles. combinatorial form CDF order statistic, exact distribution-free CIs population quantile can constructed: interval \([X_{()}, X_{(j)}]\) known coverage probability \(p\)-quantile, regardless \(F\).

heading: 2.216 Assumptions
text: classical theory assumes iid observations; formulas extend directly exchangeable data. dependent data, distribution order statistics much complicated.

heading: 2.217 R Implementation
text: first block simulates sampling distribution 75th-percentile estimate. second constructs distribution-free CI population 75th percentile binomial probabilities applied ranks.
code:
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 20
p <- 0.75
reps <- 10000

sim_q <- replicate(reps, {
  x <- rnorm(n, mean = 50, sd = 10)
  sort(x)[ceiling(p * (n + 1))]
})

true_q <- qnorm(p, mean = 50, sd = 10)
c(empirical_mean = mean(sim_q),
  true           = true_q,
  empirical_SE   = sd(sim_q))

x <- rnorm(n, mean = 50, sd = 10)
sorted_x <- sort(x)
q05 <- binom.test(x = round(p * n), n = n, p = p)
k_lo <- qbinom(0.025, n, p)
k_hi <- qbinom(0.975, n, p) + 1
c(lower = sorted_x[k_lo], upper = sorted_x[min(k_hi, n)])

heading: 2.218 Output & Results
text: sample quantile slightly biased small samples (known feature definitions like type = 7); distribution-free CI true quantile wide valid continuous distribution.
code:

         56.13           56.74           3.01

lower  upper
54.17  72.81

heading: 2.219 Interpretation
text: Order-statistic-based CIs useful distribution data unknown clearly non-normal. Reporting “median survival 27 months (distribution-free 95% CI 22 31)” appropriate summary survival analysis parametric form doubt.

heading: 2.223 Introduction
text: pivotal quantity – pivot – function data parameter whose probability distribution depend parameter. existence pivot makes exact confidence intervals possible: \(Q(X, \theta)\) known distribution, can rearrange probability statement \(Q\) probability statement \(\theta\), rearrangement CI.

heading: 2.224 Prerequisites
text: reader understand concepts confidence intervals, t-distribution, chi-squared distribution.

heading: 2.225 Theory
text: pivotal quantity \(Q = Q(X_1, \ldots, X_n; \theta)\) satisfies: every \(\theta\), distribution \(Q\) known distribution. stronger “test statistic null”: pivot’s distribution depend \(\theta\) every value \(\theta\).Canonical examples (iid data):Normal, known \(\sigma^2\): \(Z = \sqrt{n}(\bar{X} - \mu)/\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\).Normal, unknown \(\sigma^2\): \(T = \sqrt{n}(\bar{X} - \mu)/s \sim t_{n-1}\).Normal variance: \(W = (n-1)s^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{n-1}\).Ratio normal variances: \(F = s_1^2/\sigma_1^2 \cdot \sigma_2^2/s_2^2 \sim F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1}\).Uniform\((0, \theta)\): \(\max X_i / \theta \sim \text{Beta}(n, 1)\).Given pivot \(Q\) known distribution, pick quantiles \(q_{\alpha/2}\) \(q_{1 - \alpha/2}\) \(P(q_{\alpha/2} \leq Q \leq q_{1 - \alpha/2}) = 1 - \alpha\). Invert inequalities isolate \(\theta\); resulting set \(1 - \alpha\) CI exact coverage.exact pivot exists, asymptotic pivots (e.g., \((\hat{\theta} - \theta)/\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}\) asymptotically \(\mathcal{N}(0, 1)\)) give CIs approximate coverage.

heading: 2.226 Assumptions
text: Exact pivots depend distributional assumptions (normality, uniformity). fail, nominal coverage guaranteed; bootstrap methods robust asymptotic pivots alternatives.

heading: 2.227 R Implementation
text: Two exact CIs constructed: t-pivot \(\mu\), chi-squared pivot \(\sigma^2\).
code:
set.seed(2026)

n <- 25
mu <- 5
sigma <- 2

x <- rnorm(n, mu, sigma)

xbar <- mean(x)
s    <- sd(x)
t_crit <- qt(0.975, df = n - 1)

ci_mean <- xbar + c(-1, 1) * t_crit * s / sqrt(n)
ci_mean

chi_l <- qchisq(0.025, df = n - 1)
chi_u <- qchisq(0.975, df = n - 1)
ci_var <- c((n - 1) * s^2 / chi_u, (n - 1) * s^2 / chi_l)
ci_var

heading: 2.228 Output & Results
text: intervals built pivots exact distributions normal model; coverage exactly 95% across repeated sampling.
code:
[1] 4.272  6.044     # 95% CI for mu
[1] 2.197  7.115     # 95% CI for sigma^2 (true value 4)

heading: 2.229 Interpretation
text: Pivotal quantities often implicit textbook CIs. Reporting t-interval mean chi-squared interval variance reporting pivot-based construction. pivot formalism explains CIs exact coverage assumed model – lose coverage model wrong.

heading: 2.230 Practical Tips
text: exact pivot exists, prefer asymptotic normal approximation, especially small \(n\).Pivots rare outside normal family; models, asymptotic pivots bootstrap practical tools.CI variance derived chi-squared pivot asymmetric centred \(s^2\); report “\(s^2 \pm \text{margin}\)”.Pivotal CIs transformations (e.g., log-odds) can constructed transformed scale back-transformed; preserves exact coverage pivot exact.every test statistic pivot – Wald statistic , general, exactly pivotal, CIs approximate.

heading: 2.233 Introduction

Confusion two single common source overreach scientific reporting.

heading: 2.234 Prerequisites
text: reader know average comfortable simple R arithmetic. previous inferential statistics assumed.

heading: 2.235 Theory
text: target population set units want make claim: “adults Germany”, “HER2-positive breast cancer patients treated hospital 2020”, “spikes recorded V1 neurons macaque M task T”. defined first, scientific terms, easy measure.sampling frame operational list units actually drawn: register registered voters; hospital’s electronic health record; 48 neurons successfully isolated. sampling frame differs target population, sample may representative frame intended population – coverage bias.sample subset frame actually observed. summary statistics (mean, proportion, correlation) estimates corresponding population parameters. Inference machinery quantifies close statistic likely parameter, given sampling scheme observed variability.convention, Greek letters denote population parameters Latin letters denote sample statistics:Population mean \(\mu\), sample mean \(\bar{x}\).Population variance \(\sigma^2\), sample variance \(s^2\).Population proportion \(\pi\), sample proportion \(\hat{p}\).Population regression coefficient \(\beta\), estimate \(\hat{\beta}\).

heading: 2.236 Assumptions
text: Inference population sample requires sampling scheme representative – either probability sampling (unit frame known, positive probability selection) identified mechanism conclusions transfer (e.g., random assignment within convenience sample, supports causal prevalence claims).

heading: 2.237 R Implementation
text: treat population fictional census one million values. single sample \(n = 50\) gives point estimate \(\bar{x}\); repeated sampling traces sampling distribution \(\bar{x}\).
code:
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
population <- rnorm(1e6, mean = 75, sd = 10)
mu    <- mean(population)
sigma <- sd(population)

sample_n  <- 50
one_sample <- sample(population, sample_n)
xbar <- mean(one_sample)
s    <- sd(one_sample)

c(population_mean = mu, sample_mean = xbar,
  population_sd   = sigma, sample_sd   = s)

sampling_distribution <- replicate(5000, mean(sample(population, sample_n)))
quantile(sampling_distribution, c(0.025, 0.975))

Retrospective definitions invite bias.Document sampling frame, inclusion/exclusion criteria, selection probabilities. makes study reproducible.frame deviates target population (convenience sampling clinical trials, online panels, self-selected respondents), state deviation likely direction bias.Sample statistics random; reporting point estimate without SE CI gives false sense precision.Never confuse “distribution data” “sampling distribution statistic”. first describes individuals, second describes averages (summaries) across repeated samples.

heading: 2.243 Introduction
text: sampling distribution statistic distribution values across hypothetical repetitions sampling process. Every confidence interval, every p-value, every standard error statement sampling distribution statistic. Understanding concept makes inference intuitive; ignoring makes ritual incantations.

heading: 2.244 Prerequisites
text: reader know sample mean comfortable histograms R’s replicate() function.

heading: 2.245 Theory
text: Let \(T = T(X_1, \ldots, X_n)\) statistic computed sample size \(n\) drawn population distribution. sampling distribution \(T\) distribution values \(T\) take sampling process repeated many times, time drawing fresh sample size \(n\) population.Key facts:sampling distribution property \(T\) sampling design, particular observed sample.mean expectation \(E[T]\). unbiased estimators, \(E[T]\) equals population parameter.standard deviation called standard error \(T\): \(\mathrm{SE}(T) = \sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(T)}\).shape depends statistic, \(n\), population distribution. sample mean population finite variance, central limit theorem guarantees approximate normality \(n\) grows.sample mean \(\bar{X}\) \(n\) iid observations mean \(\mu\) variance \(\sigma^2\):\[E[\bar{X}] = \mu, \qquad \mathrm{SE}(\bar{X}) = \sigma/\sqrt{n}.\]standard error tells us typical distance \(\bar{X}\) \(\mu\) – unit confidence intervals measured.

heading: 2.246 Assumptions
text: theoretical sampling distribution derived textbooks assumes iid sampling population finite variance. Deviations (heavy tails, dependence, non-iid sampling) change shape formulas; bootstrap lets us approximate sampling distribution empirically without assumptions.

heading: 2.247 R Implementation
text: skewed gamma population, sampling distribution mean concentrates \(n\) grows, spread matches theoretical standard error.
code:
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
population <- rgamma(1e5, shape = 2, rate = 0.1)
mu <- mean(population)
sigma <- sd(population)

draw_mean <- function(n) mean(sample(population, n))

reps <- 10000
means_n10  <- replicate(reps, draw_mean(10))
means_n100 <- replicate(reps, draw_mean(100))

cat(sprintf("n=10  : mean=%.2f, SE=%.2f; theoretical SE=%.2f\n",
            mean(means_n10), sd(means_n10), sigma / sqrt(10)))
cat(sprintf("n=100 : mean=%.2f, SE=%.2f; theoretical SE=%.2f\n",
            mean(means_n100), sd(means_n100), sigma / sqrt(100)))

df <- data.frame(xbar = c(means_n10, means_n100),
                 n = factor(rep(c(10, 100), each = reps)))
ggplot(df, aes(x = xbar, fill = n)) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.6, bins = 50, position = "identity") +
  labs(x = expression(bar(X)), y = "Frequency") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 2.248 Output & Results
text: Increasing \(n\) factor 10 decreases standard error \(\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16\), exactly \(1/\sqrt{n}\) rule predicts.
code:
n=10  : mean=19.98, SE=4.42; theoretical SE=4.47
n=100 : mean=20.01, SE=1.41; theoretical SE=1.41

heading: 2.249 Interpretation
text: standard deviation sampling distribution – SE – confidence interval built . 95% CI mean approximately \(\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \mathrm{SE}(\bar{X})\). manuscript reports “mean 19.98 (SE 1.41, 95% CI 17.22–22.74)”, every number summary derived sampling distribution statistic.

heading: 2.250 Practical Tips
text: confuse standard deviation data standard error statistic. SD describes variability across individuals; SE describes variability across hypothetical samples size \(n\).theoretical sampling distribution unknown, approximate bootstrap: resample replacement observed sample, compute statistic, use empirical distribution resampled statistics estimate.sampling distribution depends \(n\) – larger samples yield tighter distributions, \(1/\sqrt{n}\) rule captures mean.statistics mean, sampling distribution may harder derive analytically equally well-defined; simulation bootstrap general-purpose tool.\(t\) distribution appears small-sample inference sampling distribution \((\bar{X} - \mu)/(s/\sqrt{n})\) normality unknown variance, mean .

heading: 2.253 Introduction
text: sample drawn matters much big . 500 observations can support valid inference population produce misleading summary, depending entirely sampling mechanism. Classical sampling theory names handful probability designs, characteristic strengths, variance properties, analysis requirements.

heading: 2.254 Prerequisites
text: reader know difference population sample, familiar random-number generation R.

heading: 2.255 Theory
text: Simple random sampling (SRS) draws \(n\) units frame \(N\) every subset size \(n\) equally likely. sample mean unbiased population mean, standard error \(\sigma/\sqrt{n} \cdot \sqrt{1 - n/N}\) (finite-population correction negligible \(n \ll N\)). SRS theoretical baseline designs compared.Stratified sampling partitions population non-overlapping strata – e.g., region, age band, hospital – draws SRS . pooled estimator lower variance SRS whenever strata homogeneous within population large, typical. Stratification also guarantees representation small subgroups might missed chance SRS.Cluster sampling draws whole clusters (schools, villages, GP practices) includes units within . Variance higher SRS \(n\) units within cluster correlated; loss quantified design effect \(\text{deff} = 1 + (\bar{m} - 1)\rho\), \(\bar{m}\) average cluster size \(\rho\) intra-cluster correlation. Cluster sampling trades statistical efficiency dramatically lower data-collection cost.Systematic sampling picks every \(k\)-th unit list random start. equivalent SRS list random order; list periodicity matching \(k\), can give badly biased estimates.Convenience sampling recruits whatever units available – internet panels, patients one hospital, undergraduates course. supports probability-based inference broader population; generalisation must argued non-statistical grounds (mechanism, theory, replication).

heading: 2.256 Assumptions
text: Probability designs assume complete sampling frame known selection probabilities. Analysis must incorporate design weights (\(w_i = 1/\pi_i\)) design variance; standard software ignores weighting (t-test weighted survey data, say) produces incorrect standard errors.

heading: 2.257 R Implementation
text: code draws 200-unit SRS 200-unit stratified sample population, computes design-consistent stratified estimator via weighted averages.
code:
library(sampling)

set.seed(2026)
N <- 10000
pop <- data.frame(
  id     = 1:N,
  stratum = sample(c("A", "B", "C"), N, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2)),
  y      = rnorm(N, mean = c(A = 50, B = 60, C = 70)[sample(c("A", "B", "C"), N,
           replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2))], sd = 5)
)

srs_idx <- sample(N, 200)
srs <- pop[srs_idx, ]
mean(srs$y); sd(srs$y) / sqrt(200)

str_idx <- strata(pop, stratanames = "stratum",
                  size = c(A = 100, B = 60, C = 40),
                  method = "srswor")
strat <- pop[str_idx$ID_unit, ]
tapply(strat$y, strat$stratum, mean)
weighted.mean(tapply(strat$y, strat$stratum, mean),
              w = table(pop$stratum))
