heading: 2.263 Introduction
text: Every variable dataset carries scale measurement, scale dictates statistics meaningful compute. Stevens’ 1946 taxonomy – nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio – remains default vocabulary idea, even strict version criticised, underlying distinctions unavoidable applied work. Computing mean jersey numbers nonsense jersey numbers nominal; computing median exam grades sensible grades least ordinal.

heading: 2.264 Prerequisites
text: reader know variable able tell numeric data categorical data R (numeric vs. factor character).

heading: 2.265 Theory
text: Nominal: labels without order. Operations: equality. Examples: sex, blood type, country. Meaningful summaries: mode, frequency counts.Ordinal: ordered labels without meaningful distances. Operations: \(=\), \(<\), \(>\). Examples: Likert scale, disease stage (–IV), pain rating. Meaningful summaries: median, quantiles, rank correlations.Interval: ordered, meaningful distances true zero. Operations: \(+\), \(-\). Examples: temperature Celsius, calendar dates. Ratios meaningful: 40 °C “twice hot” 20 °C.Ratio: interval true zero. Operations: \(\times\), \(/\). Examples: mass, concentration, time elapsed, counts. Ratios meaningful: 40 kg twice 20 kg.scale determines permissible summary statistics inferential procedures. Pearson correlation requires interval ratio data; Spearman correlation extends ordinal. t-test operates (minimum) interval data, though practice widely applied Likert-like composites reasonable robustness.

heading: 2.266 Assumptions
text: taxonomy descriptive rather inferential; use rests assumption chosen scale accurately characterises variable. practice, many variables sit ambiguously – 5-point Likert item ordinal interval? Standard practice treats Likert composite scales (averages many items) interval single items ordinal.

heading: 2.267 R Implementation
text: Nominal country summarised frequencies; ordinal stage median (raw factor must coerced numeric median call); interval temp_c mean; ratio mass_kg mean ratios.
code:
library(ggplot2)

df <- data.frame(
  id      = 1:8,
  country = factor(c("DE", "US", "FR", "DE", "US", "FR", "DE", "US")),
  stage   = factor(c("I", "II", "III", "II", "IV", "I", "III", "II"),
                   levels = c("I", "II", "III", "IV"), ordered = TRUE),
  temp_c  = c(36.5, 37.1, 38.2, 36.9, 37.4, 36.7, 38.0, 37.2),
  mass_kg = c(72.1, 81.3, 65.8, 74.2, 88.7, 61.4, 79.0, 84.5)
)

table(df$country)
median(as.numeric(df$stage))
mean(df$temp_c)
mean(df$mass_kg); df$mass_kg[1] / df$mass_kg[3]

heading: 2.273 Introduction
text: Slutsky’s theorem one workhorse results asymptotic statistics. says can substitute consistent estimator constant asymptotic statement, without changing limiting distribution. simple-sounding result makes Wald statistic asymptotic standard-normal distribution, justifies using sample SD place population SD t-statistic’s denominator, underlies countless routine asymptotic arguments applied work.

heading: 2.274 Prerequisites
text: reader understand convergence probability convergence distribution.

heading: 2.275 Theory
text: Slutsky’s theorem. Suppose \(Y_n \xrightarrow{d} Y\) \(Z_n \xrightarrow{P} c\), \(c\) constant. :\(Y_n + Z_n \xrightarrow{d} Y + c\),\(Y_n Z_n \xrightarrow{d} c Y\),\(Y_n / Z_n \xrightarrow{d} Y / c\) (provided \(c \neq 0\)).key requirement \(Z_n\) converges constant, another random variable. Convergence \(Z_n\) non-degenerate random variable generally justify manipulations.Typical use. Consider Wald statistic mean:\[W_n = \frac{\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_n - \mu)}{s_n}.\]know \(\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_n - \mu)/\sigma \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) CLT, \(s_n \xrightarrow{P} \sigma\) LLN applied sample variance. Writing\[W_n = \frac{\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_n - \mu)}{\sigma} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{s_n},\]Slutsky’s theorem gives \(W_n \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \cdot 1 = \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\).Wald statistic estimated SE still standard-normal limit. principle applies Wald chi-squared statistics, score statistics, almost every test statistic asymptotic theory.

heading: 2.276 Assumptions
text: Slutsky’s theorem requires () \(Z_n\) converges probability constant, random variable, (ii) combined operation (sum, product, quotient) well-defined. quotients constant must zero; dividing small random denominator can still introduce tail issues finite \(n\) Slutsky warn .

heading: 2.277 R Implementation
text: simulate Wald statistic across 5000 replications. Slutsky’s theorem predicts limiting distribution standard normal; simulation confirms empirically.
code:
set.seed(2026)

n <- 500
mu <- 3; sigma <- 2
reps <- 5000

wald_stats <- replicate(reps, {
  x <- rnorm(n, mu, sigma)
  sqrt(n) * (mean(x) - mu) / sd(x)
})

qqnorm(wald_stats, pch = ".")
qqline(wald_stats, col = "red")

c(mean = mean(wald_stats),
  sd   = sd(wald_stats))

heading: 2.278 Output & Results
text: empirical mean near zero SD near one, confirming standard-normal behaviour. Q-Q plot falls reference line.

heading: 2.279 Interpretation
text: Slutsky’s theorem rarely cited name applied papers, conclusions appear constantly. phrase “CLT, using consistency \(\hat{\sigma}\) \(\sigma\), Wald statistic asymptotically standard normal” Slutsky argument compressed single sentence.

heading: 2.283 Introduction
text: standard error statistic standard deviation sampling distribution. quantifies much statistic vary across hypothetical repetitions sampling process. Every confidence interval every t- z-statistic built estimate standard error, understanding correctly computing SEs central competent data analysis.

heading: 2.284 Prerequisites
text: reader know sampling distribution , sample standard deviation , use sd() mean() functions R.

heading: 2.285 Theory
text: iid sample \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) population variance \(\sigma^2\), sample mean \(\bar{X}\) variance \(\sigma^2/n\) therefore\[\mathrm{SE}(\bar{X}) = \sigma / \sqrt{n}.\]\(\sigma\) usually unknown, estimated sample SD \(s\), giving plug-estimator \(\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\bar{X}) = s/\sqrt{n}\).binary proportion \(\hat{p} = k/n\), standard error binomial sampling \[\mathrm{SE}(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{p(1-p)/n}, \qquad \widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}.\]difference means across two independent samples variances \(s_1^2, s_2^2\),\[\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) = \sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}.\]regression coefficients, standard errors come diagonal \(\hat{\sigma}^2 (X^\top X)^{-1}\); R returns summary(fit)$coefficients.statistics without closed-form SE (ratios, quantiles, custom summaries), bootstrap gives general-purpose estimator: resample replacement \(B\) times, compute statistic resample, use SD \(B\) bootstrap statistics \(\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}\).

heading: 2.286 Assumptions
text: formula-based SEs assume iid sampling , finite-sample versions, enough regularity CLT justify using normal-approximation CIs. bootstrap SE consistent weaker conditions inherits design: accurate resampling units independent sampling units (bootstrap cluster level cluster samples).

heading: 2.287 R Implementation
text: first two blocks use closed-form SEs mean proportion; third uses bootstrap IQR, convenient closed form.
code:
library(boot)

set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(60, mean = 75, sd = 12)

se_mean     <- sd(x) / sqrt(length(x))
se_mean

y <- c(rep(1, 24), rep(0, 76))
p_hat <- mean(y)
se_prop <- sqrt(p_hat * (1 - p_hat) / length(y))
se_prop

iqr_stat <- function(d, i) IQR(d[i])
b <- boot(x, statistic = iqr_stat, R = 5000)
se_iqr <- sd(b$t)
se_iqr

heading: 2.293 Introduction
text: statistic sufficient parameter captures information data relevant parameter. sufficient statistic computed, raw data nothing left add parameter. Sufficiency organises theory estimation: explains sample mean complete summary normal mean, sum successes suffices binomial proportion, exponential family distributions central modern statistical modelling.

heading: 2.294 Prerequisites
text: reader comfortable likelihood function, conditional probability, little abstract notation.

heading: 2.295 Theory
text: Formally, \(T(X_1, \ldots, X_n)\) sufficient \(\theta\) conditional distribution data given \(T = t\) depend \(\theta\). Equivalently (Fisher-Neyman factorisation theorem), \(T\) sufficient iff joint density factorises \[f(x_1, \ldots, x_n \mid \theta) = g\!\big(T(x), \theta\big) \cdot h(x),\]\(h\) depend \(\theta\) \(g\) depends data \(T\).Examples:Normal \((\mu, \sigma^2)\) known \(\sigma^2\): \(T = \bar{X}\) sufficient \(\mu\).Normal unknown: \((\bar{X}, \sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2)\) jointly sufficient.Bernoulli/Binomial: \(T = \sum X_i\) sufficient \(\pi\).Poisson: \(T = \sum X_i\) sufficient \(\lambda\).Uniform\((0, \theta)\): \(T = \max X_i\) sufficient \(\theta\).statistic minimal sufficient function every sufficient statistic – .e., compact sufficient summary.exponential family density \(f(x \mid \theta) = h(x) \exp[\eta(\theta)^\top T(x) - (\theta)]\) natural statistic \(T(x)\). Almost every distribution used applied statistics (normal, binomial, Poisson, gamma, beta, multinomial) belongs exponential family, \(T(x)\) minimal sufficient \(\eta(\theta)\).Rao-Blackwell theorem: given unbiased estimator \(\hat{\theta}\) sufficient statistic \(T\), conditional expectation \(E[\hat{\theta} \mid T]\) also unbiased variance larger \(\hat{\theta}\). Conditioning sufficient statistics never hurts may help.

heading: 2.296 Assumptions
text: factorisation theorem applies proper densities common support. non-regular families (uniform unknown upper endpoint) sufficiency still applies, resulting estimators inherit non-standard behaviour family.

heading: 2.297 R Implementation
text: Computing MLE two sufficient statistics \((T_1, T_2) = (\sum x, \sum x^2)\) yields point estimates computing full data vector.
code:
set.seed(2026)

n <- 50
mu <- 5
sigma <- 2
x <- rnorm(n, mean = mu, sd = sigma)

T1 <- sum(x)
T2 <- sum(x^2)

log_lik_from_sum <- function(mu, sigma, T1, T2, n) {
  -n/2 * log(2 * pi * sigma^2) -
    (T2 - 2 * mu * T1 + n * mu^2) / (2 * sigma^2)
}

mu_hat <- T1 / n
sigma2_hat <- (T2 - T1^2/n) / n

c(mu_hat = mu_hat, sigma2_hat = sigma2_hat)

c(mle_from_data_mean = mean(x),
  mle_from_data_var  = mean((x - mean(x))^2))

heading: 2.303 Introduction
text: multiple estimators available parameter, need criteria choose among . Three classical criteria – unbiasedness, consistency, efficiency – cover important properties: whether estimator correct average (unbiasedness), whether approaches truth data arrive (consistency), whether uses data effectively possible (efficiency).

heading: 2.304 Prerequisites
text: reader comfortable notions expectation, variance, convergence probability.

heading: 2.305 Theory
text: Unbiasedness. estimator \(\hat{\theta}\) unbiased \(\theta\) \(E[\hat{\theta}] = \theta\) every value \(\theta\) parameter space. Examples: sample mean unbiased population mean; sample variance divisor \(n - 1\) unbiased \(\sigma^2\); plug-variance divisor \(n\) biased.Consistency. estimator \(\hat{\theta}_n\) consistent \(\theta\) converges \(\theta\) probability \(n \\infty\):\[\hat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta.\]Unbiasedness consistency distinct: sample median consistent population median can biased small samples; MLE \(\sigma^2\) (divisor \(n\)) biased every finite sample consistent.Efficiency. Among unbiased estimators, efficient one one smallest variance. Cramer-Rao lower bound (CRLB) sets floor variance:\[\mathrm{Var}(\hat{\theta}) \geq \frac{1}{(\theta)},\]\((\theta)\) Fisher information \(\theta\). estimator attains bound UMVUE (uniformly minimum-variance unbiased estimator). regular models, MLE asymptotically efficient: variance approaches CRLB \(n\) grows.three properties can tension. biased estimator tiny variance can lower MSE unbiased estimator large variance; consistent estimator may converge slowly may exist closed form.

heading: 2.306 Assumptions
text: properties defined within model. Change model, estimator unbiased, consistent, efficient one parameter may none three another.

heading: 2.307 R Implementation
text: compare sample mean sample median estimators population mean \(\mu\) (symmetric normal, target quantity). mean efficient; median less efficient also consistent.
code:
library(ggplot2)
set.seed(2026)

simulate_props <- function(n, reps = 5000) {
  mu <- 10
  sigma <- 3
  m  <- replicate(reps, mean(rnorm(n, mu, sigma)))
  md <- replicate(reps, median(rnorm(n, mu, sigma)))
  rbind(
    data.frame(n = n, est = "mean",   bias = mean(m) - mu, var = var(m)),
    data.frame(n = n, est = "median", bias = mean(md) - mu, var = var(md))
  )
}

res <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c(10, 50, 250, 1000), simulate_props))
res

text: estimators bias near zero; variance shrinks rate \(1/n\) (mean hits \(\sigma^2/n = 9/n\); median higher factor \(\pi/2 \approx 1.57\)). mean thus efficient roughly 1.57.

heading: 2.309 Interpretation
text: normally distributed data, sample mean unbiased efficient; choosing median costs nothing gains precision. heavy-tailed data data outliers, median robust, efficiency gap can reverse opposite direction. trade-offs three criteria make explicit.

Reshape long form row one reading.long format makes trivial summarise visit, patient, 
visit--patient. plot wide format require

pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("visit"),
    names_to = c("visit", "reading"),
    names_pattern = "visit(\\d)_sbp(\\d)",
    values_to = "sbp"
  ) |>
  mutate(visit = factor(paste0("V", visit)),
         reading = as.integer(reading))
head(long)
long |>
  ggplot(aes(visit, sbp, colour = sex)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "SBP (mmHg)")

  group_by(id, visit, sex) |>
  summarise(sbp_mean = mean(sbp), .groups = "drop")

# Pivot back to wide if the downstream analysis needs it.
wide <- by_visit |> pivot_wider(names_from = visit, values_from = sbp_mean)
head(wide)

heading: 2.320 4. Check
text:
Simulate instrument known truth (say, calibrated reference
120) compare three candidate devices different
accuracy/precision profiles.
code:
n <- 500
devices <- tibble(
  device_A = rnorm(n, mean = 120, sd = 1),   # accurate & precise
  device_B = rnorm(n, mean = 125, sd = 1),   # precise, biased
  device_C = rnorm(n, mean = 120, sd = 5)    # accurate on average, imprecise
) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "device", values_to = "reading")

summary_table <- devices |>
  group_by(device) |>
  summarise(mean = mean(reading),
            sd = sd(reading),
            bias = mean(reading) - truth,
            rmse = sqrt(mean((reading - truth)^2)))
summary_table
devices |>
  ggplot(aes(reading, fill = device)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5, colour = NA) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = truth, linetype = 2) +
  labs(x = "Reading", y = "Density")

)

heading: 3.3 Introduction
text: coefficient variation (CV) ratio standard deviation mean, usually expressed percentage. provides scale-free measure dispersion, useful comparing variability across variables measured different units different magnitudes. laboratory medicine, CV standard metric assay precision; CV 5% typical clinical chemistry assay.

heading: 3.4 Prerequisites
text: reader know mean SD .

heading: 3.5 Theory
text: positive-valued variable mean \(\mu\) SD \(\sigma\),\[\mathrm{CV} = \sigma / \mu \quad \text{(} 100 \cdot \sigma/\mu \text{ percentage)}.\]CV unit-less: comparing CVs across weight--kg weight--pounds gives value. meaningful variables ratio scale (true zero) unstable mean close zero.related quantity relative standard error (RSE) = SE / mean. measures precision mean estimate rather dispersion individuals.

heading: 3.6 Assumptions
text: variable must ratio scale (mass, concentration, time, count).mean must comfortably far zero; CV approaches infinity mean approaches zero.CV comparisons across groups meaningful, variables must type.

heading: 3.7 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Two assays with different concentration ranges
low  <- rnorm(50, mean = 2.5, sd = 0.25)
high <- rnorm(50, mean = 85.0, sd = 6.8)

cv_pct <- function(x) 100 * sd(x) / mean(x)

c(low_cv  = cv_pct(low),
  high_cv = cv_pct(high))

heading: 3.8 Output & Results
text: low-concentration assay slightly higher CV (10%) high-concentration one (8%); absolute SD low 0.25 vs. 6.8 high, scaling mean two comparable.
code:
low_cv high_cv
  10.0    8.0

heading: 3.9 Interpretation
text: Report CV inter-assay variability, instrument precision, context comparing variability across scales matters. CV 10% conventional clinical-chemistry imprecision; CV 20% signals assay need optimisation.

heading: 3.10 Practical Tips
text: CV undefined nonsensical variables centred near zero; use absolute SD instead.report CV interval-scale variables (temperature Celsius).Log-CV = SD log-values approximately equal CV original scale small CVs; large CVs approximation breaks .intra-assay vs. inter-assay contexts, report CVs separately.CV mean \(\mathrm{CV}(\bar{x})\) CV(\(x\)) / \(\sqrt{n}\); connects precision sample size.

heading: 3.13 Introduction
text: contingency table cross-tabulation treated input statistical test independence. counts cells, expected counts independence null, standardised residuals localise deviation three essential components.

heading: 3.14 Prerequisites
text: reader know cross-tabulation .

heading: 3.15 Theory
text: \(r \times c\) contingency table observed counts \(n_{ij}\), row totals \(n_{+}\), column totals \(n_{+j}\), grand total \(n\):Expected count independence: \(E_{ij} = n_{+} \cdot n_{+j} / n\).Pearson residual: \(e_{ij} = (n_{ij} - E_{ij}) / \sqrt{E_{ij}}\).Standardised residual: Pearson residual divided \(\sqrt{(1 - n_{+}/n)(1 - n_{+j}/n)}\); approximately standard normal null.Chi-squared statistic: \(\chi^2 = \sum (n_{ij} - E_{ij})^2 / E_{ij}\); null, follows \(\chi^2\) \((r - 1)(c - 1)\) df.Standardised residuals beyond \(\pm 2\) flag cells substantially exceed fall short independence.

heading: 3.16 Assumptions
text: chi-squared approximation: independent observations expected counts \(\geq 5\). small expected counts, use Fisher’s exact test.

heading: 3.17 R Implementation
code:
library(vcd)

cohort <- matrix(c(24, 18, 36, 32, 18, 42, 16, 24, 40),
                 nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE,
                 dimnames = list(Stage = c("I", "II", "III"),
                                 Response = c("CR", "PR", "SD")))
cohort

chi <- chisq.test(cohort)
chi
chi$expected
chi$residuals         # Pearson residuals
chi$stdres            # standardised residuals

# Visual representation of residuals
mosaic(cohort, shade = TRUE, legend = TRUE)

heading: 3.18 Output & Results
text: cell’s standardised residual exceeds \(\pm 2.5\), none drives substantial departure independence. chi-squared p-value (\(p \approx 0.22\) ) non-significant.
code:
Response
Stage    CR PR SD
  I      24 18 36
  II     32 18 42
  III    16 24 40

Expected:
       CR   PR   SD
  I   23.5 18.3 36.2
  II  27.4 21.4 43.2
  III 21.1 16.5 33.4

Standardised residuals:
       CR    PR    SD
  I   0.15 -0.09 -0.08
  II  1.32 -1.02 -0.30
  III -1.50  2.18  1.53

heading: 3.19 Interpretation
text: Report contingency table alongside chi-squared statistic measure association (Cramer’s V). Standardised residuals reported omnibus test significant investigator wants localise effect.

heading: 3.20 Practical Tips
text: chisq.test() returns expected counts, Pearson residuals, standardised residuals attributes.Mosaic plots (vcd::mosaic) encode cell deviations colour – visual alternative residual tables.small tables sparse cells, fisher.test() (Monte Carlo chi-squared) required.stratified 2x2 tables across third variable, use Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel (mantelhaen.test).vcd::assocstats() returns phi, contingency coefficient, Cramer’s V one call.

heading: 3.23 Introduction
text: cross-tabulation shows joint distribution two categorical variables. first look whether variables associated input chi-squared, Fisher’s exact, tests independence.

heading: 3.24 Prerequisites
text: reader know one-way frequency table .

heading: 3.25 Theory
text: two categorical variables \(X\) \(r\) levels \(Y\) \(c\) levels, cross-tabulation \(r \times c\) table whose \((, j)\)-th cell holds count observations \(X = \) \(Y = j\). Typically reported forms:Joint counts: raw \(n_{ij}\).Row percentages: \(100 \cdot n_{ij} / \sum_j n_{ij}\). Answers “given \(X = \), distribution \(Y\)?”Column percentages: \(100 \cdot n_{ij} / \sum_i n_{ij}\). Answers “given \(Y = j\), distribution \(X\)?”Overall percentages: \(100 \cdot n_{ij} / n\). Joint probabilities.

heading: 3.26 Assumptions
text: Purely descriptive; distributional assumption.

heading: 3.27 R Implementation
code:
library(dplyr)
library(janitor)
library(gtsummary)

set.seed(2026)
cohort <- tibble::tibble(
  sex   = factor(sample(c("Female", "Male"), 300, replace = TRUE)),
  arm   = factor(sample(c("Placebo", "Active"), 300, replace = TRUE)),
  event = factor(sample(c("Yes", "No"), 300, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.25, 0.75)))
)

# Base R
table(cohort$arm, cohort$event)

# Percentages
addmargins(prop.table(table(cohort$arm, cohort$event), margin = 1), margin = 2)

# Tidy with janitor
cohort |>
  tabyl(arm, event) |>
  adorn_percentages("row") |>
  adorn_pct_formatting(digits = 1) |>
  adorn_ns()

# gtsummary
cohort |>
  tbl_cross(row = arm, col = event, percent = "row", margin = "column")

# Three-way via xtabs
xtabs(~ sex + arm + event, data = cohort)

heading: 3.28 Output & Results
text: typical 2x2 table:Row percentages show event rate within arm; column percentages instead show fraction events came arm.

heading: 3.29 Interpretation
text: association analysis, report counts one row, column, joint percentages – one matches causal direction research question. “Among placebo patients, 21.3% experienced event” row-percentage statement appropriate arm precedes event.

heading: 3.30 Practical Tips
text: Always state whether percentages row, column, overall; common source reader confusion.Sparse cells (count < 5) motivate Fisher’s exact test rather chi-squared.three-way higher tables, consider log-linear models (loglm MASS).gtsummary::tbl_cross() produces publication-ready output directly data frame.Report totals margins; let readers check arithmetic quickly.

heading: 3.33 Introduction
text: Every applied statistics course opens distinction sounds obvious routinely blurred papers: descriptive statistics summarise sample hand; inferential statistics make claims population sample drawn. Confusing single common reporting error published research.

heading: 3.34 Prerequisites
text: reader know sample population , encountered least one summary statistic (mean, proportion).

heading: 3.35 Theory
text: descriptive statistic numerical summary computed sample: mean, SD, median, IQR, counts, percentages. deterministic given data; dataset always produces descriptive summary.inferential statistic uses sample say something population: confidence interval, hypothesis test, regression coefficient standard error, Bayesian credible interval. carries explicit implicit uncertainty quantification.distinction maps cleanly onto notation: descriptive statistics (Latin letters \(\bar{x}\), \(s\), \(\hat{p}\)) versus population parameters (Greek letters \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(\pi\)) inferential procedures estimate.play complementary roles paper:Descriptive: Table 1 characterising sample; figures showing distributions.Inferential: Primary analysis results; p-values confidence intervals effect sizes.

heading: 3.36 Assumptions
text: Descriptive statistics require data correctly transcribed. Inferential statistics require sampling-model assumption: sample reasonably represents population (random sampling, randomised assignment, defensible non-random scheme).

heading: 3.37 R Implementation
code:
library(broom)
set.seed(2026)

trial <- data.frame(
  arm    = factor(rep(c("Placebo", "Active"), each = 60)),
  sbp    = c(rnorm(60, 138, 14), rnorm(60, 130, 14))
)

# Descriptive: summarise the sample
aggregate(sbp ~ arm, data = trial,
          FUN = function(x) c(n = length(x), mean = mean(x), sd = sd(x)))

# Inferential: make a claim about the population
fit <- t.test(sbp ~ arm, data = trial)
tidy(fit)

heading: 3.38 Output & Results
text: Descriptive: placebo arm mean SBP 137.8 mmHg (SD 14.1); active arm 130.4 (SD 13.3). Inferential: difference 7.4 mmHg 95 % CI 2.5 12.3 mmHg, \(p = 0.003\).
code:

 Placebo 60  137.82 14.05
  Active 60  130.39 13.28

# A tibble: 1 x 10
  estimate estimate1 estimate2 statistic p.value parameter conf.low conf.high

1     7.43      138.      130.      2.99 0.00333      118.     2.52      12.3

heading: 3.39 Interpretation
text: paper’s Results section typically progresses descriptive inferential: first describe sample (Table 1), test pre-specified hypotheses. Mislabelling two – treating confidence interval described sample, treating sample mean population parameter – common reviewer complaint.

heading: 3.40 Practical Tips
text: write “mean SBP 137.8 mmHg” made descriptive statement; write “SBP 137.8 mmHg population” made inferential statement (include CI).Inferential statistics depend well-defined sampling frame; sample convenience sample, inference sample , broader population.Use distinct notation equations: \(\bar{x}\) sample mean, \(\mu\) population mean.Every p-value every confidence interval accompanied descriptive summary (counts, means, proportions) reader effect context.purely descriptive paper (audit, registry summary) legitimate; pretending inferential .

heading: 3.43 Introduction
text: five-number summary compact description univariate distribution five values: minimum, first quartile, median, third quartile, maximum. popularised John Tukey exploratory data analysis tradition numeric backbone boxplot. skewed non-normal data conveys mean SD space.

heading: 3.44 Prerequisites
text: reader know quantiles sort vector R.

heading: 3.45 Theory
text: Given sorted sample \(x_{(1)} \leq \ldots \leq x_{(n)}\), five-number summary \[(x_{(1)}, Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, x_{(n)}),\]\(Q_1, Q_2, Q_3\) 0.25, 0.50, 0.75 quantiles. Tukey’s hinges slightly different Type-7 quantiles R uses default; fivenum() function returns hinges, quantile(..., probs = c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1)) returns Type-7 quantiles.interquartile range \(\mathrm{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1\) measures width central 50% data. Tukey fences \(Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}\) \(Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}\) define whiskers boxplot; points beyond flagged outliers.

heading: 3.46 Assumptions
text: five-number summary purely descriptive assumes nothing distribution.

heading: 3.47 R Implementation
text: grouped summaries:
code:
library(dplyr)

set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(80, mean = 70, sd = 12)

fivenum(x)
summary(x)

iqr_x <- IQR(x)
fences <- c(lower = quantile(x, 0.25) - 1.5 * iqr_x,
            upper = quantile(x, 0.75) + 1.5 * iqr_x)
fences
sum(x < fences["lower"] | x > fences["upper"])

boxplot(x, horizontal = TRUE,
        main = "Five-number summary of x")
trial <- tibble::tibble(
  arm = factor(rep(c("Placebo", "Active"), each = 40)),
  fpg = c(rnorm(40, 8.1, 0.9), rnorm(40, 7.0, 1.0))
)

trial |>
  group_by(arm) |>
  summarise(min = min(fpg), q25 = quantile(fpg, 0.25),
            med = median(fpg), q75 = quantile(fpg, 0.75),
            max = max(fpg))

heading: 3.48 Output & Results
text: Typical output fivenum(x):boxplot displays five numbers: whiskers min max (Tukey fence outliers flagged), hinges Q1 Q3, thick line median.

heading: 3.49 Interpretation
text: Use five-number summary skewed small-sample data. Reporting mean SD data misrepresents typicality; median IQR informative.

heading: 3.50 Practical Tips
text: boxplot default graphical display five-number summary; use early exploratory analysis.comparing groups, put boxplots side side notice whether boxes overlap medians align.Many journals accept boxplots underlying data overlaid (e.g., jittered points raincloud plots); check submission guidelines.small samples (\(n < 10\)), Tukey fences flag many normal observations outliers; rely data, fence.Report five numbers text table alongside figure, readers exact values.

heading: 3.53 Introduction
text: frequency table basic summary categorical discrete-numeric variable: distinct value, many times occurred (absolute frequency), share sample represents (relative frequency), running total including value (cumulative frequency). Frequency tables building blocks chi-squared tests, goodness--fit analyses, epidemiological prevalence reporting.

heading: 3.54 Prerequisites
text: reader know categorical variable comfortable R factors.

heading: 3.55 Theory
text: Given categorical variable \(X\) levels \(c_1, \ldots, c_k\) observed sample size \(n\):Absolute frequency \(f_j\): number times \(c_j\) appears.Relative frequency \(r_j = f_j / n\): proportion.Cumulative frequency \(F_j = f_1 + \ldots + f_j\): running count. Meaningful ordered variables.Cumulative relative frequency \(R_j = F_j / n\): running proportion, equivalent empirical CDF \(c_j\).

heading: 3.56 Assumptions
text: Frequency tables descriptive; distributional assumption. variable must categorical (nominal ordinal) discrete numeric.

heading: 3.57 R Implementation
code:
library(dplyr)
library(janitor)
library(gt)

set.seed(2026)
cohort <- tibble::tibble(
  stage = factor(sample(c("I", "II", "III", "IV"), 250, replace = TRUE,
                        prob = c(0.20, 0.35, 0.30, 0.15)),
                 levels = c("I", "II", "III", "IV"), ordered = TRUE)
)

# Base R
table(cohort$stage)
prop.table(table(cohort$stage))
cumsum(prop.table(table(cohort$stage)))

# Tidy with janitor
cohort |>
  tabyl(stage) |>
  adorn_pct_formatting(digits = 1) |>
  adorn_totals("row")

# Publication-ready via gt
cohort |> count(stage) |>
  mutate(pct = 100 * n / sum(n),
         cum_pct = cumsum(pct)) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(pct, cum_pct), decimals = 1)

heading: 3.58 Output & Results
text: ordered stages, cumulative column shows 87% patients stage III earlier.

heading: 3.59 Interpretation
text: Report frequency tables every categorical variable manuscript’s Table 1. Include counts percentages: “Stage III: 77 (30.8%)”. ordered variables, cumulative column often tells story clearly raw counts.

heading: 3.60 Practical Tips
text: Preserve factor level ordering reporting; ordered factors R maintain sequence automatically.Report denominator used percentages (total sample column total) avoid confusion.sparse levels, consider collapsing categories; report original collapsed table.Missing values: table() excludes NAs default; use useNA = "always" include .Publication tables benefit gtsummary::tbl_summary() handles frequencies continuous variables.

heading: 3.63 Introduction
text: geometric mean arithmetic mean log scale, back-transformed original scale. appropriate summary variables combine multiplicatively rather additively: antibody titres, concentrations, growth rates, investment returns. log-normal data, geometric mean median thus natural measure typicality.

heading: 3.64 Prerequisites
text: reader know arithmetic mean comfortable natural logarithms.

heading: 3.65 Theory
text: positive values \(x_1, \ldots, x_n\):\[\mathrm{GM}(x) = \left(\prod x_i\right)^{1/n} = \exp\!\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum \log x_i\right).\]second form numerically stable matches geometric mean computed practice. Key properties:\(\mathrm{GM}(x) \leq \bar{x}\), equality \(x_i\) equal (-GM inequality, consequence Jensen).log-normal data, geometric mean equals population median.ratio geometric mean arithmetic mean simple measure skewness.

heading: 3.66 Assumptions
text: values must strictly positive; single zero negative value renders geometric mean undefined.inference (confidence intervals), log-normality data typically assumed.

heading: 3.67 R Implementation
code:
library(psych)

set.seed(2026)
titres <- 2^sample(4:12, 50, replace = TRUE)  # antibody titres in 2^n form

mean(titres)
median(titres)
psych::geometric.mean(titres)
exp(mean(log(titres)))

# 95% CI for the geometric mean
n <- length(titres)
log_x <- log(titres)
log_mean <- mean(log_x)
log_se   <- sd(log_x) / sqrt(n)
exp(log_mean + c(-1, 1) * qt(0.975, n - 1) * log_se)

heading: 3.73 Introduction
text: harmonic mean reciprocal arithmetic mean reciprocals. appropriate averaging rates, speeds, ratios denominator fixed – example, traveller covers equal distances different speeds want average speed. machine learning, harmonic mean precision recall gives F1 score.

heading: 3.74 Prerequisites
text: reader know arithmetic mean comfortable taking reciprocals R.

heading: 3.75 Theory
text: positive values \(x_1, \ldots, x_n\),\[\mathrm{HM}(x) = \frac{n}{\sum 1/x_i}.\]harmonic mean always less equal geometric mean, less equal arithmetic mean: HM \(\leq\) GM \(\leq\) , equality \(x_i\) equal.F1 score binary classification harmonic mean precision recall:\[F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}\right)} \cdot \text{(rescaled)}.\]

heading: 3.76 Assumptions
text: values must strictly positive. Negative values zeros make harmonic mean undefined infinite.

heading: 3.77 R Implementation
code:
library(psych)

speeds <- c(40, 60, 80)  # km/h over three equal distances
psych::harmonic.mean(speeds)
mean(speeds)                  # incorrect for average speed over equal distances

# F1 score
precision <- 0.80
recall    <- 0.60
f1 <- 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)
f1

# Harmonic mean is appropriate for average rates
n <- 3
hm_speed <- n / sum(1 / speeds)
hm_speed

[1] 60          # arithmetic mean (incorrect for equal-distance travel)
[1] 0.686       # F1 score with P=0.80, R=0.60

heading: 3.79 Interpretation
text: Use harmonic mean averaging rates fixed numerator denominator (e.g., average speed distance constant). Use F1 form balancing two normalised scores values matter.

heading: 3.80 Practical Tips
text: Zero negative inputs break harmonic mean; guard .harmonic mean weights small values heavily; single small observation drives result .meta-analysis rates per unit time, harmonic-mean sample size sometimes used effective-sample-size calculations.F-beta score generalises F1: \(F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) PR / (\beta^2 P + R)\), \(\beta = 2\) emphasising recall \(\beta = 0.5\) emphasising precision.Reporting harmonic mean without explanation rare; specify appropriate summary methods.

heading: 3.83 Introduction
text: interquartile range (IQR) distance first third quartiles, \(Q_3 - Q_1\). measures width central 50% distribution robust outliers, since extreme values affect quartiles . IQR appears boxplots, Tukey fences, median-IQR convention reporting skewed biomedical variables.

heading: 3.84 Prerequisites
text: reader know quantiles quartiles , comfortable R’s quantile() function.

heading: 3.85 Theory
text: sample, \(Q_1\) \(Q_3\) 0.25 0.75 quantiles empirical CDF. IQR \[\mathrm{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1.\]Tukey’s hinges differ slightly R’s default quantiles; fivenum() returns hinges IQR() uses quantile() type = 7.normal distribution SD \(\sigma\), IQR equals \(2 \cdot 0.6745 \cdot \sigma \approx 1.349 \sigma\). Equivalently, dividing IQR 1.349 gives rough, robust estimate SD.Tukey fences \(Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}\) \(Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}\) define whiskers standard boxplot; points beyond flagged outliers.

heading: 3.86 Assumptions
text: IQR purely descriptive requires distributional assumption.

heading: 3.87 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(80, mean = 75, sd = 12)

quantile(x, probs = c(0.25, 0.75))
IQR(x)

# Tukey fences
q1 <- quantile(x, 0.25); q3 <- quantile(x, 0.75)
fences <- c(lower = q1 - 1.5 * IQR(x),
            upper = q3 + 1.5 * IQR(x))
fences
sum(x < fences[1] | x > fences[2])

# Robust SD from IQR (approximately)
IQR(x) / 1.349
sd(x)

lower  upper

heading: 3.90 Practical Tips

heading: 3.93 Introduction
text: Kurtosis fourth standardised moment distribution quantifies tail heaviness relative normal. High kurtosis means observations contribute disproportionately variance (“fat tails” finance). Low kurtosis means distribution fewer extreme values normal. Kurtosis matters many estimators tests assume normal-like tails; heavy tails break asymptotic approximations inflate Type error.

heading: 3.94 Prerequisites
text: reader know mean, SD, skewness , comfortable computing summary statistics R.

heading: 3.95 Theory
text:
population kurtosis \[\gamma_2 = E\!\left[\left( rac{X - \mu}{\sigma}ight)^4ight].\]standard normal distribution, \(\gamma_2 = 3\). convention report excess kurtosis \(\gamma_2 - 3\), 0 indicates normal-like tails, positive indicates heavier tails (leptokurtic), negative indicates lighter tails (platykurtic).Classical values:Normal: excess kurtosis = 0.Uniform: excess kurtosis = -1.2 (light tails).Logistic: excess kurtosis = 1.2.t-distribution \(
u\) df: excess kurtosis = \(6/(
u - 4)\) \(
u > 4\). \(
u = 5\) excess kurtosis 6; \(
u = 10\) 1.Exponential: excess kurtosis = 6.Laplace: excess kurtosis = 3.

heading: 3.96 Assumptions
text: Kurtosis descriptive statistic assumption. estimator population parameter, requires finite fourth moments.

heading: 3.97 R Implementation
code:
library(moments)

set.seed(2026)
norm_sample <- rnorm(5000)
t4_sample   <- rt(5000, df = 4)
unif_sample <- runif(5000)

c(normal  = kurtosis(norm_sample) - 3,
  t_df4   = kurtosis(t4_sample) - 3,
  uniform = kurtosis(unif_sample) - 3)

moments::anscombe.test(t4_sample)

heading: 3.98 Output & Results

heading: 3.99 Interpretation
text: Report excess kurtosis alongside skewness whenever distribution shape matters. value near zero “normal-like”; value 1 heavy-tailed distribution outlier-sensitive methods (mean, SD, OLS) may perform poorly.

heading: 3.100 Practical Tips

Highly robust; breakdown point 50%. multiplied 1.4826, consistent estimator SD normal data.R’s mad() function computes MAD median 1.4826 scaling constant default.

heading: 3.115 Theory
text: sample \(x_1, \ldots, x_n\):Range \(= \max x_i - \min x_i\). Simplest, fragile: uses two values, dominated outliers, information bulk distribution.Variance \(s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2\). average squared deviation mean. Additive independent sums. Divisor \(n - 1\) gives unbiased estimator population variance; divisor \(n\) gives MLE normality (biased).Standard deviation \(s = \sqrt{s^2}\). units data. SD normally distributed variable corresponds 68-95-99.7 rule.Median absolute deviation (MAD) \(= \text{median}|x_i - \text{median}(x)|\). Robust outliers; breakdown point 50%. Multiplied 1.4826 estimate SD normal distribution.Interquartile range (IQR) \(= Q_3 - Q_1\). width central 50% data. Reporting range informative small, clean samples summarising audit ranges.

text: dispersion measures purely descriptive; require distributional assumption. interpretation, however, depends distribution: SD 10 normal distribution implies 68% values within \(\pm 10\) mean, SD 10 heavy-tailed distribution .

heading: 3.117 R Implementation
text: mad() function defaults scaling constant 1.4826, makes consistent SD normal data. robustbase::Qn() Sn() alternative robust scale estimators higher efficiency MAD.
code:
library(dplyr)
library(robustbase)

set.seed(2026)

df <- tibble::tibble(
  symmetric    = rnorm(200, mean = 50, sd = 10),
  right_skewed = rlnorm(200, meanlog = log(50), sdlog = 0.6)
)

summarise_dispersion <- function(x) {
  tibble::tibble(
    n        = length(x),
    range    = max(x) - min(x),
    variance = var(x),
    sd       = sd(x),
    iqr      = IQR(x),
    mad      = mad(x, constant = 1.4826),
    mad_raw  = mad(x, constant = 1)
  )
}

dispersion_tbl <- df |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") |>
  group_by(variable) |>
  summarise(
    summarise_dispersion(value),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

dispersion_tbl

Qn_scale <- robustbase::Qn(df$right_skewed)
Sn_scale <- robustbase::Sn(df$right_skewed)
c(Qn = Qn_scale, Sn = Sn_scale)

heading: 3.133 Introduction
text: Central tendency dispersion alone describe distribution completely: two samples mean SD can look different one symmetric skewed, one thin tails heavy tails. Skewness kurtosis conventional third- fourth-moment summaries capture shape features.

heading: 3.134 Prerequisites
text: reader know mean, variance, standard deviation , understand histogram shows.

heading: 3.135 Theory
text: skewness distribution measures asymmetry:\[g_1 = \frac{1}{n} \sum \left(\frac{x_i - \bar{x}}{s}\right)^3.\]Positive skewness indicates right-tailed distribution (mean > median); negative skewness indicates left tail. normal distribution, \(g_1 = 0\). Absolute values greater 1 considered substantial.kurtosis measures tail heaviness:\[g_2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum \left(\frac{x_i - \bar{x}}{s}\right)^4.\]normal, \(g_2 = 3\). convention, excess kurtosis reported \(g_2 - 3\): zero means normal-like tails, positive means heavier (leptokurtic), negative means lighter (platykurtic). High kurtosis signals observations contribute large share variance; finance “fat tails” break many standard models.statistics moment-based therefore sensitive outliers. small samples, estimates noisy.

heading: 3.136 Assumptions
text: Skewness kurtosis descriptive require distributional assumption. asymptotic standard errors assume independent observations finite sixth moment skewness eighth kurtosis.

heading: 3.137 R Implementation
code:
library(moments)
library(psych)

set.seed(2026)

symmetric   <- rnorm(5000, mean = 0, sd = 1)
right_skewed <- rlnorm(5000, meanlog = 0, sdlog = 0.6)
heavy_tailed <- rt(5000, df = 4)

shape_summary <- function(x, label) {
  data.frame(
    variable = label,
    skewness = moments::skewness(x),
    kurtosis = moments::kurtosis(x),
    excess_kurtosis = moments::kurtosis(x) - 3
  )
}

rbind(
  shape_summary(symmetric,    "Normal"),
  shape_summary(right_skewed, "Log-normal"),
  shape_summary(heavy_tailed, "t, df=4")
)

psych::describe(data.frame(symmetric, right_skewed, heavy_tailed),
                skew = TRUE, ranges = FALSE)

heading: 3.143 Introduction
text: Outlier detection diagnostic step, cleaning step: flagged points investigated, automatically removed. Several rules produce flags different premises; different trade-sensitivity robustness. page covers common univariate rules assumptions.

heading: 3.144 Prerequisites
text: reader know mean, median, SD, IQR .

heading: 3.145 Theory
text: 1.5 x IQR fence (Tukey): observations outside \([Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}, \ Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}]\) mild outliers; \(3 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}\) marks extremes. Robust distribution-free.Z-score (3-sigma): \(|z_i| > 3\) \(z_i = (x_i - \bar{x})/s\). Sensitive outliers trying flag (masking).Hampel identifier: \(|x_i - \text{median}| > 3 \cdot \mathrm{MAD}\). Robust variant z-score uses median MAD.Grubbs’ test: formal test extreme value normality. Gives p-value sensitive normality assumption.Masking swamping two pitfalls: masking occurs true outliers inflate SD hide ; swamping occurs one outlier drags neighbours across threshold, falsely flagging .

heading: 3.146 Assumptions
text: IQR fence Hampel: distributional assumption; robust.Z-score: approximately normal distribution.Grubbs’ test: normality single suspected outlier.

heading: 3.147 R Implementation
code:
library(outliers)

set.seed(2026)
x <- c(rnorm(48, mean = 50, sd = 8), 95, 110)

# IQR fence
q <- quantile(x, c(0.25, 0.75))
iqr <- IQR(x)
fences <- c(q[1] - 1.5 * iqr, q[2] + 1.5 * iqr)
which(x < fences[1] | x > fences[2])

# Z-score
z <- (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
which(abs(z) > 3)

# Hampel
h <- (x - median(x)) / mad(x)
which(abs(h) > 3)

# Grubbs' test for the single most extreme
grubbs.test(x)

heading: 3.148 Output & Results
text: IQR Hampel rules identify extreme values; z-score, inflated values, identifies extreme. Grubbs’ test rejects null extreme point distribution.
code:
IQR fence flags:   [49, 50]
z-score flags:     [50]
Hampel flags:      [49, 50]
Grubbs: G = 2.81, p = 0.03

heading: 3.149 Interpretation
text: Report rule applied many observations flagged. “Two observations exceeded Tukey 1.5 x IQR upper fence; verified accurate data-entry values retained primary analysis” defensible sentence.

heading: 3.150 Practical Tips
text: Flag, investigate, decide. Never delete silently.skewed data, IQR fences still flag normal-range observations outliers; consider log-transformation first.multivariate outliers, use Mahalanobis distance; regression diagnostics, use Cook’s distance.Grubbs’ test assumes normality; non-normal data prefer IQR Hampel rule.Report sensitivity analyses re-run primary analysis without flagged observations; conclusion robust.

heading: 3.153 Introduction
text: percentile rank answers “fraction observations fall value?” converts absolute measurement relative position sample distribution. paediatrics, growth charts give child’s weight percentile; cognitive testing, IQ score comes percentile rank; bioinformatics, gene’s expression often reported percentile across reference panel.

heading: 3.154 Prerequisites
text: reader know quantile comfortable sorted vectors R.

heading: 3.155 Theory
text: sample \(x_1, \ldots, x_n\), percentile rank value \(y\) \[\mathrm{PR}(y) = 100 \cdot \frac{\#\{x_i \leq y\}}{n}.\]Several refinements exist: “mid-rank” form counts half ties; “plotting position” form uses \((- )/(n + 1 - 2a)\) various \(\) (e.g., Blom’s \(= 3/8\)).single value, computing percentile rank inverse computing quantile: 75th percentile \(q_{0.75}\), \(\mathrm{PR}(q_{0.75}) \approx 75\).

heading: 3.156 Assumptions
text: Purely descriptive; assumes reference distribution appropriate value ranked.

heading: 3.157 R Implementation
code:
library(dplyr)

set.seed(2026)
growth_chart <- rnorm(1000, mean = 15.0, sd = 2.2)   # reference weights (kg)
patient      <- 17.8                                   # a patient's weight

pr_patient <- 100 * mean(growth_chart <= patient)
pr_patient

# Percentile ranks for all observations in a cohort
cohort <- data.frame(weight = rnorm(30, 15.5, 2.0))
cohort$rank    <- rank(cohort$weight)
cohort$pr      <- 100 * cohort$rank / nrow(cohort)
cohort$pr_mid  <- 100 * (rank(cohort$weight) - 0.5) / nrow(cohort)
head(cohort)

# ECDF equivalent
ec <- ecdf(growth_chart)
100 * ec(patient)

1  13.29    1   3.3   1.67
2  14.15    6  20.0  18.33
3  15.01   11  36.7  35.00

heading: 3.159 Interpretation
text: Report percentile ranks growth, development, norming contexts. clinical note: “body weight 17.8 kg (89th percentile age/sex)”. percentile tells reader unusual value relative reference.

heading: 3.160 Practical Tips
text: Percentile ranks depend reference sample; always state reference used (national norms, internal cohort, etc.).Ties handled rank() via averaging, minimum, maximum; specify ties.method convention matters.small reference samples, percentile ranks unstable; use smoothing (LMS method) growth chart applications.percentile rank exactly 100 usually reported; “99th percentile” conventional phrasing sample maximum.confuse percentile rank (position distribution) percentile (value specified position).

heading: 3.163 Introduction
text: quantile cut-point divides distribution cumulative probability: \(p\)-quantile value proportion \(p\) data lies. median 0.5-quantile; quartiles 0.25, 0.50, 0.75 quantiles; percentiles quantiles rescaled 0-100 range. Quantiles underlie boxplots, five-number summaries, reference ranges lab medicine, non-parametric confidence intervals.

heading: 3.164 Prerequisites
text: reader know cumulative distribution function comfortable sorted vectors R.

heading: 3.165 Theory
text: sample \(x_1, \ldots, x_n\) sorted \(x_{(1)} \leq x_{(2)} \leq \ldots \leq x_{(n)}\), sample \(p\)-quantile uniquely defined \(p \cdot (n+1)\) integer. R offers nine definitions, selected via type argument quantile():type = 1: inverse empirical CDF, giving step-function quantile.type = 4: linear interpolation empirical CDF (SAS default).type = 6: linear interpolation based plotting positions (Minitab, SPSS).type = 7: R’s default; linear interpolation order statistics offset.type = 8: approximately unbiased continuous distributions (Hyndman-Fan recommendation).different types give slightly different numeric values small samples; converge \(n\) grows. Type 7 default R’s quantile(), reproducibility across software requires specifying type.

heading: 3.166 Assumptions
text: Quantiles purely descriptive; require distributional assumption.

heading: 3.167 R Implementation
code:
library(dplyr)

set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(60, mean = 75, sd = 10)

# Default quantile types
quantile(x, probs = c(0.25, 0.50, 0.75))

# Compare five R types
sapply(c(1, 4, 6, 7, 8), function(t) quantile(x, probs = 0.25, type = t))

# Five-number summary via fivenum
fivenum(x)

# Deciles (10th, 20th, ..., 90th percentiles)
quantile(x, probs = seq(0.1, 0.9, by = 0.1))

# Quantiles by group
diabetes <- tibble::tibble(
  arm = factor(rep(c("Placebo", "Active"), each = 40)),
  hba1c = c(rnorm(40, 7.4, 0.6), rnorm(40, 6.8, 0.7))
)
diabetes |> group_by(arm) |>
  summarise(q25 = quantile(hba1c, 0.25),
            q50 = quantile(hba1c, 0.50),
            q75 = quantile(hba1c, 0.75))

heading: 3.168 Output & Results
text: five types differ less 2 units \(n = 60\). diabetes, active arm’s quartiles shifted lower placebo’s, expected.

heading: 3.169 Interpretation
text: Report quantiles distribution skewed, ordinal, otherwise well-summarised mean: median IQR (25th 75th percentiles) standard form. reference-range contexts (clinical chemistry), 2.5 97.5 percentiles define central 95% “normal range”.

heading: 3.170 Practical Tips
text: Always specify type quantile() reproducing results across software.boxplot uses Type 2 (fivenum()) hinges; differs Type 7 quantiles.Quantiles respect monotonic transformations: quantile(log(x), 0.5) == log(quantile(x, 0.5)) (exactly median, approximately others depending type).small samples (\(n < 10\)), quantile estimates unstable; report order statistics directly instead.Empirical quantiles biased estimators population quantiles; use Harrell-Davis (Hmisc::hdquantile) smoother estimator.

heading: 3.173 Introduction
text: robust statistic one remains useful data deviate idealised model – heavy tails, outliers, skewed contamination. Classical estimators like mean SD efficient normality break even modest contamination. Robust alternatives sacrifice little efficiency dramatic stability, default many applied fields data quality guaranteed.

heading: 3.174 Prerequisites
text: reader know mean, median, SD, MAD. Familiarity outlier concepts helps.

heading: 3.175 Theory
text: Two key concepts robustness theory:Breakdown point. largest fraction contaminated observations estimator can tolerate giving arbitrary answer. arithmetic mean breakdown 0: single extreme point can push arbitrarily far. median breakdown 0.5: half sample can contaminated median affected.Influence function. Measures infinitesimal effect adding single observation \(x\) estimator. arithmetic mean linear, unbounded influence function; median bounded (step-shaped) influence function.Robust estimators aim high breakdown point bounded influence function, typically moderate loss efficiency normal model.Robust alternatives classical summaries:

heading: 3.176 Assumptions
text: Robustness explicit relaxation distributional assumptions; asks estimator behaves deviation, whether deviation present.

heading: 3.177 R Implementation
code:
library(robustbase)
library(MASS)

set.seed(2026)
clean <- rnorm(100, mean = 50, sd = 10)
contaminated <- c(clean[1:95], rnorm(5, mean = 200, sd = 5))

classical <- c(mean = mean(contaminated), sd = sd(contaminated))
robust    <- c(median = median(contaminated),
               mad    = mad(contaminated),
               Qn     = Qn(contaminated),
               Sn     = Sn(contaminated))

rbind(classical = c(classical, NA, NA), robust)

# Robust regression example
n <- 80
x <- rnorm(n)
y <- 2 + 1.5 * x + rnorm(n)
y[c(5, 10, 15)] <- y[c(5, 10, 15)] + c(15, -15, 20)  # inject outliers

coef(lm(y ~ x))
coef(rlm(y ~ x))          # M-estimator
coef(lmrob(y ~ x))        # MM-estimator with high breakdown

heading: 3.178 Output & Results
text: Classical mean pulled 57.8 five extreme values; every robust estimator remains near true 50.regression: OLS slope 1.23 (pulled outliers); rlm gives 1.47 (close truth 1.5); lmrob gives 1.48.

heading: 3.179 Interpretation
text: Use robust statistics default biomedical work outliers heavy tails typical. robust summary plus classical one can reported side side; divergence signals contamination warrants investigation.

heading: 3.180 Practical Tips
text: MM-estimators (lmrob) combine high breakdown high efficiency modern robust-regression default.Robust methods license ignore outliers; always investigate reporting.heavy-tailed data, Student-t likelihood (brms heavy package) alternative robust M-estimators.Robust confidence intervals usually narrower non-robust CI contaminated data, reflecting estimator’s stability.Specialised packages: robustbase, WRS2 (Wilcox), quantreg (quantile regression), MASS::rlm.

heading: 3.183 Introduction
text: Skewness third standardised moment distribution; quantifies asymmetric distribution around mean. Zero skewness corresponds perfect symmetry; positive skewness indicates long right tail; negative skewness indicates long left tail. Many biomedical variables (lab values, incubation times, survival times) right-skewed; income wealth distributions classically right-skewed long tails.

heading: 3.184 Prerequisites
text: reader know mean SD , seen histograms.

heading: 3.185 Theory
text: population skewness \[\gamma_1 = E\!\left[\left(\frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}\right)^3\right].\]Several sample estimators exist different small-sample biases. classical moment estimator \(g_1 = m_3 / m_2^{3/2}\) \(m_k = \frac{1}{n} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^k\). SAS/SPSS/psych use bias-corrected form \(G_1 = \sqrt{n(n-1)}/(n-2) \cdot g_1\). Interpretation: \(|g_1| < 0.5\) approximately symmetric, \(0.5 < |g_1| < 1\) moderately skewed, \(|g_1| > 1\) highly skewed.

heading: 3.186 Assumptions
text: descriptive statistic, skewness assumptions. estimator, requires finite third moments, fails heavy-tailed distributions like Cauchy.

heading: 3.187 R Implementation
code:
library(moments)

set.seed(2026)
sym   <- rnorm(2000, 0, 1)
rskew <- rlnorm(2000, meanlog = 0, sdlog = 0.7)
lskew <- -rlnorm(2000, meanlog = 0, sdlog = 0.7)

c(symmetric = skewness(sym),
  right     = skewness(rskew),
  left      = skewness(lskew))

# log transform reduces right skewness
skewness(log(rskew))

heading: 3.188 Output & Results
text: Log-transforming right-skewed sample drops skewness 2.1 approximately 0; standard remedy biomedical lab values.
code:
symmetric      right       left
    0.013      2.134     -2.090

heading: 3.189 Interpretation
text: Report skewness variable whose histogram departs symmetry. \(|g_1| > 1\), mean pulled away centre mass; median better point summary, variable transformed.

heading: 3.190 Practical Tips
text: Log transformation default remedy right-skewed positive data.Visual inspection beats numerical thresholds; histogram makes shape immediately visible.Skewness small samples noisy; avoid thresholds \(n = 30\).Pearson skewness coefficient \(3(\mathrm{mean} - \mathrm{median})/\mathrm{SD}\) robust alternative.formal tests skewness, use D’Agostino’s test (moments::agostino.test).

heading: 3.193 Introduction
text: z-score expresses observation distance mean units standard deviation. Standardisation often applied clustering, PCA, ridge/lasso regression, procedure whose output unit-sensitive. data outliers, robust variant using median MAD safer.

heading: 3.194 Prerequisites
text: reader know mean, SD, median, MAD .

heading: 3.195 Theory
text: classical z-score observation \(x_i\) \[z_i = (x_i - \bar{x}) / s.\]standardisation, sample mean 0 sample SD 1. values dimensionless comparable across variables measured different scales.robust z uses median scaled MAD:\[z_i^{\text{robust}} = (x_i - \text{median}(x)) / (1.4826 \cdot \mathrm{MAD}(x)).\]reduces classical z normal data resists inflation scale estimator outliers.Standardisation normalisation. Min-max normalisation rescales \([0, 1]\): \((x_i - \min(x)) / (\max(x) - \min(x))\). use; z-scores preserve relative distances better.

heading: 3.196 Assumptions
text: Purely algebraic; distributional assumption. choice classical robust z depends whether outliers present.

heading: 3.197 R Implementation
code:
library(robustbase)

set.seed(2026)
x <- c(rnorm(50, mean = 100, sd = 10), 200, 250)

z_classical <- scale(x)
z_robust    <- (x - median(x)) / (1.4826 * mad(x, constant = 1))

range(z_classical)
range(z_robust)

cohort <- data.frame(
  age  = rnorm(100, 58, 12),
  sbp  = rnorm(100, 130, 18),
  crp  = rlnorm(100, log(3), 0.6)
)
z_cohort <- scale(cohort)
colMeans(z_cohort)
apply(z_cohort, 2, sd)

heading: 3.198 Output & Results
text: robust z flags outlier 250 > 15 SDs median, classical z gives 6. Classical z understates deviation extreme point inflated sample SD.
code:
range(z_classical): -1.69   6.11
range(z_robust):    -2.02  15.04

heading: 3.199 Interpretation
text: Report z-scores comparing observations different scales. Biological examples: centred scaled gene-expression values across samples; cognitive-test scores scaled reference population.

heading: 3.200 Practical Tips
text: scale() returns matrix; wrap .data.frame() using downstream tidyverse functions.Save center scale attributes apply standardisation new test data – critical predictive modelling.skewed variables, log-transform standardising.robust z simple effective first-pass outlier filter: \(|z^{\text{robust}}| > 3\) flags candidates.standardise outcome regression interpretability desired; standardise predictors comparing coefficients.

heading: 3.203 Introduction
text: stem--leaf plot, introduced John Tukey, compact display shows distribution small sample preserving every data value. observation split “stem” (leading digits) “leaf” (trailing digit); stems listed vertically leaves written next stem order. samples 100 observations, resulting ASCII plot poor man’s histogram can typed notebook.

heading: 3.205 Theory

Modern software rarely uses stem--leaf publication figures, remains quick inspection tool R’s console.

stem(x)

heading: 3.213 Introduction
text: Split-apply-combine workhorse pattern applied data analysis: split data grouping variable, apply summary function group, combine results. produces per-group means, medians, counts, statistics populate Table 1 scientific papers.

heading: 3.214 Prerequisites
text: reader know mean, median, SD , comfortable R’s dplyr pipe syntax.

heading: 3.215 Theory
text: dataset \(n\) observations grouping variable \(g\) taking values \(g_1, \ldots, g_k\), split-apply-combine computes\[s_j = f(\{x_i : g_i = g_j\})\]group \(j\), \(f\) summary function (mean, median, quantile, aggregator). returned object \(k\) rows, one per group.R implements via base aggregate(), tapply(), (), via modern dplyr::group_by() |> summarise() data.table equivalents.

heading: 3.216 Assumptions
text: Purely descriptive; distributional assumption. grouping variable enough observations per group summary meaningful (mean based \(n = 2\) noisy).

heading: 3.217 R Implementation
code:
library(dplyr)
library(data.table)
library(gtsummary)

set.seed(2026)
trial <- tibble::tibble(
  arm      = factor(rep(c("Placebo", "Active"), each = 75)),
  sex      = factor(sample(c("F", "M"), 150, replace = TRUE)),
  age      = round(rnorm(150, 58, 12)),
  hba1c    = round(c(rnorm(75, 7.3, 0.7), rnorm(75, 6.9, 0.8)), 1),
  event    = rbinom(150, 1, 0.15)
)

# dplyr
trial |>
  group_by(arm) |>
  summarise(
    n         = n(),
    age_mean  = mean(age),
    age_sd    = sd(age),
    hba1c_med = median(hba1c),
    hba1c_iqr = IQR(hba1c),
    event_rate = mean(event)
  )

# Multi-variable grouping
trial |>
  group_by(arm, sex) |>
  summarise(across(c(age, hba1c), list(mean = mean, sd = sd)),
            .groups = "drop")

# data.table
dt <- as.data.table(trial)
dt[, .(n = .N, age_mean = mean(age), hba1c_median = median(hba1c)),
   by = .(arm)]

# Publication-ready
trial |>
  tbl_summary(by = arm, include = c(age, sex, hba1c, event),
              statistic = list(all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})"))

heading: 3.223 Introduction
text: trimmed mean drops proportion observations tail computing mean. Winsorised mean replaces observations nearest non-trimmed values averages. estimators compromise arithmetic mean (efficient sensitive outliers) median (robust inefficient). useful data heavy-tailed want retain sample’s information.

heading: 3.224 Prerequisites
text: reader know arithmetic mean median .

heading: 3.225 Theory
text: sample size \(n\) trim proportion \(\alpha \[0, 0.5)\):Trimmed mean: sort data, discard smallest largest \(\lfloor n\alpha \rfloor\) observations, average rest.Winsorised mean: replace smallest \(\lfloor n\alpha \rfloor\) values \((\lfloor n\alpha \rfloor + 1)\)-th smallest, similarly upper end; compute mean.Special cases: \(\alpha = 0\) gives arithmetic mean; \(\alpha = 0.5\) gives median (exactly odd \(n\), almost even \(n\)). 10% 20% trim common compromise.Trimmed means positive breakdown point (proportional \(\alpha\)): trim 20% tolerates 20% contamination without affected. Winsorised means breakdown retain influence replaced values “pinning” behaviour.

heading: 3.226 Assumptions
text: estimators descriptive; distributional assumption. efficiency depends distribution: 20% trimmed mean nearly efficient arithmetic mean normality much robust heavy tails.

heading: 3.227 R Implementation

library(psych)
library(DescTools)

set.seed(2026)
x <- c(rnorm(48, mean = 50, sd = 8), 200, 250)  # two outliers

mean(x)
median(x)
mean(x, trim = 0.10)
mean(x, trim = 0.20)
DescTools::Winsorize(x, probs = c(0.10, 0.90)) |> mean()

text: trimmed Winsorised means align closely median, demonstrating robustness. arithmetic mean dragged two extreme points.

[1] 50.30       # median
[1] 51.82       # 10% trimmed mean
[1] 50.78       # 20% trimmed mean
[1] 51.50       # 10% Winsorised mean

heading: 3.229 Interpretation
text: Report trimmed Winsorised mean outliers present, exclusion justified, want efficient summary median. Explicitly state trim proportion.

heading: 3.230 Practical Tips
text: 10% trim useful default; 20% stronger loses efficiency.Don’t let trim serve clean-genuine heterogeneity; investigate outliers first.Inference trimmed means requires Yuen test (WRS2::yuen) rather usual t-test.Winsorising preferred want retain sample size soften influence extremes.Combined robust scale (MAD, Sn), trimmed mean / SD pair gives fully robust location / spread summary.

heading: 3.233 Introduction
text: Variance standard deviation widely used dispersion measures applied statistics. variance mean squared deviation mean; SD square root, returning original units. underlie normal distribution, central limit theorem, regression, every parametric test refers “sigma”.

heading: 3.234 Prerequisites
text: reader know mean comfortable squaring, summing, taking square roots R.

heading: 3.235 Theory
text: sample \(x_1, \ldots, x_n\):Population variance (data whole population): \(\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum (x_i - \mu)^2\).Sample variance (data sample): \(s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2\).\(n - 1\) divisor (Bessel’s correction) makes \(s^2\) unbiased estimator \(\sigma^2\). Using \(n\) systematically underestimate \(\sigma^2\) \(\bar{x}\) estimated data.sample standard deviation \(s = \sqrt{s^2}\). Even though \(s^2\) unbiased, \(s\) biased downward square root concave (Jensen’s inequality). bias shrinks like \(1/n\) negligible \(n > 30\); unbiased estimator exists (c_4 correction SPC) rarely used applied work.R’s var() sd() use \(n - 1\) divisor default. get MLE version, compute mean((x - mean(x))^2).

heading: 3.236 Assumptions
text: Variance SD descriptive; distributional assumption required. inferential use (standard error mean \(s / \sqrt{n}\)), usual iid assumption applies.

heading: 3.237 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(60, mean = 100, sd = 15)

var(x)
sd(x)

# MLE (biased) version with divisor n
mean((x - mean(x))^2)
sqrt(mean((x - mean(x))^2))

# Bias of s: theoretical factor c_4(n) for normal data
c4 <- function(n) sqrt(2 / (n - 1)) * gamma(n / 2) / gamma((n - 1) / 2)
sd_unbiased <- sd(x) / c4(length(x))
sd_unbiased

heading: 3.238 Output & Results
text: \(n = 60\), difference biased unbiased SD fraction percent, irrelevant applied work.
code:
[1] 210.5       # s^2 with divisor n-1
[1]  14.5       # s
[1] 207.0       # MLE variance
[1]  14.4       # MLE SD (biased)
[1]  14.6       # bias-corrected SD

heading: 3.239 Interpretation
text: Report variance scale squared units interpretable (e.g., variance portfolio returns). Report SD scale original units desired. paper: “mean 100.2 (SD 14.5)” universally readable.

heading: 3.240 Practical Tips
text: Always specify divisor mean reporting; R’s default \(n - 1\), statistician’s convention.small samples, biased-vs-unbiased distinction matters; applied work (n > 30) .SD units data; variance units squared. Don’t mix single report.variance reported without associated mean, check whether actually standard error (smaller \(\sqrt{n}\)).skewed data, SD useful summary; use IQR instead.

heading: 3.243 Introduction
text: weighted mean generalisation arithmetic mean observation carries positive weight reflects importance, reliability, inclusion probability. Weighted means appear survey sampling (weights reciprocals inclusion probabilities), meta-analysis (weights inverse-variance), weighted regression.

heading: 3.244 Prerequisites
text: reader know arithmetic mean comfortable R function weighted.mean().

heading: 3.245 Theory
text: Given observations \(x_1, \ldots, x_n\) positive weights \(w_1, \ldots, w_n\), weighted mean \[\bar{x}_w = \frac{\sum w_i x_i}{\sum w_i}.\]weights equal, reduces arithmetic mean. weights re-scale observation’s contribution. inverse-variance weighting, \(w_i = 1 / s_i^2\), gives precise observations influence.weighted variance \[s^2_w = \frac{\sum w_i (x_i - \bar{x}_w)^2}{\sum w_i - 1}\](using frequency-weight convention). survey weights, different denominator used.

heading: 3.246 Assumptions
text: Weights must positive.choice weight reflects real asymmetry (inclusion probability, precision, repetition count); arbitrary weighting meaningless.

heading: 3.247 R Implementation
code:
library(survey)

# Meta-analysis: four studies with mean and SE
studies <- data.frame(
  mean = c(1.20, 0.95, 1.40, 1.10),
  se   = c(0.15, 0.25, 0.10, 0.20)
)
studies$w <- 1 / studies$se^2

pooled_mean <- weighted.mean(studies$mean, studies$w)
pooled_se   <- sqrt(1 / sum(studies$w))
c(pooled_mean = pooled_mean, pooled_se = pooled_se)

# Survey weights: 200 respondents, sample weight = 1 / inclusion probability
set.seed(2026)
resp <- data.frame(
  sbp = rnorm(200, 132, 15),
  w   = sample(c(0.5, 1.0, 2.0), 200, replace = TRUE)
)
weighted.mean(resp$sbp, resp$w)

heading: 3.248 Output & Results

      1.236        0.075

heading: 3.249 Interpretation
text: Meta-analytic pooling gives study-level weights reflect much study contributes combined evidence. Survey weights make computed mean estimate population mean rather sample mean. Always report weights used.

heading: 3.250 Practical Tips
text: Negative zero weights invalid; check input.survey data, use survey package (svymean, svydesign) rather weighted.mean() get correct SE accounting design.Inverse-variance weighting optimal random-effects meta-analysis \(\tau^2\) zero; otherwise weights use \(w_i = 1/(s_i^2 + \tau^2)\).weights frequencies (integer counts), weighted mean equals ordinary mean expanded dataset.Reporting “weighted mean” without specifying weights ambiguous; include weight definition methods.

heading: 4.3 Introduction
text: Bayes’ theorem rule inverting conditional probabilities: given \(P(B \mid )\), tells us \(P(\mid B)\). mathematical bridge diagnostic sensitivity positive predictive value, forward simulation inverse inference, likelihood function posterior distribution. Every Bayesian analysis, every clinical prediction, every probabilistic classifier application Bayes’ theorem disguise.

heading: 4.4 Prerequisites
text: Conditional probability law total probability.

heading: 4.5 Theory
text: events \(\) \(B\) \(P(B) > 0\),\[P(\mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid ) \, P()}{P(B)}.\]\(\{A_i\}\) forms partition sample space, denominator expands law total probability:\[P(A_i \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A_i) \, P(A_i)}{\sum_j P(B \mid A_j) \, P(A_j)}.\]Bayesian vocabulary:\(P()\) prior: believed observing \(B\).\(P(B \mid )\) likelihood: likely \(B\) hypothesis.\(P(\mid B)\) posterior: updated belief \(\) observing \(B\).\(P(B)\) marginal likelihood “evidence”.Posterior \(\propto\) likelihood \(\times\) prior single important slogan Bayesian statistics.

heading: 4.6 Assumptions
text: \(P(B) > 0\); otherwise conditioning undefined.prior \(P()\) must specified; choosing domain knowledge enters.

heading: 4.7 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

# Diagnostic test: prior = prevalence, likelihood = sensitivity and specificity
sensitivity <- 0.92
specificity <- 0.97
prevalences <- seq(0.001, 0.5, length.out = 100)

ppv <- sensitivity * prevalences /
       (sensitivity * prevalences + (1 - specificity) * (1 - prevalences))

df <- data.frame(prevalence = prevalences, ppv = ppv)

ggplot(df, aes(prevalence, ppv)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2A9D8F", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.01, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#F4A261") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.30, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#F4A261") +
  labs(x = "Disease prevalence",
       y = "Positive predictive value",
       title = "PPV depends strongly on prevalence (Bayes' theorem)") +
  theme_minimal()

c(prevalence_001 = ppv[which.min(abs(prevalences - 0.01))],
  prevalence_030 = ppv[which.min(abs(prevalences - 0.30))])

heading: 4.8 Output & Results
text: 1% prevalence, positive test 92%-sensitive, 97%-specific assay gives 24% probability disease. 30% prevalence, test yields 93% PPV. test characteristics unchanged; posterior depends crucially prior.
code:
prevalence_001 prevalence_030
        0.2368         0.9290

heading: 4.9 Interpretation
text: prevalence dependence PPV canonical real-world application Bayes’ theorem. Reporting “test 92% sensitivity 97% specificity” meaningless without prevalence intended use population; clinically relevant quantity PPV.

heading: 4.10 Practical Tips
text: Never cite sensitivity / specificity isolation discussing diagnostic utility; always give PPV NPV target prevalence.Bayes’ theorem scales trivially many hypotheses via partition form; continuous parameter spaces, sums become integrals.Prior choice contentious Bayesian applications; weakly informative priors (Cauchy, normal wide scale) standard defaults.Updating sequentially: posterior observation 1 becomes prior observation 2. Chains updates equivalent single update observations.likelihood ratio \(P(B \mid ) / P(B \mid ^c)\) converts prior odds \(\) posterior odds multiplicatively – often cleaner formulation raw probabilities.

heading: 4.13 Introduction
text: Bernoulli distribution models single binary trial: success vs. failure, disease vs. healthy, click vs. click. simplest non-trivial distribution building block binomial, geometric, negative binomial distributions.

heading: 4.14 Prerequisites
text: Random variables basic probability.

heading: 4.15 Theory
text: random variable \(X\) Bernoulli(\(p\)) takes values \(\{0, 1\}\) \[P(X = 1) = p, \qquad P(X = 0) = 1 - p.\]PMF: \(p_X(x) = p^x (1 - p)^{1 - x}\) \(x \\{0, 1\}\).Moments:\(E[X] = p\).\(\mathrm{Var}(X) = p(1 - p)\), maximised \(p = 0.5\) (value 0.25).\(E[X^k] = p\) every \(k \geq 1\) (since \(X^k = X\)).sum \(n\) iid Bernoulli\((p)\) variables Binomial\((n, p)\).MLE \(p\) iid sample \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\): \(\hat{p} = \bar{X}\), sample proportion.

heading: 4.16 Assumptions
text: Independence across trials common probability \(p\). Violations either produce overdispersion (\(p\) varies) correlation (trials dependent).

heading: 4.17 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

p <- 0.3
n <- 10000

# Sample from Bernoulli(p) via rbinom with size = 1
X <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = p)

# Sample mean is the MLE of p
c(p_hat = mean(X), p_true = p)

# Sample variance approximates p(1 - p)
c(sample_var = var(X), theoretical = p * (1 - p))

# 95% Wald confidence interval for p
se_p <- sqrt(mean(X) * (1 - mean(X)) / n)
c(lower = mean(X) - 1.96 * se_p, upper = mean(X) + 1.96 * se_p)

# Wilson (better for small p or small n)
binom::binom.confint(sum(X), n, conf.level = 0.95, methods = "wilson")

heading: 4.18 Output & Results
text: Empirical mean \(0.3002 \approx p\); empirical variance \(0.2101 \approx p(1-p) = 0.21\); Wald Wilson CIs agree range.
code:

   0.3002     0.3000

sample_var theoretical
   0.2101      0.2100

   lower    upper
  0.2912   0.3092

method    x    n    mean    lower    upper
Wilson 3002 10000  0.3002   0.2912   0.3094

heading: 4.19 Interpretation
text: applied work, Bernoulli underlying distribution every binary outcome: adverse event, responder status, screen-positive. Reporting usually aggregates proportions across \(n\) independent Bernoullis – .e., binomial framework.

heading: 4.20 Practical Tips
text: small samples extreme \(p\) (near 0 1), use Wilson Clopper-Pearson CIs instead Wald.Overdispersion (variance exceeding \(p(1-p)\)) grouped data signals unobserved heterogeneity; consider beta-binomial mixed-effects logistic regression.rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = p) direct way simulate Bernoullis; sample(0:1, n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(1 - p, p)) equivalent.Bernoulli likelihood foundation logistic regression; MLE \(p\) regression coefficients requires logit link.Two dependent Bernoullis require specification joint distribution – usually via copula 2x2 joint PMF.

heading: 4.23 Introduction
text: beta distribution flexible family probabilities unit interval \([0, 1]\). natural distribution proportions, success probabilities, measurements bounded two fixed limits. Bayesian inference, conjugate prior binomial Bernoulli likelihoods.

heading: 4.24 Prerequisites
text: Continuous distributions, Bayesian inference basics.

heading: 4.25 Theory
text: beta random variable shape parameters \(> 0\) \(b > 0\) PDF\[f(x) = \frac{1}{B(, b)} x^{-1} (1-x)^{b-1}, \qquad 0 \leq x \leq 1,\]\(B(, b) = \Gamma() \Gamma(b) / \Gamma(+ b)\) beta function.Moments:\(E[X] = / (+ b)\).\(\mathrm{Var}(X) = ab / [(+ b)^2 (+ b + 1)]\).Special cases:\(= b = 1\): Uniform\((0, 1)\).\(= b = 0.5\): Jeffreys prior binomial probability.\(, b > 1\): unimodal density mode \((- 1)/(+ b - 2)\).\(< 1\) \(b < 1\): bimodal (poles 0 1).Conjugacy: given Bernoulli/Binomial likelihood success probability \(\theta\) Beta\((, b)\) prior \(\theta\), posterior observing \(k\) successes \(n\) trials Beta\((+ k, b + n - k)\).

heading: 4.26 Assumptions
text: variable must bounded 0 1. probabilities near 0 1, distribution can pole; use care simulating.

heading: 4.27 R Implementation
code:
curve(dbeta(x, 2, 5),  from = 0, to = 1, col = "steelblue", lwd = 2,
      main = "Beta densities", ylab = "f(x)")
curve(dbeta(x, 5, 2),  add = TRUE, col = "#F4A261", lwd = 2)
curve(dbeta(x, 0.5, 0.5), add = TRUE, col = "#6A4C93", lwd = 2)
curve(dbeta(x, 2, 2),  add = TRUE, col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2)
legend("top", legend = c("Beta(2,5)", "Beta(5,2)", "Beta(0.5,0.5)", "Beta(2,2)"),
       col = c("steelblue", "#F4A261", "#6A4C93", "#2A9D8F"), lwd = 2)

# Moments
a <- 2; b <- 5
c(mean = a / (a + b), var = a * b / ((a + b)^2 * (a + b + 1)))

X <- rbeta(1e5, a, b)
c(emp_mean = mean(X), emp_var = var(X))

# Bayesian update example
prior_a <- 2; prior_b <- 2
k <- 15; n <- 20
post_a <- prior_a + k; post_b <- prior_b + n - k
c(posterior_mean = post_a / (post_a + post_b),
  posterior_95_CI_lower = qbeta(0.025, post_a, post_b),
  posterior_95_CI_upper = qbeta(0.975, post_a, post_b))

heading: 4.28 Output & Results
text: Empirical moments match theoretical. Bayesian update Beta(2,2) prior 15 successes 20 trials gives posterior mean 0.71 95% credible interval (0.50, 0.87).
code:

heading: 4.29 Interpretation
text: Beta distributions appear everywhere Bayesian analysis proportions. Reporting “posterior probability success 0.71 (95% credible interval 0.50 0.87)” beta-based statement.

heading: 4.30 Practical Tips
text: weakly informative priors, Beta(1, 1) (uniform) Beta(0.5, 0.5) (Jeffreys) common defaults.large \(\) \(b\) correspond strongly informative priors; check prior predictive distribution verify plausibility.Beta regression (betareg) models proportions \((0, 1)\) function covariates.Values exactly 0 1 problematic; transform via \((x (n - 1) + 0.5) / n\) use zero-one-inflated beta.quick-look sanity check, beta \(= b\) symmetric centred 0.5, scaling \((+ b)\) concentration parameter.
