heading: 4.33 Introduction
text: binomial distribution counts number successes \(n\) independent trials, probability \(p\). common discrete distribution applied statistics: responder counts trial, positive test results screen, correct classifications evaluation.

heading: 4.34 Prerequisites
text: Bernoulli trials, basic combinatorics.

heading: 4.35 Theory
text: \(X = \sum_{=1}^n B_i\) \(B_i\) iid Bernoulli\((p)\), \(X \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n, p)\).PMF: \(P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1 - p)^{n - k}\) \(k = 0, 1, \ldots, n\).Moments:\(E[X] = np\).\(\mathrm{Var}(X) = np(1 - p)\).Normal approximation: \(\mathrm{Binomial}(n, p) \approx \mathcal{N}(np, np(1 - p))\) \(np \geq 10\) \(n(1 - p) \geq 10\). Continuity-corrected: \(P(X \leq k) \approx \Phi\!\big((k + 0.5 - np)/\sqrt{np(1-p)}\big)\).Poisson approximation: large \(n\) small \(p\) \(np = \lambda\) moderate, \(\mathrm{Binomial}(n, p) \approx \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda)\).Relationship distributions:Sum independent binomials \(p\): binomial sum \(n\)’s.Conditional total successes, two independent binomials hypergeometric distribution one.

heading: 4.36 Assumptions
text: Fixed number trials \(n\).Independent trials.Constant success probability \(p\).Violations produce overdispersion (varying \(p\)) correlation (dependent trials).

heading: 4.37 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

n <- 20; p <- 0.35

k <- 0:n
pmf <- dbinom(k, n, p)

data.frame(k, pmf = round(pmf, 4))

# Moments
c(mean = n * p, var = n * p * (1 - p))
mean(rbinom(1e5, n, p)); var(rbinom(1e5, n, p))

# Normal approximation
x <- seq(0, n, length.out = 400)
norm_approx <- dnorm(x, mean = n * p, sd = sqrt(n * p * (1 - p)))

ggplot() +
  geom_col(data = data.frame(k, pmf), aes(k, pmf), fill = "#2A9D8F") +
  geom_line(data = data.frame(x, norm_approx), aes(x, norm_approx),
            colour = "#F4A261", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(x = "k", y = "probability",
       title = "Binomial(20, 0.35) with normal approximation") +
  theme_minimal()

# Interval probability and quantile
pbinom(10, n, p)        # P(X <= 10)
qbinom(0.025, n, p)     # 2.5th percentile

heading: 4.38 Output & Results
text: normal approximation tracks PMF closely \(n = 20\), \(p = 0.35\) \(np = 7\) \(n(1 - p) = 13\).

heading: 4.39 Interpretation
text: Binomial reasoning pervades applied statistics: one-proportion tests, two-proportion tests, logistic regression, every chi-squared-style comparison. Reporting proportion Wilson 95% CI reporting binomial inference.

heading: 4.40 Practical Tips
text: small \(n\) extreme \(p\), use exact binomial test / Clopper-Pearson interval instead normal-based inference.Overdispersion relative binomial signals either varying \(p\) across trials correlation among trials; beta-binomial common remedy.dbinom(k, n, p, log = TRUE) gives log-probabilities, useful likelihoods many observations.Compound inferences (e.g., comparing two binomial proportions) CIs; prefer score intervals (prop.test) Wald.R order arguments dbinom(x, size, prob) size \(n\) prob \(p\); mixing common bug.

heading: 4.43 Introduction
text: chi-squared distribution arises sum squared independent standard normal random variables. sampling distribution sample variance normality, reference distribution goodness--fit contingency tests, key ingredient F distribution used throughout ANOVA.

heading: 4.44 Prerequisites
text: Normal distribution, basic calculus.

heading: 4.45 Theory
text: \(Z_1, \ldots, Z_k\) iid standard normal, \[Q = \sum_{=1}^k Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2_k.\]parameter \(k\) degrees freedom. PDF \[f(x) = \frac{1}{2^{k/2} \Gamma(k/2)} x^{k/2 - 1} e^{-x/2}, \qquad x > 0.\]Moments: \(E[Q] = k\), \(\mathrm{Var}(Q) = 2k\).Sum property: \(Q_1 \sim \chi^2_{k_1}\) \(Q_2 \sim \chi^2_{k_2}\) independent, \(Q_1 + Q_2 \sim \chi^2_{k_1 + k_2}\).Sample variance: iid normal \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) variance \(\sigma^2\),\[\frac{(n - 1) s^2}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{n - 1}.\]basis confidence intervals normal variance.Large-df behaviour: \(\chi^2_k\) approximately normal large \(k\) (CLT applied sum squared normals).

heading: 4.46 Assumptions
text: sample-variance result: iid normal observations. goodness--fit approximation chi-squared tests: independent observations large enough expected cell counts.

heading: 4.47 R Implementation
code:
k <- 5

# PDF and CDF
x <- seq(0, 20, length.out = 400)
plot(x, dchisq(x, df = k), type = "l", col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2,
     main = paste("Chi-squared,", k, "df"), ylab = "f(x)")

# Moments
c(theoretical_mean = k, theoretical_var = 2 * k,
  empirical_mean   = mean(rchisq(1e5, k)),
  empirical_var    = var(rchisq(1e5, k)))

# Verify: sum of k squared standard normals
n <- 1e5
Q_direct <- rchisq(n, df = k)
Q_from_normals <- rowSums(matrix(rnorm(n * k), n, k)^2)
c(mean_direct = mean(Q_direct), mean_from_normals = mean(Q_from_normals))

# Confidence interval for a variance
set.seed(2026)
X <- rnorm(30, mean = 0, sd = 2)
s2 <- var(X)
n <- length(X)
chi_l <- qchisq(0.025, n - 1); chi_u <- qchisq(0.975, n - 1)
c((n - 1) * s2 / chi_u, (n - 1) * s2 / chi_l)

heading: 4.48 Output & Results
text: 95% CI true variance (4) (2.54, 7.21), captures true value. sum--squared-normals construction produces expected mean 5.
code:

            5.0             10.0           5.003           9.98

mean_direct mean_from_normals
      5.005             5.004

[1] 2.542 7.206

heading: 4.49 Interpretation
text: Chi-squared tests independence, goodness--fit, variance homogeneity reference chi-squared distribution. Reporting test statistic value like “\(\chi^2_3 = 9.5\), \(p = 0.023\)” reporting chi-squared-distributed statistic p-value.

heading: 4.50 Practical Tips
text: Chi-squared confidence intervals variance highly sensitive normality assumption.large df, use normal approximation: \(\chi^2_k \approx \mathcal{N}(k, 2k)\), accurately \(\sqrt{2 \chi^2_k} \approx \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{2k - 1}, 1)\).Chi-squared tests small expected counts poorly approximated; use exact tests Monte Carlo.Non-central chi-squared (non-centrality parameter \(\lambda\)) governs power calculations chi-squared tests.square \(t_\nu\) \(F_{1, \nu}\), chi-squared; conflate.

heading: 4.53 Introduction
text: conditional distribution describes one random variable behaves given specific value (values) another. Conditional expectations regression models estimate; conditional variances quantify residual uncertainty. Together language predictive statistics.

heading: 4.54 Prerequisites
text: Joint distributions, marginal distributions, conditional probability.

heading: 4.55 Theory
text: continuous joint density \(f_{X,Y}\) marginal \(f_Y > 0\), conditional density \(X\) given \(Y = y\) \[f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) = \frac{f_{X, Y}(x, y)}{f_Y(y)}.\]Analogous expression discrete variables using PMFs.Conditional expectation \(X\) given \(Y = y\):\[E[X \mid Y = y] = \int x f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) \, dx.\]function \(y\), \(E[X \mid Y]\) random variable.Tower property (law total expectation):\[E[X] = E[E[X \mid Y]].\]Conditional variance: \(\mathrm{Var}(X \mid Y = y) = E[X^2 \mid Y = y] - (E[X \mid Y = y])^2\).Law total variance: \(\mathrm{Var}(X) = E[\mathrm{Var}(X \mid Y)] + \mathrm{Var}(E[X \mid Y])\). Within-group variance plus -group variance.bivariate normal correlation \(\rho\): \(X \mid Y = y \sim \mathcal{N}\!\big(\mu_X + \rho \sigma_X \sigma_Y^{-1}(y - \mu_Y), \sigma_X^2 (1 - \rho^2)\big)\).

heading: 4.56 Assumptions
text: \(f_Y(y) > 0\) (conditional probability defined via alternate limiting procedure); \(E[|X|] < \infty\) conditional expectation exist.

heading: 4.57 R Implementation
code:
library(mvtnorm)
set.seed(2026)

# Bivariate normal
mu <- c(0, 0); Sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.7, 0.7, 1), 2, 2)
rho <- Sigma[1, 2] / sqrt(prod(diag(Sigma)))
XY <- rmvnorm(5e4, mean = mu, sigma = Sigma)

# Conditional on Y near y0, distribution of X
y0 <- 1.0; window <- 0.05
X_given_Y <- XY[abs(XY[, 2] - y0) < window, 1]

c(n_window    = length(X_given_Y),
  emp_mean    = mean(X_given_Y),
  theor_mean  = mu[1] + rho * (y0 - mu[2]),
  emp_sd      = sd(X_given_Y),
  theor_sd    = sqrt(Sigma[1, 1] * (1 - rho^2)))

# Tower property: E[X] = E[E[X | Y]]
c(E_X = mean(XY[, 1]),
  tower = mean(mu[1] + rho * (XY[, 2] - mu[2])))

# Law of total variance
var_total <- var(XY[, 1])
var_within <- Sigma[1, 1] * (1 - rho^2)
var_between <- rho^2 * Sigma[1, 1]
c(total = var_total,
  sum_of_parts = var_within + var_between)

heading: 4.63 Introduction
text: Conditional probability quantifies information one event updates probability another. lens every diagnostic test, every Bayesian update, every hazard calculation viewed. rules simple; interpretations subtle recurrently misunderstood applied research.

heading: 4.64 Prerequisites
text: Basic probability, sample spaces, set operations.

heading: 4.65 Theory
text: events \(\) \(B\) \(P(B) > 0\), conditional probability \(\) given \(B\) \[P(\mid B) = \frac{P(\cap B)}{P(B)}.\]Conceptually, conditioning restricts attention subset sample space \(B\) occurred, asks: among outcomes, fraction \(\)?Rearranging gives product (multiplication) rule:\[P(\cap B) = P(\mid B) \, P(B) = P(B \mid ) \, P().\]product rule extends multiple events via chain rule:\[P(A_1 \cap \ldots \cap A_n) = P(A_1) \, P(A_2 \mid A_1) \, P(A_3 \mid A_1 \cap A_2) \cdots\]Conditioning preserves axioms: fixing \(B\), function \(P(\cdot \mid B)\) valid probability measure conditional sample space. standard properties (complement rule, inclusion-exclusion) hold \(P\) replaced \(P(\cdot \mid B)\).

heading: 4.66 Assumptions
text: \(P(B) > 0\). Conditioning zero-probability event defined elementary setting; measure-theoretic extensions exist beyond scope.original probability space fixed; conditioning change \(\Omega\), measure restricted \(B\).

heading: 4.67 R Implementation
text: use 2x2 population table demonstrate conditioning mechanically.
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Simulated screening-test data: 10 000 patients
# True disease status and test result
n <- 10000
disease_prev <- 0.05
sensitivity  <- 0.90
specificity  <- 0.95

patients <- data.frame(
  disease = rbinom(n, 1, disease_prev)
) |>
  within({
    test <- ifelse(disease == 1,
                   rbinom(length(disease), 1, sensitivity),
                   rbinom(length(disease), 1, 1 - specificity))
  })

tab <- table(patients$test, patients$disease,
             dnn = c("test", "disease"))
tab

p_disease_given_test_pos <- tab["1", "1"] / sum(tab["1", ])
p_disease_given_test_pos

p_test_pos_given_disease <- tab["1", "1"] / sum(tab[, "1"])
p_test_pos_given_disease

p_joint <- tab["1", "1"] / n
c(joint = p_joint,
  formula = p_test_pos_given_disease * mean(patients$disease == 1))

heading: 4.68 Output & Results
text: product rule verified: \(P(\text{test+} \cap \text{disease}) = P(\text{test+} \mid \text{disease}) \cdot P(\text{disease})\). \(P(\text{disease} \mid \text{test+}) = 0.49\) – far sensitivity 0.90, disease prevalence low.

heading: 4.69 Interpretation
text: final ratio – 49% positive predictive value despite 90% sensitivity – classic base-rate lesson clinical testing. manuscript: “positive predictive value 49%, reflecting low prevalence disease screened population despite sensitive test.”

heading: 4.70 Practical Tips
text: \(P(\mid B) \neq P(B \mid )\) general; prosecutor’s fallacy confused legal clinical settings.Always check conditioning event positive probability; small samples, conditional probabilities based empty strata undefined.interpreting conditional probability, state conditioning event explicitly: “probability disease given test positive” clearer “positive predictive value” non-specialist readers.Chain product rule computing joint probabilities many events: \(P(\cap B \cap C) = P() P(B \mid ) P(C \mid \cap B)\).Conditioning changes denominator: moving marginal conditional probability requires dividing \(P(B)\).

heading: 4.73 Introduction
text: distribution sum independent random variables convolution. Many standard distribution families closed convolution: sums independent normals normal, sums independent Poissons Poisson, sums independent gammas (rate) gamma. closure makes convolution core operation probability.

heading: 4.74 Prerequisites
text: Joint distributions, independence, integration.

heading: 4.75 Theory
text: independent \(X\) \(Y\) densities \(f_X\) \(f_Y\), density \(Z = X + Y\) \[f_Z(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) f_Y(z - x) \, dx.\]integral convolution \(f_X * f_Y\). discrete PMFs, sum replaces integral.Characteristic functions convert convolutions products: \(\varphi_{X + Y}(t) = \varphi_X(t) \varphi_Y(t)\). makes convolutions many distributions analytically tractable via Fourier-analytic methods.Closure examples:\(\mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2) + \mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_1 + \mu_2, \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2)\) (independent).\(\mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_1) + \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_2) \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)\) (independent).\(\mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_1, \beta) + \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_2, \beta) \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2, \beta)\) (rate; independent).\(\chi^2_{n_1} + \chi^2_{n_2} \sim \chi^2_{n_1 + n_2}\) (independent).\(\mathrm{Binomial}(n_1, p) + \mathrm{Binomial}(n_2, p) \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n_1 + n_2, p)\) (\(p\); independent).Note Cauchy family also closed sums: sums independent Cauchys Cauchy – consequence stable distribution family Cauchy belongs.

heading: 4.76 Assumptions
text: Independence essential; convolution formula fails dependent \(X\) \(Y\).

heading: 4.77 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Normal + normal is normal
X <- rnorm(1e5, mean = 0, sd = 1)
Y <- rnorm(1e5, mean = 0, sd = 1)
Z <- X + Y
c(emp_mean = mean(Z), emp_sd = sd(Z),
  theoretical_mean = 0, theoretical_sd = sqrt(2))

# Poisson + Poisson is Poisson
X_pois <- rpois(1e5, lambda = 3)
Y_pois <- rpois(1e5, lambda = 2)
Z_pois <- X_pois + Y_pois
c(emp_mean = mean(Z_pois),
  emp_var  = var(Z_pois),
  theoretical_mean = 5,
  theoretical_var  = 5)

# Numerical convolution of two PDFs via integrate()
# f_X: uniform[0,1], f_Y: exponential(rate=1)
# Compute density of Z = X + Y at z = 0.5
f_convolve <- function(z) {
  integrate(function(x) dunif(x) * dexp(z - x, rate = 1),
            lower = 0, upper = min(1, z))$value
}
f_convolve(0.5)
f_convolve(1.5)

heading: 4.83 Introduction
text: copula multivariate distribution uniform marginals. captures dependence structure joint distribution independently marginals. Sklar’s theorem says every joint distribution decomposes marginals plus unique copula; decomposition cleanest way model dependence marginals non-normal.

heading: 4.84 Prerequisites
text: Joint marginal distributions; cumulative distribution function; basic multivariate probability.

heading: 4.85 Theory
text: Sklar’s theorem (1959). joint CDF \(F_{XY}\) marginals \(F_X, F_Y\), exists copula \(C: [0, 1]^2 \[0, 1]\) \[F_{XY}(x, y) = C(F_X(x), F_Y(y)).\]\(F_X, F_Y\) continuous, \(C\) unique. direction, copula \(C\) marginals \(F_X, F_Y\), composition defines valid joint CDF.Common bivariate copulas:Gaussian copula: induced bivariate normal correlation \(\rho\). Symmetric, tail dependence.t-copula: bivariate t. symmetric structure Gaussian heavier tail dependence.Clayton copula: asymmetric lower-tail dependence.Gumbel copula: asymmetric upper-tail dependence.Frank copula: symmetric, tail dependence, wider range dependence Gaussian.Tail dependence: lower upper tail-dependence coefficients \(\lambda_L = \lim_{u \0^+} C(u, u)/u\) \(\lambda_U = \lim_{u \1^-} (1 - 2u + C(u, u))/(1 - u)\) quantify strongly extreme values one variable co-occur extremes .probability integral transform turns continuous marginal uniform: \(U = F_X(X) \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)\). Copulas operate uniform scale transform back original scale via \(F_X^{-1}\).

heading: 4.86 Assumptions
text: Continuous marginals (uniqueness); otherwise copula unique joint probabilities atoms.

heading: 4.87 R Implementation
code:
library(copula)

# Fit a Gaussian copula to bivariate data
set.seed(2026)
n <- 1000

# Simulate data with non-normal marginals but Gaussian copula
cop <- normalCopula(param = 0.7, dim = 2)
mvd <- mvdc(copula = cop,
            margins = c("gamma", "exp"),
            paramMargins = list(list(shape = 3, rate = 0.8),
                                 list(rate = 0.3)))
X <- rMvdc(n, mvd)

# Visualise the dependence through the copula (uniform-scaled)
U <- pobs(X)   # empirical pseudo-observations on [0, 1]^2
plot(U, pch = 16, cex = 0.3, col = "#2A9D8F",
     main = "Empirical copula (pseudo-observations)",
     xlab = "U1", ylab = "U2")

# Fit a Gaussian copula
gc_fit <- fitCopula(normalCopula(dim = 2), data = U, method = "ml")
coef(gc_fit)

# Compare Clayton (lower tail dep) vs Gumbel (upper tail dep)
cc_fit <- fitCopula(claytonCopula(dim = 2), data = U, method = "ml")
gu_fit <- fitCopula(gumbelCopula(dim = 2), data = U, method = "ml")
c(gaussian = AIC(gc_fit), clayton = AIC(cc_fit), gumbel = AIC(gu_fit))

heading: 4.88 Output & Results

heading: 4.93 Introduction
text: correlation coefficient rescales covariance unitless number \(-1\) \(+1\), making comparisons across different measurement scales meaningful. correlation \(+1\) means perfect positive linear dependence, \(-1\) perfect negative, \(0\) linear association. single reported bivariate summary applied research.

heading: 4.94 Prerequisites
text: Variance covariance.

heading: 4.95 Theory
text: random variables \(X, Y\) finite non-zero variances,\[\rho(X, Y) = \frac{\mathrm{Cov}(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}.\]Cauchy-Schwarz guarantees \(\rho \[-1, 1]\). Equality \(\pm 1\) occurs \(Y = aX + b\) almost surely \(\mathrm{sign}() = \mathrm{sign}(\rho)\).sample correlation coefficient (Pearson’s \(r\)) plug-estimator using sample covariance SDs:\[r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}.\]Key caveats:\(\rho = 0\) imply independence unless \((X, Y)\) jointly normal.\(\rho\) measures linear component association; non-linear dependence invisible .Outliers affect \(r\) disproportionately; robust alternative Spearman rank correlation.

heading: 4.96 Assumptions
text: variances finite non-zero.Linearity interpretive assumption: \(\rho\) captures linear dependence.

heading: 4.97 R Implementation
code:
library(mvtnorm)
set.seed(2026)

# Simulate bivariate normals at several correlation levels
rho_grid <- c(-0.8, -0.3, 0, 0.3, 0.8)
n <- 500
results <- sapply(rho_grid, function(rho) {
  Sigma <- matrix(c(1, rho, rho, 1), 2, 2)
  XY <- rmvnorm(n, sigma = Sigma)
  cor(XY[, 1], XY[, 2])
})
data.frame(true = rho_grid, empirical = round(results, 3))

# Zero correlation does not imply independence
X <- rnorm(1e4)
Y <- X^2 - 1      # quadratic, mean zero
cor(X, Y)

# Outlier sensitivity
X <- rnorm(100); Y <- 0.3 * X + rnorm(100)
c(no_outlier = cor(X, Y),
  with_outlier = cor(c(X, 20), c(Y, -20)))

heading: 4.103 Introduction
text: cumulative distribution function (CDF) one representation fits every random variable, whether discrete, continuous, mixed. encodes distribution completely supports every computation PMF PDF .

heading: 4.104 Prerequisites
text: Random variables, basic probability.

heading: 4.105 Theory
text: CDF random variable \(X\) \[F_X(x) = P(X \leq x), \qquad x \\mathbb{R}.\]Every CDF following properties:Monotonic non-decreasing: \(x_1 \leq x_2 \Rightarrow F_X(x_1) \leq F_X(x_2)\).Right-continuous: \(\lim_{x \downarrow x_0} F_X(x) = F_X(x_0)\).Limits: \(\lim_{x \-\infty} F_X(x) = 0\), \(\lim_{x \+\infty} F_X(x) = 1\).Conversely, function three properties defines unique probability distribution \(\mathbb{R}\).Key computations:Interval probability: \(P(< X \leq b) = F_X(b) - F_X()\).PDF (continuous \(X\)): \(f_X(x) = F_X'(x)\).PMF (discrete \(X\)): \(p_X(x) = F_X(x) - F_X(x^-)\), jump size.Quantile function: \(Q_X(u) = \inf\{x : F_X(x) \geq u\}\), (generalised) inverse \(F_X\).R, distribution family p*() function returning CDF; quantile function q*(). empirical CDF obtained ecdf().

heading: 4.106 Assumptions
text: None beyond well-defined probability measure.

heading: 4.107 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(200, mean = 75, sd = 10)

# Empirical CDF compared to theoretical CDF
Fn <- ecdf(x)
grid <- seq(50, 100, length.out = 401)
df <- data.frame(
  x        = grid,
  F_empir  = Fn(grid),
  F_theor  = pnorm(grid, mean = 75, sd = 10)
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_step(aes(y = F_empir), colour = "#2A9D8F") +
  geom_line(aes(y = F_theor), colour = "#F4A261", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(y = "CDF", title = "Empirical (step) vs theoretical (line) CDF") +
  theme_minimal()

# Interval probability via the CDF
P_70_to_80 <- pnorm(80, 75, 10) - pnorm(70, 75, 10)
P_70_to_80

# Quantile: the value below which a given probability lies
qnorm(0.975, 75, 10)

heading: 4.108 Output & Results
text: empirical step-function close theoretical normal CDF, differences narrow \(n\) grows (Glivenko-Cantelli).

heading: 4.109 Interpretation
text: paper, CDF appears often figure showing empirical CDF theoretical reference (Q-Q plot rotation idea). Interval probabilities computed CDF standard form reporting “percentage within normal range”.

heading: 4.110 Practical Tips

heading: 4.113 Introduction
text: Random variables come two canonical kinds – discrete (countable support) continuous (uncountable support density). textbook distributions belong one . subtler third category, mixed, combines discrete atoms continuous parts arises naturally censored, truncated, zero-inflated data.

heading: 4.114 Prerequisites
text: Random variables, PMF, PDF.

heading: 4.115 Theory
text: random variable \(X\) :Discrete takes values countable set \(\{x_1, x_2, \ldots\}\). distribution described probability mass function \(p(x_i) = P(X = x_i)\), \(\sum_i p(x_i) = 1\).Continuous (absolutely continuous) exists probability density function \(f\) \(P(X \) = \int_A f(x) \, dx\) every measurable set \(\). continuous \(X\), \(P(X = x) = 0\) every single point \(x\).Mixed distribution discrete atoms (points positive probability mass) continuous part. Examples: censored survival times (mass censoring time, density elsewhere); zero-inflated counts (mass 0, positive distribution ).CDF \(F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)\) defined three. discrete \(X\), \(F\) step function; continuous \(X\), \(F\) smooth; mixed \(X\), \(F\) piecewise smooth jumps atoms.mean variance defined way (\(E[X]\) \(E[(X - E[X])^2]\)) across three cases, computed via sums discrete integrals continuous.

heading: 4.116 Assumptions
text: classification mathematical rather empirical; random variable falls one three categories.

heading: 4.117 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)
set.seed(2026)

# Discrete: Poisson counts
dis <- rpois(5000, lambda = 3)
table(dis)[1:7]

# Continuous: exponential waiting times
con <- rexp(5000, rate = 0.5)
quantile(con, c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75))

# Mixed: censored normal (values below 0 set to exactly 0)
mix <- pmax(rnorm(5000, mean = 1.0, sd = 1.5), 0)
c(mass_at_zero = mean(mix == 0),
  mean_overall = mean(mix))

# Plot all three
df <- rbind(
  data.frame(x = dis,    kind = "discrete (Poisson)"),
  data.frame(x = con,    kind = "continuous (exponential)"),
  data.frame(x = mix,    kind = "mixed (censored normal)")
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "#2A9D8F") +
  facet_wrap(~ kind, scales = "free", ncol = 1) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 4.118 Output & Results
text: Poisson histogram bars integer values (discrete), exponential smooth decaying curve (continuous), censored-normal spike zero plus continuous upper tail (mixed).

heading: 4.119 Interpretation
text: reporting, kind random variable determines appropriate summaries:Discrete: report counts, proportions, mode, PMF table.Continuous: report mean, SD, quantiles, histogram/density.Mixed: report atom probability separately continuous-part summary.paper analysing zero-inflated outcome acknowledge mixed distribution explicitly rather forcing continuous discrete mould.

heading: 4.120 Practical Tips
text: Count data “cap ” large value often mixed; report probability cap analyse remainder conditionally.Censored survival times mixed; classical survival methods (Kaplan-Meier, Cox) handle mixture natively.Continuous variables rounded , say, integer values still “almost continuous” practice; treat continuous inference.Zero-inflated Poisson (pscl::zeroinfl) canonical regression model mixed count data.doubt, plot histogram (ECDF) inspect atoms; presence spike specific value signals mixed distribution.

heading: 4.123 Introduction
text: expectation \(E[X]\) probability-weighted average values random variable can take. centre gravity distribution, long-run average repeated independent draws, first moment moment hierarchy. Along variance, single commonly reported summary distribution.

heading: 4.124 Prerequisites
text: Random variables, PMF PDF, basic summation integration.

heading: 4.125 Theory
text: discrete random variable PMF \(p_X\):\[E[X] = \sum_x x \, p_X(x).\]continuous random variable PDF \(f_X\):\[E[X] = \int x \, f_X(x) \, dx.\]Law Unconscious Statistician (LOTUS): measurable function \(g\),\[E[g(X)] = \sum_x g(x) p_X(x) \quad \text{} \quad \int g(x) f_X(x) \, dx,\]without needing find distribution \(g(X)\) first.Linearity. Expectation linear regardless dependence:\[E[aX + ] = E[X] + b E[Y], \qquad E\!\left[\sum_i X_i\right] = \sum_i E[X_i].\]Product rule independent variables: \(E[XY] = E[X] E[Y]\) \(X \perp Y\).Monotonicity: \(X \leq Y\) (pointwise) \(\Rightarrow E[X] \leq E[Y]\).Expectation need exist. Cauchy distribution finite mean integral diverges. Existence requires \(E[|X|] < \infty\).

heading: 4.126 Assumptions
text: \(E[|X|] < \infty\) expectation well-defined.Fubini/Tonelli conditions interchanging integration summation needed (automatic non-negative variables integrable ones).

heading: 4.127 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Exact expectation for discrete RV: binomial
n <- 10; p <- 0.3
k <- 0:n
E_X <- sum(k * dbinom(k, n, p))
c(formula = E_X, closed_form = n * p)

# Exact expectation for continuous RV: gamma
integrate(function(x) x * dgamma(x, shape = 3, rate = 0.5), 0, Inf)
3 / 0.5   # closed form alpha/beta

# LOTUS: E[g(X)] without the distribution of g(X)
# For X ~ Gamma(3, 0.5), compute E[log(X)]
integrate(function(x) log(x) * dgamma(x, shape = 3, rate = 0.5), 0, Inf)

# Monte Carlo estimate (LLN justifies this)
X <- rgamma(1e5, shape = 3, rate = 0.5)
mean(log(X))

# Linearity check: E[2X + 3Y] = 2E[X] + 3E[Y] regardless of dependence
X <- rnorm(1e5, 5, 2)
Y <- 0.7 * X + rnorm(1e5, 0, 1)        # dependent
c(direct = mean(2 * X + 3 * Y),
  sum_of_parts = 2 * mean(X) + 3 * mean(Y))

heading: 4.133 Introduction
text: exponential distribution models waiting times events Poisson process constant rate. continuous distribution memoryless property, simplest parametric model survival reliability analysis.

heading: 4.134 Prerequisites
text: Continuous random variables Poisson process.

heading: 4.135 Theory
text: non-negative random variable \(X\) exponential rate \(\lambda > 0\) \[f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}, \qquad x \geq 0.\]CDF: \(F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}\). Survival function: \(S(x) = e^{-\lambda x}\).Moments:\(E[X] = 1/\lambda\) (mean waiting time).\(\mathrm{Var}(X) = 1/\lambda^2\).Memoryless property: \(P(X > s + t \mid X > s) = P(X > t)\). probability waiting another \(t\) units, given already waited \(s\), unconditional probability waiting \(t\).Constant hazard: \(h(x) = f(x)/S(x) = \lambda\). instantaneous event rate depend long object already survived.Relationship Poisson: Poisson process rate \(\lambda\), inter-arrival times iid exponential rate \(\lambda\).Scale vs rate: R functions use rate (\(\lambda\)), others use scale (\(1/\lambda\)). Check documentation.

heading: 4.136 Assumptions
text: Constant hazard (ageing / improvement). Violations motivate Weibull log-normal distributions.

heading: 4.137 R Implementation
code:
lambda <- 0.5

X <- rexp(1e5, rate = lambda)
c(mean = mean(X), theoretical = 1 / lambda,
  var  = var(X),  theoretical_var = 1 / lambda^2)

# Memoryless property
P_past_2 <- mean(X > 2)
P_past_5_given_2 <- mean(X[X > 2] > 5)        # P(X > 5 | X > 2)
P_past_3 <- mean(X > 3)                        # should equal above by memorylessness
c(conditional = P_past_5_given_2, unconditional = P_past_3)

# Constant hazard
breaks <- seq(0, 10, by = 1)
hazards <- sapply(seq_len(length(breaks) - 1), function(i) {
  in_bin <- X >= breaks[i] & X < breaks[i + 1]
  sum(in_bin) / sum(X >= breaks[i])
})
round(hazards, 3)

heading: 4.138 Output & Results
text: Empirical mean variance match theoretical; memoryless property holds; empirical hazard constant \(\approx 1 - e^{-\lambda}\) per unit interval.

heading: 4.139 Interpretation
text: Exponential survival simplest hypothesis one can test reliability survival. observed hazard non-constant, report Weibull flexible parametric model instead.

heading: 4.140 Practical Tips

heading: 4.143 Introduction
text: F distribution arises ratio two independent scaled chi-squared random variables. reference distribution F-statistic ANOVA, tests variance equality two normal samples, omnibus tests linear regression. shape controlled two degree--freedom parameters, one numerator one denominator.

heading: 4.144 Prerequisites
text: Chi-squared distribution.

heading: 4.145 Theory
text: \(U \sim \chi^2_{d_1}\) \(V \sim \chi^2_{d_2}\) independent, \[F = \frac{U/d_1}{V/d_2} \sim F_{d_1, d_2}.\]numerator df \(d_1\); denominator df \(d_2\).Moments (exist):\(E[F] = d_2 / (d_2 - 2)\) \(d_2 > 2\).\(\mathrm{Var}(F) = \frac{2 d_2^2 (d_1 + d_2 - 2)}{d_1 (d_2 - 2)^2 (d_2 - 4)}\) \(d_2 > 4\).Relationships distributions:\(1/F_{d_1, d_2} \sim F_{d_2, d_1}\).\(t_\nu^2 \sim F_{1, \nu}\).\(d_2 \\infty\), \(d_1 \cdot F_{d_1, d_2} \\chi^2_{d_1}\).One-way ANOVA: \(k\) groups \(n\) total observations, F-statistic\[F = \frac{\text{MS}_{\text{}}}{\text{MS}_{\text{within}}}\]follows \(F_{k-1, n-k}\) null equal means.Variance ratio test: two independent normal samples variances \(\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2\),\[F = s_1^2 / s_2^2 \sim F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1}.\]

heading: 4.146 Assumptions
text: Independence \(U\) \(V\); normality underlying data derived sample statistics.

heading: 4.147 R Implementation
code:
# PDF visualisation
x <- seq(0, 5, length.out = 400)
plot(x, df(x, 5, 20), type = "l", col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2,
     main = "F(5, 20) density", ylab = "f(x)")
lines(x, df(x, 5, 5),   col = "#F4A261", lwd = 2)
lines(x, df(x, 20, 20), col = "#6A4C93", lwd = 2)
legend("topright", c("F(5,20)", "F(5,5)", "F(20,20)"),
       col = c("#2A9D8F", "#F4A261", "#6A4C93"), lwd = 2)

# Verify construction
n <- 1e5; d1 <- 4; d2 <- 10
U <- rchisq(n, df = d1); V <- rchisq(n, df = d2)
F_sim <- (U/d1) / (V/d2)
c(emp_mean = mean(F_sim), theoretical = d2 / (d2 - 2))

# F-statistic from ANOVA
set.seed(2026)
df <- data.frame(y = c(rnorm(30, 50, 8), rnorm(30, 55, 8), rnorm(30, 53, 8)),
                 g = factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 30)))
anova(lm(y ~ g, data = df))

heading: 4.150 Practical Tips
text: Order df correctly: F(df1, df2) F(df2, df1) general.two-sided variance tests, software uses max/min \(s_1^2/s_2^2\) avoid ambiguity.Non-central F (non-centrality parameter \(\lambda\)) governs power calculations ANOVA.F-distribution highly sensitive non-normality; skewed data prefer Welch’s ANOVA non-parametric alternatives.qf(0.95, d1, d2) gives critical value one-sided 5% test.

heading: 4.153 Introduction
text: gamma distribution flexible right-skewed distribution positive real numbers. parameterised shape rate (scale) includes exponential, Erlang, chi-squared special cases. applied work models waiting times, concentrations, response amplitudes – positive continuous variable light--moderate right skew.

heading: 4.154 Prerequisites
text: Exponential distribution, basic calculus.

heading: 4.155 Theory
text: gamma random variable shape \(\alpha > 0\) rate \(\beta > 0\) PDF\[f(x) = \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)} x^{\alpha - 1} e^{-\beta x}, \qquad x > 0.\]Scale parameterisation: \(\theta = 1/\beta\), \(f(x) = \frac{1}{\theta^\alpha \Gamma(\alpha)} x^{\alpha - 1} e^{-x/\theta}\).Moments:\(E[X] = \alpha / \beta\) (rate form) = \(\alpha \theta\) (scale form).\(\mathrm{Var}(X) = \alpha / \beta^2 = \alpha \theta^2\).Special cases:Shape \(\alpha = 1\): exponential\((\beta)\).Integer shape \(\alpha = k\): Erlang distribution, equals sum \(k\) iid exponentials rate \(\beta\).Shape \(\alpha = \nu/2\), rate \(\beta = 1/2\): chi-squared \(\nu\) df.Sum property: independent Gamma\((\alpha_1, \beta)\) Gamma\((\alpha_2, \beta)\) sum Gamma\((\alpha_1 + \alpha_2, \beta)\).Conjugacy: gamma conjugate Poisson likelihood (prior \(\lambda\)); gamma-Poisson mixture yields negative binomial.

heading: 4.156 Assumptions
text: Values must positive. flexibility comes two parameters; monotone hazard (increasing \(\alpha > 1\), decreasing \(\alpha < 1\), constant \(\alpha = 1\)).

heading: 4.157 R Implementation
code:
library(MASS)

shape <- 2.5; rate <- 0.8

X <- rgamma(1e5, shape = shape, rate = rate)
c(mean = mean(X), theoretical_mean = shape / rate,
  var  = var(X),  theoretical_var = shape / rate^2)

# Visualise the PDF
curve(dgamma(x, shape, rate), from = 0, to = 15,
      main = paste("Gamma(shape =", shape, ", rate =", rate, ")"),
      xlab = "x", ylab = "f(x)", col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2)

# MLE fit
fit <- fitdistr(X, "gamma")
fit$estimate

# Sum of independent exponentials equals Erlang (gamma with integer shape)
k <- 5; lambda <- 1
S <- replicate(5000, sum(rexp(k, rate = lambda)))
c(mean_empirical = mean(S), mean_theoretical = k / lambda,
  var_empirical  = var(S),  var_theoretical  = k / lambda^2)

heading: 4.158 Output & Results
text: Empirical moments match theoretical; MLE recovers true parameters; sum 5 iid exponentials predicted Erlang moments.
code:

  3.13             3.125     3.92            3.906

    shape    rate
  2.507   0.802       # MLE recovers true parameters

mean_empirical mean_theoretical  var_empirical  var_theoretical

heading: 4.159 Interpretation
text: Gamma default parametric model positive continuous variables fit well normal log-normal. generalised linear models, gamma family (glm(..., family = Gamma)) appropriate positive continuous outcomes variance proportional squared mean.

heading: 4.160 Practical Tips

heading: 4.163 Introduction
text: geometric distribution counts number Bernoulli trials required obtain first success. discrete analogue exponential waiting-time distribution shares memoryless property: probability trials success depend many already tried.

heading: 4.164 Prerequisites
text: Bernoulli trials.

heading: 4.165 Theory
text: Two conventions exist. Let \(X\) number Bernoulli\((p)\) trials including first success:\[P(X = k) = (1 - p)^{k - 1} p, \qquad k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots\]Alternatively, \(Y\) number failures first success:\[P(Y = k) = (1 - p)^k p, \qquad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\]R uses second convention (rgeom, dgeom return \(Y\), .e., failures first success).Moments first convention (\(X\) = trials first success):\(E[X] = 1/p\), \(\mathrm{Var}(X) = (1 - p)/p^2\).\(Y\) (R’s convention): \(E[Y] = (1 - p)/p\), \(\mathrm{Var}(Y) = (1 - p)/p^2\).Memoryless property: \(P(X > m + n \mid X > m) = P(X > n)\). waited \(m\) trials without success change distribution waiting time.

heading: 4.166 Assumptions
text: Independent Bernoulli trials constant probability \(p\).

heading: 4.167 R Implementation
code:
p <- 0.3

# R's convention: Y = failures before first success
Y <- rgeom(1e5, p)
c(mean_Y = mean(Y), theoretical = (1 - p) / p)

# Number of trials to first success X = Y + 1
X <- Y + 1
c(mean_X = mean(X), theoretical = 1 / p)

# PMF comparison
k <- 0:15
data.frame(k, pmf_Y = dgeom(k, p))

# Memoryless: P(Y > 5 + 3 | Y > 5) = P(Y > 3)
Y_past_5 <- Y[Y > 5]
c(conditional = mean(Y_past_5 > 5 + 3),
  unconditional = mean(Y > 3))

heading: 4.168 Output & Results
text: memoryless property holds empirically: conditional probability 3 failures, given already 5 failures, equals unconditional probability least 3 failures.

    3.334       3.333

1   0     0.3000

3   2     0.1470

conditional  unconditional

heading: 4.169 Interpretation
text: geometric distribution baseline model “time event” discrete case: cycles pregnancy IVF, attempts successful procedure, trials rare positive test. Memorylessness strong assumption often fails real data fatigue learning changes \(p\) time.

heading: 4.170 Practical Tips
text: Check convention (trials vs. failures) expected comparing textbook formulas.Memorylessness testable empirically: compare distribution residual trials given survival time.non-memoryless discrete waiting times, negative binomial (shape \(> 1\)) captures -dispersion relative geometric.geometric converges exponential discretising time fine intervals.clinical biological settings, censoring common; naive rgeom ignores right-censoring – use survival methods realistic data.

heading: 4.173 Introduction
text: hazard function \(h(t)\) gives instantaneous rate events occur time \(t\), given occurred \(t\). primary object survival analysis reliability engineering: proportional-hazards models, accelerated-failure-time models, competing-risks methods reason hazards rather distributions directly.

heading: 4.174 Prerequisites
text: Continuous distributions; survival function \(S(t) = P(T > t)\).

heading: 4.175 Theory
text: non-negative continuous random variable \(T\) density \(f\) survival function \(S(t) = 1 - F(t)\),\[h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{S(t)}.\]Intuitively, \(h(t) \, dt\) approximately \(P(t \leq T < t + dt \mid T \geq t)\) small \(dt\).cumulative hazard \[H(t) = \int_0^t h(u) \, du = -\log S(t).\]Conversely, \(S(t) = e^{-H(t)}\). either \(f\), \(F\), \(S\), \(h\), \(H\) known, others follow.Hazard shapes common distributions:Exponential: \(h(t) = \lambda\) (constant).Weibull shape \(k\): \(h(t) = (k/\lambda)(t/\lambda)^{k-1}\) – increasing \(k > 1\), decreasing \(k < 1\).Gompertz: \(h(t) = \alpha e^{\beta t}\) – exponentially increasing; common human mortality.Log-normal: \(h(t)\) rises falls.

heading: 4.176 Assumptions
text: \(T\) non-negative continuous random variable density. Proportional-hazards models assume \(h(t \mid x) = h_0(t) \exp(\beta^\top x)\).

heading: 4.177 R Implementation
code:
library(survival); library(ggplot2)

# Constant hazard (exponential)
x <- seq(0.01, 5, length.out = 400)
h_exp <- rep(0.5, length(x))
h_weibull_inc <- (2.0 / 1.5) * (x / 1.5)^(2.0 - 1)
h_weibull_dec <- (0.5 / 1.5) * (x / 1.5)^(0.5 - 1)

df <- data.frame(
  t  = rep(x, 3),
  h  = c(h_exp, h_weibull_inc, h_weibull_dec),
  kind = factor(rep(c("exp lambda=0.5",
                      "weibull k=2 (incr)",
                      "weibull k=0.5 (decr)"), each = length(x)))
)

ggplot(df, aes(t, h, colour = kind)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("#6A4C93", "#2A9D8F", "#F4A261")) +
  labs(x = "t", y = "h(t)", colour = "",
       title = "Hazard functions for three canonical models") +
  theme_minimal()

# Empirical cumulative hazard from censored data
set.seed(2026)
T <- rexp(100, rate = 0.3)
status <- rep(1, 100)
fit <- survfit(Surv(T, status) ~ 1)
plot(fit, fun = "cumhaz", col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "t", ylab = "H(t)",
     main = "Empirical cumulative hazard (Nelson-Aalen)")
abline(0, 0.3, col = "#F4A261", lty = 2)

heading: 4.178 Output & Results
text: plot shows three hazards: exponential (flat), increasing Weibull, decreasing Weibull. empirical cumulative hazard 100 exponential event times approximately linear slope \(\lambda = 0.3\), matching theoretical \(H(t) = 0.3 t\).

heading: 4.179 Interpretation
text: survival analysis, hazards natural target regression: “hazard death 40 % higher treated group controls (HR = 1.40, 95 % CI 1.10-1.78)”. proportional-hazards model ubiquitous avoids specifying baseline hazard shape.

heading: 4.180 Practical Tips
text: constant hazard implies exponentiality; log-log plot \(-\log S(t)\) \(\log t\) linear indicates Weibull.Empirical hazards noisy tail subjects remain; report cumulative hazard (smoother) kernel-smoothed hazard estimates.Nelson-Aalen estimator standard non-parametric estimator cumulative hazard right-censoring.competing risks, cause-specific hazards differ sub-distribution hazard Fine-Gray; two answer different questions.Time-varying hazard ratios invalidate proportional-hazards assumption; use stratification time-interactions.

heading: 4.183 Introduction
text: hypergeometric distribution models number successes sample drawn without replacement finite population. differs binomial dependence across draws: removing success reduces probability subsequent success. underlies Fisher’s exact test audit sampling.

heading: 4.184 Prerequisites
text: Combinatorics, binomial distribution.

heading: 4.185 Theory
text: population \(N\) items contains \(K\) “successes” \(N - K\) “failures”. sample size \(n\) drawn without replacement. Let \(X\) number successes sample:\[P(X = k) = \frac{\binom{K}{k}\binom{N - K}{n - k}}{\binom{N}{n}}, \qquad \max(0, n - (N - K)) \leq k \leq \min(n, K).\]Moments:\(E[X] = n K / N\) (binomial \(p = K/N\)).\(\mathrm{Var}(X) = n \cdot \frac{K(N - K)}{N^2} \cdot \frac{N - n}{N - 1}\).factor \((N - n)/(N - 1)\) finite-population correction; shrinks variance relative binomial sampling, reflecting reduced uncertainty substantial fraction population sampled.\(N \\infty\) \(K / N = p\) fixed, hypergeometric converges binomial: population large relative sample, /without replacement makes little difference.

heading: 4.186 Assumptions
text: Finite population known size \(N\).Known number successes \(K\) population.Simple random sampling without replacement.

heading: 4.187 R Implementation
code:
N <- 200; K <- 60; n <- 30

# PMF and moments
k <- 0:n
pmf <- dhyper(k, K, N - K, n)
c(mean = n * K / N,
  var  = n * K / N * (1 - K / N) * (N - n) / (N - 1),
  emp_mean = sum(k * pmf),
  emp_var  = sum((k - sum(k * pmf))^2 * pmf))

# Compare with binomial: same mean, larger variance
c(binom_var = n * (K / N) * (1 - K / N))

# Fisher's exact test uses the hypergeometric
tab <- matrix(c(12, 18, 48, 122), 2, 2)
fisher.test(tab)

heading: 4.188 Output & Results
text: hypergeometric variance smaller binomial variance (5.49 vs 6.30) finite-population correction accounts dependence. Fisher’s exact test 2x2 example hypergeometric computation null independence.
code:
mean       var   emp_mean    emp_var
      9.0     5.487       9.00      5.487

binom_var
     6.30

        Fisher's Exact Test for Count Data

data:  tab
p-value = 0.1412
alternative hypothesis: true odds ratio is not equal to 1

heading: 4.189 Interpretation
text: Hypergeometric inference appears whenever sampling without replacement matters. quality audits, exact probability given number defective items observed sample hypergeometric. contingency-table analysis small cells, Fisher’s exact test hypergeometric.

heading: 4.190 Practical Tips
text: large populations (\(N > 20n\)), binomial approximation typically adequate simpler.2x2 contingency tables, Fisher’s exact test hypergeometric-based preferable chi-squared small expected counts.dhyper(x, m, n, k) uses R’s quirky naming: m = successes population, n = failures population, k = sample size.survey sampling stratification, within-stratum variance uses finite-population correction factor.Generalisations multiple categories yield multivariate hypergeometric; useful categorical audit card-game probabilities.

heading: 4.193 Introduction
text: Two events independent knowing one occurred tells nothing whether . Independence property makes simulation tractable, factorises joint distributions, underlies iid assumption almost every classical statistical method. also one commonly mis-stated concepts: independent disjoint, pairwise independence imply joint independence.

heading: 4.194 Prerequisites
text: Basic probability, conditional probability, product rule.

heading: 4.195 Theory
text: Two events \(\) \(B\) independent \[P(\cap B) = P() \, P(B).\]Equivalently, \(P(B) > 0\): \(P(\mid B) = P()\). Knowing \(B\) update probability \(\).\(n\) events \(A_1, \ldots, A_n\), two levels independence exist:Pairwise independence: \(P(A_i \cap A_j) = P(A_i) P(A_j)\) every \(\neq j\).Mutual independence: every subset factorises. \(P(A_{i_1} \cap \ldots \cap A_{i_k}) = \prod_{j=1}^k P(A_{i_j})\) every subset.Mutual strictly stronger: possible three events pairwise independent yet mutually independent (Bernstein’s example).random variables \(X\) \(Y\):\[X \perp Y \iff F_{XY}(x, y) = F_X(x) F_Y(y) \quad \forall x, y,\]equivalently (densities exist) \(f_{XY}(x, y) = f_X(x) f_Y(y)\).Independence vs. uncorrelatedness. Independence implies zero correlation, vice versa: \(X\) \(X^2\) \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) uncorrelated yet functionally dependent.

heading: 4.196 Assumptions
text: None beyond probability space well-defined.

heading: 4.197 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Two independent Bernoulli trials
n <- 1e5
A <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.3)
B <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.6)

# Check: P(A and B) ≈ P(A) * P(B)
c(joint = mean(A == 1 & B == 1),
  product = mean(A == 1) * mean(B == 1))

# Dependence: construct two correlated Bernoullis
C <- ifelse(A == 1, rbinom(n, 1, 0.9), rbinom(n, 1, 0.2))
c(joint = mean(A == 1 & C == 1),
  product = mean(A == 1) * mean(C == 1))

# Uncorrelated but dependent example
X <- rnorm(n)
Y <- X^2
c(correlation = cor(X, Y),
  joint_factorises = abs(mean(X < 0 & Y > 2) -
                          mean(X < 0) * mean(Y > 2)))

heading: 4.198 Output & Results
text: First pair independent (joint equals product). Second pair dependent (joint much larger product). Third shows zero correlation clearly non-zero deviation factorisation.
code:
joint  product
 0.1801   0.1800

  joint  product
 0.2714   0.1798

correlation joint_factorises
     0.0028           0.1589

heading: 4.199 Interpretation
text: study design, independence usually design property: separate random assignments ensure treatment independent unmeasured confounders; random selection ensures sample independent observable sources. design guarantee independence (clustered data, repeated measures), statistical model must account .

heading: 4.200 Practical Tips
text: Independence property particular probability measure \(P\). Two events can independent one distribution dependent another.Pairwise---mutual independence rare applied settings matters cryptography combinatorial probability.“iid” (independent identically distributed) default assumption statistical methods; violations require mixed-effects models, GEE, time-series methods.Use simulation check independence analytical factorisation cumbersome; deviation \(P()P(B)\) quantifies dependence.continuous variables, formal independence test available via distance correlation (energy::dcor).

heading: 4.203 Introduction
text: joint distribution describes two random variables behave together. contains strictly information marginals alone: marginals tell variable isolation, joint tells interact. Dependence, correlation, conditional behaviour properties joint distribution.

heading: 4.204 Prerequisites
text: Random variables, PMF, PDF, independence.

heading: 4.205 Theory
text: two random variables \(X\) \(Y\):Joint CDF: \(F_{X,Y}(x, y) = P(X \leq x, Y \leq y)\).Joint PMF (discrete): \(p_{X,Y}(x, y) = P(X = x, Y = y)\).Joint PDF (continuous): \(f_{X,Y}(x, y)\) \(\iint f = 1\).joint CDF satisfies \(\partial^2 F / \partial x \partial y = f\) (continuous case derivatives exist).Independence: \(X \perp Y\) \(f_{X,Y}(x, y) = f_X(x) f_Y(y)\) \(x, y\) (PMF equivalent). Equivalently, joint CDF factorises.bivariate normal canonical joint distribution applied work:\[f_{X,Y}(x, y) = \frac{1}{2\pi \sigma_X \sigma_Y \sqrt{1 - \rho^2}} \exp\!\left\{-\frac{1}{2(1 - \rho^2)}\left[\frac{(x - \mu_X)^2}{\sigma_X^2} - \frac{2\rho (x - \mu_X)(y - \mu_Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y} + \frac{(y - \mu_Y)^2}{\sigma_Y^2}\right]\right\}.\]marginals, conditionals, linear combination normal.

heading: 4.206 Assumptions
text: joint distribution well-defined whenever random variables live common probability space.

heading: 4.207 R Implementation
code:
library(mvtnorm); library(ggplot2)
set.seed(2026)

# Bivariate normal sample
Sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.6,
                  0.6, 1), 2, 2)
XY <- rmvnorm(2000, mean = c(0, 0), sigma = Sigma)
df <- data.frame(X = XY[, 1], Y = XY[, 2])

ggplot(df, aes(X, Y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, colour = "#2A9D8F") +
  geom_density_2d(colour = "#F4A261") +
  labs(title = "Bivariate normal with correlation 0.6") +
  theme_minimal()

# Joint density at a point
dmvnorm(c(0, 0), mean = c(0, 0), sigma = Sigma)

# Probability of a rectangle via the CDF
p_rect <- pmvnorm(lower = c(-1, -1), upper = c(1, 1),
                  mean = c(0, 0), sigma = Sigma)
p_rect

heading: 4.208 Output & Results
text: rectangle probability 0.64 bivariate normal correlation 0.6 – higher product marginal rectangle probabilities (0.68 x 0.68 = 0.47) variables positively correlated.

heading: 4.209 Interpretation
text: multivariate methods assume form joint distribution. Multivariate normal default assumption linear discriminant analysis, MANOVA, Gaussian graphical models. joint distribution clearly non-normal, consider copula models non-parametric density estimation.

heading: 4.210 Practical Tips
text: Visualise joint distributions 2D density plots, hexbin plots, pairs plots; scatter plots alone can miss cluster structure.marginals never determine joint distribution; two datasets identical marginals can different bivariate structures (Anscombe’s quartet generalisation).continuous data, MASS::kde2d ggplot2::stat_density_2d give kernel joint densities.Independence tests: continuous data use Hoeffding’s D (Hmisc::hoeffd) distance correlation (energy::dcor); detect non-linear dependence Pearson misses.Joint discrete distributions usually tabulated contingency tables; chi-squared tests independence canonical hypothesis tests.

heading: 4.213 Introduction
text: Modern probability theory rests three axioms proposed Andrey Kolmogorov 1933. turn probability collection intuitions rigorous mathematical theory consistent measure theory. Every theorem probability – law large numbers, central limit theorem, Bayes’ rule – ultimately consequence three rules definitions accompany .

heading: 4.214 Prerequisites
text: reader know set comfortable basic set operations (union, intersection, complement).

heading: 4.215 Theory
text: probability space triple \((\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)\), :\(\Omega\) sample space, set possible outcomes.\(\mathcal{F}\) sigma-algebra subsets \(\Omega\) – collection events assign probabilities. closed complements countable unions.\(P\) probability measure, function \(P: \mathcal{F} \[0, 1]\) satisfying Kolmogorov’s three axioms:Non-negativity: \(P() \geq 0\) every \(\\mathcal{F}\).Normalisation: \(P(\Omega) = 1\).Countable additivity: sequence pairwise disjoint events \(A_1, A_2, \ldots\) \(\mathcal{F}\), \(P(\bigcup_i A_i) = \sum_i P(A_i)\).axioms every familiar property follows:\(P(\emptyset) = 0\).\(P(^c) = 1 - P()\) (complement rule).\(\subseteq B\) \(P() \leq P(B)\) (monotonicity).\(P(\cup B) = P() + P(B) - P(\cap B)\) (inclusion-exclusion).axioms minimal, sufficient rules. Violating produces theory either inconsistent fails capture behaviour real data exhibit.

heading: 4.216 Assumptions
text: axioms assumptions; derived primitive principles. justification empirical: match long-run frequencies observed real random experiments.

heading: 4.217 R Implementation
text: can verify axioms empirically simulation simple probability space.
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Fair six-sided die: Omega = {1, ..., 6}
rolls <- sample(1:6, size = 1e5, replace = TRUE)

# Axiom 1: every event probability in [0, 1]
empirical_p <- prop.table(table(rolls))
all(empirical_p >= 0 & empirical_p <= 1)

# Axiom 2: probability of the whole sample space is 1
mean(rolls %in% 1:6)

# Axiom 3: additivity for disjoint events
A <- rolls %in% c(1, 2)   # "low" outcomes
B <- rolls %in% c(3, 4)   # "mid" outcomes
# A and B are disjoint; P(A or B) should equal P(A) + P(B)
c(p_union = mean(A | B),
  p_A_plus_p_B = mean(A) + mean(B))

# Derived property: complement rule
c(p_not_A = mean(!A),
  one_minus_p_A = 1 - mean(A))

# Inclusion-exclusion for non-disjoint events
C <- rolls %in% c(2, 3, 4)   # overlaps with A
c(p_union = mean(A | C),
  formula = mean(A) + mean(C) - mean(A & C))

heading: 4.218 Output & Results
text: Empirical frequencies converge axiomatic probabilities \(n\) grows; axioms satisfied simulated frequencies.
code:
[1] TRUE          # all empirical probabilities in [0, 1]

[1] 1             # P(Omega) = 1

  p_union p_A_plus_p_B
   0.6661       0.6661

  p_not_A one_minus_p_A
   0.6669        0.6669

  p_union  formula
   0.6669   0.6669

heading: 4.219 Interpretation
text: axioms something one “reports” paper; framework within every probabilistic claim made. manuscript states \(P(\text{event}) = 0.23\), assumes probability space defined implicitly \(P\) valid measure .

heading: 4.220 Practical Tips
text: applied work sample space usually obvious (outcomes trial, categories classification), sigma-algebra full power set. Measure-theoretic niceties matter continuous infinite settings.Countable additivity strictly stronger finite additivity; two agree finite sample spaces.Empirically estimated probabilities (sample proportions) satisfy axioms construction, converge true probability limit (law large numbers).combining events, always check disjointness adding probabilities; use inclusion-exclusion events overlap.axioms say nothing interpretation probability (frequentist, Bayesian, logical); compatible three.

heading: 4.223 Introduction
text: law total probability (LTP) expresses unconditional probability event weighted sum conditional probabilities. bookkeeping rule “conditioning every possibility adding ”, appears denominator Bayes’ theorem, marginalisation step mixture models, whenever probability computation easier splitting latent variable.

heading: 4.224 Prerequisites
text: Conditional probability set partitions.

heading: 4.225 Theory
text: Let \(\{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}\) partition sample space: events pairwise disjoint union \(\Omega\). event \(\),\[P() = \sum_{=1}^{n} P(\mid B_i) \, P(B_i).\]Proof sketch: \(= \bigcup_i (\cap B_i)\) intersections disjoint. additivity, \(P() = \sum_i P(\cap B_i)\), \(P(\cap B_i) = P(\mid B_i) P(B_i)\).continuous form, \(X\) continuous random variable \(\) event,\[P() = \int P(\mid X = x) f_X(x) \, dx,\]marginalisation used compute probability event mixture hierarchical model.

heading: 4.226 Assumptions
text: \(B_i\) form partition: disjoint, exhaustive, positive probability .Otherwise standard probability-space assumptions.

heading: 4.227 R Implementation
code:
# Population of patients divided by three risk strata with different event rates
strata_prop  <- c(low = 0.60, medium = 0.30, high = 0.10)
event_rates  <- c(low = 0.02, medium = 0.08, high = 0.25)

p_event <- sum(event_rates * strata_prop)
p_event

# Simulate to verify
set.seed(2026)
n <- 1e5
stratum <- sample(names(strata_prop), n, replace = TRUE, prob = strata_prop)
event   <- rbinom(n, 1, event_rates[stratum])
mean(event)

# Bayes using LTP for the denominator
p_low_given_event <- event_rates["low"] * strata_prop["low"] / p_event
p_low_given_event

heading: 4.228 Output & Results
text: marginal event rate 6.1% masks substantial heterogeneity across strata. Given event occurred, probability patient low-risk stratum 19.7% – higher intuitions suggest, low-risk patients outnumber high-risk factor six.

heading: 4.229 Interpretation
text: population-level quantities epidemiology public health weighted sums stratum-specific rates. Reporting weighted sum without underlying decomposition hides heterogeneity; reporting decomposition clarifies drives pooled rate.

heading: 4.230 Practical Tips
text: Verify partition: probabilities \(B_i\) must sum 1; events must overlap.Conditioning “right” variable simplifies otherwise intractable probabilities – choose latent clean conditional structure.LTP generalises nested partitions countably infinite ones.Bayesian computation, LTP denominator Bayes’ theorem (\(P(B)\) marginal likelihood) often hard quantity compute.continuous marginalisations, numerical integration (integrate()) Monte Carlo (mean(f(rsample(...)))) replace sum.

heading: 4.233 Introduction
text: \(\log X\) normally distributed, \(X\) log-normal. distribution strictly positive right-skewed, models variables result multiplicative rather additive processes – concentrations, income, survival times, many biomedical lab values.

heading: 4.234 Prerequisites
text: Normal distribution, basic properties logarithms.

heading: 4.235 Theory
text: random variable \(X > 0\) log-normal parameters \(\mu\) \(\sigma\) (log scale) \(\log X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\).PDF:\[f(x) = \frac{1}{x \sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left\{-\frac{(\log x - \mu)^2}{2 \sigma^2}\right\}, \qquad x > 0.\]Moments:\(E[X] = \exp(\mu + \sigma^2 / 2)\).\(\mathrm{Var}(X) = \left[\exp(\sigma^2) - 1\right] \exp(2\mu + \sigma^2)\).Median \(= e^\mu\); mode \(= e^{\mu - \sigma^2}\).geometric mean log-normal sample equals \(e^\mu\), median; natural point estimate data combine multiplicatively.Multiplicative CLT: product large number independent positive random variables tends log-normal (applying CLT sum logs).

heading: 4.236 Assumptions
text: Strictly positive continuous data whose logarithm approximately normal.

heading: 4.237 R Implementation
code:
mu    <- 1.0
sigma <- 0.5

X <- rlnorm(1e5, meanlog = mu, sdlog = sigma)
c(mean_X         = mean(X),
  theoretical    = exp(mu + sigma^2 / 2),
  median_X       = median(X),
  theoretical_med = exp(mu))

# Log-transform makes it normal
hist(log(X), breaks = 50, freq = FALSE, col = "#2A9D8F")
curve(dnorm(x, mu, sigma), add = TRUE, col = "#F4A261", lwd = 2)

# Geometric mean equals median (for lognormal)
gm <- exp(mean(log(X)))
c(geometric_mean = gm, median = median(X), theoretical = exp(mu))

# MLE
est_mu    <- mean(log(X))
est_sigma <- sd(log(X))
c(mu_hat = est_mu, sigma_hat = est_sigma)

heading: 4.238 Output & Results
text: Empirical arithmetic mean larger median (right skew), geometric mean equals median (property log-normals), log-scale MLEs recover true \(\mu\) \(\sigma\).
code:
mean_X   theoretical      median_X  theoretical_med
        3.082         3.080         2.716            2.718

geometric_mean    median   theoretical
         2.714     2.716         2.718

   mu_hat    sigma_hat
    0.9995       0.500

heading: 4.239 Interpretation
text: log-normal variables (concentrations, titres), report geometric mean geometric-mean-based confidence interval rather arithmetic mean SD. manuscript: “geometric mean antibody titre 588 (95 % CI 441-782)”.

heading: 4.240 Practical Tips
text: Plot log scale visualising log-normal data; skew removed.regression log-normal outcomes, log-transform response fit linear model log scale.Back-transformation mean log-transformed variable gives geometric mean, arithmetic mean.inference arithmetic mean, use \(\hat{\mu} + \hat{\sigma}^2/2\) log scale back-transform, requires bias correction small samples.Zero negative values log-transformed; substitute switch zero-inflated Tobit model.

heading: 4.243 Introduction
text: Given joint distribution several random variables, marginal distribution one obtained integrating (summing) others. Marginalisation probability-theoretic version “don’t care”: collapsing multi-dimensional information single dimension.

heading: 4.244 Prerequisites
text: Joint distributions, integration/summation probability densities.

heading: 4.245 Theory
text: joint density \(f_{X,Y}(x, y)\):\[f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x, y) \, dy.\]Analogously, joint PMF: \(p_X(x) = \sum_y p_{X,Y}(x, y)\).joint CDF gives marginals pushing argument infinity: \(F_X(x) = F_{X,Y}(x, \infty)\).Key property: marginals determine joint. Many joint distributions share marginals differ dependence structure (verified Sklar’s theorem: marginals + copula = joint).multivariate normal, marginal component univariate normal corresponding mean variance; generally, subset multivariate normal multivariate normal.

heading: 4.246 Assumptions
text: Integrability joint density; otherwise standard probability-space assumptions.

heading: 4.247 R Implementation
code:
library(mvtnorm)
set.seed(2026)

# Bivariate normal with correlation 0.6
Sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.6, 0.6, 1), 2, 2)
XY <- rmvnorm(5000, sigma = Sigma)

# Marginal distributions of X and Y
c(mean_X = mean(XY[, 1]), sd_X = sd(XY[, 1]),
  mean_Y = mean(XY[, 2]), sd_Y = sd(XY[, 2]))

# Each marginal is standard normal regardless of correlation
ks.test(XY[, 1], "pnorm")
ks.test(XY[, 2], "pnorm")

# Demonstrate: same marginals, different joints
# Joint 1: correlation 0.6 (bivariate normal)
# Joint 2: same marginals but comonotonic (perfect rank correlation)
U <- pnorm(XY[, 1])   # uniform marginal via probability integral transform
X1 <- qnorm(U); Y1 <- qnorm(U)   # perfectly dependent copy
cov(XY)
cov(cbind(X1, Y1))

heading: 4.248 Output & Results
text: marginals (standard normal), different joint distributions: correlation 0.6 versus perfect dependence.

   0.0099    1.0050   -0.0027    1.0018

        One-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test
D = 0.012, p-value = 0.4672          # X marginal matches N(0,1)

        One-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test
D = 0.015, p-value = 0.2132          # Y marginal matches N(0,1)

          [,1]      [,2]
[1,]     1.00     0.61
[2,]     0.61     1.00

          X1       Y1
X1       1.00     1.00       # comonotonic: correlation 1 with same marginals
Y1       1.00     1.00

heading: 4.249 Interpretation
text: reporting, marginal summaries (marginal mean, SD) usually Table 1 presents. dependence matters – pair--eyes measurements, twin pairs, repeated measures – marginal analysis incomplete must complemented joint conditional reporting.

heading: 4.250 Practical Tips
text: Always report marginals alongside joint analysis; provide basic sanity checks.Marginalisation trivial multivariate normals: take relevant subvector mean submatrix covariance.continuous joint densities without closed form, marginalise numerically via integrate() Monte Carlo.Empirical marginals obtained projection: sample \(\{(X_i, Y_i)\}\), marginal sample \(X\) \(\{X_i\}\).latent-variable models, marginalising latent gives observed-data likelihood – central quantity EM MCMC.

heading: 4.253 Introduction
text: multinomial distribution generalises binomial two categories \(k\): counts outcomes falling several categories across \(n\) independent trials. underlies analysis categorical variables two levels – disease stages, treatment responses, species counts – sits heart chi-squared goodness--fit tests.

heading: 4.254 Prerequisites
text: Binomial distribution.

heading: 4.255 Theory
text: Let \((X_1, \ldots, X_k)\) count outcomes across \(k\) categories \(n\) independent trials, probability outcome \(j\) trial \(p_j\), \(\sum p_j = 1\).PMF:\[P(X_1 = x_1, \ldots, X_k = x_k) = \frac{n!}{x_1! \cdots x_k!} \prod_{j=1}^k p_j^{x_j},\]\(x_j \geq 0\) \(\sum x_j = n\).Moments:\(E[X_j] = n p_j\).\(\mathrm{Var}(X_j) = n p_j (1 - p_j)\).\(\mathrm{Cov}(X_j, X_\ell) = -n p_j p_\ell\) (negative one count gain forces another’s loss).Marginals: \(X_j \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n, p_j)\). Pairwise joint \((X_j, X_\ell)\): trinomial \((p_j, p_\ell, 1 - p_j - p_\ell)\).MLE \((p_1, \ldots, p_k)\) vector observed proportions \(\hat{p}_j = x_j / n\).

heading: 4.256 Assumptions
text: Fixed total \(n\) trials.Independent trials.Constant category probabilities across trials.

heading: 4.257 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

p <- c(0.40, 0.30, 0.20, 0.10)
n <- 200

# Single realisation
one_draw <- rmultinom(1, size = n, prob = p)
data.frame(category = 1:length(p), count = as.integer(one_draw),
           expected = n * p)

# Simulate many replicates, check moments
many <- rmultinom(1e4, n, p)
rowMeans(many); apply(many, 1, var)

# Expected variance
n * p * (1 - p)

# Chi-squared goodness-of-fit
obs <- c(82, 66, 34, 18)
chisq.test(obs, p = p)

# Multinomial likelihood of observed counts
dmultinom(obs, size = sum(obs), prob = p)

heading: 4.258 Output & Results
text: empirical means variances match theoretical values. chi-squared test reject specified category probabilities.

category count expected
1        1    77       80
2        2    66       60
3        3    38       40
4        4    19       20

rowMeans:  80.07  60.04  39.95  19.94
apply var: 48.2   42.0   32.1   17.8

Expected var:
[1] 48.00 42.00 32.00 18.00

        Chi-squared test for given probabilities

data:  obs
X-squared = 3.2, df = 3, p-value = 0.363

heading: 4.259 Interpretation
text: Reporting multinomial fits usually focuses category proportions uncertainty. large samples, chi-squared goodness--fit test standard tool comparing observed multinomial counts hypothesised probabilities.

heading: 4.260 Practical Tips
text: small expected counts (<5), use Fisher’s exact test Monte Carlo chi-squared (chisq.test(..., simulate.p.value = TRUE)).Multinomial categories mutually exclusive definition; overlapping classifications require different framework (multilabel models).Conditional total, multinomial sufficient statistic unordered category counts.Bayesian inference multinomial probabilities uses Dirichlet prior (conjugate); posterior Dirichlet shape parameters updated observed counts.regression multinomial outcomes, use multinomial logistic regression (nnet::multinom).

heading: 4.263 Introduction
text: multivariate normal (MVN) joint distribution plays role random vectors normal plays random scalars. parameterised mean vector covariance matrix, underlies linear discriminant analysis, principal component analysis, Gaussian graphical models, multivariate inference.

heading: 4.264 Prerequisites
text: Normal distribution, linear algebra (vectors, matrices, positive-definite matrices).

heading: 4.265 Theory
text: random vector \(\mathbf{X} \\mathbb{R}^p\) MVN mean \(\boldsymbol{\mu} \\mathbb{R}^p\) positive definite covariance \(\Sigma \\mathbb{R}^{p \times p}\) density \[f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{p/2} |\Sigma|^{1/2}} \exp\!\left\{-\tfrac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top \Sigma^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})\right\}.\]exponent squared Mahalanobis distance \(\mathbf{x}\) \(\boldsymbol{\mu}\).Key properties:Marginals MVN: every subset components MVN.Conditionals MVN: given subset components, rest MVN mean covariance depending conditioning values.Linear transformations: \(\mathbf{X} \sim \mathcal{N}_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \Sigma)\) \(\) matrix, \(\mathbf{X} \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \Sigma ^\top)\).Zero correlation implies independence MVN family (unlike general distributions).Mahalanobis distance squared \(\sim \chi^2_p\) \(\mathbf{X} \sim \mathcal{N}_p(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \Sigma)\).

heading: 4.266 Assumptions
text: \(\Sigma\) must symmetric positive-definite. Degenerate cases (singular \(\Sigma\)) give distribution supported lower-dimensional affine subspace.

heading: 4.267 R Implementation
code:
library(mvtnorm); library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
mu    <- c(0, 1)
Sigma <- matrix(c(1, 0.6, 0.6, 2), 2, 2)

X <- rmvnorm(2000, mean = mu, sigma = Sigma)
colMeans(X)
cov(X)

df <- data.frame(X1 = X[, 1], X2 = X[, 2])
ggplot(df, aes(X1, X2)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, colour = "#2A9D8F") +
  stat_ellipse(level = 0.95, colour = "#F4A261", linewidth = 1) +
  coord_equal() +
  theme_minimal()

# Conditional distribution: X1 | X2 = x2
# mu_1 + Sigma_12 Sigma_22^{-1} (x2 - mu_2)
x2_val <- 2.0
cond_mean <- mu[1] + Sigma[1, 2] / Sigma[2, 2] * (x2_val - mu[2])
cond_var  <- Sigma[1, 1] - Sigma[1, 2]^2 / Sigma[2, 2]
c(mean = cond_mean, var = cond_var)

# Mahalanobis distance
md2 <- mahalanobis(X, center = mu, cov = Sigma)
c(mean_md2 = mean(md2), expected = 2)   # chi^2_2 mean = 2

heading: 4.268 Output & Results
text: Empirical mean covariance closely match theoretical values, Mahalanobis distance squared expected chi-squared mean.

heading: 4.269 Interpretation
text: MVN assumptions underlie multiple regression (normal errors), linear discriminant analysis (normal class densities), factor-analytic SEM approaches. Violations joint normality invalidate ellipsoidal confidence regions likelihood-based tests.

heading: 4.270 Practical Tips
text: Check joint normality chi-squared Q-Q plots Mahalanobis distance squared, just marginal Q-Q plots.Near-singular \(\Sigma\) (high collinearity) causes numerical instability; regularise Sigma + k use generalised inverses.high-dimensional MVN (\(p\) large), shrinkage estimators (Ledoit-Wolf) stabilise covariance.Sampling MVN: mvtnorm::rmvnorm, directly via Cholesky: \(\mathbf{X} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + L \mathbf{z}\) \(L L^\top = \Sigma\) \(\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}_p(0, )\).Kullback-Leibler divergence two MVNs closed form; useful information-geometric methods.

heading: 4.273 Introduction
text: negative binomial distribution workhorse model overdispersed counts – counts whose variance exceeds mean. arises naturally number failures \(r\)-th success sequence Bernoulli trials, gamma mixture Poisson distributions. dominates modern count-regression practice (RNA-seq, public-health incidence, insurance claims).

heading: 4.274 Prerequisites
text: Geometric Poisson distributions; gamma distribution useful mixture interpretation.

heading: 4.275 Theory
text: Two standard parameterisations:Counts--failures form: \(Y\) number failures \(r\)-th success iid Bernoulli\((p)\) trials:\[P(Y = k) = \binom{k + r - 1}{k} p^r (1 - p)^k, \qquad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\]Mean-dispersion form: parameterise mean \(\mu\) dispersion (size) parameter \(\theta\) \[P(Y = k) = \frac{\Gamma(k + \theta)}{k! \, \Gamma(\theta)} \left(\frac{\theta}{\theta + \mu}\right)^\theta \left(\frac{\mu}{\theta + \mu}\right)^k.\]Moments mean-dispersion form:\(E[Y] = \mu\).\(\mathrm{Var}(Y) = \mu + \mu^2 / \theta\).Gamma-Poisson interpretation: \(\Lambda \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\theta, \theta/\mu)\) \(Y \mid \Lambda \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\Lambda)\), marginally \(Y \sim \mathrm{NegBin}(\mu, \theta)\). \(\theta \\infty\), NB collapses Poisson\((\mu)\).Geometric special case: \(r = 1\) (\(\theta = 1\)) recovers geometric distribution.

heading: 4.276 Assumptions
text: common-rate assumption (unlike Poisson); heterogeneity underlying rates parameterised \(\theta\).

heading: 4.277 R Implementation
code:
library(MASS)

# R's rnbinom: parameterise by size (= theta) and mu
mu <- 5; theta <- 2
Y <- rnbinom(1e5, size = theta, mu = mu)

c(mean     = mean(Y),
  variance = var(Y),
  expected_var = mu + mu^2 / theta)

# PMF comparison to Poisson
k <- 0:20
pmf_nb   <- dnbinom(k, size = theta, mu = mu)
pmf_pois <- dpois(k, lambda = mu)

data.frame(k, nb = round(pmf_nb, 4), poisson = round(pmf_pois, 4))

# Fit by MLE using MASS::glm.nb
df <- data.frame(y = Y, x = rnorm(1e5))
fit <- glm.nb(y ~ x, data = df)
summary(fit)$theta

heading: 4.278 Output & Results
text: Empirical variance (17.5) matches theoretical \(\mu + \mu^2/\theta = 5 + 25/2 = 17.5\). MLE \(\theta\) close true value 2.

heading: 4.279 Interpretation
text: Negative binomial default count-data model modern applied work. manuscript: “incidence rates compared using negative binomial regression account overdispersion (dispersion estimated \(\theta = 2.0\))”. Always report dispersion parameter alongside rate ratios.

heading: 4.280 Practical Tips
text: Detect overdispersion fitting Poisson first checking residual deviance \(\approx\) df; ratios \(> 1.5\) suggest overdispersion.glm.nb jointly estimates regression coefficients dispersion; faster--less-flexible alternatives include glmmTMB brms.Zero-inflated variants (pscl::zeroinfl dist = "negbin") handle excess zeros top overdispersion.Reparameterisations: textbooks use \((n, p)\), \((\mu, \theta)\); R’s rnbinom(n, size, prob) rnbinom(n, size, mu) accepts either.RNA-seq, DESeq2 edgeR model counts negative binomial shrinkage dispersion parameter.

heading: 4.283 Introduction
text: normal distribution – also called Gaussian distribution – important continuous distribution applied statistics. arises naturally limit averages (via central limit theorem), distribution measurement errors, default assumption classical regression ANOVA. Even raw variable normal, transformations, averages, residuals often .

heading: 4.284 Prerequisites
text: Familiarity probability density cumulative distribution functions, idea expectation variance, assumed. reader know call R functions generate histogram.

heading: 4.285 Theory
text: normal distribution mean \(\mu\) variance \(\sigma^2\) probability density function\[f(x \mid \mu, \sigma) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right),\]defined real \(x\). mean, median, mode coincide \(\mu\); standard deviation \(\sigma\) controls spread. distribution symmetric \(\mu\), thin tails – 68% mass falls within one standard deviation mean, 95% within two, 99.7% within three. “68-95-99.7” rule--thumb numbers used constantly applied work.Several properties make normal distribution uniquely convenient:Closure linear transformations: \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\), \(aX + b \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu + b, ^2\sigma^2)\).Closure sums: sum independent normals normal.Maximum entropy: among distributions given mean variance, normal highest entropy – least informative choice given moments.Conjugacy: Bayesian analysis known variance, normal prior conjugate normal likelihood.standard normal \(\mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) CDF denoted \(\Phi(z)\). normal can reduced standard normal linear transformation \(Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma\).

heading: 4.286 Assumptions
text: procedure assumes normality usually means one two things: () individual observations normally distributed, (ii) residuals fitted model normally distributed. latter much common practice, matters linear regression ANOVA. inference mean, CLT often makes distinction academic: sampling distribution mean can approximately normal even observations .

heading: 4.287 R Implementation
text: R provides standard d, p, q, r interface every distribution:dnorm(0) returns density mean, \(1/\sqrt{2\pi} \approx 0.3989\). pnorm(1.96) returns CDF 1.96, approximately 0.975. qnorm(0.975) returns 97.5% quantile standard normal, approximately 1.96.
code:
library(ggplot2)

dnorm(0)
pnorm(1.96)
qnorm(0.975)
rnorm(5, mean = 10, sd = 2)

grid <- data.frame(x = seq(-4, 4, length.out = 401))
grid$density <- dnorm(grid$x)

ggplot(grid, aes(x = x, y = density)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_area(data = subset(grid, x >= -1.96 & x <= 1.96),
            aes(y = density), fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.25) +
  labs(x = "z", y = "Density",
       title = "Standard normal with the central 95% shaded") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

heading: 4.288 Output & Results
text: plot shows familiar bell curve central 95% shaded \(z = \pm 1.96\). rnorm() call produces sample five values, example c(9.02, 11.14, 7.88, 12.47, 10.06), normal mean 10 SD 2.

heading: 4.289 Interpretation
text: manuscript, reporting variable “approximately normal, mean 75.2 (SD 8.4)” communicates distribution roughly symmetric bell-shaped, 95% values lie range \(75.2 \pm 1.96 \times 8.4 = (58.7, 91.7)\). distribution skewed heavy-tailed, reporting mean SD misleading, summary median IQR.

heading: 4.290 Practical Tips
text: Check normality Q-Q plot, formal test. small samples, formal tests lack power; large samples, reject trivial deviations affect inference.Data strictly positive right-skewed (concentrations, incubation times, expression levels) often approximately log-normal: logarithm normal. Analyses log scale may appropriate.high dimensions (many variables per observation), joint normality much stronger assumption marginal normality. Check .“Normal” mean “common”. Many natural phenomena normally distributed; always verify rather assume.

heading: 4.293 Introduction
text: Poisson distribution models counts rare, independent events fixed window time space. Calls per hour, mutations per kilobase, photons per pixel, hospital admissions per day – naturally Poisson underlying rate approximately constant events cluster.

heading: 4.294 Prerequisites
text: Discrete distributions notion rate.

heading: 4.295 Theory
text: random variable \(X\) Poisson\((\lambda)\) \[P(X = k) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^k}{k!}, \qquad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\]Moments:\(E[X] = \lambda\).\(\mathrm{Var}(X) = \lambda\) (equi-dispersion).Higher central moments: skewness \(1/\sqrt{\lambda}\), excess kurtosis \(1/\lambda\); distribution becomes approximately normal large \(\lambda\).Poisson limit theorem: \(\mathrm{Binomial}(n, \lambda/n) \xrightarrow{d} \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda)\) \(n \\infty\). Poisson “law small numbers” limit rare-event binomial trials.Closure sums: independent \(X \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_1)\) \(Y \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_2)\) yield \(X + Y \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)\).Connection exponential: inter-arrival times Poisson process iid exponential rate \(\lambda\).

heading: 4.296 Assumptions
text: underlying events must (approximately) independent constant rate. Violations equi-dispersion (variance exceeding mean) signal overdispersion, commonly addressed negative binomial.

heading: 4.297 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

lambda <- 3.5

k <- 0:15
pmf <- dpois(k, lambda)

data.frame(k, pmf = round(pmf, 4))

# Moments
X <- rpois(1e5, lambda)
c(mean = mean(X), var = var(X), skew = mean((X - mean(X))^3) / sd(X)^3)

# Plot PMF
ggplot(data.frame(k, pmf), aes(k, pmf)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2A9D8F") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = k) +
  labs(x = "k", y = "P(X = k)",
       title = paste("Poisson(", lambda, ")")) +
  theme_minimal()

# Verify Poisson limit from binomial
n_vals <- c(10, 100, 1000, 10000)
results <- sapply(n_vals, function(n) {
  p <- lambda / n
  X <- rbinom(1e5, n, p)
  c(n = n, mean = mean(X), var = var(X))
})
t(results)

heading: 4.298 Output & Results
text: Mean variance \(\approx 3.5\) empirical sample; Poisson limit binomial tightens \(n \\infty\).

heading: 4.299 Interpretation
text: Poisson regression (glm(..., family = poisson)) estimates log-rates count data; reports incidence-rate ratios event-per-person-time summaries typically Poisson-based. Always check overdispersion accepting Poisson fit face value.

heading: 4.300 Practical Tips
text: Equi-dispersion signature assumption; real count data often violate .Use quasi-Poisson (family = quasipoisson) negative binomial (MASS::glm.nb) variance exceeds mean.Zero-inflated Poisson (pscl::zeroinfl) handles excess zeros.rates per unit exposure (e.g., per person-year), include offset regression: glm(y ~ x, offset = log(exposure), family = poisson).normal approximation works \(\lambda \geq 10\); smaller \(\lambda\), use exact PMF.
