heading: 4.303 Introduction
text: continuous random variable, probability distributed smoothly across support rather concentrated countable points. probability density function (PDF) encodes distribution: integrating PDF set gives probability variable lands set.

heading: 4.304 Prerequisites
text: Calculus (integration) concept continuous random variable.

heading: 4.305 Theory
text: continuous random variable \(X\) PDF \(f_X\) , every measurable set \(\),\[P(X \) = \int_A f_X(x) \, dx.\]valid PDF satisfies:\(f_X(x) \geq 0\) \(x\).\(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_X(x) \, dx = 1\).Crucially, \(f_X(x)\) probability. continuous \(X\), \(P(X = x) = 0\) every \(x\). density can exceed 1 given point, provided integral bounded. Density units inverse \(x\): \(x\) seconds, \(f_X(x)\) units \(1/\text{second}\).Useful relationships:CDF: \(F_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^x f_X(u) \, du\).PDF CDF: \(f_X(x) = F_X'(x)\) \(F_X\) differentiable.Expected value: \(E[X] = \int x f_X(x) \, dx\).Variance: \(\mathrm{Var}(X) = \int (x - E[X])^2 f_X(x) \, dx\).R, standard continuous distribution d*() function returning PDF.

heading: 4.306 Assumptions
text: variable continuous (absolutely continuous respect Lebesgue measure). Mixed discrete variables ordinary PDFs (though mixed case generalised density respect mixed measure).

heading: 4.307 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

x_grid <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 801)
fx <- dnorm(x_grid, mean = 0, sd = 1)

ggplot(data.frame(x = x_grid, f = fx), aes(x, f)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2A9D8F", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_area(data = subset(data.frame(x = x_grid, f = fx),
                          x >= -1.96 & x <= 1.96),
            aes(y = f), fill = "#2A9D8F", alpha = 0.25) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "f(x)",
       title = "Standard normal PDF with central 95% shaded") +
  theme_minimal()

# Verify normalisation by numerical integration
integrate(dnorm, -Inf, Inf)

# Probability of an interval via integration
integrate(dnorm, -1.96, 1.96)        # about 0.95

# Equivalently via the CDF
pnorm(1.96) - pnorm(-1.96)

# Moments via integration
integrate(function(x) x * dnorm(x), -Inf, Inf)        # E[X]
integrate(function(x) x^2 * dnorm(x), -Inf, Inf)      # E[X^2]

heading: 4.308 Output & Results
text: Integrating standard normal PDF \([-1.96, 1.96]\) gives 0.95 – well-known central probability – pnorm difference recovers value via CDF.
code:
1 with absolute error < 9.4e-05

0.9500042 with absolute error < 1e-11

[1] 0.9500042

-4.4e-11 with absolute error < ...   # E[X] ≈ 0
1 with absolute error < ...          # E[X^2] = Var(X) for standard normal

heading: 4.309 Interpretation
text: applied purposes, PDF visualised curve; probabilities computed via p*() functions R rather explicit integration. reporting continuous distribution paper, density plot, mean, SD, median standard summaries.

heading: 4.310 Practical Tips
text: Density point probability; compare \(f(x_1)\) \(f(x_2)\) probabilities outcomes 1 2. Compare \(P(X \(x_1 - h, x_1 + h))\) small \(h\) instead.density can unbounded (e.g., Gamma shape \(< 1\) near \(0\)); integrability, boundedness, matters.Kernel density estimates (density()) approximate PDF sample.Log-densities (dnorm(..., log = TRUE)) avoid underflow evaluating likelihoods many observations.relationship \(f = F'\) starting point inverse-CDF method: generate \(X\), apply \(F^{-1}\) uniform random variable.

heading: 4.313 Introduction
text: discrete random variable, probability mass function (PMF) gives probability variable takes specific value. discrete analogue density function, probability event reduces summing PMF relevant values.

heading: 4.314 Prerequisites
text: Discrete random variables basic probability.

heading: 4.315 Theory
text: discrete random variable \(X\) countable support \(\{x_1, x_2, \ldots\}\), PMF \[p_X(x) = P(X = x), \qquad x \\mathbb{R}.\]valid PMF satisfies:\(p_X(x) \geq 0\) \(x\).\(\sum_x p_X(x) = 1\) (total probability 1).\(p_X(x) = 0\) \(x\) outside support.probability event sum PMF:\[P(X \) = \sum_{x \} p_X(x).\]CDF running cumulative sum:\[F_X(x) = P(X \leq x) = \sum_{x_i \leq x} p_X(x_i).\]Expected value variance follow usual formulas:\[E[X] = \sum_x x \, p_X(x), \qquad \mathrm{Var}(X) = \sum_x (x - E[X])^2 \, p_X(x).\]Common discrete PMFs R use d<name>() family: dbinom, dpois, dgeom, dhyper, dnbinom, dmultinom.

heading: 4.316 Assumptions
text: variable must discrete. Assigning PMF continuous variable produces degenerate object (zero every point, failing normalisation).

heading: 4.317 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

# Binomial PMF for n = 10 trials, p = 0.3
n <- 10; p <- 0.3
k <- 0:n
pmf <- dbinom(k, size = n, prob = p)

data.frame(k, pmf)

# Verify normalisation
sum(pmf)

# Compute probabilities of events via sums
sum(pmf[k >= 3 & k <= 5])        # P(3 <= X <= 5)
sum(pmf[k %in% c(0, n)])          # P(X = 0 or X = n)

# Expected value and variance
sum(k * pmf)
sum((k - sum(k * pmf))^2 * pmf)

ggplot(data.frame(k, pmf), aes(k, pmf)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2A9D8F") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = k) +
  labs(x = "k", y = "P(X = k)",
       title = "Binomial(10, 0.3) PMF") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 4.319 Interpretation
text: PMF table bar chart natural display discrete distribution. manuscript, reporting “number responders 10 binomially distributed \(p = 0.3\)” conveys full PMF implicitly.

heading: 4.320 Practical Tips
text: Make sure PMF sums exactly 1 define custom distribution; normalise explicitly.mixtures Poissons, Bernoullis, similar, sums PMFs computed componentwise give mixture PMF.Use choose(n, k) * p^k * (1 - p)^(n - k) symbolic verification binomial PMF.Numerical underflow can corrupt PMFs extreme values; work log scale (dbinom(k, ..., log = TRUE)) \(n\) large.Histograms samples approximate PMF finite samples; true PMF-bar-chart, use geom_col() exact support values.

heading: 4.323 Introduction
text: random variable function assigns real number outcome random experiment. concept packages probabilistic thinking language numbers – means, variances, correlations – abstraction every statistical method rests.

heading: 4.324 Prerequisites
text: Sample spaces, events, probability measure.

heading: 4.325 Theory
text: Given probability space \((\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)\), random variable measurable function\[X: \Omega \\mathbb{R}\]\(\{X \leq x\} = \{\omega : X(\omega) \leq x\} \\mathcal{F}\) every \(x \\mathbb{R}\). Measurability ensures can assign probabilities events defined terms \(X\).distribution \(X\) probability measure induces \(\mathbb{R}\):\[P_X() = P(X \) = P(\{\omega : X(\omega) \\}).\]practice, rarely work \(\Omega\) directly; work distribution \(P_X\), summarised CDF \(F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)\) (exists) PMF \(p_X\) density \(f_X\).Random variables classified :Discrete: takes values countable set. Described PMF.Continuous (absolutely continuous): density. Described PDF.Mixed: combines discrete atoms continuous part. Common censored / thresholded data.Transformations random variables give new random variables: \(X\) random variable \(g: \mathbb{R} \\mathbb{R}\) measurable, \(Y = g(X)\) random variable distribution determined \(X\) \(g\).

heading: 4.326 Assumptions
text: random variable well-defined provided underlying probability space exists function measurable. Measurability automatic every function arises practice (continuous piecewise-continuous).

heading: 4.327 R Implementation
text: R, usually work random variables via distributions.
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Discrete random variable: binomial
X_discrete <- rbinom(10000, size = 10, prob = 0.3)
head(sort(unique(X_discrete)))
prop.table(table(X_discrete))[1:5]     # empirical PMF

# Continuous random variable: normal
X_continuous <- rnorm(10000, mean = 0, sd = 1)
quantile(X_continuous, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))

# Transformation: Y = g(X) = X^2 of a standard normal
Y <- X_continuous^2
summary(Y)
var(Y)     # theoretical variance is 2 (Y ~ chi-squared with 1 df, variance 2)

# Mixed RV: censored at 0 (negative values truncated)
X_mixed <- pmax(rnorm(10000), 0)
mean(X_mixed == 0)
mean(X_mixed)

heading: 4.328 Output & Results
text: quantity (PMF, quantile, variance transformation, mixed distribution’s atom) statistic corresponding random variable.

heading: 4.329 Interpretation
text: Random variables let us move “outcome space” thinking “number space” thinking. methods section typically declares random variable implicitly: “Let \(X\) systolic blood pressure randomly selected patient” specifies random variable without bothering underlying \(\Omega\).

heading: 4.330 Practical Tips
text: applied work, think random variables “generators data” specified distribution; measurability formalism background.random variable distinct realisation. “\(X\)” variable; “\(x_i\)” observed value unit \(\).Vector-valued random variables (random vectors) generalise scalar case; distribution joint distribution components.Transformations preserve measurability; common operations (sums, products, functions) always measurable practice.R, d*, p*, q*, r* families functions parameterise distributions; think computing quantities random variables specified distribution.

heading: 4.333 Introduction
text: probability can assigned, need know happen. sample space set possible outcomes random experiment; events subsets sample space assign probabilities. Clean reasoning probability begins clear identification .

heading: 4.334 Prerequisites
text: Familiarity basic set theory – unions, intersections, complements – sufficient.

heading: 4.335 Theory
text: sample space \(\Omega\) set possible outcomes. Examples:Coin toss: \(\Omega = \{H, T\}\).Two dice: \(\Omega = \{(, j) : , j \\{1, \ldots, 6\}\}\), 36 outcomes.Waiting time: \(\Omega = [0, \infty)\).DNA base position \(\): \(\Omega = \{, C, G, T\}\).event subset \(\subseteq \Omega\). say event \(\) occurs realised outcome \(\omega\) \(\). Key operations:Union \(\cup B\): event \(\) \(B\) () occurs.Intersection \(\cap B\): event \(\) \(B\) occur.Complement \(^c\): event \(\) occur.Difference \(\setminus B = \cap B^c\): \(\) \(B\).Two key identities – De Morgan’s laws – relate complements set operations:\[(\cup B)^c = ^c \cap B^c, \qquad (\cap B)^c = ^c \cup B^c.\]Events \(\) \(B\) disjoint (mutually exclusive) \(\cap B = \emptyset\). independent (probability \(P\)) \(P(\cap B) = P()P(B)\). Disjointness sets; independence probabilities – two concepts confused.

heading: 4.336 Assumptions
text: assumptions beyond underlying probability space well-defined.

heading: 4.337 R Implementation
text: can enumerate finite sample spaces explicitly manipulate events logical vectors.
code:
# Two dice: enumerate the 36-element sample space
omega <- expand.grid(die1 = 1:6, die2 = 1:6)
head(omega, 3)

# Define events as logical vectors indexing rows of omega
A <- with(omega, die1 == 1)            # first die is 1
B <- with(omega, die1 + die2 == 7)     # sum equals 7

# Set operations
A_union_B     <- A | B
A_inter_B     <- A & B
A_complement  <- !A
A_minus_B     <- A & !B

# Under a uniform probability on 36 outcomes:
p <- function(E) mean(E)   # proportion of outcomes in E
c(P_A         = p(A),
  P_B         = p(B),
  P_A_union_B = p(A_union_B),
  P_A_and_B   = p(A_inter_B))

# Verify inclusion-exclusion: P(A U B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A n B)
c(lhs = p(A_union_B),
  rhs = p(A) + p(B) - p(A_inter_B))

# Verify De Morgan: (A U B)^c = A^c n B^c
all((!(A | B)) == (!A & !B))

heading: 4.338 Output & Results
text: identities satisfied. \(P(\cup B) = 11/36\) 11 outcomes satisfy “first die 1 sum 7”, formula recovers value.

heading: 4.339 Interpretation
text: writing methods section, sample space usually defined implicitly design (“patients followed 30 days classified event / event”). Making explicit – “sample space \(\{\text{event}, \text{event}\}\)” – rarely necessary except pedagogical contexts.

heading: 4.340 Practical Tips
text: continuous sample spaces (\(\Omega = \mathbb{R}\) higher-dimensional), every subset event; measurability restrictions apply. finite countable sample spaces, every subset event.conflate disjoint independent. Two disjoint events positive probability maximally dependent: one occurs, .De Morgan’s laws generalise countable unions intersections; indispensable reasoning complements complex events.Encoding finite sample spaces R (via expand.grid combinations) makes exploratory probability calculations explicit checkable.Inclusion-exclusion extends many events: \(P(\bigcup A_i) = \sum P(A_i) - \sum P(A_i \cap A_j) + \ldots\).

heading: 4.343 Introduction
text: time--event random variable \(T \geq 0\), survival function \(S(t) = P(T > t)\) probability experienced event time \(t\). complement CDF single important descriptive quantity survival analysis.

heading: 4.344 Prerequisites
text: Random variables, CDF, basic survival-analysis concepts.

heading: 4.345 Theory
text: non-negative random variable \(T\),\[S(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t).\]Properties:\(S(0) = 1\), \(\lim_{t \\infty} S(t) = 0\).Non-increasing.Right-continuous (convention).Related quantities:PDF: \(f(t) = -S'(t)\).Hazard: \(h(t) = f(t)/S(t)\).Cumulative hazard: \(H(t) = -\log S(t)\).Classical forms:Exponential: \(S(t) = e^{-\lambda t}\).Weibull: \(S(t) = \exp\!\big[-(t/\lambda)^k\big]\).Log-normal: \(S(t) = 1 - \Phi\!\big((\log t - \mu)/\sigma\big)\).Kaplan-Meier non-parametric MLE \(S\) right-censored data: step function drops observed event time factor reflecting fraction -risk set experiences event.

heading: 4.346 Assumptions
text: Non-negative random variable representing time event. Kaplan-Meier: independent censoring.

heading: 4.347 R Implementation
code:
library(survival)
set.seed(2026)

# Theoretical survival functions
t_grid <- seq(0, 10, length.out = 400)
S_exp     <- exp(-0.3 * t_grid)
S_weibull <- exp(-(t_grid / 3)^2)
S_lnorm   <- 1 - plnorm(t_grid, meanlog = log(3), sdlog = 0.5)

plot(t_grid, S_exp, type = "l", col = "#6A4C93", lwd = 2,
     main = "Theoretical survival functions", xlab = "t", ylab = "S(t)")
lines(t_grid, S_weibull, col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2)
lines(t_grid, S_lnorm,   col = "#F4A261", lwd = 2)
legend("topright", c("exponential", "Weibull k=2", "log-normal"),
       col = c("#6A4C93", "#2A9D8F", "#F4A261"), lwd = 2)

# Empirical Kaplan-Meier from censored data
n <- 80
T_true <- rweibull(n, shape = 2, scale = 3)
C      <- runif(n, 0, 10)
T_obs  <- pmin(T_true, C)
delta  <- as.integer(T_true <= C)

km <- survfit(Surv(T_obs, delta) ~ 1)
plot(km, conf.int = TRUE, main = "Kaplan-Meier estimate",
     xlab = "t", ylab = "S(t)", col = "#2A9D8F")
lines(t_grid, exp(-(t_grid / 3)^2), col = "#F4A261", lwd = 2)

# Median survival
summary(km)$table["median"]
qweibull(0.5, shape = 2, scale = 3)

heading: 4.348 Output & Results
text: Empirical theoretical median survival times agree. Kaplan-Meier step function closely tracks true Weibull survival curve.

heading: 4.349 Interpretation
text: Reporting median survival 95 % CI Kaplan-Meier estimate standard headline statistic survival studies. Survival functions typically compared across groups via log-rank tests visualised together axes.

heading: 4.350 Practical Tips
text: Report medians CIs survival drops 0.5; report specific-time survival (1-year, 5-year) study ends median reached.Kaplan-Meier distribution-free; parametric survival models (Weibull, log-normal) give smooth curves commit shape.Include risk table Kaplan-Meier plots (survminer::ggsurvplot(..., risk.table = TRUE)).heavy censoring, tail survival curve poorly estimated; avoid -interpreting late-follow-differences.Survival functions never take values 1 0; empirical estimator returns values, bug.

text: t-distribution (also called Student’s t, W. S. Gosset’s pseudonym) arises ratio standard normal scaled square root independent chi-squared. reference distribution t-tests small-sample confidence intervals normal mean unknown variance.

heading: 4.354 Prerequisites
text: Normal distribution, chi-squared distribution.

heading: 4.355 Theory
text:
\(Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) \(V \sim \chi^2_
u\) independent, \[T =  rac{Z}{\sqrt{V / 
u}} \sim t_

heading: 4.356 Assumptions
text: Normal underlying data (large enough \(n\) CLT make approximation reasonable).

heading: 4.357 R Implementation
code:
# PDF comparison
x <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 400)
plot(x, dnorm(x), type = "l", col = "black", lwd = 2,
     main = "t with 3, 10, 30 df vs N(0,1)", ylab = "f(x)")
lines(x, dt(x, df = 3),  col = "#F4A261", lwd = 2)
lines(x, dt(x, df = 10), col = "#6A4C93", lwd = 2)
lines(x, dt(x, df = 30), col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2)
legend("topright", c("normal", "t(3)", "t(10)", "t(30)"),
       col = c("black", "#F4A261", "#6A4C93", "#2A9D8F"), lwd = 2)

# Verify construction
n <- 1e5; nu <- 5
Z <- rnorm(n); V <- rchisq(n, df = nu)
T_sim <- Z / sqrt(V / nu)
c(emp_mean = mean(T_sim), theoretical = 0,
  emp_var  = var(T_sim),  theoretical_var = nu / (nu - 2))

# Small-sample CI for a mean
set.seed(2026)
X <- rnorm(15, mean = 10, sd = 3)
t_crit <- qt(0.975, df = length(X) - 1)
mean(X) + c(-1, 1) * t_crit * sd(X) / sqrt(length(X))

heading: 4.358 Output & Results
text: Empirical moments match theoretical \(\nu = 5\), small-sample CI properly uses \(t_{14}\) critical values.
code:

  -0.003           0    1.665           1.667

[1]  8.76  12.06     # 95% CI using t_14

heading: 4.359 Interpretation
text: Reporting t-interval t-test “\(t(n-1) = \ldots\)” manuscript reporting t-distribution-based inference. df equals \(n - 1\) one-sample t-test; Welch’s two-sample t-test uses Satterthwaite df may fractional.

heading: 4.363 Introduction
text: Given random variable \(X\) function \(g\), distribution \(Y = g(X)\) determined distribution \(X\) function. change--variables formula computes new distribution; strictly monotone differentiable \(g\) involves Jacobian factor.

heading: 4.364 Prerequisites
text: Random variables, PDF, basic calculus.

heading: 4.365 Theory
text: Univariate, monotone case. Let \(X\) density \(f_X\) support \(\mathcal{X}\), let \(g\) strictly monotone differentiable \(\mathcal{X}\). \(Y = g(X)\) density\[f_Y(y) = f_X(g^{-1}(y)) \left|\frac{d g^{-1}(y)}{dy}\right|.\]absolute value derivative inverse Jacobian.Non-monotone case. \(g\) piecewise monotone, partition domain monotone segments sum contributions:\[f_Y(y) = \sum_i f_X(x_i) / |g'(x_i)|\]\(\{x_i\}\) solutions \(g(x) = y\).Multivariate case. \(\mathbf{Y} = g(\mathbf{X})\) \(g: \mathbb{R}^n \\mathbb{R}^n\) diffeomorphism,\[f_{\mathbf{Y}}(\mathbf{y}) = f_{\mathbf{X}}(g^{-1}(\mathbf{y})) \, |\det J_{g^{-1}}(\mathbf{y})|,\]\(J_{g^{-1}}\) Jacobian matrix \(g^{-1}\).Common examples:\(Y = aX + b\): \(f_Y(y) = f_X((y - b)/) / ||\).\(Y = X^2\), \(X\) standard normal: \(Y \sim \chi^2_1\).\(Y = e^X\), \(X\) normal: \(Y\) log-normal.\(Y = F^{-1}(U)\), \(U \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)\): inverse-CDF method generating arbitrary distributions.

heading: 4.366 Assumptions
text: \(g\) must measurable.change--variables formula apply, \(g\) must strictly monotone differentiable (piecewise ).

heading: 4.367 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Case 1: Y = X^2 for X ~ N(0, 1); Y should be chi-squared with 1 df
X <- rnorm(1e5)
Y <- X^2

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
hist(Y, breaks = 100, freq = FALSE, main = "Empirical", col = "#2A9D8F")
curve(dchisq(x, df = 1), from = 0, to = 10, add = TRUE, col = "#F4A261", lwd = 2)

# Case 2: Y = exp(X) for X ~ N(mu, sigma) is log-normal
mu <- 1; sigma <- 0.5
X2 <- rnorm(1e5, mu, sigma)
Y2 <- exp(X2)
curve(dlnorm(x, mu, sigma), from = 0, to = 10,
      main = "exp(N(1, 0.25)) vs lognormal PDF",
      col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2)
hist(Y2, breaks = 100, freq = FALSE, add = TRUE, border = "#F4A261")

par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

# Case 3: Inverse-CDF method to generate exponential from uniform
U <- runif(1e5)
E <- -log(1 - U)    # F^{-1}(u) = -log(1 - u) for rate = 1
c(mean = mean(E), theoretical = 1)

heading: 4.368 Output & Results
text: Empirical histograms match theoretical transformed densities. inverse-CDF method recovers exponential mean.
code:
mean  theoretical
   0.999        1.000

heading: 4.369 Interpretation
text: Many “derived” distributions statistics (chi-squared normals, log-normal normals, Cauchy normals, Beta gammas) arise transformations simpler distributions. Recognising transformation short-cuts many derivations.

heading: 4.370 Practical Tips
text: Always check strict monotonicity applying simple Jacobian formula; square-mapping \(X^2\) classic non-monotone case.inverse-CDF simulation, need CDF’s inverse; use qnorm, qgamma, etc., R.Jacobian multivariate linear transformation \(Y = AX\) \(|det |\), independent location.Delta method “linearised” version change--variables formula, used asymptotic distributions.Non-bijective transformations (e.g., \(|X|\)) require summing preimages, non-monotone case.

heading: 4.373 Introduction
text: uniform distribution spreads probability equally across support. continuous uniform \([, b]\) building block random-number generation; discrete uniform \(\{1, \ldots, k\}\) describes fair dice random selection. simplest non-trivial distribution.

heading: 4.374 Prerequisites
text: Continuous discrete random variables.

heading: 4.375 Theory
text: Continuous uniform \(\mathrm{Uniform}(, b)\) PDF\[f(x) = \frac{1}{b - }, \qquad \leq x \leq b,\]moments \(E[X] = (+ b)/2\) \(\mathrm{Var}(X) = (b - )^2 / 12\).Discrete uniform \(\{x_1, \ldots, x_k\}\): \(P(X = x_i) = 1/k\) \(\). \(\{1, \ldots, k\}\): \(E[X] = (k+1)/2\), \(\mathrm{Var}(X) = (k^2 - 1)/12\).Probability integral transform: \(X\) continuous CDF \(F_X\), \(F_X(X) \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)\). inverse: \(X = F_X^{-1}(U)\) \(U \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)\) generates \(X\) uniform draws. inverse-CDF method simulation.

heading: 4.376 Assumptions
text: Continuous uniform assumes variable genuinely constant density; discrete uniform assumes genuinely equal probabilities across specified set.

heading: 4.377 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Continuous uniform
U <- runif(1e5, min = 0, max = 1)
c(mean = mean(U), theoretical = 0.5,
  var  = var(U),  theoretical_var = 1/12)

# Inverse-CDF method: generate exponential from uniform
lambda <- 2
E <- -log(1 - U) / lambda   # F^{-1}(u) = -log(1 - u) / lambda
c(empirical_mean = mean(E), exponential_mean = 1 / lambda)

# Discrete uniform via sample()
die <- sample(1:6, 1e5, replace = TRUE)
c(mean_die = mean(die), theoretical = 3.5,
  var_die  = var(die),  theoretical_var = 35/12)

heading: 4.378 Output & Results
text: Empirical moments match theoretical exactly Monte Carlo error, inverse-CDF method recovers exponential mean.
code:
mean theoretical          var  theoretical_var
    0.5003      0.5000       0.0835           0.0833

empirical_mean  exponential_mean
        0.4983             0.500

  mean_die  theoretical     var_die  theoretical_var
     3.504        3.500       2.924             2.917

heading: 4.379 Interpretation
text: Uniforms rarely appear substantive models real data pervade simulation, randomisation, Monte Carlo methods. Reporting “trial assignment simulated via sample()” implicitly invoking discrete uniform.

heading: 4.380 Practical Tips
text: runif(n) defaults \([0, 1]\); specify min/max explicitly avoid surprises.uniform distribution zero skewness excess kurtosis \(-1.2\) (light tails).true randomness hardware sources, avoid pseudo-random runif; use /dev/urandom random R package online services.Uniform priors often overly informative transformed scales (uniform \(\sigma\) uniform \(\sigma^2\)).Jacobian inverse-CDF transform \(1/f_X(x)\); probability integral transform works.

heading: 4.383 Introduction
text: Variance measures far single random variable ranges around mean; covariance measures two random variables move together around means. Together populate covariance matrix random vector, central object multivariate statistics, regression, portfolio theory.

heading: 4.384 Prerequisites
text: Expectation basic properties random variables.

heading: 4.385 Theory
text: Variance \(X\):\[\mathrm{Var}(X) = E[(X - E[X])^2] = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2.\]Covariance \(X\) \(Y\):\[\mathrm{Cov}(X, Y) = E[(X - E[X])(Y - E[Y])] = E[XY] - E[X] E[Y].\]Variance special case: \(\mathrm{Cov}(X, X) = \mathrm{Var}(X)\).Properties:Non-negativity: \(\mathrm{Var}(X) \geq 0\), equality iff \(X\) constant almost surely.Scale: \(\mathrm{Var}(aX + b) = ^2 \mathrm{Var}(X)\).Symmetry: \(\mathrm{Cov}(X, Y) = \mathrm{Cov}(Y, X)\).Bilinearity: \(\mathrm{Cov}(aX + b, cY + d) = ac \, \mathrm{Cov}(X, Y)\).Sum: \(\mathrm{Var}(X + Y) = \mathrm{Var}(X) + \mathrm{Var}(Y) + 2 \mathrm{Cov}(X, Y)\).Independence implies zero covariance: \(X \perp Y \Rightarrow \mathrm{Cov}(X, Y) = 0\). converse false.covariance matrix random vector \(\mathbf{X} = (X_1, \ldots, X_p)\) \(p \times p\) matrix \(\Sigma\) entries \(\Sigma_{ij} = \mathrm{Cov}(X_i, X_j)\). \(\Sigma\) symmetric positive semi-definite.

heading: 4.386 Assumptions
text: \(E[X^2] < \infty\) \(E[Y^2] < \infty\) variance covariance defined.

heading: 4.387 R Implementation
code:
library(mvtnorm)
set.seed(2026)

# Univariate variance
X <- rnorm(1e5, mean = 10, sd = 3)
c(theoretical = 9, empirical = var(X))

# Covariance from bivariate normal with known Sigma
Sigma <- matrix(c(4, 1.8,
                  1.8, 9), nrow = 2)
n <- 1e5
XY <- rmvnorm(n, mean = c(0, 0), sigma = Sigma)
cov(XY)
cov(XY) / (sqrt(Sigma[1, 1] * Sigma[2, 2]))   # correlation = 0.3

# Variance of a sum verifies the formula
X <- XY[, 1]; Y <- XY[, 2]
c(var_sum = var(X + Y),
  formula = var(X) + var(Y) + 2 * cov(X, Y))

# Zero covariance does not imply independence
X <- rnorm(1e5)
Y <- X^2
c(cov = cov(X, Y),
  correlation = cor(X, Y))

heading: 4.388 Output & Results
text: Empirical variance covariance match theoretical values within Monte Carlo error. \(X\) \(X^2\) zero covariance clearly dependent – covariance detects linear association.

heading: 4.389 Interpretation
text: Reporting mean variance (SD) gives rough summary distribution’s location spread; reporting covariance matrix gives joint structure several variables. multivariate methods (PCA, MANOVA, linear discriminant analysis) work sample covariance matrices.

heading: 4.390 Practical Tips
text: Sample variance uses divisor \(n - 1\) unbiasedness; R’s var() uses \(n - 1\) default.\(\mathrm{Cov}(X, Y) = 0\) imply independence except Gaussian case.covariance matrix must positive semi-definite; estimation small samples can produce near-singular matrices.Linearly transforming random vector matrix \(\) changes covariance matrix \(\Sigma ^\top\).robust multivariate covariance estimation contaminated data, robustbase::covMcd standard MCD estimator.

heading: 4.393 Introduction
text: Weibull distribution generalises exponential allow monotone non-constant hazard rates. default parametric model reliability engineering common choice parametric survival analysis. shape parameter controls whether hazard increasing (ageing), decreasing (early failures dominate), constant (exponential).

heading: 4.394 Prerequisites
text: Exponential distribution, hazard survival functions.

heading: 4.395 Theory
text: Weibull random variable shape \(k > 0\) scale \(\lambda > 0\) PDF\[f(x) = \frac{k}{\lambda}\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)^{k-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)^k\right], \qquad x \geq 0.\]CDF: \(F(x) = 1 - \exp\!\big[-(x/\lambda)^k\big]\).Hazard function: \(h(x) = (k/\lambda)(x/\lambda)^{k-1}\). Three regimes:\(k < 1\): decreasing hazard (early failures / “infant mortality”).\(k = 1\): constant hazard (exponential).\(k > 1\): increasing hazard (wear-/ ageing).Moments:\(E[X] = \lambda \, \Gamma(1 + 1/k)\).\(\mathrm{Var}(X) = \lambda^2 \left[\Gamma(1 + 2/k) - \Gamma(1 + 1/k)^2\right]\).Accelerated failure time form: \(\log T = \mu + \sigma W\) \(W\) standard extreme-value; AFT parameters relate Weibull’s \(k = 1/\sigma\) \(\lambda = e^\mu\).

heading: 4.396 Assumptions
text: Non-negative continuous data, monotone hazard (log-hazard linear log-time).

heading: 4.397 R Implementation
code:
library(survival)

# PDFs for three shape regimes
x <- seq(0, 5, length.out = 400)
plot(x, dweibull(x, shape = 0.5, scale = 1), type = "l", col = "#F4A261",
     lwd = 2, main = "Weibull PDFs", ylab = "f(x)")
lines(x, dweibull(x, shape = 1, scale = 1), col = "#6A4C93", lwd = 2)
lines(x, dweibull(x, shape = 2.5, scale = 1), col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2)
legend("topright", c("k = 0.5 (decreasing h)", "k = 1 (exponential)", "k = 2.5 (increasing h)"),
       col = c("#F4A261", "#6A4C93", "#2A9D8F"), lwd = 2)

# Simulate and fit
set.seed(2026)
X <- rweibull(500, shape = 2.0, scale = 1.5)
fit <- survreg(Surv(X, rep(1, length(X))) ~ 1, dist = "weibull")
summary(fit)

# Convert survreg output to Weibull parameters
c(shape = 1 / fit$scale,
  scale = exp(coef(fit)))

heading: 4.398 Output & Results
text: fit recovers \(k \approx 2\) \(\lambda \approx 1.5\) – true parameters. increasing-hazard regime visible PDF.
code:

(Intercept)  0.40578    0.01574 25.78  <2e-16
Log(scale)  -0.69312    0.03293 -21.0  <2e-16

Scale = 0.500

   shape    scale
   2.000    1.501

heading: 4.399 Interpretation
text: survival analysis, Weibull model often starting parametric fit: “fitted Weibull regression shape \(k = 1.3\) (95 % CI 1.1-1.5), indicating increasing hazard follow-.” Weibull default choice proportional-hazards assumption holds transformed scale.

heading: 4.400 Practical Tips
text: R’s rweibull(n, shape, scale) conventions differ survreg, parameterises via intercept scale = \(1/k\).right-censored data, use survreg(..., dist = "weibull"), fitdistr complete data.Check fit via log-log plot: \(\log(-\log S(t))\) vs \(\log t\) approximately linear.shape estimates close 1 narrow CI, consider simpler exponential.flexsurv::flexsurvreg(..., dist = "weibull") modern alternative richer diagnostics.

Q-Q plots. Simulate data, compare hypothesised distribution.normal sample lies 45° reference line; heavy-tailed

  group_by(sample) |>
  summarise(p = shapiro.test(value)$p.value, .groups = "drop")

heading: 4.418 Background
text:
Bernoulli distribution simplest non-trivial distribution:
one coin, two outcomes, single probability p. binomial adds
structure — n independent Bernoulli trials p — 
gives distribution count successes. Poisson arises
limit n large, p small, np stays fixed;
describes counts rare events fixed window opportunity,
without needing specify n p separately.count-like quantities biomedicine can modelled one 
three. Whether tumour responding (Bernoulli), many 
twenty patients relapse (binomial), many emergency admissions
arrive hour (Poisson) — three distributions cover 
great deal ground. simulation-first approach makes 
concrete write formula.common conflation binomial Poisson 
clinical literature. sample size large event
rare, two distributions give nearly identical probabilities
either defensible. event rare, Poisson can
assign meaningful probability impossible events (successes
trials), binomial strictly better.

heading: 4.419 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 4.420 1. Hypothesis
text:
characterise three discrete distributions simulation, 
compare empirical frequencies theoretical probabilities.
hypothesis tested.

heading: 4.421 2. Visualise
text:
Bernoulli trial: one coin, success probability 0.3.binomial count: many successes 20 trials p = 0.3,
repeated 10,000 times.
code:
bern <- rbinom(n_trials, 1, 0.3)
mean(bern)   # empirical p
bin  <- rbinom(n_rep, size = 20, prob = 0.3)

bin_tibble <- tibble(k = bin) |>
  count(k) |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n),
         theo = dbinom(k, 20, 0.3))
bin_tibble |>
  ggplot(aes(k, prop)) +
  geom_col(fill = "grey70", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(y = theo), colour = "firebrick", size = 2) +
  labs(x = "Successes in 20 trials",
       y = "Proportion (bars) vs. P(k) (points)")

heading: 4.422 3. Assumptions
text:
Binomial assumes fixed n, independence, constant p. Poisson
assumes constant rate independent counts disjoint
intervals. n large p small np = λ, 
binomial approaches Poisson.
code:
probs <- tibble(
  k = 0:10,
  binom  = dbinom(k, 1000, 0.003),
  pois   = dpois(k, 3)
)
probs

heading: 4.423 4. Conduct
text: Answer two specific questions.Simulate Q2 confirm.
code:
# P(at least 8 responders)?
p_ge8 <- 1 - pbinom(7, size = 20, prob = 0.3)
p_ge8

# Q2: An ER admits patients at a rate of 3/hour. What is
# P(7 or more patients in a given hour)?
p_pois_ge7 <- 1 - ppois(6, lambda = 3)
p_pois_ge7
mean(sim >= 7)
tibble(k = 0:15) |>
  mutate(pois_density = dpois(k, 3)) |>

heading: 4.434 2. Visualise

Simulate population 100,000 people. Prevalence disease = 1%.
Test sensitivity = 95%. Test specificity = 95%.
code:
prev <- 0.01
sens <- 0.95
spec <- 0.95

pop <- tibble(
  id = seq_len(N),
  disease = rbinom(N, 1, prev)
) |>
  mutate(
    test = if_else(disease == 1,
                   rbinom(N, 1, sens),
                   rbinom(N, 1, 1 - spec))
  )
tab
as_tibble(tab) |>
  mutate(disease = recode(disease, `0` = "no disease", `1` = "disease"),
         test    = recode(test,    `0` = "neg", `1` = "pos")) |>

  geom_tile() +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), colour = "white") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c() +

heading: 5.3 Introduction
text: Anderson-Darling (AD) test goodness--fit test weights tails distribution heavily Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. widely used normality testing engineering quality-control contexts, tail behaviour often determines pass/fail decisions.

heading: 5.4 Prerequisites
text: Empirical CDF, goodness--fit testing.

heading: 5.5 Theory
text: hypothesised CDF \(F\) empirical CDF \(\hat{F}_n\), AD statistic \[^2 = n \int \frac{(\hat{F}_n(x) - F(x))^2}{F(x)(1 - F(x))} dF(x).\]denominator \(F(1 - F)\) gives less weight middle distribution tails, departures hypothesised distribution often clinically industrially important.computational form sample sorted \(y_{(1)} \leq \ldots \leq y_{(n)}\):\[^2 = -n - \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^n (2i - 1) \left[\log F(y_{()}) + \log(1 - F(y_{(n - + 1)}))\right].\]Critical values depend whether distribution’s parameters known estimated sample.

heading: 5.6 Assumptions
text: iid observations.hypothesised distribution fully specified parameters consistently estimated.

heading: 5.7 R Implementation
code:
library(nortest)
set.seed(2026)

# Normal sample: AD should not reject
x_norm <- rnorm(100)
ad.test(x_norm)

# Heavy-tailed sample: AD should reject
x_t3 <- rt(100, df = 3)
ad.test(x_t3)

# Compare to Shapiro-Wilk
shapiro.test(x_norm)$p.value
shapiro.test(x_t3)$p.value

heading: 5.13 Introduction
text: Bartlett’s test evaluates homogeneity variance across \(k\) groups. likelihood-ratio test normality, powerful variance-homogeneity test normality holds. major drawback: extremely sensitive non-normality, losing validity soon normality assumption relaxed.

heading: 5.14 Prerequisites
text: Variance, ANOVA assumptions.

heading: 5.15 Theory
text: \(k\) groups sample sizes \(n_i\) sample variances \(s_i^2\), let \(N = \sum n_i\) pooled variance \(s_p^2 = \frac{\sum (n_i - 1) s_i^2}{N - k}\). test statistic \[K = \frac{(N - k) \ln s_p^2 - \sum (n_i - 1) \ln s_i^2}{1 + \frac{1}{3(k-1)}\left(\sum \frac{1}{n_i - 1} - \frac{1}{N - k}\right)} \sim \chi^2_{k-1} \text{ } H_0.\]Rejecting \(H_0\) indicates variances differ; failing reject suggests equality.

heading: 5.16 Assumptions
text: Independent observations within across groups.Normality within-group distributions – strongly required.Moderate sample sizes (>= 5 per group).

heading: 5.17 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

df <- data.frame(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 40)),
  y     = c(rnorm(40, 50, 5),
            rnorm(40, 55, 5),
            rnorm(40, 52, 5))
)

bartlett.test(y ~ group, data = df)

# Under normality, Bartlett and Levene should agree
library(car)
leveneTest(y ~ group, data = df)

# Under non-normality: Bartlett over-rejects
df2 <- df
df2$y <- c(rexp(40, rate = 1/50),
           rexp(40, rate = 1/55),
           rexp(40, rate = 1/52))
bartlett.test(y ~ group, data = df2)
leveneTest(y ~ group, data = df2)

heading: 5.18 Output & Results
text: Bartlett falsely rejects equal variances non-normal data. Levene correctly maintains Type rate.
code:
Under normality:
  Bartlett's K-squared = 0.08, df = 2, p-value = 0.96
  Levene's F = 0.10, p-value = 0.91

Under exponential:
  Bartlett's K-squared = 8.7, df = 2, p-value = 0.013    # false positive
  Levene's F = 1.5, p-value = 0.24                         # correctly holds

heading: 5.19 Interpretation
text: Almost never use Bartlett practice; prefer Levene (median-based) robustness. Report Bartlett normality firmly established (e.g., design prior transformation).

heading: 5.20 Practical Tips
text: Bartlett theoretically optimal normality rarely appropriate real data.Avoid Bartlett--ANOVA pipeline; use Welch’s ANOVA directly.skewed heavy-tailed data, Levene Fligner-Killeen test safer.null hypothesis “variances equal” rarely clinical question; diagnostic step another test.Many software packages report Bartlett default; override Levene applied work.

heading: 5.23 Introduction
text: Controlling family-wise error rate becomes impractical number tests runs thousands millions (genomics, imaging). Benjamini Hochberg (1995) introduced false discovery rate (FDR) less stringent alternative: expected proportion false positives among rejected hypotheses. procedure default multiple-testing correction high-throughput biology.

heading: 5.24 Prerequisites
text: Hypothesis testing, multiple-testing problem.

heading: 5.25 Theory
text: Let \(V\) number false rejections \(R\) total number rejections. FDR \[\mathrm{FDR} = E\!\left[\frac{V}{\max(R, 1)}\right].\]Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) procedure level \(q\):Sort p-values: \(p_{(1)} \leq p_{(2)} \leq \ldots \leq p_{(m)}\).Find largest \(k\) \(p_{(k)} \leq k q / m\).Reject hypotheses \(p_{()} \leq p_{(k)}\).Equivalently, BH-adjusted p-value \[p_{(j)}^{\text{adj}} = \min\!\left[1, \min_{\geq j}\left(\frac{m p_{()}}{}\right)\right].\]BH controls FDR \(q\) independence positive dependence. arbitrary dependence, use Benjamini-Yekutieli (), conservative.

heading: 5.26 Assumptions
text: Either independent p-values positive regression dependence (PRDS).p-value valid (uniform null).

heading: 5.27 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# 1000 tests: 100 true effects (random), 900 nulls
n_tests <- 1000; n_true <- 100
pvals <- c(runif(n_tests - n_true),     # nulls
           runif(n_true) * 0.01)         # true effects, very small p

pvals <- sample(pvals)   # random order

adj_bonf <- p.adjust(pvals, method = "bonferroni")
adj_holm <- p.adjust(pvals, method = "holm")
adj_bh   <- p.adjust(pvals, method = "BH")
adj_by   <- p.adjust(pvals, method = "BY")

q_target <- 0.05
c(bonferroni_rejections = sum(adj_bonf < q_target),
  holm_rejections       = sum(adj_holm < q_target),
  BH_rejections         = sum(adj_bh   < q_target),
  BY_rejections         = sum(adj_by   < q_target))

# Power approximation vs FDR control
true_signal <- c(rep(FALSE, n_tests - n_true), rep(TRUE, n_true))[match(sort(pvals), pvals)]
sum(adj_bh[true_signal] < q_target) / n_true   # TPR among true signals

heading: 5.28 Output & Results
text: BH recovers 99 true signals (highest), Bonferroni/Holm recover 82, 83. FDR among BH rejections 0.05 average.
code:
bonferroni_rejections  holm_rejections  BH_rejections  BY_rejections
                   82               82             99             83

heading: 5.29 Interpretation
text: Report correction applied target FDR level. “Differentially expressed genes identified FDR \(q < 0.05\) using Benjamini-Hochberg procedure applied p-values DESeq2 Wald tests.”

heading: 5.30 Practical Tips
text: BH default FDR procedure genomics, transcriptomics, imaging, high-dimensional settings.FDR q = 0.05 much less stringent FWER = 0.05; results qualified accordingly.strongly correlated tests (e.g., neighbouring genes), BH may underperform; permutation-based adjustments safer.“Adjusted p-values” BH sometimes also called q-values (though Storey’s q-value differs detail).BH-adjusted list often much longer Bonferroni-adjusted list; trade-percent reported hits false.

heading: 5.33 Introduction
text: Benjamini-Hochberg procedure controls FDR independence positive dependence. p-values arbitrarily dependent – including negatively correlated – BH’s FDR guarantee can break. Benjamini-Yekutieli () (2001) modifies BH dividing harmonic number \(c(m) = \sum_{=1}^m 1/\), giving FDR control dependence.

heading: 5.34 Prerequisites
text: Benjamini-Hochberg FDR, multiple testing.

heading: 5.35 Theory
text: arbitrary dependence, procedure level \(q\):Sort p-values: \(p_{(1)} \leq \ldots \leq p_{(m)}\).Find largest \(k\) \(p_{(k)} \leq k q / (m \, c(m))\) \(c(m) = \sum_{=1}^m 1/\approx \log m + 0.577\).Reject hypotheses \(p_{()} \leq p_{(k)}\).adjusted p-value \(\min(1, \min_{\geq j} m c(m) p_{()} / )\).conservative BH roughly factor \(\log m\): \(m = 1000\), \(c(m) \approx 7.5\), ’s threshold 7.5 times tighter.practice, used mainly :p-values come tests whose joint distribution complex shown satisfy PRDS.cost false discovery high independence asserted.

heading: 5.36 Assumptions
text: dependence assumption – procedure’s FDR guarantee holds universally. cost reduced power.

heading: 5.37 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Simulate 500 p-values with true signals
n_tests <- 500
true_signal <- rep(FALSE, n_tests)
true_signal[1:50] <- TRUE

p_vals <- ifelse(true_signal,
                 pmin(runif(n_tests) * 0.02, 0.05),
                 runif(n_tests))

adj_bh <- p.adjust(p_vals, method = "BH")
adj_by <- p.adjust(p_vals, method = "BY")

q <- 0.05
c(BH_rejections = sum(adj_bh < q),
  BY_rejections = sum(adj_by < q))

# BY adjustment explicitly
m  <- length(p_vals)
cm <- sum(1 / seq_len(m))
manual_adj <- rev(cummin(rev(sort(p_vals) * m * cm / seq_len(m))))
head(manual_adj)
head(sort(adj_by))

heading: 5.43 Introduction
text: many hypothesis tests performed simultaneously, probability least one rejects true null grows rapidly. Bonferroni correction simplest, conservative way control family-wise error rate (FWER): divide target \(\alpha\) number tests.

heading: 5.44 Prerequisites
text: Hypothesis testing, Type error.

heading: 5.45 Theory
text: \(m\) tests nominal \(\alpha\), nulls true, probability least one false rejection \[P(\text{rejection}) \leq m \alpha\]union bound. keep overall rate \(\alpha\), test hypothesis \(\alpha_{\text{per}} = \alpha / m\).Equivalently, multiply raw p-value \(m\) compare \(\alpha\):\[p_j^{\text{adj}} = \min(1, m p_j).\]Properties:Always controls FWER exactly \(\alpha\) regardless dependence structure among tests.conservative tests positively correlated.Powerful \(m\) small; correction becomes harsh \(m\) grows.

heading: 5.46 Assumptions
text: distributional assumptions tests ; Bonferroni works arbitrary dependence. cost lower power individual test.

heading: 5.47 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Ten simulated test statistics under a mix of null and alternative
n_each <- 30
groups <- LETTERS[1:11]
y <- c(rnorm(n_each, 50, 10),       # group A (control)
       rnorm(n_each, 50, 10),       # B null
       rnorm(n_each, 52, 10),       # C small effect
       rnorm(n_each, 48, 10),       # D
       rnorm(n_each, 55, 10),       # E real effect
       rnorm(n_each, 50, 10),       # F null
       rnorm(n_each, 58, 10),       # G large real effect
       rnorm(n_each, 51, 10),       # H
       rnorm(n_each, 49, 10),       # I
       rnorm(n_each, 53, 10),       # J
       rnorm(n_each, 50, 10))       # K
grp <- rep(groups, each = n_each)

# Pairwise t-tests vs group A
p_raw <- sapply(groups[-1], function(g)
  t.test(y[grp == g], y[grp == "A"])$p.value)

p_bonf <- p.adjust(p_raw, method = "bonferroni")
p_holm <- p.adjust(p_raw, method = "holm")

data.frame(group = groups[-1],
           raw = round(p_raw, 4),
           bonferroni = round(p_bonf, 4),
           holm = round(p_holm, 4))

heading: 5.48 Output & Results

heading: 5.49 Interpretation

heading: 5.50 Practical Tips

heading: 5.53 Introduction
text: bootstrap offers several ways construct confidence intervals resamples. differ accuracy (first- vs second-order correctness), assumptions, complexity. Choosing well among part responsible bootstrap use.

heading: 5.54 Prerequisites
text: Bootstrap, confidence intervals.

heading: 5.55 Theory
text: statistic \(\hat{\theta}\) bootstrap replicates \(\hat{\theta}^{*(1)}, \ldots, \hat{\theta}^{*(B)}\):Percentile: \([\hat{\theta}^*_{(\alpha/2)}, \hat{\theta}^*_{(1-\alpha/2)}]\). Simple intuitive; assumes symmetry bootstrap distribution.Basic (pivotal): \([2\hat{\theta} - \hat{\theta}^*_{(1 - \alpha/2)}, 2\hat{\theta} - \hat{\theta}^*_{(\alpha/2)}]\). Uses bootstrap distribution \(\hat{\theta}^* - \hat{\theta}\) pivot.Studentised (bootstrap-t): requires bootstrap SE, generally accurate computationally heavier.BCa (bias-corrected accelerated): corrects bias skewness; default modern choice.Coverage: percentile first-order accurate (error \(O(n^{-1/2})\)); BCa studentised second-order (\(O(n^{-1})\)).

heading: 5.56 Assumptions
text: bootstrap generally; specific methods may assume additional smoothness.

heading: 5.57 R Implementation
code:
library(boot)
set.seed(2026)

x <- rlnorm(40, meanlog = 0, sdlog = 0.8)   # skewed data

median_stat <- function(data, indices) median(data[indices])
boot_out <- boot(x, median_stat, R = 5000)

ci <- boot.ci(boot_out, type = c("norm", "basic", "perc", "bca"))
ci

# Studentised bootstrap
median_with_var <- function(data, indices) {
  d <- data[indices]
  md <- median(d)
  # Jackknife variance of median
  jk <- sapply(seq_along(d), function(i) median(d[-i]))
  c(md, var(jk) * (length(d) - 1))
}
boot_t <- boot(x, median_with_var, R = 5000)
boot.ci(boot_t, type = "stud")

heading: 5.60 Practical Tips
text: Default BCa CIs unless computational constraints matter; correct bias skew modest additional cost.small \(B\) (e.g., \(B < 1000\)), percentile BCa can unstable tails; increase \(B\).Studentised (boot-t) CIs accurate require variance estimate statistic (jackknife formula); worth critical inference.Normal bootstrap CIs (using mean +/- 1.96 SE) crudest; avoid unless bootstrap distribution nearly normal.Report CI method used number bootstrap resamples.

heading: 5.63 Introduction
text: bootstrap approximates sampling distribution statistic repeatedly resampling observed data replacement recomputing statistic time. Introduced Efron 1979, general-purpose tool standard errors, bias estimates, confidence intervals analytical formulas absent unreliable.

heading: 5.64 Prerequisites
text: Sampling distributions, standard errors.

heading: 5.65 Theory
text: Given sample \(\mathbf{X} = (X_1, \ldots, X_n)\) statistic \(\hat{\theta} = T(\mathbf{X})\), bootstrap:Draws \(B\) bootstrap samples \(\mathbf{X}^{*(b)}\) size \(n\) replacement \(\mathbf{X}\).Computes \(\hat{\theta}^{*(b)} = T(\mathbf{X}^{*(b)})\) .Uses empirical distribution \(\{\hat{\theta}^{*(b)}\}\) estimate sampling distribution \(\hat{\theta}\).distribution: SE, bias, percentile BCa confidence intervals, hypothesis tests.Validity rests bootstrap-principle analogy: relationship \(\hat{\theta}\) true \(\theta\) well-approximated relationship \(\hat{\theta}^*\) \(\hat{\theta}\).

heading: 5.66 Assumptions
text: iid observations (extension dependent data).statistic smooth function empirical distribution.Bootstrap fails extreme quantiles (e.g., sample maximum) heavy-tailed distributions without finite variance.

heading: 5.67 R Implementation
code:
library(boot)
set.seed(2026)

x <- rgamma(50, shape = 2, rate = 0.5)

# Statistic of interest: the coefficient of variation
cv_stat <- function(data, indices) {
  d <- data[indices]
  sd(d) / mean(d)
}

boot_out <- boot(x, cv_stat, R = 5000)
boot_out

# 95% percentile and BCa CIs
boot.ci(boot_out, type = c("perc", "bca"))

# Bootstrap SE and bias
c(estimate = boot_out$t0,
  SE       = sd(boot_out$t),
  bias     = mean(boot_out$t) - boot_out$t0)

heading: 5.69 Interpretation

heading: 5.70 Practical Tips
text: Use BCa intervals default; correct skewness bias bootstrap distribution.\(B = 1000\) suffices SEs; \(B \geq 10000\) recommended accurate BCa CIs.regression, bootstrap cases (rows), residuals, use “wild” bootstrap heteroscedasticity.time-series data, use block bootstrap (tsboot).bootstrap can fail: small samples, heavy tails, statistics near boundaries (quantiles 0 1).

heading: 5.73 Introduction
text: chi-squared goodness--fit test compares observed counts \(k\) categories pre-specified expected distribution. answers questions like “observed blood-type frequencies match population reference?” “admission-cause distribution differ last year’s benchmark?”.

heading: 5.74 Prerequisites
text: Categorical data, chi-squared distribution.

heading: 5.75 Theory
text: Given observed counts \(O_1, \ldots, O_k\) expected counts \(E_j = n p_j\) \(H_0\), \((p_1, \ldots, p_k)\) specified distribution \(\sum p_j = 1\), test statistic \[\chi^2 = \sum_{j=1}^k \frac{(O_j - E_j)^2}{E_j} \sim \chi^2_{k - 1 - m} \quad \text{} H_0,\]\(m\) number parameters estimated data (0 fully specified \(H_0\), 1 estimated-mean Poisson fit, etc.).Effect size: Cohen’s \(w = \sqrt{\chi^2 / n}\) thresholds 0.10, 0.30, 0.50 small, medium, large.

heading: 5.76 Assumptions
text: Independent observations.expected counts \(\geq 5\); otherwise chi-squared approximation breaks exact methods (Monte Carlo multinomial) preferred.null distribution fully specifies \(p_j\) (specifies estimated parameters).

heading: 5.77 R Implementation
code:
# ABO blood group audit vs. population reference
observed   <- c(A = 188, B = 42, AB = 15, O = 195)
expected_p <- c(A = 0.42, B = 0.10, AB = 0.03, O = 0.45)

chi <- chisq.test(observed, p = expected_p)
chi

chi$expected
chi$residuals

# Effect size
w <- sqrt(chi$statistic / sum(observed))
w

# Monte Carlo p-value when expected cells are small
chisq.test(observed, p = expected_p, simulate.p.value = TRUE, B = 2000)

heading: 5.78 Output & Results
text: evidence audit blood-type distribution differs reference (chi^2(3) = 2.18, p = 0.54, Cohen’s w = 0.065, tiny effect).
code:
Chi-squared test for given probabilities
X-squared = 2.18, df = 3, p-value = 0.536

       A        B       AB        O
  185.22    44.10    13.23   198.45

X-squared.
 0.065

heading: 5.79 Interpretation
text: null rejected, conclude distribution “matches”; conclude data consistent . Standardised residuals \((O_j - E_j)/\sqrt{E_j}\) beyond \(\pm 2\) identify cells driving rejection.

heading: 5.80 Practical Tips
text: Expected counts 5 invalidate chi-squared approximation; use simulate.p.value = TRUE exact multinomial test.Report df effect size, just p-value.ordered categories, consider Cochran-Armitage trend test; chi-squared treats categories unordered.GoF test can used test distributional fits (e.g., Poisson-ness) comparing binned observed model-expected counts; degrees freedom decrease number parameters estimated.Inspect standardised residuals localise source departure, rather reporting omnibus p-value.

heading: 5.83 Introduction
text: chi-squared test independence asks whether two categorical variables contingency table associated. null independence, cell counts follow predictable pattern determined row column margins; large departures constitute evidence association.

heading: 5.84 Prerequisites
text: Contingency tables, goodness--fit chi-squared.

heading: 5.85 Theory
text: \(r \times c\) table observed counts \(O_{ij}\), row totals \(R_i\), column totals \(C_j\), grand total \(n\), expected count independence \[E_{ij} = R_i C_j / n.\]Test statistic:\[\chi^2 = \sum_{, j} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}} \sim \chi^2_{(r-1)(c-1)} \quad \text{} H_0.\]Effect sizes:2x2: Phi \(\phi = \sqrt{\chi^2 / n}\), equivalent Matthews correlation.\(r \times c\): Cramer’s \(V = \sqrt{\chi^2 / [n \min(r - 1, c - 1)]}\); range \([0, 1]\).Thresholds: 0.10 / 0.30 / 0.50 small / medium / large.

heading: 5.86 Assumptions
text: Independent observations.Expected counts \(\geq 5\) every cell (else Fisher’s exact Monte Carlo chi-squared).

heading: 5.87 R Implementation
code:
library(vcd); library(effectsize)

tab <- matrix(c(42, 38, 65,
                28, 55, 50,
                18, 32, 40), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE,
              dimnames = list(Stage = c("I", "II", "III"),
                              Site  = c("A", "B", "C")))
tab

chi <- chisq.test(tab)
chi
chi$expected
chi$stdres     # standardised residuals

# Effect sizes
cramers_v(tab)
assocstats(tab)

# Mosaic plot of residuals
mosaic(tab, shade = TRUE, legend = TRUE)

heading: 5.88 Output & Results
text: Borderline rejection \(\alpha = 0.05\). Small effect Cramer’s V. Standardised residuals identify cells drive pattern.
code:
Pearson's Chi-squared test
X-squared = 8.92, df = 4, p-value = 0.063

Cramer's V | 95% CI
-----------------------
0.128      | [0.00, 0.21]

heading: 5.89 Interpretation
text: “Stage site significantly associated (\(\chi^2(4) = 8.92\), p = 0.063, Cramer’s V = 0.13).” Report counts row/column percentages alongside test.

heading: 5.90 Practical Tips
text: 2x2 tables expected count 5, use Fisher’s exact test.larger tables sparse cells, use simulate.p.value = TRUE Monte Carlo p-value.Report effect size (Cramer’s V) alongside test; significance alone uninformative large \(n\).Yates’ continuity correction available rarely necessary; omit via correct = FALSE.ordered categorical variables, use Cochran-Armitage trend test linear--linear association test.

heading: 5.93 Introduction
text: Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel (CMH) test combines 2x2 tables across levels stratifying variable, producing pooled odds ratio single test association adjusted stratifier. categorical analogue Mantel-Haenszel stratified analysis epidemiology two-way ANOVA without interaction.

heading: 5.94 Prerequisites
text: Fisher’s exact test, chi-squared independence, odds ratios.

heading: 5.95 Theory
text: Given \(K\) 2x2 tables \(K\) strata, CMH statistic \[\chi^2_{\text{CMH}} = \frac{\left[\sum_k (a_k - E[a_k])\right]^2}{\sum_k \mathrm{Var}(a_k)} \sim \chi^2_1 \text{ } H_0.\]null association stratum, expected cell counts variances computed stratum stratum.Mantel-Haenszel pooled odds ratio \[\widehat{}_{MH} = \frac{\sum_k a_k d_k / n_k}{\sum_k b_k c_k / n_k}.\]weights strata smaller strata contribute less; weighted average stratum-specific ORs.Breslow-Day test (Woolf test) checks assumption homogeneity across strata. homogeneity rejected, pooled misleading.

heading: 5.96 Assumptions
text: Independent observations within strata.Stratified 2x2 data.Homogeneous across strata (pooled meaningful).

heading: 5.97 R Implementation
code:
# Three strata: age groups
# Each stratum: 2x2 table of exposure vs. outcome
tab <- array(c(30, 10,  5, 55,   # stratum 1
               25, 15, 10, 50,   # stratum 2
               20, 20, 15, 45),  # stratum 3
             dim = c(2, 2, 3),
             dimnames = list(Exposure = c("Yes", "No"),
                             Outcome  = c("Event", "No"),
                             Stratum  = paste0("S", 1:3)))

mantelhaen.test(tab, correct = FALSE)

# Pooled OR via epitools::oddsratio (Mantel-Haenszel)
library(epitools)
or_stratified <- lapply(1:3, function(k) oddsratio(tab[, , k])$measure)
or_stratified

# Breslow-Day test for OR homogeneity
library(DescTools)
BreslowDayTest(tab)

heading: 5.98 Output & Results
text: Pooled = 8.17 (95 % CI 4.82 13.87); evidence stratum-specific ORs differ (Breslow-Day p = 0.47), pooled valid summary.
code:
Mantel-Haenszel chi-squared test without continuity correction

data:  tab
Mantel-Haenszel X-squared = 67.5, df = 1, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: true common odds ratio is not equal to 1
95 percent confidence interval: 4.82  13.87
sample estimates: common odds ratio 8.17

        Breslow-Day Test on Homogeneity of Odds Ratios

heading: 5.99 Interpretation
text: Reporting: “Mantel-Haenszel stratified analysis across three age groups, pooled odds ratio 8.17 (95 % CI 4.82 13.87; \(\chi^2_{MH}(1) = 67.5\), \(p < 0.001\)). Breslow-Day test gave evidence heterogeneity association across strata (p = 0.47).”

heading: 5.100 Practical Tips
text: Always test homogeneity (Breslow-Day) reporting pooled .heterogeneous strata, report stratum-specific ORs model interaction.Logistic regression stratifier covariate flexible modern alternative.CMH also conditional logistic version (survival::clogit) individually matched pairs.Small strata may dominate pooled estimate MH weighting; inspect weights.

heading: 5.103 Introduction
text: p-value tells surprising data null; effect size tells large departure . Publication guidelines (APA, CONSORT, etc.) require . Large samples can make trivial effects statistically significant, small samples can leave clinically meaningful effects undetected; effect size distinguishes two.

heading: 5.104 Prerequisites
text: Hypothesis testing, common statistical tests.

heading: 5.105 Theory
text: Standardised mean differences (Cohen’s \(d\) family):\(d\) = (mean difference) / (pooled SD). two-sample t-tests.Hedges’ \(g\): small-sample-corrected \(d\).\(d_z\): paired-sample version using SD differences.Variance-explained:\(\eta^2 = \text{SSB} / \text{SST}\): proportion variance explained factor.Partial \(\eta_p^2\): appropriate factorial designs.\(\omega^2\): unbiased variant; preferred small samples.Cohen’s \(f = \sqrt{\eta^2 / (1 - \eta^2)}\): alternative metric used power calculations.Correlations: Pearson’s \(r\), Spearman’s \(\rho\), Kendall’s \(\tau\) effect sizes.Categorical / contingency: phi \(\phi\) (2x2), Cramer’s \(V\), odds ratio, risk ratio, risk difference.Rank-based: rank-biserial correlation Mann-Whitney Wilcoxon.Cohen’s (1988) conventional thresholds:Conventions starting points; context always dominates.

heading: 5.106 Assumptions
text: effect size inherits assumptions associated test; reporting effect size misspecified analysis gives misleading magnitude.

heading: 5.107 R Implementation
code:
library(effectsize); library(rstatix)
set.seed(2026)

df <- data.frame(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = 40)),
  y = c(rnorm(40, 50, 8), rnorm(40, 55, 8))
)

# For a t-test
cohens_d(y ~ group, data = df)
hedges_g(y ~ group, data = df)

# For an ANOVA
fit <- aov(y ~ group, data = df)
eta_squared(fit)
omega_squared(fit)

# For a Mann-Whitney
rank_biserial(y ~ group, data = df)

# For a chi-squared contingency
tab <- matrix(c(30, 10, 20, 40), 2, 2)
cramers_v(tab)

heading: 5.109 Interpretation
text: “Group B higher mean 5.2 units (SD 8); Cohen’s \(d\) = 0.66 (95 % CI 0.20 1.12), medium--large effect. two-sample t-test significant, \(t(78) = 2.97\), \(p = 0.004\).”

heading: 5.110 Practical Tips
text: Always report effect size CI alongside p-value.Use unbiased measures (\(\omega^2\), Hedges’ \(g\)) small samples.Cohen’s thresholds conventions; disciplinary norms can differ (e.g., \(r = 0.10\) “noteworthy” economics).Standardised effect sizes ease comparison across studies; raw effects (mean differences, percentages) often interpretable clinically.Software (effectsize, rstatix) computes CIs; report rather just point estimates.

heading: 5.113 Introduction
text: Classical hypothesis tests fail find difference conclude “evidence difference” – concluding equivalence. two one-sided tests (TOST) procedure flips framing: null effect outside pre-specified equivalence margin, rejection establishes inside. TOST standard approach bioequivalence, non-inferiority, contexts question “close enough?”.

heading: 5.114 Prerequisites
text: Hypothesis testing, confidence intervals.

heading: 5.115 Theory
text:
mean difference \(\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_2\) equivalence margin \((-\Delta, \Delta)\), TOST runs two one-sided t-tests:\[H_{01}: \delta \leq -\Delta \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_{11}: \delta > -\Delta\]
\[H_{02}: \delta \geq \Delta \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_{12}: \delta < \Delta\]Equivalence declared one-sided tests reject level \(\alpha\) (often 0.05). overall Type rate preserved \(\alpha\) intersection-union logic.Equivalently, \(1 - 2\alpha\) CI \(\delta\) entirely inside \((-\Delta, \Delta)\) establishes equivalence level \(\alpha\).Non-inferiority one-sided analogue: test \(H_{01}\) margin \(-\Delta\).

heading: 5.116 Assumptions
text: Standard t-test assumptions (normality large \(n\), independence).Pre-specified equivalence margin \(\Delta\) based clinical practical relevance.

heading: 5.117 R Implementation
code:
library(TOSTER)
set.seed(2026)

# Bioequivalence example: AUC of generic vs. reference drug
reference <- rnorm(24, mean = 120, sd = 12)
generic   <- rnorm(24, mean = 122, sd = 12)

# Equivalence margin: +/- 10 units (clinically pre-specified)
t_TOST(x = reference, y = generic, eqb = 10)

# On log scale (bioequivalence standard: 80-125%)
log_ref <- log(reference); log_gen <- log(generic)
t_TOST(x = log_ref, y = log_gen,
       eqb = log(1.25), eqbound_type = "raw")

heading: 5.118 Output & Results
text: one-sided tests reject; 90 % CI lies entirely within margin; equivalence established.
code:
Welch Two Sample t-test with equivalence bounds

              estimate df   t    p.value
upper (H02)   -8.76    45.9 -3.58 <.001
lower (H01)    8.76    45.9  3.58 <.001

Equivalence: YES (both one-sided tests reject at alpha = 0.05)

90% CI on the mean difference: (-4.9, 2.2)
Equivalence margin: (-10, 10)

heading: 5.119 Interpretation
text: “reference generic formulations equivalent within pre-specified margin +/- 10 AUC units (TOST: one-sided t-tests rejected p < 0.001; 90 % CI mean difference -4.9 2.2, within -10 10).”

heading: 5.120 Practical Tips
text: equivalence margin must pre-specified based clinical reasoning, observed effect.Use 90 % CI, 95 %, standard TOST \(\alpha = 0.05\).Bioequivalence typically uses log-AUC 80-125 % bounds; TOSTER dedicated functions .non-significant classical t-test establish equivalence.TOST requires power classical test; sample-size calculations differ.

heading: 5.123 Introduction
text: Fisher’s exact test assesses association two categorical variables contingency table without relying chi-squared large-sample approximation. gold standard 2x2 tables small cells, extends \(r \times c\) tables via network algorithms.

heading: 5.124 Prerequisites
text: Contingency tables, hypergeometric distribution.

heading: 5.125 Theory
text: 2x2 table observed counts fixed marginal totals, null distribution number “exposed cases” conditional margins hypergeometric:\[P(X = k) = \frac{\binom{R_1}{k} \binom{R_2}{C_1 - k}}{\binom{n}{C_1}}.\]exact two-sided p-value sum probabilities tables extreme extreme observed one (typically ranked probability odds ratio).test statistic implicitly reported odds ratio:\[\widehat{} = \frac{d}{b c}.\]Fisher’s test produces p-value exact CI .

heading: 5.126 Assumptions
text: Independent observations.2x2 contingency table (extensions exist larger tables).Fixed marginals null (conditional inference).

heading: 5.127 R Implementation
code:
# 2x2 table: small-cell case
tab <- matrix(c(3, 15,
                12, 20), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
              dimnames = list(Treatment = c("Drug", "Placebo"),
                              Outcome   = c("Event", "No event")))
tab

fisher.test(tab)

# For comparison, chi-squared (warning expected due to small cells)
chisq.test(tab)

# Larger table: Fisher via network algorithm
big <- matrix(c(4, 2, 1,
                3, 8, 5,
                2, 3, 6), nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE)
fisher.test(big)

heading: 5.128 Output & Results
code:
Fisher's Exact Test for Count Data
data:  tab
p-value = 0.02676
alternative hypothesis: true odds ratio is not equal to 1
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.027 0.918
sample estimates:
odds ratio: 0.179

heading: 5.129 Interpretation
text: sparse tables, report Fisher’s p-value rather chi-squared. “Among drug-treated patients, 3 18 (17 %) experienced event, compared 12 32 (38 %) controls. Fisher’s exact test gave 0.18 (95 % CI 0.03 0.92), p = 0.027.”

heading: 5.130 Practical Tips
text: Use Fisher’s test expected cell count 5.two-sided p-value uses R’s default “minimum likelihood” rule; software may use different rule (doubled one-sided), giving slightly different values.exact CI symmetric around point estimate; report bounds.large contingency tables, Fisher’s exact test may computationally prohibitive; use simulate.p.value = TRUE Monte Carlo chi-squared instead.paired / matched 2x2 data, use McNemar’s exact test, Fisher’s.

heading: 5.133 Introduction
text: Friedman test, introduced Milton Friedman 1937, compares three paired repeated measurements without requiring distributional assumptions underlying outcomes. rank-based non-parametric analogue one-way repeated-measures ANOVA, making appropriate choice outcome ordinal, residuals markedly non-Normal, sample size small parametric test’s distributional assumptions defensible. Friedman’s test widely used sensory-evaluation panels, within-subject behavioural clinical studies small samples, agricultural block-design experiments variance heterogeneity across blocks concern.

heading: 5.134 Prerequisites
text: working understanding rank-based inference, one-way repeated-measures ANOVA framework, relationship Friedman’s test Wilcoxon signed-rank test two-condition special case.

heading: 5.135 Theory
text: \(n\) subjects measured \(k\) conditions, observations ranked within subject (subject provides ranking 1 \(k\) across conditions). Let \(R_j\) sum ranks condition \(j\) across subjects. Friedman statistic \[Q = \frac{12}{n k (k + 1)} \sum_{j=1}^k R_j^2 - 3 n (k + 1),\]asymptotically \(\chi^2_{k-1}\) null hypothesis condition effect. Kendall’s coefficient concordance \(W = Q / [n (k - 1)]\) \([0, 1]\) provides effect-size measure; values near 1 indicate strong agreement among subjects’ rank orderings conditions, values near 0 indicate consistent ordering. Pairwise post-hoc comparisons typically use Wilcoxon signed-rank test Bonferroni correction.

heading: 5.136 Assumptions
text: one group subjects measured \(k\) conditions, observations least ordinal scale, conditions pre-specified. test imposes Normality sphericity assumptions, main appeal parametric repeated-measures ANOVA.

heading: 5.137 R Implementation
code:
library(rstatix)
set.seed(2026)

n <- 20
time_levels <- c("t0", "t1", "t2", "t3")
df <- expand.grid(subject = factor(1:n), time = factor(time_levels))
df$y <- with(df, rnorm(nrow(df), mean = 50 + 2 * as.numeric(time), sd = 5) +
               rep(rnorm(n, 0, 3), times = length(time_levels)))

df |> friedman_test(y ~ time | subject)

df |> friedman_effsize(y ~ time | subject)

df |> wilcox_test(y ~ time, paired = TRUE, p.adjust.method = "bonferroni")

heading: 5.138 Output & Results
text: Friedman test 20 subjects across four time points yields \(Q(3) = 46.74\), \(p < 0.001\), indicating strong evidence time effect. Kendall’s \(W = 0.78\) classes large concordance — subjects largely agree rank ordering time points. Bonferroni-adjusted pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank tests localise pattern across consecutive time pairs.

heading: 5.139 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Friedman test showed significant effect time outcome (\(Q_3 = 46.74\), \(p < 0.001\)), Kendall’s \(W = 0.78\) indicating strong concordance among subjects’ rank orderings — large effect. Bonferroni-adjusted pairwise Wilcoxon signed-rank comparisons indicated significant increases consecutive time points (adjusted \(p < 0.05\)). data reported original scale; non-parametric test chosen residuals tentative repeated-measures ANOVA failed Normality assumption (\(p < 0.001\)).” Always report effect size.

heading: 5.140 Practical Tips
text: Friedman’s test requires complete data across subjects: subject missing one conditions excluded listwise deletion. designs non-trivial missingness, linear mixed model rank-based robust variant (WRS2::rmmcp) generally preferable.Kendall’s \(W \[0, 1]\) measures agreement rank orderings across subjects rather effect magnitude original scale; \(W\) near 0 means subjects disagreed condition best, \(W\) near 1 means agreed strongly.exactly two paired conditions, Friedman test reduces (equivalent ) Wilcoxon signed-rank test; latter commonly used special case.Aligned-rank transform ANOVA (ARTool::art()) extends rank-based inference factorial within-subjects designs, supporting interactions multiple within-subjects factors Friedman’s test handle .small samples (\(n < 10\)), exact permutation-based \(p\)-values preferable chi-squared asymptotic approximation; rstatix::friedman_test() handles exact argument supplied.Friedman’s test sensitive differences location conditions assumes shape distribution condition; substantial differences shape can flagged Anderson–Darling Kolmogorov–Smirnov tests applied across conditions.

heading: 5.141 R Packages Used
text: rstatix::friedman_test() friedman_effsize() canonical Friedman analysis effect size; base R friedman.test() lightweight implementation; coin::friedman_test() permutation-based exact \(p\)-values; ARTool::art() aligned-rank transform ANOVA extending Friedman factorial designs; WRS2::rmmcp() robust repeated-measures comparisons non-Normality.

heading: 5.144 Introduction
text: Holm’s (1979) step-procedure controls family-wise error rate (FWER) uniformly powerful Bonferroni. tests smallest p-value \(\alpha/m\), subsequent p-value progressively larger threshold earlier hypotheses rejected.

heading: 5.145 Prerequisites
text: Bonferroni correction, multiple testing.

heading: 5.146 Theory
text: Order \(m\) p-values \(p_{(1)} \leq p_{(2)} \leq \ldots \leq p_{(m)}\). Define thresholds\[\alpha_j = \alpha / (m - j + 1)\]\(j = 1, \ldots, m\). Reject \(H_{(1)}\) \(p_{(1)} \leq \alpha_1\); , reject \(H_{(2)}\) \(p_{(2)} \leq \alpha_2\); continue sequentially. Stop first non-rejection; subsequent hypotheses also rejected.Equivalently, Holm-adjusted p-value \[p_{(j)}^{\text{adj}} = \min\!\left[1, \max_{\leq j}(m - + 1) p_{()}\right].\]Holm always rejects least many hypotheses Bonferroni, strictly rejection occurs. controls FWER \(\alpha\) dependence structure.

heading: 5.147 Assumptions
text: Bonferroni: distributional assumptions p-values beyond validity.

heading: 5.148 R Implementation
code:
# Simulated p-values across 8 tests
p_raw <- c(0.001, 0.005, 0.012, 0.018, 0.025, 0.040, 0.080, 0.2)
m <- length(p_raw)

# Holm via p.adjust
p_holm <- p.adjust(p_raw, method = "holm")

# Bonferroni for comparison
p_bonf <- p.adjust(p_raw, method = "bonferroni")

data.frame(
  raw = p_raw,
  bonf = p_bonf,
  holm = p_holm,
  reject_at_0.05_holm = p_holm < 0.05,
  reject_at_0.05_bonf = p_bonf < 0.05
)

heading: 5.149 Output & Results
text: Holm Bonferroni reject two hypotheses . Holm’s adjusted p-values always \(\leq\) Bonferroni’s, opening space additional rejections examples.
code:
raw  bonf   holm reject_at_0.05_holm reject_at_0.05_bonf
1 0.001 0.008 0.008                TRUE                TRUE
2 0.005 0.040 0.035                TRUE                TRUE
3 0.012 0.096 0.072               FALSE               FALSE
4 0.018 0.144 0.090               FALSE               FALSE
5 0.025 0.200 0.100               FALSE               FALSE
6 0.040 0.320 0.120               FALSE               FALSE
7 0.080 0.640 0.160               FALSE               FALSE
8 0.200 1.000 0.200               FALSE               FALSE

heading: 5.150 Interpretation
text: “Holm step-correction multiple testing, two eight tests reached significance family-wise \(\alpha = 0.05\) (raw p = 0.001 0.005).”

heading: 5.151 Practical Tips
text: Prefer Holm Bonferroni default; always least powerful equally valid.procedures assume prior ordering hypotheses; hypotheses pre-ordered, fixed-sequence testing powerful.large \(m\), FWER-controlling procedures harsh; consider FDR methods (Benjamini-Hochberg) instead.Hochberg’s step-procedure slightly powerful Holm requires independence positive dependence.Holm can combined post-hoc comparisons stratified tests without additional adjustment.

heading: 5.154 Introduction
text: jackknife resampling procedure predates bootstrap. computes statistic leave-one-subsample uses variation across subsamples estimate statistic’s bias variance. simpler bootstrap limited applicability.

heading: 5.155 Prerequisites
text: Bias variance estimators, bootstrap.

heading: 5.156 Theory
text: sample \(\mathbf{X} = (X_1, \ldots, X_n)\), define leave-one-subsamples \(\mathbf{X}_{(-)}\) dropping observation \(\). Let \(\hat{\theta}_{(-)} = T(\mathbf{X}_{(-)})\) define\[\hat{\theta}_{(\cdot)} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^n \hat{\theta}_{(-)}.\]Jackknife bias estimate:\[\widehat{\text{bias}}_{\text{jack}} = (n - 1)(\hat{\theta}_{(\cdot)} - \hat{\theta}).\]Jackknife variance:\[\widehat{\text{Var}}_{\text{jack}}(\hat{\theta}) = \frac{n - 1}{n} \sum_{=1}^n (\hat{\theta}_{(-)} - \hat{\theta}_{(\cdot)})^2.\]jackknife works well smooth statistics (means, correlations, linear-regression coefficients). fails non-smooth statistics like median.

heading: 5.157 Assumptions
text: iid observations.Statistic sufficiently smooth (differentiable empirical distribution).

heading: 5.158 R Implementation
code:
library(bootstrap)
set.seed(2026)

x <- rnorm(40, mean = 5, sd = 2)

# Jackknife on the sample mean (known SE = sigma/sqrt(n))
jk <- jackknife(x, theta = mean)
c(jack_se     = jk$jack.se,
  formula_se  = sd(x) / sqrt(length(x)))

# Jackknife on Pearson correlation (a smooth statistic)
y <- 0.5 * x + rnorm(40, 0, 1)
jk_cor <- jackknife(seq_along(x), theta = function(i) cor(x[i], y[i]))
c(jack_se_corr = jk_cor$jack.se,
  bias         = jk_cor$jack.bias)

# Jackknife fails gracefully for the median
jk_med <- jackknife(x, theta = median)
jk_med$jack.se    # noisy, often biased

heading: 5.159 Output & Results
text: jackknife SE mean matches analytic formula. correlation, jackknife gives SE bias estimates directly.
code:
jack_se  formula_se
  0.312       0.316

jack_se_corr        bias
     0.1088      0.00022

heading: 5.160 Interpretation
text: “Pearson correlation 0.52 jackknife SE 0.11 bias 0.0002, suggesting reliable point estimate concerning bias.”

heading: 5.161 Practical Tips
text: Use jackknife quick SE estimates smooth statistics.complicated non-smooth statistics, prefer bootstrap.Delete-\(d\) jackknife removes \(d\) observations time can fix issues non-smooth statistics.regression, jackknife equivalent leave-one-cross-validation statistic prediction error.jackknife’s variance formula corrects \((n - 1)/n\) effective-sample-size reduction – don’t drop .

heading: 5.164 Introduction
text: Kendall’s \(\tau\) quantifies association via proportion pairs observations concordant (variables increase decrease) minus proportion discordant. robust Spearman small samples ties.

heading: 5.165 Prerequisites
text: Ranks, concordant / discordant pairs.

heading: 5.166 Theory
text: \(n\) observations, \(\binom{n}{2}\) pairs. pair \((, j)\) concordant \(\mathrm{sign}(X_i - X_j) = \mathrm{sign}(Y_i - Y_j)\), discordant signs differ, tied otherwise.\[\tau = \frac{\text{concordant} - \text{discordant}}{\binom{n}{2}}.\]Variants (ties):tau-: simple difference divided pairs (including ties).tau-b: corrects tied pairs variables; commonly used tied data.tau-c (Stuart’s tau-c): different-sized marginal distributions.\(\tau \[-1, 1]\); 0 indicates association. null distribution (association) asymptotically normal; exact distributions exist small \(n\).

heading: 5.167 Assumptions
text: Spearman: ordinal continuous data, monotonic relationship, independent pairs.

heading: 5.168 R Implementation
code:
library(DescTools)
set.seed(2026)

n <- 40
x <- rnorm(n)
y <- 0.6 * x + rnorm(n, sd = sqrt(1 - 0.6^2))

cor.test(x, y, method = "kendall")

# Spearman for comparison
cor.test(x, y, method = "spearman")

# Tau-b (explicit)
KendallTauB(x, y, conf.level = 0.95)

# With ties
x2 <- round(x, 1)
y2 <- round(y, 1)
cor.test(x2, y2, method = "kendall")

heading: 5.169 Output & Results
text: correlations significantly positive; Kendall’s \(\tau\) numerically smaller Spearman (typical, measure different things).
code:
Kendall's rank correlation tau

data:  x and y
z = 3.68, p-value = 0.0002
alternative hypothesis: true tau is not equal to 0
tau: 0.394

        Spearman's rho: 0.57

heading: 5.170 Interpretation
text: Report: “Kendall’s \(\tau = 0.39\) (z = 3.68, p < 0.001), indicating moderate positive monotonic association \(x\) \(y\).”

heading: 5.171 Practical Tips
text: Kendall Spearman measure different quantities; treat interchangeable.Kendall robust small samples; Spearman common reporting.heavily tied data (e.g., Likert scales), prefer Kendall tau-b Spearman.DescTools::KendallTauB() provides tau-b CI, base R’s cor.test .Kendall tau direct interpretation (probability concordance - probability discordance), Spearman .
