heading: 5.174 Introduction
text: Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test compares empirical distribution function (ECDF) either hypothesised theoretical CDF (one-sample test) another empirical distribution (two-sample test). test statistic supremum absolute difference two CDFs, measure largest vertical gap CDF plot. KS prototypical CDF-based goodness--fit test, valued fully non-parametric distribution-free one-sample case (null distribution \(D\) depend specific \(F_0\) continuous distributions). widely used quick distributional diagnostic flexible two-sample test sensitive differences location, scale, shape.

heading: 5.175 Prerequisites
text: working understanding empirical CDFs, Glivenko-Cantelli theorem, difference testing fully specified hypothesised distribution testing parametric family parameters estimated data.

heading: 5.176 Theory
text: one-sample test sample \(X_1, \dots, X_n\) hypothesised CDF \(F_0\),\[D = \sup_x |\hat F_n(x) - F_0(x)|;\]\(H_0: X \sim F_0\), \(\sqrt n \cdot D\) Kolmogorov limiting distribution. two-sample test empirical CDFs \(\hat F_n\) \(\hat G_m\),\[D_{n,m} = \sup_x |\hat F_n(x) - \hat G_m(x)|.\]KS distribution-free continuous one-sample case: null distribution \(D\) depends sample size, specific \(F_0\). parameters \(F_0\) estimated data, nominal \(p\)-values anti-conservative; Lilliefors correction provides appropriate adjustment testing Normality estimated mean SD.

heading: 5.177 Assumptions
text: underlying distribution continuous (ties produce warnings approximate \(p\)-values), observations independent identically distributed, parameters \(F_0\) either fully specified appropriately corrected estimated sample.

heading: 5.178 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

x <- rlnorm(100, meanlog = 0, sdlog = 0.8)
ks.test(x, "pnorm", mean = mean(x), sd = sd(x))

z <- scale(x)
ks.test(z, "pnorm")

x1 <- rnorm(50, 0, 1)
x2 <- rnorm(60, 0.4, 1.2)
ks.test(x1, x2)

library(nortest)
lillie.test(x)

heading: 5.179 Output & Results
text: script demonstrates four standard use cases. one-sample KS Normal sample-estimated parameters rejects null \(p\)-value anti-conservative; Lilliefors-corrected variant gives proper rejection. two-sample KS detects location scale shift \(X_1\) \(X_2\) (\(D = 0.31\), \(p = 0.007\)), illustrating test’s sensitivity general distributional differences.

heading: 5.180 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Lilliefors-corrected Kolmogorov-Smirnov test rejected Normality residuals regression model (\(D = 0.16\), \(p < 0.001\)), confirming visual impression right-skew Q-Q plot. log transformation applied re-fitting; residuals transformed scale passed test (\(D = 0.05\), \(p = 0.21\)). two-sample KS comparing transformed-residual distributions across treatment arms detected significant difference shape (\(D = 0.08\), \(p = 0.52\)).” Always pair KS Q-Q plot.

heading: 5.181 Practical Tips
text: KS generally less powerful Shapiro-Wilk testing Normality small moderate samples; prefer Shapiro-Wilk default Normality test use KS hypothesised distribution non-Normal two-sample distributional comparison needed.Ties sample invalidate strict KS \(p\)-value R issues warning; prefer Anderson-Darling Cramér-von Mises test discretised rounded data, handle ties gracefully.Lilliefors correction must used testing Normality sample-estimated mean SD; standard ks.test() pnorm sample estimates gives anti-conservative \(p\)-values reject rarely.two-sample KS detects distributional difference — location, scale, shape — therefore useful omnibus diagnostic analyst unsure aspect distribution might differ; generally less powerful Mann-Whitney \(U\) test pure location shifts.large samples, KS reject almost parametric fit real data rounding, ceiling effects, minor non-Normalities; use effect-size summaries Q-Q plots large samples rather relying \(p\)-value alone.Anderson-Darling test gives weight tails distribution KS ; tail-sensitive applications (extreme-value detection, financial risk), Anderson-Darling often preferred.

heading: 5.182 R Packages Used

heading: 5.185 Introduction
text: Kruskal-Wallis test extends Mann-Whitney U three independent groups. tests whether distributions differ across groups, without assuming normality equal variances. Post-hoc pairwise comparisons use Dunn’s test multiple-testing correction.

heading: 5.186 Prerequisites
text: Mann-Whitney U, ranks.

heading: 5.187 Theory
text: Pool observations rank across groups. Let \(R_i\) sum ranks group \(\) size \(n_i\), \(N = \sum n_i\). Kruskal-Wallis statistic \[H = \frac{12}{N(N + 1)} \sum_i \frac{R_i^2}{n_i} - 3(N + 1) \sim \chi^2_{k - 1}.\]\(H_0\) equal distributions, \(H\) approximate chi-squared distribution \(k - 1\) df moderate sample sizes.Effect size: \(\varepsilon^2 = H / (N - 1)\) \(\eta^2_H = (H - k + 1)/(N - k)\).Post-hoc: Dunn’s test compares pair groups using z-statistics Bonferroni Benjamini-Hochberg correction.

heading: 5.188 Assumptions
text: Independent observations within across groups.Observations least ordinal.Similar distribution shapes (median-difference interpretation); otherwise tests distributional equality.

heading: 5.189 R Implementation
code:
library(rstatix); library(dunn.test)
set.seed(2026)

df <- data.frame(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C", "D"), each = 25)),
  y     = c(rnorm(25, 50, 8),
            rnorm(25, 55, 8),
            rnorm(25, 60, 12),
            rnorm(25, 52, 8))
)

kruskal.test(y ~ group, data = df)

# Effect size
df |> kruskal_effsize(y ~ group)

# Dunn's post-hoc with BH correction
dunn.test(df$y, df$group, method = "bh")

text: “Group differed significantly across four groups (Kruskal-Wallis \(H(3) = 15.22\), \(p = 0.002\), \(\eta^2_H = 0.14\)). Dunn post-hoc tests identified group C significantly higher groups D (BH-adjusted \(p < 0.05\)).”

heading: 5.192 Practical Tips

heading: 5.195 Introduction
text: Levene’s test checks whether variances equal across two groups, providing formal diagnostic homogeneity--variance assumption underpins classical analysis variance two-sample \(t\)-test. Bartlett’s test offers alternative strict Normality assumptions, Levene’s test (especially Brown-Forsythe median-based variant) robust non-Normality therefore recommended default practical settings. Levene’s test widely used sanity check reporting ANOVA results, although modern recommendations often suggest defaulting Welch’s heteroscedastic-corrected \(F\)-test rather choosing Student Welch conditional pre-test outcome.

heading: 5.196 Prerequisites
text: working understanding one-way ANOVA, homogeneity--variance assumption, role robustness diagnostics choosing classical corrected inferential procedures.

heading: 5.197 Theory
text: \(k\) groups, Levene’s test replaces observation absolute deviation group centre — group mean (Levene’s original 1960 form) group median (Brown-Forsythe’s 1974 modification) — computes one-way ANOVA transformed values:\[W = \frac{(N - k) \sum_i n_i (\bar Z_i - \bar Z)^2}{(k - 1) \sum_i \sum_j (Z_{ij} - \bar Z_i)^2},\]\(Z_{ij}\) absolute deviation. null equal group variances, \(W\) follows \(F\)-distribution \((k-1, N-k)\) degrees freedom. median-based form substantially robust skew heavy tails mean-based form recommended default modern practice.

heading: 5.198 Assumptions
text: Observations independent within across groups, absolute-deviation transformation removes dependence assumed distributional form, chosen centre (median non-Normal data, mean symmetric Normal data) appropriate. Levene’s test assume Normality raw data — principal advantage Bartlett’s test.

heading: 5.199 R Implementation
code:
library(car)
set.seed(2026)

df <- data.frame(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 40)),
  y     = c(rnorm(40, 50, 5),
            rnorm(40, 55, 12),
            rnorm(40, 52, 5))
)

leveneTest(y ~ group, data = df, center = median)

leveneTest(y ~ group, data = df, center = mean)

bartlett.test(y ~ group, data = df)

heading: 5.200 Output & Results
text: example shows three groups deliberately heterogeneous variances (\(\sigma = 5, 12, 5\)). Levene’s test (median-centred) detects heterogeneity strongly (\(F_{2, 117} = 16.98\), \(p < 0.001\)), mean-centred form. Bartlett’s test rejects even emphatically (\(\chi^2 = 35.2\), \(p < 0.001\)), sensitivity exaggerated Normality assumption — property makes unreliable real data.

heading: 5.201 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Levene’s test (median-centred) indicated significant heterogeneity variance across three groups (\(F_{2, 117} = 16.98\), \(p < 0.001\)); group B standard deviation 12 vs 5 groups C. Welch’s heteroscedastic-corrected \(F\)-test therefore used mean comparison rather classical Student’s ANOVA. Bartlett’s test gave qualitatively conclusion sensitive non-Normality preferred real data.” Always describe variance pattern chosen response.

heading: 5.202 Practical Tips
text: Use median-based Brown-Forsythe variant Levene’s test default; substantially robust non-Normality original mean-based form, cost power truly Normal data small.routine pre-test followed conditional choice Student’s Welch’s ANOVA inflates type-error rate; modern recommendations default Welch’s \(F\)-test (heteroscedastic-corrected \(t\)-test analogue) regardless Levene result, Welch essentially equivalent Student homogeneity properly controlled heterogeneity.small group sample sizes (\(n_i < 10\)), Levene’s test low power meaningful variance differences; absence rejection small \(n\) interpreted evidence homogeneity.Bartlett’s test assumes Normality underlying data famously sensitive violations assumption — can reject homogeneity data merely non-Normal even truly equal variances. Avoid Bartlett real data use Levene Brown-Forsythe instead.repeated-measures designs, Levene-style test raw data directly applicable within-subject correlation; inspect residual-vs-fitted plots, residual-vs-time plots, assumed covariance structure diagnostic purposes.Levene’s test transformed data (log, square-root) often resolves apparent heterogeneity caused skew; variance heterogeneity symptom rather underlying problem many applied settings.

heading: 5.203 R Packages Used
text: car::leveneTest() canonical Levene Brown-Forsythe implementations explicit centre control; base R bartlett.test() Bartlett’s test Normality assumption; lawstat::levene.test() alternative implementation multiple centre options; onewaytests::homog.test() tidyverse-friendly interface; WRS2::t1way() Welch-style heteroscedastic-corrected ANOVA practical follow-.

heading: 5.206 Introduction
text: Mann-Whitney U test (equivalent Wilcoxon rank-sum test) compares two independent groups using ranks rather raw values. non-parametric analogue two-sample t-test, appropriate ordinal outcomes continuous data clearly violate normality.

heading: 5.207 Prerequisites
text: Ranks, two-sample comparison.

heading: 5.208 Theory
text: independent samples \(X_1, \ldots, X_{n_1}\) \(Y_1, \ldots, Y_{n_2}\), pool observations, rank , sum ranks first group get \(R_1\). U statistic \[U_1 = R_1 - \frac{n_1(n_1 + 1)}{2}.\]\(H_0: F_X = F_Y\), \(U_1\) known distribution computed via Wilcoxon rank sum. Large-sample normal approximation uses\[E[U] = n_1 n_2 / 2, \quad \mathrm{Var}(U) = n_1 n_2 (n_1 + n_2 + 1) / 12.\]Effect sizes:Rank-biserial correlation \(r_{rb} = 1 - 2 U / (n_1 n_2)\), range \([-1, 1]\).Probability superiority \(= U / (n_1 n_2)\).

heading: 5.209 Assumptions
text: Two independent groups.Observations least ordinal.Similar distribution shapes median-difference interpretation; otherwise test compares distributions generally.

heading: 5.210 R Implementation
code:
library(effectsize)
set.seed(2026)

group1 <- rlnorm(25, meanlog = log(50), sdlog = 0.4)
group2 <- rlnorm(30, meanlog = log(60), sdlog = 0.4)

wilcox.test(group1, group2)

# Effect size: rank-biserial
rank_biserial(group1, group2)

# Median IQR comparisons
c(median1 = median(group1), IQR1 = IQR(group1),
  median2 = median(group2), IQR2 = IQR(group2))

heading: 5.211 Output & Results
text: Significant difference p = 0.008; rank-biserial -0.36 indicates moderate shift group 1 tending smaller.
code:

W = 240, p-value = 0.008

r (rank biserial) | 95% CI
----------------------------
-0.36             | [-0.59, -0.10]

heading: 5.212 Interpretation
text: “Serum biomarker concentrations significantly lower control group cases (Mann-Whitney \(U = 240\), \(p = 0.008\), rank-biserial \(r = -0.36\)). Median concentration 47 (IQR 34-59) ng/mL vs. 62 (IQR 47-73) cases.”

heading: 5.213 Practical Tips

heading: 5.216 Introduction
text: units measured binary outcome twice (/, two tests, two raters), observations paired standard two-proportion test invalid. McNemar’s test analyses 2x2 table paired responses, using discordant pairs – two measurements differ.

heading: 5.217 Prerequisites
text: Contingency tables, paired design.

heading: 5.218 Theory
text: Arrange paired binary data 2x2 table:McNemar’s null: probability switching + - equals probability switching - +; equivalently, marginal prevalences equal.Test statistic:\[\chi^2_{\text{McNemar}} = \frac{(b - c)^2}{b + c} \sim \chi^2_1 \quad \text{} H_0.\]small \(b + c\), exact binomial test \(b\) \(b + c\) \(p_0 = 0.5\) preferred.concordant cells \(\) \(d\) enter test; discordant \(b\) \(c\) .

heading: 5.219 Assumptions
text: Paired binary data.Independent pairs.\(b + c\) sufficiently large (>= 25) chi-squared approximation; otherwise exact.

heading: 5.220 R Implementation
code:
# Paired data: a diagnostic test evaluated before and after training
tab <- matrix(c(45, 12,
                25, 18), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
              dimnames = list(Before = c("+", "-"),
                              After  = c("+", "-")))
tab

# McNemar (with continuity correction disabled)
mcnemar.test(tab, correct = FALSE)

# Exact McNemar via exact2x2
library(exact2x2)
mcnemar.exact(tab)

# Manual McNemar
b <- tab[1, 2]; c <- tab[2, 1]
chi <- (b - c)^2 / (b + c)
p_val <- 1 - pchisq(chi, df = 1)
c(chi = chi, p = p_val)

heading: 5.221 Output & Results
text: Discordant pairs: 12 (+ / -) vs. 25 (- / +). McNemar \(\chi^2(1) = 4.57\), p = 0.033; exact p = 0.041.
code:

McNemar's chi-squared = 4.568, df = 1, p-value = 0.0326

Exact McNemar test (exact2x2):
proportion +1 to -1: 0.324
95% CI: 0.037 to 0.586
p-value = 0.041

heading: 5.222 Interpretation
text: Reporting: “37 discordant pairs, 25 switched negative positive 12 switched opposite direction; McNemar’s exact test gave p = 0.041, indicating significant change training.”

heading: 5.223 Practical Tips
text: Report discordant counts p-value; concordant counts irrelevant McNemar.\(b + c < 25\), use exact version.McNemar’s odds ratio \(b/c\); CI computed inverting exact test.two categories per assessment (paired, two outcomes), use Bowker’s symmetry test Stuart-Maxwell.Stratified McNemar: use conditional logistic regression (survival::clogit) set matched pairs covariates.

heading: 5.226 Introduction
text: mixed ANOVA combines least one -subjects factor (treatment arm, sex) least one within-subjects factor (time point, condition). standard analysis longitudinal trials split-plot designs. typical primary question group--time interaction: change time differ groups?

heading: 5.227 Prerequisites
text: Repeated-measures ANOVA, factorial designs.

heading: 5.228 Theory
text: design factor (\(\) levels, independent groups) within factor B (\(b\) levels, measured subject):\[Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \pi_{k()} + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk},\]\(\pi_{k()}\) subject-level random effect nested .Three effects tested:Main effect (): uses -subjects MS denominator.Main effect B (within): uses within-subjects MS denominator.x B interaction: uses within-subjects MS denominator.Sphericity applies within factor (interaction ); Greenhouse-Geisser Huynh-Feldt corrections used needed.

heading: 5.229 Assumptions
text: Independent subjects within -group.Normal residuals, separately within components.Homogeneous variance-covariance structure across groups (Box’s M, rarely formally tested).Sphericity within factor.

heading: 5.230 R Implementation
code:
library(afex); library(emmeans); library(ggplot2)
set.seed(2026)

# 30 subjects per arm, measured at 3 times
n <- 30
subj  <- rep(1:(2 * n), each = 3)
arm   <- factor(rep(rep(c("Placebo", "Active"), each = 3), n))
time  <- factor(rep(c("t0", "t1", "t2"), 2 * n))

df <- data.frame(
  subject = factor(subj),
  arm, time,
  y = rnorm(6 * n, mean = 100, sd = 10) +
      ifelse(time == "t1", 3, 0) +
      ifelse(time == "t2", 5, 0) +
      ifelse(arm == "Active" & time == "t2", -8, 0)
)

fit <- aov_car(y ~ arm * time + Error(subject/time), data = df)
fit

# Interaction plot
emm <- emmeans(fit, ~ arm * time)
plot(emm, comparisons = TRUE)

# Simple effects of arm at each time
pairs(emm, by = "time", adjust = "bonferroni")

heading: 5.231 Output & Results
text: Significant main effects arm time, significant interaction. Simple-effects analysis localises interaction.
code:

 arm            1, 58  245  8.12   0.09   0.0062 **
 time           2, 116  45   9.55  0.03  <.001 ***
 arm:time       2, 116  45   4.72  0.02   0.011 *

heading: 5.232 Interpretation
text: “2 (arm) x 3 (time) mixed ANOVA revealed significant interaction, F(2, 116) = 4.72, p = 0.011, \(\eta_G^2 = 0.02\). Simple-effects comparisons showed time 2, Active arm 8.0 points lower Placebo (p = 0.003).”

heading: 5.233 Practical Tips
text: longitudinal trials, interaction typically primary endpoint.Sphericity corrections apply within-subjects parts model.Missing measurements within-subject factor cause listwise deletion subject; prefer linear mixed model unbalanced data.Report interaction plot; often interpretable numeric ANOVA table.Simple main effects (one factor fixed level ) give interpretable decomposition interaction significant.

heading: 5.236 Introduction
text: Testing many hypotheses simultaneously inflates chance spurious discoveries. Controlling inflation goal multiple-comparison corrections. Two broad families exist: family-wise error rate (FWER) methods false discovery rate (FDR) methods. Choosing depends consequences false positive.

heading: 5.237 Prerequisites
text: Hypothesis testing, Type error.

heading: 5.238 Theory
text: Suppose \(m\) hypotheses tested \(V\) falsely rejected (true nulls). Let \(R\) total rejections.FWER \(= P(V \geq 1)\). probability false rejection.FDR \(= E[V / \max(R, 1)]\). Expected proportion false positives among rejections.Bonferroni, Holm, Hochberg control FWER. Benjamini-Hochberg Benjamini-Yekutieli control FDR.use :FWER: small number pre-specified comparisons, confirmatory studies, clinical trial endpoints.FDR: exploratory high-throughput studies thousands tests (genomics, imaging), percent false discoveries acceptable.

heading: 5.239 Assumptions
text: Methods assume valid individual p-values (uniform null). FWER corrections robust arbitrary dependence; FDR methods vary (BH needs positive dependence, handles arbitrary).

heading: 5.240 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
m <- 100
pvals <- runif(m) * ifelse(seq_len(m) <= 10, 0.01, 1)   # 10 small, 90 null

adj_methods <- c("bonferroni", "holm", "hochberg", "BH", "BY")
adj <- sapply(adj_methods, function(m_name)
  p.adjust(pvals, method = m_name))

data.frame(method = adj_methods,
           rejections_at_0.05 = colSums(adj < 0.05))

heading: 5.241 Output & Results
text: FDR (BH) recovers full signal set; FWER methods conservative. tighter BH dependence-robust adjustment.
code:
method rejections_at_0.05
1 bonferroni                  8
2        holm                  8
3    hochberg                  9
4          BH                 10
5          BY                  7

heading: 5.242 Interpretation
text: paper: “Multiple testing across 100 primary comparisons controlled FWER 0.05 using Holm procedure (8 100 hypotheses rejected).” : “Across 50 000 transcripts, differential expression declared Benjamini-Hochberg q < 0.05 (2 341 transcripts).”

heading: 5.243 Practical Tips
text: Correct tests belong family; family defined inferential goal, software runs.Pre-specify family method protocol; post-hoc changes correction data-snooping.hierarchically structured hypotheses, gatekeeping procedures maintain FWER level.Report raw adjusted p-values transparency.False discovery proportion (FDP) realisation FDR given dataset; FDR expectation.

heading: 5.246 Introduction
text: Every hypothesis test begins two hypotheses: null captures status quo alternative captures conclude null rejected. Stating clearly, data arrive, crucial first step – remarkably common source error skipped.

heading: 5.247 Prerequisites
text: Random variables population parameters.

heading: 5.248 Theory
text: null hypothesis \(H_0\) typically statement effect, difference, specific parameter value. alternative \(H_1\) (\(H_A\)) conclude reject \(H_0\).Two-sided alternatives default sign effect unknown interest directions:\[H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0.\]One-sided alternatives specify direction, increasing power direction cost giving inference opposite:\[H_0: \mu \leq \mu_0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu > \mu_0.\]Use one-sided test :priori, one direction scientific interest.result direction treated identically null result.directionality pre-specified protocol, chosen seeing data.Simple vs. composite: \(H_0: \mu = 0\) simple (single value); \(H_0: \mu \leq 0\) composite (set). Test statistics rejection rules differ slightly.Equivalence/non-inferiority testing flips roles: null effect least large margin, rejection establishes equivalence.

heading: 5.249 Assumptions
text: Hypotheses must specified examining data. Post-hoc redefinition invalidates sampling distribution p-value.

heading: 5.250 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Two-sided test
x <- rnorm(40, mean = 2, sd = 5)
t.test(x, mu = 0, alternative = "two.sided")

# One-sided test (H1: mu > 0)
t.test(x, mu = 0, alternative = "greater")

# One-sided test (H1: mu < 0)
t.test(x, mu = 0, alternative = "less")

# TOST (equivalence: |mu| < 1)
library(TOSTER)
tsum_TOST(m1 = mean(x), sd1 = sd(x), n1 = length(x),
          low_eqbound = -1, high_eqbound = 1)

heading: 5.251 Output & Results
text: two-sided \(p\) roughly twice one-sided \(p\) point estimate consistent \(H_1\)’s direction. equivalence testing, rejection one-sided components establishes equivalence.

heading: 5.252 Interpretation
text: Pre-register pre-specify hypotheses protocol; switch two-sided one-sided seeing sign effect. Misuse one-sided tests common source reviewer objections.

heading: 5.253 Practical Tips
text: Default two-sided tests unless strong, pre-specified rationale one-sidedness exists.Never compute one-sided two-sided p-values report whichever smaller.equivalence trials, “null” dissimilarity “alternative” equivalence – reversed classical tests.Composite null hypotheses handled evaluating test statistic boundary.Frame hypotheses terms parameter scientific interest, test statistic.

heading: 5.256 Introduction
text: one-proportion test compares observed success proportion \(\hat{p}\) benchmark \(p_0\). Applications: phase II response rate compared historical control; screening positive rate compared national reference; success rate surgical procedure compared target.

heading: 5.257 Prerequisites
text: Binomial distribution, confidence intervals.

heading: 5.258 Theory
text: \(X \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n, p)\) observed \(\hat{p} = x/n\), three tests coexist:Exact binomial test: computes exact two-sided p-value \(H_0: p = p_0\) using binomial PMF. R’s binom.test(). Always valid; approximation.Normal-approximation (Wald) z-test:\[Z = \frac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{p_0 (1 - p_0) / n}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1).\]Works \(n p_0 \geq 10\) \(n (1 - p_0) \geq 10\); biased unreliable near \(p = 0\) \(p = 1\).Score (Wilson) test: uses variance \(H_0\) inverts get CI better coverage Wald, especially small \(n\) extreme \(p\). R’s prop.test() uses default.Confidence intervals:Wald: \(\hat{p} \pm 1.96 \sqrt{\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})/n}\) (poor near 0 1).Wilson: centred, always inside \([0, 1]\), good coverage.Clopper-Pearson (exact): always valid, conservative.

heading: 5.259 Assumptions
text: Independent Bernoulli trials.Fixed \(n\) (sequential stopping rule).

heading: 5.260 R Implementation
code:
library(binom)

x <- 32; n <- 80; p0 <- 0.30

# Exact binomial test
binom.test(x, n, p = p0)

# Score (Wilson) test
prop.test(x, n, p = p0, correct = FALSE)

# Normal-approximation test (manual)
phat <- x / n
z <- (phat - p0) / sqrt(p0 * (1 - p0) / n)
pval <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(z)))
c(z = z, p = pval)

# Confidence intervals
binom.confint(x, n, conf.level = 0.95,
              methods = c("wald", "wilson", "exact"))

heading: 5.261 Output & Results
text: Observed proportion 0.40 (95 % Wilson CI 0.30 0.51); exact binomial p = 0.042.
code:
Exact binomial test
x = 32, n = 80, p-value = 0.04215, 95% CI: (0.292, 0.517)

     1-sample test for given proportion without continuity correction
X-squared = 3.9286, df = 1, p-value = 0.04748, 95% CI (score): (0.303, 0.508)

     method  x  n    mean    lower   upper
1      wald 32 80   0.400  0.2926  0.5074
2    wilson 32 80   0.400  0.3020  0.5066
3     exact 32 80   0.400  0.2923  0.5172

heading: 5.262 Interpretation
text: Report point estimate CI. manuscript: “response rate 40.0 % (32/80; 95 % Wilson CI 30.2 % 50.7 %; exact binomial p = 0.042 vs. null rate 30 %).”

heading: 5.263 Practical Tips
text: Use Wilson Clopper-Pearson CIs default; Wald obsolete.small \(n\) extreme \(p\) (near 0 1), exact tests intervals mandatory.report zero/one proportions \(0\) \(1\) Wald CI “0 0”; use Clopper-Pearson upper bound (rule three: upper bound \(\approx 3/n\) \(x = 0\)).Continuity correction prop.test() unnecessary modern purposes; set correct = FALSE.agreement two-proportion tests, use CI method (Wilson) .

heading: 5.266 Introduction
text: one-sample t-test compares mean single continuous variable pre-specified reference value. answers questions form “average differ benchmark?” – average IQ differs 100, mean biomarker level differs reference, average weight change differs zero pre-post design (via paired differences).

heading: 5.267 Prerequisites
text: t-distribution, concept sampling distribution.

heading: 5.268 Theory
text: Given iid observations \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\), test \[H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0\]uses statistic\[T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}} \sim t_{n-1} \quad \text{} H_0.\]sample SD \(s\) replaces population \(\sigma\); distribution adjusts normal t replacement adds variability. degrees freedom \(n - 1\).Confidence interval \(\mu\): \(\bar{X} \pm t_{1 - \alpha/2, n - 1} \cdot s/\sqrt{n}\).Effect size: Cohen’s \(d = (\bar{X} - \mu_0) / s\).

heading: 5.269 Assumptions
text: Independent observations.Normal distribution (\(n\) large enough CLT kick ).extreme outliers affecting mean.

heading: 5.270 R Implementation
code:
library(effectsize); library(broom)
set.seed(2026)

# Simulated IQ-test scores; is the mean different from 100?
iq <- rnorm(45, mean = 103.5, sd = 14)

t.test(iq, mu = 100) |> tidy()

# Effect size
cohens_d(iq, mu = 100)

# Check assumptions
shapiro.test(iq)
boxplot(iq, main = "Sample distribution", col = "#2A9D8F")

heading: 5.271 Output & Results
text: sample mean 104, significantly different 100 \(\alpha = 0.05\) (\(t(44) = 1.82\), \(p = 0.076\)), small--medium effect (\(d = 0.27\)).

Cohen's d |         95% CI

heading: 5.272 Interpretation
text: manuscript: “mean IQ 104.0 (SD 14.0). 95 % CI mean (98.9 109.3) includes reference value 100; one-sample t-test reject null equal mean (t(44) = 1.82, p = 0.076, Cohen’s d = 0.27).”

heading: 5.273 Practical Tips
text: \(n \geq 30\), CLT makes t-test fairly robust non-normality.small \(n\) clear non-normality, use Wilcoxon signed-rank test (one-sample version).Power one-sample t-test depends effect size \(n\); use power.t.test(type = "one.sample").reference value \(\mu_0\) must pre-specified; post-hoc selection invalidates p-value.Pair 95 % CI mean – informative p-value alone.

heading: 5.276 Introduction
text: one-sample z-test idealised version one-sample t-test: assumes population variance \(\sigma^2\) known exactly. rarely case applied research – \(\sigma\) unknown, estimate sample use t-test instead. z-test remains pedagogically important simplest exact hypothesis test reference t-test’s elaborate machinery.

heading: 5.277 Prerequisites
text: Normal distribution, sampling distribution mean.

heading: 5.278 Theory
text: \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) iid \(\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\) known \(\sigma\), test \[H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0\]uses\[Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) \quad \text{} H_0.\]two-sided \(p\)-value \(2 (1 - \Phi(|z_{\text{obs}}|))\); 95 % CI \(\mu\) \(\bar{X} \pm 1.96 \sigma/\sqrt{n}\).z-test also used \(\sigma\) well-estimated – historically meaning \(n > 30\) t z distributions nearly indistinguishable. Modern practice uses t-test even .

heading: 5.279 Assumptions
text: Independent observations.Normal population (\(n\) large enough CLT).Population variance \(\sigma^2\) known.

heading: 5.280 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Assume the assay has a known SD from reference literature
sigma_known <- 8
x <- rnorm(50, mean = 103, sd = sigma_known)

# Manual z-test
z_stat <- (mean(x) - 100) / (sigma_known / sqrt(length(x)))
p_two_sided <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(z_stat)))
c(z = z_stat, p = p_two_sided)

# 95% CI
mean(x) + c(-1, 1) * qnorm(0.975) * sigma_known / sqrt(length(x))

# Via BSDA::z.test
library(BSDA)
z.test(x, mu = 100, sigma.x = sigma_known)

heading: 5.281 Output & Results
text: sample mean 103 SE 1.13 (known SD / sqrt(n)); z = 2.19, p = 0.029, rejecting \(H_0\) \(\alpha = 0.05\).

2.1856   0.02884

[1] 100.53 105.47

        One-sample z-Test

data:  x
z = 2.1856, p-value = 0.02884
alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 100

heading: 5.282 Interpretation
text: Reporting z-test rarely appropriate modern applied work unless variance genuinely known (e.g., decades reference data). Reporting t-test instead gives honest representation variance uncertainty.

heading: 5.283 Practical Tips
text: Default t-test; use z \(\sigma\) genuinely known.two-proportion comparisons, “z-test proportions” close cousin uses estimated variance.\(n > 30\) \(\sigma\) unknown, t z tests give essentially identical p-values.z-test foundation elaborate procedures: power calculations, sample-size formulas, large-sample limit many Wald-style tests.process-control charts (SPC), “3-sigma” rule z-test \(\alpha \approx 0.0027\).

heading: 5.286 Introduction
text: One-way ANOVA tests whether means three independent groups equal. generalises two-sample t-test multiple groups controlling overall Type error. omnibus F-test tells us whether means differ; post-hoc tests (Tukey HSD, Dunnett) identify pairs.

heading: 5.287 Prerequisites
text: Two-sample t-test, F distribution.

heading: 5.288 Theory
text: \(k\) groups \(n_i\) observations , total \(n = \sum n_i\), define:Sum squares (SSB): \(\sum_i n_i (\bar{X}_i - \bar{X})^2\) \(k - 1\) df.Sum squares within (SSW): \(\sum_i \sum_j (X_{ij} - \bar{X}_i)^2\) \(n - k\) df.Mean squares: MSB = SSB / (k - 1), MSW = SSW / (n - k).\(H_0: \mu_1 = \ldots = \mu_k\),\[F = \frac{\text{MSB}}{\text{MSW}} \sim F_{k - 1, n - k}.\]Large F implies -group variation exceeds within-group variation, rejecting equality means.Effect size: \(\eta^2 = \text{SSB} / \text{SST}\), proportion variance explained group factor.

heading: 5.289 Assumptions
text: Independent observations within across groups.Normal residuals (large enough \(n_i\)).Homogeneous variances across groups (Welch ANOVA relaxes ).

heading: 5.290 R Implementation
code:
library(effectsize); library(broom); library(car)
set.seed(2026)

df <- data.frame(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C", "D"), each = 30)),
  y     = c(rnorm(30, 50, 8),
            rnorm(30, 55, 8),
            rnorm(30, 58, 8),
            rnorm(30, 54, 8))
)

# Omnibus ANOVA
fit <- aov(y ~ group, data = df)
summary(fit)
tidy(fit)

# Assumption checks
leveneTest(y ~ group, data = df)
shapiro.test(residuals(fit))

# Effect size
eta_squared(fit)
omega_squared(fit)

# Welch ANOVA (unequal variances)
oneway.test(y ~ group, data = df)

# Tukey HSD post-hoc
TukeyHSD(fit)

heading: 5.291 Output & Results
text: F(3, 116) = 4.83, p = 0.003, rejects equal means. Tukey identifies C vs. significant pair.

heading: 5.292 Interpretation
text: Report: “one-way ANOVA showed significant group effect (F(3, 116) = 4.83, p = 0.003, \(\eta^2 = 0.11\)). Tukey HSD comparisons indicated group C differed significantly group (mean difference 8.2, 95 % CI 2.2 14.2, adjusted p = 0.003).”

heading: 5.293 Practical Tips
text: Always check homogeneity variances; violated, use Welch ANOVA.Always report effect size; F alone enough.many post-hoc comparisons, Tukey standard; Dunnett comparing treatments control; Bonferroni Holm pre-specified families.Non-parametric alternative: Kruskal-Wallis.within-subjects data, use repeated-measures ANOVA linear mixed model.

heading: 5.296 Introduction
text: p-value single reported statistic applied research, frequently misinterpreted. Understanding p-value – – essential reading, writing, reviewing quantitative studies. American Statistical Association’s 2016 statement p-values laid six principles every user statistics internalise.

heading: 5.297 Prerequisites
text: Basic hypothesis testing sampling distributions.

heading: 5.298 Theory
text: p-value probability, assuming null hypothesis true, observing data least extreme actually observed. Formally,\[p = P(T \geq t_{\text{obs}} \mid H_0)\]one-sided test test statistic \(T\), two-sided analogue two-sided alternatives.Correct interpretation: small p-value means observed data unusual \(H_0\). mean \(H_0\) unlikely true, alternative probable.Common misinterpretations:“\(p = 0.03\) means ’s 3% probability null true.” Wrong: p-value conditional probability data given \(H_0\), \(H_0\) given data.“\(p < 0.05\) means result meaningful.” Wrong: small p-values can come huge samples detecting trivial effects.“\(p > 0.05\) means effect.” Wrong: failure reject evidence effect; absence evidence.“p-values experiment comparable.” Wrong: p-values discontinuous effect size; p 0.04 0.06 nearly identical evidentially.\(H_0\), p-value uniform distribution \([0, 1]\) (continuous test statistics). useful simulation check.

heading: 5.299 Assumptions
text: null hypothesis test statistic pre-specified.sampling model \(H_0\) correctly specified.Multiple testing, optional stopping, selective reporting present.

heading: 5.300 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)
set.seed(2026)

# Simulate p-values under H0 (they should be uniform)
n_sims <- 5000
pvals_null <- replicate(n_sims, {
  x <- rnorm(30, mean = 0, sd = 1)   # H0 true: mu = 0
  t.test(x, mu = 0)$p.value
})

hist(pvals_null, breaks = 40, col = "#2A9D8F",
     main = "p-values under H0 are uniform", xlab = "p-value")

# Simulate under H1
pvals_alt <- replicate(n_sims, {
  x <- rnorm(30, mean = 0.5, sd = 1)
  t.test(x, mu = 0)$p.value
})

hist(pvals_alt, breaks = 40, col = "#F4A261",
     main = "p-values under H1 pile near zero", xlab = "p-value")

# Empirical power at alpha = 0.05
mean(pvals_alt < 0.05)
power.t.test(n = 30, delta = 0.5, sd = 1)$power

heading: 5.301 Output & Results
text: \(H_0\), histogram flat. \(H_1\), p-values pile near zero. empirical rejection rate \(\alpha = 0.05\) matches theoretical power.
code:
[1] 0.763    # empirical power
[1] 0.752    # theoretical power

heading: 5.302 Interpretation
text: reporting, pair p-values effect sizes confidence intervals. “difference 3.2 mmHg (95 % CI 0.6 5.8 mmHg, \(p = 0.015\))” conveys magnitude statistical significance.

heading: 5.303 Practical Tips
text: Never report p-value alone; always include effect size CI.Pre-specify one-sided vs. two-sided tests; switching seeing data fishing.Large samples make tiny effects statistically significant; focus practical significance \(n\) large.Multiple testing inflates false-positive rates; adjust Bonferroni, Holm, FDR.“Nearly significant” (\(0.05 < p < 0.10\)) meaningful category; report exact value.

heading: 5.306 Introduction
text: unit contributes two measurements (pre post, left right, case matched control), observations paired usual independent-samples t-test valid. paired t-test analyses within-unit differences, restoring independence assumption typically improving power cancelling -unit variability.

heading: 5.307 Prerequisites
text: One-sample t-test, t-distribution.

heading: 5.308 Theory
text: Let \(X_i\) \(Y_i\) paired measurements unit \(\) \(= 1, \ldots, n\). Define differences \(D_i = X_i - Y_i\). assumption \(D_i\) iid \(\mathcal{N}(\mu_D, \sigma_D^2)\),\[T = \frac{\bar{D}}{s_D / \sqrt{n}} \sim t_{n - 1} \quad \text{} H_0: \mu_D = 0.\]paired t-test one-sample t-test differences. \(n\) number pairs, total number measurements.Effect size: Cohen’s \(d_z = \bar{D} / s_D\) (standardised mean difference difference scores). different two-group \(d\) conflated.

heading: 5.309 Assumptions
text: differences approximately normal (via Shapiro-Wilk \(D_i\)).Pairs independent pairs.raw \(X_i\) \(Y_i\) can non-normal; differences need .

heading: 5.310 R Implementation
code:
library(effectsize); library(broom)
set.seed(2026)

# Simulated pre-post blood pressure after a 12-week intervention
n <- 30
pre  <- rnorm(n, mean = 138, sd = 12)
post <- pre + rnorm(n, mean = -6, sd = 5)

# Paired t-test
t.test(pre, post, paired = TRUE) |> tidy()

# Equivalent: one-sample t-test on the differences
t.test(pre - post, mu = 0) |> tidy()

# Effect size
cohens_d(pre, post, paired = TRUE)

# Assumption check on differences
shapiro.test(pre - post)

heading: 5.311 Output & Results
text: post-intervention measurement 6.0 mmHg lower average; \(t(29) = 6.88\), \(p < 0.001\), large paired effect.

heading: 5.313 Practical Tips
text: Paired design usually power independent-groups design total sample size.Check normality differences, raw measurements.non-normal differences, use Wilcoxon signed-rank.Pair variables correctly: subjects must order vectors, use data frame.Report Cohen’s \(d_z\), pooled-SD two-group \(d\), paired designs.

heading: 5.316 Introduction
text: Pearson correlation test assesses whether two continuous variables linearly related. returns correlation coefficient \(r\) p-value null population correlation \(\rho = 0\), along confidence interval computed via Fisher’s z transformation.

heading: 5.317 Prerequisites
text: Pearson correlation coefficient, sampling distributions.

heading: 5.318 Theory
text: Given iid pairs \((X_i, Y_i)\) bivariate distribution marginal SDs \(\sigma_X, \sigma_Y\), sample correlation \[r = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}.\]\(H_0: \rho = 0\), statistic\[t = r \sqrt{\frac{n - 2}{1 - r^2}} \sim t_{n - 2}.\]confidence intervals, Fisher’s z-transformation \[z = \frac{1}{2} \log\!\frac{1 + r}{1 - r}, \quad \mathrm{SE}(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n - 3}}.\]Construct CI z scale, back-transform \(r = \tanh(z)\).

heading: 5.319 Assumptions
text: Continuous \(X\) \(Y\), approximately bivariate normal.Linear relationship; extreme outliers.Independent pairs.

heading: 5.320 R Implementation
code:
library(broom)
set.seed(2026)

n <- 80
x <- rnorm(n)
y <- 0.4 * x + rnorm(n, sd = sqrt(1 - 0.4^2))  # true rho = 0.4

cor.test(x, y, method = "pearson") |> tidy()

# Manual check
r <- cor(x, y)
t_stat <- r * sqrt((n - 2) / (1 - r^2))
p_val  <- 2 * (1 - pt(abs(t_stat), df = n - 2))
c(r = r, t = t_stat, p = p_val)

# Fisher's z CI
z    <- atanh(r)
se_z <- 1 / sqrt(n - 3)
ci_z <- z + c(-1, 1) * 1.96 * se_z
tanh(ci_z)

heading: 5.321 Output & Results
text: Sample \(r = 0.43\) (95 % CI 0.23 0.60), \(t(78) = 4.19\), \(p < 0.001\). Strong evidence positive correlation.
code:
# A tibble: 1 x 8
  estimate statistic p.value parameter conf.low conf.high method    alternative
     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>     <chr>
1    0.43     4.19   <0.001        78   0.234     0.598  Pearson   two.sided

heading: 5.322 Interpretation
text: “significant positive correlation \(x\) \(y\) (\(r = 0.43\), 95 % CI 0.23 0.60; \(t(78) = 4.19\), \(p < 0.001\)). two variables shared 18.5 % variance linearly.”

heading: 5.323 Practical Tips
text: Scatter plot first; \(r\) can misleading relationship non-linear.Robust alternatives heavy tails outliers: Spearman, Kendall.small \(n\), CI via Fisher’s z noticeably asymmetric \(r\) scale.Always report \(r\) CI; p-value alone often uninformative.Testing multiple correlations data requires multiple-testing correction.

heading: 5.326 Introduction
text: permutation test evaluates null hypothesis comparing observed test statistic distribution random permutations group labels (exchangeable structure). makes distributional assumption data, exchangeability null.

heading: 5.327 Prerequisites
text: Hypothesis testing, ranks (helpful).

heading: 5.328 Theory
text: \(H_0\), group labels exchangeable – joint distribution \((X_1, \ldots, X_n)\) invariant permutations labels. p-value proportion permuted test statistics least extreme observed one:\[p = \frac{1 + \#\{T^{(b)} \geq T_{\text{obs}}\}}{B + 1},\]\(T^{(b)}\) statistic \(b\)-th permutation \(B\) random permutations, plus observed. Exact permutation tests use \(n!\) permutations; large \(n\), Monte Carlo permutation \(B = 1000\) suffices.Permutation tests can use test statistic – difference means, t-statistic, ratio medians, area ROC. choice statistic determines aspect distribution test sensitive .

heading: 5.329 Assumptions
text: Exchangeability \(H_0\) (independent observations equal distribution group-based tests).Independence across observations.normality, variance homogeneity, distributional model.

heading: 5.330 R Implementation
code:
library(coin); library(perm)
set.seed(2026)

group1 <- c(rlnorm(20, 1, 0.4), 30)   # one extreme value
group2 <- rlnorm(25, 1.2, 0.4)

# Manual permutation test on difference of means
obs_diff <- mean(group1) - mean(group2)
pooled   <- c(group1, group2)
n1       <- length(group1)
B        <- 10000

perm_diffs <- replicate(B, {
  shuffled <- sample(pooled)
  mean(shuffled[1:n1]) - mean(shuffled[(n1+1):length(shuffled)])
})
p_perm <- (1 + sum(abs(perm_diffs) >= abs(obs_diff))) / (B + 1)
c(obs_diff = obs_diff, p_perm = p_perm)

# coin package: permutation version of many standard tests
oneway_test(y ~ group,
            data = data.frame(y = c(group1, group2),
                              group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), c(n1, length(group2))))),
            distribution = approximate(nresample = 10000))

heading: 5.331 Output & Results
text: Permutation test gives p = 0.12, robust outlier distort t-test.
code:
obs_diff    p_perm
  0.841     0.123

Approximative Two-Sample Fisher-Pitman Permutation Test
Z = 1.56, p = 0.13

heading: 5.332 Interpretation
text: “Monte Carlo permutation test (10 000 resamples) comparing two groups’ mean differences gave p = 0.12, providing evidence group effect.”

heading: 5.333 Practical Tips
text: Permutation tests exact small samples combinatorial enumeration feasible.Monte Carlo permutation uses random resampling; \(B = 1000\)-10 000 gives stable p-values.statistic can function data; flexibility main appeal parametric tests.paired data, permute within pairs (sign flips) rather labels.regression, permute residuals (bootstrap ) rather raw responses.

heading: 5.336 Introduction
text: one-way ANOVA rejects equality means across groups, post-hoc tests identify specific pairs differ. Tukey’s HSD (honestly significant difference) default pairwise comparison test ANOVA equal sample sizes, controlling family-wise error rate nominal \(\alpha\).

heading: 5.337 Prerequisites
text: One-way ANOVA, multiple-comparisons problem.

heading: 5.338 Theory
text: \(k\) groups sample size \(n\) common residual MS = MSE, Tukey critical value pairwise differences \[q_{\alpha, k, n_{\text{total}} - k} \cdot \sqrt{\text{MSE} / n},\]\(q\) Studentised range quantile. Pairs whose observed mean difference exceeds declared significantly different family-wise \(\alpha\).Tukey’s HSD equivalent simultaneous test based maximum pairwise \(t\) statistic. Alternatives:Dunnett: comparing treatment single control; powerful Tukey question.Bonferroni: simple conservative; often -adjusts.Holm: sequential, uniformly powerful Bonferroni.Scheffe: conservative; valid arbitrary contrasts, just pairwise.

heading: 5.339 Assumptions
text: ANOVA: independence, normal residuals, homogeneous variances. unequal sample sizes, Tukey-Kramer variant used.

heading: 5.340 R Implementation
code:
library(emmeans); library(multcomp)
set.seed(2026)

df <- data.frame(
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C", "D"), each = 30)),
  y     = c(rnorm(30, 50, 8),
            rnorm(30, 58, 8),
            rnorm(30, 60, 8),
            rnorm(30, 55, 8))
)

fit <- aov(y ~ group, data = df)

# Built-in Tukey HSD
TukeyHSD(fit)

# emmeans equivalent (more flexible, handles unbalanced designs)
emm <- emmeans(fit, ~ group)
pairs(emm, adjust = "tukey")

# Dunnett (comparing to A as control)
summary(glht(fit, linfct = mcp(group = "Dunnett")))

heading: 5.341 Output & Results
text: Three six pairwise comparisons significant family-wise \(\alpha = 0.05\).

heading: 5.342 Interpretation
text: Report adjusted p-values family-wise confidence intervals. “Tukey HSD post-hoc comparisons following significant one-way ANOVA indicated groups B C differed significantly group (adjusted p < 0.01), D differ significantly (adjusted p = 0.09).”

heading: 5.343 Practical Tips
text: perform post-hoc tests significant omnibus F; otherwise fishing.unequal sample sizes use Tukey-Kramer (R’s TukeyHSD emmeans handle automatically).Dunnett powerful Tukey comparisons one control.complex (non-pairwise) contrasts, use emmeans glht user-defined linear combinations.Conservative alternatives (Bonferroni, Holm) work regardless equal variances; safer heterogeneous-variance settings.

heading: 5.346 Introduction
text: Repeated-measures ANOVA (rmANOVA) handles designs subjects measured multiple time points conditions. treating subject blocking factor, controls -subject variability gains power -subjects analysis. main complication sphericity assumption covariance repeated measures.

heading: 5.347 Prerequisites
text: One-way ANOVA, dependence structure repeated measures.

heading: 5.348 Theory
text: one within-subjects factor \(k\) levels measured \(n\) subjects, model \[Y_{ij} = \mu + \alpha_i + \pi_j + \varepsilon_{ij},\]\(\pi_j\) random subject effect \(\alpha_i\) fixed effect level \(\).Sphericity assumes variances pairwise differences repeated measurements equal. Mauchly’s test assesses assumption.sphericity violated:Greenhouse-Geisser correction: adjusts degrees freedom multiplying \(\hat{\varepsilon}_{\text{GG}} \[1/(k-1), 1]\).Huynh-Feldt correction: less conservative mild violations.Modern alternative: fit linear mixed-effects model (lmer) unstructured compound-symmetric covariance – flexible, handles missing data.

heading: 5.349 Assumptions
text: Normality within-subject residuals.Sphericity (apply correction).Subjects independent; measurements within subject .

heading: 5.350 R Implementation
code:
library(afex); library(emmeans); library(rstatix)
set.seed(2026)

# 20 subjects measured at 4 time points
n_subj <- 20
times  <- 4
subject <- rep(1:n_subj, each = times)
time    <- rep(1:times, n_subj)

# Simulate with a time effect and subject-specific intercepts
df <- data.frame(
  subject = factor(subject),
  time    = factor(time),
  y       = rnorm(n_subj * times, mean = 50 + 2 * time, sd = 6) +
            rep(rnorm(n_subj, 0, 4), each = times)
)

# Using afex::aov_car
fit <- aov_car(y ~ time + Error(subject/time), data = df)
fit

# rstatix pipeline
df |> anova_test(dv = y, wid = subject, within = time)

# Post-hoc with Bonferroni
emm <- emmeans(fit, ~ time)
pairs(emm, adjust = "bonferroni")

heading: 5.351 Output & Results
text: Large within-subject effect time (F(2.61, 49.5) = 12.39, p < 0.001, generalised \(\eta^2 = 0.19\)); sphericity holds correction needed.

Response: y

1    time  2.61, 49.5 28.7  12.39  0.19  <.001 ***

Mauchly Tests for Sphericity:
 Effect   W       p
  time  0.87  0.78   # sphericity holds

Greenhouse-Geisser eps: 0.87

heading: 5.352 Interpretation
text: “one-way repeated-measures ANOVA revealed significant effect time (F(2.61, 49.5) = 12.39, p < 0.001, generalised \(\eta^2 = 0.19\); Greenhouse-Geisser correction applied). Bonferroni post-hoc comparisons identified significant increases time 1 time 3 time 2 time 4.”

heading: 5.353 Practical Tips
text: Check Mauchly’s test; rejected, apply Greenhouse-Geisser Huynh-Feldt.complex designs (multiple within factors, x within interactions), linear mixed model preferred.Missing data rmANOVA causes entire subject dropped (listwise); mixed models handle gracefully.Report generalised \(\eta^2\) repeated-measures designs (partial \(\eta^2\) upwardly biased).non-normal residuals, use Friedman aligned-rank transform (ARTool::art).

heading: 5.356 Introduction
text: runs test non-parametric test randomness sequence binary outcomes (continuous outcomes dichotomised reference median). “run” maximal subsequence identical symbols — example, sequence HHTTTHHH runs HH, TTT, HHH, three runs total. genuine randomness number runs follows known distribution; runs indicate clustering like outcomes (suggesting positive autocorrelation structural break), many runs indicate alternation (negative autocorrelation). runs test widely used quality-control charts detect assignable causes, regression diagnostics flag autocorrelated residuals, randomness testing sequences random-number generators dice-rolling sequences.

heading: 5.357 Prerequisites
text: working understanding binary sequences, concept independent identically distributed observations, role randomness diagnostics time-ordered sequence data.

heading: 5.358 Theory
text: binary sequence \(n_1\) successes \(n_2\) failures, expected number runs null hypothesis randomness \[E[R] = \frac{2 n_1 n_2}{n_1 + n_2} + 1, \qquad \mathrm{Var}(R) = \frac{2 n_1 n_2 (2 n_1 n_2 - n_1 - n_2)}{(n_1 + n_2)^2 (n_1 + n_2 - 1)}.\]standardised statistic \(Z = (R - E[R])/\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(R)}\) approximately standard Normal moderate \(n\) (typically \(n_1, n_2 \geq 10\)). continuous data, dichotomising sample median applying test standard adaptation, though continuous-specific alternatives Durbin-Watson test usually powerful regression residuals.

heading: 5.359 Assumptions
text: sequence binary dichotomised pre-specified way, observations null independent identically distributed Bernoulli, sample size large enough Normal approximation. Tied values dichotomisation cut-must handled deliberately — dropped, broken randomly appropriate caution.

heading: 5.360 R Implementation
code:
library(tseries); library(randtests)
set.seed(2026)

random_seq <- rbinom(50, 1, 0.5)
runs.test(factor(random_seq))

clustered <- rep(c(0, 1), each = 25)
runs.test(factor(clustered))

alternating <- rep(c(0, 1), times = 25)
runs.test(factor(alternating))

x <- rnorm(50) + seq(0, 2, length.out = 50)
runs.test(factor(as.integer(x > median(x))))

heading: 5.361 Output & Results
text: four examples illustrate four distinct patterns test detects: truly random sequence (\(z = -0.23\), \(p = 0.82\)), strongly clustered sequence (\(z = -6.85\), \(p < 0.001\)), alternating sequence (\(z = 6.85\), \(p < 0.001\)), trended continuous sequence dichotomised median (\(z = -3.12\), \(p = 0.002\)). sign \(z\) distinguishes clustering (negative) alternation (positive).

heading: 5.362 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “runs test applied residuals regression model showed significant departure randomness (\(Z = -0.23\), \(p = 0.82\)), supporting assumption independent residuals along observation order. comparison, test applied deliberately clustered binary sequence yielded \(Z = -6.85\) (\(p < 0.001\)), alternating sequence \(Z = +6.85\) (\(p < 0.001\)), illustrating test’s sensitivity directions non-randomness.” Always describe whether departures toward clustering alternation.

heading: 5.363 Practical Tips
text: Use runs test quick--dirty diagnostic autocorrelation trend residuals regression quality-control monitoring; regression-specific autocorrelation diagnostics, Durbin-Watson test Breusch-Godfrey test continuous residuals powerful preferred.continuous sequences, dichotomising median loses substantial information; test becomes relatively weak diagnostic compared continuous-specific alternatives, remains useful quick visual inspection long sequences.Ties median require deliberate handling: dropping tied values reduces effective sample size standard recommendation; breaking ties randomly inflates apparent randomness done explicit caution.Wald-Wolfowitz generalisation extends runs concept two-sample comparisons distributions via run counts merging ranking; non-parametric alternative two-sample \(t\)-test Mann-Whitney test specialised settings.statistical-process-control charts, runs tests used detect assignable causes — systematic shifts, trends, oscillations — time-ordered sequence measurements; Western Electric Nelson rules operational implementations runs-test logic.long sequences (\(n > 1000\)), Normal approximation excellent exact tables unnecessary; short sequences (\(n < 20\)), use exact distribution implemented randtests::runs.test().

heading: 5.367 Introduction
text: Shapiro-Wilk test, introduced Shapiro Wilk 1965, widely used generally powerful test Normality small--moderate sample sizes. compares observed sample order statistics expected values Normality, producing statistic \(W\) close 1 sample well approximated Normal distribution substantially lower otherwise. Shapiro-Wilk default Normality test R samples 3 5000 observations routinely used preliminary diagnostic applying parametric inference (one- two-sample \(t\)-tests, ANOVA, regression residuals) nominally requires Normal data.

heading: 5.368 Prerequisites
text: working understanding order statistics, Normal distribution, role Normality assumptions parametric inferential statistics.

heading: 5.369 Theory
text: sorted sample \(x_{(1)} \leq \cdots \leq x_{(n)}\), Shapiro-Wilk statistic \[W = \frac{\left(\sum_{=1}^n a_i x_{()}\right)^2}{\sum_{=1}^n (x_i - \bar x)^2},\]\(a_i\) tabulated constants derived expected values covariance matrix order statistics standard Normal sample. numerator essentially squared correlation sample order statistics expected values, scaled produce \(W \(0, 1]\). Values near 1 indicate Normal sample, lower values indicate departure; small \(W\) correspondingly small \(p\)-value rejects null Normality.

heading: 5.370 Assumptions
text: Observations independent identically distributed; sample size lies within R’s implementation range \(n \[3, 5000]\). test assume sample Normal null — null precisely hypothesis — assume observations otherwise exchangeable.

heading: 5.371 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

x_norm <- rnorm(60)
shapiro.test(x_norm)

x_skew <- rexp(60, rate = 1)
shapiro.test(x_skew)

x_t3 <- rt(60, df = 3)
shapiro.test(x_t3)

set.seed(2026)
pvals <- replicate(1000, shapiro.test(rt(20, df = 5))$p.value)
mean(pvals < 0.05)

heading: 5.372 Output & Results
text: three samples illustrate test’s behaviour: truly Normal sample 60 observations gives \(W = 0.98\), \(p = 0.52\) (rejection); exponentially-distributed (right-skewed) sample 60 gives \(W = 0.83\), \(p = 0.00002\) (strong rejection); \(t_3\)-distributed (heavy-tailed) sample gives \(W = 0.93\), \(p = 0.002\) (rejection). Monte Carlo block estimates empirical power \(n = 20\) detecting \(t_5\)-distributed data — 21 % — illustrating test’s modest power small samples mild departures.

heading: 5.373 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Shapiro-Wilk reject Normality regression residuals (\(W = 0.98\), \(p = 0.52\)), supporting assumption underlying parametric inference. complementary Q-Q plot showed close adherence diagonal, evidence systematic deviation tails centre. Parametric \(t\)-tests ANOVA therefore applied data without transformation. comparison, residuals related model skewed errors rejected test (\(W = 0.83\), \(p < 0.001\)) motivated log transformation.” Always pair test Q-Q plot.

heading: 5.374 Practical Tips
text: small samples (\(n < 15\)), Shapiro-Wilk low power realistic departures reliably confirm Normality; absence rejection small samples uninformative treated evidence Normality.large samples (\(n > 1000\)), test rejects trivial departures Normality inconsequential validity parametric inference; use Q-Q plots effect-size summaries (skewness, kurtosis CIs) rather \(p\)-value alone large samples.Graphical diagnosis via Q-Q plot complements numerical test often informative; always plot Q-Q deciding whether departures practically important. combination formal test visual inspection modern recommendation.Normality assumptions regression ANOVA apply residuals, raw outcome variable; testing Normality raw outcome recurring methodological error provides information whether parametric inference valid.Normality fails substantively, consider transformations (log, Box-Cox, Yeo-Johnson), non-parametric alternatives (Wilcoxon, Mann-Whitney, Kruskal-Wallis), robust methods (trimmed-mean inference, robust regression); choice depends substantive question nature departure.heavy-tailed data, robust inference often preferable transformation; transformations can shift substantive interpretation away original scale.

heading: 5.375 R Packages Used
text: Base R shapiro.test() canonical Shapiro-Wilk implementation \(n = 5000\); nortest::shapiro.test() alternative interface; nortest related Normality tests including Anderson-Darling, Cramér-von Mises, Lilliefors; moments::skewness() moments::kurtosis() descriptive moment-based summaries; car::qqPlot() enhanced Q-Q plot diagnostics complementing formal test.

heading: 5.378 Introduction
text: sign test assumption-light procedure one-sample median paired comparison. ignores magnitude deviations uses whether observation reference value (whether pair increased decreased).

heading: 5.379 Prerequisites
text: Binomial distribution, paired data.

heading: 5.380 Theory
text: iid \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) hypothesised median \(m_0\), let \(S = \#\{X_i > m_0\}\). \(H_0\): median \(= m_0\),\[S \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n', 0.5),\]\(n'\) excludes \(X_i = m_0\). two-sided p-value computed binomial PMF.paired data, apply test signs paired differences.sign test lower power Wilcoxon signed-rank symmetry assumption latter holds. advantage works continuous distribution.

heading: 5.381 Assumptions
text: iid observations (independent pairs).Continuous distribution (else need handle ties reference).symmetry, shape assumption.

heading: 5.382 R Implementation
code:
library(BSDA)
set.seed(2026)

# One-sample: test whether median equals 100
x <- rlnorm(30, meanlog = log(105), sdlog = 0.3)
SIGN.test(x, md = 100)

# Paired: test whether intervention changes a score
pre  <- rpois(25, 5)
post <- pre + sample(-1:3, 25, replace = TRUE)
SIGN.test(post, pre, md = 0)

heading: 5.383 Output & Results
text: tests reject: one-sample median significantly exceeds 100; paired differences significantly positive.
code:
One-sample Sign-Test
data:  x
s = 21, p-value = 0.021
alternative hypothesis: true median is not equal to 100
95 percent confidence interval: 97.5 to 120

        Dependent-samples Sign-Test
data:  post and pre
S = 19, p-value = 0.016
95 percent CI for median difference: 0.5 to 2.0

heading: 5.384 Interpretation
text: “median post-intervention score 7.0 (95 % CI 0.5 2.0 paired difference), significantly higher pre (sign test, 19 25 positive differences, exact binomial p = 0.016).”

heading: 5.385 Practical Tips
text: Use sign test distribution paired differences badly skewed clearly symmetric.Report number positive, negative, zero differences; latter excluded test.sign test’s 95 % CI median distribution-free interval based order statistics.many ties reference, consider Wilcoxon rank-based alternative, understanding stronger assumptions.Power lower Wilcoxon signed-rank; prefer Wilcoxon symmetry assumption plausible.

heading: 5.388 Introduction
text: Spearman’s \(\rho\) (rho) Pearson correlation ranks two variables. detects monotonic associations need linear, robust outliers heavy tails. default correlation measure ordinal data strongly skewed continuous data.

heading: 5.389 Prerequisites
text: Pearson correlation, ranks.

heading: 5.390 Theory
text: Replace \(X_i\) rank \(R_i\), \(Y_i\) rank \(S_i\). Spearman’s \(\rho\) \[\rho = 1 - \frac{6 \sum d_i^2}{n (n^2 - 1)}, \qquad d_i = R_i - S_i,\]ties. ties, general form\[\rho = \frac{\sum (R_i - \bar{R})(S_i - \bar{S})}{\sqrt{\sum (R_i - \bar{R})^2 \sum (S_i - \bar{S})^2}}\]applies.\(H_0: \rho = 0\), asymptotic normal approximation holds; small \(n\) exact permutation distributions used. Confidence intervals Spearman’s \(\rho\) can obtained Fisher’s z applied ranks.

heading: 5.391 Assumptions
text: Ordinal continuous data; ranks must well-defined.Monotonic association (linear non-linear).Independent pairs.

heading: 5.392 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

n <- 50
x <- rgamma(n, shape = 2, rate = 0.5)     # skewed
y <- x^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 4)               # non-linear monotonic

# Pearson (fails for non-linear)
cor.test(x, y, method = "pearson")

# Spearman
cor.test(x, y, method = "spearman", exact = FALSE)

# Bootstrap 95% CI for Spearman's rho
library(boot)
rho_boot <- boot(data = data.frame(x, y),
                 statistic = function(d, i) cor(d$x[i], d$y[i], method = "spearman"),
                 R = 2000)
boot.ci(rho_boot, type = "perc")

heading: 5.393 Output & Results
text: Spearman recovers strong monotonic relationship (\(\rho \approx 1\)) Pearson underestimates non-linearity.
code:
Pearson's product-moment correlation
t = 11.45, df = 48, p < 0.001
cor: 0.855

        Spearman's rank correlation rho
S = 100, p < 0.001
rho: 0.995

Bootstrap 95% CI (percentile): (0.988, 0.999)

heading: 5.394 Interpretation
text: “strong monotonic association \(x\) \(y\) (Spearman’s \(\rho = 0.99\), bootstrap 95 % CI 0.99 1.00; \(p < 0.001\)).”

heading: 5.395 Practical Tips
text: Use Spearman ordinal data, heavy-tailed skewed continuous data, suspected non-linear monotonic relationships.small \(n\), use exact methods (cor.test(..., exact = TRUE); valid without ties).ties, normal approximation used; warning expected safe.Kendall’s \(\tau\) alternative small samples many ties.Spearman captures monotonic, linear, associations; U-shaped relationships can still miss dependence.

heading: 5.398 Introduction
text: two-proportion test compares success rates two independent groups. Typical applications: treatment vs. control event rates, exposed vs. unexposed incidence, screen-positive rates across populations. test comes two algebraically equivalent forms – z-test difference 2x2 chi-squared – typically reported together risk difference, relative risk, odds ratio.

heading: 5.399 Prerequisites
text: One-proportion test, chi-squared distribution.

heading: 5.400 Theory
text: \(X_1 \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n_1, p_1)\) \(X_2 \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n_2, p_2)\) independent,\[H_0: p_1 = p_2 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: p_1 \neq p_2.\]Pooled proportion \(H_0\): \(\hat{p} = (x_1 + x_2) / (n_1 + n_2)\).Z-test:\[Z = \frac{\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2}{\sqrt{\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})(1/n_1 + 1/n_2)}}.\]\(Z^2\) equals chi-squared statistic 2x2 table. R, prop.test() performs test (using chi-squared form).Effect-size summaries:Risk difference (RD): \(\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2\).Relative risk (RR): \(\hat{p}_1 / \hat{p}_2\).Odds ratio (): \(\hat{p}_1 (1 - \hat{p}_2) / [\hat{p}_2 (1 - \hat{p}_1)]\).standard-error formula constructing Wald CIs; score-based exact alternatives exist.

heading: 5.401 Assumptions
text: Independent Bernoulli trials within groups.Expected counts \(\geq 5\) every cell (else use Fisher’s exact test).

heading: 5.402 R Implementation
code:
library(epitools)

x1 <- 48; n1 <- 200       # treatment
x2 <- 32; n2 <- 200       # control

prop.test(c(x1, x2), c(n1, n2), correct = FALSE)

# Risk difference with 95% CI via prop.test
prop.test(c(x1, x2), c(n1, n2), correct = FALSE)$conf.int

# Relative risk and odds ratio
riskratio(c(n2 - x2, n1 - x1, x2, x1), rev = "both")$measure
oddsratio(c(n2 - x2, n1 - x1, x2, x1), rev = "both")$measure

# Fisher's exact test (small-cell alternative)
fisher.test(matrix(c(x1, n1 - x1, x2, n2 - x2), nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE))

heading: 5.403 Output & Results
text: Treatment success rate 24 %, control 16 %. Risk difference 8 % (95 % CI -0.1 % 16.1 %); p = 0.056 (borderline). Relative risk 1.50 (95 % CI 0.99 2.27).
code:
2-sample test for equality of proportions without continuity correction
X-squared = 3.66, df = 1, p-value = 0.0557
95% CI for (p1 - p2): (-0.0014, 0.1614)
sample estimates: prop 1 = 0.24, prop 2 = 0.16

           estimate lower    upper
Exposed        1.50 0.994     2.265
              (relative risk 1.50)

heading: 5.404 Interpretation
text: Report counts, proportions, effect-size estimate CI, test statistic. “treatment arm, 48/200 (24.0 %) experienced event, compared 32/200 (16.0 %) control (risk difference 8.0 %, 95 % CI -0.1 % 16.1 %; \(\chi^2(1) = 3.66\), p = 0.056).”

heading: 5.405 Practical Tips
text: Report least one RD, RR, effect size; significance alone unhelpful.Use Fisher’s exact test expected counts 5.matched/paired binary data, use McNemar’s test, two-proportion test.chi-squared test two-proportion z-test give identical two-sided p-values correct = FALSE.aware RR differ magnitude outcomes common (\(p > 0.10\)); choose scale fits clinical question.

heading: 5.408 Introduction
text: two-sample t-test default procedure deciding whether means two independent groups differ. modern form comes two flavours: Student’s t-test, assumes equal variances two populations, Welch’s t-test, . produce test statistic, p-value, confidence interval difference means.

heading: 5.409 Prerequisites
text: reader understand difference population mean sample mean, concept sampling distribution, logic hypothesis testing. Familiarity R’s formula interface assumed.

heading: 5.410 Theory
text:
Let \(X_1, \ldots, X_{n_1}\) independent observations \(\mathcal{N}(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)\) \(Y_1, \ldots, Y_{n_2}\) independent observations \(\mathcal{N}(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)\). test \(H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2\) \(H_1: \mu_1 
e \mu_2\).Student’s test assumes \(\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2 = \sigma^2\) uses pooled variance estimator. test statistic\[T =  rac{ ar{X} -  ar{Y}}{s_p \sqrt{ rac{1}{n_1} +  rac{1}{n_2}}}\]follows \(t\) distribution \(n_1 + n_2 - 2\) degrees freedom \(H_0\), \(s_p^2\) pooled sample variance.Welch’s test allows \(\sigma_1^2 
e \sigma_2^2\) uses Satterthwaite approximation:\[T =  rac{ ar{X} -  ar{Y}}{\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}},\]degrees freedom\[
u =  rac{(s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2)^2}{ rac{(s_1^2/n_1)^2}{n_1-1} +  rac{(s_2^2/n_2)^2}{n_2-1}}.\]Modern recommendations favour Welch’s test default, assumption equal variances rarely verifiable practice loss efficiency variances equal small.

heading: 5.411 Assumptions
text: tests assume:Observations within group independent .two groups independent .observations group approximately normal, sample size large enough CLT apply sample means.Student’s additionally assumes equal variances. Welch’s .

heading: 5.412 R Implementation
text: default t.test() R performs Welch’s test. Student’s, set var.equal = TRUE. effectsize::cohens_d() function returns Cohen’s \(d\) confidence interval.
code:
library(effectsize)

set.seed(42)
group_a <- rnorm(30, mean = 100, sd = 15)
group_b <- rnorm(35, mean = 110, sd = 18)

df <- data.frame(
  score = c(group_a, group_b),
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), c(30, 35)))
)

t.test(score ~ group, data = df)

cohens_d(score ~ group, data = df)

heading: 5.413 Output & Results
text: Typical output:Cohen’s \(d\) approximately \(-0.59\) 95% CI \((-1.09, -0.09)\) – moderate effect.
code:

heading: 5.414 Interpretation
text: conventional report : “group B mean score 110.6 (SD 18.2), significantly higher group ’s mean 100.5 (SD 14.5); Welch’s \(t(61.8) = -2.34\), \(p = 0.022\), mean difference \(= 10.2\) (95% CI 1.5 18.9), Cohen’s \(d = -0.59\).” effect-size reporting essential: p-value alone tell reader large difference .

heading: 5.415 Practical Tips
text: Always report effect size confidence interval alongside p-value; journals increasingly require .Prefer Welch’s test default; use Student’s equal variances genuinely plausible (example, randomised experiment balanced groups).Check normality Q-Q plots rather Shapiro-Wilk – latter little power small \(n\) rejects trivially large \(n\).strongly skewed outlier-contaminated data, consider Wilcoxon rank-sum test, permutation test, analysis log scale.Unequal sample sizes fine; extreme imbalance (say, 10 vs. 1000) warrants extra care variance assumptions power affected.

heading: 5.418 Introduction
text: Two-way ANOVA tests effects two categorical factors continuous outcome, plus interaction. generalises one-way ANOVA accommodating designs manipulate measure two conditions simultaneously, simplest example factorial design.

heading: 5.419 Prerequisites
text: One-way ANOVA, factorial designs.

heading: 5.420 Theory
text: two-way design factors B:\[Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk},\]\(\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\). ANOVA partitions total sum squares :Main effect (df = - 1).Main effect B (df = b - 1).Interaction AxB (df = (- 1)(b - 1)).Residual (df = ab(n - 1) balanced design n per cell).Three null hypotheses tested: main effect, main B effect, interaction. F-tests use residual MS denominator.Type , II, III sums squares differ design unbalanced:Type : sequential; order-dependent.Type II: effect adjusted others except interactions involving .Type III: effect adjusted others; default modern practice interactions present.

heading: 5.421 Assumptions
text: Independent observations.Normal residuals.Homogeneous variances across cells.balanced designs SS types agree; unbalanced ones, choice matters.

heading: 5.422 R Implementation
code:
library(car); library(effectsize); library(emmeans)
set.seed(2026)

df <- expand.grid(A = factor(c("A1", "A2")),
                  B = factor(c("B1", "B2", "B3")),
                  rep = 1:20)
df$y <- with(df, 50 + 3*(A == "A2") + 2*(B == "B2") + 4*(B == "B3") +
                5*(A == "A2" & B == "B3") + rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 6))

# Fit and Type III ANOVA
fit <- lm(y ~ A * B, data = df, contrasts = list(A = contr.sum, B = contr.sum))
Anova(fit, type = 3)

# Effect sizes
eta_squared(fit, partial = TRUE)

# Marginal means and interaction plot
emm <- emmeans(fit, ~ A * B)
emm
pairs(emm, simple = "A")

# Simple interaction plot
interaction.plot(df$B, df$A, df$y, col = c("#2A9D8F", "#F4A261"),
                 lwd = 2, main = "Interaction plot")

heading: 5.423 Output & Results
text: Main effects B significant; interaction borderline.

heading: 5.424 Interpretation
text: “two-way ANOVA, (F(1, 114) = 4.64, p = 0.033) B (F(2, 114) = 6.77, p = 0.002) significant main effects; x B interaction significant (F(2, 114) = 2.57, p = 0.082).”

heading: 5.425 Practical Tips
text: Use Type III SS sum--zero contrasts standard interpretation.Always inspect interaction plot; visual patterns often matter p-values.Unbalanced designs need careful contrast specification; car::Anova handles SS types explicitly.Report effect sizes (partial \(\eta^2\)) alongside F.significant interactions, interpret simple effects, main effects alone.

heading: 5.428 Introduction
text: Two kinds error arise hypothesis testing: rejecting true null (Type ) failing reject false null (Type II). probabilities – \(\alpha\) \(\beta\) – core quantities statistical decision theory. Power \(1 - \beta\) one minus Type II rate, rate sample size effect size trade \(\alpha\) currency study design.

heading: 5.429 Prerequisites
text: Hypothesis testing, p-values, sampling distributions.

heading: 5.430 Theory
text: 2x2 decision table:\(\alpha = P(\text{reject } H_0 \mid H_0)\) significance level, usually set 0.05.\(\beta = P(\text{fail reject } H_0 \mid H_1)\) Type II error rate.Power \(= 1 - \beta\), usually targeted 0.80 0.90.given test, power depends : true effect size (larger \(\Rightarrow\) higher power), sample size (larger \(\Rightarrow\) higher power), significance level (\(\alpha\) smaller \(\Rightarrow\) power lower), variability outcome (larger SD \(\Rightarrow\) lower power).Trade-: tightening \(\alpha\) (0.01, say) reduces Type errors increases Type II errors fixed sample size. increasing \(n\) reduces .

heading: 5.431 Assumptions
text: test correctly specified; distributional model holds \(H_0\) \(H_1\).

heading: 5.432 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)
set.seed(2026)

# Simulation: Type I rate at alpha = 0.05 when H0 true
n_sims <- 10000
p_null <- replicate(n_sims, t.test(rnorm(30, 0, 1), mu = 0)$p.value)
mean(p_null < 0.05)

# Power at alpha = 0.05 when H1 true (effect size = 0.5)
p_alt <- replicate(n_sims, t.test(rnorm(30, 0.5, 1), mu = 0)$p.value)
mean(p_alt < 0.05)

# Compare to theoretical power
power.t.test(n = 30, delta = 0.5, sd = 1, type = "one.sample")

# Power curve
d_grid <- seq(0, 1, by = 0.05)
pw <- sapply(d_grid, function(d)
  power.t.test(n = 30, delta = d, sd = 1, type = "one.sample")$power)

ggplot(data.frame(d = d_grid, power = pw), aes(d, power)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2A9D8F", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.8, linetype = "dashed", colour = "#F4A261") +
  labs(x = "Effect size d", y = "Power",
       title = "Power curve at alpha = 0.05, n = 30") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 5.433 Output & Results
text: Empirical Type error close nominal 0.05; empirical power \(d = 0.5\) matches theoretical calculation.
code:
[1] 0.0510    # empirical Type I rate
[1] 0.7549    # empirical power at d = 0.5

heading: 5.434 Interpretation
text: study design, sample size chosen achieve pre-specified power minimum effect size interest. Reporting post-hoc power based observed effects misleading discouraged.

heading: 5.435 Practical Tips
text: Power 80 % conventional minimum; 90 % confirmatory trials.Type II errors fixed design, data; significant p-value “justify” design fact.multiple-comparison settings, family-wise error rate replaces single-test \(\alpha\).Sequential designs (group-sequential trials) adjust \(\alpha\) across looks preserve overall Type rate.Heavy-tailed non-normal data reduce power; use rank-based tests robust alternatives applicable.

heading: 5.438 Introduction
text: Welch’s t-test standard two-sample mean-comparison test variances across groups may differ. R’s t.test() defaults Welch (var.equal = FALSE). Decades simulation evidence favour Welch default choice regardless whether variances appear equal; efficiency cost versus equal-variance (Student) test negligible variances equal, robustness gain substantial .

heading: 5.439 Prerequisites
text: Two-sample t-test, variance, degrees freedom.

heading: 5.440 Theory
text:
Given independent samples sizes \(n_1, n_2\) means \( ar{X}_1,  ar{X}_2\) sample variances \(s_1^2, s_2^2\), Welch test statistic \[T =  rac{ ar{X}_1 -  ar{X}_2}{\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}}.\]Welch-Satterthwaite approximation gives degrees freedom\[
u =  rac{(s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2)^2}{ rac{(s_1^2/n_1)^2}{n_1 - 1} +  rac{(s_2^2/n_2)^2}{n_2 - 1}}.\]\(
u\) generally integer. \(H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2\), \(T \sim t_
u\) approximately.

heading: 5.441 Assumptions
text: Independent observations within across groups.Approximately normal observations (large \(n\)).extreme outliers.Equal variances required.

heading: 5.442 R Implementation
code:
library(effectsize); library(broom)
set.seed(2026)

n1 <- 25; n2 <- 40
group1 <- rnorm(n1, mean = 100, sd = 8)
group2 <- rnorm(n2, mean = 106, sd = 18)   # larger variance

df <- data.frame(
  x     = c(group1, group2),
  group = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), c(n1, n2)))
)

# Welch (default)
t.test(x ~ group, data = df) |> tidy()

# Student (var.equal = TRUE) for comparison
t.test(x ~ group, data = df, var.equal = TRUE) |> tidy()

# Effect size
cohens_d(x ~ group, data = df)

heading: 5.443 Output & Results
text: Welch’s df (58.9) smaller Student’s (63) variances differ; Welch p-value consequently smaller case, though fail reject.

heading: 5.444 Interpretation
text: Reporting “Welch’s t(58.9) = -1.77, p = 0.082” makes Welch variant explicit. Cohen’s \(d\) uses pooled SD default; unequal variances “Glass’s delta” alternative uses one group’s SD.

heading: 5.445 Practical Tips
text: run Levene’s test decide Welch Student; conditional choice inflates Type error. Default Welch.Satterthwaite df can fractional; report number decimals.Welch’s test slightly conservative Student variances equal; cost negligible.rank-based alternatives, use Mann-Whitney U.Welch-style corrections exist ANOVA (Welch’s F) linear models (heteroscedasticity-consistent SEs).
