heading: 5.448 Introduction
text: Wilcoxon signed-rank test compares two paired measurements without requiring Normality differences. rank-based non-parametric analogue paired \(t\)-test appropriate choice paired differences ordinal, difference distribution skewed heavy-tailed, sample sizes small parametric test’s distributional assumptions defensible. Wilcoxon signed-rank test widely used pre-post studies, within-subject /B comparisons, sensory evaluation panels, setting paired measurements compared Normality doubt.

heading: 5.449 Prerequisites
text: working understanding paired-sample inference, rank-based testing framework, assumption symmetric distribution paired differences (rather Normal specific shape).

heading: 5.450 Theory
text: paired observations \((X_i, Y_i)\), compute differences \(D_i = X_i - Y_i\), drop zero differences, rank absolute values \(|D_i|\), sum ranks assigned positive differences (\(W_+\)) negative differences (\(W_-\)). null hypothesis median \(D\) zero (assuming distribution \(D\) symmetric), \(W_+\) \(W_-\) known discrete distribution; large \(n\), standardised statistic approximately Normal. rank-biserial correlation \(r_{rb} = (W_+ - W_-)/T\), \(T\) sum ranks, provides effect-size measure \([-1, 1]\).symmetry assumption fails substantively, sign test offers assumption-light alternative cost reduced power.

heading: 5.451 Assumptions
text: observations genuinely paired, distribution paired differences approximately symmetric around median, outcomes least ordinal. test assume Normality differences — symmetry — principal advantage paired \(t\)-test.

heading: 5.452 R Implementation
code:
library(effectsize)
set.seed(2026)

n <- 25
pre  <- sample(1:7, n, replace = TRUE)
post <- pre + sample(-1:2, n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.3))

wilcox.test(pre, post, paired = TRUE)
rank_biserial(pre, post, paired = TRUE)

c(median_diff = median(post - pre),
  IQR_diff    = IQR(post - pre))

heading: 5.453 Output & Results
text: pre-post comparison 7-point ordinal scale gives Wilcoxon signed-rank statistic \(V = 35\) (\(p = 0.003\)), paired rank-biserial correlation \(-0.72\) (95 % CI \(-0.90\) \(-0.40\)) classed large effect. median paired difference \(+1.0\) (IQR 0 1) characterises central tendency change original scale, reporting test statistic effect-size summary standard expected modern guidelines.

heading: 5.454 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Scores increased significantly pre-treatment post-treatment (Wilcoxon signed-rank \(V = 35\), \(p = 0.003\)); median paired difference \(+1.0\) point 7-point scale (IQR 0 1, range \(-1\) \(+3\)), paired rank-biserial correlation \(-0.72\) (95 % CI \(-0.90\) \(-0.40\)), classed large effect. non-parametric test chosen differences ordinal parametric paired \(t\)-test appropriate.” Always report median, IQR, effect-size measure.

heading: 5.455 Practical Tips
text: Report median IQR paired differences rather raw pre/post means; median location parameter Wilcoxon signed-rank test inferentially targets, means directly relevant rank-based test.Handle zero differences R default (drop analysis), use Pratt convention (include ranking assign sign zero); choice can matter small samples documented.small \(n\) ties absolute differences, use exact = TRUE obtain exact \(p\)-values rather Normal approximation; exact distribution accurate inference small samples.symmetry--differences assumption can checked informally histogram \(D_i\) Q-Q plot \(D_i\) Normal; differences markedly skewed, sign test assumption-light alternative.paired binary outcomes (yes/observed twice subject), McNemar’s test — Wilcoxon signed-rank — appropriate paired-sample test; conflating two recurring methodological error.Wilcoxon signed-rank test sensitive differences median location symmetry assumption; general distributional comparisons paired data, paired Cliff’s delta one-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test differences general alternatives.

heading: 5.456 R Packages Used
text: Base R wilcox.test(paired = TRUE) canonical signed-rank test; effectsize::rank_biserial() paired rank-biserial correlation effect size; coin::wilcoxsign_test() permutation-based exact \(p\)-values; rcompanion::wilcoxonPairedR() alternative effect-size implementation; BSDA::SIGN.test() assumption-light sign-test alternative.

heading: 5.466.1 Bootstrap CI for the median body mass of Gentoo
code:
B <- 2000
boot_meds <- replicate(B, median(sample(gen, replace = TRUE)))
ci_med <- quantile(boot_meds, c(0.025, 0.975))
obs_med <- median(gen)
tibble(statistic = "median body mass",
       observed = obs_med,
       ci_low = ci_med[1],
       ci_high = ci_med[2])
tibble(boot = boot_meds) |>
  ggplot(aes(boot)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "grey60", colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = obs_med, colour = "firebrick") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ci_med, linetype = 2) +
  labs(x = "Bootstrap median", y = "Count")

heading: 5.466.2 Permutation test for flipper length Gentoo vs Adelie
code:
x <- dat$flipper_length_mm
observed_diff <- mean(x[g == "Gentoo"]) - mean(x[g == "Adelie"])

perm_diff <- replicate(5000, {
  gp <- sample(g)
  mean(x[gp == "Gentoo"]) - mean(x[gp == "Adelie"])
})
p_perm <- mean(abs(perm_diff) >= abs(observed_diff))
p_perm
tibble(perm = perm_diff) |>
  ggplot(aes(perm)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "grey60", colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = observed_diff, colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(x = "Permuted mean difference", y = "Count")

heading: 5.487 Background
text:
Maximum likelihood workhorse parametric inference. Given 
parametric family data — normal, Poisson, binomial — MLE
parameter value makes observed data probable.
mild regularity conditions MLE consistent (converges
truth), asymptotically unbiased, asymptotically
normal variance given inverse Fisher information.Fisher information quantifies sharply log-likelihood peaks 
maximum. sharply peaked likelihood gives precise estimate
(small variance); flat one gives vague estimate. standard
error MLE square root 1/n times inverse 
Fisher information per observation.simple cases treat MLE coincides 
intuitive estimator — mean normal, sample rate 
Poisson — machinery generalises likelihood.

heading: 5.488 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 5.489 1. Hypothesis
text:
Estimate () mean normal (ii) rate Poisson 
maximum likelihood, recover standard errors Fisher information,

heading: 5.490 2. Visualise
text: Normal data known σ = 2.likelihood peaked sample mean — MLE.
code:
mu_true <- 5
sigma <- 2
x <- rnorm(n, mean = mu_true, sd = sigma)
grid <- tibble(mu = seq(3.5, 6.5, length.out = 300)) |>
  mutate(loglik = sapply(mu, function(m) sum(dnorm(x, m, sigma, log = TRUE))))
ggplot(grid, aes(mu, loglik)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(x), linetype = 2, colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(x = expression(mu), y = "log-likelihood")

heading: 5.491 3. Assumptions
text:
Normal model: iid, constant σ. Poisson model: iid counts, constant
rate fixed exposure. Fisher information evaluated 
MLE Wald standard error.

heading: 5.492.1 Normal mean by MLE
code:
# Fisher information for mu in normal with known sigma is n / sigma^2.
I_mu <- n / sigma^2
se_mu <- 1 / sqrt(I_mu)
ci_mu <- mle_mu + c(-1, 1) * qnorm(0.975) * se_mu
tibble(mle = mle_mu, se = se_mu,
       ci_low = ci_mu[1], ci_high = ci_mu[2])

heading: 5.492.2 Normal mean by numeric optimisation (sanity check)
code:
opt <- optim(0, nll, method = "Brent", lower = 0, upper = 10, hessian = TRUE)
opt$par        # MLE
sqrt(1 / opt$hessian[1, 1])  # SE from observed information

heading: 5.492.3 Poisson rate by MLE
code:
mle_lambda <- mean(y)
# Fisher information for lambda: n / lambda. At MLE use 1/mle_lambda per obs.
I_lambda <- length(y) / mle_lambda
se_lambda <- 1 / sqrt(I_lambda)
ci_lambda <- mle_lambda + c(-1, 1) * qnorm(0.975) * se_lambda
tibble(mle = mle_lambda, se = se_lambda,
       ci_low = ci_lambda[1], ci_high = ci_lambda[2])

heading: 5.492.4 Likelihood surface for a two-parameter model (μ, σ)
code:
mu = seq(3.5, 6.5, length.out = 80),
  s  = seq(1, 3.5, length.out = 80)
) |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(ll = sum(dnorm(x, mu, s, log = TRUE))) |>
  ungroup()
ggplot(surf, aes(mu, s, fill = ll)) +
  geom_tile() +
  geom_contour(aes(z = ll), colour = "white") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c() +
  labs(x = expression(mu), y = expression(sigma), fill = "log-L")

round(mle_mu, 3) (Fisher-information SE = round(se_mu, 3);
95% Wald CI round(ci_mu[1], 2) round(ci_mu[2], 2)),
recovering true μ = 5. MLE Poisson rate n =
100 observations round(mle_lambda, 3) (SE
round(se_lambda, 3); CI round(ci_lambda[1], 2)

heading: 5.500 Background
text:
Non-parametric tests make weaker distributional assumptions 
parametric tests dominate preceding labs. tend test
exchangeability distributions rather equality means, 
operate ranks data rather raw values. 
data heavily skewed, ordinal, small-sample, 
non-parametric alternatives often defensible default.workhorse set: Wilcoxon signed-rank paired continuous data;
Mann-Whitney U (wilcox.test paired = FALSE) two
independent groups; Kruskal-Wallis three groups; sign
test cruder paired-data option based signs 
differences alone.common mistake read tests “comparing medians”. 
compare distributions. additional assumptions — notably 
two groups’ distributions differ location shift — 
Mann-Whitney U test estimate location shift, 
stronger assumption test requires.

heading: 5.501 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 5.502 1. Hypothesis
text:
Three scenarios:. Paired: pre/post 25 patients, skewed outcome. H0: shift.
B. Independent two-sample: outcome two arms, skewed. H0: 
distribution.
C. Three-group comparison: outcome across three centres. H0: 
distribution across three.

heading: 5.503 2. Visualise
code:
n_a <- 25
pre <- exp(rnorm(n_a, 3, 0.3))
post <- pre * exp(rnorm(n_a, -0.2, 0.3))
df_a <- tibble(id = seq_len(n_a), pre, post,
               delta = post - pre)

# Scenario B: independent two groups, lognormal
n_b <- 30
grp <- rep(c("A", "B"), each = n_b)
y_b <- c(exp(rnorm(n_b, 3, 0.5)),
         exp(rnorm(n_b, 3.4, 0.5)))
df_b <- tibble(grp, y = y_b)

# Scenario C: three groups
n_c <- 25
centre <- rep(c("X", "Y", "Z"), each = n_c)
y_c <- c(exp(rnorm(n_c, 3.0, 0.4)),
         exp(rnorm(n_c, 3.2, 0.4)),
         exp(rnorm(n_c, 3.5, 0.4)))
df_c <- tibble(centre, y = y_c)
df_b |>
  ggplot(aes(grp, y, fill = grp)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, colour = "grey30") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Outcome") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

heading: 5.504 3. Assumptions
text:
Wilcoxon signed-rank: paired observations; differences symmetric
around null. Mann-Whitney U: independent observations within 
groups; want location-shift interpretation, two
distributions shape. Kruskal-Wallis: independent
observations; -shape assumption extends k groups.

heading: 5.505 4. Conduct
text: Follow-pairwise comparisons Holm correction C:
code:
wt_a <- wilcox.test(df_a$post, df_a$pre, paired = TRUE,
                    conf.int = TRUE)
# Sign test: a simple binomial test on positive differences
signs <- sum(df_a$delta > 0)
sign_test <- binom.test(signs, n_a, p = 0.5)

# B: Mann-Whitney U
wt_b <- wilcox.test(y ~ grp, data = df_b, conf.int = TRUE)

# C: Kruskal-Wallis
kw_c <- kruskal.test(y ~ centre, data = df_c)

list(wilcox_paired = wt_a,
     sign_test = sign_test,
     mann_whitney = wt_b,
     kruskal_wallis = kw_c)

heading: 5.506 5. Concluding statement
text:
Paired comparison (). 25 patients, median change =
round(median(df_a$delta), 2); Wilcoxon signed-rank p =
signif(wt_a$p.value, 2), 95% CI Hodges-Lehmann estimate
round(wt_a$conf.int[1], 2) 
round(wt_a$conf.int[2], 2). Sign test p =
signif(sign_test$p.value, 2).Two-group (B). Mann-Whitney p =
signif(wt_b$p.value, 2), 95% CI
round(wt_b$conf.int[1], 2) 
round(wt_b$conf.int[2], 2).Three-group (C). Kruskal-Wallis χ² =
round(kw_c$statistic, 2), df = kw_c$parameter,
p = signif(kw_c$p.value, 2). Pairwise Wilcoxon tests 
Holm correction indicated signal lay X vs Z contrast.Non-parametric tests free lunches. trade modest amount
power (5% vs t-test t-test’s assumptions
hold) robustness distributional misspecification.

heading: 5.526 Background
text:
Pearson’s r measures linear association. standardised
covariance two variables; sensitive outliers assumes
roughly linear, bivariate-normal relationship. Spearman’s ρ
measures monotonic association. Pearson’s r applied 
ranks variables; robust outliers non-linear
monotonic transformations.Neither coefficient captures non-linear, non-monotonic relationships
well. Anscombe’s quartet DatasauRus dozen canonical
demonstrations coefficient without plot can lie — datasets
engineered identical Pearson r radically different
shapes. conclusion abandon correlations never 
report one without first plotting data.correlation coefficient causal quantity. Two variables can
perfectly correlated without one causing — lurking
variable, common cause, coincidence sample selection. word
“correlation” often used loosely press mean “weak
causation”; course means exactly cor.test() returns.

heading: 5.527 Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(datasauRus)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 5.528 1. Hypothesis

heading: 5.529 2. Visualise
code:
x <- rnorm(n)
y_linear   <- 0.6 * x + rnorm(n, 0, 0.8)
y_monotone <- exp(x) + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)
df <- tibble(x, y_linear, y_monotone) |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("y_"), names_to = "type", values_to = "y")
df |>
  ggplot(aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ type, scales = "free") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.5)

heading: 5.530 3. Assumptions
text:
Pearson assumes linearity roughly bivariate-normal data.
Spearman assumes monotonicity. Neither handles non-monotonic
relationships. , observations must independent; 
sensitive extreme sample-size-induced significance n
huge.

heading: 5.531 4. Conduct
text:
monotonic non-linear relationship gives much higher Spearman
Pearson — rank correlation sees perfect monotonicity.
code:
p1_spearman <- cor.test(x, y_linear,   method = "spearman", exact = FALSE)
p2_pearson  <- cor.test(x, y_monotone, method = "pearson")
p2_spearman <- cor.test(x, y_monotone, method = "spearman", exact = FALSE)

tibble(
  relationship = c("linear", "linear", "monotone", "monotone"),
  method = c("Pearson", "Spearman", "Pearson", "Spearman"),
  r_or_rho = c(p1_pearson$estimate, p1_spearman$estimate,
               p2_pearson$estimate, p2_spearman$estimate),
  p = c(p1_pearson$p.value, p1_spearman$p.value,
        p2_pearson$p.value, p2_spearman$p.value)
)

heading: 5.540 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 5.541 1. Hypothesis
text:
Claim: sampling distribution sample mean 
exponential (strongly right-skewed) population approaches normal
distribution n increases, shrinking standard error.

heading: 5.542 2. Visualise
text:
Draw exponential population show distribution 
individual observations.
code:
pop_mean <- 1 / rate
pop_sd   <- 1 / rate

pop_sample <- tibble(x = rexp(2000, rate = rate))
pop_sample |>
  ggplot(aes(x)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "grey60", colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = pop_mean, linetype = 2) +
  labs(x = "x", y = "Count")

heading: 5.543 3. Assumptions
text:
CLT assumes iid sampling finite variance. Exponential qualifies.
speed convergence set skewed parent — 
skewed, larger n needed normality.
code:
# population mean, across sample sizes.
summarise_means <- function(n, B = 2000) {
  xbars <- replicate(B, mean(rexp(n, rate = rate)))
  tibble(n = n,
         bias = mean(xbars) - pop_mean,
         variance = var(xbars),
         mse = mean((xbars - pop_mean)^2),
         se = sd(xbars))
}
bv <- map_dfr(c(5, 10, 30, 100, 500), summarise_means)
bv

heading: 5.544 4. Conduct
text:
Plot sampling distribution mean across growing n.histograms widen (spread small n) strongly
right-skewed n = 2. n = 30 close symmetric.
code:
replicate(B, mean(rexp(n, rate = rate)))
}

means_df <- bind_rows(
  tibble(n = "n = 2",   mean = sim_means(2)),
  tibble(n = "n = 5",   mean = sim_means(5)),
  tibble(n = "n = 30",  mean = sim_means(30)),
  tibble(n = "n = 100", mean = sim_means(100))
) |>
  mutate(n = factor(n, levels = c("n = 2", "n = 5", "n = 30", "n = 100")))
means_df |>
  ggplot(aes(mean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "grey60", colour = "white") +
  facet_wrap(~ n, scales = "free") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = pop_mean, linetype = 2) +
  labs(x = "Sample mean", y = "Count")
bv |> mutate(expected_se = pop_sd / sqrt(n),
             ratio = se / expected_se)

heading: 5.545 5. Concluding statement
text:
Sampling exponential population mean 1 SD 1,

library(tidyverse)

text: Two--two: treatment vs control, outcome event vs none.H0: event rate independent treatment.H1: rates differ.α = 0.05.

heading: 5.555 2. Visualise
text: Simulate: 200 per arm, event rate 0.25 control, 0.15 treatment.
code:
sim <- tibble(
  arm = rep(c("control", "treatment"), each = n_per),
  event = c(rbinom(n_per, 1, 0.25),
            rbinom(n_per, 1, 0.15))
)
tab <- table(arm = sim$arm, event = sim$event)
tab
as_tibble(tab) |>
  mutate(arm = factor(arm, levels = c("control", "treatment")),
         event = recode(event, `0` = "no event", `1` = "event")) |>
  ggplot(aes(arm, n, fill = event)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Proportion", fill = NULL)

heading: 5.556 3. Assumptions
text:
Chi-square requires expected cell counts ≥ 5. Fisher 
constraint exact size. Random assignment 
representative sampling assumed. Independence observations 
essential.expected counts large; chi-square appropriate.

heading: 5.557 4. Conduct
code:
ft <- fisher.test(tab)
pt <- prop.test(x = tab[, "1"], n = rowSums(tab), correct = FALSE)
list(chisq = ct, fisher = ft, prop = pt)

heading: 5.557.1 Effect sizes: RD, RR, OR
code:
p_trt  <- tab["treatment", "1"] / sum(tab["treatment", ])

rd <- p_trt - p_ctrl

rr <- p_trt / p_ctrl
# Log-based Wald CI for RR
se_log_rr <- sqrt(
  (1 - p_trt)  / (p_trt  * sum(tab["treatment", ])) +
  (1 - p_ctrl) / (p_ctrl * sum(tab["control",   ]))
)
ci_rr <- exp(log(rr) + c(-1, 1) * qnorm(0.975) * se_log_rr)

or <- (tab["treatment", "1"] * tab["control", "0"]) /
      (tab["treatment", "0"] * tab["control", "1"])
se_log_or <- sqrt(sum(1 / tab))
ci_or <- exp(log(or) + c(-1, 1) * qnorm(0.975) * se_log_or)

tibble(effect = c("RD", "RR", "OR"),
       est = c(rd, rr, or),
       ci_low = c(pt$conf.int[1], ci_rr[1], ci_or[1]),
       ci_high = c(pt$conf.int[2], ci_rr[2], ci_or[2]))

heading: 5.557.2 Goodness-of-fit example
text:
Test whether 600 simulated genotype counts match Hardy-Weinberg
proportions allele frequency 0.3.
code:
pAA <- 0.7^2; paa <- 0.3^2; pAa <- 2 * 0.7 * 0.3
exp_probs <- c(AA = pAA, Aa = pAa, aa = paa)
gof <- chisq.test(obs, p = exp_probs)
gof

trt <- rnorm(n, mean = 135, sd = 12)

post <- pre - rnorm(n2, 5, 7)  # true mean drop of 5
df_pair <- tibble(id = seq_len(n2), pre = pre, post = post) |>
  mutate(delta = post - pre)
df_pair |>
  pivot_longer(c(pre, post), names_to = "time", values_to = "sbp") |>
  mutate(time = factor(time, levels = c("pre", "post"))) |>

  geom_line(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +

heading: 6.3 Introduction
text: Cohen’s \(d\) ratio mean difference standard deviation. standardises effects cross-study comparison power analysis. Although simple idea, several variants differ SD used denominator.

heading: 6.4 Prerequisites
text: Means standard deviations, two-sample t-test.

heading: 6.5 Theory
text: Classical definitions:Cohen’s \(d\) (two independent groups): \(d = (\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) / s_{\text{pooled}}\), \(s_{\text{pooled}}^2 = [(n_1 - 1)s_1^2 + (n_2 - 1)s_2^2]/(n_1 + n_2 - 2)\).Hedges’ \(g\): Cohen’s \(d\) multiplied small-sample bias correction factor.Glass’s \(\Delta\): \(d\) using control group’s SD (unequal variances).Cohen’s \(d_z\) (paired): mean paired difference / SD differences.Cohen’s \(d_{\text{rm}}\) (repeated measures): uses specific variance mean difference.Cohen’s thresholds: 0.20 / 0.50 / 0.80 small / medium / large. starting points; clinical context override conventions.

heading: 6.6 Assumptions
text: Approximately normal data (SD meaningful).Equal variances classical Cohen’s \(d\); use Glass Welch-style unequal.

heading: 6.7 R Implementation
code:
library(effectsize)
set.seed(2026)

g1 <- rnorm(50, 50, 10)
g2 <- rnorm(50, 55, 10)

cohens_d(g1, g2)
hedges_g(g1, g2)
glass_delta(g1, g2)

# Paired
pre  <- rnorm(30, 50, 10)
post <- pre + rnorm(30, -5, 5)
cohens_d(pre, post, paired = TRUE)

# Unequal variances -> Glass' Delta
g1_big <- rnorm(50, 50, 5)
g2_big <- rnorm(50, 55, 15)
cohens_d(g1_big, g2_big)
glass_delta(g1_big, g2_big)

heading: 6.8 Output & Results
text: two-sample example: Cohen’s \(d \approx -0.47\), Hedges’ \(g \approx -0.47\), Glass’s \(\Delta \approx -0.50\).

heading: 6.9 Interpretation
text: “intervention reduced anxiety 5.1 points (SE 2.0, Cohen’s \(d = 0.51\), 95 % CI 0.11 0.90), medium-sized effect.”

heading: 6.10 Practical Tips
text: Use Hedges’ \(g\) meta-analysis; ’s small-sample-corrected version combines across studies fairly.Paired designs use \(d_z\), two-sample \(d\); two interchangeable.Report standardised effect raw effect units matter clinically.Cohen’s thresholds vary across fields; cite field-appropriate reference.Confidence intervals \(d\) (e.g., effectsize) asymmetric; assume symmetric.

heading: 6.13 Introduction
text: Cohen’s \(h\) standard standardised effect size differences two proportions. Unlike raw risk difference (whose interpretation depends 0--1 scale comparison sits) odds ratio (whose multiplicative scale obscures absolute magnitude), Cohen’s \(h\) uses arcsine-square-root variance-stabilising transformation makes effect size approximately scale-free directly suitable input Normal-approximation power calculations. natural building block two-proportion sample-size formulas pwr package equivalent tools, recommended effect-size summary published power-analysis protocols.

heading: 6.14 Prerequisites
text: working understanding binomial proportions, arcsine variance-stabilising transformation, role standardised effect sizes sample-size power calculations.

heading: 6.15 Theory
text: two proportions \(p_1, p_2 \[0, 1]\), Cohen’s \(h\) \[h = 2 \arcsin\sqrt{p_1} - 2 \arcsin\sqrt{p_2}.\]arcsine-square-root transformation stabilises variance sample proportion approximately \(1/n\), independent proportion’s value. result, \(h\) dimensionless, scale-free, directly comparable across baseline rates. Cohen’s conventional thresholds 0.20 (small), 0.50 (medium), 0.80 (large). mapping raw proportion difference \(h\) non-linear: \(|p_1 - p_2|\) corresponds different \(h\) values depending unit interval comparison sits, extreme proportions (near 0 1) yielding larger \(h\) absolute difference mid-range comparisons.

heading: 6.16 Assumptions
text: two proportions arise independent Bernoulli outcomes; standardisation useful moderate sample sizes Normal approximation binomial holds.

heading: 6.17 R Implementation

library(pwr)

ES.h(p1 = 0.10, p2 = 0.20)
ES.h(p1 = 0.50, p2 = 0.60)
ES.h(p1 = 0.80, p2 = 0.90)

pwr.2p.test(h = ES.h(0.10, 0.20), sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)
pwr.2p.test(h = ES.h(0.40, 0.50), sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)

heading: 6.18 Output & Results
text: three example pairs involve 10 percentage-point raw difference, produce different Cohen’s \(h\) values: \(h = -0.28\) 10-vs-20 %, \(h = -0.20\) 50-vs-60 %, \(h = -0.29\) 80-vs-90 %. corresponding required sample sizes per arm 80 % power roughly 200, 388, 186 — illustrating absolute difference expensive detect near middle proportion range cheapest extremes.

heading: 6.19 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Assuming intervention increases response 50 % 60 % (Cohen’s \(h = 0.20\), classed small Cohen’s conventions), 388 participants per group required 80 % power \(\alpha = 0.05\) two-sided. 10-percentage-point increase 10 % 20 % corresponds \(h = 0.28\) (still small) requires 200 per group, 80 % 90 % similarly requires 186 per group. non-linear mapping absolute proportion difference detection cost \(h\) rather raw difference used sample-size planning.” Always translate \(h\) back clinical proportions.

heading: 6.20 Practical Tips
text: Prefer Cohen’s \(h\) raw risk difference sample-size power calculations \(h\) natural input Normal-approximation formulas accounts non-constant variance proportion across 0--1 scale.rare events (near 0) common events (near 1), even small raw differences correspond relatively large \(h\) values detectable fewer subjects mid-range proportions; counterintuitive property consequence variance-stabilising transformation explains screening trials rare conditions can detect small absolute increases efficiently.Cohen’s \(h\) symmetric: \(h(p_1, p_2) = -h(p_2, p_1)\), sign carries direction comparison; always report sign directional interpretation.reporting interpretation, translate \(h\) back raw proportions used inputs; \(h\) 0.28 means little clinical reader without corresponding “10 % 20 %” framing.\(2 \times 2\) contingency-table tests independence (rather two-proportion comparisons), use Cohen’s \(w\) rather \(h\); two related designed different test contexts.comparison single proportion fixed reference (one-sample), Cohen’s \(h\) still applies — \(\mathrm{ES.h}(p_1, p_0)\) — feeds pwr.p.test() rather pwr.2p.test().

heading: 6.24 Introduction
text: Eta-squared (\(\eta^2\)) standard effect-size measure analysis variance: proportion total variance outcome attributable factor. complements omnibus \(F\)-test, reports significance, quantifying magnitude — much variability response explained categorical factor interest. Several variants eta-squared widely used different ANOVA designs: classical \(\eta^2\) one-way designs, partial \(\eta_p^2\) factorial -subjects designs, generalised \(\eta_G^2\) mixed designs combine - within-subjects factors, omega-squared \(\omega^2\) unbiased alternative preferred small samples. Reporting appropriate variant alongside \(F\)-test result now standard psychology, neuroscience, clinical-trial publications.

heading: 6.25 Prerequisites
text: working understanding one-way factorial ANOVA, partitioning sums squares factor error components, distinction -subjects within-subjects factors.

heading: 6.26 Theory
text: Classical eta-squared \[\eta^2 = \frac{\mathrm{SS}_{\text{factor}}}{\mathrm{SS}_{\text{total}}}.\]Partial eta-squared, factorial designs, removes factors’ variance denominator:\[\eta_p^2 = \frac{\mathrm{SS}_{\text{factor}}}{\mathrm{SS}_{\text{factor}} + \mathrm{SS}_{\text{error}}}.\]Generalised eta-squared (Bakeman, 2005), mixed designs combining within- -subjects factors, uses denominator depends design comparable across - within-subjects effects.Omega-squared \(\omega^2\) corrects upward bias \(\eta^2\) small samples:\[\omega^2 = \frac{\mathrm{SS}_{\text{factor}} - \mathrm{df}_{\text{factor}} \cdot \mathrm{MSE}}{\mathrm{SS}_{\text{total}} + \mathrm{MSE}}.\]Cohen’s conventional thresholds \(\eta^2\) 0.01 (small), 0.06 (medium), 0.14 (large).

heading: 6.27 Assumptions
text: underlying ANOVA: independent observations (within stratum), Normal residuals, homogeneous variances. eta-squared family impose additional assumptions beyond ANOVA sums squares computed.

heading: 6.28 R Implementation
code:
library(effectsize)
set.seed(2026)

df <- data.frame(
  A = factor(rep(c("A1", "A2"), each = 30)),
  B = factor(rep(c("B1", "B2", "B3"), 20)),
  y = rnorm(60)
)
df$y <- df$y + 0.5 * (df$A == "A2") + 0.3 * (df$B == "B2") + 0.1 * (df$B == "B3")

fit <- aov(y ~ A * B, data = df)

eta_squared(fit)
eta_squared(fit, partial = TRUE)
eta_squared(fit, generalized = TRUE)
omega_squared(fit)

heading: 6.29 Output & Results
text: effectsize::eta_squared() omega_squared() return point estimates confidence intervals ANOVA term. output reports appropriate variant requested design, \(\eta_p^2\) interpretable variance explained factor relative plus error, holding factors constant.

heading: 6.30 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “factorial ANOVA showed small main effect factor outcome (partial \(\eta_p^2 = 0.06\), 95 % CI 0.01 0.21, \(F_{1, 54} = 3.4\), \(p = 0.07\)); terms small non-significant. Reporting partial \(\eta_p^2\) rather classical \(\eta^2\) appropriate -subjects factorial design effect’s variance share computed relative error stratum. Omega-squared values 0.04 0.00 respectively, slightly conservative expected.” Always specify \(\eta^2\) variant.

text: Report partial \(\eta_p^2\) -subjects factorial designs; classical \(\eta^2\) factorial contexts conflates factor’s variance share factors rarely right summary.Use generalised \(\eta_G^2\) (Bakeman, 2005) mixed designs combine within-subjects -subjects factors; gives effect sizes directly comparable across two types factor, partial \(\eta_p^2\) .Prefer omega-squared \(\omega^2\) small samples (typically \(n < 30\) per cell); \(\eta^2\) upward-biased small samples \(\omega^2\) corrects . Report feasible.Always report confidence interval effect size, just point estimate; CIs \(\eta^2\) non-central-\(F\)-based reported effectsize::eta_squared() directly.Cohen’s thresholds (0.01 / 0.06 / 0.14) come behavioural research may appropriate benchmarks biomedical physical-science contexts larger effects routinely expected; interpret thresholds light substantive expectations rather treating universal.related \(f^2 = \eta^2/(1 - \eta^2)\) effect size used regression power analysis, conversion direct useful designing follow-studies based existing ANOVA results.

heading: 6.35 Introduction
text: sample size study constrained budget, time, availability participants, natural planning question longer “\(n\) achieves target power?” instead “given fixed \(n\), smallest effect can reasonably hope detect?” minimum detectable effect (MDE) reverses usual sample-size calculation, solving effect size power equals target — typically 80 % — given fixed \(n\), \(\alpha\), analysis. Reporting MDE increasingly required grant applications constrained sample sizes, pilot exploratory studies, honest power-analysis reporting study afford enrol enough participants original target.

heading: 6.36 Prerequisites
text: working understanding statistical power, relationship \(\alpha\), effect size, sample size, power, standard test-specific effect-size measures (Cohen’s \(d\), \(r\), \(f^2\), \(w\)).

heading: 6.37 Theory
text: Power, effect size, significance level, sample size linked non-central distribution test statistic, solving one quantities given three standard inversion power-analysis framework. MDE defined effect size planned test achieves specified power (usually 0.80) planned \(\alpha\) available sample size. Plotted curve MDE vs. \(n\), gives clear picture design’s sensitivity across resource scenarios.

heading: 6.38 Assumptions
text: assumptions underlying test apply (Normality \(t\)-tests, bivariate Normality correlation, large expected counts chi-squared, etc.); MDE property planned analysis design, property observed data fact.

heading: 6.39 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

pwr.t.test(n = 40, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80,
           type = "two.sample", d = NULL)

pwr.r.test(n = 100, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)

n_grid <- seq(10, 200, by = 5)
d_grid <- sapply(n_grid, function(n)
  pwr.t.test(n = n, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = "two.sample")$d)

plot(n_grid, d_grid, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "#2A9D8F",
     xlab = "n per group", ylab = "MDE (Cohen's d)",
     main = "Minimum detectable effect at power = 0.80")

heading: 6.40 Output & Results
text: two-sample \(t\)-test \(n = 40\) per group, MDE 80 % power \(d = 0.64\) (medium--large); doubling \(n = 200\) per group reduces MDE \(d = 0.28\) (small--medium). correlation test \(n = 100\), MDE \(r = 0.28\). Plotting MDE-vs-\(n\) curve shows diminishing-returns relationship planners understand negotiating sample-size constraints.

heading: 6.41 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “available sample 40 participants per arm, minimum detectable standardised effect (Cohen’s \(d\)) 80 % power two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\) 0.64 — medium--large effect Cohen’s conventions. Effects smaller magnitude likely remain undetected. smallest clinically meaningful effect primary outcome \(d = 0.40\), planned study underpowered clinical target; report MDE explicitly clarify inferential limits propose larger follow-trial.” Always discuss MDE relative clinically meaningful effect.

heading: 6.46 Introduction
text: Post-hoc power – also called “observed power” – calculation power using observed effect size study just ran. common fields (especially study fails reject) near-universally criticised statisticians.

heading: 6.47 Prerequisites
text: Power analysis, hypothesis testing.

heading: 6.48 Theory
text: ’s problematic:Observed power monotone function p-value: smaller \(p\) always yields higher observed power. conveys information beyond p-value (Hoenig Heisey 2001).non-significant results, observed power bounded 50 % (exactly 50 % \(p = \alpha\)).tell whether original design underpowered clinically important effect; restates surprising data null.Legitimate alternatives:Sensitivity analysis: compute power pre-specified clinically important effect, given \(n\).Confidence intervals observed effect: communicate range effects consistent data.Minimum detectable effect (MDE): smallest effect detectable study’s \(n\) power target, study ran.

heading: 6.49 Assumptions
text: None; issue epistemological.

heading: 6.50 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

# Simulated study that fails to reject
set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(30, 0.2, 1)
t_res <- t.test(x)
c(t = t_res$statistic, p = t_res$p.value)

# Post-hoc power (what many people do)
d_obs <- mean(x) / sd(x)
pwr.t.test(n = 30, d = d_obs, sig.level = 0.05, type = "one.sample")$power

# Better: 95% CI for the mean and MDE
c(CI_lower = t_res$conf.int[1], CI_upper = t_res$conf.int[2])
pwr.t.test(n = 30, power = 0.80, type = "one.sample", sig.level = 0.05)$d

heading: 6.56 Introduction
text: Cohen’s kappa measures agreement beyond chance two raters categorical scale de facto standard inter-rater reliability categorical outcomes. Sample-size planning kappa-based reliability study addresses one two distinct questions: many subjects needed estimate kappa desired precision (target 95 % confidence-interval half-width around expected value), many subjects needed test hypothesis kappa (typically kappa exceeds benchmark value 0 0.6) adequate power. Modern reliability-study protocols increasingly precision-driven rather hypothesis-driven, “kappa greater zero” claim conveys little practical information clinical instrument.

heading: 6.57 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Cohen’s kappa, marginal-prevalence dependence kappa’s variance, distinction precision-based hypothesis-test sample-size questions.

heading: 6.58 Theory
text: expected kappa \(\kappa_1\) marginal prevalences \(\pi_1\) \(\pi_2\) two raters, asymptotic variance sample kappa given Fleiss-Cicchetti-Everitt formula. Precision-based planning targets 95 % CI half-width \(w\):\[n \approx \frac{(1 - \kappa_1^2) \, z_{0.975}^2}{w^2 (1 - p_e)^2},\]\(p_e\) chance-expected agreement. Hypothesis-test planning targets power reject \(H_0: \kappa = \kappa_0\) favour \(H_1: \kappa = \kappa_1\), using Normal-approximation \(z\)-test difference estimated hypothesised values, scaled appropriate standard errors.

heading: 6.59 Assumptions
text: Subjects independent rated two raters; expected kappa marginal prevalences pre-specified pilot data substantive expectations; rating scale categorical (ordinal weighted kappa).

heading: 6.60 R Implementation
code:
library(irr)

N.cohen.kappa(rate1 = 0.5, rate2 = 0.5,
              k1 = 0.70, k0 = 0,
              alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80,
              twosided = TRUE)

kappa_exp <- 0.70; p_e <- 0.5
w <- 0.05
n_prec <- qnorm(0.975)^2 * (1 - kappa_exp^2) / (w^2 * (1 - p_e)^2)
ceiling(n_prec)

heading: 6.62 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “estimate inter-rater Cohen’s kappa 95 % CI half-width 0.05 around anticipated value 0.70 (assumed marginal prevalences 0.5 rater), 83 subjects required. protocol enrols 100 allow 15 % unevaluable ratings. hypothesis-test framing — power reject \(\kappa = 0\) 80 % power — require 40 subjects, precision-based plan preferred substantive question magnitude agreement, whether differs zero.” Always state planning paradigm.

heading: 6.67 Introduction
text: Power analysis turns four quantities – effect size, significance level, power, sample size – single equation: given three, fourth determined. Every responsible study design pins three four advance solve fourth (usually sample size). Skipping power analysis leaves studies - -powered, reputational ethical consequences.

heading: 6.68 Prerequisites
text: Hypothesis testing, Type II errors.

heading: 6.69 Theory
text: four quantities:Effect size (\(d\), \(f\), \(r\), , RR): magnitude true effect alternative. Must pre-specified based prior evidence, clinical relevance, Cohen’s conventions.Significance level \(\alpha\): Type error rate, almost always 0.05 two-sided.Power \(1 - \beta\): probability detecting true effect. Conventionally 0.80; confirmatory trials use 0.90.Sample size \(n\): number observations (per group).Given three, fourth computed test’s non-central distribution \(H_1\).Sensitivity trade-offs:Doubling \(n\) roughly shrinks detectable effect \(\sqrt{2}\).Reducing \(\alpha\) 0.05 0.01 reduces power \(n\).Larger SD reduces power; precise measurement increases .Power one-sided test \(\alpha\) equals power two-sided test \(2\alpha\) (effect right direction).

heading: 6.70 Assumptions
text: test’s sampling distribution \(H_1\) must known (approximable). Power calculations valid assumed effect-size magnitude; plausible range often reported.

heading: 6.71 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

# Solve for n given d, alpha, power
pwr.t.test(d = 0.5, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

# Solve for d given n and power (minimum detectable effect)
pwr.t.test(n = 30, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80,
           type = "two.sample")

# Power curves across d
d_grid <- seq(0.1, 1, by = 0.05)
pw_n30  <- sapply(d_grid, function(d) pwr.t.test(n = 30, d = d)$power)
pw_n100 <- sapply(d_grid, function(d) pwr.t.test(n = 100, d = d)$power)

plot(d_grid, pw_n30, type = "l", col = "#F4A261", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Cohen's d", ylab = "Power",
     main = "Power curves: n = 30 vs n = 100 per group")
lines(d_grid, pw_n100, col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2)
abline(h = 0.80, lty = 2)
legend("bottomright", c("n = 30", "n = 100"),
       col = c("#F4A261", "#2A9D8F"), lwd = 2)

heading: 6.77 Introduction
text: Power analysis one-way ANOVA computes per-group sample size needed detect specified pattern differences among three group means adequate probability. two-sample \(t\)-test handles simple two-group comparison, one-way ANOVA generalises comparison number groups, power calculation must therefore handle multi-group effect-size measure rather single mean difference. Cohen’s \(f\) standard standardised effect size; captures spread true group means relative within-group standard deviation single number maps cleanly non-central \(F\) distribution alternative hypothesis.

heading: 6.78 Prerequisites
text: working understanding one-way ANOVA, omnibus \(F\)-test, non-central \(F\) distribution, Cohen’s \(f\) standardised effect size ANOVA.

heading: 6.79 Theory
text: Cohen’s \(f\) one-way ANOVA \[f = \frac{\sigma_{\text{}}}{\sigma_{\text{within}}},\]SD true group means divided within-group SD. relationship \(\eta^2\) \(f = \sqrt{\eta^2 / (1 - \eta^2)}\). Cohen’s conventional benchmarks 0.10 (small), 0.25 (medium), 0.40 (large). alternative hypothesis, omnibus \(F\)-statistic follows non-central \(F\) \((k-1, N - k)\) degrees freedom non-centrality \(\lambda = N f^2\), \(N\) total sample size across \(k\) groups; power tail probability non-central \(F\) critical value.

heading: 6.80 Assumptions
text: design balanced (equal \(n\) per group; modest imbalance tolerable reduces efficiency), within-group variances approximately equal (homogeneity variance), residuals approximately Normal. Welch-corrected variants accommodate variance heterogeneity cost slightly complex power calculations.

heading: 6.81 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

pwr.anova.test(k = 4, f = 0.25, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)

pwr.anova.test(k = 3, f = 0.10, power = 0.80)
pwr.anova.test(k = 3, f = 0.25, power = 0.80)
pwr.anova.test(k = 3, f = 0.40, power = 0.80)

group_means <- c(50, 55, 58)
pooled_sd   <- 8
grand_mean  <- mean(group_means)
f_est <- sqrt(mean((group_means - grand_mean)^2)) / pooled_sd
f_est

heading: 6.82 Output & Results
text: pwr.anova.test() returns per-group sample size required achieve target power specified \(f\), \(\alpha\), number groups. \(k = 4\) groups medium effect \(f = 0.25\), 45 per group (180 total) achieves 80 % power; computing \(f\) directly substantive expected group means pooled within-group SD ties calculation scientifically defensible assumptions.

heading: 6.83 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “four groups anticipated Cohen’s \(f = 0.25\) (medium effect Cohen’s convention; equivalent expected group means differing approximately 0.5 within-group SDs), \(n = 45\) participants per group (180 total) required 80 % power \(\alpha = 0.05\) using one-way ANOVA. protocol allocates 50 per group allow 10 % attrition, pre-specified pairwise Tukey HSD comparisons follow omnibus significant.” Always describe \(f\) terms underlying group-mean structure.

heading: 6.84 Practical Tips
text: Compute Cohen’s \(f\) substantive expectations group means within-group SD wherever possible, rather relying Cohen’s medium/large benchmarks; tying calculation actual expected pattern far defensible invoking generic effect-size labels.unequal group variances, power approximate standard formulas; use Welch-adjusted simulation (WebPower::wp.kanova() explicit variance specification) heterogeneity substantial.Unbalanced designs lower power balanced designs total \(N\); loss small modest imbalances grows quickly ratios extreme 2:1. Plan balance whenever feasible.many groups single pre-specified contrast primary interest (e.g., linear trend across ordered groups, treatment-vs-control contrast), pre-specified contrast tests higher power omnibus \(F\)-test used preference.Simulation-based power (simr, Superpower, faux) flexible complex designs — unbalanced cells, heterogeneous variances, non-Normal outcomes, mixed-effects structures — increasingly default approach modern protocol development.factorial ANOVA (two -subjects factors), calculation extends naturally effect sizes main effect interaction; Superpower::ANOVA_power() standard tool.

heading: 6.88 Introduction
text: Bland-Altman analysis standard method comparing two quantitative measurement methods — new instrument reference standard, two raters’ continuous scores, two clinical-laboratory assays. central output 95 % limits agreement (LoA), defined mean difference \(\pm 1.96\) SD paired differences, describe range within 95 % differences two methods expected fall. Sample-size planning Bland-Altman study therefore targets precision (CI half-width) LoA estimates rather power reject null. subjects, LoA estimated imprecisely, clinical-acceptance decisions whether methods interchangeable become unreliable.

heading: 6.89 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Bland-Altman analysis framework, calculation limits agreement, standard-error theory sample quantiles Normal-based confidence intervals.

heading: 6.90 Theory
text: standard error limit agreement approximately\[\mathrm{SE}(\mathrm{LoA}) \approx \sqrt{3} \, \sigma_d / \sqrt{n},\]\(\sigma_d\) SD paired differences methods. 95 % CI half-width LoA therefore approximately \(1.96 \sqrt{3} \sigma_d / \sqrt n\). achieve target half-width \(w\):\[n \approx \left(\frac{1.96 \sqrt{3} \sigma_d}{w}\right)^2 \approx \frac{11.5 \, \sigma_d^2}{w^2}.\]factor \(\sqrt 3\) arises LoA linear combination sample mean sample SD; formula accounts components’ uncertainty Normal-distribution theory.

heading: 6.91 Assumptions
text: paired differences approximately Normally distributed, systematic bias varies across measurement range (proportional bias), subjects independent, SD differences reasonably known pilot data. Bland-Altman 1999 extension handles repeated measurements per subject different formula.

heading: 6.92 R Implementation
code:
sigma_d <- 5; w <- 1
n_req <- 11.5 * sigma_d^2 / w^2
ceiling(n_req)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 288
diff <- rnorm(n, mean = 0, sd = sigma_d)
mean_diff <- mean(diff)
sd_diff   <- sd(diff)
LoA_lower <- mean_diff - 1.96 * sd_diff
LoA_upper <- mean_diff + 1.96 * sd_diff
SE_LoA    <- sd_diff * sqrt(3 / n)
half_ci   <- 1.96 * SE_LoA
c(LoA_lower, LoA_upper, half_ci)

heading: 6.93 Output & Results
text: closed-form calculation gives \(n \approx 288\) \(\sigma_d = 5\) target half-width 1 unit LoA. simulation block confirms empirical CI half-width matches formula prediction; reporting analytical calculation Monte Carlo verification good practice underlying distributional assumptions uncertain.

heading: 6.94 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “estimate 95 % limits agreement two methods 95 % CI half-width \(\pm 1\) unit limit (pre-specified clinical-acceptance criterion), \(n = 288\) paired measurements required, assuming within-subject difference SD 5 units pilot data. protocol enrols 320 allow 10 % unevaluable measurements. Sensitivity analyses across \(\sigma_d \[4, 6]\) yield required \(n\) 184 414.” Always report target half-width pilot-derived \(\sigma_d\).

heading: 6.95 Practical Tips
text: Precision LoA dominated SD differences \(\sigma_d\); pilot data estimate \(\sigma_d\) essential, protocols rely guessed \(\sigma_d\) values report sensitivity analyses across plausible range.repeated measurements per subject (multiple paired observations within individual), analysis sample-size formula change; use Bland-Altman 1999 extension corresponding precision formula, accounts within-subject correlation.Non-constant bias across measurement range (proportional bias) invalidates simple LoA reporting; regress differences means detect proportional bias, present, compute LoA conditional measurement value.one-sided clinical-acceptance criteria (e.g., upper LoA must tolerance threshold), adjust calculation target one-sided 95 % CI upper bound; sample-size factor changes correspondingly well-documented precision-based literature.Report target LoA half-width explicitly methods section; vague phrases like “adequate precision” longer acceptable regulatory submissions method-comparison publications, reviewers expect explicit precision targets.ratio-scale outcomes proportional measurement error, log-transform computing LoA; LoA log scale back-transforms multiplicative LoA interpretable.

heading: 6.99 Introduction
text: Power analysis chi-squared tests — goodness--fit hypothesised distribution independence two-way contingency table — uses non-central chi-squared distribution alternative hypothesis. standardised effect size tests Cohen’s \(w\), combines difference hypothesised expected cell proportions single number maps directly non-centrality parameter test statistic. Power calculations essential design survey research, epidemiological category-association studies, market-segmentation analyses, setting central question whether categorical proportions differ reference vary across rows contingency table.

heading: 6.100 Prerequisites
text: working understanding chi-squared goodness--fit contingency-table tests, chi-squared distribution non-central generalisation, Cohen’s \(w\) standardised effect-size measure.

heading: 6.101 Theory
text: Cohen’s \(w\) \[w = \sqrt{\sum_i \frac{(p_{1i} - p_{0i})^2}{p_{0i}}},\]\(p_{0i}\) cell proportions expected null hypothesis \(p_{1i}\) alternative. Equivalently, \(w = \sqrt{\chi^2 / n}\) expected effect. alternative, test statistic follows non-central chi-squared test’s standard degrees freedom non-centrality parameter \(\lambda = n w^2\). Conventional benchmarks \(w\) 0.10 (small), 0.30 (medium), 0.50 (large).

heading: 6.102 Assumptions
text: Observations independent, expected cell counts large enough chi-squared approximation valid (typically \(\geq 5\) per cell, though stricter thresholds apply small tables), cell proportions alternative hypothesis pre-specified.

heading: 6.103 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

pwr.chisq.test(w = 0.3, df = 3, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)

pwr.chisq.test(w = 0.3, df = 4, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)

p0 <- c(0.25, 0.25, 0.25, 0.25)
p1 <- c(0.40, 0.30, 0.20, 0.10)
w  <- sqrt(sum((p1 - p0)^2 / p0))
w
pwr.chisq.test(w = w, df = 3, power = 0.80)

heading: 6.104 Output & Results
text: pwr.chisq.test() returns required sample size achieve target power specified \(w\), \(\alpha\), df. medium effect \(w = 0.3\) 3 df (4-category goodness--fit), \(n = 122\); 4 df (3×3 contingency table), \(n = 133\). Computing \(w\) directly hypothesised cell proportions recommended workflow ties calculation substantive distributional assumptions.

heading: 6.105 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “detect deviation equal proportions across four categories medium magnitude (Cohen’s \(w = 0.30\), equivalent expected proportions \((0.40, 0.30, 0.20, 0.10)\) vs. uniform null) 80 % power \(\alpha = 0.05\), \(n = 122\) observations required. 5 % attrition, protocol enrols 130 participants. Sensitivity analyses across \(w \[0.20, 0.40]\) reported supplement bracket uncertainty true effect size.” Always state \(w\) underlying proportional hypothesis.

heading: 6.106 Practical Tips
text: Convert real-world quantities — relative risks, odds ratios, differences proportions — Cohen’s \(w\) via contingency-table formula rather guessing; using generic “medium” benchmark underlying scientific question implies different magnitude recurring source mis-powered studies.Required sample size grows roughly linearly degrees freedom fixed \(w\); spreading effects across cells (e.g., 5×5 vs 2×2) reduces per-cell signal inflates sample-size requirement.small expected cell counts invalidate chi-squared approximation, plan use Fisher’s exact test estimate power Monte Carlo simulation; asymptotic chi-squared power calculation unreliable small-sample regime exact tests needed.alternative-hypothesis cell proportions uncertain, pre-specify sensitivity range report power across range; increasingly expected reviewers protects -powering assumed proportions turn optimistic.Chi-squared power calculations approximate (non-central chi-squared assumes large samples); simulation-based power safer complex designs, sparse-data scenarios, test statistic deviates standard Pearson form.one-way exact-binomial tests single proportion, use specialised tools like pwr.p.test() binom::power.binom() rather chi-squared approximation; exact methods accurate small samples.

heading: 6.110 Introduction
text: cluster-randomised trials (CRTs), treatment assigned cluster (clinic, school, village) rather individual level. Within-cluster correlation inflates variance, reducing effective sample size requiring individuals individually randomised trial.

heading: 6.111 Prerequisites
text: Intraclass correlation, CRT design.

heading: 6.112 Theory
text: design effect (DE) converts individual-level sample size cluster-RCT sample size:\[\mathrm{DE} = 1 + (\bar{m} - 1) \rho_{ICC},\]\(\bar{m}\) average cluster size \(\rho_{ICC}\) intraclass correlation.Required individual-level \(n\) individually-randomised design, multiplied DE, gives cluster-RCT total. Required clusters = total / \(\bar{m}\).

heading: 6.113 Assumptions
text: Balanced nearly balanced cluster sizes.Pre-specified ICC (pilot literature).Analysis adjusts clustering (mixed models GEE).

heading: 6.114 R Implementation
code:
library(clusterPower)

# Continuous outcome: detect a 5-unit mean difference, sigma = 15
# ICC = 0.02, 30 subjects per cluster
# Individual n for same power:
n_ind <- 2 * 15^2 * (qnorm(0.975) + qnorm(0.80))^2 / 5^2
n_ind

rho <- 0.02
m_bar <- 30
DE <- 1 + (m_bar - 1) * rho
c(design_effect = DE,
  cluster_total_n = n_ind * DE,
  clusters_per_arm = ceiling(n_ind * DE / m_bar / 2))

# Direct calculation via clusterPower
cpa.normal(alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80, nclusters = NA,
           nsubjects = 30, d = 5 / 15,   # d = delta / sigma
           ICC = 0.02)

heading: 6.115 Output & Results
text: Individual \(n \approx 143\) per arm; DE = 1.58 ICC 0.02 cluster size 30, giving 226 per arm (8 clusters 30 per arm).

heading: 6.116 Interpretation
text: “assumed ICC 0.02 30 subjects per cluster, design effect 1.58. detect standardised difference 0.33 (5 units / 15 SD) 80 % power two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\), 8 clusters per arm required (total 480 subjects).”

heading: 6.117 Practical Tips
text: ICC estimates vary widely; use pilot data, literature, sensitivity ranges (0.001 0.1).Unequal cluster sizes reduce power; inflate \(n\) 10-20 % buffer.Large \(m\) increases DE linearly ICC; adding clusters efficient per-cluster recruitment.Stratified matched CRTs (pairs clusters) reduce DE.Analysis model: linear mixed model random cluster effects; GEE exchangeable correlation; cluster-level summary.

heading: 6.120 Introduction
text: Power analysis correlation tests determines sample size required detect specified population correlation \(\rho\) different zero (different non-zero reference) adequate probability. Correlation studies pervasive clinical research, psychometrics, behavioural science, biomarker validation, setting strength association two continuous variables central question. standard analytical approach uses Fisher’s \(z\)-transformation, converts sampling distribution Pearson correlation approximately Normal distribution simple variance formula, supporting clean closed-form sample-size calculations.

heading: 6.121 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Pearson Spearman correlation, Fisher \(z\)-transformation, relationship effect size required sample size inferential statistics.

heading: 6.122 Theory
text: Fisher’s \(z\)-transformation \[z = \tfrac{1}{2} \log \frac{1 + r}{1 - r} = \mathrm{atanh}(r),\]\(\mathrm{SE}(z) \approx 1/\sqrt{n - 3}\). required sample size detect \(\rho\) different 0 two-sided \(\alpha\) power \(1 - \beta\) \[n \approx \left(\frac{z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}}{\mathrm{atanh}(\rho)}\right)^2 + 3.\]Cohen’s effect-size benchmarks \(r\) 0.10 (small), 0.30 (medium), 0.50 (large). Spearman’s rank correlation slightly less efficient inference bivariate Normality (asymptotic relative efficiency \(9/\pi^2 \approx 0.91\)), 9 % inflation \(n\) converts Pearson sample-size calculation Spearman equivalent.

heading: 6.123 Assumptions
text: data bivariate Normal Pearson test, pairs independent identically distributed, population correlation genuinely well-defined. Spearman correlation tolerates monotone non-linearity power calculation approximate.

heading: 6.124 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

pwr.r.test(r = 0.30, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)

pwr.r.test(r = 0.10, power = 0.80)

r_grid <- seq(0.1, 0.6, by = 0.05)
pw <- sapply(r_grid, function(r) pwr.r.test(r = r, n = 50)$power)
data.frame(r = r_grid, power_n50 = round(pw, 2))

heading: 6.125 Output & Results
text: pwr.r.test() returns required \(n\) specified \(r\), \(\alpha\), power. \(r = 0.30\) 80 % power two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\), \(n = 85\); \(r = 0.10\), \(n = 782\) — illustrating dramatically small correlations inflate required sample size. power--correlation table fixed \(n\) shows achievable power across range plausible correlations useful supplement single-point calculation.

heading: 6.126 Interpretation

Sensitivity analyses across \(\rho \[0.20, 0.40]\) show required \(n\) ranging 47 191.” Always report sensitivity plausible \(\rho\) range.

heading: 6.127 Practical Tips
text: Testing \(\rho\) non-zero reference value requires modified Fisher-\(z\) formula accounts difference \(\mathrm{atanh}(\rho_0)\) \(\mathrm{atanh}(\rho_1)\); use pwrss::pwrss.z.corr() compute manually rather relying standard pwr.r.test() assumes \(\rho_0 = 0\).Spearman correlations, inflate Pearson-based \(n\) approximately 9 % reflect slight efficiency loss rank-based test bivariate Normality; non-Normal underlying data rank-based test can actually efficient.Correlations near \(\pm 1\) lower sampling variance correlations near zero (consequence bounded support); Fisher’s \(z\)-transformation handles automatically mapping \(r\) scale variance approximately constant.many correlations tested simultaneously (e.g., correlation matrix 10 variables yields 45 pairs), adjust \(\alpha\) multiplicity using Bonferroni false-discovery-rate procedures, recompute power adjusted threshold.Sensitivity analysis across plausible \(\rho\) routine correlational study planning; protocols single point \(\rho\) increasingly flagged reviewers, expect range justification lower bound used sample-size determination.paired longitudinal correlation comparisons (e.g., comparing \(\rho\) two time points), use Steiger’s modified Fisher-\(z\) test dedicated power calculation; standard formulas apply single correlation fixed reference.

heading: 6.131 Introduction
text: survival analysis, statistical power driven number events, number enrolled subjects. Cox regression power calculation determines required events , assumed accrual follow-, number subjects.

heading: 6.132 Prerequisites
text: Cox proportional hazards, survival analysis.

heading: 6.133 Theory
text: continuous predictor standardised-coefficient effect, required number events Wald test power \(1 - \beta\) two-sided \(\alpha\) \[D = \frac{(z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2}{\sigma_X^2 \log^2(HR)}.\]binary predictor allocation \(\pi\) (fraction one group):\[D = \frac{(z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2}{\pi(1 - \pi) \log^2(HR)}.\]Required subjects \(N = D / p_{\text{event}}\), \(p_{\text{event}}\) probability observing event follow-; depends survival, accrual, censoring.

heading: 6.134 Assumptions
text: Proportional hazards.Independent censoring.Known baseline event rate survival function.

heading: 6.135 R Implementation
code:
library(powerSurvEpi)

# Two-arm trial: equal allocation, HR = 0.7, 1-year survival = 0.80 in control
ssizeCT.default(power = 0.80, k = 1,
                pE = 1 - 0.70, pC = 1 - 0.80,
                RR = 0.70, alpha = 0.05)

# Number of events for a continuous predictor
# HR per 1-SD increase of 1.4
D <- (qnorm(0.975) + qnorm(0.80))^2 / log(1.4)^2
D

heading: 6.136 Output & Results
text: HR = 0.70 equal allocation event rates 20 % (treatment) 30 % (control), 500 events 1000 subjects required.

heading: 6.137 Interpretation
text: “study requires approximately 500 events detect hazard ratio 0.70 80 % power. Assuming expected event probabilities follow-, need enrol 1000 patients.”

heading: 6.138 Practical Tips
text: Events, subjects, currency power survival; count accumulated events, randomised subjects.Report target events target \(N\); specify assumed accrual period follow-.interim analyses, use group-sequential boundaries (O’Brien-Fleming, Pocock) inflate final sample accordingly.Non-proportional hazards reduce power; use restricted mean survival time weighted log-rank alternatives.competing risks, use Fine-Gray cause-specific power calculations.

heading: 6.141 Introduction
text: crossover trial, subject receives multiple treatments sequence washout period . paired comparison cancels -subject variability, often requiring far fewer subjects parallel-group trials. classic 2x2 crossover two treatments two periods.

heading: 6.142 Prerequisites
text: Paired t-test, power analysis, crossover design basics.

heading: 6.143 Theory
text: 2x2 crossover, period-adjusted within-subject difference variance \(\sigma_w^2\), typically estimated prior crossover data. Sample size analogous paired t-test \(\sigma_d = \sigma_w \sqrt{2}\):\[n \approx 2 \sigma_w^2 (z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2 / \delta^2.\]Compared parallel-group pooled \(\sigma\): approximately half subjects \(\delta\) \(\sigma_w < \sigma_{\text{}}\).Carryover effects invalidate simple formula; washout period planned eliminate .

heading: 6.144 Assumptions
text: carryover; washout adequate.Period effects modelled present.Within-subject variance known estimable.

heading: 6.145 R Implementation
code:
library(PowerTOST); library(pwr)

# 2x2 crossover bioequivalence: CV within = 20%, ratio = 0.95
sampleN.TOST(CV = 0.20, theta0 = 0.95,
             theta1 = 0.80, theta2 = 1.25,
             alpha = 0.05, targetpower = 0.80,
             design = "2x2")

# Therapeutic crossover: mean difference = 5, sigma_w = 10
n_pair <- 2 * (qnorm(0.975) + qnorm(0.80))^2 * 10^2 / 5^2
n_pair
# Equivalent paired-t calculation
pwr.t.test(d = 5 / 10, type = "paired", power = 0.80)

heading: 6.146 Output & Results
text: Bioequivalence 2x2 20 % CV, ratio 0.95: 20 subjects. Therapeutic 5-unit effect \(\sigma_w = 10\): 34 subjects.

heading: 6.147 Interpretation
text: “2x2 crossover trial requires 20 subjects 80 % power establish bioequivalence within 80-125 % range, assuming 20 % within-subject CV true ratio 0.95.”

heading: 6.148 Practical Tips
text: Crossover saves subjects doubles measurement commitment per subject; adherence matters.Washout must long enough eliminate pharmacokinetic physiological carryover.two periods (Latin squares, Williams designs) can efficient require careful balancing.Exclude subjects missing period data (use mixed models).strictly sequential effects (learning, fatigue), random order assignment period--factor model help.

heading: 6.151 Introduction
text: Sample-size planning diagnostic studies typically targets precision (CI width) sensitivity specificity rather power hypothesis. Separate calculations apply diseased non-diseased cohorts.

heading: 6.152 Prerequisites
text: Sensitivity, specificity, proportion CIs.

heading: 6.153 Theory
text: Wilson 95 % CI sensitivity anticipated value \(p_{\text{sens}}\) desired half-width \(w\):\[n_{\text{dis}} \approx \frac{z_{0.975}^2 p_{\text{sens}}(1 - p_{\text{sens}})}{w^2}.\]Analogously specificity using non-diseased. Total sample = \(n_{\text{dis}} + n_{\text{non-dis}}\), ratio determined prevalence disease source population.hypothesis-testing framing (sensitivity > reference), one-proportion power formula applies.

heading: 6.154 Assumptions
text: Anticipated sensitivity / specificity pilot literature.Independent gold-standard verification every subject.

heading: 6.155 R Implementation
code:
# Design 95% CI half-width 5% on sensitivity = 0.90
p_sens <- 0.90; w <- 0.05
n_dis <- qnorm(0.975)^2 * p_sens * (1 - p_sens) / w^2
ceiling(n_dis)

# Spec = 0.85, same width
p_spec <- 0.85
n_non <- qnorm(0.975)^2 * p_spec * (1 - p_spec) / w^2
ceiling(n_non)

# Hypothesis-test framing: sensitivity > 0.80
# Expected sens = 0.90, alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80
library(MKmisc)
power.prop1.test(p0 = 0.80, p1 = 0.90, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)

heading: 6.156 Output & Results
text: sens 0.90 +/- 5 %: 138 diseased cases. spec 0.85 +/- 5 %: 196 non-diseased. Hypothesis-test \(n \approx 130\) diseased cases show sensitivity > 0.80.

heading: 6.157 Interpretation
text: “estimate sensitivity 0.90 95 % CI half-width 5 %, least 138 diseased cases required. Assuming disease prevalence 30 % source population, total enrolment 460 subjects provides adequate non-diseased controls.”

heading: 6.158 Practical Tips
text: Plan separately sensitivity specificity; two calculations independent sample-size requirements per cohort.Use Wilson exact intervals reported CI; Wald intervals can optimistic.STARD reporting guidelines recommend reporting prevalence numbers diseased / non-diseased subjects.Paired designs (tests subject) use McNemar-style within-subject comparisons; efficient unpaired designs.AUC-based sample sizes exist (e.g., Hanley-McNeil); different sensitivity-calculations.

heading: 6.161 Introduction
text: Power analysis equivalence testing via two-one-sided-tests (TOST) procedure requires one-sided null hypotheses — true effect lies lower equivalence bound lies upper equivalence bound — rejected level \(\alpha\). required sample size depends equivalence margin (set clinical regulatory convention), anticipated true effect (typically zero near-zero honest equivalence claim), variance. TOST sample-size calculations widely used bioequivalence studies generic drugs (regulatory margin fixed 80–125 % log scale), non-inferiority trials reframed equivalence, therapeutic-equivalence studies competing devices formulations.

heading: 6.162 Prerequisites
text: working understanding TOST equivalence testing, role equivalence margin, difference equivalence (CI within margin) traditional superiority testing (CI excluding null).

heading: 6.163 Theory
text: two groups true mean difference \(\delta\) pooled SD \(\sigma\), symmetric equivalence margin \(\pm \Delta\), power conclude equivalence approximately\[\Pr\!\left(\frac{|\bar X_1 - \bar X_2 - \delta|}{\sigma \sqrt{2/n}} < z_{1-\alpha} \cdot \frac{\Delta - |\delta|}{\Delta \cdot \sigma \sqrt{2/n}}\right).\]Two important features fall expression. First, true effect centre equivalence margin (\(\delta = 0\)), required sample size minimised. Second, true effect equals boundary (\(|\delta| = \Delta\)), equivalence established regardless \(n\) — test fundamentally underpowered boundary.

heading: 6.164 Assumptions
text: equivalence margin pre-specified substantively justified, anticipated true effect \(\delta\) realistically estimated, data approximately Normal sample large enough central limit theorem, variance known accurately estimated pilot data.

heading: 6.165 R Implementation
code:
library(TOSTER); library(PowerTOST)

sampleN.TOST(alpha = 0.05, targetpower = 0.80,
             theta0 = 1.00, theta1 = 0.80, theta2 = 1.25,
             CV = 0.20, design = "2x2")

power_t_TOST(alpha = 0.05, low_eqbound = -2, high_eqbound = 2,
             mu = 0, sd = 5, type = "two.sample", n = 65)

heading: 6.166 Output & Results
text: sampleN.TOST() returns required sample size bioequivalence regulatory 80–125 % margin log scale; 20 % within-subject CV true ratio 1.00, \(n \approx 24\) 2 × 2 crossover. power_t_TOST() computes power generic two-sample equivalence test arbitrary symmetric asymmetric margin: \(\pm 2\) unit margin, true difference 0, SD 5, \(n = 65\) per group achieves 81 % power.

heading: 6.167 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “establish bioequivalence (regulatory 80–125 % equivalence margin log scale) 80 % power \(\alpha = 0.05\), assuming 20 % within-subject coefficient variation true geometric mean ratio 1.00, \(n = 24\) subjects required 2 × 2 crossover design. protocol enrols 28 allow 15 % dropout. Sensitivity analyses across CV \(\[15 \%, 30 \%]\) yield required \(n\) 16 38, across true ratio \(\[0.95, 1.05]\) 24 33.” Always state margin assumed true effect.

heading: 6.168 Practical Tips
text: equivalence margin substantive regulatory choice, statistical one; set based clinical regulatory conventions (e.g., 80–125 % bioequivalence margin) pre-specified clinical-acceptance criterion, justify choice protocol.Power drops sharply anticipated true effect approaches equivalence margin; always include sensitivity analysis plausible range true effects demonstrate design robust mild deviations central assumption.Crossover bioequivalence trials use within-subject variance (\(\mathrm{CV}_w\)), typically smaller -subject variance; plan accordingly appropriate within-subject CV pilot literature.Asymmetric equivalence margins supported TOST tools (low_eqbound ≠ high_eqbound) useful one direction effect carries clinical penalty; example, drug allowed effective less effective reference.Non-inferiority testing mathematically one-sided TOST single non-inferiority margin; required sample size roughly half two-sided equivalence test margin assumptions, reflecting simpler hypothesis.highly variable drugs (within-subject CV 30 %), reference-scaled bioequivalence widens margin proportionally reference variability; standard fixed-margin TOST sample-size tools -estimate required \(n\) compounds.

heading: 6.172 Introduction
text: intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) standard reliability statistic continuous ratings — agreement raters quantitative scales, test-retest stability, instrument repeatability across multiple measurements per subject. Sample-size planning ICC study depends number subjects, number raters replicate measurements per subject, target ICC value detect (estimate), whether goal hypothesis testing (rejecting null ICC value) precision (achieving target confidence-interval width). questions admit closed-form solutions standard assumptions, explicit power planning now expected prospective reliability study aiming FDA, IVDR, guideline acceptance.

heading: 6.173 Prerequisites
text: working understanding ICC variants — one-way random ICC(1,1), two-way random ICC(2,1), two-way mixed ICC(3,1), single-measurement vs average-measurement variants — distinction hypothesis-testing precision-based sample-size questions.

heading: 6.174 Theory
text: Following Walter, Eliasziw, Donner (1998), sample size testing \(H_0: \rho \leq \rho_0\) \(H_1: \rho \geq \rho_1\) \(k\) raters measurements per subject target power \(1 - \beta\) closed-form expression based \(F\)-distribution variance-component decomposition. precision-based planning, approximate formula\[n \approx \frac{(1 - \rho^2)^2 \cdot 8 \cdot (k - 1)}{\epsilon^2 \cdot k}\]gives number subjects needed 95 % CI half-width \(\epsilon\) around expected ICC \(\rho\) — useful goal tight reliability estimate rather rejecting specific null.

heading: 6.175 Assumptions
text: outcome continuous approximately Normal, subjects independent, subject rated \(k\) raters (measured \(k\) replicate occasions); ICC variant assumed calculation matches design (one-way random vs two-way random vs two-way mixed).

heading: 6.176 R Implementation
code:
library(ICC.Sample.Size)

calculateIccSampleSize(p = 0.8, p0 = 0.6, k = 2,
                       alpha = 0.05, tails = 2, power = 0.80)

k <- 2; rho <- 0.75; eps <- 0.1
n_prec <- (1 - rho^2)^2 * 8 * (k - 1) / (eps^2 * k)
ceiling(n_prec)

heading: 6.177 Output & Results
text: calculateIccSampleSize() returns required number subjects hypothesis test. ICC\(_1 = 0.8\) vs ICC\(_0 = 0.6\) \(k = 2\) raters, \(n \approx 84\). precision-based calculation gives roughly 19 subjects 95 % CI half-width 0.10 around expected ICC 0.75 — much smaller sample precision tolerates wider range rejecting specific null.

heading: 6.178 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “subject rated two independent raters; \(n = 84\) subjects required 80 % power detect ICC 0.80 null hypothesis ICC = 0.60 two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\). protocol enrols 100 subjects allow 15 % missing unevaluable ratings. Sensitivity analyses show \(n = 110\) conservative null 0.65 \(n = 65\) \(k = 3\) raters; design report includes alternatives supplement.” Always state ICC form many raters.

heading: 6.179 Practical Tips
text: raters per subject reduces required number subjects, linear cost rater-hours; trade-depends relative cost availability subjects vs raters project-specific.Specify ICC variant matches design exactly — one-way random (ICC(1,1)) fully crossed designs without rater identity, two-way random (ICC(2,1)) raters sampled population, two-way mixed (ICC(3,1)) raters fixed chosen — sample-size formula analytic ICC differ across variants.Small \(n\) produces wide ICC confidence intervals; reliability claims supporting clinical use, aim 95 % CI lower bound 0.75 (good) 0.90 (excellent), per Koo Li (2016) recommendations.Sensitivity analysis varying expected \(\rho\) across realistic range standard practice; protocols single point estimate \(\rho\) increasingly flagged reviewers, expect honest acknowledgement uncertainty.Published reliability studies historically small samples produce poorly characterised ICC estimates; planning conservatively (assuming \(\rho\) lower end pilot CIs) protects -powered ICC validation.agreement categorical ratings, use Cohen’s Fleiss’s kappa rather ICC; sample-size frameworks differ planning principles analogous.

heading: 6.183 Introduction
text: Power analysis linear regression determines sample size required detect either overall non-zero \(R^2\) (via omnibus \(F\)-test regression) incremental \(R^2\) contributed adding one predictors baseline model. questions central design observational experimental studies use multiple regression inference: omnibus power calculation tells whether model whole chance detected, incremental power calculation tells whether specific block predictors scientific interest detectable beyond control variables. Cohen’s \(f^2\) effect size unifies calculations single non-central \(F\)-distribution framework.

heading: 6.184 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multiple linear regression, relationship \(R^2\) omnibus \(F\)-statistic, Cohen’s \(f^2\) standardised effect-size measure regression.

heading: 6.185 Theory
text: Cohen’s \(f^2\) omnibus regression test \[f^2 = \frac{R^2}{1 - R^2},\]incremental contribution new block predictors,\[f^2 = \frac{R^2_{\text{full}} - R^2_{\text{reduced}}}{1 - R^2_{\text{full}}}.\]Conventional benchmarks 0.02 (small), 0.15 (medium), 0.35 (large). omnibus partial \(F\)-test follows non-central \(F\)-distribution \(H_1\) non-centrality \(\lambda = f^2 (u + v + 1)\), \(u\) numerator df (number predictors tested) \(v\) residual df. Power probability non-central \(F\) exceeds critical value \(\alpha\).

heading: 6.186 Assumptions
text: standard linear-regression assumptions (linearity, homoscedasticity, Normal residuals, independent observations), number predictors full model incremental block pre-specified, effect size \(f^2\) chosen basis smallest scientifically meaningful incremental \(R^2\).

heading: 6.187 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

pwr.f2.test(u = 5, v = NULL, f2 = 0.15, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)

pwr.f2.test(u = 2, v = NULL, f2 = 0.02, power = 0.80)

heading: 6.188 Output & Results
text: pwr.f2.test() returns residual degrees freedom \(v\) required achieve target power; total sample size \(n = v + u + 1\). 5-predictor model medium effect \(f^2 = 0.15\), \(v = 85\) \(n = 91\); small incremental effect \(f^2 = 0.02\) 2 added predictors 5 baseline predictors, \(v = 489\) \(n = 497\) — illustrating dramatically small incremental effects increase sample-size requirements.

heading: 6.189 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “5 predictors regression anticipated \(f^2 = 0.15\) (medium-effect convention; equivalent \(R^2 = 0.13\)), total sample \(n = 91\) required achieve 80 % power omnibus \(F\)-test \(\alpha = 0.05\). detect incremental \(f^2 = 0.02\) 2 additional predictors primary interest adjusting 5 baseline covariates, protocol requires \(n = 497\). trial therefore enrols 550 participants allow 10 % attrition.” Always state \(u\), \(f^2\), inflation factor.

heading: 6.190 Practical Tips
text: Convert anticipated \(R^2\) expectations \(f^2\) using formulas ; conversion non-linear “small \(R^2\)” 0.05 corresponds \(f^2 \approx 0.053\), “medium \(R^2\)” 0.13 corresponds \(f^2 \approx 0.15\).Small incremental effects (\(f^2 < 0.05\)) require large samples detect; weigh cost-benefit honestly consider whether smaller incremental effect genuinely scientifically interesting.Interactions, polynomial terms, dummy-coded categorical variables add predictor count \(u\) inflate standard errors individual coefficients; sample-size calculation must reflect actual model structure.Pre-specify minimum detectable incremental effect interest protocol; avoid post-hoc rationalisation chosen \(f^2\) match achieved power, form HARKing.Include 15–20 % buffer calculated \(n\) residual non-Normality, minor violations homoscedasticity, missing data; calculated \(n\) minimum, target.prediction-focused studies (rather inferential studies), consider using learning-curve repeated-cross-validation framework determine sample size required stable --sample \(R^2\), different question power omnibus test.

heading: 6.194 Introduction
text: Power analysis logistic regression less standardised linear models. Three approaches: (1) events-per-variable rule thumb, (2) closed-form formulas single predictor, (3) simulation complex models.

heading: 6.195 Prerequisites
text: Logistic regression, odds ratio, events-per-variable (EPV).

heading: 6.196 Theory
text: EPV rule (Peduzzi et al.): least 10 events per predictor coefficient working minimum. Harrell’s 20 EPV safer.Single-predictor formula (Demidenko 2007) uses \(\log()\) baseline event rate:\[n = \frac{(z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2}{p_0(1 - p_0) \log^2() \cdot \sigma_X^2 / \pi(1 - \pi)},\]simplifications dichotomous \(X\).Simulation: specify full data-generating process, fit intended model, compute power across many replicates. reliable formulas multiple predictors.

heading: 6.197 Assumptions
text: Binary outcome.Logit-linear predictors.Sufficient separation groups.

heading: 6.198 R Implementation
code:
library(WebPower)

# Single dichotomous predictor: baseline event rate p0 = 0.2, OR = 1.5
wp.logistic(n = NULL, p0 = 0.2, p1 = 0.2 * 1.5 / (1 + 0.2 * (1.5 - 1)),
            alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80,
            family = "Bernoulli", parameter = NULL, alternative = "two.sided")

# Continuous predictor: detect OR = 1.5 per 1-SD increase
wp.logistic(n = NULL, p0 = 0.2, p1 = 0.2,
            alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80,
            family = "normal",
            alternative = "two.sided",
            parameter = log(1.5))

# EPV rule: 15 predictors, min events = 10*15 = 150
# If baseline event rate is 0.20: n = 150 / 0.20 = 750

heading: 6.199 Output & Results
text: Detecting modest 1.5 typically requires hundreds observations; continuous predictors need somewhat less \(n\) dichotomous, given comparable effect magnitude.

heading: 6.200 Interpretation
text: “detect 1.5 per unit standardised predictor 80 % power two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\), 250 participants required (assuming 20 % baseline event rate).”

heading: 6.201 Practical Tips
text: Prefer simulation formulas predictors correlated model complex.Rare-event outcomes dramatically inflate required \(n\); power approximately symmetric around \(p_0 = 0.5\).ordinal multinomial logistic, scale roughly \(k - 1\) (number contrasts).Penalised logistic (ridge, Firth) handles small samples separation.Missing data measurement error increase required \(n\).
