heading: 6.204 Introduction
text: log-rank test standard non-parametric test equality two () survival functions widely used inference tool survival analysis. central feature power calculation distinguishes sample-size analyses \(t\)-tests, ANOVAs, proportion comparisons: power depends total number observed events across two groups, sample size directly. Event-based sample-size planning therefore separates recruitment question (many subjects enrol) inferential question (many events observe), links assumptions event rates, accrual time, follow-duration. decoupling essential realistic planning survival trials.

heading: 6.205 Prerequisites
text: working understanding log-rank test, Kaplan-Meier survival estimator, proportional-hazards assumption, relationship hazard ratio, event rate, follow-time.

heading: 6.206 Theory
text: alternative hypothesis \(\mathrm{HR} = \theta\) equal allocation, required total number events \[D = \frac{4 (z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2}{\log^2 \theta}.\]unequal allocation arm fractions \(\pi_1, \pi_2\) summing one, multiplier 4 replaced \(1/(\pi_1 \pi_2)\). required sample size \(n\) depends event probability trial duration, turn depends underlying hazard rates, planned accrual period, post-accrual follow-period. relationship captured standard formulas (Schoenfeld, Freedman, Lakatos) implemented dedicated power-analysis packages.

heading: 6.207 Assumptions
text: proportional-hazards assumption holds (approximately) trial duration, censoring independent non-informative, accrual pattern follow-duration pre-specified, underlying hazard rates reasonably well-estimated prior data.

heading: 6.208 R Implementation

library(powerSurvEpi); library(gsDesign)

D <- 4 * (qnorm(0.975) + qnorm(0.80))^2 / log(0.65)^2
D

ssizeCT.default(power = 0.80, k = 1,
                pE = 0.40, pC = 0.55,
                RR = 0.65, alpha = 0.05)

gs <- gsDesign(k = 3, test.type = 2, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.20)
gsBoundSummary(gs)

text: closed-form calculation gives roughly 88 events required detect HR = 0.65 80 % power two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\). assumed event rates 40 % experimental arm 55 % control arm planned follow-window, corresponds roughly 185 subjects (just 100 per arm). group-sequential design extends multiple looks appropriate alpha-spending.

heading: 6.210 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “trial requires 88 events detect hazard ratio 0.65 80 % power two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\) log-rank test. Assuming 24 months uniform accrual 12 months additional follow-, expected event rates 55 % (control) 40 % (treatment) trial duration, planned enrolment 180 subjects (90 per arm). protocol triggers primary analysis 88 events observed, regardless calendar time, ensuring planned power achieved.” Always state event-driven analysis trigger.

heading: 6.211 Practical Tips
text: Power depends total number events observed, number subjects enrolled; plan accrual follow-deliver required event count, trigger primary analysis count reached rather fixed calendar time. survival trials report “event-driven” stopping rules.Non-proportional hazards (delayed effects, crossing survival curves, time-varying treatment effects) reduce log-rank test’s power; Fleming-Harrington weighted log-rank tests improve detection early late differences can pre-specified trials non-proportionality expected.interim monitoring, use alpha-spending group-sequential designs (gsDesign, rpact) information-fraction-based analysis timing — typically every \(D/4\) events four-stage design — rather fixed calendar-time analyses.Report assumed accrual pattern expected follow-duration explicitly; determine event yield given enrolment target, trial outcomes vary substantially assumptions wrong.competing risks, log-rank test appropriate inferential tool; use Fine-Gray subdistribution-hazard test corresponding power-analysis tools (cmprsk, crrSC::power.crr()).Sensitivity analysis range plausible hazard ratios standard; protocols single point HR increasingly flagged reviewers, expect defensible bracket.

heading: 6.215 Introduction
text: Power McNemar’s test depends rate discordant pairs, overall sample size alone. concordant pairs contribute nothing test, studies high agreement need proportionally pairs.

heading: 6.216 Prerequisites
text: McNemar’s test, paired binary data.

heading: 6.217 Theory
text: Let \(p_{10}\) = probability (+, -) pairs \(p_{01}\) = probability (-, +). null hypothesis \(p_{10} = p_{01}\). \(H_1\), total proportion discordant pairs \(p_{\text{disc}} = p_{10} + p_{01}\); odds ratio “+ test 1” given discordance \(p_{10}/p_{01}\).two-sided test \(\alpha\) power \(1 - \beta\):\[n \approx \frac{(z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta})^2}{p_{\text{disc}} \cdot \left(\frac{p_{10} - p_{01}}{p_{10} + p_{01}}\right)^2}.\]discordance drops, \(n\) grows rapidly.

heading: 6.218 Assumptions
text: Independent paired observations.Pre-specified \(p_{10}, p_{01}\) pilot.

heading: 6.219 R Implementation
code:
library(pwrss)

# Expected p10 = 0.15, p01 = 0.05, alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80
pwrss.z.mcnemar(p10 = 0.15, p01 = 0.05,
                alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80)

# Manual calculation
p10 <- 0.15; p01 <- 0.05
p_disc <- p10 + p01
OR <- (p10 - p01) / (p10 + p01)
n_manual <- (qnorm(0.975) + qnorm(0.80))^2 / (p_disc * OR^2)
n_manual

heading: 6.220 Output & Results
text: \(n \approx 79\) pairs required. discordance lower (say \(p_{10} = 0.10\), \(p_{01} = 0.05\)), required \(n\) roughly doubles.

heading: 6.221 Interpretation
text: “expected proportion (+, -) pairs 0.15 (-, +) pairs 0.05, McNemar’s test requires 79 paired observations 80 % power two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\).”

heading: 6.222 Practical Tips
text: Plan total sample, just discordant pairs; concordant pairs expected non-informative.formula sensitive assumed discordance rates; sensitivity analysis essential.high agreement (concordant pairs >> discordant), large samples needed; consider redesigning comparison.Exact McNemar conservative small samples; simulate exactness matters.Extension Bowker Stuart-Maxwell multi-category paired data requires simulation-based power.

heading: 6.223 Reporting
text: Always report assumed discordance rates alongside resulting sample size. Reviewers ethics committees expect see number pairs derived marginal probabilities, total sample can yield different power depending split \(p_{10}\) \(p_{01}\). pilot estimate discordance uncertain, present small grid \(n\) across plausible values rather single point estimate, state value drove final target. sequential design anticipated, note formula single fixed analysis group-sequential adaptive variants require separate boundary calculations.

heading: 6.226 Introduction
text: Non-inferiority trials aim establish new treatment “meaningfully worse” active comparator. non-inferiority margin pre-specified loss defines boundary. Power calculations use one-sided test margin.

heading: 6.227 Prerequisites
text: Equivalence testing, confidence intervals.

heading: 6.228 Theory
text: continuous outcomes true difference \(\delta\) (new minus reference), margin \(-\Delta\) (loss), pooled SD \(\sigma\), non-inferiority null \(\mu_{\text{new}} - \mu_{\text{ref}} \leq -\Delta\). Power \(\alpha\) one-sided \[1 - \beta = \Phi\!\left(\frac{\delta + \Delta}{\sigma \sqrt{2/n}} - z_{1-\alpha}\right).\]binary outcomes (proportions), analogous formulas using Cohen’s \(h\) risk-difference variance apply.Sample size increases tighter margin, larger allowed effect, reduced power.

heading: 6.229 Assumptions
text: Pre-specified non-inferiority margin based regulatory clinical guidance.Assumed true \(\delta\) (often zero, assuming equivalence).Normal large-sample approximations.

heading: 6.230 R Implementation
code:
library(gsDesign)

# Continuous outcome: margin -5 units, true delta = 0, sigma = 15
# One-sided alpha = 0.025, power = 0.80
delta  <- 0; margin <- 5; sigma <- 15; alpha <- 0.025; beta <- 0.20
n_per_arm <- 2 * sigma^2 * (qnorm(1 - alpha) + qnorm(1 - beta))^2 / (delta + margin)^2
n_per_arm

# Binary outcome: reference rate 70% cure, margin -10%, true new = 70%
pC <- 0.70; pE <- 0.70; margin_prop <- 0.10
# Farrington-Manning or score-based calculations are standard; simplified Wald here:
p_bar <- (pC + pE) / 2
n_bin <- (qnorm(1 - alpha) + qnorm(1 - beta))^2 *
         (pC*(1-pC) + pE*(1-pE)) / (pE - pC + margin_prop)^2
n_bin

# Group-sequential non-inferiority design
ni <- gsDesign(k = 2, test.type = 1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.20,
               sfu = "OF")
gsBoundSummary(ni)

heading: 6.231 Output & Results
text: Continuous: \(n \approx 140\) per arm. Binary: \(n \approx 196\) per arm.

heading: 6.232 Interpretation
text: “establish non-inferiority 5-unit margin, assuming true zero difference SD 15, 80 % power one-sided \(\alpha = 0.025\), 140 patients per arm required.”

heading: 6.233 Practical Tips
text: Margin selection important decision non-inferiority design; regulators require explicit justification.Use one-sided \(\alpha = 0.025\) keep consistency two-sided 0.05 superiority conventions.Report per-protocol analysis alongside intention--treat; dilution effects ITT make non-inferiority easier show artificially.Include interim futility stops ethical reasons.Conservative margins high power requirements inflate \(n\) quickly.

heading: 6.236 Introduction
text: Power analysis one-proportion test computes sample size required detect departure null proportion \(p_0\) adequate probability. one-proportion test arises constantly clinical research — testing whether single-arm response rate exceeds historical control benchmark, whether quality-control defect rate departs tolerance threshold, whether survey-derived proportion differs reference. Two complementary approaches widely used: Normal-approximation power formula, gives clean closed-form expressions terms Cohen’s \(h\) effect size, exact-binomial power, enumerates binomial distribution preferred small samples extreme proportions Normal approximation unreliable.

heading: 6.237 Prerequisites
text: working understanding one-proportion test, binomial distribution, Normal approximation binomial, Cohen’s \(h\) standardised effect-size measure proportion tests.

heading: 6.238 Theory
text: test \(H_0: p = p_0\) versus \(H_1: p = p_1\), Cohen’s standardised proportion-difference effect size \[h = 2 \arcsin\sqrt{p_1} - 2 \arcsin\sqrt{p_0},\]arc-sine transforms proportion onto scale variance approximately constant. Normal-approximation sample-size formula two-sided \(\alpha\) power \(1 - \beta\) \[n \approx \left(\frac{z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}}{h}\right)^2.\]Cohen’s conventional benchmarks \(h\) 0.20 (small), 0.50 (medium), 0.80 (large). Exact binomial power evaluates binomial CDF \(p_1\) sums probabilities rejection region defined binomial CDF \(p_0\).

heading: 6.239 Assumptions
text: Trials independent Bernoulli constant success probability; Normal-approximation formula, \(n p_0 \geq 10\) \(n (1 - p_0) \geq 10\) ensure approximation adequate; exact binomial power conditions needed.

heading: 6.240 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

h <- ES.h(p1 = 0.40, p2 = 0.25)
pwr.p.test(h = h, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, alternative = "two.sided")

exact_binom_power <- function(n, p0, p1, alpha = 0.05) {
  k_reject <- sum(dbinom(0:n, n, p0) <= alpha)
  crit_lo <- qbinom(alpha/2, n, p0)
  crit_hi <- qbinom(1 - alpha/2, n, p0)
  power_lo <- pbinom(crit_lo - 1, n, p1)
  power_hi <- 1 - pbinom(crit_hi, n, p1)
  power_lo + power_hi
}
exact_binom_power(n = 80, p0 = 0.25, p1 = 0.40)

heading: 6.241 Output & Results
text: pwr.p.test() returns sample size required Normal-approximation formula; custom exact-binomial function verifies calculation direct enumeration. example, Normal approximation gives \(n = 82\) exact calculation confirms 80 % power \(n = 80\) — close agreement typical Normal-approximation conditions satisfied.

heading: 6.242 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “detect response rate 40 % historical-control null 25 % 80 % power two-sided \(\alpha = 0.05\), \(n = 82\) participants required (Normal-approximation calculation, Cohen’s \(h = 0.32\)). Exact binomial power \(n = 80\) 80 %, confirming Normal-approximation result. protocol enrols 90 allow 10 % attrition. Sensitivity analyses across \(p_1 \[0.35, 0.45]\) reported supplement.” Always report sensitivity plausible \(p_1\).

heading: 6.247 Introduction
text: Power analysis one-sample \(t\)-test determines sample size required detect specified departure population mean reference value given probability, equivalently power achievable fixed sample size. one-sample \(t\)-test arises many practical contexts: validating quality-control measurement target, comparing single-arm pilot study historical benchmark, testing whether within-subject change differs zero. every case, planning question — given smallest effect scientific interest assumed within-population standard deviation, many observations needed adequately-powered test? Pre-specifying calculation protocol now standard prospective study.

heading: 6.248 Prerequisites
text: working understanding one-sample \(t\)-test, concept statistical power type-II error, Cohen’s \(d\) standardised effect-size measure one-mean tests.

heading: 6.249 Theory
text: alternative hypothesis \(H_1: \mu = \mu_1 \neq \mu_0\), \(t\)-statistic follows non-central \(t\)-distribution \(n - 1\) degrees freedom non-centrality parameter\[\lambda = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} = d \sqrt{n},\]\(d = (\mu_1 - \mu_0)/\sigma\) Cohen’s standardised effect. Power probability \(P(|T| > t_{1-\alpha/2, n-1})\) computed non-central \(t\)-distribution. Closed-form sample-size solutions invert expression produce smallest \(n\) achieving target power specified \(d\), \(\alpha\), one-sided / two-sided test direction.

heading: 6.250 Assumptions
text: population approximately Normal sample size large enough central limit theorem apply, within-population standard deviation \(\sigma\) reasonably known (estimated pilot data adequate precision), effect size \(d\) pre-specified — usually smallest clinically scientifically meaningful difference rather expected value.

heading: 6.251 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

pwr.t.test(d = 0.4, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = "one.sample")

pwr.t.test(n = 25, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = "one.sample")

n_grid <- seq(10, 100, by = 5)
pw <- sapply(n_grid, function(n)
  pwr.t.test(n = n, d = 0.4, type = "one.sample")$power)
plot(n_grid, pw, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "#2A9D8F",
     xlab = "n", ylab = "Power", main = "One-sample t-test, d = 0.4")
abline(h = 0.80, lty = 2)

heading: 6.252 Output & Results

heading: 6.253 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “sample 51 participants required detect standardised effect \(d = 0.4\) 80 % power \(\alpha = 0.05\) (two-sided) using one-sample \(t\)-test reference value 10. planned sample 25 participants, minimum detectable effect 80 % power \(d = 0.58\), exceeds smallest clinically meaningful effect 0.4 leave study underpowered. protocol therefore stipulates 51 participants, 60 enrolled allow 15 % attrition.” Always state \(d\), \(\alpha\), power, attrition margin.

heading: 6.258 Introduction
text: Paired designs achieve statistical power gain cancelling -subject variability treatment contrast. subject acts control, -subject component variance — typically largest source noise clinical-pharmacology, behavioural, clinical-laboratory studies — drops within-subject difference. result paired designs substantially powerful independent-groups designs equal total sample size, benefit growing within-subject correlation increases. Power analysis paired \(t\)-test must therefore explicitly account correlation; ignoring -uses design’s main analytic advantage inflates planned sample size unnecessarily.

heading: 6.259 Prerequisites
text: working understanding paired \(t\)-test, relationship within-subject correlation \(\rho\) difference standard deviation \(\sigma_D\), standardised effect-size measures Cohen’s \(d\) (-subject) \(d_z\) (within-subject paired).

heading: 6.260 Theory
text: paired differences \(D_i = X_i - Y_i\) within-subject correlation \(\rho\), difference standard deviation \[\sigma_D = \sigma \sqrt{2(1 - \rho)},\]appropriate paired-design effect size \[d_z = \frac{\mu_D}{\sigma_D}.\]Higher within-subject correlation reduces \(\sigma_D\), inflating \(d_z\) fixed raw effect, dramatically reducing required sample size. \(\rho = 0\) paired design reduces independent-groups case (half per-condition sample size); \(\rho \1\) paired design approaches infinite efficiency.

heading: 6.261 Assumptions
text: design genuinely produces paired observations (subject contributes one value per condition), differences approximately Normal (sample large enough central limit theorem), within-subject correlation reasonably known pilot data literature.

heading: 6.262 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

pwr.t.test(d = 0.4, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = "paired")

sigma_diff <- function(sigma, rho) sigma * sqrt(2 * (1 - rho))
d_z_from_d <- function(d, rho) d / sqrt(2 * (1 - rho))

d_z_from_d(d = 0.5, rho = 0.7)

pwr.t.test(d = 0.5, power = 0.80, type = "two.sample")$n
pwr.t.test(d = d_z_from_d(0.5, 0.7), power = 0.80, type = "paired")$n

heading: 6.263 Output & Results
text: script reports required number pairs \(d_z = 0.4\), conversion raw \(d\) paired \(d_z\) correlation 0.7, side--side sample-size comparison independent-groups paired designs raw effect. paired design needs roughly 21 pairs (42 observations) versus 128 observations independent comparison — six-fold efficiency gain \(\rho = 0.7\).

heading: 6.264 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Assuming within-subject correlation 0.7 (estimated pilot data) raw mean difference 0.5 SD, paired design requires 21 paired observations 80 % power \(\alpha = 0.05\) (two-sided), corresponding paired effect size \(d_z = 0.65\). raw effect require 128 observations (64 per arm) independent-groups design — 6-fold efficiency gain. protocol therefore enrols 25 subjects accommodate 15 % attrition.” Always report assumed \(\rho\) efficiency gain.

heading: 6.265 Practical Tips

heading: 6.269 Introduction
text: Repeated-measures designs achieve substantial statistical-power gains independent-groups designs exploiting within-subject correlation across multiple measurements per subject. subject contributes information every time point condition, within-subject component variance — typically smaller two variance components — drives sensitivity test. Power calculations repeated-measures ANOVA must therefore explicitly account within-subject correlation; ignoring leads substantially -estimated sample-size requirements wastes resources, assuming unrealistically high correlation -powers trial reality intervenes.

heading: 6.270 Prerequisites
text: working understanding repeated-measures ANOVA, sphericity assumption diagnostics, Cohen’s \(f\) standardised effect-size measure ANOVA, role within-subject correlation design efficiency.

heading: 6.271 Theory
text: within-subjects design \(k\) measurements per subject, effect size \(f\), average within-subject correlation \(\rho\), non-centrality parameter omnibus \(F\)-test approximately\[\lambda = n f^2 \frac{k}{1 - \rho},\]\(n\) number subjects. Power tail probability non-central \(F\) distribution evaluated conventional critical value. Higher \(\rho\) inflates \(\lambda\) reduces required sample size; near-zero \(\rho\) gives little efficiency advantage equivalent independent-groups ANOVA. Cohen-conventional benchmarks \(f\) 0.10 (small), 0.25 (medium), 0.40 (large).

heading: 6.272 Assumptions
text: Sphericity holds (equal pairwise variances differences across time points), residuals approximately Normal, within-subject correlation reasonably known pilot data literature. Sphericity violations require Greenhouse-Geisser Huynh-Feldt corrections, slightly inflate required sample size.

heading: 6.273 R Implementation
code:
library(WebPower)

wp.rmanova(f = 0.25, ng = 1, nm = 4, nscor = 1,
           alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = 1)

rho_grid <- seq(0.2, 0.9, by = 0.1)
ns <- sapply(rho_grid, function(rho) {
  wp.rmanova(f = 0.25, ng = 1, nm = 4, nscor = rho,
             alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80, type = 1)$n
})
data.frame(rho = rho_grid, n = round(ns))

heading: 6.274 Output & Results

Sensitivity analyses across \(\rho \[0.30, 0.70]\) yield required \(n\) 47 23 respectively; protocol enrols 50 accommodate conservative scenario plus 15 % attrition.” Always state \(\rho\) report sensitivity.

heading: 6.280 Introduction
text: Stepped-wedge cluster-randomised trials (SW-CRT) progressively introduce intervention across clusters time every cluster receives . design efficient intervention expected help must rolled anyway. Power calculations use Hussey-Hughes (2007) formula.

heading: 6.281 Prerequisites
text: Cluster-RCT, ICC, time effects.

heading: 6.282 Theory
text: \(\) clusters \(J\) time periods (one cluster per step crossing ), Hussey-Hughes variance treatment effect complex depends ICC, cluster-period correlation, cluster size, total steps. key insight: adding time periods increases efficiency cluster contributes control intervention observations.Software (swCRTdesign, SWSamp) computes needed number clusters given ICC, cluster-autocorrelation, cluster size, effect.

heading: 6.284 R Implementation

library(swCRTdesign)

# Design: 8 clusters, 6 time periods, cluster size 20, ICC = 0.05
swDsn <- swDsn(clusters = c(rep(1, 7), 2))
swDsn

# Power for effect size 0.3, sigma = 1
swPwr(design = swDsn,
      distn = "gaussian",
      n = 20,
      mu0 = 0, mu1 = 0.3,
      tau = 0.05, sigma = 1,
      alpha = 0.05,
      retDATA = FALSE)

heading: 6.290 Introduction
text: Comparing proportions two independent groups one common designs clinical research, public-health epidemiology, quality-control. Whenever primary outcome binary — event vs event, response vs non-response, success vs failure — trial’s sample-size calculation reduces two-proportion power analysis. Several closely-related approaches widely used: Cohen’s arcsine-transformed effect size \(h\) maps cleanly Normal-approximation formula, direct sample-size formulas original-proportion scale (e.g., Kelsey’s formula continuity correction), exact Fisher-based methods rare events. choice depends expected event rates, desired allocation ratio, regulatory methodological context.

heading: 6.291 Prerequisites
text: working understanding two-proportion test, arcsine variance-stabilising transformation, Cohen’s \(h\) standardised effect-size measure proportion-difference designs.

heading: 6.292 Theory
text: Cohen’s \(h\) arcsine-transformed proportion difference\[h = 2 \arcsin\sqrt{p_1} - 2 \arcsin\sqrt{p_2}.\]balanced two-arm designs, sample size per group two-sided \(\alpha\) power \(1 - \beta\) approximately\[n \approx 2 \left(\frac{z_{1-\alpha/2} + z_{1-\beta}}{h}\right)^2 \text{ per group}.\]unequal allocation ratio \(k = n_2 / n_1\), total sample size grows factor \((1 + 1/k)(1 + k)/4\) relative balanced minimum. Cohen’s benchmarks \(h\) 0.20 (small), 0.50 (medium), 0.80 (large).

heading: 6.293 Assumptions
text: two groups independent, standard Normal-approximation conditions \(np \geq 10\) \(n(1-p) \geq 10\) satisfied group, proportions alternative hypothesis pre-specified.

heading: 6.294 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr); library(Hmisc)

pwr.2p.test(h = ES.h(0.20, 0.10), sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80)

bsamsize(p1 = 0.20, p2 = 0.10, alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80)

pwr.2p2n.test(h = ES.h(0.20, 0.10), n1 = 100, sig.level = 0.05)
n1_fix <- 100
n2_vals <- seq(40, 300, 10)
pw <- sapply(n2_vals, function(n2)
  pwr.2p2n.test(h = ES.h(0.20, 0.10), n1 = n1_fix, n2 = n2)$power)
n2_vals[which(pw >= 0.80)[1]]

heading: 6.295 Output & Results
text: pwr.2p.test() returns per-group sample size specified \(h\), \(\alpha\), power; Hmisc::bsamsize() provides calculation directly original-proportion scale. \(p_1 = 0.20\) vs \(p_2 = 0.10\) 80 % power, \(n \approx 199\) per group (398 total). unequal-allocation analysis shows fixing \(n_1 = 100\) requires roughly \(n_2 = 280\) maintain 80 % power, illustrating inefficiency imbalanced designs.

heading: 6.296 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “detect difference event rates 10 % (historical control) 20 % (active treatment) \(\alpha = 0.05\) two-sided 80 % power, 199 patients per arm required (398 total) 1:1 allocation. protocol enrols 440 patients allow 10 % attrition. Sensitivity analyses across \(p_2 \[0.15, 0.25]\) yield required per-arm \(n\) 392 105, bracketing assumed effect; conservative end range used justify operational sample size.” Always report assumed proportions allocation ratio.

heading: 6.297 Practical Tips
text: Equal allocation (1:1) efficient detecting difference two proportions; unequal allocation (2:1, 3:1) costs 10–30 % total \(n\) power justified cost feasibility two arms differs substantially.rare events (\(p < 0.01\) either arm), Normal-approximation formulas unreliable; use exact Fisher’s-test-based power calculations simulation, available Exact::power.exact.test() via Monte Carlo enumeration.Continuity correction (Yates) inflates required sample size slightly historical default \(2 \times 2\) chi-squared tests; modern practice often omits likelihood-ratio score tests, verify software computing.cluster-randomised designs, inflate calculated \(n\) design effect \(1 + (\bar m - 1) \mathrm{ICC}\), \(\bar m\) average cluster size ICC intraclass correlation; ignoring clustering grossly -powers cluster trials.Cohen’s \(h\) benchmarks (0.20 / 0.50 / 0.80) useful translating effect-size language sample-size expectations, tying calculation actual expected proportions via ES.h(p1, p2) defensible invoking benchmarks directly.non-inferiority equivalence designs, calculation differs superiority test; use pwr.2p.test() appropriate margin specialised tools like bsamsize() non-inferiority arguments.

heading: 6.298 R Packages Used
text: pwr::pwr.2p.test() pwr::pwr.2p2n.test() canonical Cohen’s \(h\) Normal-approximation calculations; Hmisc::bsamsize() direct-proportion-scale calculations optional continuity correction; Exact::power.exact.test() exact-binomial power across small-sample two-proportion designs; clusterPower cluster-randomised two-proportion power; Mediana trial-design simulation including two-proportion power across complex designs.

heading: 6.301 Introduction
text: running two-group comparison, investigator needs know many observations per group required detect difference scientific importance acceptable probability. Power analysis two-sample t-test answers question linking four quantities: true effect size, significance level, power, sample size. Given three, R can solve fourth.

heading: 6.302 Prerequisites
text: Familiarity two-sample t-test concepts type type II error assumed. reader know “power” means \(1 - \beta\), probability rejecting \(H_0\) \(H_1\) true.

heading: 6.303 Theory
text: \(H_0\), t statistic follows central t distribution \(\nu = n_1 + n_2 - 2\) degrees freedom (Student’s test, equal variances). \(H_1\) \(\mu_1 - \mu_2 = \delta\), statistic follows non-central t distribution non-centrality parameter\[\lambda = \frac{\delta}{\sigma \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}}}.\]Power probability test statistic exceeds critical value non-central distribution. Cohen’s standardised effect size \(d = \delta/\sigma\) equal group sizes \(n_1 = n_2 = n\), simplifies \(\lambda = d\sqrt{n/2}\).four power quantities trade-offs:Effect size \(d\): minimum standardised difference considered scientifically meaningful. Cohen’s conventions, \(d = 0.2\) small, \(0.5\) medium, \(0.8\) large – conventions field-dependent used judgement.Significance level \(\alpha\): usually 0.05 two-sided testing.Power: conventionally 0.80 exploratory work, 0.90 confirmatory registered trials.Sample size per group \(n\): typically solve .

heading: 6.304 Assumptions
text: Power analysis inherits assumptions t-test plans : independent groups, approximately normal data (large enough \(n\) CLT), equal variances Student’s test (unequal Welch’s).

heading: 6.305 R Implementation
text: first pwr.t.test() call asks: many subjects per group need detect medium-sized effect (\(d = 0.5\)) 80% power? second asks: given \(n = 30\) per group, minimum detectable effect 80% power? third asks: given \(n = 30\) \(d = 0.5\), power? pwrss::pwrss.t.2means() call works directly raw-mean terms, often natural planning clinical trial.
code:
library(pwr)

pwr.t.test(d = 0.5, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

pwr.t.test(n = 30, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

pwr.t.test(n = 30, d = 0.5, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

library(pwrss)
pwrss.t.2means(mu1 = 110, mu2 = 100, sd1 = 18, sd2 = 15,
               power = 0.80, alpha = 0.05)

heading: 6.306 Output & Results
text: first call returns \(n \approx 64\) per group – 128 subjects total. second returns minimum detectable \(d \approx 0.735\), corresponding raw difference 11 points \(\sigma = 15\). third returns power approximately 0.48, showing 30 per group badly underpowered medium effect.
code:
Two-sample t test power calculation

    alternative = two.sided

heading: 6.307 Interpretation
text: recommended manuscript phrasing : “Assuming -group standardised mean difference \(d = 0.5\) (medium effect Cohen’s criteria), two-sided \(\alpha\) 0.05, power 0.80, required sample size 64 per group. Allowing 10% loss follow-, aim enrol 72 per group (144 total).” power analysis reported methods section grant protocol, buried appendix.

heading: 6.308 Practical Tips
text: Base effect size published data pilot, convention like \(d = 0.5\). published effect \(d = 0.3\), planning \(d = 0.5\) leave underpowered.Use Welch’s sample size unequal variances (pwr.t2n.test() pwrss.t.2means() sd1 != sd2). slightly conservative, want planning stage.Plan loss follow-, noncompliance, data exclusion inflating computed \(n\) realistic percentage (typically 10-25%).minimum clinically important difference (MCID) known raw units, use directly rather translating \(d\). interpretable clinicians.Always produce sensitivity analysis: curve power \(n\), tornado plot sample size plausible values effect size SD.

heading: 6.311 Introduction
text: Every power calculation rests assumptions investigator know exactly advance: true effect size, outcome SD, dropout rate, ICC cluster design. Sensitivity analysis reports sample size across realistic range assumptions, rather single point estimate.

heading: 6.312 Prerequisites
text: Power analysis.

heading: 6.313 Theory
text: sensitivity analysis two-dimensional (higher) grid required sample sizes, varying one two assumptions holding others fixed. Tabular graphical (contour / heatmap) presentation lets readers see robustness chosen \(n\).

heading: 6.314 Assumptions
text: chosen assumption grid covers plausible parameter space. -narrow grids miss important regions; -wide grids unfocused.

heading: 6.315 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr); library(ggplot2)

# 2D sensitivity: n per group vs d and power
d_grid <- seq(0.2, 0.8, by = 0.05)
power_grid <- c(0.70, 0.80, 0.90)

res <- expand.grid(d = d_grid, power = power_grid)
res$n <- mapply(function(d, p)
  pwr.t.test(d = d, power = p, sig.level = 0.05, type = "two.sample")$n,
  res$d, res$power)

ggplot(res, aes(d, n, colour = factor(power))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("#F4A261", "#6A4C93", "#2A9D8F")) +
  labs(x = "Cohen's d", y = "n per group (log scale)",
       colour = "Power",
       title = "Sample size sensitivity: d x power") +
  theme_minimal()

# Inflation for dropout
n_base <- 64
dropout_rates <- c(0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20)
n_inflated <- round(n_base / (1 - dropout_rates))
data.frame(dropout = dropout_rates, n_enrol = n_inflated)

heading: 6.316 Output & Results
text: \(d = 0.5\): \(n\) ranges 51 power 0.70 86 power 0.90. \(d = 0.3\): 138 234. Dropout inflation 15 % pushes \(n\) 64 76 per group.

heading: 6.317 Interpretation
text: “Sensitivity analysis shows plausible effect sizes (Cohen’s \(d\) 0.35 0.55), required sample size ranges 72 164 per arm 80 % power. plan enrol 180 per arm, accommodating upper end range allowing 10 % attrition.”

heading: 6.318 Practical Tips
text: Always report sensitivity analysis alongside headline sample-size calculation grant proposals.Present calculation underpins chosen \(n\) bold; show neighbouring values context.Two-dimensional grids (effect size vs. power, effect size vs. variance) typical; higher dimensions confuse readers.Include dropout inflation factor explicitly; reviewers frequently check.adaptive sequential designs, sensitivity interim analyses alpha spending deserves separate reporting.

heading: 6.319 Reporting
text: useful sensitivity table small enough reviewer can read every row large enough chosen design point clearly bracketed less conservative alternatives. State inputs varied held fixed, justify ranges terms pilot data published evidence, identify row drives final enrolment target audit trail assumption commitment explicit. dropout inflation applied, write formula assumed completion proportion protocol, reviewers ethics committees rely line confirm analysis sample consistent powered effect.

library(tidyverse)
library(pwr)
library(lme4)
library(lmerTest)
library(simr)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 6.326 1. Hypothesis
text:
Detect standardised mean difference d = 0.4 two
groups 80% power 5% two-sided significance level. 
power mixed model 20 clusters 10 observations 
detect fixed effect 0.3.

heading: 6.327 2. Visualise
code:
pw <- map_dbl(ns, \(n) pwr.t.test(
  n = n / 2, d = 0.4, sig.level = 0.05, type = "two.sample"
)$power)

tibble(n = ns, power = pw) |>
  ggplot(aes(n, power)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.8, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  labs(x = "Total sample size", y = "Power")

heading: 6.328 3. Assumptions
text:
Closed-form assumes equal variance across arms, independent
observations, nominal effect size. simulation adds
random intercepts cluster level therefore relaxes
independence.

heading: 6.329 4. Conduct
text:
Fifty simulations enough teaching demo; practice use 
least 1000 stable estimate.
code:
res <- pwr.t.test(d = 0.4, power = 0.8, sig.level = 0.05, type = "two.sample")
res
J   <- 20                              # clusters
nj  <- 10                              # obs per cluster
dat <- tibble(
  cluster = factor(rep(seq_len(J), each = nj)),
  arm     = rep(rep(c(0, 1), each = nj), length.out = J * nj),
  y       = rnorm(J * nj)              # placeholder; simr overwrites
)

m0 <- makeLmer(
  y ~ arm + (1 | cluster),
  fixef   = c(0, 0.3),
  VarCorr = 0.5,
  sigma   = 1.0,
  data    = dat
)
m0
ps <- powerSim(m0, nsim = 50, test = fixed("arm"), progress = FALSE)
ps

heading: 6.330 5. Concluding statement
text:
two-sample t-test targeting d = 0.4 80% power requires
ceiling(res$n) * 2 participants total. mixed
model 20 clusters size 10 fixed effect 0.3 
estimated power round(summary(ps)$mean * 100)% 
simulation (50 replicates).

heading: 6.338 Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(pwr)
library(gtsummary)
library(broom)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 6.339 1. Goal
text:
Plan hypothetical two-arm trial comparing new antihypertensive 
placebo, choose sample size 80% power detect 5 mmHg
difference α = 0.05, verify simulation, produce reporting
table can paste protocol.

heading: 6.340 2. Approach
text:
Closed-form first. Cohen’s d 5 mmHg effect scale SD
10 mmHg d = 0.5 — medium effect convention.formula calls ceiling(pwr_calc$n) per arm.
code:
d = 0.5, power = 0.80, sig.level = 0.05,
  type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided"
)
pwr_calc
ns <- seq(10, 200, by = 5)
powers <- sapply(ns, function(n) {
  pwr.t.test(n = n, d = 0.5, sig.level = 0.05,
             type = "two.sample")$power
})
tibble(n_per_arm = ns, power = powers) |>
  ggplot(aes(n_per_arm, power)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.8, linetype = 2, colour = "grey50") +
  labs(x = "N per arm", y = "Power",
       title = "Power curve for d = 0.5, α = 0.05")

Verify simulation. function runs trial returns
p-value:Within Monte-Carlo error analytic 0.80, expected.

Now imagine data collected. simulate one realisation 
fit planned analysis.
code:
trial <- tibble(
  arm = rep(c("placebo", "drug"), each = n),
  sbp_change = c(rnorm(n, 0, 10), rnorm(n, -5, 10))
)

fit <- t.test(sbp_change ~ arm, data = trial, var.equal = FALSE)
broom::tidy(fit)

heading: 6.343 5. Report

simulated two-arm trial (n = n per arm), mean change 
systolic blood pressure lower drug arm placebo 
round(-diff(fit$estimate), 1) mmHg (95% CI:
round(-fit$conf.int[2], 1) round(-fit$conf.int[1], 1);
p = signif(fit$p.value, 2)).
code:
trial |>
  tbl_summary(
    by = arm,
    statistic = list(all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})"),
    digits    = all_continuous() ~ 1,
    label     = list(sbp_change = "Change in SBP (mmHg)")
  ) |>
  add_p() |>

heading: 7.3 Introduction
text: ggplot2 built Wilkinson’s grammar graphics, separates two concerns ad-hoc plotting languages tend conflate. aesthetic mapping column data visual property: x-position, y-position, colour, fill, shape, size. geom geometric object drawn using aesthetics: point, line, bar, histogram, smoother. internalise split — “variable becomes visual property” versus “shape want drawn” — ggplot2 becomes Lego kit swappable parts rather dialect memorise.

heading: 7.4 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge basic ggplot2 syntax (ggplot(), aes(), + geom_*), tidy data conventions row observation column variable.

heading: 7.5 Theory
text: aesthetic mapping lives inside aes() binds column visual property:mapping triggers automatic scale construction, legend generation, axis labelling — ggplot2 knows continuous numeric column needs continuous scale categorical column needs discrete one.geom consumes aesthetics draws corresponding marks. Common geoms include geom_point() scatter, geom_line() lines, geom_bar() geom_col() bars, geom_histogram() geom_density() distributions, geom_smooth() trend lines.Mapping vs setting important distinction ggplot2. Inside aes() binds visual property variable, producing one value per row legend. Outside aes() sets constant rows legend. geom_point(colour = "blue") makes every point blue; geom_point(aes(colour = species)) colours points species.Multiple geoms plot share base aesthetics declared ggplot(aes(...)) may add . layering heart grammar: complex figure just stack compatible geoms reading data.
code: aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, colour = species)

heading: 7.6 Assumptions
text: Tidy data: one observation per row, one variable per column. Long-format frames standard input ggplot2; wide-format data reshaped via tidyr::pivot_longer() plotting.

heading: 7.7 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(palmerpenguins)
data(penguins, package = "palmerpenguins")

# Basic mapping: x, y, colour
ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, colour = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  theme_minimal()

# Adding a smoother geom on top
ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, colour = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal()

# Aesthetics differ between geoms: shape is mapped only for points
ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = species, shape = sex), alpha = 0.7) +
  theme_minimal()

# Constant aesthetics outside aes()
ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point(colour = "steelblue", size = 3, alpha = 0.6) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.8 Output & Results
text: Four versions scatter: basic colour-coded plot, plot per-species linear smoothers added, four-aesthetic plot mapping species colour sex shape simultaneously, single-colour version every aesthetic except position held constant. illustrates different combination mapping setting.

heading: 7.9 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Body mass flipper length (n = 333 penguins, three species); colour encodes species shape encodes sex. Per-species linear smoothers overlaid right panel.” explicit aesthetics carry information constant; readers infer mapping.

heading: 7.10 Practical Tips
text: Put aesthetics shared across geoms base ggplot() call; put geom-specific aesthetics inside geom. Repetition fine clarity rarely necessary.Reach alpha mitigate overplotting dense scatter plots; values 0.2 0.5 typically reveal density structure.fill controls interior geoms area (bars, boxplots, polygons); colour controls edges points. Mixing one common ggplot2 errors.categorical colour scales, prefer viridis::scale_colour_viridis_d() RColorBrewer::scale_colour_brewer(); avoid default rainbow.Use shape two six groups; beyond , shapes become indistinguishable facet text label communicates better.want constant value ggplot2 keeps drawing legend, forgot move assignment outside aes(); single common debugging step.

heading: 7.14 Introduction
text: figure shows data without annotation forces reader interpretive work alone. figure many annotations buries data labels, arrows, ornamental boxes. middle ground — well-placed labels, arrow points one observation worth singling , rich-text title names comparison — separates publication-quality plots informative--amateurish ones. ggplot2 ecosystem provides three families annotation tools cover virtually every requirement: annotate() single hand-placed items, geom_text / geom_label (repelling ggrepel variants) data-driven labels, ggtext rich Markdown HTML inside titles, axes, strip text.

heading: 7.15 Prerequisites

heading: 7.18 R Implementation
code: library(ggplot2); library(ggrepel); library(ggtext) p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) + geom_point() + theme_minimal() # Single annotation p + annotate("text", x = 3, y = 30, label = "Lightweight, efficient", size = 5) # Labelled points with repel p + geom_text_repel(aes(label = rownames(mtcars)), size = 3) # Arrow with annotation p + annotate("segment", x = 5, xend = 4, y = 25, yend = 20, arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.3, "cm"))) + annotate("text", x = 5.1, y = 25.5, label = "Outlier") # Markdown in titles p + labs(title = "Weight vs *mpg*") + theme(plot.title = element_markdown())

heading: 7.21 Practical Tips
text: Prefer ggrepel::geom_text_repel() geom_text() whenever labels can overlap; repelling variants always look polished robust data updates.annotate() right tool single fixed label; using geom_text() one-row tibble unnecessarily verbose.small set labels style, build small data frame map aesthetics geom_text() rather stacking many annotate() calls.Keep annotations away data marks, legends, figure margins; , increase plot size re-styling labels.Use ggtext::element_markdown() inline italics scientific names, bold statistical thresholds, colour paired emphasis text data; far cleaner expression()-based plotmath.Save labelled unlabelled versions figure separately; reviewers occasionally request “clean” version supplement.

heading: 7.25 Introduction
text: Bar charts workhorse categorical data geom frequently misused. confusion almost always stems single distinction: bar can show many observations fall category (count) summary statistic computed category (mean, percentage, proportion). ggplot2 separates two cases geom_bar() counts geom_col() pre-computed values, getting right geom data hand first step sensible figure.

heading: 7.26 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge categorical variables, basic summary statistics (mean, standard error), ggplot2’s aesthetic mappings.

heading: 7.27 Theory
text: geom_bar() defaults stat = "count": tabulates rows x-aesthetic draws bars whose heights equal counts. y-aesthetic required; supplying one along stat = "identity" equivalent using geom_col() directly.geom_col() plots explicit y-aesthetic bar height — useful data already summarised. group means uncertainty, pair geom_col() geom_errorbar() whose ymin ymax come confidence interval standard error column.cognitive risk bar-plus-error-bar plots well documented: hide within-group distribution overemphasise mean. showing distributional information, raincloud plots, dot plots, boxplots transparent. Bars remain right choice genuine counts cumulative quantities (proportions, percentages, totals).

heading: 7.28 Assumptions
text: count bars: x-axis categorical discrete. summary bars: y-aesthetic meaningful summary data, ideally one respects bar’s “starts zero” implication.

heading: 7.29 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(dplyr)

# Counts
ggplot(mpg, aes(class)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "#2A9D8F") +
  theme_minimal()

# Ordered bars by count
ggplot(mpg, aes(forcats::fct_infreq(class))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "#2A9D8F") +
  labs(x = "Class") +
  theme_minimal()

# Summarised values (e.g., mean mpg by class)
mpg_summary <- mpg |> group_by(class) |>
  summarise(mean_hwy = mean(hwy), se = sd(hwy)/sqrt(n()))
ggplot(mpg_summary, aes(class, mean_hwy)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2A9D8F") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_hwy - se, ymax = mean_hwy + se),
                width = 0.2) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.30 Output & Results
text: Three bar charts: count cars per class alphabetical order, data categories ordered descending frequency, summary chart mean highway mileage per class \(\pm 1\) standard-error bars. Reordering explicit summary radically improve readability default.

heading: 7.31 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Bar heights show mean highway fuel efficiency per class (n varies, see Table 1); error bars \(\pm 1\) standard error mean. Classes ordered alphabetically.” Always state summary statistic uncertainty measure caption — bars self-explanatory.

heading: 7.32 Practical Tips
text: Order categories value (forcats::fct_reorder() fct_infreq()) rather alphabetically; sorted bars communicate ranking instantly.Use geom_col() already summary statistics geom_bar() (default stat = "count") want raw frequencies.two-category comparisons, simple sentence often beats bar chart; reserve bars three groups.Add underlying observations jittered points (geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.4)) top summary bars recover distributional information bars hide.Always label y-axis units bars summarise quantities (“Mean fuel economy (mpg)”) rather counts.Avoid 3D bars, gradient fills along bar length, ornamental flourishes; distort perceptual mapping bar length value.

heading: 7.33 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 geom_bar(), geom_col(), geom_errorbar(); dplyr forcats summary category-ordering pipeline; ggdist want overlay distributional summaries (slabintervals) top bar charts communicate spread alongside mean.

heading: 7.36 Introduction
text: Bland-Altman plot standard graphical tool assessing agreement two measurement methods, two raters, two devices. Unlike correlation coefficient 45-degree scatter, conflate agreement linear association, Bland-Altman plot displays difference paired measurements mean, exposing systematic bias, proportional bias, limits within 95 % differences expected fall. Since Bland Altman’s 1986 Lancet paper, become canonical method-comparison figure clinical chemistry laboratory medicine.

heading: 7.37 Prerequisites
text: working understanding paired measurements, mean standard deviation, distinction agreement (two methods give value?) correlation (two methods rank observations way?).

heading: 7.38 Theory
text: paired measurements \((X_i, Y_i)\), compute mean \(M_i = (X_i + Y_i)/2\) difference \(D_i = X_i - Y_i\). Plot \(M\) x-axis \(D\) y-axis, three horizontal reference lines:Bias: \(\bar D\), mean differences. Non-zero bias indicates systematic disagreement.Upper limit agreement (LoA): \(\bar D + 1.96 \cdot \mathrm{SD}(D)\).Lower LoA: \(\bar D - 1.96 \cdot \mathrm{SD}(D)\).differences approximately Normal, 95 % points fall within LoA. Trends \(D\) function \(M\) — typically tested regressing \(D\) \(M\) — indicate proportional bias, disagreement scales magnitude. Heteroscedasticity (fan-shaped pattern) suggests LoA expressed percentage mean rather absolute interval.

heading: 7.39 Assumptions
text: Differences approximately Normal, proportional bias (constant spread across measurement range), independent paired observations. repeated measures within subjects, modified Bland-Altman 1999 method partitions within- -subject variance.

heading: 7.40 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(blandr)

set.seed(2026)
m1 <- rnorm(80, 100, 15)
m2 <- m1 + rnorm(80, 2, 5)

d <- data.frame(M = (m1 + m2)/2, D = m1 - m2)
bias <- mean(d$D)
loa <- bias + c(-1, 1) * 1.96 * sd(d$D)

ggplot(d, aes(M, D)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = bias, colour = "#2A9D8F", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = loa, colour = "#F4A261", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Mean of methods", y = "Difference (M1 - M2)") +
  theme_minimal()

# With blandr
blandr.draw(m1, m2, plotter = "ggplot")

heading: 7.41 Output & Results
text: scatter differences vs means three reference lines: solid line bias approximately 2 units two dashed lines upper lower limits agreement approximately \(-7.8\) \(11.8\) units. 4 80 points (5 %) fall outside LoA Normality assumption.

heading: 7.42 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Bland-Altman analysis showed bias \(+2.0\) units (95 % CI 0.9 3.1) 95 % limits agreement \(-7.8\) \(11.8\) units; clinical equivalence requires LoA fall within \(\pm 5\) units, methods interchangeable currently calibrated.” Always report LoA 95 % confidence intervals — LoA endpoints uncertain, especially \(n < 100\).

heading: 7.43 Practical Tips
text: Check proportional bias regressing \(D\) \(M\); significant slope indicates disagreement scales magnitude constant LoA misrepresents data.Report LoA confidence intervals; standard formulae give intervals \(\pm 1.71 \cdot \mathrm{SE}\) around LoA.repeated measures within subjects, use Bland-Altman’s 1999 modified method splits variance within- -subject components; ignoring structure underestimates LoA.45-degree scatter complements (replace) Bland-Altman plot; reveals correlation hides level-dependent bias Bland-Altman exposes.heteroscedastic differences, log-transform measurements first; resulting Bland-Altman log scale LoA become percentages.blandr::blandr.statistics() returns full statistical summary including bias, LoA, CIs, Shapiro-Wilk test Normality differences.

heading: 7.44 R Packages Used
text: blandr blandr.statistics() blandr.draw(); BlandAltmanLeh alternative interface; ggplot2 hand-built versions; epiR::epi.ccc() concordance correlation coefficient complementary single-number agreement summary.

heading: 7.47 Introduction
text: boxplot — also called box--whisker plot — compresses continuous distribution five-number summary: minimum, first quartile, median, third quartile, maximum, Tukey’s fences \(\pm 1.5 \times \mathrm{IQR}\) defining whiskers flagging anything beyond outlier. Boxplots workhorse compact group--group comparisons put many groups side side without ink overload. also geom often misused: hide bimodality, hide sample size, hide raw observations behind tidy rectangle, can mislead readers realise box actually summarises.

heading: 7.48 Prerequisites
text: working understanding quartiles, interquartile range, standard \(\pm 1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}\) outlier convention.

heading: 7.49 Theory
text: standard Tukey boxplot following components per group:box spanning first third quartiles (IQR).horizontal line median inside box.Whiskers extending extreme observation within \(\pm 1.5 \times \mathrm{IQR}\) box edges.Individual points beyond whiskers drawn outliers.Variants:Notched boxplot: notches \(\pm 1.57 \cdot \mathrm{IQR} / \sqrt n\) give approximate 95 % confidence interval median; non-overlapping notches groups suggest real difference medians.Variable-width boxplot: box width proportional \(\sqrt n\), encoding group size visually.Letter-value plot: replaces single box nested boxes decreasing depth, suitable large \(n\) single Tukey box hides tail structure.

heading: 7.50 Assumptions
text: descriptive use, none. notched-boxplot CI interpretation, approximation assumes approximately symmetric within-group distributions; heavy skew notches mislead.

heading: 7.51 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

# Standard
ggplot(mtcars, aes(factor(cyl), mpg, fill = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

# Notched
ggplot(iris, aes(Species, Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

# Boxplot + jittered points (show data and summary)
ggplot(mtcars, aes(factor(cyl), mpg)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, fill = "#2A9D8F", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.5) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.52 Output & Results
text: Three views: standard side--side boxplot mpg cylinder count, notched boxplot iris sepal length per species (notches show approximate median CIs), hybrid plot hides default outlier dots overlays jittered raw points boxplot’s summary underlying data visible.

heading: 7.53 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Boxplots fuel efficiency cylinder count (n = 11, 7, 14 4, 6, 8 cylinders); boxes span Q1 Q3, line marks median, whiskers extend extreme observation within \(1.5 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}\), dots outliers.” Always describe whisker definition; software uses 5 %/95 % quantiles min/max instead Tukey rule.

heading: 7.54 Practical Tips
text: Hide default outliers (outlier.shape = NA) whenever overlay geom_jitter(); otherwise extreme points appear twice.Notches can overshoot box small samples; R prints warning happens, plot becomes uninterpretable.Use varwidth = TRUE encode group sizes via box width groups unbalanced; visual cue subtle informative.small samples (\(n < 10\)), prefer dot plot strip plot boxplot; quartile-based summaries unstable tiny groups.Boxplots hide multi-modality; always inspect density histogram alongside concluding group unimodal.large samples, letter-value plot (lvplot::geom_lv()) preserves tail structure single Tukey box.

heading: 7.55 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 geom_boxplot() varwidth / notch parameters; lvplot letter-value plots large \(n\); ggbeeswarm ggdist richer alternatives combine boxplot summaries raw points distributional shape.

heading: 7.58 Introduction
text: bubble plot scatter plot enriched third continuous variable encoded point size. standard idiom business analytics (“revenue × growth × market share”) international development reporting (Hans Rosling’s life-expectancy × income × population). third dimension comes real perceptual cost — humans judge area less accurately position — bubble plot’s size aesthetic reserved variables rank order rough magnitude matter precise comparison.

heading: 7.59 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge scatter plots, ggplot2’s aesthetic mappings, perceptual principles position vs area encoding.

heading: 7.60 Theory
text: bubble plot maps three continuous variables: \(x\), \(y\), size. size mapping must use area scale — scale_size_area() — perceived magnitude (area) proportional data value. default scale_size() maps data point radius, means doubled data value occupies four times visual area; long-standing bug--feature many bubble-chart implementations produces misleading impressions magnitude. Colour can encode fourth variable, four-variable bubble plots tend overwhelm readers; alternatives include facet categorical fourth variable animated time slider longitudinal data.

heading: 7.61 Assumptions
text: three (four) variables continuous (treated ). size variable non-negative scale zero means “absent”; mapping size centred signed quantity undefined.

heading: 7.62 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, size = hp, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 12, name = "Horsepower") +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Cylinders") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.63 Output & Results
text: scatter weight vs. miles-per-gallon bubble area proportional horsepower colour encoding cylinder count. Heavy, high-horsepower cars cluster lower-right corner; light four-cylinder cars cluster upper-left. combination size colour makes structural patterns visible require four-panel facet communicate without bubbles.

heading: 7.64 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Bubble plot vehicle weight vs fuel economy (n = 32); bubble area proportional horsepower (scale_size_area, max area 12), colour encodes cylinder count.” Always specify area (radius) encoding; readers familiar poorly designed bubble charts otherwise assume wrong mapping.

heading: 7.65 Practical Tips
text: Always use scale_size_area() visual area proportional data; default radius mapping double-counts magnitude.Set max_size deliberately (typically 8–15) avoid enormous bubbles dominating panel obscuring smaller observations.many overlapping bubbles, lower alpha 0.4–0.6 prefer sequential fill density still reads transparency.map size negative signed values; area undefined negatives, visual mapping ambiguous.fourth dimension, prefer facet colour aesthetic top size; combining fights reader’s attention.time series bubbles (“Rosling-style”), use gganimate::transition_time(year) rather static panel; temporal dimension variable makes bubble plots compelling.

heading: 7.66 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 underlying scatter, scale_size_area() correct area encoding, gganimate time-animated bubble charts, ggrepel non-overlapping bubble labels individual observations need named.

heading: 7.69 Introduction
text: Roughly 8 % men European descent 0.5 % women form colour-vision deficiency. Red-green deficiency (deuteranopia protanopia) far common; blue-yellow (tritanopia) rarer still present large readership. figure relies red-versus-green contrast unreadable meaningful fraction every audience, including journal reviewers. Designing colour-blind safety therefore optional politeness — basic requirement accessible scientific communication, increasingly mandated journal style guides web-accessibility standards (WCAG 2.x AA).

heading: 7.70 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2’s colour fill scales, basic familiarity palette functions, understanding “colour” includes qualitative palettes (categorical groups) sequential / diverging palettes (ordered signed values).

heading: 7.71 Theory
text: Safe design strategies fall three categories:Perceptually uniform palettes: viridis, cividis, magma, inferno designed equal steps colour space correspond equal steps perceived brightness, even simulated colour-blindness. handle continuous case well.Hand-curated qualitative palettes: RColorBrewer’s Set2, Dark2, Paired explicitly tested colour-blind safety certain number categories (six eight); beyond , distinguishability degrades.Redundant encoding: pair colour shape, linetype, line weight, direct text label reader distinguish colours can still tell groups apart second visual channel.Simulation tools colorBlindness::cvdPlot() dichromat re-render ggplot deuteranopia, protanopia, tritanopia simulations; treat colour equivalent spell-checker run submission.

heading: 7.72 Assumptions
text: statistical assumptions; constraint figure’s information content survives loss one colour channel.

heading: 7.73 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(viridis); library(colorBlindness)

# Unsafe: default red-green
p_bad <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("red", "orange", "green")) +
  theme_minimal()

# Safe: viridis + shape redundancy
p_good <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl), shape = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "D", end = 0.85) +
  theme_minimal()

cvdPlot(p_good)   # simulates deuteranopia, protanopia, tritanopia

heading: 7.74 Output & Results
text: cvdPlot() returns four-panel composite showing figure normal vision, deuteranopia, protanopia, tritanopia. safely designed plot remains interpretable four panels; unsafe one collapses indistinguishable groups least one.

heading: 7.75 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (methods figure-preparation note): “figures use viridis palette ordered fills RColorBrewer::Set2 qualitative colours; categorical groups encoded redundantly shape colour-blind readers can distinguish . Plots simulated deuteranopia protanopia (colorBlindness::cvdPlot) submission.” Mentioning accessibility check pre-empts reviewer requests redo figures.

heading: 7.76 Practical Tips
text: Default viridis cividis continuous scales; perceptually uniform remain ordered common colour-blindness simulations.categorical groups, pair colour shape (shape = group) linetype (linetype = group); colour alone insufficient.Avoid red--green success/failure annotations; use blue--orange categorical palette like Okabe-Ito.Use colorBlindness::cvdPlot() (Color Oracle desktop tool) every figure submission; simulating colour-vision deficiencies takes seconds catches problems human eye .Beyond colour, ensure adequate contrast (WCAG AA minimum 4.5:1 body text), avoid pure red pure green thin lines, keep font sizes large enough remain legible greyscale photocopies.network flow diagrams, prefer position topology colour primary information channel; colour reinforcement, signal.

heading: 7.77 R Packages Used
text: viridis perceptually uniform sequential palettes, RColorBrewer qualitative diverging palettes (brewer.pal, display.brewer.(colorblindFriendly = TRUE)), colorBlindness cvdPlot() simulations, paletteer unified access dozens curated palettes across R ecosystem.

heading: 7.80 Introduction
text: Colour data visualisation decoration — encodes information. right palette makes ordered values read ordered, signed values read signed, categorical values read distinct without implying ranking. wrong palette (infamous rainbow) creates false visual hotspots hue cycle bends obscures structure perceptually uniform palette reveal. Mastering three palette families choosing among deliberately one highest-payoff skills figure design.

heading: 7.81 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2’s colour fill scales, awareness colour-vision deficiencies affect roughly 8 % male readers.

heading: 7.82 Theory
text: Three palette families serve three different data types:Sequential: monotone hue saturation gradient low high (viridis, plasma, magma, YlOrRd, Blues); ordered data without meaningful zero centre.Diverging: two hues meeting neutral midpoint (RdBu, PuOr, BrBG); signed data positive negative deviations centre meaningful.Qualitative: distinct, equally salient hues (Set1, Set2, Dark2, Okabe-Ito); unordered categorical groups, ideally limited eight fewer.viridis modern default continuous scales perceptually uniform (equal data steps look like equal visual steps), colour-blind safe deuteranopia protanopia, degrades gracefully greyscale. ColorBrewer palettes label colour-blind-friendly variants explicitly (display.brewer.(colorblindFriendly = TRUE)), Okabe-Ito eight-colour palette designed specifically accessibility.

heading: 7.83 Assumptions
text: data type matches palette family — sequential ordered values, diverging signed, qualitative nominal.

heading: 7.84 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(viridis); library(RColorBrewer)

# Continuous: viridis
ggplot(faithfuld, aes(waiting, eruptions, fill = density)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  theme_minimal()

# Continuous: diverging
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = hp - mean(hp))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_colour_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "grey80", high = "red",
                         midpoint = 0) +
  theme_minimal()

# Qualitative: Brewer Set2
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

# Show colour-blind-safe palettes
display.brewer.all(colorblindFriendly = TRUE)

heading: 7.85 Output & Results
text: Four illustrative figures: 2D density tile plot using plasma sequential encoding, centred--zero scatter using gradient2 signed deviations, categorical scatter using Brewer Set2 cylinder counts, panel colour-blind-friendly Brewer palettes reference.

heading: 7.86 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Cell colour encodes 2D kernel density viridis::plasma perceptually uniform palette; positive negative horsepower deviations mean use red-blue diverging scale centred zero.” Always state palette name non-default; readers guess.

heading: 7.87 Practical Tips
text: Use viridis (variants magma, plasma, inferno, cividis) default continuous scales; perceptually uniform colour-blind safe.Limit qualitative palettes 8 colours fewer; beyond , switch shapes, linetypes, facets disambiguate groups.Use scale_colour_gradient2() (scale_fill_gradient2()) signed quantity; centred palette communicates symmetry one-sided sequentials .greyscale fallback (photocopying, monochrome printing), viridis translates almost losslessly; rainbow hues collapse similar greys become unreadable.Verify colour-blind accessibility every figure colorBlindness::cvdPlot(); simulating deuteranopia protanopia takes seconds prevents costly resubmissions.heatmaps, perceptual uniformity non-negotiable — non-uniform palette creates apparent hotspots wherever hue cycle bends.

heading: 7.88 R Packages Used
text: viridis viridis, magma, plasma, inferno, cividis continuous scales; RColorBrewer Brewer family qualitative, sequential, diverging palettes; colorspace HCL-based custom palettes; paletteer unified access dozens curated palettes across R ecosystem.

heading: 7.91 Introduction
text: contour plot draws isolines — curves along scalar field constant — 2D domain. geom serves two related purposes: visualising joint density paired observations, contours level sets kernel-density estimate, visualising deterministic function \(z = f(x, y)\), contours mathematical isolines. uses share common visual grammar — peaks, ridges, saddles — contour plots remain one compact ways communicate probability mass concentrates function attains specific values.

heading: 7.92 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2, basic kernel-density estimation two dimensions, convention closer contour lines indicate steeper gradients.

heading: 7.93 Theory
text: Two distinct workflows feed contour layers ggplot2:Density contours computed raw paired observations via geom_density_2d() stat_density_2d(). Internally, 2D kernel density estimate produces smoothed surface MASS::kde2d() ggplot2’s built-estimator returns level sets.Functional contours require complete grid \((x, y, z)\) values supplied user; geom_contour() computes isolines z exactly. grid resolution determines smoothness; coarse grids produce jagged contours.Filled variants — geom_density_2d_filled() geom_contour_filled() — render closed bands rather lines demand sequential diverging colour palette ordering preserved.

heading: 7.94 Assumptions
text: density contours, input sample large enough kernel density meaningful (least dozen points; ideally hundreds). functional contours, grid covers domain interest adequate resolution.

heading: 7.95 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(metR)

# Contour of 2D density
ggplot(faithful, aes(eruptions, waiting)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_density_2d(colour = "#F4A261") +
  theme_minimal()

# Filled contour
ggplot(faithful, aes(eruptions, waiting)) +
  geom_density_2d_filled(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.5, colour = "white") +
  theme_minimal()

# Function surface: z = sin(x) * cos(y)
grid <- expand.grid(x = seq(-3, 3, 0.1), y = seq(-3, 3, 0.1))
grid$z <- sin(grid$x) * cos(grid$y)
ggplot(grid, aes(x, y, z = z)) +
  geom_contour_filled() +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.96 Output & Results
text: Three figures: density contours Old Faithful eruption / waiting joint distribution showing two well-known modes, density rendered filled bands underlying points overlaid, filled-contour view \(z = \sin(x) \cos(y)\) \(61 \times 61\) grid showing alternating positive negative regions.

heading: 7.97 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Top: 2D kernel density eruption duration vs waiting time Old Faithful (n = 272), raw observations background points; bimodality reflects two distinct eruption regimes. Bottom: deterministic surface \(z = \sin(x)\cos(y)\) filled contours default levels.” Mention kernel bandwidth choice density contours used, since shape visible structure.

heading: 7.98 Practical Tips
text: sparse data, density contours can misleading — estimator extrapolates low-mass regions produces artefactual closed loops. Use bins = 5 bins = 8 sparse samples; let default handle dense ones.Always overlay raw points (low alpha) density contour plots; points anchor contours actual observations let readers spot extrapolation.functional surfaces, increase grid resolution contours visibly smooth; -resolved grids produce stair-stepped lines.metR::geom_contour_fill() metR::geom_text_contour() add labelled contours read clearly filled backgrounds.Use sequential palettes (viridis, magma) ordered density diverging palettes (RdBu, BrBG) surfaces cross zero; qualitative palettes destroy level ordering wrong filled contours.published figures multiple contour layers, label key isolines level values readers can read heights without consulting separate legend.

heading: 7.99 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 geom_density_2d(), geom_density_2d_filled(), geom_contour(), geom_contour_filled(); metR geom_contour_fill() labelled contours; MASS::kde2d() underlying kernel density estimator finer control needed.

heading: 7.102 Introduction
text: correlation heatmap renders correlation matrix grid colour-encoded cells, cell colour mapping correlation coefficient diverging palette centred zero. natural overview figure multivariate dataset: dozens pairwise correlations impossible read numeric table become single image blocks similarly correlated variables stand colour. Hierarchical reordering rows columns brings related variables together reveals cluster structure, often suggesting candidate factors downstream analysis.

heading: 7.103 Prerequisites
text: working understanding correlation coefficients (Pearson, Spearman, Kendall), basic familiarity hierarchical clustering, ggplot2 colour scales.

heading: 7.104 Theory
text: correlation heatmap displays \(r_{ij}\) cell \((, j)\). Standard variants:Symmetric numbers shown — useful matrix small (≤ 8 variables).Upper lower triangle — avoids redundancy saves visual space.Significance overlay — marks non-significant correlations blank cross, eye drawn noise.Hierarchical reordering — uses correlation distance (\(1 - |r|\)) cluster related variables reveal block structure.colour scale must diverging (typically red–white–blue Brewer RdBu) centred zero positive negative correlations visually symmetric. Sequential qualitative palettes destroy sign information wrong correlations.

heading: 7.105 Assumptions
text: valid correlation matrix (kind: Pearson linear, Spearman monotone, polychoric ordinal). matrix positive-semi-definite feed downstream methods; automatic computed raw data cor().

heading: 7.106 R Implementation
code:
library(corrplot); library(ggcorrplot)

corr_mat <- cor(mtcars)

# corrplot
corrplot(corr_mat, method = "color", order = "hclust",
         addCoef.col = "black", tl.col = "black")

# ggcorrplot with p-value overlay
p_mat <- cor_pmat(mtcars)
ggcorrplot(corr_mat, hc.order = TRUE, type = "lower",
           p.mat = p_mat, insig = "blank",
           lab = TRUE, lab_size = 3)

heading: 7.107 Output & Results
text: Two correlation heatmaps 11-variable mtcars dataset: full symmetric heatmap hierarchical reordering numeric cell labels, lower-triangle version non-significant cells masked. first emphasises full structure; second cleaner publication figure.

heading: 7.108 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Pearson correlation matrix 11 vehicle attributes (n = 32), reordered hierarchically using \(1 - |r|\) distance; cells \(p > 0.05\) blanked. two visible blocks correspond size-related variables (weight, displacement, horsepower) efficiency-related variables (mpg, quarter-mile time).” Always state correlation type reordering method.

heading: 7.109 Practical Tips
text: Use diverging palette centred zero; Brewer RdBu viridis::cividis (centre stretched) work well print.Hierarchical reordering (hc.order = TRUE ggcorrplot, order = "hclust" corrplot) brings related variables together almost always preferable alphabetical order.Mask non-significant cells (insig = "blank") direct reader’s attention robust signals; alternatively, mark crosses show test performed.30 variables, figure becomes coloured pixel grid without readable labels; consider summarising via principal components factor analysis instead.State correlation type (Pearson, Spearman, Kendall) test correction () caption; readers guess.partial correlations, use ppcor::pcor() feed resulting matrix heatmap geom; partial correlations often reveal structure pairwise correlations obscure.

heading: 7.110 R Packages Used
text: corrplot canonical correlation heatmap reordering significance overlays; ggcorrplot ggplot2-native interface; Hmisc::rcorr() correlation matrices associated \(p\)-values; psych::corPlot() alternative rich cell formatting.

heading: 7.113 Introduction
text: density plot estimates underlying probability density continuous variable via kernel smoothing, producing smooth curve instead binned bars histogram. Densities easier compare across groups (overlaid translucent fills legible dodged transparent bars) sidestep histogram’s bin-boundary arbitrariness — cost introducing different arbitrary parameter, bandwidth. Choosing sensible bandwidth explicit main craft density-plot design.

heading: 7.114 Prerequisites
text: working understanding histograms, kernel density estimation basics, role bandwidth smoothness parameter.

heading: 7.115 Theory
text: kernel density estimate (KDE) \[\hat f(x) = \frac{1}{n h} \sum_{=1}^n K\!\left(\frac{x - x_i}{h}\right),\]\(K\) kernel (Gaussian default) \(h\) bandwidth. bandwidth controls smoothness: small \(h\) undersmooths produces noisy curve follows individual observations, large \(h\) oversmooths hides modes skew. R’s default uses Silverman’s rule thumb (bw = "nrd0"), works well unimodal Normal-like data can oversmooth multi-modal distributions; Sheather–Jones plug-(bw = "SJ") flexible default adapts local variability.choice kernel (\(K\)) matters far less bandwidth practice — Gaussian, Epanechnikov, rectangular kernels produce visually indistinguishable density curves \(h\).

heading: 7.116 Assumptions
text: Observations independent identically distributed; underlying distribution density (discrete spike). bounded variables (proportions, durations), kernel density spills past boundary reflection truncation method required keep estimate inside support.

heading: 7.117 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

# Basic density
ggplot(mtcars, aes(mpg)) +
  geom_density(fill = "#2A9D8F", alpha = 0.5) +
  theme_minimal()

# Density by group
ggplot(iris, aes(Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

# Bandwidth control
ggplot(faithful, aes(eruptions)) +
  geom_density(bw = 0.05, colour = "red") +      # undersmoothed
  geom_density(bw = 0.5,  colour = "black") +    # default-ish
  geom_density(bw = 2.0,  colour = "blue") +     # oversmoothed
  theme_minimal()

# Histogram + density overlay
ggplot(mtcars, aes(mpg)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 15,
                 fill = "#2A9D8F", alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_density(linewidth = 1) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.118 Output & Results
text: Four illustrative views: single-group density, three-group overlay iris dataset, bandwidth comparison bimodal Old Faithful data showing bandwidth choices alternately reveal hide second mode, histogram density overlay aligned common density y-axis.

heading: 7.119 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Kernel density estimate sepal length per species (n = 50 ), Gaussian kernel Silverman bandwidth; setosa well-separated two species, overlap considerably.” Always state bandwidth method (value) kernel; shape visible structure.

heading: 7.120 Practical Tips
text: Show histogram alongside density exploratory work; density alone can hide binning artefacts histogram makes visible.bounded data (proportions \([0, 1]\), response times bounded zero), use density(..., = 0, = 1) reflection-based estimator; default kernels spill past boundary produce artificially low density edges.small samples (\(n < 30\)), density estimate noisy misleading; show strip plot, dot plot, histogram instead.Compare densities across groups via fill (alpha = 0.4) translucent overlays colour line-renderings; latter scales better many groups.2D joint densities, geom_density_2d() draws contours stat_density_2d(aes(fill = after_stat(level)), geom = "polygon") renders filled level sets.doubt, plot data three bandwidths (Silverman, Sheather-Jones, one tighter) pick whichever respects structure already know exists; let bandwidth invent features.

heading: 7.121 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 geom_density() stat_density(); KernSmooth advanced bandwidth selectors; ks multivariate kernel densities; ggdist slabinterval geoms combine density credible-interval annotation.
