heading: 7.124 Introduction
text: “Dot plot” refers two distinct chart types share name geometry answer different questions. Cleveland dot plots show one value per category single point, replacing bar charts ranked comparisons; popularised William Cleveland’s 1985 Elements Graphing Data grounds perceiving position accurate perceiving length. Wilkinson dot plots show one point per observation, stacked relevant x-value, act hand-drawn histogram exposes individual observations rather binned counts.

heading: 7.125 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2, idea prefer position length encoding, basic familiarity histograms bar charts.

heading: 7.126 Theory
text: Cleveland dot plots (geom_point() category y value x) eliminate visual noise bar lengths make small differences across many categories easy compare. faint horizontal segment zero point — geom_segment() — preserves bar-like sense “distance origin” without committing visual heaviness filled bar.Wilkinson dot plots (geom_dotplot()) bin observations along one axis stack equal-sized dots bin. method = "histodot" argument anchors bins data range; method = "dotdensity" adapts bin centres local density. useful small samples (n < 100) every observation visible; larger samples, jittered strip plots swarm plots scale better.

heading: 7.127 Assumptions
text: Cleveland dot plots: one value per category (clearly defined summary). Wilkinson dot plots: binwidth makes sense data range — small dots stack spikes; large merge bars.

heading: 7.128 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

# Cleveland dot plot: mean mpg by car class
mpg_summary <- aggregate(hwy ~ class, mpg, mean)
ggplot(mpg_summary, aes(hwy, reorder(class, hwy))) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = hwy, yend = class), colour = "grey80") +
  geom_point(size = 3, colour = "#2A9D8F") +
  labs(y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

# Wilkinson dot plot: distribution of a small sample
ggplot(mtcars, aes(mpg)) +
  geom_dotplot(method = "histodot", binwidth = 1, fill = "#2A9D8F") +
  theme_minimal()

# Grouped dot plot
ggplot(iris, aes(Species, Sepal.Length)) +
  geom_dotplot(binaxis = "y", stackdir = "center", binwidth = 0.1,
               fill = "#2A9D8F") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.129 Output & Results
text: Three views: Cleveland dot plot ranking car classes mean highway mpg faint baseline segments, Wilkinson dot plot 32 mtcars mpg values stacked histogram, grouped Wilkinson plot showing sepal-length distributions three iris species side side.

heading: 7.130 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Cleveland dot plot mean highway fuel economy (mpg) class 234 vehicles; categories ordered descending mean. Wilkinson dot plot mpg values 32 vehicles, dot representing single car.” showing summary points, always describe summary (mean, median, count) dot represents.

heading: 7.131 Practical Tips
text: Order Cleveland-dot categories value forcats::fct_reorder() reorder(); alphabetic orderings rarely communicate ranking.Combine Cleveland dot plots horizontal error bars (geom_errorbarh() geom_segment() confidence intervals) uncertainty important.Choose Wilkinson dot binwidth match data resolution; integer data, set binwidth equal unit step.grouped Wilkinson plots, set stackdir = "center" symmetric stacks "" one-sided; binaxis = "y" rotates dotplot vertical y-axis layouts.Dot plots presentation slides work better bar charts small sizes; bar’s length advantage becomes length-perception headache bar pixels tall.hundred observations per category, switch Wilkinson dot plots strip plots, swarm plots (ggbeeswarm), violin plots; stacked dots longer scale.

heading: 7.132 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 geom_dotplot(), geom_point(), geom_segment(); forcats category reordering; ggbeeswarm swarm-style dot plots scale larger samples; ggdist slabinterval geoms combine dot-plot density idioms.

heading: 7.135 Introduction
text: Small multiples — grid identical plots, one per subgroup — one powerful visualisation idioms modern data analysis. let reader compare relationship across many conditions without overlaying everything single set axes, becomes unreadable beyond three four series. ggplot2 calls faceting provides two complementary functions: facet_wrap() one-dimensional set panels arranged rows columns, facet_grid() strict row--column matrix indexed two grouping variables.

heading: 7.136 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2’s grammar graphics, layered geoms, distinction aesthetic mappings faceting variables.

heading: 7.137 Theory
text: facet_wrap(~ var) partitions data var, creates one panel per level, arranges single block whose dimensions can specified nrow ncol. facet_grid(rows ~ cols) produces matrix panels indexed two variables, leaving empty cell combinations data. functions share scales argument controlling axis behaviour:"fixed" (default): panels share x y axes — right choice comparative question headline."free_x", "free_y", "free": panel sets axis range — useful scales differ enough fixed axes hide structure within panels cost cross-panel comparability.Strip labels (panel headers) themed via labeller; label_both combines variable name level, label_parsed interprets level math expression.

heading: 7.138 Assumptions
text: statistical assumptions; faceting display convention. practical constraint number panels remains small enough readable final print size — usually 9–12 panels per page.

heading: 7.139 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(palmerpenguins)
data(penguins)

# facet_wrap: one factor
ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = species), alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ sex) +
  theme_minimal()

# facet_grid: two factors (rows = island, cols = sex)
ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = species), alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_grid(island ~ sex) +
  theme_minimal()

# Free scales
ggplot(penguins, aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = species), alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ species, scales = "free") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.140 Output & Results
text: Three faceted views: two-panel split sex, 3--3 grid island sex, three-panel split species free axes per panel. version answers different question — “relationship differ sexes”, “across island sex”, “look like inside species ignoring scale”.

heading: 7.141 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Body mass flipper length faceted sex (facet_wrap(~ sex)); colour distinguishes three penguin species.” Always describe faceting variable caption — readers infer grid axis strip labels alone.

heading: 7.142 Practical Tips
text: Reach facet_wrap() one grouping variable; use facet_grid() row/column structure meaningful balanced.Keep scales = "fixed" whenever cross-panel comparison inferential focus; switch free scales within-panel magnitudes differ enough obscure shape.Limit panel count: 9–12 practical upper bound journal-sized figure; beyond , faceting fragments rather illuminates.Use labeller = labeller(var = c("M" = "Male", "F" = "Female")) spell short factor levels reader.aligned axes across facet_wrap() panels different ranges, fix one axis (scales = "free_y") rather freeing — preserves comparability matters.coord_cartesian(xlim = ..., ylim = ...) zooms leaving underlying data unfiltered; combine facet_grid() want fixed-scale comparisons tighter view natural data range.

heading: 7.143 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 facet_wrap() facet_grid(); ggh4x nested facets per-panel scale control; palmerpenguins small dataset rich faceting structure examples.

heading: 7.146 Introduction
text: forest plot signature visualisation meta-analysis subgroup analyses clinical trials. row shows one study (one subgroup) point estimate horizontal confidence interval, study name sample size margin column pooled summary diamond bottom. compact layout invites direct visual comparison: heterogeneity reads horizontal scatter across rows, consistency reads alignment near vertical position. Done well, forest plot communicates meta-analysis accompanying numerical table.

heading: 7.147 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge point estimates, confidence intervals, difference fixed-effect random-effects pooling, basic ggplot2.

heading: 7.148 Theory
text: forest plot encodes three quantities per study: point estimate (marker, size optionally proportional inverse-variance weight), lower upper confidence-interval limits (horizontal segment), study label. Conventional additions:vertical reference line null value (1 ratios, 0 differences).diamond rather circle pooled estimates, diamond width equal CI.Effect-size CI text right-margin column readers need numbers well positions.Ratios (HR, , RR) belong log axis visual width CI corresponds multiplicative width intervals symmetric log scale appear symmetric.

heading: 7.149 Assumptions
text: Effect estimates confidence intervals available comparable scale across studies; weighting () computed appropriately meta-analytic model.

heading: 7.150 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

studies <- data.frame(
  study = c("Trial A", "Trial B", "Trial C", "Trial D", "Pooled"),
  estimate = c(0.85, 0.70, 0.95, 0.88, 0.85),
  lo = c(0.65, 0.50, 0.75, 0.66, 0.75),
  hi = c(1.10, 0.98, 1.20, 1.16, 0.96),
  is_pooled = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

ggplot(studies, aes(x = estimate, y = factor(study, levels = rev(study)))) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = lo, xmax = hi), height = 0.2) +
  geom_point(aes(size = is_pooled, shape = is_pooled), colour = "#2A9D8F") +
  scale_size_manual(values = c(3, 5), guide = "none") +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(16, 18), guide = "none") +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(x = "Hazard ratio (log scale)", y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.151 Output & Results
text: five-row forest plot: four trials displayed circles horizontal error bars, pooled summary larger diamond bottom, vertical null line HR = 1 log-x axis. dispersion four trial estimates around pooled diamond communicates -study heterogeneity glance.

heading: 7.152 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Forest plot hazard ratios four trials random-effects pooled estimate (REML, \(^2 = 23 \%\)); horizontal error bars show 95 % confidence intervals log scale, diamond marks pooled estimate, dashed vertical line corresponds effect (HR = 1).” Always state pooling model heterogeneity statistic.

heading: 7.153 Practical Tips
text: Log-transform ratios x-axis confidence-interval widths correspond multiplicative width; otherwise small effects look disproportionately large.Make marker size proportional inverse-variance weight meta-analysis (size = 1/SE^2); convention readers expect.Use diamond pooled estimates width equal CI; geom_polygon() produces cleanly.Order studies meaningfully — chronologically, size, point estimate, subgroup — disclose ordering choice caption.Add right-margin column numerical effect estimates CIs (geom_text() fixed x, mapped y); readers expect visual numeric information.automated meta-analysis output, metafor::forest.rma() forestplot produce publication-ready figures weighted markers, columns, heterogeneity annotations without hand-coding ggplot.

heading: 7.154 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 hand-built forest plots; forestplot two-column publication-style forests; metafor::forest.rma() meta-analysis output forests; meta::forest() random-effects forests prediction intervals; ggforestplot tidy hazard-ratio displays regression models.

heading: 7.157 Introduction
text: funnel plot canonical visualisation diagnosing publication bias meta-analysis. plots study’s effect size precision (standard error), forming inverted-funnel shape null bias: large precise studies cluster near pooled estimate narrow top funnel, smaller imprecise studies scatter wider base. Asymmetry funnel — typically missing wedge small null negative studies — suggests studies suppressed non-statistical reasons, often field’s preferred direction positive significant.

heading: 7.158 Prerequisites
text: working understanding meta-analysis, relationship sample size standard error, difference heterogeneity publication bias competing explanations asymmetry.

heading: 7.159 Theory
text: funnel plot axes:x: effect size (log odds ratio, log hazard ratio, standardised mean difference, etc.).y: standard error (smaller SEs top, funnel narrows upward) precision \(1/\mathrm{SE}\).null publication bias small-study effects, studies fall symmetrically inside triangular pseudo-confidence funnel centred pooled estimate. Asymmetry — commonly absence small studies one side — visual cue bias. Formal tests quantify : Egger’s regression standardised effect precision widely used; Begg–Mazumdar’s rank correlation robust sparse data. Trim--fill (metafor::trimfill()) imputes missing studies estimate bias-adjusted effect.

heading: 7.160 Assumptions
text: meta-analysis least 10 studies (fewer makes asymmetry impossible judge), study-level effects standard errors comparable scale, working assumption sources (e.g. true heterogeneity correlated study size) explain asymmetry.

heading: 7.161 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

# Meta-analysis of 10 hypothetical studies
dat <- data.frame(
  yi = c(-0.1, -0.3, -0.5, -0.4, -0.2, -0.6, -0.7, -0.3, -0.9, -1.2),
  vi = c(0.04, 0.03, 0.08, 0.05, 0.02, 0.09, 0.07, 0.06, 0.10, 0.12)
)
res <- rma(yi = yi, vi = vi, data = dat, method = "REML")
funnel(res, main = "Funnel plot of 10 studies")

# Egger test
regtest(res, model = "lm")

heading: 7.162 Output & Results
text: funnel plot study marked \((y_i, \mathrm{SE}_i)\) position, vertical line pooled estimate, pseudo-95 % confidence funnel boundaries widen standard error increases. regtest() returns Egger’s test slope \(p\)-value; significant slope conventional evidence asymmetry.

heading: 7.163 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “funnel plot 10 studies showed visible asymmetry (Egger’s regression slope \(-2.1\), \(p = 0.04\)); trim--fill imputed three additional studies right shifted pooled estimate \(-0.55\) (95 % CI \(-0.78\) \(-0.32\)) \(-0.42\) (95 % CI \(-0.66\) \(-0.17\)), suggesting publication bias inflates apparent effect roughly 25 %.” Always pair visual formal test; single test rarely provides strong evidence .

heading: 7.164 Practical Tips
text: Require least 10 studies drawing conclusions funnel plot; fewer studies support meaningful asymmetry test.True -study heterogeneity correlated study size (smaller studies different populations) can mimic publication-bias asymmetry; consider explanations.metafor::trimfill() adjusts detected asymmetry cure — one several sensitivity analyses report.Contour-enhanced funnel plots (funnel(res, level = c(90, 95, 99))) overlay significance contours, helping distinguish bias heterogeneity.binary outcomes, plot log-log-RR scale; raw axes distort symmetry null.Custom ggplot2 funnel plots easy build (geom_point() plus geom_segment() funnel boundaries) journal style requires consistent typography across figures.

heading: 7.165 R Packages Used
text: metafor rma(), funnel(), regtest(), trimfill(); meta metabias() alternative funnel implementations; ggplot2 hand-built funnel plots default base-graphics output match publication’s style; dmetar additional bias diagnostics.

heading: 7.168 Introduction
text: ggplot2 theme controls everything plot data — panel background, axis ticks, gridlines, font sizes, legend placement, plot margins. Two layers control coexist: complete theme (theme_minimal(), theme_bw(), theme_classic(), etc.) sets every non-data element usually right starting point, individual theme() calls override specific elements publication polish. Mastering layers turns default plot one looks home journal article polished report.

heading: 7.169 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2’s grammar graphics, layered geoms, aesthetic mappings.

heading: 7.170 Theory
text: Complete themes functions return fully populated theme object. Built-choices include theme_minimal() (white background, light gridlines, de facto modern default), theme_bw() (white panel thin black border), theme_classic() (axes , gridlines, mimicking print journals), theme_void() (axes gridlines, useful maps decorative composites). ggthemes package adds journal-flavoured options (theme_tufte(), theme_economist(), theme_few()).Element overrides come theme(), accepts named arguments every theme element. element built one four constructors: element_text() text (size, family, face, colour, hjust, vjust, margin), element_line() lines, element_rect() rectangles, element_blank() hide element entirely. Common overrides include legend.position, axis.title, panel.grid.minor, plot.title.theme_set() applies theme globally every subsequent plot session, useful enforcing house style across multi-figure report.

heading: 7.171 Assumptions
text: statistical assumptions; practical constraint font sizes, line widths, margins must remain legible final print size, best verified saving plot target dimensions judging screen.

heading: 7.172 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(ggthemes)

p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3)

# Complete themes
p + theme_minimal()
p + theme_bw()
p + theme_classic()
p + theme_tufte()

# Custom theme
p +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(title = "MPG vs weight", x = "Weight (1000 lbs)", y = "MPG",
       colour = "Cylinders")

# Set a global theme
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 7.173 Output & Results
text: Side--side comparisons scatterplot four complete themes plus fully customised version. custom variant combines theme_minimal() base overrides title weight, axis title size, legend position, gridline visibility — typical recipe publication-ready figure.

heading: 7.174 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “figures use theme_minimal(base_size = 12) custom title axis label sizes; legend position moved bottom horizontal compositions.” Mention theme affects interpretation (e.g. removing gridlines deliberately emphasise contrast); otherwise can left methods section “figure preparation” line.

heading: 7.175 Practical Tips
text: Set global theme top analysis script via theme_set(); rest figures inherit automatically stay consistent.biomedical publications, theme_minimal(base_size = 12) panel.grid.minor = element_blank() sensible default; reads well print screen.Use element_blank() hide element entirely; setting colour = NA makes invisible still occupying space.Keep font choices simple: theme(text = element_text(family = "Helvetica")) (corresponding sans-serif default) avoids rendering surprises come fancier typefaces.Always export ggsave() final intended width, height, dpi (300 raster, 600+ line art); judging plot screen wrong dimensions hides legibility problems.bespoke journal styles, build wrapper function returns fully configured theme use instead repeating theme() overrides every script.

heading: 7.176 R Packages Used

heading: 7.179 Introduction
text: final step figure pipeline exporting plot right dimensions resolution. also step figures go wrong: plot designed previewed 1024 × 768 pixels screen looks fine dropped manuscript reprinted journal column width, fonts shrunk illegibility raster edges blurred. ggsave() ggplot2’s export function, discipline invoke explicit dimensions explicit DPI early workflow separates publication-ready figures ones re-made night submission.

heading: 7.180 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2, idea final figure used (print, web, slide), basic familiarity vector vs raster graphics.

heading: 7.181 Theory
text: File-format choice depends destination:PDF vector infinite-resolution; standard journal submissions safest default unless told otherwise.SVG also vector ideal figure edited Illustrator Inkscape final use.PNG raster lossless compression; use 300 dpi higher print, 96 dpi web.TIFF raster required several biomedical journals; use lossless compression = "lzw" avoid quality loss.EPS vector required shrinking minority journals; PDF generally better choice allowed.Dimensions specified width height units ("", "cm", "mm"); DPI applies raster formats. Font sizes scale base_size theme; setting base_size = 12 6 × 4 inch figure produces fonts print legibly journal column width.

heading: 7.182 Assumptions
text: graphics device chosen format available (Cairo PNG anti-aliasing, ragg high-quality raster output, svglite compact SVG); modern R installations include verify specific format fails.

heading: 7.183 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

# Standard PDF for journal submission
ggsave("figure1.pdf", p, width = 6, height = 4, units = "in")

# High-resolution PNG
ggsave("figure1.png", p, width = 6, height = 4, units = "in", dpi = 300)

# Journal-standard column width (typically 85 mm for single column)
ggsave("figure1.tiff", p, width = 85, height = 60, units = "mm",
       dpi = 300, compression = "lzw")

# Vector SVG for editing
ggsave("figure1.svg", p, width = 6, height = 4)

heading: 7.184 Output & Results
text: Four export variants ggplot object: PDF journal submission, 300-dpi PNG web embedding, LZW-compressed TIFF 85 mm single-column width, SVG downstream editing. file identical content differs format-specific properties.

heading: 7.185 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (methods figure-preparation note): “figures rendered ggplot2 3.5 (base_size = 12) exported PDF (vector) TIFF (LZW-compressed, 300 dpi) single-column width (85 mm); raster previews use ragg device anti-aliased text.” Documenting export pipeline aids reproducibility pre-empts reviewer questions figure resolution.

heading: 7.186 Practical Tips
text: Always export final publication size; resizing saved figure word processor distorts fonts produces blurry raster edges.multi-panel figures composed patchwork, export composed object directly; save panels separately stitch editor.Use ragg::agg_png() (set options(ragg.background = "white")) anti-aliased PNG output handles UTF-8 emoji correctly.TIFF submissions, compression = "lzw" produces lossless files typically half size uncompressed; journals also accept "zip".Save small reproducible script next every published figure (figure1.R); regenerating figure six months later otherwise exercise archaeology.vector figures thousands overlapping elements (large hexbins, dense scatter), file sizes balloon; rasterise busy layer ggrastr::rasterise() keeping axes text vector hybrid output prints cleanly.

heading: 7.187 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 ggsave(); ragg fast, high-quality raster devices; svglite compact SVG; Cairo legacy anti-aliased rendering; ggrastr selectively rasterising vector layers otherwise vector exports.

heading: 7.190 Introduction
text: ggplot2 dominant plotting system R built principled grammar rather ad-hoc collection plot types. Every plot constructed seven building blocks: data, aesthetic mappings, geometric objects (geoms), statistical transformations, scales, coordinate systems, faceting. grammar internalised, vast library plot types reduces small set decisions common vocabulary.

heading: 7.191 Prerequisites
text: reader know R vectors, data frames, %>% |> pipe. prior ggplot2 knowledge assumed.

heading: 7.192 Theory
text: layered grammar expresses every plot stack layers added base specification. layer may override inherit data mappings base. seven components:Data: data frame whose rows observations whose columns variables.Mappings: assignments variables data visual aesthetics (x-position, y-position, colour, size, shape, alpha, etc.).Geoms: geometric objects drawn plot – points, lines, bars, polygons, text.Stats: statistical transformations applied data drawing (identity, binning histograms, smoothing trend lines, summary means error bars).Scales: functions translate data values aesthetic values – continuous scale numeric x-axis, discrete ordinal scale factor colour mapping.Coord: coordinate system, usually Cartesian occasionally polar, map projections, fixed aspect ratios.Facets: subplots defined one categorical variables, laid rows, columns, grid.plot rendered evaluating stats data, mapping results scales aesthetic values, drawing geoms chosen coordinate system, replicating whole thing across facets.

heading: 7.193 R Implementation
text: palmerpenguins package provides tidy biological dataset demonstration:single plot uses seven grammar components: data penguins, mappings send flipper_length_mm x, body_mass_g y, species colour; two geoms (point smooth) layered; lm stat fits linear trend within species; Brewer scale converts species factor colours; Cartesian coordinate system implicit; faceting sex produces panel per level.
code:
library(ggplot2)
library(palmerpenguins)

ggplot(data = penguins,
       mapping = aes(x = flipper_length_mm,
                     y = body_mass_g,
                     colour = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  facet_wrap(~ sex, nrow = 1) +
  labs(x = "Flipper length (mm)",
       y = "Body mass (g)",
       colour = "Species",
       title = "Body mass vs. flipper length by species and sex") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

heading: 7.194 Output & Results
text: plot shows three colour-coded point clouds two panels (one per sex). Linear trends drawn within species, Brewer “Set2” palette colour-blind-safe. Axis labels title provide context; legend placed bottom give maximum plot area data.

heading: 7.195 Interpretation
text: layered-grammar view turns plot design checklist:data?variables go aesthetics?geoms best display relationship?statistical transformations needed?scales (colour palette, axis transformation) best carry intended comparison?Coordinate system – usually Cartesian, check.Faceting – story separate across grouping variable?Going questions order produces plots intentional rather stumbled .

heading: 7.196 Practical Tips
text: Start geom_point() add layers; try build final plot one shot.Map aesthetics inside aes() depend data; set outside aes() (e.g. colour = "steelblue") constant.Use colour-blind-safe palettes default: scale_colour_viridis_d(), scale_colour_brewer(), colorblindr package.Export figures ggsave(filename, width = 7, height = 5, dpi = 300) get publication-grade output. journals accept PDF TIFF 300+ DPI.Avoid dual-y-axis plots unless two scales genuinely comparable; almost always misleading.

heading: 7.199 Introduction
text: heatmap displays matrix grid coloured cells, numerical values encoded colour. geom workhorse across genomics (gene-expression matrices), clinical research (cross-tabulations conditions measurements), classifier evaluation (confusion matrices), exploratory data analysis (correlation matrices). Two design choices dominate readability: colour palette (sequential, diverging, qualitative) row / column order, often comes hierarchical clustering rows, columns, .

heading: 7.200 Prerequisites
text: working understanding matrices, hierarchical clustering, colour palette selection (sequential vs diverging).

heading: 7.201 Theory
text: Two ecosystems handle heatmaps R:ggplot2 + geom_tile(): flexible requires long-format data manual handling clustering. Best heatmap one panel larger composition.pheatmap ComplexHeatmap: dedicated packages handle clustering, dendrograms, row/column annotations, z-scoring natively. Best heatmap figure customisation focus annotation tracks.colour scale must match data semantics: sequential (viridis, Blues) ordered values without meaningful zero, diverging (RdBu, BrBG) z-scored data quantity centred zero. Heatmaps one-sided palette applied centred data misleading visual midpoint match numerical midpoint.

heading: 7.202 Assumptions
text: matrix matrix-like structure rows columns admit sensible reordering. clustering, rows columns must comparable scales (pre-scaled per row).

heading: 7.203 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

# ggplot tile heatmap
d <- as.data.frame(scale(as.matrix(mtcars)))
d$car <- rownames(d)
d_long <- reshape2::melt(d, id.vars = "car")
ggplot(d_long, aes(variable, car, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red",
                       midpoint = 0) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

# pheatmap with hierarchical clustering
library(pheatmap)
pheatmap(scale(mtcars), clustering_method = "ward.D2",
         color = colorRampPalette(c("blue", "white", "red"))(100))

heading: 7.204 Output & Results
text: Two heatmaps z-scored mtcars matrix: hand-built ggplot2 tile heatmap (clustering, alphabetical row order) pheatmap version Ward’s hierarchical clustering rows columns plus accompanying dendrograms. clustered version reveals two clear groups cars distinguished efficiency-versus-power profile; unclustered version requires reader find structure unaided.

heading: 7.205 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Heatmap z-scored vehicle attributes (n = 32 cars × 11 variables); colour encodes z-score blue-white-red diverging scale centred zero, rows columns clustered Ward’s method Euclidean distance.” Always state scaling, distance metric, linkage method; results depend three.

heading: 7.206 Practical Tips
text: Centre scale rows (z-score per row) clustering whenever variables different scales; otherwise, large-magnitude variables dominate distance metric.Use diverging palette explicit midpoint z-scored data; one-sided sequential palettes collapse visual midpoint obscure structure.large matrices (thousands rows columns), ComplexHeatmap scales better pheatmap supports complex annotation tracks (categorical, continuous, mark-driven).Add side annotations (group, condition, batch) help readers spot whether clustering tracks biology technical artefacts.Cluster dimensions ordered; force dimension’s order biological temporal meaning matters.Use cluster_rows = FALSE (cluster_cols = FALSE) existing ordering carries information want preserved (e.g. chromosome position).

heading: 7.207 R Packages Used

heading: 7.210 Introduction
text: scatterplot hundred points informative; scatterplot hundred thousand points mostly black ink. \(n\) exceeds thousand, individual points overplot point hiding density structure: ridges, modes, non-linear boundaries vanish cloud. Hexbin plots solve dividing plane hexagonal cells, counting points per cell, colour-encoding count. result preserves joint distribution sample size renders quickly even millions observations.

heading: 7.211 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge scatterplots, basic 2D density estimation, ggplot2’s aesthetic mappings.

heading: 7.212 Theory
text: Hexagonal bins tile plane efficiently squares every hexagon six equidistant neighbours; isotropy reduces visual aliasing affects rectangular bins along diagonal density boundaries. ggplot2::geom_hex() calls hexbin package internally construct bins aggregate counts. bins argument sets resolution along axis; binwidth alternative fixes cell size data units. colour scale respect count distribution: linear scales roughly uniform counts, logarithmic scales (scale_fill_viridis_c(trans = "log")) highly skewed counts span orders magnitude.

heading: 7.213 Assumptions
text: x y continuous; categorical variables need different geom (heatmap, mosaic, jitter). plot assumes joint distribution inferential target, individual points — one-outliers, overlay labelled geom_point().

heading: 7.214 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(hexbin)

# Diamonds dataset: 54 000 observations
ggplot(diamonds, aes(carat, price)) +
  geom_hex(bins = 40) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c() +
  theme_minimal()

# Log-scale for skewed data
ggplot(diamonds, aes(carat, price)) +
  geom_hex(bins = 40) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(trans = "log") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.215 Output & Results
text: 40--40 hexbin grid showing joint distribution carat price across 54,000 diamonds. linear-scale version emphasises dense middle distribution; log-log version log-scaled fill reveals sparse tails — small low-priced diamonds large high-priced ones — invisible original scale.

heading: 7.216 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Hexbin density diamond carat versus price (n = 53,940), 40 bins per axis, fill log-count scale; axes log-transformed expose price-vs-carat relationship across full range.” Always state bin count transformations, since shape visual conclusion.

heading: 7.217 Practical Tips
text: bins = 30 bins = 60 sensible default tens thousands observations; finer grids reveal detail cost noisier counts per cell.Log-transform fill scale counts span orders magnitude; otherwise dense centre saturates palette tails vanish.square bins, use geom_bin2d(); choice hex square largely aesthetic, hex bins reduce diagonal-aliasing artefacts.Overlay smoother (geom_smooth(method = "gam")) top hexbin model-based summary; bin colour shows smoother well-supported.extremely large \(n\) (millions points), hexbin remains fast effective individual scatter crash renderer; underlying counts stored small array regardless input size.thousand points, prefer alpha-blended scatter 2D density contours; hexbin overkill small samples reads cluttered.

heading: 7.218 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 geom_hex() geom_bin2d(), hexbin underlying hexagonal binning, viridis perceptually uniform fill scales, ggdensity want combine binning model-based density contours.

heading: 7.221 Introduction
text: histogram simplest distributional display: bin range continuous variable, count observations bin, draw bars proportional counts. canonical first plot exploratory data analysis. also geom often misled settings — impression “skewness” “bimodality” can conjured hidden entirely choice bin width, good histogram one inspected several resolutions committed print.

heading: 7.222 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge continuous variables, difference counts densities, ggplot2 aesthetic mappings.

heading: 7.223 Theory
text: Three choices shape every histogram:Bin count (binwidth). bins hide modes skew; many exaggerate sampling noise. Common rules thumb: Sturges (\(\log_2 n + 1\), optimal Normal data moderate size), Freedman–Diaconis (binwidth \(= 2 \cdot \mathrm{IQR}/n^{1/3}\), robust outliers), Scott (\(3.5 \sigma / n^{1/3}\), optimised Normal data).Frequency vs density y-axis. Frequency (raw count) default; density (count divided binwidth, integrating 1) essential comparing groups different sample sizes overlaying density curve.Bin boundaries. first bin starts affects appearance, especially small samples; -called “boundary effect”, invisible dense data obvious sparse.shape impression depends three; histogram always reproduced least two bin counts conclusion drawn.

heading: 7.224 Assumptions
text: continuous (discrete--numerous) variable. Categorical data go bar charts; small ordinal scales (Likert) often look better bar charts even technically histograms apply.

heading: 7.225 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

# Basic histogram
ggplot(mtcars, aes(mpg)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "#2A9D8F", colour = "white") +
  theme_minimal()

# Density y-axis for group comparison
ggplot(iris, aes(Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)),
                 position = "identity", alpha = 0.5, bins = 20) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

# Automatic binwidth via Freedman-Diaconis
ggplot(diamonds, aes(price)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 2 * IQR(diamonds$price) / nrow(diamonds)^(1/3),
                 fill = "#2A9D8F", colour = "white") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.226 Output & Results
text: Three histograms: 32-row mtcars mileage 15 bins, three-species iris overlay density y-axis (groups comparable despite identical sample sizes), Freedman–Diaconis-binwidth view diamond prices handles long right tail gracefully default 30-bin choice.

heading: 7.227 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Distribution fuel efficiency (mpg) across 32 cars, 15 equal-width bins covering range 10–35 mpg.” Always state bin count binwidth caption; histogram without information impossible reproduce.

heading: 7.228 Practical Tips
text: Always inspect several bin counts committing one; modes survive doubling bins real, modes disappear artefacts.Use after_stat(density) (formerly ..density..) overlaying multiple groups different sizes; raw counts conflate distribution shape sample-size differences.long right tails (waiting times, prices, gene expression), log-transform x-axis (scale_x_log10()) binning; resulting histogram reveals structure invisible linear scale.small samples (\(n < 30\)) prefer dot plot strip plot; histogram 30 points binned 10 bars mostly empty.Overlay geom_density() (aes(y = after_stat(density))) smooth summary complements bins; two together communicate discrete bins smooth shape.Avoid mixing colour (group) fill aesthetics column drives ; pick one let legend speak .

heading: 7.232 Introduction
text: plotly translates ggplot2 figures separate JavaScript engine, ggiraph keeps original ggplot2 rendering adds SVG event handlers individual elements — points, bars, polygons. result interactivity preserves ggplot2’s native typography, themes, palettes, sharp vector output scales cleanly print screen alike. Tooltips, click events, hover highlighting, Shiny linkage come small set *_interactive geoms mirror ggplot2 counterparts.

heading: 7.233 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2 output environment supports HTML — Quarto, RMarkdown, Shiny, browser-based viewer.

heading: 7.234 Theory
text: interactive geom (geom_point_interactive(), geom_col_interactive(), geom_polygon_interactive(), etc.) accepts additional aesthetics top ggplot2 base:tooltip: text (HTML) shown hover.data_id: identifier used cross-element highlighting selection.onclick: arbitrary JavaScript invoked element clicked.girafe() function wraps resulting plot HTML widget; opts_* helpers tune hover CSS, selection behaviour, zoom, toolbar visibility, download options. output plain SVG, every interactive plot remains standalone vector graphic can exported, embedded, re-styled external CSS.

heading: 7.235 Assumptions
text: output target supports HTML SVG rendering. PDF PNG output, ggiraph falls back static SVG snapshot — interactivity lost typography preserved, unlike plotly’s rasterised exports.

heading: 7.236 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(ggiraph)

p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg,
                        tooltip = rownames(mtcars),
                        data_id = rownames(mtcars))) +
  geom_point_interactive(size = 3, colour = "#2A9D8F") +
  theme_minimal()

girafe(ggobj = p,
       options = list(opts_hover(css = "fill:#F4A261;stroke:black"),
                      opts_selection(type = "multiple")))

heading: 7.237 Output & Results
text: interactive SVG scatterplot hovering point highlights configured CSS rule, clicking selects , shift-clicking adds selection. Inside Shiny, selection propagates input$<plot_id>_selected reactive, enabling brushed cross-filtering charts tables.

heading: 7.238 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Figure 2 (rendered ggiraph): point shows vehicle; hover reveals model name selected points propagate linked summary table .” Mention technology selection semantics caption — readers know whether interactivity decorative part analytical workflow.

heading: 7.239 Practical Tips
text: geom_*_interactive() accepts arguments non-interactive parent plus new aesthetics, refactoring existing plot one-word swap per layer.Use data_id group elements highlight together (points one country, bars one facet); SVG event system propagates data_id automatically.Inside Shiny, swap plotOutput() renderPlot() girafeOutput() renderGirafe(); selection hover events become reactive inputs without additional plumbing.Customise hover selection CSS opts_hover(css = ...) opts_selection(css = ...); plot can present different interactive feedback depending context.Avoid ggiraph plots many tens thousands elements; SVG renders every element DOM node, browsers slow beyond thousand. large data, hexbin canvas-rendered alternatives (scattermore, plotly scattergl) scale better.static publication, ggplot object renders without modification ggsave() — write interactive code normal ggplot get versions free.

heading: 7.243 Introduction
text: static figure journal prints; interactive figure colleague needs exploring result screen. plotly::ggplotly() converts existing ggplot object HTML widget supports hover tooltips, drag--zoom, panning, selection brushing — without requiring JavaScript. result fits naturally Quarto RMarkdown reports, Shiny dashboards, standalone HTML pages, gives reviewers collaborators way interrogate data without exporting CSVs.

heading: 7.244 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2 (geoms, aesthetics, themes) output context supports HTML rendering — Quarto, RMarkdown, Shiny, RStudio’s Viewer pane.

heading: 7.245 Theory
text: ggplotly() walks structure ggplot object — layers, scales, aesthetic mappings — translates component corresponding plotly.js primitives. Hover tooltips default whatever aesthetic mappings scope; custom tooltips come mapping aes(text = ...) passing tooltip = "text" ggplotly(). translation faithful common geoms (point, line, bar, ribbon, density), partial statistical layers (geom_smooth, faceting free scales), weakest non-standard extensions may need drop native plot_ly() syntax.

heading: 7.246 Assumptions
text: output environment renders HTML. Static targets (PDF, PNG, EPS) lose interactivity, journal submission retain original ggplot re-render ggsave(); plotly screens, paper.

heading: 7.247 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(plotly)

p <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl),
                        text = rownames(mtcars))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")

# Link two plots
p2 <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(hp, mpg)) + geom_point()
subplot(ggplotly(p), ggplotly(p2))

heading: 7.248 Output & Results
text: interactive HTML scatter marker shows car name hover, legend clickable toggle cylinder groups, drag-zoom narrows axes. subplot() arranges multiple ggplotly objects side side synchronised axes — useful cross-filtering related views.

heading: 7.249 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Figure 2 interactive scatterplot rendered plotly::ggplotly(); hovering marker shows car name cylinder count, clicking legend entry toggles corresponding group.” Mention technology figure caption interactivity carries part figure’s meaning static fallbacks (screenshot print version) mistaken whole story.

Render static ggplot ggplotly version source; way printed online versions stay sync.custom tooltips, include variables matter: many columns cursor become wall text. aes(text = paste("Name:", rownames(data), "
HP:", hp)) common pattern.subplot() combines multiple ggplotly objects optional shared axes; many panels, build via lapply() combine .call(subplot, ...).Performance degrades beyond thousand points; switch plot_ly(..., type = "scattergl") WebGL rendering working tens thousands.Inside Shiny, swap plotOutput() / renderPlot() plotlyOutput() / renderPlotly() rest app remains unchanged.Use config(displayModeBar = FALSE) suppress toolbar embedded reports toolbar clutter feature.

heading: 7.254 Introduction
text: Line plots connect ordered observations exploit powerful perceptual cue: human eye reads slope rate change effortlessly. default geom time-series, dose-response curves, individual-trajectory (“spaghetti”) displays, cumulative distributions, context x-axis carries meaningful sequence. simplicity makes effective also makes dangerous x-axis lacks order — connecting unordered categories line implies trajectory exist misleads readers seeing trends noise.

heading: 7.255 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2’s aesthetic mappings, clear distinction continuous, ordered, unordered variables, understanding group aesthetics control points get joined.

heading: 7.256 Theory
text: geom_line() joins points order appear within group defined group aesthetic; default groups come discrete colour, fill, linetype mapping. paired repeated-measures data, group aesthetic must set explicitly subject identifier individual trajectories connected, observations across subjects. Variants include:geom_step() cumulative quantities ECDFs Kaplan–Meier survival curves, underlying process changes observed events.geom_path() trajectories ordered row position rather x-aesthetic, useful state-space plots.geom_ribbon() paired line confidence prediction bands.many overlapping lines, alpha-blending faceting prevents “spaghetti pile” hides individual trajectories.

heading: 7.257 Assumptions
text: x-axis values ordered meaningfully — time, dose, position, rank. Connecting nominal categories line presentation error, stylistic choice.

heading: 7.258 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

# Single time series
ggplot(economics, aes(date, unemploy)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2A9D8F", linewidth = 1) +
  theme_minimal()

# Multiple groups
ggplot(airquality, aes(Day, Ozone, group = Month, colour = factor(Month))) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

# Paired trajectories (pre/post style)
df <- data.frame(
  subject = rep(1:20, each = 2),
  time    = rep(c("pre", "post"), 20),
  value   = c(rnorm(20, 50, 10), rnorm(20, 55, 10))
)
ggplot(df, aes(time, value, group = subject)) +
  geom_line(colour = "grey70") +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  theme_minimal()

# Step line (cumulative)
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt)) +
  stat_ecdf(geom = "step", colour = "#2A9D8F", linewidth = 1) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.259 Output & Results
text: Four illustrative line plots: long-running unemployment series, monthly ozone levels coloured month, individual subject trajectories pre post, empirical cumulative distribution function car weights step line. shows different idiomatic use line geom.

heading: 7.260 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Individual subject responses pre post connected grey lines, overlaid points indicating measurement times; group-mean changes (shown) reported Table 1.” Always describe unit analysis line represents (subject, country, replicate); line plot without context ambiguous.

heading: 7.261 Practical Tips
text: Set group aesthetic explicitly paired repeated-measures data; default group inferred colour linetype rarely matches intended unit.Add geom_point() top geom_line() x-values sparse individual observations remain visible; pure lines hide irregular sampling.uncertainty bands, layer geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), alpha = 0.3) line line draws top.Use geom_step() ECDFs survival curves — step functions, smooth ones, smooth interpolation misrepresents underlying mathematics.dozens overlapping trajectories, lower alpha (0.2–0.4) add single bold line group mean median; pure spaghetti loses readability 30–40 trajectories.y-axis spans orders magnitude multiplicative growth, use scale_y_log10() constant-rate growth reads straight line.

heading: 7.265 Introduction
text: pairs plot — also called scatterplot matrix — shows every pairwise scatter set continuous variables single grid figure. Modern variants enrich matrix marginal histograms densities along diagonal correlation coefficients (pairwise summaries) upper triangle. result single efficient exploratory diagnostic small--moderate set variables: surfaces non-linearity, outliers, subgroup separation, multicollinearity one pass.

heading: 7.266 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge scatter plots, correlation, group-coloured aesthetics; familiarity GGally package’s extensions ggplot2.

heading: 7.267 Theory
text: \(p\) variables matrix \(p^2\) cells. diagonal shows marginal distribution variable (histogram, density, labelled bar factors). lower triangle shows pairwise scatter plots; upper triangle shows summary statistics — commonly Pearson, Spearman, Kendall correlations, optionally annotated significance. GGally::ggpairs() canonical implementation, arguments lower, upper, diag accepting list cell-type specifications. Group structure added via aes(colour = group) propagates every panel provides immediate insight -group differences.

heading: 7.268 Assumptions
text: pairwise cell inherits assumptions relevant geom: scatter plots assume continuous data; correlation cells assume meaningful linear (rank) association. Categorical variables displayed bar charts contingency tables -diagonal cells.

heading: 7.269 R Implementation
code:
library(GGally)

# Basic pairs plot
ggpairs(iris, columns = 1:4, aes(colour = Species, alpha = 0.5)) +
  theme_minimal()

# Custom lower-triangle geoms
ggpairs(mtcars, columns = c("mpg", "wt", "hp", "qsec"),
        lower = list(continuous = "smooth"))

heading: 7.270 Output & Results
text: Two pairs plots: four-variable matrix iris dataset coloured species, four-variable matrix mtcars numerical columns LOESS smoothers lower triangle. first reveals species-driven separation across nearly every pairwise plot; second emphasises curvilinear weight–mpg horsepower–quarter-mile relationships single scatter obscure.

heading: 7.271 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Pairs plot four iris measurements (n = 150), coloured species; diagonal shows kernel densities, lower triangle shows pairwise scatter, upper triangle shows Pearson correlations within species (GGally::ggpairs).” Always state correlation type used upper triangle geom chosen lower; defaults differ versions.

heading: 7.272 Practical Tips
text: Keep matrix 3–6 variables; cells shrink quadratically become unreadable beyond . variables, switch correlation heatmap focused PCA.Add aes(colour = group) surface -group structure; colour propagates diagonal densities lower-triangle scatters automatically.large \(n\), replace scatter cells hexbin via lower = list(continuous = "hex"); 50--50 grid scales millions points individual scatters fail.diag = list(continuous = "barDiag") shows histograms instead densities; useful discrete-looking continuous variables (e.g. integer scores).Save pairs plots large dimensions (ggsave(..., width = 10, height = 10, dpi = 300)); shrinking single column makes cells unreadable.posterior simulation diagnostics, bayesplot::mcmc_pairs() Bayesian-flavoured analogue overlays divergent transitions -diagonal cells.

heading: 7.273 R Packages Used

heading: 7.276 Introduction
text: Almost every journal-worthy figure composition: two panels arranged shared legends, alphabetical tags, unifying title, aligned axes. Building figures hand gridExtra cowplot verbose brittle layout changes. patchwork package replaces workflow arithmetic-like operators mirror way figure read: + “next ”, / “”, | “row”. Combined plot_layout() relative sizes plot_annotation() shared titles tags, now standard tool assembling multi-panel ggplot2 figures.

heading: 7.277 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2 — geoms, scales, themes — idea combinations plots actually communicate (rather merely fill space).

heading: 7.278 Theory
text: patchwork treats ggplot object supports small algebra:p1 + p2 places two plots side side equal width.p1 / p2 stacks vertically.Parentheses control nesting: (p1 | p2) / p3 puts p1 p2 horizontally p3 spanning .plot_layout(guides = "collect") deduplicates shared legends, moving single copy figure margin.plot_annotation(title = ..., tag_levels = "") adds global title panel tags (, B, C, …) automatically.& theme_minimal() applies theme every panel composition simultaneously, useful individual panels built without consistent theme.package handles axis alignment automatically plots share axis ranges, avoids tiny horizontal offsets bedevil hand-aligned multi-panel figures.

heading: 7.279 Assumptions
text: statistical assumptions; practical constraint combined panels must remain legible final printed size, usually means three four panels per row .

heading: 7.280 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(patchwork)

p1 <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg))       + geom_point() + theme_minimal()
p2 <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(factor(cyl)))   + geom_bar()   + theme_minimal()
p3 <- ggplot(mtcars, aes(hp))             + geom_histogram(bins = 15) + theme_minimal()

# Two side by side
p1 + p2

# Stacked
p1 / p2

# Complex layout
(p1 | p2) / p3 +
  plot_annotation(title = "mtcars overview", tag_levels = "A")

# Collect shared legends
p1 + p2 + plot_layout(guides = "collect")

heading: 7.281 Output & Results
text: Four progressively complex layouts: two horizontal panels, two vertical panels, 2--1-arrangement global title panel tags , B, C, side--side composition one shared legend collected right margin. Saving ggsave() produces single file specified dimensions, ready journal submission.

heading: 7.282 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Figure 2 (assembled patchwork): ) weight vs miles-per-gallon, B) cylinder distribution, C) horsepower distribution; panels share common minimal theme single legend.” Caption-level credit composition tool unnecessary, tags one-line description panel essential multi-panel figure.

heading: 7.283 Practical Tips
text: Use plot_layout(widths = c(2, 1)) give one panel twice horizontal space another; heights vertically.tag_levels = "" produces , B, C tags; "1" produces 1, 2, 3; "" produces lowercase Roman numerals.& theme_minimal() (note &) applies theme globally every panel; + applies recently added plot.many small panels structure, prefer facet_wrap() inside single ggplot rather composing dozens plots; faceting efficient produces aligned axes default.Always save composed plot single file via ggsave(); assembling separate exports vector editor produces fragile artwork.shared legend many entries, place right via plot_layout(guides = "collect") & theme(legend.position = "right") rather letting stack vertically inside one panel.

heading: 7.287 Introduction
text: raincloud plot modern alternative bar--error-bar chart comparing continuous distributions across groups. Proposed Allen colleagues 2019, combines three pieces information one panel: half-violin showing kernel density (“cloud”), jittered raw observations (“rain”), small boxplot encoding median quartiles. result preserves distributional information bars throw away — modes, skew, outliers, sample sizes — still presenting central tendency drives reporting decisions.

heading: 7.288 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge boxplots, violin plots, kernel density estimation, ggplot2 aesthetic mappings.

heading: 7.289 Theory
text: raincloud plot stacks three layers per group:Half-violin drawn one side (stat_halfeye() ggdist geom_violinh() gghalves); displays density shape.Jittered raw points (geom_point() position_jitter() ggbeeswarm::geom_beeswarm()); shows individual observations.Narrow boxplot hidden outliers (geom_boxplot(width = 0.12, outlier.shape = NA)); communicates median, IQR, whisker range.coord_flip() rotates layout horizontal, makes long category names easier read. half-violin opens away data column cloud, rain, boxplot sit side side rather stacked.

heading: 7.290 Assumptions
text: continuous outcome categorical (ordered) grouping variable. group enough observations (least 20–30) kernel density meaningful; , drop half-violin show points alone.

heading: 7.291 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(ggdist)

ggplot(iris, aes(Species, Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5, justification = -0.2, .width = 0, point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.12, outlier.shape = NA, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_point(position = position_jitter(width = 0.05, seed = 1),
             size = 1.3, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.292 Output & Results
text: horizontal raincloud plot iris dataset: species occupies one row density rendered half-violin right, small boxplot middle, jittered raw points left. full distribution, summary statistics, individual observations visible simultaneously.

heading: 7.293 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Raincloud plots sepal length species (n = 50 per group): half-violin shows kernel-density estimates (adjust = 0.5), jittered points show individual observations, boxplot summarises median IQR. Setosa well-separated two species, overlap considerably.” Mention bandwidth (adjust) used; shapes visible structure cloud.

heading: 7.294 Practical Tips
text: Use coord_flip() long group names; vertical raincloud plots crowd labels waste space.Drop boxplot layer group sizes small (< 30); adds visual clutter without summarising much.large per-group \(n\) (thousands), replace jittered points single hexbin column alpha-blended strip; thousands dots stack noise.Set outlier.shape = NA boxplot individual outliers appear twice (rain, flagged box whiskers).Use ggbeeswarm::geom_beeswarm() instead position_jitter() collisions matter; produces tidy spread along category axis.ggdist modern implementation integrates Bayesian posterior summaries; gghalves older alternative still active use.

heading: 7.295 R Packages Used
text: ggdist stat_halfeye(), stat_dots(), slabinterval geoms; ggplot2 underlying grammar; gghalves older alternative half-violins; ggbeeswarm collision-free jittered rain layers.

heading: 7.298 Introduction
text: ridge plot stacks kernel-density curves multiple groups vertically small overlap, evoking cover 1979 Joy Division album (hence alternative name “joy plot”). idiom particularly effective comparing shape distributions across many ordered groups — months year, dose levels, time periods, ranked categories — panel separate density plots sparse violin plot lose detail overlap.

heading: 7.299 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge kernel density estimation, grouped distributional comparisons, ggplot2’s aesthetic mappings.

heading: 7.300 Theory
text: group’s density curve computed independently offset vertically group’s position y-axis. scale argument controls much curves overlap: scale 1 means tallest curve group exactly reaches next baseline, values 1 produce overlap values 1 produce gaps. fill aesthetic can encode group (categorical fill) mapped x-value via after_stat(x) produce gradient fills highlight quantiles thresholds. horizontal axis carries variable interest; vertical axis carries group identity, ideally ordered.

heading: 7.301 Assumptions
text: continuous outcome variable, categorical (ordered) grouping variable, least dozen observations per group kernel density estimate meaningful. fewer 20–30 observations per group, ridge plots can produce noisy, misleading curves.

heading: 7.302 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(ggridges)

# Classic ridge plot
ggplot(iris, aes(Sepal.Length, Species, fill = Species)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_ridges()

# With gradient fill encoding quantiles
ggplot(diamonds, aes(price, cut, fill = after_stat(x))) +
  stat_density_ridges(geom = "density_ridges_gradient", scale = 2) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c() +
  scale_x_continuous(trans = "log10") +
  theme_ridges()

heading: 7.303 Output & Results
text: Two ridge plots: iris data showing three species along sepal-length axis translucent fills, diamonds data showing price distribution per cut log scale gradient fill encodes price . second example particularly good use ridges — five ordered groups, log-transformed x, gradient fill emphasises right tail.

heading: 7.304 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Density log-price per cut 53,940 diamonds, computed via kernel density estimation default bandwidth; fill encodes log price highlight quantile structure across cuts.” Mention bandwidth transformations shape visible structure.

heading: 7.305 Practical Tips
text: Choose scale deliberately: 1.5–2 typical sweet spot 6–10 groups; tighter ranges work fewer groups, wider ranges many.Order groups meaningfully (forcats::fct_reorder(), fct_relevel()); alphabetical ordering rarely communicates structure want show.highly skewed distributions, log-transform x-axis (scale_x_continuous(trans = "log10")); ridges capture multiplicative differences across groups.theme_ridges() removes axis lines adjusts margins suit stacked densities; pair sequential qualitative palette.Ridge plots struggle groups (two three) violin plots paired density plots communicate better; reach ridges six groups need compared simultaneously.groups different sample sizes, consider showing density comparable scale (scale = 0.95) small group dominate figure visually.

heading: 7.306 R Packages Used
text: ggridges geom_density_ridges(), stat_density_ridges(), theme_ridges(); ggplot2 underlying grammar; viridis RColorBrewer sequential qualitative fill scales appropriate ordered group comparisons.

heading: 7.309 Introduction
text: receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve traces binary classifier’s sensitivity \(1 -\) specificity decision threshold sweeps one end score range . canonical diagnostic-performance figure medicine machine learning, summarising trade-true-positive false-positive rates without committing single threshold. area curve (AUC) condenses curve one number, equal probability classifier ranks randomly chosen positive case higher randomly chosen negative one.

heading: 7.310 Prerequisites
text: working understanding sensitivity, specificity, decision thresholds, difference calibration discrimination classifier evaluation.

heading: 7.311 Theory
text: continuous score \(s\) binary outcome \(y\), ROC curve plots\[\bigl(\mathrm{FPR}(t), \mathrm{TPR}(t)\bigr) = \bigl(P(s > t \mid y = 0), P(s > t \mid y = 1)\bigr)\]thresholds \(t\). perfect classifier passes \((0, 1)\); random one follows diagonal. AUC equals Mann-Whitney U statistic divided sample sizes; lies \([0, 1]\) \(0.5\) representing chance \(1\) perfect rank-ordering. Conventional benchmarks: AUC \(> 0.7\) acceptable, \(> 0.8\) good, \(> 0.9\) excellent. DeLong’s test compares two AUCs correlated samples.

heading: 7.312 Assumptions
text: binary outcome continuous (ordinal) score. ROC curve invariant monotone transformations score, calibration irrelevant curve ; thresholding decisions, however, depend calibration.

heading: 7.313 R Implementation
code:
library(pROC); library(plotROC); library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
disease <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)
score   <- rnorm(n, mean = disease, sd = 1)

# pROC
roc_obj <- roc(disease, score, quiet = TRUE)
auc(roc_obj); ci.auc(roc_obj)
plot(roc_obj, legacy.axes = TRUE, col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2)

# ggplot via plotROC
df <- data.frame(D = disease, score = score)
ggplot(df, aes(d = D, m = score)) +
  geom_roc(labelsize = 3, labelround = 2, colour = "#2A9D8F", size = 1) +
  style_roc() +
  labs(title = paste0("AUC = ", round(auc(roc_obj), 3)))

heading: 7.314 Output & Results
text: monotone curve rising \((0, 0)\) toward \((1, 1)\), optional threshold labels selected points AUC reported either title via ci.auc() 95 % confidence interval. plotROC package adds ggplot2-native styling; pROC::plot() produces fast base-R version.

heading: 7.315 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “classifier achieved AUC 0.85 (95 % CI 0.79 0.91), indicating good discrimination; Youden-optimal threshold 0.42, sensitivity 0.78 specificity 0.81.” Always pair AUC confidence interval least one operating point readers can judge clinical utility.

heading: 7.316 Practical Tips
text: Report AUC 95 % CI, just point estimate; bootstrap interval (ci.auc(roc_obj)) handles typical correlated-sample structure.Compare two ROC curves DeLong’s test (pROC::roc.test); pairwise AUC differences \(p\)-values standard way claim one classifier outperforms another.Avoid smoothed ROC curves (smooth = TRUE) primary reporting; smoothing hides finite-sample irregularities. Show smoothed raw side side smoothing aids interpretation.severe class imbalance, precision-recall curves often communicate operating-point trade-offs better ROC; report positives rare.Always draw diagonal reference line (\(y = x\)); without , readers judge far chance classifier .threshold selection, mark Youden index, equal-error-rate point, clinically motivated sensitivity / specificity target curve quote threshold.

heading: 7.317 R Packages Used
text: pROC ROC objects, AUC, confidence intervals, DeLong’s test; plotROC ggplot2-native ROC layers (geom_roc(), style_roc()); ROCit alternative API bootstrap CIs; yardstick tidy classifier metrics within tidymodels ecosystem.

heading: 7.320 Introduction
text: Scales coordinates two layers ggplot2 turn raw data visual marks page. Scales map data values visual properties — numbers positions, factor levels colours, ordered values sizes — coordinates describe geometry plot area — Cartesian, polar, log, fixed aspect ratio. figure customisation happens scale level; coordinates change less often, understanding difference lets switch zooming, transforming, projecting without breaking rest plot.

heading: 7.321 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2 aesthetics geoms, basic familiarity aes() mappings flow scale choices.

heading: 7.322 Theory
text: Every aesthetic family scales:scale_x_continuous, scale_x_discrete, scale_x_log10, scale_x_date, scale_x_datetime x; scale_y_* y.scale_colour_* scale_fill_* colour fill, continuous, discrete, viridis, brewer, gradient, manual variants.scale_size_*, scale_alpha_*, scale_shape_*, scale_linetype_* remaining aesthetics.scale exposes breaks, labels, limits, trans, (continuous) expand arguments control tick placement, label formatting, axis range, monotone transformations, padding.Coordinates change geometry panel:coord_cartesian() default; supplying xlim / ylim zooms without dropping data.coord_flip() swaps x y axes scales applied.coord_polar() produces pie charts radial layouts.coord_fixed(ratio = 1) enforces equal aspect ratio, essential maps structural diagrams.coord_sf() coord_map() apply geographic projections.crucial distinction: scale_x_continuous(limits = c(, b)) filters observations outside [, b] recomputes statistics remaining data; coord_cartesian(xlim = c(, b)) zooms without filtering, leaving statistics untouched.

heading: 7.323 Assumptions
text: data supplied valid chosen scale (negative values log scales, fractional dates date scales).

heading: 7.324 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(scales)

# Log-scale y with sensible breaks
ggplot(diamonds, aes(carat, price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.1) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::dollar_format()) +
  theme_minimal()

# Manual colour and custom axis formatting
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("4" = "#2A9D8F",
                                 "6" = "#F4A261",
                                 "8" = "#6A4C93")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:5, labels = paste0(1:5, "K lbs")) +
  theme_minimal()

# Coord flip: horizontal bar chart
ggplot(mpg, aes(class)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "#2A9D8F") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()

# Zoom without filtering
ggplot(diamonds, aes(carat, price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 2)) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.325 Output & Results
text: Four illustrative plots: log-scale price axis currency formatting, manual colour scale custom x-axis labels, horizontal bar chart via coord_flip(), zoomed-scatter preserves full underlying dataset accurate statistical layers.

heading: 7.326 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Price shown base-10 log scale (scale_y_log10); linear relationship log-price carat reflects approximately power-law pricing structure.” Note non-default scale coordinate figure caption — log-scale axis easy overlook can mislead readers expecting linear interpretation.

heading: 7.327 Practical Tips
text: Reach scale_y_log10() whenever y-axis spans orders magnitude; constant-rate growth reads straight line.Use coord_cartesian() zoom — scale_x_continuous(limits = ...) removes filtered data recomputes smoother summary, can mislead.Always label axis units; “Weight” alone ambiguous, “Weight (1000 lbs)” informative.date axes, use scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") pick break frequencies match publication width.Reverse scale scale_x_reverse() (continuous) scale_x_discrete(limits = rev) (categorical); avoid hand-flipping data frames.diverging numeric scales centred zero, combine scale_fill_gradient2() explicit low, mid, high colours; avoid one-sided palettes misrepresent symmetry.

heading: 7.328 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 scale coordinate functions; scales label formatters (dollar_format, percent_format, comma_format, label_log); ggh4x nested axis labels per-panel scale control; cowplot theme_cowplot() paired scales publication look.

heading: 7.331 Introduction
text: scatter plot default visualisation relationship two continuous variables: observation becomes point, position encodes value pair, eye reads correlation, clustering, outliers directly. first graphic make whenever regression correlation contemplated. simplicity also trap: modest sample sizes scatter informative, large \(n\) overplotting hides density, small \(n\) points easy misread strong relationship disappears reasonable significance test.

heading: 7.332 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2’s aesthetic mappings idea bivariate distribution differs marginal univariate summaries.

heading: 7.333 Theory
text: core geom geom_point(), point size, shape, alpha, colour controllable aesthetics. Overplotting — multiple observations stacking pixel — universal challenge \(n\) exceeds hundred. Standard remedies:Alpha blending: lower per-point opacity density translates colour intensity.Jittering: position_jitter() adds small random offset; useful one axes discrete.Hexagonal binning: geom_hex() divides plane hexagons coloured count; appropriate \(n \ge 5{,}000\).Density colouring: ggpointdensity::geom_pointdensity() keeps individual points colours local density.2D density contours: geom_density_2d() stat_density_2d_filled() overlay smooth level sets top sparse scatter.Trend layers come geom_smooth(), LOESS default exploratory work method = "lm" ("gam", "glm") parametric fits.

heading: 7.334 Assumptions
text: x y continuous; one categorical variable encoded colour shape rather placed axis. statistical assumptions smoother fitted line added.

heading: 7.335 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(ggpointdensity)

# Simple scatter
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, colour = "#2A9D8F") +
  theme_minimal()

# Overplotted data with alpha
ggplot(diamonds, aes(carat, price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.05) +
  theme_minimal()

# Point density colouring
ggplot(diamonds[sample(nrow(diamonds), 2000), ], aes(carat, price)) +
  geom_pointdensity() +
  scale_colour_viridis_c() +
  theme_minimal()

# Scatter with trend line
ggplot(mtcars, aes(wt, mpg)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", colour = "#F4A261") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.336 Output & Results
text: Four scatter plots showing idea different scales: small clean scatter 32-row mtcars, density-revealing alpha-blended scatter 53,940-row diamonds, density-coloured point--point view intermediate sample, small scatter OLS smoother confidence band overlay.

heading: 7.337 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Higher carat values associated higher price (Spearman \(\rho = 0.93\), \(n = 53{,}940\)); relationship approximately linear log-log scale alpha-blended scatter reveals heteroscedasticity right tail.” Always inspect scatter computing correlation: non-linearity, subgroup structure, outliers visible hidden single summary number.

heading: 7.338 Practical Tips
text: \(n > 1{,}000\) use alpha (0.1–0.3 typical); \(n > 10{,}000\) switch hexbin density colouring outright.Always label axes units; “Weight” ambiguous, “Weight (1000 lbs)” informative.Add smoother relationship plausible; -eager LOESS curves random scatter create illusion structure.paired samples, connect points geom_line(aes(group = subject)) within-subject change visible alongside cross-sectional spread.Use stat_ellipse() draw 95 % data ellipses group boundaries colour-coded clusters need extra visual hint.Avoid 3D scatter plots print; rotation kills depth perception third dimension better encoded colour, size, faceting variable.

heading: 7.339 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 geom_point(), geom_smooth(), stat_ellipse(); ggpointdensity density-coloured scatter; hexbin geom_hex() large \(n\); ggrepel labelling individual points without overlap.

heading: 7.342 Introduction
text: bar chart needs display two categorical variables , three layout options compete: stacking sub-bars within main bar (showing totals composition), placing side side (showing within-category comparison), normalising 100 % (showing proportions independent total). layout answers different question, choosing right one important tweaking colours fonts. position argument geom_bar() geom_col() selects three.

heading: 7.343 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge bar charts, distinction geom_bar() (counts) geom_col() (pre-computed values), ggplot2 aesthetic mappings.

heading: 7.344 Theory
text: position = "stack" default bar geoms fill aesthetic: sub-bars stack vertically inside main bar, bar’s total height equals sum sub-counts, segments encode composition.position = "dodge" (position_dodge(width = ...) finer control) places sub-bars side side, one per fill level, x-tick. sub-bar’s height equals count, making within-x comparison direct.position = "fill" stacks normalises every main bar height 1 (100 %), hiding absolute totals expose proportional structure across x-axis.choice maps directly onto analytical question: “big group?” → stack; “subgroup compare subgroup B within main category?” → dodge; “proportion subgroup shift across main categories?” → fill.

heading: 7.345 Assumptions
text: Two categorical variables (one categorical one ordered) count summary makes sense add across sub-bars.

heading: 7.346 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

d <- data.frame(
  stage = factor(rep(c("I", "II", "III"), 2)),
  sex   = factor(rep(c("F", "M"), each = 3)),
  count = c(30, 45, 25, 25, 50, 30)
)

# Stacked
ggplot(d, aes(stage, count, fill = sex)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

# Dodged
ggplot(d, aes(stage, count, fill = sex)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

# Filled (proportions)
ggplot(d, aes(stage, count, fill = sex)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.347 Output & Results
text: Three views disease-stage--sex data: stacked bar showing total cases per stage decomposed sex, dodged bar enabling direct sex-vs-sex comparison within stage, filled bar showing female--male proportion balance across stages y-axis percentage.

heading: 7.348 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Stacked bars show total case counts per stage decomposed sex; dodged bars display male female counts side side direct comparison within stage; filled bars normalise 100 % expose sex proportion across stages.” Always describe layout chosen ; common source bar-chart misreading.

heading: 7.349 Practical Tips
text: Stacked bars communicate totals cleanly obscure within-stage comparisons segments first sub-bar start zero — readers judge lengths accurately.Dodged bars need adequate bar width stage spacing; three fill levels, figure widens quickly benefits coord_flip() horizontal layouts.Filled bars hide absolute counts entirely; pair text table summary giving per-stage \(n\).four fill levels, stacking dodging become hard read; consider heatmap, faceted small-multiple, Sankey diagram instead.Order fill levels deliberately (forcats::fct_relevel()); legend reads top--bottom bar reads bottom--top, visual flow depends factor order.Use geom_text(aes(label = count), position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5)) label segments counts total alone enough.

heading: 7.350 R Packages Used
text: ggplot2 geom_bar(), geom_col(), position_* family; forcats factor reordering fill levels; scales percent comma label formatters; ggalluvial flow-style alternatives stacked bars three levels become confusing.
