heading: 7.353 Introduction
text: Kaplan-Meier survival curve recognisable figure clinical biostatistics. estimates probability surviving (remaining event-free) past time point displays result step function dropping every observed event. publication-grade KM plot rarely just curve, however — carries risk table beneath panel showing many subjects remain landmark time, log-rank \(p\)-value annotating -group comparison, 95 % confidence bands shaded behind curve. survminer package combines single ggsurvplot() call works directly survfit objects survival package.

heading: 7.354 Prerequisites
text: working understanding survival analysis, Kaplan-Meier estimator, censoring, log-rank test. Familiarity ggplot2 helps customising resulting object.

heading: 7.355 Theory
text: KM estimator \[\hat S(t) = \prod_{t_i \le t} \!\left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right),\]\(d_i\) number events \(n_i\) number risk just time \(t_i\). plot displays \(\hat S(t)\) y-axis, time x-axis, steps event tick-marks (shorter steps) censoring times. Multiple groups overlaid different colours, risk-table panel shows \(n_i\) chosen landmark times group. log-rank \(p\)-value summarises -group difference proportional hazards.

heading: 7.356 Assumptions
text: Right-censored survival data, non-informative censoring (censoring mechanism depend prognosis), — log-rank annotation — approximately proportional hazards across groups. Severely non-proportional hazards make log-rank test underpowered restricted-mean comparison may appropriate.

heading: 7.357 R Implementation
code:
library(survival); library(survminer)

fit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung)

ggsurvplot(fit,
  data = lung,
  conf.int = TRUE,
  pval = TRUE,
  risk.table = TRUE,
  legend.labs = c("Male", "Female"),
  palette = c("#2A9D8F", "#F4A261"),
  xlab = "Days",
  ylab = "Survival probability",
  risk.table.y.text.col = TRUE
)

# Cumulative event curve (1 - S)
ggsurvplot(fit, data = lung, fun = "event")

heading: 7.358 Output & Results
text: two-panel figure: upper panel shows two survival curves (male female) shaded 95 % confidence bands log-rank \(p\)-value corner; lower panel risk table tabulating number risk group six evenly spaced time points. cumulative-event variant (fun = "event") inverts y-axis show \(1 - \hat S(t)\), useful event rate natural reporting scale.

heading: 7.359 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Kaplan–Meier overall-survival curves sex (survival::survfit); shaded bands 95 % point-wise confidence intervals, log-rank \(p\)-value compares two groups, risk table reports number subjects risk labelled time.” Always state time-zero anchor (date randomisation, surgery, diagnosis) methods x-axis unambiguous.

heading: 7.360 Practical Tips
text: Always include risk table; without , curve late time points misleading estimate based tiny remaining cohort.Use fun = "event" cumulative incidence framing communicating clinical audiences think event rates rather survival probabilities.competing risks, switch cumulative incidence function (CIF) plot via cmprsk::cuminc() ggcompetingrisks(); KM curves overestimate event interest competing events non-trivial.Pair log-rank \(p\)-value hazard-ratio estimate (e.g. univariate Cox model); test alone gives effect size.multi-arm trials, lay subgroups panels (facet.= "subgroup") rather crowding many curves one panel.Export 300 dpi (higher line art) journal’s column width risk-table text remains legible.

heading: 7.361 R Packages Used
text: survival survfit() Surv(); survminer ggsurvplot(), ggsurvtable(), risk-table support; ggplot2 downstream customisation returned object; cmprsk competing-risks extensions KM appropriate.

heading: 7.364 Introduction
text: Time-series plots deceptively easy make surprisingly easy ruin. data ordered, x-axis date, single line often right geom — choice tick spacing, transformation, accompanying decomposition forecast layer determines whether plot communicates underlying dynamics hides . Trend, seasonality, forecast uncertainty three distinct stories series can tell, good time-series figure usually shows least two .

heading: 7.365 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge ggplot2, understanding R’s date date-time classes (Date, POSIXct), basic familiarity classical time-series concepts (trend, seasonality, forecast intervals).

heading: 7.366 Theory
text: Three structural elements appear nearly every published time-series figure:continuous date axis carefully chosen date_breaks date_labels. Defaults often produce overcrowded sparse ticks; choose explicitly.line geom observed series, optionally smooth fitted-value overlay.uncertainty layer — typically geom_ribbon() forecast prediction intervals faint shaded region seasonal subsamples.multivariate decompositions, classical STL splits series trend, seasonal, remainder components shown stacked panel. forecast::autoplot() feasts::autoplot() methods produce decomposition forecast plots automatically; tidy-friendly fit naturally modern reporting workflows.

heading: 7.367 Assumptions
text: Time variable monotonically ordered, regular grid (explicitly handled irregular), class scale_x_date() scale_x_datetime() understands.

heading: 7.368 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(forecast); library(ggfortify)

# Standard time series plot
ggplot(economics, aes(date, unemploy / 1000)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2A9D8F", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "10 years", date_labels = "%Y") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Unemployment (thousands)") +
  theme_minimal()

# Forecast with prediction intervals
fit <- auto.arima(AirPassengers)
fc  <- forecast(fit, h = 24)
autoplot(fc) +
  theme_minimal()

# STL decomposition
decomp <- stl(AirPassengers, s.window = "periodic")
autoplot(decomp) + theme_minimal()

heading: 7.369 Output & Results
text: Three figures: long historical line plot US unemployment decade ticks, ARIMA forecast 80 % 95 % prediction ribbons attached end historical series, four-panel STL decomposition monthly air-passenger counts showing rising trend, twelve-month seasonal cycle, remaining residual variation.

heading: 7.370 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Top: unemployment series 1967–2015 (thousands persons). Middle: ARIMA(2,1,1)(0,1,0)\[12\] forecast 24 months ahead 80 % 95 % prediction intervals. Bottom: STL decomposition (s.window = "periodic") monthly air-passenger counts.” Always show forecast intervals; forecast line without uncertainty misleading.

heading: 7.375 Introduction
text: violin plot displays kernel-density estimate vertically reflects around central axis, producing symmetric outline gives geom name. combines clarity density plot side--side grouping boxplots provide, — crucially — reveals multi-modality skew boxplots flatten single rectangle. comparing shape distributions across categories, violin plots often right default; comparing median quartile structure, boxplots remain compact.

heading: 7.376 Prerequisites
text: working understanding kernel density estimation, boxplots, ggplot2 aesthetic mappings.

heading: 7.377 Theory
text: group, geom_violin() computes kernel density \(\hat f(x)\) response variable, draws density curve vertically, mirrors across group’s central axis. resulting width y-value proportional \(\hat f(y)\), wide regions correspond high density. scale argument controls groups sized relative : "area" makes violins equal area, "count" scales width proportional group’s sample size, "width" makes maximum width. trim argument decides whether density tails truncated data extremes (TRUE, default) extrapolated beyond (FALSE).

heading: 7.378 Assumptions
text: continuous outcome categorical (ordered) grouping variable, enough observations per group (least 20–30) kernel density meaningful. threshold shape becomes unstable strip plot jittered scatter honest.

heading: 7.379 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

# Basic violin
ggplot(iris, aes(Species, Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_violin() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

# Violin + boxplot overlay
ggplot(iris, aes(Species, Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.5, trim = FALSE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.2, fill = "white", outlier.shape = NA) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

# Violin + jittered points
ggplot(iris, aes(Species, Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 7.380 Output & Results
text: Three views iris sepal-length data: plain violin per species, violin narrow boxplot overlay showing median quartiles, violin jittered raw points overlaid full transparency. third version informative audience unfamiliar kernel-density visualisations.

heading: 7.381 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Sepal length species (n = 50 per group); violin width proportional kernel-density estimate (scale = "area"), inset boxplot summarises median IQR, jittered points show individual observations.” Always describe violin’s width encodes; readers unfamiliar geom default thinking represents count.

heading: 7.382 Practical Tips
text: multi-modal distributions (e.g. Old Faithful eruption times), violin reveals second mode boxplot hide entirely; strongest single argument geom.Set trim = FALSE density tails biologically meaningful; otherwise default trim avoids implausible extrapolation.Combine narrow boxplot (width = 0.15) readers expect numeric summaries; inset preserves quartile information without dominating visual.paired matched groups, introdataviz::geom_split_violin() produces left-right halves two conditions share x-position.large \(n\), prefer violins jittered points; violins scale millions observations whereas individual points stack uninformative blobs.small \(n\) (\(< 20\) per group), strip plot boxplot honest violin; kernel density estimate noisy trust.

heading: 8.3 Introduction
text: added-variable plot (AVP), also called partial regression plot, displays relationship single predictor outcome adjusting predictors model. geometric expression regression coefficient: slope OLS line AVP equals coefficient full multiple regression. AVPs standard tool diagnosing whether partial linear effect well-supported, whether non-linearity missed, whether one two observations driving particular coefficient.

heading: 8.4 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multiple regression, partial correlation, residualisation interpretation partial effects.

heading: 8.5 Theory
text: predictor \(X_j\) model predictors \(X_{-j}\):Regress \(Y\) \(X_{-j}\) save residuals \(e_{Y \mid X_{-j}}\).Regress \(X_j\) \(X_{-j}\) save residuals \(e_{X_j \mid X_{-j}}\).Plot \(e_{Y \mid X_{-j}}\) \(e_{X_j \mid X_{-j}}\).OLS slope plot equals coefficient \(X_j\) full multiple regression — famous Frisch-Waugh-Lovell theorem. Patterns AVP reveal partial-effect non-linearity, leverage, outliers specific \(X_j\) bivariate scatter plots show.

heading: 8.6 Assumptions
text: Standard multiple regression assumptions; residualisation interpretation requires predictors correctly specified.

heading: 8.7 R Implementation
code:
library(car)

fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp + disp + drat, data = mtcars)
avPlots(fit)

# Single AVP
avPlot(fit, variable = "hp")

heading: 8.8 Output & Results
text: avPlots() draws one scatter plot per predictor, fitted partial regression line. slope line equals corresponding multiple-regression coefficient. Outlier leverage diagnostics specific predictor become visible.

heading: 8.9 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “added-variable plot horsepower showed clear negative slope adjusting weight, displacement, rear-axle ratio, evidence non-linearity individual influential observations; AVP slope \(-0.030\) matches multiple-regression coefficient (\(p = 0.005\)). Diagnostic plots support reporting linear partial effect horsepower.” Use AVPs check shape partial relationship flag observations driving specific coefficients.

heading: 8.10 Practical Tips
text: AVPs informative raw bivariate scatter plots checking partial effects; bivariate plots ignore predictors can mislead.Non-linear patterns AVP suggest transformation, polynomial term, spline specific predictor.Outliers AVP indicate observations influencing particular coefficient; cross-check DFBETAs.car::avPlots(fit) draws AVPs single grid compact diagnostic display.Distinguish component-plus-residual plots (crPlots), add predictor’s full effect rather partialling predictors; two answer related different diagnostic questions.GLMs mixed models, AVP analogues exist; see car::avPlots() effects::predictorEffects().

heading: 8.14 Introduction
text: Best subset selection enumerates \(2^p\) possible predictor subsets identifies best subset chosen criterion (adjusted \(R^2\), Mallows’ \(C_p\), BIC, cross-validation error). exhaustive search therefore avoids sequence-dependence plagues stepwise methods, combinatorial cost limits practical use modest \(p\) — typically 30 predictors branch--bound algorithms (leaps).

heading: 8.15 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multiple regression, model-selection criteria, bias-variance trade-choosing model complexity.

heading: 8.16 Theory
text: subset size \(k \\{0, 1, \dots, p\}\), find subset lowest residual sum squares (equivalently, highest \(R^2\)). compare across sizes using criterion penalises complexity:Adjusted \(R^2\): \(1 - (1 - R^2)(n-1)/(n-k-1)\); rewards fit per parameter.Mallows’ \(C_p\): \(\mathrm{RSS}_k / \hat\sigma^2 - n + 2k\); near \(k\) subset adequate.AIC: \(-2 \ell + 2k\); favours predictive accuracy.BIC: \(-2 \ell + k \log n\); parsimonious AIC.Cross-validation: directly estimates predictive error.leaps::regsubsets uses branch--bound avoid evaluating every subset explicitly, making moderate-\(p\) best-subset feasible.

heading: 8.17 Assumptions
text: Standard linear-regression assumptions; criteria like AIC, BIC assume Gaussian residuals (forms exist GLMs).

heading: 8.18 R Implementation
code:
library(leaps)

d <- mtcars
fit <- regsubsets(mpg ~ ., data = d, nvmax = 5)
sum_fit <- summary(fit)

data.frame(
  n_pred = 1:5,
  adj_R2 = sum_fit$adjr2,
  Cp     = sum_fit$cp,
  BIC    = sum_fit$bic
)

# Show the best subset at each size
sum_fit$which

# Plot the criterion
plot(fit, scale = "bic")

heading: 8.19 Output & Results
text: regsubsets() returns best subset size nvmax. summary provides criterion values; sum_fit$shows predictors included size; plot displays subsets ordered chosen criterion.

heading: 8.20 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Best-subset selection mtcars (nvmax = 5) identified weight (wt), quarter-mile time (qsec), transmission () preferred three-predictor model BIC (\(\mathrm{BIC} = -84\)); cross-validated mean squared error confirmed best subset, substantially worse performance smaller larger models.” Always state criterion pair cross-validation stakes high.

heading: 8.21 Practical Tips
text: Best subset computationally feasible \(p = 30\) branch--bound; beyond , switch penalised methods (lasso, elastic net).Lasso elastic net often perform similarly best-subset selection better scalability less -fitting; benchmark three high-dimensional problems.Like stepwise selection, best-subset chooses criteria computed data — cross-validate assess true predictive performance.regsubsets() returns single best subset per size; near-ties can missed selection becomes unstable across resamples. Bootstrap assess stability.Pre-specification subset based substantive theory generally beats data-driven search; reserve best-subset genuinely exploratory work.GLMs, use bestglm::bestglm stepwise AIC; pure best-subset enumeration OLS-specific leaps.

heading: 8.22 R Packages Used

heading: 8.25 Introduction
text: Beta regression models continuous outcomes constrained open unit interval \((0, 1)\) — proportions, percentages expressed fractions, biomarker ratios range 0 1. Ordinary least squares fails data: can predict values outside \((0, 1)\), residuals typically heteroscedastic asymmetric, confidence intervals boundary unreliable. Beta regression respects bound, models heteroscedasticity precision parameter, returns interpretable coefficients logit scale (supported link).

heading: 8.26 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Beta distribution, generalised linear models, logit link transformation probabilities real line.

heading: 8.27 Theory
text: model parameterises \(Y\) Beta mean \(\mu\) precision \(\phi\):\[Y \mid x \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\mu\phi, (1 - \mu)\phi),\]\(\mathrm{logit}(\mu) = x^\top \beta\) \(\phi > 0\) precision parameter (larger \(\phi\) means tighter concentration around \(\mu\)). Variable-dispersion beta regression allows \(\phi\) depend covariates well, capturing heteroscedasticity directly. Coefficients interpretable log-odds ratios proportion: unit increase \(x_j\) multiplies odds “success-vs-failure” proportion \(\exp(\beta_j)\).Values exactly 0 1 excluded standard model; common remedies include small linear shift (\(y^* = (y(n-1) + 0.5)/n\)) zero-one-inflated beta model adds explicit point-mass components.

heading: 8.28 Assumptions
text: Outcome strictly \((0, 1)\) (exact 0 1 unless using zero-one-inflated extensions); logit (chosen) link appropriate; independent observations.

heading: 8.29 R Implementation
code:
library(betareg)

set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(200)
eta <- 0.2 + 0.8 * x
mu  <- plogis(eta)
phi <- 10
y <- rbeta(200, mu * phi, (1 - mu) * phi)

fit <- betareg(y ~ x)
summary(fit)

# Predictions on the probability scale
head(predict(fit, type = "response"))

heading: 8.30 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) reports coefficients logit scale (Wald \(z\)-tests), precision parameter \(\hat\phi\), pseudo-\(R^2\). predict(..., type = "response") returns predictions back \((0, 1)\) scale.

heading: 8.31 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Beta regression bounded outcome \(x\) estimated \(\hat\beta = 0.79\) logit scale (odds ratio 2.20, 95 % CI 1.79 2.71, \(p < 0.001\)); precision parameter \(\hat\phi = 9.6\), indicating moderate concentration around conditional mean. 1-SD increase \(x\) associated roughly two-fold increase odds higher proportion.” Always report coefficient precision; precision controls implied uncertainty.

heading: 8.32 Practical Tips
text: Outcomes exactly equal 0 1 need either small shift transformation (\(y^* = (y(n-1) + 0.5)/n\)) zero-one-inflated beta model (gamlss::gamlss(family = BEINF) zoib::zoib).Variable-dispersion beta regression — modelling \(\phi\) covariates — handles heteroscedasticity directly via betareg(y ~ x | z) syntax.longitudinal clustered proportion data, glmmTMB(family = beta_family()) adds random effects beta GLM.default link logit; alternatives (probit, cloglog) available via link argument rarely change conclusions materially.Pearson quantile residuals right diagnostic tools; standard residuals can misleading bounded outcomes.Compare beta regression quasi-binomial GLM logit link; latter sometimes simpler gives similar conclusions moderate dispersion.

heading: 8.36 Introduction
text: Centring (subtracting mean) scaling (dividing standard deviation) simple transformations continuous predictors change coefficient interpretation without changing model fit. Centring shifts intercept meaningful value (predicted outcome mean every predictor) reduces collinearity main effects interaction terms. Scaling makes coefficient magnitudes comparable across predictors different units. transformations routine pre-processing steps regression interactions analysis benchmarks predictor importance coefficient size.

heading: 8.37 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multiple linear regression, intercepts, geometric meaning regression coefficients.

heading: 8.38 Theory
text: Centring: \(X^c = X - \bar X\). intercept becomes predicted \(Y\) mean every centred predictor; slopes unchanged. models interactions \(X \cdot Z\), centring reduces artefactual correlation \(X\), \(Z\), \(X \cdot Z\) inflates standard errors complicates interpretation.Scaling: \(X^s = X / \mathrm{SD}(X)\). coefficient \(X^s\) predicted change \(Y\) per one-standard-deviation increase \(X\), whatever scale \(Y\) measured. Combined centring, result standard \(z\)-score; standardised coefficients directly comparable across predictors different units.Neither transformation changes \(R^2\), predictions, residual structure, significance tests; coefficient values interpretation change.

heading: 8.39 Assumptions
text: Continuous predictors. categorical (factor) predictors, “centring” via sum--zero contrasts achieves analogous effect intercept interpretation.

heading: 8.40 R Implementation
code:
d <- mtcars

# Centred predictors
d$wt_c <- d$wt - mean(d$wt)
d$hp_c <- d$hp - mean(d$hp)

# Centred + scaled (z-scored)
d$wt_z <- scale(d$wt)[, 1]
d$hp_z <- scale(d$hp)[, 1]

fit_raw  <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp,     data = d)
fit_cent <- lm(mpg ~ wt_c + hp_c, data = d)
fit_std  <- lm(mpg ~ wt_z + hp_z, data = d)

# Intercept differs; slopes for raw & centred equal; slopes for standardised are per-SD
coef(fit_raw);  coef(fit_cent);  coef(fit_std)

heading: 8.41 Output & Results
text: Three fits identical \(R^2\), \(F\)-statistic, residuals different coefficient interpretations. raw model’s intercept \(Y\) \(X = 0\) (often outside data range); centred model’s intercept \(Y\) mean every predictor; standardised model’s slopes per-SD changes \(Y\).

heading: 8.42 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Standardised coefficients show 1-SD increase vehicle weight associated 4.2-mpg decrease fuel economy, 1-SD increase horsepower associated 2.3-mpg decrease (95 % CI \(-5.1\) \(-3.3\) \(-3.5\) \(-1.0\) respectively); per standard deviation, weight stronger predictor fuel economy sample.” Standardised coefficients right summary comparing strengths predictors different units.

heading: 8.43 Practical Tips
text: Centre predictors adding interactions; reduces mechanical correlation main interaction terms stabilises SEs.Scale predictors benchmarking importance across units; coefficient 0.001 annual income looks small might strongest predictor per-SD basis.scale() returns matrix attributes; index [, 1] get plain numeric vector.Store means SDs training data applying standardisation test data; using test-set mean/SD leaks information.centre scale outcome unless interpretation requires ; transformations \(Y\) change predictions \(R^2\) ways may undesirable.penalised regression (lasso, ridge, elastic net), glmnet standardises predictors internally returns coefficients original scale; pre-standardise.

heading: 8.44 R Packages Used

heading: 8.47 Introduction
text: contrast matrix maps factor’s levels numeric indicator columns enter regression design matrix. R ships four built-schemes — treatment, sum, Helmert, polynomial — testing different hypotheses group differences. Beyond , custom contrasts let analysts test pre-specified comparisons (e.g. “first arm vs average two”) directly coefficient table, without post-hoc multiple comparisons. Choosing reporting contrast scheme routine consequential modelling decision.

heading: 8.48 Prerequisites
text: working understanding dummy coding, role design matrix regression, basic familiarity factor handling R.

heading: 8.49 Theory
text: factor \(k\) levels, contrast scheme produces \(k - 1\) numeric columns. Built-choices:Treatment (contr.treatment): first level reference; coefficients level-vs-reference differences. Default unordered factors.Sum (contr.sum): sum--zero deviation coding; intercept becomes grand mean coefficients level-vs-grand-mean deviations.Helmert (contr.helmert): orthogonal successive comparisons (level \(j\) vs mean levels \(1, \dots, j-1\)). Useful ordered sequential designs.Polynomial (contr.poly): linear, quadratic, cubic, etc. trends ordered factors. Default ordered factors.Custom contrasts matrices whose columns define comparisons interest; resulting coefficients represent exactly comparisons. orthogonal contrasts (columns sum zero pairwise products sum zero), tests coefficient independent multiplicity adjustment needed within pre-planned set.

heading: 8.50 Assumptions
text: Standard regression assumptions; contrast choice change fit predictions, coefficient interpretation.

heading: 8.51 R Implementation
code:
d <- mtcars
d$cyl <- factor(d$cyl, levels = c("4", "6", "8"))

# Compare built-in contrasts
contr.treatment(3)
contr.sum(3)
contr.helmert(3)
contr.poly(3)

# Apply a specific contrast to a factor
contrasts(d$cyl) <- contr.helmert(3)
fit <- lm(mpg ~ cyl, data = d)
summary(fit)

# Custom contrast: 4 vs (6 + 8), and 6 vs 8
custom <- cbind("4_vs_68" = c(2, -1, -1),
                "6_vs_8"  = c(0, 1, -1))
contrasts(d$cyl) <- custom
fit_custom <- lm(mpg ~ cyl, data = d)
summary(fit_custom)

heading: 8.52 Output & Results
text: coefficient regression output tests comparison defined corresponding column contrast matrix. Treatment contrasts produce level-vs-reference comparisons; sum contrasts produce level-vs-grand-mean; custom contrasts produce whatever comparisons user specifies.

heading: 8.53 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Custom orthogonal contrasts compared 4-cylinder cars average 6- 8-cylinder cars (estimate \(-9.36\), 95 % CI \(-12.0\) \(-6.7\), \(p < 0.001\)) 6-cylinder 8-cylinder cars (estimate \(-4.65\), 95 % CI \(-7.6\) \(-1.7\), \(p = 0.003\)); orthogonal contrasts allow two pre-specified hypotheses tested without multiplicity adjustment.” Always state contrast scheme.

heading: 8.54 Practical Tips
text: Match contrast scheme scientific question rather software default; treatment contrasts convenient rarely optimal ANOVA-style questions.Custom contrasts must linearly independent (rank \(k-1\)); orthogonal contrasts add property pairwise within-column products sum zero.Type III sums squares unbalanced ANOVA designs, use sum--zero (contr.sum) Helmert; treatment contrasts produce non-orthogonal SS confound main effects interactions.ordered factors, polynomial default tests linear, quadratic, etc. trends; want pairwise group differences, override contr.treatment.Centring continuous predictors conceptually analogous using effect-coding factors; shift intercept meaningful reference reduce collinearity interactions.Document contrast scheme methods section: “treatment contrasts placebo reference” “Helmert contrasts” leaves room misreading.

heading: 8.58 Introduction
text: Cook’s distance widely used single-number summary observation’s influence regression fit. answers direct question: much fitted values change removed observation? Cook’s distance combines residual (badly model fits point) leverage (unusual point predictor space) one quantity, making first port call influence diagnostics linear regression.

heading: 8.59 Prerequisites
text: working understanding leverage (hat values), standardised residuals, leave-one-perspective regression diagnostics.

heading: 8.60 Theory
text: observation \(\) standardised residual \(r_i\) hat value \(h_{ii}\),\[D_i = \frac{r_i^2}{p + 1} \cdot \frac{h_{ii}}{(1 - h_{ii})^2},\]\(p + 1\) number regression parameters. product structure makes Cook’s distance large residual sizable leverage non-trivial; either alone insufficient.Conventional thresholds: \(D_i > 1\) indicates strong influence (regression effectively hinges point); \(D_i > 4/n\) indicates moderate influence warrants investigation. relative magnitude across observations often informative absolute thresholds — points dominating Cook’s distance distribution deserve scrutiny regardless cutoffs.

heading: 8.61 Assumptions
text: Classical OLS assumptions, including residual Normality (Cook’s distance sensitive outliers response). robust alternatives, DFBETA (per-coefficient influence) robust regression leverage diagnostics preferable.

heading: 8.62 R Implementation
code:
fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)

cooks <- cooks.distance(fit)
head(sort(cooks, decreasing = TRUE))

# Visualise
plot(fit, which = 4)
abline(h = 4 / nrow(mtcars), col = "red", lty = 2)

# Influential cars
mtcars[cooks > 4 / nrow(mtcars), ]

heading: 8.63 Output & Results
text: cooks.distance(fit) returns vector \(D_i\) every observation. diagnostic plot (plot(fit, = 4)) displays sticks, largest values labelled. Subsetting threshold identifies candidate influential observations inspection.

heading: 8.64 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Three vehicles Cook’s distance exceeding \(4/n = 0.125\) — Maserati Bora, Cadillac Fleetwood, Lincoln Continental — refitting without changed coefficient weight \(-3.88\) \(-3.65\) (95 % CI \(-5.65\) \(-1.65\) vs \(-5.20\) \(-2.10\) originally), conclusions unchanged.” Always run sensitivity analysis identifying influential points; never silently remove .

heading: 8.65 Practical Tips
text: Cook’s distance sensitive residual outliers well leverage; robust alternatives, examine DFBETAs consider robust regression.observation \(D_i > 1\) essentially guarantees regression hinges ; investigate data point errors deciding whether retain exclude.Report sensitivity analyses influential points removed; silently exclude.Cook’s distance summarises overall influence; DFBETAs identify specific coefficients affected observation.GLMs, use influence.measures(fit), returns DFBETAs, hat values, Cook’s-distance analogues chosen family.Influence diagnostics substitute substantive judgement — high-leverage observation may informative data point sample correct.

heading: 8.69 Introduction
text: Dirichlet regression models compositional outcomes — vectors proportions sum 1, relative cell-type composition tissue, time budgets across activities, microbial relative abundances, budget shares across categories. Dirichlet distribution multivariate generalisation Beta lives unit simplex; Dirichlet regression places covariate effects component preserving unit-sum constraint inherent compositional data.

heading: 8.70 Prerequisites
text: working understanding beta regression, unit simplex, compositional data fundamentally different independent proportions.

heading: 8.71 Theory
text: composition \(Y = (Y_1, \dots, Y_k)\) \(\sum_j Y_j = 1\) \(Y_j > 0\) follows Dirichlet distribution \(\mathrm{Dir}(\alpha_1, \dots, \alpha_k)\). Dirichlet regression parameterises component means \[\mu_j = \frac{\exp(x^\top \beta_j)}{\sum_l \exp(x^\top \beta_l)},\]\(\alpha_j = \mu_j \phi\) \(\phi\) precision parameter. structure mirrors multinomial logistic regression, one reference component fixed identifiability \(k - 1\) sets coefficients estimated.small samples high precision, Dirichlet can unstable; weakly informative Bayesian priors (brms family = dirichlet()) alternative.

heading: 8.72 Assumptions
text: Components sum 1 components strictly positive (zeros require perturbation zero-inflated extensions); independent observations; Dirichlet variance structure appropriate data.

heading: 8.73 R Implementation
code:
library(DirichletReg)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 150
x <- rnorm(n)
a1 <- exp(0.3 * x); a2 <- exp(-0.2 * x); a3 <- exp(0.5 * x)
Y <- t(apply(cbind(a1, a2, a3), 1,
             function(a) {
               phi <- 20
               rd <- rgamma(length(a), a * phi, 1)
               rd / sum(rd)
             }))

d <- data.frame(Y = DR_data(as.data.frame(Y)), x = x)
fit <- DirichReg(Y ~ x, data = d)
summary(fit)

heading: 8.74 Output & Results
text: DirichReg() returns coefficients per non-reference component multinomial logit scale plus precision parameter. Predictions back simplex come predict(); always satisfy unit-sum constraint construction.

heading: 8.75 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Dirichlet regression showed \(x\) positively associated components 1 3 (multinomial-logit slopes 0.5 0.7 vs reference) negatively component 2 (\(-0.4\)); predicted compositions \(x = -1, 0, +1\) shifted (0.30, 0.40, 0.30) (0.33, 0.30, 0.37) (0.36, 0.22, 0.42), preserving unit-sum constraint.” Always communicate compositional results predicted compositions; raw multinomial-logit coefficients difficult interpret.

heading: 8.76 Practical Tips
text: Exact zeros exact ones composition need perturbation fitting; compositions::clr() zCompositions::cmultRepl() provide standard zero-replacement algorithms.compositional data many components (\(k > 6\)), consider aggregating fewer meaningful categories using additive-log-ratio (ALR) centred-log-ratio (CLR) transformations followed ordinary multivariate regression.zero-inflated compositions (many exact zeros), use zero-inflated Dirichlet Zadeh-Aitchison ZI-logratio models.Interpretation cleanest via predicted compositions plotted covariate values; raw coefficients multinomial-logit scale rarely communicative.paired longitudinal compositional data, paired Dirichlet model mixed-effects extension needed; ordinary Dirichlet regression assumes independence across observations.Bayesian Dirichlet regression weakly informative priors, brms(family = dirichlet()) provides familiar interface.

heading: 8.77 R Packages Used
text: DirichletReg canonical DirichReg() implementation; compositions zCompositions compositional data pre-processing; brms Bayesian Dirichlet regression; aldex2 differential-abundance compositional analysis microbiome work.

heading: 8.80 Introduction
text: Categorical predictors regression must encoded numeric indicator variables enter linear predictor. R’s default treatment-contrast coding (also called dummy coding), one level implicit reference remaining \(k - 1\) levels get 0/1 indicator. choice reference level partly arbitrary partly substantive: reference becomes baseline levels compared, level makes scientific sense — placebo clinical trial, “symptom” symptom-based predictors, largest stratum observational studies.

heading: 8.81 Prerequisites
text: working understanding categorical variables, factor handling R, role design matrices regression.

heading: 8.82 Theory
text: factor \(k\) levels, treatment coding produces \(k \times (k-1)\) contrast matrix. reference level -zero indicator entries; level 1 column 0 elsewhere. Substituting linear model:Intercept = mean reference level (model factor predictor).Coefficient indicator \(j\) = difference \(\mu_j - \mu_{\mathrm{ref}}\).Alternative coding schemes — sum--zero (contr.sum), Helmert (contr.helmert), polynomial (contr.poly) — change meaning intercept coefficients produce identical fits. Ordered factors default polynomial contrasts R, decompose trend linear, quadratic, etc. components; override contrasts(f) <- contr.treatment want familiar group differences.

heading: 8.83 Assumptions
text: underlying regression: linearity linear predictor, etc. choice contrast affects coefficient interpretation model fit, residuals, predictions.

heading: 8.84 R Implementation
code:
# Default: treatment contrasts with first level as reference
fit <- lm(mpg ~ factor(cyl), data = mtcars)
summary(fit)

# Change reference level
mtcars$cyl <- factor(mtcars$cyl)
mtcars$cyl <- relevel(mtcars$cyl, ref = "6")
fit2 <- lm(mpg ~ cyl, data = mtcars)
summary(fit2)

# Inspect contrast matrix
contrasts(mtcars$cyl)

heading: 8.85 Output & Results
text: fitted intercept equals reference-level mean; coefficient equals difference reference. Re-leveling changes intercept coefficients change predictions residuals.

heading: 8.86 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Compared 4-cylinder cars (reference; mean 26.7 mpg), 6-cylinder cars averaged 6.9 mpg less (\(p < 0.001\)) 8-cylinder cars averaged 11.6 mpg less (\(p < 0.001\)). Treatment contrasts 4-cylinder reference used throughout.” Always state reference level explicitly; reviewers reanalysts tell coefficients alone.

heading: 8.87 Practical Tips
text: Choose reference level match scientific question — placebo, baseline, control, “common” — rather relying alphabetic default.Use relevel(f, ref = "...") forcats::fct_relevel() set specific reference; lm()/glm(), first factor level always reference.ordered factors, R defaults polynomial contrasts (contr.poly); override contrasts(f) <- contr.treatment want pairwise group differences.ANOVA-style hypothesis testing, sum--zero contrasts (contr.sum) make intercept grand mean produce orthogonal Type II / Type III sums squares; specify options(contrasts = c("contr.sum", "contr.poly")).Inspect contrast matrices contrasts(f) confirm actually fitted; mismatch expected actual contrasts common source misinterpreted output.Document contrast type methods: “treatment contrasts placebo reference” leaves room ambiguity.

heading: 8.91 Introduction
text: Effect coding — also called deviation coding sum--zero coding — represents factor’s levels deviations grand mean rather differences single reference level. intercept becomes grand mean coefficient becomes level-vs-grand-mean deviation, level deviations summing zero. symmetric interpretation right framing factorial ANOVA analysis single level naturally privileged “reference”.

heading: 8.92 Prerequisites
text: working understanding dummy (treatment) coding, factorial ANOVA, difference Type , II, III sums squares unbalanced designs.

heading: 8.93 Theory
text: \(k\)-level factor, effect coding produces \(k - 1\) coded columns. column codes one non-reference level \(+1\), implicit reference level \(-1\), levels \(0\). intercept equals grand mean cell means; coefficient column deviation level grand mean. implicit reference level’s deviation negative sum explicit coefficients (since deviations sum zero construction).Effect coding matters factorial ANOVA unbalanced designs: Type III sums squares (standard ANOVA approach many disciplines) require orthogonal contrasts give interpretable interaction tests, effect coding (Helmert) right choice. Treatment contrasts give Type II SS confound main effects interactions.

heading: 8.94 Assumptions
text: regression; contrast choice change fit predictions, coefficient interpretation.

heading: 8.95 R Implementation
code:
d <- mtcars
d$cyl <- factor(d$cyl)

# Default: treatment contrasts
contrasts(d$cyl)

# Effect (sum-to-zero) contrasts
contrasts(d$cyl) <- contr.sum(3)
contrasts(d$cyl)

fit_eff <- lm(mpg ~ cyl, data = d)
summary(fit_eff)

# Intercept = grand mean of cell means
mean(tapply(d$mpg, d$cyl, mean))

heading: 8.96 Output & Results

Effect coding used support Type III SS interpretation interactions factorial design.” Always state contrast choice reporting ANOVA-style coefficients.

heading: 8.102 Introduction
text: Elastic net combines L1 penalty lasso L2 penalty ridge single hybrid regulariser. performs variable selection (like lasso) retaining stability correlated predictors lasso famously lacks (ridge provides). mixing parameter \(\alpha \[0, 1]\) moves continuously two extremes — \(\alpha = 0\) pure ridge, \(\alpha = 1\) pure lasso, intermediate values blend behaviours. high-dimensional biomedical data groups correlated predictors (gene expression, multiple imaging features), elastic net now standard default.

heading: 8.103 Prerequisites
text: working understanding ridge regression, lasso regression, differing geometries L1 versus L2 penalties.

heading: 8.104 Theory
text: Elastic net minimises\[\|y - X\beta\|^2 + \lambda \!\left[\alpha \|\beta\|_1 + (1 - \alpha) \|\beta\|_2^2\right].\]L1 component drives coefficients exactly zero (selection), L2 component shares shrinkage across correlated predictors --nothing selection failures avoided. lasso pick one two perfectly correlated predictors arbitrarily zero , elastic net keeps equal coefficients (“grouping effect”), often honest reflection underlying signal.Two hyperparameters need tuning: \(\alpha\) (ratio L1 total penalty) \(\lambda\) (overall strength). Cross-validation grid \(\alpha\) values, \(\lambda\) tuned within \(\alpha\) via cv.glmnet(), finds joint optimum.

heading: 8.105 Assumptions
text: Standard linear-model assumptions residuals; predictors standardised (glmnet internally). inference selected coefficients, post-selection adjustments apply just lasso.

heading: 8.106 R Implementation
code:
library(glmnet)

X <- as.matrix(mtcars[, -1])
y <- mtcars$mpg

set.seed(2026)
# Two-dimensional CV: choose alpha and lambda jointly
alpha_grid <- c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1)
results <- lapply(alpha_grid, function(a) {
  cv <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = a)
  c(alpha = a, lambda_min = cv$lambda.min, cvm_min = min(cv$cvm))
})
do.call(rbind, results)

# Best alpha:
best_alpha <- 0.5
fit_best <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = best_alpha)
coef(fit_best, s = "lambda.min")

heading: 8.107 Output & Results
text: grid cross-validated mean squared errors across \(\alpha\) values shows best joint tuning; refitting chosen \(\alpha\) produces shrunken coefficients zero. combination typically achieves lower CV error either lasso ridge alone correlated predictors.

heading: 8.108 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Elastic net \(\alpha = 0.5\) cross-validated \(\lambda_{1\mathrm{se}}\) retained four ten predictors shrunken coefficients; resulting cross-validated MSE 6.0 mpg², lower lasso (\(\alpha = 1\), MSE 6.3) ridge (\(\alpha = 0\), MSE 7.2) alternatives, reflecting grouping benefit correlated engine-size predictors.” Report three CV errors justifying elastic-net choice.

heading: 8.109 Practical Tips
text: common starting value \(\alpha = 0.5\); tune jointly \(\lambda\) grid.Elastic net default high-dimensional biomedical data correlated predictors (gene expression, imaging features); rarely pure lasso pure ridge chosen without trying elastic net.caret::train(method = "glmnet") tidymodels automate joint tuning across hyperparameters.mostly uncorrelated sparse predictors, pure lasso (\(\alpha = 1\)) may suffice computationally cheaper.maximum stability severe multicollinearity, lean closer ridge (\(\alpha\) near 0); variable-selection benefit small nearly every predictor matters.non-Gaussian outcomes, family argument extends elastic net logistic, Poisson, Cox regression.

heading: 8.113 Introduction
text: Generalised additive models (GAMs) replace strict linear predictor GLMs sum smooth functions predictors: \(g(\mathbb E[Y]) = \beta_0 + \sum_j s_j(X_j)\). \(s_j\) smooth, data-driven function estimated alongside parametric components, GAMs combine interpretability linear regression (additivity, coefficient-style summaries) flexibility non-parametric smoothers. standard tool whenever continuous predictor’s effect suspected non-linear theoretical grounds specific functional form absent.

heading: 8.114 Prerequisites
text: working understanding generalised linear models, basis functions smooth curves (B-splines, thin-plate splines), bias-variance trade-motivates smoothing penalties.

heading: 8.115 Theory
text: smooth function \(s_j\) represented linear combination basis functions smoothing penalty controlling wiggliness. mgcv::gam() defaults penalised thin-plate regression splines, smoothing parameter selected automatically REML (recommended) generalised cross-validation (GCV). effective degrees freedom (edf) smooth quantify fitted complexity: edf = 1 means smooth reduced straight line, higher edf indicates curvature. penalty trades fit smoothness prevents -fitting high basis dimensions.categorical predictors, factor effects enter ordinary parametric terms; interactions continuous predictors, tensor product smooths (te()) ti() pure interactions available.

heading: 8.116 Assumptions
text: Standard GLM assumptions conditional distribution outcome; smooth effects additive (automatic interaction detection); basis dimension large enough capture underlying smoothness (gam.check() diagnoses ).

heading: 8.117 R Implementation
code:
library(mgcv)

set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(
  x = runif(200, 0, 10),
  z = rnorm(200)
)
d$y <- 2 + sin(d$x) * 2 + 0.4 * d$z + rnorm(200, 0, 1)

fit <- gam(y ~ s(x) + z, data = d)
summary(fit)
plot(fit, pages = 1, shade = TRUE)

# Check and predict
check.gam <- gam.check(fit)
predict(fit, newdata = data.frame(x = 5, z = 0))

heading: 8.118 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) reports parametric coefficients (\(t\) tests) approximate \(F\) tests smooth (edf reference degrees freedom). plot(fit, shade = TRUE) displays smooth shaded confidence band around estimated curve. gam.check() reports residual diagnostics basis-dimension k-index, flags basis functions needed.

heading: 8.119 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “GAM smooth function \(x\) (edf 8.5, \(F = 22.4\), \(p < 0.001\)) linear effect \(z\) (\(\hat\beta = 0.41\), \(p < 0.001\)) recovered simulated sinusoidal pattern \(x\) additive linear contribution \(z\); deviance explained 76 %, \(\hat R^2_{\mathrm{adj}}\) = 0.74.” Report edf smooths parametric coefficients linear terms; never quote single \(p\)-value smooth without edf.

heading: 8.120 Practical Tips
text: s(x) uses thin-plate regression splines default; s(x, bs = "cr") cubic regression splines (faster one-dimensional smooths); te(x, z) tensor product interactions two continuous predictors.basis dimension k defaults work smooth functions; raise gam.check() reports low k-index, lower parsimony.Use method = "REML" smoothing-parameter selection; reliable default GCV finite samples.non-Gaussian outcomes, switch family: gam(y ~ s(x), family = binomial), family = poisson, etc.gratia::draw(fit) produces ggplot-style smooth-effect plots cleaner formatting base plot.gam().large datasets, use mgcv::bam() discrete = TRUE substantial speed-ups; API otherwise identical.

heading: 8.124 Introduction
text: Generalised additive mixed models (GAMMs) bring together two powerful regression frameworks: smooth non-linear effects generalised additive models (GAMs) random effects linear / generalised linear mixed models. mgcv::gam() interface handles single unified framework treating random effects specific type penalised smooth, making combined model harder fit plain GAM. result natural choice clustered data also exhibit non-linear effects continuous covariates — longitudinal trajectories, growth curves, ecological time series site-level structure.

heading: 8.125 Prerequisites
text: working understanding generalised additive models (smooth effects, basis functions, penalised regression splines), linear mixed models, role random effects clustered data.

heading: 8.126 Theory
text: mgcv, random effects smooths basis specification bs = "re". smooth grouping factor basis mathematically equivalent random intercept; smoothing penalty plays role random-effect variance, REML estimation smoothing parameter recovers variance estimate. complex random structures — random slopes, factor--smooth interactions — constructed via tensor-product smooths s(group, bs = "re") combined appropriate factor coding.non-Gaussian outcomes, change family argument; random-effect basis trick generalises GLMM territory automatically.

heading: 8.127 Assumptions
text: Smooth-function assumptions (true relationship reasonably represented penalised splines), random-effect Normality, conditional independence given random effects, appropriate family outcome.

heading: 8.128 R Implementation
code:
library(mgcv)

set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(
  subject = factor(rep(1:20, each = 30)),
  time    = rep(seq(0, 10, length.out = 30), 20)
)
d$y <- rnorm(nrow(d)) + sin(d$time) * 2 +
       rep(rnorm(20, 0, 1.5), each = 30) +
       0.3 * d$time

fit <- gam(y ~ s(time) + s(subject, bs = "re"), data = d)
summary(fit)
plot(fit, pages = 1)

heading: 8.129 Output & Results

heading: 8.130 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “GAMM smooth time (edf 7.2, \(F = 24.5\), \(p < 0.001\)) subject-level random intercept (variance 2.18) explained 72 % deviance; smooth recovered simulated sine-plus-linear trend faithfully random-intercept SD matched simulation parameter within Monte Carlo error.” Always report edf smooths random-effect variance.

text: bs = "re" turns smooth random effect; s(group, bs = "re") random intercept; s(group, x, bs = "re") random slope \(x\).Tensor product smooths (te(x, z)) model smooth interactions two continuous predictors; ti(x, z) builds pure interaction without main effects, useful ANOVA-like decomposition.GLMM-type responses (binomial, Poisson, beta), specify family = binomial(link = "logit") (etc.) random-effect smooth syntax works.Use mgcv::gam.check(fit) diagnostic plots residuals basis-dimension adequacy; raise k basis dimensions small.gratia::draw(fit) produces ggplot-style visualisations model components, far cleaner base plot.gam() publications.large datasets (\(n > 10{,}000\)), use mgcv::bam() discrete = TRUE substantial speed-ups; API otherwise identical.

heading: 8.135 Introduction
text: Generalised estimating equations (GEE) fit marginal regression models clustered data — repeated measures, longitudinal outcomes, familial correlations — without specifying random effects. mixed models target subject-specific (conditional) effects, GEE targets population-averaged (marginal) effects, answering question “average effect across population?” rather “effect typical subject?” two interpretations coincide linear models identity link diverge non-linear links (logistic, log).

heading: 8.136 Prerequisites
text: working understanding generalised linear models, difference conditional marginal effects, basic familiarity cluster-robust standard errors.

heading: 8.137 Theory
text: GEE specifies mean structure \(g(\mu_{ij}) = X_{ij}^\top \beta\) observation \(j\) cluster \(\) working correlation matrix \(R(\alpha)\) governing within-cluster dependence. Standard choices:Independence: ignore correlation; coefficients still consistent.Exchangeable: equal correlation pairs within cluster.AR(1): correlation decays geometrically time gap.Unstructured: pairwise correlation estimated separately.Coefficient estimates consistent even correlation misspecification; standard errors use sandwich (robust) variance estimator. working-correlation choice affects efficiency asymptotic consistency.QIC (quasi-likelihood information criterion) generalises AIC GEE used selecting working correlation mean structure.

heading: 8.138 Assumptions
text: Independent clusters, correctly specified mean model, missing-completely--random (MCAR) missing observations. MCAR requirement stricter MAR assumption mixed models, heavy missingness can favour latter.

heading: 8.139 R Implementation
code:
library(geepack)

data(respiratory, package = "geepack")
fit <- geeglm(outcome ~ treat + age + sex, id = id, data = respiratory,
              family = binomial, corstr = "exchangeable")
summary(fit)

# AR(1) correlation
fit_ar <- geeglm(outcome ~ treat + age + sex, id = id, data = respiratory,
                  family = binomial, corstr = "ar1")

# QIC for correlation structure selection
QIC(fit); QIC(fit_ar)

heading: 8.140 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) returns marginal coefficients sandwich-robust standard errors Wald \(z\)-tests. QIC() provides quasi-likelihood information criterion comparing models different working correlations different mean structures.

heading: 8.141 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “GEE logistic regression exchangeable working correlation estimated population-averaged odds ratio treatment 3.7 (95 % CI 2.0 6.6, \(p < 0.001\)); standard errors used sandwich variance estimator protect working-correlation misspecification, QIC favoured exchangeable AR(1) (\(\Delta\mathrm{QIC} = 4\)).” Always specify working-correlation structure use sandwich SEs.

heading: 8.142 Practical Tips
text: Sandwich SEs standard inferential tool GEE; rely naive model-based SEs.Use QIC selecting working correlation; AIC BIC apply GEE quasi-likelihood, likelihood.Choose mixed models GEE based inferential question: mixed subject-specific effects, GEE population-averaged.longitudinal binary outcomes, exchangeable working correlation sensible default; AR(1) measurements evenly spaced correlation decays time.small numbers clusters (\(< 40\)), sandwich SEs can biased downward; use small-sample corrections (geesmv package) fall back mixed models appropriate denominator df.Missing data: GEE requires MCAR unbiased estimation, mixed models tolerate MAR; consider trade-choosing.

heading: 8.143 R Packages Used
text: geepack geeglm() QIC; gee alternative implementation; geeM high-performance GEE large datasets; geesmv small-sample SE corrections; multgee GEE multinomial outcomes.

heading: 8.146 Introduction
text: lme4::glmer() fits generalised linear mixed models (GLMMs) — binary, count, non-Gaussian outcomes random effects. function inherits random-effects formula syntax lmer() family / link conventions glm(), combining single estimation problem. binary outcomes (logistic mixed models), Poisson rates cluster structure, GLM-style problems repeated measures hierarchical sampling, glmer() standard tool. newer glmmTMB package faster, flexible (handles overdispersed zero-inflated families natively), increasingly recommended default.

heading: 8.147 Prerequisites
text: working understanding generalised linear models, mixed-effects models, link functions; familiarity lme4 formula syntax random effects.

heading: 8.148 Theory
text: GLMM \[g\bigl(\mathbb E[Y \mid u]\bigr) = X^\top \beta + Z^\top u, \qquad u \sim \mathcal N(0, \Sigma),\]\(g\) link function (logit binary, log Poisson, log-odds-cumulative ordinal). random effects placed linear-predictor scale, interpreting variance requires caution: random-intercept SD 1 logit scale corresponds substantial -cluster variability baseline probability (\(\pm 1\) SD spans 0.27--0.73 baseline range centre 0.50).Estimation uses Laplace approximation default; adaptive Gauss-Hermite quadrature (nAGQ > 1) gives better accuracy higher computational cost.

heading: 8.149 Assumptions
text: GLM-family assumptions conditional distribution, multivariate Normal random effects, conditional independence given random effects, appropriate link function outcome family.

heading: 8.150 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4); library(glmmTMB)

set.seed(2026)
# Binary outcome: 20 subjects x 10 trials
d <- expand.grid(subject = factor(1:20), trial = 1:10)
d$x <- rnorm(nrow(d))
subj_int <- rnorm(20, 0, 1.2)
d$lp <- -0.5 + subj_int[as.integer(d$subject)] + 0.5 * d$x
d$y <- rbinom(nrow(d), 1, plogis(d$lp))

# lme4 glmer (binomial)
fit <- glmer(y ~ x + (1 | subject), data = d, family = binomial)
summary(fit)

# glmmTMB equivalent with more features
fit_tmb <- glmmTMB(y ~ x + (1 | subject), data = d, family = binomial)

heading: 8.151 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) reports fixed-effect coefficients linear-predictor (logit, log) scale standard errors, Wald \(z\) statistics, \(p\)-values, plus random-effect variances covariances AIC. Exponentiating fixed-effect coefficients gives odds ratios (binomial), rate ratios (Poisson), family-appropriate effect-size measures.

heading: 8.152 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “binomial GLMM estimated population-level slope 0.51 log-odds scale (= 1.66, 95 % CI 1.34 2.04, \(p < 0.001\)); random-intercept standard deviation 1.10, indicating substantial -subject variability baseline log-odds. Coefficients conditional subject; marginal effects use ggeffects::ggpredict.” Always state whether fixed-effect interpretations conditional marginal — different scales.

heading: 8.153 Practical Tips
text: Laplace approximation default; binary outcomes small clusters, raise nAGQ 7 11 better accuracy.Convergence warnings common binary data; try glmmTMB robust optimiser, switch Bayesian fitting (brms).Fixed-effect coefficients conditional random effects; marginal (population-averaged) effects, use generalised estimating equations (GEE) post-process ggeffects::ggpredict() marginaleffects::avg_predictions().overdispersed counts, glmmTMB(family = nbinom2) far cleaner deprecated glmer.nb().zero-inflated outcomes, glmmTMB handles zero-inflation component natively; lme4 .Monitor singular fits — random-effect variances estimated near zero — simplify random structure appear.

heading: 8.154 R Packages Used
text: lme4 glmer() Laplace adaptive Gauss-Hermite quadrature; glmmTMB fast TMB-based GLMM fitting overdispersed zero-inflated families; pbkrtest parametric-bootstrap LRTs variance components; ggeffects marginaleffects marginal effects; brms Bayesian GLMMs.

heading: 8.157 Introduction
text: Hurdle models split count outcomes two distinct processes: Bernoulli decision zero versus non-zero, zero-truncated count distribution positive counts. appropriate question “anything happen?” conceptually different “given something happened, much?” — example, healthcare visits (decided see doctor , number visits), insurance claims, hospital admissions rare condition.

heading: 8.158 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Poisson negative binomial regression, zero-inflated alternative, conceptual distinction two-part mixture models zero-heavy counts.

heading: 8.159 Theory
text: hurdle model \[\mathrm P(Y = 0) = 1 - \pi, \qquad \mathrm P(Y = y) = \pi \cdot \frac{f(y)}{1 - f(0)}, \quad y \ge 1,\]\(\pi\) probability clearing hurdle (logistic regression covariates) \(f(y)\) count distribution (Poisson NB) truncated zero. two parts estimated jointly maximum likelihood; covariates can differ zero count submodels.contrast zero-inflated models conceptual: hurdle treats zeros arising one Bernoulli process, zero-inflated treats zeros “structural” (always zero) others “sampling” (positive happened zero). Choose based scientific story.

heading: 8.160 Assumptions
text: count outcome meaningful two-stage data-generating process; chosen count distribution (Poisson NB) appropriate positive counts.

heading: 8.161 R Implementation
code:
library(pscl)

set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(x = rnorm(300))
d$y <- ifelse(rbinom(300, 1, plogis(0 + 0.4 * d$x)) == 0, 0,
              rpois(300, lambda = exp(1 + 0.5 * d$x)))

fit_hurdle <- hurdle(y ~ x | x, data = d)
summary(fit_hurdle)

# Zero-inflated for comparison
fit_zi <- zeroinfl(y ~ x | x, data = d)
AIC(fit_hurdle, fit_zi)

heading: 8.162 Output & Results
text: summary(fit_hurdle) returns two coefficient tables: count part (zero-truncated Poisson NB) zero hurdle (binomial). part intercept slope estimates Wald \(z\)-tests. AIC comparison zeroinfl quantifies framework better matches data.

heading: 8.163 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “hurdle model decomposed data binomial hurdle (unit increase \(x\) raised log-odds positive count 0.40, \(p < 0.001\)) zero-truncated Poisson count model (rate ratio 1.65 per unit increase \(x\) among positive counts, 95 % CI 1.40 1.95). Hurdle favoured zero-inflated AIC (\(\Delta = 4\)).” Always describe parts use AIC comparison justify framework choice.

heading: 8.164 Practical Tips
text: Hurdle cleaner zero-inflated scientific story clear two-stage interpretation; mixing two approaches without justification confuses readers.overdispersed positive counts, use dist = "negbin" count part; essential real count data.Different covariates can drive two parts; use y ~ x_count | x_zero specify separately.Zero-inflated assumes zeros structural value; hurdle assumes zeros come one decision process.AIC BIC compare hurdle zero-inflated objectively well-fitted; rely visual inspection alone.hierarchical hurdle models, glmmTMB(family = truncated_nbinom2(), ziformula = ~ ...) provides random-effect extensions.

heading: 8.165 R Packages Used
text: pscl::hurdle() canonical hurdle models Poisson, NB, geometric count parts; glmmTMB hurdle zero-inflated mixed-effects extensions; countreg additional count-model variants rootogram diagnostics; DHARMa residual diagnostics across hurdle zero-inflated families.

heading: 8.168 Introduction
text: interaction term — product two predictors entered alongside main effects — expresses substantive idea effect one predictor depends value another. Without interactions, linear model assumes purely additive effects: unit increase \(X_1\) produces change \(Y\) regardless \(X_2\). Interactions relax assumption often capture actual scientific hypothesis (treatment effect varies age, biomarker effect varies sex, dose-response curve depends baseline severity). Reporting interactions correctly requires moving beyond main-effect coefficients conditional effects specific covariate values.

heading: 8.169 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multiple linear regression, geometric meaning regression coefficients, centring continuous predictors tool reducing collinearity.

heading: 8.170 Theory
text: two continuous predictors \(X_1, X_2\) interaction:\[Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \beta_3 X_1 X_2 + \varepsilon.\]conditional effect \(X_1\) \(\beta_1 + \beta_3 X_2\) — function \(X_2\) rather single number. “main effect” \(\beta_1\) conditional effect \(X_1\) \(X_2 = 0\); \(X_2 = 0\) outside data range meaningful reference, main-effect coefficient uninterpretable.Centring continuous predictors adding interactions makes \(X_2 = 0\) correspond mean \(X_2\) gives main-effect coefficient interpretation “effect average predictor”. also reduces mechanical collinearity \(X_1, X_2\), \(X_1 X_2\).categorical--continuous interactions, continuous coefficient effect within reference category interaction difference non-reference category.

heading: 8.171 Assumptions
text: underlying regression: linearity linear predictor (interaction included), independent observations, etc. interaction model choice, assumption.

heading: 8.172 R Implementation
code:
library(emmeans); library(ggeffects)

d <- mtcars
d$wt_c <- scale(d$wt, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE)[, 1]
d$hp_c <- scale(d$hp, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE)[, 1]

fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt_c * hp_c, data = d)
summary(fit)

# Marginal effects at selected levels
emtrends(fit, ~ hp_c, var = "wt_c",
         at = list(hp_c = c(-50, 0, 50)))

# Interaction plot
ggpredict(fit, terms = c("wt_c", "hp_c [-50, 0, 50]")) |> plot()

heading: 8.173 Output & Results
text: interaction coefficient product scale. emtrends() returns conditional slope one variable chosen values . ggpredict() plots predicted \(Y\) \(X_1\) multiple levels \(X_2\), canonical visualisation interaction.

heading: 8.174 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “effect weight mpg negative high horsepower low (interaction coefficient 0.030, 95 % CI 0.007 0.053, \(p = 0.01\)); mean horsepower, 1000-lb increase weight reduced mpg 3.5 (95 % CI \(-4.6\) \(-2.4\)); horsepower 50 mean, slope \(-4.9\), 50 \(-2.0\).” Always report simple slopes meaningful covariate values, just interaction coefficient.

heading: 8.175 Practical Tips
text: Centre continuous predictors adding interactions; gives interpretable main effects (“effect mean ”) reduces collinearity main interaction terms.Never interpret “main effects” significant interaction present without specifying reference value moderator; main effect alone incomplete.Use emmeans::emtrends() marginaleffects::slopes() compute simple slopes meaningful covariate values; pair confidence intervals.Visualise interactions ggeffects::ggpredict() plots showing \(Y\) focal predictor several values moderator; communicates interaction far better coefficient tables.categorical--categorical interactions, interaction plot separate lines per group standard display; categorical--continuous, use facets colour groups.Higher-order interactions (three-way beyond) require much larger samples careful interpretation; usually best avoided confirmatory analyses.

heading: 8.176 R Packages Used
text: emmeans marginal means, simple slopes, contrast-stable post-hoc inference; ggeffects ggplot-style predicted-effect plots; marginaleffects unified marginal-effect computation across many model classes; interactions purpose-built interaction probing tools.

heading: 8.179 Introduction
text: lasso (least absolute shrinkage selection operator) adds L1 penalty ordinary least squares, simultaneously shrinking coefficients driving exactly zero. L1 geometry — absolute-value penalty corners axis — makes lasso canonical method high-dimensional regression simultaneous estimation variable selection required. dominates ridge regression truth sparse (small number predictors carry signal) competes stepwise selection dramatically better statistical properties.

heading: 8.180 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear regression, ridge regression L2-penalised counterpart, cross-validation, bias-variance trade-.

heading: 8.181 Theory
text: lasso minimises\[\|y - X\beta\|^2 + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j|.\]Larger \(\lambda\) produces shrinkage zero coefficients. L1 penalty’s non-differentiability zero produces sparse solutions: optimisation finds corner points constraint region \(\hat\beta_j = 0\) exactly. Tuning \(\lambda\) via \(k\)-fold cross-validation standard; package glmnet returns prediction-optimal \(\lambda_{\min}\) conservative \(\lambda_{1\mathrm{se}}\) within one standard error.

heading: 8.182 Assumptions
text: Standard linear-model assumptions residual structure; predictors standardised (glmnet internally back-transforms). statistical inference selected coefficients, naive \(p\)-values invalid post-selection bias; tools like selectiveInference provide adjusted intervals.

heading: 8.183 R Implementation
code:
library(glmnet)

X <- as.matrix(mtcars[, -1])
y <- mtcars$mpg

set.seed(2026)
cvfit <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = 1)   # alpha = 1 -> lasso

plot(cvfit)

# Selected predictors at lambda.1se
as.matrix(coef(cvfit, s = "lambda.1se"))

heading: 8.184 Output & Results
text: cv.glmnet() returns cross-validation curve (mean squared error vs. log \(\lambda\)) plus two recommended \(\lambda\) values. plot(cvfit) displays CV curve confidence intervals vertical lines marking \(\lambda_{\min}\) \(\lambda_{1\mathrm{se}}\). selected predictors non-zero coefficients chosen \(\lambda\).

heading: 8.185 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Lasso regression cross-validation-selected \(\lambda_{1\mathrm{se}}\) retained three ten predictors (weight, horsepower, year); remaining seven coefficients shrunk zero exactly. Cross-validated mean squared error 6.3 mpg² chosen \(\lambda\).” Always report \(\lambda\) rule used; \(\lambda_{\min}\) tends -fit, \(\lambda_{1\mathrm{se}}\) parsimonious.

heading: 8.186 Practical Tips
text: lasso unstable predictors strongly correlated — tends pick one zero others arbitrarily; elastic net combines L1 L2 penalties mitigate .Report selected subset cross-validated fit, never report naive \(p\)-values lasso selection; invalid.inference selected coefficients, use selectiveInference::fixedLassoInf() properly adjusted confidence intervals; alternatively, debias lasso hdi::lasso.proj().binary count outcomes, switch family: cv.glmnet(X, y, family = "binomial") logistic lasso, family = "poisson" Poisson lasso.relaxed lasso (re-fitting OLS selected variables) often improves predictive performance; available via glmnet’s relax = TRUE.Always set random seed cv.glmnet(); cross-validation folds affect chosen \(\lambda\) reproducibility requires fixed seed.

heading: 8.187 R Packages Used
text: glmnet canonical lasso, ridge, elastic-net implementations cross-validated tuning; selectiveInference post-selection confidence intervals; hdi high-dimensional inference; caret tidymodels unified cross-validation alternative penalised methods.

heading: 8.190 Introduction
text: Leverage influence diagnostics identify observations whose removal substantially change regression fit. two concepts distinct: leverage measures unusual observation predictor space, influence measures much actually affects fitted coefficients. High leverage without large residual harmless; high residual without leverage produces poor fit influential coefficients; combination produces observation disproportionately drives regression.

heading: 8.191 Prerequisites
text: working understanding residual diagnostics, hat matrix, leave-one-perspective regression.

heading: 8.192 Theory
text: Hat values \(h_{ii}\) diagonal projection matrix \(H = X(X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top\). measure observation’s leverage predictor space; trace \(\sum h_{ii} = p + 1\) (number parameters), average \((p+1)/n\). Conventional threshold: \(h_{ii} > 2(p+1)/n\) high leverage.DFBETA coefficient \(j\) observation \(\): standardised change \(\hat\beta_j\) observation \(\) removed. Threshold: \(|\mathrm{DFBETA}_{ij}| > 2/\sqrt n\).Cook’s distance: combines residual leverage single influence summary; thresholds \(D_i > 4/n\) \(D_i > 1\).DFFITS: standardised change fitted value observation \(\); analogous DFBETA predictions rather coefficients.

heading: 8.193 Assumptions
text: Classical OLS assumptions; diagnostics linear-regression-specific. GLMs, influence.measures() provides analogues based one-step approximations.

heading: 8.194 R Implementation
code:
library(car)

fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)

# Hat values
h <- hatvalues(fit)
which(h > 2 * (length(coef(fit))) / nrow(mtcars))

# DFBETAs
dfb <- dfbetas(fit)
which(apply(abs(dfb), 1, max) > 2 / sqrt(nrow(mtcars)))

# Cook's distance
cooks <- cooks.distance(fit)
which(cooks > 4 / nrow(mtcars))

# Influence plot
influencePlot(fit)

heading: 8.195 Output & Results
text: diagnostic flags candidate observations. car::influencePlot() produces single integrated plot leverage x-axis, studentised residual y-axis, point size proportional Cook’s distance — canonical three-way diagnostic.

heading: 8.196 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Influence diagnostics flagged three observations candidates inspection (Maserati Bora: high leverage large residual; Cadillac Fleetwood, Lincoln Continental: high leverage moderate residuals); refitting without observations changed coefficient weight less 0.3 SE units, substantive conclusions unchanged.” Sensitivity analyses right way report influence diagnostics; pointing flagged observations without consequence checks adds noise rather signal.

heading: 8.197 Practical Tips
text: Leverage determined predictor matrix alone; outliers response residual problems, leverage.combination leverage large residual produces influence; investigate flagged points removing .Never delete influential observations without investigating cause — may informative data points signal genuine measurement errors.Report sensitivity analyses influential points removed; transparency robustness builds reader confidence.GLMs, influence.measures(fit) returns DFBETAs, hat values, Cook’s-distance equivalents based one-step approximations.Robust regression (MASS::rlm, robustbase::lmrob) automatically downweights influential observations; useful influential points genuine retained dominate fit.

heading: 8.198 R Packages Used
text: Base R hatvalues(), dfbetas(), dffits(), cooks.distance(), influence.measures(); car::influencePlot() three-way diagnostic visualisation; performance::check_outliers() tidy diagnostics; MASS::rlm() robustbase::lmrob() robust regression alternatives.

heading: 8.201 Introduction
text: lme4::lmer() standard R implementation linear mixed-effects models de facto reference formula syntax used across broader mixed-models ecosystem (lmerTest, glmmTMB, brms, nlme). Mastering random-effects syntax — (1 | group), (x | group), (x || group), nested vs. crossed grouping notation actually mean — prerequisite fluent mixed-models work. output structure equally important: block (fixed effects, random effects, residual variance) carries different inferential weight must reported correctly.

heading: 8.202 Prerequisites
text: working understanding mixed-effects models, difference fixed random effects, basic familiarity lme4 formula notation.

heading: 8.203 Theory
text: Core random-effects syntax lmer():(1 | g): random intercept per level \(g\).(x | g): random intercept correlated random slope \(x\) within \(g\).(x || g) equivalently (1 | g) + (0 + x | g): uncorrelated random intercept random slope.(1 | g/h): \(h\) nested within \(g\) (\(h\) value belongs one \(g\)).(1 | g) + (1 | h): \(g\) \(h\) crossed random factors.(0 + x | g): random slope without random intercept; rarely scientifically motivated.fixed-effects part formula (Reaction ~ Days) interpreted lm(); random-effects part adds variance components.

heading: 8.204 Assumptions
text: Linearity linear predictor, conditional independence given random effects, multivariate Normal random effects, Normal residuals constant variance, independence residuals random effects.

heading: 8.205 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4); library(lmerTest)

data(sleepstudy, package = "lme4")

# Random intercept only
m1 <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (1 | Subject), data = sleepstudy)

# Random intercept + correlated slope
m2 <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject), data = sleepstudy)

# Random intercept + uncorrelated slope
m3 <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (1 | Subject) + (0 + Days | Subject),
           data = sleepstudy)

# Nested example (sleepstudy doesn't have nesting; use simulated)
set.seed(2026)
dat <- data.frame(g = gl(5, 20), subj = factor(1:100),
                  x = rnorm(100), y = rnorm(100))
m_nest <- lmer(y ~ x + (1 | g/subj), data = dat)

anova(m1, m2, m3)
summary(m2)

heading: 8.206 Output & Results
text: summary() reports four blocks: fixed-effect coefficients standard errors (lmerTest) Satterthwaite degrees freedom \(p\)-values; random-effect variance components correlations ; residual variance; overall fit statistics including REML criterion. anova() models compares via likelihood-ratio tests fitted ML.

heading: 8.207 Interpretation
text: Reading summary(m2):Fixed effects: population-level intercept slope standard errors (lmerTest) \(p\)-values.Random effects: per-subject intercept slope variances, plus correlation; SD entries quantify -subject heterogeneity.Residual SD: within-subject (partialling random effects) error.Number obs / groups: confirms model fitting structure intended.reporting sentence: “random-intercept--slope model gave population-level reaction-time slope 10.5 ms per day sleep deprivation (95 % CI 7.6 13.4) substantial -subject heterogeneity slope (\(\mathrm{SD} = 5.92\), correlation random intercept \(-0.04\)) residual SD 25.6 ms.” Report variance components alongside fixed effects.

heading: 8.208 Practical Tips
text: Use lmerTest Satterthwaite Kenward-Roger degrees freedom; lme4 alone returns \(p\)-values fixed effects.convergence warnings, rescale predictors (scale() continuous variables) check allFit() across multiple optimisers; stable estimate across optimisers reassuring.Singular fits (correlation random effects estimated \(\pm 1\)) usually mean random structure -specified data; simplify removing slope-intercept correlations random slopes.likelihood-ratio tests variance components, null boundary; use half-half \(\chi^2\) mixture parametric bootstrap (pbkrtest::PBmodcomp) rather standard \(\chi^2\).non-Gaussian outcomes, glmer() extends formula syntax family = binomial, poisson, etc.Bayesian fits identical formulas, brms::brm() near drop-replacement adds proper credible intervals.

heading: 8.212 Introduction
text: Logistic regression diagnostics fall three families probe different aspects model adequacy: calibration (predicted probabilities match observed event rates across probability range?), discrimination (well model separate cases controls?), functional form continuous predictors (linearity logit scale reasonable assumption?). three checked reporting hazard ratios recommending clinical prediction model.

heading: 8.213 Prerequisites
text: working understanding logistic regression, logit link, distinction calibration discrimination separate aspects predictive performance.

heading: 8.214 Theory
text: Calibration plot: observed event proportion predicted probability, typically binned deciles; perfect calibration lies identity line. Deviations indicate - -prediction specific probability ranges.Hosmer-Lemeshow test: chi-squared comparison observed vs. expected counts deciles predicted probability. known weaknesses (low power, bin-choice dependence) relied upon alone.Deviance residuals: approximately Normal model; plotted fitted logit, reveal mean-structure misspecification.DFBETA / Cook’s distance: influence diagnostics influence.measures() flag observations disproportionately affect coefficients.Functional form (linearity logit): component-plus-residual plots mgcv::gam smooth terms expose non-linearity continuous predictors.

heading: 8.215 Assumptions
text: Standard logistic-regression assumptions: binary outcome, independent observations, linearity logit scale, severe multicollinearity.

heading: 8.216 R Implementation
code:
library(ResourceSelection); library(performance)

d <- mtcars
d$am <- factor(d$am)
fit <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, data = d, family = binomial)

# Hosmer-Lemeshow
hoslem.test(as.numeric(d$am) - 1, fitted(fit))

# Calibration plot via performance
check_model(fit)

# Deviance residuals
resid(fit, type = "deviance") |> plot()

heading: 8.217 Output & Results
text: hoslem.test() returns chi-squared statistic \(p\)-value. performance::check_model() produces multi-panel diagnostic figure including calibration, posterior predictive checks, residual plots, influence diagnostics. Deviance residuals plotted fitted values reveal mean-structure problems.

heading: 8.218 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Calibration plots showed observed expected event probabilities aligned across deciles, Hosmer-Lemeshow test rejecting adequate calibration (\(\chi^2_8 = 5.4\), \(p = 0.72\)); deviance residuals approximately Normal systematic pattern fitted logit, supporting linearity assumption continuous predictors.” Always pair Hosmer-Lemeshow calibration plot; test alone unreliable.

heading: 8.219 Practical Tips
text: Hosmer-Lemeshow well-known weaknesses (low power, sensitive bin choice); prefer calibration plots Brier score primary calibration assessment.non-linearity continuous predictors, add restricted cubic splines (rms::rcs(x, 4)) fit mgcv::gam s(x) compare AICs.Outliers logistic regression jointly driven leverage residual; use influence.measures(fit) dfbetas(fit) identify candidate points run sensitivity analyses removed.Report discrimination (AUC) calibration; independent aspects model quality model can excellent one poor .small samples, Hosmer-Lemeshow test can fail reject simply low power; visual calibration informative.rms::val.prob() function gives comprehensive calibration summary including intercept, slope, bias-corrected \(E_{\max}\).
