heading: 8.223 Introduction
text: Logistic regression models binary outcomes via logit link, mapping probability “event” entire real line linear regression machinery applies log-odds scale. widely used GLM clinical, epidemiological, behavioural research, producing odds ratios directly answer question “odds outcome change covariate?” fit iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS), maximising binomial likelihood.

heading: 8.224 Prerequisites
text: working understanding binomial distribution, maximum likelihood estimation, logit link, distinction odds ratios relative risks.

heading: 8.225 Theory
text: model \[Y \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(p), \qquad \mathrm{logit}(p) = \log\frac{p}{1-p} = \beta_0 + \sum_j \beta_j X_j.\]IRLS produces \(\hat\beta\) log-odds scale; exponentiating gives odds ratios, interpretable multiplicative change odds outcome per unit increase covariate, else equal. rare events (prevalence ~10 %), odds ratio approximates relative risk; common events two diverge odds ratios overstate relative risk.

heading: 8.226 Assumptions
text: Binary outcome, independent observations, linearity logit scale (log-odds linear function covariates), absence severe multicollinearity, adequate events per variable (EPV \(\ge 10\) working rule stable estimation).

heading: 8.227 R Implementation
code:
library(broom); library(pROC); library(performance)

d <- mtcars
d$am <- factor(d$am)

fit <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, data = d, family = binomial)
tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE)
glance(fit)

# Discrimination
pred <- predict(fit, type = "response")
roc(d$am, pred, quiet = TRUE) |> auc()

check_model(fit)

heading: 8.228 Output & Results
text: tidy(fit, exponentiate = TRUE) returns odds ratios confidence intervals; glance() provides AIC, deviance, pseudo-\(R^2\) summaries. AUC quantifies discrimination data; honest assessment, cross-validate hold test set.

heading: 8.229 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “multivariable logistic regression transmission type weight horsepower estimated additional 1000 lbs weight reduced odds manual transmission factor 0.10 (95 % CI 0.02 0.43, \(p = 0.003\)), adjusted horsepower; model’s -sample AUC 0.93 (95 % CI 0.84 1.00).” Always report odds ratios, raw coefficients log-odds scale.

heading: 8.230 Practical Tips
text: Report odds ratios confidence intervals; coefficients log-odds scale rarely communicative summary clinical readers.rare events (prevalence < 10 %), ≈ RR; common events, overstates RR. Switch logbin modified Poisson direct relative-risk regression needed.Small samples can produce complete separation, MLE drifts \(\pm\infty\); use Firth correction (logistf::logistf) weakly informative Bayesian priors (brms) finite estimates.Calibration matters much discrimination; assess performance::check_model() calibration plots Hosmer-Lemeshow test (caveats known limitations).matched case-control data, use conditional logistic regression (survival::clogit) preserves matched-pair structure.high-dimensional correlated predictors, switch penalised logistic regression via glmnet(family = "binomial").

heading: 8.234 Introduction
text: Mixed-effects models (also called multilevel hierarchical models) handle data clustered nested structure: patients within hospitals, repeated measures within subjects, plants within plots. model fixed effects population-level structure random effects capturing cluster-specific deviations.

heading: 8.235 Prerequisites
text: Linear regression.

heading: 8.236 Theory
text: Simplest form: random intercept model\[Y_{ij} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{ij} + u_i + \varepsilon_{ij}, \quad u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_u^2), \; \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2).\]\(u_i\) subject-specific deviations; \(\varepsilon_{ij}\) residuals. Intraclass correlation: \(\rho = \sigma_u^2 / (\sigma_u^2 + \sigma^2)\).Extensions: random slopes (\(u_{1i}\)), nested crossed groupings, non-normal responses (GLMM).

heading: 8.237 Assumptions
text: Clusters random sample population clusters.Random effects normal; residuals normal.Linearity, independence across clusters.

heading: 8.238 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4); library(lmerTest)

data(sleepstudy, package = "lme4")
head(sleepstudy)

# Random intercept
fit_ri <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (1 | Subject), data = sleepstudy)
summary(fit_ri)

# Random intercept + slope
fit_rs <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject), data = sleepstudy)
summary(fit_rs)

# Test random slope vs. intercept only
anova(fit_ri, fit_rs)

heading: 8.239 Output & Results
text: Fixed-effect table estimates, SEs, df (approximated), p-values; random-effect variance / SD summary; likelihood-ratio test nested random-effects structures.

heading: 8.240 Interpretation
text: “day sleep deprivation increased reaction time 10.5 ms (SE = 1.5, p < 0.001). -subject variation intercept substantial (SD = 37 ms), random-slope comparison showed better fit model allowing slope variation (chi-squared = 42, p < 0.001).”

heading: 8.241 Practical Tips
text: Specify random effects grouping factor.Use REML default; switch ML likelihood-ratio tests fixed-effect specifications.Singular fits often arise -parameterised random-effects structures; simplify.p-values, use lmerTest (Kenward-Roger Satterthwaite approximations) – standard lme4 provide .hierarchical binary count outcomes, use glmer glmmTMB.

heading: 8.244 Introduction
text: AIC (Akaike information criterion) BIC (Bayesian information criterion) penalised likelihood scores comparing models data. reward fit (high likelihood) penalise complexity (number parameters). differ aggressively penalise: BIC’s penalty grows sample size, BIC favours simpler models large samples AIC retains constant per-parameter cost. essential tools model-selection workflow goes beyond likelihood-ratio tests.

heading: 8.245 Prerequisites
text: working understanding maximum likelihood, log-likelihood measure fit, bias-variance trade-model complexity.

heading: 8.246 Theory
text: \[\mathrm{AIC} = -2 \log L + 2 k, \qquad \mathrm{BIC} = -2 \log L + k \log n,\]\(k\) number estimated parameters \(n\) sample size. Lower better. Conventional thresholds AIC differences (\(\Delta\mathrm{AIC}\)): \(< 2\) inconclusive, \(4\)–\(7\) moderate preference, \(> 10\) decisive. Relative model weights \(\exp(-\Delta\mathrm{AIC} / 2)\) give relative likelihood model among compared.AIC asymptotically equivalent leave-one-cross-validation regularity conditions; targets predictive accuracy. BIC approximates posterior model probability specific prior; targets identification true model one candidate set. BIC’s \(\log n\) penalty makes consistent (picks true model \(n \\infty\) true model among candidates) conservative finite samples.

heading: 8.247 Assumptions
text: Models compared must fitted data, response (transformation differences). nested models, AIC likelihood-ratio tests apply; non-nested, AIC BIC make sense.

heading: 8.248 R Implementation
code:
fit1 <- lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)
fit2 <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)
fit3 <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp + cyl + disp, data = mtcars)

AIC(fit1, fit2, fit3)
BIC(fit1, fit2, fit3)

# Relative likelihood of a model
delta_aic <- AIC(fit1, fit2, fit3)$AIC - min(AIC(fit1, fit2, fit3)$AIC)
exp(-delta_aic / 2)     # relative likelihood

heading: 8.249 Output & Results
text: AIC() BIC() return tabular comparisons degrees freedom criterion value. relative likelihoods, computed \(\exp(-\Delta\mathrm{AIC} / 2)\), give proportional support model data.

heading: 8.250 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Model 2 (mpg ~ wt + hp) lowest AIC 164.0; Model 1 (wt ) differed \(\Delta\mathrm{AIC} = 8.4\), decisive evidence Model 1, Model 3 (cyl disp added) \(\Delta\mathrm{AIC} = 1.2\), indicating meaningful improvement Model 2 despite additional predictors. BIC also selected Model 2 (\(\Delta\mathrm{BIC} = 6.1\) vs Model 1; Model 3 worse 5.5).” Always quote \(\Delta\) values rather absolute AICs; differences interpretable across model sets.

heading: 8.251 Practical Tips
text: AIC BIC can disagree; AIC favours larger models, BIC smaller. Choose question: AIC prediction, BIC parsimony near-true-model selection.Compare models fitted data response scale; AICs across log-transformed raw outcomes comparable.nested models, likelihood-ratio tests AIC work; non-nested models, AIC BIC options.Model averaging (MuMIn::dredge model.avg) often outperforms single-model selection prediction; resulting predictions average across high-AIC-weight models.AICc (corrected AIC) adds finite-sample correction preferable small \(n\) relative \(k\); formula \(\mathrm{AICc} = \mathrm{AIC} + 2k(k+1)/(n-k-1)\).Reporting one fit’s AIC alone uninformative; always quote comparison set.

heading: 8.255 Introduction
text: Multicollinearity describes situation predictors regression strongly linearly related . Coefficient estimates remain unbiased multicollinearity, standard errors inflate, making individual effects hard attribute reliably producing classic “model significant overall individual coefficient ” pattern. Diagnosing collinearity, deciding whether matters inferential question, choosing appropriate remedy routine parts every multivariable regression workflow.

heading: 8.256 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multiple linear regression, geometric interpretation regression coefficients, bias-variance trade-.

heading: 8.257 Theory
text: variance inflation factor predictor \(j\) \[\mathrm{VIF}_j = \frac{1}{1 - R^2_j},\]\(R^2_j\) comes regressing \(X_j\) predictors. \(\mathrm{VIF}_j \ge 5\) conventionally flags concern; \(\ge 10\) indicates severe collinearity. square root VIF multiplier coefficient’s standard error: VIF 16 inflates SE factor 4 relative uncorrelated predictor.condition number \(\kappa\) (scaled) design matrix ratio largest smallest singular value; \(\kappa > 30\) signals strong collinearity. Pairwise correlations expose specific offending pairs miss multivariate collinearity VIF condition number catch.Remedies include dropping one collinear predictors, combining (sum, average, principal component), shifting ridge elastic-net regression penalises coefficient norm stabilises estimates collinearity.

heading: 8.258 Assumptions
text: Standard multiple-regression setup; VIF defined predictor set used focal regression.

heading: 8.259 R Implementation
code:
library(car)

fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp + cyl + disp + drat, data = mtcars)

vif(fit)

# Condition number
X <- model.matrix(fit)[, -1]
kappa(X)

# Correlation matrix of predictors
cor(mtcars[, c("wt", "hp", "cyl", "disp", "drat")])

heading: 8.260 Output & Results
text: vif() returns one VIF per predictor; values 10 signal severe collinearity. kappa() returns condition number scaled design matrix; pairwise correlations identify specific predictors colinear .

heading: 8.261 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Cylinder count displacement VIFs 12.4 14.8 respectively, Pearson correlation \(r = 0.90\); retained cylinders biological interpretability dropped displacement, reducing maximum VIF 4.5 tightening SE retained predictors approximately 30 %.” Always report action taken response high VIF.

heading: 8.262 Practical Tips
text: High VIF always problem: prediction goal individual coefficient interpretation , leaving collinear predictors acceptable can even improve prediction.Centring predictors adding interactions reduces mechanical collinearity main interaction terms; routinely.Ridge elastic-net regression handle collinearity gracefully L2 penalty; shrink coefficients collinear predictors proportionally rather picking one inflating SE.Check VIF model change; adding removing predictors can change collinearity structure dramatically.polynomial spline terms, use orthogonal polynomials (poly(x, degree)) avoid inherent collinearity \(x, x^2, x^3, \dots\).Report VIF model diagnostics whenever multicollinearity plausible; reviewers often request .

heading: 8.263 R Packages Used

heading: 8.266 Introduction
text: Multinomial logistic regression extends binary logistic regression outcomes three unordered categories. binary logistic models log-odds one category vs another, multinomial models log-relative-risk-ratios non-reference category chosen reference, simultaneously across \(k - 1\) contrasts. standard tool predicting voting choice, transportation mode, disease subtype, categorical outcome whose categories inherent ordering.

heading: 8.267 Prerequisites
text: working understanding binary logistic regression, softmax function, relative-risk-ratio interpretation exponentiated coefficients.

heading: 8.268 Theory
text: outcome \(Y \\{1, \dots, k\}\) category 1 reference:\[\log \frac{\mathrm P(Y = j)}{\mathrm P(Y = 1)} = \beta_0^{(j)} + \sum_l \beta_l^{(j)} X_l, \qquad j = 2, \dots, k.\]model fits \(k - 1\) linear equations simultaneously maximum likelihood. Exponentiating \(\beta_l^{(j)}\) gives relative-risk ratio: multiplicative change \(\mathrm P(Y = j) / \mathrm P(Y = 1)\) per unit increase \(X_l\), holding predictors fixed.independence--irrelevant-alternatives (IIA) assumption — ratio probabilities two categories depend categories available — key restriction. IIA fails, nested mixed logit alternatives generalise model.

heading: 8.269 Assumptions
text: Unordered categorical outcome (use ordinal logistic ordered outcomes), independent observations, IIA, sufficiently large sample estimate \(k - 1\) sets coefficients (rule thumb: \(\ge 10\) events smallest category per coefficient).

heading: 8.270 R Implementation
code:
library(nnet); library(broom); library(gtsummary)

data(iris)
fit <- multinom(Species ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width, data = iris, trace = FALSE)

# Coefficients (log-RRR)
summary(fit)

# Exponentiated as relative-risk ratios
tidy(fit, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE)

# Predicted probabilities
head(predict(fit, type = "probs"))

# Publication-ready table
tbl_regression(fit, exponentiate = TRUE)

heading: 8.271 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) returns coefficient tables per non-reference category; tidy(fit, exponentiate = TRUE) provides relative-risk ratios confidence intervals. predict(..., type = "probs") returns predicted category probabilities new observations.

heading: 8.272 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Compared setosa (reference), 1-cm increase sepal length multiplied relative risk versicolor setosa 10.8 (95 % CI 3.7 31.2, \(p < 0.001\)); corresponding RRR virginica 9.0 (95 % CI 3.1 26.0, \(p < 0.001\)). reference category (setosa) chosen one three iris species serves baseline group.” Always state reference category assumed IIA.

heading: 8.273 Practical Tips
text: Test IIA Hausman-McFadden test (mlogit::hmftest); violated, switch nested mixed logit (mlogit).Choose reference category interpretable contrasts — typically common category substantively meaningful baseline.nnet::multinom() uses BFGS optimisation; large problems many categories predictors, mclogit mlogit scale better.ordered outcomes, ordinal logistic regression efficient multinomial; check ordering levels defaulting multinomial.Communicate results via predicted probability plots (ggeffects::ggpredict); readers absorb absolute probabilities better relative-risk ratios.Variable selection multinomial models awkward predictor produces \(k - 1\) coefficients; consider penalised multinomial logistic (glmnet(family = "multinomial")).

heading: 8.277 Introduction
text: Multiple linear regression extends simple regression include two predictors. coefficient represents partial effect predictor, holding others constant. allows confounding adjustment, multi-factor prediction, partial correlations.

heading: 8.278 Prerequisites
text: Simple linear regression.

heading: 8.279 Theory
text: Model: \(Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \ldots + \beta_p X_p + \varepsilon\), \(\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\).OLS estimates: \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^\top X)^{-1} X^\top Y\). coefficient partial slope: change \(E[Y]\) per unit \(X_j\), holding predictors fixed.Collinearity: highly correlated predictors inflate SE individual coefficients. Diagnose variance inflation factors (VIF): \(\mathrm{VIF}_j = 1 / (1 - R^2_j)\), \(R^2_j\) \(R^2\) regressing \(X_j\) others. VIF > 5 concerning; > 10 severe.\(R^2\) proportion variance explained; adjusted \(R^2\) penalises model complexity.

heading: 8.280 Assumptions
text: Linearity predictor, independence, homoscedasticity, normality residuals, severe collinearity.

heading: 8.281 R Implementation
code:
library(broom); library(car); library(performance)

data(mtcars)
fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp + cyl + disp, data = mtcars)

tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE)
glance(fit)
vif(fit)

check_model(fit)

heading: 8.282 Output & Results

heading: 8.283 Interpretation

heading: 8.284 Practical Tips

heading: 8.287 Introduction
text: Negative binomial (NB) regression standard remedy overdispersed count data — counts whose variance exceeds mean, real-world count data unobserved heterogeneity present. generalises Poisson regression adding dispersion parameter \(\theta\) explicitly models additional variance, confidence intervals tests respect true uncertainty rather artificially narrow Poisson ones. epidemiological count data, hospital admissions, ecological abundance counts, almost context Poisson dispersion checks fail, NB natural next step.

heading: 8.288 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Poisson regression, log link, concept overdispersion failure \(\mathrm{Var}(Y) = \mathbb E[Y]\).

heading: 8.289 Theory
text: NB distribution arises Poisson-Gamma mixture: \(Y \mid \lambda \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda)\) \(\lambda \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\theta, \theta/\mu)\). Marginalising Gamma yields \(Y \sim \mathrm{NegBin}(\mu, \theta)\) mean \(\mu\) variance\[\mathrm{Var}(Y) = \mu + \mu^2 / \theta.\]dispersion parameter \(\theta\) controls extra-Poisson variance; \(\theta \\infty\), NB reduces Poisson, \(\theta \0\), variance grows quadratically mean.regression model uses log link \(\log \mu = X^\top \beta\). Coefficients interpreted log-rate ratios identically Poisson; difference standard errors inference reflect genuine -Poisson variance.

heading: 8.290 Assumptions
text: Non-negative integer outcomes, independent observations conditional covariates, NB variance structure \(\mu + \mu^2/\theta\). excess zeros beyond NB predicts, zero-inflated extensions needed.

heading: 8.291 R Implementation
code:
library(MASS)

set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(x = rnorm(300))
d$y <- rnbinom(300, mu = exp(1 + 0.5 * d$x), size = 2)

fit <- glm.nb(y ~ x, data = d)
summary(fit)
exp(coef(fit))   # rate ratios
fit$theta

heading: 8.292 Output & Results
text: glm.nb() returns regression coefficients log scale (Wald \(z\)-tests, confidence intervals) plus estimated dispersion parameter \(\hat\theta\). Exponentiating coefficients gives rate ratios; comparing AIC Poisson fit data confirms whether NB justified.

heading: 8.293 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Negative binomial regression estimated dispersion \(\hat\theta = 2.1\) (indicating substantial overdispersion relative Poisson, \(\Delta\mathrm{AIC} = 87\)) estimated rate ratio 1.65 per unit increase \(x\) (95 % CI 1.40 1.95, \(p < 0.001\)); Poisson fit underestimated standard error 22 %, illustrating consequence ignoring overdispersion.” Always report \(\hat\theta\) alongside rate ratios; carries information residual heterogeneity.

heading: 8.294 Practical Tips
text: Compare NB Poisson AIC likelihood-ratio test (lmtest::lrtest); NB additional parameter \(\theta\) well-defined inference.zero-inflation addition overdispersion, use pscl::zeroinfl(..., dist = "negbin") glmmTMB(family = nbinom2(), ziformula = ...).count data hierarchical structure, glmmTMB(family = nbinom2()) adds random effects top NB likelihood; lme4::glmer.nb() older, less reliable alternative.large \(\hat\theta\) (\(> 100\)) suggests Poisson suffice NB extension unnecessary; small values (\(< 1\)) indicate severe overdispersion may signal mis-specification rather just Poisson failure.NB1 parameterisation (glmmTMB(family = nbinom1())) gives variance proportional mean (linear); NB2 (default MASS::glm.nb) gives quadratic variance. Choose based variance-mean relationship residual plots.Pearson residuals NB fit look approximately uniform; systematic patterns reveal model misspecification.

heading: 8.298 Introduction
text: Mixed-effects models require careful specification random-effects structure, common confusion nested crossed designs. Nested means one grouping factor’s levels sit strictly within another’s: patients within hospitals, schools within districts, plots within fields. Crossed means levels one factor appear across levels another: subjects rate items, items rated multiple subjects, students take set exams. Misspecifying one produces wrong variance components biased standard errors.

heading: 8.299 Prerequisites
text: working understanding mixed-effects models, random intercepts, geometric meaning “grouping factor” hierarchical crossed design.

heading: 8.300 Theory
text: Nested random effects: level inner factor belongs exactly one level outer factor. Patient 17 hospital different conceptual entity patient 17 hospital B even IDs coincide. lme4 syntax: (1 | outer/inner) , equivalently, (1 | outer) + (1 | outer:inner). forward-slash shorthand expands automatically; explicit interaction notation removes ambiguity readers.Crossed random effects: level one factor combines multiple levels . Subjects rate items, items rated subjects. lme4 syntax: (1 | subject) + (1 | item). variance components two grouping factors estimated independently, implied hierarchy.practical heuristic: can ask “\(\) \(B\) belong ?” get unique answer, design nested. \(B\)s reused across \(\)s, design crossed.

heading: 8.301 Assumptions
text: grouping structure correctly identified; random effects within grouping factor independent Normal; crossed designs, two grouping factors conditionally independent given model.

heading: 8.302 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4)

# Nested example (simulated)
set.seed(2026)
hosp <- gl(5, 20)           # 5 hospitals
pt   <- factor(seq_along(hosp))   # 100 unique patients
y    <- rnorm(100) + rep(rnorm(5, 0, 3), each = 20)

fit_nested <- lmer(y ~ 1 + (1 | hosp))
# Crossed
item <- gl(10, 10, length = 100)
y2 <- rnorm(100) + rep(rnorm(10, 0, 2), length.out = 100) +
                   rep(rnorm(10, 0, 1), length.out = 100)
fit_crossed <- lmer(y2 ~ 1 + (1 | hosp) + (1 | item))
summary(fit_crossed)

heading: 8.303 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) reports variance components random effect. nested designs variance decomposed hierarchical level; crossed designs two grouping factors contribute independently. Likelihood-ratio tests simpler models confirm whether random effect needed.

heading: 8.304 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Crossed random effects subject item revealed substantial subject-level variation (\(\hat\sigma_{\mathrm{subject}} = 2.1\)) smaller item-level variation (\(\hat\sigma_{\mathrm{item}} = 1.0\)); residual SD 1.5. two grouping factors conceptually independent — every subject saw every item — structure correctly specified crossed rather nested.” Always describe grouping structure explicitly methods.

heading: 8.305 Practical Tips
text: Nested IDs must unique within parent level; patient IDs repeat across hospitals (e.g. patient 17 hospital different patient 17 hospital B), use explicit hospital:patient interaction disambiguate.Crossed designs small per-factor cluster counts (< 5 levels) can struggle; verify convergence watch singular fits.(1 | /B) shorthand convenient can opaque; documenting structure (1 | ) + (1 | :B) explicitly removes ambiguity.glmmTMB handles many complex random structures (crossed + non-Normal outcomes, structured covariance) gracefully lme4.longitudinal data crossed item / occasion structure, include random factors plus temporal autocorrelation within-subject correlation term.common diagnosis tool: xtabs(~ + B, data = d) shows crossing pattern directly. diagonal-block table indicates nesting; dense table indicates crossing.

heading: 8.306 R Packages Used
text: lme4 lmer() glmer() nested crossed random effects; lmerTest \(p\)-values via Satterthwaite Kenward-Roger; glmmTMB complex random structures non-Normal outcomes; pbkrtest parametric-bootstrap LRTs variance components.

heading: 8.309 Introduction
text: count regression, offset predictor whose coefficient fixed 1. standard mechanism modelling rates per unit exposure: events per person-year follow-, infections per 1000 hospital admissions, mutations per kilobase DNA. Without offset, model fits expected counts treats exposure confounder; offset, model fits expected rates exposure correctly accounted . Forgetting offset leading cause biased coefficient estimates epidemiological count-data work.

heading: 8.310 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Poisson negative-binomial regression, log link, distinction counts rates inferential targets.

heading: 8.311 Theory
text: events \(Y_i\) observed exposure \(E_i\), modelling rate \(Y_i / E_i\) via Poisson regression equivalent modelling count \(Y_i\) \(\log E_i\) known offset:\[\log \mathbb E[Y_i] = \log E_i + X_i^\top \beta \quad\Leftrightarrow\quad \log \mathbb E[Y_i / E_i] = X_i^\top \beta.\]offset \(\log E_i\) enters linear predictor fixed coefficient 1, contribution treated known rather estimated. remaining coefficients \(\beta\) interpretable log-rate ratios; exponentiating gives rate ratios per unit covariate change.construction extends negative-binomial regression (overdispersion), logistic regression offset = log(odds_baseline) known baseline rates, Cox regression person-time risk plays analogous role.

heading: 8.312 Assumptions
text: Exposure \(E_i > 0\) every observation; rate proportional exposure (doubling exposure doubles expected count, else equal); count distribution (Poisson, NB) appropriate data.

heading: 8.313 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(
  x = rnorm(200),
  person_years = runif(200, 0.5, 5)
)
d$events <- rpois(200, lambda = d$person_years * exp(-2 + 0.5 * d$x))

# With offset
fit <- glm(events ~ x, data = d, family = poisson,
           offset = log(person_years))
summary(fit)
exp(coef(fit))      # rate ratios per unit x

# WITHOUT offset (wrong!): models expected count ignoring exposure
fit_no_off <- glm(events ~ x, data = d, family = poisson)
summary(fit_no_off)

heading: 8.314 Output & Results
text: offset, slope coefficient log-rate ratio per unit increase \(x\); exponentiating gives rate ratio (close data-generating value \(\exp(0.5) = 1.65\)). Without offset, coefficients absorb variation exposure biased; comparison illustrates importance correctly specifying rate model.

heading: 8.315 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “accounting variable follow-via offset \(\log(\text{person-years})\), unit increase \(x\) associated 65 % higher event rate per person-year (rate ratio 1.65, 95 % CI 1.32 2.05, \(p < 0.001\)); omitting offset gives misleading ‘count ratio’ 1.43 conflates rate effect average follow-.” Always state exposure unit (person-years, 1000 admissions, kilobases) reporting rate ratios.

heading: 8.316 Practical Tips
text: offset \(\log(\text{exposure})\), exposure ; using exposure directly common bug.offset = log(exposure) argument glm() keeps coefficient fixed 1; including log(exposure) regular predictor estimates coefficient produces different model.Offsets generalise negative-binomial, zero-inflated, survival models; check family-specific syntax (glmmTMB, MASS::glm.nb, survival::coxph).binomial regression, analogous construction uses offset = log(p_baseline / (1 - p_baseline)) known baseline respected.Always inspect whether exposure varies meaningfully across observations deciding include omit offset; ignoring varying exposure biases coefficient estimates.Report rate ratios exponentiating; coefficient log scale rarely communicative summary clinical readers.

heading: 8.317 R Packages Used
text: Base R glm() offset = family = poisson; MASS::glm.nb() negative-binomial regression offsets; glmmTMB mixed-effects count models offsets overdispersion; survival::coxph() survival analyses exposure enters time risk.

heading: 8.320 Introduction
text: Ordinal logistic regression — commonly proportional-odds model — handles ordered categorical outcomes Likert-scale responses, disease severity grades, NYHA functional class, cancer stage. exploits ordering via cumulative-probability model: cumulative odds category share set regression coefficients across cut-points. Multinomial logistic ignores ordering estimates separate coefficients non-reference category, wasteful ordering real informative.

heading: 8.321 Prerequisites
text: working understanding binary logistic regression, ordered categorical variables, cumulative-probability framing ordinal data.

heading: 8.322 Theory
text: proportional-odds model \[\log \frac{\mathrm P(Y \le j)}{\mathrm P(Y > j)} = \alpha_j - X^\top \beta, \qquad j = 1, \dots, k - 1,\]\(k\) ordered categories. cut-points \(\alpha_j\) shift \(j\), regression coefficients \(\beta\) constrained identical across cut-points — proportional-odds assumption. Exponentiated coefficients cumulative odds ratios: per unit increase \(X\), odds higher category multiplied \(\exp(\beta)\), regardless threshold crossed.proportional-odds assumption testable Brant’s test likelihood-ratio test flexible partial-proportional-odds generalised ordered logit model.

heading: 8.323 Assumptions
text: Ordered categorical outcome least three levels, proportional odds across cut-points, independent observations, adequate counts category (rule thumb: least 5–10 events per coefficient smallest category).

heading: 8.324 R Implementation
code:
library(MASS); library(brant)

d <- data.frame(
  y = factor(sample(c("low", "med", "high"), 200, replace = TRUE),
             levels = c("low", "med", "high"), ordered = TRUE),
  x = rnorm(200)
)
d$y <- factor(as.character(d$y)[sample(1:200,
                                        prob = 1 + plogis(d$x), replace = TRUE)],
              levels = c("low", "med", "high"), ordered = TRUE)

fit <- polr(y ~ x, data = d, Hess = TRUE)
summary(fit)
exp(coef(fit))

# Test proportional odds
brant(fit)

heading: 8.325 Output & Results
text: polr() returns coefficients cumulative-logit scale plus cut-point estimates. Exponentiating gives cumulative odds ratios. brant() reports omnibus test proportional odds plus per-coefficient tests; significant results indicate assumption fails covariate.

heading: 8.326 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Ordinal logistic regression estimated 1-SD increase \(x\) multiplied cumulative odds higher category 2.10 (95 % CI 1.50 2.94, \(p < 0.001\)); Brant’s test reject proportional-odds assumption (omnibus \(p = 0.44\), per-covariate \(p > 0.20\)). cumulative odds-ratio interpretation applies uniformly across low-vs-(med + high) (low + med)-vs-high cut-points.” Always test report proportional-odds assumption.

heading: 8.327 Practical Tips
text: Always test proportional-odds assumption Brant’s test LRT partial-proportional-odds model; reporting cumulative ORs without check incomplete.proportional odds fails one covariates, use partial-proportional-odds (VGAM::vglm cumulative(parallel = FALSE ~ x)) generalised ordered logit (oglmx).three four categories severe non-proportionality, multinomial logistic regression sometimes simpler partial-proportional-odds, cost ignoring ordering.ordinal::clm() provides modern implementation mixed-effects extensions (clmm) richer post-hoc tools.Report cumulative odds ratios confidence intervals; phrasing “odds higher category” clearest way communicate cumulative-interpretation.predictions absolute-probability scale, use predict(fit, type = "probs"); readers absorb predicted category probabilities better cumulative odds ratios.

heading: 8.328 R Packages Used
text: MASS::polr() canonical proportional-odds implementation; ordinal::clm() modern alternative mixed-effects extensions; brant proportional-odds testing; VGAM partial-proportional-odds ordinal extensions; oglmx generalised ordered logit; gtsummary::tbl_regression() publication-ready tables.

heading: 8.331 Introduction
text: Partial correlation measures linear association two variables removing linear influence one covariates . ordinary correlation captures bivariate association including indirect effect confounders, partial correlation isolates direct linear relationship. bivariate analogue adjusted regression coefficient one simplest tools thinking confounding observational data.

heading: 8.332 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Pearson correlation, multiple linear regression, concept confounding.

heading: 8.333 Theory
text: three variables \(X, Y, Z\), partial correlation \(X\) \(Y\) adjusted \(Z\) \[r_{XY \cdot Z} = \frac{r_{XY} - r_{XZ} r_{YZ}}{\sqrt{(1 - r_{XZ}^2)(1 - r_{YZ}^2)}}.\]equals Pearson correlation residuals regressing \(X\) \(Z\) \(Y\) \(Z\). construction generalises controlling multiple covariates: regress vector covariates , correlate residuals.semi-partial (part) correlation removes influence \(Z\) \(X\) , retaining \(Y\)’s association \(Z\). multiple regression, partial \(r\) predictor \(j\) equals \(\mathrm{sign}(\hat\beta_j) \sqrt{t_j^2 / (t_j^2 + \mathrm{df}_{\mathrm{res}})}\).

heading: 8.334 Assumptions
text: Linearity pairwise relationship; multivariate Normality (standard inference test); accurately measured covariates; partial-correlation interpretation causal additional assumptions covariate set.

heading: 8.335 R Implementation
code:
library(ppcor)

# Partial correlation of wt and mpg given hp
pcor.test(mtcars$wt, mtcars$mpg, mtcars$hp)

# All partial correlations in a dataset
pcor(mtcars[, c("mpg", "wt", "hp", "disp")])$estimate

# Manual via residuals
res_wt  <- resid(lm(wt ~ hp, data = mtcars))
res_mpg <- resid(lm(mpg ~ hp, data = mtcars))
cor(res_wt, res_mpg)

heading: 8.336 Output & Results
text: pcor.test() returns partial correlation estimate, \(t\)-statistic, \(p\)-value. pcor() returns full partial-correlation matrix, useful many adjustments simultaneously interest. manual residual-based computation confirms algebraic identity.

heading: 8.337 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “adjusting horsepower, weight miles-per-gallon partial correlation \(-0.66\) (\(p < 0.001\), \(n = 32\)); negative association persists strongly even controlling engine’s power output, suggesting independent role vehicle weight beyond correlation engine size.” Distinguish partial semi-partial correlation reports; readers often confuse .

heading: 8.338 Practical Tips
text: Partial correlation bivariate analogue adjusted regression coefficient; already fitted multiple regression, corresponding partial \(r\) comes free \(t\)-statistic.Semi-partial correlations describe “unique variance contribution” multiple regression; often reported \(\Delta R^2\) adding one predictor existing model.non-Normal ordinal data, use partial Spearman correlation (ppcor::pcor(method = "spearman")); rank-based version robust outliers.graphical / network analyses, partial correlations natural input Gaussian graphical models (qgraph, huge); encode conditional dependencies multivariate Normal.Causal interpretation partial correlations requires conditioning set blocks confounding paths; statistical independence conditioning imply causation.high-dimensional data (\(p\) near \(n\)), partial correlations inverse covariance matrix unstable; use regularised graphical-lasso estimators (glasso).

heading: 8.339 R Packages Used
text: ppcor pcor() pcor.test(); psych::partial.r() alternative interface; qgraph huge partial-correlation networks; glasso regularised inverse-covariance estimation high dimensions.

heading: 8.342 Introduction
text: Poisson regression models count data log link, making canonical GLM events per unit exposure: hospital admissions per month, mutations per kilobase, falls per resident-year. log-linear structure means coefficients interpretable log-rate ratios, model accommodates varying exposures naturally offset term. defining assumption — equidispersion, \(\mathrm{Var}(Y) = \mathbb E[Y]\) — also main limitation: real count data typically overdispersed, either quasi-Poisson, negative binomial, zero-inflated extensions required.

heading: 8.343 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Poisson distribution, generalised linear models, log link, role offset modelling rates rather counts.

heading: 8.344 Theory
text: model \(Y \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\mu)\) \(\log \mu = X^\top \beta\). Exponentiated coefficients rate ratios: \(\exp(\beta_j)\) multiplies expected count per unit increase \(X_j\), else equal. rate-per-exposure problems, offset \(\log(\text{exposure})\) converts model rate model:\[\log \mathbb E[Y] = \log(\text{exposure}) + X^\top \beta.\]offset’s coefficient fixed 1, remaining \(\beta\) represent log-rate ratios per unit covariate change.Equidispersion central assumption; variance exceeds mean, standard errors underestimated inference anti-conservative. Pearson chi-squared divided residual degrees freedom standard dispersion check; values much greater 1 motivate quasi-Poisson negative binomial.

heading: 8.345 Assumptions
text: Non-negative integer outcomes, independent observations, equidispersion (variance equal mean), linear relationship covariates log-rate.

heading: 8.346 R Implementation
code:
library(broom)

# Simulated: event counts per patient-year
set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(
  age = rnorm(300, 60, 12),
  smoker = rbinom(300, 1, 0.3),
  exposure_years = runif(300, 0.5, 5)
)
d$events <- rpois(nrow(d),
  lambda = d$exposure_years * exp(-2 + 0.02 * d$age + 0.6 * d$smoker))

fit <- glm(events ~ age + smoker, data = d, family = poisson,
           offset = log(exposure_years))
tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE)
glance(fit)

# Check overdispersion
dispersion <- sum(resid(fit, type = "pearson")^2) / fit$df.residual
dispersion

heading: 8.347 Output & Results
text: tidy(fit, exponentiate = TRUE) returns rate ratios confidence intervals; glance() provides AIC, deviance, model-level summaries. dispersion ratio close 1 confirms Poisson appropriate; values \(\gg 1\) suggest overdispersion needs different family handling.

heading: 8.348 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “adjusting age (rate ratio 1.02 per year, 95 % CI 1.01 1.03), smokers event rate 1.81 times non-smokers (95 % CI 1.40 2.34, \(p < 0.001\)); dispersion ratio 1.05 consistent Poisson assumption.” Always report rate ratios multiplicative scale check dispersion.

heading: 8.349 Practical Tips
text: Check dispersion statistic immediately fitting; \(\gg 1\), switch quasi-Poisson (family = quasipoisson) inflated SEs negative binomial (MASS::glm.nb) explicit overdispersion modelling.Include offset whenever exposure varies across observations; without , coefficients absorb exposure variation biased.Coefficients log-rate scale; exponentiate rate ratios publication-friendly units.excessive zero counts beyond Poisson predicts, fit zero-inflated hurdle model (pscl::zeroinfl, glmmTMB).count data hierarchical structure (repeated measures, clustered observations), use Poisson mixed models (glmer(family = poisson) glmmTMB(family = poisson)).Pearson residuals look approximately uniformly scattered; systematic patterns suggest model misspecification (missing covariates, non-linear effects, overdispersion).

heading: 8.353 Introduction
text: Polynomial regression fits outcome sum powers predictor: linear, quadratic, cubic, higher. simplest way model non-linear relationship without moving outside linear-model framework. Quadratic cubic terms can capture curvature biological dose-response, growth, saturation curves, resulting model retains familiar OLS inference tools. principal weakness — wild extrapolation outside data range instability high degrees — motivates disciplined alternative restricted cubic splines.

heading: 8.354 Prerequisites
text: working understanding simple linear regression, geometric meaning coefficients, bias-variance trade-model complexity grows.

heading: 8.355 Theory
text: polynomial model \[Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 X^2 + \dots + \beta_d X^d + \varepsilon.\]Two parameterisations coexist:Orthogonal polynomials (poly(X, d)): basis functions mutually uncorrelated. Numerically stable high degrees; individual coefficients lose intuitive interpretation predictions overall fit identical raw polynomials.Raw polynomials (poly(X, d, raw = TRUE) (X^2)): retain natural “per unit” interpretation introduce collinearity inflates standard errors individual coefficients.High-degree polynomials -fit extrapolate poorly; degrees 2 3 cover applications, beyond splines (natural cubic, restricted cubic) usually preferable constrain tail behaviour.

heading: 8.356 Assumptions
text: Standard regression assumptions residuals; polynomial degree high enough capture underlying curvature high chases noise.

heading: 8.357 R Implementation
code:
# Simulated quadratic relationship
set.seed(2026)
x <- runif(100, 0, 10)
y <- 2 + 3 * x - 0.4 * x^2 + rnorm(100, 0, 2)

# Orthogonal quadratic
fit_ortho <- lm(y ~ poly(x, 2))
summary(fit_ortho)

# Raw quadratic (interpretable coefficients)
fit_raw <- lm(y ~ poly(x, 2, raw = TRUE))
summary(fit_raw)

# Visualise
plot(x, y, pch = 20)
curve(predict(fit_raw, newdata = data.frame(x)), add = TRUE,
      col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2)

heading: 8.358 Output & Results
text: fits produce identical \(R^2\), residuals, predictions; coefficient values differ parameterisation. Visualising fitted curve overlaid data primary inferential output, since coefficients individual polynomial terms directly interpretable.

heading: 8.359 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “quadratic regression \(y\) \(x\) recovered simulated curvature (\(R^2 = 0.78\)); raw-polynomial coefficients give peak \(\hat x = -\hat\beta_1 / (2\hat\beta_2) = 3.75\), consistent data-generating function. linear-fit performed substantially worse (\(R^2 = 0.30\)) missed curvature entirely.” Always show fitted curve; coefficient tables alone communicate non-linear structure.

heading: 8.360 Practical Tips
text: Degrees 2 3 usually suffice biological clinical applications; beyond , splines disciplined.Orthogonal polynomials numerically stable high degrees avoid matrix-conditioning problems raw polynomials produce.Use anova(fit1, fit2) test whether higher-order term improves fit; partial \(F\)-test right tool.extrapolate beyond range \(X\); polynomials diverge \(\pm\infty\) rapidly outside support.multiple predictors non-linear effects, GAMs (mgcv::gam) automate smoothness selection avoid manual choice polynomial degree.Restricted cubic splines (rms::rcs) usually preferable high-order polynomials clinical reporting constrain tail behaviour linear.

heading: 8.364 Introduction
text: Probit regression models binary outcome inverse standard Normal cumulative distribution function — idea logistic regression Normal-tailed link instead logistic-tailed one. two methods produce essentially identical predicted probabilities; coefficients differ fixed scaling factor 1.6. Probit natural choice latent Normal-variable interpretation matches substantive theory; logistic common clinical reporting odds ratios easier communicate.

heading: 8.365 Prerequisites
text: working understanding logistic regression, standard Normal CDF, latent-variable interpretation binary regression.

heading: 8.366 Theory
text: probit model \[\mathrm P(Y = 1 \mid X) = \Phi(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \dots + \beta_p X_p),\]\(\Phi\) standard Normal CDF. Coefficients interpretable probit scale: one-unit increase \(X_j\) corresponds \(\beta_j\)-standard-deviation change underlying latent Normal propensity. inverse relationship \(\Phi^{-1}(p) = X^\top \beta\) probit link.Probit coefficients approximately logistic coefficients divided 1.6 (\(\sigma_{\mathrm{logistic}} = \pi / \sqrt 3 \approx 1.81\) vs. \(\sigma_{\mathrm{Normal}} = 1\)). link functions agree near \(p = 0.5\) diverge slightly tails — logistic heavier tails — coefficient comparisons across two scales require rescaling.

heading: 8.367 Assumptions
text: Binary outcome, independent observations, latent linear relationship probit scale, adequate sample size relative predictors (events-per-variable \(\ge 10\)).

heading: 8.368 R Implementation
code:
d <- mtcars
d$am <- factor(d$am)

fit_probit <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, data = d,
                  family = binomial(link = "probit"))
summary(fit_probit)

# Compare with logit
fit_logit <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, data = d, family = binomial)

# Predictions should be similar
pred_p <- predict(fit_probit, type = "response")
pred_l <- predict(fit_logit, type = "response")
cor(pred_p, pred_l)

heading: 8.369 Output & Results
text: summary(fit_probit) returns coefficients probit scale Wald \(z\)-tests. correlation probit logit predicted probabilities typically 0.999, confirming two methods interchangeable prediction purposes.

heading: 8.370 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Probit regression estimated 1000-lb increase vehicle weight reduced latent propensity manual transmission 1.8 SDs (probit coefficient \(-1.8\), 95 % CI \(-3.0\) \(-0.7\), \(p = 0.002\)); average marginal effect probability scale \(-0.27\) per 1000 lbs (95 % CI \(-0.42\) \(-0.13\), computed via margins).” Always pair probit coefficients average marginal effects communicating non-statistical audiences.

heading: 8.371 Practical Tips
text: Probit logistic give essentially identical predicted probabilities; choose based convention latent-variable interpretation, prediction accuracy.Probit coefficients lack clean odds-ratio interpretation logistic; report average marginal effects (margins::margins, marginaleffects::avg_predictions) clinical communication.multilevel Bayesian binary models, probit links sometimes computationally simpler logistic; brms Stan handle .probit-vs-logit choice almost always matter reporting convention rather statistical superiority.ordinal extensions, probit gives cumulative-probit (threshold) model; survival analysis, probit link appears accelerated-failure-time formulations.Predictions models agree within 1–2 percentage points across full range fitted probabilities; large discrepancies signal model misspecification.

heading: 8.372 R Packages Used
text: Base R glm() family = binomial(link = "probit"); margins::margins() marginaleffects::avg_predictions() average marginal effects; mfx::probitmfx() direct marginal-effect output; mvProbit multivariate probit multiple binary outcomes jointly modelled.

heading: 8.375 Introduction
text: Quantile regression estimates chosen conditional quantile outcome (median, lower percentile, upper percentile) rather conditional mean. result richer description covariates affect outcome distribution: covariate may different effect 10th percentile (shifts lower tail) 90th (shifts upper tail). Median regression L1 alternative OLS robust outliers; multi-quantile fits expose heteroscedasticity asymmetric effects mean regression hides.

heading: 8.376 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear regression, conditional quantiles, difference modelling mean versus median percentiles distribution.

heading: 8.377 Theory
text: Mean regression minimises squared residuals; quantile regression minimises asymmetric absolute-value loss:\[\sum_i \rho_\tau(y_i - x_i^\top \beta), \qquad \rho_\tau(u) = u(\tau - \mathbf 1\{u < 0\}).\]Setting \(\tau = 0.5\) gives median regression. objective convex non-differentiable; standard solvers use linear programming. Standard errors come rank-based bootstrap methods. Inference multiple \(\tau\) values produces quantile process — trajectory coefficient \(\tau\) varies — informative summary covariates shift conditional distribution.

heading: 8.378 Assumptions
text: Fewer OLS: independence correct specification chosen quantile. Normality, homoscedasticity, constant variance assumption. robustness comes bounded influence single observation.

heading: 8.379 R Implementation
code:
library(quantreg)

fit_median <- rq(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, tau = 0.5)
summary(fit_median)

# Multiple quantiles
fit_q <- rq(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars, tau = c(0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.9))
summary(fit_q)
plot(summary(fit_q))

# Comparison to OLS
fit_lm <- lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)
coef(fit_lm)
coef(rq(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars, tau = 0.5))

heading: 8.380 Output & Results
text: rq() returns coefficient estimates per requested \(\tau\) rank-based standard errors. plot(summary()) method shows coefficient function \(\tau\), confidence bands — canonical visualisation covariate effects shift across conditional distribution.

heading: 8.381 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Quantile regression mpg weight five quantiles (\(\tau \\{0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.9\}\)) showed 1000-lb increase weight reduced mpg 6.8 10th percentile, 5.7 median, 4.2 90th percentile (\(p < 0.001\)); gradient indicates heteroscedasticity ordinary mean regression obscure.” Always plot quantile process reporting multi-\(\tau\) results; communicates coefficient table.

heading: 8.382 Practical Tips
text: Median regression (\(\tau = 0.5\)) L1 alternative OLS robust outliers; reach residual diagnostics show heavy tails.Multi-quantile fits expose heteroscedasticity distributional shape changes; report least three quantiles (\(\tau \\{0.25, 0.5, 0.75\}\)) characterise conditional distribution.Bootstrap standard errors (se = "boot") reliable rank-based small samples; specify R = 999 resamples.non-linear effects, quantreg::nlrq() quantreg::rqss() add non-linear smoothing-spline extensions.longitudinal clustered data, lqmm::lqmm() qrLMM::QRLMM() extend quantile regression mixed-effects.Quantile regression require homoscedasticity often informative OLS plus robust SEs heteroscedastic data.

heading: 8.383 R Packages Used
text: quantreg rq(), rqss(), bootstrap SEs; lqmm linear quantile mixed-effects models; qrLMM quantile regression linear mixed models random intercepts; quantregForest random-forest extensions quantile prediction.

heading: 8.386 Introduction
text: Quasi-Poisson regression simplest fix overdispersion count data: keep Poisson mean structure \(\log \mu = X^\top \beta\), introduce free dispersion parameter \(\phi\) scales variance \(\phi \mu\) rather Poisson-mandated \(\mu\). Coefficient estimates identical Poisson; standard errors change, multiplied \(\sqrt{\phi}\). workhorse “quick fix” Poisson dispersion check fails moderate amount need likelihood-based comparison.

heading: 8.387 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Poisson regression, generalised linear models, concept overdispersion.

heading: 8.388 Theory
text: Quasi-Poisson uses quasi-likelihood: specifies first two moments \(\mathbb E[Y] = \mu\) \(\mathrm{Var}(Y) = \phi \mu\), without committing particular distribution. dispersion parameter estimated Pearson chi-squared divided residual degrees freedom. Coefficient estimates unchanged Poisson; standard errors inflated \(\sqrt{\hat\phi}\), confidence intervals widen accordingly.Unlike negative binomial — genuine likelihood AIC — quasi-Poisson quasi-likelihood model: AIC, BIC, likelihood-ratio tests usual sense. F-type tests via anova(fit, test = "F") appropriate comparing nested quasi-Poisson models.

heading: 8.389 Assumptions
text: Non-negative integer outcomes, variance-mean relationship \(\mathrm{Var}(Y) = \phi \mu\) (linear mean), independent observations. quadratic variance-mean (typical strongly overdispersed counts), negative binomial appropriate.

heading: 8.390 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(x = rnorm(200))
d$y <- rnbinom(200, mu = exp(1 + 0.5 * d$x), size = 2)   # overdispersed

fit_pois <- glm(y ~ x, data = d, family = poisson)
fit_qp   <- glm(y ~ x, data = d, family = quasipoisson)

# Dispersion estimate
summary(fit_qp)$dispersion
# Compare SE
summary(fit_pois)$coefficients
summary(fit_qp)$coefficients

heading: 8.391 Output & Results
text: quasipoisson family produces point estimates Poisson inflated standard errors. dispersion estimate summary(fit)$dispersion; comparing two coefficient summaries shows much SEs change.

heading: 8.392 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Quasi-Poisson regression estimated dispersion \(\hat\phi = 2.8\), indicating substantial overdispersion; standard errors inflated \(\sqrt{2.8} = 1.67\) relative Poisson, giving 95 % confidence interval rate ratio per unit \(x\) 1.30 2.00 (Poisson misleadingly gave 1.45 1.85).” Always quote dispersion alongside inflated SEs.

heading: 8.393 Practical Tips
text: Quasi-Poisson acceptable estimation confidence intervals, lacks proper likelihood — AIC, BIC, LRTs usual sense.formal model selection AIC, switch negative binomial (MASS::glm.nb glmmTMB(family = nbinom2)); additional dispersion parameter genuinely estimated.Quasi-Poisson assumes linear variance-mean (\(\mathrm{Var}(Y) = \phi \mu\)); NB assumes quadratic (\(\mathrm{Var}(Y) = \mu + \mu^2/\theta\)). Inspect Pearson residuals fitted values choose .large dispersion (\(\hat\phi > 3\)), neither quasi-Poisson NB may suffice; consider zero-inflated mixture models.Use F-tests (anova(fit, test = "F")) nested quasi-Poisson model comparisons; LRTs available.longitudinal clustered counts, glmmTMB(family = nbinom2) preferable quasi-Poisson provides random effects proper likelihood.

heading: 8.394 R Packages Used
text: Base R glm() family = quasipoisson; MASS::glm.nb() negative binomial recommended alternative; glmmTMB mixed-effects count models proper likelihoods; DHARMa residual diagnostics across count-model families.

heading: 8.397 Introduction
text: \(R^2\) proportion variance outcome explained regression — commonly reported summary regression fit. weakness well known: \(R^2\) never decreases predictors added, distinguish useful predictor noise variable, high \(R^2\) alone imply useful model. Adjusted \(R^2\) penalises number predictors decreases added predictor earn complexity. predictive purposes, cross-validated \(R^2\) honest still.

heading: 8.398 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multiple regression, residual sum squares, bias-variance trade-model selection.

heading: 8.399 Theory
text: \[R^2 = 1 - \frac{\mathrm{SS}_{\mathrm{res}}}{\mathrm{SS}_{\mathrm{tot}}}.\]equals squared correlation observed fitted values OLS intercept. Always 0 1; always non-decreasing predictors added.Adjusted \(R^2\) corrects penalty:\[R^2_{\mathrm{adj}} = 1 - (1 - R^2) \cdot \frac{n - 1}{n - p - 1},\]\(p\) number predictors. Adjusted \(R^2\) can decrease added predictor improve fit enough compensate lost degree freedom; can even negative poorly fitting models.prediction, -sample summaries; cross-validated \(R^2\) (1 - SSE_{CV} / SS_{tot}) replaces residual sum CV-based estimate --sample error corrects optimism -sample fit.

heading: 8.400 Assumptions
text: Standard regression assumptions underlying linear model. \(R^2\) well-defined model intercept; without intercept, definitions differ across software.

heading: 8.401 R Implementation
code:
fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp + disp, data = mtcars)
s <- summary(fit)
c(R2 = s$r.squared, R2_adj = s$adj.r.squared)

# Cross-validated R^2
library(caret)
set.seed(2026)
cv <- train(mpg ~ wt + hp + disp, data = mtcars,
            method = "lm",
            trControl = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 10))
cv$results

heading: 8.402 Output & Results
text: summary(fit)$r.squared $adj.r.squared give -sample values; caret::train() returns cross-validated \(R^2\) RMSE 10-fold CV. gap -sample CV \(R^2\) quantifies optimism — large gaps indicate overfitting.

heading: 8.403 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “model explained 83 % variance mpg (\(R^2 = 0.83\), adjusted \(R^2 = 0.81\)); 10-fold cross-validated \(R^2\) 0.79, indicating modest optimism -sample estimate. Adjusted \(R^2\) confirmed three predictors contributed beyond penalty.” Always report adjusted \(R^2\) alongside \(R^2\) multi-predictor models, CV \(R^2\) prediction goal.

heading: 8.404 Practical Tips
text: Report adjusted \(R^2\), just \(R^2\), whenever predictors number one; unadjusted version misleads model parsimony.Adjusted \(R^2\) can negative poorly fitting models; informative, bug.predictive modelling, prefer cross-validated \(R^2\) RMSE; -sample \(R^2\) optimistic design.compare \(R^2\) across different datasets different outcome variance; comparison meaningless.Avoid causal language: “model explains 83 % variance” correct; “model predicts 83 % outcome” .GLMs, pseudo-\(R^2\) measures (Cox-Snell, Nagelkerke, McFadden) generalise concept; report specific variant used give different numerical answers.

heading: 8.405 R Packages Used
text: Base R summary.lm() summary.glm(); performance::r2() tidy \(R^2\) across model classes; caret tidymodels cross-validated \(R^2\); MuMIn::r.squaredGLMM() marginal conditional \(R^2\) mixed models.

heading: 8.408 Introduction
text: random-intercept model allows cluster (subject, hospital, school) baseline intercept constraining clusters share regression slope. simplest mixed-effects model natural starting point repeated-measures, longitudinal, clustered data. single new parameter beyond ordinary regression — variance random intercept — captures -cluster heterogeneity baseline level primary handle partial pooling intraclass correlation.

heading: 8.409 Prerequisites
text: working understanding mixed-effects models, difference fixed random effects, basic familiarity lme4 formula syntax.

heading: 8.410 Theory
text: random-intercept model \[Y_{ij} = \beta_0 + u_i + \beta_1 X_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}, \qquad u_i \sim \mathcal N(0, \sigma_u^2), \qquad \varepsilon_{ij} \sim \mathcal N(0, \sigma^2).\]cluster-specific intercept \(\beta_0 + u_i\) varies around population intercept \(\beta_0\) variance \(\sigma_u^2\). intraclass correlation coefficient \[\rho = \frac{\sigma_u^2}{\sigma_u^2 + \sigma^2},\]interpretable proportion total variance attributable -cluster differences. high ICC (\(> 0.30\)) indicates substantial cluster-level structure; low ICC (\(< 0.05\)) suggests random effect unnecessary ordinary regression suffices.

heading: 8.411 Assumptions
text: Random intercepts independent Normally distributed across clusters; residuals independent Normal conditional random effects; clusters share common slope (relax via random slopes fails).

heading: 8.412 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4); library(lmerTest)

data(sleepstudy, package = "lme4")
fit <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (1 | Subject), data = sleepstudy)
summary(fit)

# ICC
vc <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(fit))
icc <- vc$vcov[1] / sum(vc$vcov)
icc

heading: 8.413 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) reports fixed effects (common slope population intercept) lmerTest-supplied \(p\)-values, plus random-intercept SD residual SD. ICC computed variance components primary single-number summary cluster-level structure.

heading: 8.414 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “random-intercept model estimated population-level slope 10.47 ms per day sleep deprivation (95 % CI 8.92 12.02, \(p < 0.001\)); -subject variation baseline reaction time substantial (\(\hat\sigma_u = 37\) ms), intraclass correlation 0.39 indicating 39 % total variance attributable -subject differences.” Always report ICC alongside fixed effects.

heading: 8.415 Practical Tips
text: Always report ICC; headline measure cluster-level structure tells readers whether random-intercept model warranted.small numbers clusters (\(< 5\)), random-effect variance estimates unstable; consider simpler model fixed effects per cluster, move Bayesian estimation weakly informative priors.Compare -random-effects model via likelihood-ratio test (\(\chi^2_0\) / \(\chi^2_1\) mixture reference boundary null).Use confint(fit, method = "profile") profile-likelihood confidence intervals random-effect variances; Wald intervals unreliable boundary.slopes clearly vary across clusters (visible spaghetti plots), add random slopes via (1 + X | cluster); reporting random intercepts slopes vary biases inference.non-Gaussian outcomes, syntax extends via glmer() appropriate family.

heading: 8.416 R Packages Used
text: lme4 lmer(), glmer(), VarCorr(); lmerTest Satterthwaite Kenward-Roger \(p\)-values; performance::icc() tidy ICC computation; pbkrtest::PBmodcomp() parametric-bootstrap LRTs; glmmTMB alternative implementation handles complex error structures.

heading: 8.419 Introduction
text: Random-slope models allow cluster slope one predictors, capturing -cluster heterogeneity covariate affects outcome. random-intercept models assume clusters share effect \(X\) differ baseline, random-slope models drop shared-slope assumption let slope vary. essential longitudinal analysis individual trajectories diverge time population-average slope may hide substantial subject--subject variability.

heading: 8.420 Prerequisites
text: working understanding random-intercept models, mixed-effects model syntax lme4, bivariate Normal distribution joint random effects.

heading: 8.421 Theory
text: predictor \(X\) varying within clusters:\[Y_{ij} = (\beta_0 + u_{0i}) + (\beta_1 + u_{1i}) X_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij},\]\((u_{0i}, u_{1i})^\top \sim \mathcal N(0, \Sigma)\) \(\Sigma\) contains intercept variance \(\sigma_0^2\), slope variance \(\sigma_1^2\), correlation \(\rho_{01}\). “correlated random slope” formulation (X | cluster) estimates three parameters jointly; “uncorrelated” form (X || cluster) constrains \(\rho_{01} = 0\).non-zero \(\rho_{01}\) indicates systematic dependence baseline level rate change — instance, subjects start higher progress faster (positive correlation) slower (negative correlation).

heading: 8.422 Assumptions
text: Random effects bivariate Normal, independent across clusters; conditional Normal residuals; slope variation real driven influential clusters.

heading: 8.423 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4)

data(sleepstudy, package = "lme4")

# Random intercept + slope (correlated)
fit_rs <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject), data = sleepstudy)
summary(fit_rs)

# Uncorrelated random slope
fit_rs_unc <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (1 | Subject) + (0 + Days | Subject),
                   data = sleepstudy)

# Compare with random-intercept-only
fit_ri <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (1 | Subject), data = sleepstudy)
anova(fit_ri, fit_rs)

heading: 8.424 Output & Results
text: summary(fit_rs) reports population-level fixed effects, standard deviations random intercept random slope, correlation. anova() nested models compares via likelihood-ratio test (boundary-mixture reference variance components).

heading: 8.425 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Adding random slope Days substantially improved fit (\(\chi^2(2) = 42.1\), \(p < 0.001\)); -subject standard deviation slope 5.9 ms per day, intercept-slope correlation 0.07. population-level slope 10.5 ms per day (95 % CI 7.6 13.4).” Always report random-slope variance intercept-slope correlation alongside fixed effects.

heading: 8.426 Practical Tips
text: Random slopes require enough observations per cluster (rule thumb: least 10 per cluster); fewer, slope variance unstable may produce singular fits.Near-zero random-slope variance signals -specification; drop random slope constrain \(\rho_{01} = 0\) via || syntax.Centring predictor (X - mean(X)) often reduces intercept-slope correlation arises mechanically parameterisation rather data.Check random-effects variance-covariance VarCorr(fit); correlation estimates near \(\pm 1\) red flag -fitting.Use parametric bootstrap (pbkrtest::PBmodcomp) small-sample LRTs random-slope variance; asymptotic \(\chi^2\) mixture can unreliable.non-Gaussian outcomes, syntax extends via glmer() appropriate family.

heading: 8.427 R Packages Used
text: lme4 lmer() glmer(); lmerTest \(p\)-values fixed effects; pbkrtest parametric-bootstrap LRTs variance components; glmmTMB alternative implementation handles complex error structures; merTools prediction intervals respect random-slope uncertainty.

heading: 8.430 Introduction
text: Linear regression rests four classical assumptions — linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, Normality residuals — characteristic diagnostics remedies violated. Distinguishing four essential failures different consequences: non-linearity biases coefficients, dependence deflates standard errors, heteroscedasticity gives unbiased coefficients wrong SEs, Normality matters mainly small-sample inference. responsible analysis checks four reports verdict explicitly.

heading: 8.431 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear regression, residuals, standard regression diagnostics.

heading: 8.432 Theory
text: Linearity: \(\mathbb E[Y \mid X] = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_j X_j\). Failure produces biased coefficients regardless sample size.Independence: residuals uncorrelated (predictors). Time-series, repeated-measures, clustered data violate ; consequence downwardly biased standard errors inflated Type error.Homoscedasticity: \(\mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon \mid X) = \sigma^2\) constant across predictor space. Failure produces unbiased coefficients wrong standard errors; fix robust (sandwich) SEs weighted least squares.Normality residuals: needed exact small-sample \(t\)-test confidence-interval validity. central limit theorem makes assumption mild large \(n\), coefficient sampling distributions approximately Normal regardless residual distribution.

heading: 8.433 Assumptions
text: four assumptions. Diagnostics aim detect violations, “prove” assumptions hold.

heading: 8.434 R Implementation
code:
library(car); library(performance); library(lmtest)

fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)

# Residuals-vs-fitted: linearity and homoscedasticity
plot(fit, which = 1)

# Q-Q plot of residuals: normality
plot(fit, which = 2)

# Scale-location: homoscedasticity
plot(fit, which = 3)

# Residuals vs leverage: influence
plot(fit, which = 5)

# Formal tests
bptest(fit)     # Breusch-Pagan for heteroscedasticity
shapiro.test(residuals(fit))
dwtest(fit)     # Durbin-Watson for autocorrelation

# performance::check_model combines all
check_model(fit)

heading: 8.435 Output & Results
text: Four diagnostic plots plot.lm() plus three formal tests: Breusch-Pagan heteroscedasticity, Shapiro-Wilk residual Normality, Durbin-Watson first-order autocorrelation. performance::check_model() produces integrated multi-panel diagnostic annotations.

heading: 8.436 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Diagnostic plots showed systematic patterns: residuals-vs-fitted scattered randomly around zero, scale-location approximately horizontal, Q-Q plot showed mild tail deviation. Formal tests confirmed: Breusch-Pagan \(p = 0.41\) (evidence heteroscedasticity), Shapiro-Wilk \(p = 0.32\) (residuals consistent Normality), Durbin-Watson 1.95 (autocorrelation). four classical assumptions reasonably supported.” Always report checked verdict.

heading: 8.437 Practical Tips
text: Graphical diagnostics informative formal tests, especially small samples; formal tests low power \(n = 50\) reject easily \(n = 1{,}000\).Heteroscedasticity common; use robust (HC3) SEs sandwich::vcovHC combined lmtest::coeftest detected. Alternatively, transform response use weighted least squares.Clustering repeated measures invalidate independence assumption; fit linear mixed model (lme4::lmer) rather ordinary regression.Non-linearity often fixed transformation (log, square root), polynomial terms, splines. right fix depends substantive functional form.Gross departures Normality large \(n\) typically still allow valid CLT-based inference; assumption matters \(t\)-tests CIs small \(n\).Reporting “assumptions met” without specifying checked uninformative; cite specific diagnostics used.

heading: 8.438 R Packages Used
text: Base R plot.lm() shapiro.test(); lmtest::bptest() dwtest() heteroscedasticity autocorrelation; car::ncvTest() durbinWatsonTest() alternatives; performance::check_model() integrated diagnostics; sandwich lmtest::coeftest() robust SE inference.

heading: 8.441 Introduction

heading: 8.443 Theory
text: Residuals-vs-fitted: checks linearity equal variance simultaneously. Random scatter around zero indicates linear-predictor structure adequate; systematic trend suggests missed non-linearity; funnel shape indicates heteroscedasticity.Residuals-vs-fitted: checks linearity equal variance simultaneously. heading: 8.444 Assumptions

heading: 8.445 R Implementation
code:
library(car); library(performance)

fit <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)

# Built-in 4-panel diagnostic
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(fit)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

# performance's modern alternative
check_model(fit)

# Individual residual types
resid(fit)              # raw
rstandard(fit)          # standardised
rstudent(fit)           # studentised

heading: 8.452 Introduction
text: Ridge regression adds L2 penalty \(\lambda \sum_j \beta_j^2\) residual sum squares. shrinks coefficients toward zero — never exactly zero — reducing variance cost small bias producing stable fits even predictors highly correlated. classical motivation multicollinearity: \(X^\top X\) near-singular, OLS estimates extremely variable, ridge penalty regularises inverse yields well-defined estimator. Modern uses extend high-dimensional regression \(p > n\) makes OLS undefined.

heading: 8.453 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear regression, bias-variance trade-, multicollinearity, role cross-validation tuning regularisation parameters.

heading: 8.454 Theory
text: ridge estimator minimises\[\|y - X\beta\|^2 + \lambda \|\beta\|_2^2,\]closed-form solution \(\hat\beta_{\text{ridge}} = (X^\top X + \lambda )^{-1} X^\top y\). Larger \(\lambda\) shrinks coefficients aggressively; \(\lambda = 0\) recovers OLS. coefficient paths — coefficient values \(\lambda\) varies log scale — show predictor contributes; coefficients shrink continuously rather dropping abruptly lasso.Cross-validation (typically \(k = 10\) folds) selects \(\lambda\). Two common rules: \(\lambda_{\min}\) minimises CV error, \(\lambda_{1\mathrm{se}}\) takes largest \(\lambda\) within one standard error minimum (parsimonious shrinkage).

heading: 8.455 Assumptions
text: Standard linear-model assumptions residuals; predictors standardised (glmnet internally back-transforms). statistical inference shrunken coefficients, bias regularisation must considered; bootstrap debiased lasso/ridge methods provide adjusted intervals.

heading: 8.456 R Implementation
code:
library(glmnet)

X <- as.matrix(mtcars[, -1])
y <- mtcars$mpg

set.seed(2026)
cvfit <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = 0)   # alpha = 0 -> ridge

plot(cvfit)
coef(cvfit, s = "lambda.min")
coef(cvfit, s = "lambda.1se")

heading: 8.457 Output & Results
text: cv.glmnet() alpha = 0 fits ridge regression cross-validated \(\lambda\). plot shows mean CV error log \(\lambda\) two selection lines. coef() returns shrunken coefficients chosen \(\lambda\); non-zero, contrast lasso.

heading: 8.458 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Ridge regression CV-optimal \(\lambda_{1\mathrm{se}} = 0.5\) produced shrunken coefficient estimates remained stable despite strong correlations displacement, weight, horsepower; cross-validated mean squared error 7.2 mpg² versus 9.8 mpg² OLS, reflecting variance reduction.” Always report chosen \(\lambda\) cross-validated error.

heading: 8.459 Practical Tips
text: Always standardise predictors ridge; glmnet internally standardize = TRUE (default).Use \(\lambda_{1\mathrm{se}}\) shrinkage \(\lambda_{\min}\); parsimonious choice typically generalises better.variable selection rather pure shrinkage, switch lasso (alpha = 1) elastic net (0 < alpha < 1); ridge keeps every predictor.non-Gaussian outcomes, set family = "binomial", "poisson", etc.; ridge penalty extends naturally.Set random seed cv.glmnet() ensure reproducible \(\lambda\) selection.Compare ridge lasso elastic net cross-validation folds right level sparsity unclear; modern penalised regression workflows benchmark three.

heading: 8.463 Introduction
text: Robust regression estimators -weight observations large residuals reduce influence outliers heavy-tailed errors. ordinary least squares minimises squared residual — making single large residual contribute much many moderate ones — robust methods cap smoothly bound loss function extreme observations dominate fit. Two main families coexist: M-estimators (Huber, bisquare) MM-estimators combine high-breakdown initial fit high-efficiency M-step.

heading: 8.464 Prerequisites
text: working understanding OLS, residual diagnostics, leverage influence, breakdown-vs-efficiency trade-robust statistics.

heading: 8.465 Theory
text: M-estimators minimise \(\sum \rho(r_i / s)\) \(\rho\) robust loss function: quadratic small residuals (acts like OLS) bounded transitioning linear large residuals (capping influence outliers). Huber’s \(\rho\) transitions 1.345 SDs; bisquare’s \(\rho\) exactly zero beyond cutoff, eliminating large outliers entirely.MM-estimators combine high-breakdown initial fit (S-estimator breakdown 50 %, maximum possible) high-efficiency M-estimator using S-estimate starting point. result breakdown 50 % 95 % efficiency Normal errors — modern default robust linear regression.

heading: 8.466 Assumptions
text: Outliers response variable; outliers predictors (high-leverage points), MM-estimator still resists breakdown safeguard weaker. Errors symmetric near-symmetric; severely skewed errors, transformation may preferable robust regression.

heading: 8.467 R Implementation
code:
library(MASS); library(robustbase)

# Simulated data with 10% outliers
set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(100)
y <- 2 + 1.5 * x + rnorm(100)
y[sample(100, 10)] <- y[sample(100, 10)] + rnorm(10, 0, 10)

fit_ols <- lm(y ~ x)
fit_rlm <- rlm(y ~ x)                      # Huber M-estimator
fit_mm  <- lmrob(y ~ x, method = "MM")     # MM-estimator

rbind(ols = coef(fit_ols), rlm = coef(fit_rlm), mm = coef(fit_mm))

heading: 8.468 Output & Results
text: OLS coefficients pulled contamination; robust estimators recover values close data-generating slope. MM-estimator typically delivers tighter standard errors Huber M-estimator level robustness, reflecting better efficiency.

heading: 8.469 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “MM-regression gave slope 1.48 (SE 0.11), close true 1.5; OLS 10 %-contaminated data produced 1.35 (SE 0.16), pulled toward zero outliers. Reporting fits illustrates robustness benefit cost ignoring outliers.” Pair OLS robust fit outliers suspected; large discrepancies signal influential observations need investigation.

heading: 8.474 Introduction
text: receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve area--curve (AUC) summary quantify well logistic regression’s predicted probabilities rank observed events non-events. AUC ranges 0.5 (chance) 1.0 (perfect ranking) conventional discrimination metric binary classifiers clinical epidemiological work. invariant threshold chosen class assignment, making property model’s ranking ability rather specific cut-.

heading: 8.475 Prerequisites
text: working understanding logistic regression, classification thresholds, sensitivity, specificity.

heading: 8.476 Theory
text: binary outcome \(Y \\{0, 1\}\) continuous score (predicted probability) \(\hat p\), AUC equals probability randomly chosen positive case higher predicted probability randomly chosen negative case:\[\mathrm{AUC} = \mathrm P(\hat p_i > \hat p_j \mid Y_i = 1, Y_j = 0).\]mathematically equivalent Mann-Whitney U statistic divided sample sizes. Rough conventional benchmarks: 0.5–0.7 poor, 0.7–0.8 acceptable, 0.8–0.9 excellent, 0.9 outstanding (worth scrutinising -fitting small samples). DeLong’s test compares two correlated AUCs models fit data.

heading: 8.477 Assumptions
text: binary outcome continuous score (predicted probability monotone transformation thereof). AUC invariant monotone transformations score, calibration irrelevant AUC; thresholding decisions, however, depend calibration.

heading: 8.478 R Implementation
code:
library(pROC)

d <- mtcars
d$am <- factor(d$am)
fit <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, data = d, family = binomial)
probs <- predict(fit, type = "response")

roc_obj <- roc(d$am, probs, quiet = TRUE)
auc(roc_obj); ci.auc(roc_obj)
plot(roc_obj, col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2)

# DeLong test: compare two models' AUCs
fit2 <- glm(am ~ wt, data = d, family = binomial)
roc2 <- roc(d$am, predict(fit2, type = "response"), quiet = TRUE)
roc.test(roc_obj, roc2)

heading: 8.479 Output & Results
text: auc(roc_obj) returns point estimate, ci.auc() bootstrap confidence interval. plot displays ROC curve diagonal reference chance. roc.test() performs DeLong’s test AUC difference two correlated ROC curves.

heading: 8.480 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “full logistic regression -sample AUC 0.93 (95 % CI 0.84 1.00); reduced model weight AUC 0.89, DeLong’s test difference yielded \(\Delta\mathrm{AUC} = 0.04\) (\(p = 0.12\)), suggesting horsepower contributes little additional discrimination beyond weight.” Report AUC confidence interval pair least one operating-point summary (sensitivity / specificity clinically chosen threshold).

heading: 8.481 Practical Tips
text: Always report AUC 95 % confidence interval; point estimate alone uninterpretable assessing precision.honest internal validation, use bootstrap 0.632+ correction optimism; package rms::validate automates .severe class imbalance, precision-recall curves communicate operating-point trade-offs better ROC; report positives rare.AUC independent prevalence; calibration predictive values (PPV, NPV) . Report calibration alongside AUC clinical prediction tool.survival outcomes, use time-dependent ROC (timeROC::timeROC) time-specific AUCs clinically meaningful horizons.Compare nested models via DeLong’s test (pROC::roc.test); non-nested models, treat comparison exploratory avoid hypothesis-test framing.
