heading: 8.485 Introduction
text: Simple linear regression models mean continuous outcome linear function single continuous predictor. workhorse applied statistics: half reported analyses linear regressions disguise, concepts introduces (least squares estimation, residuals, fit-vs-data trade-, R-squared, confidence prediction bands) generalise every complex model.

heading: 8.486 Prerequisites
text: reader know mean, variance, correlation , comfortable reading scatter plot. prior regression experience required.

heading: 8.487 Theory
text: Given pairs \((x_i, y_i)\) \(= 1, \ldots, n\), simple linear regression model \[y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i, \qquad \varepsilon_i \overset{iid}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2).\]parameters \(\beta_0\) (intercept) \(\beta_1\) (slope) estimated ordinary least squares – minimising sum squared residuals \(\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\). Closed-form solutions \[\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}, \qquad \hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}.\]standard error slope \(SE(\hat{\beta}_1) = s / \sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}\), \(s\) residual standard error. normality assumption, \(\hat{\beta}_1\) normally distributed around \(\beta_1\), \(\hat{\beta}_1 / SE(\hat{\beta}_1)\) follows \(t\) distribution \(n - 2\) degrees freedom null \(\beta_1 = 0\).coefficient determination \(R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}\) – proportion variance \(y\) explained linear relationship \(x\). simple regression, \(R^2\) equals squared Pearson correlation.

heading: 8.488 Assumptions
text: OLS inference machinery relies four assumptions:Linearity: true relationship \(x\) mean \(y\) linear.Independence: errors \(\varepsilon_i\) independent .Homoscedasticity: errors constant variance \(\sigma^2\) regardless \(x\).Normality errors: errors normally distributed – important small-sample inference, less large samples.

heading: 8.489 R Implementation
text: tidy() function returns one-row-per-term table estimates, standard errors, t statistics, p-values, confidence intervals. glance() returns one-row model-level summary: R-squared, adjusted R-squared, residual standard error, F statistic, log-likelihood. check_model() produces standard diagnostic panel.
code:
library(broom)
library(ggplot2)
library(performance)

set.seed(7)
n <- 80
x <- runif(n, 0, 10)
y <- 2 + 1.5 * x + rnorm(n, sd = 1.5)
df <- data.frame(x, y)

fit <- lm(y ~ x, data = df)
tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE)
glance(fit)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", colour = "steelblue") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

check_model(fit)

heading: 8.490 Output & Results
text: Typical coefficient output:\(R^2 = 0.904\), residual SE = 1.47, F(1, 78) = 737.4.

heading: 8.491 Interpretation
text: slope estimate 1.512 (95% CI 1.401 1.623) says one-unit increase \(x\) associated 1.51-unit increase conditional mean \(y\), holding else equal (vacuous simple regression, phrasing generalises). intercept 1.982 predicted \(y\) \(x = 0\); scientifically meaningful quantity \(x = 0\) within range data.manuscript: “simple linear regression showed \(y\) increased significantly \(x\), \(b = 1.51\) (95% CI 1.40 1.62), \(t(78) = 27.2\), \(p < 0.001\). model explained 90.4% variance \(y\).”

heading: 8.492 Practical Tips
text: Always plot data fitted line; numbers alone can hide gross misfit.Residuals-vs-fitted Q-Q plots two diagnostics reflexively inspect.\(R^2\) 0.9 stellar physics, mediocre psychology, suspect epidemiology – interpret context.confidence interval mean response given \(x\); prediction interval, wider, new individual observation.Extrapolating beyond range observed \(x\) risky: linear relationship held \(x \[0, 10]\) may nonsense \(x = 100\).

heading: 8.495 Introduction
text: Splines piecewise polynomials joined smoothly points called knots. give flexible non-linear fits without wild extrapolation behaviour high-degree polynomials, makes canonical tool modelling non-linear continuous predictor effects regression. Natural cubic splines restricted cubic splines workhorses clinical epidemiological work; constraint linear behaviour beyond boundary knots prevents implausible curls ordinary polynomials produce tails.

heading: 8.496 Prerequisites
text: working understanding polynomial regression, limitations high-degree polynomials extremes data, concept basis functions regression.

heading: 8.497 Theory
text: natural cubic spline \(k\) internal knots piecewise cubic polynomial continuous together first second derivatives knot, linear beyond boundary knots. uses \(k + 1\) degrees freedom non-linear effect. Knot placement typically quantiles \(X\) (3 knots: 10/50/90; 4 knots: 5/35/65/95; 5 knots: 5/27.5/50/72.5/95 percentiles). Three five knots adequate biomedical applications; invites -fitting.Restricted cubic splines (RCS) impose linearity beyond outer knots, preventing polynomial curling away tails — useful clinical reporting extrapolation low-data regions real concern.

heading: 8.498 Assumptions
text: Standard regression assumptions residuals; residuals vs. fitted show remaining structure spline included; chosen number placement knots adequate underlying functional form.

heading: 8.499 R Implementation
code:
library(splines); library(rms)

set.seed(2026)
x <- runif(200, 0, 10)
y <- 2 + sin(x) * 2 + rnorm(200, 0, 0.5)

# Natural cubic spline via splines::ns
fit_ns <- lm(y ~ ns(x, df = 4))
summary(fit_ns)

# Visualise
plot(x, y, pch = 20)
xg <- seq(0, 10, length.out = 200)
lines(xg, predict(fit_ns, newdata = data.frame(x = xg)),
      col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2)

# rms::rcs for restricted cubic splines with plot
dd <- datadist(data.frame(x, y)); options(datadist = "dd")
fit_rcs <- ols(y ~ rcs(x, 4))
plot(Predict(fit_rcs, x = seq(0, 10, 0.2)))

heading: 8.500 Output & Results

heading: 8.501 Interpretation

heading: 8.506 Introduction
text: Stepwise regression — forward, backward, bidirectional automated selection predictors based \(p\)-values, AIC, BIC — workhorse model selection decades remains widely used despite extensive criticism. Frank Harrell’s Regression Modeling Strategies, TRIPOD guideline clinical prediction models, long methodological literature documented serious statistical pathologies make stepwise reporting best misleading worst actively wrong. Modern practice increasingly substitutes penalised regression (lasso, elastic net) pre-specified predictor sets.

heading: 8.507 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multiple regression, model selection, bias-variance trade-.

heading: 8.508 Theory
text: well-documented problems stepwise selection:Selection bias: \(R^2\) inflated coefficients biased away zero selection testing done data.Invalid \(p\)-values: final model’s “significant” predictors include false positives rate far nominal \(\alpha\); candidate predictors, worse gets.Instability: small data perturbations (single observation, different random sample) change selected predictor set substantially.Reduced --sample performance: stepwise-selected models often predict worse pre-specified penalised alternatives.Forward / backward / bidirectional give different answers: procedure path-dependent.Better alternatives include pre-specified predictor sets based substantive theory, penalised regression cross-validated tuning (lasso, elastic net), cross-validated model comparison rather naive selection--fit-statistics.

heading: 8.509 Assumptions
text: Standard regression framework; issues selection procedure, underlying model.

heading: 8.510 R Implementation
code:
library(MASS)

# Stepwise (for illustration; not recommended in practice)
full <- lm(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars)
step_model <- stepAIC(full, direction = "both", trace = FALSE)
summary(step_model)

# Better: penalised regression
library(glmnet)
X <- as.matrix(mtcars[, -1])
y <- mtcars$mpg
cvfit <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = 1)
coef(cvfit, s = "lambda.min")

heading: 8.511 Output & Results
text: stepAIC() selects subset based AIC; reporting resulting coefficients naive standard errors \(p\)-values produces invalid inference. Lasso cross-validated \(\lambda\) provides principled alternative shrunken coefficients defensible variable-selection mechanism.

heading: 8.512 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “pre-specified predictor set based prior literature; stepwise selection used known instability inflated Type error. exploratory secondary analyses, report cross-validated lasso results selected variables CV mean squared error, naive \(p\)-values.” phrasing places responsible alternative methods section explicitly.

heading: 8.513 Practical Tips
text: Avoid reporting naive \(t\)-statistics \(p\)-values stepwise-selected models; biased miscalibrated.Use stepwise procedures exploratory purposes, never confirmatory inference.Pre-specify predictor set based substantive theory; strongest defence selection--significance.high-dimensional correlated predictors, ridge, lasso, elastic net handle variable-selection problem proper regularisation; cross-validate choose penalty.TRIPOD reporting guideline clinical prediction models specifically discourages stepwise selection; reviewers clinical journals increasingly enforce .must report stepwise results, honestly caveats: report selection procedure, candidate predictor set, cross-validated estimate --sample performance.

text: Cox proportional-hazards regression models time--event outcomes right censoring de facto standard regression tool survival analysis. page gives regression-modelling-focused summary; Survival Analysis section contains detailed treatment diagnostics, residuals, stratification. headline insight Cox regression family GLMs — coefficients, standard errors, confidence intervals, likelihood-ratio tests behave familiarly — response Surv object encoding event time censoring status.

heading: 8.518 Prerequisites
text: working understanding survival analysis, hazard function, censoring, partial-likelihood approach inference.

heading: 8.519 Theory
text: Cox model writes hazard \[h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \exp(X^\top \beta),\]baseline hazard \(h_0(t)\) left unspecified partial likelihood providing \(\hat\beta\) without estimating . Exponentiated coefficients hazard ratios; \(\hat\beta_j = 0.02\) age corresponds 2 % multiplicative increase hazard per year age, \(\exp(0.02) \approx 1.02\).proportional-hazards assumption — hazard ratios two covariate patterns constant time — model’s defining restriction. fails, options include stratification, time-varying coefficients via tt() survSplit(), switching AFT RMST-based summaries.

heading: 8.520 Assumptions
text: Proportional hazards across covariate levels (test via Schoenfeld residuals), independent non-informative censoring, unmodelled time-varying covariate effects, adequate events per variable stable estimation (rule thumb: 10–15 events per regression coefficient).

heading: 8.521 R Implementation
code:
library(survival); library(broom)

data(lung)
fit <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + ph.ecog, data = lung)
tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE)

# PH check
cox.zph(fit)

heading: 8.522 Output & Results
text: tidy() broom returns tibble hazard ratios, confidence intervals, Wald \(p\)-values. summary(fit) adds likelihood-ratio test, score test, Wald test joint null. cox.zph() reports proportional-hazards diagnostic covariate plus global test.

heading: 8.523 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “multivariable Cox model adjusted age, sex, ECOG performance status estimated additional year age increased hazard death 2 % (HR 1.02, 95 % CI 1.00 1.04, \(p = 0.04\)); female sex associated 40 % lower hazard (HR 0.60, 95 % CI 0.42 0.85, \(p = 0.004\)). proportional-hazards assumption supported (global Schoenfeld \(p = 0.55\)).” Always pair coefficient reporting PH diagnostics.

heading: 8.524 Practical Tips
text: Always run cox.zph() fitting; reporting Cox HRs without PH diagnostics longer acceptable clinical-trial publications.Use Efron’s tie-handling method (ties = "efron") tied event times; default older software Breslow’s, less accurate.Report HRs 95 % CIs alongside \(p\)-values; CI includes 1.0 informative marginal \(p\)-value alone.time-varying covariates, use counting-process format (Surv(tstart, tstop, event)) cluster standard errors subject id.continuous covariates non-linear effects, add restricted cubic splines (rms::rcs(x, 4)) rather dichotomising.See Survival Analysis section’s dedicated Cox tutorial extended coverage residuals, stratification, influence diagnostics.

heading: 8.528 Introduction
text: Tobit regression handles outcomes observed boundary value cases fall outside censoring threshold. Income top-coded $200,000 (right-censored cap), laboratory values limit detection (left-censored LOD), drug refill rates cap 100 %, share structure: real underlying value exists, observation records censoring threshold rather true value. OLS observed outcome biased toward zero cases; Tobit recovers latent-model coefficients via appropriate Normal likelihood.

heading: 8.529 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear regression, difference censoring truncation, Normal distribution latent error model.

heading: 8.530 Theory
text: latent \(Y^*_i = X_i^\top \beta + \varepsilon_i\) \(\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal N(0, \sigma^2)\), observed value \(Y_i = \max(L, Y^*_i)\) left-censoring \(Y_i = \min(U, Y^*_i)\) right-censoring. Maximum-likelihood estimation combines two contributions:\[L(\beta, \sigma) = \prod_{\notin \mathrm{cens}} \phi\!\left(\frac{Y_i - X_i^\top \beta}{\sigma}\right) \cdot \prod_{\\mathrm{cens}} \Phi\!\left(\frac{L - X_i^\top \beta}{\sigma}\right).\]first product standard Normal likelihood non-censored observations; second probability censoring boundary observations. Tobit model assumes coefficients govern threshold decision observed values; two-part models relax processes differ.

heading: 8.531 Assumptions
text: Latent outcome Normally distributed; censoring threshold known observations (known per observation); standard linear-model assumptions latent regression.

heading: 8.532 R Implementation
code:
library(AER)

set.seed(2026)
x <- rnorm(200)
y_star <- 2 + 1.5 * x + rnorm(200)
y <- pmax(y_star, 0)    # left-censored at 0

fit <- tobit(y ~ x, left = 0, right = Inf)
summary(fit)

# Compare with OLS on observed y
coef(lm(y ~ x))

heading: 8.533 Output & Results
text: tobit() returns coefficient estimates latent scale, recovering data-generating parameters. OLS observed (censored) data attenuates slope toward zero, illustrating bias ignoring censoring structure.

heading: 8.534 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Tobit regression left-censoring 0 estimated latent-scale slope 1.47 (95 % CI 1.19 1.75, \(p < 0.001\)), close true 1.5; OLS data gave 0.98 (95 % CI 0.78 1.18), biased toward zero unaccounted censoring.” Always describe censoring rule threshold methods.

heading: 8.535 Practical Tips
text: Tobit assumes coefficients govern censoring observed values; processes differ (observations structurally boundary, others observed underlying behaviour), use two-part / hurdle model instead.right-censored time--event data, use survival regression (Cox, parametric AFT), Tobit; censoring mechanism differs.Check Normality latent residuals via quantile residuals (fitdistrplus diagnostics); Tobit sensitive non-Normal latent errors.Tobit common economics (income, expenditure); biomedicine often uses two-part models detection-limit data “LOD” decision biologically distinct actual concentration.mixed-effects censored outcomes, survreg(... , dist = "gaussian") extended random effects via survival workarounds, crch heteroscedastic censored regression.Predictions can target censored observation (type = "response") latent value (type = "linear"); choose based question requires.

heading: 8.539 Introduction
text: Truncated regression applies observations outside range absent entirely dataset rather censored threshold. distinction matters: censored data record value threshold observations fall outside, truncated data simply omit . Examples: study limited hypertensive patients (normotensive observations), income survey households threshold (low-income data), registry high-grade tumours (low-grade entries). Ordinary least squares truncated data biased conditional mean inside truncation region non-linear, even latent model linear.

heading: 8.540 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear regression, difference censoring truncation, truncated-Normal distribution.

heading: 8.541 Theory
text: latent \(Y^* = X^\top \beta + \varepsilon\) \(\varepsilon \sim \mathcal N(0, \sigma^2)\), truncation \(L\) means observe \(Y\) \(Y^* > L\). conditional density truncated region \[f(y \mid x, Y > L) = \frac{\phi((y - x^\top \beta)/\sigma)/\sigma}{1 - \Phi((L - x^\top \beta)/\sigma)}.\]conditional mean inside truncation region depends non-linearly \(x\) via truncation point, OLS coefficients attenuated. Maximum-likelihood estimation correct truncated-Normal density recovers latent-model coefficients. corresponding model covariate-dependent truncation rules Heckman selection model (sampleSelection::selection).

heading: 8.542 Assumptions
text: known truncation threshold (known truncation rule), Normal latent errors, correctly specified latent linear model. Heavy truncation makes inference fragile observations remain.

heading: 8.543 R Implementation
code:
library(truncreg)

set.seed(2026)
x_all <- rnorm(300)
y_all <- 2 + 1.5 * x_all + rnorm(300)
keep  <- y_all > 0                 # truncation: keep only y > 0
x <- x_all[keep]; y <- y_all[keep]

fit_trunc <- truncreg(y ~ x, point = 0, direction = "left")
summary(fit_trunc)

# Biased OLS
coef(lm(y ~ x))

heading: 8.544 Output & Results
text: truncreg() returns coefficient estimates recover latent-model parameters (close data-generating values 2 1.5). Naive OLS truncated data attenuates slope toward zero, illustrating bias.

heading: 8.545 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Truncated regression accounting selection rule (\(y > 0\)) recovered slope 1.48 (95 % CI 1.20 1.76), close data-generating value 1.5; naive OLS truncated sample produced attenuated slope 0.91 (95 % CI 0.65 1.17), demonstrating consequence ignoring truncation.” Always check whether observations dropped reaching analysis dataset; truncation data-collection process invisible standard regression diagnostics.

heading: 8.546 Practical Tips
text: Truncation stronger censoring: truncated values unobserved, censored values observed boundary. Use right model data structure.covariate-dependent truncation rules, Heckman two-step sampleSelection::selection() jointly models selection outcome equations.Truncated regression sensitive Normality assumption; check residuals truncated scale consider semi-parametric alternatives Normality fails.heavily truncated samples (latent distribution unobserved), inference becomes fragile; report proportion latent observations retained.Bayesian alternatives informative priors stabilise truncated fits data alone weak.Confirm truncation point (often study design parameter) fitting; misstating \(L\) produces systematically biased estimates.

heading: 8.550 Introduction
text: Zero-inflated models handle count data contain zeros Poisson negative binomial distribution predict — often called “structural zeros” arise process distinct count-generating process. model mixture: one component decides whether observation structural zero (always zero) sampling unit (non-negative count); component generates count sampling units. Insurance claims, healthcare-utilisation counts, ecological abundance absent species — share structure.

heading: 8.551 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Poisson negative-binomial regression, logistic regression, conceptual distinction mixture models two-part hurdle models.

heading: 8.552 Theory
text: zero-inflated mixture density \[\mathrm P(Y = 0) = \pi + (1 - \pi) f(0), \qquad \mathrm P(Y = y) = (1 - \pi) f(y) \text{ } y > 0,\]\(\pi\) structural-zero probability (logistic regression covariates) \(f\) count distribution (Poisson NB). two sub-models linear predictors can use different covariate sets; observed zeros come structural-zero process count-process zero.contrast hurdle models conceptual: zero-inflated treats zeros structurally always-zero, hurdle treats zeros arising one Bernoulli decision count distribution truncated strictly positive.

heading: 8.553 Assumptions
text: Count outcome, two distinct processes generating zeros non-zero counts, independent observations, chosen count distribution (Poisson NB) appropriate non-structural component.

heading: 8.554 R Implementation
code:
library(pscl); library(glmmTMB)

set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(x = rnorm(300))
# Simulate with 40% structural zeros
pi_zero <- 0.4
d$y <- ifelse(rbinom(300, 1, pi_zero) == 1, 0,
              rpois(300, lambda = exp(0.5 + 0.6 * d$x)))

# Zero-inflated Poisson
fit_zip <- zeroinfl(y ~ x | x, data = d)
summary(fit_zip)

# Zero-inflated negative binomial
fit_zinb <- zeroinfl(y ~ x | x, data = d, dist = "negbin")

AIC(fit_zip, fit_zinb)

# glmmTMB version
fit_tmb <- glmmTMB(y ~ x, ziformula = ~ x, data = d, family = poisson)

heading: 8.555 Output & Results
text: summary(fit_zip) returns two coefficient blocks: count-component coefficients log-rate scale zero-inflation coefficients logit scale. AIC() compares ZIP ZINB; substantially overdispersed counts favour ZINB strongly.

heading: 8.556 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “zero-inflated negative binomial model decomposed observed zeros structural sampling sources: count component estimated rate ratio 1.75 per unit increase \(x\) (95 % CI 1.40 2.18, \(p < 0.001\)); zero-inflation component estimated 1-unit increase \(x\) reduced odds structural zero 45 % (0.55, 95 % CI 0.36 0.84, \(p = 0.005\)). ZINB favoured ZIP AIC (\(\Delta = 12\)).” Always report components framework rationale.

heading: 8.557 Practical Tips
text: Vuong test (pscl::vuong) compares zero-inflated regular Poisson/NB often inconclusive; AIC BIC reliable.Different covariates can drive count zero-inflation components; use y ~ x_count | x_zero specify separately.Hurdle models (pscl::hurdle) alternative scientific story “anything happen, much”; ZIP/ZINB fits “zeros structural others sampling”.Overdispersed counts plus excess zeros typically warrant ZINB; pure overdispersion without structural zeros just NB.Interpretation cleaner hurdle models two-stage scientific story clear; ZIP/ZINB preferable zeros come genuine mixture.random-effects extensions, glmmTMB(..., ziformula = ~ ...) adds clustered hierarchical structure either component.

heading: 8.558 R Packages Used
text: pscl::zeroinfl() hurdle() canonical implementations; glmmTMB mixed-effects zero-inflated hurdle models cleaner overdispersion handling; countreg additional count-model variants rootogram diagnostics; DHARMa residual diagnostics across zero-inflated families.

heading: 8.564 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 8.565 1. Hypothesis
text:
Simulate trial baseline follow-correlated r = 0.6,
compare three analyses.

heading: 8.566 2. Visualise
code:
baseline <- rnorm(n, 140, 15)
arm <- rep(c("placebo", "active"), each = n / 2)
# true treatment effect on follow-up: -5
followup <- 0.6 * (baseline - 140) + 140 +
  ifelse(arm == "active", -5, 0) + rnorm(n, 0, 12)
dat <- tibble(baseline, followup, arm,
              change = followup - baseline) |>
  mutate(arm = factor(arm, levels = c("placebo", "active")))

ggplot(dat, aes(baseline, followup, colour = arm)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Baseline", y = "Follow-up")

heading: 8.567 3. Assumptions
text:
Randomisation; linear relationship baseline follow-;
equal slopes across arms (tested interaction term).interaction small, pool slopes ANCOVA.
code: tidy(fit_int) |> filter(term == "armactive:baseline")

heading: 8.568 4. Conduct
text:
three estimates around −5; ANCOVA standard error
smallest.
code:
fit_change <- lm(change ~ arm, data = dat)
fit_ancova <- lm(followup ~ arm + baseline, data = dat)

bind_rows(
  tidy(fit_post, conf.int = TRUE) |> mutate(model = "post only"),
  tidy(fit_change, conf.int = TRUE) |> mutate(model = "change"),
  tidy(fit_ancova, conf.int = TRUE) |> mutate(model = "ANCOVA")
) |>
  filter(term == "armactive") |>
  dplyr::select(model, estimate, std.error, conf.low, conf.high)

simulated trial (n = nrow(dat)), ANCOVA estimated 
treatment effect 
round(coef(fit_ancova)["armactive"], 2) units (95% CI
round(confint(fit_ancova)["armactive", 1], 2) 
round(confint(fit_ancova)["armactive", 2], 2)) smaller
standard error either post-change-score analyses.
ANCOVA prespecified primary analysis continuous-outcome
RCTs.

library(tidyverse)

heading: 8.591 1. Hypothesis
text:
simulate two noisy measurements underlying
quantity — say, body size measured two instruments — ask 
OLS SMA differ.Null hypothesis: two measurements uncorrelated.
Working alternative: positively associated, slope near
1 instruments agree.

heading: 8.592 2. Visualise
text:
cloud points climbs lower-left upper-right. 
instruments carry error, neither deserves called truth.
code:
true_size <- rnorm(n, mean = 50, sd = 10)
x <- true_size + rnorm(n, 0, 3)
y <- true_size + rnorm(n, 0, 3)
dat <- tibble(x, y)

ggplot(dat, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, colour = "grey30") +
  labs(x = "Instrument 1", y = "Instrument 2")

heading: 8.593 3. Assumptions
text:
OLS assumes X measured without error residuals Y 
approximately normal constant variance. SMA assumes errors 
variables comparable scale. assume linearity.Residuals look patternless; QQ plot close diagonal. 
OLS diagnostics fine; just capture fact X 
also noisy.
code:
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(fit_ols, which = c(1, 2))
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

heading: 8.594 4. Conduct
text:
Now hand-coded SMA slope. SMA slope ratio standard
deviations, signed correlation:SMA slope closer 1. OLS attenuated treats X 
fixed .
code:
fit_ols <- lm(y ~ x, data = dat)
tidy(fit_ols, conf.int = TRUE)
sma_intercept <- function(x, y) mean(y) - sma_slope(x, y) * mean(x)

b_sma <- sma_slope(dat$x, dat$y)
a_sma <- sma_intercept(dat$x, dat$y)
b_ols <- coef(fit_ols)[2]

c(ols_slope = unname(b_ols), sma_slope = b_sma)
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, colour = "grey40") +
  geom_abline(intercept = coef(fit_ols)[1], slope = b_ols,
              colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_abline(intercept = a_sma, slope = b_sma,
              colour = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.9, linetype = 2) +
  labs(x = "Instrument 1", y = "Instrument 2",
       title = "OLS (blue) vs SMA (red)")

heading: 8.595 5. Concluding statement

1 (Pearson r = round(cor(dat$x, dat$y), 2); OLS slope
round(unname(b_ols), 2); SMA slope round(b_sma, 2)).

heading: 8.600 Learning objectives
text:
Compute net benefit draw decision-curve plot two competing
risk models.Compute category-free NRI IDI read adds beyond
ROC/AUC.Decide three report setting.

heading: 8.601 Prerequisites
text:
Calibration discrimination, plus working
logistic-regression fit.

heading: 8.602 Background
text:
prediction model useful decisions, weighted clinical
consequence, beat alternative policy. Decision-curve analysis makes
comparison explicit: across range threshold probabilities, 
plots net benefit — true positives minus false positives, weighted 
threshold — “treat ” “treat none” default
policies. model whose decision curve dominates defaults across 
clinically plausible range thresholds providing decision value.net reclassification improvement (NRI) integrated discrimination
improvement (IDI) category-free alternatives comparing AUCs.
NRI summarises well new model moves events upwards non-events
downwards risk; IDI summarises mean change predicted
probabilities. noisy small samples prone 
optimistic interpretation, report alongside, instead , 
decision curve.

heading: 8.603 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 8.604 1. Goal
text:
Compare baseline logistic model model adds new marker,
using decision curves, NRI, IDI.

heading: 8.605 2. Approach
code:
x1 <- rnorm(n)
x2 <- rnorm(n) + 0.3 * x1
lp <- -1 + 0.9 * x1 + 0.6 * x2
y  <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(lp))
df <- tibble(y = y, x1 = x1, x2 = x2)

base_fit <- glm(y ~ x1,       data = df, family = binomial)
new_fit  <- glm(y ~ x1 + x2,  data = df, family = binomial)
df <- df |>
  mutate(p_base = predict(base_fit, type = "response"),
         p_new  = predict(new_fit,  type = "response"))

heading: 8.606 3. Execution — decision curve (hand-coded)
code:
n <- length(y)
  pred_pos <- p >= thr
  tp <- sum(pred_pos &  y == 1)
  fp <- sum(pred_pos &  y == 0)
  tp / n - fp / n * (thr / (1 - thr))
}

thresholds <- seq(0.05, 0.6, by = 0.01)
dc <- tibble(
  threshold    = thresholds,
  base_model   = sapply(thresholds, \(t) net_benefit(df$y, df$p_base, t)),
  new_model    = sapply(thresholds, \(t) net_benefit(df$y, df$p_new,  t)),
  treat_all    = sapply(thresholds, \(t)
                         mean(df$y) - (1 - mean(df$y)) * t / (1 - t)),
  treat_none   = 0
) |>
  pivot_longer(-threshold, names_to = "strategy", values_to = "net_benefit")
ggplot(dc, aes(threshold, net_benefit, colour = strategy)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-0.05, max(dc$net_benefit))) +
  labs(x = "Threshold probability", y = "Net benefit",
       colour = NULL)

heading: 8.607 4. Check — NRI and IDI
code:
up   <- mean((p_new > p_old)[y == 1]) - mean((p_new < p_old)[y == 1])
  down <- mean((p_new < p_old)[y == 0]) - mean((p_new > p_old)[y == 0])
  c(events = up, non_events = down, overall = up + down)
}

idi <- function(y, p_old, p_new) {
  ev <- mean((p_new - p_old)[y == 1])
  ne <- mean((p_old - p_new)[y == 0])
  c(events = ev, non_events = ne, overall = ev + ne)
}

nri(df$y, df$p_base, df$p_new)
idi(df$y, df$p_base, df$p_new)

heading: 8.608 5. Report
text:
Adding new marker baseline logistic model yielded higher net
benefit baseline across clinically plausible threshold
probabilities. category-free NRI positive events 
non-events; IDI also positive overall. Absolute magnitudes
interpreted alongside decision curve, isolation.

heading: 8.609 Common pitfalls
text:
Reporting NRI without interval decision-curve context.Choosing threshold range post-hoc maximise apparent gain.Forgetting decision curves assume cost/benefit ratio implied
threshold clinically reasonable.

heading: 8.610 Further reading
text:
Vickers AJ, Elkin EB. (2006). Decision curve analysis: novel
method evaluating prediction models. Medical Decision Making.Pencina MJ et al. (2008). Evaluating added predictive ability
new marker.

heading: 8.616 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(car)
library(performance)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 8.617 1. Hypothesis
text:
Simulate regression mild heteroscedasticity, high-leverage
point, two correlated predictors. Ask diagnostics flag 
problem.

heading: 8.618 2. Visualise
code:
x1 <- rnorm(n, 0, 1)
x2 <- x1 + rnorm(n, 0, 0.3)      # x1 and x2 correlated
x3 <- rnorm(n, 0, 1)
y  <- 1 + 2 * x1 - 1.5 * x2 + 0.5 * x3 + rnorm(n, 0, 1 + 0.4 * abs(x1))
# add one influential point
x1[1] <- 5; x2[1] <- 5; y[1] <- 0
dat <- tibble(y, x1, x2, x3)

ggplot(dat, aes(x1, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(x = "x1", y = "y")

heading: 8.619 3. Assumptions
text:
residuals-vs-fitted plot show trend. QQ plot 
track line. Scale-location flat. Residuals-vs-leverage
points outside Cook’s-distance contours.
code:
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(fit)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

heading: 8.620 4. Conduct
text:
Influence diagnostics:VIF:Performance summary:VIF x1 x2 high; x3 fine. first row
(injected leverage point) dominate Cook’s distance.
code:
infl |> arrange(desc(.cooksd)) |> head(5) |>
  select(row, .fitted, .resid, .hat, .cooksd)

check_model(fit, check = c("vif", "qq", "outliers", "linearity"))

heading: 8.621 5. Concluding statement
text:
Regression diagnostics identified one high-influence observation
(Cook’s D = round(max(infl$.cooksd), 2)) collinearity
x1 x2 (VIF = round(max(vif(fit)), 1)). Refitting
without influential point combining collinear
predictors next step reporting coefficients.

heading: 8.622 Common pitfalls
text:
Treating single Cook’s distance number verdict. Always look
plot.Solving VIF problem dropping one variable time 
underlying issue two predictors measure thing.Declaring assumptions met numbers pass. Plot first.

heading: 8.629 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 8.630 1. Hypothesis
8.631 2. Visualise
code:
x <- rnorm(n)
y <- 0.3 * x + rnorm(n, 0, 1)
dat1 <- tibble(x, y, x_bin = factor(ifelse(x > median(x), "high", "low")))

ggplot(dat1, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(x = "x (continuous)", y = "y")

heading: 8.632 3. Assumptions
text: Independence, approximate normality; standard regression assumptions.

heading: 8.633 4. Conduct
text:
Power loss dichotomisation:p-value x_bin larger x; standard
error larger estimate attenuated.Regression mean:“high baseline” group shows large negative mean change; rest
sample shows small positive change. intervention occurred.fitted line flatter identity line; flattening 
RTM.
code:
tidy(lm(y ~ x_bin, data = dat1)) # median split
pre  <- rnorm(n2, 100, 15)
post <- 0.7 * (pre - 100) + 100 + rnorm(n2, 0, 11)
dat2 <- tibble(pre, post) |>
  mutate(high_baseline = pre > quantile(pre, 0.8))

dat2 |>
  group_by(high_baseline) |>
  summarise(pre_mean = mean(pre), post_mean = mean(post),
            change = mean(post - pre))
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = 2, colour = "grey50") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(x = "Baseline", y = "Follow-up")

heading: 8.634 5. Concluding statement
text:
Dichotomising continuous predictor inflated p-value 
signif(tidy(lm(y ~ x, data = dat1))$p.value[2], 2) 
signif(tidy(lm(y ~ x_bin, data = dat1))$p.value[2], 2) 
widened standard error. pre-post simulation 
intervention, top-baseline quintile showed mean decline 
round(dat2 |> filter(high_baseline) |> summarise(mean(post - pre)) |> pull(), 1)
units, entirely attributable regression mean.

heading: 8.655 Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(MASS)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 8.656 1. Hypothesis
text:
Using MASS::Pima.tr, can plasma glucose (glu) predict diabetes
status (type)?

heading: 8.657 2. Visualise
code:
mutate(type = factor(type, levels = c("No", "Yes")))

ggplot(pima, aes(type, glu, fill = type)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, colour = "grey30") +
  labs(x = "Diabetes", y = "Plasma glucose") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

heading: 8.658 3. Assumptions
text:
Binary outcome, independent observations, log-odds linear 
predictors (handled explicitly smooth/transformation).
code:
# residuals are less informative in GLMs; check for linearity on the logit scale
pima |>
  mutate(p = fitted(fit),
         logit = qlogis(p)) |>
  ggplot(aes(glu, logit)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, colour = "steelblue") +
  labs(x = "Plasma glucose", y = "Fitted logit")

heading: 8.659 4. Conduct
code:
glance(fit)
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_prob > 0.5, "Yes", "No")
table(pred_class, truth = pima$type)

heading: 8.660 5. Concluding statement

nrow(pima) women, 10 mg/dL increase plasma glucose
associated odds ratio diabetes 
round(exp(10 * coef(fit)["glu"]), 2) (95% CI profile:
round(exp(10 * confint(fit)["glu", 1]), 2) 
round(exp(10 * confint(fit)["glu", 2]), 2)). 0.5 threshold
classifier 
round(100 * mean(pred_class == pima$type), 1)% accuracy; 
threshold-free assessment appears later lab.

heading: 8.668 Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 8.669 1. Hypothesis
text:
Simulate scenario confounder masks effect interest,
add interaction show effect modification.

heading: 8.670 2. Visualise
text:
lines climb age; smoker line sits higher may slope
steeply.
code:
age <- rnorm(n, 55, 10)
# smoker more common among older people in this simulation (confounding)
smoker <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-3 + 0.05 * age))
# outcome depends on both; smoker effect is stronger at older ages (interaction)
y <- 120 + 0.6 * age + 5 * smoker + 0.2 * smoker * (age - mean(age)) +
  rnorm(n, 0, 8)
dat <- tibble(age, smoker = factor(smoker, labels = c("no", "yes")), y)

ggplot(dat, aes(age, y, colour = smoker)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Age (years)", y = "Outcome", colour = "Smoker")

heading: 8.671 3. Assumptions
text:
usual linear-model assumptions, plus implicit assumption 
relevant confounders model.
code:
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(fit_full)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

heading: 8.672 4. Conduct
text:
Crude smoker effect, adjusted age, interaction:Now centred age, makes smokeryes coefficient 
smoker effect mean age:
code:
adjusted <- lm(y ~ smoker + age, data = dat)
inter    <- lm(y ~ smoker * age, data = dat)

bind_rows(
  tidy(crude, conf.int = TRUE) |> mutate(model = "crude"),
  tidy(adjusted, conf.int = TRUE) |> mutate(model = "adjusted"),
  tidy(inter, conf.int = TRUE) |> mutate(model = "interaction")
) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  select(model, term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value)
inter_c <- lm(y ~ smoker * age_c, data = dat_c)
tidy(inter_c, conf.int = TRUE)

heading: 8.673 5. Concluding statement

adjusting age, estimated smoker-effect sample
mean age 
round(coef(inter_c)["smokeryes"], 1) units (95% CI:
round(confint(inter_c)["smokeryes", 1], 1) 
round(confint(inter_c)["smokeryes", 2], 1)), evidence 
effect modification age (interaction coefficient
round(coef(inter_c)["smokeryes:age_c"], 2)).

heading: 8.681 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 8.682 1. Hypothesis
text: Simulate dose-response data fit three-parameter logistic.

heading: 8.683 2. Visualise
code:
dose <- rep(c(0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30), each = 10)
# true params: asym = 100, xmid = log(3), scal = 0.7 (log-scale)
true_resp <- 100 / (1 + exp(-(log(dose) - log(3)) / 0.7))
resp <- true_resp + rnorm(n, 0, 5)
dat <- tibble(dose, resp)

ggplot(dat, aes(dose, resp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(x = "Dose (log scale)", y = "Response")

heading: 8.684 3. Assumptions
text:
Correct model form, independent normal errors constant variance
response scale, informative starting values.
code:
resid_plot <- tibble(fitted = fitted(fit), resid = resid(fit))
ggplot(resid_plot, aes(fitted, resid)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, colour = "grey50") +
  labs(x = "Fitted", y = "Residual")

heading: 8.685 4. Conduct
code:
confint(fit)

pred <- tibble(dose = 10 ^ seq(-1, 1.5, length.out = 100))
pred$resp <- predict(fit, newdata = pred)

ggplot(dat, aes(dose, resp)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_line(data = pred, colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(x = "Dose (log scale)", y = "Response")

heading: 8.686 5. Concluding statement
text:
three-parameter logistic fitted log-dose scale returned 
asymptote round(coef(fit)["Asym"], 1) units mid-dose
(EC50) round(exp(coef(fit)["xmid"]), 2). Confidence intervals
obtained via likelihood profile (see confint).

heading: 8.687 Common pitfalls
text:
Starting values put optimiser local minimum.Reporting symmetric SE-based intervals likelihood 
asymmetric; prefer confint.-interpreting parameters data span asymptote.

heading: 8.691 Learning objectives

heading: 8.692 Prerequisites
text: t-tests; linear regression basics.

heading: 8.693 Background
text:
Analysis variance linear regression categorical predictor
tradition. omnibus F-test asks whether group means
differ; follow-contrasts ask ones. correct order 
omnibus first, targeted contrasts, preferably multiple
testing correction respects family tests intended
seeing data.emmeans package made post-hoc machinery substantially
cleaner. emmeans(fit, ~ group) returns estimated marginal means
response scale; pairs() produces pairwise differences
Tukey-adjusted p-values default.common mistake run pairwise t-tests without correction 
report one smallest p-value. less common 
insidious mistake run omnibus F, see significant, 
stop — reporting single p-value without naming groups
differ much.

heading: 8.694 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(emmeans)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 8.695 1. Hypothesis
text:
Using ToothGrowth, ask whether guinea-pig tooth length depends 
dose vitamin C.Null: mean tooth length equal across three doses.
Alternative: least one dose differs.

heading: 8.696 2. Visualise
code:
ggplot(tg, aes(dose, len, fill = dose)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, colour = "grey30") +
  labs(x = "Dose (mg/day)", y = "Tooth length") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

heading: 8.697 3. Assumptions
text: Approximate normality within groups equal variances.
code:

plot(fit)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
bartlett.test(len ~ dose, data = tg)

heading: 8.698 4. Conduct

emm <- emmeans(fit, ~ dose)
emm
pairs(emm, adjust = "tukey")

heading: 8.699 5. Concluding statement
text:
Tooth length increased dose vitamin C (one-way ANOVA
F = round(summary(fit)[[1]]$"F value"[1], 2); p =
signif(summary(fit)[[1]]$"Pr(>F)"[1], 2)). Tukey-adjusted
contrasts indicated 2 mg/day dose gave longer teeth 
lower doses (adjusted p < 0.05).

Stopping significant F without reporting contrasts drive
.Reporting unadjusted pairwise p-values omnibus test.Treating non-significant Bartlett test proof equal variances.

heading: 8.705 Prerequisites

heading: 8.706 Background
text:
Ordinal outcomes — pain scales, tumour stages, Likert items — carry
information dichotomised version usually less 
continuous version. proportional-odds model (polr) assumes 
effect predictor across cumulative splits 
response; single coefficient log cumulative odds ratio.
Multinomial logistic regression makes constraint fits 
separate logit non-reference category.proportional-odds assumption strong. Brant test (
brant package) simply comparing polr fit multinomial
fit deviance can detect violations. fails, options include
partial proportional-odds model, multinomial model, 
continuation-ratio model.Multinomial models notoriously hungry data: non-reference
category requires full set coefficients. Reporting list
coefficient tables global test (likelihood ratio) rather
single p-value.

heading: 8.707 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(MASS)
library(nnet)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 8.708 1. Hypothesis
text:
Simulate ordinal outcome (symptom severity three levels) driven
single continuous exposure, contrast ordinal 
multinomial analyses.

heading: 8.709 2. Visualise
code:
x <- rnorm(n)
eta <- 1.2 * x
# cumulative cutpoints
probs <- cbind(
  plogis(-0.5 - eta),
  plogis(1.0 - eta) - plogis(-0.5 - eta),
  1 - plogis(1.0 - eta)
)
y <- apply(probs, 1, function(p) sample(1:3, 1, prob = p))
dat <- tibble(x, y = factor(y, levels = 1:3,
                            labels = c("mild", "moderate", "severe"),
                            ordered = TRUE))

ggplot(dat, aes(y, x, fill = y)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, colour = "grey30") +
  labs(x = "Severity", y = "Exposure x") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

heading: 8.710 3. Assumptions
text:
Proportional-odds (β across cumulative splits) polr;
independent observations models.two slopes close, proportional odds plausible.
code:
splits <- tibble(cut = c("mild vs rest", "mild+mod vs severe")) |>
  mutate(coef = c(
    coef(glm(I(as.numeric(y) >= 2) ~ x, data = dat, family = binomial))[2],
    coef(glm(I(as.numeric(y) >= 3) ~ x, data = dat, family = binomial))[2]
  ))
splits

heading: 8.711 4. Conduct
text: Multinomial:
code:
summary(fit_polr)
# approximate p-values
coef_summary <- coef(summary(fit_polr))
p <- pnorm(abs(coef_summary[, "t value"]), lower.tail = FALSE) * 2
cbind(coef_summary, "p value" = round(p, 3))
exp(coef(fit_polr))  # cumulative odds ratio
summary(fit_multi)
z <- summary(fit_multi)$coefficients / summary(fit_multi)$standard.errors
p_multi <- (1 - pnorm(abs(z), 0, 1)) * 2
round(p_multi, 3)

heading: 8.712 5. Concluding statement
text:
proportional-odds model returned cumulative odds ratio per
unit x round(exp(coef(fit_polr))[1], 2), meaning larger x
shifted symptom severity upward. multinomial model returned two
logits (moderate vs mild severe vs mild) effects 
direction; deviance-based comparison reject 
proportional-odds simplification.

estimates similar NB standard error larger 
honest.

  tidy(fit_p, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE) |> mutate(model = "Poisson"),

) |>
  filter(term == "x") |>

round(exp(coef(fit_nb))["x"], 2); 95% CI
round(exp(confint(fit_nb))["x", 1], 2) 
round(exp(confint(fit_nb))["x", 2], 2)). Poisson residual
deviance indicated overdispersion; therefore report

heading: 8.750 4. Conduct
text: OLS, robust, weighted fits:Sandwich SEs OLS fit:
code:
# variance grows roughly with x; weight by 1 / (fitted variance proxy)
w <- 1 / (0.3 + 0.2 * dat$x)^2
fit_wls <- lm(y ~ x, data = dat, weights = w)

bind_rows(
  tidy(fit_ols) |> mutate(model = "OLS"),
  tibble(term = c("(Intercept)", "x"),
         estimate = coef(fit_rlm),
         std.error = sqrt(diag(vcov(fit_rlm))),
         model = "robust"),
  tidy(fit_wls) |> mutate(model = "WLS")
) |>
  filter(term == "x") |>
  dplyr::select(model, estimate, std.error)

(approximately
round(coef(fit_ols)[2], 2),
round(coef(fit_rlm)[2], 2),

standard errors biased heteroscedasticity; HC3 sandwich
standard errors preferred inference.

heading: 9.3 Introduction
text: Bray-Curtis dissimilarity default index comparing ecological communities described species-abundance data. ranges 0 two samples share composition abundances 1 share species , unlike binary indices makes use abundance information rather collapsing presence/absence. Microbial ecology, plant community work, compositional dataset rare abundant species contribute meaningful information typically reach Bray-Curtis first dissimilarity compute.

heading: 9.4 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge distance dissimilarity matrices, abundance data structures, ordination methods (NMDS, PCoA) consume dissimilarity matrices.

heading: 9.5 Theory
text: two samples \(\) \(j\) abundances \(x_{ik}\) across species \(k\):\[\mathrm{BC}_{ij} = \frac{\sum_k |x_{ik} - x_{jk}|}{\sum_k (x_{ik} + x_{jk})}.\]dissimilarity, strict metric — triangle inequality can fail, Bray-Curtis matrices best visualised non-metric MDS (NMDS) rather principal coordinates. binary form (presence-absence input) reduces Sørensen dissimilarity. Bray-Curtis sensitive dominant species; log Hellinger transformation abundance matrix computing index -weights dominance lets rare species contribute .

heading: 9.6 Assumptions
text: Count abundance data, entries non-negative, comparable sampling effort across samples (normalisation relative abundance remove effort difference).

heading: 9.7 R Implementation
code:
library(vegan)

data(dune)
d_bc <- vegdist(dune, method = "bray")
as.matrix(d_bc)[1:3, 1:3]

# Log-transform to down-weight dominant species
d_bc_log <- vegdist(log1p(dune), method = "bray")

# NMDS visualisation
nmds <- metaMDS(dune, distance = "bray", k = 2, trymax = 50)
plot(nmds, type = "t")

heading: 9.8 Output & Results
text: vegdist() returns dist object containing lower-triangular Bray-Curtis matrix. metaMDS() runs NMDS resulting matrix returns coordinates, stress, convergence diagnostics. Stress values 0.1 indicate excellent representation; 0.1–0.2 acceptable; 0.2 poor.

heading: 9.9 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Bray-Curtis dissimilarity averaged 0.55 across pairs (range 0.32 0.84), indicating substantial community turnover; NMDS two dimensions converged stress 0.07, PERMANOVA (vegan::adonis2) detected significant management-type effect (\(F = 3.6\), \(p = 0.001\), \(R^2 = 0.21\)).” Always pair Bray-Curtis ordination formal group test PERMANOVA ANOSIM.

heading: 9.10 Practical Tips
text: Log-transform abundance (log1p()) use Hellinger distance (vegdist(..., method = "hellinger")) reduce dominance abundant species.Bray-Curtis default distance vegan::metaMDS(); passing data matrix directly equivalent vegdist(data, "bray") followed metaMDS() dist.phylogenetic-aware distances microbial communities, use UniFrac (GUniFrac::GUniFrac) instead; accounts evolutionary relationships among taxa.Pair NMDS PERMANOVA (adonis2) group comparison PERMDISP (betadisper) check homogeneity multivariate dispersion; significant PERMDISP can confound PERMANOVA conclusions.vegdist() supports many alternatives — Jaccard, Manhattan, Euclidean, Canberra, Gower, Hellinger — change method switch.large community matrices, sparse implementations parallelDist scale Bray-Curtis computation across cores without code changes.

heading: 9.11 R Packages Used
text: vegan vegdist(), metaMDS(), adonis2(), betadisper(); GUniFrac phylogenetic UniFrac variants microbiome data; parallelDist fast multi-core distance computation; phyloseq microbiome workflows integrate Bray-Curtis sequence tree data.

heading: 9.14 Introduction
text: Canonical correlation analysis (CCA) finds pairs linear combinations — one two variable sets — maximally correlated. multiple regression one outcome many predictors, CCA generalises many outcomes many predictors simultaneously: first canonical pair \((U_1, V_1)\) linear combination predictor side linear combination outcome side whose correlation highest. Successive pairs constrained uncorrelated previous ones, mirroring orthogonality principal components.

heading: 9.15 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multiple regression, principal components analysis, geometry correlation multivariate spaces.

heading: 9.16 Theory
text: Given two sets variables \(X\) (\(p\) columns) \(Y\) (\(q\) columns), CCA finds pairs canonical variates \(U_k = X a_k\) \(V_k = Y b_k\) maximising \(\mathrm{cor}(U_k, V_k)\) subject \(\mathrm{Var}(U_k) = \mathrm{Var}(V_k) = 1\) orthogonality previous pairs. \(\min(p, q)\) canonical pairs, indexed canonical correlations \(\rho_1 \ge \rho_2 \ge \dots\) non-increasing.Inference multivariate Normality uses Wilks’ lambda Pillai’s trace test whether canonical correlations beyond given index zero. Canonical loadings (correlations original variables canonical variates) help interpret structure canonical pair.

heading: 9.17 Assumptions
text: Multivariate Normality (asymptotic significance tests), linear associations variable sets, adequate sample size relative \(p + q\). CCA sensitive outliers; one two extreme observations can dominate canonical structure.

heading: 9.18 R Implementation
code:
library(CCA)

# Two sets: first two and last two iris variables (artificial split)
X <- scale(iris[, 1:2])
Y <- scale(iris[, 3:4])

cc_res <- cc(X, Y)
cc_res$cor      # canonical correlations
cc_res$xcoef    # canonical coefficients for X
cc_res$ycoef    # canonical coefficients for Y

# Significance via Wilks' lambda
wilks <- (1 - cc_res$cor^2)
wilks

heading: 9.19 Output & Results
text: cc() returns canonical correlations (sorted decreasing order), canonical coefficients (weights variable set), canonical variate scores. Wilks’ lambda statistic associated \(F\)-approximation test joint significance canonical correlations given index onwards.

heading: 9.20 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Canonical correlation analysis iris measurements found first canonical correlation \(\rho_1 = 0.94\) (\(p < 0.001\), Wilks’ \(\lambda = 0.077\)); canonical loadings showed petal dimensions dominating \(Y\)-side combination, indicating overall flower size primary axis association sepal petal measurements.” Report canonical loadings (correlations) rather raw weights interpretation; latter scale-dependent.

heading: 9.21 Practical Tips
text: Interpret first canonical correlations; later pairs typically capture little signal.high-dimensional settings (\(p + q\) approaching exceeding \(n\)), use regularised CCA (CCA::rcc); standard CCA breaks rank-deficient covariance matrices.Partial CCA (CCA::pcc CCP::p.asym) controls covariates removed testing canonical structure.predictive use, partial least squares (pls::plsr mixOmics::spls) often preferable; PLS optimises predictive covariance rather mutual correlation.Canonical loadings (correlations variables canonical variates) interpret cleaner raw weights scale-independent.Always pre-screen outliers; CCA highly sensitive extreme observations, robust CCA variant (rrcov::CcaClassic vs CcaRobust) worth comparing.

heading: 9.25 Introduction
text: silhouette widely used internal validation metric clustering. observation, compares mean distance others cluster mean distance nearest competing cluster, returning value \(-1\) \(+1\). Values near 1 indicate observations comfortably inside assigned cluster; values near 0 indicate ambiguous assignments cluster boundary; negative values indicate observations fit better different cluster. Averaging silhouettes across observations gives single number summarising cluster quality without needing labels external references.

heading: 9.26 Prerequisites
text: working understanding distance metrics, clustering algorithms (\(k\)-means, hierarchical, PAM), distinction internal external validation criteria.

heading: 9.27 Theory
text: observation \(\) cluster \(C_k\), define:\(a_i\) = mean distance \(\) observations \(C_k\).\(b_i\) = minimum, clusters \(C_l \neq C_k\), mean distance \(\) observations \(C_l\).silhouette width \[s_i = \frac{b_i - a_i}{\max(a_i, b_i)}, \qquad s_i \[-1, 1].\]cluster silhouette mean \(s_i\) across observations cluster; overall silhouette mean across observations. Conventional thresholds: \(> 0.7\) indicates strong cluster structure, \(0.5\)–\(0.7\) reasonable, \(0.25\)–\(0.5\) weak, \(< 0.25\) substantial structure.

heading: 9.28 Assumptions
text: meaningful distance metric; unambiguous cluster assignments. Silhouette assumes convex clusters can misleading density-defined non-convex shapes (rings, half-moons), DBSCAN-style validation appropriate.

heading: 9.29 R Implementation
code:
library(cluster); library(factoextra)

X <- scale(iris[, 1:4])

# k-means with silhouette
km <- kmeans(X, centers = 3, nstart = 25)
sil <- silhouette(km$cluster, dist(X))
summary(sil)

fviz_silhouette(sil)

# Choose k by silhouette
fviz_nbclust(X, kmeans, method = "silhouette")

heading: 9.30 Output & Results
text: silhouette() returns per-observation \(s_i\), assigned cluster, nearest-competing cluster. summary() aggregates cluster overall. fviz_silhouette() produces canonical horizontal-bar silhouette plot. fviz_nbclust() automates \(k\) selection maximising overall silhouette.

heading: 9.31 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “\(k\)-means \(k = 3\) standardised iris data produced overall silhouette width 0.51 (cluster widths 0.79, 0.39, 0.45), observations negative silhouette, 12 observations near versicolor / virginica boundary values 0.20.” Always report cluster-specific silhouettes, just overall mean — poorly-fitting cluster can hidden inside high overall average.

heading: 9.32 Practical Tips
text: Use silhouette choose \(k\) alongside elbow method gap statistic; concordance across three strongest argument chosen partition.Negative silhouettes flag misassigned observations; investigate whether represent genuine intermediate cases, data errors, inadequate \(k\).Silhouette misleading non-convex clusters (rings, manifolds); density-based methods (DBSCAN, OPTICS), prefer density-aware validation indices.Plot silhouette per cluster compare cluster-level means; single low-silhouette cluster pulls overall mean without per-cluster breakdown making visible.Alternative internal indices (Davies-Bouldin, Calinski-Harabasz, Dunn) capture different aspects cluster quality; NbClust computes 30+ reports consensus optimum.Silhouette computation \(O(n^2)\) distance evaluations; large \(n\), use sub-sample-based silhouette via cluster::silhouette() random subset.

heading: 9.49 Theory

library(dbscan)

# Choose eps from k-distance plot

heading: 9.58 Introduction
text: choice distance dissimilarity foundation multivariate methods: clustering algorithms (k-means, hierarchical, PAM, DBSCAN) consume distance, multidimensional scaling PCoA produce coordinates approximate one, \(k\)-nearest neighbours computes every prediction. Different metrics capture different notions proximity, right choice depends data type — continuous vs. categorical, abundances vs. presence/absence, magnitudes vs. directions — inferential question. Mismatched metrics produce results look correct answer wrong question.

heading: 9.59 Prerequisites
text: working understanding vectors, difference distance dissimilarity (metric vs. semi-metric), common multivariate methods consume distance matrices.

heading: 9.60 Theory
text: Standard continuous distances \(p\)-dimensional vectors:Euclidean: \(\sqrt{\sum_i (x_i - y_i)^2}\) — straight-line distance, default uses.Manhattan (\(L_1\)): \(\sum_i |x_i - y_i|\) — robust outliers, appropriate grid-like geometry.Minkowski (\(L_p\)): \((\sum_i |x_i - y_i|^p)^{1/p}\) — generalises Euclidean (\(p=2\)) Manhattan (\(p=1\)).Canberra: \(\sum_i |x_i - y_i| / (|x_i| + |y_i|)\) — sensitive small values; useful compositional data.Cosine: \(1 - (x \cdot y) / (\|x\| \|y\|)\) — measures direction, magnitude; standard text gene-expression contexts.Mahalanobis: scale- correlation-aware, weights deviation inverse covariance matrix.categorical mixed data: Gower distance handles mixed types via per-variable scaling; Jaccard Bray-Curtis suit presence-absence abundance data respectively.

heading: 9.61 Assumptions
text: Metrics satisfy triangle inequality symmetry; dissimilarities may relax . downstream methods (classical MDS, \(k\)-means) implicitly assume metric distances; others (NMDS, hierarchical clustering non-Euclidean distances) handle dissimilarities gracefully.

heading: 9.62 R Implementation
code:
library(cluster)

x <- rbind(c(1, 2, 3), c(2, 4, 5), c(5, 0, 1))

dist(x, method = "euclidean")
dist(x, method = "manhattan")
dist(x, method = "minkowski", p = 3)
dist(x, method = "canberra")

# Gower for mixed data
d <- data.frame(num = 1:3, cat = c("A", "B", "A"))
daisy(d, metric = "gower")

heading: 9.63 Output & Results
text: dist() returns dist object containing lower-triangular pairwise distance matrix. Different methods produce numerically different matrices emphasise different features geometry. cluster::daisy() extends mixed numeric-categorical data via Gower distance.

heading: 9.64 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Euclidean distances 3.0 rows 1 2 5.7 rows 1 3 reflect continuous-valued separation; Gower distance mixed data scaled variable \([0, 1]\) combining produced unitless dissimilarity range.” Always state metric used; clustering ordination output uninterpretable without .

heading: 9.65 Practical Tips
text: Standardise variables (scale()) Euclidean distance whenever scales differ across columns; otherwise large-variance variables dominate distance bias every downstream method.mixed numeric categorical data, Gower standard; scales variable \([0, 1]\) via type-specific rules combines weighted average.binary presence/absence, prefer Jaccard Sørensen-Dice (ignore joint absences) Hamming (counts matches).sparse high-dimensional data (text, gene expression), cosine distance often meaningful Euclidean magnitudes vary widely direction encodes signal.Mahalanobis essential predictor correlations matter; underlies discriminant function LDA Hotelling’s \(T^2\).Always match metric data type downstream method; Euclidean mixed scales presence/absence data frequent mistake invalidates clustering results.

library(factoextra)

X <- scale(iris[, 1:4])

# Elbow plot

heading: 9.80 Introduction
text: Confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) tests pre-specified factor structure data: items load factors, loadings constrained zero, factors correlate . measurement-model portion structural equation modelling standard tool validating questionnaire structures, latent-trait models psychometrics, multi-indicator latent constructs clinical behavioural research. Unlike exploratory factor analysis, CFA estimate items belong factor — decision model — instead estimates strength predetermined relationships tests model-data fit.

heading: 9.81 Prerequisites
text: working understanding exploratory factor analysis, regression, lavaan model-syntax conventions specifying latent variables.

heading: 9.82 Theory
text: CFA specifies complete pattern matrix advance: item either loads freely assigned factor constrained zero every factor. Fit assessed via:Chi-squared test: non-significant \(\chi^2\) formally accepts model, test rejects easily large \(n\).Incremental fit indices: CFI TLI compare baseline null model; values 0.95 indicate good fit, 0.90–0.95 acceptable.Absolute fit: RMSEA measures discrepancy per degree freedom; 0.06 good, 0.06–0.08 acceptable. accompanying 90 % CI quantifies precision.SRMR: standardised root-mean-square residual; 0.08 indicates good fit.well-fitting model satisfies multiple criteria simultaneously — single index sufficient.

heading: 9.83 Assumptions
text: correctly specified factor structure, multivariate Normal continuous indicators (robust / WLSMV estimators non-Normal ordinal data), sufficient sample size (rule thumb: least 10 observations per estimated parameter, \(n \ge 200\)).

heading: 9.84 R Implementation
code:
library(lavaan); library(semTools)

# Three-factor model on HolzingerSwineford1939 dataset
model <- '
  visual  =~ x1 + x2 + x3
  textual =~ x4 + x5 + x6
  speed   =~ x7 + x8 + x9
'
fit <- cfa(model, data = HolzingerSwineford1939)
summary(fit, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE)

# Modification indices for potential improvements
head(modificationIndices(fit, sort = TRUE), 10)

heading: 9.85 Output & Results
text: summary(fit, fit.measures = TRUE, standardized = TRUE) reports unstandardised standardised loadings, factor correlations, residual variances, full panel fit indices (chi-squared, CFI, TLI, RMSEA CI, SRMR). modificationIndices() lists potential parameter additions ordered expected chi-squared improvement.

heading: 9.86 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “three-factor CFA fit data adequately (\(\chi^2(24) = 85.3\), \(p < 0.001\); CFI 0.93; RMSEA 0.092 [90 % CI 0.071 0.114]; SRMR 0.065); standardised loadings exceeded 0.50 statistically significant (\(p < 0.001\)). RMSEA exceeded conventional 0.06 threshold, suggesting modest local misspecification — modification indices indicated residual correlation \(x_7\) \(x_9\) improve fit substantively justified.” Always report multiple fit indices; single one decisive.

heading: 9.87 Practical Tips
text: follow modification indices blindly; unconstrained parameter moves analysis confirmatory exploratory inflates Type error pre-specified.ordinal Likert items, use diagonally-weighted least squares (cfa(..., estimator = "DWLS") "WLSMV"); ordinary ML Likert items violates Normality assumptions.non-Normal continuous data, use robust ML (estimator = "MLR"); standard errors chi-squared corrected non-Normality.Measurement-invariance testing across groups (configural, metric, scalar invariance) standard follow-comparing latent means; use semTools::measurementInvariance().Report chi-squared degrees freedom full set fit indices; never report single index alone.Standardised loadings (factor-loading correlations) communicate effect size clearly unstandardised; always include .

heading: 9.91 Introduction
text: Exploratory factor analysis (EFA) searches small number latent factors explain correlations among larger set observed variables. cornerstone psychometric scale development, standard tool validating questionnaire structures, useful exploratory technique context many correlated indicators believed reflect smaller number latent constructs. output — rotated factor loadings together factor correlations — directly informs item-selection, scale-construction, CFA model-specification decisions.

heading: 9.92 Prerequisites
text: working understanding correlation matrices, principal components analysis, conceptual difference observed indicators latent constructs.

heading: 9.93 Theory
text: common factor model \[X = \Lambda F + U,\]\(X\) observed item matrix, \(\Lambda\) loading matrix, \(F\) common factors, \(U\) unique (specific) variance. Unlike PCA, lumps common specific variance together, EFA explicitly partitions variance.standard EFA workflow five steps:Check factorability: Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO > 0.6 acceptable, > 0.8 good) Bartlett’s sphericity test confirm correlation matrix enough structure factor.Choose number factors: parallel analysis modern gold standard; obsolete Kaiser eigenvalue-greater--1 rule subjective scree plot still seen supplemented.Extract: maximum likelihood (ML) inferential purposes, principal-axis factoring (PAF) robustness, principal-components quick exploration.Rotate: varimax (orthogonal) oblimin / promax (oblique) interpretable loadings.Interpret: examine rotated loadings, factor correlations, item communalities.

heading: 9.94 Assumptions
text: Multivariate Normal continuous data ML extraction; linearity item-factor relationships; sufficient sample size (rule thumb: least 5–10 cases per item, \(n \ge 200\) floor).

heading: 9.95 R Implementation
code:
library(psych); library(GPArotation)

d <- bfi[, 1:25]   # 25-item big-five inventory

# Factorability
KMO(d)
cortest.bartlett(cor(d, use = "pairwise"), n = nrow(d))

# Number of factors
fa.parallel(d, fa = "fa")

# EFA with 5 factors, oblique rotation
efa <- fa(d, nfactors = 5, rotate = "oblimin", fm = "ml")
print(efa, cut = 0.3)

heading: 9.96 Output & Results
text: KMO() returns overall per-item factorability indices; cortest.bartlett() returns sphericity test. fa.parallel() overlays observed eigenvalues simulated null eigenvalues determine number factors. fa() returns rotated loadings, communalities, factor correlations (oblique rotation), explained variance per factor.

heading: 9.97 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “EFA 25 personality items (n = 2,800; KMO = 0.85, Bartlett’s \(\chi^2 = 18{,}245\), \(p < 0.001\)) supported five-factor solution parallel analysis; oblimin-rotated loadings aligned Big Five structure (extraversion, neuroticism, conscientiousness, agreeableness, openness), explaining 45 % common variance, factor correlations ranged 0.15 0.42.” Always report KMO, Bartlett, number--factors method, extraction method, rotation.

heading: 9.98 Practical Tips
text: Use parallel analysis (fa.parallel) number--factors decision; avoid obsolete “eigenvalue > 1” Kaiser rule -extracts settings.Prefer oblique rotation (oblimin, promax) default; uncorrelated factors rare real psychological biological data.Watch Heywood cases — loadings 1 communalities 1 — indicate -extraction model misspecification.ordinal Likert items, use polychoric correlations (fa(..., cor = "poly")); Pearson correlations Likert items underestimate true factor structure.confirmatory tests resulting structure, run separate CFA (lavaan::cfa) held-sample; never confirm data used extract.Report item communalities alongside loadings; communalities 0.20 indicate items contribute little factor may need removal.

heading: 9.102 Introduction

heading: 9.104 Theory
text: Orthogonal rotations keep factors uncorrelated; rotated loading matrix retains property factors share variance.Varimax: maximises variance squared loadings within column, producing “simple structure” variable loads strongly one factor weakly others.Quartimax: maximises variance per row; tends produce single general factor.Oblique rotations allow factors correlate; realistic psychological, biological, social phenomena underlying constructs artificially independent.Oblimin: direct minimisation cross-factor loadings controlled obliquity.Promax: applies power transformation varimax solution produce oblique structure.Geomin: alternative oblique rotation popular IRT contexts.Oblique rotations produce pattern matrix (regression-like loadings variables factors) structure matrix (correlations variables factors); two coincide orthogonal rotation.

heading: 9.105 Assumptions
text: Rotation leaves fit unchanged; interpretation changes. chosen rotation respects substantive theory whether factors correlate.

heading: 9.106 R Implementation
code:
library(psych); library(GPArotation)

d <- bfi[, 1:25]

# Varimax (orthogonal)
efa_v <- fa(d, nfactors = 5, rotate = "varimax", fm = "ml")
print(efa_v, cut = 0.3)

# Oblimin (oblique)
efa_ob <- fa(d, nfactors = 5, rotate = "oblimin", fm = "ml")
print(efa_ob, cut = 0.3)

# Factor correlations after oblique rotation
efa_ob$Phi

heading: 9.107 Output & Results
text: fa() returns rotated loading matrix; oblique rotation, $Phi contains factor correlation matrix. Setting cut = 0.3 print() suppresses small loadings cleaner display. Comparing varimax oblimin loadings data shows dramatically interpretation can shift.

heading: 9.108 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Oblimin-rotated factors showed moderate correlations (range 0.15 0.42), justifying oblique rotation; resulting pattern matrix gave clear simple structure loadings 0.40 aligning hypothesised Big Five domains cross-loadings predominantly 0.20.” Always report factor correlations oblique rotation; justify choice constrain interpretation.

heading: 9.109 Practical Tips
text: Oblique rotation (oblimin promax) default modern psychometrics; orthogonal rotation justified genuine reason expect uncorrelated factors.Report factor correlations Phi matrix; 0.20, orthogonal solution may acceptable.Varimax produces simpler-looking loading structures can misleading factors truly correlate; apparent simplicity may hide systematic cross-loadings.Quartimax tends produce strong general factor; useful hierarchical structure expected (Spearman’s \(g\), hierarchical personality models).Rotation numerical optimisation; start multiple random initialisations confirm solution stable.psych::fa.diagram() visualises rotated structure path diagram, often easier communicate loadings table.

heading: 9.113 Introduction
text: gap statistic, introduced Tibshirani, Walther, Hastie 2001, principled way choose number clusters \(k\) partitioning method. elbow method relies visual judgement silhouette method per-observation neighbour comparisons, gap statistic compares within-cluster dispersion data expected null reference distribution cluster structure. point data deviate null optimal \(k\), bootstrap-based standard errors quantify certainty choice.

heading: 9.114 Prerequisites
text: working understanding clustering algorithms, within-cluster sum squares, bootstrap resampling standard error estimation.

heading: 9.115 Theory
text: candidate \(k\), define \(W_k\) log within-cluster dispersion (sum squared distances centroids) actual data running \(k\)-means another partitioning algorithm. Generate \(B\) datasets null reference — uniformly distributed data’s bounding box, principal-components box — compute average within-cluster dispersion \(\mathbb E[W_k^*]\) null. gap statistic \[\mathrm{Gap}(k) = \mathbb E[W_k^*] - W_k.\]optimal \(k\) smallest value satisfying \(\mathrm{Gap}(k) \ge \mathrm{Gap}(k+1) - s_{k+1}\), \(s_{k+1}\) standard deviation null reference \(k+1\) (-called “first-SE-max” criterion). rule rewards parsimonious choices: picks smallest \(k\) significantly worse larger values.

heading: 9.116 Assumptions
text: meaningful distance metric data; null reference distribution sensible (uniform bounding box reasonable low--moderate dimensions; PC-aligned bounding boxes better high dimensions).

heading: 9.117 R Implementation
code:
library(cluster); library(factoextra)

X <- scale(iris[, 1:4])

set.seed(2026)
gap <- clusGap(X, FUN = kmeans, nstart = 25, K.max = 10, B = 100)
print(gap, method = "firstSEmax")

fviz_gap_stat(gap)

heading: 9.118 Output & Results
text: clusGap() returns matrix \(W_k\), \(\mathbb E[W_k^*]\), gap value, bootstrap standard error \(k\). print(..., method = "firstSEmax") applies Tibshirani’s selection rule identify optimal \(k\). fviz_gap_stat() plots gap curve error bars.

heading: 9.119 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “gap statistic, computed \(B = 100\) bootstrap replicates uniform reference, identified \(k = 3\) optimal number clusters using firstSEmax criterion (\(\mathrm{Gap}(3) = 0.59\), \(\mathrm{Gap}(4) - s_4 = 0.55\)); matches underlying species structure iris data.” Report criterion explicitly (firstSEmax, Tibs2001SEmax, globalmax); sometimes disagree.

heading: 9.120 Practical Tips
text: Use \(B = 100\) \(500\) bootstrap replicates; fewer can produce unstable optima, rarely worth runtime cost.default uniform-bounding-box reference works well low dimensions; high dimensions, switch PC-aligned reference (spaceH0 = "scaledPCA").Combine gap statistic elbow silhouette methods; concordance across three strongest argument chosen \(k\).Gap performs best well-separated clusters; overlapping hierarchical structures can favour large \(k\) fail find clear optimum.large datasets, subsample applying gap statistic; bootstrap step otherwise dominates runtime.Selection criteria differ: firstSEmax (Tibshirani’s parsimonious choice), globalmax (absolute maximum, often larger), Tibs2001SEmax (original 2001 rule). Quote criterion report.

heading: 9.121 R Packages Used
text: cluster clusGap() underlying clustering algorithms; factoextra fviz_gap_stat() fviz_nbclust() ggplot-style visualisation; NbClust 30+ alternative cluster-validation indices one call.

heading: 9.124 Introduction
text: Gaussian mixture models (GMMs) represent data weighted sum several multivariate Normal distributions, component characterising latent cluster. Unlike \(k\)-means, essentially hard-assignment heuristic, GMMs fully probabilistic generative models: observation posterior probability belonging cluster, clusters can different shapes (covariance structures), model-selection criteria (BIC) handle choice cluster count without ad-hoc heuristics. mclust package enumerates small library covariance structures selects best automatically.

heading: 9.125 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multivariate Normal distribution, EM algorithm latent-variable models, BIC model-selection criterion.

heading: 9.126 Theory
text: mixture density \[p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^K \pi_k \, \mathcal N(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k),\]mixing proportions \(\pi_k \ge 0\) summing 1. Maximum-likelihood estimation alternates E-step (compute posterior responsibilities \(\gamma_{ik} = \pi_k \mathcal N(x_i \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) / p(x_i)\)) M-step (update \(\pi_k, \mu_k, \Sigma_k\) responsibilities). mclust parameterises covariance via 14 structures spherical-equal-volume fully-unconstrained-different-shape orientation, indexed three letters: volume, shape, orientation, Equal Varying. BIC selects best combination structure component count.

heading: 9.127 Assumptions
text: Multivariate Normal mixture components, independent observations, sample size large enough estimate component covariances stably (flexible models require \(\Omega(K p^2)\) observations \(K\) clusters \(p\) dimensions).

heading: 9.128 R Implementation
code:
library(mclust)

X <- iris[, 1:4]
fit <- Mclust(X)
summary(fit)

# Soft assignments
head(fit$z)

# Plot
plot(fit, what = "classification")

# BIC across models and K
plot(fit, what = "BIC")

heading: 9.129 Output & Results
text: Mclust() returns best model code (e.g. “VEV”), number components, BIC values across model space, fitted parameters, matrix soft assignment probabilities fit$z. plot(fit, = "BIC") shows BIC component count covariance structure, making selection rationale visible.

heading: 9.130 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “mclust selected VEV (varying volume, equal shape, varying orientation) model three components (\(\mathrm{BIC} = -560.4\), \(\Delta\mathrm{BIC}\) second-best model 18.2); three components closely match iris species (overall accuracy 97 %), soft assignments quantify uncertainty seven observations classified differently species labels.” Always report chosen covariance structure BIC value.

heading: 9.131 Practical Tips
text: Use BIC model selection across \(K\) covariance structure jointly; comparing \(K\) fixed structure vice versa misses joint optimum.Soft assignments (fit$z) useful downstream uncertainty propagation; rather committing one cluster label, propagate posterior probabilities subsequent analyses.high-dimensional data (\(p > n\)), regularised mixture models (HDclassif, pgmm) variational EM needed; standard mclust unstable \(\Sigma_k\) parameters observations.Outliers can distort Normal components; consider \(t\)-mixture models (teigen::teigen) heavier-tailed components, trimmed EM (tclust) robustness.Log-likelihood can become unbounded unconstrained covariances component covers single observation; mclust adds small prior prevent , watch warnings.large \(n\), mini-batch EM online variants scale millions observations, mclust defaults handle thousand comfortably.

heading: 9.132 R Packages Used
text: mclust Mclust(), BIC selection across 14-model library, visualisation; flexmix finite mixtures regressions generalised mixture models; teigen \(t\)-mixture models robust outliers; tclust trimmed mixture models explicit contamination handling.
