heading: 9.135 Introduction
text: Hierarchical clustering builds nested family partitions: bottom every observation cluster, top observations form single cluster, intermediate levels correspond partitions might consider. Agglomerative methods (practical default) merge two closest clusters step everything connected; divisive methods reverse process. output dendrogram — tree can cut height produce partition corresponding number clusters. dendrogram headline visual distinguishes hierarchical clustering partitioning methods.

heading: 9.136 Prerequisites
text: working understanding distance metrics, difference distance observations distance clusters, dendrogram graphical representation merging sequence.

heading: 9.137 Theory
text: Linkage criteria define distance two clusters \(\) \(B\):Single linkage: \(\min_{\, b \B} d(, b)\). Prone chaining: joins clusters nearest pair, producing long stringy structures.Complete linkage: \(\max_{\, b \B} d(, b)\). Produces compact, roughly equal-size clusters.Average linkage: average pairwise distances; intermediate behaviour.Ward’s linkage: merge, choose pair minimises increase within-cluster sum squares. Produces compact spherical clusters popular default.dendrogram cut user-chosen height (\(k\) clusters via cutree()); height cut diagnostic — short merges cut indicate tight clusters, tall merges suggest meaningful separation.

heading: 9.138 Assumptions
text: meaningful distance metric data roughly independent observations. chosen linkage criterion matches expected cluster geometry; Ward’s assumes Euclidean distance.

heading: 9.139 R Implementation
code:
library(factoextra)

X <- scale(iris[, 1:4])
d <- dist(X)

hc_ward <- hclust(d, method = "ward.D2")
fviz_dend(hc_ward, k = 3, rect = TRUE, palette = "Set2")

# Compare linkages
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
for (m in c("single", "complete", "average", "ward.D2")) {
  plot(hclust(d, method = m), main = m, hang = -1)
}

# Cluster assignments at k = 3
cutree(hc_ward, k = 3)

heading: 9.140 Output & Results
text: hclust() returns merging sequence per-merge heights hclust object. fviz_dend() plots dendrogram cluster boxes colour palette. cutree() extracts cluster assignments chosen \(k\). four-panel comparison shows dramatically linkage choice changes resulting tree.

heading: 9.141 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Ward-linkage agglomerative clustering standardised iris data, cut \(k = 3\), produced three clusters matching species labels 95 % agreement; single linkage produced chained 1-iris-vs-149 partition uninformative dataset, illustrating linkage choice’s importance.” Always disclose linkage method distance metric used.

heading: 9.142 Practical Tips
text: Ward’s linkage (ward.D2) reliable default spherical clusters similar size; agrees \(k\)-means spirit produces tree.Single linkage often produces stringy chains; reach chain-like structures substantively meaningful (e.g. minimum spanning trees network analysis).Visualise dendrogram choosing \(k\); height merges cut suggests meaningful versus arbitrary splits.large datasets, hierarchical clustering \(O(n^2)\) memory time; switch \(k\)-means DBSCAN tens thousands observations.mixed numeric-categorical data, compute Gower distance cluster::daisy() feed hclust(); Ward’s needs method = "ward.D" (D2).Consider cluster::agnes() hclust() agglomerative coefficient quantifies strength clustering structure (close 1 = strong structure).

heading: 9.146 Introduction
text: Independent component analysis (ICA) canonical approach recovering statistically independent latent signals observed linear mixtures. classic application “cocktail-party problem” — separating individual conversations microphone recordings overlapping speakers — technique generalises setting multiple independent sources mix linearly smaller set observed channels. EEG, fMRI, gene-expression data, audio source separation use ICA primary decomposition method.

heading: 9.147 Prerequisites
text: working understanding principal components analysis, statistical independence (stronger condition uncorrelatedness), concept linear mixing model.

heading: 9.148 Theory
text: ICA model assumes observations \(X\) generated \(X = S\), \(\) unknown \(n \times n\) mixing matrix \(S\) contains independent latent sources. goal estimate \(\) (inverse, unmixing matrix \(W\)) \(\hat S = W X\) recovers sources permutation scale ambiguities.Two principles drive ICA algorithms:Non-Gaussianity maximisation: central limit theorem, sums independent variables become Gaussian; unmixing direction maximises non-Gaussianity (kurtosis, negentropy) recovers independent source.Statistical independence maximisation: minimise mutual information recovered components.FastICA widely used algorithm uses fixed-point iteration kurtosis negentropy. Infomax alternative principle implemented domain-specific tools.key contrast PCA: PCA produces uncorrelated components maximising variance; ICA produces independent components maximising non-Gaussianity. two coincide Gaussian data, ICA undefined.

heading: 9.149 Assumptions
text: Sources statistically independent non-Gaussian (one Gaussian source can recovered); mixing linear instantaneous; number sources equals number observed channels (equals chosen number components).

heading: 9.150 R Implementation
code:
library(fastICA)

set.seed(2026)
# Simulate two sources: sine and sawtooth
t <- seq(0, 8, length.out = 1000)
s1 <- sin(2 * pi * t)
s2 <- ((2 * t) %% 2) - 1      # sawtooth

# Mix
A <- matrix(c(1, 0.5, 0.5, 1), 2, 2)
S <- cbind(s1, s2)
X <- S %*% t(A)

# Run ICA
ic <- fastICA(X, n.comp = 2)

par(mfrow = c(3, 1))
matplot(S, type = "l", main = "True sources")
matplot(X, type = "l", main = "Mixed signals")
matplot(ic$S, type = "l", main = "Recovered ICA components")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

heading: 9.151 Output & Results
text: fastICA() returns recovered sources $S, estimated mixing matrix $, unmixing matrix $W. three-panel plot shows original sine sawtooth sources, mixed signals observed, ICA-recovered components — visually almost identical originals sign permutation.

heading: 9.152 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “FastICA two channels mixed sine sawtooth signals recovered sources cleanly (visual inspection), demonstrating blind source separation linear-mixing model; recovered components rescaled unit variance permuted, reflecting inherent ICA ambiguities.” Mention algorithm pre-processing (whitening, mean-centring) methods section.

heading: 9.153 Practical Tips
text: Pre-whiten data (PCA-based decorrelation followed unit-variance scaling) FastICA; fastICA package automatically.ICA essentially rotation whitened data — order sign components arbitrary; interpret component indices meaningful.one Gaussian source can recovered; sources Gaussian, ICA separate .fMRI EEG, specialised implementations (MELODIC FSL, runica EEGLAB) handle domain-specific challenges large channel counts physiological artefacts.non-square mixing problems (sources channels), compressive ICA multi-modal extensions exist require specialised tools.ICA unsupervised; evaluating result requires interpreting recovered signals substantively, often expert judgement.

heading: 9.154 R Packages Used
text: fastICA standard FastICA implementation; ica additional ICA variants including extended Infomax; mlbench synthetic mixing examples; JADE joint approximate diagonalisation eigen-matrices, alternative algebraic approach ICA.

heading: 9.157 Introduction
text: Jaccard Dice dissimilarities standard distance measures binary (presence/absence) data: ecological community composition, disease symptom panels, gene set membership, document term frequencies thresholding. indices share key property: ignore joint absences focus agreements disagreements among presences. exactly needed absence carries little information — species absent two sites might absent habitat unsuitable, simply detected — wrong absences diagnostic.

heading: 9.158 Prerequisites
text: working understanding binary data, set-based similarity reasoning, conventional 2×2 contingency table two binary vectors.

heading: 9.159 Theory
text: two binary vectors \(x, y\) counts \(\) (present), \(b\) (\(x\)), \(c\) (\(y\)), \(d\) (absent):Jaccard index: \(J = / (+ b + c)\), ratio intersection union present-set. Jaccard dissimilarity \(1 - J\).Dice / Sørensen coefficient: \(D = 2a / (2a + b + c)\), Dice dissimilarity \(1 - D\). Dice doubles weight joint presences relative mismatches.Sørensen Dice equivalent binary data.ignore \(d\) (joint absences), making appropriate absences uninformative. Indices include \(d\) — simple matching, Hamming distance — preferable joint absences carry meaning (e.g., binary medical diagnoses “neither condition X” informative).

heading: 9.160 Assumptions
text: Binary data (count data thresholded binary), joint absences meaningful inferential question. data different absence-meaning assumptions can yield different distances.

heading: 9.161 R Implementation
code:
library(vegan)

# Species abundance data (reduce to presence/absence)
data(dune)
dune_pa <- (dune > 0) * 1
head(dune_pa)

# Jaccard dissimilarity
d_jac <- vegdist(dune_pa, method = "jaccard", binary = TRUE)
as.matrix(d_jac)[1:3, 1:3]

# Sorensen (= Dice)
d_sor <- vegdist(dune_pa, method = "bray", binary = TRUE)

heading: 9.162 Output & Results
text: Two pairwise dissimilarity matrices dune presence/absence data, one Jaccard one Sørensen-Dice. Jaccard distances systematically larger Dice data Dice double-counts joint presences denominator.

heading: 9.163 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Jaccard dissimilarity averaged 0.40 across pairs sites (range 0.16 0.65), indicating 40 % species presences shared randomly chosen pair; corresponding Sørensen-Dice dissimilarity averaged 0.25, reflecting Dice’s higher weight joint presences.” Always state index used; data produce different conclusions Jaccard versus Dice.

heading: 9.164 Practical Tips
text: Use Jaccard Dice joint absences biologically ecologically uninformative (rare species, sparse term-document data, gene-set membership).abundance rather presence-absence data, Bray-Curtis standard; Bray-Curtis binarised data reduces Sørensen-Dice.microbiome data phylogenetic information, UniFrac (GUniFrac::GUniFrac) preferable weights distances evolutionary relationships, just shared OTUs.binary medical data joint absences meaningful (neither patient condition), use simple matching (dist(x, method = "binary") Hamming-style) rather Jaccard.large sparse binary matrices (text, single-cell sparse counts), use sparse-matrix-aware implementations proxy parallelDist avoid memory blow-.Pair dissimilarity matrices NMDS PCoA visualisation; ordinations consume Jaccard Dice matrices directly.

heading: 9.165 R Packages Used
text: vegan vegdist() method = "jaccard" "bray" broader ecology workflow; proxy general dissimilarity functions including binary metrics; philentropy distance similarity coefficients across many families; GUniFrac phylogenetic UniFrac alternatives microbiome analysis.

heading: 9.168 Introduction
text: Kernel principal component analysis performs PCA high-dimensional feature space implicitly defined kernel function, capturing non-linear structure linear PCA misses. classic example — two concentric circles — illustrates point: linear PCA fails separate straight axis distinguishes two classes, kernel PCA radial-basis-function kernel finds radial coordinate implicit infinite-dimensional space separates rings cleanly. technique generalises PCA data lying curved manifolds preserving eigen-decomposition mathematical structure.

heading: 9.169 Prerequisites
text: working understanding principal components analysis, kernel trick, Gram matrices, difference linear non-linear feature spaces.

heading: 9.170 Theory
text: kernel function \(K(x, y) = \langle \phi(x), \phi(y) \rangle\) defines inner product feature space without ever computing \(\phi\) explicitly (“kernel trick”). Standard choices:RBF / Gaussian: \(K(x, y) = \exp(-\gamma \|x - y\|^2)\), infinite-dimensional feature space.Polynomial: \(K(x, y) = (x^\top y + c)^d\), polynomial feature space degree \(d\).Sigmoid: \(K(x, y) = \tanh(\alpha x^\top y + c)\), related neural-net activations.Construct \(n \times n\) Gram matrix \(K\), centre feature space, compute top eigenvectors. projection observation onto kernel principal component weighted sum kernel evaluations training observations — making kernel PCA \(O(n^2)\) space time, becomes prohibitive \(n \gg 10{,}000\).

heading: 9.171 Assumptions
text: kernel encodes similarity meaningful inferential question; Gram matrix positive semi-definite (true valid Mercer kernels); sufficient observations stable eigendecomposition.

heading: 9.172 R Implementation
code:
library(kernlab)

# Data on two concentric circles (linear PCA fails)
set.seed(2026)
theta <- runif(200, 0, 2 * pi)
r <- c(rep(1, 100), rep(3, 100))
X <- cbind(r * cos(theta), r * sin(theta)) + matrix(rnorm(400, 0, 0.1), 200)
class <- factor(r)

# Linear PCA
pca <- prcomp(X)
plot(pca$x[, 1:2], col = class, main = "Linear PCA")

# Kernel PCA with RBF
kpca <- kpca(X, kernel = "rbfdot", kpar = list(sigma = 0.1), features = 2)
plot(rotated(kpca), col = class, main = "Kernel PCA (RBF)")

heading: 9.173 Output & Results
text: Two side--side scatterplots: linear PCA leaves two concentric rings overlapping PC space, kernel PCA RBF kernel \(\sigma = 0.1\) separates cleanly along first kernel principal component.

heading: 9.174 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Kernel PCA RBF kernel (\(\sigma = 0.1\)) separated two concentric rings (n = 100 ) linear PCA , demonstrating underlying class structure non-linear original feature space; first kernel principal component captured radial coordinate distinguishes classes.” kernel PCA succeeds linear PCA fails, name geometric feature captured.

heading: 9.175 Practical Tips
text: Kernel choice hyperparameters matter; RBF median-heuristic bandwidth reasonable default, tune cross-validation downstream supervised task.Kernel PCA components directly interpretable original feature space; weighted sums kernel evaluations training points.Computation scales \(O(n^2)\) space \(O(n^3)\) time eigendecomposition; \(n > 10{,}000\), use Nyström approximation (kernlab::kha, RSpectra partial eigendecomposition).Modern alternatives non-linear visualisation — UMAP, t-SNE, autoencoders — often outperform kernel PCA cluster separation two dimensions.Kernel PCA useful feature engineering step downstream supervised learning \(p\) small; \(p \gg n\) regimes, sparse linear methods (PLS, lasso) typically efficient.preserving local geometry, prefer Laplacian eigenmaps diffusion maps; kernel PCA preserves variance implicit feature space, local distances.

heading: 9.176 R Packages Used

heading: 9.179 Introduction
text: \(k\)-means workhorse partitioning cluster analysis: assign \(n\) observations one \(k\) clusters, cluster centres multivariate means objective minimise total within-cluster sum squared distances. scales large datasets, conceptually simple, produces interpretable centres original feature space. limitations — sensitivity outliers, dependence Euclidean geometry, need pre-specify \(k\), assumption approximately spherical equal-size clusters — equally well known motivate alternatives (PAM, DBSCAN, Gaussian mixtures) category.

heading: 9.180 Prerequisites
text: working understanding distance metrics, multivariate means, trade-within-cluster compactness -cluster separation.

heading: 9.181 Theory
text: Lloyd’s algorithm alternates two steps convergence:Initialise \(k\) centres, either randomly via \(k\)-means++ heuristic spreads initial centres apart better convergence.Assign observation cluster nearest centre.Update centre mean assigned observations.Repeat cluster assignments longer change.objective \[\mathrm{WSS} = \sum_{j=1}^k \sum_{x \C_j} \|x - \bar x_j\|^2,\]monotonically decreasing across iterations. algorithm converges local optimum, multiple random starts (nstart >= 25) needed find good solution. Choice \(k\) uses elbow method, silhouette analysis, gap statistic, composite tools like NbClust.

heading: 9.182 Assumptions
text: Continuous variables comparable scales (standardise first); roughly spherical clusters similar size; Euclidean distance meaningful. Outliers can drag means produce unbalanced clusters; PAM preferable outliers present.

heading: 9.183 R Implementation
code:
library(factoextra)

X <- scale(iris[, 1:4])

# 3 clusters
set.seed(2026)
km <- kmeans(X, centers = 3, nstart = 25)
km$size
km$centers

fviz_cluster(km, data = X, palette = "Set2", ggtheme = theme_minimal())

# Elbow and silhouette
fviz_nbclust(X, kmeans, method = "wss")
fviz_nbclust(X, kmeans, method = "silhouette")

heading: 9.184 Output & Results
text: kmeans() returns cluster assignments, cluster centres standardised feature space, total within-cluster sum squares, -cluster sum squares, convergence diagnostics. fviz_cluster() plots clusters first two principal components cluster ellipses; fviz_nbclust() automates elbow silhouette diagnostics.

heading: 9.185 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “\(k\)-means \(k = 3\) 25 random starts partitioned standardised iris data total within-cluster sum squares 140; resulting clusters closely matched three species (overall accuracy 89 %, versicolor virginica contributing misclassifications).” Always state \(k\), number starts, quality metric (silhouette WSS).

heading: 9.186 Practical Tips
text: Always standardise predictors \(k\)-means; variables larger variance dominate Euclidean distance bias partition.Use nstart = 25 higher avoid poor local optima; algorithm non-convex single random start can converge sub-optimal solution.non-spherical clusters, switch hierarchical clustering, DBSCAN (density-based), Gaussian mixture models (model-based elliptical clusters).categorical data, \(k\)-means apply directly; use \(k\)-modes (klaR::kmodes) PAM Gower distance.Cross-validate chosen \(k\) via stability measures (fpc::clusterboot); clusters recur bootstrapping trustworthy change resample.Outliers can drag means; outlier-contaminated data, prefer PAM (cluster::pam), trimmed \(k\)-means (tclust::tkmeans), robust mixture models.

heading: 9.190 Introduction
text: Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) canonical Gaussian classifier two classes. assumption within-class distributions multivariate Normal common covariance matrix, optimal Bayes classifier linear predictors, corresponding posterior probabilities simple closed-form functions class means pooled covariance. Fisher’s complementary derivation gives projection onto \(K - 1\) axes maximally separates \(K\) class centroids; useful low-dimensional visualisation class structure.

heading: 9.191 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multivariate Normal distributions, classification metrics, Bayes-optimal classifier known generative assumptions.

heading: 9.192 Theory
text: Assume class \(k\) prior \(\pi_k\), mean \(\mu_k\), common covariance \(\Sigma\). Bayes-optimal log-discriminant \[\delta_k(x) = x^\top \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k - \tfrac{1}{2} \mu_k^\top \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k + \log \pi_k.\]Assigning \(x\) class largest \(\delta_k\) gives LDA classifier. decision boundary two classes linear \(x\), makes model interpretable: classification depends small number linear combinations predictors.Fisher’s LDA, conceptually identical, finds directions -class variance large relative within-class variance; projecting onto top \(K - 1\) directions gives canonical low-dimensional visualisation class structure.

heading: 9.193 Assumptions
text: Multivariate Normal within class, equal class covariance matrices (otherwise QDA preferred), independent observations. Outliers can pull class means inflate pooled covariance, distorting linear boundary.

heading: 9.194 R Implementation
code:
library(MASS)

set.seed(2026)
train_idx <- sample(nrow(iris), 100)
train <- iris[train_idx, ]; test  <- iris[-train_idx, ]

fit_lda <- lda(Species ~ ., data = train)
pred <- predict(fit_lda, test)
table(pred$class, test$Species)

# Project onto LDA axes
plot(fit_lda, col = as.integer(train$Species))

heading: 9.195 Output & Results
text: lda() returns class priors, class means, scaling matrix (coefficients linear discriminants), proportion trace axis explains. predict() provides class labels posterior probabilities. classifier projects test observations onto LDA axes assigns nearest class centroid projected space.

heading: 9.196 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Linear discriminant analysis iris training set classified held-observations 98 % accuracy (49/50 correct); first discriminant axis accounted 99 % -group variance, indicating single linear combination four measurements largely separates three species.” Compare LDA QDA class covariances differ; otherwise LDA efficient choice.

heading: 9.197 Practical Tips
text: class-specific covariance differences, switch quadratic discriminant analysis (QDA); Box’s M test (biotools::boxM) provides formal check, sensitive non-Normality.LDA sensitive outliers; screen use robust variants MASS::lda(... , method = "mve") rrcov::Linda().Prior class probabilities affect decision boundary; specify via prior = c(...) training-set proportions differ population proportions.high-dimensional data \(p\) approaching exceeding \(n\), regularise via klaR::rda() (regularised discriminant analysis) sparse LDA (MASS::lda PCA pre-step, sparseLDA).Multiclass alternatives include multinomial logistic regression (nnet::multinom) random forests; LDA remains competitive low-dimensional problems Gaussian-like classes.Visualise LDA projection first two discriminants; 2D plot class colours quickly shows whether classes linearly separable.

heading: 9.198 R Packages Used
text: MASS lda() qda(); klaR rda() (regularised) partimat() partition diagnostics; caret cross-validated comparison across discriminant variants; rrcov robust LDA; sparseLDA high-dimensional sparse extensions.

heading: 9.201 Introduction
text: Mahalanobis distance multivariate generalisation “many standard deviations mean” respects scale correlation variables. Euclidean distance treats axis equally ignores covariance, Mahalanobis pre-multiplies deviation inverse covariance matrix, directions high variance contribute less correlated directions decorrelated measurement. foundation multivariate outlier detection, discriminant function LDA QDA, Hotelling’s \(T^2\) statistic.

heading: 9.202 Prerequisites
text: working understanding covariance matrices, multivariate Normal distributions, chi-squared distribution.

heading: 9.203 Theory
text: point \(x \\mathbb R^p\), mean vector \(\mu\), covariance \(\Sigma\):\[D_M(x, \mu, \Sigma) = \sqrt{(x - \mu)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu)}.\]multivariate Normality, \(D_M^2\) follows chi-squared distribution \(p\) degrees freedom. basis probabilistic outlier flagging: points \(D_M^2\) exceeding \(1 - \alpha\) chi-squared quantile unlikely null. Substituting sample mean sample covariance gives standard estimator, sample covariance influenced outliers — leading “masking” extreme points hide behind . Robust estimators (Minimum Covariance Determinant, MCD; Minimum Volume Ellipsoid, MVE) trim -influential observations estimating \(\Sigma\).

heading: 9.204 Assumptions
text: Multivariate Normality chi-squared reference distribution; positive-definite \(\Sigma\) (pseudo-inverse rank-deficient); sufficient sample size relative \(p\) stable covariance estimation.

heading: 9.205 R Implementation
code:
library(robustbase)

X <- iris[, 1:4]
mu <- colMeans(X)
Sigma <- cov(X)
MD2 <- mahalanobis(X, mu, Sigma)

# Threshold at 97.5% chi-squared quantile
cutoff <- qchisq(0.975, df = 4)
which(MD2 > cutoff)

# Robust version (MCD)
mcd <- covMcd(X)
MD2_rob <- mahalanobis(X, mcd$center, mcd$cov)
which(MD2_rob > cutoff)

heading: 9.206 Output & Results
text: mahalanobis() returns squared distances supplied centre covariance. Indexing chi-squared cutoff identifies candidate outliers. robust MCD-based version typically flags outliers classical estimator distorted points flag.

heading: 9.207 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Classical Mahalanobis distance flagged 4 iris observations \(\chi^2_4\) 97.5 % cutoff, robust MCD-based estimator flagged 12; additional observations masked joint influence classical covariance estimate, illustrating robust methods preferable outlier diagnostics non-trivial multivariate data.” Always pair classical robust Mahalanobis reporting outliers; agreement increases confidence, disagreement signals masking.

heading: 9.208 Practical Tips
text: Classical Mahalanobis suffers masking — outliers distort covariance used detect . Always pair MCD MVE non-trivial outlier-detection task.high-dimensional data (\(p \gg n\)), covariance rank-deficient; use regularised estimators (corpcor::cov.shrink) first reduce dimensionality.Mahalanobis distance underlies LDA QDA: discriminant function Mahalanobis distance class centre, weighted class priors.multivariate Normality assessment, plot ordered Mahalanobis distances chi-squared quantiles; deviations diagonal indicate non-Normality outliers.cutoff \(\chi^2_p\) assumes Normality; non-Normal data, calibrate cutoff via simulation use robust universal cutoff.Use squared form \(D_M^2\) reporting; chi-squared reference distribution avoids square-root computation.

heading: 9.209 R Packages Used
text: Base R mahalanobis(); robustbase::covMcd() MCD-based robust covariance; MASS::cov.mve() MVE; rrcov::CovMcd() CovMve() additional robust estimators formal classes; mvoutlier::aq.plot() adjusted quantile plots compare classical robust Mahalanobis distances.

heading: 9.212 Introduction
text: MANOVA — multivariate analysis variance — tests whether group means differ across several dependent variables simultaneously, accounting correlations among outcomes. powerful running separate one-way ANOVAs outcomes correlated, pools information across response set instead testing isolation. also controls Type error family-wise level joint hypothesis, removing need multiple-comparison correction across battery univariate ANOVAs.

heading: 9.213 Prerequisites
text: working understanding one-way ANOVA, multivariate Normal distributions, covariance matrices.

heading: 9.214 Theory
text: MANOVA decomposes total cross-product matrix hypothesis (\(H\)) error (\(E\)) components constructs four standard multivariate test statistics joint structure:Wilks’ lambda: \(\Lambda = |E| / |H + E|\), classical choice; small values reject null.Pillai’s trace: \(V = \mathrm{tr}(H (H + E)^{-1})\), robust covariance heterogeneity recommended default.Hotelling–Lawley trace: \(T = \mathrm{tr}(H E^{-1})\), powerful one dimension dominates.Roy’s greatest root: $= $ largest eigenvalue \(H E^{-1}\), powerful group differences concentrated one direction liberal otherwise.four test omnibus null hypothesis slightly different operating characteristics non-Normality unequal covariances.

heading: 9.215 Assumptions
text: Multivariate Normal within groups, homogeneous covariance matrices across groups (Box’s M test), independent observations.

heading: 9.216 R Implementation
code:
fit <- manova(cbind(Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width, Petal.Length, Petal.Width) ~
              Species, data = iris)
summary(fit)                     # Pillai default
summary(fit, test = "Wilks")
summary(fit, test = "Hotelling-Lawley")

# Follow-up univariate ANOVAs
summary.aov(fit)

# car Anova for Type II/III
library(car)
Manova(lm(cbind(Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width) ~ Species, data = iris))

heading: 9.217 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) returns Pillai trace default, \(F\) approximation, degrees freedom, \(p\)-value. Specifying tests via test argument produces Wilks, Hotelling-Lawley, Roy variants. summary.aov(fit) runs follow-univariate ANOVAs outcome.

heading: 9.218 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “MANOVA showed significant multivariate species effect (Wilks’ \(\Lambda = 0.02\), \(F(8, 288) = 199\), \(p < 0.001\), multivariate \(\eta^2 = 0.86\)); univariate follow-ANOVAs confirmed significant species differences four flower dimensions (\(p < 0.001\) ), petal length carrying strongest effect (\(\eta^2 = 0.94\)).” Always pair MANOVA univariate follow-ups multivariate effect-size measure.

heading: 9.219 Practical Tips
text: Pillai’s trace recommended default — robust covariance heterogeneity unequal sample sizes.significant MANOVA, follow univariate ANOVAs outcome identify dimensions drive effect; corrected \(p\)-values (Bonferroni Holm) appropriate stage.Test homogeneity covariance Box’s M (biotools::boxM) relying Wilks; significant Box’s M motivates Pillai.unequal sample sizes multifactorial designs, use car::Manova() explicit Type II Type III sums squares; base R’s summary.manova defaults sequential (Type ).Discriminant analysis follows naturally post-MANOVA exploration; linear discriminants visualise multivariate group differences live.groups many highly-correlated outcomes, MANOVA powerful Bonferroni-corrected univariate tests; many groups outcomes, separate ANOVAs may interpretable.

heading: 9.220 R Packages Used
text: Base R manova() summary.manova(); car::Manova() Type II / III sums squares; biotools::boxM() homogeneity--covariance test; MASS::lda() discriminant-analysis follow-; heplots visualising hypothesis-error decompositions.

heading: 9.223 Introduction
text: Metric multidimensional scaling — also known classical MDS principal coordinates analysis (PCoA) — takes pairwise distance matrix produces low-dimensional Euclidean configuration whose pairwise distances approximate input closely possible. workhorse visualising similarity distance data raw coordinates unavailable: think reconstructing map inter-city distances, projecting microbial-community Bray-Curtis dissimilarities, visualising kernel-derived similarity matrix 2D plot.

heading: 9.224 Prerequisites
text: working understanding distance similarity matrices, principal components analysis, basic linear algebra (eigendecomposition).

heading: 9.225 Theory
text: Given \(n \times n\) distance matrix \(D\), form doubly-centred inner-product matrix\[B = -\tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(- J/n\bigr) D^{(2)} \bigl(- J/n\bigr),\]\(D^{(2)}\) elements \(d_{ij}^2\) \(J\) -ones matrix. Eigendecompose \(B = U \Lambda U^\top\). top \(k\) eigenvectors scaled \(\sqrt{\lambda}\) give \(k\)-dimensional configuration whose Euclidean inter-point distances approximate original distances. \(D\) comes Euclidean distances raw data, classical MDS equivalent PCA centred data — eigenvalues match squared singular values centred data matrix.non-Euclidean distances, \(B\) may negative eigenvalues; configuration still uses top positive eigenvalues, discarded negative mass quantifying embedding error.

heading: 9.226 Assumptions
text: pairwise distance matrix least approximately Euclidean-embeddable. Severe violations (negative eigenvalues dominating) suggest non-metric MDS appropriate alternative.

heading: 9.227 R Implementation
code:
# Classical MDS on US city distances
data(UScitiesD)
mds <- cmdscale(UScitiesD, k = 2, eig = TRUE)
plot(mds$points, type = "n")
text(mds$points, labels = labels(UScitiesD))

# Eigenvalues and variance explained
mds$eig[1:5]
cumsum(mds$eig[1:5] / sum(mds$eig[1:5]))

heading: 9.228 Output & Results
text: cmdscale() returns low-dimensional configuration ($points), eigenvalues, indicator well chosen \(k\) approximates original distances. plot US-city MDS coordinates recovers recognisable geographical structure near-perfect rotation reflection actual map.

heading: 9.229 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Classical MDS 10 inter-city distances produced two-dimensional configuration captured 99 % eigen-mass; resulting layout matches actual geography reflection, east-west north-south structure immediately visible.” Always report proportion eigen-mass retained displayed dimensions; poor approximation signals dimensions non-metric MDS help.

heading: 9.230 Practical Tips
text: Euclidean distances, classical MDS equivalent PCA centred data; raw data, run PCA directly cleaner output.non-Euclidean distances (Bray-Curtis, Jaccard, network distances), check eigenvalue structure — large negative eigenvalues indicate non-Euclidean metric motivate non-metric MDS instead.ecology microbiome research, method called principal coordinates analysis (PCoA); algorithm identical.Add Procrustes analysis (vegan::procrustes) comparing MDS solutions across related datasets; aligns configurations rotation, reflection, scaling.configurations beyond two dimensions, plot pairs axes; factoextra::fviz_mds() provides ggplot-style alternative.Pair configuration stress measure proportion variance retained communicate fit quality alongside visual.

heading: 9.231 R Packages Used
text: Base R cmdscale() classical MDS; vegan::wcmdscale() weighted classical MDS ecology; MASS::isoMDS() vegan::metaMDS() non-metric alternatives; factoextra::fviz_mds() ggplot-style visualisation; smacof advanced MDS variants including weighted constrained versions.

heading: 9.234 Introduction
text: Non-metric multidimensional scaling (NMDS) preserves rank order pairwise dissimilarities rather absolute magnitudes. robustness makes standard ordination method ecological community data, Bray-Curtis Jaccard dissimilarities Euclidean distance values carry less meaning ordering similarities. classical MDS works squared distances directly, NMDS minimises stress function depends whether configuration’s distances respect input ranking.

heading: 9.235 Prerequisites
text: working understanding metric MDS (classical / PCoA), rank-based statistics, distinction metric ordinal distance information.

heading: 9.236 Theory
text: NMDS finds configuration \(Y\) \(k\) dimensions whose pairwise Euclidean distances \(d(Y_i, Y_j)\) monotonically related input dissimilarities \(\delta_{ij}\). optimisation minimises Kruskal stress:\[\mathrm{Stress} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{< j} (d_{ij} - \hat d_{ij})^2}{\sum_{< j} d_{ij}^2}},\]\(\hat d_{ij}\) disparities — values fitted via isotonic regression non-decreasing function \(\delta_{ij}\). Shepard diagram plots \(\delta_{ij}\) \(d_{ij}\) isotonic fit overlaid; tight scatter around smooth monotone curve indicates good ordination.Stress interpretation (Kruskal): 0.05 excellent, 0.05–0.10 good, 0.10–0.20 fair, 0.20 poor configuration trusted.

heading: 9.237 Assumptions
text: Dissimilarity ranks meaningful; sufficient observations stable configuration; chosen number dimensions (\(k\)) large enough accommodate rank structure.

heading: 9.238 R Implementation
code:
library(MASS); library(vegan)

# Non-metric MDS on UScities
data(UScitiesD)
iso <- isoMDS(UScitiesD, k = 2)
iso$stress

# ecology example with vegan
data(varespec)
nmds <- metaMDS(varespec, distance = "bray", k = 2, trymax = 50)
nmds$stress
plot(nmds, type = "t")

heading: 9.239 Output & Results
text: isoMDS() metaMDS() return 2D configuration plus stress value. metaMDS() also runs multiple random starts (trymax) returns best solution; essential NMDS non-convex prone local minima.

heading: 9.240 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “NMDS vegetation community (Bray-Curtis dissimilarity, two dimensions, 50 random starts) achieved stress 0.18 — fair fit — configuration revealed two dominant environmental gradients corresponding moisture nutrient availability site-level covariates overlaid via envfit().” Always report stress value number starts; single-start fit can land poor local minimum.

heading: 9.241 Practical Tips
text: Always check stress; values 0.2 indicate configuration unreliable higher-dimensional solution may needed.Use multiple random starts (trymax = 50 metaMDS); NMDS non-convex single start often lands local minimum.NMDS standard non-Euclidean ecological dissimilarities; metric MDS forces Euclidean embedding may respect underlying geometry.Procrustes-align configurations across related datasets (vegan::procrustes); NMDS solutions defined rotation, reflection, uniform scaling.Pair NMDS vegan::envfit() ordisurf() overlay environmental covariates arrows contours, exposing gradients ordination captured.Interpret relative positions ; absolute distances NMDS plot single fixed scale.

heading: 9.242 R Packages Used

heading: 9.245 Introduction
text: Multiple correspondence analysis (MCA) categorical analogue principal components analysis. PCA reduces matrix continuous variables small number orthogonal axes capturing maximal variance, MCA reduces set categorical variables axes capturing maximal chi-squared inertia. natural exploratory tool survey data, lifestyle patterns, ICD code panels, context several categorical variables jointly characterise units observation.

heading: 9.246 Prerequisites
text: working understanding correspondence analysis two-way contingency table, indicator (disjunctive) coding categorical variables, chi-squared distance interpretation.

heading: 9.247 Theory
text: MCA operates disjunctive-coded indicator matrix represents categorical variable one binary column per category. Two equivalent computations exist:Indicator-matrix CA: apply standard correspondence analysis \(n \times \sum K_q\) indicator matrix.Burt-matrix CA: apply CA Burt table, cross-tabulation pairs variables; gives identical row column structure sometimes intuitive geometry.category becomes point resulting low-dimensional space; individual also placed space (“cloud individuals”). Inertia per axis typically low — much lower typical PCA explained variance — indicator column contributes one dimension variation. Benzécri’s Greenacre’s adjusted inertia formulas correct give meaningful variance-explained percentages.

heading: 9.248 Assumptions
text: Categorical variables (strong zero cells), sufficient sample size relative number categories, meaningful joint structure extract.

heading: 9.249 R Implementation
code:
library(FactoMineR); library(factoextra)

data(hobbies)
mca <- MCA(hobbies[, 1:8], graph = FALSE)
fviz_mca_biplot(mca)
fviz_mca_var(mca)

summary(mca)

heading: 9.250 Output & Results
text: MCA() returns coordinates individuals category points, inertia per axis, contributions, squared cosines (quality representation). fviz_mca_biplot() displays individuals categories first two dimensions; fviz_mca_var() zooms category cloud.

heading: 9.251 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “MCA 8 hobby variables (n = 8,403 respondents) extracted two interpretable dimensions: first contrasts active vs sedentary hobbies (Benzécri-adjusted inertia 28 %), second social vs solitary hobbies (adjusted inertia 19 %); supplementary projection age gender showed clear age gradient first dimension weak gender effect second.” Always report adjusted inertia rather raw inertia; raw values systematically understate explained variation.

heading: 9.252 Practical Tips
text: Use Benzécri’s Greenacre’s adjusted inertia (FactoMineR::MCA() reports ) variance-explained percentages; raw inertias pessimistic misleading.Supplementary categorical variables (quali.sup) project onto axes without affecting estimation; useful adding demographic markers post-hoc.mixed continuous-categorical data, switch factor analysis mixed data (FAMD); generalises PCA MCA jointly.Visualise category cloud separately individual cloud latter dense; fviz_mca_var() gives cleaner view category geometry.ordinal categorical data, MCA loses ordering — consider polychoric PCA graded-response IRT models instead.Pair MCA hierarchical clustering principal component scores (HCPC()) single workflow combines exploration partitioning.

heading: 9.253 R Packages Used

heading: 9.256 Introduction
text: Partitioning around medoids (PAM) canonical \(k\)-medoids clustering algorithm. Like \(k\)-means, partitions \(n\) observations \(k\) clusters; unlike \(k\)-means, cluster centres actual observations (medoids) rather coordinate means. single change makes PAM robust outliers (single extreme value drag medoid away cluster bulk), enables clustering arbitrary dissimilarity matrices including derived non-Euclidean distances mixed numeric-categorical data, produces interpretable centre observations can quoted clinical biological contexts (“typical patient cluster 2 patient 47”).

heading: 9.257 Prerequisites
text: working understanding \(k\)-means, distance dissimilarity matrices, difference mean-based median-like cluster centres.

heading: 9.258 Theory
text: PAM alternates two steps:Build / swap: cluster, choose medoid minimises total dissimilarity cluster members.Assign: assign observation cluster nearest medoid.objective sum dissimilarities assigned medoid, summed observations. algorithm converges local minimum; multiple random initialisations help find better local optimum. PAM consumes dissimilarity matrix, works Gower distance mixed numeric-categorical data, Manhattan distance outlier-robust clustering, correlation-based distances gene-expression patterns, custom dissimilarity analyst can define.

heading: 9.259 Assumptions
text: meaningful dissimilarity (distance) input space. Approximately equal cluster sizes preferable; PAM handle imbalanced clusters gracefully model-based methods (Gaussian mixtures).

heading: 9.260 R Implementation
code:
library(cluster)

# Mixed-type data with Gower distance
d_iris <- iris
set.seed(2026)
d_iris$Category <- factor(sample(c("A", "B", "C"), 150, replace = TRUE))

g <- daisy(d_iris, metric = "gower")
pam_fit <- pam(g, k = 3)

pam_fit$medoids
table(pam_fit$clustering, iris$Species)

# Silhouette
summary(silhouette(pam_fit))

heading: 9.261 Output & Results
text: pam() returns medoid identifiers (actual case indices), cluster assignments, within-cluster summaries. silhouette() returns per-observation silhouette widths average — primary internal validation metric.

heading: 9.262 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Partitioning around medoids Gower-distance dissimilarities produced three clusters closely match iris species (Cluster 1: setosa; Cluster 2: 92 % versicolor; Cluster 3: 86 % virginica) average silhouette width 0.45 (range \(-0.10\) \(0.78\)).” Always pair PAM silhouette analysis verify chosen \(k\) produces well-separated clusters.

heading: 9.263 Practical Tips
text: Choose PAM \(k\)-means outliers likely data mixed-type genuinely non-Euclidean; medoid robust mean .cluster::pam() accepts either data matrix (computing Euclidean distance internally) pre-computed dist object via diss = TRUE argument.large datasets (\(n > 1{,}000\)), cluster::clara() runs PAM sub-samples combines results, scaling tens thousands observations.Use fpc::pamk() automatically select \(k\) via silhouette method; returns optimal \(k\) along fit.Gower distances mixed numeric categorical data, cluster::daisy(metric = "gower") standard input; categorical variables become indicator distances, numeric ones become Manhattan range normalisation.large \(n\), alternatives CLARANS, BIRCH, mini-batch \(k\)-means scale better; price reduced robustness interpretability.

heading: 9.264 R Packages Used
text: cluster pam(), clara(), daisy(), silhouette(); fpc pamk() automatic \(k\) selection; factoextra fviz_cluster() ggplot-style cluster visualisation; klaR::kmodes() analogous categorical-\(k\)-modes algorithm.

heading: 9.267 Introduction
text: PLS discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) extends partial least squares regression classification dummy-coding class variable binary indicator matrix. become standard tool metabolomics, transcriptomics, omics disciplines handles \(p \gg n\) regime gracefully , sparse variant, performs simultaneous classification feature selection. output — small set latent components plus discriminant boundary projected space — interpretable way black-box classifiers (random forests, deep nets) often .

heading: 9.268 Prerequisites
text: working understanding PLS regression, classification metrics, high-dimensional (\(p \gg n\)) regime motivates latent-component methods.

heading: 9.269 Theory
text: Encode class variable one-hot indicators \(Y \\{0, 1\}^{n \times K}\) \(K\) classes. PLS regression \((X, Y)\) produces latent components maximise covariance indicator matrix; predicted indicators continuous, new sample assigned class largest prediction. number components selected cross-validation (mixOmics::perf()).Sparse PLS-DA (sPLS-DA) imposes L1 penalty per component, retaining keepX predictors per component. produces signature — small list variables driving classification — can reported alongside classifier .

heading: 9.270 Assumptions
text: Linear relationships latent components outcome probabilities, sample sizes large enough per class estimate components stably (rule thumb: least 5–10 observations per class), meaningful covariance predictors encoded indicators.

heading: 9.271 R Implementation
code:
library(mixOmics)

data(srbct)
X <- srbct$gene
Y <- srbct$class

# PLS-DA
fit_plsda <- plsda(X, Y, ncomp = 3)
plotIndiv(fit_plsda, ind.names = FALSE, legend = TRUE)

# Sparse PLS-DA: select 50 variables per component
fit_splsda <- splsda(X, Y, ncomp = 3, keepX = c(50, 50, 50))
selectVar(fit_splsda, comp = 1)$name |> head()

heading: 9.272 Output & Results
text: plsda() returns latent component scores per sample, loadings predictor, predicted class labels. splsda() adds variable-selection-per-component, selectVar() extracting chosen predictors. plotIndiv() displays samples latent component space, coloured class.

heading: 9.273 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Sparse PLS-DA 2,308-gene SRBCT dataset selected 50 genes per component across three components, achieving 100 % balanced accuracy 10-fold cross-validation; resulting 150-gene signature substantially overlaps published transcriptomic markers four cancer subtypes.” Always report classification performance (accuracy, balanced accuracy, AUC) selected feature signature.

heading: 9.274 Practical Tips
text: Use mixOmics::perf() cross-validation select ncomp keepX; fixed choice without tuning -fits high-dimensional omics.two-class problems, logistic regression elastic net regularisation often performs comparably familiar; reserve sPLS-DA multi-class covariance structure strongly multivariate.Sparse PLS-DA can produce unstable variable selections presence highly correlated predictors; report selection stability via bootstrap resampling (mixOmics::perf() nrepeat = 50).selected signature headline result biological audiences; pair pathway-enrichment analysis interpret functionally.Compare alternative classifiers (random forest, elastic-net logistic, sPLS-DA) identical CV folds; modern omics papers typically report multiple methods.imbalanced classes, use near.zero.var = TRUE balanced cross-validation prevent majority class dominating component construction.

heading: 9.275 R Packages Used
text: mixOmics plsda(), splsda(), perf(), selectVar(), broader multi-block extensions; caret unified cross-validated benchmarking alternative classifiers; ropls alternative PLS-DA implementation popular metabolomics; glmnet elastic-net logistic regression complementary high-dimensional classifier.

heading: 9.278 Introduction
text: Partial least squares (PLS) regression latent-component method designed regime predictors numerous, correlated, possibly outnumber observations — exactly situation ordinary least squares fails. extracts components predictor matrix simultaneously high-variance strongly correlated outcome, uses components regression basis. Chemometrics metabolomics use PLS default regression workhorse, bioinformatics competes elastic net principal-components regression high-dimensional prediction tasks.

heading: 9.279 Prerequisites
text: working understanding principal-components regression, multiple linear regression, cross-validation, trade-variance explanation prediction.

heading: 9.280 Theory
text: PCA finds orthogonal directions \(X\) maximise variance \(X\). PLS finds directions maximise covariance \(Y\) — latent components simultaneously high-variance \(X\) predictive \(Y\). \(\) components, model \[\hat Y = T_a Q_a^\top, \qquad T_a = X W_a,\]\(W_a\) PLS weights \(T_a\) resulting latent scores. number components chosen cross-validation, typically minimising root-mean-square error prediction (RMSEP). PLS regression coefficients original predictor space recovered \(\hat\beta_{\text{PLS}} = W_a (P_a^\top W_a)^{-1} Q_a^\top\).

heading: 9.281 Assumptions
text: Linear relationships latent components outcome, independent observations, (inference) homoscedastic Normal residuals. PLS fairly assumption-light prediction; cross-validated RMSEP primary quality criterion.

heading: 9.282 R Implementation
code:
library(pls)

data(mtcars)
fit <- plsr(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars, validation = "CV", ncomp = 5)
summary(fit)
validationplot(fit, val.type = "RMSEP")

# Use the best number of components
n_comp <- 2
pred <- predict(fit, ncomp = n_comp)

heading: 9.283 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) reports cumulative explained variance \(X\) \(Y\) component count, plus cross-validated RMSEP. validationplot() shows RMSEP curve component count, clear minimum optimal number components.

heading: 9.284 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “PLS regression two latent components yielded cross-validated RMSEP 2.3 mpg 32-row mtcars dataset; two components captured 85 % variance predictors 92 % variance outcome, additional components gave diminishing returns.” Report \(X\)- \(Y\)-variance explained; PLS optimises latter, readers familiar PCA may expect former.

text: Always use cross-validation choose number components; -sample fit monotonically better components selects many.classification, PLS-DA (mixOmics::plsda()) dummy-codes outcome applies PLS regression; remains popular metabolomics.Sparse PLS (mixOmics::spls()) adds variable selection component, useful interpretability individual predictors matters.truly wide data (\(p \gg n\)), PLS sparse PLS core tools alongside ridge, lasso, elastic net; cross-validate four benchmarking.Interpret latent components cautiously; linear combinations (many) predictors rarely direct biological interpretations.non-linear extensions, kernel PLS PLS spline-transformed predictors generalise non-linear response surfaces.

heading: 9.289 Introduction
text: Principal component analysis (PCA) takes data matrix many correlated variables re-expresses smaller number uncorrelated components capture variance. used exploratory visualisation, compression, preprocessing step regression classification, quality-control tool high-dimensional experiments RNA-seq. model data; change basis.

heading: 9.290 Prerequisites
text: reader familiar vectors, variance, covariance, correlation. understanding eigenvalues eigenvectors helps essential.

heading: 9.291 Theory
text: Given data matrix \(\mathbf{X}\) \(n\) rows (observations) \(p\) columns (variables), PCA finds orthogonal directions \(p\)-dimensional space variables successively maximise projected variance. first principal component (PC1) direction greatest variance; PC2 direction greatest variance orthogonal PC1; \(\min(n-1, p)\).Algebraically, \(\mathbf{X}\) centred (column mean zero), PCA eigendecomposition covariance matrix \(\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}\):\[\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V} \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{V}^\top,\]columns \(\mathbf{V}\) principal-component directions (loadings), \(\mathbf{\Lambda}\) diagonal matrix variances along direction (eigenvalues). scores – coordinates observations new basis – \(\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{V}\).practical issue: variables different scales, variance-maximisation criterion dominated whichever variable happens biggest numeric range. heterogeneous data, PCA performed correlation matrix instead – equivalent standardising variable unit variance decomposition.

heading: 9.292 Assumptions
text: PCA formal probabilistic assumptions, interpretation rests :relationships interest linear variables.Variance reasonable proxy “importance”.first components contain useful signal.first violated relationships non-linear; alternative methods (kernel PCA, UMAP) may needed. second violated small-variance components encode scientific signal (example, subtle gene regulators bulk RNA-seq study).

heading: 9.293 R Implementation
text: PCA() FactoMineR handles centring scaling default. fviz_eig() draws scree plot; fviz_pca_biplot() draws biplot observations coloured species variable-loading arrows overlaid. summary() produces textual summary including variance explained, variable loadings, contribution percentages.
code:
library(FactoMineR)
library(factoextra)

data(iris)
X <- iris[, 1:4]

pca <- PCA(X, scale.unit = TRUE, graph = FALSE)

fviz_eig(pca, addlabels = TRUE)
fviz_pca_biplot(pca,
                habillage = iris$Species,
                palette = "Set2",
                addEllipses = TRUE,
                label = "var")

summary(pca)

heading: 9.294 Output & Results
text: iris data, first component captures approximately 73% variance, second another 23%; together first two components retain 96% information. biplot shows three species separate cleanly along PC1, heavily loaded petal length, petal width, sepal length.

heading: 9.295 Interpretation
text: manuscript, PCA usually reported two pieces: proportion variance explained first \(k\) components, biological physical interpretation component’s loadings. “PC1 (73% variance) size component, loading positively length measurements; PC2 (23%) contrasts sepal width measurements separates . setosa two species.”

heading: 9.296 Practical Tips
text: Always standardise (scale.unit = TRUE) unless variables share units reason preserve absolute-scale differences.Inspect scree plot keep components elbow, enough 80-90% cumulative variance, interpretable pattern emerges. universal rule.sign component arbitrary (eigenvectors unique sign), “PC1 goes ” “PC1 goes ” equivalent representations; -interpret direction.high-dimensional sparse data (e.g., scRNA-seq), use randomised SVD (irlba, rsvd) PCA implementations Seurat scran rather dense eigendecomposition.PCA sensitive outliers; robust variants (ROBPCA, projection pursuit PCA) available rrcov package.

heading: 9.299 Introduction
text: Quadratic discriminant analysis (QDA) extends linear discriminant analysis (LDA) allowing class covariance matrix instead pooling across classes. relaxation transforms decision boundary hyperplane (LDA) conic section (ellipse, parabola, hyperbola) gives classifier flexibility handle classes whose spread differs shape orientation. cost parameter count: \(K\) separate covariance matrices instead one, means training data required estimate precisely.

heading: 9.300 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear discriminant analysis, multivariate Normal distributions, role class covariance matrices shaping decision boundaries.

heading: 9.301 Theory
text: QDA discriminant class \(k\) \[\delta_k(x) = -\tfrac{1}{2}(x - \mu_k)^\top \Sigma_k^{-1} (x - \mu_k) - \tfrac{1}{2}\log|\Sigma_k| + \log \pi_k,\]\(\mu_k, \Sigma_k, \pi_k\) class mean, class covariance, class prior probability respectively. test observation assigned class largest \(\delta_k(x)\). quadratic term \(x\) produces non-linear decision boundaries; pooling covariances across classes (LDA ) eliminates quadratic term recovers linearity.

heading: 9.302 Assumptions
text: Multivariate Normal within class, possibly different covariance matrices across classes; independent observations; sufficient observations per class estimate \(\Sigma_k\) stably (rule thumb: least 5–10 observations per dimension per class).

heading: 9.303 R Implementation
code:
library(MASS)

set.seed(2026)
train_idx <- sample(nrow(iris), 100)
train <- iris[train_idx, ]; test <- iris[-train_idx, ]

fit_qda <- qda(Species ~ ., data = train)
pred <- predict(fit_qda, test)
table(pred$class, test$Species)

heading: 9.304 Output & Results
text: qda() returns class priors, class means, class-specific group counts. predict() provides class labels posterior probabilities. confusion matrix (table(pred$class, test$Species)) summarises classification accuracy held-fold.

heading: 9.305 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “QDA classified iris test set 94 % accuracy (47/50 correct); LDA reached 98 % split, suggesting equal-covariance assumption reasonable additional flexibility QDA warranted.” Compare QDA LDA whenever class covariances obviously different; simpler model often wins.

heading: 9.306 Practical Tips
text: Prefer QDA within-class covariance structures clearly differ scale orientation; pooled covariance good approximation, LDA generalises better.QDA requires training data LDA per-class covariance estimation; high dimensions small classes, LDA regularised discriminant analysis (RDA) reliable.RDA (klaR::rda) interpolates LDA QDA via shrinkage parameters; cross-validate shrinkage find data’s preferred point spectrum.QDA decision boundaries conic — ellipses, parabolas, hyperbolas; visualise 2D projection (klaR::partimat) see curvature.high-dimensional non-Gaussian data, compare flexible classifiers (random forests, SVMs RBF kernels); QDA’s quadratic boundary may match true structure.Diagnose equal-covariance assumption Box’s M test (biotools::boxM); significant test motivates QDA LDA, test sensitive non-Normality.

heading: 9.307 R Packages Used
text: MASS qda() lda(); klaR rda() (regularised discriminant analysis) partimat() partition diagnostics; caret cross-validated comparison across discriminant variants; biotools::boxM() Box’s M test homogeneity covariance.

heading: 9.310 Introduction
text: Structural equation modelling (SEM) unified framework integrates measurement (observed items reflect underlying latent constructs) structure (latent constructs relate ) single estimation problem. generalises confirmatory factor analysis (measurement ), path analysis (regressions among observed variables ), multivariate regression. Modern psychometrics, organisational research, increasingly clinical-outcomes work depend SEM primary analytical tool isolates relationships among true constructs distorted measurement error.

heading: 9.311 Prerequisites
text: working understanding confirmatory factor analysis, multiple regression, latent-variable thinking, basic familiarity lavaan syntax.

heading: 9.312 Theory
text: SEM estimates measurement structural parameters jointly maximum likelihood (robust ML, weighted least squares ordinal indicators). Components lavaan syntax:Measurement model (=~): latent factor defined observed indicators.Structural regressions (~): latent--latent latent--observed regressions.Variances / covariances (~~): residual correlations factor covariances.Defined parameters (:=): derived quantities indirect effects.Standard fit indices (CFI, TLI, RMSEA, SRMR) parallel CFA. Indirect effects confidence intervals computed level MCMC bootstrap draws.

heading: 9.313 Assumptions
text: Correct specification measurement structural components, multivariate Normal continuous indicators (robust estimators non-Normal data), sufficient sample size — least 200 observations rough rule, complex models.

heading: 9.314 R Implementation
code:
library(lavaan); library(semPlot)

model <- '
  # Measurement
  ind60 =~ x1 + x2 + x3
  dem60 =~ y1 + y2 + y3 + y4
  dem65 =~ y5 + y6 + y7 + y8

  # Structural
  dem60 ~ ind60
  dem65 ~ ind60 + dem60

  # Residual correlations
  y1 ~~ y5
  y2 ~~ y4 + y6
  y3 ~~ y7
  y4 ~~ y8
  y6 ~~ y8
'
fit <- sem(model, data = PoliticalDemocracy)
summary(fit, standardized = TRUE, fit.measures = TRUE)

semPaths(fit, "std", layout = "tree2")

heading: 9.315 Output & Results
text: summary(fit, standardized = TRUE) reports unstandardised standardised coefficients, factor loadings, residual covariances, full set fit indices. semPaths() renders path diagram arrow widths proportional standardised coefficients.

heading: 9.316 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “structural equation model fit Political Democracy data well (CFI 0.99, RMSEA 0.04 [90 % CI 0.02 0.06], SRMR 0.05); industrialisation 1960 predicted 1960 1965 democracy (\(\beta = 0.45\) \(0.39\) respectively), 1960 democracy strongly predicted 1965 democracy (\(\beta = 0.84\), \(p < 0.001\)).” Always report standardised coefficients alongside fit indices.

heading: 9.317 Practical Tips
text: Check measurement model isolation (run CFA) adding structural paths; weak factors produce uninterpretable structural relationships.Use modificationIndices() cautiously; data-driven modifications blur confirmatory status analysis inflate Type error.mediation analysis, define indirect effects := request bootstrap CIs (sem(..., se = "bootstrap")); indirect effect product coefficients non-Normal sampling distribution.ordinal indicators, switch weighted least squares (estimator = "WLSMV"); ordinary ML Likert items violates Normality assumptions.complex models need large samples; \(n < 5\) per estimated parameter, results unstable simpler path model may robust.cross-cultural multi-group comparisons, fit measurement-invariance hierarchies (configural, metric, scalar) comparing latent means across groups.

heading: 9.318 R Packages Used
text: lavaan sem(), cfa(), growth(), lavInspect(); semPlot path diagrams; semTools measurement-invariance tests, reliability coefficients, modification heuristics; blavaan Bayesian SEM handles small samples complex priors.

heading: 9.321 Introduction
text: UMAP (uniform manifold approximation projection) t-SNE (t-distributed stochastic neighbour embedding) dominant non-linear dimensionality-reduction techniques visualising high-dimensional data — single-cell RNA sequencing, large image embeddings, language-model representations. produce striking 2D 3D scatter plots similar observations cluster together, now near-default visualisations many computational-biology machine-learning pipelines. also well-documented pitfalls, learning show important learning apply .

heading: 9.322 Prerequisites
text: working understanding principal components analysis, distance metrics, trade-preserving local global structure low-dimensional embedding.

heading: 9.323 Theory
text: methods optimise 2D 3D embedding pairwise similarities computed original high-dimensional space preserved well possible.t-SNE computes Gaussian-weighted neighbour probabilities high-dimensional space, t-distributed neighbour probabilities low-dimensional space, minimises KL divergence . Student-\(t\) distribution low dimensions prevents “crowding problem” (compressing many high-dimensional neighbours small low-dimensional area).UMAP constructs fuzzy topological representation high-dimensional data using local distance scaling, optimises similar fuzzy structure low dimensions via cross-entropy loss. UMAP faster, scales better large \(n\), often considered preserve global structure better t-SNE.algorithms stochastic; setting random seed required reproducible figures.

heading: 9.324 Assumptions
text: similarity-preserving 2D / 3D embedding informative inferential question. algorithms assume meaningful local structure exists data; assume Gaussianity, linearity, specific distance metric (UMAP’s metric configurable).

heading: 9.325 R Implementation
code:
library(umap); library(Rtsne)

set.seed(2026)
X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4])

# UMAP
u <- umap(X)
plot(u$layout, col = iris$Species, pch = 16,
     main = "UMAP of iris")

# t-SNE
t <- Rtsne(X, perplexity = 15, check_duplicates = FALSE)
plot(t$Y, col = iris$Species, pch = 16,
     main = "t-SNE of iris")

heading: 9.326 Output & Results
text: methods produce 2D coordinates per observation. UMAP t-SNE separate three iris species clearly; differences layout (relative cluster positions, cluster sizes) reflect algorithm-specific choices rather data-driven structure.

heading: 9.327 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “UMAP t-SNE embeddings four iris measurements separate three species, setosa well-isolated versicolor / virginica close distinguishable; UMAP preserves inter-cluster gap faithfully t-SNE, consistent better global-structure properties.” Always describe algorithm key hyperparameters figure caption; readers guess.

heading: 9.328 Practical Tips
text: Treat distances embedding relative neighbourhoods ; absolute distances cluster sizes meaningful, despite visualisation looks.t-SNE’s perplexity parameter (typically 5–50) sets effective neighbourhood scale; vary across least three values confirm structure robust.UMAP’s n_neighbors min_dist control trade-offs; defaults work data tuned small large \(n\).high-dimensional input, run PCA first reduce noise top 50 components, UMAP/t-SNE PCA scores; standard pipeline single-cell analysis.Cluster sizes embedding meaningless; interpret “small cluster” “large cluster” biologically real.Reproducibility requires fixed seed fixed software versions; UMAP results particular can shift releases.

library(tidyverse)

text:

heading: 9.337 2. Approach

code:
rep(mu, each = n)
X <- rbind(
  make_blob(60, c(0, 0, 0, 0, 0)),
  make_blob(40, c(3, 0, 0, 0, 0)),
  make_blob(30, c(0, 3, 0, 0, 0))
)

  ggplot(aes(PC1, PC2, colour = group)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.7)

})

  pivot_longer(-k) |>
  ggplot(aes(k, value)) + geom_line() + geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ name, scales = "free_y")

  tibble(method = "UMAP", x = emb_umap[, 1], y = emb_umap[, 2], lbl = lbl),

  ggplot(aes(x, y, colour = lbl)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ k, scales = "free")

heading: 10.4 Prerequisites
text: working understanding autoregressive (AR) moving-average (MA) processes, difference correlation partial correlation, stationarity precondition meaningful ACF interpretation.

heading: 10.5 Theory
text: ACF \(\rho_k\) measures correlation \(y_t\) \(y_{t-k}\). PACF \(\phi_{kk}\) measures correlation \(y_t\) \(y_{t-k}\) removing effects shorter lags \(1, 2, \dots, k-1\). Box-Jenkins identification rules:dashed lines R’s ACF plot show approximate 95 % confidence bands (\(\pm 1.96/\sqrt n\)) white-noise null; values outside bands statistically significant 5 % level.seasonal data, expect spikes multiples seasonal period; multiplicative seasonal patterns produce decaying spikes \(s, 2s, 3s, \dots\) ACF.

heading: 10.6 Assumptions
text: series stationary; , difference first becomes stationary, read ACF / PACF differenced series.

heading: 10.7 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

set.seed(2026)
ar_series <- arima.sim(list(ar = 0.7), n = 200)
ma_series <- arima.sim(list(ma = -0.5), n = 200)

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
acf(ar_series, main = "ACF of AR(1)")
pacf(ar_series, main = "PACF of AR(1)")
acf(ma_series, main = "ACF of MA(1)")
pacf(ma_series, main = "PACF of MA(1)")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

ggtsdisplay(ar_series)

heading: 10.8 Output & Results
text: four-panel display shows characteristic Box-Jenkins patterns: AR(1) ACF tails geometrically PACF cuts lag 1; MA(1) ACF cuts lag 1 PACF tails . ggtsdisplay() integrates time-series plot ACF PACF single ggplot-style view.

heading: 10.9 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “ACF simulated AR(1) tailed geometrically (lag-1 correlation 0.69, decaying smoothly); PACF cut sharply lag 1 (0.69 lag 1, well within bands lags 2 onward), consistent AR(1) data-generating process. MA(1) showed reverse pattern, confirming Box-Jenkins identification rules.” Identify model class patterns fitting model.

heading: 10.10 Practical Tips
text: Always difference series stationary reading ACF / PACF levels; non-stationary series produce decaying ACFs mimic AR.Use forecast::ggtsdisplay() combined time-series + ACF + PACF view; faster three separate plots.Significance bands assume white-noise null; model fitting, residual ACF key diagnostic approximately within bands.Seasonal patterns appear lags equal seasonal period; spikes \(s, 2s, 3s\) ACF indicate seasonal AR/MA structure.Complex ACF / PACF patterns (mixed ARMA, seasonal-plus-non-seasonal) difficult identify eye; reach auto.arima verify chosen orders ACF.long series, even tiny correlations statistically significant; focus magnitude pattern, lags fall just outside bands.

heading: 10.14 Introduction
text: augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test canonical test unit roots time series. unit root corresponds random-walk-like non-stationarity shocks permanent effects level series; standard regression ARIMA(p, 0, q) models give nonsense results applied data. ADF test asks whether unit root present , rejected, supports modelling series stationary (trend-stationary). Pairing ADF KPSS gives complementary information: two tests flipped null hypotheses, agreement strengthens verdict.

heading: 10.15 Prerequisites
text: working understanding stationarity, autoregressive models, difference mean-reverting random-walk processes, role differencing inducing stationarity.

heading: 10.16 Theory
text: ADF test fits regression\[\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma y_{t-1} + \sum_{=1}^p \delta_i \Delta y_{t-} + \varepsilon_t,\]tests \(\gamma = 0\) (unit root) \(\gamma < 0\) (stationary). “augmented” \(p\) lagged differences absorb autocorrelation residuals validate test general AR processes original Dickey-Fuller. unit-root null, test statistic follow standard normal distribution; tabulated Dickey-Fuller critical values used.Three deterministic specifications common: intercept (rare), intercept (“drift”), intercept plus linear trend. choice reflect visible behaviour series.

heading: 10.17 Assumptions
text: linear autoregressive structure; structural breaks (invalidate test); lag order \(p\) large enough whiten residuals.

heading: 10.18 R Implementation
code:
library(tseries); library(urca)

x <- cumsum(rnorm(100))     # random walk = unit root

adf.test(x)                # default lag order
adf.test(diff(x))          # stationary after differencing

# urca version with formal output
summary(ur.df(x, type = "drift", selectlags = "AIC"))

heading: 10.19 Output & Results
text: adf.test() returns ADF statistic \(p\)-value computed Dickey-Fuller distribution. urca::ur.df() provides richer output critical values multiple significance levels automatic lag selection AIC BIC.

heading: 10.20 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “ADF test random-walk series gave statistic \(-1.50\) (\(p = 0.52\), fail reject unit root); first-differencing, ADF gave \(-9.83\) (\(p < 0.01\), reject unit root favour stationarity), confirming series integrated order 1.” Always state deterministic specification (drift, trend, none) lag order chosen.

heading: 10.21 Practical Tips
text: Include intercept (type = "drift") series fluctuate around non-zero mean; add trend (type = "trend") series visible linear trend.Use selectlags = "AIC" "BIC" urca::ur.df() automatic lag selection; default tseries::adf.test() trunc(...) sometimes small.Structural breaks invalidate ADF; break plausible, use Zivot-Andrews (urca::ur.za()) extends test allow one endogenous break.Pair KPSS complementary perspective: agreement (ADF rejects, KPSS ) confirms stationarity; reverse confirms unit root.Results sensitive specification; try several deterministic options lag orders, report robust summary conclusions hold across .multi-series unit-root testing panel data, use Im-Pesaran-Shin (plm::purtest) Levin-Lin-Chu.

heading: 10.22 R Packages Used
text: tseries::adf.test() canonical ADF; urca::ur.df() richer output lag-selection tools; urca::ur.za() Zivot-Andrews test structural breaks; forecast::ndiffs() integrated workflow determines required differencing order KPSS / ADF / Phillips-Perron.

heading: 10.25 Introduction
text: ARIMA models classical workhorse time-series forecasting. combine three ideas: autoregression (current value depends lagged values), differencing (current value depends changes rather levels), moving averages errors. ARIMA model specified three integers \((p, d, q)\): order autoregression, order differencing, order moving averages. seasonal extension (SARIMA), model captures weekly, monthly, annual cycles.

heading: 10.26 Prerequisites
text: reader understand concepts stationary time series, autocorrelation, white noise, seen ACF/PACF plot .

heading: 10.27 Theory
text: ARMA\((p, q)\) model stationary series \(Y_t\) \[Y_t = c + \phi_1 Y_{t-1} + \ldots + \phi_p Y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \ldots + \theta_q \varepsilon_{t-q},\]\(\varepsilon_t\) white noise. series non-stationary, differencing applied: \(\Delta Y_t = Y_t - Y_{t-1}\), generally \(\Delta^d Y_t\). ARIMA\((p, d, q)\) model ARMA\((p, q)\) model fit \(d\)-th-order differenced series.seasonal data period \(s\), SARIMA\((p, d, q)(P, D, Q)_s\) adds seasonal autoregressive, differencing, moving-average terms lag \(s\). monthly retail sales series might well described SARIMA\((1, 1, 1)(0, 1, 1)_{12}\).Model identification traditionally follows Box-Jenkins methodology:Check stationarity; difference stationary.Inspect ACF PACF differenced series; decay patterns suggest \(p\) \(q\).Fit candidate models choose AIC/BIC.Diagnose residuals: white noise.practice, auto.arima() automates steps 1-3 reliably finds good model.

heading: 10.28 Assumptions
text: ARIMA models assume:differenced series stationary (constant mean, constant variance, autocorrelation depending lag).Errors uncorrelated constant variance (homoscedastic).Errors approximately normally distributed (prediction-interval validity).Violations normality affect interval coverage point forecasts; violations stationarity invalidate whole model.

heading: 10.29 R Implementation
text: forecast package provides classical interface; newer fable package integrates tidyverse.auto.arima() searches range \((p, d, q)(P, D, Q)_{12}\) specifications returns best corrected AIC. checkresiduals() plots residual time series, ACF, histogram, runs Ljung-Box test remaining autocorrelation.
code:
library(forecast)

data(AirPassengers)
plot(AirPassengers)

fit <- auto.arima(AirPassengers)
summary(fit)

checkresiduals(fit)

fc <- forecast(fit, h = 24)
autoplot(fc) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Air passengers (thousands)",
       title = "24-month ahead forecast with 80/95% PIs")

heading: 10.30 Output & Results
text: AirPassengers series, auto.arima() selects ARIMA\((0, 1, 1)(0, 1, 1)_{12}\) model log scale (automatic Box-Cox transformation). Ljung-Box test residuals gives \(p \approx 0.5\), indicating remaining autocorrelation. forecast plot shows point predictions 80% 95% prediction intervals fan 24-month horizon.

heading: 10.31 Interpretation
text: model says seasonal regular differencing log scale, series well described simple MA(1) structure regular seasonal dimensions. growing width prediction intervals reflects accumulating uncertainty – feature, bug, honest forecast.

heading: 10.32 Practical Tips
text: Always inspect series visually differencing; automated tools can miss obvious issues like level shifts outlier clusters.Use information criteria (AICc preferred small samples) model comparison, p-values.Validate forecasts held-tail series (train/test split time), -sample fit.series shows clear seasonality, try SARIMA ETS (ets()) let cross-validated accuracy choose.long series complex seasonality, consider prophet, fable::fable, structural time-series models instead ARIMA.

heading: 10.35 Introduction
text: Bayesian changepoint detection returns posterior probability changepoint time, rather hard list detected change times. richer output quantifies uncertainty existence location changes, making preferable classical methods (PELT, binary segmentation) analyst wants communicate “signal probably shifted near time 200, uncertainty exact time” rather committing arbitrary threshold. Bayesian approaches also support online detection data stream .

heading: 10.36 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Bayesian inference, classical changepoint detection, difference offline (batch) online (streaming) analysis.

heading: 10.37 Theory
text: Offline Bayesian changepoint detection (Barry-Hartigan 1993, Fearnhead 2006) places prior number locations changepoints plus segment parameters samples joint posterior via Gibbs sampling, particle filters, recursive forward-backward algorithms. output marginal posterior probability changepoint time posterior summaries segment parameters.Online Bayesian changepoint detection (BOCD) (Adams & MacKay 2007) computes run-length distribution — posterior probability time since last changepoint equals \(r\) — recursively new data point arrives. run-length distribution updates \(O(t)\) per step, making method viable streaming applications.

heading: 10.38 Assumptions
text: Segment-parameter priors chosen appropriately data; conditional independence segments given changepoint structure; chosen segment model (Normal mean, regression coefficients) matches underlying signal.

heading: 10.39 R Implementation
code:
library(bcp)

set.seed(2026)
x <- c(rnorm(100, 0, 1), rnorm(100, 3, 1), rnorm(100, -1, 1))

fit <- bcp(x)
plot(fit)
fit$posterior.prob   # per-time changepoint probability

heading: 10.40 Output & Results
text: bcp() returns posterior changepoint probability time posterior mean segmented signal. plot displays — smooth signal estimate posterior probability — making easy identify clearly supported changepoints (probability spikes near 1) versus uncertain candidates.

heading: 10.41 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Bayesian changepoint detection returned posterior changepoint probabilities exceeding 0.95 times 100 200, recovering simulated three-segment structure; second changepoint slightly tighter posterior distribution (probability mass concentrated within \(\pm 1\) time step) first, reflecting larger -segment mean difference point.” Communicate posterior probabilities, hard threshold-based detections.

heading: 10.42 Practical Tips
text: Tune hyperparameter w0 (prior -changepoint variance) stability; larger values favour fewer, sharper changepoints.Online variants (ocp package) implement BOCD real-time detection; useful monitoring applications decisions must made data arrive.Visualise posterior probability time series rather extracting hard changepoints thresholding; thresholding throws away uncertainty information motivates Bayesian approach.Small samples give diffuse posteriors; tight cases, posterior probability true changepoint may peak 0.5 0.7. data sharpens posterior.multivariate changepoint detection, MultiBNPRiver mbcp extend framework vector-valued series, substantially higher computational cost.Compare classical methods (PELT) data; Bayesian methods richer output classical methods typically much faster.
