heading: 10.46 Introduction
text: Changepoint detection identifies times statistical properties time series — mean, variance, regression coefficients, full distribution — change abruptly. Applications range monitoring physiological signals (heart-rate EEG state changes), detecting regime shifts financial economic series, segmenting long sensor recordings homogeneous epochs. output list changepoint times together within-segment summaries ; methodological choices concern change type, detection algorithm, penalty controlling sensitivity.

heading: 10.47 Prerequisites
text: working understanding hypothesis testing, segmentation, trade-detecting true changepoints false positives.

heading: 10.48 Theory
text: Three algorithm classes dominate:Binary segmentation: recursively detect single changepoint, partition series, apply procedure segment. Fast greedy; can miss configurations multiple nearby changepoints.PELT (Pruned Exact Linear Time): finds optimal segmentation penalty \(O(n)\) penalty satisfies regularity condition. standard modern method.Segment Neighbourhood: dynamic programming optimal partition fixed maximum number changepoints.penalty (BIC, MBIC, Hannan-Quinn, AIC) controls many changepoints reported; larger penalty yields fewer changepoints. Different change types different test statistics: mean changes use likelihood-ratio test Normal model; variance changes use \(\sigma\) parameter; meanvar combines ; regression changepoints generalise coefficient breaks.

heading: 10.49 Assumptions
text: Independent observations within segments (correctly specified within-segment dependence structure); change type matches underlying signal (mean shifts vs variance shifts vs regression breaks).

heading: 10.50 R Implementation
code:
library(changepoint)

set.seed(2026)
x <- c(rnorm(100, 0, 1), rnorm(100, 3, 1), rnorm(100, -1, 1))

# Mean changes via PELT
cp <- cpt.mean(x, method = "PELT", penalty = "BIC")
cpts(cp)
plot(cp)

# Variance changes
cp_v <- cpt.var(x, method = "PELT")
cpts(cp_v)

heading: 10.51 Output & Results
text: cpt.mean() returns cpt object changepoint times accessible via cpts(), within-segment means via param.est(), plot showing detected segments. simulated three-segment structure recovered changepoints times 100 200.

heading: 10.52 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “PELT BIC penalty detected two changepoints times 100 200, recovering simulated three-segment mean structure (segment means \(0.04\), \(2.95\), \(-0.97\)); sensitivity analysis MBIC Hannan-Quinn penalties yielded changepoints.” Always report algorithm, penalty, sensitivity check across penalties.

heading: 10.53 Practical Tips
text: Choose right change type: cpt.mean mean shifts, cpt.var variance shifts, cpt.meanvar , regression-based methods coefficient breaks.penalty controls false positives; try several (BIC, MBIC, Hannan-Quinn) check conclusions robust.non-stationary noise within segments, model noise (ARIMA) first apply changepoint detection residuals.Bayesian alternatives (bcp::bcp) give per-time changepoint posterior probabilities, informative hard list detected changepoints.PELT offline — requires full series. online (real-time) detection, use CUSUM, Page-Hinkley, Bayesian online methods.multivariate changepoint detection, ecp InspectChangepoint extend PELT-style methods vector time series.

heading: 10.57 Introduction
text: Diebold-Mariano test compares forecasting accuracy two competing models held-test window. naive accuracy comparisons (one model lower MSE another) ignore statistical noise difference, DM test treats loss-difference series stochastic process asks whether mean significantly non-zero. test works loss function accommodates autocorrelation multi-step-ahead forecast errors typically exhibit, making standard inferential tool forecast comparisons.

heading: 10.58 Prerequisites
text: working understanding forecast accuracy metrics, difference -sample --sample evaluation, basic familiarity autocorrelation residual series.

heading: 10.59 Theory
text: two forecasts series errors \(e_{1,t}\) \(e_{2,t}\) test window length \(T\), define \(d_t = L(e_{1,t}) - L(e_{2,t})\) \(L\) chosen loss (squared error, absolute error, log-squared error). null \(\mathbb E[d_t] = 0\) — equal expected forecast accuracy. test statistic standardised mean \(d_t\) long-run-variance estimator accounts natural autocorrelation loss-difference series.Harvey-Leybourne-Newbold (HLN) small-sample correction adjusts asymptotic Normal reference finite \(T\) forecast horizon \(h\), reducing -rejection original DM test small samples.

heading: 10.60 Assumptions
text: loss-difference series covariance-stationary; forecast errors observable overlapping sequential test sets; chosen loss appropriate forecasting question.

heading: 10.61 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

train <- window(AirPassengers, end = c(1957, 12))
test  <- window(AirPassengers, start = c(1958, 1))

e1 <- test - forecast(auto.arima(train), h = length(test))$mean
e2 <- test - forecast(ets(train), h = length(test))$mean

dm.test(e1, e2, alternative = "two.sided", h = 1, power = 2)

heading: 10.62 Output & Results
text: dm.test() returns DM statistic, \(p\)-value, chosen lag autocovariance estimation. HLN correction applied default forecast::dm.test().

heading: 10.63 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “held-test window three years (1958–1960), Diebold-Mariano test comparing ARIMA ETS forecasts one-step-ahead horizon gave \(\mathrm{DM} = -1.42\) HLN small-sample correction (\(p = 0.16\)); significant difference forecast accuracy squared-error loss.” Always state test window, forecast horizon, loss function.

heading: 10.64 Practical Tips
text: Use power = 2 squared-error loss (default), power = 1 absolute error; choose based substantive forecasting cost function.multi-step-ahead horizons, set h forecast horizon; long-run variance uses \(h\) determine truncation lag autocovariance estimation.Report test window length, forecast horizon, chosen loss; results depend three.model selection across many candidate models, DM test pairwise; model-confidence-set approach (MCS::mcsTest) extends many models multiple-comparison correction.DM test compares forecast methods, models per se; methods produce point forecast (different prediction-interval widths) may DM-equivalent despite different underlying assumptions.nested models, conventional DM test -covers; use Clark-West West tests instead.

heading: 10.65 R Packages Used
text: forecast::dm.test() canonical implementation HLN correction; MCS model-confidence-set extension multi-model comparisons; multDM multivariate DM tests; multMCS MCS extensions multivariate forecasts.

heading: 10.68 Introduction
text: Differencing simplest tool making non-stationary series stationary: replace observation difference previous one. First differences remove linear trends; seasonal differences remove fixed-period seasonality; combined regular seasonal differencing handles . order differencing \(d\) ARIMA\((p, d, q)\) exactly count first differences applied; seasonal differencing order \(D\) SARIMA\((p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s\) counts seasonal differences.

heading: 10.69 Prerequisites
text: working understanding stationarity, AR models, relationship trend, seasonality, spectrum series.

heading: 10.70 Theory
text: Standard differencing operations:First differences: \(\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}\). Removes linear (level-shift) non-stationarity.Second differences: \(\Delta^2 y_t = \Delta y_t - \Delta y_{t-1}\). Removes quadratic trend. Rarely needed practice; second differences typically -difference inflate residual variance.Seasonal differences period \(s\): \(\Delta_s y_t = y_t - y_{t-s}\). Removes constant seasonal patterns frequency \(1/s\).Combined regular seasonal: \(\Delta \Delta_s y_t = (y_t - y_{t-1}) - (y_{t-s} - y_{t-s-1})\).-differencing introduces unnecessary moving-average structure (differenced series strong negative lag-1 autocorrelation); always use minimum differencing order needed stationarity. KPSS- ADF-based automated selection (forecast::ndiffs nsdiffs) chooses required orders.

heading: 10.71 Assumptions
text: non-stationarity kind differencing addresses (stochastic trend fixed-period seasonality); deterministic polynomial trends better handled detrending regression.

heading: 10.72 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

x <- AirPassengers
plot(x)

# First difference
plot(diff(x))

# Seasonal difference (lag = 12 for monthly)
plot(diff(x, lag = 12))

# Both
plot(diff(diff(x, lag = 12)))

# Automatic: number of differences needed
ndiffs(log(x))         # regular
nsdiffs(log(x))        # seasonal

heading: 10.73 Output & Results
text: diff(x, lag = s, differences = d) produces \(\Delta^d_s y\). ndiffs() returns required regular differencing order based KPSS default; nsdiffs() returns seasonal differencing order. log-AirPassengers series, regular seasonal differencing produces stationary residual.

heading: 10.74 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Monthly air-passenger counts log scale required one regular difference one seasonal difference lag 12 achieve stationarity (KPSS \(p > 0.10\) doubly-differenced series); resulting series motivates ARIMA\((0, 1, 1)(0, 1, 1)_{12}\) model log scale, equivalent multiplicative seasonal ARIMA original scale.” Always confirm stationarity differencing.

heading: 10.75 Practical Tips
text: Log-transform first series variance grows level; log scale additive differencing well-behaved.ARIMA\((p, d, q)\) encodes differencing order \(d\); corresponding Arima() call automatically applies difference works original series prediction.forecast::ndiffs() nsdiffs() automate choice regular seasonal differencing orders; useful starting point auto.arima.-differencing inflates residual variance introduces strong negative MA(1) coefficient; see \(\theta_1\) near \(-0.95\) differenced model, consider whether differencing excessive.Always confirm stationarity differencing ADF KPSS; either test still indicates non-stationarity, examine data structural breaks non-linear trends.deterministic polynomial trends, detrending regression efficient differencing; ARIMA’s stochastic-trend interpretation differs.

heading: 10.79 Introduction
text: ETS models — Error, Trend, Seasonal — express exponential-smoothing family unified state-space form. component (error, trend, seasonal) independently classified additive, multiplicative, absent, giving small library well-understood models. state-space framework allows proper likelihood-based estimation, AIC-based model selection, principled prediction intervals derived underlying stochastic structure rather ad-hoc heuristics.

heading: 10.80 Prerequisites
text: working understanding exponential smoothing (simple, Holt’s, Holt-Winters), state-space models, additive vs multiplicative distinction time-series components.

heading: 10.81 Theory
text: notation ETS(E, T, S) classifies component:Error: (additive) M (multiplicative).Trend: N (none), (additive), Ad (damped additive), M (multiplicative), Md (damped multiplicative).Seasonal: N (none), (additive), M (multiplicative).Examples: ETS(, N, N) simple exponential smoothing; ETS(, , N) Holt’s linear trend; ETS(, , ) additive Holt-Winters; ETS(M, , M) gives multiplicative errors plus additive trend plus multiplicative seasonality. forecast::ets() enumerates valid candidates selects AIC.state-space form provides exact likelihood, AIC model selection, analytic (simulation-based) prediction intervals. Multiplicative components require strictly positive data; AIC-based selection avoids choosing invalid model.

heading: 10.82 Assumptions
text: chosen ETS specification matches data’s components; prediction intervals, innovations approximately Normal (simulated fitted residual distribution).

heading: 10.83 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

x <- AirPassengers

fit <- ets(x)
summary(fit)
autoplot(fit)

fc <- forecast(fit, h = 24)
autoplot(fc)

# Residual diagnostics
checkresiduals(fit)

heading: 10.84 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) returns chosen model code (e.g., “M, , M”), smoothing parameters \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\), AIC, BIC. forecast() produces point forecasts 80 % / 95 % prediction intervals fitted state-space form. checkresiduals() provides Ljung-Box graphical diagnostics.

heading: 10.85 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “ets() selected ETS(M, , M) airline series (\(\alpha = 0.34\), \(\beta = 0.01\), \(\gamma = 1.0\), AIC = 1{,}393); model captures upward trend multiplicative seasonality whose amplitude grows level. Standardised residuals passed Ljung-Box lag 24 (\(p = 0.61\)).” Always state chosen ETS specification smoothing parameters.

heading: 10.86 Practical Tips
text: ets() automatically selects optimal ETS variant AIC; override model = "AAA" etc. specific form required replication.Multiplicative components require strictly positive data; AIC-based selection avoids invalid choices automatically.complex multi-seasonality (e.g. weekly + yearly), TBATS (forecast::tbats()) extends ETS multiple seasonal layers Box-Cox transformation.ETS prediction intervals can wide long horizons multiplicative variance compounds; nuanced view, simulate many sample paths via forecast(fit, simulate = TRUE, npaths = 1000).Compare ETS ARIMA via rolling-origin cross-validation; strong baseline forecasters better choice depends series.damped trend (extrapolates conservatively long horizons), use model = "AAdA" let ets() discover AIC.

heading: 10.87 R Packages Used
text: forecast::ets() canonical ETS implementation AIC-based selection; fable::ETS() modern tidyverts equivalent; forecast::tbats() multiple seasonalities Box-Cox transformation; smooth::es() advanced ETS variants including ETS plus regressors.

heading: 10.90 Introduction
text: Exponential smoothing family forecasting methods produces predictions weighted averages past observations geometrically declining weights. simplest form (simple exponential smoothing, SES) handles series trend seasonality; Holt’s method adds linear trend; Holt-Winters extends seasonal series additive multiplicative seasonality. methods competitive ARIMA short horizons, conceptually simpler, natural choice series structure matches one supported variants.

heading: 10.91 Prerequisites
text: working understanding moving averages, forecasting basics, trade-adaptiveness smoothness.

heading: 10.92 Theory
text: Simple exponential smoothing recursively updates level estimate:\[\hat y_{t+1 \mid t} = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha) \hat y_{t \mid t-1},\]smoothing parameter \(\alpha \(0, 1)\). Larger \(\alpha\) adapts quickly recent values; smaller \(\alpha\) smooths aggressively.Holt’s method adds trend component smoothing parameter \(\beta\). Holt-Winters adds seasonal component parameter \(\gamma\) either additive (constant seasonal amplitude) multiplicative (seasonal amplitude grows level) form. equivalent state-space representations form ETS family — Error/Trend/Seasonal — coded ETS(, , ), ETS(M, Ad, M), , = additive, M = multiplicative, N = none, Ad = damped additive trend.

heading: 10.93 Assumptions
text: chosen ETS specification matches data’s components; smoothing parameters constant time; prediction intervals, innovations approximately Normal.

heading: 10.94 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

x <- AirPassengers

# Holt-Winters multiplicative (for multiplicative seasonality)
hw <- HoltWinters(x, seasonal = "multiplicative")
plot(hw)
fc <- forecast(hw, h = 24)
plot(fc)

# Equivalent via ets()
fit_ets <- ets(x)
summary(fit_ets)
plot(forecast(fit_ets, h = 24))

heading: 10.95 Output & Results
text: HoltWinters() returns smoothing parameters fitted level, trend, seasonal components. ets() automatically selects ETS variant AIC produces equivalent output prediction intervals state-space form.

heading: 10.96 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Holt-Winters multiplicative smoothing captured growing seasonal amplitude airline passengers (smoothing parameters \(\alpha = 0.34\), \(\beta = 0.01\), \(\gamma = 1.0\)); 24-month forecasts extend trend realistic seasonal variation, 95 % prediction intervals widen \(\pm 4 \%\) 1 month \(\pm 18 \%\) 24 months.” Always state ETS variant chosen smoothing parameter values.

heading: 10.97 Practical Tips
text: ets() automatically selects ETS family AIC; specify model argument explicitly force specific structure.multiplicative seasonality, either fit ETS(…,M) directly log-transform series fit ETS(…,); approaches give equivalent forecasts.Exponential smoothing competitive ARIMA short horizons (1–4 periods); ARIMA tends win longer horizons.Prediction intervals forecast() assume Normal innovations; check residuals skewness heavy tails.short series (\(T < 30\)), simple SES Holt often beats Holt-Winters parameter estimation unstable complex models.damped-trend ETS(…,Ad) variant sensible default trend plausibly slowing forecast horizon; pure additive trends extrapolate forever.

heading: 10.101 Introduction
text: Forecast accuracy evaluated error metrics compare predictions held-actuals. metric encodes different loss function different units, choice metric depends substantive cost structure forecasting errors. four widely reported MAE, RMSE, MAPE, MASE; understanding differences trade-offs essential fair comparison across forecasting methods across series.

heading: 10.102 Prerequisites
text: working understanding forecasting, difference -sample --sample evaluation, basic loss-function concepts.

heading: 10.103 Theory
text: Let \(e_t = y_t - \hat y_t\) forecast error test window length \(n\).Mean Absolute Error: \(\mathrm{MAE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum |e_t|\). Scale-dependent, units data; treats errors equally.Root Mean Squared Error: \(\mathrm{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum e_t^2}\). Scale-dependent, penalises large errors heavily; natural metric squared loss cost function.Mean Absolute Percentage Error: \(\mathrm{MAPE} = \frac{100}{n} \sum |e_t / y_t|\). Scale-independent percentage; undefined \(y_t = 0\) asymmetric (penalises -forecasts -forecasts absolute level).Mean Absolute Scaled Error: \(\mathrm{MASE} = \mathrm{MAE} / \bar e_{\text{naive}}\), scaled -sample MAE naive forecast. Scale-independent, symmetric, defined data (including zeros). Values 1 mean “better naive seasonal forecast sample”.probabilistic forecasts, continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) generalises MAE forecast distributions standard scoring rule prediction intervals.

heading: 10.104 Assumptions
text: comparable held-test window sufficient length give stable error estimates; chosen metric matches substantive loss function forecasting errors.

heading: 10.105 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

x <- AirPassengers
train <- window(x, end = c(1957, 12))
test  <- window(x, start = c(1958, 1))

fit  <- auto.arima(train)
fc   <- forecast(fit, h = length(test))
accuracy(fc, test)

heading: 10.106 Output & Results
text: accuracy() returns table (mean error, indicates bias), RMSE, MAE, MPE, MAPE, MASE, ACF1 (lag-1 autocorrelation residuals) training test sets. training-set numbers measure -sample fit; test-set numbers genuine --sample predictive performance.

heading: 10.107 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “held-three-year window, ARIMA forecast achieved test-set RMSE 22.8 passengers, MAE 18.5, MASE 0.42; MASE 1 confirms substantially better predictive performance naive seasonal forecast.” Always report multiple metrics — RMSE squared-error context, MAE absolute-error context, MASE scale-free comparison.

heading: 10.108 Practical Tips
text: Report multiple metrics; reward different error properties model can dominate one losing another.MAPE popular business reporting misleading series values near zero negative values; use sMAPE MASE instead.MASE recommended scale-free metric academic forecasting evaluation; symmetric, defined zeros, benchmarked meaningful baseline.Evaluate held-tail (chronological train/test split), never -sample; -sample errors systematically optimistic.comparing many forecasts across many series, aggregate metrics via rolling-origin cross-validation; single train/test split sensitive choice cutoff.probabilistic forecasts, report CRPS interval scores; point-forecast metrics ignore prediction-interval calibration.

heading: 10.109 R Packages Used

heading: 10.112 Introduction
text: GARCH (Generalised Autoregressive Conditional Heteroscedasticity) models capture time-varying conditional variance — volatility clustering pattern quiet periods alternate volatile ones. Financial returns prototypical application: stock prices show stretches low daily variability punctuated high-volatility crisis episodes, structure conditional-Normal ARMA models accommodate. GARCH extends ARMA adding autoregressive equation conditional variance , making volatility forecastable quantity.

heading: 10.113 Prerequisites
text: working understanding ARMA models, conditional distributions, distinction unconditional conditional variance.

heading: 10.114 Theory
text: GARCH(1, 1) model decomposes returns \[r_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t, \quad z_t \sim \mathcal N(0, 1),\]conditional variance evolving \[\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta \sigma_{t-1}^2.\]mean structure can combined ARMA component; GARCH specifically models variance innovations. Stationary unconditional variance requires \(\alpha + \beta < 1\); values close 1 indicate highly persistent volatility (typical daily financial returns).Extensions handle asymmetric responses positive vs negative shocks (EGARCH, TGARCH, APARCH), variance feedback mean (GARCH-M), multivariate volatility (DCC, BEKK). non-Normal returns, \(t\)- skewed-\(t\)-distributed innovations standard.

heading: 10.115 Assumptions
text: Conditional Normality (\(t\), skewed-\(t\)) innovations \(z_t\); stationarity conditional variance (\(\alpha + \beta < 1\)); chosen GARCH order appropriate data.

heading: 10.116 R Implementation
code:
library(rugarch)

# Simulate GARCH(1,1) returns
spec <- ugarchspec(variance.model = list(model = "sGARCH", garchOrder = c(1, 1)),
                   mean.model = list(armaOrder = c(0, 0), include.mean = TRUE),
                   distribution.model = "norm")

set.seed(2026)
sim <- ugarchpath(spec = ugarchspec(
  variance.model = list(garchOrder = c(1, 1)),
  mean.model = list(armaOrder = c(0, 0))),
  n.sim = 500)
r <- as.numeric(fitted(sim))

# Fit
fit <- ugarchfit(spec, data = r)
show(fit)
plot(fit, which = 3)   # conditional sd

heading: 10.117 Output & Results
text: ugarchfit() returns parameter estimates (\(\omega, \alpha, \beta\), mean parameters), log-likelihood, AIC/BIC, Ljung-Box-style tests standardised squared standardised residuals. conditional-standard-deviation plot shows time-varying volatility extracted model.

heading: 10.118 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “GARCH(1, 1) fit daily returns estimated \(\hat\omega = 0.0001\), \(\hat\alpha = 0.08\), \(\hat\beta = 0.91\) (\(\hat\alpha + \hat\beta = 0.99\), persistent volatility); standardised residuals showed remaining ARCH effects (Ljung-Box squared residuals \(p = 0.42\)). Conditional-volatility forecasts widen modestly 30-day horizon.” Always report persistence sum standardised-residual diagnostics.

heading: 10.119 Practical Tips
text: Check standardised residuals (residuals(fit, standardize = TRUE)); approximately iid often heavier-tailed Normal, motivating \(t\) skewed-\(t\) innovations.asymmetric responses positive vs negative shocks (leverage effect equity returns), use EGARCH TGARCH; standard GARCH symmetric misses .rugarch industry standard univariate GARCH; multivariate volatility (covariance matrices), rmgarch extends DCC, BEKK, copula-GARCH.Value--Risk forecasts come conditional-variance forecast plus quantile innovation distribution; ugarchforecast() ugarchroll() support rolling VaR estimation.GARCH returns conventional, framework applies series volatility clustering — physiological signals, atmospheric data, traffic counts.long series, parameter estimates can stable conditional-volatility series may need recursive re-estimation; use ugarchroll() genuine --sample evaluation.

heading: 10.120 R Packages Used
text: rugarch univariate GARCH many extensions; rmgarch multivariate GARCH (DCC, BEKK); fGarch alternative implementation; bayesGARCH Bayesian GARCH; tsgarch modern tidyverse-flavoured interface.

heading: 10.123 Introduction
text: Granger causality tests whether past values one time series improve prediction another, beyond already explained latter’s history. statistical concept, causal one philosophical sense: series Granger-causes another including lags reduces residual variance predicted series, useful limited notion. Two series can Granger-cause without direct causal link driven common unobserved factor.

heading: 10.124 Prerequisites
text: working understanding vector autoregressive (VAR) models, F-tests joint coefficient hypotheses, difference predictive causal claims.

heading: 10.125 Theory
text: Series \(X\) Granger-causes series \(Y\) lag \(p\) lagged values \(X_{t-1}, \dots, X_{t-p}\) significantly improve regression \(Y_t\) lags \(Y_{t-1}, \dots, Y_{t-p}\). test F-test joint hypothesis coefficients lagged \(X\) zero. Within VAR system \(K\) series, Granger causality one series another (others) computed similarly residuals jointly estimated.non-trivial extension “instantaneous” causality: \(X\) \(Y\) contemporaneously correlated conditioning lagged values . included implementations (e.g., vars::causality).

heading: 10.126 Assumptions
text: Stationary series — test invalid unit-root processes. unmeasured common cause drives series; otherwise Granger-causal direction uninterpretable.

heading: 10.127 R Implementation
code:
library(lmtest); library(vars)

data(ChickEgg)   # eggs / chickens

# Does egg production Granger-cause chicken population?
grangertest(chicken ~ egg, order = 3, data = ChickEgg)
grangertest(egg ~ chicken, order = 3, data = ChickEgg)

# Within a VAR
fit <- VAR(ChickEgg, p = 3)
causality(fit, cause = "egg")$Granger
causality(fit, cause = "chicken")$Granger

heading: 10.128 Output & Results
text: grangertest() returns F-statistic, degrees freedom, \(p\)-value joint hypothesis zero coefficients cause’s lags. vars::causality() extends within VAR framework, handling multivariate residual structure correctly adding instantaneous-causality test.

heading: 10.129 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Egg production Granger-caused chicken population lag 3 (\(F(3, 48) = 4.9\), \(p = 0.005\)); reverse test significant (\(p = 0.39\)). suggests egg counts contain predictive information future chicken populations beyond past chicken counts, establish direct causal mechanism.” Always frame Granger results predictive, causal.

heading: 10.130 Practical Tips
text: Granger causality requires stationarity; difference detrend first using KPSS / ADF guidance.Results sensitive lag order; try several plausible lags report chosen value justification.Granger causality imply causation; common confounder produces apparent bidirectional Granger causality two effects.two series, use VAR-based vars::causality() rather pairwise grangertest(); joint estimation handles cross-series correlations.Non-linear Granger causality exists (bootGran, NlinTS); standard tests linear miss non-linear predictive relationships.frequency-domain Granger causality, vars::fevd() Geweke decomposition tools partition predictive contribution frequency band.

heading: 10.131 R Packages Used
text: lmtest::grangertest() bivariate test; vars::VAR() causality() VAR-based multivariate Granger causality; tsDyn non-linear extensions; NlinTS non-linear information-theoretic causality measures.

heading: 10.134 Introduction
text: Holt-Winters method extends exponential smoothing handle trend seasonality via three coupled smoothing equations — level, trend, seasonal — smoothing parameter. seasonal generalisation Holt’s linear method conceptual ancestor modern ETS family. strength interpretability competitive forecasting performance relatively parameters; weakness assumption fixed seasonal period stable parameters across entire series.

heading: 10.135 Prerequisites
text: working understanding simple exponential smoothing, Holt’s linear-trend method, additive-vs-multiplicative seasonal distinction.

heading: 10.136 Theory
text:
additive Holt-Winters equations \[\begin{aligned}
L_t &= \alpha (y_t - S_{t-s}) + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}), \\
T_t &= \beta (L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) T_{t-1}, \\
S_t &= \gamma (y_t - L_t) + (1 - \gamma) S_{t-s}.
\end{aligned}\]forecast \(\hat y_{t+h} = L_t + h T_t + S_{t-s+1+(h-1) \bmod s}\). multiplicative variant replaces seasonal additions multiplications appropriate seasonal amplitude grows level.three smoothing parameters \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\) lie \([0, 1]\): small values give slow adaptation (smoother estimates), large values give fast adaptation (responsive noisier).

heading: 10.137 Assumptions
text: seasonal period \(s\) fixed known; smoothing parameters stable series; prediction intervals, residuals approximately Normal.

heading: 10.138 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

x <- AirPassengers

# Multiplicative
hw_m <- HoltWinters(x, seasonal = "multiplicative")
hw_m$alpha; hw_m$beta; hw_m$gamma

fc <- forecast(hw_m, h = 24)
plot(fc)

heading: 10.139 Output & Results
text: HoltWinters() returns three smoothing parameters, fitted level / trend / seasonal components, fitted values original series. forecast() extends multi-step-ahead predictions 80 % / 95 % intervals.

heading: 10.140 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Holt-Winters multiplicative smoothing monthly air-passenger counts estimated \(\hat\alpha = 0.20\), \(\hat\beta = 0.03\), \(\hat\gamma = 0.80\); resulting 24-month forecast continues upward trend proportionally growing seasonal amplitude. slow \(\beta\) indicates stable trend slope, fast \(\gamma\) allows seasonal shape adapt time.” Always state smoothing parameters additive-vs-multiplicative choice.

heading: 10.141 Practical Tips
text: Use additive Holt-Winters stable seasonal amplitude; multiplicative seasonal amplitude scales level.Log-transforming series often allows additive Holt-Winters substitute multiplicative; produce essentially equivalent forecasts.Parameters \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\) near 0 give slow updates (highly smoothed); near 1 give fast adaptation (responsive noisy).complex seasonality (multiple periods, irregular spacing), older Holt-Winters fails; use BATS TBATS (forecast::bats(), tbats()) handle cases.forecast::ets() automates choice additive, multiplicative, damped variants AIC; reach HoltWinters() new analyses.Diagnostic check: residuals look like white noise; checkresiduals(fc) runs Ljung-Box visual diagnostics.

heading: 10.142 R Packages Used
text: Base R HoltWinters() classical implementation; forecast::ets() modern state-space ETS family includes Holt-Winters ETS(…,,) ETS(…,M,M); forecast::tbats() multi-seasonal extensions; fable::ETS() tidyverts equivalent.

heading: 10.145 Introduction
text: Kalman filter workhorse algorithm state-space models linear-Gaussian observation transition equations. recursively combines previous state estimate new observation produce updated state estimate together uncertainty, closed-form Gaussian update provably optimal linear-Gaussian assumptions. Originally developed aerospace tracking, now underpins virtually every classical time-series filtering application — economic indicators, signal processing, target tracking, financial volatility estimation.

heading: 10.146 Prerequisites
text: working understanding state-space models, linear algebra, multivariate Normal distribution.

heading: 10.147 Theory
text:
linear-Gaussian state-space model observation \(y_t = F x_t + v_t\) transition \(x_t = G x_{t-1} + w_t\), Kalman filter alternates two steps time:Predict:
\[x_{t \mid t-1} = G x_{t-1 \mid t-1}, \qquad P_{t \mid t-1} = G P_{t-1 \mid t-1} G^\top + W.\]Update:
\[x_{t \mid t} = x_{t \mid t-1} + K_t (y_t - F x_{t \mid t-1}), \qquad P_{t \mid t} = (- K_t F) P_{t \mid t-1},\]Kalman gain \(K_t = P_{t \mid t-1} F^\top (F P_{t \mid t-1} F^\top + V)^{-1}\).smoother (Rauch-Tung-Striebel) backward recursion combines forward filter estimates future observations produce smoothed estimates \(x_{t \mid T}\). Non-linear extensions include extended Kalman filter (linearisation around current estimate) unscented Kalman filter (sigma-point sampling); particle filters handle fully non-Gaussian non-linear models via Monte Carlo.

heading: 10.148 Assumptions
text: Linear Gaussian observation transition equations known parameters \((F, G, V, W)\). Parameters can estimated MLE innovations.

heading: 10.149 R Implementation
code:
library(dlm)

# Local-level model on Nile
mod <- dlmModPoly(order = 1, dV = 15100, dW = 1470)
filt <- dlmFilter(Nile, mod)

plot(Nile)
lines(dropFirst(filt$m), col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Smoother
smo <- dlmSmooth(filt)
lines(dropFirst(smo$s), col = "blue", lwd = 2)

heading: 10.150 Output & Results
text: dlmFilter() returns filtered state estimates (\(x_{t \mid t}\)) covariances. dlmSmooth() produces retrospective smoothed estimates (\(x_{t \mid T}\)). Plotting original series shows filter’s adaptation real time smoother’s retrospective best estimate.

heading: 10.151 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Kalman filter applied Nile river-flow series produced one-step-ahead forecasts (filtered states) adapt new observation; smoothed estimate, using full series retrospect, identified sharp level drop near 1899 forward filter takes several years track.” Distinguish filtered (real-time) smoothed (retrospective) estimates report.

heading: 10.152 Practical Tips
text: non-linear models, use extended Kalman filter (KFAS::EKF hand-coded) mild non-linearity, unscented Kalman filter stronger non-linearity, particle filters (SMCSamplers, SMC2) arbitrary non-linear / non-Gaussian models.Uncertainty propagates naturally predict update steps; report state estimates estimated standard errors.log-likelihood model can computed innovations (dlmLL dlm) used MLE-based parameter estimation; Kalman filter engine, MLE parameter-fitting wrapper.choice initial state \(x_{0 \mid 0}\) initial variance \(P_{0 \mid 0}\) matters first time steps; diffuse initialisation (\(P_{0 \mid 0}\) large) standard choice prior information available.extremely high-dimensional state spaces (large meteorological models), ensemble Kalman filter approximates covariance low-rank ensemble; standard Kalman fails setting.Pair filtering diagnostic checks innovations (one-step-ahead residuals); approximately white noise correct model specification.

heading: 10.153 R Packages Used
text: dlm dlmFilter(), dlmSmooth(), dlmLL(); KFAS fast filtering non-Gaussian observations; FKF fast Kalman filter implementation; bsts Bayesian structural time-series alternatives; nimble hand-built non-linear filters via probabilistic programming.

heading: 10.156 Introduction
text: Kwiatkowski-Phillips-Schmidt-Shin (KPSS) test natural complement augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) test assessing stationarity. two tests flipped null hypotheses: ADF tests unit-root null stationary alternative; KPSS tests stationary null unit-root alternative. Running comparing conclusions gives robust verdict either alone, single test fails reject uninformative — failure reject unit-root null evidence stationarity, vice versa.

heading: 10.157 Prerequisites
text: working understanding stationarity, unit-root processes (random walks), augmented Dickey-Fuller test complementary stationarity diagnostic.

heading: 10.158 Theory
text: KPSS framework decomposes \(y_t = r_t + \beta t + \varepsilon_t\), \(r_t = r_{t-1} + u_t\) random walk. null stationarity, variance random-walk innovations zero (\(\sigma^2_u = 0\)); alternative, \(r_t\) contributes stochastic trend. test statistic based partial sums residuals regressing \(y_t\) (optionally) constant trend; large partial-sum behaviour indicates unit root test rejects stationarity.“Level” version tests stationarity around constant mean; “Trend” version tests stationarity around deterministic trend. Choosing depends whether series visible time trend.

heading: 10.159 Assumptions
text: Correct model specification (level vs trend stationarity); lag truncation parameter adequate underlying autocorrelation structure (default works applications).

heading: 10.160 R Implementation
code:
library(tseries)

# Stationary series
y1 <- rnorm(100)
kpss.test(y1, null = "Level")

# Random walk (unit root)
y2 <- cumsum(rnorm(100))
kpss.test(y2, null = "Level")

# Trend-stationary series
y3 <- 0.5 * (1:100) + rnorm(100)
kpss.test(y3, null = "Trend")

heading: 10.161 Output & Results
text: kpss.test() returns KPSS statistic \(p\)-value (note truncation lag type stationarity tested). Small statistics correspond large \(p\)-values indicate insufficient evidence reject stationarity; large statistics reject support unit root.

heading: 10.162 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “stationary white-noise series gave KPSS statistic 0.08 (\(p > 0.10\), fail reject stationarity); random-walk series gave statistic 2.34 (\(p < 0.01\), reject stationarity).” Always state null tested (Level vs Trend) pair ADF test complementary perspective.

heading: 10.163 Practical Tips
text: null = "Level" plain stationarity around constant mean; "Trend" stationarity around deterministic trend. Choose based whether time-trend visible series.Bandwidth (lag-truncation) choice affects test size; default adequate series. long-memory strongly autocorrelated series, increase truncation manually.Combine ADF test: agree (ADF rejects unit root, KPSS fails reject stationarity), stationarity well-supported; disagree (ADF fails, KPSS rejects), unit root well-supported.tests give ambiguous answers (ADF fails, KPSS fails), series borderline additional diagnostics (autocorrelation structure, Phillips-Perron, fractional integration) help.multivariate panel data, use Im-Pesaran-Shin Levin-Lin-Chu tests; KPSS univariate.Differencing series re-testing standard workflow unit root detected; differenced series pass KPSS ADF.

heading: 10.164 R Packages Used
text: tseries::kpss.test() canonical implementation; urca::ur.kpss() alternative finer control deterministic components bandwidth selection; tseries::adf.test() complementary ADF test; forecast::ndiffs() integrated workflow automatically applies KPSS determine required differencing order.

text: Time series basics

heading: 10.167 Introduction
text: Ljung-Box test standard portmanteau test residual autocorrelation time-series models. fitting ARIMA, ETS, time-series model, question whether residuals white noise; Ljung-Box test pools sample autocorrelations first several lags single chi-squared statistic. non-significant result supports model adequacy; significant result indicates missed dynamics motivate refining model.

heading: 10.168 Prerequisites
text: working understanding autocorrelation functions, ARIMA models, white-noise assumption time-series residuals.

heading: 10.169 Theory
text: \[Q = n(n + 2) \sum_{k=1}^h \frac{\hat\rho_k^2}{n - k} \sim \chi^2_{h - p - q} \text{ white noise},\]\(\hat\rho_k\) sample ACF lag \(k\), \(h\) maximum lag, \(p + q\) number ARMA parameters fit. fitdf argument Box.test() adjusts degrees freedom parameters estimated data; residuals raw data (model fitted), set fitdf = 0.Ljung-Box improves older Box-Pierce statistic introducing small-sample correction \(n(n+2)\) prevents -rejection small \(n\). seasonal models, maximum lag span least one seasonal period.

heading: 10.170 Assumptions
text: Residuals iid null; maximum lag \(h\) small relative series length \(n\).

heading: 10.171 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
x <- arima.sim(list(ar = 0.5), n = 200)
fit <- arima(x, order = c(1, 0, 0))
e <- residuals(fit)

# Ljung-Box at lag 20, 1 parameter fit
Box.test(e, lag = 20, type = "Ljung-Box", fitdf = 1)

# On an unfit series (no fitdf adjustment)
Box.test(x, lag = 20, type = "Ljung-Box")

heading: 10.172 Output & Results
text: Box.test() returns chi-squared statistic \(p\)-value chosen lag. Pair ACF PACF plots residuals visual confirmation; isolated significant autocorrelations may move omnibus test still substantively meaningful.

heading: 10.173 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Residuals AR(1) fit gave Ljung-Box statistic lag 20 \(Q = 18.9\) 19 degrees freedom (\(p = 0.45\)), consistent white noise. ACF PACF residuals showed isolated significant lags, supporting model adequacy.” Always pair test result lag chosen parameters subtracted.

heading: 10.174 Practical Tips
text: Choose maximum lag \(h\) around \(\min(10, n/5)\) based domain knowledge; monthly data annual seasonality, \(h \ge 24\) sensible.Use fitdf = p + q ARMA(\(p\), \(q\)) fitted models; test ignores parameter cost otherwise anti-conservative.seasonal models, set maximum lag least one full seasonal period detect missed seasonal autocorrelation.Rejection lag indicates missed dynamics; re-examine model order, add seasonal terms, consider different family (state-space, GARCH).forecast::checkresiduals() shows Ljung-Box result alongside ACF time-series plots, integrating numerical visual diagnostics.Ljung-Box sensitive autocorrelation non-stationarity distributional assumptions; complement KPSS / ADF residual normality plot.

heading: 10.175 R Packages Used
text: Base R Box.test() canonical implementation; forecast::checkresiduals() integrated diagnostic time-series ACF plots; feasts::ljung_box() modern tidyverts interface; astsa::sarima() provides automatic Ljung-Box reporting diagnostic output.

heading: 10.176 For Reviewers

heading: 10.178 Introduction
text: moving average smooths time series replacing observation average nearby values. oldest simplest tool highlighting trend suppressing high-frequency noise; underpins sophisticated techniques (X-11 decomposition, Henderson trend filters) fundamental financial chart analysis. choice trailing, centred, weighted moving averages governs trade-smoothness lag, choosing right window size application principal craft moving-average smoothing.

heading: 10.179 Prerequisites
text: working understanding time series, trade-smoothness lag, role window size noise suppression.

heading: 10.180 Theory
text: Three main variants:Simple moving average: arithmetic mean \(k\) consecutive observations. Trailing alignment uses last \(k\) values time \(t\) (one-sided, introduces lag); centred alignment averages \((k-1)/2\) (two-sided, lag undefined boundaries).Weighted moving average: assigns weights observation window — triangular, Hamming, Spencer 15-term (used classical seasonal adjustment), user-defined kernel.Exponential moving average: geometrically declining weights, \(\hat y_t = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha) \hat y_{t-1}\). Closely related simple exponential smoothing.centred MA window equal seasonal period eliminates seasonal component, leaving trend plus residual; foundation classical seasonal decomposition.

heading: 10.181 Assumptions
text: Regular sampling intervals (irregular spacing requires alternative methods); stationarity required, moving-average filter assumes slowly-varying trend.

heading: 10.182 R Implementation
code:
library(zoo); library(TTR)

x <- AirPassengers

# Trailing mean
sm_3 <- rollmean(x, 3, align = "right", fill = NA)

# Centred 12-month MA (removes annual seasonality)
sm_12 <- rollmean(x, 12, align = "center", fill = NA)

# Weighted MA
sm_w <- TTR::WMA(x, n = 12, wts = 1:12)

plot(x); lines(sm_12, col = "red", lwd = 2)

heading: 10.183 Output & Results
text: rollmean() returns smoothed series NA boundaries (trailing alignment leaves NAs start; centred alignment leaves NAs ends). TTR::WMA() produces weighted alternatives. Plotting centred 12-month MA top raw series reveals trend seasonal cycle hides.

heading: 10.184 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “centred 12-month moving average revealed secular trend monthly air-passenger counts, smoothed series showing approximately 5 % annual growth seasonal cycle largely eliminated averaging full period.” Quote window size alignment report.

heading: 10.185 Practical Tips
text: centred moving average window equal seasonal period eliminates seasonal cycle exactly; X-11 first step.Trailing moving averages introduce lag proportional half window size; use real-time estimation needed lag acceptable.smoother curves minimal lag, exponential smoothing, LOESS, spline smoothers often preferable wide moving averages.Centred MA undefined start end series; boundary, use one-sided MA, asymmetric weights, end-effects correction.Weighted MAs carefully chosen kernels (Spencer’s 15-term, Henderson’s filters) used official-statistics seasonal adjustment preserve cubic polynomial trends suppressing irregular components.Pair MA smoothing original series plot; gap seasonal-plus-irregular component.

heading: 10.186 R Packages Used

heading: 10.189 Introduction
text: Vector autoregression (VAR) multivariate generalisation autoregressive model: variable \(k\)-variate time series depends linearly lags lags every variable system. workhorse empirical macroeconomics, modelling GDP, inflation, interest rates jointly produces forecasts impulse-response analyses single-equation models . Outside macro, VAR natural tool whenever multiple time series believed jointly determined forecasting goal.

heading: 10.190 Prerequisites
text: working understanding univariate AR models, multivariate data, concept system-wide forecasting identification.

heading: 10.191 Theory
text: VAR(\(p\)) model \[\mathbf y_t = \mathbf c + A_1 \mathbf y_{t-1} + A_2 \mathbf y_{t-2} + \dots + A_p \mathbf y_{t-p} + \mathbf u_t,\]\(\mathbf y_t\) \(k\)-vector, \(A_i\) \(k \times k\) coefficient matrices, \(\mathbf u_t\) jointly white-noise innovations covariance \(\Sigma_u\). model estimated equation--equation OLS (multivariate OLS spherical errors), lag length \(p\) chosen AIC, BIC, HQ, sequential LRT.VAR feeds three key downstream analyses: multi-step forecasts system-coherent uncertainty bands, impulse-response functions trace dynamic effect shock one variable system, Granger-causality tests predictive relationships among variables.

heading: 10.192 Assumptions
text: Stationary series (difference first , use VECM cointegrated (1) series); independent, homoscedastic, jointly white-noise innovations; correctly specified lag order.

heading: 10.193 R Implementation
code:
library(vars)

data(Canada)   # four-variate quarterly macroeconomic series
VARselect(Canada, lag.max = 10, type = "const")$selection

fit <- VAR(Canada, p = 2, type = "const")
summary(fit)

# Forecast
fc <- predict(fit, n.ahead = 8)
plot(fc)

heading: 10.194 Output & Results
text: VARselect() returns suggested lag orders four information criteria; differing recommendations across criteria common. summary() returns equation--equation coefficient tables. predict() produces multi-step forecasts system-coherent confidence bands.

heading: 10.195 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “VARselect identified \(p = 2\) AIC- HQ-optimal lag order four-variate Canada series; VAR(2) model produced eight-quarter forecasts prediction intervals widen \(\pm 0.5\) % one quarter \(\pm 1.8\) % eight, multivariate covariance structure preserving empirical co-movements employment, GDP, inflation.” Always state lag selection criterion deterministic specification (constant, trend, ).

heading: 10.196 Practical Tips
text: Select lag order AIC, BIC, HQ information criteria; differing recommendations common, conservative HQ BIC choice usually preferable forecasting.Check residual cross-correlations serial.test(fit); significant -diagonals indicate missed cross-series dynamics.cointegrated (1) series, use VECM (urca::ca.jo()) levels rather VAR differences; differencing throws away long-run equilibrium relationship.Structural VARs impose identifying restrictions (Cholesky, sign, long-run) \(\Sigma_u\) give impulse responses causal interpretation; standard reduced-form VAR .Forecast-error variance decompositions (fevd(fit)) partition forecast uncertainty across innovations; useful understanding variables drive forecast errors horizon.high-dimensional VARs (\(k > 10\)), use Bayesian VARs Minnesota stochastic-search variable-selection priors; reduced-form OLS becomes unstable.

heading: 10.200 Introduction
text: Phillips-Perron (PP) test unit-root test modifies Dickey-Fuller statistic account autocorrelation heteroscedasticity errors non-parametrically. augmented Dickey-Fuller test handles serial correlation including lagged differences regression, PP applies Newey-West long-run variance correction directly test statistic. two approaches usually agree; PP preferable lag specification ADF uncertain heteroscedasticity suspected.

heading: 10.201 Prerequisites
text: working understanding augmented Dickey-Fuller test, Newey-West long-run variance estimator, unit-root vs stationarity distinction.

heading: 10.202 Theory
text: PP fits unit-root regression \(\Delta y_t = \alpha + \gamma y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t\) (without lagged differences) computes test statistic non-parametrically corrected residual autocorrelation heteroscedasticity using Newey-West estimator long-run variance. Two flavours exist: \(Z(\alpha)\) (based OLS coefficient) \(Z(t)\) (based \(t\)-statistic). asymptotic distribution unit-root null Dickey-Fuller distribution ADF, critical values tabulated.non-parametric correction sidesteps ADF lag-order choice requires bandwidth choice long-run variance estimator; defaults work series.

heading: 10.203 Assumptions
text: Errors stationary unit-root transformation; long-run variance bandwidth appropriate autocorrelation horizon.

heading: 10.204 R Implementation
code:
library(tseries)

x <- cumsum(rnorm(100))

pp.test(x)                     # default
pp.test(x, type = "Z(alpha)")
pp.test(diff(x))

# Compare with ADF
adf.test(x)

heading: 10.205 Output & Results
text: pp.test() returns test statistic \(p\)-value computed Dickey-Fuller distribution. Comparing PP ADF series useful robustness check; large discrepancies signal autocorrelation heteroscedasticity issues one test handles better .

heading: 10.206 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Phillips-Perron test random-walk series gave \(p = 0.58\), failing reject unit-root null; augmented Dickey-Fuller agreed (\(p = 0.52\)). first-differencing tests rejected null \(p < 0.01\), confirming series integrated order 1.” Always pair PP ADF ideally KPSS robust unit-root verdict.

heading: 10.207 Practical Tips
text: PP ADF usually agree; disagree, examine residual autocorrelation structure identify test appropriate.PP’s non-parametric correction avoids ADF lag-order sensitivity introduces bandwidth choice; default Newey-West truncation usually fine.PP ADF low power near-unit-root stationary processes (e.g., AR(1) coefficient 0.95); borderline series, test gives confident answer.Combine PP KPSS two-sided perspective: PP / ADF test unit-root null KPSS tests stationarity null.series structural breaks, PP invalid; use Zivot-Andrews (urca::ur.za()) instead.panel data, panel unit-root tests (Im-Pesaran-Shin, Levin-Lin-Chu) extend framework multiple series simultaneously.

heading: 10.208 R Packages Used
text: tseries::pp.test() canonical Phillips-Perron implementation; urca::ur.pp() richer output multiple critical values; tseries::adf.test() urca::ur.df() complementary ADF; urca::ur.za() Zivot-Andrews structural breaks plausible.

heading: 10.211 Introduction
text: Standard \(k\)-fold cross-validation shuffles observations across folds, violates time ordering leaks future information training data. time series, invalidates predictive performance estimates entirely. Rolling-origin cross-validation (also called time-series CV walk-forward validation) preserves temporal ordering training cutoff forecasting subsequent horizon, sliding cutoff forward repeating. valid CV scheme time-series forecast evaluation.

heading: 10.212 Prerequisites
text: working understanding cross-validation, time-series forecasting, difference -sample fit --sample predictive performance.

heading: 10.213 Theory
text: series length \(T\), choose initial training-window size \(t_0\), forecast horizon \(h\), (optionally) step size cutoff advancement. cutoff \(t = t_0, t_0 + s, \dots, T - h\):Train \(\{y_1, \dots, y_t\}\).Forecast \(\hat y_{t+1}, \dots, \hat y_{t+h}\).Compute error observed \(y_{t+1}, \dots, y_{t+h}\).Average errors across cutoffs horizon \(1, 2, \dots, h\) obtain horizon-specific accuracy estimates. Two windowing variants exist: expanding window (training set grows) rolling window (fixed-size training set slides forward). Expanding efficient underlying process stable; rolling robust regime changes.

heading: 10.214 Assumptions
text: Sufficient history initial training window estimates model adequately; model can refit cutoff (parameters updated efficiently computational savings).

heading: 10.215 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

x <- AirPassengers
h <- 12

# tsCV: rolling-origin with one-step expanding window
f_ets <- function(y, h) forecast(ets(y), h = h)$mean
err_ets <- tsCV(x, f_ets, h = h)

# RMSE by horizon
sqrt(colMeans(err_ets^2, na.rm = TRUE))

# Compare ARIMA
f_arima <- function(y, h) forecast(auto.arima(y), h = h)$mean
err_arima <- tsCV(x, f_arima, h = h)
sqrt(colMeans(err_arima^2, na.rm = TRUE))

heading: 10.216 Output & Results
text: tsCV() returns matrix forecast errors one row per cutoff one column per horizon. Column-wise summaries give horizon-specific RMSE, MAE, metrics. Comparing matrices across models identifies performs best.

heading: 10.217 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Rolling-origin CV one-step expanding window forecast horizon 12 months showed ARIMA outperforming ETS short horizons (1–3 months: RMSE 22 vs 28) ETS accurate longer horizons (10–12 months: RMSE 38 vs 45). horizon-specific differences justify reporting CV-RMSE per horizon rather single averaged metric.” Report horizon-specific errors; collapsing hides structure informs model choice.

heading: 10.218 Practical Tips
text: forecast::tsCV() implements expanding-window CV one-step cutoff advancement; rolling fixed-window, write small loop.computational cost, increase initial window skip cutoffs (advance one observation per step); trades estimation precision speed.Always report horizon-specific errors; single averaged RMSE hides structure determines model wins horizon.Refitting model every cutoff expensive; ARIMA, fixed-parameter updates (forecast(Arima(y, model = fit), h = h)) faster ignore changing dynamics.fable::stretch_tsibble() provides tidy rolling-CV splits integrate modern tidyverts forecasting workflow.nested hierarchical forecasts, run rolling-origin CV bottom level aggregate; never CV aggregated level alone.

heading: 10.219 R Packages Used
text: forecast::tsCV() expanding-window time-series CV; fable::stretch_tsibble() accuracy() tidyverts CV workflows; tibbletime::rollify() general rolling functions; slider::slide_dbl() sliding-window operations.

heading: 10.222 Introduction
text: Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA) extends standard ARIMA framework series periodic seasonality. standard notation \(\mathrm{ARIMA}(p, d, q)(P, D, Q)_s\) lower-case letters non-seasonal orders, upper-case seasonal orders, \(s\) seasonal period (12 monthly annual seasonality, 4 quarterly, 24 hourly daily seasonality). SARIMA workhorse parametric forecasting tool economic, financial, many biomedical time series stable seasonal patterns.

heading: 10.223 Prerequisites
text: working understanding ARIMA models, differencing, backshift operator, seasonal decomposition.

heading: 10.224 Theory
text: full SARIMA equation \[\Phi_P(B^s) \phi_p(B) (1 - B)^d (1 - B^s)^D y_t = \Theta_Q(B^s) \theta_q(B) \varepsilon_t,\]\(B\) backshift operator (\(B y_t = y_{t-1}\)). non-seasonal AR, differencing, MA components compose multiplicatively seasonal counterparts lag \(s\). model accommodates trends differencing (\(d\)) seasonal patterns seasonal differencing (\(D\)) seasonal AR/MA terms.famous “airline model” \((0, 1, 1)(0, 1, 1)_{12}\) monthly airline passengers — Box Jenkins’s headline example — demonstrates even highly trending strongly seasonal series can fit SARIMA family modest order.

heading: 10.225 Assumptions
text: series stationary regular seasonal differencing; residuals white noise; seasonal period \(s\) correctly specified frequency() input ts object.

heading: 10.226 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

x <- AirPassengers

fit <- auto.arima(log(x), seasonal = TRUE)
summary(fit)

fc <- forecast(fit, h = 24)
autoplot(fc)

checkresiduals(fit)

heading: 10.227 Output & Results
text: auto.arima() searches candidate orders using AICc returns chosen SARIMA model coefficients standard errors. forecast() produces point forecasts 80 % / 95 % prediction intervals. checkresiduals() reports Ljung-Box test, ACF, time-series plot residuals.

heading: 10.228 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “auto.arima log-transformed AirPassengers selected ARIMA\((0,1,1)(0,1,1)_{12}\) structure (AICc = \(-483\), \(\sigma^2 = 0.0014\)); residuals passed Ljung-Box lag 24 (\(p = 0.61\)). Forecasts next 24 months show continued multiplicative growth stable seasonal pattern, 95 % prediction intervals widening \(\pm 5 \%\) 1 month \(\pm 18 \%\) 24 months.” Always check residual diagnostics reporting forecasts.

heading: 10.229 Practical Tips
text: Use auto.arima() automatic order selection; double-check result ACF/PACF inspection differenced series.Log-transform multiplicative seasonality (seasonal amplitude grows level); SARIMA log scale additive well-behaved.long seasonal periods (\(s \ge 52\)), SARIMA becomes computationally heavy; consider regression Fourier seasonal terms (forecast::tslm fourier()) instead.Always use chronological train/test splits; random CV invalid time series leaks future information past folds.Box-Jenkins residual diagnostics (Ljung-Box, ACF, PACF, normality plot) essential fitting; checkresiduals() aggregates .multiple seasonalities (weekly + yearly), use TBATS (forecast::tbats) mstl() decomposition non-seasonal forecasting adjusted series.

heading: 10.230 R Packages Used

heading: 10.233 Introduction
text: STL (Seasonal Trend decomposition using Loess) flexible classical decomposition method time series. separates series trend, seasonal, remainder components using iterative locally-weighted regression (loess) smoothers. Three properties distinguish STL alternatives: handles kind seasonality (just monthly quarterly), allows seasonal pattern change time, robust outliers run robust option enabled.

heading: 10.234 Prerequisites
text: working understanding time-series decomposition, additive vs multiplicative model distinction, basic familiarity loess smoothers.

heading: 10.235 Theory
text: STL alternates two loess smoothing operations convergence: seasonal smoother operates within cycle position (Januaries together, Februaries together, etc.) trend smoother operates across de-seasonalised series. Two window parameters control resulting decomposition:s.window: seasonal-window length. "periodic" forces constant seasonal pattern across series; integer (typical 7, 9, 11) allows seasonal pattern change gradually.t.window: trend-window length. Larger values produce smoother trend estimates.multiplicative seasonality (seasonal amplitude grows level), apply STL log series; result interpretable additive decomposition log scale.

heading: 10.236 Assumptions
text: Additive components chosen scale; seasonal cycle correctly specified (frequency() ts object); sufficient series length estimate decomposition (least two full seasonal cycles).

heading: 10.237 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

# log-transform for multiplicative data
x <- log(AirPassengers)

# STL with fixed seasonal
stl_fit <- stl(x, s.window = "periodic")
autoplot(stl_fit)

# Seasonal adjustment
seasadj <- seasadj(stl_fit)
plot(seasadj)

# Forecast via STL + ETS
fc <- forecast(stl_fit, method = "ets", h = 24)
autoplot(fc)

heading: 10.238 Output & Results
text: stl() returns stl object decomposition. autoplot() produces four-panel display: original series, trend, seasonal, remainder. seasadj() extracts seasonally adjusted series (original minus seasonal); forecast(stl_fit) applies chosen method (ETS, ARIMA) seasonally adjusted component adds back seasonal pattern.

heading: 10.239 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “STL decomposition log-AirPassengers showed smooth upward trend (annual growth approximately 5 %), stable multiplicative seasonality (constant log scale, multiplicative original), remainder series consistent white noise (Ljung-Box \(p = 0.32\)); seasonal pattern peaked July–August, consistent summer holiday travel.” Always describe trend seasonality separately check remainder approximately white noise.

heading: 10.240 Practical Tips
text: s.window = "periodic" forces constant seasonal pattern across series; integer values (7, 11) allow seasonal pattern evolve. Use integer seasonality visibly shifted series.Set robust = TRUE outlier-resistant fitting; standard STL sensitive single extreme observations.forecasting, stlf() one-call wrapper applies STL + automatic ETS ARIMA forecast seasonally adjusted series.STL handles seasonal period; multiple seasonalities (e.g. weekly yearly), use forecast::mstl() decomposes multiple seasonal layers.Residuals (remainder) look like white noise; systematic structure indicates trend seasonal windows need tuning.Compare STL classical seasonal decomposition (decompose) X-13 (seasonal::seas); STL generally flexible X-13 regulatory standard official statistics.

heading: 10.241 R Packages Used
text: Base R stl() canonical implementation; forecast::stlf() STL-based forecasting; forecast::mstl() multi-seasonal decomposition; seasonal::seas() X-13 ARIMA-SEATS official-statistics alternative; feasts::STL() modern tidyverts interface.

heading: 10.244 Introduction
text: Spectral analysis examines time series frequency domain rather time domain. Periodicities appear peaks power spectrum, slow trends dominate low-frequency end, white noise flat spectrum. view complementary autocorrelation-based time-domain analysis: spectral density autocovariance function Fourier-transform pairs, neither view contains information lacks, makes different features easier see.

heading: 10.245 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Fourier analysis, autocovariance function, time-frequency duality stationary processes.

heading: 10.246 Theory
text: stationary process autocovariance function \(\gamma_k\), spectral density \[S(f) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \gamma_k e^{-2\pi f k},\]defined \(f \[-1/2, 1/2]\) unit-spaced data. periodogram sample-based estimator, computed squared magnitude FFT centred series divided series length. Raw periodograms inconsistent (variance decrease \(n\)), smoothing — Bartlett, Welch, kernel-based, multi-taper — applied produce stable spectral estimates.peak frequency \(f\) indicates cyclic component period \(1/f\) time units; area frequency band variance contribution band.

heading: 10.247 Assumptions
text: series approximately stationary (made detrending differencing); sufficient length frequency resolution \(1/n\) distinguish nearby peaks.

heading: 10.248 R Implementation
code:
# Simulated series with two cycles
set.seed(2026)
t <- 1:500
y <- sin(2 * pi * t / 12) + 0.5 * sin(2 * pi * t / 4) + rnorm(500, 0, 0.3)

# Periodogram
spec_p <- spectrum(y, plot = FALSE)
plot(spec_p, main = "Raw periodogram")

# Smoothed
spec_s <- spectrum(y, spans = c(5, 5), plot = FALSE)
plot(spec_s, main = "Smoothed periodogram")
abline(v = 1/12, col = "red", lty = 2)
abline(v = 1/4,  col = "red", lty = 2)

heading: 10.249 Output & Results
text: spectrum() returns periodogram frequencies spectral-density estimates, plus chosen smoothing-window structure. plot shows distinct peaks \(f = 1/12\) \(f = 1/4\), matching simulated cycles.

heading: 10.250 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Spectral analysis simulated series two embedded cycles produced peaks frequencies \(f = 0.083\) (period 12) \(f = 0.25\) (period 4) smoothed periodogram spans = c(5, 5); relative peak heights match relative amplitudes simulated components.” Always state smoothing window figure caption; raw periodograms noisy often misread.

heading: 10.251 Practical Tips
text: Smoothing (spans = c(5, 5) similar) trades variance bias; tune balance peak detection resolution.Frequency resolution \(1/n\); closely spaced periodicities, longer series essential.Use log-log plot frequency vs spectral density expose power-law behaviour broadband features.Multi-taper estimation (multitaper::spec.mtm) provides better bias-variance trade-spans-based smoothing short series.Spectral analysis complements ACF / PACF; use , especially looking cyclic versus trend structure.non-stationary series, time-frequency methods (short-time Fourier, wavelets) generalise stationary spectral framework.

heading: 10.252 R Packages Used
text: Base R spectrum() periodograms smoothing; multitaper::spec.mtm() multi-taper estimation; astsa::mvspec() multivariate spectral analysis; WaveletComp time-frequency localisation stationarity fails.

heading: 10.255 Introduction
text: State-space models describe time series via latent state vector evolves time, observations noisy function state. general framework encompasses essentially every classical time-series model — ARIMA, exponential smoothing (ETS), structural time series, hidden Markov models, dynamic linear models — special cases. unified framework provides consistent inference machinery (Kalman filter linear Gaussian models; particle extended-Kalman filters non-linear non-Gaussian extensions) flexible specification language custom models.

heading: 10.256 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear algebra, Bayesian likelihood inference, basic familiarity classical ARIMA / ETS forecasting.

heading: 10.257 Theory
text: standard linear Gaussian state-space model two equations:Observation equation: \(y_t = F_t x_t + v_t, \quad v_t \sim \mathcal N(0, V_t)\).State transition: \(x_t = G_t x_{t-1} + w_t, \quad w_t \sim \mathcal N(0, W_t)\).matrices \((F_t, G_t, V_t, W_t)\) specify model — typically constant time can vary deterministically. Inference proceeds three modes: filtering estimates \(x_t \mid y_{1:t}\) real time; smoothing estimates \(x_t \mid y_{1:T}\) using full series; prediction extends state forward beyond data.Kalman filter computes filtered smoothed states recursive Gaussian updates; non-linear non-Gaussian extensions use extended unscented Kalman filter, particle filter, fully Bayesian MCMC.

heading: 10.258 Assumptions
text: standard Kalman filter: linear Gaussian observation state equations. Non-linearity non-Gaussianity requires extended methods.

heading: 10.259 R Implementation
code:
library(dlm)

# Local level model (random walk + noise)
build_dlm <- function(theta)
  dlmModPoly(order = 1, dV = exp(theta[1]), dW = exp(theta[2]))

set.seed(2026)
y <- Nile
fit <- dlmMLE(y, parm = c(0, 0), build = build_dlm)
dlm_mod <- build_dlm(fit$par)

# Filtering
filt <- dlmFilter(y, dlm_mod)
smo  <- dlmSmooth(filt)

plot(y)
lines(dropFirst(smo$s), col = "red", lwd = 2)

heading: 10.260 Output & Results
text: dlmMLE() estimates variance components maximum likelihood. dlmFilter() dlmSmooth() produce filtered smoothed state estimates. smoothed latent series captures gradual level evolution; Nile, shows famous step-around 1900 corresponding Aswan Low Dam construction.

heading: 10.261 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “local-level state-space model Nile river flow fit maximum likelihood estimated observation variance 15{,}100 state-transition variance 1{,}470 (signal--noise ratio 0.10), favouring gradual state evolution; smoothed latent series captured clear level shift late 1890s.” Always state model structure (local level, local linear trend, etc.) estimated variance components.

heading: 10.262 Practical Tips
text: ARIMA ETS exact state-space representations; forecast::Arima() ets() use internally.Unknown latent states estimated automatically Kalman filter; user typically specifies model structure estimates variance components MLE.non-linear non-Gaussian models, use extended Kalman, unscented Kalman, particle filters; bayesforecast bsts provide Bayesian alternatives.Parameter estimation MLE (dlmMLE) Bayesian MCMC (bsts); Bayesian setups, weakly informative priors variance components stabilise inference data weak.State-space general framework; choose specific model (local level, local linear trend, structural seasonal, dynamic regression) based substantive domain knowledge.diagnostics, examine standardised innovations (filter residuals); approximately white noise correct model specification.

heading: 10.263 R Packages Used
text: dlm canonical dynamic linear model framework; KFAS fast Kalman filtering / smoothing including non-Gaussian extensions; bsts Bayesian structural time series; MARSS multivariate ARMA state-space models; fable::SSM() tidyverts state-space modelling.

heading: 10.266 Introduction
text: Stationarity — constant mean, variance, autocovariance time — fundamental prerequisite classical time-series models (ARIMA, VAR, spectral analysis). Non-stationary series typically need made stationary differencing, detrending, transformation model fitting; forgetting step produces nonsense estimates invalid forecasts. Formal stationarity testing combines several tests complementary nulls give robust verdict.

heading: 10.267 Prerequisites
text: working understanding time-series basics, autocorrelation, difference strict weak stationarity, role differencing inducing stationarity.

heading: 10.268 Theory
text: standard test battery comprises:Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF): null = unit root (non-stationary), alternative = stationary trend-stationary. Rejection supports stationarity.KPSS: null = stationarity (level trend), alternative = unit root. Rejection supports non-stationarity.Phillips-Perron (PP): non-parametric variant ADF, robust autocorrelation heteroscedasticity errors.Pair : stationary ADF rejects KPSS ; non-stationary ADF fails reject KPSS rejects. Disagreement (reject, neither rejects) ambiguous may indicate near-unit-root behaviour, structural breaks, fractional integration.series structural breaks, standard tests invalid; use Zivot-Andrews (urca::ur.za()) extends framework allow one endogenous break.

heading: 10.269 Assumptions
text: Choice test depends alternative — level vs trend stationarity KPSS, drift vs trend specification ADF. Adequate sample size asymptotic critical values.

heading: 10.270 R Implementation
code:
library(tseries); library(urca)

x <- AirPassengers

# ADF
adf.test(log(x))

# KPSS
kpss.test(log(x))

# Phillips-Perron
pp.test(log(x))

# Differencing to achieve stationarity
ndiffs(log(x))   # forecast::ndiffs

heading: 10.271 Output & Results
text: test returns statistic \(p\)-value. forecast::ndiffs() nsdiffs() automate differencing decision running KPSS tests candidate differencing order stationarity supported.

heading: 10.272 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “log-transformed AirPassengers series, ADF failed reject unit-root null (\(p = 0.99\)) KPSS rejected level stationarity (\(p < 0.05\)); agree series non-stationary. first-differencing plus seasonal differencing (order \((0, 1, 0)(0, 1, 0)_{12}\)), three tests supported stationarity, justifying ARIMA modelling doubly-differenced series.” Always pair ADF KPSS report verdicts.

heading: 10.273 Practical Tips
text: Run ADF KPSS together; conclusions agree, disagreement diagnostic information.series clear trend, test trend-stationarity explicitly (kpss.test(x, null = "Trend") adf.test trend specification).Structural breaks invalidate standard tests; use Zivot-Andrews (urca::ur.za()) break plausible.forecast::ndiffs() nsdiffs() automate choice regular seasonal differencing orders ARIMA model preparation.Visual inspection series — plotting looking trends, level shifts, changing variance — usually suggests right transformation test run.panel data (multiple time series), use Im-Pesaran-Shin Levin-Lin-Chu panel unit-root tests; running univariate tests series ignores panel structure.

heading: 10.274 R Packages Used
text: tseries::adf.test(), kpss.test(), pp.test() standard battery; urca::ur.df(), ur.kpss(), ur.pp(), ur.za() richer output structural-break extensions; forecast::ndiffs() nsdiffs() automated differencing decisions; feasts::unitroot_kpss() unitroot_ndiffs() modern tidyverts interface.

heading: 10.277 Introduction
text: time series sequence observations indexed time. Time-series analysis decomposes series four interpretable components — trend, seasonality, cyclicality, noise — models . decomposition framework underpins virtually every classical method: ARIMA models noise differencing trend seasonality; ETS captures four components state-space form; Bayesian structural time series isolates latent processes. Understanding four-component vocabulary foundational step reaching specific model.

heading: 10.278 Prerequisites
text: working understanding basic statistics, ts class R, difference cross-sectional longitudinal data structures.

heading: 10.279 Theory
text: classical additive decomposition \[y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \varepsilon_t,\]components: trend \(T\) (long-term level change), seasonal \(S\) (regular fixed-period variation, e.g. monthly cycle), cycle \(C\) (irregular longer--seasonal swings, e.g. business cycles), noise \(\varepsilon\) (residual stochastic variation). multiplicative form \(y_t = T_t \cdot S_t \cdot C_t \cdot \varepsilon_t\) applies seasonal amplitude grows level; additive log scale.practice, cycle often combined trend (giving “trend-cycle” one component), leaving three components decomposition implementations: trend-cycle, seasonal, remainder.

heading: 10.280 Assumptions
text: Time order preserved (shuffling); regular sampling intervals (irregular sampling requires zoo / xts modelling spacing); chosen decomposition (additive vs multiplicative) matches data.

heading: 10.281 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

data(AirPassengers)
plot(AirPassengers, main = "Monthly airline passengers")

# Classical decomposition
dec <- decompose(AirPassengers, type = "multiplicative")
plot(dec)

# STL: loess-based decomposition
stl_dec <- stl(log(AirPassengers), s.window = "periodic")
plot(stl_dec)

heading: 10.282 Output & Results
text: decompose() returns classical additive multiplicative decomposition; stl() returns loess-based seasonal-trend decomposition. four-panel plot shows observed, trend, seasonal, remainder components stacked vertically.

heading: 10.283 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “monthly air-passenger series exhibited clear upward trend (approximately 5 % per year), stable annual seasonal cycle peaking summer, noise grew level (motivating log transformation). STL decomposition log scale produced trend, seasonal, approximately white-noise remainder components.” introducing time series, describe component visible data.

heading: 10.284 Practical Tips
text: Log-transform series whose variance grows level; resulting decomposition log scale additive well-behaved.STL (stl()) robust classical decompose(); latter sensitive outliers assumes fixed seasonal patterns.decomposition, examine remainder series ACF / PACF plots Ljung-Box; substantial autocorrelation indicates missed structure.modern tidy workflow uses tsibble time-aware tibbles, feasts decomposition diagnostics, fable forecasting; together replace much older forecast workflow.irregular sampling intervals, use zoo xts time series; classical decomposition assumes regular sampling.multiple seasonalities (e.g. weekly + yearly retail data), forecast::mstl() produces multi-layer decomposition single-seasonal STL .

heading: 10.285 R Packages Used
text: Base R ts(), decompose(), stl(); forecast broader classical workflow including auto.arima, forecast, mstl; tsibble, feasts, fable modern tidyverts ecosystem; zoo xts irregularly-spaced time series.

heading: 10.288 Introduction
text: Two non-stationary time series cointegrated linear combination stationary. classic example interest rates different maturities: rate non-stationary (random-walk-like), spreads mean-reverting. vector error-correction model (VECM) captures short-run dynamics cointegrated series respecting long-run equilibrium relationship; Johansen procedure tests many cointegrating relations exist estimates jointly dynamic parameters.

heading: 10.289 Prerequisites
text: working understanding vector autoregressive (VAR) models, unit roots, (1)/(0) terminology integrated stationary series.

heading: 10.290 Theory
text: VECM \[\Delta y_t = \Pi y_{t-1} + \sum_{=1}^{p-1} \Gamma_i \Delta y_{t-} + \varepsilon_t.\]matrix \(\Pi\) encodes long-run relationships; rank equals number cointegrating relations. Johansen’s approach tests rank via two statistics:Trace test: \(\lambda_{\text{trace}}(r) = -T \sum_{=r+1}^k \log(1 - \hat\lambda_i)\) tests \(H_0: \mathrm{rank} \le r\).Maximum eigenvalue test: \(\lambda_{\max}(r) = -T \log(1 - \hat\lambda_{r+1})\) tests \(H_0: \mathrm{rank} = r\) vs \(H_1: \mathrm{rank} = r + 1\).two often agree; disagree, trace test generally preferred. rank determined, cointegrating vectors adjustment speeds can estimated.

heading: 10.291 Assumptions
text: series individually (1) (verifiable ADF KPSS); \(k\)-variate Gaussian innovations; lag length \(p\) correctly specified.

heading: 10.292 R Implementation
code:
library(urca); library(vars)

data(Canada)
jo <- ca.jo(Canada, ecdet = "const", type = "eigen", K = 2, spec = "transitory")
summary(jo)

# Fit VECM with rank r = 1 (if supported by test)
vecm <- cajorls(jo, r = 1)
vecm

heading: 10.293 Output & Results
text: ca.jo() returns eigenvalue trace test statistics per candidate rank, along critical values 10/5/1 % levels. cajorls() fits VECM chosen rank returns cointegrating vector adjustment coefficients.

heading: 10.294 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Johansen’s trace test supported one cointegrating relationship Canada macroeconomic data (test statistic 32.8 vs 5 % critical value 25.3, \(p < 0.05\)); corresponding cointegrating vector linked money demand interest rates adjustment speeds \(-0.10\) money demand toward equilibrium.” Always report trace max-eigenvalue results, chosen lag length.

heading: 10.295 Practical Tips
text: Confirm series (1) via ADF / KPSS cointegration testing; cointegration meaningful integrated series.ecdet argument specifies whether deterministic terms (constant, trend) enter inside cointegrating relation; choose based visible long-run drift.Engle-Granger two-step simpler two series gives essentially equivalent conclusions; Johansen generalises \(k\) variables.Report trace max-eigenvalue tests; typically agree can disagree one two cointegrating relations weak.short samples (100 observations) many variables, Johansen loses power; reduce dimension use bias-corrected critical values.VECM fitted, impulse-response functions forecast-error variance decompositions vec2var(vecm) extend standard VAR diagnostic toolkit.

heading: 10.296 R Packages Used
text: urca ca.jo(), cajorls(), Johansen-related cointegration tests; vars VAR modelling vec2var() convert VECM back VAR form IRF analysis; tsDyn::VECM() alternative VECM implementation; urca::ca.po() Phillips-Ouliaris cointegration tests.
