heading: 10.299 Introduction
text: Wavelet analysis decomposes time series components localised time frequency simultaneously. Unlike Fourier transform, assumes stationarity produces single spectrum entire series, wavelets capture cyclic components appear, shift, disappear time — essential non-stationary signals EEG recordings, climate proxies, series whose periodic content evolves. output scalogram, 2D map power across time frequency reveals time-varying structure Fourier methods miss.

heading: 10.300 Prerequisites
text: working understanding spectral analysis Fourier transforms, concept time-frequency localisation, basic familiarity trade-time frequency resolution.

heading: 10.301 Theory
text: continuous wavelet transform (CWT) convolves signal scaled shifted copies mother wavelet \(\psi\):\[W(, b) = \int y(t) \, \bar\psi\!\left(\frac{t - b}{}\right) \frac{\mathrm d t}{\sqrt{||}}.\]scale \(\) controls wavelet’s “width” corresponds inversely frequency; \(b\) time shift. squared magnitude \(|W(, b)|^2\) wavelet power time \(b\) scale \(\). Common mother wavelets include Morlet (oscillatory, good time-frequency localisation), Haar (step-function), Daubechies (orthogonal, used compression). scalogram visualises \(|W|^2\) heatmap time-frequency plane.cone influence delimits regions scalogram free edge artefacts; power within cone reliable, power outside discounted.

heading: 10.302 Assumptions
text: Depends wavelet choice; minimal beyond sufficiently long series. discrete wavelet transform (DWT) requires series length power 2; CWT .

heading: 10.303 R Implementation
code:
library(WaveletComp)

set.seed(2026)
t <- 1:500
y <- sin(2 * pi * t / 50) * (t < 250) + sin(2 * pi * t / 20) * (t >= 250) +
     rnorm(500, 0, 0.3)

df <- data.frame(date = t, x = y)
wt <- analyze.wavelet(df, "x", loess.span = 0, dt = 1,
                      dj = 1/50, lowerPeriod = 8,
                      upperPeriod = 128, make.pval = FALSE, verbose = FALSE)

wt.image(wt, main = "Wavelet power spectrum")

heading: 10.304 Output & Results
text: analyze.wavelet() returns list containing wavelet power, phase, statistical-significance information. wt.image() plots scalogram 2D heatmap cone influence overlaid.

heading: 10.305 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “wavelet power spectrum revealed 50-time-step cycle first half series 20-time-step cycle second half — non-stationary feature invisible global Fourier analysis. cone influence excluded first last 30 time points interpretation due edge effects.” Always describe time-frequency structure visible acknowledge cone influence.

heading: 10.306 Practical Tips
text: Choose wavelet suit signal: Morlet oscillatory components, Mexican-hat localised peaks, Daubechies compression denoising.Power outside cone influence unreliable; mask interpret caution.Statistical significance via red-noise surrogates (make.pval = TRUE WaveletComp) tests power AR(1) null scale time.discrete wavelet transform (DWT, wavethresh) supports compression denoising; CWT visualisation analysis.EEG, fMRI, neurophysiological data, wavelets standard time-frequency tool; specialised packages (eegkit, fmri.wavelets) extend framework.multi-channel time-frequency analysis, cross-wavelet coherence wavelet phase quantify time-varying co-movements series.

heading: 10.310 Introduction
text: fitting time-series model — ARIMA, ETS, regression ARIMA errors — residuals look like white noise: zero mean, constant variance, autocorrelation. White-noise tests check properties formally standard residual-diagnostic battery time-series modelling. Significant rejection indicates model missed structure motivate refinement; failure reject supports model adequacy.

heading: 10.311 Prerequisites
text: working understanding ARIMA models, residual diagnostics, autocorrelation function (ACF) visual counterpart formal autocorrelation tests.

heading: 10.312 Theory
text: Three test families dominate practice:Portmanteau tests (Box-Pierce, Ljung-Box) aggregate squared sample autocorrelations first \(h\) lags single chi-squared statistic. white-noise null, \(Q \sim \chi^2_{h - p - q}\) \(p + q\) number fitted ARMA parameters. Ljung-Box variant small-sample correction preferred Box-Pierce.Turning-points test: counts number local maxima minima residual series; iid, known mean \(2(n-2)/3\) variance, giving approximate Normal test independence.Runs test: counts runs consecutive -sign residuals; tests independence without distributional assumptions.failure Ljung-Box lag indicates missed autocorrelation model address; failure runs turning-points test indicates general non-randomness.

heading: 10.313 Assumptions
text: Null: residuals iid white noise. tests different power autocorrelation, heteroscedasticity, non-linearity; single test detects departures, multiple tests run battery.

heading: 10.314 R Implementation
code:
library(forecast)

set.seed(2026)
fit <- arima(AirPassengers, order = c(0, 1, 1), seasonal = c(0, 1, 1))
resid_fit <- residuals(fit)

# Ljung-Box
Box.test(resid_fit, lag = 24, type = "Ljung-Box", fitdf = 2)

# Visual + ARIMA diagnostic
checkresiduals(fit)

heading: 10.315 Output & Results
text: Box.test() returns test statistic \(p\)-value; checkresiduals() aggregates Ljung-Box test ACF, histogram, time-series plots single diagnostic display.

heading: 10.316 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Ljung-Box lag 24 residuals ARIMA(0,1,1)(0,1,1)\[12\] fit gave \(Q = 28.1\) 22 degrees freedom (\(p = 0.17\)); ACF showed isolated significant lags, histogram approximately Normal, residual time-series plot showed remaining structure. Residuals consistent white noise.” Always pair numerical test ACF plot.

heading: 10.317 Practical Tips
text: Choose maximum lag \(h\) around \(\log n\) small multiple thereof; seasonal models, set \(h\) least one full seasonal period detect missed seasonal autocorrelation.Use fitdf = p + q ARMA(\(p\), \(q\)) fitted models; test ignores parameter cost otherwise anti-conservative.significant Ljung-Box model fitting indicates missed structure; add AR MA terms, seasonal terms, consider different model family.Pair formal Ljung-Box test visual ACF inspection; isolated significant lags can substantively meaningful even omnibus test passes.forecast::checkresiduals() convenient one-call diagnostic battery; use routinely every time-series fit.non-Normal residuals, Jarque-Bera Shapiro-Wilk test residuals complements ACF-based tests.

heading: 10.318 R Packages Used
text: Base R Box.test() Ljung-Box Box-Pierce; forecast::checkresiduals() integrated diagnostic; tseries::runs.test() runs test; randtests::turning.point.test() turning-points test; feasts::ljung_box() modern tidyverts implementation.

heading: 10.321 Introduction
text: X-13ARIMA-SEATS seasonal-adjustment workhorse official statistics. Census Bureau standard, used Eurostat, Bank England, national statistical agencies producing officially published seasonally adjusted economic time series. combination RegARIMA-based pre-adjustment (handling outliers, trading-day effects, Easter effects) either X-11 filter-based SEATS model-based decomposition makes sophisticated decomposition tool routinely available — standard simpler methods (STL, classical decomposition) benchmarked.

heading: 10.322 Prerequisites

heading: 10.323 Theory
text: X-13 two main computational stages:RegARIMA pre-adjustment: fit regression--ARIMA-errors model absorbs outliers (additive, level shift, temporary change), trading-day effects, leap-year effects, Easter effects. pre-adjustment removes original series.RegARIMA pre-adjustment: fit regression--ARIMA-errors model absorbs outliers (additive, level shift, temporary change), trading-day effects, leap-year effects, Easter effects. pre-adjustment removes original series.Decomposition: either X-11-style iterative moving averages SEATS (Signal Extraction ARIMA Time Series), model-based decomposition derived RegARIMA spectral structure. SEATS preferred series stable seasonal patterns; X-11 robust deviations fitted ARIMA.Decomposition: either X-11-style iterative moving averages SEATS (Signal Extraction ARIMA Time Series), model-based decomposition derived RegARIMA spectral structure. SEATS preferred series stable seasonal patterns; X-11 robust deviations fitted ARIMA.output includes seasonally adjusted series, trend-cycle, seasonal component, irregular component, plus diagnostics stability, residual autocorrelation, seasonal-adjustment quality.

heading: 10.324 Assumptions

heading: 10.325 R Implementation

library(seasonal)

x <- AirPassengers
m <- seas(x)
summary(m)

plot(m)

text:
time series differs cross-sectional data one crucial way:
observations close time correlated one another, 
standard errors based independence wrong. Classical
decomposition splits series slowly varying trend, 
periodic seasonal component, residual. ARIMA models capture
residual autocorrelation autoregressive moving-average
terms, possibly differencing remove unit root.Change-point detection asks different question: given series,
(ever) underlying mean variance shift?

epidemiology, change-points flag outbreaks, policy changes,

Seasonal adjustment removes regular period; detrending
removes slow evolution. anomalies care (

heading: 10.335 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(forecast)
library(changepoint)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 10.336 1. Hypothesis
text:
simulated monthly series linear trend, yearly seasonal
cycle, mean shift month 80. Decomposition, ARIMA, 
change-point detection recover interpretable piece.

heading: 10.337 2. Visualise
code:
t  <- seq_len(months)
trend <- 0.08 * t
season <- 4 * sin(2 * pi * t / 12)
shift  <- if_else(t >= 80, 5, 0)
y      <- 20 + trend + season + shift + rnorm(months, 0, 1.5)
ts_y   <- ts(y, frequency = 12, start = c(2014, 1))

autoplot(ts_y) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Simulated rate")

heading: 10.338 3. Assumptions
text:
STL decomposition assumes seasonal period fixed known.
ARIMA assumes linear, time-invariant generating process 
differencing. cpt.mean assumes variance approximately
constant — change-points variance need cpt.var.

heading: 10.339 4. Conduct
code:
autoplot(decomp)
fit_arima
autoplot(fc) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Simulated rate")
cpts(cpt)
tibble(t = t, y = as.numeric(ts_y)) |>
  ggplot(aes(t, y)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cpts(cpt), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(x = "Month index", y = "Rate")

heading: 10.340 5. Concluding statement

STL cleanly separated linear trend, 12-month seasonal cycle,
residual containing simulated mean shift. auto.arima
selected fit_arima$arma |> paste(collapse = ", ")
(p, q, P, Q, frequency, d, D). PELT detected change-points 
months paste(cpts(cpt), collapse = ", "), close 
simulated truth 80.

heading: 11 Bayesian Statistics
text: Posterior inference via brms Stan: priors, hierarchical models, posterior predictive checks, leave-one-cross-validation, Bayes factors. chapter opinionated: treats Bayesian inference default, alternative.chapter contains 35 method pages 2 labs. sure method read, return Chapter 0 follow decision tree right node.

heading: 11.3 Introduction
text: Bayes factor compares two models ratio plausible observed data , integrating parameters model respect prior. Bayesian counterpart likelihood-ratio test, carries richer interpretation: rather rejecting failing reject null, returns continuous strength--evidence measure can equally support null model data inconsistent alternative. symmetry — quantifying support either side — one main reasons Bayes factors recur applied work “effect” substantively interesting conclusion.

heading: 11.4 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Bayes’ theorem, role marginal likelihood (evidence) Bayesian inference, difference point-null composite hypotheses.

heading: 11.5 Theory
text: two models \(M_0\) \(M_1\) parameter vectors \(\theta_0\) \(\theta_1\) priors \(p(\theta_0 \mid M_0)\) \(p(\theta_1 \mid M_1)\), Bayes factor favour \(M_1\) \[\mathrm{BF}_{10} = \frac{p(y \mid M_1)}{p(y \mid M_0)} = \frac{\int p(y \mid \theta_1, M_1) p(\theta_1 \mid M_1) \, \mathrm d \theta_1}{\int p(y \mid \theta_0, M_0) p(\theta_0 \mid M_0) \, \mathrm d \theta_0}.\]Posterior odds equal Bayes factor times prior odds; equal prior odds, posterior probability \(M_1\) \(\mathrm{BF}_{10} / (\mathrm{BF}_{10} + 1)\). Jeffreys’ interpretive scale calls \(\mathrm{BF} > 3\) substantial, \(> 10\) strong, \(> 30\) strong, \(> 100\) decisive evidence; thresholds apply, mirrored, evidence favour \(M_0\).

heading: 11.6 Assumptions
text: models well-specified proper priors assigned parameters differ two; improper priors leave marginal likelihoods undefined arbitrary constant Bayes factor inherits indeterminacy. Numerical estimation must stable — bridge sampling, Chib’s method, Savage-Dickey ratios nested models.

heading: 11.7 R Implementation
code:
library(BayesFactor)

# Simple example: one-sample t-test equivalent
x <- rnorm(40, mean = 0.5, sd = 1)
ttestBF(x)

# Regression
data(mtcars)
full <- lmBF(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars)
reduced <- lmBF(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)
full / reduced

heading: 11.8 Output & Results
text: BayesFactor package returns Bayes factor estimated error percentage. regression-type models output also includes implied posterior model probabilities; ratios BFBayesFactor objects deliver pairwise comparisons directly.

heading: 11.9 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “default-prior \(t\)-test Bayes factor \(\mathrm{BF}_{10} = 4.7\) (\(\pm 0.001 \%\)), interpreted substantial evidence population mean differs zero.” reporting, always include prior (BayesFactor) prior scale parameter rscale; readers otherwise judge much evidence data-driven versus prior-driven.

heading: 11.10 Practical Tips
text: Wider priors mechanically shift Bayes factors toward null (Lindley–Bartlett paradox); sensitivity analysis across least two prior scales mandatory Bayes-factor conclusion.bridgesampling::bridge_sampler() works directly brms Stan fits; general way compute marginal likelihoods non-trivial models.nested null hypotheses, Savage-Dickey density ratio faster, stable, avoids marginal-likelihood estimation entirely.Bayes factors near 1 genuinely inconclusive; resist temptation declare evidence \(\mathrm{BF} = 1.5\) either direction.Bayes factors substitute predictive performance; pair loo() comparing models prediction, since two answer different questions.

heading: 11.14 Introduction
text: Bayesian decision-making effects splits two cultural traditions complement rather replace . Bayes-factor school computes ratio marginal likelihoods reads strength evidence Jeffreys’ scale. Region Practical Equivalence (ROPE) school, popularised Kruschke, defines range effects practically null asks much posterior lies inside . produce decisions hypotheses, answer slightly different questions: Bayes factors compare models, ROPE asks whether data ruled substantively negligible effect.

heading: 11.15 Prerequisites
text: working understanding posterior distributions, credible intervals, marginal likelihoods, difference statistical significance practical importance.

heading: 11.16 Theory
text: ROPE. Specify, looking data, parameter range \([, b]\) inside effect considered practically zero — example \(\beta \(-0.1, 0.1)\) standardised scale. fitting, compute proportion 95 % HPD (full posterior) inside range. Conventional rules: \(> 95 \%\) inside means accept null practical purposes, \(0 \%\) inside means reject , intermediate values inconclusive.Bayes factor. Ratio marginal likelihoods two models; point null nested continuous alternative, Savage-Dickey density ratio gives answer ratio posterior prior densities null. quantities depend prior alternative, disclosing prior part report.

text: ROPE assumes domain-relevant equivalence margin set advance; chasing margins seeing posterior Bayesian variant \(p\)-hacking. Bayes factors assume proper priors likelihood admits stable marginal-likelihood computation.

heading: 11.18 R Implementation
code:
library(bayestestR); library(brms)

set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(y = rnorm(200), x = rnorm(200))
fit <- brm(y ~ x, data = d, chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)

rope(fit, range = c(-0.1, 0.1))
bayesfactor_parameters(fit)
describe_posterior(fit)

heading: 11.19 Output & Results
text: rope() returns percentage HPD (full posterior, depending chosen ci) inside equivalence region parameter. bayesfactor_parameters() returns Savage-Dickey Bayes factor point null. describe_posterior() one-stop summary combines two point estimates credible intervals.

heading: 11.25 Introduction
text: Bayesian inference , mechanically, application Bayes’ theorem parameter rather event. prior encodes whatever believed data arrived, likelihood data-generating model evaluated observed data, posterior updated belief. conceptual leap frequentist statistics small consequential: parameters random quantities whose posterior distribution can summarised, predicted , acted upon, without invoking long-run sampling thought experiments.

heading: 11.26 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Bayes’ theorem events, concept likelihood function, difference probability density probability mass.

heading: 11.27 Theory
text: parameter \(\theta\) data \(y\), Bayes’ theorem reads\[p(\theta \mid y) = \frac{p(y \mid \theta) p(\theta)}{p(y)} \propto p(y \mid \theta) p(\theta),\]four quantities posterior, likelihood, prior, marginal likelihood \(p(y) = \int p(y \mid \theta) p(\theta) \, \mathrm d \theta\). marginal likelihood appears normalisation constant single-model perspective plays central role Bayes-factor-based model comparison. closed-form (conjugate) priors, posterior comes family prior updated hyperparameters; otherwise MCMC produces draws un-normalised right-hand side.

heading: 11.28 Assumptions
text: prior reflects genuine pre-data information defensible weak-regularisation choice, likelihood correctly captures data-generating process, posterior integrability established (proper priors guarantee ; improper priors must checked).

heading: 11.29 R Implementation
code:
# Beta-Binomial: prior Beta(2, 2), 8 successes in 10 trials
prior_a <- 2; prior_b <- 2
k <- 8; n <- 10
post_a <- prior_a + k
post_b <- prior_b + n - k

theta <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 400)
plot(theta, dbeta(theta, prior_a, prior_b), type = "l",
     main = "Prior -> Posterior", ylab = "Density", col = "grey")
lines(theta, dbeta(theta, k + 1, n - k + 1), col = "orange")  # likelihood scaled
lines(theta, dbeta(theta, post_a, post_b), col = "steelblue", lwd = 2)
legend("topleft", c("Prior", "Likelihood", "Posterior"),
       col = c("grey", "orange", "steelblue"), lty = 1)

# Posterior summaries
c(mean = post_a / (post_a + post_b),
  ci95 = qbeta(c(0.025, 0.975), post_a, post_b))

heading: 11.30 Output & Results

heading: 11.38 Theory

Two parameterisations common: fixed-effects parameterisation weakly informative priors contrasts, hierarchical parameterisation \(\alpha_i \sim \mathcal N(0, \tau^2)\) pools group estimates toward grand mean. hierarchical version preferable group counts large groups represent samples broader population. Pairwise contrasts linear combination \(\mu_i\) computed level MCMC draws, draw produces contrast value posterior contrast empirical distribution values.

heading: 11.49 Theory

Smaller groups rely population mean; larger groups stand data. formal expression “shrinkage” “borrowing strength.”

heading: 11.50 Assumptions

heading: 11.51 R Implementation
code:
library(brms)

# 30 hospitals, events and trials per hospital
set.seed(2026)
n_hosp <- 30
n_per  <- sample(5:50, n_hosp, replace = TRUE)
true_rates <- rbeta(n_hosp, 3, 12)
events <- rbinom(n_hosp, n_per, true_rates)

d <- data.frame(hospital = factor(1:n_hosp),
                events = events, trials = n_per)

fit <- brm(events | trials(trials) ~ 1 + (1 | hospital),
           data = d, family = binomial,
           chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)
summary(fit)

heading: 11.52 Output & Results

heading: 11.53 Interpretation

heading: 11.58 Introduction
text: Mediation analysis decomposes treatment effect part flows intermediate variable (mediator) part . Frequentist approaches build confidence intervals around product \(a_1 b_2\) via Sobel tests non-parametric bootstrapping; compensate fact product two normally distributed estimates non-normal. Bayesian formulation sidesteps issue: joint posterior \((a_1, b_2)\) sampled directly, indirect effect computed draw--draw product, quantile resulting posterior valid credible interval without distributional assumptions.

heading: 11.59 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multiple regression, concept mediation causal inference, Bayesian regression. Familiarity multivariate brms formulas accelerates implementation step.

heading: 11.60 Theory
text: classical Baron-Kenny system two regressions\[M = a_0 + a_1 X + \varepsilon_M, \qquad Y = b_0 + b_1 X + b_2 M + \varepsilon_Y,\]errors typically taken Normal. direct effect \(X\) \(Y\) \(b_1\), indirect effect \(M\) \(a_1 b_2\), total effect \(b_1 + a_1 b_2\). Bayesian formulation, two equations fitted jointly posterior carries joint uncertainty \((a_1, b_2)\). indirect-effect posterior empirical distribution \(\{a_1^{(s)} b_2^{(s)}\}_{s=1}^S\); credible interval comes quantiles. symmetry assumption needed posterior captures skew induced product.

heading: 11.61 Assumptions
text: Linearity equations, Normal residuals (whatever family specify), unmeasured confounders \(M \Y\) relation, correctly ordered causal direction \(X \M \Y\). Mediation analysis causal claim; Bayesian machinery make weak design choices stronger.

heading: 11.62 R Implementation
code:
library(brms)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
X <- rnorm(n)
M <- 0.5 * X + rnorm(n)
Y <- 0.3 * X + 0.6 * M + rnorm(n)
d <- data.frame(X, M, Y)

# Joint model using brms multivariate syntax
bf1 <- bf(M ~ X)
bf2 <- bf(Y ~ X + M)

fit <- brm(bf1 + bf2 + set_rescor(FALSE), data = d,
           chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)

post <- as_draws_df(fit)
indirect <- post$b_M_X * post$b_Y_M
quantile(indirect, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))

heading: 11.63 Output & Results
text: multivariate brms fit jointly estimates two regressions stores draws . Computing elementwise product posterior draws \(a_1\) \(b_2\) produces indirect-effect posterior; quantiles posterior give credible interval directly. total effect, direct effect, proportion mediated come draw matrix without re-fitting.

heading: 11.64 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Bayesian mediation estimated indirect effect \(X\) \(Y\) \(M\) 0.30 (95 % credible interval 0.18 0.45) direct effect 0.28 (95 % CrI 0.14 0.42); indirect path explained approximately 52 % total effect (95 % CrI 35 % 68 %).” Always report direct indirect effects credible intervals; reporting indirect product hides whether direct path remains substantive.

heading: 11.65 Practical Tips
text: Use multivariate brms formulas (bf(M ~ X) + bf(Y ~ X + M) + set_rescor(FALSE)) two regressions fitted jointly joint posterior preserved; running separately throws away cross-equation correlation needed honest indirect-effect intervals.Place priors regression’s coefficients individually; weakly informative defaults (\(\mathcal N(0, 2.5)\) standardised scales) usually appropriate.Sensitivity analysis unmeasured confounding important choice estimator; consider causalsens Bayesian instrumental-variable extensions assumption weak.binary count mediators / outcomes, change family corresponding bf() indirect-effect computation generalises; just use posterior_epred() translate natural-effects scale.Reporting proportion mediated (\(a_1 b_2 / (b_1 + a_1 b_2)\)) requires care denominator can small change sign across draws; truncate report indirect total effects separately proportion unstable.

heading: 11.69 Introduction
text: Meta-analysis \(k\) studies awkward frequentist random-effects estimators \(k\) small: REML DerSimonian-Laird rely asymptotic approximations break five six studies contribute, -study variance \(\tau^2\) can collapse zero wildly imprecise. Bayesian formulation puts explicit prior \(\tau\) propagates uncertainty pooled effect prediction interval new study, exactly inferential question drives meta-analyses clinical biomedical work.

heading: 11.70 Prerequisites
text: working understanding fixed- random-effects meta-analysis, exchangeability assumptions, hierarchical Bayesian models. Familiarity brms formula syntax accelerates implementation step.

heading: 11.71 Theory
text: hierarchical model \[y_i \sim \mathcal N(\theta_i, s_i^2), \qquad \theta_i \sim \mathcal N(\mu, \tau^2),\]\(y_i\) \(s_i\) observed effect estimate standard error study \(\), \(\theta_i\) latent study-specific effect, \(\mu\) population effect, \(\tau\) -study standard deviation. Typical priors weakly informative: \(\mu \sim \mathcal N(0, 2)\) standardised-mean-difference scale \(\tau \sim \mathrm{half\text{-}Normal}(0, 0.5)\) \(\mathrm{half\text{-}Cauchy}(0, 0.3)\). half-Cauchy gives heavier tail favoured large heterogeneity plausible. posterior delivers \(\mu\), \(\tau\), every \(\theta_i\) shrunk toward \(\mu\), predictive distribution \(\mathcal N(\mu, \tau^2)\) next study.

heading: 11.72 Assumptions
text: Exchangeable studies, accurate within-study standard errors \(s_i\), proper priors. within-study sampling distribution taken known; \(s_i\) estimated patients, additional level hierarchy needed.

heading: 11.73 R Implementation
code:
library(brms)

studies <- data.frame(
  yi = c(0.2, 0.3, -0.05, 0.4, 0.25),
  sei = c(0.10, 0.15, 0.08, 0.12, 0.20)
)

fit <- brm(yi | se(sei) ~ 1 + (1 | seq_len(nrow(studies))),
           data = studies,
           prior = prior(normal(0, 1), class = "Intercept") +
                   prior(cauchy(0, 0.5), class = "sd"),
           chains = 4, iter = 4000, refresh = 0)

summary(fit)

heading: 11.74 Output & Results

heading: 11.75 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Bayesian random-effects meta-analysis half-Cauchy(0, 0.5) prior \(\tau\) pooled five studies effect 0.23 (95 % credible interval 0.07 0.39); -study heterogeneity small (\(\tau = 0.09\), 95 % CrI 0.01 0.25), 95 % predictive interval future trial \(-0.05\) \(0.51\).” Reporting predictive interval together pooled estimate avoids false sense precision credible interval \(\mu\) alone can convey.

heading: 11.80 Introduction
text: Bayesian mixed models treat random-effect variance components parameters priors instead plug-estimates REML. pay-visible small samples boundary: ML REML routinely return zero variance estimates handful clusters contribute, half-Normal half-Cauchy prior keeps variance away zero principled way propagates resulting uncertainty every fixed-effect interval. cost computational — sampling instead optimising — analysis depends credible variance components, cost worthwhile investment.

heading: 11.81 Prerequisites
text: working understanding frequentist mixed-effects models (lme4-style formulas), Bayesian regression, role half-distributions priors standard deviations.

heading: 11.82 Theory
text: random-intercept--slope model form\[y_{ij} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{ij} + u_{0i} + u_{1i} X_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij},\]\(\bigl(u_{0i}, u_{1i}\bigr) \sim \mathcal N(0, \Sigma)\) \(\varepsilon_{ij} \sim \mathcal N(0, \sigma^2)\). Priors placed every component: weakly informative Normal priors \(\beta\), half-Normal half-Cauchy diagonal \(\Sigma\), LKJ prior correlation, exponential half-\(t\) \(\sigma\). LKJ\((\eta)\) prior \(\eta > 1\) pulls correlations toward zero gently still allowing strong correlations data demand . Posterior draws \(u_i\) shrunk toward zero amount depends within-cluster signal--noise — partial-pooling behaviour makes mixed models attractive first place.

heading: 11.83 Assumptions
text: Conditional independence observations given random effects, exchangeability clusters, approximately Normal random effects prior structure , proper priors every variance correlation parameter. Convergence diagnostics (\(\hat R\), divergent transitions, ESS) part assumptions practice.

heading: 11.84 R Implementation
code:
library(brms)

data(sleepstudy, package = "lme4")

fit <- brm(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject), data = sleepstudy,
           prior = prior(normal(0, 30), class = "b") +
                   prior(normal(250, 50), class = "Intercept") +
                   prior(exponential(0.1), class = "sd") +
                   prior(exponential(0.1), class = "sigma") +
                   prior(lkj(2), class = "cor"),
           chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)
summary(fit)

# Visualise random effects
ranef(fit)

heading: 11.85 Output & Results

heading: 11.86 Interpretation

heading: 11.91 Introduction
text: beta-binomial conjugate pair textbook entry point Bayesian inference: Beta prior probability combined binomial likelihood yields Beta posterior whose parameters obtained adding successes one shape parameter failures . closed form makes entire workflow — prior elicitation, posterior, credible interval, posterior predictive — tractable calculator. also conceptual backbone hierarchical models proportions, use Beta prior population level share strength across many groups.

heading: 11.92 Prerequisites
text: Familiarity Beta distribution flexible density \([0, 1]\), binomial likelihood independent Bernoulli trials, basic mechanics Bayesian updating.

heading: 11.93 Theory
text: prior \(\theta \sim \mathrm{Beta}(, b)\) likelihood \(X \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n, \theta)\), posterior \[\theta \mid x \sim \mathrm{Beta}(+ x, b + n - x).\]shape parameters prior direct interpretation pseudo-counts: \(\) pseudo-successes \(b\) pseudo-failures observed data arrived, prior effective sample size \(+ b\). posterior mean \[\mathbb E[\theta \mid x] = \frac{+ x}{+ b + n},\]weighted average prior mean \(/ (+b)\) sample proportion \(x / n\), weights determined prior pseudo-count actual sample size. Conjugacy also gives closed-form expressions posterior variance, credible intervals, predictive distribution future Bernoulli trials.

heading: 11.94 Assumptions
text: Independent Bernoulli trials constant success probability \(\theta\), Beta prior plausible enough tails exclude reasonable values, sample size fixed advance — informative censoring stopping rules invalidate binomial likelihood.

heading: 11.95 R Implementation
code:
# Prior Beta(2, 2) -- weakly informative around 0.5
# Data: 15 successes in 25 trials
a <- 2; b <- 2
x <- 15; n <- 25

post_a <- a + x
post_b <- b + n - x

c(prior_mean = a / (a + b),
  sample_prop = x / n,
  posterior_mean = post_a / (post_a + post_b),
  ci95 = qbeta(c(0.025, 0.975), post_a, post_b))

theta <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 400)
plot(theta, dbeta(theta, a, b), type = "l", col = "grey",
     ylab = "Density", main = "Beta-Binomial update")
lines(theta, dbeta(theta, post_a, post_b), col = "steelblue", lwd = 2)

heading: 11.102 Introduction
text: brms widely used high-level interface R Stan. accepts familiar lme4 formula syntax fixed random effects, automatically translates model Stan, compiles , runs -U-Turn Sampler (NUTS), returns posterior together diagnostic predictive helpers. means can fit Bayesian generalised linear, mixed, multinomial, ordinal, censored, distributional, non-linear models without writing Stan hand, still able inspect override generated code situation demands .

heading: 11.103 Prerequisites
text: Comfort classical regression, understanding priors posteriors, working rstan cmdstanr toolchain (C++ compiler required first use). Familiarity lme4 formulas accelerates learning curve dramatically almost every random-effects expression carries unchanged.

heading: 11.104 Theory
text: brms model three pieces: formula (mean structure plus optional distributional terms), family (likelihood link), priors (per parameter class). Random effects use (slope | group) syntax, || uncorrelated terms. Distributional models, set bf(y ~ ..., sigma ~ ...), let parameter family depend predictors. Priors attached class: b population-level slopes, Intercept centred intercept, sd random-effect standard deviations, sigma residual scale, cor correlation matrices via LKJ distribution. Defaults weakly informative reviewed analysis intended publication.

heading: 11.105 Assumptions
text: Inherits assumptions chosen family — gaussian means linearity linear predictor, additivity, Normal residuals partial pooling. Convergence diagnostics (\(\hat R < 1.01\), divergent transitions, sensible effective sample size) also precondition trusting posterior summary.

heading: 11.106 R Implementation
code:
library(brms)

data(sleepstudy, package = "lme4")

fit <- brm(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject),
           data = sleepstudy,
           family = gaussian,
           prior = prior(normal(0, 30), class = "b") +
                   prior(exponential(0.1), class = "sigma") +
                   prior(exponential(0.1), class = "sd") +
                   prior(lkj(2), class = "cor"),
           chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)

summary(fit)
pp_check(fit)

heading: 11.113 Introduction
text: MCMC made arbitrary Bayesian models tractable, conjugate priors provided route tractable posterior. prior conjugate likelihood resulting posterior belongs distributional family prior – prior posterior share form, updated parameters simple closed-form expressions. Conjugate pairs remain pedagogically central make Bayesian update arithmetic, remain practically useful building blocks within larger hierarchical models.

heading: 11.114 Prerequisites
text: reader know Bayes’ theorem comfortable beta, normal, gamma distributions. Familiarity basic R plotting assumed.

heading: 11.115.1 Beta-Binomial
text: binomial observation \(Y \sim \text{Binomial}(n, \theta)\) success probability \(\theta\) beta prior \(\theta \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)\), posterior \[\theta \mid Y = y \;\sim\; \text{Beta}(\alpha + y, \beta + n - y).\]update rule nothing “add successes first shape parameter failures second”. posterior mean \((\alpha + y)/(\alpha + \beta + n)\), weighted average prior mean \(\alpha/(\alpha + \beta)\) observed proportion \(y/n\), weights determined prior’s “effective sample size” \(\alpha + \beta\).

heading: 11.115.2 Normal-Normal with known variance
text: observations \(Y_1, \ldots, Y_n \overset{iid}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\) \(\sigma^2\) known, normal prior \(\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \tau_0^2)\), posterior \[\mu \mid y \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}(\mu_n, \tau_n^2),\]posterior precision \(1/\tau_n^2 = 1/\tau_0^2 + n/\sigma^2\), posterior mean precision-weighted average \(\mu_n = \tau_n^2 (\mu_0/\tau_0^2 + n \bar{y}/\sigma^2)\).

heading: 11.115.3 Gamma-Poisson
text: independent counts \(Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\) gamma prior \(\lambda \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)\) (shape-rate parameterisation), posterior \[\lambda \mid y \;\sim\; \text{Gamma}\!\left(\alpha + \sum y_i,\; \beta + n\right).\]updates can memorised “add data prior parameters”, precise form determined sufficient statistics likelihood.

heading: 11.116 Assumptions
text: Conjugate analyses assume independence distributional conditions corresponding likelihoods. addition, assume prior genuinely expression prior belief, mathematical convenience chosen conjugacy alone. informative conjugate prior contradicts data produces posterior compromise two; intended, prior bad choice.

heading: 11.117 R Implementation
text: visualisation beta-binomial update makes idea concrete:Beta(2, 2) prior (symmetric, weakly informative around 0.5) combined 8 successes 10 trials gives Beta(10, 4) posterior concentrated around 0.71.
code:
library(ggplot2)

alpha_0 <- 2
beta_0  <- 2
y       <- 8
n       <- 10

alpha_n <- alpha_0 + y
beta_n  <- beta_0 + n - y

theta <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 401)
df <- data.frame(
  theta = rep(theta, 3),
  density = c(dbeta(theta, alpha_0, beta_0),
              dbinom(y, n, theta) * n,
              dbeta(theta, alpha_n, beta_n)),
  kind = factor(rep(c("Prior", "Likelihood (scaled)", "Posterior"),
                    each = 401),
                levels = c("Prior", "Likelihood (scaled)", "Posterior"))
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = theta, y = density, colour = kind, linetype = kind)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(x = expression(theta), y = "Density",
       title = "Beta-Binomial update with y = 8 of n = 10") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

heading: 11.118 Output & Results
text: Posterior summaries beta distribution :Mean: \((\alpha + y)/(\alpha + \beta + n) = 10/14 \approx 0.714\)95% equal-tailed credible interval: qbeta(c(0.025, 0.975), 10, 4) \(\approx (0.47, 0.91)\)Probability \(\theta > 0.5\): 1 - pbeta(0.5, 10, 4) \(\approx 0.95\).

heading: 11.119 Interpretation
text: posterior conjugate model approximation; exact posterior stated prior. Bayesian reporting analysis write: “weakly informative \(\text{Beta}(2, 2)\) prior data 8 successes 10 trials, posterior \(\theta\) \(\text{Beta}(10, 4)\), posterior mean 0.71 (95% CrI 0.47 0.91). posterior probability \(\theta\) exceeds 0.5 0.95.”

heading: 11.123 Introduction
text: credible interval Bayesian summary posterior uncertainty often reported alongside point estimate. Unlike confidence interval, whose probability statement refers long-run frequency hypothetical resampling, credible interval supports direct claim “given data prior, 95 % probability parameter lies inside interval.” single sentence reason students consistently misinterpret confidence intervals — reason credible intervals increasingly preferred applied biomedical reporting.

heading: 11.124 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Bayesian inference, posterior distributions, distinction posterior sampling distribution.

heading: 11.125 Theory
text: Two definitions dominate practice:Equal-tailed interval (ETI): interval bounded \(\alpha/2\) \(1 - \alpha/2\) quantiles posterior. invariant monotone reparameterisation easy compute MCMC draws.Highest posterior density (HPD) interval: narrowest set \(1 - \alpha\) posterior mass; unimodal posteriors single interval centred region highest density, always least short ETI.symmetric posteriors two intervals coincide; skewed posteriors can differ markedly. 95 % level conventional arbitrary — Bayesian methodologists advocate 89 % discourage misreading threshold hypothesis test.

heading: 11.126 Assumptions
text: proper posterior distribution either MCMC draws (post-warm-, adequate effective sample size) closed-form posterior. ETI endpoints quantiles tolerate small samples better HPD endpoints, depend density estimation tails.

heading: 11.127 R Implementation
code:
library(HDInterval)

set.seed(2026)
theta <- rbeta(10000, 10, 4)

# Equal-tailed
quantile(theta, c(0.025, 0.975))

# HPD
hdi(theta, credMass = 0.95)

heading: 11.128 Output & Results
text: quantile() returns ETI; hdi() returns HPD. Beta(10, 4) posterior ETI approximately \((0.50, 0.90)\) HPD approximately \((0.53, 0.93)\) — slightly shifted toward right posterior left-skewed HPD chases highest density region rather splitting tail probability evenly.

heading: 11.129 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “posterior probability 95 % equal-tailed credible interval 0.50 0.90; highest-posterior-density interval 0.53 0.93. 95 % posterior probability true proportion falls either interval, given prior observed data.” Always state interval reported; mixing definitions across tables paper common reproducibility headache.

heading: 11.130 Practical Tips
text: Report ETIs symmetric near-symmetric posteriors; familiar reparameterisation-invariant.Report HPDs skewed posteriors additional precision matters density estimate stable.HPD intervals can disjoint multimodal posteriors; set allowSplit = TRUE HDInterval::hdi() structure preserved.Disclose prior reporting sentence; credible interval property data alone.Use post-warm-MCMC draws check ESS tails — credible-interval endpoints depend quantile estimation, needs effective draws mean.

heading: 11.135 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, leapfrog integration, geometric origins divergent transitions; familiarity bayesplot::mcmc_pairs() visualising joint posteriors.

heading: 11.136 Theory
text: leapfrog integrator approximates Hamiltonian dynamics finite step size \(\epsilon\). regions high curvature (typically funnels hierarchical models, small variance parameter shrinks dependent effects), even moderate \(\epsilon\) produces Hamiltonian error large enough proposal biased. Stan flags proposals divergent refuses use , chain effectively avoids problem region instead exploring . classical remedy non-centred reparameterisation: replacing \(\beta_i \sim \mathcal N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) \(\tilde z_i \sim \mathcal N(0, 1)\) \(\beta_i = \mu + \sigma \tilde z_i\) decouples funnel geometry makes posterior easier sample.

heading: 11.137 Assumptions
text: HMC NUTS sampling; divergent-transition flag specific algorithms. Random-walk samplers produce divergences suffer different pathologies (slow mixing, mode-trapping) need separate diagnostics.

heading: 11.138 R Implementation
code:
library(brms); library(bayesplot)

data(sleepstudy, package = "lme4")

fit <- brm(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject), data = sleepstudy,
           chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0,
           control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99))

# Divergence diagnostics
rstan::check_hmc_diagnostics(fit$fit)

# Pairs plot of parameters with divergences coloured
np <- nuts_params(fit)
mcmc_pairs(as.array(fit), pars = c("b_Days", "sd_Subject__Days"),
           np = np, np_style = pairs_style_np(div_color = "red"))

heading: 11.145 Introduction
text: Gamma–Poisson conjugate pair count-data analogue Beta–Binomial model: Gamma prior Poisson rate yields Gamma posterior whose parameters obtained adding observed event count observed exposure prior shape rate respectively. natural starting point whenever inferring event rate per unit time, area, population, generalises directly Poisson regression predictors enter linear predictor.

heading: 11.146 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Poisson distribution, Gamma distribution, concept conjugacy.

heading: 11.147 Theory
text: prior \(\lambda \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_0, \beta_0)\) likelihood \(y_i \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda t_i)\) \(t_i\) exposure observation \(\), posterior \[\lambda \mid y \sim \mathrm{Gamma}\!\left(\alpha_0 + \sum_{} y_i, \, \beta_0 + \sum_{} t_i\right).\]prior shape \(\alpha_0\) behaves like pseudo-count events prior rate \(\beta_0\) like pseudo-exposure, \(\mathrm{Gamma}(2, 1)\) prior equivalent two prior events observed one unit exposure. posterior mean \((\alpha_0 + \sum y_i) / (\beta_0 + \sum t_i)\) posterior variance posterior mean divided posterior rate. Poisson–Gamma compound distribution Negative-Binomial, gives posterior predictive distribution closed form.

heading: 11.148 Assumptions
text: Independent Poisson counts conditional rate, exposure \(t_i\) known estimated data, constant rate observation window (conditioning covariates). Overdispersion relative Poisson — variance exceeding mean — motivates Negative-Binomial likelihood instead.

heading: 11.149 R Implementation
code:
# Prior Gamma(2, 1) -- 2 events per 1 unit of exposure
alpha0 <- 2; beta0 <- 1

# Data: 15 events in 10 units of exposure
y <- 15; t <- 10

alpha_n <- alpha0 + y
beta_n  <- beta0 + t

c(posterior_mean = alpha_n / beta_n,
  posterior_sd   = sqrt(alpha_n / beta_n^2),
  ci95 = qgamma(c(0.025, 0.975), alpha_n, beta_n))

heading: 11.150 Output & Results
text: Closed-form summaries: posterior mean approximately 1.55 events per unit exposure, posterior SD approximately 0.38, 95 % equal-tailed credible interval roughly \((0.94, 2.28)\). posterior right-skewed Gamma densities heavier right tail left, report median HPD asymmetry matters.

heading: 11.151 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “\(\mathrm{Gamma}(2, 1)\) prior combined 15 events 10 units exposure yielded \(\mathrm{Gamma}(17, 11)\) posterior mean 1.55 events per unit exposure (95 % credible interval 0.94 2.28); prior contributed equivalent two pseudo-events one pseudo-unit exposure, data overwhelmed.” Quote posterior mean credible interval, note skew.

heading: 11.152 Practical Tips
text: Weakly informative defaults: \(\mathrm{Gamma}(0.5, 0.5)\) \(\mathrm{Gamma}(1, 1)\); latter prior mean 1 prior variance 1.Poisson regression, place priors regression coefficients log-rate scale (typically \(\mathcal N(0, 1)\) \(\mathcal N(0, 2.5)\)) rather rate ; linear predictor naturally constrained.posterior predictive distribution Negative-Binomial: \(\tilde y \mid y \sim \mathrm{NegBinom}\bigl(\alpha_n, t_{\text{new}} / (\beta_n + t_{\text{new}})\bigr)\), prediction intervals come free without simulation.Persistent overdispersion fitting motivates Negative-Binomial likelihood separate dispersion parameter; Gamma–Poisson conjugate root.zero-inflation (zeros Poisson predicts), augment zero-inflation component rather tightening prior.

heading: 11.156 Introduction
text: Gibbs sampling one oldest practical MCMC algorithms remains instructive special case general samplers. exploits structural property multidimensional posteriors: even joint distribution intractable, conditional distribution one parameter given others can often derived closed form. Cycling parameters updating full conditional generates Markov chain whose stationary distribution joint posterior, acceptance step tuning proposals.

heading: 11.157 Prerequisites
text: working understanding MCMC, conditional probability, conjugate-prior structure makes full conditionals tractable canonical models (Normal–Normal, Beta–Binomial, Gamma–Poisson, Dirichlet–Multinomial).

heading: 11.158 Theory
text: joint posterior \(p(\theta_1, \dots, \theta_K \mid y)\), algorithm initialises \(\theta^{(0)}\) , iteration \(t\), sweeps parameters order:\[\theta_k^{(t)} \sim p\bigl(\theta_k \mid \theta_1^{(t)}, \dots, \theta_{k-1}^{(t)}, \theta_{k+1}^{(t-1)}, \dots, \theta_K^{(t-1)}, y\bigr)\]\(k = 1, \dots, K\). standard regularity conditions, chain irreducible aperiodic converges joint posterior. update draw one-dimensional low-dimensional conditional, often via standard distribution libraries; Metropolis acceptance needed proposal conditional. conditionals available analytically, affected blocks updated Metropolis step embedded inside Gibbs sweep (“Metropolis-within-Gibbs”).

heading: 11.159 Assumptions
text: Every full conditional must either recognisable distribution distribution one can sample Metropolis step. Strongly correlated parameters update slowly coordinate-wise sweep; central pathology Gibbs reason HMC now preferred general continuous models.

heading: 11.160 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Conjugate normal-normal model: mu and sigma2 both unknown
# Priors: mu ~ N(mu0, tau0^2); 1/sigma2 ~ Gamma(alpha, beta)
y <- rnorm(50, 3, 2)
n <- length(y)
mu0 <- 0; tau0 <- 10
alpha <- 2; beta <- 1

n_iter <- 5000
mu_chain    <- numeric(n_iter)
sig2_chain  <- numeric(n_iter)
sig2_chain[1] <- var(y)

for (t in 2:n_iter) {
  # Sample mu | sigma^2, y
  prec <- 1/tau0^2 + n/sig2_chain[t-1]
  mean_mu <- (mu0/tau0^2 + sum(y)/sig2_chain[t-1]) / prec
  mu_chain[t] <- rnorm(1, mean_mu, sqrt(1/prec))

  # Sample sigma^2 | mu, y  (inverse gamma)
  alpha_n <- alpha + n/2
  beta_n  <- beta + sum((y - mu_chain[t])^2)/2
  sig2_chain[t] <- 1/rgamma(1, alpha_n, beta_n)
}

# Summaries
c(mean_mu = mean(mu_chain[-(1:1000)]),
  mean_sigma = mean(sqrt(sig2_chain[-(1:1000)])))

heading: 11.161 Output & Results
text: chain produces 5,000 draws \(\mu\) \(\sigma^2\). Discarding first 1,000 burn-summarising rest gives posterior means \(\mu\) near 3.1 \(\sigma\) near 2.0 — close data-generating parameters within expected Monte Carlo error.

heading: 11.162 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Gibbs sampler conjugate Normal–Inverse-Gamma model produced 4,000 post-burn-draws posterior mean \(\mu = 3.10\) (95 % credible interval 2.55 3.65) posterior mean \(\sigma = 2.01\) (95 % CrI 1.65 2.49).” pedagogical examples, also report rejection rate (Gibbs none) autocorrelation function readers can see per-iteration efficiency.

heading: 11.163 Practical Tips
text: Block-update correlated parameters jointly conditional available closed form; reduces autocorrelation dramatically compared coordinate-wise updates.non-conjugate components, embed Metropolis step (Metropolis-within-Gibbs); JAGS automatically resolve node analytically.Check convergence \(\hat R\) across multiple chains trace plots; single chain reveal mode-trapping.continuous, smooth posteriors moderate--high dimension, HMC (Stan) typically mixes far better Gibbs follows gradient instead cycling coordinates.Gibbs remains algorithm choice discrete-state-space models, latent-allocation samplers, setting conjugacy makes single sweep almost free.

heading: 11.167 Introduction
text: Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) current state art sampling continuous Bayesian posteriors moderate--high dimension. random-walk Metropolis takes small, undirected steps Gibbs cycles coordinates, HMC uses gradient information propose long-distance moves almost always land regions high posterior density. -U-Turn Sampler (NUTS), Stan’s default, automates choice trajectory length users need tune step counts hand. result mixing rates orders magnitude better coordinate-wise samplers posteriors strong correlations.

heading: 11.168 Prerequisites
text: working understanding MCMC, basic calculus (gradients), relationship log-posterior gradients geometry posterior surface. Familiarity Stan brms makes implementation step trivial.

heading: 11.169 Theory
text: HMC introduces auxiliary momentum variables \(p\) samples joint distribution \(p(\theta, p) = p(\theta \mid y) \mathcal N(p \mid 0, M)\), \(M\) mass matrix. Hamiltonian dynamics conserve joint Hamiltonian \(H(\theta, p) = -\log p(\theta \mid y) + \tfrac{1}{2} p^T M^{-1} p\), trajectories simulated leapfrog integration drift level sets constant log-density explore posterior efficiently. \(L\) leapfrog steps step size \(\epsilon\), proposal accepted Metropolis correction compensates integrator error. NUTS replaces manual choice \(L\) “-U-turn” criterion terminates trajectory starts doubling back, eliminating error-prone tuning parameter plain HMC.

heading: 11.170 Assumptions
text: continuous, almost-everywhere differentiable log-posterior. Discrete parameters must marginalised . mass matrix approximate posterior covariance — Stan adapts warm-— step size must small enough keep integrator error check.

heading: 11.171 R Implementation
code:
library(brms)

set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(x = rnorm(100))
d$y <- 2 + 1.5 * d$x + rnorm(100)

fit <- brm(y ~ x, data = d,
           chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0,
           control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95, max_treedepth = 10))

summary(fit)
# Diagnostics: R-hat, divergences, energy
rstan::check_hmc_diagnostics(fit$fit)

heading: 11.172 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) reports posterior summaries \(\hat R\), ESS, credible intervals. rstan::check_hmc_diagnostics() reports divergent transitions, tree-depth saturation, energy diagnostics (E-BFMI), first-line check geometry navigable. Healthy HMC delivers \(\hat R\) near 1.00, divergent transitions, ESS substantial fraction post-warm-draws.

heading: 11.173 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “HMC/NUTS produced 4,000 post-warm-draws across four chains divergent transitions \(\hat R = 1.00\) every parameter; slope posterior 1.52 (95 % credible interval 1.31 1.74).” divergences occur, report describe remedy (reparameterisation, raising adapt_delta); divergent transition missed region posterior, tuning curiosity.

heading: 11.174 Practical Tips
text: hierarchical models funnel geometry, switch non-centred parameterisation; brms automatically specify prior(... , class = "sd", ...).Raising adapt_delta 0.95 0.99 reduces divergences cost smaller step sizes slower sampling; reparameterisation usually better fix.Tree-depth saturation (treedepth__ hitting max_treedepth) signals NUTS turn around within budget — increase max_treedepth simplify model.Reparameterise constrained variables onto unconstrained scales can: log(sigma) rather sigma > 0 keeps leapfrog integrator stable.HMC scales thousands parameters Gibbs random-walk MH unworkable, rescue ill-conditioned posterior; geometry matters algorithm.

heading: 11.178 Introduction
text: highest posterior density (HPD) interval narrowest range contains chosen probability mass — typically 95 % — posterior distribution. Two intervals can cover 95 % posterior, yet HPD always least short equal-tailed interval (ETI) include parameter values whose density threshold. unimodal posteriors two intervals similar; skewed bimodal posteriors can disagree dramatically, choice shapes readers perceive result.

heading: 11.179 Prerequisites
text: working understanding posterior distributions credible intervals, access MCMC draws (via brms, rstanarm, raw Stan, sampler).

heading: 11.180 Theory
text: posterior \(p(\theta \mid y)\), \((1-\alpha)\) HPD region \[\{\theta : p(\theta \mid y) \ge h\}, \quad \text{} h \text{ } P(\theta \\text{region}) = 1 - \alpha.\]unimodal posterior region single interval centred near mode; multimodal posterior can union disjoint intervals, one per mode exceeds threshold. ETI, contrast, places tail probability \(\alpha/2\) tail, skewed posterior shifts toward heavier tail wider. HPD therefore favours shape posterior; ETI favours interpretability invariance monotone transformations.

heading: 11.181 Assumptions
text: Adequate MCMC draws (warm-, \(\hat R\) near 1.00) closed-form posterior density. HPD sensitive noisy density estimates tails, short chains can yield unstable interval endpoints.

heading: 11.182 R Implementation
code:
library(HDInterval)

set.seed(2026)
theta <- c(rbeta(5000, 5, 15), rbeta(5000, 15, 5))  # bimodal

# Equal-tailed (may span both modes)
quantile(theta, c(0.025, 0.975))

# HPD (disjoint regions)
hdi(theta, credMass = 0.95, allowSplit = TRUE)

heading: 11.183 Output & Results
text: hdi() returns numeric vector two endpoints HPD single interval, matrix begin / end pairs allowSplit = TRUE posterior multimodal. quantile() returns ETI direct comparison; gap ETI HPD widths direct numerical signal posterior asymmetry.

heading: 11.184 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “95 % HPD interval \(\theta\) \((0.13, 0.38) \cup (0.60, 0.89)\), reflecting bimodal posterior; equal-tailed interval \((0.13, 0.89)\) obscures structure including low-density valley modes.” posterior unimodal roughly symmetric, two intervals agree choice matter; disagree, prefer one whose definition matches inferential question — HPD “posterior concentrate?” ETI “range values 2.5 % / truth?”

heading: 11.185 Practical Tips
text: Set allowSplit = TRUE whenever multimodality plausible; single interval reported across modes can misleading.HPD invariant non-linear reparameterisation: HPD \(\sigma^2\) general square HPD \(\sigma\), ETI quantile-based interval . Decide reporting scale computing interval.bounded parameters (probabilities, variances) HPD often touches boundary; report explicitly readers know posterior symmetric .Use post-warm-MCMC draws check chains mixed; HPD endpoints noisier means, bigger effective sample sizes help.bayestestR::hdi() provides similar interface integrates model objects brms, rstanarm, posterior.

heading: 11.189 Introduction
text: informative prior encodes genuine pre-data knowledge: quantitative summary previous studies, mechanistic constraint plausible parameter must respect, structured elicitation domain experts. Done well, informative priors stabilise inference small samples, integrate evidence across studies, let single analysis quantify new data update already known. Done badly, paper weak data make conclusions look certain . discipline explicit source, justify choice quantitatively, run sensitivity analysis whenever prior meaningful work.

heading: 11.190 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Bayesian inference, role weakly informative priors default, basic familiarity meta-analytic summaries relevant effect scale.

heading: 11.191 Theory
text: Three classical sources informative priors:Meta-analytic priors condense effect estimates previous trials Normal (log-Normal, \(t\)) prior relevant scale: \(\beta \sim \mathcal N(\bar\beta, \tau^2)\) \(\bar\beta\) \(\tau^2\) come published random-effects meta-analysis.Mechanistic priors derived physical, biological, chemical constraints; example, drug half-lives positive bounded metabolic limits, log-Normal truncated prior appropriate.Expert-elicited priors capture structured beliefs clinicians subject-matter specialists, typically via SHELF protocol similar formal procedure reduce influence single dominant expert.power prior commensurate prior -weights prior contribution conflicts current data — principled way guard unverified historical assumptions.

heading: 11.192 Assumptions
text: prior information relevant current analysis (population, outcome definition, scale) derived data analysed (double-counting). Mechanistic constraints must physically defensible, stylistic.

heading: 11.193 R Implementation
code:
library(brms)

# Meta-analytic prior: previous studies gave OR of 0.8 (95% CI 0.7 to 0.9)
# On log-OR scale: mean -0.223, SD ~ 0.05 (narrow informative prior)
set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(x = rnorm(100), y = rbinom(100, 1, 0.3))

fit_inf <- brm(y ~ x, data = d, family = bernoulli,
               prior = prior(normal(-0.223, 0.05), class = "b", coef = "x"),
               chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)

# Compare with weakly informative
fit_weak <- brm(y ~ x, data = d, family = bernoulli,
                prior = prior(normal(0, 2.5), class = "b"),
                chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)

# Posterior summaries
fixef(fit_inf); fixef(fit_weak)

heading: 11.194 Output & Results
text: fixef(fit_inf) returns posterior mean credible interval meta-analytic prior; fixef(fit_weak) weakly informative prior. two posteriors differ proportion relative precision prior data: \(\mathcal N(-0.223, 0.05)\) prior carries weight roughly \(1 / 0.05^2 = 400\) “pseudo-observations” log-odds scale, dominating small simulated dataset.

heading: 11.195 Interpretation

heading: 11.200 Introduction
text: Jeffreys’ prior classical “objective” “reference” prior Bayesian inference. Defined proportional square root Fisher information, elegant property invariant monotone reparameterisation: prior transformation \(\eta = g(\theta)\) derived Jeffreys prior \(\theta\) equals Jeffreys prior derived directly \(\eta\). invariance — absent flat prior, parameterisation-dependent — historically made Jeffreys’ rule default attempt non-informative prior. modern Bayesian practice, however, weakly informative priors largely displaced regularise small-sample inference reliably behave better hierarchical models.

heading: 11.201 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Fisher information, role reparameterisation inference, difference proper improper priors.

heading: 11.202 Theory
text: scalar parameter \(\theta\) likelihood \(p(y \mid \theta)\), Jeffreys prior \[p(\theta) \propto \sqrt{(\theta)}, \qquad (\theta) = -\mathbb E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \log p(y \mid \theta)}{\partial \theta^2}\right].\]Standard examples:Binomial\((n, \theta)\): \((\theta) \propto 1 / [\theta(1-\theta)]\), Jeffreys prior \(\mathrm{Beta}(\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2})\).Poisson\((\lambda)\): \((\lambda) \propto 1 / \lambda\), \(p(\lambda) \propto \lambda^{-1/2}\).Normal mean (known variance): flat prior \(p(\mu) \propto 1\).Normal scale (known mean): \(p(\sigma) \propto 1 / \sigma\).Jeffreys priors often improper, posterior usually proper long sample size sufficient. multivariate parameters, \((\theta)\) matrix prior \(\sqrt{|(\theta)|}\).

heading: 11.203 Assumptions
text: regular likelihood (twice differentiable \(\theta\), finite Fisher information across parameter space). Boundary problems parameter-dimension growth complicate construction; reference priors (Bernardo) generalise Jeffreys’ rule hierarchical high-dimensional cases.

heading: 11.204 R Implementation
code:
# Binomial: posterior under Jeffreys is Beta(1/2 + x, 1/2 + n - x)
n <- 10; x <- 8
post_a <- 0.5 + x
post_b <- 0.5 + n - x
c(mean = post_a / (post_a + post_b),
  ci95 = qbeta(c(0.025, 0.975), post_a, post_b))

# Compare with uniform Beta(1, 1) and Beta(2, 2)
data.frame(
  prior = c("Uniform(0,1)", "Jeffreys", "Beta(2,2)"),
  post_mean = c((1 + x)/(2 + n), (0.5 + x)/(1 + n), (2 + x)/(4 + n))
)

heading: 11.205 Output & Results
text: Closed-form summaries three priors. 8 successes 10 trials Jeffreys posterior mean approximately 0.77 (vs. 0.75 uniform \(\mathrm{Beta}(1, 1)\) 0.71 informative \(\mathrm{Beta}(2, 2)\)), 95 % credible interval roughly \((0.47, 0.94)\). comparison highlights mildly Jeffreys differs flat prior setting.

heading: 11.206 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Jeffreys’ prior \(\mathrm{Beta}(0.5, 0.5)\) success probability combined 8 successes 10 trials yielded posterior mean 0.77 (95 % credible interval 0.47 0.94); result essentially equivalent uniform prior sample size, justifying reference-prior framing.” Jeffreys differs materially weakly informative prior, prior real work explicit chose .

heading: 11.207 Practical Tips
text: invariance property mathematically attractive practice rarely affects applied conclusions; weakly informative priors flexible default modern workflows.Jeffreys priors can behave badly hierarchical models — multivariate version often produces improper posteriors variance components boundary. Reach weakly informative half-Cauchy half-Normal priors instead.Always check resulting posterior proper, especially prior improper; back---envelope check confirm posterior integrates finite quantity.Reference priors (Bernardo) generalise Jeffreys’ rule ordered parameters interest nuisance components; modern theoretical successor objective prior genuinely required.pedagogical purposes, Jeffreys’ prior invaluable makes invariance reparameterisation hands-exercise; applied analyses, prefer weakly informative defaults unless invariance publication requirement.

heading: 11.211 Introduction
text: Bayesian linear regression keeps linear predictor Normal residual structure ordinary least squares treats every parameter random variable prior. Inference returns joint posterior coefficients residual scale rather point estimate plus standard errors. practical gain direct: credible intervals carry probability statement students often (incorrectly) attribute confidence intervals, predictions inherit propagated uncertainty automatically, small-sample analyses longer rely asymptotic approximations \(t\) distributions.

heading: 11.212 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multiple linear regression, comfort idea likelihood, basic sense priors. Stan-capable backend (rstan cmdstanr) needed brms compiles models demand; rstanarm ships pre-compiled models compile time concern.

heading: 11.213 Theory
text: model \[y_i = X_i \beta + \varepsilon_i, \qquad \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal N(0, \sigma^2),\]priors \(\beta_j \sim \mathcal N(0, \tau_j)\) \(\sigma \sim \mathrm{Exponential}(\lambda)\) half-\(t\). weakly informative priors reasonable sample size, posterior mean close OLS estimate small-\(n\) inference regularised toward prior. default sampler Hamiltonian Monte Carlo -U-Turn termination criterion (NUTS); convergence assessed \(\hat R\), bulk tail effective sample size, divergent-transition counts.

heading: 11.214 Assumptions
text: Linearity linear predictor, additive Normal residuals constant variance, independence observations, proper priors. Convergence diagnostics part model assumptions practice — chain mixed deliver credible posterior summary.

heading: 11.215 R Implementation
code:
library(brms)

set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(x = rnorm(100), y = 2 + 1.5 * rnorm(100))

fit <- brm(y ~ x, data = d,
           prior = prior(normal(0, 2.5), class = "b") +
                   prior(normal(0, 5),   class = "Intercept") +
                   prior(exponential(1), class = "sigma"),
           chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)

summary(fit)
plot(fit)

heading: 11.216 Output & Results
text: summary(fit) returns posterior mean, standard error mean, posterior standard deviation, 95 % credible interval, \(\hat R\), bulk tail effective sample size every parameter. plot(fit) shows trace plots (one line per chain) alongside marginal density plots; well-mixed chains overlap throughout warm-produce indistinguishable density plots.

heading: 11.217 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Bayesian linear regression weakly informative priors estimated slope 1.53 (95 % CrI 1.23 1.84) residual scale 1.02 (95 % CrI 0.90 1.17); \(\hat R = 1.00\) parameters, \(n_{\text{eff}} > 3{,}500\).” Always report convergence statistic alongside interval — without , reader tell whether posterior summary trustworthy.

heading: 11.223 Prerequisites
text: Familiarity classical logistic regression odds ratios, idea prior posterior , access Stan-capable backend (rstan cmdstanr).

heading: 11.224 Theory
text: model \[y_i \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}\bigl(\mathrm{logit}^{-1}(X_i \beta)\bigr),\]priors \(\beta \sim \mathcal N(0, \sigma_\beta^2)\) regression coefficients separate, often wider prior intercept average log-odds success can lie outside typical predictor scale. \(\mathcal N(0, 2.5)\) prior standardised slopes default rstanarm sensible starting point brms. separation likelihood becomes monotone \(\beta\), proper prior — even wide one — yields well-defined posterior finite credible intervals; Bayesian analogue Firth’s penalised likelihood.

heading: 11.225 Assumptions
text: Independent Bernoulli observations conditional covariates, logit link reasonable approximation true response surface, valid (proper) priors. MLE logistic regression, linear predictor contain redundant columns.

heading: 11.226 R Implementation
code:
library(brms)

set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(x = rnorm(200))
d$y <- rbinom(200, 1, plogis(-1 + 1.2 * d$x))

fit <- brm(y ~ x, data = d, family = bernoulli,
           prior = prior(normal(0, 2.5), class = "b") +
                   prior(normal(0, 5), class = "Intercept"),
           chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)
summary(fit)

# Odds ratios
exp(fixef(fit))

heading: 11.227 Output & Results

heading: 11.233 Introduction
text: Leave-one-cross-validation (LOO-CV) gold standard estimating --sample predictive performance, exact LOO requires refitting model per observation — prohibitive Bayesian model takes seconds sample. Pareto-Smoothed Importance Sampling LOO (PSIS-LOO), introduced Vehtari, Gelman, Gabry, approximates exact LOO single full-data posterior using importance weights, providing per-observation diagnostics flag approximation breaks . become standard model-comparison tool brms, rstanarm, raw Stan workflows.

heading: 11.234 Prerequisites
text: working understanding cross-validation, importance sampling, role log pointwise predictive density (elpd) measuring predictive performance.

heading: 11.235 Theory
text: expected log predictive density leave-one-\[\mathrm{elpd}_{\mathrm{loo}} = \sum_{=1}^n \log p(y_i \mid y_{-}),\]\(y_{-}\) data observation \(\) removed. PSIS approximates \(p(y_i \mid y_{-})\) via importance weights \(w_i^{(s)} \propto 1 / p(y_i \mid \theta^{(s)})\) applied full-data posterior draws \(\theta^{(s)}\). stabilise right tail importance weights, PSIS fits generalised Pareto distribution largest weights replaces order-statistic estimates. shape parameter \(\hat k\) fit acts per-observation diagnostic: \(\hat k < 0.5\) excellent, \(0.5 \le \hat k < 0.7\) acceptable, \(\hat k > 0.7\) indicates PSIS-LOO unreliable point.

heading: 11.236 Assumptions
text: Exchangeable observations posterior sufficient effective sample size estimate importance-weight tail; hierarchical temporal data, replace standard LOO leave-one-cluster-leave-future-variants whose theoretical properties match dependence structure.

heading: 11.237 R Implementation
code:
library(loo); library(brms)

data(mtcars)
fit1 <- brm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars, chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)
fit2 <- brm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)

loo1 <- loo(fit1)
loo2 <- loo(fit2)

loo_compare(loo1, loo2)

# k-hat diagnostic
plot(loo2)

heading: 11.238 Output & Results
text: loo() returns elpd, effective parameter count \(p_{\mathrm{loo}}\), vector per-observation \(\hat k\) values. loo_compare() aligns two loo objects returns elpd difference standard error, ordered best worst. plot(loo) displays \(\hat k\) values observation index, immediately revealing influential points.

heading: 11.239 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “PSIS-LOO favoured two-predictor model \(\Delta\mathrm{elpd} = 6\) (SE 2.3); \(\hat k < 0.5\), importance-sampling approximation reliable, observation needs exact refit.” \(\hat k\) values exceed 0.7, rerun loo(fit, reloo = TRUE) refit model affected leave-folds; elpd estimate hybrid PSIS safe folds exact fits troublesome ones.

heading: 11.240 Practical Tips
text: Check \(\hat k\) values interpreting elpd comparison; single high-leverage observation can dominate ranking.time-series, use leave-future-CV via loo::loo_subsample() loo_moment_match(); standard LOO leaks future information past folds biases elpd downward.hierarchical models clusters, leave-one-cluster-CV appropriate generalisation: answers prediction question matters practice (“model perform new cluster?”).Differences elpd interpretable absolute terms: \(\Delta\mathrm{elpd} > 4 \times \mathrm{SE}\) decisive; \(|\Delta\mathrm{elpd}| < 2 \times \mathrm{SE}\) inconclusive.LOO well-defined non-nested models, unlike likelihood-ratio tests, makes natural tool comparing models differ family, link, predictor set.

heading: 11.244 Introduction
text: Metropolis-Hastings algorithm original general-purpose MCMC sampler remains conceptual reference subsequent algorithms explained. Given unnormalised target density, produces Markov chain whose stationary distribution target repeatedly proposing candidate value accepting rejecting based simple density-ratio rule. trade-universality versus efficiency: MH works target one can evaluate, tuning proposals high-dimensional strongly correlated posteriors becomes painful, HMC displaced random-walk MH modern Bayesian software.

heading: 11.245 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Markov chains, conditional probability, role unnormalised posterior Bayesian inference. Familiarity R’s distribution functions (rnorm, dunif) needed example.

heading: 11.246 Theory
text: Given current state \(\theta\), one iteration MH proceeds :Propose \(\theta^*\) proposal kernel \(q(\theta^* \mid \theta)\).Compute acceptance probability \(\alpha = \min\!\left(1, \dfrac{p(\theta^*) q(\theta \mid \theta^*)}{p(\theta) q(\theta^* \mid \theta)}\right)\).probability \(\alpha\) set next state \(\theta^*\); otherwise keep \(\theta\).symmetric proposal (Gaussian random walk centred current state) proposal terms cancel acceptance ratio simply ratio target densities. Detailed balance ensures chain target invariant distribution; ergodicity (irreducibility plus aperiodicity) ensures convergence regardless starting value.

heading: 11.247 Assumptions
text: unnormalised target density computable everywhere support. proposal kernel must give chain positive probability reaching every part support; otherwise chain reducible never explores full posterior.

heading: 11.248 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Target: Beta(4, 8) (unnormalised density is fine)
log_target <- function(x) {
  if (x <= 0 || x >= 1) return(-Inf)
  (4 - 1) * log(x) + (8 - 1) * log(1 - x)
}

n_iter <- 10000
theta <- numeric(n_iter)
theta[1] <- 0.5
sigma_prop <- 0.1

for (i in 2:n_iter) {
  prop <- rnorm(1, theta[i-1], sigma_prop)
  log_ratio <- log_target(prop) - log_target(theta[i-1])
  if (log(runif(1)) < log_ratio) theta[i] <- prop
  else theta[i] <- theta[i-1]
}

# Diagnostic: trace + histogram
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(theta, type = "l")
hist(theta[-(1:1000)], breaks = 50, freq = FALSE, main = "Posterior samples")
curve(dbeta(x, 4, 8), add = TRUE, col = "red", lwd = 2)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

mean(theta[-(1:1000)])     # posterior mean

heading: 11.249 Output & Results
text: 10,000-step chain whose post-burn-histogram aligns analytic Beta(4, 8) density. trace plot, histogram, posterior mean (around \(4 / (4+8) = 0.33\)) confirm convergence; autocorrelation function tells many independent draws chain worth.

heading: 11.250 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “random-walk Metropolis-Hastings sampler proposal \(\mathcal N(\theta^{(t)}, 0.1^2)\) produced 9,000 post-burn-draws; histogram tracked analytic Beta(4, 8) target, posterior mean 0.33 matched closed-form value within Monte Carlo error.” Quote acceptance rate readers can judge whether proposal tuned reasonably.

heading: 11.251 Practical Tips
text: Tune proposal scale acceptance rate near 25–40 % one-dimensional random walks (Roberts–Gelman–Gilks rule); higher dimensions, lower rates optimal.Discard burn-1,000–5,000 iterations inspect trace plot visible drift computing summaries.parameters correlated, coordinate-wise random walk poor mixing; switch block updates multivariate Normal proposal whose covariance approximates posterior, move HMC.Use \(\hat R\) across multiple chains rather relying single trace; single chain reveal mode-trapping.tall posteriors many parameters, modern HMC samplers (Stan, NUTS) almost always mix dramatically better basic MH; reach MH target hard differentiate discrete components.

heading: 11.255 Introduction
text: Normal-Normal model simplest example conjugate Bayesian updating continuous parameter: Normal prior mean, Normal likelihood known variance, Normal posterior obtained transparent precision-weighted average. classical pedagogical example, despite simplicity sits heart every hierarchical model contains Normal level — random-effects meta-analysis, partial pooling mixed models, mean structure Gaussian processes rely closed-form update.

heading: 11.256 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Normal distribution, concept conjugacy, difference variance precision (inverse variance). Basic algebra enough follow derivation.

heading: 11.257 Theory
text: prior \(\mu \sim \mathcal N(\mu_0, \tau_0^2)\) likelihood \(y_i \sim \mathcal N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) \(= 1, \dots, n\) known \(\sigma\), posterior \[\mu \mid y \sim \mathcal N(\mu_n, \tau_n^2),\]posterior precision sum prior precision data precision,\[\tau_n^{-2} = \tau_0^{-2} + n / \sigma^2,\]posterior mean precision-weighted average\[\mu_n = \tau_n^2 \left(\tau_0^{-2} \mu_0 + n \bar y / \sigma^2\right).\]posterior mean lies prior mean sample mean, weight depends relative precisions. precision-additive structure makes prior’s “pseudo-sample-size” interpretable: \(\tau_0^2\) corresponds prior sample size \(\sigma^2 / \tau_0^2\).

heading: 11.258 Assumptions
text: Known \(\sigma^2\) — unknown mean — Normal prior. variance also unknown, conjugate setup uses Normal-Inverse-Gamma prior posterior \(\mu\) \(t\) distribution.

heading: 11.259 R Implementation
code:
# Prior: mu ~ N(100, 10^2); data: xbar = 105, sigma = 15, n = 20
mu0 <- 100; tau0 <- 10
xbar <- 105; sigma <- 15; n <- 20

prec_post <- 1/tau0^2 + n/sigma^2
tau_post  <- 1/sqrt(prec_post)
mu_post   <- tau_post^2 * (mu0/tau0^2 + n*xbar/sigma^2)

c(posterior_mean = mu_post,
  posterior_sd   = tau_post,
  ci95 = mu_post + c(-1, 1) * 1.96 * tau_post)

heading: 11.260 Output & Results
text: Posterior mean approximately 104.2 posterior standard deviation 2.4 95 % credible interval roughly \((99.6, 108.9)\). shrinkage sample mean 105 toward prior mean 100 small data precision (\(n / \sigma^2 \approx 0.089\)) much larger prior precision (\(1 / \tau_0^2 = 0.01\)).

heading: 11.261 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Combining \(\mathcal N(100, 10^2)\) prior 20 observations sample mean 105 (known \(\sigma = 15\)) yielded posterior mean 104.2 (95 % credible interval 99.6 108.9); prior contributed equivalent approximately 2.25 additional observations.” “equivalent observations” framing one intuitive ways communicate prior strength non-Bayesians.

heading: 11.262 Practical Tips
text: \(n \\infty\), posterior mean tends sample mean posterior variance tends \(\sigma^2 / n\); prior’s influence vanishes asymptotically can dominate small samples.\(\sigma\) unknown, switch Normal–Inverse-Gamma conjugate pair; marginal posterior \(\mu\) \(t\) tails.precision-form update generalises directly multivariate Normals covariance matrices place variances; everything scalar becomes matrix.Use model sanity check software: general-purpose Bayesian engine (Stan, JAGS, brms) reproduce closed-form posterior within Monte Carlo error.Hierarchical mean structures built update every level, understanding deeply pays dividends mixed models meta-analysis.

heading: 11.268 Theory
text: posterior \(p(\theta \mid y)\), standard summaries :Posterior mean: \(\mathbb E[\theta \mid y]\) minimises expected squared-error loss; Bayes estimator quadratic loss natural target MCMC averaging.Posterior median: minimises expected absolute-error loss; preferred skewed posteriors chase long tail.Posterior mode (MAP): maximises \(p(\theta \mid y)\) often coincides penalised maximum-likelihood estimate prior interpreted penalty.Posterior variance: \(\mathrm{Var}(\theta \mid y)\) measures spread; standard deviation \(\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}}\) original scale \(\theta\).mean variance always exist proper integrable posteriors; mode may unique multimodal posteriors median unique continuous distributions.

heading: 11.269 Assumptions

heading: 11.270 R Implementation

set.seed(2026)

# Simulated samples from a posterior (e.g., from MCMC)
theta <- rbeta(5000, shape1 = 10, shape2 = 4)

c(mean = mean(theta),
  median = median(theta),
  mode = density(theta)$x[which.max(density(theta)$y)],
  sd = sd(theta),
  var = var(theta))

# Credible interval
quantile(theta, c(0.025, 0.975))

heading: 11.273 Practical Tips

heading: 11.279 Theory

heading: 11.281 R Implementation
code:
library(brms); library(bayesplot)

set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(y = rnorm(200, 3, 1.5))

fit <- brm(y ~ 1, data = d, family = gaussian,
           chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)

# Density overlay
pp_check(fit)

# Test-statistic check: SD of simulated vs observed
pp_check(fit, type = "stat", stat = "sd")

# Interval check
pp_check(fit, type = "intervals")

heading: 11.292 R Implementation
code:
library(brms)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 100
d <- data.frame(x = rnorm(n), y = NA)
d$y <- 2 + 1.5 * d$x + rnorm(n)

fit <- brm(y ~ x, data = d, chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)

# Posterior predictive samples
pp <- posterior_predict(fit, newdata = data.frame(x = 0:3))
apply(pp, 2, quantile, c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))

heading: 11.299 Introduction
text: Choosing prior consequential modelling decision Bayesian analysis choice likelihood. prior shapes posterior strongly data sparse, almost imperceptibly data abundant, unpredictably middle ground applied work occupies. Three categories cover needs: flat improper priors, weakly informative priors regularise without imposing substantive content, informative priors incorporate genuine pre-data knowledge. Modern Bayesian practice converged weakly informative priors default stabilise sampling, regularise small-sample inference, remain agnostic enough data dominate posterior whenever informative.
