Standard choices \(\mathcal N(0, 2.5)\) standardised slopes, \(\mathcal N(0, 10)\) intercepts, \(\mathrm{half\text{-}Cauchy}(0, 5)\) \(\mathrm{half\text{-}Normal}(0, 2.5)\) variance components, \(\mathrm{LKJ}(2)\) correlation matrices. regularise estimates without injecting domain claims.Informative priors carry substantive content previous studies, theoretical bounds, expert elicitation. appropriate information defensible reproducible, inappropriate chosen post-hoc push posterior somewhere convenient.prior predictive check — simulating prior, propagating likelihood, inspecting implied range y — single useful sanity check. prior implies blood pressures 1,000 mm Hg odds ratios \(10^6\), wide.

library(brms)

                                          x1 = rnorm(100), x2 = rnorm(100)),
          family = bernoulli)

library(brms); library(posterior)

data(sleepstudy, package = "lme4")

# Summary with R-hat and ESS

# Per-parameter

summarise_draws(post_draws)

Override defaults prior, prior_intercept, prior_aux, prior_covariance arguments, accepting one normal(), student_t(), cauchy(), laplace(), exponential(), decov(). priors scaled predictors, numerical scale (e.g. normal(0, 2.5)) sensible across different predictor ranges.

heading: 11.325 R Implementation
code:
library(rstanarm)

set.seed(2026)
d <- data.frame(y = rnorm(100), x = rnorm(100))

# Same syntax as lm() but Bayesian
fit <- stan_glm(y ~ x, data = d,
                prior = normal(0, 2.5),
                prior_intercept = normal(0, 5),
                prior_aux = exponential(1),
                chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)
summary(fit)
posterior_interval(fit, prob = 0.95)

heading: 11.334 Theory
text: point null \(H_0 : \theta = \theta_0\) nested continuous alternative \(H_1 : \theta \\Theta\), prior \(H_1\) taken marginal prior \(p(\theta)\) parameter interest, Bayes factor favour null \[\mathrm{BF}_{01} = \frac{p(\theta_0 \mid y)}{p(\theta_0)}.\]posterior puts density null prior , data support \(H_0\); puts less, support \(H_1\). quantity interpretable Jeffreys’ evidence scale invariant monotone reparameterisations nuisance parameters alternative’s prior must set consistently. identity assumes prior \(H_1\) reduces prior \(H_0\) \(\theta = \theta_0\); automatic nested-null setups can fail improper hierarchical priors.

heading: 11.335 Assumptions
text: continuous parameter, null nested inside alternative, proper prior whose density \(\theta_0\) finite known. Improper priors invalidate construction Bayes factor depends normalisation constant thrown away.

heading: 11.336 R Implementation
code:
library(polspline)

set.seed(2026)
# Posterior samples (e.g., from MCMC)
theta_post <- rnorm(5000, mean = 0.2, sd = 0.3)
theta_prior_density <- dnorm(0, mean = 0, sd = 1)   # N(0, 1) prior at 0

# Posterior density at 0 via logspline fit
logspl <- logspline(theta_post)
post_density_at_0 <- dlogspline(0, logspl)

BF01 <- post_density_at_0 / theta_prior_density
BF01; 1/BF01

Increase chain length reporting strong evidence \(H_0\).interval nulls composite hypotheses, Savage-Dickey identity apply; use bridge sampling (bridgesampling::bridge_sampler()) instead.Prefer sensitivity analysis: report Bayes factor least two prior scales, readers can judge prior-driven conclusion .

heading: 11.356 Theory
text: posterior joint distribution parameters represented \(S\) MCMC draws. tidybayes::spread_draws() returns one row per draw per parameter, model \(K\) regression coefficients yields \(S \times K\) rows. gather_draws() produces even longer format .variable column, useful faceting. package adds geoms — stat_halfeye, stat_pointinterval, stat_lineribbon — compute density interval summaries fly, data frame stays untransformed. Predictive draws come epred_draws() (expectation posterior predictive) predicted_draws() (full predictive distribution including residual variation).

heading: 11.357 Assumptions

heading: 11.358 R Implementation
code:
library(brms); library(tidybayes); library(ggplot2)

data(mtcars)
fit <- brm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, chains = 4, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)

# Tidy draws
draws <- fit |>
  spread_draws(b_wt, b_hp)

head(draws)

# Visualise posterior
draws |>
  pivot_longer(c(b_wt, b_hp), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "value") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = value, y = parameter)) +
  stat_halfeye(fill = "#2A9D8F") +
  theme_minimal()

# Conditional effects
fit |>
  epred_draws(newdata = data.frame(wt = 2.5, hp = seq(50, 300, 10))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = hp, y = .epred)) +
  stat_lineribbon(alpha = 0.3) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 11.359 Output & Results

heading: 11.365 Introduction
text: widely applicable information criterion (WAIC), introduced Watanabe, estimates expected --sample prediction score posterior samples without holding data. Bayesian successor AIC DIC: AIC requires single point estimate (assumes regularity fails hierarchical models); DIC plug-ins posterior mean deviance unstable mixed models. WAIC instead averages pointwise log-posterior predictive density across posterior draws penalises effective-parameter term, making directly applicable model well-defined pointwise likelihood.

heading: 11.366 Prerequisites
text: working understanding information criteria, posterior predictive distributions, relationship -sample fit --sample prediction.

heading: 11.367 Theory
text: data \(y_1, \dots, y_n\) posterior draws \(\theta^{(s)}\), log pointwise predictive density \[\widehat{\mathrm{lpd}} = \sum_{=1}^n \log \!\left(\frac{1}{S} \sum_{s=1}^S p(y_i \mid \theta^{(s)})\right).\]effective parameter count \(p_{\text{waic}}\) sum pointwise posterior variances log-likelihood. \[\mathrm{WAIC} = -2\bigl(\widehat{\mathrm{lpd}} - p_{\text{waic}}\bigr).\]Lower better. Watanabe’s asymptotic theory shows regular models WAIC asymptotically equivalent leave-one-cross-validation. standard error WAIC difference two models comes sample standard deviation pointwise differences scaled \(\sqrt n\), providing principled threshold “meaningful” differences.

heading: 11.368 Assumptions
text: pointwise likelihood well-defined (observation contributes one term sum) posterior well-mixed. WAIC inherits exchangeability assumption standard cross-validation — clustered temporal data, replace leave-one-cluster-leave-future-variants.

heading: 11.369 R Implementation
code:
library(brms); library(loo)

data(mtcars)
fit1 <- brm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars, chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)
fit2 <- brm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0)

w1 <- waic(fit1)
w2 <- waic(fit2)

loo_compare(w1, w2)

heading: 11.370 Output & Results
text: waic() returns WAIC value, \(p_{\text{waic}}\), vector pointwise contributions. loo_compare() aligns two WAIC objects, reports differences standard errors, orders models best (top) worst (bottom).

heading: 11.371 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “WAIC favoured two-predictor model \(\Delta\mathrm{WAIC} = -12 \pm 4\) (standard error difference); rule--thumb differences exceeding \(2 \times \mathrm{SE}\) meaningful, data clearly support including horsepower addition weight.” Always quote difference standard error — “12-point improvement” without context uninformative.

library(brms)

# Default brms priors are weakly informative
data_ex <- data.frame(y = rnorm(100), x = rnorm(100))

centre <- rep(seq_len(n_centre), each = per_centre)

  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

heading: 12.3 Introduction
text: Accelerated failure time (AFT) models express covariate effects directly time scale: covariate scales expected survival time multiplicative factor. model \(\log T = x^\top \beta + \sigma W\) \(W\) standardised noise (extreme-value, Normal, logistic, depending family). exponentiated coefficient \(\exp(-\beta_j)\) “acceleration factor” — multiplier time scale per unit increase \(x_j\). AFT natural framework communicating effects clinical audiences reason survival times rather hazards, remains valid non-proportional hazards Cox regression fails.

heading: 12.4 Prerequisites
text: working understanding parametric survival distributions (Weibull, log-Normal, log-logistic) difference hazard-ratio acceleration-factor interpretations.

heading: 12.5 Theory
text: Common AFT families:Weibull: log-time errors extreme-value distributed; unique family PH AFT interpretations, \(\beta_{\mathrm{PH}} = -k \beta_{\mathrm{AFT}}\) \(k\) Weibull shape.Log-Normal: errors Normal log-time scale; produces non-monotone hazards.Log-logistic: errors logistic log-time scale; produces non-monotone hazards heavier tails log-Normal closed-form quantile function.Generalised Gamma: three-parameter family nests Weibull, log-Normal, exponential; useful selecting among nested AFT alternatives via likelihood-ratio tests.acceleration-factor interpretation direct: \(\exp(\beta_j) = 0.7\) means survival times 30 % shorter unit increase \(x_j\); \(\exp(\beta_j) = 1.3\) means 30 % longer.

heading: 12.6 Assumptions
text: chosen parametric family correct (verifiable residual diagnostics AIC comparisons), independent non-informative censoring, absence unmodelled time-varying covariate effects.

heading: 12.7 R Implementation
code:
library(survival); library(flexsurv)

data(lung)

# Weibull AFT
aft_w <- survreg(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex, data = lung,
                 dist = "weibull")
summary(aft_w)

# Log-normal AFT
aft_ln <- survreg(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex, data = lung,
                  dist = "lognormal")

# flexsurv alternative
flex_w <- flexsurvreg(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex, data = lung, dist = "weibull")
flex_w

# AFT interpretation
exp(coef(aft_w)[-1])   # acceleration factors (time ratio)

heading: 12.8 Output & Results
text: survreg() returns AFT coefficients log-time scale plus scale parameter; flexsurvreg() provides equivalent estimates cleaner prediction interfaces. Exponentiating slope coefficients gives acceleration factors interpretable time ratios.

heading: 12.9 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Weibull AFT model estimated additional year age accelerated event factor 0.98 (95 % CI 0.97 1.00) — equivalently, 2 % shorter expected time per year — females 30 % longer expected survival males (acceleration factor 1.30, 95 % CI 1.06 1.59).” Choose AFT-vs-PH framing based audience: clinicians think survival times prefer acceleration factors, used hazard ratios prefer PH.

heading: 12.10 Practical Tips
text: Weibull AFT equivalent Weibull PH fixed transformation; choose parameterisation matches audience.Use AIC across distributions (exponential, Weibull, log-Normal, log-logistic, generalised gamma) select family; AICs differ substantially hazard shape varies.Check goodness fit Cox-Snell residuals (flexsurv::resid()) — Cox-Snell residual \(\exp(1)\) follow unit exponential.log–log survival plot standard diagnostic Weibull suitability; clear non-linearity rules motivates different family.non-monotone hazards (log-Normal, log-logistic), AFT mandatory — PH analogue without time-varying coefficient.small samples, AFT models efficient Cox parametric structure adds information; large samples PH, Cox competitive without distributional assumptions.

heading: 12.14 Introduction
text: Brier score survival prediction-loss metric combines calibration discrimination one number, evaluated chosen time horizon \(t\). generalises binary Brier score right-censored data via inverse-probability--censoring weights, model predicts survival probability \(\hat S_i(t)\) subject can assessed actual observed status \(t\). Unlike C-index, captures ranking, Brier score penalises miscalibrated probabilities — model ranks well consistently overpredicts survival score worse Brier concordance.

heading: 12.15 Prerequisites
text: working understanding survival predictions, calibration vs discrimination, censoring, binary Brier score \((\hat p - y)^2\).

heading: 12.16 Theory
text: time \(t\), censoring-weighted Brier score \[\mathrm{BS}(t) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^n w_i(t) \bigl(Y_i(t) - \hat S_i(t)\bigr)^2,\]\(Y_i(t) = \mathbf 1\{T_i > t\}\) observed survival indicator (defined) \(w_i(t)\) inverse-probability--censoring weight adjusts missing \(Y_i(t)\) subject \(\) censored \(t\). integrated Brier score (IBS, also called continuous ranked probability score survival) averages \(\mathrm{BS}(t)\) follow-window gives single-number summary suited model selection.useful reference Brier score marginal Kaplan-Meier estimator (covariates); model delivers lower IBS null KM produced informative predictions.

heading: 12.17 Assumptions
text: censoring weights \(w_i(t)\) correctly estimated, typically via Kaplan-Meier censoring distribution, assumes non-informative censoring conditional covariates.

heading: 12.18 R Implementation
code:
library(pec); library(survival)

data(lung)
fit <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex, data = lung, x = TRUE, y = TRUE)
pec_out <- pec(list(fit), data = lung,
               formula = Surv(time, status) ~ 1,
               times = seq(100, 800, by = 100))

plot(pec_out)
crps(pec_out)

heading: 12.20 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Cox model achieved integrated Brier score 0.16 100–800 days, compared 0.21 null Kaplan-Meier; predictive improvement largest 200 500 days, model’s time-specific Brier 30 % reference.” Pair IBS C-index; together cover calibration discrimination.

heading: 12.21 Practical Tips

heading: 12.25 Introduction
text: Censoring defining feature survival data: exact event time unknown partially observed, follow-ended, event screened periodically, subject lost observation. Handling censoring correctly separates survival analysis ordinary regression time--event endpoints. wrong censoring assumption one consequential analytic errors clinical studies, identifying censoring type choosing methods respect first step time--event workflow.

heading: 12.26 Prerequisites
text: working understanding time--event concepts, follow-windows, difference event end--follow-timestamp.

heading: 12.27 Theory
text: main censoring categories:Right censoring: event yet occurred observation ended. common type, default clinical trials.Left censoring: event occurred first observation; exact time unknown \([0, t_{\text{first observation}}]\).Interval censoring: event occurred within known interval \((L, R]\), typical studies periodic check-ups.Two design-based subtypes right censoring:Type : fixed follow-time; censoring time identical administratively censored subjects.Type II: follow pre-specified number events occur; censoring time depends data realisation.Informative censoring dangerous failure mode: censoring mechanism related underlying event risk (e.g., subjects drop badly). violates independence assumption nearly every standard method requires can severely bias estimates without warning.

heading: 12.28 Assumptions
text: Non-informative censoring conditional covariates included model. censoring depends prognosis unobserved variables, sensitivity analyses (informative-censoring bounds, multiple imputation) needed.

heading: 12.29 R Implementation
code:
library(survival)

# Right-censored data: time and status
Surv(time = c(5, 8, 12, 6), event = c(1, 1, 0, 1))

# Interval-censored: event occurred between left and right
Surv(time = c(5, 8), time2 = c(10, 12), type = "interval2")

# Counting-process style (time-varying covariates): start, stop, status
Surv(time = c(0, 5, 10), time2 = c(5, 10, 15), event = c(0, 0, 1),
     type = "counting")

heading: 12.30 Output & Results
text: Surv objects display censoring + suffix right-censored times, brackets interval-censored intervals, pairs counting-process intervals. Every downstream survival function (survfit, coxph, survreg) takes Surv object response dispatches censoring type.

heading: 12.31 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “228 patients, 165 (72 %) experienced event 63 (28 %) administratively right-censored study end (median follow-310 days, range 5 1,022). interval-censored left-censored events present. Censoring assumed non-informative; sensitivity analysis tipping-point assumptions confirmed conclusions robust plausible departures.” Always quantify censoring discuss assumed mechanism.

heading: 12.32 Practical Tips
text: Right censoring default; specifying types requires type argument Surv().Interval-censored data require icenReg survival Surv(L, R, type = "interval2"); treating intervals exact event times biases estimates.Check informative censoring comparing baseline characteristics censored vs event subjects; large differences warning sign.Distinguish administrative censoring (study end) loss follow-(subject left); two different mechanisms warrant separate sensitivity analyses.Plot censoring patterns: histogram censoring times alongside event times often reveals administrative cut-offs dropout clusters affect interpretation.competing risks, treating competing events censoring censoring choice; cumulative incidence analysis avoids .

heading: 12.36 Introduction
text: competing risk event whose occurrence prevents event interest happening — death non-disease cause prevents disease-specific death, retirement prevents return--work, . setting standard Kaplan-Meier estimator overestimates cumulative incidence event interest, treats competing events censoring rather absorbing states actually . cumulative incidence function (CIF), estimated non-parametrically Aalen-Johansen, correct summary: gives probability experienced cause time \(t\) presence competing causes.

heading: 12.37 Prerequisites
text: working understanding survival analysis, censoring, Kaplan-Meier estimator; familiarity multi-state thinking (“alive → cause-1 event” vs “alive → cause-2 event”) helps.

heading: 12.38 Theory
text: CIF cause \(k\) \[\mathrm{CIF}_k(t) = \mathrm P(T \le t, \text{cause} = k) = \int_0^t S(u^-) h_k(u) \, \mathrm d u,\]\(h_k\) cause-specific hazard \(S\) overall (-cause) survival. Summing CIFs across causes recovers \(1 - S(t)\). Aalen-Johansen estimator generalises Nelson-Aalen multi-state processes; Gray’s test compares CIFs across groups, null CIF event interest equal across groups. regression, Fine-Gray subdistribution-hazards model targets covariate effects CIF directly, whereas cause-specific Cox models target effects cause-specific hazard — two answer different scientific questions.

heading: 12.39 Assumptions
text: Non-informative censoring (independent competing causes), one event per subject (first event ends follow-), accurate cause classification.

heading: 12.40 R Implementation
code:
library(cmprsk)

# Simulated: event causes 1 (event of interest), 2 (competing), 0 (censored)
set.seed(2026)
t_time <- rexp(300, 0.3)
cause <- sample(0:2, 300, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.3, 0.5, 0.2))
group <- factor(sample(c("A", "B"), 300, replace = TRUE))

fit <- cuminc(t_time, cause, group)
plot(fit, xlab = "Time", ylab = "Cumulative incidence",
     color = c("red", "blue", "green", "orange"))

# Gray's test for equality of CIFs
print(fit)

heading: 12.41 Output & Results
text: cuminc() returns CIF estimates combination cause group, plus Gray’s test \(\chi^2\) statistics equality across groups within cause. plot overlays four CIF curves; cumulative incidences two causes within group sum less corresponding \(1 - S\) every time.

heading: 12.42 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “five-year cumulative incidence event 1 25 % group 40 % group B (Gray’s test \(p = 0.01\)); event 2 incidences 20 % vs 18 % (\(p = 0.65\)). KM analysis estimated event-1 incidence group B 52 %, illustrating upward bias ignoring competing cause.” Always present CIFs every competing cause explain KM inappropriate.

heading: 12.43 Practical Tips

heading: 12.47 Introduction
text: Harrell’s concordance index (C-index) generalises ROC AUC censored survival data. probability , randomly chosen pair subjects, one higher model-predicted risk actually fails first. C-index therefore headline measure discrimination survival model — Cox regression, parametric survival, machine-learning predictors — default reporting standard alongside model’s coefficients clinical prediction-model papers.

heading: 12.48 Prerequisites
text: working understanding survival analysis, Cox regression, censoring, AUC binary classifiers; familiarity distinction calibration discrimination.

heading: 12.49 Theory
text: Define pair \((, j)\) comparable can determined subject failed first — least one observed event subject either failing later surviving past first failure. C-index proportion comparable pairs higher predicted risk corresponds earlier observed event:\[C = \mathrm{P}(\hat\eta_i > \hat\eta_j \mid T_i < T_j, \delta_i = 1).\]\(C = 0.5\) corresponds random ranking \(C = 1.0\) perfect discrimination. Variants address specific limitations Harrell’s original index: Uno’s C-statistic uses inverse-probability--censoring weights unbiased estimation heavy non-uniform censoring; time-dependent AUCs (Heagerty, Uno) describe discrimination changes across follow-period; integrated Brier score complementary calibration-discrimination compound.

heading: 12.50 Assumptions

heading: 12.58 Introduction
text: Conditional survival probability surviving additional time \(t\) given patient already survived time \(s\). answers clinically relevant question absolute survival : five-year survival probability 30 % diagnosis says little patient already lived three years. conditional five-year--now estimate may 70 % higher, dramatically updating prognosis. Survivorship clinics, oncology long-term follow-, patient counselling rely conditional rather unconditional survival.

heading: 12.59 Prerequisites
text: working understanding survival function \(S(t)\), conditional probability, Kaplan-Meier estimator.

heading: 12.60 Theory
text: definition conditional probability,\[\mathrm P(T > t + s \mid T > s) = \frac{\mathrm P(T > t + s)}{\mathrm P(T > s)} = \frac{S(t + s)}{S(s)}.\]patient survived \(s\) years now risk set \(s\); next-\(t\)-year survival ratio survival probabilities. Kaplan-Meier estimates, computed \(\hat S(s)\) \(\hat S(t + s)\) directly. Confidence intervals require variance propagation ratio; log log-log transformation gives intervals respect \([0, 1]\) bounds.

heading: 12.61 Assumptions
text: reliable Kaplan-Meier parametric survival estimate \(s\) \(t + s\); practice means sufficient follow-\(\hat S(t + s)\) based tiny remaining cohort.

heading: 12.62 R Implementation
code:
library(survival)

data(lung)
fit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ 1, data = lung)

# Unconditional 2-year survival
summary(fit, times = c(500, 1000))$surv   # approximate 2- and 3-year

# Conditional 1-year survival given 1-year survivorship
times <- summary(fit, times = c(365, 730))
cond_surv <- times$surv[2] / times$surv[1]
cond_surv

heading: 12.63 Output & Results
text: ratio two KM survival estimates chosen landmark times. confidence intervals, transform onto log-log scale, compute SEs delta method, back-transform. condsurv package automates computation including bootstrap intervals.

heading: 12.64 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Unconditional one-year survival 48 %; conditional survived one year, probability surviving additional year 66 % (95 % CI 56 % 75 %); conditional survived two years, next-year survival rose 78 %.” Always report landmark time \(s\) explicitly; conditional survival meaningless without .

heading: 12.69 Introduction
text: fitted Cox model good assumptions: proportional hazards across covariate levels, right functional form continuous predictors, absence one two highly influential observations driving coefficient estimates. Three residual families address turn — Schoenfeld proportional hazards, martingale functional form, DFBETA-style influence diagnostics unduly leveraged observations. Reporting three now standard clinical-trial Cox analysis.

heading: 12.70 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Cox proportional-hazards regression, residual-based diagnostics, difference proportional-hazards assumption linearity---linear-predictor assumption.

heading: 12.71 Theory
text: Schoenfeld residuals computed event time difference observed covariate value expected value across risk set. proportional hazards, systematic relationship time. cox.zph() tests correlation scaled residuals time transform (default \(g(t) = \log t\)).Martingale residuals observed minus expected events subject. Plotting continuous covariate exposes non-linearity: smooth trend indicates linear-predictor assumption fails covariate.Deviance residuals normalise martingale residuals make outlying observations visible roughly symmetric scale.DFBETA measures much coefficient changes individual observation removed; large absolute values flag influential observations whose exclusion changes conclusions.

heading: 12.72 Assumptions
text: Cox model fitted, sufficient events estimate residuals reliably (typically least 10 events per covariate, substantially stable diagnostic plots).

heading: 12.73 R Implementation
code:
library(survival); library(survminer)

data(lung)
fit <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + ph.ecog, data = lung)

# PH test
zph <- cox.zph(fit)
print(zph)
ggcoxzph(zph)

# Linearity check for continuous age
ggcoxfunctional(Surv(time, status) ~ age,
  data = na.omit(lung[, c("time", "status", "age")]))

# Influential observations
ggcoxdiagnostics(fit, type = "dfbeta")

heading: 12.74 Output & Results
text: cox.zph() returns per-covariate \(\chi^2\) tests global test proportional hazards. ggcoxzph() plots smoothed scaled Schoenfeld residuals horizontal reference constant-HR null. ggcoxfunctional() plots martingale residuals continuous covariate smoother. ggcoxdiagnostics() displays DFBETA coefficient.

heading: 12.75 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Diagnostics Cox model showed PH violations (Schoenfeld global \(p = 0.08\), per-covariate \(p > 0.10\)), approximately linear effects age (martingale residuals showed systematic trend), three influential observations (DFBETA \(> 0.1\) absolute value) investigated retained sensitivity analysis showed coefficients changed less 5 %.” Always report global PH test, per-covariate tests, sensitivity analysis ran.

heading: 12.80 Introduction
text: Cox proportional hazards regression widely used survival regression model clinical epidemiological research. central insight hazard ratios subjects can estimated without specifying estimating baseline hazard \(h_0(t)\). partial likelihood, introduced D. R. Cox 1972, conditions risk set event time depends regression coefficients, leaving baseline hazard unspecified. result semi-parametric model combines interpretability regression flexibility non-parametric survival analysis.

heading: 12.81 Prerequisites
text: working understanding hazard functions, proportional-hazards assumption, partial-likelihood concept.

heading: 12.82 Theory
text: Cox model \[h(t \mid x) = h_0(t) \exp(x^\top \beta).\]partial likelihood event times \[L_p(\beta) = \prod_{: \delta_i = 1} \frac{\exp(x_i^\top \beta)}{\sum_{j \R(t_i)} \exp(x_j^\top \beta)},\]\(R(t_i)\) risk set just \(t_i\). Maximising \(L_p\) produces \(\hat\beta\) whose exponentiated components hazard ratios. Tied event times require handling rule: Breslow’s approximation (default) fast consistent ties, Efron’s accurate ties common, exact-marginal method gold standard discrete-time data.model assumes hazard ratio two covariate patterns constant time — proportional hazards. fails, options include stratification, time-varying coefficients, switching parametric AFT model.

heading: 12.83 Assumptions
text: Proportional hazards across covariate levels, independent non-informative censoring, absence unmodelled time-varying covariate effects. Adequate events-per-variable (EPV) ratios — least 10–15 events per regression coefficient — stable inference.

heading: 12.84 R Implementation
code:
library(survival); library(broom)

data(lung)
fit <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + ph.ecog, data = lung)
tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE)

# Check PH assumption
cox.zph(fit)

heading: 12.85 Output & Results

heading: 12.86 Interpretation

heading: 12.91 Introduction
text: competing-risks data, two questions often co-exist: “covariate’s mechanistic effect event interest” “effect population-level probability experiencing event time \(t\)?” Cause-specific Cox regression answers first; Fine-Gray subdistribution-hazards regression answers second. two questions can disagree dramatically — covariate can leave cause-specific hazard unchanged still raising lowering cumulative incidence changes competing-event hazard. Reporting one relevant common analytical mistake.

heading: 12.92 Prerequisites
text: working understanding competing-risks data, cumulative incidence functions, cause-specific vs subdistribution-hazards distinction.

heading: 12.93 Theory
text: subdistribution hazard cause 1 \[h_1^{\text{sub}}(t \mid x) = \lim_{\Delta t \0} \frac{\mathrm P\bigl(t \le T < t + \Delta t, \ C = 1 \mid T \ge t \text{ } (T < t \text{ } C \neq 1), \ x\bigr)}{\Delta t}.\]risk set keeps subjects already experienced competing event, diminishing weights. Fine-Gray model\[h_1^{\text{sub}}(t \mid x) = h_{10}(t) \exp(x^\top \beta),\]exponentiated coefficient \(\exp(\beta_j)\) subdistribution hazard ratio maps directly onto changes cumulative incidence: \(\mathrm{sHR} > 1\) means higher cumulative incidence cause 1 per unit covariate. model fitted modified partial likelihood inverse-probability--censoring weights.

heading: 12.94 Assumptions
text: Proportional subdistribution hazards (analogue proportional cause-specific hazards), independent censoring (correctly weighted), accurate cause classification.

heading: 12.95 R Implementation
code:
library(cmprsk)

set.seed(2026)
t_time <- rexp(300, 0.3)
cause  <- sample(0:2, 300, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.3, 0.5, 0.2))
x1     <- rnorm(300); x2 <- rbinom(300, 1, 0.5)

fg <- crr(ftime = t_time, fstatus = cause, cov1 = cbind(x1, x2),
          failcode = 1, cencode = 0)
summary(fg)

heading: 12.96 Output & Results
text: crr() returns subdistribution coefficients log-sHR scale, standard errors, \(p\)-values, (summary()) exponentiated subdistribution hazard ratios confidence intervals. output structure matches Cox regression interpretation refers cumulative incidence rather cause-specific hazard.

heading: 12.97 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Fine-Gray subdistribution-hazards regression cause 1 estimated \(\mathrm{sHR} = 1.45\) \(x_1\) (95 % CI 1.10 1.90), meaning 45 % higher cumulative incidence cause 1 per unit increase \(x_1\); cause-specific Cox model data gave HR 1.32, indicating part effect cumulative incidence comes via competing-event hazard.” Always report subdistribution cause-specific HRs differ; answer different questions.

heading: 12.98 Practical Tips
text: Subdistribution hazard ratios equal cause-specific hazard ratios; treating interchangeable common error.Use cause-specific Cox etiologic questions (“affects disease mechanism?”); use Fine-Gray prognostic / prediction questions (“affects probability disease time?”).prediction tools (nomograms, risk calculators), Fine-Gray correct framework absolute risk depends cumulative incidence.Report approaches questions matter; transparency difference prevents readers misinterpreting one .riskRegression::FGR() offers modern alternative cleaner diagnostics better small-sample handling.Check proportional subdistribution hazards via crskdiag simulated residual plots; assumption can fail even proportional cause-specific hazards holds.

heading: 12.102 Introduction
text: Royston-Parmar models bridge gap rigid parametric survival families (exponential, Weibull, log-normal) assumption-light Cox semi-parametric model. place restricted cubic splines log cumulative hazard (related transforms) baseline can take essentially shape data demand, keeping full parametric likelihood delivers smooth survival, hazard, quantile predictions covariate pattern. epidemiological work — particularly population-based cancer studies — become preferred default combine Cox-like flexibility parametric extrapolation.

heading: 12.103 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Cox regression, parametric survival families, restricted cubic splines smooth functional forms.

heading: 12.104 Theory
text: Royston-Parmar model writes\[\log H(t \mid x) = s\bigl(\log t; \gamma\bigr) + x^\top \beta,\]\(s\) restricted cubic spline \(\log t\) knots placed quantiles log event-time distribution. \(k\) internal knots, spline \(k + 1\) parameters; common choices 1–4 knots. Setting scale = "hazard" produces proportional-hazards model; scale = "odds" gives proportional-odds parameterisation; scale = "normal" gives probit parameterisation, equivalent generalised log-normal model. Time-dependent effects can modelled interacting covariates spline basis functions.Predictions natural time scale smooth available horizon, including extrapolation beyond maximum observed event time, Cox predictions undefined.

heading: 12.105 Assumptions
text: chosen scale (PH, PO, probit) appropriate; number knots large baseline overfitted small underlying shape missed. AIC conventional knot-selection criterion.

heading: 12.106 R Implementation
code:
library(flexsurv)

data(lung)

# 2 internal knots (default placement at quantiles of log-event times)
fit <- flexsurvspline(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex, data = lung,
                      k = 2, scale = "hazard")
fit
plot(fit, type = "hazard")
plot(fit, type = "survival")

heading: 12.107 Output & Results
text: flexsurvspline() returns spline coefficients \(\gamma_0, \gamma_1, \dots\), covariate effects \(\hat\beta\) log-hazard scale (PH), fully smooth model object. plot(fit, type = "hazard") overlays model-implied hazard non-parametric reference, plot(fit, type = "survival") survival function. smoothness headline visual difference Cox-based survfit step function.

heading: 12.108 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Royston-Parmar PH model two internal knots estimated hazard ratio female sex 0.60 (95 % CI 0.42 0.85), close Cox estimate 0.59 (0.42 0.83); spline baseline captured hazard peak around 9 months Weibull model missed entirely (AIC: Royston-Parmar 1{,}890, Weibull 1{,}908).” Reporting AIC across knots simpler parametric alternatives makes choice transparent.

heading: 12.109 Practical Tips
text: Start 2–3 knots compare AIC across 1–4; 4 internal knots tends overfit small datasets.Use scale = "hazard" (PH) goal matches Cox interpretation; "odds" proportional-odds analogue; "normal" probit / log-normal-like fit.Coefficient estimates close Cox model PH; large discrepancies indicate either Cox tied-event handling differences issue spline baseline.Time-dependent covariate effects come gamma1 ~ x interactions inside flexsurvspline(); lets test PH non-parametrically within model.predictions fine time grid, summary(fit, type = "survival", t = seq(0, 1000, 10)) produces smooth survival probabilities ready plotting.population-based cancer epidemiology, Royston-Parmar models largely replaced Cox relative-survival excess-mortality work; parametric baseline gives interpretable hazard shapes clean extrapolation.

heading: 12.113 Introduction
text: frailty model adds unobserved multiplicative effect — frailty — hazard, allowing survival analogue random effect. Frailty captures two things: unmeasured heterogeneity individual level (population mixture “robust” “fragile” subjects different baseline hazards), clustered survival data observations within unit (family, hospital, patient recurrent events) share unobserved common factor. Without , standard errors wrong clustered data, population-level hazard ratios biased toward null selection--survivors effect.

heading: 12.114 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Cox regression, random-effects models linear generalised linear settings, concept unobserved heterogeneity.

heading: 12.115 Theory
text: shared-frailty Cox model \[h_{ij}(t) = h_0(t) u_i \exp(x_{ij}^\top \beta),\]\(u_i\) positive random variable (frailty) shared across observations \(j\) within cluster \(\) conventionally constrained mean 1. Gamma distribution textbook default — conjugate structure partial likelihood makes estimation tractable — though log-Normal frailties increasingly used Laplace-approximation likelihood implemented.frailty variance \(\theta = \mathrm{Var}(u_i)\) measures strength within-cluster correlation; \(\theta = 0\) corresponds clustering reduces standard Cox model. likelihood-ratio test \(\theta = 0\) boundary parameter space, conventional \(\chi^2_1\) reference replaced 50-50 mixture \(\chi^2_0\) \(\chi^2_1\).

heading: 12.116 Assumptions

heading: 12.117 R Implementation

library(survival)

data(kidney)   # two events per patient (recurrent catheter infections)

# Shared frailty
fit <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + frailty(id), data = kidney)
summary(fit)

heading: 12.118 Output & Results

heading: 12.119 Interpretation

heading: 12.124 Introduction
text: time--event distribution admits three mutually equivalent representations: survival function \(S(t)\), hazard function \(h(t)\), cumulative hazard \(H(t)\). contain information emphasise different aspects process — surviving probability, instantaneous risk, accumulated risk respectively. Switching freely among one foundational fluencies survival analysis, form natural plotting inferential conventions software exposes three.

heading: 12.125 Prerequisites
text: working understanding survival analysis basics, difference probability rate, integration non-negative functions.

heading: 12.126 Theory
text: non-negative continuous time--event \(T\) density \(f(t)\):Survival function: \(S(t) = \mathrm P(T > t)\). probability surviving past time \(t\).Hazard function: \(h(t) = f(t) / S(t)\). instantaneous event rate time \(t\) given survival \(t\). Hazard rate (per unit time), probability.Cumulative hazard: \(H(t) = \int_0^t h(u) \, \mathrm d u = -\log S(t)\). Accumulated risk time \(t\).one three determines two: \(S(t) = e^{-H(t)}\), \(h(t) = -\mathrm d \log S(t) / \mathrm d t\). Kaplan-Meier estimator targets \(S(t)\) directly; Nelson-Aalen estimator targets \(H(t)\) preferable right tail KM step function unstable. hazard never estimated directly without smoothing, instantaneous rates require kernel methods parametric assumptions.

heading: 12.127 Assumptions
text: non-negative, almost surely continuous time--event proper density (, discrete time, discrete analogue mutual recoverability).

heading: 12.128 R Implementation
code:
library(survival)

data(lung)
fit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ 1, data = lung)

# Survival curve
plot(fit, conf.int = TRUE, main = "S(t)")

# Cumulative hazard
plot(fit, fun = "cumhaz", main = "H(t)")

heading: 12.129 Output & Results
text: Two complementary curves: Kaplan-Meier survival function point-wise confidence bands, cumulative hazard estimated via \(-\log \hat S(t)\) (agrees Nelson-Aalen small-sample corrections). Reading together makes underlying hazard pattern visible — steep \(S(t)\) corresponds rising \(H(t)\), slope \(H(t)\) time local hazard rate.

heading: 12.130 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Median survival 310 days (95 % CI 280 360); cumulative hazard one year approximately 0.85, corresponding \(S(365) = \mathrm{exp}(-0.85) = 0.43\), consistent KM estimate 42 % one year.” Quoting representations communicates fact two complementary forms readers may prefer.

heading: 12.135 Introduction
text: Interval censoring arises whenever subjects observed discrete intervals exact event time therefore unknown — interval contains known. Cavity detected routine dental check-, HIV seroconversion identified quarterly clinic visit, breakdown part discovered periodic inspection: case event time lies last “event” inspection first “event observed” inspection. Treating exact event times biases standard survival methods; Turnbull non-parametric maximum-likelihood estimator (NPMLE) parametric semi-parametric extensions handle interval structure correctly.

heading: 12.136 Prerequisites
text: working understanding survival analysis, distinction right, left, interval censoring, basic Kaplan-Meier estimation gold-standard right-censored analogue.

heading: 12.137 Theory
text: subject contributes interval \((L_i, R_i]\) within event occurred; right-censored subjects \(R_i = \infty\), exact events \(L_i = R_i\). Turnbull’s algorithm finds NPMLE survival function grid unique left right endpoints via EM iteration: step apportions probability mass across candidate intervals proportional current estimates updates survival estimate weighted contributions. Convergence yields NPMLE indeterminacy regions data provide information (-called Turnbull intervals).regression, parametric models (Weibull, log-normal) semi-parametric extensions (PH PO monotone splines baseline) accept interval-censored input directly. likelihood \(\prod_i [S(L_i) - S(R_i)]\), reduces standard product right-censored special case.

heading: 12.138 Assumptions
text: Independent interval censoring (inspection schedule depend subject’s underlying time--event), accurate interval boundaries.

heading: 12.139 R Implementation

library(icenReg); library(survival)

data(miceData)
head(miceData)

# NPMLE
fit_np <- ic_np(cbind(l, u) ~ grp, data = miceData)
plot(fit_np)

# Parametric: Weibull PH
fit_w <- ic_par(cbind(l, u) ~ grp, data = miceData, model = "ph", dist = "weibull")
summary(fit_w)

heading: 12.146 Introduction
text: Joint longitudinal-survival models bring together two submodels — mixed model longitudinally measured biomarker survival model time event — via shared random effects evolving biomarker part hazard. solves two problems simultaneously: time-varying-covariate Cox model uses last-observation-carried-forward biomarker values ignores measurement error timing irregularity, informative dropout (subjects censored biomarker deteriorating) biases naive survival estimates. joint formulation models processes together integrates shared random effects, yielding consistent hazard ratios underlying biomarker trajectory.

heading: 12.147 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear mixed models, Cox regression, time-varying covariates, concept informative dropout.

heading: 12.148 Theory
text: longitudinal submodel \[y_{ij} = \mu_i(t_{ij}) + \varepsilon_{ij}, \qquad \mu_i(t) = X_{ij}^\top \gamma + Z_{ij}^\top b_i,\]\(b_i \sim \mathcal N(0, D)\) subject-specific random effects. survival submodel uses latent trajectory:\[h_i(t) = h_0(t) \exp\!\bigl(\alpha \mu_i(t) + x_i^\top \beta\bigr).\]association parameter \(\alpha\) measures strongly underlying biomarker drives hazard. joint likelihood integrates random effects submodels simultaneously, typically via Gauss-Hermite quadrature; makes joint models computationally heavier fitting two submodels separately.

heading: 12.149 Assumptions
text: Multivariate Normal random effects, correctly specified mean covariance structures, model misspecification either submodel, proper proportional-hazards form (flexible alternative) survival component.

heading: 12.150 R Implementation
code:
library(JM)

data(pbc2)
lm_fit <- lme(log(serBilir) ~ year + drug, random = ~ year | id,
              data = pbc2)
surv_fit <- coxph(Surv(years, status2) ~ drug, data = pbc2.id, x = TRUE)

joint_fit <- jointModel(lm_fit, surv_fit, timeVar = "year",
                        method = "piecewise-PH-aGH")
summary(joint_fit)

heading: 12.151 Output & Results
text: summary(joint_fit) returns longitudinal-submodel coefficients (fixed effects, random-effect covariance), survival-submodel coefficients (\(\beta\)), association parameter \(\alpha\) standard error 95 % confidence interval. Diagnostics include Gauss-Hermite quadrature convergence criterion integrated likelihood.

heading: 12.152 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “joint longitudinal-survival model linking log-bilirubin trajectory time death (PBC dataset, 312 subjects) estimated association parameter \(\alpha = 0.74\) (\(\mathrm{SE} = 0.10\), \(p < 0.001\)); unit increase underlying log-bilirubin level corresponded 2.1-fold higher hazard death, accounting measurement error informative dropout.” Always describe submodels methods joint structure reproducible.

heading: 12.153 Practical Tips

heading: 12.157 Introduction
text: Kaplan-Meier (KM) estimator non-parametric maximum-likelihood estimator survival function right-censored time--event data. signature step-function plot single recognisable figure clinical research. estimator makes distributional assumption time--event variable; simply accumulates conditional probability surviving observed event time.

heading: 12.158 Prerequisites
text: reader understand survival analysis studies time event, “right censoring” means subjects observed point event occurred. Familiarity R’s survival package required helps.

heading: 12.159 Theory
text: Let \(t_1 < t_2 < \ldots < t_k\) denote distinct event times observed data. time \(t_j\), let \(d_j\) number events \(n_j\) number subjects still risk (yet event yet censored). Kaplan-Meier estimator survival function \[\hat{S}(t) = \prod_{j : t_j \leq t} \left(1 - \frac{d_j}{n_j}\right).\]estimator step function drops event time fraction -risk set experiencing event. Censored subjects contribute risk set censoring time cause drops.standard-error estimator due Greenwood \[\widehat{\text{Var}}[\hat{S}(t)] = \hat{S}(t)^2 \sum_{j : t_j \leq t} \frac{d_j}{n_j (n_j - d_j)}.\]Pointwise confidence intervals typically constructed complementary log-log scale ensure lie \([0, 1]\).log-rank test compares survival two groups. sums observed-minus-expected events event time across groups, yielding test statistic follows \(\chi^2_{k-1}\) distribution null hypothesis equal survival functions.

heading: 12.160 Assumptions
text: Kaplan-Meier estimation assumes:Censoring non-informative – subjects censored time \(t\) future risk remain observation. Violated , example, patients drop feel worse.Subjects independent.Risk event depend calendar time beyond covariates capture (important accrual spans long period).

heading: 12.161 R Implementation
text: Surv() call packages time event-indicator columns single survival object; survfit() right-hand side sex produces separate KM curves sex. ggsurvplot() draws curves pointwise confidence bands, log-rank p-value, risk table .
code:
library(survival)
library(survminer)

data(lung, package = "survival")

fit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung)

print(fit)

ggsurvplot(fit,
           data = lung,
           conf.int = TRUE,
           pval = TRUE,
           risk.table = TRUE,
           legend.labs = c("Male", "Female"),
           xlab = "Days from diagnosis",
           ylab = "Survival probability",
           palette = "Set2")

heading: 12.162 Output & Results
text: lung dataset 138 male 90 female patients advanced lung cancer. KM median survival 270 days males (95% CI 212 310) 426 days females (95% CI 348 550). log-rank test gives \(\chi^2_1 = 10.3\), \(p = 0.0013\), indicating significantly higher survival females.

heading: 12.163 Interpretation
text: Reporting follows CONSORT conventions: “Median overall survival 270 days (95% CI 212-310) males 426 days (95% CI 348-550) females; log-rank \(p = 0.001\) (Figure X).” figure always shown risk table beneath – curve nearly meaningless number risk drops handful.

heading: 12.167 Introduction
text: Landmark analysis standard fix immortal-time bias arises subjects classified post-baseline exposure (treatment response, biomarker change, secondary event). Naively grouping subjects covariate measured follow-begins assigns “responder” status subjects survived long enough assessed, automatically biasing comparisons favour. Landmark analysis sidesteps selecting fixed landmark time, restricting subjects alive observable time, classifying covariate value landmark, analysing post-landmark survival.

heading: 12.168 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Cox regression, time-varying covariates, immortal-time bias makes naive responder-vs-nonresponder analyses invalid.

heading: 12.169 Theory
text: chosen landmark time \(L\):Exclude subjects event \(L\) — classified status \(L\).Classify remaining subject covariate value \(L\).Treat \(L\) new time origin analyse residual survival \(T_i - L\) \(T_i > L\).Fit standard Cox model post-landmark data.Multiple landmarks can combined “super model” id-clustered variance smooth function \(L\) covariate, gaining efficiency cost additional model complexity (Van Houwelingen 2007).

heading: 12.170 Assumptions
text: covariate value landmark \(L\) relevant exposure prognosis; sufficient follow-beyond \(L\) remains; censoring mechanism \(L\) non-informative.

heading: 12.171 R Implementation
code:
library(survival)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
d <- data.frame(
  id = 1:n,
  time = rexp(n, 0.1),
  status = rbinom(n, 1, 0.6),
  response = rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)      # binary time-varying covariate
)

L <- 6   # landmark at 6 months

# Subjects at risk at L
at_risk <- d[d$time >= L, ]
at_risk$time_from_L <- at_risk$time - L
at_risk$status_from_L <- at_risk$status

fit <- coxph(Surv(time_from_L, status_from_L) ~ response, data = at_risk)
summary(fit)

heading: 12.172 Output & Results
text: standard Cox regression post-landmark cohort: hazard ratio covariate (response) measured landmark, 95 % confidence interval Wald test. number events -risk subjects shrinks landmark, confidence intervals wider full-cohort analysis.

heading: 12.173 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Among 152 patients alive six-month landmark, treatment responders (n = 78) 50 % lower hazard subsequent death non-responders (HR 0.50, 95 % CI 0.30 0.83); analysis avoids immortal-time bias inherent unrestricted responder-vs-non-responder comparisons.” Always state landmark time post-landmark sample size.

heading: 12.178 Introduction
text: Left truncation, also called delayed entry, occurs subjects become observable surviving certain age calendar time. Recruiting middle-aged adults cohort study means anyone died child young adult structurally absent — contribute. Treating delayed-entry data every subject followed time zero overestimates survival, early period observed survivors. counting-process formulation survival::Surv(tstart, tstop, event) handles naturally restricting risk sets event time subjects actually observation .

heading: 12.179 Prerequisites
text: working understanding survival analysis, difference censoring (subject leaves event) truncation (subject enters specific conditions), counting-process formulation survival data.

heading: 12.180 Theory
text: left-truncated subject enters time \(\tau_i\) exits time \(T_i\) (event indicator \(\delta_i\)), contribution partial likelihood event time \(t\) appear \(\tau_i < t \le T_i\). standard \(\mathrm{Surv}(\mathrm{time}, \mathrm{event})\) object encode entry time \(\tau_i\) silently sets zero, biasing estimates upward. two-argument form \(\mathrm{Surv}(\tau, T, \mathrm{event})\) (counting-process style) carries endpoints; survfit() coxph() use construct correct risk sets.age--timescale analyses (natural framing chronic-disease epidemiology), every subject left-truncated age enrolment.

heading: 12.181 Assumptions
text: truncation mechanism independent event interest given covariates: subjects become observable reasons related underlying risk profile.

heading: 12.182 R Implementation
code:
library(survival)

# Age-as-timescale cohort: enrolled between ages 40 and 70
set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
age_enroll <- runif(n, 40, 70)
event_age  <- age_enroll + rexp(n, 0.05)
end_age    <- pmin(event_age, age_enroll + runif(n, 5, 20))
status     <- as.integer(event_age <= end_age)

# Left-truncated Cox: tstart = entry age, tstop = exit age
fit <- coxph(Surv(age_enroll, end_age, status) ~ 1)
summary(fit)

# Kaplan-Meier with delayed entry
km <- survfit(Surv(age_enroll, end_age, status) ~ 1)
plot(km)

heading: 12.183 Output & Results
text: survfit() returns Kaplan-Meier curve chosen time scale (age, calendar year, time-since-event) conditions subject’s entry time. Cox model counting-process Surv object produces unbiased hazard-ratio estimates even entry times heavily truncated.

heading: 12.184 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Using age time scale left truncation enrolment, conditional survival age 40 0.75 age 60 (95 % CI 0.68 0.82); ignoring delayed entry inflates estimate 0.84 treating mid-life enrolees followed since age 0.” Always state time scale truncation mechanism methods section.

heading: 12.185 Practical Tips
text: Always specify tstart tstop arguments Surv() entry times vary across subjects; single-argument form silently assumes subjects entered zero.Age--timescale cohort studies always left-truncated; common scenario chronic-disease epidemiology ageing research.Distinguish left truncation (subject observable certain time) left censoring (event occurred observation began subject observable now); two handled differently.Without left-truncation correction, overestimate survival cohorts enrolled older ages early-life mortality invisible data.survfit() delayed entry produces correct conditional survival curves; check reported time scale starts minimum entry time, zero.competing risks delayed entry, cmprsk::cuminc tidycmprsk::cuminc accept counting-process input via entry arguments.

heading: 12.189 Introduction
text: log-rank test standard non-parametric test comparing survival across two groups randomised trials observational studies. accumulates differences observed expected event counts event time across follow-window reports chi-squared statistic. test powerful proportional hazards — alternative designed — variants (weighted log-rank, stratified log-rank) extend applicability non-proportional covariate-adjusted settings.

heading: 12.190 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Kaplan-Meier estimation, censoring, proportional-hazards alternative natural target log-rank test.

heading: 12.191 Theory
text: unique event time \(t_j\) across groups, test compares observed number events group \(k\) expected number null equal hazards: \(E_{kj} = d_j \cdot n_{kj} / n_j\), \(d_j\) total events \(t_j\) \(n_{kj}, n_j\) numbers risk. standardised cumulative deviation across event times forms chi-squared statistic \(k - 1\) degrees freedom, \(k\) number groups.Weighted variants (Wilcoxon, Tarone-Ware, Fleming-Harrington \(G^{\rho, \gamma}\)) give weight early late differences recover power hazards non-proportional. stratified log-rank applies test within strata defined categorical covariate pools contributions, controlling covariate testing main effect.

heading: 12.192 Assumptions
text: Proportional hazards across groups (standard log-rank), independent non-informative censoring, accurate event-time recording.

heading: 12.193 R Implementation
code:
library(survival)

data(lung)
fit <- survdiff(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung)
fit

# Stratified log-rank
survdiff(Surv(time, status) ~ sex + strata(ph.ecog), data = lung)

heading: 12.194 Output & Results
text: survdiff() returns observed expected events per group, chi-squared test statistic, degrees freedom, asymptotic \(p\)-value. stratified version partitions comparison within levels strata variable, producing output structure stratification controlled .

heading: 12.195 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “log-rank test rejected null equal survival sexes (\(\chi^2_1 = 10.3\), \(p = 0.001\)); female arm 53 observed events compared 86 expected null, male arm 112 observed compared 79 expected.” Always pair test hazard-ratio estimate (Cox) Kaplan-Meier curves; test alone gives effect size.

heading: 12.196 Practical Tips
text: Use stratification (+ strata(var)) controlling covariate without testing ; stratified log-rank tests within strata pools.hazards cross, standard log-rank can fail detect real difference; use Fleming-Harrington \(G^{1, 0}\) test late differences \(G^{0, 1}\) early ones.many groups, log-rank gives omnibus test; pairwise comparisons follow pairwise_survdiff() survminer apply Bonferroni Hochberg correction.Report Kaplan-Meier curves alongside test result; \(p\)-value without curve uninterpretable clinical readers.Non-informative censoring assumption fails often observational studies; censoring depends prognosis, log-rank test biased.severely non-proportional hazards (clearly crossing curves), consider RMST primary summary instead relying log-rank.

heading: 12.200 Introduction
text: multi-state model describes subject’s movement discrete set states time — healthy diseased dead, pre-transplant post-transplant rejected, clinical pathway intermediate landmarks. generalises ordinary survival (two states: alive, dead), competing risks (one origin, several absorbing states), illness-death model (one intermediate, one absorbing state). framework gives complete probabilistic picture disease course rather collapsing single time--first-event summary.

heading: 12.201 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Cox regression, competing-risks analysis, concept transition intensities (state-specific hazards) multi-state generalisation single survival hazard.

heading: 12.202 Theory
text: multi-state model represents states nodes possible transitions directed edges. transition \(j \k\) associated transition intensity (hazard) \(\alpha_{jk}(t)\). Transition-specific Cox models estimate covariate effects \(\alpha_{jk}\) separately, stratification transition number ensuring baseline can differ. Predicted transition probabilities \(P_{jk}(s, t)\) — probability state \(k\) time \(t\) given state \(j\) time \(s\) — obtained cumulative-hazard matrix via product-integral, implemented mstate::probtrans().Markov property assumes future depends current state time, path taken reach ; semi-Markov extensions condition time-since-entry--current-state instead.

heading: 12.203 Assumptions
text: Markov property (often, semi-Markov extensions available), proportional hazards within transition (relaxed via spline-based stratified models), accurate state classification time point.

heading: 12.204 R Implementation
code:
library(mstate)

# Example: illness-death model (3 states)
# Load example data
data(ebmt3)

tmat <- trans.illdeath()     # transition matrix
d_ms <- msprep(data = ebmt3, trans = tmat,
               time = c(NA, "prtime", "rfstime"),
               status = c(NA, "prstat", "rfsstat"))

# Fit Cox models per transition
fit <- coxph(Surv(Tstart, Tstop, status) ~ dissub + age + strata(trans),
             data = d_ms)
summary(fit)

heading: 12.205 Output & Results
text: msprep() reshapes wide-format clinical data transition-stratified long format multi-state Cox models expect. Cox fit produces transition-specific coefficients (interactions strata(trans) included). mstate::msfit() mstate::probtrans() deliver predicted cumulative hazards per transition transition probabilities \(P_{jk}(s, t)\) horizon.

heading: 12.206 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Increasing age associated elevated transition hazards healthy relapsed (HR 1.02 per year, 95 % CI 1.01 1.04) healthy death (HR 1.04, 95 % CI 1.02 1.06); predicted probability relapsed state five years 28 % 30-year-old patient 41 % 60-year-old.” Predictions absolute probabilities communicate multi-state results far better underlying transition-specific hazard ratios alone.

heading: 12.207 Practical Tips
text: Draw state diagram analysis; clear picture allowed transitions prevents misspecified transition matrices.msprep() converts wide patient-level data long format one row per allowed transition per subject; common entry barrier new users.Use probtrans() predictions communicate results absolute terms — readers absorb “20 % probability death year 5” much better three transition hazard ratios.Markov vs semi-Markov choice matters sojourn-time-dependent hazards; mstate supports via Tstart argument time-origin handling.high-dimensional state spaces, sparse-state extensions msm (continuous-time Markov chains panel data) often tractable full-likelihood multi-state models.Validate predictions held-data; multi-state Cox models can overfit transition-specific hazards individual transitions events.

heading: 12.211 Introduction
text: Nelson-Aalen estimator standard non-parametric estimator cumulative hazard \(H(t)\) right-censored data. natural counterpart Kaplan-Meier estimator: KM directly targets survival function, Nelson-Aalen targets cumulative hazard, two linked \(S(t) = \exp(-H(t))\). Nelson-Aalen slightly better small-sample properties Kaplan-Meier right tail natural input analysis framed terms hazards rather survival probabilities, including diagnostics proportional hazards across groups.

heading: 12.212 Prerequisites
text: working understanding cumulative hazards, Kaplan-Meier estimator, relationship \(S(t) = \exp(-H(t))\).

heading: 12.213 Theory
text: Nelson-Aalen estimator \[\hat H(t) = \sum_{t_i \le t} \frac{d_i}{n_i},\]\(d_i\) number events \(t_i\) \(n_i\) number risk just \(t_i\). event time contributes empirical hazard increment \(d_i / n_i\), cumulative hazard running sum. variance estimator \[\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}\bigl(\hat H(t)\bigr) = \sum_{t_i \le t} d_i / n_i^2,\]gives pointwise confidence bands log-log scale. Nelson-Aalen-based survival estimator \(\hat S(t) = \exp(-\hat H(t))\) differs slightly Kaplan-Meier product-limit estimator preferred cumulative-hazard scale primary reporting target.

heading: 12.214 Assumptions
text: Independent, non-informative censoring. Adequate -risk sets across follow-window — right tail estimator wide variance handful subjects remain.

heading: 12.215 R Implementation
code:
library(survival)

data(lung)
fit <- survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ 1, data = lung)

# Cumulative hazard via NA
plot(fit, fun = "cumhaz", main = "Nelson-Aalen cumulative hazard")

# Underlying values
summary(fit)$cumhaz |> head()

heading: 12.222 Introduction
text: log-normal survival model assumes \(\log T\) follows Normal distribution. distinctive feature among parametric survival models non-monotone hazard — instantaneous risk rises early follow-, peaks, declines. shape suits diseases processes susceptible individuals fail relatively quickly hardy subset survives indefinitely: example, post-surgical mortality (early peri-operative risk wanes), cancer recurrences, time--engagement processes behavioural data. Weibull exponential models lock monotone hazards, log-normal accommodates rise--fall pattern naturally.

heading: 12.223 Prerequisites
text: working understanding log-normal distribution, parametric survival regression, accelerated-failure-time (AFT) parameterisation alternative proportional-hazards form.

heading: 12.224 Theory
text: log-normal survival function \[S(t) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\frac{\log t - \mu}{\sigma}\right),\]hazard\[h(t) = \frac{(1/(t \sigma)) \phi((\log t - \mu)/\sigma)}{1 - \Phi((\log t - \mu)/\sigma)}.\]hazard unique maximum near \(t \approx \exp(\mu - \sigma^2)\) \(\sigma > 1\) decreases thereafter. AFT parameterisation \(\log T = X\beta + \sigma W\) (\(W \sim \mathcal N(0, 1)\)) makes regression coefficients interpretable multiplicative effects event time — one-unit increase covariate scales time scale \(\exp(-\beta)\).

heading: 12.225 Assumptions
text: non-monotone (rising--falling) hazard, log-Normal residual structure log time scale, independent observations, non-informative censoring.

heading: 12.226 R Implementation
code:
library(survival); library(flexsurv)

data(lung)
fit <- survreg(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex, data = lung, dist = "lognormal")
summary(fit)

# flexsurv
flex <- flexsurvreg(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex, data = lung, dist = "lnorm")
plot(flex, type = "hazard")

heading: 12.227 Output & Results
text: survreg() returns AFT coefficients log-time scale plus scale parameter \(\sigma\). flexsurvreg() produces equivalent estimates cleaner survival, hazard, quantile prediction methods. plot(flex, type = "hazard") draws model-implied hazard curve, making rise-peak-decline shape visible KM-based hazard estimates.

heading: 12.228 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “log-normal AFT model estimated additional year age shortened expected event time approximately 2 % (\(\hat\beta_{\mathrm{age}} = -0.02\), 95 % CI \(-0.04\) \(-0.00\)); model-implied hazard peaked approximately 9 months declined thereafter, consistent observed concentration events first year follow-.” Always plot implied hazard alongside Kaplan-Meier verify non-monotone shape supported data.

heading: 12.233 Introduction
text: Weibull distribution flexible parametric survival family monotone hazard. Hazard increasing shape parameter \(k > 1\), constant (exponential) \(k = 1\), decreasing \(k < 1\). Among common parametric families unique admitting proportional-hazards accelerated-failure-time interpretations, makes natural starting point parametric survival analysis whenever PH plausible. Cleaner extrapolation, smaller standard errors, explicit hazard shape practical pay-offs semi-parametric Cox model assumptions hold.

heading: 12.234 Prerequisites

heading: 12.235 Theory
text: Weibull survival function \[S(t) = \exp\!\left(-(t/\lambda)^k\right),\]hazard \(h(t) = (k/\lambda)(t/\lambda)^{k-1}\). hazard monotone: increasing \(k > 1\) (positive ageing), decreasing \(k < 1\) (early-failure regimes), constant \(k = 1\) (exponential).Two parameterisations coexist:Accelerated-failure-time (AFT) survreg(dist = "weibull"): \(\log T = X\beta + \sigma W\) \(W\) standard extreme-value variate; scale \(\sigma = 1/k\).Shape-scale flexsurv::flexsurvreg(dist = "weibull"): explicit shape \(k\) scale \(\lambda\), PH-style covariate effects via interactions \(\lambda\).conversion PH AFT forms \(\beta_{\mathrm{PH}} = -k \cdot \beta_{\mathrm{AFT}}\).

heading: 12.236 Assumptions
text: monotone hazard. standard diagnostic log–log survival plot: \(\log[-\log \hat S(t)]\) \(\log t\) approximately linear slope \(k\) Weibull holds.

fit_flex <- flexsurvreg(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex, data = lung,
                        dist = "weibull")
fit_flex

heading: 12.244 Introduction
text: Recurrent-event analysis applies whenever single subject can experience event interest multiple times follow-— hospital admissions, seizures, infections, asthma exacerbations, recurrent tumours. Treating first event collapses information wastes power; treating events independent inflates effective sample size produces invalid standard errors. Several modelling frameworks balance extremes, suited different scientific question.

heading: 12.245 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Cox regression, counting-process data formats, within-subject correlation source dependent observations.

heading: 12.246 Theory
text: main approaches:Andersen-Gill (AG): counting-process Cox model treats events equally uses cluster-robust standard errors account within-subject dependence. Estimates single baseline hazard recurrent process.Prentice-Williams-Peterson (PWP): stratifies event number, allowing separate baseline hazards first, second, third, etc. event. Two flavours: total time (gap study start) gap time (gap previous event). Use hazard structure changes event order.Wei-Lin-Weissfeld (WLW): fits separate marginal Cox model event order, using first event \(K=1\), first two \(K=2\), etc. Conceptually cleaner increasingly favour conditions survived enough events risk.Shared-frailty: Cox model subject-level random effect \(u_i\) captures unmeasured individual heterogeneity. Combine AG frailty natural interpretation within-subject correlation.

heading: 12.247 Assumptions
text: chosen correlation structure matches data, hazard specification (PH within strata marginally) appropriate, counting-process intervals correctly constructed (overlaps, gaps).

heading: 12.248 R Implementation
code:
library(survival)

# Counting-process data: id, tstart, tstop, status (1 = event)
data(cgd, package = "survival")

# Andersen-Gill
ag <- coxph(Surv(tstart, tstop, status) ~ treat + inherit,
            data = cgd, cluster = id)
summary(ag)

# PWP stratified by event number
cgd$event.num <- ave(cgd$status, cgd$id, FUN = cumsum) + 1
pwp <- coxph(Surv(tstart, tstop, status) ~ treat + inherit + strata(event.num),
             data = cgd, cluster = id)
summary(pwp)

heading: 12.249 Output & Results
text: fits return hazard-ratio estimates cluster-robust standard errors. AG produces single HR per covariate covering full recurrent-event process; PWP produces strata-specific baselines sharing covariate effects across strata, allowing different baseline hazards per event order.

heading: 12.250 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Andersen-Gill counting-process Cox model estimated prophylactic treatment reduced recurrent-infection hazard 60 % (HR 0.40, 95 % CI 0.25 0.63), cluster-robust SEs accounting within-subject correlation; PWP model stratified event order produced consistent estimate (HR 0.43, 95 % CI 0.26 0.70), suggesting treatment effect vary materially across event order.” Compare AG PWP whenever data structure permits.

heading: 12.251 Practical Tips
text: AG default starting point applications easiest interpret.PWP (gap-time) appropriate hazard depends time since previous event; PWP (total time) depends time since study start.WLW fallen favour conditions event order way produces awkward causal interpretations.unmeasured individual heterogeneity captured covariates, add shared frailty term AG model (+ frailty(id)).Always use cluster-robust SEs (cluster = id); ignoring within-subject correlation produces SEs small Type error inflated.Pair hazard ratios absolute counts events, expected rates, cumulative mean functions; recurrent-event audiences typically reason absolute frequencies rather hazard ratios.

heading: 12.255 Introduction
text: restricted mean survival time (RMST) expected survival time truncated clinically meaningful horizon \(\tau\). hazard ratio Cox model summarises relative effect requires proportional hazards interpretable, RMST quantifies absolute time difference (days, months, years) remains valid even hazard ratio constant. become increasingly standard endpoint oncology trials immunotherapy studies, crossing delayed survival curves common regulators clinicians want number expressed natural time scale.

heading: 12.256 Prerequisites
text: working understanding survival functions, Kaplan-Meier estimator, issues proportional-hazards assumptions modern clinical trials.

heading: 12.257 Theory
text: survival function \(S(t)\) horizon \(\tau\), RMST \[\mathrm{RMST}(\tau) = \mathbb E[\min(T, \tau)] = \int_0^\tau S(t) \, \mathrm d t.\]Geometrically area KM curve time zero \(\tau\). Group differences summarised difference \(\mathrm{RMST}_1(\tau) - \mathrm{RMST}_0(\tau)\) ratio. Standard errors come asymptotic theory developed Royston Parmar; bootstrap confidence intervals alternative asymptotic approximation suspect. Crucially, construction makes assumption relationship hazards two groups — non-proportionality, treatment-effect waning, crossing curves handled gracefully.

heading: 12.258 Assumptions
text: Sufficient follow-estimate \(S(t)\) reliably \(\tau\); explicit choice \(\tau\) clinically meaningful within support data; non-informative censoring \(\tau\).

heading: 12.259 R Implementation

library(survRM2)

data(lung)

# Truncation at 600 days
rmst2(time = lung$time, status = lung$status,
      arm = lung$sex - 1, tau = 600)

heading: 12.261 Interpretation

library(survival)

heading: 12.288 Introduction
text: Simulating censored time--event data essential three classes work: power sample-size calculations trials non-standard designs, validation new statistical methods (estimator recover truth?), pedagogy. inverse-hazard method generates event times specified hazard function lets build realistic datasets match target survival pattern, censoring distribution, covariate-effect structure.

heading: 12.289 Prerequisites
text: working understanding hazard cumulative hazard functions, inverse-CDF method random-number generation, relationship proportional-hazards models exponentiated covariate effects.

heading: 12.290 Theory
text: hazard \(h(t \mid x)\) cumulative hazard \(H(t \mid x)\) uniform variate \(U \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)\),\[T = H^{-1}\bigl(-\log U \mid x\bigr)\]target distribution. proportional hazards \(H(t \mid x) = H_0(t) \exp(x^\top \beta)\), \[T_i = H_0^{-1}\!\left(-\log U_i / \exp(x_i^\top \beta)\right).\]Censoring added drawing independent censoring time \(C_i\) chosen distribution (administrative, exponential, empirical real cohort) recording \(\min(T_i, C_i)\) observation time \(\mathbf 1\{T_i \le C_i\}\) event indicator.

heading: 12.291 Assumptions
text: specified baseline hazard covariate-effect structure truth simulated; censoring distribution independent \(T_i\) unless simulation explicitly handles informative censoring.

heading: 12.292 R Implementation
code:
library(simsurv)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
x <- data.frame(id = 1:n, arm = factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = n/2)))
x$arm_num <- as.numeric(x$arm) - 1

# Weibull baseline: lambda = 0.1, gamma = 1.2
sim <- simsurv(lambdas = 0.1, gammas = 1.2,
               x = x, betas = c(arm_num = -0.5),
               maxt = 10)

# Merge with covariates
d <- merge(sim, x, by = "id")
head(d)

# Kaplan-Meier
library(survival)
fit_km <- survfit(Surv(eventtime, status) ~ arm, data = d)
plot(fit_km)

heading: 12.293 Output & Results
text: simsurv() returns tibble one row per subject containing simulated event time, censoring indicator (taken maxt = 10), subject id. Merging back covariate frame gives complete simulated dataset ready analysis. Kaplan-Meier plot shows two arms separating designed effect size.

heading: 12.294 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Simulated Weibull data shape 1.2, baseline scale 0.1, treatment hazard ratio \(\exp(-0.5) = 0.61\); Cox model simulated data recovered HR 0.59 (95 % CI 0.46 0.76), confirming simulation correctly calibrated.” Always confirm analysis pipeline recovers simulation parameters using simulator power calculations.

heading: 12.299 Introduction
text: Time-dependent ROC extends classical ROC analysis right-censored survival data. chosen evaluation time \(t\), analysis treats subjects observed fail \(t\) cases subjects known survive past \(t\) controls; subjects censored \(t\) reweighted via inverse-probability--censoring weights. resulting time-specific AUC summarises well continuous risk score (Cox linear predictor) discriminates failures survivors \(t\). natural extension C-index “AUC exactly one year” reporting.

heading: 12.300 Prerequisites
text: working understanding ROC curves, C-index, Cox regression linear predictors, inverse-probability--censoring weighting.

heading: 12.301 Theory
text: Two definitions cases controls compete:Incident / dynamic (\(/D\)): cases subjects fail time \(t\) exactly; controls still risk past \(t\). Useful hazard-style discrimination.Cumulative / dynamic (\(C/D\)): cases subjects fail time \(\le t\); controls still risk past \(t\). common choice clinical risk-score reporting.Inverse-probability--censoring weights compensate subjects censored comparison can made; censoring distribution typically estimated Kaplan-Meier censoring times. result time-dependent AUC requested \(t\) bootstrap analytical confidence intervals.

heading: 12.302 Assumptions
text: Non-informative censoring (correctly conditional covariates weighting = "cox" "aalen" used) continuous risk marker meaningful ranking score.

heading: 12.303 R Implementation
code:
library(timeROC); library(survival)

data(lung)
fit <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ age + sex + ph.ecog, data = lung)
lp <- predict(fit, type = "lp")

tROC <- timeROC(T = lung$time, delta = lung$status, marker = lp,
                times = c(180, 365, 730), cause = 1,
                weighting = "marginal")
tROC
plot(tROC, time = 365, col = "steelblue")

heading: 12.310 Introduction
text: Real survival studies routinely include covariates whose value changes follow-: treatment initiation, laboratory measurements repeated visit, secondary events alter prognosis. Including time-fixed baseline values misrepresents exposure history; pretending baseline value applied throughout follow-biases hazard-ratio estimates leading cause immortal-time errors. standard remedy counting-process data layout, splits subject’s follow-intervals covariate values constant lets partial likelihood combine contributions correctly.

heading: 12.312 Theory
text: counting-process format row represents one interval \((\tau_{ij}^{\text{start}}, \tau_{ij}^{\text{stop}}]\) covariate values subject \(\) constant. columns :tstart: interval start.tstop: interval end.event: 1 event occurred tstop, otherwise 0.Covariates values interval.partial likelihood evaluates subject’s risk-set contribution using whichever interval includes event time, event time naturally compares subjects -current covariate values. Robust (“sandwich”) variance estimators, requested via cluster = id, account within-subject correlation induced multiple rows per subject.

heading: 12.313 Assumptions
text: Covariate values constant within interval accurately measured. covariate “internal” trajectory affected event interest (e.g., biomarker changes treatment), “external” determined exogenously (e.g., scheduled treatment dose); inference internal covariates requires careful causal interpretation.
