heading: 12.321 Introduction
text: standard log-rank test gives every event time equal weight comparison groups. optimal weighting proportional hazards loses power difference groups concentrated one end follow-— early differences wane (typical immunotherapy run-effects), late differences emerge delay (typical immune-checkpoint inhibitors), crossing-curve patterns. Weighted log-rank tests recover power settings emphasising time region difference expected.

heading: 12.322 Prerequisites
text: working understanding log-rank test, proportional-hazards assumption, relationship hazard ratios survival curves different follow-times.

heading: 12.323 Theory
text: general form weighted log-rank test \[\chi^2 = \frac{\bigl(\sum_j w(t_j) [O_j - E_j]\bigr)^2}{\sum_j w(t_j)^2 V_j},\]\(O_j - E_j\) observed-minus-expected event count \(t_j\) \(V_j\) variance. Common weighting schemes:Log-rank: \(w(t) = 1\). Optimal proportional hazards.Wilcoxon / Gehan-Breslow: \(w(t_j) = n_j\) (size risk set). Emphasises early differences.Peto-Peto: \(w(t) = \hat S(t)\). Similar Wilcoxon uses pooled survival estimator.Fleming-Harrington \(G^{\rho, \gamma}\): \(w(t) = \hat S(t^-)^\rho \, [1 - \hat S(t^-)]^\gamma\). \(G^{1, 0}\) emphasises early differences; \(G^{0, 1}\) emphasises late differences; \(G^{1, 1}\) emphasises mid-follow-differences.Combination tests (max-combo) compute several weights simultaneously apply multiple-testing correction; preserves Type control gaining power across range alternative shapes.

heading: 12.324 Assumptions
text: Independent non-informative censoring. chosen weight reflects hazard pattern actually expected; post-hoc weight selection inflates Type error statistical malpractice.

heading: 12.325 R Implementation
code:
library(survival)

data(lung)
# Standard log-rank (rho = 0)
survdiff(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung, rho = 0)

# Peto-Peto (rho = 1): weights early differences
survdiff(Surv(time, status) ~ sex, data = lung, rho = 1)

# Fleming-Harrington via nphRCT or similar package (conceptually)

heading: 12.326 Output & Results
text: survdiff() reports chi-squared statistic, degrees freedom, \(p\)-value, observed-vs-expected counts chosen weight (rho argument). nph nphRCT packages add Fleming-Harrington \(G^{\rho, \gamma}\) tests combination tests formal Type control.

heading: 12.327 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “pre-specified Peto-Peto weighted log-rank test rejected null equal survival (\(\chi^2_1 = 12.8\), \(p < 0.001\)); standard log-rank produced similar conclusion (\(\chi^2_1 = 10.3\), \(p = 0.001\)). Peto-Peto weighting chosen priori based prior evidence treatment effect concentrated first 18 months.” Always state weight advance justify ; weight-shopping common abuse tests.

heading: 12.328 Practical Tips
text: Pre-specify weighting scheme protocol; switching weights seeing data inflates Type error far nominal level.trials expected delayed treatment effects (immunotherapy), Fleming-Harrington \(G^{0, 1}\) max-combo test increasingly standard.Restricted-mean survival time (RMST) complementary approach non-proportional hazards; report proportional hazards question.Combination tests (max-combo: standard log-rank, \(G^{1, 0}\), \(G^{0, 1}\), \(G^{1, 1}\)) preserve Type control via Hochberg-style adjustment; nph::nphRCT implements .Inspect Kaplan-Meier curve choosing weight; clearly delayed effect rules standard log-rank primary test.Modern immuno-oncology trials routinely pre-specify max-combo RMST primary analysis; era one-size-fits-log-rank reporting closing.

# Keep death records (etype == 2) + recurrence merged; for teaching
# we build a two-event dataset:
dat <- colon |>
  filter(etype == 1) |>                       # recurrence row per subject

protime + stage, data = d)

                   protime + stage,
                 data = as.data.frame(d), ntree = 500,
                 importance = TRUE)

  arrange(desc(imp)) |>

heading: 12.352 4. Check
text: Compare concordance held-data.DeepSurv conceptual sketch.
code:
tr <- d[idx, ]; te <- d[-idx, ]
cox2 <- coxph(Surv(time, status) ~ bili + albumin + age + edema +
                protime + stage, data = tr)
rsf2 <- rfsrc(Surv(time, status) ~ bili + albumin + age + edema +
                protime + stage,
              data = as.data.frame(tr), ntree = 500)
c_cox <- survival::concordance(cox2, newdata = te)$concordance
p_rsf <- predict(rsf2, newdata = as.data.frame(te))$predicted
c_rsf <- survival::concordance(Surv(te$time, te$status) ~ p_rsf,
                               reverse = TRUE)$concordance
c(cox = c_cox, rsf = c_rsf)
library(torch)
# A DeepSurv-style loss computes the partial log-likelihood on the
# sorted risk set. The network output h(x) replaces the Cox linear
# predictor; the loss is  -(sum_{i:event}  h(x_i) - log sum_{j in R_i} exp(h(x_j))).
deepsurv_loss <- function(risk, time, event) {
  ord <- order(time, decreasing = TRUE)
  risk <- risk[ord]; event <- event[ord]
  log_cumsum <- torch_logcumsumexp(risk, dim = 1)
  -torch_sum(event * (risk - log_cumsum)) / torch_sum(event)
}

heading: 12.360 Background
text:
Survival analysis handles outcomes times event, 
subjects yet event observation ends — 
familiar right-censoring problem. Ignoring censoring throwing 
censored observations biases estimator; pretending censored
observations event-free biases way. survival
machinery handles censoring correctly reasoning event time
still risk.Three tools cover clinical applications: Kaplan-Meier
estimator non-parametric survival curve, log-rank test 
two-group comparison, Cox proportional-hazards regression 
adjustment multivariable modelling. PH assumption — 
hazard ratio constant time — deserves explicit checking; 
fails, time-varying coefficient stratified model usually
right fix.

heading: 12.374 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(survival)
library(pROC)

heading: 12.375 1. Hypothesis
text:
Cox model fit development cohort retains calibration 
discrimination external cohort shifted covariate
distribution.

heading: 12.376 2. Visualise
code:
x <- rnorm(n, mu_x)
  lp <- beta * x
  t <- rexp(n, rate = exp(lp - 1))
  c <- rexp(n, rate = 0.1)
  time <- pmin(t, c)
  event <- as.integer(t <= c)
  tibble(x = x, time = time, event = event)
}
dev  <- make_cohort(500, mu_x = 0)
ext  <- make_cohort(500, mu_x = 1.0)   # distribution shift
bind_rows(dev |> mutate(cohort = "dev"),
          ext |> mutate(cohort = "ext")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x, fill = cohort)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, alpha = 0.7, position = "identity")

heading: 12.377 3. Assumptions

Independent right censoring; Cox proportional hazards 
development cohort; time-varying covariates.

heading: 12.378 4. Conduct
text: Fit dev; predict survival fixed horizon cohorts.calibration plot horizon.
code:
horizon <- 3

predict_surv <- function(fit, newdata, t) {
  bh <- basehaz(fit, centered = FALSE)
  H0 <- approx(bh$time, bh$hazard, xout = t, rule = 2)$y
  lp <- predict(fit, newdata = newdata, type = "lp")
  exp(-H0 * exp(lp))
}

s_dev <- predict_surv(fit, dev, horizon)
s_ext <- predict_surv(fit, ext, horizon)

# Observed indicator (for simple Brier w/out censoring at horizon)
obs_dev <- dev$event == 1 & dev$time <= horizon
obs_ext <- ext$event == 1 & ext$time <= horizon

brier <- function(p_event, obs) mean((p_event - obs)^2)
brier_null <- function(obs) mean((mean(obs) - obs)^2)
b_dev <- brier(1 - s_dev, obs_dev); b0_dev <- brier_null(obs_dev)
b_ext <- brier(1 - s_ext, obs_ext); b0_ext <- brier_null(obs_ext)
ipa_dev <- 1 - b_dev / b0_dev
ipa_ext <- 1 - b_ext / b0_ext

auc_dev <- as.numeric(auc(roc(obs_dev, 1 - s_dev, quiet = TRUE)))
auc_ext <- as.numeric(auc(roc(obs_ext, 1 - s_ext, quiet = TRUE)))

tibble(cohort = c("dev", "ext"),
       auc = c(auc_dev, auc_ext),
       brier = c(b_dev, b_ext),
       ipa  = c(ipa_dev, ipa_ext))  tibble(p = p, y = y, cohort = lab) |>
    mutate(bin = cut(p, quantile(p, seq(0, 1, by = 0.1)),
                     include.lowest = TRUE)) |>
    group_by(cohort, bin) |>
    summarise(mean_pred = mean(p), obs = mean(y), .groups = "drop")
}
bind_rows(mk_cal(1 - s_dev, obs_dev, "dev"),
          mk_cal(1 - s_ext, obs_ext, "ext")) |>
  ggplot(aes(mean_pred, obs, colour = cohort)) +
  geom_point() + geom_line() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "grey50") +
  labs(x = "predicted event prob at horizon",
       y = "observed event proportion")

heading: 12.385 Prerequisites
text: Survival basics; coxph syntax.

heading: 12.386 Background
text:
Immortal-time bias arises period outcome
occur (definition exposure) misclassified 
follow-one exposure groups. classic example: “users
drug X” defined anyone ever received prescription;
people must definition survived first
prescription, start follow-baseline rather
prescription, give “immortal” time biases
hazard ratio toward protection.Two standard fixes exist. Landmark analysis picks time 
baseline classifies exposure based happened 
time; follow-starts landmark bias disappears.
Time-dependent Cox models treat exposure function time 
let patients change exposure group actually start 
drug, using counting-process (start, stop, event) data.Time-dependent covariates can external (calendar time, drug
availability) internal (lab value evolves 
disease). Internal time-dependent covariates can introduce 
bias conditioning — may mediators 
effect studying.

heading: 12.387 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(survival)
library(ggsurvfit)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 12.388 1. Hypothesis
text:
simulate cohort every patient true hazard.
patients start drug random later time. naive analysis
counts drug users baseline produce spurious
protective effect.

heading: 12.389 2. Visualise
code:
dat <- tibble(
  id = seq_len(n),
  t_event = rexp(n, rate = 0.02),        # true event time
  t_drug  = rexp(n, rate = 0.05),        # time of drug start
  ever_drug = t_drug < t_event,          # did they start drug before event?
  t_drug_obs = pmin(t_drug, t_event),
  t_obs   = pmin(t_event, 100),          # admin censoring at 100
  event   = as.integer(t_event <= 100)
)

dat |>
  ggplot(aes(t_obs, fill = ever_drug)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, alpha = 0.6,
                 position = "identity") +
  labs(x = "Observed time", y = "Count", fill = "Ever drug?")

heading: 12.390 3. Assumptions
text:
Proportional hazards (Cox), non-informative censoring, 
independence event times across patients. “ever drug” split
deliberately biased construction.

heading: 12.391 4. Conduct
code:
fit_naive <- coxph(Surv(t_obs, event) ~ ever_drug, data = dat)
summary(fit_naive)
# classify by drug status as of 20.
landmark <- 20
dat_lm <- dat |>
  filter(t_obs > landmark) |>
  mutate(drug_lm = t_drug < landmark,
         t_obs_lm = t_obs - landmark)

fit_lm <- coxph(Surv(t_obs_lm, event) ~ drug_lm, data = dat_lm)
summary(fit_lm)
td <- dat |>
  transmute(
    id,
    tstart = 0,
    tstop  = if_else(ever_drug, t_drug_obs, t_obs),
    event  = if_else(ever_drug, 0L, event),
    drug   = 0L
  ) |>
  bind_rows(
    dat |>
      filter(ever_drug) |>
      transmute(id,
                tstart = t_drug_obs,
                tstop  = t_obs,
                event  = event,
                drug   = 1L)
  ) |>
  filter(tstop > tstart) |>
  arrange(id, tstart)

fit_td <- coxph(Surv(tstart, tstop, event) ~ drug, data = td)
summary(fit_td)

heading: 12.392 5. Concluding statement
text:
naive Cox analysis produced hazard ratio 
round(exp(coef(fit_naive)), 2) ever-drug — spuriously
protective, immortal time counted exposed
follow-. landmark analysis time 20 gave HR
round(exp(coef(fit_lm)), 2), time-dependent Cox model
gave HR round(exp(coef(fit_td)), 2), close null
expected simulated truth.

heading: 13.3 Introduction
text: Ensembl’s Variant Effect Predictor (VEP) assigns variant predicted consequence (missense, synonymous, splice-donor, intronic, etc.), gene transcript IDs, pathogenicity scores predictors (SIFT, PolyPhen, CADD, REVEL). standard annotator variant interpretation clinical genetics population genomics.

heading: 13.4 Prerequisites
text: VCF file; Ensembl RefSeq transcript database.

heading: 13.5 Theory
text: VEP traverses variant, intersects transcript structures, emits one record per overlapping transcript predicted functional consequence. Gene-level summaries pick severe consequence across transcripts (MANE-select canonical transcript standard clinical use).

heading: 13.6 Assumptions
text: Reference build matches VCF (GRCh37 vs GRCh38); transcript database current.

heading: 13.7 R Implementation
text: VEP runs command line (Perl). programmatic annotation R, use VariantAnnotation::locateVariants() TxDb plus predictCoding() protein consequences.representative VEP command (run ):
code:
library(VariantAnnotation)
library(TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene)
library(BSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19)

vcf_file <- system.file("extdata", "chr22.vcf.gz", package = "VariantAnnotation")
vcf <- readVcf(vcf_file, "hg19")
seqlevelsStyle(vcf) <- "UCSC"

txdb <- TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene
loc  <- locateVariants(rowRanges(vcf), txdb, CodingVariants())
table(loc$LOCATION)

pc <- predictCoding(vcf, txdb, Hsapiens)
head(mcols(pc)[, c("CONSEQUENCE", "REFAA", "VARAA")])vep -i input.vcf -o output.vcf --cache --sift p --polyphen p --canonical

heading: 13.8 Output & Results

heading: 13.9 Interpretation

heading: 13.10 Practical Tips

heading: 13.13 Introduction
text: ATAC-seq (Assay Transposase-Accessible Chromatin using sequencing) measures genome-wide chromatin accessibility tagging accessible DNA regions hyperactive Tn5 transposase. Peaks resulting alignment density correspond open chromatin — regulatory elements promoters enhancers — differential accessibility analysis conditions reveals cell-state-specific regulatory changes. ATAC-seq largely replaced DNase-seq accessibility profiling lower input requirement (10{,}000 cells vs millions) simpler protocol.

heading: 13.14 Prerequisites
text: working understanding read alignment, BAM files, peak-calling concepts (typically MACS2), regulatory genomics (promoters, enhancers, transcription factor binding).

heading: 13.15 Theory
text: standard ATAC-seq pipeline:QC: TSS enrichment score, fragment-length distribution characteristic nucleosomal peaks (mono-, di-, tri-nucleosomal approximately 150, 300, 450 bp).Duplicate mitochondrial-read removal: typical mitochondrial fraction 20–50 % reads must removed peak calling.Peak calling: MACS2 Tn5-specific flags (--nomodel --shift -75 --extsize 150) treats end fragment Tn5 cut site.Consensus peak set: combine peaks across replicates conditions joint quantitative analysis.Differential accessibility: DiffBind, edgeR, DESeq2 peak-level read counts.Peak annotation: ChIPseeker assigns peaks nearest gene annotates promoter vs distal categories.

heading: 13.16 Assumptions
text: Tn5 tagmentation approximately uniform across accessible regions; sequencing depth sufficient (\(> 50\) million reads per sample typical); mitochondrial reads removed peak calling.

heading: 13.17 R Implementation
code:
library(ATACseqQC); library(ChIPseeker)
library(TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene)

# Fragment-length diagnostic from an example ATAC bam shipped with ATACseqQC
bam <- system.file("extdata", "GL1.bam", package = "ATACseqQC")
# fragSize <- fragSizeDist(bam, bamFiles.labels = "example")

# Peak annotation example with a toy peak GRanges
set.seed(2026)
peaks <- GRanges(
  seqnames = rep("chr1", 50),
  ranges = IRanges(start = sort(sample(1e7:1.05e7, 50)),
                   width = 500)
)
anno <- annotatePeak(peaks,
                     TxDb = TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene,
                     level = "gene",
                     verbose = FALSE)
as.data.frame(anno)[1:5, c("seqnames", "start", "annotation", "geneId")]

heading: 13.18 Output & Results
text: ATACseqQC::fragSizeDist() produces diagnostic fragment-size distribution; visible peaks nucleosomal lengths confirm successful tagmentation. ChIPseeker::annotatePeak() assigns peak nearest gene categorises promoter, intronic, intergenic, etc.

heading: 13.19 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “ATAC-seq CD8 regulatory T cells (n = 4 per group) identified 25{,}412 reproducible peaks IDR < 0.05; ChIPseeker annotation showed 35 % promoter-proximal 65 % distal-regulatory locations. Differential accessibility analysis identified 1{,}240 peaks FDR < 0.05, enriched AP-1 family motifs (Jun, Fos).” Always report TSS enrichment, peak counts, consensus peak strategy.

heading: 13.20 Practical Tips
text: Remove duplicates mitochondrial reads samtools markdup chromosome filtering peak calling; mitochondrial reads dominate ATAC libraries confound peak calling retained.Check TSS enrichment score (computed ATACseqQC chrom.sizes-aware tools); scores 7 typical fresh tissue, scores 4 acceptable frozen samples.differential accessibility, define consensus peak set across replicates (DiffBind-style) quantify samples regions; calling peaks separately per sample produces unstable comparisons.Motif enrichment via chromVAR provides per-sample transcription-factor activity scores; pair differential accessibility identify TFs driving observed changes.single-cell ATAC-seq (scATAC-seq), use Signac (Seurat-flavoured) ArchR; workflow differs substantially bulk ATAC.Use BlackList regions (ENCODE) exclude artefact-prone genomic regions downstream analysis.

heading: 13.24 Introduction
text: Batch effects — systematic technical variation introduced different sequencing dates, instruments, library-prep operators, reagent lots — bias gene-expression measurements ways can swamp biological signal addressed. ComBat widely used batch-correction method, applying empirical-Bayes adjustment centre scale gene’s distribution per batch. appropriate batches known condition confounded batch; alternative — including batch covariate differential-expression models — preserves information downstream analysis explicitly models batch.

heading: 13.25 Prerequisites
text: working understanding normalisation, distinction technical biological variation, basic empirical-Bayes shrinkage.

heading: 13.26 Theory
text: ComBat models gene’s expression \[Y_{ij} = \alpha_j + X\beta_j + \gamma_{bj} + \delta_{bj} \varepsilon_{ij},\]sample \(\), gene \(j\), batch \(b\), additive batch effect \(\gamma_{bj}\), multiplicative batch effect \(\delta_{bj}\). empirical-Bayes step shrinks per-gene batch-effect estimates toward common distribution across genes, stabilising estimation batches contain samples. output expression matrix batch effects removed biological condition (specified via mod) preserved.ComBat-Seq (sva) count-data analogue, designed raw RNA-seq counts normalisation; standard ComBat assumes log-normalised input.

heading: 13.27 Assumptions
text: Batches known; biological condition perfectly confounded batch (otherwise correction impossible); sufficient samples per batch stable empirical-Bayes shrinkage.

heading: 13.28 R Implementation
code:
library(sva)

set.seed(2026)
# Simulated: 10 genes x 20 samples, 2 batches, 2 conditions
expr <- matrix(rnorm(10 * 20), 10, 20)
batch <- c(rep(1, 10), rep(2, 10))
condition <- rep(c("A", "B"), 10)

# Add batch effect
expr[, batch == 2] <- expr[, batch == 2] + 2

mod <- model.matrix(~ condition)
expr_corrected <- ComBat(dat = expr, batch = batch, mod = mod)

# PCA before/after
# prcomp(t(expr))$x[, 1:2]; prcomp(t(expr_corrected))$x[, 1:2]

heading: 13.29 Output & Results
text: ComBat() returns batch-corrected expression matrix; ComBat_seq() returns batch-corrected counts. PCA correction standard QC check: condition-driven clustering remain strengthen, batch-driven separation diminish substantially.

heading: 13.30 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “ComBat correction condition included covariate (mod = model.matrix(~ condition)) reduced batch variance 35 % PC1 5 %, preserving condition-driven separation explains 28 % remaining variance. corrected matrix used downstream visualisation clustering.” Always report covariates preserved correction.

heading: 13.31 Practical Tips
text: run ComBat condition fully confounded batch — correction remove biological signal along batch effect.Use ComBat_seq() raw RNA-seq counts; standard ComBat log-normalised data.differential-expression analysis, consider including batch covariate DE design (design = ~ batch + condition DESeq2/edgeR/limma) rather running ComBat first; model-based approach generally preferred.Always report batch-correction methods explicitly publications; reviewers expect clarity batch handled.Alternative methods include RUVSeq (latent factor extraction), svaseq (surrogate variable analysis), limma::removeBatchEffect() (simple linear adjustment); choice depends whether batches known.Pair correction PCA routine QC step; visible improvement validation whether correction worked.

heading: 13.32 R Packages Used
text: sva::ComBat() ComBat_seq() canonical empirical-Bayes batch correction; RUVSeq unsupervised factor-based correction; limma::removeBatchEffect() simple linear batch removal; harmony batch correction single-cell RNA-seq workflows.

heading: 13.35 Introduction
text: biomaRt provides programmatic interface Ensembl BioMart converting gene identifier systems (Ensembl gene IDs, Entrez IDs, HGNC symbols, RefSeq, UniProt), retrieving genomic coordinates, transcript structures, GO term annotations, orthologues across species. indispensable whenever gene list — differential expression, GWAS, genomic analysis — needs enriched curated annotation. biomaRt workflow builds queries filter, attribute, merge single call, replacing dozens manual lookups across web interfaces.

heading: 13.36 Prerequisites

heading: 13.37 Theory
text: biomaRt query targets Mart (database) dataset (species), specifying:Filters: genes query (input IDs type).Attributes: annotations return.Values: actual identifier values.core call getBM(attributes, filters, values, mart) issues query returns data frame. reproducibility, pin Ensembl release host = "<month><year>.archive.ensembl.org"; default host = "www.ensembl.org" points current release, changes time.biomaRt also supports cross-species queries via getLDS() (linked datasets) orthologue lookup, transcript-level queries splicing analyses, SNP queries Ensembl variation.

heading: 13.46 Introduction
text: Bulk RNA-seq asks genes change expression conditions. DESeq2 widely used framework analysis: models counts negative binomial distribution, shares dispersion information across genes stabilise estimates small samples, tests gene’s log-fold change significance multiple-testing correction. standard DESeq2 workflow goes raw count matrix list differentially expressed genes dozen lines R.

heading: 13.47 Prerequisites
text: reader understand count matrix (rows = genes, columns = samples), basic biology RNA-seq (reads mapped transcripts, summed per gene), idea generalised linear model. Familiarity Bioconductor data structures (SummarizedExperiment) helpful.

heading: 13.48 Theory
text: DESeq2 assumes read count \(K_{ij}\) gene \(\) sample \(j\) follows negative binomial distribution\[K_{ij} \sim \text{NB}(\text{mean} = \mu_{ij},\; \text{dispersion} = \alpha_i),\]\(\mu_{ij} = s_j q_{ij}\) product sample-specific size factor \(s_j\) (sequencing depth normalisation) gene-specific expected count \(q_{ij}\) depends experimental design log-linear model \(\log_2 q_{ij} = \sum_k x_{jk} \beta_{ik}\).Three ideas make DESeq2 work well small-sample studies:Size factors computed median--ratios method, robust highly expressed genes dominating normalisation.Dispersion shrinkage borrows strength across genes: gene’s dispersion estimate shrunk toward mean-dispersion trend fitted across genes, noisy per-gene estimates small samples produce wild inference.Log-fold-change shrinkage (via apeglm ashr) pulls LFCs low-count genes toward zero, preventing dominating ranked gene lists.Wald test compares estimated coefficient interest zero, standard errors reflect design dispersion. Benjamini-Hochberg procedure controls false discovery rate across thousands genes tested.

heading: 13.49 Assumptions
text: Read counts follow negative binomial distribution – usually reasonable bulk mRNA-seq; may fail -low-coverage heavily PCR-amplified libraries.dispersion-mean trend smooth across genes.Replicates within condition exchangeable accounting modelled covariates.confounding biological condition technical covariates (batch, library prep date). , include technical covariate design.

heading: 13.50 R Implementation
text: toy simulation creates 20,000 genes 6 samples, upregulates first 200 genes treated condition, runs standard DESeq2 pipeline: construct DESeqDataSet, filter -low-count genes, estimate size factors dispersion, fit model, shrink LFCs reporting.
code:
library(DESeq2)
library(apeglm)
library(ggplot2)

counts <- matrix(rnbinom(20000 * 6, mu = 100, size = 10),
                 nrow = 20000, ncol = 6)
rownames(counts) <- paste0("gene", seq_len(20000))
colnames(counts) <- paste0("sample", seq_len(6))
coldata <- data.frame(condition = factor(rep(c("control", "treated"), each = 3)),
                      row.names = colnames(counts))

counts[1:200, 4:6] <- counts[1:200, 4:6] * 3

dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(countData = counts,
                              colData = coldata,
                              design = ~ condition)

dds <- dds[rowSums(counts(dds) >= 10) >= 3, ]

dds <- DESeq(dds)

res <- lfcShrink(dds, coef = "condition_treated_vs_control",
                 type = "apeglm")

summary(res)
head(res[order(res$padj), ])

plotMA(res, ylim = c(-3, 3))

heading: 13.51 Output & Results
text: summary(res) output reports number genes - -regulated default alpha (0.1): typically hundred simulated upregulated set, small number false positives FDR 10%. MA plot shows shrunken LFCs compressed toward zero low mean expression, desired.

heading: 13.52 Interpretation
text: manuscript: “Differential expression treated control samples assessed DESeq2 (v1.44). Size factors estimated median--ratios method; dispersions shared across genes via DESeq2 trend; log-fold changes shrunk using apeglm. 212 genes differentially expressed FDR 5% (154 , 58 ).”

heading: 13.53 Practical Tips
text: Use vst() rlog() transformations downstream visualisation (PCA, heatmaps); use raw counts DESeq2’s pipeline.Filter low-count genes running DESeq (e.g., keep genes \(\geq 10\) counts \(\geq\) smallest group size). improves multiple-testing adjusted p-values without inflating false discoveries.Always inspect PCA normalised samples differential expression. samples cluster batch rather condition, revise design formula interpreting results.apeglm shrinkage recommended default LFC reporting. ashr handles contrasts; normal legacy method.Report shrunken LFCs ranking visualisation, use Wald test p-values significance calling.

heading: 13.56 Introduction
text: BWA-MEM Bowtie2 dominant short-read DNA aligners modern sequencing pipelines. use Burrows-Wheeler-transformed (BWT) index reference genome achieve rapid exact approximate matching. BWT compresses reference preserving ability query substrings efficiently — algorithmic insight brought genome-scale alignment hours per sample minutes. Production alignment runs command line; R wrappers (Rbowtie2, BWA via Rsubread) integrate downstream Bioconductor analyses.

heading: 13.57 Prerequisites
text: working understanding FASTQ format, reference genomes, SAM/BAM alignment format.

heading: 13.58 Theory
text: BWA-MEM (Burrows-Wheeler Aligner, Maximal Exact Matches) seeds local alignments finding maximal exact matches, extends via banded Smith-Waterman. default aligner whole-genome DNA sequencing accuracy long-insert paired-end reads handling indels.Bowtie2 offers end--end alignment (read must align contiguously) local alignment (soft-clipping allowed read ends). fast accurate short reads common choice ChIP-seq, ATAC-seq, small-genome DNA sequencing.write SAM (text) BAM (binary) output, including alignment scores, mapping quality, mate-pair information, tags soft-clipping, mismatches, features. long reads (PacBio, Nanopore), use minimap2 instead — designed higher error rates longer alignment lengths.

heading: 13.59 Assumptions
text: Short reads (typically ~300 bp; longer, switch minimap2); reference genome appropriate species assembly version experiment.

heading: 13.60 R Implementation

# Read a short segment
# scanBam("aln_sorted.bam", param = ScanBamParam(which = GRanges("chr1", IRanges(1, 1000))))

heading: 13.61 Output & Results

heading: 13.62 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “BWA-MEM (v0.7.17) alignment GRCh38 achieved 97 % overall alignment rate 30× whole-genome library, <1 % ambiguously mapped reads (multi-mappers); Picard MarkDuplicates, duplicate rate 8 %, consistent well-optimised PCR-based library prep.” Always state aligner version, reference assembly, alignment / duplicate rates.

heading: 13.63 Practical Tips
text: Use minimap2 long reads (PacBio HiFi, Oxford Nanopore); BWA-MEM Bowtie2 short-read tools lose accuracy beyond 300 bp.Report alignment statistics (flagstat) alignment routine QC step; declines alignment rate signal contamination, library-prep problems, reference mismatch.Index BAM files (samtools index Rsamtools::indexBam()) random-access downstream tools (variant calling, peak calling, browser visualisation).Deduplicate alignment Picard MarkDuplicates samtools markdup; PCR duplicates inflate variant-calling false positives removed.paired-end reads, specify mate files; aligner uses insert-size constraints improve mapping accuracy near repeats.ChIP-seq ATAC-seq, Bowtie2 ---sensitive standard; WGS variant calling, BWA-MEM preferred.

heading: 13.67 Introduction
text: ChIP-seq (chromatin immunoprecipitation followed sequencing) localises protein-DNA binding events genome-wide — transcription factor binding sites, histone modifications, chromatin remodellers. resulting alignment density forms peaks binding loci; sharp peaks correspond point-source binders (TFs), broad domains correspond histone-mark territories (H3K27me3, H3K9me3 spanning kilobases). standard pipeline alignment, peak calling MACS2, annotation ChIPseeker, motif enrichment HOMER motifmatchr, differential binding DiffBind.

heading: 13.68 Prerequisites
text: working understanding read alignment (BWA, Bowtie2), peak calling, regulatory genomics including transcription factor binding sites histone modifications.

heading: 13.69 Theory
text: Two peak-calling modes:Narrow peaks (TF ChIP, transcription start sites): MACS2 defaults tuned sharp, point-source binding events.Broad peaks (H3K27me3, H3K9me3, H3K36me3): use --broad call diffuse domains spanning kilobases.matched input control (IgG total chromatin) essential background normalisation; ChIP enrichment measured ChIP-vs-input fold enrichment per peak region. Standard QC includes:FRiP (fraction reads peaks): > 1 % indicates successful IP; lower values suggest weak antibody insufficient enrichment.TSS enrichment: read coverage transcription start sites; high values confirm chromatin accessibility preserved.Cross-correlation: relative strand cross-correlation peak fragment length, indicating successful IP.

heading: 13.70 Assumptions
text: Cross-linking immunoprecipitation efficient specific; control input matches ChIP depth; duplicates removed (essential ChIP-seq peak calling).

heading: 13.71 R Implementation
code:
library(ChIPseeker)
library(TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene)

# Example: annotate a toy peak set
set.seed(2026)
peaks <- GRanges(
  seqnames = rep("chr1", 100),
  ranges = IRanges(start = sort(sample(1e7:5e7, 100)), width = 400)
)

anno <- annotatePeak(peaks,
                     TxDb = TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene,
                     level = "gene",
                     verbose = FALSE)
as.data.frame(anno)[1:5, c("seqnames", "annotation", "distanceToTSS")]

# Feature-type distribution
plotAnnoBar(anno)

# (Motif-enrichment example would require sequence extraction with
#  BSgenome and motifmatchr::matchMotifs against a JASPAR PWM set.)

heading: 13.72 Output & Results
text: annotatePeak() assigns peak nearest gene categorises promoter, intron, intergenic, etc. plotAnnoBar() summarises feature-type distribution; typical TF ChIP-seq shows ~30 % promoter, ~40 % intron, ~25 % distal-intergenic.

heading: 13.73 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “MACS2 (input control, \(q < 0.01\)) called 18{,}230 high-confidence STAT1 ChIP peaks IFN-γ-stimulated cells; 42 % promoter-proximal, 35 % intronic, 18 % distal-intergenic. De novo motif analysis (HOMER) recovered canonical STAT1 GAS motif (TTCN3GAA) \(p < 10^{-100}\), confirming antibody specificity. FRiP 12 %, TSS enrichment 16, well within QC standards.” Always report FRiP, TSS enrichment, motif validation.

heading: 13.74 Practical Tips
text: Always use matched input control (IgG, sonicated input, -antibody mock); without , MACS2 falls back generic background assumptions inflates false positives.Check FRiP per sample; values 1 % indicate weak IP, incorrect peak-calling mode, sample failure.histone marks (especially H3K27me3, H3K36me3, H3K9me3), use --broad; sharp peak callers miss diffuse domains entirely.Validate antibody specificity de novo motif enrichment (HOMER’s findMotifsGenome.pl MEME-ChIP); recovering expected motif gold standard ChIP success.differential binding across conditions, use DiffBind builds consensus peak sets runs DESeq2 edgeR peak-level read counts.CUT&RUN CUT&Tag (modern higher-resolution alternatives), downstream tools apply; specialised peak callers (SEACR) handle lower-background data accurately.

heading: 13.75 R Packages Used
text: ChIPseeker peak annotation visualisation; DiffBind differential-binding analysis; motifmatchr motif enrichment PWM databases; chromVAR chromatin-accessibility-style motif activity scores; ChIPQC quality control ChIP-seq experiments.

heading: 13.78 Introduction
text: Copy number variations (CNVs) large-scale gains losses genomic regions, critical oncology (tumour aneuploidy), developmental disorders (trisomies, deletions), population genetics. WGS, exome, array-based readouts feed segmentation algorithms partition genome constant-copy-number segments.

heading: 13.79 Prerequisites
text: Coverage intensity data (read depth, log R ratio); genome windowing.

heading: 13.80 Theory
text: Read-depth CNV calling: normalised read counts windows scale linearly copy number. log-ratio series smoothed segmented via Circular Binary Segmentation (CBS) (DNAcopy), HMMs (HMMcopy), Bayesian segmentation.tumour sequencing, methods like ASCAT FACETS jointly estimate purity, ploidy, allele-specific copy number B-allele frequencies.

heading: 13.81 Assumptions
text: Coverage uniformity (controlled GC / mappability correction); matched normal tumour calling (ideal); segments consistent copy number within.

heading: 13.82 R Implementation
code:
library(DNAcopy)

set.seed(2026)
n_probes <- 1000
chrom <- rep(1:5, each = n_probes / 5)
maploc <- rep(seq(1, 1e7, length.out = n_probes / 5), 5)

# Simulated log ratios with two CNV segments
logR <- rnorm(n_probes, 0, 0.2)
logR[201:300] <- logR[201:300] + 0.8    # duplication on chr2
logR[601:680] <- logR[601:680] - 1.0    # deletion   on chr4

cna <- CNA(genomdat = logR, chrom = chrom, maploc = maploc,
           data.type = "logratio", sampleid = "tumor1")
smoothed <- smooth.CNA(cna)
seg <- segment(smoothed, verbose = 0)
seg$output                     # segment means and boundaries

plot(seg)

heading: 13.83 Output & Results
text: segment table chromosome, start, end, number probes, mean log ratio; simulated duplication deletion recovered distinct segments.

heading: 13.84 Interpretation
text: “CNV segmentation tumour identified gain 17q (log2 ratio 0.85) loss 13q (log2 ratio -1.1); 13q deletion spans RB1, consistent retinoblastoma diagnosis.”

heading: 13.85 Practical Tips
text: GC bias correction (QDNAseq) essential low-coverage sequencing; uncorrected data produces spurious segments.tumour studies, always use matched normal control germline CNVs sequencing artefacts.Integer copy-number calls require ploidy purity estimates (ASCAT, FACETS); log ratios alone give relative copy number.Report CNV frequencies across cohorts GISTIC2, controls background aneuploidy.rare-CNV studies, focus large events (\(> 100\) kb); small events dominated noise array data.

heading: 13.88 Introduction
text: read alignment, next step RNA-seq pipelines summarising aligned reads per-gene (per-feature) count matrices differential-expression analysis. featureCounts (Rsubread) HTSeq-count two standard tools; consume BAM files plus GTF annotation produce gene--sample count matrix. choices matter — strand-specificity, multi-mapper handling, paired-end mode, meta-feature aggregation — directly affect downstream conclusions must match experimental library prep.

heading: 13.89 Prerequisites
text: working understanding BAM alignment files, GTF/GFF annotation formats, difference transcript-level gene-level expression quantification.

heading: 13.90 Theory
text: aligned read assigned feature (gene, exon, annotated region) overlaps. Key configuration choices:Strand-specificity: strandSpecific = 0 (unstranded), = 1 (forward-stranded, dUTP), = 2 (reverse-stranded, TruSeq SR). Wrong setting halves assigned counts.Multi-mapper handling: usually counted primary alignment; fractional counting (fraction = TRUE) splits credit across alignments multi-mapper.Paired-end: count fragments rather reads; mates must align consistently.Meta-feature: aggregate exon-level counts gene-level gene_id; alternatively, count exon level splicing analyses.transcript-level counts, pseudo-alignment tools (Salmon, Kallisto) skip BAM-producing alignment step provide isoform-level abundance estimates directly.

heading: 13.91 Assumptions
text: GTF annotation matches genome reference used alignment; strand-specificity setting matches library prep; aligned BAMs coordinate-sorted (sortable featureCounts).

heading: 13.92 R Implementation
code:
library(Rsubread)

# Build counts from BAM + GTF
# counts <- featureCounts(files = c("s1.bam", "s2.bam"),
#                         annot.ext = "annotation.gtf",

#                         GTF.featureType = "exon",
#                         GTF.attrType = "gene_id",
#                         strandSpecific = 2,

# counts$counts: gene x sample matrix

heading: 13.93 Output & Results
text: featureCounts() returns list $counts (gene--sample matrix), $annotation (per-gene metadata including length used FPKM/TPM normalisation), $stat (per-sample summary assigned vs ambiguous vs unassigned reads).

heading: 13.94 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “featureCounts (Rsubread v2.16) assigned 82 % mapped reads GENCODE v44 gene features (strandSpecific = 2, isPairedEnd = TRUE); 8 % ambiguous due multi-gene overlap 10 % unassigned feature. high assignment rate confirms strand setting matches dUTP library prep.” Always quote assignment rate; sub-50 % rates signal serious problems (wrong strand, annotation mismatch).

heading: 13.95 Practical Tips
text: Report $stat summaries alongside count matrix; low assignment rates (60 % typical RNA-seq) signal alignment, annotation, strand-setting problems.Strand-specificity must match library protocol exactly; check infer_experiment.py RSeQC visual inspection IGV uncertain.Use primaryOnly = TRUE ChIP-seq ATAC-seq, multi-mappers typically excluded.transcript-level (isoform) counts, switch Salmon, Kallisto, RSEM; gene-level counting collapses isoform-resolved information.Bundle counts metadata SummarizedExperiment object downstream Bioconductor workflows (DESeq2, edgeR, limma accept SummarizedExperiment).Include feature length output use normalisation reporting TPM/FPKM; raw counts alone control gene length differences.

heading: 13.96 R Packages Used

heading: 13.99 Introduction
text: Drug-target interaction data – compounds bind proteins affinity – underpins target discovery drug repositioning. Public databases (ChEMBL, DrugBank, PubChem, BindingDB, PharmGKB) archive millions bioassay measurements; programmatic access lets users assemble custom interaction tables.

heading: 13.100 Prerequisites
text: Familiarity compound identifiers (SMILES, InChI, ChEMBL ID) protein identifiers (UniProt).

heading: 13.101 Theory
text:
Key bioassay quantities:
- IC50 – inhibitor concentration halving biological response.
- Ki – equilibrium inhibition constant.
- Kd – dissociation constant.
- EC50 – effect concentration (agonist activity).Lower = potent. Values typically reported nM uM; low-nM sub-nM bindings considered high-affinity drug-like hits.

heading: 13.102 Assumptions
text: Assays comparable across studies (unreliable practice – format, conditions, species differ); target compound annotations correct.

heading: 13.103 R Implementation
code:
library(httr); library(jsonlite); library(dplyr)

# Query ChEMBL's REST API for bioactivities of a target (ERBB2 / HER2 = CHEMBL1824)
# This illustrative snippet is internet-free pseudo-code:
chembl_url <- paste0("https://www.ebi.ac.uk/chembl/api/data/activity.json",
                     "?target_chembl_id=CHEMBL1824&limit=100")
# res <- fromJSON(content(GET(chembl_url), "text"))
# activities <- res$activities

# Instead, simulate a small bioactivity table for illustration
set.seed(2026)
df <- data.frame(
  compound = paste0("CMP", 1:200),
  target   = sample(c("HER2", "EGFR", "MET"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  type     = sample(c("IC50", "Ki", "Kd"), 200, replace = TRUE),
  value_nM = round(10^runif(200, 0, 4))
)

# Potent binders (< 100 nM) per target
df %>%
  filter(value_nM < 100) %>%
  count(target, type, sort = TRUE)

# Rank compounds by median potency across multiple assays
df %>%
  group_by(compound, target) %>%
  summarise(median_pot = median(value_nM), n = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(median_pot) %>%
  head(5)

heading: 13.104 Output & Results
text: ranked table potent drug-target interactions per target per compound. practice replaces simulated data ChEMBL pull > 10,000 measurements.

heading: 13.105 Interpretation
text: “ChEMBL mining identified 26 HER2 inhibitors IC50 < 10 nM across independent assays; potent (CMP_X, median IC50 = 2 nM) selected -silico docking.”

heading: 13.106 Practical Tips
text: Aggregate across assays using median geometric mean smooth inter-lab variance.Convert IC50 pIC50 (\(-\log_{10}\)) symmetric, Gaussian-like distributions.Filter assay confidence score using ChEMBL (confidence \(\geq\) 7).Use BindingDB affinity-focused data; DrugBank approved-drug information.Cross-reference compounds via InChIKey merge records across databases (SMILES unique).

heading: 13.107 Reporting
text: reproducible drug-target-interaction analysis names database snapshot, date access, activity types retained, confidence quality filter applied raw assay records. State whether replicate measurements compound-target pair aggregated median, mean pIC50, geometric mean, since choices materially shift downstream rankings. potency thresholds conventional sub-100-nanomolar cutoff used define hits, justify threshold terms assay distribution rather treating universal constant, report compounds mixed agonist antagonist annotations resolved ranking step.

heading: 13.110 Introduction
text: edgeR one three dominant RNA-seq differential-expression frameworks (alongside DESeq2 limma-voom). fits negative-binomial generalised linear model gene’s counts, empirical-Bayes shrinkage borrowing information across genes stabilise per-gene dispersion estimates. combination NB likelihood shrinkage gives edgeR characteristic strength small samples competitive accuracy standard RNA-seq experiments.

heading: 13.111 Prerequisites
text: working understanding negative-binomial regression, RNA-seq normalisation (TMM), generalised linear models offsets, empirical-Bayes shrinkage approach.

heading: 13.112 Theory
text: Per gene \(\) sample \(j\):\[Y_{ij} \sim \mathrm{NB}(\mu_{ij}, \phi_i), \qquad \log \mu_{ij} = \log S_j + x_j^\top \beta_i,\]\(S_j\) TMM-normalised library size offset \(\phi_i\) gene-wise dispersion. Empirical-Bayes shrinkage (estimateDisp) pulls per-gene dispersions toward common trend across genes, stabilising estimation.Three test choices:exactTest: NB-exact test two-group comparisons; rarely used today.glmQLFTest: quasi-likelihood F-test; recommended default nearly designs accounts uncertainty dispersion estimation.glmLRT: classical likelihood-ratio test; aggressive anti-conservative sample sizes small.

heading: 13.113 Assumptions
text: Counts approximately negative-binomial conditional covariates; design matrix encodes biological technical factors correctly; sufficient sample size stable dispersion estimation (typically \(\ge 3\) per group).

heading: 13.114 R Implementation
code:
library(edgeR)

set.seed(2026)
counts <- matrix(rpois(20000 * 12, lambda = 30), 20000, 12)
group <- factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = 6))

y <- DGEList(counts = counts, group = group)
y <- calcNormFactors(y)

design <- model.matrix(~ group)
y <- estimateDisp(y, design)

fit <- glmQLFit(y, design)
qlf <- glmQLFTest(fit, coef = 2)

topTags(qlf)
summary(decideTests(qlf))

heading: 13.115 Output & Results
text: topTags() returns top differentially-expressed genes ordered FDR-adjusted \(p\)-value, \(\log_2\) fold change, log-CPM, \(F\)-statistic, BH-adjusted \(p\)-value. decideTests() summarises significance calls per direction (/ / significant) cross-method comparison.

heading: 13.116 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “edgeR’s quasi-likelihood F-test identified 342 differentially expressed genes FDR < 0.05 conditions B (n = 6 per group), 180 upregulated 162 downregulated condition B; median absolute \(\log_2\) fold change among significant genes 1.4. Filtering filterByExpr retained 14{,}500 20{,}000 genes testing.” Always state FDR threshold, sample sizes, pre-filtering decisions.

heading: 13.117 Practical Tips
text: Use glmQLFTest() default nearly designs; better Type error control glmLRT() small sample sizes.Filter low-count genes filterByExpr() dispersion estimation; testing 20{,}000 mostly-zero genes wastes power produces unstable shrinkage.paired blocked designs, include blocking factor design matrix; QLF test handles corresponding contrast correctly.Use glmTreat() fold-change-thresholded inference (testing |LFC| > chosen cutoff rather LFC ≠ 0); biologically meaningful testing strict zero.Visualise MA plot, volcano plot, heatmap top genes; Glimma::glimmaMA() provides interactive versions exploration.Compare edgeR results DESeq2 limma-voom data; concordance across methods reassuring, disagreement signals model-sensitive genes warrant investigation.

heading: 13.118 R Packages Used
text: edgeR DGEList, calcNormFactors, estimateDisp, glmQLFit, glmQLFTest, canonical NB workflow; limma voom alternative; DESeq2 third major DE method; Glimma interactive MA volcano plots; EnhancedVolcano publication-ready volcano plots.

heading: 13.121 Introduction
text: Every high-throughput sequencing analysis starts FASTQ quality control. alignment, quantification, downstream interpretation, inspect per-base quality, adapter contamination, GC content, duplicate rate decide trimming filtering strategies. cost skipping step high: low-quality data quietly degrade alignment accuracy, inflate variant-calling false positives, bias differential-expression conclusions ways hard detect retrospectively.

heading: 13.122 Prerequisites
text: working understanding FASTQ format, Phred quality scores, basic Illumina sequencing biology.

heading: 13.123 Theory
text: Phred quality score \(Q = -10 \log_{10} P(\text{error})\), Q30 means 1 1000 probability base-call error Q40 means 1 10{,}000. Modern Illumina runs aim Q30 higher across read, quality typically dropping toward 3’ end.Standard FastQC-style metrics:Per-base sequence quality: boxplot Phred scores position; declines toward 3’ normal drop Q20.Per-sequence quality: histogram mean read quality; bi-modal histograms suggest mixed-quality libraries.GC content: match expected genomic GC; deviations indicate contamination biased library prep.Adapter contamination: presence known adapter sequences 3’ ends.Overrepresented sequences: top recurring sequences often pinpoint adapters contamination.

heading: 13.124 Assumptions
text: Modern Illumina FASTQ files Sanger-encoded Phred (Phred+33). Older Illumina encodings (Phred+64) require explicit specification.

heading: 13.125 R Implementation
code:
library(ShortRead); library(Rqc)

# Assume demo FASTQ file path
fq_file <- system.file("extdata", "ex1.fq", package = "ShortRead")

# Basic inspection
reads <- readFastq(fq_file)
length(reads)
head(width(reads))
mean(as.numeric(encoding(quality(reads))))   # mean Phred base

# Rqc for systematic QC
qc <- rqcQA(fq_file, workers = 1)
rqcReport(qc)

heading: 13.126 Output & Results
text: Rqc::rqcQA() produces interactive HTML report covering per-position quality, GC content, duplicate rate, length distribution, adapter flags. ShortRead::readFastq() provides programmatic access custom diagnostics.

heading: 13.127 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “FastQC-style QC 12 RNA-seq samples showed mean per-base Phred ranging 35 38 across read length, adapter contamination 1 %, GC content centred 49 % (within expected range human transcriptome). fastp trimming Q15 adapter removal, approximately 95 % bases retained per sample.” Always report pre- post-trim QC.

heading: 13.128 Practical Tips
text: Use fastp trim_galore command line production QC trimming; R tools (Rqc, ShortRead) useful exploration slower dedicated command-line utilities large datasets.Report pre- post-trim QC; QC improvement validates trimming step.GC content far expected genomic value suggests contamination, library prep biases, species mismatch; investigate alignment.Overrepresented sequences often pinpoint adapter sequences, rRNA contamination, PCR duplicates single dominant transcripts.multiple samples, MultiQC aggregates per-sample reports single dashboard; essential reviewing dozens hundreds samples consistently.Phred Q20 conventional threshold “good” bases; trimming Q15 retains data removing worst calls.

heading: 13.129 R Packages Used
text: ShortRead general FASTQ manipulation R; Rqc systematic QC reports; Rfastp wrapper around fastp fast trimming QC; ngsReports parsing aggregating MultiQC FastQC outputs R objects.

heading: 13.132 Introduction
text: Gene Ontology (GO) enrichment asks whether list differentially-expressed genes disproportionately annotated specific GO terms — biological processes, molecular functions, cellular components. common first-pass functional interpretation RNA-seq, proteomics, single-cell DE list entry point pathway-level analysis. dominant method -representation analysis (ORA) using Fisher’s exact hypergeometric tests; gene-set enrichment analysis (GSEA) extends ranked gene lists without requiring significance threshold.

heading: 13.133 Prerequisites
text: working understanding differential-expression analysis, GO annotations three branches (biological process, molecular function, cellular component), basic Fisher’s exact test framework.

heading: 13.134 Theory
text: ORA constructs 2×2 contingency table GO term:Fisher’s exact test (hypergeometric distribution) tests whether DE genes fall term expected random sampling universe. universe set genes tested — typically genes passed pre-filtering — entire genome.Multiple testing across thousands GO terms controlled via Benjamini-Hochberg FDR; nested GO term hierarchies introduce correlated tests, BH adjustment remains conservative dependence.

heading: 13.135 Assumptions
text: Genes sampled uniformly universe given non-DE status; GO annotations correct (recently updated); universe matches tested set (testing genes genome inflates apparent enrichment tested set).

heading: 13.136 R Implementation
code:
library(clusterProfiler); library(org.Hs.eg.db)

# Example with a small simulated DE list
set.seed(2026)
all_genes <- keys(org.Hs.eg.db, keytype = "SYMBOL")
de_genes  <- sample(all_genes, 200)

ego <- enrichGO(gene = de_genes,
                universe = all_genes,
                OrgDb = org.Hs.eg.db,
                keyType = "SYMBOL",
                ont = "BP",
                pAdjustMethod = "BH",
                pvalueCutoff = 0.05,
                qvalueCutoff = 0.2)

head(as.data.frame(ego))[, c("ID", "Description", "p.adjust", "Count")]
dotplot(ego, showCategory = 15)

heading: 13.137 Output & Results
text: enrichGO() returns ranked table GO terms BH-adjusted \(p\)-values, gene counts, contributing gene symbols. dotplot() visualises top hits horizontal dot plot gene-count size adjusted-\(p\)-value colour.

heading: 13.138 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “GO -representation analysis (BH-FDR < 0.05) identified 28 biological-process terms among 200 DE genes; top terms — response stimulus (45 genes), immune response (32), cell cycle regulation (28) — reflect experimental manipulation. simplify() reduced redundant hierarchy 12 representative terms.” Always state universe, multiple-testing correction, term-redundancy reduction.

heading: 13.139 Practical Tips
text: Define universe correctly — always set genes passed pre-filtering tested DE, never whole genome; using latter inflates apparent enrichment.Use simplify() enrichResult object collapse redundant nested GO terms; unsimplified output often contains many parent-child duplications.Direction-agnostic ORA loses signal; biology-aware interpretation, run - -regulated lists separately.ranked gene lists continuous statistics, prefer GSEA (gseGO()) ORA; GSEA require arbitrary significance threshold.goseq adjusts gene-length bias RNA-seq (longer genes power DE detection); use length bias concern.Pair GO KEGG (enrichKEGG) Reactome (enrichPathway) complementary functional coverage.

heading: 13.140 R Packages Used
text: clusterProfiler::enrichGO(), gseGO(), simplify() canonical GO enrichment; org.Hs.eg.db species-specific packages annotation; topGO goseq alternative implementations; enrichplot dotplot(), cnetplot(), emapplot() visualisations enrichment results.

heading: 13.143 Introduction
text: Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) tests whether gene set concentrated top bottom ranked gene list (e.g., log fold change signed p-value). Unlike -representation analysis, uses full ranking sensitive coordinated modest changes within pathway.

heading: 13.144 Prerequisites
text: Ranked gene statistic (LFC signed -log10 p); curated gene-set database (MSigDB, Reactome, KEGG).

heading: 13.145 Theory
text: GSEA walks ranked list, incrementing running-sum statistic encountering set member (weighted rank statistic) decrementing otherwise. enrichment score (ES) maximum deviation zero; permutation-based null distribution yields p-values. fgsea uses fast adaptive multi-level permutation scheme.Normalised ES (NES) adjusts gene-set size allows cross-set comparison.

heading: 13.146 Assumptions
text: ranking reflects biological signal; gene sets well-curated; permutation null captures null distribution ES.

heading: 13.147 R Implementation
code:
library(fgsea); library(msigdbr)

set.seed(2026)
n_genes <- 5000
genes <- paste0("g", 1:n_genes)
stats <- rnorm(n_genes); names(stats) <- genes

# Inject a pathway signal
pathway_A <- sample(genes, 30); stats[pathway_A] <- stats[pathway_A] + 1.5
pathway_B <- sample(genes, 20); stats[pathway_B] <- stats[pathway_B] - 1.2

pathways <- list(pathway_A = pathway_A, pathway_B = pathway_B,
                 pathway_C = sample(genes, 40))

fg <- fgsea(pathways = pathways, stats = sort(stats, decreasing = TRUE),
            minSize = 10, maxSize = 500)
fg[, .(pathway, NES, pval, padj)]
plotEnrichment(pathways$pathway_A, sort(stats, decreasing = TRUE))

heading: 13.148 Output & Results
text: table NES, p-value, adjusted p-value per pathway. enrichment plot pathway_A shows strong positive leading edge.

heading: 13.149 Interpretation
text: “GSEA MSigDB Hallmark revealed positive enrichment inflammatory response (NES = 1.8, BH-FDR < 0.01) negative enrichment oxidative phosphorylation (NES = -1.5).”

heading: 13.150 Practical Tips
text: Use stat column DESeq2 t limma ranking; signed -log10(p-value) alternative.Remove NAs ranking; fgsea sensitive ties NAs.Report leading-edge subset biologically driven interpretation.MSigDB Hallmark (50 curated), C2 (curated pathways), C5 (GO), C7 (immune); match collection biological question.GSEA tests concentrated enrichment, direction-specific effects; combine ORA complementary evidence.

heading: 13.151 For Reviewers

heading: 13.153 Introduction
text: GSVA (Gene Set Variation Analysis) transforms gene--sample expression matrix pathway--sample matrix assigning sample per-pathway enrichment score. transformation enables pathways treated features downstream analysis — Cox regression survival, clustering, classification, differential analysis pathway level. -representation analysis (GO, KEGG -representation) tells pathways enriched hit list, GSVA tells individual sample stands relative panel pathways.

heading: 13.154 Prerequisites
text: working understanding gene-set enrichment, log-normalised expression matrices, difference sample-level contrast-level enrichment.

heading: 13.155 Theory
text: sample, GSVA computes empirical CDF expression values across genes (rank-based) derives Kolmogorov-Smirnov-like statistic gene set. result per-sample, per-pathway score, typically roughly \([-1, 1]\) scale. ssGSEA (single-sample GSEA) related method differing primarily weighting scheme; implemented GSVA package.transformed pathway--sample matrix can analysed standard tool: limma differential pathway expression, Cox regression survival, hierarchical clustering, supervised classification.

heading: 13.156 Assumptions
text: Expression consistently normalised across samples; gene sets accurately curated relevant biology; scores interpretable relative within study absolute scale across studies.

heading: 13.157 R Implementation
code:
library(GSVA)

set.seed(2026)
n_genes <- 500; n_samples <- 20
expr <- matrix(rnorm(n_genes * n_samples), n_genes, n_samples)
rownames(expr) <- paste0("g", 1:n_genes)

pathways <- list(
  path_A = paste0("g", sample(1:n_genes, 30)),
  path_B = paste0("g", sample(1:n_genes, 40)),
  path_C = paste0("g", sample(1:n_genes, 25))
)

gsva_scores <- gsva(expr, pathways, method = "gsva", kcdf = "Gaussian",
                    verbose = FALSE)
dim(gsva_scores)        # pathways x samples
round(gsva_scores[, 1:4], 2)

heading: 13.158 Output & Results
text: gsva() returns pathway--sample matrix enrichment scores. column one sample’s pathway profile; high scores indicate pathway’s genes coordinately -regulated sample relative others, low scores indicate opposite.

heading: 13.159 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “GSVA pathway scores (Hallmark v7.5 gene sets, log-CPM input, Gaussian kernel) served features Cox regression overall survival across 200 patients; inflammatory-response pathway emerged strongest independent predictor (HR 1.7 per SD increase, 95 % CI 1.2 2.4, \(p = 0.002\)) adjustment age, stage, treatment arm.” Always state gene-set source, kernel, input data.

heading: 13.160 Practical Tips
text: Use kcdf = "Gaussian" log-CPM, kcdf = "Poisson" raw counts; wrong setting biases scores systematically.Downstream differential-pathway analysis works standard limma fit GSVA score matrix design used gene-level analysis.single-cell expression, per-cell GSVA noisy; aggregate cells pseudobulk first, use signed-rank alternatives (AUCell, UCell) designed single-cell context.Always compute GSVA single, consistently normalised expression matrix; mixing normalisations across input matrix produces invalid scores.Scores relative within study; compare raw GSVA scores across independent datasets without re-running combined matrix.pathway exploration, MSigDB Hallmark, KEGG, Reactome gene sets (via msigdbr::msigdbr()) standard starting libraries.

heading: 13.161 R Packages Used
text: GSVA::gsva() canonical GSVA ssGSEA implementation; msigdbr MSigDB gene-set retrieval; AUCell UCell single-cell-friendly enrichment alternatives; singscore alternative rank-based single-sample method; escape Seurat-integrated GSVA workflow.

heading: 13.164 Introduction
text: Heatmaps compactly display expression matrix colour grid rows (genes) columns (samples) optionally clustered hierarchically. RNA-seq standard visualisation differential-expression signature, curated marker panel, pathway--interest gene set: colour intensity encodes scaled expression, dendrograms reveal structure, annotation bars link sample columns experimental metadata. Done well, heatmap communicates biological structure experiment dozens pages statistical tables.

heading: 13.165 Prerequisites
text: working understanding log-CPM variance-stabilised counts, hierarchical clustering, role row scaling (z-scoring) making heatmaps interpretable.

heading: 13.166 Theory
text: Raw counts unsuitable heatmaps one strongly expressed gene dominates colour range obscures pattern lower-expression genes. standard pre-processing :Log-transform counts (use VST/rlog DESeq2).Row z-score gene unit variance zero mean across samples; scales gene’s display range comparably.Cluster rows columns hierarchical clustering, typically Euclidean distance Ward complete linkage.Annotation bars (sample group, batch, time point) overlay metadata column dendrogram, making immediately visible whether sample clustering corresponds biology technical artefacts.

heading: 13.167 Assumptions
text: Row scaling assumes comparable behaviour genes (one z-score unit means thing across genes); column clustering assumes samples comparable normalisation; chosen distance metric linkage match cluster structure.

heading: 13.168 R Implementation
code:
library(pheatmap)

set.seed(2026)
n_genes <- 50; n_samples <- 20
mat <- matrix(rnorm(n_genes * n_samples), n_genes, n_samples)
rownames(mat) <- paste0("gene_", 1:n_genes)
colnames(mat) <- paste0("S", 1:n_samples)

# Introduce a DE pattern
group <- rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = 10)
mat[1:10, group == "trt"] <- mat[1:10, group == "trt"] + 3

annot <- data.frame(group = factor(group))
rownames(annot) <- colnames(mat)

pheatmap(mat, scale = "row",
         clustering_distance_rows = "euclidean",
         clustering_method = "ward.D2",
         annotation_col = annot,
         color = colorRampPalette(c("#2A9D8F", "white", "#E76F51"))(50),
         main = "Top DE genes (row-scaled z-scores)")

heading: 13.169 Output & Results
text: pheatmap() produces coloured grid row column dendrograms, annotation strip group factor, colour legend. two sample clusters separate control treatment groups based 10 simulated DE genes; row dendrogram groups DE genes coherent block distinct noise rows.

heading: 13.170 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Row-scaled heatmap top 50 differentially-expressed genes (z-scores per row, blue-white-red diverging colour scale, Ward linkage Euclidean distance); samples cluster two distinct groups corresponding experimental conditions, top 10 row-cluster shows coherent -regulation treatment group.” Always describe scaling, distance metric, linkage method.

heading: 13.171 Practical Tips
text: Always row-scale (z-score) DE heatmaps; otherwise high-expression genes swamp colour range pattern lower-expression genes invisible.Use ComplexHeatmap::Heatmap() richer annotations, row/column splits cluster, multi-panel composition; pheatmap simpler one-plots.Truncate extreme values (e.g., cap z-scores \(\pm 3\)) prevent one outlier dominating colour scale.Annotate samples batch, condition, time-point information column annotations; visual separation match biology, technical structure.Use variance-stabilised counts (DESeq2 vst() rlog()) rather raw log-CPM robust heatmaps dataset wide expression range.Save figure final publication size (ggsave() using ggplot, pdf()/png() base plots); shrinking heatmaps word processor produces unreadable cell labels.

heading: 13.172 R Packages Used
text: pheatmap quick standard heatmaps; ComplexHeatmap advanced layouts including multiple annotations, row/column splits, composition panels; DESeq2::vst() rlog() variance-stabilising input transformations; RColorBrewer viridis colour palettes.

heading: 13.175 Introduction
text: limma-voom one three dominant differential-expression methods RNA-seq (alongside DESeq2 edgeR). voom step transforms raw counts onto log-CPM scale computes per-observation precision weights account mean-variance trend characteristic count data. result suitable input limma’s full linear-modelling framework, adds empirical-Bayes shrinkage variance estimates drives limma’s power small samples complex designs.

heading: 13.176 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear models, RNA-seq normalisation (TMM via edgeR), mean-variance relationship count data, empirical-Bayes shrinkage approach.

heading: 13.177 Theory
text: voom() transforms raw counts via \(\log_2(\mathrm{CPM} + 0.5)\) fits smooth mean-variance trend across genes. fitted trend produces precision weight observation; downstream lmFit() regression uses weights low-expression genes (high relative variance) contribute less joint estimation. empirical-Bayes step (eBayes()) shrinks per-gene variance estimates toward global trend, yielding moderated \(t\)-statistics better small-sample properties ordinary \(t\)-tests.full limma-voom pipeline supports arbitrarily complex designs (interactions, blocking, batch adjustment) standard model.matrix notation, contrast machinery handles multi-condition comparisons cleanly via contrasts.fit().

heading: 13.178 Assumptions
text: Counts TMM-normalised (via calcNormFactors edgeR); chosen design matrix correctly encodes biological technical factors; mean-variance trend approximately monotone (true typical bulk RNA-seq).

heading: 13.179 R Implementation
code:
library(limma); library(edgeR)

set.seed(2026)
counts <- matrix(rpois(20000 * 12, lambda = 30), 20000, 12)
group <- factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = 6))

dge <- DGEList(counts = counts); dge <- calcNormFactors(dge)
design <- model.matrix(~ group)

v <- voom(dge, design, plot = FALSE)
fit <- lmFit(v, design)
fit <- eBayes(fit)

topTable(fit, coef = 2, n = 10)
summary(decideTests(fit))

heading: 13.180 Output & Results
text: topTable() returns top differentially-expressed genes \(\log_2\) fold change, average expression, moderated \(t\)-statistic, raw \(p\)-value, Benjamini-Hochberg adjusted \(p\)-value. decideTests() summarises significance calls per contrast, useful cross-method comparison.

heading: 13.181 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “limma-voom identified 380 genes FDR < 0.05 conditions B (n = 6 per group), mean fold change among significant genes 1.7 (range 0.4 3.8); corresponding edgeR analysis data identified 342 genes, 320 genes (84 %) shared methods, indicating substantial concordance.” Always state FDR threshold design matrix structure.

heading: 13.182 Practical Tips
text: limma-voom competitive edgeR’s glmQLFit DESeq2’s negative-binomial Wald test; choose based workflow conventions personal preference rather expected accuracy gains.voomWithQualityWeights() additionally -weights noisy samples, useful one two samples visibly poor QC; combine two weight types sample-level variability substantial.complex designs (interactions, multiple blocking factors), standard model.matrix contrasts.fit() machinery applies; limma’s design language among flexible.Voom well-suited scRNA-seq pseudobulk: aggregate cells per cluster pseudo-bulk counts, run voom resulting matrix.samples per group (n < 3), variance estimates unstable; consider DESeq2 variance-stabilising shrinkage, rely heavily empirical Bayes step limma.Pair analysis voomQCPlot meanvariancetrend() diagnostic plots confirm variance trend well-fitted.

heading: 13.183 R Packages Used
text: limma voom(), lmFit(), eBayes(), topTable(), broader linear-modelling framework; edgeR DGEList, calcNormFactors (TMM normalisation); Glimma interactive MA volcano plots; EnhancedVolcano publication-ready volcano plots limma output.

heading: 13.186 Introduction
text: MA plot — also known mean-difference plot Bland-Altman-style plot genomics — displays log fold change (M) y-axis mean expression intensity () x-axis. standard diagnostic differential-expression results: bulk points (non-DE genes) clusters around zero independent mean intensity, normalisation succeeded; systematic curvature vertical drift indicates intensity-dependent bias contaminates DE calls. MA plot complements volcano plot emphasising relationship effect size expression level rather effect size statistical significance.

heading: 13.187 Prerequisites
text: working understanding log fold change, average expression log scale, differential-expression output (DESeq2, edgeR, limma).

heading: 13.188 Theory
text: two samples (two groups) \(X, Y\):\[M = \log_2(Y / X), \qquad = \tfrac{1}{2} \log_2(X Y).\]well-normalised comparison shows \(M\) scattered around zero independently \(\); lowess curve \(M\) vs \(\) approximately flat zero. Curvature, drift, systematic offset indicates scaling compositional bias normalisation failed remove. “trumpet” shape — wider scatter low expression, narrower high — expected low-expression genes noisier fold-change estimates; information precision, bias.

heading: 13.189 Assumptions
text: Counts normalised (TMM, RLE, similar); majority genes non-differentially expressed (lowess \(M\) pass zero); dataset sufficiently large estimate lowess curve stably.

heading: 13.190 R Implementation
code:
library(limma); library(edgeR)

set.seed(2026)
counts <- matrix(rpois(10000 * 6, lambda = 30), 10000, 6)
group  <- factor(rep(c("A", "B"), each = 3))
dge    <- DGEList(counts); dge <- calcNormFactors(dge)

design <- model.matrix(~ group)
v <- voom(dge, design)
fit <- lmFit(v, design); fit <- eBayes(fit)

plotMD(fit, coef = 2, status = decideTests(fit)[, 2],
       values = c(-1, 1), hl.col = c("#2A9D8F", "#E76F51"),
       main = "MA plot (limma)")
abline(h = 0, lty = 2)

heading: 13.191 Output & Results
text: limma::plotMD() plots logFC vs mean log-CPM significant genes highlighted colour horizontal reference line zero. DESeq2::plotMA() produces plot results object single call. non-DE genes cluster tightly around zero trumpet shape opening toward low mean expression.

heading: 13.192 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “MA plot (Fig. X) confirms TMM normalisation log fold changes centred zero across full mean-expression range; lowess curve deviate zero, indicating intensity-dependent bias supporting validity differential-expression calls. Significant genes (highlighted) distributed across expression range.” Always pair MA plots volcano plots reporting DE results.

heading: 13.193 Practical Tips
text: Always produce MA plot alongside volcano plot reporting DE results; two communicate complementary information.Overlay lowess curve (lines(lowess(, M))) diagnose intensity-dependent bias formally; visible drift signals normalisation problems.DESeq2, plotMA(res) produces standard MA plot single call; function automatically applies LFC shrinkage results object contains shrunken estimates.Use shrinkage (DESeq2::lfcShrink apeglm ashr) stabilise noisy log-fold-change estimates low expression; shrunken MA plot interpretable raw version.Label top genes (5–10); labelling significant hits makes plot unreadable.large MA plots, alpha-blending hexbin (smoothScatter) prevents overplotting dense central column.

heading: 13.194 R Packages Used
text: limma::plotMD() MA plots limma fits; DESeq2::plotMA() MA plots DESeq2 results; edgeR::plotMD() edgeR analogue; Glimma::glimmaMA() interactive MA plots embeddable HTML; ggplot2 hand-built versions custom styling.

heading: 13.197 Introduction
text: Shotgun metagenomics sequences DNA sample rather marker gene, enabling species-level taxonomic functional profiling. Two dominant approaches: MetaPhlAn (marker-gene alignment species-specific genes) Kraken (k-mer matching reference database).

heading: 13.198 Prerequisites
text: Shotgun sequencing basics; read QC; familiarity amplicon methods.

heading: 13.199 Theory
text: MetaPhlAn maps reads ~1M clade-specific markers; species abundances inferred relative marker coverage. Output per-species relative abundance table.Kraken2 classifies reads via k-mer matches database (e.g., RefSeq Bacteria). Fast comprehensive prone false positives low-abundance species; pair Bracken abundance re-estimation.Functional profiling: HUMAnN3 pathway-level abundances (MetaCyc); eggNOG-mapper gene-function annotation.

heading: 13.200 Assumptions
text: Reads adapter-trimmed host-decontaminated; reference database covers expected taxa; relative abundances interpretable within sample.

heading: 13.201 R Implementation
text: MetaPhlAn Kraken run command line; R ingests results downstream diversity DA analysis:Example command-line steps (run ):
code:
library(phyloseq)

set.seed(2026)
# Simulated MetaPhlAn-style output
species <- paste0("s__Species_", 1:50)
samples <- paste0("S", 1:20)
abund <- matrix(rgamma(length(species) * length(samples), 0.5, 2),
                length(species), length(samples))
abund <- sweep(abund, 2, colSums(abund), "/")   # relative abundances
rownames(abund) <- species; colnames(abund) <- samples

# Coerce to a phyloseq object for diversity analyses
tax_tab <- matrix(species, length(species), 1,
                  dimnames = list(species, "Species"))
ps <- phyloseq(otu_table(round(abund * 1e6), taxa_are_rows = TRUE),
               tax_table(tax_tab))
ps

# Top species by mean abundance
head(sort(rowMeans(abund), decreasing = TRUE), 5)metaphlan sample.fastq --input_type fastq --bowtie2db metaphlan_db \
          -o sample_profile.txt
kraken2 --db kraken_db --paired R1.fq R2.fq --report sample.kreport

heading: 13.202 Output & Results
text: species--sample abundance table; phyloseq, standard diversity DA methods apply.

heading: 13.203 Interpretation
text: “MetaPhlAn profiling revealed 420 species; top differentially abundant species gut inflammation cases controls included Faecalibacterium prausnitzii (depleted) Escherichia coli (enriched).”

heading: 13.204 Practical Tips
text: Host-read removal (Bowtie2 vs host genome) essential; residual host DNA biases classification.Kraken2 + Bracken gives per-species quantification reduced false positives vs Kraken alone.functional analysis, HUMAnN3 provides pathway abundances suitable differential pathway analysis MaAsLin2 LEfSe.ONT long reads enable better species resolution complex communities.mix relative absolute abundances across analyses; compositional data require log-ratio transforms (CLR) specific methods (ANCOM-BC).

heading: 13.207 Introduction
text: DNA methylation CpG dinucleotides stable epigenetic mark regulates gene expression varies age, tissue type, environmental exposures, disease state. two dominant assays Illumina arrays (450K, 850K EPIC) measuring methylation hundreds thousands CpGs whole-genome bisulfite sequencing (WGBS) covering nearly CpGs higher cost. Differential methylation analysis identifies CpG positions regions whose methylation differs groups, often serving biomarkers (e.g., cancer subtype classification, age estimation).

heading: 13.208 Prerequisites
text: working understanding bisulfite conversion chemistry (unmethylated C → T, methylated C → C), difference beta-values M-values methylation summaries, basic linear-modelling continuous outcomes.

heading: 13.209 Theory
text: Two methylation summaries:Beta-value: \(\beta = M / (M + U + \alpha)\), proportion methylated reads (\(\alpha\) small offset stabilise low-coverage estimates). Intuitive (0 = fully unmethylated, 1 = fully methylated) heteroscedastic — variance largest near 0.5.M-value: \(\log_2(M / U)\), log-ratio methylated unmethylated. Linear approximately homoscedastic; preferred regression.standard pipeline:Read raw IDAT files (minfi::read.metharray.exp).Normalise (SWAN, functional normalisation, BMIQ).Detect remove outlier samples cross-reactive probes.Differential methylation probe level via limma M-values.Region-level analysis (DMRcate, bumphunter) smoother small-effect detection.

heading: 13.210 Assumptions
text: Array probes well-designed (major cross-reactivity); batch effects identified modelled; cell-type composition adjusted blood heterogeneous samples — failure creates spurious associations.

heading: 13.211 R Implementation
code:
library(limma)

set.seed(2026)
n_cpgs <- 1000; n_samples <- 12
M_values <- matrix(rnorm(n_cpgs * n_samples, mean = 0, sd = 2),
                   n_cpgs, n_samples)
rownames(M_values) <- paste0("cg", 1:n_cpgs)
colnames(M_values) <- paste0("S", 1:n_samples)

group <- factor(rep(c("ctrl", "case"), each = 6))
# Inject differential methylation in 50 CpGs
M_values[1:50, group == "case"] <- M_values[1:50, group == "case"] + 1.5

design <- model.matrix(~ group)
fit <- lmFit(M_values, design)
fit <- eBayes(fit)

topTable(fit, coef = 2, n = 5)
summary(decideTests(fit))

heading: 13.212 Output & Results
text: topTable() returns per-CpG table log fold change M-value, raw \(p\)-value, BH-adjusted \(p\)-value. 50 injected CpGs appear top ranked list largest absolute fold changes significant \(p\)-values.

heading: 13.213 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Epigenome-wide differential methylation analysis (limma M-values, design adjusting age estimated cell-type composition) identified 1{,}240 differentially methylated CpGs Bonferroni \(p < 10^{-7}\); 62 % hypermethylated cases, concentrated CpG islands known tumour-suppressor loci. Region-level analysis (DMRcate) identified 87 differentially methylated regions overlapping 64 unique genes.” Always state multiple-testing correction, cell-type adjustment strategy, use M-values vs beta-values.

heading: 13.214 Practical Tips
text: Fit regression models M-values (homoscedastic), report effect sizes beta-values (interpretable “% methylation difference”) clinical audiences.Cell-type deconvolution (EpiDISH, FlowSorted.Blood.450k) mandatory blood-based EWAS; unadjusted results predominantly reflect composition differences.Region-level analysis (DMRcate, bumphunter) powerful probe-level small consistent effects across nearby CpGs; also reduces multiple-testing burden.Batch effects (array plate, chip, scan date) strong methylation data; include covariates run ComBat.age-related studies, Horvath, Hannum, PhenoAge clocks give DNA methylation age estimates biologically interpretable; report alongside chronological age.Standardised EWAS pipelines (meffil, PaCES) offer one-call QC analysis sensible defaults.

heading: 13.215 R Packages Used
text: minfi IDAT reading, normalisation, basic differential analysis; limma linear-modelling M-values; DMRcate region-level differential methylation; EpiDISH FlowSorted.Blood.450k cell-type deconvolution; bumphunter alternative region detector; meffil end--end EWAS pipeline; methylKit WGBS analysis.
