heading: 13.218 Introduction
text: DADA2 (Divisive Amplicon Denoising Algorithm 2) infers exact amplicon sequence variants (ASVs) 16S rRNA, 18S, , amplicon sequencing data. replaces older OTU-clustering approach (lumps similar sequences together , typically, 97 % identity) single-nucleotide resolution. ASVs reproducible across studies — sequence variant regardless runs analysis — allow finer taxonomic assignment threshold-based OTU representation.

heading: 13.219 Prerequisites
text: working understanding amplicon sequencing (paired-end single-end Illumina), primer trimming, basic microbial ecology including OTU vs ASV distinction.

heading: 13.220 Theory
text: DADA2 fits position- quality-aware error model observed FASTQ data, uses distinguish real biological variants sequencing errors. intuition: sequence appearing often error model predicts candidate ASV; sequences explained errors abundant variant merged . final output per-sample ASV count table (analogous gene count matrix RNA-seq) feeds downstream pipeline — alpha beta diversity, differential-abundance testing, ordination.standard pipeline: filter trim FASTQs, learn error rates, denoise per sample, merge paired-end reads, construct sequence table, remove chimeras, assign taxonomy.

heading: 13.221 Assumptions
text: Primers removed DADA2 input; paired-end reads overlap sufficiently merging; error rates sample-specific (DADA2 estimates per run).

heading: 13.222 R Implementation
text: DADA2 processes FASTQs disk; simulate post-DADA2 ASV count table downstream analysis:actual DADA2 calls (commented simulation example):
code:
library(phyloseq)

set.seed(2026)
n_asv <- 80; n_samples <- 20
otu_tab <- matrix(rpois(n_asv * n_samples, lambda = 10), n_asv, n_samples)
rownames(otu_tab) <- paste0("ASV", 1:n_asv)
colnames(otu_tab) <- paste0("S", 1:n_samples)

tax_tab <- matrix(sample(c("Firmicutes", "Bacteroidetes", "Proteobacteria"),
                          n_asv, replace = TRUE), n_asv, 1)
rownames(tax_tab) <- rownames(otu_tab); colnames(tax_tab) <- "Phylum"

meta <- data.frame(group = factor(rep(c("ctrl", "case"), each = 10)),
                   row.names = colnames(otu_tab))

ps <- phyloseq(otu_table(otu_tab, taxa_are_rows = TRUE),
               tax_table(tax_tab),
               sample_data(meta))

sort(sample_sums(ps), decreasing = TRUE)[1:5]   # per-sample depth
taxa_sums(ps)[1:5]                              # per-ASV total
# filterAndTrim(fnFs, filtFs, fnRs, filtRs, truncLen = c(240, 160),
#               maxEE = c(2, 2), truncQ = 2, compress = TRUE)
# errF <- learnErrors(filtFs); errR <- learnErrors(filtRs)
# dadaFs <- dada(filtFs, err = errF); dadaRs <- dada(filtRs, err = errR)
# mergers <- mergePairs(dadaFs, filtFs, dadaRs, filtRs)
# seqtab <- makeSequenceTable(mergers)
# seqtab.nochim <- removeBimeraDenovo(seqtab, method = "consensus")

heading: 13.223 Output & Results
text: DADA2 output ASV--sample count matrix together taxonomy assignments SILVA GTDB. Wrapping count table, taxonomy, sample metadata phyloseq object provides standard input downstream community ecology analyses.

heading: 13.224 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “DADA2 inferred 2{,}148 amplicon sequence variants across 50 samples chimera removal; 980 ASVs assigned phylum level using SILVA v138 reference, median read depth 42{,}300 reads per sample. resulting phyloseq object served input alpha-diversity (Shannon, Simpson) beta-diversity (Bray-Curtis NMDS) analyses.” Always state reference database version read-retention rate.

heading: 13.225 Practical Tips
text: Always run removeBimeraDenovo() sequence-table construction strip chimeric ASVs; accumulate PCR can substantial fraction count.Assign taxonomy SILVA (v138 later 16S) GTDB; species-level assignment short amplicons usually unreliable due limited resolution.Filter ASVs present fewer 2 samples reduce noise; rare ASVs often sequencing artefacts.DECIPHER::IdTaxa() provides faster alternative DADA2’s assignTaxonomy() supports SILVA reference.Always check per-sample read-retention DADA2 step (filtering, denoising, merging, chimera removal); large drops step usually mean -strict parameters.16S V4 region overlapping 250 bp paired-end reads, truncLen = c(240, 160) sensible starting point; tune based quality profiles.

heading: 13.226 R Packages Used
text: dada2 ASV inference canonical workflow; phyloseq downstream community-ecology analysis ASV count table; DECIPHER fast taxonomy assignment; vegan alpha beta diversity; ANCOMBC ALDEx2 compositional differential-abundance testing.

heading: 13.229 Introduction
text: Diversity metrics summarise microbial community composition. Alpha diversity measures richness evenness within sample; beta diversity measures compositional dissimilarity samples. essential comparing communities detecting treatment, disease, niche effects.

heading: 13.230 Prerequisites
text: ASV OTU count table; phylogenetic tree (UniFrac); sample metadata.

heading: 13.231 Theory
text:
Alpha diversity:
- Richness (# ASVs observed).
- Shannon index \(H = -\sum p_i \log p_i\) – evenness-weighted.
- Simpson \(1 - \sum p_i^2\) – inverse concentration.
- Faith’s PD – phylogenetic tree branch length sum.Beta diversity:
- Bray-Curtis – abundance-weighted Manhattan dissimilarity.
- Jaccard – presence/absence.
- UniFrac (weighted / unweighted) – phylogeny-aware.Permutational MANOVA (adonis2) tests whether group membership explains beta diversity.

heading: 13.232 Assumptions
text: Counts non-negative; rarefaction equivalent normalisation applied alpha diversity depths vary widely; phylogenetic tree matches ASV labels.

heading: 13.233 R Implementation
code:
library(vegan)

set.seed(2026)
n_samples <- 20; n_asv <- 50
counts <- matrix(rpois(n_asv * n_samples, lambda = 10),
                 n_samples, n_asv)   # samples x ASVs

# Inject group differences
group <- factor(rep(c("ctrl", "case"), each = 10))
counts[group == "case", 1:15] <- counts[group == "case", 1:15] + 20

shannon <- diversity(counts, index = "shannon")
simpson <- diversity(counts, index = "simpson")
richness <- rowSums(counts > 0)

by(shannon, group, median)

bray <- vegdist(counts, method = "bray")
adonis_res <- adonis2(bray ~ group, permutations = 999)
adonis_res

heading: 13.234 Output & Results
text: Shannon / Simpson / richness per sample (group medians printed), PERMANOVA Bray-Curtis showing whether group explains dissimilarity.

heading: 13.235 Interpretation
text: “Case samples lower Shannon diversity (median 2.1 vs 2.9, Wilcoxon p = 0.004) PERMANOVA Bray-Curtis revealed significant community-level differences (\(R^2\) = 0.18, p = 0.001).”

heading: 13.236 Practical Tips
text: Rarefy equal depth alpha diversity avoid depth-driven confounding; alternatively use rarefaction curves confirm coverage.phyloseq::estimate_richness computes many alpha metrics one call.Weighted UniFrac captures abundance-weighted phylogenetic distance; unweighted presence-.PERMANOVA sensitive dispersion heterogeneity; run betadisper confirm equal dispersion interpreting.PCoA visualisation beta dissimilarity matrix complements PERMANOVA.

heading: 13.239 Introduction
text: Multiple sequence alignment (MSA) precursor phylogeny, motif discovery, structural modelling: arranges residues homologous positions align columns. msa Bioconductor package offers ClustalW, ClustalOmega, Muscle unified R interface.

heading: 13.240 Prerequisites
text: FASTA sequences; substitution scoring (PAM, BLOSUM proteins; match/mismatch DNA).

heading: 13.241 Theory
text: Progressive alignment (ClustalW): build guide tree pairwise distances, align sequences following tree leaves root. Iterative refinement (Muscle, MAFFT) revisits partial alignments improve scores. Consistency-based methods (T-Coffee) combine information multiple library alignments.Accuracy depends sequence divergence: highly divergent sequences, structural alignment (DALI proteins) outperforms sequence-methods.

heading: 13.242 Assumptions
text: Sequences homologous; single substitution model approximates evolutionary process; insertions/deletions rare enough gap parameters meaningful.

heading: 13.243 R Implementation
code:
library(Biostrings); library(msa)

# Example amino-acid sequences (hemoglobin across species)
aas <- readAAStringSet(
  system.file("examples", "exampleAA.fasta", package = "msa"))
aas
aln <- msa(aas, method = "ClustalW", verbose = FALSE)
aln

# Summary of alignment
print(aln, show = "complete")

# Convert to phangorn for phylogeny
library(phangorn)
aln_phy <- as.phyDat(as(aln, "AAStringSet"), type = "AA")
dm <- dist.ml(aln_phy)
tree <- NJ(dm)
plot(tree, main = "NJ tree from MSA", cex = 0.8)

heading: 13.244 Output & Results
text: MSA object aligned sequences gap characters; consensus derivable via consensusString.

heading: 13.245 Interpretation

heading: 13.246 Practical Tips
text: > 1000 sequences, MAFFT (outside R) substantially faster Biostrings/msa.Alignment quality matters tree-building algorithm; bad MSA produces bad phylogeny.Trim alignment columns many gaps (e.g., trimAl) tree inference.Visualise alignments ggmsa identify problematic columns.Amino-acid alignments often reliable codon alignments distantly related sequences; use back-translation codon-level analysis needed.

heading: 13.249 Introduction
text: Multi-omics experiments measure transcriptome, methylome, proteome, etc. samples. Joint analysis exposes coordinated variation across layers single-layer analyses miss – cis-regulatory effects methylation, mRNA-protein correspondence, integrated disease subtypes.

heading: 13.250 Prerequisites
text: Single-omic analysis (DE, methylation, proteomics); PCA / factor analysis basics.

heading: 13.251 Theory
text: MOFA (Multi-Omics Factor Analysis) learns latent factors explaining variation across omics layers. factor per-layer weights: factors loading strongly multiple layers capture shared signal; single-layer factors reflect layer-specific variation. MOFA2 adds sparsity interpretability.Similarity network fusion (SNF) iCluster+ offer alternative approaches; DIABLO (mixOmics) builds supervised multi-omic classifiers.

heading: 13.252 Assumptions
text: Samples matched across layers; layer-specific noise scaling handled model; sufficient sample size factor inference (typically \(> 50\)).

heading: 13.253 R Implementation
code:
library(MOFA2)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 50
# Latent factor drives correlated variation across 2 layers
z <- rnorm(n)

# Layer 1: 200 features (e.g., RNA)
m_rna <- matrix(rnorm(200 * n, 0, 1), 200, n)
m_rna[1:20, ] <- m_rna[1:20, ] + outer(rep(1.5, 20), z)

# Layer 2: 100 features (e.g., methylation)
m_meth <- matrix(rnorm(100 * n, 0, 1), 100, n)
m_meth[1:10, ] <- m_meth[1:10, ] - outer(rep(1.2, 10), z)

colnames(m_rna) <- colnames(m_meth) <- paste0("S", 1:n)
rownames(m_rna)  <- paste0("rna_",  1:200)
rownames(m_meth) <- paste0("meth_", 1:100)

mofa <- create_mofa(list(RNA = m_rna, Methylation = m_meth))
# Illustrative: actual fitting requires Python backend (reticulate + mofapy2).
# model_opts <- get_default_model_options(mofa)
# model_opts$num_factors <- 3
# mofa_trained <- run_mofa(mofa, training_data_options = list(),
#                          model_options = model_opts, outfile = tempfile())

mofa

heading: 13.254 Output & Results
text: trained MOFA model latent-factor scores per sample, per-layer feature weights, variance decomposition per factor x layer.

heading: 13.255 Interpretation
text: “MOFA identified 5 latent factors; factor 1 explained 32 % RNA 18 % methylation variance, linked cancer subtype; factor 2 RNA-specific reflected proliferation signature.”

heading: 13.256 Practical Tips
text: Standardise layer MOFA (mean = 0, SD = 1) one layer dominates.Layer-specific factors often reflect technical artefacts; use diagnose batch effects.DIABLO mixOmics better choice integration supervised outcome.MOFA requires Python via reticulate; test install run_mofa2_docs() large runs.Interpret factor weights domain knowledge; algorithmic factors without biology uninformative.

heading: 13.259 Introduction
text: KEGG (Kyoto Encyclopedia Genes Genomes) curated database metabolic signalling pathways represented directed graphs genes gene products. KEGG pathway enrichment asks whether genes differential-expression hit list -represented curated pathways, identifying functional themes differential signal. companion visualisation tool pathview overlays log-fold-change values directly onto canonical KEGG pathway diagrams, making straightforward communicate specific genes within pathway drive enrichment signal.

heading: 13.260 Prerequisites
text: working understanding differential-expression analysis, Entrez gene identifiers, basic -representation analysis using hypergeometric Fisher’s exact tests.

heading: 13.261 Theory
text: KEGG enrichment uses statistical framework GO -representation analysis: hypergeometric (Fisher’s exact) test pathway compares proportion DE genes assigned pathway proportion expected chance universe annotated genes. Adjusted \(p\)-values (BH Benjamini-Hochberg) control false-discovery rate across many pathways tested.Pathway visualisation via pathview rendering colours gene node canonical KEGG diagram user-supplied log fold change (continuous statistic), producing annotated PNG/PDF pathway map. visual immediately exposes whether enrichment driven coordinated - -regulation across pathway versus isolated hits.

heading: 13.262 Assumptions
text: DE genes correctly mapped Entrez IDs; KEGG pathway annotations current (KEGG updated regularly; old annotations miss recent additions); tests independent across pathways (BH adjustment conservative typical correlated-pathway structure).

heading: 13.263 R Implementation
code:
library(clusterProfiler); library(org.Hs.eg.db)

set.seed(2026)
all_entrez <- keys(org.Hs.eg.db, keytype = "ENTREZID")
de_entrez  <- sample(all_entrez, 200)

ek <- enrichKEGG(gene = de_entrez,
                 organism = "hsa",
                 pvalueCutoff = 0.1)
head(as.data.frame(ek))[, c("ID", "Description", "p.adjust", "Count")]

# Visualise a pathway with LFC overlay
# library(pathview)
# lfc_vec <- setNames(rnorm(length(de_entrez)), de_entrez)
# pathview(gene.data = lfc_vec, pathway.id = "hsa04110", species = "hsa")

heading: 13.264 Output & Results
text: enrichKEGG() returns enrichResult object one row per pathway, including pathway ID description, count DE genes, BH-adjusted \(p\)-value, comma-separated list contributing gene symbols. pathview() writes PNG pathway diagram gene node coloured supplied log-fold-change value.

heading: 13.265 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “KEGG enrichment clusterProfiler::enrichKEGG (organism = hsa, BH-adjusted \(p < 0.05\)) identified cell cycle (hsa04110, \(p_{\mathrm{adj}} = 0.001\), 23 genes) p53 signalling (hsa04115, \(p_{\mathrm{adj}} = 0.008\), 14 genes) top enriched pathways; pathview overlay hsa04110 showed consistent -regulation cyclins CDKs alongside -regulation CDK inhibitors.” Always state species code, universe background genes, multiple-testing correction.

heading: 13.266 Practical Tips
text: KEGG functions clusterProfiler require Entrez IDs; convert gene symbols via bitr(symbols, fromType = "SYMBOL", toType = "ENTREZID", OrgDb = org.Hs.eg.db).enrichMKEGG() uses KEGG modules (smaller, specific functional units); preferable metabolic studies broader KEGG pathway granularity coarse.pathview() requires internet access writes output files working directory; treat rendering one-rather running automated pipeline.KEGG organism codes: hsa human, mmu mouse, rno rat, dme fly, cel worm; complete list KEGG website.Combine KEGG (broad pathways) Reactome (ReactomePA::enrichPathway) complementary coverage; two databases overlap emphasise different aspects biology.genes associated fold-change estimates, GSEA KEGG pathways (gseKEGG()) powerful -representation analysis uses full ranked gene list rather thresholding hit non-hit sets.

heading: 13.270 Introduction
text: Sample-level PCA transformed RNA-seq counts standard quality-control step differential-expression analysis. reveals whether biological samples cluster experimental condition (desired outcome), batch technical covariates (confounding signal needs correction), neither (can suggest excessive noise sample mix-ups). Running PCA early — modelling — prevents consequential downstream errors motivates design choices differential-expression step.

heading: 13.271 Prerequisites
text: working understanding principal components analysis, RNA-seq count data, role variance-stabilising transformations equalising variance-mean relationship count data.

heading: 13.272 Theory
text: Raw RNA-seq counts variance grows mean, violating assumption equal variance PCA implicitly relies . Variance-stabilising transformations (VST older rlog DESeq2) apply non-linear transformation approximately equalises variance across expression range; result suitable PCA, clustering, downstream methods assume homoscedastic input.PCA top-variance genes (typically top 500 ) highlights dominant sources variation. proportion variance explained PC indicates much total variability captured, score plot — samples coloured metadata variables — exposes design factors drive variability.

heading: 13.273 Assumptions
text: Sufficient samples (rule thumb: least 6, ideally ) meaningful PCA; counts variance-stabilised; chosen number top-variance genes large enough capture structure small enough suppress noise.

heading: 13.274 R Implementation
code:
library(DESeq2); library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
counts <- matrix(rpois(20000 * 12, lambda = 30), nrow = 20000)
colnames(counts) <- paste0("s", 1:12)
coldata <- data.frame(condition = rep(c("A", "B"), each = 6),
                      batch = rep(c("X", "Y"), 6))

dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(counts, colData = coldata, design = ~ condition)
vsd <- vst(dds, blind = TRUE)

plotPCA(vsd, intgroup = c("condition", "batch"))

heading: 13.275 Output & Results
text: plotPCA() returns ggplot samples projected onto PC1 PC2 axis labels showing proportion variance explained. Colouring condition shape batch (vice versa) makes factors visible simultaneously.

heading: 13.276 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Sample-level PCA VST-transformed counts (top 500 variance genes) showed PC1 capturing 40 % variance clearly separating treatment conditions, PC2 (12 %) showed weaker batch effect. included batch differential-expression design adjust .” Always report proportion variance explained PC corresponds substantively.

heading: 13.277 Practical Tips
text: Use blind = TRUE QC PCA variance-stabilisation shrink toward design; downstream DE, design-aware (blind = FALSE) transformation appropriate.batch dominates PC1, include batch DESeq2 (limma) design; batch overlaps perfectly condition, experiment confounded salvaged.Plot multiple metadata colourings (sex, age, RIN, library prep date) PCA scatter reveal hidden structure; outlier samples often correspond specific covariates.Complement PCA sample-sample distance heatmap (pheatmap(.matrix(dist(t(assay(vsd)))))); two views agree broad story emphasise different details.Outliers PCA often indicate QC failures (low library complexity, contamination); investigate consider exclusion differential analysis.Compare PCA raw counts, log-counts, VST; raw counts variance dominated highly expressed genes, log-counts overcorrect, VST calibrated downstream analysis.

heading: 13.278 R Packages Used
text: DESeq2 vst(), rlog(), plotPCA() broader RNA-seq workflow; pheatmap sample-distance heatmaps; ggplot2 custom PCA plots; PCAtools richer PCA visualisations including biplots loading plots.

heading: 13.281 Introduction
text: Phylogenetic trees depict evolutionary relationships among sequences (species, strains, cells). central taxonomy, molecular epidemiology, cancer-clone tracing. ape standard R package tree construction manipulation, phangorn adds maximum-likelihood parsimony methods.

heading: 13.282 Prerequisites
text: Multiple sequence alignment; nucleotide amino-acid substitution models.

heading: 13.283 Theory
text: Distance-based methods (neighbour-joining, UPGMA) compute pairwise distances alignment build tree approximates distances; fast statistically crude.Maximum-likelihood (ML) methods posit substitution model (JC69, K80, GTR + Gamma + ) find tree topology + branch lengths maximising likelihood observed alignment.Bootstrapping resamples alignment columns assess topology confidence; bootstrap values \(\geq\) 70 % commonly considered well-supported.

heading: 13.284 Assumptions
text: Alignment columns homologous independent; substitution model captures key features (transition/transversion bias, rate heterogeneity).

heading: 13.285 R Implementation
code:
library(ape)

# Load an example alignment shipped with ape
data(woodmouse)          # DNAbin alignment of 15 sequences, 965 sites

# Distance matrix under K80 model
d <- dist.dna(woodmouse, model = "K80")

# Neighbour-joining tree
nj_tree <- nj(d)
plot(nj_tree, main = "Neighbour-joining tree (woodmouse)",
     cex = 0.6, font = 1)
add.scale.bar()

# Bootstrap support
bp <- boot.phylo(nj_tree, woodmouse,
                 function(x) nj(dist.dna(x, model = "K80")),
                 B = 100, quiet = TRUE)
plot(nj_tree, main = "NJ tree with bootstrap support",
     cex = 0.6, font = 1)
nodelabels(bp, cex = 0.5, frame = "none", adj = c(1.3, -0.3))

heading: 13.286 Output & Results
text: neighbour-joining tree branch lengths substitutions per site; bootstrap values annotated internal nodes.

heading: 13.287 Interpretation
text: “neighbour-joining tree cytochrome b sequences recovers regional subclades bootstrap support > 80 %, consistent geographic sampling structure.”

heading: 13.288 Practical Tips
text: genome-scale data, use ML tools like RAxML-NG IQ-TREE externally import trees ape::read.tree().NJ fast sensitive model misspecification; ML gold standard small-moderate alignments.Use phangorn::modelTest() pick substitution model AIC/BIC running ML.Annotate trees ggtree publication-ready figures including metadata overlays.large strain trees (SARS-CoV-2), use Nextstrain / treetime rather base ape.

heading: 13.291 Introduction
text: Population genetics quantifies genetic variation within populations. Allele frequencies, Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (HWE), F-statistics form foundational toolkit diagnosing genotyping errors, detecting population structure, assessing selection drift.

heading: 13.292 Prerequisites
text: Mendelian inheritance; genotype coding (0/1/2 minor-allele dosage).

heading: 13.293 Theory
text: Allele frequency. biallelic SNP minor-allele counts \(n_{AA}, n_{Aa}, n_{aa}\), \(p = (2 n_{AA} + n_{Aa}) / (2 n)\).Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (HWE). random mating selection, expected genotype frequencies \(p^2, 2pq, q^2\). Exact HWE tests (HWExact) preferred chi-square rare variants.F-statistics. \(F_{ST} = (H_T - H_S) / H_T\) partitions heterozygosity within populations, quantifying structure (Wright, 1951). Typical human \(F_{ST}\) continents \(\approx 0.1\).

heading: 13.294 Assumptions
text: Random mating, selection, mutation, migration, infinite population size; genotyping unbiased.

heading: 13.295 R Implementation
code:
library(HardyWeinberg)

set.seed(2026)
# Simulate HWE: p = 0.3, n = 200 diploid individuals
p <- 0.3
n_AA <- rbinom(1, 200, p^2)
n_aa <- rbinom(1, 200, (1 - p)^2)
n_Aa <- 200 - n_AA - n_aa

gt <- c(AA = n_AA, AB = n_Aa, BB = n_aa)
HWExact(gt, verbose = TRUE)

# Simulate HWE violation (excess heterozygotes)
gt_violate <- c(AA = 20, AB = 160, BB = 20)
HWExact(gt_violate, verbose = TRUE)

# F_ST between two populations (manual calculation)
p1 <- 0.2; p2 <- 0.5
p_bar <- mean(c(p1, p2))
H_T <- 2 * p_bar * (1 - p_bar)
H_S <- mean(c(2 * p1 * (1 - p1), 2 * p2 * (1 - p2)))
Fst <- (H_T - H_S) / H_T
Fst

heading: 13.296 Output & Results
text: HWE exact test p-value (large simulated HWE case; ~0 violated case). \(F_{ST} \approx 0.1\) two-population toy example.

heading: 13.297 Interpretation
text: “HWE exact tests excluded 1,240 variants (p < 1e-6) association analysis likely genotyping artefacts. -cohort \(F_{ST} = 0.012\) indicates minimal population stratification ancestry matching.”

heading: 13.298 Practical Tips
text: Always HWE-filter variants GWAS pre-processing; strong HWE violations usually indicate genotyping errors.Test HWE controls (cases) true disease associations can cause HWE departures cases.low-MAF variants (<5%), asymptotic chi-square unreliable; use exact test.adegenet hierfstat provide comprehensive F-statistics population-structure utilities.\(F_{ST}\) samples can inflated small sample size; apply Weir-Cockerham estimator bootstrap CIs.

heading: 13.301 Introduction
text: AlphaFold 2 (2021) achieved near-experimental accuracy predicting protein 3-D structure amino-acid sequence, transforming structural biology. RoseTTAFold ESMFold offer competitive alternatives different speed/accuracy trade-offs. Downstream analyses include structural alignment, docking, mutation impact prediction.

heading: 13.302 Prerequisites
text: Amino-acid sequence; familiarity protein structure (secondary/tertiary elements).

heading: 13.303 Theory
text: AlphaFold uses attention-based deep learning trained PDB structures multiple-sequence-alignment (MSA) features. model outputs per-residue (x, y, z) coordinates plus per-residue confidence score (pLDDT, 0-100) inter-residue confidence (PAE).pLDDT > 70 = confident; 50-70 = low confidence; < 50 = disordered/unreliable.

heading: 13.304 Assumptions
text: protein evolutionary neighbours (sufficient MSA) unusually divergent; post-translational modifications co-factors modelled.

heading: 13.305 R Implementation
text: AlphaFold prediction runs outside R (ColabFold, AlphaFold Docker, OpenFold). R used structural analysis via bio3d:
code:
library(bio3d)

# Read a PDB (here a built-in example: human CDK2)
pdb <- read.pdb(system.file("examples/hivp.pdb", package = "bio3d"))
summary(pdb)

# Secondary structure, residue count, chains
head(pdb$atom[, c("resno", "resid", "chain", "elety")])

# Torsion angles
tor <- torsion.pdb(pdb)
head(tor$phi)

# Structural alignment of two chains (illustrative)
chain_a <- atom.select(pdb, chain = "A", elety = "CA")
chain_b <- atom.select(pdb, chain = "B", elety = "CA")
xyz_a <- pdb$xyz[chain_a$xyz]
xyz_b <- pdb$xyz[chain_b$xyz]
c(n_a = length(xyz_a) / 3, n_b = length(xyz_b) / 3)

heading: 13.306 Output & Results
text: parsed PDB structure atoms, residues, chains accessible programmatically; torsion angles (phi, psi) define backbone conformation.

heading: 13.307 Interpretation
text: “AlphaFold prediction novel kinase scored pLDDT 88.3 overall; activation loop (residues 185-210) pLDDT < 60, consistent conformational flexibility seen homologous kinases.”

heading: 13.308 Practical Tips
text: Always check pLDDT per residue; low-confidence regions unreliable structural analysis.PAE plots reveal inter-domain uncertainty; two confident domains high PAE means relative orientation unreliable.mutation impact, combine AlphaFold + molecular-dynamics simulation Gromacs AMBER.AlphaFold-Multimer handles complexes; standard AlphaFold monomer-focused.ESMFold faster less accurate novel folds; use screening large sequence sets.

heading: 13.311 Introduction
text: MSstats standard Bioconductor package statistical analysis bottom-mass-spectrometry proteomics. ingests peptide-level intensity search engine (MaxQuant, Skyline, OpenMS, Spectronaut) fits mixed-effects models protein-level contrasts label-free (LFQ), TMT, SRM/PRM workflows.

heading: 13.312 Prerequisites
text: Peptide-level MS data; experimental-design matrix (condition, bioreplicate, run).

heading: 13.313 Theory
text: protein, MSstats fits linear mixed-effects model run/feature random effects condition fixed effects; contrast produces log-fold change moderated t-test analogue. Missing-value handling uses accelerated failure-time imputation censoring (AFT). LOG2 intensity response scale.

heading: 13.314 Assumptions
text: Peptide intensity valid surrogate protein abundance; missingness either completely random (MCAR) left-censored detection limit (MNAR).

heading: 13.315 R Implementation
code:
library(MSstats)

# Simulated MSstats-format table: peptide intensity x run x condition
set.seed(2026)
proteins <- paste0("P", 1:20)
pep_per_prot <- 3
runs <- paste0("R", 1:6)
conditions <- rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = 3)

df <- expand.grid(Protein = proteins,
                  Feature = 1:pep_per_prot,
                  Run = runs,
                  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
df$Condition    <- conditions[match(df$Run, runs)]
df$BioReplicate <- df$Run
df$Intensity    <- 2^(rnorm(nrow(df), mean = 20, sd = 1))

# Inject DE in first 5 proteins
trt_rows <- df$Condition == "trt" & df$Protein %in% proteins[1:5]
df$Intensity[trt_rows] <- df$Intensity[trt_rows] * 2

# Mock up the MSstats-required column names and minimum columns
msdf <- data.frame(
  ProteinName = df$Protein,
  PeptideSequence = paste0("PEP", df$Feature),
  PrecursorCharge = 2,
  FragmentIon = NA, ProductCharge = NA,
  IsotopeLabelType = "L",
  Condition = df$Condition,
  BioReplicate = df$BioReplicate,
  Run = df$Run,
  Intensity = df$Intensity
)

# MSstats workflow: processing + protein-level model + contrast
# processed <- dataProcess(msdf)
# comparison <- matrix(c(-1, 1), nrow = 1,
#                      dimnames = list("trt_vs_ctrl", c("ctrl", "trt")))
# results <- groupComparison(contrast.matrix = comparison, data = processed)
# head(results$ComparisonResult)
head(msdf, 3)

heading: 13.316 Output & Results
text: Per-protein log-fold change, standard error, p-value, adjusted p-value; MSstats also outputs processed data diagnostic plots (profile plots, condition plots) QC.

heading: 13.317 Interpretation
text: “MSstats identified 142 proteins differentially abundant BH-FDR < 0.05 treatment control; top hit (log2FC = 3.2) corresponds direct drug target.”

heading: 13.318 Practical Tips
text: Provide replicate structure via BioReplicate; technical replicates must distinct biological replicates.Normalise within MSstats (dataProcess(normalization = "equalizeMedians")) rather pre-normalising, keep pipeline single-entry.TMT, use MSstatsTMT; mixed-effects model differs due isobaric design structure.Filter peptides unique--protein modelling; shared peptides inflate -protein correlation.Report LFC adjusted p-value; high LFC wide CI (peptides) weak evidence.

heading: 13.321 Introduction
text: FASTQ-level QC identified low-quality bases adapter contamination, read trimming removes alignment. motivation straightforward: sequencing-quality scores typically degrade toward 3’ end reads, adapter sequences appear insert shorter read length. produce mismatched bases bias alignment increase mapping rates incorrectly. Rfastp package provides R-friendly interface fastp, fast C++ tool handles trimming, adapter removal, quality filtering, polytail cleanup single pass.

heading: 13.322 Prerequisites
text: working understanding FASTQ format, Phred quality scores, Illumina adapter sequences (TruSeq, Nextera).

heading: 13.323 Theory
text: Standard trimming operations:Quality trimming: scan 3’ end (optionally 5’) remove bases falling Phred threshold; sliding-window variants trim mean quality window drops cutoff.Adapter removal: detect known adapter sequences via overlap matching read 3’ end trim . Auto-detection (matching library common adapters) usually reliable.Length filtering: discard reads fall minimum length trimming; short reads align ambiguously waste downstream resources.Polytail trimming: remove poly-poly-G contamination, common NextSeq/NovaSeq dark-cycle artefacts.

heading: 13.324 Assumptions
text: Adapter sequences known auto-detectable; quality scores standard Phred+33 encoding (true modern Illumina output).

heading: 13.325 R Implementation
code:
library(Rfastp)

in_file  <- system.file("extdata", "reads1.fastq.gz", package = "Rfastp")
out_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".fastq.gz")

# Trim with fastp default heuristics
out <- rfastp(read1 = in_file, outputFastq = out_file,
              thread = 2,
              trimAdapter = TRUE, overlapDiff = 5)

out$summary

heading: 13.326 Output & Results
text: rfastp() returns summary list /statistics: reads passing filter, reads adapters trimmed, mean read length , base content. HTML report (auto-generated fastp) provides richer visualisation trimming impact.

heading: 13.327 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Read trimming fastp removed Illumina TruSeq adapters 3.2 % reads quality-trimmed bases Phred 15; 98 % reads retained trimming, mean read length decreasing 150 145 bp.” Always report adapter set, quality threshold, retention rate.

heading: 13.328 Practical Tips
text: Keep trimming settings mild — aggressive quality filtering removes data without proportional gains downstream accuracy.Adapter auto-detection fastp usually reliable; specify adapter sequences explicitly working non-standard library prep kits.paired-end reads, trim mates simultaneously retain properly-paired survivors; Rfastp::rfastp() read1 read2 arguments handles automatically.Validate downstream QC (FastQC, MultiQC) trimming; successful trim improves per-base quality, per-base content, adapter-content panels.Record trimming parameters methods document workflow file (Snakemake, Nextflow); reproducibility requires exact tool versions parameters.UMI-tagged libraries (single-cell, low-input), use UMI-aware tools (umi_tools, fastp’s UMI mode) quality trimming preserve UMI integrity.

heading: 13.329 R Packages Used
text: Rfastp canonical fastp wrapper; ShortRead general FASTQ manipulation R; Biostrings adapter-sequence handling; QuasR integrated alignment workflow includes trimming step.

heading: 13.332 Introduction
text: Raw RNA-seq read counts depend library size library composition; normalisation removes technical sources variation samples can compared equal footing. standard methods — TMM (edgeR), RLE (DESeq2), upper-quartile, CPM — differ aggressively handle compositional differences assumption make bulk genes. Choosing right normalisation foundational; downstream differential-expression tools incorporate normalisation factors offsets GLM, poor choice undermines every conclusion follows.

heading: 13.333 Prerequisites
text: working understanding count data, library size, difference technical (sequencing depth) biological (true expression) variation samples.

heading: 13.334 Theory
text: four standard methods:CPM (counts per million): simple division count total library size millions. Adjusts sequencing depth; address compositional differences (one sample highly expressed genes pulling resources others).TMM (Trimmed Mean M-values): edgeR’s default. Computes log-fold-changes sample reference, trims extreme values, uses trimmed mean normalisation factor. Robust highly expressed compositional outliers.RLE (Relative Log Expression): DESeq2’s estimateSizeFactors. Takes median ratio gene’s count geometric mean across samples. Equivalent TMM spirit; adjust composition.Upper-quartile: scaling 75th-percentile count; less robust TMM/RLE useful sparse data.TMM RLE de facto standards; CPM appropriate visualisation, statistical comparison.

heading: 13.335 Assumptions
text: genes differentially expressed (“majority unchanged” assumption motivates trimmed-mean median-based normalisation); samples technical replicates comparable biological material.

heading: 13.336 R Implementation
code:
library(edgeR); library(DESeq2)

counts <- matrix(rpois(1000, lambda = 10), 100, 10)
rownames(counts) <- paste0("g", 1:100)

# edgeR TMM
dge <- DGEList(counts = counts)
dge <- calcNormFactors(dge, method = "TMM")
dge$samples$norm.factors

# DESeq2 size factors
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(counts, colData = DataFrame(id = 1:10),
                              design = ~ 1)
dds <- estimateSizeFactors(dds)
sizeFactors(dds)

heading: 13.337 Output & Results
text: calcNormFactors() returns per-sample TMM normalisation factors (centred near 1, deviations indicating compositional differences); estimateSizeFactors() returns DESeq2 size factors similar interpretation. used offsets downstream GLMs edgeR, DESeq2, limma-voom.

heading: 13.338 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “TMM normalisation factors ranged 0.85 1.18 across 10 samples, factor exceeding conventional ±20 % concern threshold; corresponding DESeq2 RLE size factors agreed within 5 %, confirming normalisation robust method choice.” Quote range normalisation factors; extreme values signal compositional anomalies.

heading: 13.339 Practical Tips

DE method handles automatically.TMM refColumn set specific reference sample useful one sample known representative; default chooses automatic reference.mixed-tissue heterogeneous experiments, “majority unchanged” assumption can fail; consider quantile normalisation (limma::normalizeQuantiles) condition-aware alternatives.

heading: 13.356 Theory

heading: 13.357 Assumptions

heading: 13.358 R Implementation
code:
library(SingleR); library(celldex)

# Reference: built-in Monaco immune cells
ref <- MonacoImmuneData()

# Toy query: simulate a cell-by-gene expression matrix
set.seed(2026)
query <- matrix(rpois(200 * 100, lambda = 2), 200, 100)
rownames(query) <- sample(rownames(ref), 200)   # match gene names
colnames(query) <- paste0("c", 1:100)

pred <- SingleR(test = query, ref = ref,
                labels = ref$label.main,
                de.method = "wilcox")

table(pred$labels)
head(pred[, c("labels", "pruned.labels")])

heading: 13.359 Output & Results
text: SingleR() returns per-cell predicted labels, pruned labels (high-confidence subset), underlying per-reference-label scores. Summary tables show predicted cell-type counts; pruneScores() allows fine-tuning confidence threshold.

heading: 13.360 Interpretation

heading: 13.365 Introduction
text: Single-cell RNA-seq clustering groups cells putative cell types transcriptional states based transcriptome similarity. dominant approach graph-based community detection: construct shared-nearest-neighbour (SNN) graph cells PCA space, apply Louvain Leiden algorithm identify densely connected communities. approach scales millions cells, produces interpretable cluster boundaries, standard Seurat Bioconductor scRNA-seq workflows.

heading: 13.366 Prerequisites
text: working understanding normalised scRNA-seq counts, dimensionality reduction (PCA), concept community detection graphs.

heading: 13.367 Theory
text: standard pipeline:K-nearest-neighbour graph reduced PCA space: cell connected \(k\) nearest neighbours (typically \(k = 20\)).Shared-nearest-neighbour (SNN) graph: edge weights cell pairs determined number shared nearest neighbours, emphasising robust local structure noisy individual neighbour relationships.Community detection: Louvain optimises modularity; Leiden refines Louvain guarantee well-connected communities become modern default.resolution parameter controls granularity: higher resolution yields , smaller clusters; lower resolution yields fewer, larger ones. universally correct resolution — choice depends biological question typically validated marker-gene analysis.

heading: 13.368 Assumptions
text: Cell types states form reasonably discrete communities PCA space; SNN graph captures local biological structure; chosen number PCs (typically 10–50) captures major biological variation.

heading: 13.369 R Implementation
code:
library(Seurat)

set.seed(2026)
counts <- matrix(rpois(300 * 200, lambda = 2), 200, 300)
rownames(counts) <- paste0("g", 1:200)
colnames(counts) <- paste0("c", 1:300)

so <- CreateSeuratObject(counts)
so <- NormalizeData(so, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindVariableFeatures(so, nfeatures = 100, verbose = FALSE)
so <- ScaleData(so, verbose = FALSE)
so <- RunPCA(so, npcs = 10, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindNeighbors(so, dims = 1:10, verbose = FALSE)

so <- FindClusters(so, resolution = 0.3, verbose = FALSE)
n_low <- nlevels(Idents(so))

so <- FindClusters(so, resolution = 1.2, verbose = FALSE)
n_high <- nlevels(Idents(so))

c(low_res = n_low, high_res = n_high)

heading: 13.370 Output & Results
text: FindClusters() returns Seurat object cluster identities added active idents per-cell metadata. Different resolutions produce different cluster counts; clustree package visualises cells move clusters resolution changes.

heading: 13.371 Interpretation

heading: 13.372 Practical Tips

Always cluster PCA space.“correct” number clusters biological judgement, statistical one; use marker-gene analysis confirm whether clusters correspond known plausible cell types.large datasets (\(> 500{,}000\) cells), use Seurat v5 sketch-based clustering Bioconductor’s bluster package subsampling tractable computation.Compare clustering integration-aware methods (harmony, Symphony) batch effects span multiple datasets; clustering uncorrected data confounds batch biology.

heading: 13.376 Introduction
text: Datasets different batches, donors, technologies cluster batch rather biology without correction. Integration methods align shared cell types across datasets preserving dataset-specific populations. Harmony iteratively centres cell embeddings batch; Seurat’s CCA/RPCA anchors cells batches.

heading: 13.377 Prerequisites
text: Multiple scRNA-seq datasets shared cell populations; normalised expression; PCA embeddings.

heading: 13.378 Theory
text: Harmony alternates () soft cluster assignment PCA space, (ii) per-cluster batch centroid computation, (iii) shifting cells within cluster batch-specific correction vector. Converges iterations scales millions cells.Seurat CCA/RPCA integration finds anchor pairs across datasets via mutual nearest neighbours shared low-dimensional space uses anchors transform embeddings.

heading: 13.379 Assumptions
text: Shared cell types span batches; batch effects corrective PCA space; biology batch perfectly confounded.

heading: 13.380 R Implementation
code:
library(harmony); library(Seurat)

set.seed(2026)
# Simulate two batches with shared biology + batch shift
n <- 150
batch <- rep(c("A", "B"), each = n)

counts <- matrix(rpois(300 * 200, lambda = 2), 200, 300)
rownames(counts) <- paste0("g", 1:200)
colnames(counts) <- paste0("c", 1:300)
counts[, batch == "B"] <- counts[, batch == "B"] + 3   # batch effect

so <- CreateSeuratObject(counts)
so$batch <- batch
so <- NormalizeData(so, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindVariableFeatures(so, nfeatures = 100, verbose = FALSE)
so <- ScaleData(so, verbose = FALSE)
so <- RunPCA(so, npcs = 10, verbose = FALSE)

so <- RunHarmony(so, group.by.vars = "batch", dims.use = 1:10,
                 verbose = FALSE)
so <- RunUMAP(so, reduction = "harmony", dims = 1:10, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindNeighbors(so, reduction = "harmony", dims = 1:10, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindClusters(so, resolution = 0.3, verbose = FALSE)

table(Batch = so$batch, Cluster = Idents(so))

heading: 13.381 Output & Results
text: Harmony-corrected embedding; clustering, batches represented cluster (shared cell types) rather segregating batch.

heading: 13.382 Interpretation
text: “Harmony integration, seven major cell types contained cells donors (mixing LISI = 1.84 / 2.0), indicating successful batch correction without -mixing.”

heading: 13.383 Practical Tips
text: Use Harmony speed; CCA/RPCA heavier anchor-based correction batches unique cell types.Quantify integration quality kBET, LISI, silhouette batch vs cell type.Never integrate across tissues; integrate within tissue donors/batches covariates.-integration destroys biology; always inspect cell-type marker expression post-integration.Batch fixed effect DE model alternative explicit embedding integration focused comparisons.

heading: 13.386 Introduction
text: Marker genes genes differentially expressed one cluster relative others; drive cell-type annotation, functional interpretation, connection unsupervised clustering known biology. Seurat’s FindMarkers FindAllMarkers implement standard differential-expression tests (Wilcoxon rank-sum, MAST hurdle, DESeq2) log-normalised SCT-transformed data, per cluster, either cells specified comparison cluster.

heading: 13.387 Prerequisites
text: working understanding clustered scRNA-seq output, log-fold-change interpretation, difference cluster-vs-pairwise cluster comparisons.

heading: 13.388 Theory
text: cluster, marker test compares cells cluster cells clusters (specified subset) gene--gene. output gene includes:avg_log2FC: average log-fold change target cluster reference.pct.1: fraction cells target cluster expressing gene.pct.2: fraction cells reference expressing gene.p_val_adj: Benjamini-Hochberg adjusted \(p\)-value.Wilcoxon rank-sum default test Seurat (fast, robust); MAST handles zero-inflation rigorously via hurdle model; DESeq2 applies negative-binomial framework (slow principled).Conventional marker criteria: \(\log_2 \mathrm{FC} > 0.5\), adjusted \(p < 0.05\), pct.1 > 0.25 (gene expressed least quarter cluster’s cells).

heading: 13.389 Assumptions
text: Cells within cluster reasonably homogeneous; reference set valid comparison; multiple testing corrected across genes within comparison (across joint cluster set, -conservative).

heading: 13.390 R Implementation
code:
library(Seurat)

set.seed(2026)
counts <- matrix(rpois(300 * 200, lambda = 2), 200, 300)
rownames(counts) <- paste0("g", 1:200)
colnames(counts) <- paste0("c", 1:300)
# Inject a marker pattern in cells 1-50
counts[1:10, 1:50] <- counts[1:10, 1:50] + 10

so <- CreateSeuratObject(counts)
so <- NormalizeData(so, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindVariableFeatures(so, nfeatures = 100, verbose = FALSE)
so <- ScaleData(so, verbose = FALSE)
so <- RunPCA(so, npcs = 10, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindNeighbors(so, dims = 1:10, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindClusters(so, resolution = 0.5, verbose = FALSE)

markers_all <- FindAllMarkers(so, only.pos = TRUE, min.pct = 0.25,
                              logfc.threshold = 0.25, verbose = FALSE)
head(markers_all[, c("cluster", "gene", "avg_log2FC", "p_val_adj", "pct.1")])

heading: 13.391 Output & Results
text: FindAllMarkers() returns data frame one row per significant marker per cluster, including cluster identity, gene name, log-fold change, adjusted \(p\)-value, percentage cells expressing target vs reference. Filtering .pos = TRUE retains -regulated markers, typically interpretable cell-type identification.

heading: 13.392 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “FindAllMarkers (Wilcoxon test, min.pct = 0.25, logfc.threshold = 0.25, .pos = TRUE) identified 1{,}240 unique markers across 12 clusters; cluster 2’s top markers (CD8A, GZMB, PRF1) identify cytotoxic T cells, cluster 5’s markers (MS4A1, CD79A) identify B cells. Annotation followed manual review Human Cell Atlas reference.” Always state test, thresholds, annotation source.

heading: 13.393 Practical Tips
text: Use .pos = TRUE retain -regulated markers; -regulated markers less interpretable scRNA-seq genes dropouts given cluster.Set min.pct = 0.25 filter genes limited expression target cluster; quality gate prevents spurious markers low-detection genes.publication-quality marker calls, use MAST (test.use = "MAST"); handles zero-inflation rigorously Wilcoxon gives conservative \(p\)-values.Always annotate clusters using markers clustering — never adjust clusters fit pre-conceived markers; latter circular reasoning.perform DE testing clusters produced clustering data; form double-dipping inflates significance. Use post-hoc validation independent test data instead.automated cell-type annotation, pair markers reference-based tools (SingleR, scType, Azimuth); reduce manual annotation effort substantially.

heading: 13.397 Introduction
text: Single-cell RNA-seq cells differ total UMI count orders magnitude — typical 10x Chromium experiment ranges hundred tens thousands UMIs per cell. library-size variation swamps biological signal corrected. Normalisation removes library-size effect, variance stabilisation goes flattening mean-variance relationship downstream PCA clustering driven biology rather count magnitude.

heading: 13.398 Prerequisites
text: working understanding count data, library-size effects, negative-binomial distribution standard model count overdispersion.

heading: 13.399 Theory
text: Three approaches dominate:LogNormalize (Seurat default): divide cell’s count total, multiply 10{,}000, take \(\log_{1p}\). Simple, fast, well understood; stabilise variance.SCTransform: regularised negative-binomial regression sequencing depth explanatory variable, producing Pearson residuals normalised output. Variance stabilised construction, regularised parameter pooling allows rare cell types recovered better LogNormalize.Pool-based size factors (scran::computeSumFactors): pool cells robust size-factor estimation low-depth settings, addressing dropout problem simple library-size normalisation .

heading: 13.400 Assumptions
text: Library-size effects multiplicative count expectations; counts follow (possibly cell-specific) negative-binomial distribution gene-specific dispersion.

heading: 13.401 R Implementation
code:
library(Seurat)

set.seed(2026)
counts <- matrix(rpois(300 * 200, lambda = 2), 200, 300)
rownames(counts) <- paste0("g", 1:200)
colnames(counts) <- paste0("c", 1:300)

so <- CreateSeuratObject(counts)

# Standard LogNormalize
so <- NormalizeData(so, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindVariableFeatures(so, nfeatures = 100, verbose = FALSE)
so <- ScaleData(so, verbose = FALSE)

# Alternative: SCTransform
so_sct <- SCTransform(so, verbose = FALSE)

head(GetAssayData(so, assay = "RNA", slot = "data")[1:5, 1:4])
head(GetAssayData(so_sct, assay = "SCT", slot = "data")[1:5, 1:4])

heading: 13.402 Output & Results

heading: 13.403 Interpretation

SCT-based clustering identified two additional rare populations (mast cells, plasmacytoid dendritic cells) LogNormalize merged larger clusters.” Always state normalisation method regressed-covariates.

heading: 13.404 Practical Tips

heading: 13.408 Introduction
text: Single-cell RNA-seq experiments routinely contain empty droplets, stressed apoptotic cells, doublets (two cells captured one droplet), various technical artefacts enter downstream clustering differential-expression analysis. Per-cell QC filtering library size, detected-gene count, mitochondrial-read percentage removes worst offenders. trade-delicate: -filtering excludes real biology (e.g. plasma cells naturally low gene counts), -filtering produces clusters damaged cells mimic real cell types.

heading: 13.409 Prerequisites
text: working understanding scRNA-seq counts, structure droplet-based experiments, role mitochondrial reads cell-health indicator.

heading: 13.410 Theory
text: Three canonical per-cell metrics dominate QC:nCount_RNA (total UMI count per cell): low values indicate empty droplets debris; high values often indicate doublets.nFeature_RNA (number detected genes): lowest quantile typically corresponds broken empty droplets.percent.mt (percentage reads mapping mitochondrial genes): elevated values indicate apoptotic stressed cells whose cytoplasmic mRNA degraded preferentially.Thresholds dataset-specific; fixed cutoffs (percent.mt < 15 10x human samples) work typical experiments, adaptive thresholds based median absolute deviation (scater::isOutlier) generalise better across datasets.doublet detection, dedicated tools (scDblFinder, DoubletFinder) provide reliable identification naive UMI-based heuristics.

heading: 13.411 Assumptions
text: Mitochondrial percentage valid proxy cell health (fails tissue types — cardiomyocytes naturally high mitochondrial content); doublets show abnormally high UMI counts average; chosen filtering thresholds preserve biological diversity.

heading: 13.412 R Implementation
code:
library(Seurat)

set.seed(2026)
counts <- matrix(rpois(300 * 200, lambda = 2), 200, 300)
rownames(counts) <- c(paste0("MT-", 1:10),
                       paste0("gene_", 11:200))
colnames(counts) <- paste0("cell_", 1:300)
counts[1:10, 1:30] <- counts[1:10, 1:30] + 20   # 30 cells with high MT

so <- CreateSeuratObject(counts)
so[["percent.mt"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(so, pattern = "^MT-")

VlnPlot(so, c("nFeature_RNA", "nCount_RNA", "percent.mt"),
        ncol = 3, pt.size = 0.1)

so_f <- subset(so,
               subset = nFeature_RNA > 50 &
                        nFeature_RNA < 180 &
                        percent.mt   < 15)
dim(so_f)       # cells x genes after QC

heading: 13.413 Output & Results
text: PercentageFeatureSet() adds mitochondrial percentage per-cell metadata; VlnPlot() shows distribution QC metrics across cells. subset() applies thresholds returns filtered Seurat object. Inspecting violin plots filtering confirms chosen thresholds remove tails without truncating bulk distribution.

heading: 13.414 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Per-cell QC removed 4{,}166 12{,}400 cells (33.6 %) fell outside chosen thresholds (nFeature < 500, nFeature > 6{,}000, percent.mt > 15); doublet detection scDblFinder flagged additional 312 cells (3.8 %), yielding 7{,}922 high-quality cells clustering. Thresholds chosen inspecting violin plots per sample.” Always state thresholds rationale.

heading: 13.415 Practical Tips
text: Inspect QC metrics per sample batch separately pooling; differing distributions reflect technical variation inform sample-specific thresholds.Use median-absolute-deviation-based thresholds (scater::isOutlier(metric, nmads = 3)) reproducibility across datasets; fixed cutoffs sample-specific.Run doublet detection (scDblFinder modern recommendation, DoubletFinder older popular alternative) separately count-based QC; doublets often pass naïve UMI thresholds.Remove cells failing multiple metrics (low UMI low gene count high mitochondrial percentage) rather aggressively filtering single metric; combinations reliable thresholds individual metrics.Document QC parameters methods section; results highly sensitive filter choice reproducibility requires explicit thresholds.tissues mitochondrial percentage biologically meaningful (heart, skeletal muscle), adjust thresholds accordingly use alternative QC metrics.

heading: 13.416 R Packages Used

heading: 13.419 Introduction
text: Trajectory inference orders single cells along continuous developmental activation path, producing pseudotime value per cell represents progress along trajectory. Biological processes cell differentiation, cell-cycle progression, T-cell activation, disease progression continuous nature; clustering imposes discrete categories conceal underlying continuous structure. Trajectory methods recover continuity fitting smooth curves graphs data dimensional-reduced space.

heading: 13.420 Prerequisites
text: working understanding scRNA-seq normalisation, dimensional reduction (PCA, UMAP), clustering, conceptual difference discrete cell types continuous developmental trajectories.

heading: 13.421 Theory
text: two dominant trajectory frameworks:Slingshot constructs minimum spanning tree cluster centroids reduced-dimensional space (typically PCA), specifies infers starting cluster, fits smooth principal curves lineage start. Pseudotime arc length along curve, computed per cell.Monocle3 uses reverse-graph-embedding approach UMAP coordinates, fitting principal graph ordering cells along . Monocle3 handles complex branching topologies naturally Slingshot.methods require either specified root cluster inferred one (biological-marker-based validation strongly recommended). Pseudotime values used continuous covariate downstream gene--pseudotime models (tradeSeq, generalised additive models) identify genes whose expression varies along trajectory.

heading: 13.422 Assumptions
text: Cells lie low-dimensional biological manifold; cluster topology approximates trajectory topology; chosen root cluster represents developmental starting state.

heading: 13.423 R Implementation
code:
library(slingshot); library(SingleCellExperiment)

set.seed(2026)
n_cells <- 200
x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = n_cells)
pc1 <- x + rnorm(n_cells, 0, 0.05)
pc2 <- x^2 + rnorm(n_cells, 0, 0.05)

sce <- SingleCellExperiment(
  assays = list(counts = matrix(rpois(n_cells * 50, 3), 50, n_cells))
)
reducedDim(sce, "PCA") <- cbind(pc1, pc2)
sce$cluster <- factor(cut(x, breaks = 4, labels = 1:4))

sce <- slingshot(sce, clusterLabels = sce$cluster,
                 reducedDim = "PCA", start.clus = "1")
head(slingPseudotime(sce))

plot(reducedDim(sce, "PCA"), col = sce$cluster, pch = 16, asp = 1,
     main = "Slingshot lineage")
lines(SlingshotDataSet(sce), lwd = 2, col = "black")

heading: 13.424 Output & Results
text: slingshot() returns SingleCellExperiment object augmented pseudotime values (one column per lineage). plot overlays smooth principal curves PCA scatter, cells coloured cluster, making inferred lineage structure visible.

heading: 13.425 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Slingshot trajectory inference cluster-resolved PCA embedding recovered single lineage cluster 1 (root, validated quiescent stem cells SOX2 expression) clusters 2 3 cluster 4 (terminally activated cells); pseudotime correlated strongly known maturation marker BMI1 (\(\rho = 0.84\), \(p < 0.001\)).” Always validate trajectory direction known biology.

heading: 13.426 Practical Tips
text: Specify start.clus explicitly biology supports clear initial state; automatic root inference often unreliable complex datasets.Compute trajectories PCA space, UMAP; UMAP distorts distances produces unstable trajectory inference.Test genes differential expression along pseudotime tradeSeq (generalised additive models count data) principled identification trajectory-driven genes.Monocle3 preferred trajectory complex branching structure (multiple lineages diverging); Slingshot simpler works well linear weakly branching trajectories.Always validate inferred direction known biological markers (start-state vs end-state); pseudotime direction-less without defined root.Pair trajectory analysis cluster annotation; lineage structure connect biologically meaningful states, arbitrary clusters.

heading: 13.427 R Packages Used
text: slingshot principal-curve-based trajectory inference; monocle3 graph-based trajectory inference branching support; tradeSeq differential gene expression along pseudotime; condiments trajectory testing multiple conditions; dyno unified access dozens trajectory inference methods.

heading: 13.430 Introduction
text: Sequence alignment foundational operation bioinformatics: comparing DNA, RNA, protein sequences find similarities, detect mutations, identify conserved regions, annotate functional domains. two canonical algorithms Needleman-Wunsch (global, full-length alignment) Smith-Waterman (local, best-matching subregion). modern aligners — BWA, Bowtie, BLAST, minimap2 — heuristic approximations dynamic-programming gold standards, optimised speed cost theoretical optimality.

heading: 13.431 Prerequisites
text: working understanding DNA protein sequences, FASTA format, basic dynamic programming algorithmic technique behind alignment.

heading: 13.432 Theory
text: Global alignment (Needleman-Wunsch) finds optimal alignment across entire length sequences. Appropriate sequences believed homologous full length, e.g. comparing two complete protein sequences related species.Local alignment (Smith-Waterman) finds optimal alignment substring sequence. Appropriate region conserved, e.g. searching domain match within longer protein.Scoring schemes combine match rewards, mismatch penalties, gap penalties. proteins, substitution matrices encode biological similarity:BLOSUM62: derived blocks distantly related proteins; BLAST default.PAM250: derived closely related proteins; appropriate close evolutionary distances.Affine gap penalties (gap-opening + gap-extension) better model real biological gaps constant penalties.

heading: 13.433 Assumptions
text: Sequences biologically comparable (molecule type, similar functional category); chosen scoring scheme reflects evolutionary distance involved.

heading: 13.434 R Implementation
code:
library(Biostrings)

s1 <- DNAString("ATCGATCGTACGTACG")
s2 <- DNAString("ATCAATCATACGTGG")

# Global alignment
ga <- pairwiseAlignment(s1, s2, type = "global", substitutionMatrix = nucleotideSubstitutionMatrix(),
                        gapOpening = 10, gapExtension = 4)
ga

# Local alignment
la <- pairwiseAlignment(s1, s2, type = "local",
                        substitutionMatrix = nucleotideSubstitutionMatrix(),
                        gapOpening = 10, gapExtension = 4)
la

# Protein alignment with BLOSUM62
p1 <- AAString("ACDEFGHI")
p2 <- AAString("ACDFGHI")
data(BLOSUM62)
pairwiseAlignment(p1, p2, substitutionMatrix = BLOSUM62, type = "global",
                  gapOpening = 10, gapExtension = 4)

heading: 13.435 Output & Results
text: pairwiseAlignment() returns object aligned sequences (gaps inserted), alignment score, percent identity. Subset accessors (pattern(), subject(), score(), pid()) extract specific components.

heading: 13.436 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Needleman-Wunsch global alignment two 16-bp DNA sequences scored 6 (13/16 matches, two mismatches single-base gap); Smith-Waterman local alignment returned best-matching 13-character substring gaps. protein alignment BLOSUM62 highlighted conserved core (8/9 identity) despite single deletion.” Always state scoring scheme gap penalties.

heading: 13.437 Practical Tips
text: Choose scoring scheme match question: BLOSUM distant protein relatives, PAM close ones; nucleotide alignment, simple match/mismatch usually adequate.short-read alignment (NGS), use BWA, Bowtie2, minimap2 — heuristic vastly faster Needleman-Wunsch millions reads.multiple sequence alignment (MSA), use msa package wraps Clustal Omega, ClustalW, MUSCLE; external tools (MAFFT, T-Coffee) large alignments.Affine gap penalties (gap-open gap-extension) better model biological gaps constant penalties; BLAST default gap-open 10, gap-extension 4 nucleotides.long reads (PacBio, Nanopore), use minimap2 — designed higher error rates longer alignment lengths.sequence search rather pairwise comparison, BLAST DIAMOND scale large databases via heuristic seeding.

heading: 13.438 R Packages Used
text: Biostrings pairwiseAlignment(), BLOSUM/PAM matrices, sequence-handling classes; msa multiple sequence alignment; Rsamtools GenomicAlignments BAM-aware alignment workflows; Rsubread short-read alignment integrated broader RNA-seq Bioconductor workflow.

heading: 13.441 Introduction
text: Seurat widely used R framework single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) analysis. standard Seurat workflow loads count matrix Cell Ranger similar source, performs quality control per-cell metrics, normalises expression, selects variable features, scales, runs dimensionality reduction (PCA + UMAP/t-SNE), clusters cells, identifies marker genes. framework’s strength end--end coverage large user community; alternative Bioconductor stack (SingleCellExperiment + scran) offers comparable functionality tighter integration broader Bioconductor ecosystem.

heading: 13.442 Prerequisites
text: working understanding single-cell RNA-seq experimental design, count matrices droplet-based protocols (10x Chromium), basic clustering dimensionality-reduction concepts.

heading: 13.443 Theory
text: Seurat stores cells × genes counts derived matrices Seurat object structured slots raw counts, normalised data, variable features, scaled data, dimensional reductions, per-cell metadata. standard pipeline:QC: filter cells minimum gene count, maximum percent mitochondrial reads, library size thresholds.Normalisation: log-normalisation (NormalizeData) regularised regression (SCTransform).Variable-feature selection: identify genes biologically interesting variability.Scaling: centre unit-variance scale variable features.PCA: linear dimensionality reduction scaled variable features.Graph-based clustering: shared-nearest-neighbour graph + Louvain Leiden community detection.UMAP / t-SNE: non-linear visualisation — used clustering decisions.

heading: 13.444 Assumptions
text: droplet mostly single-cell (low doublet rate, typically 5 %); per-cell library size correlates mRNA content; variable genes capture biological signal rather technical noise.

heading: 13.445 R Implementation
code:
library(Seurat)

set.seed(2026)
# Minimal simulated count matrix (300 cells x 200 genes)
counts <- matrix(rpois(300 * 200, lambda = 2), 200, 300)
rownames(counts) <- paste0("gene_", 1:200)
colnames(counts) <- paste0("cell_", 1:300)

so <- CreateSeuratObject(counts = counts, min.cells = 3, min.features = 10)
so <- NormalizeData(so, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindVariableFeatures(so, nfeatures = 100, verbose = FALSE)
so <- ScaleData(so, verbose = FALSE)
so <- RunPCA(so, npcs = 10, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindNeighbors(so, dims = 1:10, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindClusters(so, resolution = 0.5, verbose = FALSE)
so <- RunUMAP(so, dims = 1:10, verbose = FALSE)

table(Idents(so))
DimPlot(so, reduction = "umap", label = TRUE)

heading: 13.446 Output & Results
text: Seurat object containing per-cell cluster assignments, dimensional-reduction coordinates (PCA, UMAP), gene-level statistics. DimPlot() produces canonical UMAP visualisation cluster boundaries; FeaturePlot() overlays gene expression UMAP.

heading: 13.447 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Standard Seurat processing 300-cell dataset (NormalizeData, FindVariableFeatures, ScaleData, RunPCA, Louvain clustering resolution 0.5) produced 5 clusters; cluster 3 uniquely expressed CD8A CD8B, consistent cytotoxic T cells, cluster 1 expressed CD79A MS4A1, consistent B cells.” Always describe QC thresholds, normalisation method, number PCs, clustering resolution.

heading: 13.448 Practical Tips
text: Run QC normalisation; low-quality cells dominate variable-feature selection contaminate downstream clustering.Use SCTransform instead NormalizeData + FindVariableFeatures + ScaleData principled variance-stabilising approach; SCTransform modern default new analyses.clustering resolution controls cluster granularity; explore values 0.2 1.2 pick based biological interpretability rather single statistical criterion.Document Seurat version sessionInfo(); API changed substantially major versions (v3, v4, v5), old code may run current versions.large datasets (\(> 100{,}000\) cells), use Seurat v5’s sketch-based subsampling switch Bioconductor stack (SingleCellExperiment, scran) memory efficiency.Pair clustering results marker-gene analysis (FindAllMarkers) biological annotation (SingleR, scType) reporting cluster identities.

heading: 13.449 R Packages Used
text: Seurat canonical end--end scRNA-seq workflow; SingleCellExperiment Bioconductor data structure; scran scater Bioconductor-style normalisation clustering; harmony batch correction; SingleR automatic cell-type annotation; DoubletFinder doublet detection.

heading: 13.452 Introduction
text: Spatial transcriptomics assays (10x Visium, Slide-seq, Stereo-seq, MERFISH) measure gene expression spatial coordinates. bridge histology scRNA-seq: tissue architecture preserved, analyses like spatial domain detection, neighbourhood effects, tumour-immune interface mapping become possible.

heading: 13.453 Prerequisites
text: scRNA-seq analysis basics; familiarity tissue histology.

heading: 13.454 Theory
text: Visium spots contain ~10-100 cells , giving spot-resolution (single-cell) expression. Analysis proceeds like scRNA-seq two additions: () spatial variable-gene detection (Moran’s ), (ii) spatial domain inference (BayesSpace, Giotto, spatial-aware clustering).Deconvolution (cell2location, RCTD, SpatialExperiment) decomposes spot cell-type proportions using matched scRNA-seq reference.

heading: 13.455 Assumptions
text: Spatial coordinates correctly registered H&E; spot-level expression linear combination underlying cell types; tissue processing preserves mRNA integrity.

heading: 13.456 R Implementation
code:
library(Seurat)

set.seed(2026)
# Minimal spatial-like object: spots on a grid
n_spots <- 100
coords <- expand.grid(x = 1:10, y = 1:10)
counts <- matrix(rpois(200 * n_spots, lambda = 3), 200, n_spots)
rownames(counts) <- paste0("g", 1:200)
colnames(counts) <- paste0("spot_", 1:n_spots)

# Inject spatial pattern: top-left vs bottom-right
region <- ifelse(coords$x + coords$y < 10, "A", "B")
counts[1:20, region == "A"] <- counts[1:20, region == "A"] + 8

so <- CreateSeuratObject(counts)
so$x <- coords$x; so$y <- coords$y; so$region <- region

so <- NormalizeData(so, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindVariableFeatures(so, nfeatures = 100, verbose = FALSE)
so <- ScaleData(so, verbose = FALSE)
so <- RunPCA(so, npcs = 10, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindNeighbors(so, dims = 1:10, verbose = FALSE)
so <- FindClusters(so, resolution = 0.5, verbose = FALSE)

plot(coords, col = factor(Idents(so)), pch = 15, cex = 2,
     main = "Spot clusters on tissue coordinates",
     xlab = "x", ylab = "y")

heading: 13.457 Output & Results
text: clustered spatial object; 2-D scatter shows clusters aligning spatial regions, recapitulating tissue architecture.

heading: 13.458 Interpretation
text: “Spatial clustering Visium section identified six domains corresponding tumour core, tumour edge, stroma, three immune niches; interface enrichment analysis revealed T-cell accumulation tumour-stroma boundary.”

heading: 13.459 Practical Tips
text: Always overlay clusters H&E validate biological interpretation.Moran’s (SpatialExperiment::spatialCorrelation Seurat::FindSpatiallyVariableFeatures) finds spatially variable genes.Deconvolution essential Visium spots cover multiple cell types; use matched scRNA-seq reference.BayesSpace SpaGCN integrate spatial structure clustering, giving smoother domain boundaries.Stereo-seq MERFISH offer sub-cellular resolution; analysis pipelines differ slightly spot-based Visium.

heading: 13.462 Introduction
text: STAR (Spliced Transcripts Alignment Reference) de facto standard short-read RNA-seq aligner. designed specifically splice-aware alignment problem: reads mature mRNA cross exon-exon junctions, naive aligner requires contiguous matches misses split alignments misclassifies multi-mappers unaligned. STAR’s seed-extend algorithm splits reads candidate junctions, aligns segments reference genome, assembles spliced alignments — including across novel (unannotated) junctions.

heading: 13.463 Prerequisites
text: working understanding RNA-seq experimental design, difference mRNA DNA sequencing, basic familiarity sequence alignment concepts.

heading: 13.464 Theory
text: STAR’s two-pass mapping standard mode: first pass aligns read independently accumulates list candidate splice junctions; second pass adds junctions reference index re-aligns, producing accurate spliced alignments especially across novel junctions. output BAM file plus tab-separated splice-junction summary (SJ..tab).STAR also supports chimeric alignment fusion-gene detection (reads spanning two distinct genomic loci), expectation-maximisation-based multi-mapper resolution, stranded alignment library protocols preserve transcript orientation.

heading: 13.465 Assumptions
text: Short RNA-seq reads (typically 50–150 bp Illumina); reference genome (preferably) gene annotation GTF format; sufficient memory — STAR’s index human genome takes 30 GB RAM.

heading: 13.466 R Implementation
code:
library(Rsubread)

# Rsubread::align is Subjunc (splice-aware) or align
# index <- "subread_index"
# buildindex(basename = index, reference = "genome.fa")

# Splice-aware alignment
# align(index = index, readfile1 = "reads.fastq.gz",
#       output_file = "aligned.bam",
#       type = "rna", useAnnotation = TRUE,
#       annot.inbuilt = "hg38")

heading: 13.467 Output & Results
text: primary output BAM file containing spliced alignments SJ..tab file summarising splice junctions detected (including support count annotation status). typical RNA-seq experiments, 90–95 % reads align uniquely; much lower rate signals contamination, library-prep problems, annotation mismatch.

heading: 13.468 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “STAR (v2.7.10a) two-pass alignment GRCh38 GENCODE v44 annotation aligned 94 % reads uniquely; 2.3 million splice junctions detected, 98 % matched annotated junctions, suggesting limited novel splicing experimental conditions.” Always report genome assembly, annotation version, unique-mapping rate.

heading: 13.469 Practical Tips
text: STAR needs 30 GB RAM human genome index; smaller genomes (yeast, bacteria), runs comfortably laptop.Two-pass mode (twopassMode = "Basic") standard novel-junction detection; first-pass-mode faster less accurate.transcript-level quantification, pair STAR alignments RSEM, Salmon, Kallisto; BAM input abundance estimation.HISAT2 faster, lower-memory alternative aligner similar accuracy; use memory constrained.Pseudo-aligners (Salmon, Kallisto) skip BAM-producing alignment entirely; faster less suitable isoform-level alignments needed (variant calling, fusion detection).Use reference GTF annotation improve junction accuracy; novel-junction detection works without error-prone.

heading: 13.470 R Packages Used
text: Rsubread provides splice-aware alignment via subjunc feature counting via featureCounts; Rsamtools GenomicAlignments downstream BAM manipulation; tximport importing transcript-level abundance estimates Salmon, Kallisto, RSEM Bioconductor; STAR command-line tool output integrates standard R/Bioconductor RNA-seq workflows.

heading: 13.473 Introduction
text: Structural variants (SVs) – deletions, duplications, inversions, translocations, complex rearrangements – genomic events \(\geq\) 50 bp. often larger phenotypic impact single-nucleotide variants harder call involve breakpoints across long distances. Short-read callers like Manta Delly, plus long-read callers (Sniffles, pbsv), standard.

heading: 13.474 Prerequisites
text: BAM files high-coverage WGS; reference genome.

heading: 13.475 Theory
text:
Short-read SV callers combine three signals:
- Read pairs abnormal insert size orientation.
- Split reads whose two halves map distant loci.
- Read-depth anomalies (CNV overlap).Long-read callers directly observe breakpoint-spanning reads, dramatically improving resolution repeat-rich SVs.

heading: 13.476 Assumptions
text: Sequencing depth adequate (\(\geq\) 30x WGS); insert-size distribution approximately normal; reference matches sample ancestry.

heading: 13.477 R Implementation
text: Manta/Delly run command line producing VCF; R ingests annotates:Example command-line calls:
code:
library(VariantAnnotation)
library(StructuralVariantAnnotation)

# Use a VariantAnnotation-shipped VCF as a proxy
vcf_file <- system.file("extdata", "chr22.vcf.gz", package = "VariantAnnotation")
vcf <- readVcf(vcf_file, "hg19")
vcf_sv <- vcf[1:10, ]   # illustrative subset; treat as SV records

# breakpointRanges builds a GRanges of breakpoints from SV VCF INFO fields
gr <- breakpointRanges(vcf_sv)
length(gr)
head(as.data.frame(gr))manta configManta.py --bam sample.bam --reference ref.fa --runDir manta_out
delly call -g ref.fa -o sample.bcf sample.bam

heading: 13.478 Output & Results
text: VCF SV calls type (DEL, DUP, INV, INS, BND), coordinates, supporting-read counts; annotation genes yields per-gene SV impact.

heading: 13.479 Interpretation
text: “WGS SV calling identified 4,120 events (2,800 deletions, 1,100 duplications, 180 inversions, 40 translocations) per sample. germline 17q12 deletion spans RCAD concordant clinical suspicion.”

heading: 13.480 Practical Tips
text: Use two callers intersect (high-precision) union (high-sensitivity); single caller misses events.Filter breakpoint support; single-read evidence almost always false.Long-read WGS resolves complex rearrangements short reads ; use structural-disease phenotyping.Tumour SV calling requires matched normal remove germline events; somatic callers: Manta + DELLY (cancer mode), GRIDSS.Annotate breakpoints StructuralVariantAnnotation identify gene fusions breakpoint-disrupted regions.

heading: 13.483 Introduction
text: Surrogate variable analysis (SVA) estimates latent factors capture unmodelled heterogeneity high-dimensional expression data – unknown batch effects, sample-processing artefacts, unmeasured biology correlated technical variables. Including surrogate variables covariates downstream regression restores power controls false positives ComBat apply (batch variable unknown).

heading: 13.484 Prerequisites
text: Linear models expression data, batch effects, ComBat.

heading: 13.485 Theory
text: SVA decomposes residual expression matrix (regressing primary model) latent factors using bootstrap-based singular value decomposition. number surrogate variables \(k\) chosen permutation test (num.sv). surrogate added covariate downstream limma/edgeR model.RNA-seq count data, use svaseq, applies SVA log-transformed scale suitable negative-binomial outcomes.

heading: 13.486 Assumptions
text: primary variable interest modelled correctly; surrogate variables orthogonal ; residual variation reflects technical unmodelled factors.

heading: 13.487 R Implementation
code:
library(sva); library(limma)

set.seed(2026)
n_genes <- 3000; n_samples <- 20
group   <- factor(rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = 10))
batch   <- factor(rep(c("A", "B"), times = 10))           # hidden

y <- matrix(rnorm(n_genes * n_samples), n_genes, n_samples)
y <- y + matrix(rep(as.numeric(batch) * 0.6, each = n_genes),
                n_genes, n_samples)

mod  <- model.matrix(~ group)
mod0 <- model.matrix(~ 1, data = data.frame(group))

n_sv <- num.sv(y, mod, method = "be")
svobj <- sva(y, mod, mod0, n.sv = n_sv)

mod_sv <- cbind(mod, svobj$sv)
fit <- lmFit(y, mod_sv); fit <- eBayes(fit)
topTable(fit, coef = 2, n = 5)

heading: 13.488 Output & Results
text: svobj$sv contains \(n_sv\) surrogate variables per sample. Including typically recovers differential expression batch confounding otherwise hides.

heading: 13.489 Interpretation
text: “estimated \(k\) surrogate variables using SVA included limma model, adjusting unmodelled heterogeneity without needing specify batch variable.”

heading: 13.490 Practical Tips
text: Use svaseq RNA-seq count data.num.sv(..., method = "") Buja-Eyuboglu estimator conservative; "leek" aggressive.Surrogates correlate variable interest; sva orthogonalises construction.Always inspect surrogates correlate (library size, processing date) interpretation.batch known, prefer ComBat-seq direct covariate inclusion; SVA hidden factors.

heading: 13.493 Introduction
text: Pseudo-alignment tools (Salmon, Kallisto) EM-based quantifiers (RSEM) produce transcript-level (isoform-level) counts. differential-expression workflows operate gene level, transcript counts must aggregated genes via transcript--gene mapping derived annotation. tximport package handles aggregation cleanly produces output ready DESeq2, edgeR, limma-voom; related tximeta extends automatic metadata reference-genome tracking.

heading: 13.494 Prerequisites
text: working understanding transcript-level vs gene-level expression quantification, RNA-seq annotation files (GTF), difference counts, TPM, effective length.

heading: 13.495 Theory
text: simplest transcript--gene aggregation sums transcript counts within gene: \(\mathrm{gene\ count} = \sum_{\mathrm{transcripts \gene}} \mathrm{transcript\ count}\). tximport adds two important refinements:Length-scaled TPM (countsFromAbundance = "lengthScaledTPM"): combines transcript abundance length information produce gene-level counts respect isoform-resolution gene-level interpretation.Effective-length offsets: passes effective-length information downstream DE tools (DESeq2 tximeta::tximeta() integration) accurate normalisation.Direct transcript-level DE (without aggregation) supported tools like swish (fishpond) DRIMSeq differential transcript usage; aggregation right choice question gene-level expression.

heading: 13.496 Assumptions
text: transcript--gene map matches annotation used transcript quantification; sample-file ordering consistent metadata.
