# Example: salmon outputs
# files <- list.files("salmon_out", pattern = "quant.sf", recursive = TRUE, full.names = TRUE)
# tx2gene <- read.table("tx2gene.tsv", header = TRUE)

#                 countsFromAbundance = "lengthScaledTPM")

heading: 13.498 Output & Results
text: tximport() returns list counts (gene--sample matrix), abundance (gene-level TPM), length (effective lengths used downstream normalisation). matrix ready input DESeq2::DESeqDataSetFromTximport() starting point edgeR / limma workflows.

heading: 13.499 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “tximport aggregated 190{,}000 transcript-level Salmon estimates 22{,}000 gene-level counts using GENCODE v44 transcript--gene map; countsFromAbundance = 'lengthScaledTPM' produced gene counts DESeq2 analysed effective-length offsets passed via txi$length matrix.” Always document aggregation method tx--gene source.

heading: 13.500 Practical Tips
text: countsFromAbundance = "lengthScaledTPM" standard DE analysis; scales raw counts transcript length preserving count interpretation.Use tximeta instead raw tximport possible — automatically retrieves transcript--gene map Salmon index’s metadata, eliminating annotation-mismatch errors.differential transcript usage (DTU), use DRIMSeq DEXSeq transcript-level matrix; aggregation collapses isoform-level information.differential transcript expression (DTE), use swish (fishpond::swish) transcript-level matrix bootstrap inferential replicates Salmon.Verify sample order files matches rows metadata table; --one errors corrupt entire downstream analysis silently.large studies, tximport reads files sequentially; parallelise BiocParallel substantial speedups.

heading: 13.504 Introduction
text: Variant calling turns aligned reads list single-nucleotide variants (SNVs) small insertions/deletions (indels). GATK’s HaplotypeCaller de facto standard: locally reassembles regions evidence variation emits probabilistic genotype calls VCF format.

heading: 13.505 Prerequisites
text: Sequence alignment, BAM format, reference genome indexing.

heading: 13.506 Theory
text: HaplotypeCaller proceeds four stages: define active regions (loci potential variation), perform local de novo assembly haplotypes, align reads haplotypes via pair-HMM, genotype using likelihoods. cohorts, sample first emitted per-sample GVCF, joint-genotyped GenotypeGVCFs – preserves reference evidence scales linearly samples.Downstream filtering done variant-quality score recalibration (VQSR) whole-genome data hard filters smaller studies.

heading: 13.507 Assumptions
text: Reads duplicate-marked, base-quality-recalibrated (BQSR), aligned standard reference; ploidy specified.

heading: 13.508 R Implementation

library(VariantAnnotation)

# Sample VCF from the package

vcf                                      # summary (SNPs, indels, samples)

head(info(vcf)[, c("AF", "DP")])         # population AF, depth

heading: 13.514 Introduction
text: VCF (Variant Call Format) standard tab-delimited format representing genotype calls genomic positions. Whether data come whole-genome sequencing, exome sequencing, genotyping arrays, imputation, downstream analysis nearly always begins reading, filtering, subsetting VCF. VariantAnnotation Bioconductor package provides programmatic access R; bcftools command-line workhorse cohort-scale operations.

heading: 13.515 Prerequisites
text: working understanding VCF format basics (CHROM, POS, REF, ALT, FILTER, INFO, FORMAT, per-sample GT), difference phased unphased genotypes, typical variant-level QC metrics (QUAL, depth, missingness, allele frequency).

heading: 13.516 Theory
text: VCF row represents variant one genomic position; columns include INFO (variant-level annotations: allele frequency, depth, functional consequence) per-sample FORMAT fields (genotype GT, depth DP, genotype quality GQ). Standard filtering steps:PASS filter: keep variants flagged PASS calling pipeline; non-PASS variants typically calibration artefacts.Allele frequency: minimum minor-allele frequency (MAF) threshold retain common variants association testing.Depth missingness: minimum per-sample depth maximum site-level missingness.Hardy-Weinberg: large deviations unrelated controls indicate genotyping errors.Multi-allelic normalisation: split multi-allelic sites bi-allelic rows left-align indels (bcftools norm).

heading: 13.517 Assumptions
text: VCF bgzipped (.vcf.gz) tabix-indexed random access; reference build matches calling pipeline; sample identifiers consistent across cohorts merging.

heading: 13.518 R Implementation
code:
library(VariantAnnotation)

vcf_file <- system.file("extdata", "chr22.vcf.gz", package = "VariantAnnotation")

# Targeted reading (region + fields only; faster than full read)
rng <- GRanges("22", IRanges(50301422, width = 50000))
param <- ScanVcfParam(which = rng,
                      info = c("AF", "DP"),
                      geno = "GT")
vcf_sub <- readVcf(vcf_file, "hg19", param = param)
vcf_sub

# Filter by MAF
af <- unlist(info(vcf_sub)$AF)
af <- pmin(af, 1 - af)
vcf_common <- vcf_sub[af > 0.05, ]
length(vcf_common)

# Extract and reshape genotypes
gt <- geno(vcf_common)$GT
head(gt)

heading: 13.519 Output & Results
text: readVcf() returns VCF Bioconductor object structured slots variant ranges, INFO fields, FORMAT fields, sample information. Accessor functions (info(), geno(), rowRanges()) extract specific components, indexing (vcf[af > 0.05, ]) filters row.

heading: 13.520 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “filtering PASS variants MAF > 5 %, missingness < 5 %, Hardy-Weinberg \(p > 10^{-6}\) 1{,}532 unrelated controls, retained 412{,}380 high-quality common autosomal variants association testing.” Always describe filtering pipeline detail; cohort-comparison studies require precisely matched filtering.

heading: 13.521 Practical Tips
text: cohort-scale operations (millions variants, thousands samples), use bcftools command line; orders magnitude faster R-based reading writing.Use ScanVcfParam() read regions fields need; full WGS VCFs can tens GB reading entirely wasteful.Filter FILTER == "PASS" early; non-PASS variants accumulate downstream analyses removed.Normalise variants bcftools norm -m --f reference.fa merging cohorts; multi-allelic representations differ across callers produce silent merge errors.association studies, export PLINK BGEN formats via snpStats plink2 efficient large-scale regression.functional annotation (consequence prediction, conservation scores), use VariantAnnotation::predictCoding(), Ensembl VEP, SnpEff.

heading: 13.525 Introduction
text: volcano plot summarises differential-expression result single scatter: log fold change x-axis, \(-\log_{10}\) adjusted \(p\)-value y-axis. Genes upper-left upper-right corners — large effect high precision — targets interest; genes near bottom plot fail significance criterion regardless effect size, genes near zero x-axis fail biological-relevance criterion regardless significance. combined visual makes standard two-criterion ranking immediately apparent canonical figure differential-expression report.

heading: 13.526 Prerequisites
text: working understanding differential-expression analysis output (DESeq2, edgeR, limma-voom), log fold change, FDR-adjusted \(p\)-values.

heading: 13.527 Theory
text: volcano plot’s signature shape arises relationship effect-size estimates standard errors: extreme fold changes typically reported genes low expression (therefore noisier estimates worse \(p\)-values), “wings” high fold change low significance sparse. dense central column near zero fold change, \(p\)-values uniformly distributed high moderately significant, represents bulk unchanged genes.Standard cutoffs combine fold-change significance: \(|\log_2 \mathrm{FC}| > 1\) (two-fold change) adjusted \(p < 0.05\) jointly. Either criterion alone misleading — large effects poor precision (e.g. low-expression genes) small effects extreme significance (e.g. high-expression genes tiny reproducible changes) produce different scientific conclusions.

heading: 13.528 Assumptions
text: DE analysis correctly specified; \(p\)-values calibrated (DE methods generally produce well-calibrated \(p\)-values assumptions); fold-change sign interpretable directional effect.

heading: 13.529 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 5000
logFC <- c(rnorm(n - 100), rnorm(100, 2))
pval  <- c(runif(n - 100), 10^(-runif(100, 2, 6)))
padj  <- p.adjust(pval, "BH")

df <- data.frame(logFC = logFC, padj = padj)
df$cat <- with(df, ifelse(padj < 0.05 & abs(logFC) > 1,
                          ifelse(logFC > 0, "up", "down"), "ns"))

ggplot(df, aes(logFC, -log10(padj), colour = cat)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = 2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1, 1), linetype = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(up = "#E76F51",
                                  down = "#2A9D8F",
                                  ns = "grey70")) +
  labs(x = "log2 fold change", y = "-log10 adjusted p",
       title = "Volcano plot (DE results)") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 13.530 Output & Results
text: scatter - -regulated genes highlighted distinct colours dashed lines chosen FC FDR thresholds. visual immediately conveys many genes pass criteria, symmetry vs regulation, distribution effect sizes among significant hits.

heading: 13.531 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence (figure caption): “Volcano plot differential expression conditions B (n = 6 per group); 100 genes passed FDR < 0.05 \(|\log_2 \mathrm{FC}| > 1\) (45 , 55 ); five significant genes labelled. Dashed lines mark FDR fold-change thresholds.” Always state cutoffs sample sizes.

heading: 13.532 Practical Tips
text: Use EnhancedVolcano::EnhancedVolcano() publication-ready volcano plots automatic gene labelling, customisable thresholds, ggrepel-based label placement.Truncate extreme \(-\log_{10} p\) values (e.g. cap 50) readability; small \(p\)-values otherwise stretch y-axis compress bulk plot.Report raw FDR-adjusted \(p\)-value thresholds transparently figure caption.Add gene labels top-ranked hits biologically-curated genes interest; many labels obscure plot.Volcano plots show absolute expression magnitude; pair MA plots expression-dependent effects suspected.comparing multiple contrasts, use facets different colour schemes; overlaying multiple contrasts single volcano usually fails.

heading: 13.539 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 13.540 1. Goal
text:
Demonstrate hypergeometric ORA hand, sketch fgsea
call pattern running-sum GSEA.

heading: 13.541 2. Approach
text:
Simulate universe 5 000 genes; pathway 80 members 
enriched differentially expressed (DE) genes.
code:
pathway <- sample(seq_len(N), 80)
true_de <- unique(c(sample(pathway, 40), sample(setdiff(seq_len(N), pathway), 60)))

tibble(in_pathway = seq_len(N) %in% pathway,
       de = seq_len(N) %in% true_de) |>
  count(in_pathway, de) |>
  ggplot(aes(in_pathway, n, fill = de)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  labs(x = "gene in pathway?", y = "count")

heading: 13.542 3. Execution
text: Hypergeometric test hand.Make ranked gene list (DE) sketch fgsea.fgsea sketch.
code:
k_in <- sum(true_de %in% pathway)
p_hyper <- phyper(k_in - 1, 80, N - 80, n_de, lower.tail = FALSE)
c(overlap = k_in, expected = n_de * 80 / N, p = p_hyper)
stat[pathway] <- stat[pathway] + 1.2    # shift pathway genes up
names(stat) <- paste0("g", seq_len(N))
stat <- sort(stat, decreasing = TRUE)

# Running-sum demonstration by hand.
in_set <- names(stat) %in% paste0("g", pathway)
p_hit <- abs(stat) / sum(abs(stat[in_set]))
p_miss <- 1 / sum(!in_set)
run_sum <- cumsum(ifelse(in_set, p_hit, -p_miss))
ES <- run_sum[which.max(abs(run_sum))]
tibble(i = seq_along(run_sum), rs = run_sum) |>
  ggplot(aes(i, rs)) + geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey50") +
  labs(x = "rank", y = "running enrichment score")
library(fgsea)
gene_sets <- list(pathway = paste0("g", pathway))
fgsea_res <- fgsea(pathways = gene_sets, stats = stat,
                   minSize = 15, maxSize = 500)

code:

  k <- sum(short %in% paste0("g", pathway))
  phyper(k - 1, 80, N - 80, top, lower.tail = FALSE)
})

(contrasts, interactions, F-tests). large complex designs,

heading: 13.552 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(MASS)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 13.553 1. Goal
text:
Simulate small RNA-seq count matrix two conditions 
demonstrate DESeq2/edgeR pipeline pattern alongside base-R
negative-binomial GLM actually runs.

heading: 13.554 2. Approach
text:
Simulate 500 genes × 10 samples (5 per group). Ten genes truly
differentially expressed.
code:
group <- rep(c("A", "B"), each = 5)
mu <- 2 * runif(n_gene, 1, 10)   # baseline mean per gene
lfc <- c(rep(log(3), 10), rep(0, n_gene - 10))
counts <- sapply(seq_len(n_samp), function(j) {
  rate <- mu * exp(ifelse(group[j] == "B", lfc, 0))
  rnbinom(n_gene, mu = rate, size = 5)
})
rownames(counts) <- paste0("g", seq_len(n_gene))
colnames(counts) <- paste0("s", seq_len(n_samp))

tibble(mean = rowMeans(counts), var = apply(counts, 1, var)) |>
  ggplot(aes(mean, var)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  scale_x_log10() + scale_y_log10() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "grey50") +
  labs(title = "mean–variance (log–log)")

heading: 13.555 3. Execution
text: DESeq2 call pattern (run).edgeR call pattern (run).per-gene negative-binomial GLM runs.
code:
library(DESeq2)
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = counts,
  colData   = data.frame(group = group),
  design    = ~ group
)
dds <- DESeq(dds)
res <- results(dds, contrast = c("group", "B", "A"))
head(res[order(res$padj), ])
library(edgeR)
dge <- DGEList(counts = counts, group = group)
dge <- calcNormFactors(dge)
design <- model.matrix(~ group)
dge <- estimateDisp(dge, design)
fit <- glmQLFit(dge, design)
qlf <- glmQLFTest(fit, coef = 2)
topTags(qlf)
logfcs <- numeric(n_gene)
for (g in seq_len(n_gene)) {
  y <- counts[g, ]
  fit <- try(glm.nb(y ~ group), silent = TRUE)
  if (inherits(fit, "try-error")) { pvals[g] <- NA; next }
  s <- summary(fit)
  pvals[g]  <- s$coefficients[2, 4]
  logfcs[g] <- s$coefficients[2, 1]
}
padj <- p.adjust(pvals, method = "BH")

heading: 13.556 4. Check
text: Volcano plot truly DE genes flagged.
code:
tibble(lfc = logfcs, padj = padj, truth = truth) |>
  drop_na() |>
  ggplot(aes(lfc, -log10(padj), colour = truth)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(x = "log fold change (B vs A)", y = "-log10(padj)")
mean(padj[11:n_gene] < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE)

heading: 13.557 5. Report

simulated 500-gene, 10-sample RNA-seq dataset 10 truly
differentially expressed genes (log fold change ≈ 1.1),
per-gene negative-binomial GLMs followed Benjamini–Hochberg
adjustment recovered
round(mean(padj[1:10] < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE) * 100, 0)% 
true DE genes FDR < 0.05 yielded false-discovery rate
among null genes 
round(mean(padj[11:n_gene] < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE) * 100, 1)%.DESeq2 edgeR improve power shrinking gene-wise
dispersion across experiment; per-gene fit shown 
transparent low-power version idea.

heading: 13.565 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(uwot)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 13.566 1. Goal
text:
Walk scRNA-seq analysis conceptually small simulated
cell × gene matrix, ending 2-D embedding cluster labels.

heading: 13.567 2. Approach
text:
300 cells, 500 genes, three cell types partly overlapping
expression programmes.
code:
cell_type <- rep(c("T", "B", "myeloid"), each = n_cell / 3)
mu_base <- exp(rnorm(n_gene, 1, 1))
X <- matrix(0, n_cell, n_gene)
for (i in seq_len(n_cell)) {
  program <- rep(1, n_gene)
  if (cell_type[i] == "T")       program[1:20]  <- 5
  if (cell_type[i] == "B")       program[21:40] <- 5
  if (cell_type[i] == "myeloid") program[41:60] <- 5
  X[i, ] <- rnbinom(n_gene, mu = mu_base * program, size = 2)
}
tibble(lib = rowSums(X), ct = cell_type) |>
  ggplot(aes(ct, lib)) + geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "total counts per cell")

heading: 13.568 3. Execution
text: Log-normalise, select variable genes, PCA, UMAP, cluster.Seurat call pattern (run).
code:
gene_var <- apply(logX, 2, var)
keep <- order(gene_var, decreasing = TRUE)[1:100]
pcs <- prcomp(logX[, keep])$x[, 1:10]
emb <- umap(pcs, n_neighbors = 20, min_dist = 0.1)

# A simple k-means cluster on the PCs as a stand-in.
km <- kmeans(pcs, centers = 3, nstart = 10)
tibble(x = emb[, 1], y = emb[, 2],
       cluster = factor(km$cluster), truth = cell_type) |>
  ggplot(aes(x, y, colour = cluster, shape = truth)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8, size = 1) +
  labs(x = "UMAP1", y = "UMAP2")
library(Seurat)
obj <- CreateSeuratObject(counts = t(X))
obj <- NormalizeData(obj)
obj <- FindVariableFeatures(obj, nfeatures = 100)
obj <- ScaleData(obj)
obj <- RunPCA(obj, npcs = 10)
obj <- FindNeighbors(obj, dims = 1:10)
obj <- FindClusters(obj, resolution = 0.5)
obj <- RunUMAP(obj, dims = 1:10)
DimPlot(obj, label = TRUE)

UMAP, k-means) recovered three types high agreement.

underlies Seurat pipeline sketched accompanying code.real dataset critical choices normalisation
(SCTransform vs NormalizeData), number PCs (elbow 
JackStraw), clustering resolution. Perturb confirm

heading: 14.3 Introduction
text: Anomaly detection flags observations deviate typical patterns. used fraud detection, sensor fault monitoring, network intrusion, adverse-event surveillance. Unlike classification, labels anomalies often unavailable training time – methods unsupervised semi-supervised.

heading: 14.4 Prerequisites
text: Multivariate data; distance / density concepts.

heading: 14.5 Theory
text: Distance-based: kNN distance average distance neighbours; high = anomaly.Density-based: LOF (Local Outlier Factor) compares local density neighbours.One-class SVM: learns boundary enclosing training data; points outside anomalies.Isolation Forest: exploits anomalies easier isolate random trees (shorter path length terminal node).Autoencoders: train reconstruction network normal data; anomalies high reconstruction error.

heading: 14.6 Assumptions
text: Training data mostly “normal”; anomaly pattern differs quantifiable way normal.

heading: 14.7 R Implementation
code:
library(e1071)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
# Normal data is a ball; add some outliers
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = c(rnorm(n, 0, 1), runif(20, -5, 5)),
  x2 = c(rnorm(n, 0, 1), runif(20, -5, 5))
)
labels <- c(rep("normal", n), rep("anomaly", 20))

# One-class SVM
oc <- svm(df, type = "one-classification", nu = 0.1, kernel = "radial")
pred <- predict(oc, df)                # TRUE = inlier, FALSE = anomaly

table(truth = labels, flagged = ifelse(pred, "inlier", "anomaly"))

heading: 14.8 Output & Results
text: Confusion matrix one-class SVM ground truth; correctly flags 20 injected anomalies outliers.

heading: 14.9 Interpretation
text: “One-class SVM detected 17 20 injected anomalies (sensitivity 0.85) 6 % false-positive rate normal data. Isolation Forest gave similar results less tuning.”

heading: 14.10 Practical Tips
text: Start Isolation Forest; fast, robust, hyperparameters.nu one-class SVM approximates expected anomaly fraction.Autoencoders competitive complex high-dimensional anomalies (images, signals).Validate held-labelled anomaly set; unsupervised evaluation hard.Combine multiple detectors robustness; anomalies rare single-method false-positive rates compound.

heading: 14.13 Introduction
text: Bagging — bootstrap aggregation, introduced Breiman 1996 — trains \(B\) models \(B\) bootstrap samples training data averages predictions. averaging dramatically reduces variance high-variance, low-bias learners (deep decision trees, kNN small \(k\)) without increasing bias. Random forests bagged trees additional feature-randomisation step decorrelates individual trees amplifies variance reduction.

heading: 14.14 Prerequisites
text: working understanding bootstrap resampling, bias-variance trade-, decision trees canonical high-variance base learner.

heading: 14.15 Theory
text: \(B\) predictions \(\hat y_b\) variance \(\sigma^2\) pairwise correlation \(\rho\), variance mean \[\mathrm{Var}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{B} \sum_b \hat y_b\right) = \rho \sigma^2 + \frac{(1 - \rho) \sigma^2}{B}.\]\(B\) grows, second term vanishes; first term residual variance limited correlation. Bagging alone reduces variance via \((1-\rho)/B\) contribution; adding decorrelation (feature subsampling, random forest) lowers \(\rho\) produces variance reduction.--bag (OOB) observations — roughly 37 % rows selected bootstrap — provide free held-validation set: row, average predictions trees include training.

heading: 14.16 Assumptions
text: base learner benefits variance reduction; bagging effective high-variance learners (deep trees, kNN small \(k\), neural networks) essentially nothing stable learners like linear regression.

heading: 14.17 R Implementation
code:
library(ipred); library(rpart)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 400
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n),
  y  = factor(ifelse(plogis(1 * rnorm(n) + 0.5 * rnorm(n)^2) > runif(n),
                     "yes", "no"))
)

# Bagged trees (no feature subsampling -- contrast with random forest)
m <- bagging(y ~ ., data = df, nbagg = 100, coob = TRUE)
print(m)

heading: 14.18 Output & Results
text: ipred::bagging() returns fitted bagged ensemble OOB misclassification rate coob = TRUE. OOB error standard generalisation estimate; comparing single tree’s CV error quantifies variance reduction bagging.

heading: 14.19 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “100 bagged trees achieved OOB misclassification rate 0.21, improvement single tree’s CV error 0.28; switching random forest feature subsampling reduced OOB 0.18, illustrating decorrelation contributes additional variance reduction beyond plain bagging.” Always compare bagged ensembles single-base-learner baselines.

heading: 14.20 Practical Tips
text: Bagging primarily benefits high-variance learners (trees, kNN small \(k\)); linear models stable gain little.Use \(B = 100\) \(500\) bootstrap replicates; returns diminish quickly beyond , computation grows linearly.OOB error nearly unbiased moderate large \(n\) free compute alongside training; prefer cross-validation rapid iteration.Random forests (bagging + feature subsampling) almost always outperform plain bagged trees; reach ranger directly rather tuning ipred::bagging.classification, average class probabilities (soft voting) rather class labels (hard voting); soft voting consistently accurate.Bagging extends beyond trees: learner can bagged via ipred::bagging bf set custom training function.

heading: 14.21 R Packages Used
text: ipred::bagging() canonical bagging arbitrary base learners; ranger randomForest bagged trees feature subsampling (random forests); parsnip::bag_tree() tidymodels integration; mlr3pipelines::po("subsample") bagging within mlr3 pipelines.

heading: 14.24 Introduction
text: Every supervised-learning error decomposes structural bias (wrong model class fit truth), variance (sampling noise fit), irreducible noise. bias-variance tradeoff single important mental model picking rigid flexible estimator.

heading: 14.25 Prerequisites
text: Regression basics; expected value variance estimators.

heading: 14.26 Theory
text: squared loss target \(f(x) + \varepsilon\) \(\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0\), \(\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2\):\[\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \underbrace{(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x))^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2]}_{\text{Variance}} + \sigma^2.\]Complex models: low bias, high variance. Simple models: high bias, low variance. Regularisation, averaging (bagging), dropout explicitly shift along curve.

heading: 14.27 Assumptions
text: Squared loss iid samples; trained estimator random function training set.

heading: 14.28 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

true_f <- function(x) sin(x)
x_test <- seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = 100)
f_true <- true_f(x_test)

n_reps  <- 200; n_train <- 30

simulate_fit <- function(degree) {
  preds <- replicate(n_reps, {
    x_train <- runif(n_train, 0, 2 * pi)
    y_train <- true_f(x_train) + rnorm(n_train, 0, 0.3)
    mdl <- lm(y_train ~ poly(x_train, degree))
    predict(mdl, newdata = data.frame(x_train = x_test))
  })
  bias <- rowMeans(preds) - f_true
  variance <- apply(preds, 1, var)
  list(bias2 = mean(bias^2), variance = mean(variance))
}

sapply(c(1, 3, 7, 15), simulate_fit)

heading: 14.29 Output & Results
text: polynomial degree grows, bias^2 falls variance rises; sum (excess MSE beyond noise) minimised intermediate flexibility.

heading: 14.30 Interpretation
text: “Polynomial degree 3 balanced bias (0.12) variance (0.09), minimising excess MSE; degree 15 reduced bias 0.001 inflated variance 0.45, hurting generalisation.”

heading: 14.31 Practical Tips
text: Overfitting corresponds high variance (structure fits differently across training resamples).Underfitting corresponds high bias (structurally restrictive capture signal).Ensembling (bagging, random forests) reduces variance without increasing bias.Regularisation (ridge, lasso) increases bias reduces variance – tune CV.Deep learning’s “double descent” modern complication; classical curve best starting intuition.

heading: 14.34 Introduction
text: calibration plot (reliability diagram) plots predicted probability observed frequency. well-calibrated classifier’s curve lies diagonal: predicts 0.7, events occur 70 % time. Poor calibration means probabilities trusted decision thresholds risk communication.

heading: 14.35 Prerequisites
text: Classification; predicted probabilities; binning.

heading: 14.36 Theory
text: Bin predictions predicted probability; compute observed frequency bin. Plot bin midpoints vs observed frequencies. perfect classifier follows \(y = x\); curves indicate -confidence, curves indicate -confidence.Summary metrics: Brier score \(= \mathbb{E}[(p - y)^2]\), Expected Calibration Error (ECE) \(= \sum_k w_k |\text{acc}_k - \text{conf}_k|\).

heading: 14.37 Assumptions
text: Predictions valid probabilities \([0, 1]\); sufficient data per bin (10-20 bins \(n > 1000\)).

heading: 14.38 R Implementation
code:
library(probably); library(yardstick); library(dplyr); library(tibble); library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 1000
# Simulate mis-calibrated predictions
true_y <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.4)
raw_p  <- plogis(-1 + 2 * true_y + rnorm(n, 0, 1.5))
# Inflate confidence to produce over-confident predictions
p_overconf <- raw_p^0.6
p_overconf <- pmin(pmax(p_overconf, 0.02), 0.98)

df <- tibble(truth = factor(true_y, levels = c(1, 0)),
             pred  = p_overconf)

cal_df <- cal_plot_breaks(df, truth, pred, num_breaks = 10)

# Manual reliability data + plot
df$bin <- cut(df$pred, seq(0, 1, 0.1), include.lowest = TRUE)
reliability <- df %>%
  group_by(bin) %>%
  summarise(mean_pred = mean(pred),
            obs_freq  = mean(as.integer(truth == "1")),
            n = n())

ggplot(reliability, aes(mean_pred, obs_freq)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, colour = "#2A9D8F") +
  geom_line() +
  geom_abline(linetype = 2) +
  labs(x = "Predicted probability", y = "Observed frequency",
       title = "Reliability diagram (simulated over-confident model)") +
  theme_minimal()

mean((df$pred - as.integer(df$truth == "1"))^2)   # Brier score

heading: 14.39 Output & Results
text: reliability diagram (scatter bin-means) Brier score. simulated -confident model lies diagonal low predicted probabilities.

heading: 14.40 Interpretation
text: “classifier -predicts low probabilities -predicts high probabilities; Brier score 0.22 indicates moderate calibration error. Platt isotonic recalibration validation set flatten curve toward diagonal.”

heading: 14.41 Practical Tips
text: Tree ensembles (RF, XGBoost) usually mis-calibrated; apply Platt scaling isotonic regression validation set.Neural networks modern training (high-capacity, low label smoothing) often -confident; temperature scaling fixes .Use stratified bins reliable plots probabilities concentrate near 0 1.Report Brier score ECE alongside ROC AUC; AUC rank-based reveal calibration.clinical applications, calibration matters discrimination; always verify deployment-era data.

heading: 14.44 Introduction
text: CatBoost (Prokhorenkova et al., 2018) focuses principled handling categorical features via ordered target encoding – computing target statistics prior rows avoid target leakage. also uses symmetric (oblivious) trees: trees every split given depth uses feature threshold, yielding fast inference natural regularisation.

heading: 14.45 Prerequisites
text: Gradient boosting; categorical encoding basics.

heading: 14.46 Theory
text: Ordered target encoding: categorical feature, encoding row \(\) uses target statistics computed rows 1..-1 (random permutation). prevents target leakage present ordinary mean-target encoding.Ordered boosting: tree trained residuals computed using independent prefix data, reducing prediction-shift bias.Symmetric trees: fixed structure across layers; inference reduces look-tables, making CatBoost extremely fast inference time.

heading: 14.47 Assumptions
text: Categorical features correctly flagged; data reasonably uniform (temporal drift within training set).

heading: 14.54 Introduction
text: Class weighting addresses imbalanced classification scaling observation’s loss contribution inversely class frequency, giving minority class influence gradient split decision. achieves similar effects SMOTE-style oversampling without synthesising new data: simpler, deterministic, easier reproduce, natively supported classifiers (logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, neural networks). imbalanced problems, class weights right starting point reaching resampling.

heading: 14.55 Prerequisites
text: working understanding classification loss functions, accuracy paradox imbalanced classification, basic familiarity resampling alternatives (oversampling, undersampling, SMOTE).

heading: 14.56 Theory
text: weighted loss \[L = \sum_i w_{y_i} \cdot \ell\bigl(f(x_i), y_i\bigr),\]\(w_k\) weight class \(k\). Typical balanced weights use inverse class frequency: \(w_k = n / (K n_k)\) \(K\) number classes \(n_k\) count class \(k\). makes total weight per class equal, class contributes equally loss regardless sample size.tree-based methods, class weights enter split-impurity computation; gradient-boosted methods, enter gradient Hessian; neural networks, multiply per-example loss summing.

heading: 14.57 Assumptions
text: problem genuinely classification task imbalanced classes; imbalance reflects population (just artefact training-data collection transfer deployment).

heading: 14.58 R Implementation
code:
library(ranger)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 500
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n),
  y  = factor(sample(c("pos", "neg"), n, replace = TRUE,
                     prob = c(0.05, 0.95)))
)

idx <- sample(n, n * 0.7)
train <- df[idx, ]; test <- df[-idx, ]

rf_unw <- ranger(y ~ ., data = train, num.trees = 200,
                 classification = TRUE)
pred_unw <- predict(rf_unw, test)$predictions

# Inverse-frequency class weights
cw <- setNames(as.numeric(1 / prop.table(table(train$y))),
               names(table(train$y)))
rf_w <- ranger(y ~ ., data = train, num.trees = 200,
               classification = TRUE, class.weights = cw)
pred_w <- predict(rf_w, test)$predictions

table(truth = test$y, unweighted = pred_unw)
table(truth = test$y, weighted   = pred_w)

heading: 14.59 Output & Results
text: Two confusion matrices: unweighted random forest predicts almost exclusively majority class (rational behaviour given 95:5 imbalance accuracy-maximising training); weighted RF recovers minority-class examples cost additional false positives.

heading: 14.60 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Inverse-frequency class weighting 95:5 imbalanced dataset improved minority-class recall 0.12 0.56 modest decrease majority precision (0.97 0.93); weighted classifier useful screening applications missing minority cases costly false positives.” Always state weighting scheme trade-terms application’s cost structure.

heading: 14.61 Practical Tips
text: Always try class weights SMOTE-style oversampling; deterministic, faster, produce reproducible results.Tune weights cross-validation; inverse-frequency sensible default necessarily optimal cost structure every application.major classifiers support class weights natively: glmnet(weights = ...), ranger(class.weights = ...), xgboost(scale_pos_weight = ...), keras::compile(... loss_weights = ...).Weighting shifts decision boundary; inspect calibration plots ROC curves check probabilistic quality weighting.severely imbalanced problems (1 %), combine class weights threshold tuning validation set; default 0.5 cutoff rarely optimal heavy imbalance.problems cost errors asymmetric known, build cost-sensitive classifier directly (mlr3::msr("classif.costs")) rather tuning weights heuristically.

heading: 14.62 R Packages Used
text: ranger random forest native class weights; glmnet penalised logistic regression weights = ...; xgboost::xgboost(scale_pos_weight = ...) gradient-boosted trees; themis SMOTE-style alternatives within tidymodels; parsnip::set_args(class_weights = ...) class-weight support across multiple engines.

heading: 14.65 Introduction
text: DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering Applications Noise) groups points densely packed together flags isolated points noise. Unlike \(k\)-means require specifying number clusters, handles non-convex cluster shapes, produces outlier-detection signal side effect — every point flagged noise candidate anomaly. machine-learning workflows natural cluster discovery outlier flagging useful, DBSCAN right starting point.

heading: 14.66 Prerequisites
text: working understanding distance metrics, density-based reasoning, limitations partitioning algorithms (\(k\)-means) non-convex cluster shapes.

heading: 14.67 Theory
text: DBSCAN two hyperparameters: eps (neighbourhood radius) minPts (minimum number points required dense region). Three categories point emerge algorithm:Core point: least minPts neighbours within eps.Border point: within eps core point core point.Noise point: neither core border.Clusters formed maximally connected sets core points (associated border points). Choosing eps practical challenge; standard heuristic \(k\)-distance plot, shows distance \(k\)-th nearest neighbour point sorted ascending — knee curve marks sensible eps.

heading: 14.68 Assumptions
text: Cluster density roughly uniform; chosen eps matches natural scale data; Euclidean distance meaningful (another distance substituted).

heading: 14.69 R Implementation
code:
library(dbscan)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
x1 <- c(rnorm(n/2, -2, 0.3), rnorm(n/2, 2, 0.3))
x2 <- c(rnorm(n/2, -2, 0.3), rnorm(n/2, 2, 0.3))
outliers_x1 <- runif(10, -4, 4)
outliers_x2 <- runif(10, -4, 4)
df <- data.frame(x1 = c(x1, outliers_x1),
                 x2 = c(x2, outliers_x2))

# Choose eps via the k-distance plot knee
kNNdistplot(df, k = 5); abline(h = 0.5, lty = 2, col = "red")

db <- dbscan(df, eps = 0.5, minPts = 5)
table(cluster = db$cluster)

plot(df, col = db$cluster + 1, pch = 16, asp = 1,
     main = "DBSCAN clustering (cluster 0 = noise)",
     xlab = "x1", ylab = "x2")

heading: 14.70 Output & Results
text: dbscan() returns vector cluster labels 0 indicating noise. \(k\)-distance plot guides eps selection; cluster scatter shows densely-packed groups separated noise points.

heading: 14.71 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “DBSCAN eps = 0.5 minPts = 5 recovered two natural clusters injected simulation (96 100 cluster-1 points 98 100 cluster-2 points correctly assigned) flagged 10 10 injected outliers noise (cluster 0); remaining noise calls 6 boundary points local density genuinely lower.” Always state eps, minPts, noise count.

heading: 14.72 Practical Tips
text: Set eps via \(k\)-distance plot’s knee; sensitive hyperparameter poor choice fails algorithm completely.`minPts = 2 d$ (dimensions) sensible starting heuristic; inspect cluster counts adjust unreasonably many.varying-density clusters, switch HDBSCAN (dbscan::hdbscan); adapts density threshold per region robust.Use noise label (cluster == 0) built-outlier detector; anomaly-detection tasks, often simplest interpretable approach.Scale features clustering (scale() recipes::step_normalize()); DBSCAN’s Euclidean distance sensitive feature scales.high-dimensional data, distance concentration weakens DBSCAN; reduce dimensionality PCA UMAP first.

heading: 14.76 Introduction
text: \(k\)-means usually taught stand-alone unsupervised clustering method, ML pipelines also versatile feature-engineering primitive. Pre-clustering observations using cluster membership (distance--centroid) new features can substantially improve downstream supervised models, especially underlying signal non-linear clustering captures local structure linear models miss. \(k\)-means also workhorse vector quantisation, image segmentation, customer-segmentation tasks.

heading: 14.77 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Euclidean distances, centroids, within-cluster sum--squares objective, bias-variance trade-feature engineering.

heading: 14.78 Theory
text:
\(k\)-means minimises within-cluster sum squares (WCSS):\[\min_C \sum_{k=1}^K \sum_{\C_k} \|x_i - \mu_k\|^2.\]Lloyd’s algorithm alternates assignment (point nearest centroid) update (centroid mean assigned points) convergence. algorithm non-convex; multiple random starts needed find good local optimum. number clusters \(K\) hyperparameter chosen elbow, silhouette, gap statistic methods.feature engineering, two derived quantities \(k\)-means fit useful:
- Cluster membership (one-hot label): categorical feature indicating cluster observation belongs .
- Distance centroid: \(K\) continuous features often carry predictive information cluster label alone.

heading: 14.79 Assumptions
text: Clusters roughly spherical equal-sized (implicit Voronoi-cell geometry); features scaled comparable units; chosen \(K\) matches meaningful structure data.

heading: 14.80 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
df <- data.frame(x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n), x3 = rnorm(n))

# Scale for kmeans
df_s <- scale(df)

# Fit with K=4 and use cluster membership as a feature
km <- kmeans(df_s, centers = 4, nstart = 20)
df$cluster <- factor(km$cluster)

# Distance to each centroid also becomes a feature
dist_to_centroids <- as.matrix(dist(rbind(df_s, km$centers)))
dist_features <- dist_to_centroids[1:n, (n + 1):(n + 4)]
colnames(dist_features) <- paste0("dist_c", 1:4)

head(cbind(df, dist_features))
km$tot.withinss

heading: 14.81 Output & Results
text: augmented data frame contains cluster membership plus four distance--centroid features. model’s WCSS quantifies clustering tightness; lower values indicate compact clusters.

heading: 14.82 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “\(k\)-means \(K = 4\) standardised features reduced WCSS 900 (one cluster, equivalent total variance) 620 (four clusters); using cluster membership distance--centroid additional features downstream XGBoost classifier improved cross-validated RMSE 3 %.” Always state \(K\), scaling, number random starts.

heading: 14.83 Practical Tips
text: Scale features \(k\)-means; unscaled features different units dominate Euclidean distance bias partition.Use nstart = 20 higher escape local minima; \(k\)-means non-convex single start often produces sub-optimal solutions.\(k\)-means-style feature engineering works best clusters actually exist data; truly random data adds noise features can hurt downstream performance.Distance--centroid features often informative raw cluster label capture continuous geometry discrete label discards.high-dimensional data, reduce dimensionality PCA \(k\)-means; Euclidean distances concentrate clusters lose meaning high dimensions.categorical mixed data, \(k\)-means apply directly; use \(k\)-modes PAM Gower distance instead.

heading: 14.87 Introduction
text: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs; LeCun, 1989) replace fully-connected layers local, weight-sharing convolutional filters. shared weights encode translation invariance: feature detector (edge, texture) learned applies every spatial location. CNNs dominate image classification, segmentation, many signal-processing tasks.

heading: 14.88 Prerequisites
text: Feedforward networks; convolutions cross-correlations; image tensors.

heading: 14.89 Theory
text: Convolutional layer: set learned filters \(K_c\); filter slides across input, producing feature map. Output channel count equals number filters.Pooling: max average spatial window; reduces dimensionality increases receptive field.Typical architecture (classic): conv -> ReLU -> pool, repeated; flatten fully-connected classification. Modern architectures (ResNet, EfficientNet) add residual connections batch normalisation.

heading: 14.90 Assumptions
text: Inputs spatial structure (images, spectrograms, time series); sufficient training data pretrained backbone.

heading: 14.91 R Implementation
code:
library(torch)

torch_manual_seed(2026)

# Simulate a small batch of "images"
x <- torch_randn(4, 1, 28, 28)         # 4 images, 1 channel, 28x28
y <- torch_randint(0, 10, size = list(4), dtype = torch_long())

# Simple CNN: 2 conv layers + FC head
cnn <- nn_module(
  initialize = function() {
    self$conv1 <- nn_conv2d(1, 16, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1)
    self$conv2 <- nn_conv2d(16, 32, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1)
    self$pool  <- nn_max_pool2d(2)
    self$fc1   <- nn_linear(32 * 7 * 7, 64)
    self$fc2   <- nn_linear(64, 10)
  },
  forward = function(x) {
    x <- self$pool(nnf_relu(self$conv1(x)))
    x <- self$pool(nnf_relu(self$conv2(x)))
    x <- x$view(c(x$size(1), -1))
    nnf_relu(self$fc1(x)) %>% self$fc2()
  }
)
model <- cnn()
logits <- model(x)
logits$shape                # [4, 10]

heading: 14.92 Output & Results
text: Logits shape [batch, classes] ready cross-entropy loss training.

heading: 14.93 Interpretation
text: “two-layer CNN produced 10-class logits batch 28x28 inputs. real MNIST pipeline architecture reach ~99 % accuracy training.”

heading: 14.94 Practical Tips
text: Pre-trained CNN backbones (ResNet50, EfficientNet) fine-tuning outperform training scratch tasks < 1M images.Data augmentation (flip, rotate, crop) essential image classification; use torchvision::transform_*.Batch normalisation accelerates training stabilises gradients; include every conv layer.1-D signals (ECG, audio), use 1-D convolutions (nn_conv1d).Vision transformers (ViT) now rival exceed CNNs large datasets; CNNs remain preferred small--moderate data.

heading: 14.97 Introduction
text: Cross-validation primary tool estimating well predictive model generalise unseen data. -sample error – error fitted model data used train – almost always optimistic. Cross-validation repeatedly splits data training held-portions, fits model training portion, evaluates held-portion, averages held-performance estimate true generalisation error.

heading: 14.98 Prerequisites
text: reader understand concepts overfitting, bias variance, training vs. test-set distinction. Familiarity tidymodels helpful assumed.

heading: 14.99 Theory
text: model’s generalisation error expected loss new observation drawn population training data. training error loss training data . difference – optimism training error – grows model flexibility shrinks sample size.

heading: 14.99.1 k-fold cross-validation
text: data partitioned \(k\) approximately equal folds. fold \(j = 1, \ldots, k\), model fitted \(k-1\) folds evaluated held-\(j\)-th fold. \(k\) held-losses averaged. common choice \(k = 5\) \(k = 10\), balances bias (folds means training set noticeably smaller full data) variance (many folds means estimates correlated).

heading: 14.99.2 Leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV)
text: extreme case \(k = n\): fold exactly one observation. LOOCV low bias (training sets nearly full data) high variance high computational cost. right choice \(n\) small model fast fit.

heading: 14.99.3 Stratified cross-validation
text: classification class imbalance, folds constructed preserve class proportions. reduces variability estimated performance avoids folds zero minority-class examples.

heading: 14.99.4 Repeated k-fold
text: \(k\)-fold CV repeated different random partitions, results averaged. reduces Monte Carlo variability estimate without computational cost LOOCV.

heading: 14.99.5 Nested cross-validation
text: hyperparameters tuned CV, tuned performance estimate optimistically biased hyperparameters chosen maximise metric. Nested CV uses outer loop honest generalisation estimation inner loop tuning: outer folds never see data used choose hyperparameters.

heading: 14.100 Assumptions
text: CV general technique, assumes:splits representative data-generating process – training test folds come distribution.observations exchangeable (CV scheme respects dependence structure – see ).performance metric well-estimated average folds.time-series clustered data, standard CV leaks information folds overstates performance. Use time-series CV (expanding window) group-blocked CV (subject, site, etc.) cases.

heading: 14.101 R Implementation
text: performs stratified 10-fold CV multinomial logistic regression penguins dataset. fit_resamples() handles CV loop; collect_metrics() summarises fold-level performance.
code:
library(tidymodels)

data(penguins, package = "palmerpenguins")
penguins <- tidyr::drop_na(penguins)

set.seed(2026)
split <- initial_split(penguins, prop = 0.75, strata = species)
train <- training(split)
test  <- testing(split)

folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 10, strata = species)

rec <- recipe(species ~ bill_length_mm + bill_depth_mm +
              flipper_length_mm + body_mass_g, data = train) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

spec <- multinom_reg() |> set_engine("nnet")

wf <- workflow() |> add_recipe(rec) |> add_model(spec)

cv_results <- fit_resamples(wf, resamples = folds,
                            metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc))

collect_metrics(cv_results)

heading: 14.102 Output & Results
text: typical output:mean across folds point estimate; standard error Monte Carlo uncertainty across folds.

heading: 14.103 Interpretation
text: 10-fold cross-validated accuracy 0.981 (SE 0.008), ROC AUC 0.999 (SE < 0.001). estimates --sample performance penguin species classification task. held-test set still unused; reserved final, honest evaluation modelling decisions locked.

heading: 14.104 Practical Tips
text: Preprocessing (scaling, imputation, encoding) must go inside resampling loop prevent leakage. recipes workflow() correctly default.Report mean standard error across folds readers know confident estimate .hyperparameter tuning, use nested CV (train/validation/test split) – tuning CV report biased.time series, use rolling_origin() sliding_window() rsample; never use vfold_cv() serial data.Small datasets high-variance CV estimates – consider reporting 95% CI across folds using repeated CV.

heading: 14.107 Introduction
text: Decision trees partition feature space recursive binary splits, fitting constant prediction terminal region (leaf). Classification Regression Trees (CART) algorithm Breiman, Friedman, Olshen Stone (1984) dominant implementation. Trees interpretable (single fitted tree can read sequence -else rules), handle mixed numeric categorical data without preprocessing, require feature scaling, serve building blocks random forests gradient boosting. main weakness high variance — small data perturbations produce different trees — motivates ensemble methods aggregate many trees.

heading: 14.108 Prerequisites
text: working understanding classification regression problems, impurity measures (Gini, entropy, variance), bias-variance trade-model complexity.

heading: 14.109 Theory
text: node, CART searches features candidate split points, choosing split maximally reduces impurity:Regression: minimise within-node variance.Classification: minimise Gini \(1 - \sum_k p_k^2\) entropy \(-\sum_k p_k \log p_k\).Trees grow recursively leaves pure minimum-size constraint hit, pruned back using cost-complexity pruning. cost-complexity criterion \[R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha |T|,\]\(R(T)\) training error \(|T|\) number terminal leaves. pruning parameter \(\alpha\) chosen cross-validation; standard rule selects smallest tree within one CV-standard-error minimum.

heading: 14.110 Assumptions
text: Splits axis-aligned (single tree captures interactions combining splits different depths, limits ability model rotated oblique decision boundaries); observations independent.

heading: 14.111 R Implementation
code:
library(rpart); library(rpart.plot)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 400
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = runif(n), x2 = runif(n),
  y  = factor(ifelse(runif(n) < plogis(-2 + 3 * runif(n) + 2 * runif(n)),
                     "pos", "neg"))
)

tree <- rpart(y ~ x1 + x2, data = df,
              method = "class",
              control = rpart.control(cp = 0.001))

printcp(tree)                  # CV error vs complexity
tree_pruned <- prune(tree,
                     cp = tree$cptable[which.min(tree$cptable[, "xerror"]), "CP"])
rpart.plot(tree_pruned, box.palette = c("#F4A261", "#2A9D8F"))

heading: 14.112 Output & Results
text: rpart() returns fitted tree object; printcp() reports cross-validation error candidate complexity parameter; prune() produces cost-complexity-pruned tree chosen \(\alpha\). rpart.plot() visualises tree split conditions leaf class proportions.

heading: 14.113 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “CART cost-complexity pruning CV-optimal \(\alpha\) retained 5 leaves; dominant split \(x_1\) 0.52, partitioning 78 % positive cases one branch 80 % negatives ; sub-splits \(x_2\) refined predictions within branch.” Always state pruning method resulting tree size.

heading: 14.114 Practical Tips
text: Always prune using xerror column CP table; unpruned trees -fit severely.single tree high variance; predictive accuracy, prefer random forests, gradient boosting, extra trees.Trees handle missing data via surrogate splits (rpart’s default); one methods without explicit imputation.Visualise rpart.plot() ggplot-friendly output; partykit::.party() provides richer object print plot methods.regression, use method = "anova"; classification, method = "class"; survival, method = "exp" rpart package built-support.interpretability better accuracy, use small ensemble (e.g., 50 trees random forest) aggregate variable-importance measures across trees.

heading: 14.118 Introduction

heading: 14.120 Theory

Acts like training ensemble \(2^n\) sub-networks shared weights.Early stopping: train training set, monitor validation loss; halt validation loss stops improving “patience” window. Implicitly regularises – final model parameters closer initialisation full convergence reach.

heading: 14.128 Introduction
text: Extremely Randomised Trees — usually shortened “Extra Trees” — variant random forests introduced Geurts, Ernst, Wehenkel 2006. defining innovation: instead optimising split threshold node, Extra Trees draws threshold random feature’s range. additional source randomness increases bias slightly reduces variance , often producing models accuracy competitive random forests fraction training time.

heading: 14.129 Prerequisites
text: working understanding random forests, decision-tree splitting criteria (Gini, variance), bias-variance trade-ensemble methods.

heading: 14.130 Theory
text: node, Extra Trees:Choose \(m\) random features (random forest).chosen feature, draw random threshold uniformly observed range — optimal split.Split whichever (feature, random-threshold) pair gives lowest impurity among candidates.Trees typically fit entire training data without bootstrap sampling (replace = FALSE); variance reduction comes random thresholds instead bootstrap. combination feature subsampling threshold randomisation produces highly decorrelated trees, averaging many produces low-variance ensemble.

heading: 14.131 Assumptions
text: Feature importance approximately diffuse — random thresholds work poorly dominant informative features need precise split points; settings, optimising thresholds (random forest) outperforms.

heading: 14.132 R Implementation
code:
library(ranger)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 500
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n), x3 = rnorm(n), x4 = rnorm(n),
  y  = sin(2 * rnorm(n)) + 0.3 * rnorm(n)
)

# ranger implements extra-trees via `splitrule = "extratrees"`
rf <- ranger(y ~ ., data = df,
             num.trees = 500, mtry = 2,
             splitrule = "variance")

et <- ranger(y ~ ., data = df,
             num.trees = 500, mtry = 2,
             splitrule = "extratrees",
             num.random.splits = 5)

c(rf_oob_rmse = sqrt(rf$prediction.error),
  et_oob_rmse = sqrt(et$prediction.error))

heading: 14.133 Output & Results
text: OOB-based RMSE random forest Extra Trees data. Extra Trees typically produces slightly lower RMSE noisy datasets variance reduction dominates; random forest typically wins structured signals threshold optimisation captures sharper boundaries.

heading: 14.134 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Extra Trees num.random.splits = 5 produced marginally lower OOB RMSE (0.38) random forest (0.41) noisy regression task; variance reduction random thresholds outweighed small bias cost. Training approximately 3 × faster random forest.” Always state number random splits compare random forest baseline.

heading: 14.139 Introduction
text: Feature engineering – creating informative inputs – often largest driver predictive performance. Even deep learning benefits domain-informed features. Standard techniques: log/sqrt transforms, interactions, polynomial bases, categorical encodings, date features, lag features time series.

heading: 14.140 Prerequisites
text: Regression / classification basics; understanding target’s scale distribution.

heading: 14.141 Theory
text:
Key transformations:
- Log / sqrt stabilise right-skewed variables.
- Box-Cox / Yeo-Johnson learn optimal power transform.
- Polynomial spline bases expose non-linear relationships linear models.
- Interactions (\(x_1 x_2\)) surface effects whose direction depends another variable.
- Target encoding high-cardinality categoricals: replace category smoothed target mean.
- Lag / rolling features time series.

heading: 14.142 Assumptions
text: Transformations preserve monotonicity needed; target encoding uses --fold means avoid leakage.

heading: 14.143 R Implementation
code:
library(recipes); library(embed)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
df <- tibble::tibble(
  x1 = exp(rnorm(n)),                    # right-skewed
  x2 = runif(n, 0, 10),
  cat = sample(letters[1:20], n, replace = TRUE),
  date = as.Date("2024-01-01") + sample(0:364, n, replace = TRUE),
  y = runif(n)
)

rec <- recipe(y ~ ., data = df) %>%
  step_log(x1) %>%
  step_interact(~ x1:x2) %>%
  step_ns(x2, deg_free = 4) %>%
  step_date(date, features = c("dow", "month")) %>%
  step_holiday(date) %>%
  step_rm(date) %>%
  step_lencode_mixed(cat, outcome = vars(y))   # mixed-effects target encoding

baked <- prep(rec) %>% bake(new_data = NULL)
colnames(baked)[1:10]

heading: 14.144 Output & Results
text: transformed data frame log-x1, interaction term, natural spline basis x2, day--week / month features, smoothly target-encoded category.

heading: 14.145 Interpretation
text: “Feature engineering added 12 derived columns: log-transformed skew, spline bases, date features, target-encoded category. pipeline reproducible applied identically train test.”

heading: 14.146 Practical Tips
text: feature engineering inside recipe (equivalent) avoid leakage.Log-transform right-skewed variables linear models; trees handle skew fine.High-cardinality categoricals benefit hugely target encoding (proper smoothing / CV handling).Date features (day--week, hour, month, holiday) often critical temporal prediction.Don’t -engineer – features means noise; cross-validate additions.

heading: 14.149 Introduction
text: Feature selection retains predictors relevant target discarding rest. benefits simpler models, reduced -fitting, faster inference, improved interpretability, reduced annotation cost applications. Three families methods coexist: filter (univariate tests, cheap fast), wrapper (fit--evaluate, slow thorough), embedded (selection happens fitting, balancing speed quality). Modern ML pipelines almost always use embedded methods (lasso, tree importance, Boruta) integrate naturally cross-validation.

heading: 14.150 Prerequisites
text: working understanding regression classification basics, regularisation (L1/lasso), bias-variance trade-.

heading: 14.151 Theory
text: Filter methods rank features univariate statistics — Pearson Spearman correlation, mutual information, chi-squared, ANOVA F-statistic — select top \(k\). Fast ignore feature interactions joint information.Wrapper methods treat model black box, fitting evaluating across feature subsets. Forward selection adds features one time; backward elimination starts features removes ; recursive feature elimination (RFE) cycles cross-validation. Slow capable finding complementary feature combinations.Embedded methods integrate selection fitting. Lasso drives coefficients exactly zero (selection); tree-based methods (random forest, XGBoost) provide importance scores; Boruta compares feature random permuted shadows many resamples test genuinely predictive features. Stability selection runs lasso many subsamples ranks features selection frequency.

heading: 14.152 Assumptions
text: Selected features generalise training deployment data; selection done within CV folds prevent leakage test-fold information chosen feature set.

heading: 14.153 R Implementation
code:
library(Boruta); library(glmnet)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300; p <- 20
X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p)
colnames(X) <- paste0("x", 1:p)
y <- X %*% c(rep(2, 5), rep(0, 15)) + rnorm(n)

# Embedded: Lasso selects non-zero features
lasso <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = 1)
nonzero_lasso <- rownames(coef(lasso, s = "lambda.min"))[
  which(as.vector(coef(lasso, s = "lambda.min")) != 0)
][-1]
cat("Lasso selected:", length(nonzero_lasso), "\n")

# Wrapper-ish: Boruta compares features to shadow permutations
bor <- Boruta(X, y, maxRuns = 50, doTrace = 0)
confirmed <- getSelectedAttributes(bor, withTentative = FALSE)
cat("Boruta confirmed:", length(confirmed), "\n")

heading: 14.154 Output & Results
text: Lasso retains features whose coefficients survive L1 penalty cross-validation-optimal \(\lambda\). Boruta confirms rejects features based whether importance exceeds randomly permuted shadow features across many resampling runs. sparse-signal dataset (5 true features, 15 noise), methods typically recover 4–5 true features near-zero false positives.

heading: 14.155 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Lasso (cross-validated \(\lambda = 0.08\)) Boruta (50 runs, BH-adjusted importance shadow features) independently identified 5 predictive features matching data-generating model, false positives either; correlation-based filter selection (top 5 absolute correlation \(y\)) missed one feature marginal correlation masked noise collinear predictors.” Always describe selection method cross-validation embedding.

heading: 14.156 Practical Tips
text: Perform feature selection inside cross-validation folds; selection full dataset CV leaks information test folds chosen feature set.Lasso fast principled linear near-linear relationships; random forest importance handles non-linear effects better.Boruta’s multiple-run shadow-feature comparison statistically cleaner single-pass importance provides confidence statements selection.\(p > n\) regimes, lasso (elastic net) nearly mandatory; random forest importance alone becomes unreliable high-dimensional settings.Stability selection (stabs::stabsel) fits many lassos data subsamples ranks features selection frequency; robust alternative single CV run unstable.Pre-screen cheap filter methods (variance, correlation) \(p\) millions; reduces candidate set applying expensive wrapper embedded methods.

heading: 14.160 Introduction
text: torch R native R port PyTorch: automatic-differentiation library GPU support, comprehensive layer library, modern optimisers (Adam, AdamW). enables building arbitrary neural-network architectures scratch training APIs Python users know. companion package luz wraps torch scikit-learn-style fit/predict interface, abstracting away manual training loop routine cases.

heading: 14.161 Prerequisites
text: working understanding neural networks (forward propagation, backpropagation), gradient descent, chain rule, basic mini-batch training concepts.

heading: 14.162 Theory
text: torch model nn_module — composition parameterised layers forward-pass method. Training iterates epochs mini-batches:Forward pass: compute predictions loss mini-batch.Backward pass: compute gradients via reverse-mode automatic differentiation.Optimiser step: update parameters using gradient (Adam adjusts step sizes per parameter).Modern training loops add learning-rate scheduling (cosine annealing, warmup), gradient clipping, weight decay, early stopping. luz packages patterns named callbacks.

heading: 14.163 Assumptions
text: Sufficient data chosen model capacity (overparameterised models tiny datasets memorise rather generalise); features standardised input; mini-batch size appropriate available memory bias-variance trade-.

heading: 14.164 R Implementation
code:
library(torch)

set.seed(2026)
torch_manual_seed(2026)

# Simulate a simple regression task
n <- 1000
x <- torch_randn(n, 3)
y <- (x[, 1] * 2 + x[, 2]^2 - x[, 3] * 0.5)$unsqueeze(2)

net <- nn_module(
  initialize = function() {
    self$fc1 <- nn_linear(3, 16)
    self$fc2 <- nn_linear(16, 16)
    self$fc3 <- nn_linear(16, 1)
  },
  forward = function(x) {
    x %>% self$fc1() %>% nnf_relu() %>%
          self$fc2() %>% nnf_relu() %>%
          self$fc3()
  }
)

model <- net()
opt   <- optim_adam(model$parameters, lr = 1e-2)

for (epoch in 1:50) {
  opt$zero_grad()
  pred <- model(x)
  loss <- nnf_mse_loss(pred, y)
  loss$backward()
  opt$step()
  if (epoch %% 10 == 0)
    cat(sprintf("epoch %d  loss %.4f\n", epoch, loss$item()))
}

heading: 14.165 Output & Results
text: Per-epoch MSE loss decrease monotonically network learns non-linear regression. 3-layer MLP ReLU activations rapidly fits simulated quadratic--\(x_2\) structure linear regression capture.

heading: 14.166 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “3-layer feedforward network (16-16-1 hidden units, ReLU activations, Adam optimiser, learning rate 0.01) trained 50 epochs reduced MSE 3.1 0.04 simulated regression quadratic interactions; predictions closely tracked noisy targets, residuals consistent irreducible simulation noise level.” Always state architecture, optimiser, learning rate, training epochs.

heading: 14.171 Introduction
text: Gradient boosting, formalised Friedman 2001, builds additive ensemble \(F_M(x) = \sum_{m=1}^M \nu h_m(x)\) fitting weak learner \(h_m\) negative gradient loss current predictions. Decision trees canonical weak learner, learning rate \(\nu\) shrinks tree’s contribution control regularisation, resulting framework dominates structured tabular-data benchmarks. Modern implementations (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) add second-order information, regularisation, missing-value handling, improvements original Friedman gradient boosting.

heading: 14.172 Prerequisites
text: working understanding decision trees, gradient descent, bias-variance trade-ensemble methods.

heading: 14.173 Theory
text:
generic gradient-boosting algorithm:Initialise \(F_0(x) = \arg\min_\gamma \sum_i L(y_i, \gamma)\) — constant prediction minimising loss.\(m = 1, \dots, M\):
Compute pseudo-residuals \(r_{im} = -\partial L(y_i, F(x_i)) / \partial F(x_i) |_{F = F_{m-1}}\).
Fit weak learner \(h_m\) pseudo-residuals.
Update \(F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu h_m(x)\).
Compute pseudo-residuals \(r_{im} = -\partial L(y_i, F(x_i)) / \partial F(x_i) |_{F = F_{m-1}}\).Fit weak learner \(h_m\) pseudo-residuals.Update \(F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu h_m(x)\).squared-error loss pseudo-residuals ordinary residuals; binary log-loss score residuals; Huber loss combine two regimes. learning rate \(\nu\) trades fit quality -fit risk: smaller \(\nu\) requires trees generalises better.

heading: 14.174 Assumptions
text: chosen weak learner appropriate loss (shallow trees depth 3–6 losses); learning rate small enough stable optimisation; sufficient sample size boosting interactions supported data.

heading: 14.175 R Implementation
code:
library(gbm)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 500
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n), x3 = rnorm(n),
  y  = sin(rnorm(n)) + 0.5 * rnorm(n)^2
)

m <- gbm(y ~ ., data = df,
         distribution = "gaussian",
         n.trees = 2000,
         interaction.depth = 3,
         shrinkage = 0.01,
         cv.folds = 5,
         verbose = FALSE)

best_iter <- gbm.perf(m, method = "cv", plot.it = FALSE)
cat("Best iter:", best_iter, "\n")
summary(m, n.trees = best_iter, plotit = FALSE)

heading: 14.176 Output & Results
text: gbm() cv.folds returns cross-validated boosting iteration count along variable-importance summary. gbm.perf() finds iteration minimises CV error; predictions iteration count typically used deployment.

heading: 14.177 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Gradient boosting shrinkage = 0.01, interaction.depth = 3, 5-fold cross-validated early stopping converged 1{,}240 trees (CV RMSE 0.34); variable-importance scores attributed 52 % relative influence \(x_1\), 28 % \(x_2\), 20 % \(x_3\), consistent simulated data structure.” Always state shrinkage, depth, CV-optimal iteration count, importance scores.

heading: 14.178 Practical Tips
text: Smaller learning rate (0.01–0.05) many trees almost always generalises better larger rate trees; canonical regularisation trade-boosting.Use shallow trees (depth 3–6) standard weak learners; deeper trees approach single-tree model lose boosting benefit.cv.folds = 5 gbm() produces early-stopping estimate without separate plumbing; gbm.perf(method = "cv") finds optimal iteration count.structured prediction problems, prefer XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost base gbm; add regularisation, missing-value handling, GPU support, substantially faster training.Monotone constraints (supported XGBoost LightGBM) embed domain knowledge boosting; useful regulated industries (credit, insurance, healthcare).Pair gradient boosting feature-importance partial-dependence plots interpretation; raw boosted trees otherwise opaque.

heading: 14.179 R Packages Used

heading: 14.182 Introduction
text: Isolation Forest (Liu, Ting, Zhou, 2008) tree-based unsupervised anomaly-detection method built single intuition: outliers easier isolate random splits densely packed normal points. algorithm builds many random trees, partitions data random feature--split-value choices, uses observation’s average path length terminal node anomaly score. Short paths mean easy isolation, means point anomalous; long paths mean point sits dense regions take many splits isolate.

heading: 14.183 Prerequisites
text: working understanding decision trees, random partitioning, basic anomaly-detection concepts.

heading: 14.184 Theory
text: Build \(T\) isolation trees: node, pick random feature random split value uniformly \([\min, \max]\) feature. Tree depth capped \(\log_2 n\) computational efficiency. observation \(x\), anomaly score \[s(x) = 2^{-\mathbb E[h(x)] / c(n)},\]\(h(x)\) average path length across trees \(c(n)\) normalising constant equal average path length unsuccessful BST search \(n\) points. Scores close 1 indicate clear anomalies; scores around 0.5 indicate normal points; scores well 0.5 deep dense regions.

heading: 14.185 Assumptions
text: Anomalies distinct dense normal points; subsampling size sufficient isolate (\(\psi = 256\) canonical default works well practice).

heading: 14.186 R Implementation
code:
library(solitude)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = c(rnorm(n), runif(20, -5, 5)),
  x2 = c(rnorm(n), runif(20, -5, 5))
)

iforest <- isolationForest$new(num_trees = 200)
iforest$fit(df)

scores <- iforest$predict(df)$anomaly_score
flagged <- scores > quantile(scores, 0.9)

plot(df, col = ifelse(flagged, "red", "grey60"),
     pch = 16, asp = 1,
     main = "Isolation Forest: top-10% scored observations flagged",
     xlab = "x1", ylab = "x2")

heading: 14.187 Output & Results
text: isolationForest$predict() returns tibble anomaly scores per observation. Thresholding chosen quantile (e.g., 90th percentile) flags candidates; scatter shows flagged points red normal points grey.

heading: 14.188 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Isolation Forest 200 trees default subsampling scored 14 20 injected anomalies (sensitivity 0.70) 90th-percentile threshold, 16 false positives among 300 normal points (false-positive rate 5.3 %); score distribution showed clear bimodal structure separating anomalies normal points.” Always state number trees, threshold, per-class accuracy.

heading: 14.189 Practical Tips
text: Tune contamination threshold (top-\(k\) percentile) match expected anomaly rate; 5 % threshold typical fraud detection, 1 % rare events.num_trees = 100-200 usually sufficient; returns diminish beyond 200 computational cost grows linearly.Isolation Forest handles high-dimensional data well rely distance computations; one main advantages distance-based methods (kNN-distance, LOF).Excellent default choice anomaly detection; requires feature scaling hyperparameters tune.improved separation non-axis-aligned anomaly structures, Extended Isolation Forest adds random oblique splits; available via isotree package.Pair anomaly scores domain expertise; flagged observations investigated, automatically removed actioned.

heading: 14.190 R Packages Used
text: solitude canonical R6-based isolation forest interface; isotree Extended Isolation Forest oblique splits additional anomaly-detection variants; dbscan::lof() Local Outlier Factor distance-based alternative; mlr3 tidymodels cross-validated anomaly detection labelled data.

heading: 14.193 Introduction
text: Isotonic regression fits monotone non-decreasing step function classifier scores observed probabilities. non-parametric counterpart Platt scaling: flexible (sigmoid assumption required) requiring validation data prone -fitting. standard post-hoc calibration method tree-ensemble classifiers (random forest, gradient boosting), whose mis-calibration patterns often non-sigmoidal.

heading: 14.194 Prerequisites
text: working understanding calibration plots, pool-adjacent-violators (PAV) algorithm, Platt scaling parametric alternative.

heading: 14.195 Theory
text: Given sorted score-label pairs \((f_i, y_i)\), isotonic regression finds non-decreasing step function \(\hat g\) minimising \(\sum_i (y_i - \hat g(f_i))^2\). pool-adjacent-violators algorithm solves \(O(n)\) time: walks sorted scores, pooling adjacent observations single steps whenever monotonicity violated, result non-decreasing.fitted step function maps future score observed probability rate within matching score range validation set. non-parametric flexibility allows fitting monotone calibration curve, cost statistical efficiency relative Platt’s two-parameter sigmoid.

heading: 14.196 Assumptions
text: mis-calibration monotone (almost always case well-trained classifiers); enough validation data estimate step function without -fitting (rule thumb: least 500 examples).

heading: 14.197 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
n <- 1000
y <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.3)
f <- 2 * y + rnorm(n, 0, 1.2)

val_idx <- 1:500
te_idx  <- 501:1000

# Isotonic regression on validation; apply to test
iso <- isoreg(f[val_idx], y[val_idx])
iso_fn <- stepfun(sort(f[val_idx]), c(0, iso$yf))
iso_p  <- iso_fn(f[te_idx])
iso_p  <- pmin(pmax(iso_p, 0), 1)

naive_p <- plogis(f[te_idx])
platt   <- glm(y[val_idx] ~ f[val_idx], family = "binomial")
platt_p <- plogis(coef(platt)[1] + coef(platt)[2] * f[te_idx])

c(naive = mean((naive_p - y[te_idx])^2),
  platt = mean((platt_p - y[te_idx])^2),
  isotonic = mean((iso_p  - y[te_idx])^2))

heading: 14.198 Output & Results
text: Brier scores three methods: naive sigmoid (calibration), Platt scaling (parametric sigmoid), isotonic regression (non-parametric step function). Isotonic typically wins underlying calibration curve non-sigmoidal.

heading: 14.199 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Isotonic regression held-test set produced lowest Brier score (0.168) compared Platt scaling (0.181) naive sigmoid (0.213); isotonic captured non-sigmoidal calibration curve parametric sigmoid . ROC AUC unchanged across three methods rescaling preserves rank.” Always compare calibration methods report Brier score plus reliability diagram.

heading: 14.200 Practical Tips
text: Isotonic regression needs validation data Platt scaling (rule thumb: least 500 examples; imbalanced data, minority class).Prone -fit validation set small; step function can track validation-specific noise, especially tails. Cross-validate calibration step small validation sets.tree-ensemble classifiers (random forest, GBM, XGBoost), isotonic regression usually preferred Platt; mis-calibration rarely sigmoidal.SVMs, Platt scaling usually sufficient; SVM decision-value distribution closer logistic.Step-function output produces piecewise-constant probabilities, can look strange sensitive applications; spline-smoothed isotonic exists rarely implemented production.Always pair calibration reliability diagram (probably::cal_plot, gbm::calibrate.plot) showing pre- post-calibration curves; verify visually calibration improved.

heading: 14.201 R Packages Used
text: Base R isoreg() canonical PAV-based isotonic regression; caret::calibrate() probably::cal_estimate_isotonic() integrated tidymodels-style calibration; betacal parametric Beta calibration alternative; mlr3calibration calibration extensions mlr3.

heading: 14.204 Introduction
text: \(k\)-nearest-neighbours classification assigns test observation majority class among \(k\) nearest training points feature space. simplest instance-based learning method: training step beyond storing data, prediction search--vote operation. kNN competitive low-dimensional, well-separated data serves useful baseline complex methods compared. Performance depends critically distance metric \(k\), require attention.

heading: 14.205 Prerequisites
text: working understanding distance metrics (Euclidean, Manhattan, Mahalanobis), train-test splits, bias-variance trade-supervised learning.

heading: 14.206 Theory
text: query point \(x^*\) training set \(\{(x_i, y_i)\}\), prediction mode labels \(k\) nearest neighbours: \(\hat y(x^*) = \mathrm{mode}\{y_i : x_i \N_k(x^*)\}\). Distance-weighted variants weight votes inversely distance, giving closer neighbours influence often improving boundary smoothness.bias-variance trade-\(k\) direct: small \(k\) produces high-variance, low-bias decisions (prediction depends neighbours adapts local noise); large \(k\) produces high-bias, low-variance decisions (prediction averages larger neighbourhood misses local structure). Cross-validation chooses optimal \(k\).choice distance metric matters: Euclidean default, Manhattan robust outliers, Mahalanobis accounts feature covariance, Gower distance handles mixed numeric-categorical inputs.

heading: 14.207 Assumptions
text: Features appropriately scaled (distances scale-sensitive); labels sufficiently clean local majority voting informative; chosen distance metric reflects meaningful similarity.

heading: 14.208 R Implementation
code:
library(class)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 400
x1 <- rnorm(n); x2 <- rnorm(n)
y <- factor(ifelse(x1^2 + x2^2 < 1, "in", "out"))
df <- data.frame(x1 = scale(x1)[, 1], x2 = scale(x2)[, 1], y = y)

idx <- sample(n, n * 0.7)
train <- df[idx, ]; test <- df[-idx, ]

# kNN at different k
accs <- sapply(c(1, 5, 15, 51), function(k) {
  pred <- knn(train[, 1:2], test[, 1:2], train$y, k = k)
  mean(pred == test$y)
})
setNames(accs, paste0("k=", c(1, 5, 15, 51)))

heading: 14.209 Output & Results
text: Test accuracy across grid \(k\) values reveals U-shaped bias-variance curve: small \(k\) overfits training noise, large \(k\) oversmooths decision boundary, moderate \(k\) minimises test error.

heading: 14.210 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “\(k\)-NN classification achieved best test accuracy 0.91 \(k = 15\) circular-decision-boundary task; \(k = 1\) -fit noise accuracy 0.82, \(k = 51\) -smoothed dropped 0.80. Cross-validation across \(k\) confirmed \(k = 15\) optimum.” Always state chosen \(k\), distance metric, test-set accuracy.

heading: 14.211 Practical Tips
text: Always standardise features kNN; unscaled features different units dominate distance computation bias predictions.Tune \(k\) cross-validation; U-shaped accuracy-vs-\(k\) curve clear optimum datasets.high-dimensional data (\(d > 20\)), kNN suffers curse dimensionality: distances concentrate discriminating power collapses. Reduce dimensionality PCA use parametric / tree-based model.kknn::train.kknn() implements kernel-weighted kNN (closer neighbours weighted heavily); often slightly accurate uniform-weight kNN.kNN lazy learner: prediction cost \(O(n d)\) per query naive search; use FNN kd-tree ball-tree indexing \(O(\log n)\) queries large training sets.mixed numeric-categorical inputs, use Gower distance via cluster::daisy() instead Euclidean; standard distance breaks categorical features.

heading: 14.212 R Packages Used
text: class::knn() canonical implementation; FNN::knn() fast kd-tree-based search; kknn::train.kknn() weighted kNN cross-validated \(k\) kernel selection; caret tidymodels cross-validated kNN within ML pipelines; RANN approximate nearest-neighbour search scale.
