heading: 14.215 Introduction
text: \(k\)-Nearest Neighbours regression predicts continuous outcome query point mean (weighted mean) \(k\) nearest training targets feature space. simplest non-parametric regression method, making assumption global functional form requiring distance metric. trade-straightforward: small \(k\) produces high variance (prediction depends neighbours), large \(k\) produces high bias (predictions average wide region miss local structure).

heading: 14.216 Prerequisites
text: working understanding distance metrics, bias-variance trade-, train-test evaluation supervised learning.

heading: 14.217 Theory
text: kNN regression prediction query point \(x^*\) \[\hat y(x^*) = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{\N_k(x^*)} y_i,\]\(N_k(x^*)\) set indices \(k\) training points nearest \(x^*\). Distance-weighted variants use weights \(w_i \propto 1 / \|x^* - x_i\|\) give closer neighbours influence far ones, producing smoother predictions reducing discontinuities plain kNN exhibits boundaries Voronoi cells.bias-variance trade-\(k\) direct: \(k = 1\) memorises training noise (high variance, low bias); \(k = n\) predicts global mean (low variance, high bias). Cross-validation chooses optimal \(k\) data.

heading: 14.218 Assumptions
text: Features appropriately scaled (unscaled features different units dominate distance computation); regression surface locally smooth (kNN extrapolate model abrupt changes well).

heading: 14.219 R Implementation
code:
library(FNN)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 400
x <- runif(n, 0, 2 * pi)
y <- sin(x) + rnorm(n, 0, 0.3)
df <- data.frame(x = scale(x)[, 1], y = y)

idx <- sample(n, n * 0.7)
train <- df[idx, ]; test <- df[-idx, ]

rmses <- sapply(c(1, 5, 15, 51), function(k) {
  pred <- knn.reg(train = train[, "x", drop = FALSE],
                  test  = test[,  "x", drop = FALSE],
                  y = train$y, k = k)$pred
  sqrt(mean((test$y - pred)^2))
})
setNames(rmses, paste0("k=", c(1, 5, 15, 51)))

heading: 14.220 Output & Results
text: Test RMSE across grid \(k\) values reveals U-shaped bias-variance curve: small \(k\) overfits noise, large \(k\) oversmooths sinusoid, moderate \(k\) (around 15 example) minimises test error.

heading: 14.221 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “\(k\)-NN regression \(k = 15\) achieved test RMSE 0.31 held-30 % simulated sinusoid, matching irreducible noise level (\(\sigma = 0.3\)); \(k = 1\) -fitted RMSE 0.42, \(k = 51\) -fitted RMSE 0.45. Cross-validation across \(k\) confirmed \(k = 15\) optimum.” Always state \(k\) selected test-set RMSE.

heading: 14.222 Practical Tips
text: Scale features (scale() recipes::step_normalize()); unscaled features different units dominate distance computation produce biased predictions.Use distance-weighted kNN (kknn::train.kknn) smoother predictions slightly better accuracy cost one extra hyperparameter.Prediction cost \(O(n d)\) per query naive search; use kd-trees (default FNN::knn.reg) faster lookups low-dimensional feature spaces.high-dimensional data (\(d > 20\)), kNN’s accuracy collapses due curse dimensionality; reduce dimensionality PCA use parametric / tree-based model instead.kNN native handling categorical predictors; encode dummy variables use Gower distance mixed numeric-categorical inputs.large datasets, consider approximate nearest-neighbour search (RANN, Annoy); exact \(k\)-NN becomes computationally prohibitive beyond million points.

heading: 14.223 R Packages Used
text: FNN::knn.reg() fast kd-tree-based exact \(k\)-NN regression; kknn::train.kknn() weighted \(k\)-NN cross-validated kernel \(k\) selection; caret tidymodels cross-validated \(k\)-NN within broader ML workflow; RANN approximate nearest-neighbour search scale.

heading: 14.226 Introduction
text: LightGBM (Ke et al., 2017) gradient-boosting framework engineered large datasets. introduces several optimisations XGBoost: histogram-based split finding (substantially faster sorted-value enumeration), leaf-wise tree growth (deeper trees per leaf count level-wise), Gradient-based One-Side Sampling (GOSS) prioritises high-gradient instances training, Exclusive Feature Bundling (EFB) combines mutually exclusive sparse features. result typically 3–5× faster XGBoost comparable slightly better accuracy large tabular data.

heading: 14.227 Prerequisites
text: working understanding gradient boosting, XGBoost similar boosted-tree workflow, bias-variance trade-tree depth leaf count.

heading: 14.228 Theory
text: Three core innovations distinguish LightGBM earlier gradient-boosting libraries:Histogram binning: pre-compute feature-value bins (default 255); split finding iterates bins rather sorted values, giving substantial speed memory gains.Leaf-wise growth: iteration, expand leaf maximum loss reduction. Produces deeper, asymmetric trees given fixed leaf count compared level-wise growth, higher -fit risk.GOSS: sample instances gradient magnitude, keeping top-\(\) fraction largest gradients random sample rest. classifier focuses hard examples contribute gradient.

heading: 14.229 Assumptions
text: Sufficient data histogram bins informative; regularisation (max_depth, num_leaves, min_data_in_leaf) tuned prevent -fit risk inherent leaf-wise growth.

heading: 14.230 R Implementation
code:
library(lightgbm)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 600
X <- matrix(rnorm(n * 5), n, 5)
y <- as.numeric(plogis(X[, 1] + 0.5 * X[, 2]^2) > runif(n))

idx <- sample(n, n * 0.8)
d_tr <- lgb.Dataset(X[idx, ], label = y[idx])
d_te <- lgb.Dataset.create.valid(d_tr, X[-idx, ], label = y[-idx])

params <- list(objective = "binary", metric = "binary_logloss",
               learning_rate = 0.05, num_leaves = 31,
               feature_fraction = 0.8, bagging_fraction = 0.8,
               bagging_freq = 5, min_data_in_leaf = 20)

model <- lgb.train(params, d_tr,
                   nrounds = 500, valids = list(val = d_te),
                   early_stopping_rounds = 20, verbose = -1)

cat("Best iter:", model$best_iter,
    "   best logloss:", round(model$best_score, 4), "\n")

imp <- lgb.importance(model, percentage = TRUE)
head(imp)

heading: 14.231 Output & Results
text: lgb.train() returns fitted model early-stopping iteration, best validation metric, tree structure. lgb.importance() ranks features gain (split count, cover) optional percentage display.

heading: 14.232 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “LightGBM learning_rate = 0.05, num_leaves = 31, feature bagging fractions 0.8 converged 184 boosting rounds early stopping (validation log-loss 0.39); feature 1 contributed 53 % total gain, confirming dominant predictor line simulated data-generating process. Training approximately 4× faster XGBoost data.” Always state hyperparameters early-stopping criteria.

heading: 14.233 Practical Tips
text: Constrain num_leaves (default 31) max_depth prevent -fit risk leaf-wise growth; deeper trees accurate per round prone -fitting.LightGBM typically 3–5× faster XGBoost number trees; speed gap widens \(>\) 1M rows.categorical features, pass integer-encoded factors plus categorical_feature argument; LightGBM uses split-aware categorical handling often outperforms one-hot encoding.learning_rate 0.01–0.05 sensible starting range; smaller values need rounds generalise better, larger values train faster.GPU training (device_type = "gpu") provides drop-3–10× acceleration large data; CPU performance already excellent.Compare LightGBM XGBoost data; results usually close, better model varies task.

heading: 14.234 R Packages Used
text: lightgbm canonical R interface; xgboost closely-related alternative; catboost third major gradient-boosting library (native categorical handling); parsnip::boost_tree(engine = "lightgbm") tidymodels integration; bonsai tidymodels-LightGBM bridge.

heading: 14.237 Introduction
text: LIME (Ribeiro, Singh, Guestrin 2016) explains single prediction fitting sparse linear model locally around point interest. Samples perturbed near observation, labelled black-box model, weighted distance; linear surrogate’s coefficients describe local behaviour model.

heading: 14.238 Prerequisites
text: Regression / classification; weighted linear regression.

heading: 14.239 Theory
text:
observation \(x\):
1. Sample \(x'_k\) perturbing \(x\) feature space.
2. Predict black-box: \(\hat{y}_k = f(x'_k)\).
3. Weight samples proximity: \(w_k = \exp(-d(x, x'_k)^2 / \sigma^2)\).
4. Fit sparse linear model \(g\) \((x'_k, \hat{y}_k)\) weights \(w_k\).
5. Coefficients \(g\) local explanation.

heading: 14.240 Assumptions
text: local linear approximation captures model’s behaviour neighbourhood; perturbation scheme appropriate feature type (Gaussian continuous, resampling categorical, word removal text).

heading: 14.241 R Implementation
code:
library(lime); library(ranger)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 500
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n), x3 = rnorm(n),
  y  = factor(sample(c("a", "b"), n, replace = TRUE))
)
df$y <- factor(ifelse(plogis(0.7 * df$x1 - 0.4 * df$x2) > runif(n), "a", "b"))

rf <- ranger(y ~ ., data = df, num.trees = 200, probability = TRUE,
             classification = TRUE)

# LIME setup
explainer <- lime(df[1:450, -4], rf,
                  bin_continuous = TRUE, n_bins = 4)
expl      <- explain(df[451:452, -4],
                     explainer, labels = "a",
                     n_features = 3, n_permutations = 1000)

expl[, c("feature", "feature_weight", "feature_desc")]

heading: 14.242 Output & Results
text: Per-observation feature weights: strongly feature pushes prediction toward explained class, human-readable feature description.

heading: 14.243 Interpretation
text: “LIME attributed observation 1’s predicted class ‘’ \(x_1 > 0.8\) (weight 0.42) \(x_2 < -0.3\) (weight 0.18), providing interpretable local rule.”

heading: 14.244 Practical Tips
text: LIME unstable: different runs can produce different explanations due random perturbations; set seed run several times.LIME’s linear surrogate local approximation; globally highly non-linear models, explanations drift across neighbourhoods.Compare LIME SHAP observations; large disagreements indicate explanation fragility.text, LIME removes words recomputes predictions; tabular, use binned perturbation.Weighted sampling bandwidth \(\sigma\) strongly affects results; tune inspection.

heading: 14.247 Introduction
text: Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), introduced Ronald Fisher 1936, one oldest classification methods remains strong baseline. models class multivariate Gaussian class-specific mean shared covariance matrix, assigns observation class highest posterior probability Gaussians. resulting decision boundary linear predictors. LDA simple, well-calibrated construction, often competitive logistic regression small moderate-sized datasets class-conditional Gaussianity approximately holds.

heading: 14.248 Prerequisites
text: working understanding multivariate Gaussian distributions, Bayes’ rule posterior class probabilities, basic linear algebra (matrix inversion).

heading: 14.249 Theory
text: class-conditional multivariate Normality shared covariance \(\Sigma\) class priors \(\pi_k\), posterior \[P(y = k \mid x) \propto \pi_k \, \phi(x; \mu_k, \Sigma).\]Taking log dropping constants common classes yields linear discriminant function\[\delta_k(x) = x^\top \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k - \tfrac{1}{2} \mu_k^\top \Sigma^{-1} \mu_k + \log \pi_k,\]linear \(x\). decision boundary two classes thus hyperplane. Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA) relaxes shared-covariance assumption give class-specific \(\Sigma_k\), producing quadratic boundaries cost parameters.Fisher’s geometric derivation arrives linear projection maximising ratio -class within-class scatter; connection makes LDA classifier supervised dimensionality-reduction tool.

heading: 14.250 Assumptions
text: Class-conditional features multivariate Normal shared covariance; \(n > p\) stable covariance estimation (use shrinkage LDA \(p\) approaches \(n\)).

heading: 14.251 R Implementation
code:
library(MASS)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
x1 <- c(rnorm(n/2, 0), rnorm(n/2, 2))
x2 <- c(rnorm(n/2, 0), rnorm(n/2, 2))
y <- factor(rep(c("a", "b"), each = n/2))
df <- data.frame(x1, x2, y)

idx <- sample(n, n * 0.7)
lda_fit <- lda(y ~ ., data = df[idx, ])
pred <- predict(lda_fit, df[-idx, ])$class
mean(pred == df[-idx, "y"])

# Coefficients of the linear discriminant function
lda_fit$scaling

heading: 14.252 Output & Results
text: lda() returns prior probabilities, class means, scaling matrix (linear discriminant coefficients), proportion trace axis explains. predict() provides class assignments posterior probabilities. linear coefficients, interpreted geometrically, point direction greatest -class separation within-class whitening.

heading: 14.253 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “LDA achieved 89 % test accuracy two-class Gaussian problem shared covariance (\(n_{\mathrm{train}} = 210\), \(n_{\mathrm{test}} = 90\)); linear-discriminant direction (0.82, 0.57) corresponds -class mean difference within-class scatter whitening; logistic regression gave 87 %, indicating LDA’s Gaussian assumption well-supported.” Compare LDA logistic regression sanity check Gaussian assumption.

heading: 14.254 Practical Tips
text: classes clearly Gaussian similar covariances, LDA nearly Bayes-optimal outperforms logistic regression finite samples stronger model.\(p\) approaching \(n\), use shrinkage LDA (klaR::rda() sda package); unregularised LDA unstable covariance estimates poor predictions.LDA’s posterior probabilities well-calibrated construction model holds; Platt scaling typically needed.QDA relaxes shared-covariance assumption needs data per class; use class covariances differ visibly (Box’s M test gives formal check).LDA also serves supervised dimensionality-reduction tool: project onto top \(K - 1\) discriminants low-dimensional class-aware visualisation.high-dimensional sparse data (text, genomics), regularised LDA (sparseLDA, penalizedLDA) handles \(p \gg n\) via L1 grouped penalties.

heading: 14.255 R Packages Used
text: MASS::lda() qda() canonical implementations; klaR::rda() regularised discriminant analysis; sda shrinkage LDA; penalizedLDA high-dimensional sparse extensions; caret tidymodels::discrim_linear() cross-validated LDA within ML pipelines.

heading: 14.258 Introduction
text: Logistic regression models class probability \(P(y = 1 \mid x) = \sigma(w^\top x + b)\), \(\sigma\) logistic function, fits parameters minimising cross-entropy loss. ML workhorse well-calibrated construction, interpretable coefficients log-odds scale, regularisable L1, L2, elastic-net penalties, scales millions features — making standard baseline text classification, click-prediction, medical risk scoring.

heading: 14.259 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear models, cross-entropy / log-loss objective, regularisation (L1 vs L2 penalties).

heading: 14.260 Theory
text: regularised log-loss objective \[L(w) = -\sum_i \bigl[y_i \log \sigma(w^\top x_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \sigma(w^\top x_i))\bigr] + \lambda R(w),\]\(R(w) = \|w\|_2^2\) L2 (ridge), \(\|w\|_1\) L1 (lasso), convex combination elastic net. Optimisation uses Newton-Raphson (IRLS) unregularised case coordinate descent (glmnet) L1/elastic-net case. penalty parameter \(\lambda\) tuned cross-validation, lambda.min minimising CV deviance lambda.1se providing parsimonious choice within one SE minimum.

heading: 14.261 Assumptions
text: Log-odds linear predictors; observations independent; sufficient sample size per feature (rule thumb: \(n > 10p\) unregularised, less regularised models).

heading: 14.262 R Implementation
code:
library(glmnet)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 400; p <- 30
X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p)
beta <- c(rep(1, 5), rep(0, 25))
y <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(X %*% beta - 1))

# Cross-validated lasso
cv_fit <- cv.glmnet(X, y, family = "binomial",
                    alpha = 1, nfolds = 10)
plot(cv_fit)

coef_min <- as.vector(coef(cv_fit, s = "lambda.min"))
head(coef_min, 10)
cat("# nonzero:", sum(coef_min != 0) - 1, "\n")

heading: 14.263 Output & Results
text: cv.glmnet() returns cross-validation deviance curve \(\lambda\), recommended lambda.min lambda.1se, fitted coefficient paths. Lasso correctly recovered 5 non-zero coefficients data-generating model; deviance plot shows U-shaped CV-error curve.

heading: 14.264 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Lasso logistic regression cross-validated \(\lambda_{\min} = 0.03\) retained 5 non-zero features exactly match data-generating \(\beta\); AUC held-test set 0.84 calibration intercept \(-0.02\) slope 0.97 — well-calibrated. sparser lambda.1se solution retained 3 features AUC 0.79.” Always report regularisation choice calibration check.

heading: 14.265 Practical Tips
text: Standardise features regularisation; glmnet internally back-transforms, explicit pre-scaling makes workflow transparent.Use lambda.1se sparser, interpretable model; lambda.min typically accurate less parsimonious.Logistic regression produces well-calibrated probabilities correctly-specified models; SVMs, trees, neural networks typically need Platt scaling isotonic regression recover calibration.massively sparse data (text bags--words, click logs), LiblineaR dramatically faster glmnet; handles millions features routinely.multi-class problems, family = "multinomial" glmnet extends framework natively; alternatively, nnet::multinom() unregularised multinomial regression.imbalanced classes, combine logistic regression class weights (weights = ...) threshold tuning validation set.

heading: 14.269 Introduction
text: mlr3 modern R6-based successor mlr, feature-complete machine-learning framework abstracts around five core objects: Tasks (data + target + roles), Learners (algorithms), Resamplings (CV schemes), Tuners (search strategies), Benchmarks (multi-learner comparisons). Like tidymodels, mlr3 provides unified interface across dozens underlying engines; unlike tidymodels, uses R6 object-oriented classes rather tibbles, giving flexibility steeper learning curve.

heading: 14.270 Prerequisites
text: working understanding R6 classes, machine-learning pipeline concepts, cross-validation-based hyperparameter tuning.

heading: 14.271 Theory
text: core mlr3 workflow:Task: wraps data target feature-role specifications.Learner: algorithm (e.g., lrn("classif.ranger") random forest classification).Resampling: cross-validation scheme (rsmp("cv", folds = 5) 5-fold CV).Tuner AutoTuner: wraps learner plus search space, inner resampling, search strategy self-tuning learner.Outer resampling: evaluate AutoTuner outer folds honest nested-CV performance estimation.mlr3pipelines extends framework preprocessing graphs meta-learners (stacking, bagging) composable nodes — equivalent role tidymodels recipes workflows.

heading: 14.272 Assumptions
text: Task roles (target, predictors, stratification variables) correctly specified; hyperparameter search spaces cover plausible ranges; resampling matches inferential question (random IID, blocked time series, spatial geography).

heading: 14.273 R Implementation
code:
library(mlr3); library(mlr3learners); library(mlr3tuning)

set.seed(2026)
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(500), x2 = rnorm(500),
  x3 = rnorm(500), x4 = rnorm(500),
  y  = factor(sample(c("yes", "no"), 500, replace = TRUE))
)

task <- as_task_classif(df, target = "y")

# Tunable random forest
lrn_rf <- lrn("classif.ranger",
              mtry = to_tune(1, 4),
              min.node.size = to_tune(5, 20),
              num.trees = 300)

at <- AutoTuner$new(
  learner    = lrn_rf,
  resampling = rsmp("cv", folds = 3),
  measure    = msr("classif.ce"),
  search_space = NULL,
  terminator = trm("evals", n_evals = 10),
  tuner      = tnr("random_search")
)

outer <- rsmp("cv", folds = 3)
rr <- resample(task, at, outer, store_models = TRUE)
rr$aggregate(msr("classif.ce"))

heading: 14.274 Output & Results
text: AutoTuner performs hyperparameter tuning inner resampling folds selects best configuration. resample() evaluates AutoTuner outer folds, producing nested-CV estimates generalisation performance. rr$aggregate() returns chosen metric (classification error ) averaged across outer folds bootstrap-based standard errors.

heading: 14.275 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Nested 3 × 3 cross-validation mlr3 AutoTuner (10-evaluation random-search inner tuning random forest hyperparameters) estimated classification error 0.25 ± 0.02 tuned random forest; nested wrapping prevents optimistic bias tuning evaluating folds.” Always state outer/inner CV scheme search budget.

Compose nodes Graphs complex pre/post-processing.mlr3tuningspaces provides sensible default search spaces per learner; useful don’t know right ranges.benchmark_grid() compares multiple learners task identical resampling, giving fair head--head performance comparisons.big data, use mlr3spatiotempcv mlr3temporal time-aware resamplings; standard random CV leaks information time-series problems.Parallelise tuning resampling future::plan(multisession); mlr3 respects future framework.

heading: 14.280 Introduction
text: Naive Bayes simplest probabilistic classifier one oldest. applies Bayes’ theorem strong assumption features conditionally independent given class label. independence assumption almost always wrong practice, yet Naive Bayes performs surprisingly well — often competitively sophisticated methods — text classification, spam filtering, high-dimensional sparse problems. trains linear time serves strong baseline classification pipeline.

heading: 14.281 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Bayes’ theorem, conditional probability, standard distributions (Gaussian, multinomial, Bernoulli) used per-feature class-conditional models.

heading: 14.282 Theory
text: classifier \[\hat y(x) = \arg\max_y P(y) \prod_{j=1}^p P(x_j \mid y),\]\(P(y)\) class prior (estimated class frequencies) \(P(x_j \mid y)\) per-feature class-conditional density. Three standard variants:Gaussian Naive Bayes: \(P(x_j \mid y)\) Normal class-conditional mean variance, suitable continuous features.Multinomial Naive Bayes: word counts text classification.Bernoulli Naive Bayes: binary features.Training reduces computing class frequencies per-feature class-conditional statistics — extremely fast even millions examples.

heading: 14.283 Assumptions
text: Features conditionally independent given class (almost always violated classifier remains useful); chosen distribution family matches feature’s behaviour.

heading: 14.284 R Implementation
code:
library(e1071)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 400
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n),
  x3 = sample(c("A", "B"), n, replace = TRUE),
  y  = factor(sample(c("pos", "neg"), n, replace = TRUE))
)

# Inject a relationship
df$y <- factor(ifelse(df$x1 + 0.5 * df$x2 +
                      as.numeric(df$x3) * 0.5 > runif(n, -1, 1),
                      "pos", "neg"))

idx <- sample(n, n * 0.7)
nb <- naiveBayes(y ~ ., data = df[idx, ])
pred <- predict(nb, df[-idx, ])

mean(pred == df[-idx, "y"])
nb$tables$x1         # Gaussian parameters for x1 per class

heading: 14.285 Output & Results
text: naiveBayes() returns fitted classifier per-class priors per-feature conditional distribution parameters. predict() provides class labels (type = "raw") class probabilities. accuracy held-fold gives baseline complex models can compared.

heading: 14.286 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Gaussian Naive Bayes (Laplace smoothing categorical predictor) achieved 74 % test accuracy, competitive logistic regression’s 76 % fraction training cost; per-class feature tables identify \(x_1\) discriminative feature (class-conditional mean difference 0.8).” Always state variant used smoothing parameter categorical features.

heading: 14.287 Practical Tips
text: Naive Bayes competitive high-dimensional sparse data (text classification, spam filtering); often wins small training sizes complex models -fit.Apply Laplace smoothing (laplace = 1) categorical features avoid zero-probability cases otherwise propagate make entire posterior zero.Probability calibration often poor (independence assumption produces overconfident predictions); apply Platt scaling isotonic regression calibrated probabilities needed.continuous features Gaussian, transform (log, Box-Cox) bin ordinal fitting; alternatively, use kernel-density NB variant.naivebayes package provides faster implementations supports kernel density estimation non-Gaussian continuous features.text classification specifically, multinomial NB TF-IDF features remains strong baseline; neural methods reliably beat large enough corpora.

heading: 14.291 Introduction
text: hyperparameters tuned data used evaluation, performance estimates optimistically biased — chosen hyperparameters fit underlying signal also noise validation folds, producing CV errors lower model achieves truly new data. Nested cross-validation separates two concerns: inner loop tunes hyperparameters training fold, outer loop evaluates resulting tuned model held-test folds. outer-loop mean honest, low-bias estimate generalisation error.

heading: 14.292 Prerequisites
text: working understanding \(k\)-fold cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning, optimism bias arises tuning evaluating data.

heading: 14.293 Theory
text: standard nested-CV scheme:Outer loop: \(K\)-fold CV. fold splits data outer-training outer-test sets.Inner loop: outer-training set, run \(K'\)-fold CV pick best hyperparameter configuration.Outer evaluation: refit chosen configuration full outer-training set; predict score outer-test set.Aggregate: average \(K\) outer-test scores; mean generalisation estimate, spread quantifies uncertainty.Typical values: outer \(K = 5\) \(10\), inner \(K' = 5\). Computational cost scales \(K \times K'\) times cost fitting one model.

heading: 14.294 Assumptions
text: Observations iid (resampling respects dependence structure time-series, clustered, grouped data); preprocessing (imputation, scaling, feature engineering) refit within inner fold prevent test-set leakage.

heading: 14.295 R Implementation
14.296 Output & Results
text: vector \(K\) outer-fold RMSE values; mean estimates generalisation error standard deviation quantifies much estimate varies across resampling.

heading: 14.297 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Nested 5 × 5 cross-validation estimated test RMSE 1.04 ± 0.08 ridge regression hyperparameter \(\lambda\) tuned inner folds; single-CV tuning hyperparameter grid produced RMSE 0.92 — -estimating approximately 12 %, optimism cost tuning evaluating folds.” Always report outer-fold mean standard deviation, never single CV estimate tuning involved.

heading: 14.298 Practical Tips
text: Fit preprocessing (imputation, scaling, feature engineering, target encoding) inside inner fold; preprocessing full outer-training set leaks information validation.tidymodels::nested_cv() mlr3::resampling_nested_cv() formalise structure prevent manual loop errors nested CV famous .small datasets (\(n < 200\)), repeated nested CV (repeating outer split different random seeds averaging) reduces outer-fold variance small samples introduce.final deployment model tuned full dataset chosen hyperparameter; nested CV informs uncertainty generalisation, deployed model .Parallelise inner loop future doParallel; outer loop typically fast enough leave serial.Computational budget can substantial: 5 × 5 nested CV 5-point hyperparameter grid runs \(5 \times 5 \times 5 = 125\) model fits; account planning.

heading: 14.302 Introduction
text: Neural networks compositions linear maps non-linear activation functions. feed-forward network \(L\) layers computes \(f(x) = g_L(W_L g_{L-1}(\cdots W_2 g_1(W_1 x + b_1) \cdots) + b_L)\) \(W_l\) weight matrices, \(b_l\) biases, \(g_l\) activation functions. Universal Approximation Theorem (Cybenko, 1989) shows even single hidden layer enough width can approximate continuous function. Modern deep learning stacks hundreds layers learn powerful representations images, text, audio, tabular data implicit feature engineering.

heading: 14.303 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear algebra (matrix multiplication), gradient descent, chain rule backpropagation, basic machine-learning concepts (loss functions, train-test evaluation).

heading: 14.304 Theory
text:
Perceptron: \(\hat y = \mathrm{sign}(w^\top x + b)\). Linear decision boundary; provably solve XOR.Multi-layer perceptron (MLP): two layers linear + non-linear transformations. Universal approximator one hidden layer sufficient width.Activation functions:
- Sigmoid: \(\sigma(x) = 1 / (1 + e^{-x})\), smooth vanishing gradients deep nets.
- tanh: zero-centred sigmoid; better sigmoid hidden layers still suffers vanishing gradients.
- ReLU: \(\max(0, x)\), modern default hidden layers; faster training avoids vanishing gradients positive regions.
- Leaky ReLU, GELU, SiLU: variants address ReLU’s “dead-neuron” problem.Training: minimise loss (MSE regression, cross-entropy classification) gradient descent (modern variants Adam, AdamW). Backpropagation computes gradients efficiently via chain rule.

heading: 14.305 Assumptions
text: Sufficient data relative model capacity (overparameterised models tiny datasets memorise rather generalise); features normalised; loss differentiable almost everywhere.

heading: 14.306 R Implementation
code:
library(nnet)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 400
x1 <- rnorm(n); x2 <- rnorm(n)
y  <- factor(ifelse(x1^2 + x2^2 > 1.5, "out", "in"))
df <- data.frame(x1 = scale(x1)[, 1], x2 = scale(x2)[, 1], y = y)

idx <- sample(n, n * 0.7)
nn <- nnet(y ~ ., data = df[idx, ],
           size = 5, decay = 1e-3, maxit = 200,
           trace = FALSE)

pred <- predict(nn, df[-idx, ], type = "class")
mean(pred == df[-idx, "y"])

heading: 14.307 Output & Results
text: nnet() fits small MLP one hidden layer size neurons. test accuracy concentric-rings task demonstrates network’s ability learn non-linear decision boundary linear classifiers represent.

heading: 14.308 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “5-neuron single-hidden-layer MLP weight decay 1e-3 trained 200 iterations achieved 91 % test accuracy concentric-rings classification task; logistic regression data managed 52 %, confirming non-linear decision boundary requires non-linear modelling capacity.” Always state architecture, regularisation, linear baseline.

heading: 14.309 Practical Tips
text: Always standardise inputs; unscaled features different units make training unstable slow.Combine multiple regularisers: weight decay (L2), dropout, early stopping based validation loss.Initialise weights carefully: Xavier (tanh) (ReLU) deep networks; nnet uses small uniform initialisation suffices shallow nets.tabular data, tree-based ensembles (XGBoost, LightGBM) usually outperform MLPs; neural networks dominate structure matters (images, text, audio, time series).Modern R neural-network workflows use torch keras rather nnet; latter fine shallow nets pedagogy lacks GPU support modern optimisers needed production.small datasets, regularise heavily (high decay, small networks, dropout) fall back linear / tree models; deep nets need data generalise.

heading: 14.313 Introduction
text: Partial dependence plots (PDPs), introduced Friedman 2001, show average model prediction feature varies holding features observed values. provide visual answer “model’s prediction change \(x_j\)?” — exposing non-linear effects interactions black-box models variable-importance scores alone reveal. Combined variable importance (features matter) SHAP values (per-prediction explanations), PDPs third pillar interpretation opaque ML models.

heading: 14.314 Prerequisites
text: working understanding trained black-box classifiers regressors (random forest, gradient boosting, neural networks), variable importance, difference marginal conditional effects.

heading: 14.315 Theory
text: Partial dependence value \(x_j = v\) \[\mathrm{PD}_j(v) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^n \hat f(v, x_{-j,}),\].e., set feature interest \(v\) across rows dataset, predict, average. PDP curve \(\mathrm{PD}_j(v)\) \(v\) varies observed range \(x_j\).Individual Conditional Expectation (ICE) plots show one curve per observation rather average; PDP mean ICE curves. ICE plots expose heterogeneity feature effects: ICE curves roughly parallel, effect \(x_j\) homogeneous; diverge, interaction another feature implied.Accumulated Local Effects (ALE) plots address PDP’s extrapolation problem features correlated; integrate prediction’s local derivative rather averaging predictions potentially --distribution feature combinations.

heading: 14.316 Assumptions
text: PDP interpretation, features approximately independent; otherwise average \(x_{-j}\) fixed \(x_j = v\) may include low-density regions joint distribution produce misleading curves. correlated features, use ALE plots instead.

heading: 14.317 R Implementation
code:
library(pdp); library(ranger)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 500
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n), x3 = rnorm(n),
  y  = sin(rnorm(n)) + 0.3 * rnorm(n)^2
)

rf <- ranger(y ~ ., data = df, num.trees = 300)
pd <- partial(rf, pred.var = "x1", plot = FALSE, train = df)

plot(pd$x1, pd$yhat, type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "#2A9D8F",
     xlab = "x1", ylab = "PD(x1)",
     main = "Partial dependence of x1")

heading: 14.318 Output & Results
text: pdp::partial() returns data frame feature value partial-dependence prediction. Plotting curve shows average prediction varies feature; non-linear shapes reveal effects linear models miss.

heading: 14.319 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “partial-dependence plot revealed non-monotone sinusoidal relationship \(x_1\) outcome (peak prediction near \(x_1 = -0.5\), trough near \(x_1 = 1.5\)); structure visible variable importance alone, simply ranked \(x_1\) strongest predictor. ICE curves broadly parallel, indicating major interaction features.” Always pair PDP ICE heterogeneity detection.

heading: 14.320 Practical Tips
text: Pair PDP variable importance: importance answers “features matter?”, PDP answers “affect prediction?”.Use ICE plots detect interactions: ICE curves diverging PDP indicate heterogeneous effects driven another feature.PDPs extrapolate low-density regions features correlated; correlated-feature contexts, use Accumulated Local Effects (ALE) plots via iml::FeatureEffect ALEPlot.Two-dimensional PDPs (pair features) reveal interactions computationally expensive; use sampled feature grid large datasets.DALEX::model_profile() offers unified interpretation interface across tree, linear, deep models consistent API.per-prediction explanations rather global ones, use SHAP values (shapviz, iml); SHAP PDP answer related distinct interpretation questions.

heading: 14.324 Introduction
text: Platt scaling (Platt, 1999) post-hoc calibration method maps classifier’s raw scores onto well-calibrated probabilities fitting logistic regression scores true labels held-validation set. simplest often best calibration method SVMs, neural networks, classifiers whose mis-calibration approximately monotone. idea leave classifier’s ranking untouched rescaling score axis predicted probability class 1 matches empirical class-1 rate.

heading: 14.325 Prerequisites
text: working understanding calibration plots, difference discrimination calibration, logistic regression, use held-validation sets.

heading: 14.326 Theory
text: Given validation pairs \((f_i, y_i)\) \(f_i\) classifier score (decision value, logit, raw probability), fit\[P(y = 1 \mid f) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(f + B)},\]maximum likelihood. New predictions \(P(y = 1 \mid f(x))\) fitted sigmoid. transformation two parameters preserves rank order — therefore ROC curve AUC — original classifier. Label smoothing \((y + 1)/(N + 2)\) avoids numerical issues near 0 1 validation set small.

heading: 14.327 Assumptions
text: Mis-calibration monotone (sigmoid family appropriate); separate validation set available fitting calibration; classifier’s scores rank observations meaningfully (Platt scaling fix classifier poor discrimination).

heading: 14.328 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
n <- 1000
# Well-ranked but mis-calibrated scores
y <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.3)
f <- 2 * y + rnorm(n, 0, 1.2)         # decision function

# Naive sigmoid of f does not match observed frequencies
naive_p <- plogis(f)

# Platt scaling: fit logistic regression y ~ f on validation
val_idx <- 1:500
te_idx  <- 501:1000
fit <- glm(y[val_idx] ~ f[val_idx], family = "binomial")
A <- coef(fit)[2]; B <- coef(fit)[1]
platt_p <- plogis(A * f[te_idx] + B)

# Brier scores
c(naive = mean((naive_p[te_idx] - y[te_idx])^2),
  platt = mean((platt_p - y[te_idx])^2))

heading: 14.329 Output & Results
text: Brier score (mean squared error probability vs label) drops Platt scaling, reflecting improved calibration. AUC remains unchanged rescaling monotone preserves rank.

heading: 14.330 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Platt scaling reduced Brier score 0.21 (naive sigmoid SVM decision values) 0.18 (Platt-rescaled) held-test set; ROC AUC unchanged 0.81 rescaling preserves rank order. reliability diagram shifted toward diagonal every probability decile recalibration.” Always report Brier score reliability diagram calibration.

heading: 14.331 Practical Tips
text: Platt scaling works best mis-calibration smooth monotone; wiggly non-monotone calibration curves, isotonic regression preferred.Use separate validation set (training data) fit calibration; -sample calibration self-reinforcing biased.Platt scaling adds 2 parameters extremely data-efficient — hundred validation points usually sufficient.multi-class problems, fit one-vs-rest Platt scaling per class renormalise resulting probabilities sum 1.Pair reliability diagram (ggcalibrate hand-built) calibration verify visual improvement.Platt scaling improve discrimination; AUC poor, fix underlying classifier rather chasing better calibration.

heading: 14.335 Introduction
text: Random forests, introduced Breiman 2001, average predictions many bootstrapped decision trees, trained random subset features considered every split. Two sources randomness — bootstrap row sampling feature subsampling — decorrelate trees dramatically reduce variance compared single tree. result strong --shelf baseline tabular data: minimal hyperparameter tuning, robust outliers irrelevant features, providing free --bag (OOB) error estimation side effect bootstrap.

heading: 14.336 Prerequisites
text: working understanding decision trees, bagging (bootstrap aggregating), bias-variance trade-ensemble methods.

heading: 14.337 Theory
text: \(B\) trees:Draw bootstrap sample (sampling replacement, size original) training data.Grow deep tree sample; split, consider random subset \(m\) features (typically \(m = \sqrt p\) classification, \(m = p/3\) regression).Predict tree; aggregate predictions averaging (regression) majority voting (classification).--bag (OOB) error uses observations selected bootstrap held-validation set; aggregating per-tree predictions across forest gives nearly unbiased generalisation estimate zero additional computational cost.

heading: 14.338 Assumptions
text: Observations independent identically distributed; features informative (random forests robust irrelevant features synthesise signal ); default \(m\) value starting point may need tuning specific datasets.

heading: 14.339 R Implementation
code:
library(ranger)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 500
df <- data.frame(x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n), x3 = rnorm(n),
                 y  = factor(sample(c("pos", "neg"), n, replace = TRUE)))
df$y <- factor(ifelse(plogis(0.8 * df$x1 - 0.6 * df$x2) > runif(n), "pos", "neg"))

rf <- ranger(y ~ ., data = df,
             num.trees = 500,
             mtry = 1,
             importance = "impurity",
             classification = TRUE)

rf$prediction.error         # OOB misclassification error
rf$variable.importance

heading: 14.340 Output & Results
text: ranger() returns fitted random forest OOB error (prediction.error), per-feature variable importance (variable.importance), underlying tree structures. OOB error standard generalisation estimate; variable importance ranks features average contribution prediction quality.

heading: 14.341 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “500-tree random forest mtry = 1 achieved OOB classification error 0.17; variable importance identified \(x_1\) (impurity decrease 32) \(x_2\) (decrease 18) top predictors, consistent data-generating logistic process. held-test set confirmed OOB-based estimate (test error 0.16).” Always report OOB error held-test error top variable importances.

heading: 14.342 Practical Tips

heading: 14.346 Introduction
text: recipes package tidymodels defines preprocessing pipelines sequence named steps — impute missing values, normalise numeric predictors, encode categoricals, apply PCA — learn parameters training data apply consistently prediction time. principal benefit leak-free preprocessing: imputation means, scaling parameters, PCA loadings estimated training set, never test validation folds. Applying preprocessing globally splitting (frequent mistake) leaks information test training produces optimistically biased performance estimates.

heading: 14.347 Prerequisites
text: working understanding tidymodels, train-test splits, cross-validation, concept data leakage.

heading: 14.348 Theory
text: recipe two phases:Define: chain recipe() step_*() functions pipeline order.Prep / bake: prep() learns parameters training data; bake() applies new data.statistics (centring means, scaling SDs, imputation values, PCA loadings) come data passed prep(). recipe applied test data via bake() via workflow(), parameters used — preserving principle test data must influence preprocessing.Steps executed declared order; ordering matters substantively (impute scale, ; encode categoricals operation requires numeric inputs).

heading: 14.349 Assumptions
text: Training data representative enough estimate transform parameters reliably; step ordering correct chosen transformations.

heading: 14.350 R Implementation
code:
library(recipes); library(dplyr)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
df <- tibble(
  num1 = rnorm(n),
  num2 = rnorm(n),
  cat  = sample(c("a", "b", "c"), n, replace = TRUE),
  y    = rnorm(n)
)
df$num1[sample(n, 10)] <- NA           # introduce missingness

rec <- recipe(y ~ ., data = df) %>%
  step_impute_mean(num1) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors())

prepped <- prep(rec, training = df[1:150, ])
test_baked <- bake(prepped, new_data = df[151:200, ])
head(test_baked)

heading: 14.351 Output & Results
text: prep() returns fitted recipe learnt parameters stored. bake() applies prepared recipe new data, returning transformed tibble imputed, normalised, one-hot-encoded predictors. transformations held-50 rows use parameters learned first 150.

heading: 14.352 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “recipe imputed missing values num1 training-set mean (-0.02), centred scaled numeric predictors, one-hot encoded cat factor three levels; parameters learned 150-row training set applied identically 50-row test set, avoiding data leakage occurs preprocessing applied globally splitting.” Always describe preprocessing steps methods section.

heading: 14.353 Practical Tips
text: Place imputation steps scaling; meaningfully standardise column contains NAs.step_normalize() centres scales; step_range() rescales \([0, 1]\) via min-max.tree-based models (random forest, XGBoost), skip normalisation — trees scale-invariant — keep imputation one-hot encoding.Use step_zv() step_nzv() remove zero-variance near-zero-variance predictors modelling; contribute information can destabilise algorithms.Pair recipe model specification via workflow() preprocessing prediction happen one pipeline; prevents accidental leakage predict time.PCA dimensionality reduction, step_pca() learns loadings training data; loadings applied test data, preserving train-test independence.

heading: 14.357 Introduction
text: Regularisation adds complexity penalty training loss, preventing overfitting shrinking coefficients. Ridge (L2) shrinks coefficients toward zero; lasso (L1) also performs variable selection; elastic net combines . three standard tools across regression classification.

heading: 14.358 Prerequisites
text: Linear / logistic regression; convex optimisation.

heading: 14.359 Theory
text:
Objective: \(\min_\beta \sum_i L(y_i, x_i^\top \beta) + \lambda \cdot P(\beta)\), 
- \(P(\beta) = \|\beta\|^2\) (ridge, L2)
- \(P(\beta) = \|\beta\|_1\) (lasso, L1)
- \(P(\beta) = \alpha \|\beta\|_1 + (1 - \alpha) \|\beta\|^2 / 2\) (elastic net)Lasso produces sparse solutions (many \(\hat{\beta}_j = 0\)); ridge shrinks rarely zeros; elastic net balances sparsity grouping effect correlated features.

heading: 14.360 Assumptions
text: Features standardised penalty comparable across predictors; \(\lambda\) tuned CV.

heading: 14.361 R Implementation
code:
library(glmnet)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300; p <- 50
X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p)
beta <- c(rep(2, 5), rep(0, 45))
y <- X %*% beta + rnorm(n)

cv_ridge <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = 0,   nfolds = 10)   # L2
cv_lasso <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = 1,   nfolds = 10)   # L1
cv_enet  <- cv.glmnet(X, y, alpha = 0.5, nfolds = 10)

nz <- function(fit) sum(as.vector(coef(fit, s = "lambda.min")) != 0) - 1
cat("nonzero: ridge=", nz(cv_ridge),
    "  lasso=", nz(cv_lasso),
    "  elastic=", nz(cv_enet), "\n")
c(ridge = cv_ridge$cvm[cv_ridge$lambda == cv_ridge$lambda.min],
  lasso = cv_lasso$cvm[cv_lasso$lambda == cv_lasso$lambda.min],
  enet  = cv_enet $cvm[cv_enet $lambda == cv_enet $lambda.min])

heading: 14.362 Output & Results
text: Non-zero counts: ridge retains 50, lasso selects ~5, elastic net selects ~7. Elastic net combines sparsity (lasso-like) stability (ridge-like) correlated features.

heading: 14.363 Interpretation
text: “Lasso CV-selected \(\lambda\) retained 5 true non-zero features zero-ed 45 irrelevant ones; elastic net gave similar sparsity handled correlated predictors gracefully.”

heading: 14.364 Practical Tips
text: Always standardise features (glmnet internally); otherwise penalty scale-dependent.Use lambda.1se sparser, parsimonious model within one SE CV minimum.Lasso picks one correlated-feature groups arbitrarily; elastic net picks – often stable.classification, extend via family = "binomial"; rules apply.Gradient-boosting frameworks expose L1/L2 regularisation leaf weights; identical glmnet’s lasso/ridge motivation.

heading: 14.367 Introduction
text: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) process sequences maintaining hidden state updates every time step. Long Short-Term Memory units (LSTM; Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997) add gating mechanisms combat vanishing gradients, enabling long-range dependencies. GRUs (Cho, 2014) simpler gated variant.

heading: 14.368 Prerequisites
text: Feedforward networks; gradient-based training; sequence tasks.

heading: 14.369 Theory
text:
Vanilla RNN: \(h_t = \tanh(W_h h_{t-1} + W_x x_t + b)\). Gradients time vanish explode, limiting long-range dependence.LSTM adds input, forget, output gates, plus memory cell:
\[c_t = f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot \tilde{c}_t, \quad h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(c_t).\]
Gates control written, read, forgotten memory cell.Bidirectional RNNs concatenate forward backward hidden states – useful sequence classification.

heading: 14.370 Assumptions
text: Inputs sequential order-dependent; padding/masking handles variable-length sequences correctly; sufficient context hidden state.

heading: 14.371 R Implementation
code:
library(torch)

torch_manual_seed(2026)

# Batch of 4 sequences, each length 10 and feature dim 5
x <- torch_randn(4, 10, 5)

lstm <- nn_lstm(input_size = 5, hidden_size = 8, num_layers = 2,
                batch_first = TRUE)
out  <- lstm(x)             # list: output tensor, (h_n, c_n)

out[[1]]$shape              # [4, 10, 8]
out[[2]][[1]]$shape         # h_n: [2, 4, 8]

# Simple forecasting model: LSTM + linear head
forecaster <- nn_module(
  initialize = function() {
    self$lstm <- nn_lstm(1, 16, batch_first = TRUE)
    self$fc   <- nn_linear(16, 1)
  },
  forward = function(x) {
    out <- self$lstm(x)[[1]]
    self$fc(out[, -1, ])       # predict from last time step
  }
)

model <- forecaster()
y_seq <- torch_randn(4, 10, 1)
model(y_seq)$shape          # [4, 1]

heading: 14.372 Output & Results
text: LSTM returns per-timestep hidden states (shape [batch, seq, hidden]) plus final hidden cell states.

heading: 14.373 Interpretation
text: “2-layer LSTM 8 hidden units processed 10-step sequences; final hidden state feeds linear classifier. Transformers now exceed LSTMs NLP tasks, LSTMs remain strong short-window forecasting low-data regimes.”

heading: 14.374 Practical Tips
text: Use LSTMs GRUs vanilla RNNs; latter hard train real-world sequence length.Pack sequences (nn_utils_rnn_pack_padded_sequence) skip padding computations.Gradient clipping (nn_utils_clip_grad_norm_) almost always needed RNN training stability.short tasks, 1-D CNN sequence often matches beats LSTM faster training.long sequences global dependencies, transformers beat LSTMs; need data.

heading: 14.377 Introduction
text: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations; Lundberg & Lee, 2017) attributes prediction input features via Shapley value cooperative game theory. attribution method satisfying combined axioms local accuracy, consistency, missingness. SHAP become standard black-box model interpretability.

heading: 14.378 Prerequisites
text: Variable importance; cooperative game theory basics helpful.

heading: 14.379 Theory
text:
prediction \(f(x)\), feature \(j\) gets SHAP value \(\phi_j\) satisfying
\[f(x) = \mathbb{E}[f(X)] + \sum_j \phi_j.\]Shapley values average marginal contribution feature \(j\) across possible subsets features, producing fair attribution. Exact computation exponential; Tree SHAP (Lundberg, 2020) computes polynomial time tree ensembles.

heading: 14.380 Assumptions
text: Features sensible “absence” interpretations (TreeSHAP integrates feature distributions); additive decomposition meaningfully captures model behaviour.

heading: 14.381 R Implementation
code:
library(xgboost); library(shapviz)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 500
X <- matrix(rnorm(n * 4), n, 4); colnames(X) <- paste0("x", 1:4)
y <- as.numeric(X[, 1] > 0) + 0.5 * as.numeric(X[, 2] > 0) +
     rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)

dtrain <- xgb.DMatrix(X, label = y)
model <- xgb.train(list(objective = "reg:squarederror",
                         max_depth = 3, eta = 0.1),
                    data = dtrain, nrounds = 200, verbose = 0)

sv <- shapviz(model, X_pred = X, X = X)

# Summary (importance ranking)
sv_importance(sv)

# Per-observation waterfall (first prediction)
sv_waterfall(sv, row_id = 1)

heading: 14.382 Output & Results
text: importance plot (mean |SHAP| per feature) waterfall plot showing feature pushes specific prediction baseline.

heading: 14.383 Interpretation
text: “SHAP ranked \(x_1\) dominant driver (mean |SHAP| = 0.36); waterfall showed observation 1’s prediction pushed 0.42 baseline primarily due \(x_1 = 1.8\).”

heading: 14.384 Practical Tips
text: Use Tree SHAP (fast, exact) tree models; fallback Kernel SHAP arbitrary models (approximate, slower).SHAP dependence plots (SHAP value vs feature value) reveal non-linear effects interactions.SHAP values sum \(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[f(X)]\), per-observation explanations additive.deep learning, use DeepSHAP integrated gradients; TreeSHAP apply.Global importance = mean |SHAP| across observations; usually close permutation importance tree ensembles.

heading: 14.387 Introduction
text: imbalanced classification problems, classifiers trained standard loss optimise majority class -predict minority. SMOTE (Chawla et al., 2002) addresses synthesising new minority examples via interpolation minority neighbours, balancing training set without duplicating samples.

heading: 14.388 Prerequisites
text: Classification; understanding imbalanced data; kNN.

heading: 14.389 Theory
text:
SMOTE algorithm:
1. minority sample \(x_i\), find \(k\) nearest minority neighbours.
2. Select random neighbour \(x_{nn}\).
3. Generate synthetic sample \(x_{\text{new}} = x_i + \lambda (x_{nn} - x_i)\), \(\lambda \sim U(0, 1)\).Apply within training set (never test set). Variants: Borderline-SMOTE, ADASYN (focus hard minority regions).

heading: 14.390 Assumptions
text: Feature space continuous (SMOTE works poorly categoricals without adaptation); minority class enough samples define meaningful neighbourhoods.

heading: 14.391 R Implementation
code:
library(themis); library(recipes); library(dplyr)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 500
df <- tibble(
  x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n),
  y  = factor(sample(c("pos", "neg"), n, replace = TRUE,
                     prob = c(0.05, 0.95)))
)
table(df$y)

rec <- recipe(y ~ ., data = df) %>%
  step_smote(y, over_ratio = 1, neighbors = 5)

baked <- prep(rec) %>% bake(new_data = NULL)
table(baked$y)

heading: 14.392 Output & Results
text: Original class counts (~475 neg, 25 pos); SMOTE minority upsampled via synthetic examples match majority.

heading: 14.393 Interpretation
text: “SMOTE upsampled 5 % minority class 50 % synthetic examples, enabling downstream logistic regression learn better boundary. Test AUC improved 0.74 0.83 held-set.”

heading: 14.394 Practical Tips
text: Apply SMOTE training set (inside CV fold), never test/validation.over_ratio = 1 creates fully balanced training set; smaller ratios -sample less.SMOTE works numeric features; mixed data, use SMOTE-NC mixed-type variants.Alternatives: class weighting loss function often effective simpler.Evaluate using class-balanced metrics (balanced accuracy, F1, AUC, PR-AUC) – plain accuracy misleads imbalance.

heading: 14.397 Introduction
text: Stacking, also called stacked generalisation (Wolpert 1992), combines predictions several diverse base learners using second-level meta-learner trained predict target base-model predictions. Stacking consistently wins competitive ML benchmarks base models individually decent sufficiently diverse — meta-learner captures complementary strengths single model can match. generalises bagging (one type model averaged) boosting (one type model sequentially trained) arbitrary heterogeneous ensembles.

heading: 14.398 Prerequisites
text: working understanding cross-validation, bias-variance trade-ensembling, difference -sample --fold predictions.

heading: 14.399 Theory
text: standard stacking workflow:Train base learners \(M_1, M_2, \dots, M_K\) training data; compute --fold (OOF) predictions \(\hat y^{\mathrm{oof}}_k\) via cross-validation.Train meta-learner \(g\) (typically linear regularised regression / logistic regression) predict target OOF predictions: \(\hat y = g(\hat y^{\mathrm{oof}}_1, \dots, \hat y^{\mathrm{oof}}_K)\).Refit base learners full training set; produce final predictions \(\hat y(x) = g(M_1(x), \dots, M_K(x))\).Using OOF predictions meta-learner prevents leakage occur meta-learner trained -sample base predictions.

heading: 14.400 Assumptions
text: Base learners sufficiently diverse (trees + linear + kNN combination usually outperforms five different tree variants); meta-learner simple enough -fit small set base predictions.

heading: 14.401 R Implementation
code:
library(tidymodels); library(stacks)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 500
df <- tibble(x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n), x3 = rnorm(n),
             y  = factor(sample(c("pos", "neg"), n,
                                replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.3, 0.7))))

split <- initial_split(df, strata = y)
train <- training(split); test <- testing(split)

folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 5)
ctrl <- control_stack_grid()

rec <- recipe(y ~ ., data = train)
rf_spec  <- rand_forest(mtry = 2, trees = 300) %>%
  set_engine("ranger") %>% set_mode("classification")
log_spec <- logistic_reg(penalty = 0.01) %>%
  set_engine("glmnet") %>% set_mode("classification")

rf_wf  <- workflow() %>% add_recipe(rec) %>% add_model(rf_spec)
log_wf <- workflow() %>% add_recipe(rec) %>% add_model(log_spec)

rf_res  <- fit_resamples(rf_wf,  folds, control = ctrl)
log_res <- fit_resamples(log_wf, folds, control = ctrl)

st <- stacks() %>% add_candidates(rf_res) %>% add_candidates(log_res)
st_blend <- blend_predictions(st)
st_fit   <- fit_members(st_blend)

st_fit

heading: 14.402 Output & Results
text: fit_members() returns trained stack meta-learner coefficients weighting base model. Inspecting st_blend shows chosen blending weights — typically non-negative least-squares penalised regression solution emphasises base models distinctive predictive signal.

heading: 14.403 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Stacking random forest regularised logistic regression via penalised linear meta-learner achieved test ROC AUC 0.89, exceeding either base model alone (RF 0.85, logistic 0.80); meta-learner weighted RF 0.62 logistic 0.38, contributions retained non-zero values.” Always report base-model performances, meta-learner choice, blending weights.

heading: 14.404 Practical Tips
text: Base-model diversity (tree + linear + kNN + neural net) matters many similar learners; stack five XGBoost variants rarely better single well-tuned XGBoost.Use regularised linear meta-learner (lasso non-negative LS); unregularised meta-learners -fit relatively small set OOF predictions.Pass --fold predictions meta-learner, -sample predictions; central correctness guarantee stacking.Stacking plus bagging hybrids (super learner van der Laan, Polley, Hubbard) perform best moderate--large data.stacks package (tidymodels) automates workflow integrates broader tune ecosystem cross-validated base-model tuning.SuperLearner classical alternative outside tidymodels; produce equivalent results given base models.

heading: 14.405 R Packages Used
text: stacks tidymodels-based stacking; SuperLearner classical implementation; tune::fit_resamples() cross-validated base-learner predictions; parsnip unified base-model specifications; glmnet ranger standard base-learner choices.

heading: 14.408 Introduction
text: Supervised learning uses labelled data \((x_i, y_i)_{=1}^{n}\) learn function \(f\) predicts \(y\) new \(x\). enterprise defined three choices: hypothesis class (tree, linear model, neural net), loss function (squared error, cross-entropy), optimisation procedure. Generalisation – performance unseen data – goal, bias-variance decomposition classical lens understanding models fail.

heading: 14.409 Prerequisites
text: Probability, linear algebra, basic statistics.

heading: 14.410 Theory
text:
Empirical risk minimisation (ERM): \(\hat{f} = \arg\min_{f \\mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{n}\sum_i L(y_i, f(x_i))\).Loss functions:
- Regression: squared loss \((y - f)^2\), absolute loss \(|y - f|\).
- Classification: 0/1 (intractable), log loss (cross-entropy), hinge (SVMs).Bias-variance decomposition squared loss:
\[\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\text{noise}}.\]Flexible models low bias, high variance; rigid models reverse. Regularisation trades one .

heading: 14.411 Assumptions
text: \((x, y)\) pairs drawn iid joint distribution; deployment distribution matches training distribution (distribution shift).

heading: 14.412 R Implementation
code:
library(tidymodels)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
x <- runif(n, 0, 2 * pi)
y <- sin(x) + rnorm(n, 0, 0.3)
df <- tibble(x = x, y = y)

split <- initial_split(df, prop = 0.7)
train <- training(split); test <- testing(split)

# Two models of different flexibility
m_rigid <- lm(y ~ x, data = train)
m_flex  <- loess(y ~ x, data = train, span = 0.2)

rmse <- function(yhat, y) sqrt(mean((yhat - y)^2))
rmse(predict(m_rigid, test), test$y)
rmse(predict(m_flex,  test), test$y)

heading: 14.413 Output & Results
text: flexible loess model lower test RMSE non-linear signal; rigid linear model -fits.

heading: 14.414 Interpretation
text: “sinusoidal signal captured loess (test RMSE 0.31) missed linear regression (test RMSE 0.68), illustrating bias dominates hypothesis class restrictive.”

heading: 14.415 Practical Tips
text: Always report test performance, never training performance, generalisation estimate.-free-lunch theorem: single model wins everywhere; match model data-generating process.Stratify classification splits preserve class proportions.Use cross-validation small--moderate data; single train/test split high-variance.Track multiple metrics: RMSE MAE together informative either alone.

heading: 14.418 Introduction
text: Kernel SVMs extend linear SVM framework non-linear decision boundaries via kernel trick: replace every inner product \(x_i^\top x_j\) dual formulation kernel function \(K(x_i, x_j)\) corresponds inner product high-dimensional (often infinite-dimensional) implicit feature space. Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel workhorse, capable representing essentially smooth decision boundary given enough training data appropriate hyperparameter tuning.

heading: 14.419 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear SVMs, inner-product spaces, dual formulation constrained optimisation, Mercer’s theorem (conditions function must satisfy valid kernel).

heading: 14.420 Theory
text: Common kernels:RBF (Gaussian): \(K(x, x') = \exp(-\gamma \|x - x'\|^2)\). Universal approximator; default choice non-linear problems.Polynomial: \(K(x, x') = (x^\top x' + c)^d\). Useful polynomial structure expected domain knowledge.Linear: \(K(x, x') = x^\top x'\). Reduces standard linear SVM.Sigmoid: \(K(x, x') = \tanh(\alpha x^\top x' + c)\), related single-layer perceptrons.Two hyperparameters dominate: cost parameter \(C\) (margin tightness, inherited linear SVM) kernel-specific parameter (\(\gamma\) RBF, \(d\) \(c\) polynomial). RBF \(\gamma\) trades bias variance: small \(\gamma\) produces smoother decision boundary, large \(\gamma\) produces wigglier one high variance.

heading: 14.421 Assumptions
text: Features appropriately scaled; sufficient data support kernel’s implicit dimensionality (RBF particular grows effective complexity \(\gamma\)); \(C\) \(\gamma\) tuned jointly correlated.

heading: 14.422 R Implementation
code:
library(e1071)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
theta <- runif(n, 0, 2 * pi)
r <- c(rep(1, n/2), rep(2.5, n/2)) + rnorm(n, 0, 0.3)
df <- data.frame(x1 = r * cos(theta), x2 = r * sin(theta),
                 y  = factor(rep(c("inner", "outer"), each = n/2)))
df[, 1:2] <- scale(df[, 1:2])

svm_rbf <- svm(y ~ ., data = df,
               kernel = "radial", cost = 1, gamma = 0.5,
               scale = FALSE)
table(predict(svm_rbf, df), df$y)

# Grid-search C x gamma via tune()
tune_out <- tune(svm, y ~ ., data = df, kernel = "radial",
                 ranges = list(cost = c(0.1, 1, 10),
                               gamma = c(0.1, 0.5, 1)))
tune_out$best.parameters

heading: 14.423 Output & Results
text: RBF-kernel SVM separates two concentric classes — non-linear pattern linear classifier can solve. tune() performs grid-search cross-validation \(C\) \(\gamma\) returns best combination CV accuracy.

heading: 14.424 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “RBF-kernel SVM correctly separated concentric-rings classes grid-CV-selected \(C = 1\) \(\gamma = 0.5\) (CV accuracy 0.96, test accuracy 0.94); linear-kernel baseline managed 0.51, confirming non-linear structure captured without kernel trick.” Always state hyperparameters tuned.

heading: 14.425 Practical Tips
text: Always standardise features; kernel distances scale-sensitive unscaled features bias geometry.RBF, \(\gamma\) trades bias (small \(\gamma\), smoother boundary) variance (large \(\gamma\), wigglier boundary); tune jointly \(C\) logarithmic 2D grid.\(C\) \(\gamma\) correlated; larger \(C\) paired smaller \(\gamma\) often gives similar performance smaller \(C\) larger \(\gamma\).High-degree polynomial kernels rarely useful practice; RBF subsumes cases numerically stable.large \(n\) (\(> 100{,}000\)), kernel SVMs become computationally expensive (\(O(n^2)\) \(O(n^3)\) training); consider Nyström approximation (kernlab::kha) fall back primal linear SVM engineered features.probability output, set probability = TRUE e1071::svm(); fits Platt-scaling layer top SVM decision values.

heading: 14.426 R Packages Used

heading: 14.429 Introduction
text: Linear support vector machines (SVMs) find hyperplane maximally separates classes largest possible margin. soft-margin formulation introduced Cortes Vapnik 1995 added slack variables tolerate misclassified margin-violating points, governed regularisation parameter \(C\). Linear SVMs scale large sparse feature spaces (common text classification, bag--words gives millions features) competitive logistic regression tabular classification tasks.

heading: 14.430 Prerequisites
text: working understanding linear classification, convex optimisation, geometric concept margin supervised learning.

heading: 14.431 Theory
text: soft-margin primal optimisation problem \[\min_{w, b, \xi} \tfrac{1}{2} \|w\|^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_i (w^\top x_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \ge 0.\]first term maximises margin (minimising \(\|w\|\)); second penalises slack. Larger \(C\) penalises slack , favouring low training error cost higher variance; smaller \(C\) allows margin violations produces smoother boundary. dual formulation exposes support-vector structure: points inside margin contribute \(w\), trained model effectively determined sparse subset training points.

heading: 14.432 Assumptions
text: Classes approximately linearly separable chosen \(C\); features appropriately scaled (SVMs distance-based unscaled features dominate geometry).

heading: 14.433 R Implementation
code:
library(e1071)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
x1 <- rnorm(n); x2 <- rnorm(n)
y <- factor(ifelse(x1 + x2 + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5) > 0, "pos", "neg"))
df <- data.frame(x1, x2, y)

# Scale the features
df[, 1:2] <- scale(df[, 1:2])

svm_fit <- svm(y ~ ., data = df,
               kernel = "linear",
               cost = 1,
               scale = FALSE)

table(pred = predict(svm_fit, df), truth = df$y)

# Vary C and observe support-vector count
cs <- c(0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100)
sapply(cs, function(c) {
  f <- svm(y ~ ., data = df, kernel = "linear", cost = c, scale = FALSE)
  c(C = c, n_sv = f$tot.nSV)
})

heading: 14.434 Output & Results
text: svm() returns fitted classifier support vectors, dual coefficients, learned weight vector. Varying \(C\) shows trade-: small \(C\) retains many support vectors (soft boundary), large \(C\) retains (tight boundary, high variance).

heading: 14.435 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “linear SVM cross-validated \(C = 1\) achieved 92 % test accuracy 124 support vectors 200 training points; support-vector count indicates classes overlap moderately soft boundary appropriate. Higher \(C\) values reduced support-vector counts increased test error, confirming bias-variance trade-.” Always report \(C\), support-vector counts, \(C\) tuned.

heading: 14.436 Practical Tips
text: Standardise features fitting; unscaled features different units bias geometry margin.Tune \(C\) cross-validation logarithmic grid (typical range \(10^{-3}\) \(10^{3}\)); U-shaped CV-error curve clear optimum.class imbalance, use class.weights -weight minority class; alternatively, balance via -sampling.large sparse feature spaces (text classification millions features), LiblineaR dramatically faster e1071::svm equally accurate.Linear SVM logistic regression often produce nearly identical decision boundaries data; SVM uses hinge loss logistic uses log-loss, tasks choice convention rather substance.non-linearly separable data, switch kernel SVM (RBF, polynomial); linear SVM represent non-linear boundaries regardless \(C\).

heading: 14.440 Introduction
text: tidymodels R meta-package wraps rsample, recipes, parsnip, workflows, tune, yardstick consistent, tidyverse-friendly interface machine-learning pipelines. complete tidymodels workflow composes data split, preprocessing recipe, model specification, hyperparameter tuning cross-validation resamples, final evaluation held-test set. framework’s strength syntax handles regression, classification, time-series tasks across dozens underlying engines (lm, glm, random forest, xgboost, neural networks).

heading: 14.441 Prerequisites
text: working knowledge R basics, tidyverse, standard model-fitting concepts (training-test split, cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning).

heading: 14.442 Theory
text: canonical tidymodels pipeline:Split data rsample::initial_split() training test sets.Define preprocessing recipes: imputation, normalisation, dummy encoding, feature engineering.Specify model parsnip, choosing engine hyperparameters tune.Combine workflow bundles recipe model preprocessing applied consistently prediction time.Tune hyperparameters cross-validation resamples (tune::tune_grid Bayesian tune_bayes) using metric set (yardstick).Finalise best hyperparameters refit training data.Evaluate test set, never touching step.

heading: 14.443 Assumptions
text: Data tidy (one row per observation, one column per variable); roles (outcome vs predictor) clearly identified; cross-validation splits appropriate problem (random IID, stratified imbalance, blocked time series).

heading: 14.444 R Implementation
code:
library(tidymodels)

set.seed(2026)
df <- tibble(
  x1 = rnorm(500), x2 = rnorm(500),
  x3 = rnorm(500), x4 = rnorm(500),
  y  = factor(sample(c("yes", "no"), 500, replace = TRUE,
                     prob = c(0.3, 0.7)))
)

split <- initial_split(df, strata = y)
train <- training(split); test <- testing(split)
folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 5, strata = y)

rec <- recipe(y ~ ., data = train) %>%
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

spec <- rand_forest(trees = 300, mtry = tune(), min_n = tune()) %>%
  set_engine("ranger") %>% set_mode("classification")

wf <- workflow() %>% add_recipe(rec) %>% add_model(spec)

grid <- expand_grid(mtry = c(1, 2, 3), min_n = c(5, 15))
tuned <- tune_grid(wf, folds, grid = grid, metrics = metric_set(roc_auc))

best <- select_best(tuned, metric = "roc_auc")
final_fit <- finalize_workflow(wf, best) %>% last_fit(split)

collect_metrics(final_fit)

heading: 14.445 Output & Results
text: tune_grid() returns tibble one row per combination resample fold hyperparameter setting, including metric value (ROC AUC). last_fit() refits workflow full training set chosen hyperparameters evaluates held-test set one call.

heading: 14.446 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “tidymodels random-forest workflow 5-fold stratified cross-validation selected mtry = 2, min_n = 5 (best CV ROC AUC 0.81 across 6-point grid); held-test set final model achieved ROC AUC 0.79, honest generalisation estimate.” Always state resampling scheme, tuning metric, test-set performance.

heading: 14.447 Practical Tips
text: Use workflow() rather direct fit() calls; workflow ensures preprocessing applied consistently prediction time without leaking test-set information transformations.last_fit() refits full training set evaluates test set single command; cleanest pattern final-model step.Stratify splits CV folds outcome (strata = y) imbalanced classification; otherwise rare classes can missing folds.hardhat::extract_workflow(), extract_fit_engine(), extract_recipe() retrieve internals fitted workflow inspection downstream use.Parallelise tuning doParallel::registerDoParallel(cores = 8) future framework; tuning often embarrassingly parallel across resamples hyperparameter combinations.Bayesian hyperparameter optimisation, use tune_bayes() instead tune_grid(); converges optimum fewer evaluations smooth performance landscapes.

heading: 14.451 Introduction
text: train-test split reserves random subset data honest evaluation model generalisation, separating cleanly data used fitting tuning. simplest possible evaluation protocol foundation every supervised-learning workflow. trade-straightforward: larger test set gives stable error estimates leaves less data fitting; small datasets, cross-validation extracts value available data single fixed split.

heading: 14.452 Prerequisites

heading: 14.453 Theory
text: \(n\) observations, reserve \(n_{\text{test}}\) test. Typical ratios 80/20, 70/30, 90/10 depending dataset size. test-set error point estimate generalisation variance \(O(1/n_{\text{test}})\) — stable error estimates test set least hundred observations.Stratified splits sample within class class proportions match train test; essential imbalanced classification, random sampling can leave minority class severely - -represented either fold.Grouped splits keep observations subject, site, sample together prevent leakage; ignoring grouping exists silently inflates performance estimates.

heading: 14.454 Assumptions
text: Observation pairs \((x_i, y_i)\) independent identically distributed (dependence structure respected chosen split scheme); deployment distribution matches training distribution.

heading: 14.455 R Implementation
code:
library(rsample); library(dplyr)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 1000
df <- tibble(x = rnorm(n),
             y = factor(sample(c("pos", "neg"), n,
                               replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.1, 0.9))))

# Random 80/20 split
split1 <- initial_split(df, prop = 0.8)

# Stratified split preserving y proportions
split2 <- initial_split(df, prop = 0.8, strata = y)

tbl_noStrata <- table(training(split1)$y) / nrow(training(split1))
tbl_strata   <- table(training(split2)$y) / nrow(training(split2))

round(rbind(noStrata = tbl_noStrata, strata = tbl_strata), 3)

heading: 14.462 Introduction
text: Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017) replaced recurrent networks dominant sequence model. core mechanism self-attention: token attends every token sequence learned weights. Transformers underpin BERT, GPT, T5, modern LLMs, expanded vision (ViT), audio (wav2vec2), tabular domains.

heading: 14.463 Prerequisites
text: Neural networks; attention concept; tokenisation basics.

heading: 14.464 Theory
text:
Scaled dot-product attention:
\[\text{Attn}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V,\]
\(Q, K, V\) query, key, value projections input.Multi-head attention runs \(h\) parallel attention heads concatenates outputs.Transformer block = multi-head self-attention + feedforward + residual + layer norm, stacked many times.Positional encoding injects sequence order (sinusoidal learned).

heading: 14.465 Assumptions
text: Sufficient data (transformers data-hungry); proper tokenisation (BPE WordPiece text); positional encoding matches task.

heading: 14.466 R Implementation
code:
library(torch)

torch_manual_seed(2026)

# Minimal transformer block: multi-head attention + feedforward
block <- nn_module(
  initialize = function(d_model = 32, n_heads = 4, d_ff = 64) {
    self$mha <- nn_multihead_attention(embed_dim = d_model,
                                       num_heads = n_heads,
                                       batch_first = TRUE)
    self$ln1 <- nn_layer_norm(d_model)
    self$ff  <- nn_sequential(nn_linear(d_model, d_ff),
                              nn_relu(),
                              nn_linear(d_ff, d_model))
    self$ln2 <- nn_layer_norm(d_model)
  },
  forward = function(x) {
    attn <- self$mha(x, x, x)[[1]]
    x <- self$ln1(x + attn)
    x <- self$ln2(x + self$ff(x))
    x
  }
)

model <- block()
seq <- torch_randn(2, 10, 32)        # batch=2, seq=10, d_model=32
out <- model(seq)
out$shape                             # [2, 10, 32]

heading: 14.467 Output & Results
text: Output sequence shape input; block transforms representations letting token attend full context.

heading: 14.468 Interpretation
text: “single transformer block 4-head self-attention feedforward sublayers transformed (10-token, 32-dim) sequence. Stacking 6-48 blocks gives modern NLP models; pretrained backbones (BERT, RoBERTa) practical starting point.”

heading: 14.469 Practical Tips
text: Transformers need lot data; small-data tasks, fine-tune pretrained backbone rather training scratch.Use efficient attention variants (FlashAttention, sparse attention) long sequences.Masking (causal GPT, padding BERT) essential; forgetting silently breaks training.Positional encoding: sinusoidal classical transformers, rotary (RoPE) modern LLMs.R, practical transformer pipelines often call HuggingFace via reticulate; torch R implements building blocks lacks huge pretrained-model ecosystem.

heading: 14.472 Introduction
text: Variable importance ranks features contribution model accuracy. common first-pass interpretability tool black-box models. Tree ensembles offer two native measures: impurity-based (mean decrease Gini variance) permutation-based (drop accuracy feature shuffled).

heading: 14.473 Prerequisites
text: Decision trees; random forests; cross-validation.

heading: 14.474 Theory
text: Impurity importance: sum impurity decrease splits using feature \(j\). Biased toward high-cardinality features correlated features.Permutation importance: shuffle feature \(j\) held-data; measure drop performance. Unbiased correlated features group can split importance correlated pairs.Conditional permutation SHAP values handle correlation rigorously (higher cost).

heading: 14.475 Assumptions
text: Sufficient held-data permutation; features correctly treated continuous categorical.

heading: 14.476 R Implementation
code:
library(ranger); library(vip)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 500
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n), x3 = rnorm(n),
  x4 = rnorm(n), x5 = rnorm(n),
  y  = sin(rnorm(n)) + 0.3 * rnorm(n)^2
)

rf <- ranger(y ~ ., data = df, num.trees = 500,
             importance = "permutation")
imp <- sort(rf$variable.importance, decreasing = TRUE)
print(round(imp, 3))

vip(rf, num_features = 5) +
  ggplot2::labs(title = "Permutation importance (ranger RF)")

heading: 14.477 Output & Results
text: Sorted importance scores; top feature dominates, others close zero mostly-noise data.

heading: 14.478 Interpretation
text: “Permutation importance identified \(x_1\) dominant predictor (12 % RMSE increase shuffled); features contributed negligibly, consistent data-generating process.”

heading: 14.479 Practical Tips
text: Use permutation importance correctness matters; impurity importance cheap biased.Drop correlated features use grouped importance groups correlated variables share signal.Importance alone reveal direction effect; pair partial dependence SHAP.Normalise importance baseline model error express relative increase.deep learning, use integrated gradients SHAP; RF-style permutation standard.

heading: 14.480 Reporting
text: reporting variable importance, always state flavour used (impurity, permutation, drop-column, SHAP), loss importance measured, whether held-test set training data drove calculation. Permutation importance computed training data overstates apparent role variables model overfits , test-set version defensible default. manuscript, include small bar chart table top features note explicitly importance measure model reliance, causal effect biological mechanism. features correlated, mention shared signal can split importance across columns grouped-permutation summaries may interpretable.
