heading: 14.483 Introduction
text: XGBoost (eXtreme Gradient Boosting), introduced Chen Guestrin 2016, extends classical gradient boosting L1 L2 regularisation leaf weights, second-order Taylor expansion loss, sparsity-aware split finding, histogram-based methods scale billions rows. dominated competitive machine-learning benchmarks structured tabular data nearly decade remains strongest single --shelf model regression classification problems tables.

heading: 14.484 Prerequisites
text: working understanding gradient boosting, decision trees, regularisation (L1/L2 penalties), cross-validation hyperparameter tuning.

heading: 14.485 Theory
text: XGBoost objective \[\mathcal L = \sum_i L(y_i, \hat y_i) + \sum_k \Omega(f_k), \qquad \Omega(f) = \gamma T + \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda \|w\|^2,\]\(L\) per-observation loss (squared error, log-loss, etc.), \(f_k\) \(k\)-th regression tree, \(T\) number leaves, \(w\) leaf weights, \(\gamma\) penalises tree complexity, \(\lambda\) regularises leaf weights. second-order Taylor expansion \(L\) yields analytic optimal leaf weights tractable split score.Key hyperparameters: eta (learning rate), max_depth, min_child_weight, lambda alpha (L2 L1 leaf-weight regularisation), gamma (minimum loss reduction split), subsample (row sampling), colsample_bytree (column sampling tree level).

heading: 14.486 Assumptions
text: Dataset tabular; labels suit chosen objective (binary, multiclass, count, regression); sufficient sample size support tree-based interactions depth.

heading: 14.487 R Implementation
code:
library(xgboost)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 600
X <- matrix(rnorm(n * 5), n, 5)
y <- as.numeric(plogis(X[, 1] + 0.5 * X[, 2]^2) > runif(n))

idx <- sample(n, n * 0.8)
d_tr <- xgb.DMatrix(X[idx, ], label = y[idx])
d_te <- xgb.DMatrix(X[-idx, ], label = y[-idx])

params <- list(objective = "binary:logistic", eta = 0.05,
               max_depth = 4, subsample = 0.8,
               colsample_bytree = 0.8,
               eval_metric = "logloss")

bst <- xgb.train(params = params, data = d_tr,
                 nrounds = 500,
                 watchlist = list(val = d_te),
                 early_stopping_rounds = 20,
                 verbose = 0)

cat("Best iter:", bst$best_iteration,
    "  best logloss:", round(bst$best_score, 4), "\n")

xgb.importance(model = bst)[1:5, ]

heading: 14.488 Output & Results
text: xgb.train() returns fitted xgb.Booster early-stopping iteration count, best validation metric, tree structure. xgb.importance() ranks features gain (average loss reduction attributed splits feature), cover, frequency.

heading: 14.489 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “XGBoost eta = 0.05, max_depth = 4, row column subsampling 0.8, converged 217 boosting rounds early stopping 20-round patience; validation log-loss 0.38, ROC AUC 0.92. Feature-gain importance identified \(x_1\) \(x_2\) strongest contributors, consistent simulated data-generating process.” Always state hyperparameters, early-stopping settings, validation metrics.

heading: 14.490 Practical Tips
text: Always use early stopping (early_stopping_rounds) avoid -training; without , XGBoost trains nrounds regardless whether validation loss plateaued.Choose objective match target: "reg:squarederror" regression, "binary:logistic" binary classification, "multi:softprob" multiclass, "count:poisson" counts, "survival:cox" time--event.Tune Bayesian optimisation (ParBayesianOptimization, tune::tune_bayes()) rather grid search; XGBoost many correlated knobs grid search wastes evaluations.tree_method = "hist" accelerates training 5–10× large data; "gpu_hist" adds another order magnitude GPU.Monotone constraints (monotone_constraints) enforce domain-knowledge directions predictor-outcome relationships; useful regulated industries (credit risk, healthcare).interpretation beyond feature importance, use SHAP values (xgboost::predict(..., predcontrib = TRUE) shapviz package); SHAP gives per-prediction explanations.

heading: 14.497 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(glmnet)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 14.498 1. Goal
text:
Estimate generalisation error lasso moderate p, small
n simulated regression problem, first naively, proper
nested cross-validation.

heading: 14.499 2. Approach
text:
simulate n = 120 observations p = 40 features, 
5 true signal. () fit single cv.glmnet, (ii)
report CV-minimising error, (iii) wrap outer 5-fold
loop show inner CV error optimistic.
code:
X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p)
beta <- c(rep(1.5, 5), rep(0, p - 5))
y <- as.numeric(X %*% beta + rnorm(n, sd = 1.0))

tibble(yhat_naive = as.numeric(X %*% beta), y = y) |>
  ggplot(aes(yhat_naive, y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "grey50") +
  labs(x = "True linear predictor", y = "Observed y")

heading: 14.500 3. Execution
text:
Naive single-layer CV.Nested CV. inner cv.glmnet picks lambda; outer 5 folds
produce reported MSE.
code:
naive_mse <- min(cv1$cvm)
naive_mse
folds <- sample(rep(1:K, length.out = n))
outer_mse <- numeric(K)
for (k in 1:K) {
  tr <- folds != k; te <- folds == k
  fit_k <- cv.glmnet(X[tr, ], y[tr], alpha = 1, nfolds = 5)
  yhat  <- as.numeric(predict(fit_k, newx = X[te, ], s = "lambda.min"))
  outer_mse[k] <- mean((y[te] - yhat)^2)
}
nested_mse <- mean(outer_mse)
c(naive = naive_mse, nested = nested_mse)

heading: 14.501 4. Check
text: Bootstrap .632+ error cross-check.
code:
errs_in <- errs_oob <- numeric(B)
for (b in 1:B) {
  idx <- sample.int(n, n, replace = TRUE)
  oob <- setdiff(seq_len(n), unique(idx))
  fit_b <- cv.glmnet(X[idx, ], y[idx], alpha = 1, nfolds = 5)
  yhat_in  <- as.numeric(predict(fit_b, newx = X[idx, ],  s = "lambda.min"))
  yhat_oob <- as.numeric(predict(fit_b, newx = X[oob, ],  s = "lambda.min"))
  errs_in[b]  <- mean((y[idx] - yhat_in)^2)
  errs_oob[b] <- mean((y[oob] - yhat_oob)^2)
}
err_app <- mean(errs_in); err_oob <- mean(errs_oob)
err_632 <- 0.368 * err_app + 0.632 * err_oob
c(apparent = err_app, oob = err_oob, boot632 = err_632,
  nested = nested_mse)

heading: 14.502 5. Report
text:

heading: 14.510 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 14.511 1. Goal
text:
Simulate 1 000 tests, null alternative, compare
uncorrected, Bonferroni, BH discovery sets.

heading: 14.512 2. Approach
code:
m1 <- 50
is_alt <- c(rep(TRUE, m1), rep(FALSE, m - m1))
mu <- ifelse(is_alt, 3, 0)
z <- rnorm(m, mu, 1)
p <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(abs(z)))

tibble(p = p, is_alt = is_alt) |>
  ggplot(aes(p, fill = is_alt)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, position = "identity", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(x = "p-value", y = "count")

heading: 14.513 3. Execution
code:
bh   <- p.adjust(p, method = "BH")

res <- tibble(
  method = c("raw p<0.05", "Bonferroni<0.05", "BH q<0.05"),
  discovered  = c(sum(p < 0.05), sum(bonf < 0.05), sum(bh < 0.05)),
  true_pos    = c(sum(p[is_alt] < 0.05),
                  sum(bonf[is_alt] < 0.05),
                  sum(bh[is_alt] < 0.05)),
  false_pos   = c(sum(p[!is_alt] < 0.05),
                  sum(bonf[!is_alt] < 0.05),
                  sum(bh[!is_alt] < 0.05))
) |> mutate(fdp = false_pos / pmax(discovered, 1))
res

heading: 14.514 4. Check
text: Knockoff discussion — idea, words.
code:
library(knockoff)
# For a linear regression y ~ X with X gaussian, construct
# Xk matching the second-order structure of X but conditionally
# independent of y given X. Then fit a penalised regression on
# [X, Xk]; the per-feature statistic W_j = |beta_j| - |beta_{j+p}|
# controls the FDR among selected features.
ko <- knockoff.filter(X, y, fdr = 0.1)
ko$selected

cut-0.05 produced res$discovered[1] discoveries 
false-discovery proportion round(res$fdp[1], 2). Bonferroni
correction produced res$discovered[2] discoveries; BH
procedure q < 0.05 produced res$discovered[3] 
false-discovery proportion round(res$fdp[3], 2).Knockoffs give similar FDR guarantee via different route,
remain valid correlation features provided
joint distribution can modelled.

library(tidyverse)

heading: 14.524 1. Goal

inside trained model.

heading: 14.525 2. Approach
text: Create toy 16×16 image bright square.
code:
img[5:10, 5:10] <- 1
img <- img + matrix(rnorm(256, 0, 0.1), 16, 16)

image(t(img[16:1, ]), col = grey.colors(100), main = "toy image")

heading: 14.526 3. Execution
text: manual 3×3 edge filter convolved image.1-D convolution toy sequence.tiny CNN torch (sketch ).1-D convolutional sequence model sketch.
code:
conv2d <- function(img, k) {
  kh <- nrow(k); kw <- ncol(k)
  out <- matrix(0, nrow(img) - kh + 1, ncol(img) - kw + 1)
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(out))) for (j in seq_len(ncol(out)))
    out[i, j] <- sum(img[i:(i + kh - 1), j:(j + kw - 1)] * k)
  out
}
feat <- conv2d(img, kernel)
image(t(feat[nrow(feat):1, ]), col = grey.colors(100),
      main = "edge-filter feature map")
conv1d <- function(x, k) {
  out <- numeric(length(x) - length(k) + 1)
  for (i in seq_along(out)) out[i] <- sum(x[i:(i + length(k) - 1)] * k)
  out
}
feat1d <- conv1d(seq1, c(-1, 1))
tibble(i = seq_along(feat1d), v = feat1d) |>
  ggplot(aes(i, v)) + geom_line() +
  labs(x = "position", y = "edge response")
library(torch)
cnn <- nn_sequential(
  nn_conv2d(1, 8, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1),
  nn_relu(),
  nn_max_pool2d(2),
  nn_conv2d(8, 16, kernel_size = 3, padding = 1),
  nn_relu(),
  nn_max_pool2d(2),
  nn_flatten(),
  nn_linear(16 * 4 * 4, 10)
)
library(torch)
seq_model <- nn_sequential(
  nn_conv1d(1, 8, kernel_size = 5, padding = 2),
  nn_relu(),
  nn_adaptive_avg_pool1d(1),
  nn_flatten(),
  nn_linear(8, 1)
)

heading: 14.562 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 14.563 1. Goal
text:
Fit small regression manual gradient descent, show 
equivalent torch code #| eval: false.

heading: 14.564 2. Approach
code:
X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p)
beta <- c(2, -1, 0.5, rep(0, p - 3))
y <- as.numeric(X %*% beta + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5))
tibble(i = seq_len(n), y = y) |>
  ggplot(aes(i, y)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 0.6)

heading: 14.565 3. Execution
text: Base-R gradient descent problem.equivalent torch code.
code:
losses <- numeric(200)
for (step in 1:200) {
  yhat <- X %*% w
  resid <- as.numeric(y - yhat)
  grad <- -2 * t(X) %*% resid / n
  w <- w - lr * grad
  losses[step] <- mean(resid^2)
}
tibble(step = 1:200, loss = losses) |>
  ggplot(aes(step, loss)) + geom_line() +
  labs(x = "iteration", y = "mean squared error")
library(torch)

x_t <- torch_tensor(X, dtype = torch_float())
y_t <- torch_tensor(matrix(y, ncol = 1), dtype = torch_float())

net <- nn_sequential(
  nn_linear(p, 16), nn_relu(),
  nn_linear(16, 1)
)
optimizer <- optim_adam(net$parameters, lr = 1e-2)
loss_fn <- nn_mse_loss()

for (epoch in 1:200) {
  optimizer$zero_grad()
  pred <- net(x_t)
  loss <- loss_fn(pred, y_t)
  loss$backward()
  optimizer$step()
}

  geom_point() + geom_line(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(x = "feature index", y = "coefficient")

heading: 14.575 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(MASS)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 14.576 1. Goal
text:
Fit elastic-net logistic regression predict diabetes status 
Pima.tr, using proper tidymodels pipeline.

heading: 14.577 2. Approach
code: ggplot(d, aes(glu, bmi, colour = type)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.7)

heading: 14.578 3. Execution
text: Split, recipe, model, workflow, tune.
code:
d_train <- training(d_split); d_test <- testing(d_split)
folds <- vfold_cv(d_train, v = 5, strata = type)

rec <- recipe(type ~ ., data = d_train) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

mod <- logistic_reg(penalty = tune(), mixture = tune()) |>
  set_engine("glmnet")

wf  <- workflow() |> add_recipe(rec) |> add_model(mod)

grid <- grid_regular(penalty(), mixture(), levels = 5)
res  <- tune_grid(wf, resamples = folds, grid = grid,
                  metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, accuracy))
collect_metrics(res) |>
  filter(.metric == "roc_auc") |>
  arrange(desc(mean)) |>
  head(5)

heading: 14.579 4. Check
text: Select best, finalise, evaluate test set.
code:
wf_final <- finalize_workflow(wf, best)
fit_final <- fit(wf_final, data = d_train)
preds <- predict(fit_final, d_test, type = "prob") |>
  bind_cols(predict(fit_final, d_test), d_test)
roc_auc(preds, truth = type, .pred_Yes, event_level = "second")
accuracy(preds, truth = type, estimate = .pred_class)
  autoplot()

heading: 14.580 5. Report
text:
elastic-net logistic regression tuned 5-fold CV, using 
5×5 grid penalty mixture, achieved test ROC-AUC 
round(roc_auc(preds, truth = type, .pred_Yes, event_level = "second")$.estimate, 3)
MASS::Pima.tr.pipeline pattern — recipe + workflow + tune + finalise — 
template reuse every predictive-model lab hereafter.

  data = as.matrix(d[, -1]), label = d$mpg,
  nrounds = 100, eta = 0.05, max_depth = 3,
  objective = "reg:squarederror", verbose = 0
)

heading: 14.592 4. Check
text: Honest error: CV tree xgboost, OOB ranger.
code:
folds <- sample(rep(1:K, length.out = nrow(d)))
mse <- function(y, yhat) mean((y - yhat)^2)

get_err <- function() {
  err_tree <- err_rf <- err_xg <- numeric(K)
  for (k in 1:K) {
    tr <- folds != k; te <- folds == k
    f_tr <- d[tr, ]; f_te <- d[te, ]
    t1 <- rpart(mpg ~ ., data = f_tr, cp = 0.01)
    r1 <- ranger(mpg ~ ., data = f_tr, num.trees = 500)
    x1 <- xgboost(
      data = as.matrix(f_tr[, -1]), label = f_tr$mpg,
      nrounds = 100, eta = 0.05, max_depth = 3,
      objective = "reg:squarederror", verbose = 0
    )
    err_tree[k] <- mse(f_te$mpg, predict(t1, f_te))
    err_rf[k]   <- mse(f_te$mpg, predict(r1, f_te)$predictions)
    err_xg[k]   <- mse(f_te$mpg, predict(x1, as.matrix(f_te[, -1])))
  }
  c(tree = mean(err_tree), rf = mean(err_rf), xgb = mean(err_xg))
}
errs <- get_err()
errs       imp = as.numeric(rf_fit$variable.importance)) |>
  arrange(desc(imp)) |>
  ggplot(aes(reorder(var, imp), imp)) + geom_col() +
  coord_flip() + labs(x = NULL, y = "permutation importance")

importance random forest identified weight displacement

heading: 15.3 Introduction
text: Adaptive designs allow pre-specified modifications – sample size, randomisation ratios, treatment-arm selection – based data accrued trial. promise efficiency require careful statistical control preserve Type error. FDA EMA provide detailed guidance acceptable adaptive modifications.

heading: 15.4 Prerequisites
text: Group-sequential trials; alpha spending; sample-size calculation.

heading: 15.5 Theory
text:
Common adaptive features:
- Sample-size re-estimation (blinded unblinded).
- Early stopping efficacy futility.
- Arm selection (drop inferior arms multi-arm trials).
- Response-adaptive randomisation (shift allocation toward effective arms).
- Population enrichment (restrict responsive subgroup).Maintaining Type error pre-specified adaptations requires methods like group-sequential boundaries, combination tests, conditional error functions.

heading: 15.6 Assumptions
text: Adaptation rules fully pre-specified (protocol, SAP); unblinded information access tightly controlled (Data Monitoring Committee); adjustments statistically valid.

heading: 15.7 R Implementation
code:
library(rpact)

# Group-sequential design with O'Brien-Fleming boundary, 3 analyses
design <- getDesignGroupSequential(
  sided = 2, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.2,
  typeOfDesign = "OF",
  informationRates = c(0.5, 0.75, 1)
)
kable_summary <- summary(design)
print(design)

# Plan sample size for a two-arm trial with continuous outcome
ssr <- getSampleSizeMeans(design = design,
                          alternative = 0.3, stDev = 1)
print(ssr)

heading: 15.8 Output & Results
text: Group-sequential boundaries associated sample sizes per stage; cumulative Type error preserved alpha.

heading: 15.9 Interpretation
text: “adaptive design applied O’Brien-Fleming boundaries 50 %, 75 %, 100 % information; stage-1 interim inefficacy boundary stop p > 0.0002, preserving overall alpha = 0.025.”

heading: 15.10 Practical Tips
text: Pre-specify every adaptation (including decision rule) protocol.FDA/EMA require detailed justification adaptive feature operating characteristics.Independent data monitoring committee essential efficacy futility stopping.Simulation often used verify operating characteristics; report .Post-hoc adaptations (“seamless” trial extensions) exploratory, confirmatory.

heading: 15.13 Introduction
text: Alpha-spending functions (Lan & DeMets, 1983) allow interim analyses unscheduled times preserving overall Type error. spending function \(f(t)\) specifies cumulative alpha budget information fraction \(t \[0, 1]\); nominal alpha analysis increment \(f(t_k) - f(t_{k-1})\).

heading: 15.14 Prerequisites
text: Group-sequential designs; information fraction.

heading: 15.15 Theory
text:
Common spending functions:
- -type: \(f(t) = 2 - 2\Phi(z_{\alpha/2} / \sqrt{t})\). Approximates boundary.
- Pocock-type: \(f(t) = \alpha \log(1 + (e - 1) t)\). Approximates Pocock.
- Power family: \(f(t) = \alpha t^\rho\) \(\rho > 0\).
- Custom: non-decreasing \(f\) \(f(0) = 0, f(1) = \alpha\).Flexibility: interim analyses can occur arbitrary information fractions, re-solving boundary time.

heading: 15.16 Assumptions
text: Information fractions known (approximately); stopping rule applied specified; test statistic normal.

heading: 15.17 R Implementation
code:
library(rpact)

# OF spending, flexible information fractions
design <- getDesignGroupSequential(
  sided = 1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.2,
  typeOfDesign = "asOF",          # alpha-spending OF-type
  informationRates = c(0.4, 0.75, 1)
)

print(design$stageLevels)
plot(design, type = 1)

# Compare spending functions
f_of  <- getDesignGroupSequential(sided = 1, alpha = 0.025,
                                  typeOfDesign = "asOF",
                                  informationRates = seq(0.1, 1, 0.1))
f_poc <- getDesignGroupSequential(sided = 1, alpha = 0.025,
                                  typeOfDesign = "asP",
                                  informationRates = seq(0.1, 1, 0.1))
cbind(OF = cumsum(f_of$stageLevels),
      Pocock = cumsum(f_poc$stageLevels))

heading: 15.18 Output & Results
text: Cumulative alpha information fraction spending families; delays alpha consumption, Pocock distributes earlier.

heading: 15.19 Interpretation
text: “alpha-spending design information fractions 0.4, 0.75, 1.0 -type spending allocated cumulative alpha 0.001, 0.013, 0.025 respectively, enabling flexibility interim timing without inflating Type error.”

heading: 15.20 Practical Tips
text: Alpha spending standard modern confirmatory group-sequential trials.Re-estimate information fractions interim accrual deviates plan.Information usually subject-count continuous outcomes, event-count time--event.Never spend alpha planned cumulative function current information fraction.futility, use separate beta-spending functions (non-binding boundaries standard).

heading: 15.23 Introduction
text: Analysis Covariance (ANCOVA) regresses outcome treatment baseline value jointly. Compared naive change--baseline t-test, ANCOVA gains precision, handles regression mean correctly, reduces SE roughly factor \(\sqrt{1 - \rho^2}\) \(\rho\) baseline-outcome correlation.

heading: 15.24 Prerequisites
text: Linear regression; correlation; change scores.

heading: 15.25 Theory
text: Naive change-score analysis: compare \(\Delta = Y_{\text{post}} - Y_{\text{baseline}}\) across arms. Unbiased randomisation less efficient ANCOVA baseline correlates outcome.ANCOVA: \(Y_{\text{post}} = \alpha + \beta_{\text{trt}} \cdot T + \beta_{\text{base}} \cdot Y_{\text{baseline}} + \varepsilon\). Treatment effect \(\beta_{\text{trt}}\) lower SE change-score test.Regression mean: groups different baselines chance, change scores bias toward difference; ANCOVA corrects .

heading: 15.26 Assumptions
text: Linear relationship baseline outcome; treatment--baseline interaction (common extension: stratify add interaction term).

heading: 15.27 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
baseline <- rnorm(n, 10, 2)
arm      <- factor(rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = n/2))

# Outcome correlated with baseline; true trt effect = 1
outcome <- 0.7 * baseline + ifelse(arm == "trt", 1, 0) +
           rnorm(n, 0, 1)

# Naive change-score analysis
change <- outcome - baseline
t.test(change ~ arm)

# ANCOVA
fit <- lm(outcome ~ arm + baseline)
summary(fit)$coefficients

# SE comparison
sd(change[arm == "trt"] - mean(change[arm == "trt"]))

heading: 15.28 Output & Results
text: ANCOVA estimates treatment effect substantially smaller SE change-score t-test baseline-outcome correlation non-trivial.

heading: 15.29 Interpretation
text: “ANCOVA estimated treatment effect 1.02 (95 % CI 0.74-1.30, p < 0.001) ~40 % lower SE change-score analysis, leveraging 0.7 baseline-outcome correlation.”

heading: 15.30 Practical Tips
text: Use ANCOVA continuous outcome baseline measurement available.Even small baseline-outcome correlation (0.3), ANCOVA improves power.“change baseline” outcome special case ANCOVA \(\beta_{\text{base}} = 1\) forced; ANCOVA free \(\beta\) preferred.Pre-specify baseline adjustment SAP; post-hoc addition risks bias.EMA guidance baseline adjustment: stratification ANCOVA acceptable; ANCOVA efficient baseline continuous.

heading: 15.33 Introduction
text: Bland-Altman plot (1986) compares two measurement methods plotting difference mean. exposes systematic bias, proportional bias, 95 % limits agreement within differences fall. standard graphical summary method-comparison studies largely replaced correlation-based summaries.

heading: 15.34 Prerequisites
text: Paired measurements; method-comparison basics.

heading: 15.35 Theory
text:
paired measurements \((A_i, B_i)\):
- Bias: mean difference \(\bar{d} = \overline{A_i - B_i}\).
- Limits agreement: \(\bar{d} \pm 1.96 \cdot s_d\).95 % differences lie within limits limits clinically acceptable, two methods can used interchangeably. Proportional bias shows trend scatter.

heading: 15.36 Assumptions
text: Differences approximately normal; differences systematically depend mean (check regression); replicates handled appropriately present.

heading: 15.37 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 100
truth <- rnorm(n, 10, 2)
A <- truth + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)            # method A
B <- truth + 0.3 + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)      # method B (small bias)

df <- data.frame(A, B,
                 mean = (A + B) / 2,
                 diff = A - B)

bias <- mean(df$diff)
sd_d <- sd(df$diff)
loa  <- c(bias - 1.96 * sd_d, bias + 1.96 * sd_d)

ggplot(df, aes(mean, diff)) +
  geom_point(colour = "#2A9D8F") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = bias, linetype = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = loa, linetype = 2) +
  labs(x = "Mean of A and B", y = "A - B",
       title = "Bland-Altman plot",
       subtitle = sprintf("Bias %.2f; 95%% LoA [%.2f, %.2f]",
                          bias, loa[1], loa[2])) +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 15.38 Output & Results
text: scatter differences vs means solid bias line dashed LoA; simulated systematic bias -0.3 recovered.

heading: 15.39 Interpretation
text: “Bland-Altman analysis revealed bias -0.3 units 95 % limits agreement (-1.7, 1.1). clinically acceptable limit +-2 units, methods interchangeable practical purposes.”

heading: 15.40 Practical Tips
text: Always show bias LoA; correlation alone reveal systematic bias.Check proportional bias regressing differences means; non-zero slope indicates non-constant bias.replicated measurements per subject, adjust LoA calculation (Bland-Altman 1999 extension).Report clinical acceptance criterion calculating LoA; otherwise post-hoc thresholding biases conclusions.Paired ICC, Bland-Altman gives quantitative visual summary agreement.

heading: 15.43 Introduction
text: Blinding (masking) keeps trial participants personnel unaware treatment assignments minimise performance, assessment, analyst biases. Single, double, triple, quadruple blinding refer stakeholder groups masked; layer blocks specific bias pathway.

heading: 15.44 Prerequisites
text: RCT design; sources bias clinical research.

heading: 15.45 Theory
text: Single blinding: participant unaware; investigator aware.Double blinding: participant investigator unaware. Standard drug vs placebo.Triple blinding: adds blinded outcome assessors (PROBE designs invert ).Quadruple blinding: adds blinded statisticians / analysts.additional layer addresses bias also increases operational complexity.

heading: 15.46 Assumptions
text: Identical appearance, taste, packaging active placebo; emergency unblinding procedures place.

heading: 15.47 R Implementation
text: Blinding operational – statistical analysis per se – success blinding audited.
code:
# Simulate an end-of-study blinding-integrity questionnaire
set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
guess <- factor(sample(c("active", "placebo", "don't know"),
                        n, replace = TRUE,
                        prob = c(0.4, 0.35, 0.25)))
true  <- factor(rep(c("active", "placebo"), each = n/2))

# James blinding index (range 0-1; 0.5 = good blinding)
tab <- table(guess, true)
n <- sum(tab)
# Simpler chi-square test of correct-guess rate
correct <- sum(guess == true)
binom.test(correct, length(guess), p = 0.5)

heading: 15.48 Output & Results
text: Binomial test whether correct-guess rate exceeds chance; p > 0.05 consistent effective blinding.

heading: 15.49 Interpretation
text: “End--study unblinding revealed 56 % correct guesses (95 % CI 49-62 %, p = 0.12 vs chance), consistent successful blinding. study reports result per CONSORT recommendations.”

heading: 15.50 Practical Tips
text: Match active placebo precisely (taste, colour, packaging, schedule); difference leaks information.difficult--blind interventions (surgery, behavioural), blind least outcome assessment.Test blinding study end (e.g., James/Bang blinding index); report result.Pre-specify emergency unblinding procedures; document unblinding events.statistician blinded allocation prevents analysis-choice bias even open-label trials.

heading: 15.53 Introduction
text: Block randomisation allocates clinical-trial participants random permutations within fixed-size blocks, guaranteeing equal numbers subjects treatment arm every block boundary. standard alternative simple randomisation clinical trials prevents substantial arm-size imbalance simple randomisation can produce small trials, early enrolment phases, moment trial paused interim analysis. Block randomisation now mandated strongly recommended ICH-E9 statistical-principles guideline, CONSORT randomisation reporting, virtually every regulatory authority’s clinical-trial guidance.

heading: 15.54 Prerequisites
text: working understanding randomisation foundation causal inference trials, allocation concealment procedural safeguard selection bias, difference simple, blocked, stratified randomisation.

heading: 15.55 Theory
text: block size \(B\) two equally-allocated arms, block contains \(B/2\) assignments arm random order. every block boundary arm counts exactly balanced; boundaries maximum imbalance \(B/2\). fixed block size, investigator knows block size observes \(B - 1\) allocations within block can predict final allocation — serious threat open-label trials. Variable block sizes (mixing, e.g., blocks 4 6) defeat predictability preserving boundary-balance guarantee.multi-arm trials \(k\) arms equal allocation, blocks must multiples \(k\); unequal allocation (e.g., 2 : 1), blocks multiples sum allocation ratios.

heading: 15.56 Assumptions
text: Allocation concealment preserved (randomisation list prepared advance, kept -site, never available enrolling investigators), block-size distribution documented statistical analysis plan withheld exploit , randomisation implemented interactive web-response system (IWRS) equivalent audit trail.

heading: 15.57 R Implementation
code:
library(blockrand)

set.seed(2026)

fix <- blockrand(n = 40, num.levels = 2,
                 levels = c("A", "B"),
                 block.sizes = 2)
table(fix$treatment)
head(fix, 8)

var_b <- blockrand(n = 40, num.levels = 2,
                   levels = c("A", "B"),
                   block.sizes = c(2, 3))
head(var_b, 10)

heading: 15.59 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Treatment allocation used block randomisation variable block sizes 4 6, generated blockrand managed via trial’s interactive web-response system. Allocation stratified site disease severity, blocks nested within strata. block-size distribution documented SAP concealed enrolling investigators throughout trial. Final arm counts balanced (200 arm 400-patient trial).” Always report block-size distribution concealment procedure.

heading: 15.60 Practical Tips
text: Avoid fixed block size alone open-label trials; variable block sizes now de facto standard randomised clinical trials explicitly recommended ICH-E9 prevent end--block predictability without sacrificing balance.Document block-size distribution SAP withhold actual block sequence enrolling investigators; sharing block sequence (even informally) compromises allocation concealment recurring cause CONSORT-cited methodological flaws.stratified designs (site, disease severity, age category), nest blocks within strata stratum maintains independent arm balance; standard practice multicentre trials prevents centre--treatment confounding.large blocks reduce guessability also relax boundary-balance guarantee given moment enrolment; small moderate blocks (2 6 two-arm trials) standard compromise adequately balance trials.Commercial randomisation services (IWRS / IRT) manage list, preserve concealment, provide tamper-proof audit trail; sponsor-led trials cost justified regulatory protection.trials two arms, use stratified blocked randomisation appropriately sized blocks; permuted-block randomisation extends naturally number arms equal unequal allocation ratios.

heading: 15.64 Introduction
text: Cluster-randomised trials (CRTs) randomise groups – clinics, schools, villages – rather individuals. Used intervention must delivered cluster level (implementation, educational campaign) contamination individuals bias standard RCT. Clustering inflates variance must accounted sample-size analysis.

heading: 15.65 Prerequisites
text: Intraclass correlation (ICC); mixed-effects models.

heading: 15.66 Theory
text: Design effect: \(DE = 1 + (m - 1) \rho\), \(m\) average cluster size \(\rho\) ICC. Effective sample size = actual \(N\) / \(DE\). Sample-size calculations inflate \(DE\) relative individually-randomised trials.Analysis accounts clustering via mixed-effects models (random cluster intercept) GEE cluster-robust SE.

heading: 15.67 Assumptions
text: Clusters exchangeable; intervention applied uniformly within cluster; ICC estimate pilot / literature approximately correct.

heading: 15.68 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4); library(lmerTest)

set.seed(2026)
# 20 clusters, avg 15 patients per cluster
n_clusters <- 20
m_per <- 15
cluster <- factor(rep(1:n_clusters, each = m_per))
arm     <- factor(rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = (n_clusters/2) * m_per))
clust_re <- rep(rnorm(n_clusters, 0, 0.8), each = m_per)

y <- clust_re + ifelse(arm == "trt", 0.5, 0) +
     rnorm(n_clusters * m_per, 0, 1)

df <- data.frame(cluster, arm, y)
fit <- lmer(y ~ arm + (1 | cluster), data = df)
summary(fit)$coefficients

# Empirical ICC
vc <- as.data.frame(VarCorr(fit))
icc <- vc$vcov[1] / sum(vc$vcov)
cat("Estimated ICC:", round(icc, 3), "\n")

heading: 15.69 Output & Results
text: Cluster-random-effect-adjusted treatment effect (~0.5) SE accounting clustering; ICC estimate ~0.4.

heading: 15.70 Interpretation
text: “Cluster-randomised analysis estimated 0.49 SD improvement (95 % CI 0.21-0.77, p = 0.001), accounting intra-cluster correlation 0.42 via random-cluster intercept.”

heading: 15.71 Practical Tips
text: Even small ICC (0.01) inflates required sample size substantially; budget accordingly.Pilot data literature usually provides ICC; report planning value observed value.clusters (\(< 30\)), mixed-effects SE underestimates; use Kenward-Roger Satterthwaite DF.Report per CONSORT extension cluster trials; include number clusters, cluster sizes, ICC.Stratified matched-pair cluster designs improve balance cluster count small.

heading: 15.72 Reporting
text: defensible cluster-trial report names unit randomisation, unit analysis, planning ICC, achieved ICC, explains analytical model handles potential mismatch. number clusters thirty, state small-sample correction used standard errors degrees freedom, since naive likelihood-based intervals anti-conservative regime. clusters varied substantially size, mention whether weights applied missing clusters partial cluster dropout handled, differential cluster attrition can bias estimated treatment effect even individual-level missingness modest.

heading: 15.75 Introduction
text: Cohen’s kappa, introduced Jacob Cohen 1960, measures agreement two raters categorical scale, correction agreement expected chance alone. Raw percent agreement can look impressive even reflects coincidence — two raters diagnose 90 % patients healthy agree least 81 % cases purely chance. Kappa subtracts chance baseline, leaving honest measure genuine signal inter-rater agreement. now de facto standard inter-rater reliability nominal categorical outcomes, widely used imaging-rater studies, pathology grading, diagnostic-criteria validation, reliability assessment two raters categorical scale.

heading: 15.76 Prerequisites
text: working understanding categorical data, contingency-table summaries, observed agreement percentage, concept chance-expected agreement independent raters.

heading: 15.77 Theory
text: Cohen’s kappa \[\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e},\]\(p_o\) observed proportion cases two raters agreed \(p_e\) proportion expected chance, computed \(p_e = \sum_k p_{1k} p_{2k}\) marginal proportions. Kappa ranges \(-1\) \(+1\): \(\kappa = 1\) perfect agreement, \(\kappa = 0\) exactly chance-level, negative values indicate systematic disagreement worse chance.Landis Koch (1977) proposed widely used (equally widely criticised) benchmarks: 0.01–0.20 slight, 0.21–0.40 fair, 0.41–0.60 moderate, 0.61–0.80 substantial, 0.81–1.00 almost perfect. thresholds descriptive heuristics, strict cut-offs.

heading: 15.78 Assumptions
text: exactly two raters, categorical scale mutually exclusive exhaustive categories, ratings two raters independent (one see influence ), raters classify set subjects.

heading: 15.79 R Implementation
code:
library(psych)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 100
rater1 <- factor(sample(c("pos", "neg"), n, replace = TRUE))
agree <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.8)
rater2 <- ifelse(agree == 1, as.character(rater1),
                 ifelse(rater1 == "pos", "neg", "pos"))
rater2 <- factor(rater2, levels = levels(rater1))

tab <- table(rater1, rater2)
tab

cohen.kappa(cbind(rater1, rater2))

heading: 15.80 Output & Results
text: cohen.kappa() returns unweighted (weighted, applicable) kappa statistic, standard error, confidence interval. Reporting contingency table alongside kappa value gives readers raw evidence; small imbalanced contingency table can produce surprising kappa behaviour table diagnostic reveals .

heading: 15.81 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Inter-rater agreement binary classification substantial (Cohen’s \(\kappa = 0.58\), 95 % CI 0.41 0.75) per Landis-Koch benchmarks, observed agreement 80 % chance-expected agreement 52 %. contingency table showed approximately balanced marginals (rater 1: 51 % positive; rater 2: 53 % positive), kappa-paradox concern affects skewed-marginal samples apply .” Always report observed agreement, marginals, kappa value together.

heading: 15.82 Practical Tips
text: Kappa depends prevalence balance categories — well-known “kappa paradox”: low-prevalence categories can produce small kappa values even observed agreement high, chance-expected agreement also high. Always report kappa alongside observed agreement marginals readers can diagnose .nominal scales two categories, unweighted kappa treats every disagreement equally; ordinal scales (mild / moderate / severe) use weighted kappa credit partial agreement, quadratic weights conventional default.two raters, use Fleiss’s kappa (generalisation Cohen’s kappa) , rating interval-like scale, intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC); handle multi-rater designs Cohen’s kappa .Confidence intervals kappa via delta method routinely reported psych::cohen.kappa(); bootstrap CIs preferable small samples marginal distributions imbalanced.Distinguish inter-rater reliability (different raters subjects) intra-rater reliability (rater different occasions); can assessed kappa, design inferential implications differ.continuous outcomes use Bland-Altman analysis ICC; kappa appropriate categorical scales misleading applied continuous data dichotomisation.

heading: 15.83 R Packages Used
text: psych::cohen.kappa() canonical Cohen’s weighted kappa confidence intervals; irr::kappa2() irr::kappam.fleiss() alternative interface multi-rater extensions; vcd::Kappa() kappa within vcd contingency-table ecosystem; epibasix kappa epidemiological reporting; DescTools::CohenKappa() fast computation alongside related descriptive statistics.

heading: 15.86 Introduction
text: Conditional power probability rejecting null final analysis given observed interim data assumed treatment effect remainder. standard tool futility stopping: low conditional power indicates trial unlikely succeed even favourable future data.

heading: 15.87 Prerequisites
text: Group-sequential designs; interim analyses; power calculations.

heading: 15.88 Theory
text:
two-sided Z-test fraction \(t\) information, conditional power assumption \(\theta\) 
\[\text{CP}(\theta) = 1 - \Phi\left(\frac{z_{1-\alpha} \sqrt{1} - \sqrt{t} \, Z_t - (1 - t) \theta / \sqrt{V}}{\sqrt{1 - t}}\right)\]
\(Z_t\) observed interim statistic \(V\) information.Typical futility trigger: stop CP(assumed effect) < 20 %.

heading: 15.89 Assumptions
text: Assumed effect remainder trial appropriate (observed, target, conservative); test Z-like.

heading: 15.90 R Implementation
code:
library(rpact)

design <- getDesignGroupSequential(
  sided = 1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.2,
  typeOfDesign = "OF",
  informationRates = c(0.5, 1)
)

# Interim analysis results: observed Z = 0.4 (weak signal)
results <- getDataSet(n1 = c(50, 50), means1 = c(0.1, NA),
                      stDevs1 = c(1, NA), n2 = c(50, 50),
                      means2 = c(0, NA), stDevs2 = c(1, NA))

ana <- getAnalysisResults(design = design, dataInput = results,
                          thetaH0 = 0, stage = 1)

# Conditional power at planned effect (delta = 0.3) and observed trend
cond <- getConditionalPower(ana,
                            nPlanned = c(50, 50),
                            thetaH1 = c(0.1, 0.3))
cond

heading: 15.91 Output & Results
text: Conditional power two assumed future effects; CP planned effect low (say < 20 %), DMC might recommend futility stopping.

heading: 15.92 Interpretation
text: “Interim conditional power originally planned effect 0.18; observed interim trend, 0.11. DMC recommended futility stopping pre-specified < 20 % threshold.”

heading: 15.93 Practical Tips
text: Always pre-specify futility threshold assumed effect SAP.CP observed effect “optimistic” view; CP zero effect conservative view.Predictive power (Bayesian analogue) averages CP posterior effect – often preferred modern trials.Futility boundaries typically non-binding (can overridden DMC) preserve alpha.CP-based futility can save substantial cost otherwise failing trials.

heading: 15.94 Reporting

Quote threshold prospectively recorded statistical analysis plan state whether crossing triggered binding stop recommendation data monitoring committee override. Bayesian predictive power computed, report prior used effect explain prior deemed plausible design stage, since futility decision defensible assumptions feeding .

heading: 15.97 Introduction
text: continuous diagnostic test’s discrimination established, clinical cutpoint must chosen turn continuous scores binary decisions. optimal cutpoint depends trade-sensitivity specificity relative costs false positives vs false negatives.

heading: 15.98 Prerequisites
text: Sensitivity specificity; ROC curves.

heading: 15.99 Theory
text:
Common criteria:
- Youden’s J: maximise \(\text{Sens} + \text{Spec} - 1\). Implicitly assumes equal cost FN FP.
- Closest (0, 1): minimise \(\sqrt{(1 - \text{Sens})^2 + (1 - \text{Spec})^2}\).
- Cost-weighted: minimise \(c_{FN}(1 - \text{Sens}) \cdot p_D + c_{FP}(1 - \text{Spec}) \cdot (1 - p_D)\) \(p_D\) prevalence.
- Target specificity (sensitivity): fix one maximise .

heading: 15.100 Assumptions
text: Target population known prevalence; costs misclassification elicited set convention; cutpoint generalise new sample.

heading: 15.101 R Implementation
code:
library(pROC); library(cutpointr)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
disease <- factor(sample(c(0, 1), n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)))
score   <- rnorm(n, mean = ifelse(disease == 1, 1.2, 0))

roc_obj <- roc(response = disease, predictor = score,
               levels = c("0", "1"), direction = "<")
youden_thr <- coords(roc_obj, "best", best.method = "youden",
                     transpose = FALSE)
youden_thr

# cutpointr package: multiple criteria in one call
cp <- cutpointr(data.frame(score, disease),
                x = score, class = disease,
                method = maximize_metric, metric = youden)
summary(cp)

heading: 15.102 Output & Results
text: Cutpoint Youden’s J associated sensitivity/specificity. Common cutpoint simulation: ~0.6 sensitivity ~0.7 specificity ~0.7.

heading: 15.103 Interpretation
text: “Maximum Youden’s J achieved cutpoint 0.63 (sensitivity 0.72, specificity 0.71). population 40 % prevalence, yields PPV 0.62 NPV 0.80.”

heading: 15.104 Practical Tips
text: Internal cutpoints overfit training data; cross-validate use separate holdout set.Clinical cutpoints stable, rounded meaningful precision, validated prospectively.screening tests, favour sensitivity; confirmatory tests, favour specificity.Report cutpoint downstream metrics (Sens, Spec, PPV, NPV, LR+, LR-).Decision-curve analysis (Vickers-Elkin) incorporates clinical utility across range threshold probabilities.

heading: 15.107 Introduction
text: new diagnostic test proposed, first question : accurately classify disease status reference standard? answer comes several complementary numbers – sensitivity, specificity, positive negative predictive values, likelihood ratios, area ROC curve. answers different question, reporting one alone inadequate.

heading: 15.108 Prerequisites
text: reader understand distinction test result (positive/negative) disease status (truly positive/negative), comfortable 2x2 tables proportions.

heading: 15.109 Theory
text: Given binary test binary gold-standard diagnosis, every study produces 2x2 table:table:Sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN). probability test positive diseased person.Specificity = TN / (TN + FP). probability test negative healthy person.Positive predictive value (PPV) = TP / (TP + FP). probability disease given positive test.Negative predictive value (NPV) = TN / (TN + FN). probability disease given negative test.Positive likelihood ratio (LR+) = sensitivity / (1 - specificity). many times likely positive test diseased versus healthy people.Negative likelihood ratio (LR-) = (1 - sensitivity) / specificity.Sensitivity specificity properties test (approximately) invariant prevalence. Predictive values depend strongly prevalence: test 99% sensitivity 99% specificity still PPV 50% disease prevalence 1%. Likelihood ratios, via Bayes’ theorem, convert pre-test probability post-test probability thus tie test-level quantities clinical reasoning doctor actually .continuous marker, possible threshold produces pair (sensitivity, 1 - specificity). Plotting across thresholds gives receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve. area ROC curve (AUC) probability randomly chosen diseased person higher marker value randomly chosen healthy person – interpretable summary discrimination independent threshold.

heading: 15.110 Assumptions
text: reference standard true gold standard (otherwise sensitivity specificity biased).test evaluated representative spectrum diseased healthy individuals (spectrum bias can inflate apparent accuracy).Test results read blinded reference standard (verification review bias two common threats reporting).

heading: 15.111 R Implementation
text: pROC::roc() constructs ROC object marker values disease labels. auc() ci.auc() give point estimate 95% CI. cutpointr package finds threshold maximises Youden’s index (sensitivity + specificity - 1) reports operating characteristics threshold.
code:
library(pROC)
library(cutpointr)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
disease <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.3)
marker <- ifelse(disease == 1,
                 rnorm(n, mean = 60, sd = 12),
                 rnorm(n, mean = 45, sd = 10))

df <- data.frame(disease = factor(disease, levels = c(0, 1),
                                  labels = c("healthy", "diseased")),
                 marker = marker)

roc_obj <- roc(df$disease, df$marker,
               levels = c("healthy", "diseased"), direction = "<")

auc(roc_obj)
ci.auc(roc_obj)

plot(roc_obj, print.auc = TRUE, ci = TRUE)

cp <- cutpointr(df, marker, disease,
                pos_class = "diseased",
                method = maximize_metric,
                metric = youden)
summary(cp)
plot(cp)

heading: 15.112 Output & Results
text: simulated example gives AUC approximately 0.85 (95% CI 0.79 0.90). Youden-optimal cutoff around 52, sensitivity ~0.75 specificity ~0.80 threshold.

heading: 15.113 Interpretation
text: manuscript table report sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, LR+, LR-, AUC, 95% confidence intervals. binary test:“Sensitivity 75% (95% CI 66-83%), specificity 80% (74-86%), PPV 62% (52-72%), NPV 88% (82-93%), LR+ 3.8 (2.7-5.2), LR- 0.31 (0.22-0.44), AUC 0.85 (0.79-0.90).”Crucially, PPV depends disease prevalence study population. intended clinical use lower-prevalence setting, report projected PPV prevalence using Bayes’ theorem.

heading: 15.114 Practical Tips
text: Always report reference standard explicitly justify gold standard.Report sensitivity specificity predictive values, instead . Predictive values clinician uses; sensitivity specificity test provides.Use 95% Wilson Clopper-Pearson intervals proportions, Wald interval, can extend outside \([0, 1]\) poor coverage near 0 1.Avoid choosing threshold data report performance; hold validation set use cross-validation.Follow STARD reporting guidelines: flow diagram, blinding, reference-standard description, thresholds, indeterminate results.

heading: 15.117 Introduction
text: Clinical equivalence trials test whether two treatments clinically interchangeable within pre-specified equivalence margin — narrow enough difference inside margin regarded clinically meaningless. Bioequivalence studies, used support generic-drug approval, archetypal application: regulators require ratio pharmacokinetic parameters generic reference product fall within 80 % 125 % unity, demonstrating generic delivers essentially exposure originator. Equivalence frameworks also apply clinical contexts two interventions competing safety convenience investigator wishes show “clinically meaningful difference” rather usual “test product better”.

heading: 15.118 Prerequisites
text: working understanding non-inferiority trial design, confidence-interval logic, distinction absence evidence (high \(p\)-value) evidence absence (CI within equivalence margin).

heading: 15.119 Theory
text: two one-sided tests (TOST) procedure rejects null non-equivalence two-sided 90 % confidence interval treatment effect lies entirely within equivalence margin \([-\Delta, +\Delta]\). equivalent two one-sided tests, \(\alpha = 0.05\), lower upper non-equivalence boundaries; overall type-error preserved 0.05 one two boundaries can violated single state world.bioequivalence log-transformed pharmacokinetic parameters \(\mathrm{AUC}\) \(C_{\max}\), ratio \(\mu_T / \mu_R\) must lie within \((0.80, 1.25)\), corresponding \(\pm \log(1.25) \approx \pm 0.223\) natural-log scale. Log-transformation mandated regulators makes test--reference ratio symmetric around unity renders inference Normal-theory tractable.

heading: 15.120 Assumptions
text: outcome (typically log-transformed pharmacokinetic parameter) approximately Normal, design crossover adequate washout eliminate carryover, within-subject variance reasonably estimated, observations independent across subjects.

heading: 15.121 R Implementation
code:
library(PowerTOST)

n <- sampleN.TOST(alpha = 0.05, targetpower = 0.80,
                  theta0 = 0.95,
                  theta1 = 0.80, theta2 = 1.25,
                  CV = 0.20,
                  design = "2x2")
n

set.seed(2026)
n_sub <- 30
subject_re <- rnorm(n_sub, 0, 0.15)
period1 <- exp(subject_re + rnorm(n_sub, 0, 0.1))
period2 <- exp(subject_re + log(0.95) + rnorm(n_sub, 0, 0.1))

log_diff <- log(period1) - log(period2)
m  <- mean(log_diff); sd_d <- sd(log_diff)
ci <- m + c(-1, 1) * qt(0.95, df = n_sub - 1) * sd_d / sqrt(n_sub)
exp(ci)

heading: 15.122 Output & Results
text: sampleN.TOST() returns sample size required achieve target power bioequivalence hypothesis specified true ratio within-subject coefficient variation. simulation block computes 90 % confidence interval test--reference ratio, regulatory-relevant inference. Reporting point ratio CI standard expected FDA EMA.

heading: 15.123 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “90 % confidence interval test--reference ratio 0.92 1.08, fully within regulatory bioequivalence window 0.80 1.25 AUC \(C_{\max}\). geometric mean ratio 1.00, within-subject coefficient variation 18 %. Bioequivalence therefore demonstrated standard FDA EMA criteria; formal TOST procedure rejected non-equivalence boundaries \(\alpha = 0.05\).” Always report 90 % CI back-transformed scale.

heading: 15.124 Practical Tips
text: Always analyse log-transformed PK parameters rather raw values; log-ratios symmetric around unity, regulatory equivalence window translates symmetric range log scale, Normal-theory inference tractable log scale.Use 90 % confidence interval, 95 %; TOST procedure \(\alpha = 0.05\) corresponds exactly two-sided 90 % CI lying entirely within equivalence margin, regulatory standard.bioequivalence margin (0.80 1.25) fixed FDA EMA regulation; clinical equivalence margins non-PK outcomes must pre-specified justified clinically, equivalence claim hinges entirely margin width.Reference-scaled bioequivalence (RSABE) used highly variable drugs within-subject CV 30 %; equivalence margin widened proportionally reference-product variability, preserving statistical feasibility inherently variable products.Replicate crossover designs (subject receives test reference twice) reduce within-subject variance improve efficiency; standard highly variable drugs increasingly default modern bioequivalence trials.Pre-specify equivalence margin, washout period, analysis model protocol; FDA EMA scrutinise choices closely, post-hoc margin selection grounds rejection.

heading: 15.128 Introduction
text: Factorial trials test two interventions single experiment: participants randomised factor independently, producing 2x2 (higher) cells. design efficient interventions act independently (interaction); otherwise interaction becomes primary finding.

heading: 15.129 Prerequisites
text: ANOVA; interactions.

heading: 15.130 Theory
text: 2x2 design, four cells: control, , B , + B. Main effects estimated averaging factor; interaction compares observed + B effect sum individual effects.interaction negligible, factorial efficient: power two separate trials half total sample size.

heading: 15.131 Assumptions
text: Treatments interact (interaction inferential target); randomisation factor independently; outcome measured identical conditions across cells.

heading: 15.132 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
n_per_cell <- 40
A <- factor(rep(c("no", "yes"), each = 2 * n_per_cell))
B <- factor(rep(rep(c("no", "yes"), each = n_per_cell), 2))

# Simulate additive effects, mild positive interaction
y <- rnorm(4 * n_per_cell) +
     ifelse(A == "yes", 0.5, 0) +
     ifelse(B == "yes", 0.3, 0) +
     ifelse(A == "yes" & B == "yes", 0.2, 0)

fit <- lm(y ~ A * B)
summary(fit)$coefficients
anova(fit)

heading: 15.133 Output & Results
text: Main-effect estimates B plus interaction term. interaction small, consistent simulated +0.2.

heading: 15.134 Interpretation
text: “factorial analysis estimated main effect = 0.48, B = 0.31, small positive interaction (0.22, p = 0.14). Main-effect analyses interpretable absence significant interaction.”

heading: 15.135 Practical Tips
text: Pre-specify interaction test interpretation; non-significant test guarantee additivity.Factorial trials -powered interactions unless specifically designed .Report main effects (averaged factor) cell means.Partial factorial (‘unbalanced’) designs drop problematic cells – useful certain combinations unethical impractical.> 3 factors, fractional factorial designs (Taguchi) reduce cell count cost confounding higher-order interactions.

heading: 15.138 Introduction
text: Subgroup forest plots display, single figure, treatment effect estimate confidence interval pre-specified subgroup clinical trial alongside overall effect. standard tool visually communicating effect modification — whether intervention works differently men women, older younger patients, mild severe disease — now near-universal element CONSORT-compliant trial reporting. compact horizontal-error-bar layout makes magnitude precision subgroup-specific effect immediately legible, vertical reference line null anchors interpretation. plot’s strength also risk: readers eye-ball heterogeneity glance may infer effect modification formal interaction test support .

heading: 15.139 Prerequisites
text: working understanding pre-specified subgroup analysis, difference within-subgroup tests test interaction, confidence-interval visualisation.

heading: 15.140 Theory
text: row forest plot shows subgroup name, sample sizes per arm, point estimate treatment effect (within-subgroup analogue), 95 % confidence interval horizontal whisker. overall effect — marginal estimate across full trial population — appears top bottom plot reference. vertical line null value (0 differences, 1 ratios) anchors interpretation, formal test interaction (whether effect varies across subgroups beyond chance) reported either figure alongside .

heading: 15.141 Assumptions
text: Subgroups pre-specified trial protocol statistical analysis plan rather chosen post hoc, effect estimates confidence intervals correctly computed subgroup, formal interaction test (rather within-subgroup \(p\)-value comparisons) basis claim effect modification.

heading: 15.142 R Implementation
code:
library(ggplot2); library(dplyr)

set.seed(2026)
subgroups <- data.frame(
  group   = c("Overall", "Male", "Female",
              "Age < 65", "Age >= 65",
              "Mild", "Moderate", "Severe"),
  n1      = c(200, 105, 95, 90, 110, 70, 80, 50),
  n2      = c(200, 98, 102, 92, 108, 72, 79, 49),
  effect  = c(0.30, 0.18, 0.43, 0.22, 0.38, 0.12, 0.32, 0.55),
  lower   = c(0.15, -0.02, 0.21, 0.02, 0.18, -0.14, 0.10, 0.28),
  upper   = c(0.45, 0.38, 0.65, 0.42, 0.58, 0.38, 0.54, 0.82)
)

ggplot(subgroups, aes(y = factor(group, levels = rev(group)),
                      x = effect)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 2, colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper), height = 0.15) +
  geom_point(size = 3, colour = "#2A9D8F") +
  labs(x = "Treatment effect (with 95% CI)", y = NULL,
       title = "Subgroup forest plot") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

heading: 15.143 Output & Results
text: resulting plot vertical list subgroups horizontal confidence intervals referenced null line. Combining column sample sizes interaction-test \(p\)-value column produces publication-ready forest plot. clinical-trial reports also include formal interaction \(p\)-value right figure figure caption.

heading: 15.144 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “forest plot showed treatment effect present across eight pre-specified subgroups; directional heterogeneity observed sex (point estimate 0.43 women vs 0.18 men) disease severity (0.55 severe vs 0.12 mild patients). Formal tests interaction significant (\(p_{\mathrm{sex}} = 0.11\), \(p_{\mathrm{severity}} = 0.07\)), suggesting observed within-subgroup differences may reflect sampling rather genuine effect modification. Subgroups pre-specified SAP.” Always report formal interaction tests, within-subgroup \(p\)-values.

heading: 15.145 Practical Tips
text: Always display subgroup sample sizes alongside row; small subgroup wide CI can look extreme plot carry little weight overall conclusion, readers benefit seeing precision context directly.Order subgroups category (sex, age, disease severity, geographic region) — never point estimate. Post-hoc ordering recurring source biased visual interpretation increasingly flagged trial reviewers.Show overall trial effect prominently, top bottom plot, reference subgroup deviations read; subgroup forest plots without overall reference line difficult interpret.odds ratios, hazard ratios, risk ratios, use logarithmic scale horizontal axis; linear scale, ratios appear asymmetric visual judgements “large” vs “small” effects become misleading.Include formal interaction \(p\)-value figure directly beside ; discourages well-known fallacy comparing within-subgroup \(p\)-values, always -powered routinely yield “significant one subgroup, ” patterns chance alone.Keep number subgroups manageable (typically 5–10); many subgroups crowd plot create false-positive risk multiple comparisons even correct interaction-test reporting.

heading: 15.149 Introduction
text: Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC) quantifies inter-rater reliability continuous measurements. Unlike Pearson’s correlation, penalises systematic rater bias (two raters differ constant still Pearson = 1 ICC < 1). Several ICC forms reflect different study designs questions.

heading: 15.150 Prerequisites
text: Variance components; random vs fixed effects.

heading: 15.151 Theory
text:
Shrout-Fleiss (1979) forms:
- ICC(1, 1): one-way random effects; subject rated different random rater.
- ICC(2, 1): two-way random effects; absolute agreement raters.
- ICC(3, 1): two-way mixed effects; consistency (ignores systematic rater bias).Single rater vs average \(k\) raters: ICC(x, 1) vs ICC(x, k).

heading: 15.152 Assumptions
text: Subjects raters drawn appropriate populations; ratings independent given subject; ICC form matches intended use.

heading: 15.153 R Implementation
code:
library(psych)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 30
subject_re <- rnorm(n, 0, 1)

# 3 raters, each with own systematic bias
r1 <- subject_re + rnorm(n, 0, 0.4)
r2 <- subject_re + 0.3 + rnorm(n, 0, 0.4)    # rater 2 higher by 0.3
r3 <- subject_re - 0.2 + rnorm(n, 0, 0.4)

df <- cbind(r1, r2, r3)

icc_res <- ICC(df, lmer = FALSE)
icc_res$results[, c("type", "ICC", "lower bound", "upper bound")]

heading: 15.154 Output & Results
text: Six ICC forms 95 % CIs. Agreement ICC (2, 1) lower consistency ICC (3, 1) raters systematic biases.

heading: 15.155 Interpretation
text: “Single-rater absolute-agreement ICC(2, 1) = 0.78 (95 % CI 0.63-0.88); consistency ICC(3, 1) = 0.83. Systematic rater differences moderately lowered absolute agreement.”

heading: 15.156 Practical Tips
text:
Choose ICC form study question:
ICC(2, 1) want quantify agreement including systematic rater differences.
ICC(3, 1) remove systematic rater effects practice (e.g., calibrate rater).
ICC(2, 1) want quantify agreement including systematic rater differences.ICC(3, 1) remove systematic rater effects practice (e.g., calibrate rater).clinical usability, average--\(k\)-raters ICCs interpretable.Thresholds (Koo-Li): < 0.5 poor, 0.5-0.75 moderate, 0.75-0.9 good, \(>\) 0.9 excellent.ICC \(<\) 0.7 usually insufficient individual decision-making.Pair ICC Bland-Altman graphical assessment rater-specific bias.

heading: 15.159 Introduction
text: Group-sequential designs schedule sequence interim analyses pre-specified information fractions, efficacy /futility boundaries. Trials can stop early benefit, harm, futility preserving overall Type error. standard confirmatory trials ethical imperatives early stopping.

heading: 15.160 Prerequisites
text: Type error; sample-size calculation; information fraction.

heading: 15.161 Theory
text:
\(K\) analyses overall two-sided alpha \(\alpha\), boundaries chosen union rejection events probability \(\alpha\) null.Common families:
- O’Brien-Fleming: conservative early, near-nominal late – preserves nominal alpha final analysis.
- Pocock: constant boundary across looks – early stopping harder reach final.
- Alpha-spending (Lan-DeMets): flexible timing; spending function \(f(t)\) information fraction \(t\).

heading: 15.162 Assumptions
text: Analyses occur pre-specified information fractions; test statistic approximately normal look.

heading: 15.163 R Implementation
code:
library(rpact)

# Group-sequential design: 3 analyses, O'Brien-Fleming, alpha=0.025 one-sided
design <- getDesignGroupSequential(
  sided = 1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.2,
  informationRates = c(0.33, 0.67, 1),
  typeOfDesign = "OF"
)
print(design)

# Corresponding sample sizes for two-mean comparison
ss <- getSampleSizeMeans(design = design,
                         alternative = 0.3, stDev = 1)
print(ss)

heading: 15.164 Output & Results
text: Three-stage design cumulative alpha budgets per stage summing 0.025; stage sample sizes scale chosen information fractions.

heading: 15.165 Interpretation
text: “group-sequential design O’Brien-Fleming boundary allocated little alpha first two interims (< 0.001 ), preserving nearly full alpha final analysis. Early stopping extremely unlikely unless effect large.”

heading: 15.166 Practical Tips
text: boundaries standard confirmatory trials; Pocock may suit exploratory ones single-arm phase II.Alpha-spending (Lan-DeMets) flexible – timing need exact, pre-specified.Independent DMC must oversee interim analyses; investigators remain blinded.Adjust point estimates CIs stopping (median-unbiased, repeated CI).Futility boundaries (betagamma spending) complement efficacy can non-binding.

heading: 15.169 Introduction
text: intent--treat (ITT) principle analyses participants according randomised assignment, irrespective actual treatment received adherence. estimates effect prescribing intervention real-world conditions. Per-protocol (PP) restricts analysis adherent participants estimates effect receiving intervention – different estimand.

heading: 15.170 Prerequisites
text: Randomisation; estimands; missing data.

heading: 15.171 Theory
text: ITT preserves randomisation tends conservative superiority trials (dilution non-adherers). PP can exaggerate efficacy introduce selection bias adherence post-randomisation.Modified ITT (mITT) excludes participants never started post-baseline data; common can reintroduce bias exclusion correlates arm.Non-inferiority trials: PP ITT reported; conservative result (less close margin) drives inference.

heading: 15.172 Assumptions
text: Randomisation properly concealed; adherence classification unaffected knowledge outcome; missingness mechanism characterised.

heading: 15.173 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
n_per <- 100
arm <- rep(c("trt", "ctrl"), each = n_per)

# 20 % non-adherence in trt arm; 5 % in ctrl
adhered <- ifelse(arm == "trt", rbinom(n_per * 2, 1, 0.8),
                  rbinom(n_per * 2, 1, 0.95))
adhered[1:n_per] <- rbinom(n_per, 1, 0.8)
adhered[(n_per+1):(2 * n_per)] <- rbinom(n_per, 1, 0.95)

# True effect when actually received
true_effect <- ifelse(adhered == 1 & arm == "trt", 0.7, 0)
y <- rnorm(2 * n_per) + true_effect

# ITT analysis (analyse as randomised)
itt <- t.test(y ~ arm)
# Per-protocol analysis (adherers only)
pp  <- t.test(y[adhered == 1] ~ arm[adhered == 1])

rbind(ITT = c(est = diff(itt$estimate), p = itt$p.value),
      PP  = c(est = diff(pp $estimate), p = pp $p.value))

heading: 15.174 Output & Results
text: ITT effect diluted non-adherence; PP effect recovers -treatment effect subject selection bias.

heading: 15.175 Interpretation
text: “primary ITT analysis estimated 0.56 point advantage intervention (95 % CI 0.28-0.84, p < 0.001); PP analysis gave 0.71 (CI 0.38-1.04). ITT primary inference; PP supportive.”

heading: 15.176 Practical Tips
text: Pre-specify ITT primary SAP; never switch post-hoc.Report flow diagram (CONSORT) showing participants classified.Handle missing outcome data multiple imputation, naive exclusion.Complier-average causal effect (CACE) via instrumental variables estimates effect among adherers without PP’s selection bias.Non-inferiority trials commonly report ITT PP; must meet margin.

heading: 15.179 Introduction
text: Likelihood ratios (LR+ LR-) summarise information content diagnostic test way independent disease prevalence. Sensitivity specificity describe test performs known-disease known--disease populations, directly tell clinician believe observing positive negative test result; predictive values , depend prevalence therefore portable across populations. Likelihood ratios bridge gap: combine multiplicatively pre-test odds yield post-test odds via Bayes’ theorem, making natural building blocks clinical Bayesian reasoning. Modern evidence-based-medicine guidance core teaching texts present LRs preferred diagnostic-performance summary precisely prevalence-independence.

heading: 15.180 Prerequisites
text: working understanding sensitivity specificity, relationship probability odds, Bayes’ theorem odds form.

heading: 15.181 Theory
text: two basic likelihood ratios \[\mathrm{LR}^+ = \frac{\mathrm{Sens}}{1 - \mathrm{Spec}}, \qquad \mathrm{LR}^- = \frac{1 - \mathrm{Sens}}{\mathrm{Spec}}.\]Bayes-theorem update \[\mathrm{Odds}_{\text{post}} = \mathrm{Odds}_{\text{pre}} \times \mathrm{LR}.\]Conventional clinical interpretation: \(\mathrm{LR}^+ > 10\) \(\mathrm{LR}^- < 0.1\) often decisive; 5–10 0.1–0.2 moderate evidence; 2–5 0.2–0.5 weak; near 1 uninformative. Fagan’s nomogram graphically converts pre-test probability LR directly post-test probability useful bedside tool.

heading: 15.182 Assumptions
text: assumptions sensitivity specificity: reliable gold-standard reference disease status, accurate test classification, test characteristics estimated one population generalise patients LR applied. Verification bias spectrum bias threaten generalisation.

heading: 15.183 R Implementation
code:
library(epiR)

tab <- as.table(matrix(c(80, 20,
                         20, 880),
                        nrow = 2, byrow = FALSE,
                        dimnames = list(Test = c("+", "-"),
                                        Disease = c("yes", "no"))))
epi.tests(tab)$detail[c("lrpos", "lrneg"), ]

sens <- 0.8; spec <- 0.978
lr_pos <- sens / (1 - spec); lr_neg <- (1 - sens) / spec

prior_odds <- 0.1 / 0.9
post_odds  <- prior_odds * lr_pos
post_prob  <- post_odds / (1 + post_odds)
c(lr_pos = lr_pos, lr_neg = lr_neg, post_prob_after_pos = post_prob)

heading: 15.184 Output & Results
text: epi.tests() reports LR+ LR- confidence intervals input contingency table. Bayesian update example shows 10 % pre-test probability rises roughly 80 % post-test positive result LR+ = 36, illustrating multiplicative--odds nature update.

heading: 15.185 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “diagnostic test sensitivity 80 % (95 % CI 71 87 %) specificity 97.8 % (96.4 98.7), corresponding LR+ = 36 (95 % CI 16 81) LR- = 0.20 (95 % CI 0.13 0.31). positive test raised pre-test probability 10 % post-test probability 80 %, negative test reduced roughly 2 %. test therefore strongly informative directions typical pre-test probability range clinical setting.” Always report LRs CIs.

heading: 15.186 Practical Tips
text: Report likelihood ratios 95 % confidence intervals; wide CIs indicate fragile diagnostic estimates temper interpretation, especially small sample sizes rare disease drive uncertainty sensitivity specificity.multi-category ordinal tests (rating scales, semi-quantitative biomarker results), compute stratum-specific LRs score level rather collapsing single binary LR; preserves information content gradation.Likelihood ratios generalise across populations long test characteristics (sensitivity, specificity) hold new population; primary advantage predictive values, depend local prevalence transfer.Clinical decision thresholds often pre-specified terms required LR (e.g., LR+ ≥ 10 justify initiating treatment, LR- ≤ 0.1 confidently rule disease); building thresholds diagnostic pathway operational analogue Bayesian reasoning bedside.Chain multiple test results multiplying LRs tests conditionally independent given disease status; practice assumption often violated (second test type correlated first), joint LRs multivariable predictor often honest.complex multi-variable diagnostic tools (clinical prediction rules), LR concept generalises naturally — rule’s score corresponds stratum-specific LR — useful way communicate rule’s discrimination decision-relevant cut-points.

heading: 15.190 Introduction
text: Minimisation, introduced Taves (1974) refined Pocock Simon (1975), covariate-adaptive allocation procedure assigns new participant treatment arm prospectively makes distribution pre-specified prognostic covariates balanced across arms. Unlike stratified block randomisation, requires separate randomisation list every stratum quickly becomes impractical two three covariates need balancing, minimisation handles many prognostic factors simultaneously single allocation framework. now widely used trials several important baseline covariates small--moderate sample sizes, stratified randomisation create many empty strata useful.

heading: 15.191 Prerequisites
text: working understanding simple, block, stratified randomisation; balance metrics cross-tabulated baseline covariates; regulatory framework around covariate-adaptive allocation.

heading: 15.192 Theory
text: candidate arm assignment, algorithm computes balance score — typically sum across covariates marginal imbalances result assignment. new participant allocated balance-minimising arm high probability \(p\) (commonly 0.8 0.9), arm probability \(1 - p\) preserve degree allocation unpredictability. probabilistic element matters: deterministic minimisation always chooses balance-minimising arm becomes predictable investigators know algorithm previous assignments, compromising allocation concealment.

heading: 15.193 Assumptions
text: covariates balanced pre-specified protocol, allocation performed centrally (typically interactive web-response system) rather manually, trial’s analysis model includes minimisation covariates fixed effects preserve valid inference randomisation theory.

heading: 15.194 R Implementation
code:
library(Minirand)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 60
covmat <- data.frame(
  centre = sample(c("A", "B", "C"), n, replace = TRUE),
  sex    = sample(c("M", "F"), n, replace = TRUE),
  age_g  = sample(c("young", "old"), n, replace = TRUE)
)

res <- character(n)
for (j in 1:n) {
  res[j] <- Minirand(covmat = covmat, covwt = rep(1, 3),
                     ntrt = 2, trtseq = c("A", "B"),
                     ratio = c(1, 1),
                     p = 0.9, j = j, result = res)
}

table(trt = res, centre = covmat$centre)
table(trt = res, sex = covmat$sex)

heading: 15.195 Output & Results

heading: 15.196 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Treatment allocation used Pocock-Simon minimisation balancing three pre-specified covariates (centre, sex, age category), equal weight; random component used probability 0.9 allocation balance-minimising arm. trial’s analysis model retained centre, sex, age category fixed-effect covariates preserve valid inference minimisation; final 60-subject sample, maximum marginal arm imbalance covariate 1 subject.” Always state random-component probability analysis-model covariates.

heading: 15.201 Introduction
text: Missing data ubiquitous RCTs can invalidate inference handled carefully. Three missingness mechanisms characterise problem: MCAR (missingness random), MAR (missingness depends observed variables), MNAR (missingness depends unobserved value). Valid analyses require assumptions mechanism.

heading: 15.202 Prerequisites
text: Basic probability; estimands framework (ICH E9 R1).

heading: 15.203 Theory
text: MCAR: \(P(\text{missing}) = P(\text{missing} \mid X, Y)\). Complete-case analysis unbiased inefficient.MAR: \(P(\text{missing} \mid X, Y) = P(\text{missing} \mid X)\). Multiple imputation, ML, weighting unbiased.MNAR: \(P(\text{missing})\) depends unobserved \(Y\). Sensitivity analyses assumed MNAR mechanisms needed.Missingness rarely MCAR practice; MAR default operating assumption, MNAR sensitivity.

heading: 15.204 Assumptions
text: Missingness pattern characterised analyst; auxiliary variables included imputation model; mechanism assumption matches method.

heading: 15.205 R Implementation
code:
library(mice); library(naniar)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
baseline <- rnorm(n, 5, 1)
arm <- rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = n/2)
outcome <- baseline + ifelse(arm == "trt", 1, 0) + rnorm(n, 0, 1)

# MAR: missingness depends on baseline
prob_missing <- plogis(-2 + 0.3 * baseline)
outcome[rbinom(n, 1, prob_missing) == 1] <- NA

df <- data.frame(arm = factor(arm), baseline, outcome)

# Missing-data summary
miss_var_summary(df)

# Multiple imputation
imp <- mice(df, m = 10, method = "pmm", printFlag = FALSE)
pool(with(imp, lm(outcome ~ arm + baseline))) %>% summary()

heading: 15.206 Output & Results
text: Missing-data summary (outcome ~20 % missingness); pooled MI estimate treatment effect adjusting baseline.

heading: 15.207 Interpretation
text: “MAR, multiple imputation 10 imputations gave treatment effect 0.94 (95 % CI 0.62-1.26, p < 0.001); complete-case analysis gave similar estimate, consistent MAR assumption.”

heading: 15.208 Practical Tips
text: Prevent missing data design (pre-specified follow-, low attrition) analysis tricks.Distinguish missing random missing-completely--random; CCA needs stronger MCAR.Always report missingness rate pattern arm; differential missingness red flag.Pre-specify primary analysis MAR; sensitivity analyses MNAR (tipping-point).ICH E9 R1 estimands framework formalises missing data interacts estimand; align analysis estimand, vice versa.

heading: 15.211 Introduction
text: Multiple imputation (MI; Rubin 1987) replaces missing value \(m > 1\) plausible values drawn posterior predictive distribution missing data given observed. analysis run \(m\) completed datasets; Rubin’s rules combine results single inference reflecting within-imputation -imputation uncertainty.

heading: 15.212 Prerequisites
text: Missing-data mechanisms (MAR); Bayesian predictive distributions.

heading: 15.213 Theory
text:
MI procedure:
1. Impute: create \(m\) completed datasets via predictive model.
2. Analyse: apply intended model dataset.
3. Pool: combine \(\bar{\hat{\beta}} = (1/m) \sum \hat{\beta}_k\); total variance \(T = \bar{W} + (1 + 1/m) B\), \(\bar{W}\) mean within-imputation variance \(B\) -imputation variance.mice uses chained equations: iteratively impute variable using others predictors.

heading: 15.214 Assumptions
text: Missing random (MAR); imputation model correctly specified (correct functional form, includes predictors might correlate missingness).

heading: 15.215 R Implementation
code:
library(mice)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 300
df <- data.frame(
  x1 = rnorm(n), x2 = rnorm(n),
  x3 = sample(c("a", "b", "c"), n, replace = TRUE),
  y  = rnorm(n)
)
df$y[sample(n, 60)] <- NA       # 20% missing
df$x2[sample(n, 30)] <- NA      # 10% missing

# Chained-equations imputation, 20 imputations
imp <- mice(df, m = 20, method = c("pmm", "pmm", "polyreg", "pmm"),
            printFlag = FALSE)

# Fit the model on each imputation and pool
fit <- with(imp, lm(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3))
pooled <- pool(fit)
summary(pooled)

heading: 15.216 Output & Results
text: Pooled regression coefficients SEs correctly reflect imputation uncertainty; fmi (fraction missing information) indicates much variance comes imputation.

heading: 15.217 Interpretation
text: “Multiple imputation (m = 20) MAR gave pooled coefficient 0.94 (SE 0.10, 95 % CI 0.74-1.14); fraction missing information 0.18 suggests reasonable efficiency.”

heading: 15.218 Practical Tips
text: Include variables used substantive model, plus auxiliary variables correlated missingness, imputation model.\(m\) \(\geq\) 100 large fraction missing; 20 minimum exploratory work.regression interactions non-linearity, include terms imputation model (impute model matches).Use predictive mean matching (pmm) continuous variables avoid unrealistic extrapolations.Always check convergence chained equations via trace plots.

heading: 15.221 Introduction
text: Non-inferiority (NI) trials test whether new treatment worse active comparator pre-specified margin \(\Delta\). Margin selection consequential scrutinised part NI trial design: loose genuinely inferior treatment approved; tight study infeasible.

heading: 15.222 Prerequisites
text: Hypothesis testing; active-controlled trials; historical-control data.

heading: 15.223 Theory
text:
Two common approaches:
- Fixed-margin (synthesis) method: margin \(\Delta\) chosen based historical effect active comparator vs placebo, typically preserving 50-75 % effect. Example: active reduces mortality 10 % vs placebo, \(\Delta\) might set 5 %.
- Clinical margin: clinically judged threshold practical importance, independent historical data.require regulatory justification; FDA EMA typically require synthesis approach supporting clinical judgement.

heading: 15.224 Assumptions
text: Historical active-vs-placebo effect consistent generalisable current trial population; assay sensitivity (ability detect difference truly present) preserved.

heading: 15.225 R Implementation
code:
# Synthesis-method margin calculation
# Historical effect: placebo-controlled active gives risk reduction 10% (95% CI 7%-13%)
# Conservative estimate: lower bound 7%
preservation <- 0.5                    # preserve at least 50% of effect
margin_synthesis <- 0.07 * (1 - preservation)
cat("Synthesis-based NI margin:", margin_synthesis, "\n")

# Sample size for an NI trial with continuous outcome
# Expected true difference 0; margin 0.25 SD; alpha=0.025; power=0.80
library(pwr)
pwr.t.test(d = 0.25, sig.level = 0.025, power = 0.80,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "greater")

heading: 15.226 Output & Results
text: Synthesis-based margin (0.035 = 3.5 % preserving 50 % historical effect); sample-size calculation gives ~252/arm 0.25 SD margin.

heading: 15.227 Interpretation
text: “non-inferiority margin prospectively set 3.5 percentage points, preserving least 50 % historical benefit active comparator (7 % lower bound historical effect). Sample size 504 based 80 % power rule margin one-sided alpha 0.025.”

heading: 15.228 Practical Tips
text: Margin selection must pre-specified, regulator-reviewed, clinically justified.Run ITT per-protocol analyses; NI requires consistency.Beware “biocreep”: repeated non-inferiority approvals without placebo anchoring drift effective therapy.Assay sensitivity hard demonstrate without placebo arm; three-arm trials (NI + placebo) ideal often unethical.Non-inferiority claims report effect estimate CI, just “p < 0.025”.
