heading: 15.231 Introduction
text: O’Brien-Fleming () boundary, introduced Peter O’Brien Thomas Fleming 1979, widely used efficacy stopping boundary group-sequential clinical trials de facto regulatory default confirmatory phase-3 trials. conservative early liberal late: little type-error budget spent early interim analyses, stopping early efficacy requires unusually large effect, final analysis uses nearly full nominal alpha. practical consequence interim stops rare convincing — happen treatment effect much larger powered alternative — final analysis suffers tiny multiplicity penalty early stop occurs.

heading: 15.232 Prerequisites
text: working understanding group-sequential trial design, alpha-spending framework, trade-early-stopping ease final-analysis stringency repeated-look hypothesis testing.

heading: 15.233 Theory
text: O’Brien-Fleming boundary standardised Wald-statistic scale takes form \(c_k = c / \sqrt{t_k}\) information fraction \(t_k\), nominal \(p\)-value threshold required interim \(k\) shrinks \(1/\sqrt{t_k}\). practice, five-look equally-spaced design nominal \(\alpha\) values approximately \(5 \times 10^{-6}\), \(0.0013\), \(0.008\), \(0.018\), \(0.041\) five sequential analyses (one-sided \(\alpha = 0.025\)). final analysis therefore uses 0.041 instead 0.025 — mild penalty option stop early — early looks protected premature rejection.

heading: 15.234 Assumptions
text: Information accrues planned (regular interim spacing alpha-spending implementation handles irregular timing), test statistic approximately Normal look, multiplicity correction pre-specified data unblinded.

heading: 15.235 R Implementation
code:
library(rpact)

design <- getDesignGroupSequential(
  sided = 1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.2,
  typeOfDesign = "OF",
  informationRates = seq(0.2, 1, by = 0.2)
)

print(design$stageLevels)

print(design$criticalValues)

plot(design, type = 1)

heading: 15.236 Output & Results
text: rpact returns stage-specific nominal alphas (conservative early looks, near-nominal final analysis) corresponding critical values standardised test-statistic scale. boundary plot makes asymmetric “high early, low late” shape visually obvious standard supplementary figure trial design documents.

heading: 15.237 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “five-stage group-sequential design O’Brien-Fleming efficacy boundaries required nominal \(p < 5 \times 10^{-6}\) 20 % information interim, relaxing \(p < 0.041\) final analysis maintain overall one-sided \(\alpha = 0.025\). Early stopping therefore triggered treatment effects substantially larger powered alternative; final analysis suffered 0.009 multiplicity penalty (0.041 vs unadjusted 0.025) earlier stop occurred. maximum sample size 6 % larger fixed-design equivalent.” Always justify boundary choice.

heading: 15.238 Practical Tips
text: O’Brien-Fleming default efficacy boundary confirmatory phase-3 trials virtually always regulatory expectation; deviations justified protocol explicit reasoning different boundary (Pocock, Hwang-Shih-DeCani, custom) preferred.Pair O’Brien-Fleming efficacy boundaries non-binding futility boundary (gamma-spending beta-spending) detect hopeless trials early; combination preserves type-error efficacy analysis allowing trial stop futility conditional power low.Alpha-spending implementations O’Brien-Fleming (Lan-DeMets -shape spending function) preserve behaviour irregular interim timing modern default handling unscheduled looks.Post-stopping treatment-effect point estimates upwardly biased — trial stopped early precisely random fluctuation effect large. Report repeated confidence intervals (Jennison-Turnbull) median-unbiased estimates rather naive maximum-likelihood estimate stopping efficacy.Compared Pocock boundary, makes early stopping substantially harder preserves near-nominal final-analysis alpha smaller maximum sample size; Pocock makes early stopping easier higher final-analysis cost. Choice reflect trial’s ethical practical priorities.Stage-specific information fractions need equally spaced; alpha-spending accommodates whatever schedule data-monitoring committee prefers, schedule pre-specified generated pre-specified spending function.

heading: 15.242 Introduction
text: Pocock boundary, introduced Stuart Pocock 1977, first systematic group-sequential efficacy stopping rule clinical trials. uses nominal type-error level every interim final analysis, distributing type-error budget approximately uniformly across looks. result design makes early stopping efficacy comparatively easy — constant relatively-low nominal threshold across looks — cost requiring unusually stringent nominal \(p\)-value final analysis earlier look stopped trial. Pocock boundaries conceptually simple, ethically attractive early stopping priority, somewhat costly terms maximum sample size compared conservative O’Brien-Fleming alternative.

heading: 15.243 Prerequisites
text: working understanding group-sequential trial designs, alpha-spending framework, trade-early-stopping ease final-analysis stringency repeated-look hypothesis testing.

heading: 15.244 Theory
text: \(K\) planned analyses overall two-sided type-error \(\alpha\), Pocock boundary uses constant nominal alpha \(\alpha^*\) every look probability rejection event null exactly equals \(\alpha\). \(K = 5\) \(\alpha = 0.05\) (two-sided), \(\alpha^* \approx 0.0158\) every look — substantially unadjusted 0.05 multiplicity correction. Compared O’Brien-Fleming, Pocock rejects easily early interim analyses (O’Brien-Fleming demands extreme test statistics) less easily final analysis (O’Brien-Fleming approaches unadjusted threshold).

heading: 15.245 Assumptions
text: Information accrues planned (regular interim spacing alpha-spending implementation handles irregular timing), test statistic approximately Normal look, multiplicity correction pre-specified data unblinded.

heading: 15.246 R Implementation
code:
library(rpact)

design <- getDesignGroupSequential(
  sided = 1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.2,
  typeOfDesign = "P",
  informationRates = seq(0.2, 1, by = 0.2)
)

print(design$stageLevels)
print(design$criticalValues)

plot(design, type = 1)

ss_pocock <- getSampleSizeMeans(design = design,
                                alternative = 0.3, stDev = 1)

design_of <- getDesignGroupSequential(
  sided = 1, alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.2,
  typeOfDesign = "OF",
  informationRates = seq(0.2, 1, by = 0.2)
)
ss_of <- getSampleSizeMeans(design = design_of,
                            alternative = 0.3, stDev = 1)

c(pocock_max_n = ss_pocock$maxNumberOfSubjects,
  of_max_n    = ss_of   $maxNumberOfSubjects)

heading: 15.247 Output & Results
text: rpact returns constant nominal alpha stage, corresponding critical values test-statistic scale, maximum sample size needed maintain targeted overall power. Comparing Pocock O’Brien-Fleming sample-size calculations side side quantifies cost aggressive early-stopping property.

heading: 15.248 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “five-stage group-sequential design Pocock boundaries required nominal \(p < 0.0158\) every interim final analysis maintain overall one-sided \(\alpha = 0.025\). design enables earlier efficacy stopping equivalent O’Brien-Fleming design, requires approximately 15 % maximum sample size early stop triggered. choice justified ethical imperative halt enrolment soon clinically meaningful benefit established, given trial’s seriously-ill population.” Always justify boundary choice ethically.

Pocock remains useful phase-2 trials futility-stopping contexts early termination stronger priority.hybrid Hwang-Shih-DeCani family interpolates Pocock O’Brien-Fleming behaviour via single shape parameter \(\gamma\), allowing trial designers dial trade-explicitly.Pre-specify boundary type, spending function, contingency unscheduled interims protocol; ad hoc adjustments data inspection inflate type-error increasingly flagged regulators.

heading: 15.253 Introduction
text: Sensitivity specificity properties diagnostic test, clinical utility. Positive predictive value (PPV) – probability positive test indicates disease – negative predictive value (NPV) depend disease prevalence. test can high PPV high-prevalence setting low PPV screening setting.

heading: 15.254 Prerequisites
text: Sensitivity specificity; Bayes’ theorem.

heading: 15.255 Theory
text:
\[\text{PPV} = \frac{\text{Sens} \cdot \text{Prev}}{\text{Sens} \cdot \text{Prev} + (1 - \text{Spec}) \cdot (1 - \text{Prev})}.\]
\[\text{NPV} = \frac{\text{Spec} \cdot (1 - \text{Prev})}{(1 - \text{Sens}) \cdot \text{Prev} + \text{Spec} \cdot (1 - \text{Prev})}.\]fixed test, PPV rises prevalence NPV falls. high-specificity test essential screening low-prevalence populations.

heading: 15.256 Assumptions
text: Test characteristics (Sens, Spec) generalise target population; prevalence correctly estimated.

heading: 15.257 R Implementation
code:
library(epiR)

# 2x2 table from a diagnostic study
tab <- as.table(matrix(c(90, 10,     # TP, FP
                         20, 880),   # FN, TN
                        nrow = 2, byrow = FALSE,
                        dimnames = list(Test = c("+", "-"),
                                        Disease = c("yes", "no"))))

epi.tests(tab, conf.level = 0.95)

# What happens when prevalence is only 1%?
sens <- 0.82; spec <- 0.99
for (p in c(0.5, 0.2, 0.05, 0.01)) {
  ppv <- sens * p / (sens * p + (1 - spec) * (1 - p))
  npv <- spec * (1 - p) / ((1 - sens) * p + spec * (1 - p))
  cat(sprintf("Prev=%.2f  PPV=%.3f  NPV=%.3f\n", p, ppv, npv))
}

heading: 15.258 Output & Results
text: Test statistics including PPV/NPV sample; manual computation shows PPV dropping sharply prevalence falls, 0.98 50 % prevalence 0.45 1 %.

heading: 15.259 Interpretation
text: “population 1 % prevalence, positive test (sens 82 %, spec 99 %) PPV 0.45; positives false. screening, test specificity drives PPV far sensitivity .”

heading: 15.263 Introduction
text: Randomisation defining feature RCT: participants allocated chance rather choice. Proper randomisation prevents selection bias (investigators predict allocation) ensures balance known unknown confounders expectation. Several schemes trade simplicity, balance, unpredictability.

heading: 15.264 Prerequisites
text: Probability; allocation concealment.

heading: 15.265 Theory
text: Simple randomisation: participant flips fair coin. Easy can produce imbalance small trials.Block randomisation: within blocks size \(B\), allocate equal counts arm. Guarantees balance block boundaries.Stratified randomisation: block within strata defined baseline covariates (sex, age, centre). Balances stratification variables without post-hoc adjustment.Minimisation (covariate-adaptive): allocate new participant minimise covariate imbalance; quasi-random, less transparent.

heading: 15.266 Assumptions
text: Allocation concealed participant enrolled; blocks / strata definitions pre-specified.

heading: 15.267 R Implementation
code:
library(blockrand)

set.seed(2026)
# Block randomisation with variable block sizes (4, 6)
alloc <- blockrand(n = 60, num.levels = 2,
                   levels = c("ctrl", "trt"),
                   block.sizes = c(2, 3))
head(alloc, 10)
table(alloc$treatment)

# Stratified randomisation: stratify by sex
library(randomizeR)
pbr <- pbrPar(rb = c(4, 6), K = 2, ratio = c(1, 1))
rand_m <- genSeq(pbr, r = 1, seed = 2026)
rand_f <- genSeq(pbr, r = 1, seed = 2027)

heading: 15.268 Output & Results
text: Allocation sequence approximately equal arm counts; variable block sizes prevent predictability block boundaries.

heading: 15.269 Interpretation
text: “Block-randomisation variable block sizes (4, 6) used allocate 60 participants, guaranteeing equal arm counts every 20-participant batch. Stratification centre ensured balance across sites.”

heading: 15.270 Practical Tips
text: Use variable block sizes (e.g., 4 6) prevent predictability; investigators guessing next allocation defeats concealment.Stratify small number strong prognostic variables (typically 2-3); -stratification creates empty cells.Centralise allocation schedule; local administration risks unblinding.Document randomisation method concealment published paper trial registry.small trials, stratified block randomisation usually best choice.

heading: 15.273 Introduction
text: crossover randomised controlled trial assigns participant receive two treatments sequence, separated washout period, every subject acts control across treatment comparison. within-subject comparison removes -subject variability — typically dominant source variance clinical-pharmacology chronic-disease studies — treatment estimate, giving crossover design substantially power parallel-group RCT equal total sample size. Crossover designs particularly common early-phase clinical pharmacology, bioequivalence studies, sleep migraine research, contexts underlying condition stable, treatment effects reversible, adequate washout can eliminate pharmacological carryover.

heading: 15.274 Prerequisites
text: working understanding parallel-group RCT design, within-subject paired comparisons, mixed-effects models subject random effect, concepts period, sequence, carryover effects.

heading: 15.275 Theory
text: standard \(2 \times 2\) crossover randomises participants sequence AB (treatment period 1, B period 2) BA. within-subject treatment contrast primary inference; analysis Grizzle’s classical \(t\)-test within-subject differences, — preferably — mixed-effects model subject random intercept fixed effects period treatment. model \[y_{ijk} = \mu + \pi_j + \tau_k + s_i + \varepsilon_{ijk},\]period \(\pi_j\), treatment \(\tau_k\), subject random intercept \(s_i\), residual error \(\varepsilon_{ijk}\). Carryover — residual treatment effect period 1 lingering period 2 — biases within-subject estimate; formally tested sequence × period interaction test -powered, adequate washout primary defence.

heading: 15.276 Assumptions
text: carryover (washout long enough eliminate first-period treatment’s effect), condition stable periods (progressive disease, natural recovery), treatment effects independent period, observations within subject share Normal distribution constant variance.

heading: 15.277 R Implementation
code:
library(nlme)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 20
sequence <- sample(c("AB", "BA"), n, replace = TRUE)
subj_eff <- rnorm(n, 0, 1)

y_A <- subj_eff + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)
y_B <- subj_eff + 0.5 + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)

df <- data.frame(
  subject  = rep(1:n, each = 2),
  period   = rep(1:2, n),
  treatment = unlist(lapply(sequence, function(s) strsplit(s, "")[[1]])),
  sequence = rep(sequence, each = 2),
  y        = unlist(lapply(1:n, function(i)
    if (sequence[i] == "AB") c(y_A[i], y_B[i]) else c(y_B[i], y_A[i])))
)

fit <- lme(y ~ treatment + period, random = ~ 1 | subject, data = df)
summary(fit)$tTable

heading: 15.278 Output & Results
text: mixed-effects fit returns treatment effect within-subject standard error separate period effect adjusts drift two periods. random subject intercept absorbs -subject variation source crossover design’s power advantage; reporting variance components alongside fixed-effect estimate makes design’s gain explicit.

heading: 15.279 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “two-period crossover analysis mixed-effects modelling estimated B–treatment difference 0.48 (95 % CI 0.22 0.74, \(p = 0.002\)), achieving three-fold precision equivalent parallel-group design number subjects. period effect small non-significant (\(p = 0.51\)), sequence × period interaction (carryover diagnostic) non-significant (\(p = 0.78\)), supporting -carryover assumption. Reporting follows CONSORT extension crossover trials.” Always report period carryover.

heading: 15.280 Practical Tips
text: Test carryover hypothesis formally via sequence × period interaction, rely design — adequately long washout, conventionally least five half-lives active compound — primary defence rather underpowered post-hoc test.Unbalanced sequences (different counts AB BA participants) reduce design efficiency complicate analysis; aim sequence balance via stratified randomisation sequence.two periods (Latin-square Williams designs) improve power allow comparison two treatments, cost complexity, longer trial duration, potential dropout — crossover designs handle poorly dropouts lose paired information.underlying condition evolves substantially within trial timeframe (progressive disease, recovery, growth), crossover design inappropriate; stability assumption hard defend biases estimate.Report per CONSORT extension crossover trials, including trial flow diagram (per period), sequence allocation, washout duration, carryover diagnostic.ordinal binary outcomes crossover design, generalised mixed-effects models (glmer) paired analyses within-subject contingency table (McNemar) appropriate analysis approaches.

heading: 15.281 R Packages Used
text: nlme::lme() lme4::lmer() mixed-effects analysis subject random intercepts; Crossover canonical crossover-design construction including Williams squares higher-order designs; crossdes systematic generation balanced crossover layouts; bear end--end bioequivalence analysis crossover data; Mediana trial-design simulation including crossover designs.

heading: 15.284 Introduction
text: parallel-group randomised controlled trial (RCT) gold standard evaluating interventions. Participants randomly allocated one two () arms – typically new intervention vs control – followed pre-specified outcome. Randomisation balances known unknown confounders expectation; blinding reduces bias.

heading: 15.285 Prerequisites
text: Randomisation; hypothesis testing; sample-size calculation.

heading: 15.286 Theory
text:
Essential elements:
- Primary outcome (continuous, binary, time--event) clinically meaningful effect size.
- Allocation ratio (usually 1:1, occasionally 2:1 3:1 rare interventions).
- Randomisation list (pre-generated, concealed allocation time).
- Blinding (single, double, triple).
- Pre-registered statistical analysis plan.Primary analysis typically intent--treat (ITT), comparing groups randomised.

heading: 15.287 Assumptions
text: Participants exchangeable post-randomisation; allocation fully concealed; outcome assessment blinded; follow-complete MAR.

heading: 15.288 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

# Sample-size for two-arm comparison of means
# Effect: difference of 0.5 SD, alpha = 0.05, power = 0.80
pwr.t.test(d = 0.5, sig.level = 0.05, power = 0.80,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

# Simulate an RCT with continuous outcome
set.seed(2026)
n_per_arm <- 64
arm <- rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = n_per_arm)
y   <- rnorm(2 * n_per_arm, mean = ifelse(arm == "trt", 0.5, 0))

# ITT analysis: two-sample t-test
t.test(y ~ arm, var.equal = TRUE)

# Adjusted analysis with a pre-specified covariate (ANCOVA)
covar <- rnorm(2 * n_per_arm)
summary(lm(y ~ arm + covar))$coefficients

heading: 15.289 Output & Results
text: Sample size ~64 per arm 80 % power; ITT t-test recovers effect; ANCOVA-adjusted analysis gives similar point estimate smaller SE covariate prognostic.

heading: 15.290 Interpretation
text: “trial randomised 128 participants 1:1 intervention vs control; intervention arm showed 0.52 SD improvement (95 % CI 0.18-0.86, p = 0.003) primary outcome, analysed ANCOVA adjusting baseline value.”

heading: 15.291 Practical Tips
text: Register protocol SAP enrolment (clinicaltrials.gov, EUDRACT).Pre-specify primary outcome analysis; secondary outcomes exploratory.Blinding protective; document broken (unblinding events, assessment).Report per CONSORT 2010 guidelines; flow diagram mandatory.ITT primary analysis; supportive per-protocol analyses sensitivity.

heading: 15.294 Introduction
text: Cronbach’s alpha, introduced Lee Cronbach 1951, summarises internal consistency multi-item scale quantifying strongly items co-vary accounting total number items. intuition items intended measure underlying construct correlate ; alpha rises average inter-item correlation number items. Cronbach’s alpha now ubiquitous questionnaire validation, patient-reported-outcome (PRO) instrument development, psychometric evaluation clinical scales, setting composite score formed multiple ordinal continuous items. Despite well-known statistical limitations, remains single reported reliability statistic clinical-research literature.

heading: 15.295 Prerequisites
text: working understanding classical test theory, concept true score plus measurement error, construction composite scores multi-item rating scales.

heading: 15.296 Theory
text: Cronbach’s alpha \[\alpha = \frac{k}{k - 1}\left(1 - \frac{\sum_{=1}^k \sigma_i^2}{\sigma_T^2}\right),\]\(k\) number items, \(\sigma_i^2\) variance item \(\), \(\sigma_T^2\) variance total summed score. statistic ranges \(-\infty\) (theoretically; practice 0) 1. Conventional thresholds 0.70 (acceptable), 0.80 (good), 0.90 (excellent), values 0.95 typically indicating redundant items rather superior reliability. McDonald’s omega flexible alternative assumption tau-equivalence (equal true-score variances across items) doubtful.

heading: 15.297 Assumptions
text: Items tau-equivalent (item measures true construct loading), scale unidimensional (single underlying factor explains inter-item covariance structure), items continuous quasi-continuous (Likert least 5 levels). Violations common, McDonald’s omega hierarchical-omega estimators give honest reliability estimate assumptions met.

heading: 15.298 R Implementation
code:
library(psych)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
theta <- rnorm(n)
items <- sapply(1:8, function(i) 0.7 * theta + rnorm(n, 0, 0.7))
colnames(items) <- paste0("q", 1:8)

alpha_res <- psych::alpha(items)
alpha_res$total
alpha_res$alpha.drop[, c("raw_alpha", "std.alpha")]

heading: 15.299 Output & Results
text: psych::alpha() returns raw standardised Cronbach’s alpha full scale, plus “alpha drop” table showing alpha change item removed. large positive drop (alpha increases without item) flags item inconsistent rest scale candidate revision removal. output also includes 95 % confidence intervals (via Feldt’s method) average inter-item correlation, often informative alpha .

heading: 15.300 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “eight-item PRO scale Cronbach’s alpha 0.82 (95 % CI 0.78 0.86, Feldt method), indicating good internal consistency. average inter-item correlation 0.36, supporting tau-equivalence assumption qualitatively. single item substantially changed alpha dropped (largest drop \(-0.01\)), items retained final scale. McDonald’s omega-total 0.83, close agreement alpha.” Always report alpha omega feasible.

heading: 15.301 Practical Tips
text: Alpha depends number items: long scales inflate alpha mechanically, even items weakly inter-correlated. Reporting average inter-item correlation alongside alpha gives readers fairer picture true item coherence.Low alpha (< 0.70) may reflect multi-dimensional scale rather unreliable items. Always check factor structure exploratory confirmatory factor analysis concluding items unreliable.high alpha (> 0.95) suggests redundant items measuring essentially content; consider trimming scale removing redundant items, improves administrative efficiency without sacrificing reliability.ordinal items fewer five categories, standard Pearson-correlation-based alpha biased downward; use ordinal alpha (computed polychoric correlations) McDonald’s omega instead.Pair Cronbach’s alpha confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) verify assumed unidimensional structure; alpha computed multidimensional scale uninterpretable reliability statistic.test-retest reliability inter-rater reliability, appropriate statistics intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) Cohen’s Fleiss’s kappa, respectively; alpha measures internal consistency, stability agreement.

heading: 15.302 R Packages Used
text: psych::alpha() canonical Cronbach’s alpha drop analysis, psych::omega() McDonald’s omega hierarchical-omega; ltm::cronbach.alpha() lightweight alternative; MBESS::ci.reliability() advanced CIs (Feldt, bootstrap, Bonett); lavaan confirmatory factor analysis verify unidimensional assumption.

heading: 15.305 Introduction
text: receiver-operating-characteristic (ROC) curve traces sensitivity $1 - $ specificity across possible decision thresholds continuous-valued diagnostic test risk score. single sensitivity-specificity pair characterises performance one cut-, ROC curve summarises performance across every cut-therefore captures discriminative ability underlying continuous measurement independently chosen threshold. area curve (AUC) reduces curve single number clean probabilistic interpretation: equals probability randomly chosen diseased case higher biomarker value randomly chosen non-diseased case. AUC values 0.5 indicate chance-level discrimination 1.0 indicates perfect separation; values 0.7 0.9 typical clinically useful biomarkers.

heading: 15.306 Prerequisites
text: working understanding sensitivity specificity, role decision thresholds diagnostic-test performance, trade-true false positive rates ROC curve makes explicit.

heading: 15.307 Theory
text: continuous biomarker \(X\) binary disease status \(D\), ROC curve parametric plot\[\mathrm{ROC}(c) = \bigl(\,1 - F_0(c),\, 1 - F_1(c)\,\bigr) \quad\text{} c,\]\(F_0\) \(F_1\) CDFs \(X\) non-diseased diseased populations, respectively. AUC equivalent representation\[\mathrm{AUC} = P(X_1 > X_0),\]\(X_1\) random observation diseased \(X_0\) non-diseased population — probability correct ranking. Confidence intervals AUC typically computed via DeLong non-parametric method bootstrap; bootstrap also standard approach inference curve .

heading: 15.308 Assumptions
text: Test values measured continuous scale (least ordinal scale many levels), disease status correctly classified reliable gold-standard reference, observations independent. interpretation discrimination measure independent disease prevalence — feature AUC distinguishes predictive values.

heading: 15.309 R Implementation
code:
library(pROC)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
disease <- factor(sample(c(0, 1), n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.6, 0.4)))
biomarker <- rnorm(n, mean = ifelse(disease == 1, 1.0, 0), sd = 1)

roc_obj <- roc(response = disease, predictor = biomarker,
               levels = c("0", "1"), direction = "<")
auc(roc_obj)
ci.auc(roc_obj, method = "delong")

plot(roc_obj, col = "#2A9D8F", lwd = 2, legacy.axes = TRUE,
     main = "ROC curve for biomarker")
abline(0, 1, lty = 2, col = "grey60")

heading: 15.310 Output & Results
text: roc() constructs ROC object; auc() returns area curve ci.auc() provides DeLong bootstrap confidence interval. standard plot shows sensitivity vertical axis $1 - $ specificity horizontal axis, chance-diagonal reference line. Points along curve correspond sensitivity-specificity trade-offs different cut-values.

heading: 15.311 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “biomarker showed good discrimination binary disease outcome AUC 0.77 (95 % CI 0.70 0.84, DeLong method); exceeds conventional ‘fair’ threshold 0.70 falls short ‘excellent’ (\(\geq 0.90\)). Youden-optimal cut-(biomarker \(\geq 0.42\)), sensitivity 73 % specificity 70 %; alternative cut-offs prioritising specificity (e.g., \(\geq 1.0\), sensitivity 51 %, specificity 87 %) may preferred screening applications.” Always report AUC least one operating point.

heading: 15.312 Practical Tips
text: Report AUC 95 % confidence interval — DeLong’s non-parametric method standard paired comparisons bootstrap preferable small imbalanced samples; AUC without uncertainty bounds uninterpretable.Test AUC 0.5 (chance) using pROC::roc.test(); compare two AUCs cases using paired DeLong test, respects correlation induced shared subjects.Partial AUC clinically relevant region — example, AUC restricted specificity 0.9 screening context — often informative full AUC, clinical use rarely spans full operating range.AUC threshold-independent; combine calibration analysis (Hosmer-Lemeshow, calibration intercept slope, calibration plot) decision-curve analysis threshold-dependent decisions matter clinical context.heavily imbalanced data (rare disease, screening contexts), precision-recall AUC often informative ROC AUC ROC can look deceptively good subjects non-diseased; PRAUC focuses positive class.comparing biomarkers, include increment AUC (\(\Delta\) AUC), integrated discrimination index (IDI), net reclassification improvement (NRI); captures different facet incremental performance.

heading: 15.316 Introduction
text: Sample-size re-estimation (SSR) adaptive-design technique updates clinical trial’s planned sample size mid-study using interim estimates nuisance parameters within-group standard deviation (continuous outcomes) control-arm event rate (binary outcomes). one common least controversial trial adaptations , done blinded form, access treatment-effect information therefore preserves type-error rate mild well-understood conditions. SSR now standard practice pilot-data estimates nuisance parameters uncertain planning stage trial otherwise large enough consequences - -estimated nuisance parameter substantial.

heading: 15.317 Prerequisites
text: working understanding sample-size calculation, adaptive trial design, distinction blinded unblinded interim analyses, regulatory framework around protocol-pre-specified adaptations.

heading: 15.318 Theory
text: Blinded SSR uses interim estimates nuisance parameters pooled (across-arm) data , without revealing arm-level information. continuous outcomes pooled standard deviation suffices; binary outcomes, pooled event rate. Blinded SSR inflate type-error standard conditions widely accepted regulators minimal formal control.Unblinded SSR lets Data Monitoring Committee see interim results arm, supporting flexible re-estimation rules cost formal multiplicity control. standard implementation combination-test promising-zone design (Mehta Pocock, 2011) preserves conditional type-error explicit weighting interim final test statistics.

heading: 15.319 Assumptions
text: relevant nuisance parameter unknown planning estimable interim data; interim analysis preserves blinding required; SSR rule pre-specified protocol statistical analysis plan; maximum sample size cap set advance avoid unlimited re-estimation.

heading: 15.320 R Implementation
code:
library(rpact)

n_initial <- ceiling(
  getSampleSizeMeans(alternative = 0.3, stDev = 1,
                     alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.2,
                     groups = 2)$numberOfSubjects
)
n_initial

n_updated <- ceiling(
  getSampleSizeMeans(alternative = 0.3, stDev = 1.4,
                     alpha = 0.025, beta = 0.2,
                     groups = 2)$numberOfSubjects
)
n_updated

c(planned = n_initial, revised = n_updated)

heading: 15.322 Interpretation

Per pre-specified rule, sample size increased 350 680 maintain 80 % power originally specified treatment effect 0.3 SD. increase access treatment-arm information therefore inflate type-error rate. blinded SSR conducted unblinded statistician within DMC operating manual.” Always report blinding status cap.

text: Use blinded SSR whenever possible; operationally simpler, less controversial regulators, adequate common SSR application (revising within-group SD pooled event rate).Unblinded SSR requires statistical method explicitly preserves type-error rate — typically combination-test design (Cui-Hung-Wang Mehta-Pocock promising-zone) weights interim final test statistics pre-specified way.Pre-specify SSR trigger condition, re-estimation rule, maximum sample-size cap protocol SAP; unlimited re-estimation without cap acceptable regulators creates open-ended commitment sponsors rarely want make.Document SSR decision rationale final trial report — whether SSR triggered, interim nuisance-parameter estimate , revised sample size became — reviewers can assess decision.SSR especially valuable pilot-data estimates nuisance parameters uncertain trial population expected differ pilot ways affect variance event rate; reliable prior data make SSR less necessary.Combine SSR group-sequential efficacy futility boundaries fully adaptive design handles nuisance-parameter uncertainty effect-size revision; combination now standard many phase-3 trials.

heading: 15.327 Introduction
text: Sensitivity analyses complement primary analysis varying key assumptions – missing-data mechanism, model form, inclusion criteria – assess robust conclusions . Tipping-point analyses identify extreme assumptions must primary conclusion flips, providing concrete interpretation.

heading: 15.328 Prerequisites
text: Primary analysis; missing-data mechanisms; multiple imputation.

heading: 15.329 Theory
text: Tipping-point analysis MI: impute missing outcomes experimental arm increasing shift \(\delta\) (less favourable); re-pool. smallest \(\delta\) makes treatment effect longer significant “tipping point”. large tipping point means conclusion robust.sensitivity analyses: different imputation methods, PP vs ITT, different covariate adjustments, varying inclusion criteria, alternative parametric models.

heading: 15.330 Assumptions
text: Sensitivity analyses pre-specified; tipping points interpreted clinically, mechanically.

heading: 15.331 R Implementation
code:
library(mice)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 200
arm <- factor(rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = n/2))
baseline <- rnorm(n, 5, 1)
outcome  <- 0.6 * baseline + ifelse(arm == "trt", 0.8, 0) +
            rnorm(n, 0, 1)
outcome[sample(n, 40)] <- NA

df <- data.frame(arm, baseline, outcome)

# Base analysis under MAR
imp <- mice(df, m = 20, method = "pmm", printFlag = FALSE)
summary(pool(with(imp, lm(outcome ~ arm + baseline))))$estimate[2]

# Tipping-point analysis: penalise imputed trt-arm outcomes by delta
deltas <- seq(0, 2, by = 0.25)
effs <- sapply(deltas, function(d) {
  imp2 <- imp
  for (k in 1:20) {
    idx <- which(is.na(df$outcome) & df$arm == "trt")
    imp2$imp$outcome[[k]][df$arm[idx] == "trt"] <-
      imp2$imp$outcome[[k]][df$arm[idx] == "trt"] - d
  }
  summary(pool(with(imp2, lm(outcome ~ arm + baseline))))$estimate[2]
})
data.frame(delta = deltas, trt_effect = round(effs, 3))

heading: 15.332 Output & Results
text: Treatment effect declines linearly \(\delta\); tipping point effect crosses zero (loses significance).

heading: 15.333 Interpretation
text: “delta shift 1.6 imputed intervention-arm outcomes required eliminate significance; clinically require intervention dropouts fare 1.6 SD worse MAR predicts. primary conclusion robust plausible MNAR mechanisms.”

heading: 15.334 Practical Tips
text: Pre-specify sensitivity analyses SAP; post-hoc analyses exploratory.Tipping points informative expressed clinical scale.ICH E9 R1 promotes sensitivity-analysis thinking tied estimand.Running many sensitivity analyses fine; interpret holistically.Reporting tipping-point figure primary paper increasingly standard high-missingness trials.

heading: 15.337 Introduction
text: Stepped-wedge cluster-randomised trials (SW-CRTs) randomise whether cluster receives intervention time cluster transitions control intervention. end trial clusters received intervention, gives design particular ethical pragmatic appeal: well suited settings investigators believe intervention likely beneficial (withholding clusters indefinitely ethically uncomfortable), logistical constraints prevent simultaneous rollout across clusters, programme rollout happening anyway trial exploiting staggered implementation learn effect. Analysis must carefully separate secular time trend affects clusters treatment effect realised different calendar times different clusters.

heading: 15.338 Prerequisites
text: working understanding cluster-randomised trials, time-trend modelling, mixed-effects models cluster random intercepts (optionally) cluster-time random effects.

heading: 15.339 Theory
text: Clusters randomised sequence rather arm; step (period) new subset clusters crosses control intervention. resulting data structure provides two complementary contrasts: -cluster comparison period (like parallel-arm CRT period) within-cluster -comparison around cluster’s switch-point. standard analysis mixed-effects model fixed effects time period treatment status random intercept cluster:\[y_{ijk} = \mu + \tau_t + \beta \cdot X_{jk} + u_j + \varepsilon_{ijk},\]\(\tau_t\) period effect, \(X_{jk}\) treatment indicator cluster \(j\) time \(k\), \(u_j \sim N(0, \sigma_c^2)\) cluster random effect. Including period fixed effects mandatory secular trends confound treatment estimate.

heading: 15.340 Assumptions
text: Secular time trends common across clusters (cluster-specific time trend modelled explicitly), treatment effect immediate stable switch (fade-/fade-explicitly modelled), within-cluster correlation structure correctly specified.

heading: 15.341 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4); library(lmerTest)

set.seed(2026)
n_cl <- 10; n_per <- 20; n_period <- 5
cluster <- rep(1:n_cl, each = n_period * n_per)
period  <- rep(rep(1:n_period, each = n_per), n_cl)

start_t <- sample(2:5, n_cl, replace = TRUE)
trt <- as.numeric(period >= rep(start_t, each = n_period * n_per))

time_trend <- 0.1 * (period - 1)
cl_re      <- rep(rnorm(n_cl, 0, 0.5), each = n_period * n_per)

y <- cl_re + time_trend + 0.4 * trt +
     rnorm(length(cluster), 0, 1)

df <- data.frame(cluster = factor(cluster),
                 period  = factor(period),
                 trt     = trt, y = y)

fit <- lmer(y ~ trt + period + (1 | cluster), data = df)
summary(fit)$coefficients["trt", ]

heading: 15.342 Output & Results
text: mixed-effects model returns treatment effect estimate standard error reflects within-cluster -cluster information available staggered design. Including period fixed effects absorbs secular time trend; random cluster intercept absorbs -cluster baseline variation; residual captures within-cluster, within-period noise.

Reporting follows CONSORT extension stepped-wedge CRTs.” Always report secular trend treatment effect.

heading: 15.359 Introduction
text: Subgroup analyses clinical trials assess whether overall treatment effect varies across pre-specified baseline characteristics — age, sex, disease severity, comorbidity, biomarker status. provide important insight treatment heterogeneity, support guideline development, inform precision-medicine decisions. also notoriously prone -interpretation: enough subgroups enough cut-points, false-positive heterogeneity findings appear chance alone, literature replete cautionary tales subgroup claims failed replicate. Comparing within-subgroup \(p\)-values across strata (“significant one, ” fallacy) statistically incorrect within-subgroup test -powered comparison ignores multiplicity.Pre-specification key safeguard: small number biologically-motivated subgroups documented SAP carry interpretable evidentiary weight, post-hoc subgroup discovery best hypothesis-generating worst misleading.

heading: 15.363 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
n <- 400
arm <- factor(rep(c("ctrl", "trt"), each = n/2))
sex <- factor(sample(c("M", "F"), n, replace = TRUE))

y <- ifelse(arm == "trt",
            ifelse(sex == "F", 1.0, 0.3), 0) +
     rnorm(n)

fit <- lm(y ~ arm * sex)
summary(fit)$coefficients

by(data.frame(y, arm), sex, function(df) {
  t.test(y ~ arm, data = df)$estimate
})

heading: 15.370 Introduction
text: Weighted kappa extends Cohen’s kappa ordinal data crediting partial agreement: near-miss disagreements (mild vs moderate) weigh less dramatic disagreements (mild vs severe). standard inter-rater agreement statistic Likert-type scales, radiology grading, symptom-severity ratings.

heading: 15.371 Prerequisites
text: Cohen’s kappa; ordinal measurement.

heading: 15.372 Theory
text:
\[\kappa_w = 1 - \frac{\sum_{ij} w_{ij} f_{ij}}{\sum_{ij} w_{ij} e_{ij}},\]
\(w_{ij}\) disagreement weight category \(\) \(j\), \(f_{ij}\) observed cell frequency, \(e_{ij}\) expected chance.Weight schemes:
- Linear \(w_{ij} = |- j| / (k - 1)\) \(k\) categories.
- Quadratic \(w_{ij} = (- j)^2 / (k - 1)^2\) – forgiving near-miss disagreements.Quadratic common multi-category ordinal scales.

heading: 15.373 Assumptions
text: Category ordering meaningful equally spaced; two raters; independent ratings.

heading: 15.374 R Implementation
code:
library(psych)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 100
# Two raters on a 5-point ordinal scale
rater1 <- sample(1:5, n, replace = TRUE,
                 prob = c(0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.2, 0.1))
# Rater 2 agrees within +-1 with prob 0.8, otherwise random
rater2 <- ifelse(rbinom(n, 1, 0.8) == 1,
                 pmax(1, pmin(5, rater1 + sample(-1:1, n, replace = TRUE))),
                 sample(1:5, n, replace = TRUE))

cohen.kappa(cbind(rater1, rater2))$kappa    # unweighted
cohen.kappa(cbind(rater1, rater2),
            w = "squared")$weighted.kappa
cohen.kappa(cbind(rater1, rater2),
            w = "linear")$weighted.kappa

heading: 15.375 Output & Results
text: Unweighted kappa (~0.35), linear-weighted (~0.55), quadratic-weighted (~0.70); quadratic weights reward near-miss agreement heavily.

heading: 15.376 Interpretation
text: “Weighted kappa quadratic weights 0.72 (95 % CI 0.62-0.82), consistent substantial agreement; unweighted kappa 0.35 understates agreement crediting near-miss ratings.”

heading: 15.377 Practical Tips
text: Use quadratic weights clinical ordinal scales; reflect 1-step disagreements matter far less 3-step.Linear weights appropriate category spacing uniform-linear.Always specify weight scheme reporting weighted kappa.continuous data measurement error, use intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) instead.Quadratic-weighted kappa equals ICC(3, 1) certain assumptions – two methods converge ordinal Likert scales.

heading: 15.383 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(pROC)
library(MASS)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 15.384 1. Hypothesis
text:
Can logistic model Pima.tr (glucose, BMI, age) distinguish
diabetic non-diabetic patients well enough support screening
decision?

heading: 15.385 2. Visualise
code: ggplot(d, aes(glu, bmi, colour = type)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.7)

heading: 15.386 3. Assumptions
text:
Independence observations; probability diabetes monotone 
linear predictor; missingness.

heading: 15.387 4. Conduct
text: Fit simple logistic regression compute discrimination.NRI glucose-baseline.manual decision curve.
code:
d$p <- predict(fit, type = "response")
r  <- roc(d$type, d$p, direction = "<", quiet = TRUE)
auc(r)
coords(r, "best", ret = c("threshold", "sensitivity", "specificity",
                          "youden"), transpose = FALSE)
p0 <- predict(fit0, type = "response")
p1 <- d$p
# Continuous NRI
case   <- d$type == "Yes"
nri_up <- mean(p1[case]  > p0[case])   - mean(p1[case]  < p0[case])
nri_dn <- mean(p1[!case] < p0[!case])  - mean(p1[!case] > p0[!case])
nri <- nri_up + nri_dn
c(nri_cases = nri_up, nri_noncases = nri_dn, nri_total = nri)
dca <- sapply(thr, function(t) {
  treat <- p1 > t
  tp <- sum(treat &  case); fp <- sum(treat & !case); N <- length(case)
  tp / N - (fp / N) * (t / (1 - t))
})
nb_all <- sapply(thr, function(t) {
  tp <- sum(case); fp <- sum(!case); N <- length(case)
  tp / N - (fp / N) * (t / (1 - t))
})
tibble(threshold = thr, model = dca, treat_all = nb_all, treat_none = 0) |>
  pivot_longer(-threshold) |>
  ggplot(aes(threshold, value, colour = name)) + geom_line() +
  labs(x = "threshold probability", y = "net benefit")

heading: 15.388 5. Concluding statement

logistic model using glucose, BMI, age discriminated diabetic
non-diabetic patients MASS::Pima.tr AUC
round(.numeric(auc(r)), 3). Youden-optimal cut-point
occurred predicted probability 
round(coords(r, "best", ret = "threshold", transpose = FALSE)[1, 1], 2).
Adding BMI age glucose-baseline produced NRI 
round(nri, 2); decision curve showed net benefit 
“treat ” threshold probabilities roughly 0.15 0.5.Decision curves give clinical context: decision 
intervene , say, p = 0.2 discussion, model useful;
p = 0.05 p = 0.7, barely distinguishable treat-
treat-none.

heading: 15.400 4. Conduct
text: Convert pre-test odds post-test odds LR.Sketch ROC sweeping cutoff.
code:
FP <- tab["0", "1"]; TN <- tab["0", "0"]

sens <- TP / (TP + FN)
spec <- TN / (TN + FP)
ppv  <- TP / (TP + FP)
npv  <- TN / (TN + FN)
lrp  <- sens / (1 - spec)
lrn  <- (1 - sens) / spec

diag_tbl <- tibble(
  quantity = c("Sensitivity", "Specificity",
               "PPV", "NPV", "LR+", "LR-"),
  value = c(sens, spec, ppv, npv, lrp, lrn)
)
diag_tbl
pre_odds <- pre_prob / (1 - pre_prob)
post_odds_pos <- pre_odds * lrp
post_prob_pos <- post_odds_pos / (1 + post_odds_pos)
post_odds_neg <- pre_odds * lrn
post_prob_neg <- post_odds_neg / (1 + post_odds_neg)

tibble(
  pre_prob,
  post_prob_if_positive = post_prob_pos,
  post_prob_if_negative = post_prob_neg
)  cut = seq(min(pop$biomarker), max(pop$biomarker), length.out = 200)
) |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    tp = sum(pop$biomarker > cut & pop$disease == 1),
    fn = sum(pop$biomarker <= cut & pop$disease == 1),
    fp = sum(pop$biomarker > cut & pop$disease == 0),
    tn = sum(pop$biomarker <= cut & pop$disease == 0),
    sens = tp / (tp + fn),
    fpr  = fp / (fp + tn)
  ) |>
  ungroup()
ggplot(roc, aes(fpr, sens)) +
  geom_path(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_abline(linetype = 2, colour = "grey50") +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(x = "False positive rate (1 - specificity)",
       y = "Sensitivity")

Measurement-agreement studies ask whether two raters, two methods,

adjusts simple percent agreement agreement expected chance
given marginal frequencies; ranges −1 1 common

prevalence.continuous measurements, intraclass correlation (ICC)

single-number summary reliability, defined several flavours
(one-way, two-way, consistency vs absolute). Bland–Altman plot
shows pattern: plots difference two raters

SD). reveals bias, proportional bias, heteroscedasticity

heading: 15.409 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(psych)
library(irr)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 15.410 1. Goal
text:
Build two small rater datasets — one categorical, one continuous — 
compute matching agreement statistics.

heading: 15.411 2. Approach
text:
categorical example, simulate 100 radiograph classifications
(3 categories) two readers substantial perfect
agreement. continuous example, simulate 60 measurements 
two instruments, one small constant bias.
code:
cats <- c("normal", "mild", "severe")
truth <- sample(cats, 100, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2))
r1 <- ifelse(runif(100) < 0.2, sample(cats, 100, replace = TRUE), truth)
r2 <- ifelse(runif(100) < 0.25, sample(cats, 100, replace = TRUE), truth)
kap_tbl <- tibble(r1 = factor(r1, levels = cats),
                  r2 = factor(r2, levels = cats))

# continuous
n <- 60
true_val <- rnorm(n, 100, 15)
inst1 <- true_val + rnorm(n, 0, 3)
inst2 <- true_val + 2 + rnorm(n, 0, 3)    # small positive bias
meas <- tibble(inst1, inst2)

heading: 15.412 3. Execution
text: Cohen’s kappa:ICC via psych:Bland–Altman:
code:
mutate(mean_val = (inst1 + inst2) / 2,
         diff_val = inst2 - inst1)
loa <- mean(ba$diff_val) + c(-1.96, 0, 1.96) * sd(ba$diff_val)

ggplot(ba, aes(mean_val, diff_val)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = loa[1], linetype = 2, colour = "firebrick") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = loa[2], linetype = 1, colour = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = loa[3], linetype = 2, colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(x = "Mean of two instruments",
       y = "Difference (inst2 − inst1)")

heading: 15.413 4. Check
text:
ICC high (> 0.9) raters well correlated,
Bland–Altman plot shows small positive bias (inst2 reads
2 units higher average).

heading: 15.414 5. Report
text:
Cohen’s kappa two radiograph readers 
round(kappa2(kap_tbl[, c("r1","r2")])$value, 2). two
instruments, ICC (absolute agreement, two-way random) 
round(ICC(.matrix(meas))$results$ICC[2], 2), 
Bland–Altman plot revealed mean bias 
round(mean(ba$diff_val), 1) units 95% limits agreement
round(loa[1], 1) round(loa[3], 1).

Audit logistic prediction model Pima.tr simulated
subgroup attribute, sketch targets pipeline full

heading: 15.424 2. Approach
text:
Attach synthetic subgroup label — imagine clinic 
enrolment — compare performance.
code:
mutate(subgroup = sample(c("A", "B"), n(), replace = TRUE,
                           prob = c(0.7, 0.3)))
ggplot(d, aes(glu, fill = subgroup)) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.7, bins = 20, position = "identity")

heading: 15.425 3. Execution
text: Calibration stratified subgroup.minimal targets pipeline (sketch).
code:
d$p <- predict(fit, type = "response")

auc_overall <- as.numeric(auc(roc(d$type, d$p, quiet = TRUE)))
auc_by <- d |>
  group_by(subgroup) |>
  summarise(auc = as.numeric(auc(roc(type, p, quiet = TRUE))),
            n   = n(), .groups = "drop")
auc_by
  mutate(bin = cut(p, quantile(p, seq(0, 1, by = 0.2)),
                   include.lowest = TRUE)) |>
  group_by(subgroup, bin) |>
  summarise(pred = mean(p), obs = mean(type == "Yes"),
            n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(pred, obs, colour = subgroup)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = n)) + geom_line() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, colour = "grey50") +
  labs(x = "mean predicted", y = "observed proportion")
library(targets)
tar_script({
  library(tidyverse); library(MASS); library(pROC)
  list(
    tar_target(raw, as_tibble(MASS::Pima.tr)),
    tar_target(fit, glm(type ~ glu + bmi + age, data = raw, family = binomial())),
    tar_target(auc_overall,
               as.numeric(auc(roc(raw$type, predict(fit, type = "response"), quiet = TRUE)))),
    tar_target(report, tibble(auc = auc_overall))
  )
})
tar_make()
tar_read(report)

heading: 16.3 Introduction
text: summary ROC (SROC) curve visualises relationship pooled sensitivity specificity across meta-analysis diagnostic studies. Modern SROCs derived bivariate HSROC models properly account correlation sensitivity specificity thresholds vary.

heading: 16.4 Prerequisites
text: Sensitivity specificity; bivariate meta-analysis.

heading: 16.5 Theory
text:
bivariate model fits
\[\begin{pmatrix} \text{logit}(\text{Sens}_i) \\ \text{logit}(1 - \text{Spec}_i) \end{pmatrix} \sim N(\mu, \Sigma).\]SROC curve derived mapping range logit(1 - Spec) axis back expected logit(Sens), given bivariate mean covariance. curve shows sensitivity trades specificity threshold varies.

heading: 16.6 Assumptions
text: Logit sensitivity specificity bivariate normal; studies vary threshold; -study heterogeneity captured covariance matrix.

heading: 16.7 R Implementation
code:
library(mada)

set.seed(2026)
df <- data.frame(
  TP = c(45, 60, 32, 80, 28, 55, 70, 50),
  FN = c( 5, 10,  8, 20, 12,  5,  8, 15),
  FP = c(10, 15,  8, 20, 10, 12, 18,  9),
  TN = c(140, 165, 152, 180, 150, 138, 200, 175)
)

fit <- reitsma(df)

# Summary statistics
summary(fit)$coefficients[c("tsens", "tfpr"), ]

# SROC plot
plot(fit, sroclty = 1, sroclwd = 2, predict = TRUE, predlty = 2,
     main = "Bivariate SROC with 95% confidence and prediction regions")
points(fpr(df), sens(df), cex = 0.8, pch = 19, col = "grey40")

heading: 16.8 Output & Results
text: Pooled sensitivity FPR estimates; SROC curve confidence (mean) prediction (future-study) regions; individual study points overlaid.

heading: 16.9 Interpretation
text: “SROC curve AUC = 0.94, pooled sensitivity 0.84 specificity 0.91 summary operating point. Individual studies clustered tightly along curve, consistent threshold variation rather diagnostic accuracy variation.”

heading: 16.10 Practical Tips

heading: 16.13 Introduction
text: Pearson correlation coefficients bounded \([-1, 1]\), sampling distributions skewed heteroscedastic variance — variance depends underlying \(\rho\) \(n\). properties make raw correlations unsuitable inverse-variance meta-analysis. Fisher’s z transformation converts correlation approximately Normal scale variance independent \(\rho\), enabling standard random-effects pooling. pooling z scale, result back-transformed correlation interpretation.

heading: 16.14 Prerequisites
text: working understanding Pearson correlation, variance-stabilising transformations, inverse-variance meta-analysis random effects.

heading: 16.15 Theory
text: Fisher z transformation \[z = \tfrac{1}{2} \log\!\left(\frac{1 + r}{1 - r}\right) = \tanh^{-1}(r), \qquad \mathrm{Var}(z) \approx \frac{1}{n - 3}.\]variance constant (depends \(n\), \(\rho\)), inverse-variance weighting meta-analysis well-defined. Pool \(z\) values across studies via fixed-effect random-effects meta-analysis, back-transform \(r = \tanh(z)\) reporting natural correlation scale.back-transformation preserves coverage original scale provided transformed pooling correctly weighted; reporting \(z\) (SE) \(r\) (CI) standard.

heading: 16.16 Assumptions
text: Bivariate-Normal data study (basis variance formula); independent studies; sufficient sample size per study (\(n > 10\) rough minimum) asymptotic distribution reasonable.

heading: 16.17 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 10
# Simulated correlations and sample sizes
df <- data.frame(
  ri = c(0.25, 0.32, 0.18, 0.40, 0.28, 0.35, 0.22, 0.30, 0.45, 0.38),
  ni = sample(50:300, k, replace = TRUE)
)

es <- escalc(measure = "ZCOR", ri = ri, ni = ni, data = df)
re <- rma(yi, vi, data = es, method = "REML")

# Convert pooled z back to correlation
pooled_z  <- re$b
pooled_r  <- tanh(pooled_z)
ci_r      <- tanh(c(re$ci.lb, re$ci.ub))

c(pooled_r = round(pooled_r, 3),
  lower = round(ci_r[1], 3), upper = round(ci_r[2], 3))
re$I2

heading: 16.18 Output & Results
text: escalc(measure = "ZCOR") computes Fisher z variance per study; rma() fits random-effects model z scale. pooled correlation approximately 0.31 tight 95 % CI; \(^2\) quantifies heterogeneity.

heading: 16.19 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Random-effects meta-analysis 10 studies (REML, n total = 1{,}523) gave pooled correlation \(r = 0.31\) (95 % CI 0.25 0.37) modest heterogeneity (\(^2 = 28 \%\), \(\tau^2 = 0.012\)); pooling Fisher z scale back-transforming maintains correct coverage despite \([-1, 1]\) bound \(r\).” Always pool z scale report Fisher z back-transformed \(r\).

heading: 16.20 Practical Tips
text: Always pool Fisher z scale; pooling raw \(r\) biases estimates high correlations produces incorrect coverage.metafor::escalc(measure = "ZCOR") handles Fisher z transformation variance computation single call.Report z (SE) back-transformed r (CI); r interpretable substantive readers.partial correlations, apply approach degrees--freedom-adjusted sample size \(n - p\) \(p\) number partialled-variables.Hartung-Knapp small-sample adjustment (rma(..., test = "knha")) especially important studies unevenly sized; standard inverse-variance gives -confident intervals settings.meta-analysis intraclass correlations correlation-like statistics, Fisher z framework applies appropriate variance formulae.

heading: 16.24 Introduction
text: Cumulative meta-analysis plots running pooled estimate studies added one one chronological (pre-specified) order. successive pool incorporates one study, producing sequence estimates traces body evidence evolved time. resulting forest plot reveals cumulative effect first crossed meaningful threshold — clinical relevance, statistical significance, stability — whether later studies overturned, attenuated, simply added precision early consensus. Cumulative meta-analysis widely used retrospective evidence syntheses assess research efficiency: evidence pooled real time, answer clear, many subsequent confirmatory trials arguably redundant?

heading: 16.25 Prerequisites
text: working understanding inverse-variance meta-analytic pooling, sequential-analysis idea, awareness repeated looks accumulating evidence raise multiplicity concerns goal decision-making rather description.

heading: 16.26 Theory
text: ordering studies \(1, \dots, k\), cumulative estimate step \(t\) pooled effect computed studies \(1, \dots, t\) chosen meta-analysis model. Plotting \(\hat\theta_{(1:t)}\) confidence intervals along ordering axis (typically year) reveals stability convergence: horizontal band overlapping CIs indicates answer settled early, shifting estimate signals later evidence materially changed conclusions.procedure can use precision-based ordering (largest studies first) assess sensitivity small-study effect, risk--bias ordering (low-risk studies first) assess whether pooled conclusions hinge lower-quality evidence.

heading: 16.27 Assumptions
text: Study ordering correctly recorded; studies independent; meta-analysis model used step full pool. Cumulative meta-analysis descriptive, inferential — formal sequential-testing methods trial-sequential analysis adjust repeated looks; ordinary cumulative meta-analysis .

heading: 16.28 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 12
year <- sort(sample(1990:2023, k))
yi <- rnorm(k, 0.35, 0.2)
vi <- 1 / sample(50:250, k, replace = TRUE)

df <- data.frame(year, yi, vi)

res <- rma(yi, vi, data = df, method = "REML")
cum <- cumul(res, order = df$year)

plot(cum, main = "Cumulative meta-analysis by year",
     xlab = "Year")

heading: 16.29 Output & Results
text: cumul() returns running-pooled estimate, confidence interval, heterogeneity statistics step specified ordering. associated plot() method renders forest-style display row corresponds “evidence \(t\)-th study”, making convergence visually obvious. Many pooled effects stabilise four six well-powered studies; remaining studies typically tighten CI without shifting centre.

heading: 16.30 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Cumulative meta-analysis ordered publication year showed pooled effect became statistically significant third study (1994) remained essentially unchanged 2023; 18 trials published thereafter added precision alter direction magnitude conclusion, suggesting opportunity earlier evidence-based decision-making living synthesis place.” Always describe evidence stabilised later studies contributed.

heading: 16.31 Practical Tips
text: Order primarily publication date temporal interpretation; precision-based risk--bias orderings useful complementary sensitivity analyses.Cumulative meta-analysis descriptive — inferential stopping rules repeated looks, use trial-sequential analysis (TSA) appropriate alpha-spending.Convergence stability first studies often reveals research waste: many subsequent trials confirmed already known.Combine meta-regression year formally test whether effect sizes drifted time, e.g. practice patterns populations changed.Plot point estimate confidence interval; shrinking CI without estimate movement different signal moving estimate.Use cumulative meta-analysis motivate living-systematic-review infrastructure: continuous evidence updating prevents “many years late” problem retrospective cumulative plots often expose.

heading: 16.32 R Packages Used
text: metafor::cumul() canonical cumulative pooling plot() method; meta::metacum() alternative interface aligned meta package’s forest-plot conventions; RTSA gsDesign trial-sequential analysis inferential adjustment multiple looks required; metaviz richer cumulative-forest visualisations.

heading: 16.35 Introduction
text: Meta-analysis diagnostic test accuracy pools per-study sensitivity specificity summarise well test discriminates disease non-disease across multiple primary studies. Unlike meta-analysis treatment effects, two paired outcomes intrinsically correlated: sensitivity specificity determined jointly test threshold, study choosing stricter cut-trade higher specificity lower sensitivity. Naively pooling metric separately ignores trade-produces estimates misrepresent test’s underlying performance. modern approach bivariate random-effects modelling (Reitsma et al., 2005) hierarchical summary ROC modelling (Rutter Gatsonis, 2001), respect correlation structure yield interpretable summary points curves.

heading: 16.36 Prerequisites

heading: 16.37 Theory
text: bivariate random-effects model jointly fits logit-sensitivity logit-specificity using bivariate Normal random effect across studies, estimating pooled sensitivity, pooled specificity, -study variances , correlation . HSROC model parameterises underlying ROC curve directly random study-specific thresholds accuracy parameters, producing summary curve explicitly accommodates threshold variation. Reitsma’s bivariate model HSROC model mathematically equivalent standard parameterisation; choice mainly one presentation — pooled point confidence ellipse versus summary curve.

Random effects logit scale bivariate Normal; threshold variation across studies captured implicitly random-effects structure; case ascertainment reference-standard verification comparable across studies (otherwise applicability concerns enter, captured QUADAS-2 framework).

Reporting follows PRISMA-DTA.” Always report summary points SROC curve, plus -study correlation.

heading: 16.46 Introduction
text: single meta-analysis often spans studies report effect sizes different metrics: randomised trials report standardised mean differences (Cohen’s \(d\) Hedges’s \(g\)), observational analyses report correlations (\(r\)), binary-outcome studies report odds ratios () risk ratios (RR). Pooling heterogeneous evidence requires converting every per-study effect common metric — typically \(d\) studies continuous, binary, \(r\) correlational. Analytical conversion formulas, derived standard latent-distribution assumptions, map metrics together variances. conversion correctly — documenting assumptions — critical naively recycling effect size scales without proper variance propagation produces falsely precise pooled estimates.

heading: 16.47 Prerequisites
text: working understanding principal effect-size metrics (\(d\), \(r\), , RR), variance formulas, delta method propagating variance non-linear transformations.

heading: 16.48 Theory
text: standard conversion formulas, derived assumption underlying latent variable approximately Normal, :\[d \r: \quad r = \frac{d}{\sqrt{d^2 + }}, \qquad = \frac{(n_1 + n_2)^2}{n_1 n_2};\]\[\log\mathrm{} \d: \quad d = \log(\mathrm{}) \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\pi};\]\[r \d: \quad d = \frac{2r}{\sqrt{1 - r^2}}.\]conversion corresponding variance formula obtained delta method; using (rather un-transformed variance) essential, non-linear mapping changes precision resulting estimate.

heading: 16.49 Assumptions
text: underlying latent distributions approximately Normal (\(d \leftrightarrow r\) conversions) follow standard logistic-Normal correspondence (conversions), dichotomisation underlying reported approximately median latent variable, variance propagated conversion using appropriate formula rather original metric’s variance.

heading: 16.50 R Implementation
code:
library(esc)

set.seed(2026)
d <- 0.5; var_d <- 0.04; n1 <- 50; n2 <- 50
conv_dr <- esc::convert_d2r(d = d, v = var_d,
                            grp1n = n1, grp2n = n2)
conv_dr

log_or <- log(2.5); var_logor <- 0.15
conv_od <- esc::convert_or2d(or = exp(log_or), v = var_logor)
conv_od

r <- 0.3; var_r <- 0.004
conv_rd <- esc::convert_r2d(r = r, v = var_r)
conv_rd

heading: 16.51 Output & Results
text: esc package returns list converted point estimate, delta-method variance, corresponding standard error confidence interval. conversion preserves central effect properly propagated variance, allowing inverse-variance pooling harmonised metric without artificially inflating precision.

heading: 16.52 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Effect sizes harmonised standardised mean differences. Continuous-outcome studies contributed \(d = 0.50\) directly; binary-outcome studies converted \(\mathrm{} = 2.5\) \(d = 0.51\) using \(d = \log(\mathrm{}) \cdot \sqrt{3}/\pi\); correlational studies converted \(r = 0.30\) \(d = 0.63\) using \(d = 2r/\sqrt{1 - r^2}\). Variances propagated delta method. common metric enabled inverse-variance pooling across study designs documented PRISMA Supplementary Table S2.” Always document every conversion explicitly.

heading: 16.53 Practical Tips
text: Use validated conversion functions esc, compute.es, metafor::escalc(); manual arithmetic formulas highly error-prone, especially variance propagation.Always propagate variance conversion using delta-method-derived formulas; using original metric’s variance produces falsely narrow confidence intervals converted scale.Converting binary continuous metrics assumes logistic-Normal latent-variable structure; outcomes obviously violate — categorical two levels treated binary, skewed continuous variables — conversion approximate best.highly skewed continuous outcomes (e.g., counts, costs, biomarkers long tails), \(d\)-based conversions may misrepresent substantive effect; consider rank-based effect sizes per-domain pooling instead.Document every conversion PRISMA supplementary table, including formula used, input quantities, converted estimate, propagated variance — methodological reviewers routinely check .meta-analysis mostly one metric studies another, conversion appropriate; metrics evenly split, consider whether combined-metric analysis (e.g., generalised linear mixed model underlying outcome) might defensible.

heading: 16.54 R Packages Used

heading: 16.57 Introduction
text: Egger’s regression test, introduced Egger colleagues 1997, provides formal hypothesis test funnel-plot asymmetry — commonly cited indirect symptom publication bias small-study effects. funnel plot offers visual diagnostic depends eye beholder, Egger’s test reduces question regression intercept whose distribution symmetry known. statistically significant intercept indicates small studies systematically report effects different magnitude larger studies, may reflect selective publication, methodological heterogeneity confounded sample size, genuine small-study heterogeneity. Although interpretation requires nuance, Egger’s test remains de facto standard quantitative complement funnel plot recommended Cochrane Handbook meta-analyses least ten studies.

heading: 16.58 Prerequisites
text: Familiarity funnel plots, small-study-effect concept, ordinary least-squares regression weighted variants.

heading: 16.59 Theory
text: test regresses standardised effect \(y_i / \mathrm{SE}(y_i)\) precision \(1 / \mathrm{SE}(y_i)\). null hypothesis funnel symmetry regression line passes origin; intercept tests directly. non-zero intercept indicates standardised effect depends precision — exactly pattern produced small, imprecise studies -represented one tail funnel selective publication.Statistical power low fewer ten studies, inflated -study heterogeneity large; \(k \geq 10\) conventional minimum interpretable use, test never sole basis declaring publication bias.

heading: 16.60 Assumptions
text: Studies independent; effect estimate approximately normally distributed analysis scale; relationship standardised effect precision linear null; meta-analysis contains enough studies asymmetry detectable random-sampling noise. binary outcomes standard Egger test biased — Harbord’s Peters’s modifications preferred.

heading: 16.61 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 20
yi <- rnorm(k, 0.4, 0.2)
vi <- 1 / sample(20:300, k, replace = TRUE)

keep <- !(sqrt(vi) > 0.1 & abs(yi) < 0.2)
yi_b <- yi[keep]; vi_b <- vi[keep]

res <- rma(yi_b, vi_b, method = "REML")

egger <- regtest(res, model = "lm")
print(egger)

fsn(yi_b, vi_b)

heading: 16.62 Output & Results
text: regtest() returns regression intercept, standard error, test statistic, two-sided \(p\)-value null funnel symmetry. companion fail-safe-\(N\) statistic fsn() reports many additional null-effect studies needed drive pooled estimate non-significance — complementary, intuitive sensitivity measure helps readers gauge robustness.

heading: 16.63 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Egger’s regression suggested significant funnel asymmetry (intercept 1.28, 95 % CI 0.21 2.35, \(p = 0.02\)), consistent small-study effects publication bias. fail-safe \(N\) 42 indicates pooled conclusion diluted non-significance 42 unpublished null studies, suggesting moderate unlimited robustness; trim--fill PET-PEESE sensitivity analyses reported supplement.” Always pair test result least one bias-adjusted sensitivity analysis.

heading: 16.64 Practical Tips
text: Egger’s test low power \(k < 10\); Cochrane Handbook explicitly discourages use threshold recommends visual funnel inspection .binary outcomes, prefer Harbord’s modified test (continuous data) Peters’s test (rare events); standard Egger test inflates type-error rate effect measure log-log-RR.significant intercept synonymous publication bias — true -study heterogeneity correlated study size (small studies systematically using different populations methods) can produce signal.Sensitivity-analyse trim--fill PET-PEESE; adjust pooled estimate apparent asymmetry reported alongside unadjusted estimate Egger’s test significant.low-prevalence binary outcomes, asymmetry can arise continuity-correction artefacts rather genuine bias; Mantel–Haenszel without correction useful diagnostic alternative.Report intercept estimate CI, \(p\)-value; magnitude asymmetry conveys dichotomous significance.

heading: 16.65 R Packages Used

heading: 16.68 Introduction
text: meta-analysis pools effect-size estimates multiple studies single summary. two classical approaches – fixed-effect model random-effects model – rest different assumptions studies represent. Choosing technical afterthought; substantive decision scope inference, can change point estimate uncertainty.

heading: 16.69 Prerequisites
text: reader know effect size (mean difference, standardised mean difference, log-odds ratio, etc.), standard error computed, weighted average works. Familiarity meta metafor packages helpful.

heading: 16.70 Theory
text: Let \(\hat{\theta}_i\) effect-size estimate study \(\) variance \(v_i\), \(= 1, \ldots, k\).

heading: 16.70.1 Fixed-effect model
text: Assumes studies estimate true effect \(\theta\):\[\hat{\theta}_i = \theta + \varepsilon_i, \qquad \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, v_i).\]optimal pooled estimate inverse-variance-weighted mean\[\hat{\theta}_{\text{FE}} = \frac{\sum w_i \hat{\theta}_i}{\sum w_i}, \qquad w_i = 1/v_i,\]standard error \(1 / \sqrt{\sum w_i}\). Scope inference: set studies included.

heading: 16.70.2 Random-effects model
text: Assumes study estimates study-specific true effect, drawn distribution true effects across population plausible studies:\[\hat{\theta}_i = \theta_i + \varepsilon_i, \qquad \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2), \qquad \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, v_i).\]weights become \(w_i^* = 1/(v_i + \tau^2)\), \(\tau^2\) -study variance, estimated DerSimonian-Laird, REML, Paule-Mandel, Sidik-Jonkman. pooled estimate \(\hat{\mu} = \sum w_i^* \hat{\theta}_i / \sum w_i^*\). Scope inference: population studies included ones sample.\(\tau^2 = 0\) (heterogeneity), random-effects model reduces fixed-effect model. \(\tau^2\) large, random-effects weights balanced (small studies get relatively weight), random-effects CI wider fixed-effect CI – properly , since reflects -study variability.

heading: 16.71 Assumptions
text: models assume:Study-level effect estimates approximately normal known variance.Studies independent .publication bias selection distorting set included studies.fixed-effect model additionally assumes single true effect. random-effects model assumes normal distribution true effects; Hartung-Knapp adjustment corrects CIs small \(k\) imprecise \(\tau^2\) estimates.

heading: 16.72 R Implementation
text: metacont() handles continuous outcomes mean differences. Setting method.tau = "REML" uses REML \(\tau^2\); method.random.ci = "HK" applies Hartung-Knapp correction random-effects confidence interval.
code:
library(meta)

dat <- data.frame(
  study = paste("Study", 1:8),
  mean_t = c(11.2, 9.8, 12.5, 10.1, 13.0, 8.7, 11.6, 10.9),
  sd_t   = c(2.1, 2.4, 2.0, 2.6, 1.9, 2.8, 2.2, 2.3),
  n_t    = c(40, 35, 50, 30, 45, 25, 38, 42),
  mean_c = c(9.8, 9.5, 10.1, 9.9, 10.5, 9.0, 10.0, 10.2),
  sd_c   = c(2.0, 2.3, 2.1, 2.5, 2.0, 2.7, 2.1, 2.2),
  n_c    = c(40, 35, 50, 30, 45, 25, 38, 42)
)

m <- metacont(n.e = n_t, mean.e = mean_t, sd.e = sd_t,
              n.c = n_c, mean.c = mean_c, sd.c = sd_c,
              studlab = study, data = dat,
              sm = "MD",
              method.tau = "REML",
              method.random.ci = "HK")

summary(m)

forest(m, leftcols = c("studlab", "n.e", "mean.e", "sd.e",
                        "n.c", "mean.c", "sd.c"))

heading: 16.73 Output & Results
text: Typical output:fixed-effect random-effects point estimates similar (1.21 vs. 1.20), random-effects confidence interval 50% wider, reflecting moderate heterogeneity (\(^2 = 53\%\)).

heading: 16.74 Interpretation
text: Report models \(^2\) non-negligible, emphasise random-effects result Hartung-Knapp CI primary inference heterogeneity plausible substantive grounds. Include \(\tau^2\) estimate prediction interval – latter answers “true effect expect future, similar study?” often informative summary clinician.

heading: 16.75 Practical Tips
text: Choose model based subject-matter plausibility, whether heterogeneity statistically significant – \(Q\)-tests underpowered studies.Report prediction interval alongside pooled estimate; honest summary pooled result implies new study.Use REML Paule-Mandel \(\tau^2\); DerSimonian-Laird historical default understates \(\tau^2\) studies.Never pool fewer 3 studies without explicit caveat; 5 fewer, consider narrative synthesis rather meta-analysis.heterogeneity high, investigate subgroup analysis meta-regression pooling.

heading: 16.78 Introduction
text: forest plot canonical meta-analysis figure single important visual artefact systematic review delivers. included study appears row point estimate, confidence interval, pooling weight; overall summary rendered bottom diamond whose width represents pooled confidence interval. compact layout simultaneously conveys per-study effects, study-level precision, -study scatter, overall conclusion — glance, without needing consult separate table. Editorial standards major clinical journals routinely require forest plot every meta-analytic synthesis, reviewers rely judge whether pooled estimate fairly represents constituent studies.

heading: 16.79 Prerequisites
text: Inverse-variance pooling, distinction fixed-effect random-effects models, understanding meta-analytic weights translate study precision influence summary estimate.

heading: 16.80 Theory
text: study’s square plotted point estimate size proportional meta-analytic weight (inverse variance fixed-effect, DerSimonian–Laird / REML weights random-effects). Horizontal whiskers extend 95 % confidence interval. pooled estimate diamond centred summary, horizontal extent equal pooled CI; random-effects models, additional prediction interval often overlaid dashed bar convey next study’s true effect plausibly expected.Standard columns include: study label (often author + year), sample sizes per arm, point estimate CI, percent weight contribution. Heterogeneity statistics (\(Q\), \(^2\), \(\tau^2\)) typically appear figure footer.

heading: 16.81 Assumptions
text: Study-level estimates variances scale, expressed direction (“right zero” “right one” consistent clinical meaning), pooled stated model. Forest plots impose extra assumptions — visualise whatever model produced inputs.

heading: 16.82 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 8
yi <- c(0.32, 0.51, 0.28, 0.45, 0.38, 0.22, 0.60, 0.35)
vi <- c(0.02, 0.03, 0.015, 0.025, 0.04, 0.018, 0.05, 0.022)
authors <- paste("Study", LETTERS[1:k])

res <- rma(yi, vi, method = "REML")

forest(res, slab = authors,
       header = c("Study", "SMD [95% CI]"),
       xlab = "Standardised mean difference (Hedges' g)",
       mlab = "Random-effects (REML)",
       cex = 0.9)

heading: 16.83 Output & Results
text: forest() method renders study-wise rows squares sized random-effects weight, whiskers showing 95 % CIs, summary diamond bottom. header label arguments customise axis label column heads, addpred = TRUE overlays random-effects prediction interval dashed bar around diamond.

heading: 16.84 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “forest plot summarises eight randomised trials; seven favour intervention confidence intervals excluding null, random-effects pooled SMD 0.39 (95 % CI 0.28 0.50, \(^2 = 27 \%\), \(\tau^2 = 0.012\)). 95 % prediction interval (0.10 0.68) suggests true effect future trial likely positive variable magnitude.” Always pair forest plot prose quantifies central effect heterogeneity context.

heading: 16.85 Practical Tips
text: Sort studies publication year (temporal reading), effect size (visual scanning dispersion), subgroup (pre-specified comparisons); choice match scientific question.Always include heterogeneity statistics (\(^2\), \(\tau^2\), \(Q\)) figure footer; readers judge “pool reasonable?” without .Overlay prediction interval (addpred = TRUE metafor); CI describes uncertainty mean, prediction interval readers actually want know future studies.complex layouts — multiple subgroups, side--side outcomes, risk--bias columns — forestplot, forester, metafor::forest() custom ilab arguments give finer control default meta::forest().Always include clear directional legend (“favours intervention ← → favours control”); ambiguity common cause reader misinterpretation.Round sensible number digits (typically 2 SMDs, 2 log-ratios back-transformed); excessive precision suggests false confidence clutters plot.

heading: 16.86 R Packages Used
