heading: 16.89 Introduction
text: funnel plot, introduced Light Pillemer 1984, classical visual diagnostic publication bias small-study effects meta-analysis. study contributes single point whose horizontal coordinate effect estimate whose vertical coordinate precision (typically standard error inverse). null bias homogeneous true effect, plot resembles symmetric inverted funnel: large, precise studies cluster near pooled estimate top smaller, less precise studies fan symmetrically . Asymmetry — visible empty zone one two lower corners — canonical visual red flag smaller studies effects one direction may missing synthesis, often never published.

heading: 16.90 Prerequisites
text: working understanding pooled meta-analysis, relationship sample size standard error, small-study-effect concept links study size magnitude reported effects.

heading: 16.91 Theory
text: horizontal axis carries effect estimate analysis scale (log-, log-RR, SMD); vertical axis carries precision, typically standard error inverted large, precise studies sit top. Pseudo-confidence-interval lines fan symmetrically pooled estimate, giving funnel name. symmetry, small studies scatter sides; empty lower corner suggests small studies null opposite-direction effects may missing synthesis, visual signature publication bias.central conceptual point: asymmetry indicates small-study effect, publication bias one possible cause among several. Treating asymmetry synonym bias common interpretive error.

heading: 16.92 Assumptions
text: Studies independent; null bias effect estimates scatter symmetrically around true pooled effect; precision well measured. interpretation requires enough studies asymmetry detectable sampling noise — typically least ten.

heading: 16.93 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 20
yi <- rnorm(k, 0.4, 0.2)
vi <- 1 / sample(20:300, k, replace = TRUE)

keep <- !(sqrt(vi) > 0.1 & abs(yi) < 0.2)
yi_biased <- yi[keep]; vi_biased <- vi[keep]

res_biased <- rma(yi_biased, vi_biased, method = "REML")
funnel(res_biased, main = "Funnel plot (potentially biased)")

regtest(res_biased, model = "lm")

heading: 16.94 Output & Results
text: funnel() renders canonical scatter pseudo-CI lines. Visual asymmetry — typically empty lower corner — combined significant Egger Harbord regression test data quantifies impression. Reporting views together (visual plus formal test) standard approach two complementary diagnostics rather redundant.

heading: 16.95 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “funnel plot visibly asymmetric empty region small-study, low-effect quadrant; Egger’s regression test confirmed asymmetry (intercept 1.28, \(p = 0.02\)). Publication bias plausible explanation, true heterogeneity correlated study size ruled ; trim--fill PET-PEESE sensitivity analyses reported supplement showed attenuated still positive pooled effect.” Always describe visual pattern formal-test result, acknowledge alternative explanations.

heading: 16.96 Practical Tips
text: Funnel plots require least ten studies meaningful interpretation; fewer studies, visual dominated sampling noise asymmetry impressions unreliable.Asymmetry many possible causes besides publication bias: true -study heterogeneity correlated size, selective outcome reporting, methodological quality differences, chance small \(k\). Discuss explicitly reporting asymmetric funnel.Combine visual inspection least one formal test: Egger’s regression continuous outcomes, Harbord’s modification binary outcomes (log-), Peters’s test rare events.binary outcomes, plot log() versus standard error log scale; raw funnels intrinsically asymmetric mathematical reasons unrelated bias.Include sensitivity analyses — trim--fill, PET-PEESE, Vevea–Hedges weight-function selection models — asymmetry detected; difference adjusted unadjusted estimates readers actually need.Contour-enhanced funnel plots (Peters et al., 2008) overlay significance contours help distinguish missingness near null (suggestive publication bias) missingness elsewhere (suggestive explanations).

heading: 16.97 R Packages Used

heading: 16.100 Introduction
text: Heterogeneity meta-analysis refers variation true effects across studies exceeds expected sampling variability alone. Cochran’s \(Q\) test asks whether heterogeneity present ; \(^2\) quantifies much total observed variability attributable genuine -study differences rather within-study sampling noise; \(\tau^2\) provides actual variance true effects analysis scale. Together three statistics inform choice fixed-effect random-effects models, width prediction intervals, appropriateness subgroup meta-regression follow-analyses. Reporting three now universal standard systematic-review publications.

heading: 16.101 Prerequisites
text: working understanding fixed-effect random-effects meta-analytic pooling, chi-squared distribution, inverse-variance weighting.

heading: 16.102 Theory
text: Cochran’s \(Q\) weighted sum squared deviations study effects fixed-effect pooled estimate:\[Q = \sum_i w_i (\theta_i - \hat\theta_{\text{FE}})^2.\]null homogeneity, \(Q\) follows \(\chi^2_{k-1}\) distribution. \(^2\) statistic rescales \(Q\) proportion:\[^2 = \max\!\left\{0, \, \frac{Q - (k - 1)}{Q}\right\} \cdot 100\%,\]interpretable proportion total variation attributable -study heterogeneity rather within-study error. Higgins’s benchmarks : 0–40 % low, 30–60 % moderate, 50–90 % substantial, 75–100 % considerable. variance component \(\tau^2\) — typically estimated REML, DerSimonian–Laird, Paule–Mandel — gives heterogeneity effect-size scale feeds directly prediction-interval computation.

heading: 16.103 Assumptions
text: Studies independent within-study variances known (precisely estimated). \(Q\) test famously underpowered small numbers studies famously -powered large meta-analyses precise studies, \(p\)-value never sole criterion heterogeneity decision.

heading: 16.104 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 12
theta <- rnorm(k, 0.4, 0.3)
n_per <- sample(50:400, k, replace = TRUE)
var_i <- 4 / n_per

yi <- theta + rnorm(k, 0, sqrt(var_i))
vi <- var_i

res <- rma(yi, vi, method = "REML")
cat("Q =", round(res$QE, 2),
    "(df=", res$k - 1, ", p =", round(res$QEp, 3), ")\n")
cat("I^2 =", round(res$I2, 1), "%;  tau^2 =", round(res$tau2, 3), "\n")

heading: 16.105 Output & Results
text: rma() returns \(Q\) statistic \(p\)-value, \(^2\) percentage, \(\tau^2\) effect-size scale. Confidence intervals \(\tau^2\) \(^2\) obtained confint(res), prediction interval comes predict(res). Reporting four — \(Q\) (df \(p\)), \(^2\), \(\tau^2\), prediction interval — now standard practice.

heading: 16.106 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “-study heterogeneity substantial: \(Q = 31.4\) (df \(= 11\), \(p = 0.001\)), \(^2 = 65 \%\), \(\tau^2 = 0.07\) (\(\tau = 0.26\)). 95 % prediction interval true effect future study \([-0.10, 0.85]\), far wider confidence interval pooled mean (\([0.21, 0.55]\)). random-effects model therefore appropriate, pre-specified meta-regression study year population age conducted explain heterogeneity.” Always combine three statistics prediction interval.

heading: 16.107 Practical Tips
text: rely \(Q\)’s \(p\)-value alone; underpowered \(k\) small -powered individual studies precise. Always interpret alongside \(^2\) \(\tau^2\).Report \(Q\), \(^2\), \(\tau^2\), 95 % prediction interval together; tells different complementary story heterogeneity.Substantial heterogeneity prompt follow-analyses: pre-specified meta-regression continuous moderators, subgroup analysis categorical ones, explicit reporting prediction intervals communicate practical range true effects.\(^2\) depends precision individual studies well \(\tau^2\): large, precise studies inflate \(^2\) true heterogeneity. variance component \(\tau^2\) therefore directly comparable across meta-analyses \(^2\).Confidence intervals \(^2\) \(\tau^2\) via confint() metafor routinely reported; wide CI \(^2\) temper interpretation single point estimate.Use REML \(\tau^2\) default; DerSimonian–Laird convenient traditional can biased downward heterogeneity large, Paule–Mandel estimator also widely accepted.

heading: 16.108 R Packages Used
text: metafor::rma() canonical \(Q\), \(^2\), \(\tau^2\) output, confint() variance-component intervals predict() prediction intervals; meta::metagen() related meta functions alternative interface; dmetar Knapp–Hartung-corrected heterogeneity reporting; metafor permutest() permutation-based heterogeneity inference small \(k\).

heading: 16.111 Introduction
text: Individual participant data (IPD) meta-analysis pools raw participant-level data multiple studies, enabling analyses aggregate-data meta-analyses – time--event across studies, individual-level effect modification, harmonised outcome definitions. gold standard feasible requires data-sharing agreements.

heading: 16.112 Prerequisites
text: Meta-analysis pooling; mixed-effects models.

heading: 16.113 Theory
text: Two-stage: analyse study separately obtain effect variance; pool standard meta-analysis methods.One-stage: fit single hierarchical model combined data study random effect. Allows individual-participant-level interactions.approaches typically yield similar results main effects; one-stage preferred individual-level moderator analyses.

heading: 16.114 Assumptions
text: Data-sharing agreements place; common outcome definitions across studies; correct handling study-level heterogeneity.

heading: 16.115 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4)

set.seed(2026)
n_studies <- 5
participants_per <- 100

# Simulate IPD
df <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:n_studies, function(s) {
  n <- participants_per
  data.frame(
    study = s,
    arm = factor(sample(c("trt", "ctrl"), n, replace = TRUE)),
    age = rnorm(n, 50, 10)
  )
}))
df$arm_num <- as.integer(df$arm == "trt")
df$y <- 0.4 * df$arm_num + 0.05 * df$age +
        rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 1) +
        rep(rnorm(n_studies, 0, 0.3), each = participants_per)

# One-stage mixed-effects model
one_stage <- lmer(y ~ arm + age + (1 | study), data = df)
summary(one_stage)$coefficients

# Two-stage: per-study effects, then pool
per_study <- split(df, df$study) |> sapply(function(s) {
  m <- lm(y ~ arm + age, data = s)
  c(est = coef(m)["armtrt"], var = vcov(m)["armtrt", "armtrt"])
})
library(metafor)
ma <- rma(yi = per_study["est.armtrt", ], vi = per_study["var", ],
          method = "REML")
ma$b

heading: 16.116 Output & Results
text: One-stage arm coefficient equivalent two-stage pooled estimate; typically close agreement simple models.

heading: 16.117 Interpretation
text: “IPD meta-analysis estimated intervention effect 0.42 (95 % CI 0.28-0.56, p < 0.001); two-stage one-stage approaches agreed closely, supporting finding’s robustness analytic framing.”

heading: 16.118 Practical Tips
text: Prospective IPD consortium studies avoid selection bias data-availability issues.Harmonise outcomes pooling; different outcome definitions across studies cause bias.One-stage model allows individual-level interactions (effect modifiers); valuable ecological moderators.survival endpoints, IPD required (aggregate hazard ratios difficult pool correctly).Report PRISMA-IPD checklist transparency.

heading: 16.121 Introduction
text: Leave-one-(LOO) meta-analysis systematically removes study re-computes pooled estimate using remaining \(k - 1\) studies. resulting per-removal estimates show much study drives overall conclusion. Studies whose removal substantially changes pooled effect — magnitude, significance, — influential warrant careful interpretation. LOO routine sensitivity analysis modern meta-analysis reporting.

heading: 16.122 Prerequisites
text: working understanding pooled meta-analysis (fixed random effects) concept influence diagnostics regression analysis.

heading: 16.123 Theory
text: study \(= 1, \dots, k\), compute \(\hat\theta_{(-)}\) pooling \(k - 1\) studies meta-analysis model. Plot \(\hat\theta_{(-)}\) confidence interval across \(\); large deviations full-model pooled estimate indicate influential studies.LOO complements influence measures: Cook’s distance DFBETAS quantify much study perturbs overall fit, Baujat plots show study’s contribution heterogeneity (\(Q\)) versus contribution pooled estimate. Used together, diagnostics provide comprehensive picture studies conclusion depends .

heading: 16.124 Assumptions
text: underlying random-effects meta-analysis; leave-one-fit uses model method full pooling.

heading: 16.125 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 10
yi <- c(0.35, 0.42, 0.25, 0.55, 0.28, 0.39, 0.95, 0.31, 0.44, 0.36)
vi <- c(0.02, 0.03, 0.015, 0.02, 0.025, 0.018, 0.01, 0.04, 0.02, 0.022)

res <- rma(yi, vi, method = "REML")
loo <- leave1out(res)

print(loo[, c("estimate", "ci.lb", "ci.ub", "I2")])

# Influence diagnostics: Cook's distance, DFBETAS
inf <- influence(res)
print(inf$inf)

heading: 16.126 Output & Results
text: leave1out() returns per-study table recomputed pooled estimate, confidence interval, \(^2\) study omitted. influence() provides Cook’s distance, DFBETAS, hat values, diagnostics computed jointly.

heading: 16.127 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Leave-one-analysis revealed study 7 (SMD = 0.95, largest single estimate) highly influential; removing reduced pooled SMD 0.41 0.35 shrunk \(^2\) 38 % 18 %. Conclusions robust removal study; sensitivity analysis without study 7 reported supplementary.” Always describe influential study removal effect size heterogeneity.

heading: 16.128 Practical Tips
text: Pair LOO Cook’s distance DFBETAS metafor::influence(); combination flags effect-size influence overall fit influence.removing single study crosses significance line, conclusion fragile reported .LOO descriptive — correct bias. Use inform readers robustness, “clean ” meta-analysis post-hoc.Report LOO results least one study markedly influential; otherwise table conveys useful information beyond headline pooled estimate.Combine Baujat plots (metafor::baujat()) richer visualisation: studies upper-right corner contribute disproportionately heterogeneity effect.meta-analyses fewer 5 studies, LOO unstable largely uninformative; report interpret cautiously.

heading: 16.129 R Packages Used
text: metafor::leave1out() canonical LOO output; metafor::influence() Cook’s distance, DFBETAS, hat values; metafor::baujat() Baujat plots; meta analogous sensitivity tools alternative interface; dmetar::infA() combined influence outlier diagnostics integrated workflow.

heading: 16.132 Introduction
text: binary outcomes, odds ratio () widely reported effect measure across case-control studies, randomised trials binary endpoints, observational comparative studies. bounded zero long right tail, sampling distribution markedly skewed inverse-variance pooling natural scale poorly behaved. standard approach therefore pool log scale, distribution approximately Normal, back-transform pooled estimate confidence interval scale reporting. Pooling log scale stabilises variance, allows symmetric confidence intervals analysis scale, keeps multiplicative--back-transform interpretation clinicians epidemiologists expect.

heading: 16.133 Prerequisites
text: working understanding odds ratios measure association 2×2 tables, inverse-variance meta-analytic weighting, distinction fixed-effect random-effects models.

heading: 16.134 Theory
text: study \(a_i, b_i, c_i, d_i\) four cells exposure--outcome 2×2 table, per-study log-variance \[\log \mathrm{}_i = \log \frac{a_i d_i}{b_i c_i}, \qquad \mathrm{Var}(\log \mathrm{}_i) = \frac{1}{a_i} + \frac{1}{b_i} + \frac{1}{c_i} + \frac{1}{d_i}.\]fixed-effect pool \(\hat\theta = \sum w_i \theta_i / \sum w_i\) \(w_i = 1/\mathrm{Var}(\log\mathrm{}_i)\). random-effects pool adds -study variance \(\tau^2\) study’s weight, widening confidence interval reflect -study uncertainty underlying effect. pools fit log scale back-transformed via \(\exp(\cdot)\) reporting.

heading: 16.135 Assumptions
text: Studies independent; log-approximately Normal (typically holds cell ≥5 events); within-study variance accurately estimated; pooled population reasonably homogeneous (fixed-effect) , commonly, heterogeneous effects drawn Normal distribution around common mean (random-effects).

heading: 16.136 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 10
df <- data.frame(
  ai = rpois(k, 30), bi = rpois(k, 170),
  ci = rpois(k, 20), di = rpois(k, 180)
)

es <- escalc(measure = "OR", ai = ai, bi = bi,
             ci = ci, di = di, data = df)

fe <- rma(yi, vi, data = es, method = "FE")
re <- rma(yi, vi, data = es, method = "REML")

cat("FE pooled OR:", round(exp(fe$b), 2),
    " 95% CI:", paste(round(exp(c(fe$ci.lb, fe$ci.ub)), 2), collapse = "-"), "\n")
cat("RE pooled OR:", round(exp(re$b), 2),
    " 95% CI:", paste(round(exp(c(re$ci.lb, re$ci.ub)), 2), collapse = "-"), "\n")

heading: 16.137 Output & Results
text: escalc(measure = "") computes per-study log-variance, applying continuity correction zero cells needed. rma() fits meta-analysis log scale; back-transformation via exp() yields pooled confidence interval natural scale. random-effects model additionally returns \(\tau^2\) \(^2\) describing -study heterogeneity.

heading: 16.138 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Random-effects meta-analysis 10 studies (REML, total \(n = 4{,}000\)) gave pooled odds ratio 1.56 (95 % CI 1.31 1.87) modest -study heterogeneity (\(^2 = 22 \%\), \(\tau^2 = 0.01\)); fixed-effect estimate almost identical (1.54), confirming heterogeneity materially shift central effect.” Always report pooled estimate heterogeneity metrics; readers judge pool without .

heading: 16.139 Practical Tips
text: Always pool log scale; ORs multiplicative pooling natural scale produces biased asymmetrically distributed estimates.Apply continuity correction (typically add 0.5 every cell study zero cell) studies zero events; metafor::escalc() handles automatically default.Choose fixed-effect random-effects models based substantive research question expected sources heterogeneity, \(Q\)-test \(p\)-value alone — \(^2\) \(\tau^2\) informative magnitude heterogeneity.Always report heterogeneity statistics (\(\tau^2\), \(^2\), prediction interval) alongside pooled point estimate.Apply Hartung–Knapp–Sidik–Jonkman adjustment random-effects pooling small \(k\) (usually fewer 20 studies); produces wider, conservative CIs better match coverage simulations.Mantel–Haenszel pooling (metafor::rma.mh()) preferable sparse-data settings many zero cells, continuity correction biases standard inverse-variance pool.

heading: 16.140 R Packages Used
text: metafor::escalc() rma() canonical log-pooling workflow; metafor::rma.mh() Mantel–Haenszel pooling rma.peto() Peto method rare events; meta::metabin() alternative interface cleaner forest-plot defaults; metafor Hartung–Knapp via test = "knha".

heading: 16.143 Introduction
text: Meta-regression extends random-effects meta-analysis allowing study-level covariates — moderators publication year, mean participant age, average dose, risk--bias score — enter model predictors underlying effect. fitted slope quantifies much true study effect changes per unit moderator, residual -study variance \(\tau^2\) describes whatever heterogeneity moderator explain. Meta-regression appropriate tool whenever candidate moderator continuous, generally powerful dichotomising subgroups, discards information can produce categorisation-dependent answer. now standard practice meta-analysis \(k \geq 10\) studies pre-specified hypothesis effect modification.

heading: 16.144 Prerequisites
text: working understanding random-effects meta-analysis (notably role \(\tau^2\)), ordinary linear regression, difference fixed-effect, random-effects, mixed-effects models meta-analytic context.

heading: 16.145 Theory
text: mixed-effects meta-regression model \[y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + u_i + \varepsilon_i, \qquad u_i \sim N(0, \tau^2), \quad \varepsilon_i \sim N(0, s_i^2),\]\(u_i\) -study residual heterogeneity \(\varepsilon_i\) within-study sampling error known variance \(s_i^2\). REML estimates \(\tau^2\) jointly regression coefficients; Wald likelihood-ratio tests assess moderator effects. pseudo-\(R^2 = 1 - \tau^2_{\text{residual}}/\tau^2_{\text{null}}\) summarises proportion -study variance explained moderator analogue regression \(R^2\).

heading: 16.146 Assumptions
text: moderator pre-specified rather data-mined; functional form (linear unless explicitly modelled otherwise) correct; residual heterogeneity normal; sufficient studies — frequently-cited rule thumb least ten studies per moderator coefficient — support reliable estimation.

heading: 16.147 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 15
year <- sample(1995:2023, k, replace = TRUE)
mean_age <- runif(k, 40, 70)

yi <- 0.5 - 0.02 * (year - 1995) + rnorm(k, 0, 0.15)
vi <- 1 / sample(50:250, k, replace = TRUE)

df <- data.frame(yi, vi, year, mean_age)

res1 <- rma(yi, vi, mods = ~ year, data = df, method = "REML")
summary(res1)

res2 <- rma(yi, vi, mods = ~ year + mean_age, data = df, method = "REML")
summary(res2)

cat("R^2:", round(res1$R2, 3), "\n")

heading: 16.148 Output & Results
text: rma() mods formula returns moderator coefficients confidence intervals \(p\)-values, residual heterogeneity statistics (\(\tau^2\), \(^2\), residual \(Q_E\)), omnibus moderator test (\(Q_M\)), pseudo-\(R^2\) summarising explained heterogeneity. Reporting four elements — slope, residual \(\tau^2\), \(Q_M\), \(R^2\) — standard practice.

heading: 16.149 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Year publication significantly moderated pooled effect (slope \(-0.017\), 95 % CI \(-0.029\) \(-0.005\), \(p = 0.01\)): additional year reduced standardised mean difference 0.017 units. Meta-regression explained 52 % -study variance (\(R^2\)); residual heterogeneity remained moderate (\(^2_{\text{residual}} = 21 \%\)). temporal trend pre-specified PROSPERO protocol.” Always report slope residual heterogeneity convey moderator explain.

heading: 16.150 Practical Tips
text: Restrict roughly one moderator per ten studies; meta-regression overfits aggressively -study sample sizes tiny regression standards.Pre-specify moderators PROSPERO protocol; post-hoc moderators best exploratory flagged reporting.Centre continuous moderators (e.g., year minus mean year) intercept meaningful interpretation effect mean moderator value.small \(k\), permutation tests via permutest() provide conservative inference Wald-based default widely recommended.Beware ecological fallacy: study-level moderator effect imply effect individual-participant level; requires IPD meta-analysis.Plot bubble plots (metafor::regplot()) showing study effects, moderator value, weighted regression line; make moderator relationship visible reveal influential outliers.

heading: 16.154 Introduction
text: Network meta-analysis (NMA), mixed-treatment comparison (MTC), pools evidence across studies comparing different pairs treatments estimate coherent set relative effects. enables comparisons direct head--head trials exist, provided indirect paths evidence network connect treatments.

heading: 16.155 Prerequisites
text: Pairwise meta-analysis; graph structure; consistency concept.

heading: 16.156 Theory
text: network nodes (treatments) edges (pairwise comparisons least one study). Direct evidence = studies comparing treatments directly; indirect evidence = inferred via common comparator. pooled estimate combines assumption consistency (direct = indirect estimates).Implementation: frequentist (netmeta) Bayesian (gemtc, BUGSnet).

heading: 16.157 Assumptions
text:
Transitivity: studies network sufficiently similar indirect comparisons valid.
Consistency: direct indirect estimates contrast agree within sampling error.

heading: 16.158 R Implementation
code:
library(netmeta)

# Example dataset
set.seed(2026)
net_df <- data.frame(
  study = c("S1", "S1", "S2", "S2", "S3", "S3", "S4", "S4", "S5", "S5"),
  treat = c("A", "B", "A", "C", "B", "C", "A", "D", "C", "D"),
  TE    = c(0, -0.3, 0, -0.5, 0.4, 0.2, 0, -0.2, 0, 0.3),
  seTE  = c(0.2, 0.2, 0.25, 0.25, 0.3, 0.3, 0.2, 0.2, 0.22, 0.22)
)
# Rearrange to pairs
p_df <- pairwise(treat = treat, TE = TE, seTE = seTE,
                 studlab = study, data = net_df)

net <- netmeta(p_df, common = FALSE, reference = "A")
summary(net)
netgraph(net)

heading: 16.159 Output & Results
text: network graph, pooled treatment effects relative reference, overall ^2.

heading: 16.160 Interpretation
text: “Network meta-analysis 5 studies covering 4 treatments (reference, B, C, D) yielded pooled SMDs: B vs -0.22 (-0.41, -0.03); C vs -0.35 (-0.58, -0.12); D vs 0.05 (-0.15, 0.25). Transitivity plausible; consistency violated.”

heading: 16.161 Practical Tips
text: Always produce network graph first; disconnected components compared.Check transitivity via study characteristics (populations, doses, outcomes).Use SUCRA rank treatments probability best.Report PRISMA-NMA checklist; network meta-analyses complex demand transparency.Bayesian NMA via gemtc flexible hierarchical models missing-arm data.

heading: 16.164 Introduction
text: Network meta-analysis combines direct evidence head--head trials indirect evidence inferred common comparators. validity synthesis hinges consistency assumption: across closed loops network, direct indirect estimates pairwise contrast must agree sampling error. disagree systematically — , example, direct -vs-C estimate head--head trials differs materially indirect estimate inferred via B — network inconsistent, pooled NMA estimates interpreted face value. Detecting characterising inconsistency therefore mandatory step rigorous NMA workflow, modern guidance (NICE DSU, ISPOR, PRISMA-NMA) requires consistency reported alongside league table SUCRA results.

heading: 16.165 Prerequisites
text: working understanding network meta-analysis structure (treatments, designs, contrasts), difference direct indirect evidence, transitivity assumption underpins indirect comparisons.

heading: 16.166 Theory
text: Three complementary tests address different scales inconsistency. loop-specific Bucher test, triangle -B-C, compares direct -vs-C estimate indirect estimate \(\hat\theta_{AB} + \hat\theta_{BC}\) via Wald-type difference. Node-splitting (Dias et al., 2010) generalises contrast: splits evidence one contrast direct-indirect-components tests whether agree. design--treatment interaction model (Higgins et al., 2012) provides global test via generalised-inconsistency framework, comparing consistent NMA model unconstrained model allows different contrasts take different values across study designs.

heading: 16.167 Assumptions
text: network contains least one closed loop (otherwise consistency tested), transitivity holds principle, underlying meta-analytic model correctly specified. Inconsistency tests low power loops short sparsely populated; absence detected inconsistency prove consistency.

heading: 16.168 R Implementation
code:
library(netmeta)

set.seed(2026)
net_df <- data.frame(
  study = c("S1", "S1", "S2", "S2", "S3", "S3", "S4", "S4",
            "S5", "S5", "S6", "S6"),
  treat = c("A", "B", "A", "C", "B", "C", "A", "D",
            "B", "D", "C", "D"),
  TE    = c(0, -0.3, 0, -0.5, 0.4, 0.2, 0, -0.2,
            0.1, 0.3, 0, 0.4),
  seTE  = rep(0.25, 12)
)

p_df <- pairwise(treat = treat, TE = TE, seTE = seTE,
                 studlab = study, data = net_df)
net  <- netmeta(p_df, common = FALSE, reference = "A")

netsplit(net)

decomp.design(net)

heading: 16.169 Output & Results
text: netsplit() returns per-comparison direct vs indirect estimates, difference, Wald test difference. decomp.design() decomposes heterogeneity statistic \(Q\) within-design -design components reports global design--treatment interaction test. Non-significant results diagnostics support consistency assumption.

heading: 16.170 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Node-splitting (netsplit) showed agreement direct indirect estimates six pairwise comparisons (smallest two-sided \(p\)-value \(= 0.15\)). global design--treatment interaction test (decomp.design) non-significant (\(p = 0.36\)), -design heterogeneity component negligible (\(Q_{\text{inc}} = 1.2\), df \(= 3\)). consistency assumption therefore supported, random-effects league table can interpreted reflecting joint direct indirect evidence.” Always report tests.

heading: 16.171 Practical Tips
text: Always test consistency; reporting NMA without consistency check longer acceptable major journals HTA submissions.inconsistency detected, investigate study-level effect modifiers (population age, baseline severity, dose, year, risk bias) differ across designs; meta-regression within NMA framework can partially resolve inconsistency.netsplit provides per-comparison tests (high resolution, low power per test); decomp.design provides global test (low resolution, higher power overall) — complementary, redundant.simple triangles sufficient direct evidence, Bucher test quick first-pass diagnostic; node-splitting reduces three-treatment case.Inconsistency can local (single loop) global (whole network); diagnostic patterns differ, discussion section reflect kind found.Bayesian implementations gemtc multinma provide UME (unrelated mean effects) consistency models compare consistent NMA via DIC; Bayesian analogue design--treatment test.

heading: 16.175 Introduction
text: league table standard summary display network meta-analysis (NMA). packs every pairwise treatment contrast — direct, indirect, mixed — single triangular square grid, making relative ranking interventions visible glance. diagonal cell labels treatment; -diagonal cell reports pooled contrast row column 95 % confidence (credible) interval. League tables now standard reporting requirement PRISMA-NMA extension communicate entire network’s information way single forest plot can.

heading: 16.176 Prerequisites

heading: 16.177 Theory
text: \(T\) treatments league table \(T \times T\) matrix. convention used netmeta software places random- common-effect mixed estimates upper triangle direct-pooled estimates lower triangle, treatment ordering axes. -diagonal cell reports pooled contrast (row-vs-column column-vs-row, convention noted caption) uncertainty.Ordering treatments rank (e.g., SUCRA, P-score, median ranking Bayesian NMA) groups best-performing treatments one corner, making table substantially easier interpret alphabetical ordering.

heading: 16.179 R Implementation

library(netmeta)

set.seed(2026)
net_df <- data.frame(
  study = c("S1", "S1", "S2", "S2", "S3", "S3", "S4", "S4",

                    separator = " to ", digits = 2)
league$random

Treatment C consistently effective: C vs pooled effect \(-0.55\) (95 % CI \(-0.88\) \(-0.22\)) C vs B \(-0.34\) (95 % CI \(-0.67\) \(-0.01\)); contrasts excluded null. lower-triangle direct-estimates qualitatively concordant, suggesting consistency direct indirect evidence.” Always pair league-table reading node-splitting consistency check.

heading: 16.186 Introduction
text: SUCRA — Surface Cumulative Ranking curve, introduced Salanti colleagues 2011 — summarises network meta-analysis’s treatment ranking single interpretable number 0 1. SUCRA 1 means treatment always ranked best across posterior simulation distribution; SUCRA 0 means always ranked worst. clinicians, guideline panels, patients want know “treatment use?”, triangular league table answer directly, SUCRA quickly became -cited summary NMA papers. widespread use also brings risks: SUCRA sensitive number treatments included, can exaggerate small effect-size differences, must always interpreted alongside absolute effect estimates uncertainty.

heading: 16.187 Prerequisites
text: working understanding network meta-analysis, posterior simulated rank distributions, conceptual difference treatment ranking treatment effect.

heading: 16.188 Theory
text: treatment \(j\) network \(T\) treatments, rank distribution gives \(P(\mathrm{rank}_j = k)\) \(k = 1, \dots, T\) — typically derived Bayesian posterior MCMC frequentist parametric simulation. SUCRA \[\mathrm{SUCRA}_j = \frac{1}{T-1} \sum_{k=1}^{T-1} P(\mathrm{rank}_j \leq k),\]area cumulative rank curve scaled \([0, 1]\). Rücker Schwarzer’s P-score (2015) frequentist analogue computed directly NMA point estimates standard errors; require MCMC reproduces SUCRA values closely networks.

heading: 16.189 Assumptions
text: underlying NMA valid (transitivity across studies, consistency direct indirect evidence), network connected rankings estimable. clinical interpretability “best treatment” requires treatments exchangeable indication contraindications make high-ranked option unusable patient groups.

heading: 16.190 R Implementation
code:
library(netmeta)

set.seed(2026)
net_df <- data.frame(
  study = c("S1", "S1", "S2", "S2", "S3", "S3", "S4", "S4",
            "S5", "S5", "S6", "S6"),
  treat = c("A", "B", "A", "C", "B", "C", "A", "D",
            "B", "D", "C", "D"),
  TE    = c(0, -0.3, 0, -0.5, 0.4, 0.2, 0, -0.2,
            0.1, 0.3, 0, 0.4),
  seTE  = rep(0.25, 12)
)

p_df <- pairwise(treat = treat, TE = TE, seTE = seTE,
                 studlab = study, data = net_df)
net  <- netmeta(p_df, common = FALSE, reference = "A",
                small.values = "good")

rankings <- netrank(net, method = "P-score", small.values = "good")
rankings$ranking.random

heading: 16.191 Output & Results
text: netrank() returns P-score per treatment (0 1) common-effect random-effects models. Higher scores indicate better ranking chosen analysis scale. Bayesian NMA, packages gemtc multinma produce SUCRA values directly, along rank probabilities \(P(\mathrm{rank} = k)\) \(k\).

heading: 16.192 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Treatment C highest P-score (0.89) random-effects NMA, identifying likely best four candidates. However, pairwise contrast C vs B (P-score 0.61) 95 % CI crossed null, ranking uncertain absolute differences top-ranked options small. Rankograms reporting full distribution ranks accompany SUCRA summary supplement.” Always report rankings together absolute effect estimates surrounding uncertainty.

heading: 16.193 Practical Tips
text: Always report SUCRA P-score alongside pairwise effect estimates CIs; rankings alone can exaggerate small differences obscure clinically irrelevant gaps.Treatments similar point estimates can show different SUCRA values uncertainty differs; precise treatment rank higher even mean effect better. feature, bug — must interpreted carefully.Use rankograms (bar plots \(P(\mathrm{rank} = k)\) \(k\)) visualise entire rank distribution; convey uncertainty single SUCRA number compresses.Frequentist P-scores Bayesian SUCRA values typically agree within rounding error connected networks; choice mainly methodological preference.regulatory reimbursement decisions, absolute pairwise effect CI matter ranking; SUCRA clinical-communication tool, decision rule.Check small.values carefully: misspecifying whether small effect sizes “good” (e.g., harm outcome) “bad” inverts entire ranking one common analytic errors published NMAs.

heading: 16.197 Introduction
text: 95 % confidence interval pooled meta-analytic estimate describes uncertainty mean true effect across included studies. prediction interval (PI), contrast, describes plausible range true effect future study drawn population, propagating uncertainty mean -study heterogeneity \(\tau^2\). non-trivial heterogeneity PI substantially wider CI, far clinically meaningful: clinician treating next patient cares less uncertainty average across past studies range effects might plausibly see clinical context. Modern meta-analysis reporting standards (PRISMA, GRADE) increasingly require PI alongside CI random-effects synthesis.

heading: 16.198 Prerequisites
text: working understanding random-effects meta-analysis, role -study variance \(\tau^2\), conceptual difference uncertainty mean uncertainty future realisation.

heading: 16.199 Theory
text: 95 % prediction interval random-effects meta-analysis \(k\) studies \[\mathrm{PI}_{95\%} = \hat\theta \pm t_{0.975, k-2} \sqrt{\hat\tau^2 + \mathrm{SE}(\hat\theta)^2}.\]Two features distinguish CI: use Student’s \(t\) \(k - 2\) degrees freedom rather normal quantile, addition \(\hat\tau^2\) inside square root. heterogeneity grows PI widens considerably CI comparatively unchanged, gap useful diagnostic much pooling — — clarified underlying effect.

heading: 16.200 Assumptions
text: random-effects model valid; \(\tau^2\) reasonably estimated (typically REML, recommended default); future studies exchangeable included ones, drawing true effects Normal distribution centred pooled mean.

heading: 16.201 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 10
yi <- rnorm(k, 0.4, 0.25)
vi <- 1 / sample(50:200, k, replace = TRUE)

res <- rma(yi, vi, method = "REML")
summary(res)

pi <- predict(res, addx = NULL)
pi[, c("pred", "pi.lb", "pi.ub")]

heading: 16.202 Output & Results
text: predict() rma object returns pooled estimate, confidence interval (mean), 95 % prediction interval (future study). Whenever \(\tau^2 > 0\) PI strictly wider CI, gap informative: small gap means heterogeneity negligible pool informative future studies, large gap means pool tells average expect next.

heading: 16.203 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “pooled standardised mean difference 0.38 (95 % CI 0.20 0.56) random-effects REML pooling. 95 % prediction interval wider, \([-0.10, 0.86]\), indicating new study true effect plausibly range small negative value large positive value, consistent moderate heterogeneity observed (\(^2 = 41 \%\), \(\tau^2 = 0.04\)). Clinical interpretation account dispersion rather rely solely pooled mean.” Always report CI PI together random-effects pools.

heading: 16.204 Practical Tips
text: Always report CI (uncertainty mean) PI (uncertainty future study) random-effects meta-analysis; answer different questions difference informative.wide PI alongside narrow CI distinctive pattern indicating heterogeneity diminish pooling; acknowledged explicitly discussion limitation synthesis.meta-analyses studies (typically \(k < 5\)), PI unstable wide \(\tau^2\) poorly estimated; report interpret caution consider Hartung–Knapp adjustment.PI crosses null CI common important pattern: means pooled mean “significant” meaningful proportion future studies might find effect . finding typically reshapes interpretation highlighted, buried.Hartung–Knapp–Sidik–Jonkman adjustment widens CI small samples modestly affects PI; \(k \leq 20\), recommended default.Display PI graphically forest plot dashed bar around pooled diamond (addpred = TRUE metafor::forest()); seeing visually conveys reporting numerically.

heading: 16.208 Introduction
text: Risk ratios (RR) — also called relative risks — interpretable odds ratios common outcomes; directly express ratio event probabilities two arms. RR approximates events rare (probability ~10 %); common outcomes, two diverge substantially. Meta-analytic pooling binary outcomes cohort studies randomised trials therefore typically proceeds log-RR scale, analogously log-pooling.

heading: 16.209 Prerequisites
text: working understanding risk ratios, log transformations ratio measures, inverse-variance meta-analysis random effects.

heading: 16.210 Theory
text: study \(a_i\) events \(a_i + b_i = n_{1i}\) exposed \(c_i\) events \(c_i + d_i = n_{2i}\) unexposed:\[\log \mathrm{RR}_i = \log \frac{a_i / n_{1i}}{c_i / n_{2i}}, \qquad \mathrm{Var}(\log \mathrm{RR}_i) = \frac{1}{a_i} - \frac{1}{n_{1i}} + \frac{1}{c_i} - \frac{1}{n_{2i}}.\]Inverse-variance pooling log-RR scale follows fixed-effect random-effects distinction meta-analysis. Back-transformation natural RR scale via \(\exp\) produces interpretable summaries confidence intervals respect multiplicative scale.

heading: 16.211 Assumptions
text: Studies independent; log-RR approximately Normal (typically good approximation study least 5 events per arm); population RR across studies (fixed-effect) distributed around mean -study variance \(\tau^2\) (random-effects).

heading: 16.212 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 10
df <- data.frame(
  ai = rpois(k, 30), n1i = rep(100, k),
  ci = rpois(k, 20), n2i = rep(100, k)
)

es <- escalc(measure = "RR",
             ai = ai, n1i = n1i, ci = ci, n2i = n2i,
             data = df)

re <- rma(yi, vi, data = es, method = "REML")
cat("Pooled RR:", round(exp(re$b), 2),
    " 95% CI:", paste(round(exp(c(re$ci.lb, re$ci.ub)), 2), collapse = "-"), "\n")
cat("tau^2:", round(re$tau2, 3), "  I^2:", round(re$I2, 1), "%\n")

heading: 16.213 Output & Results
text: escalc(measure = "RR") computes per-study log-RR variance. rma() fits random-effects model log scale; back-transformation gives pooled RR confidence intervals natural scale, plus \(\tau^2\) \(^2\) heterogeneity.

heading: 16.214 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Random-effects meta-analysis 10 studies (REML, \(n\) total = 2{,}000) gave pooled risk ratio 1.48 (95 % CI 1.24 1.77) moderate heterogeneity (\(^2 = 35 \%\), \(\tau^2 = 0.02\)); intervention associated 48 % higher event rate, 95 % CI excluding null.” Always report heterogeneity statistics alongside pooled estimate.

heading: 16.215 Practical Tips
text: Use RR cohort studies randomised controlled trials, baseline risk meaningful; use case-control studies, RR estimable design.rare events (event rate 10 %), RR ≈ ; common events, two diverge choice matters substantively.Zero-event studies require continuity correction (escalc(..., add = 0.5)) rare-events method (Peto , Mantel-Haenszel without correction); standard log-RR undefined cell zero.Present forest plots reports natural scale (RR), log scale; readers reason multiplicatively ratios.Mantel-Haenszel pooling (metafor::rma.mh()) common alternative sparse-data settings; require log-transformation handles zero events gracefully.individual-participant-data meta-analysis, fit logistic regression study random effect; powerful aggregate-data pooling IPD available.

heading: 16.216 R Packages Used

heading: 16.219 Introduction
text: Selection models address publication bias rigorously trim--fill simple regression-based asymmetry tests. posit explicit weight function describing probability publication depends study’s \(p\)-value effect magnitude, re-estimate pooled effect assumed selection mechanism recover bias-adjusted estimate. trim--fill descriptive triangular assumption, selection models formally model-based yield likelihood-ratio tests selection hypothesis . cost stronger parametric assumptions form selection process, means selection models useful meta-analysis large enough selection function can reasonably identified data.

heading: 16.220 Prerequisites
text: working understanding publication bias selection process, maximum-likelihood estimation, logic inverse-probability weighting sampled population.

heading: 16.221 Theory
text: Given observed effects \(y_i\) standard errors \(\mathrm{SE}_i\) published studies, observed density \[p_{\text{obs}}(y) = \frac{w(y, \mathrm{SE}) \, p(y)}{\int w(y, \mathrm{SE}) \, p(y) \, dy},\]\(p(y)\) population density true effects \(w(\cdot)\) selection weight function — typically step function one-sided \(p\)-value, selection probability dropping conventional thresholds (0.025, 0.05, 0.10). Maximum-likelihood estimation joint \((\mu, \tau^2, w)\) parameters inverts selection mechanism recover bias-adjusted pooled effect, likelihood-ratio test null \(w(\cdot) \equiv 1\) formally tests selection.

heading: 16.222 Assumptions
text: functional form selection weight correctly specified (least reasonable approximation), meta-analysis contains enough studies identify selection parameters (typically 15–20+), studies exchangeable conditional effect size standard error.

heading: 16.223 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 20
yi <- rnorm(k, 0.3, 0.15)
vi <- 1 / sample(40:250, k, replace = TRUE)

res <- rma(yi, vi, method = "REML")

sel <- selmodel(res, type = "stepfun",
                steps = c(0.025, 0.10, 0.50, 1.0),
                alternative = "greater")
sel

heading: 16.224 Output & Results
text: selmodel() returns bias-adjusted pooled estimate, standard error confidence interval, estimated selection weights step, likelihood-ratio test null selection. Comparing adjusted unadjusted estimates side side quantifies much conclusion shifts assumed selection mechanism.

heading: 16.225 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “step-function selection model thresholds \(p = 0.025, 0.10, 0.50\) provided strong evidence selective publication (likelihood-ratio test vs -selection, \(\chi^2 = 13.1\), df \(= 3\), \(p = 0.004\)). bias-adjusted standardised mean difference 0.19 (95 % CI 0.05 0.33), substantially attenuated unadjusted estimate 0.32 (0.21 0.43). result suggests publication bias inflates unadjusted pooled effect roughly 40 %.” Always report estimates LR test.

heading: 16.226 Practical Tips
text: Selection models require least 15–20 studies stable estimates; threshold selection function poorly identified confidence intervals become uninformatively wide.Try several weight-function structures (different step thresholds, smooth-monotone forms) sensitivity analyses; bias-adjusted estimate can quite sensitive assumed selection mechanism, convergent results across forms reassuring.Always report unadjusted selection-adjusted estimates, plus LR test selection; unadjusted estimate remains primary, selection-adjusted estimate sensitivity.Modern alternatives — PET-PEESE regression-based correction, \(p\)-uniform \(p\)-curve, Vevea–Hedges weight-function models — useful standard step-function selection models fit poorly fail converge.Bayesian selection models informative priors weight parameters (e.g., RoBMA related packages) stable alternative frequentist MLE unstable.Selection models adjust selection mechanism told assume; forms bias (selective outcome reporting, dependent effect-size selection) require separate handling.

heading: 16.230 Introduction
text: Hedges’ \(g\) small-sample-corrected standardised mean difference (SMD), preferred effect-size metric meta-analysis continuous outcomes. Cohen’s \(d\) — unstandardised SMD — -estimates population effect small samples; Hedges’ correction factor \(J\) de-biases . two nearly identical studies combined sample size 20 , \(g\) used routinely whenever included study small.

heading: 16.231 Prerequisites
text: working understanding standardised effect sizes (Cohen’s \(d\)), pooled standard deviation, bias \(d\) small samples.

heading: 16.232 Theory
text: Cohen’s \(d\) \[d = \frac{\bar X_1 - \bar X_2}{s_{\mathrm{pool}}}, \qquad s_{\mathrm{pool}} = \sqrt{\frac{(n_1 - 1) s_1^2 + (n_2 - 1) s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}}.\]Hedges’ correction \(J = 1 - 3 / (4 \, \mathrm{df} - 1)\) (\(\mathrm{df} = n_1 + n_2 - 2\)), \(g = J d\). correction close 1 \(\mathrm{df} > 20\) can substantially 1 small studies. variance \(g\) \[\mathrm{Var}(g) = J^2 \!\left[\frac{n_1 + n_2}{n_1 n_2} + \frac{d^2}{2(n_1 + n_2)}\right].\]

heading: 16.233 Assumptions
text: Outcomes approximately Normal within groups; group variances approximately equal (use SMDH unequal-variance variants); independent observations.

heading: 16.234 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 10                  # small study
x1 <- rnorm(n, mean = 1, sd = 1)
x2 <- rnorm(n, mean = 0, sd = 1)

# Compute g and its variance via metafor
es <- escalc(measure = "SMD",
             m1i = mean(x1), sd1i = sd(x1), n1i = n,
             m2i = mean(x2), sd2i = sd(x2), n2i = n)
es

# Manual computation
s_pool <- sqrt(((n - 1) * var(x1) + (n - 1) * var(x2)) / (2 * n - 2))
d <- (mean(x1) - mean(x2)) / s_pool
J <- 1 - 3 / (4 * (2 * n - 2) - 1)
c(d = d, g = d * J)

heading: 16.235 Output & Results
text: escalc(measure = "SMD") returns Hedges’ \(g\) (yi column) variance (vi) per study, ready inverse-variance meta-analysis. manual calculation confirms \(J\)-correction; \(n_1 = n_2 = 10\), \(J \approx 0.92\), reducing \(d \approx 1.04\) \(g \approx 0.96\).

heading: 16.236 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Hedges’ \(g\) simulated small study 0.92 (95 % CI 0.01 1.83), corresponding large effect Cohen’s benchmarks (\(d = 0.5\) medium, \(d = 0.8\) large); small-sample correction reduced raw SMD 1.04 0.92, reflecting upward bias inherent \(d\) small studies.” Always state use \(g\) vs \(d\), especially sample sizes small.

heading: 16.237 Practical Tips
text: Always use Hedges’ \(g\) rather Cohen’s \(d\) meta-analysis included study \(n_1 + n_2 < 20\); bias correction meaningful range.metafor::escalc(measure = "SMD") computes \(g\) variance single call; standard workflow.unequal variances (Levene’s test rejects), use measure = "SMDH" computes \(g\) using pooled SD based separate group SDs.Report 95 % CIs alongside \(g\); effect size alone convey precision, meta-analysis weighting depends precision.pre-post within-subject designs, use SMCR SMCC variants metafor::escalc(); account within-subject correlation ordinary SMD ignores.binary outcomes, switch log-odds ratio risk ratio; SMD continuous outcomes .

heading: 16.238 R Packages Used
text: metafor::escalc() rma() canonical effect-size meta-analysis workflow; meta::metacont() alternative interface continuous-outcome conventions; metan tidymodels-flavoured wrapper; dmetar additional meta-analysis tools visualisations.

heading: 16.241 Introduction
text: Subgroup meta-analysis partitions included studies according pre-specified categorical moderator — intervention intensity (low vs high dose), population (paediatric vs adult), risk bias (low vs unclear vs high), clinically meaningful split — pools within level. within-subgroup pooled effects describe intervention behaves stratum, formal -subgroup test assesses whether moderator explains measurable share -study heterogeneity. Subgroup analysis standard tool categorical effect modification evidence synthesis required Cochrane Handbook whenever protocol pre-specifies moderator hypothesis.

heading: 16.242 Prerequisites
text: working understanding pooled meta-analysis (preferably random-effects), heterogeneity statistics \(\tau^2\), \(^2\), \(Q\), difference within-group -group sources variation stratified analyses.

heading: 16.243 Theory
text: Within subgroup \(g = 1, \dots, G\) studies pooled obtain \(\hat\theta_g\) standard error. -subgroup heterogeneity statistic \[Q_B = \sum_g w_g (\hat\theta_g - \hat\theta)^2,\]\(G - 1\) degrees freedom; significant \(Q_B\) indicates moderator accounts -study variance. Equivalently, fitting mixed-effects meta-regression subgroup categorical moderator produces \(Q_M\) test recovers exactly comparison modern preferred implementation, can also share common \(\tau^2\) across subgroups stable inference.

heading: 16.244 Assumptions
text: moderator pre-specified rather chosen post hoc, within-subgroup studies reasonably homogeneous (small within-group \(\tau^2\)), subgroup contains enough studies — typically least four five — support stable within-group pool. Subgroup analyses one two studies per stratum largely uninformative reported descriptively.

heading: 16.245 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 16
subgroup <- factor(rep(c("low_dose", "high_dose"), each = k/2))
yi <- c(rnorm(k/2, 0.2, 0.15),
        rnorm(k/2, 0.5, 0.15))
vi <- 1 / sample(50:200, k, replace = TRUE)

df <- data.frame(yi, vi, subgroup)

lapply(split(df, df$subgroup), function(d) {
  rma(yi, vi, data = d, method = "REML")$b
})

res_mod <- rma(yi, vi, mods = ~ subgroup, data = df, method = "REML")
summary(res_mod)

heading: 16.246 Output & Results
text: script returns per-subgroup pooled estimates independent random-effects pools joint mixed-effects meta-regression subgroup moderator. latter reports omnibus \(Q_M\) test -subgroup heterogeneity, per-level coefficients, residual heterogeneity remaining moderator included — canonical reporting set subgroup analyses.

heading: 16.247 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Pre-specified subgroup analysis showed significant -subgroup heterogeneity (\(Q_M = 18.4\), df \(= 1\), \(p < 0.001\)): high-dose studies pooled standardised mean difference 0.52 (95 % CI 0.39 0.65) compared 0.18 (0.08 0.28) low-dose studies, supporting hypothesis dose moderates treatment effect. moderator explained 47 % -study heterogeneity (\(R^2\)); residual \(^2\) 18 %.” Always report within-subgroup pools -subgroup test.

heading: 16.248 Practical Tips
text: Pre-specify subgroup analyses PROSPERO protocol; post-hoc subgrouping exploratory flagged — never primary inference.Small per-subgroup study counts produce unstable pooled estimates; Cochrane Handbook recommends least 10 studies per subgroup drawing conclusions, warns explicitly single-stratum subgroup pools.Prefer meta-regression subgroup analysis whenever moderator continuous (age, dose, year); dichotomising loses information depends arbitrary cutpoint.Report -subgroup \(Q_M\) \(Q_B\) \(p\)-value primary inference; comparing within-subgroup \(p\)-values across strata (“significant one, ”) well-known statistical fallacy tests small subgroups underpowered.Use shared \(\tau^2\) across subgroups (default mixed-effects meta-regression) rather separate estimates per subgroup unless heterogeneity clearly different ; shared specification efficient conservative.subgroup analysis exploratory, apply multiplicity adjustment interpret explicit caution; false-positive rate inflates rapidly number subgroup tests.

heading: 16.249 R Packages Used
text: metafor::rma() mods = ~ factor(subgroup) canonical mixed-effects implementation; meta::metabin() meta::metacont() subgroup = built-subgroup pooling forest-plot grouping; dmetar Knapp–Hartung-adjusted subgroup analyses; robumeta robust-variance subgroup pooling correlated effects.

heading: 16.252 Introduction
text: random-effects meta-analysis requires estimate \(\tau^2\), -study variance. Several estimators exist different bias-variance trade-offs; choice affects pooled estimates, CIs, downstream inference. REML modern default; DerSimonian-Laird (DL) historical default.

heading: 16.253 Prerequisites
text: Random-effects meta-analysis; method--moments vs maximum likelihood.

heading: 16.254 Theory
text:
Common estimators:
- DerSimonian-Laird (DL): method--moments; fastest, historically default.
- REML (Restricted Maximum Likelihood): likelihood-based, generally less biased DL; modern default.
- Paule-Mandel (PM): iterative method--moments; often similar REML.
- Sidik-Jonkman (SJ): analytic, robust model misspecification.
- Empirical Bayes (EB): iterative Bayes-style estimator.DL systematically underestimates \(\tau^2\) moderate heterogeneity; REML close unbiased.

heading: 16.255 Assumptions
text: True effects drawn distribution (typically normal) common variance \(\tau^2\); within-study variances known.

heading: 16.256 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 10
n_per <- sample(30:200, k, replace = TRUE)
true_theta <- rnorm(k, 0.3, 0.25)
yi <- true_theta + rnorm(k, 0, sqrt(4 / n_per))
vi <- 4 / n_per

methods <- c("DL", "REML", "PM", "SJ", "EB")
tau2_est <- sapply(methods, function(m) {
  rma(yi, vi, method = m)$tau2
})
round(tau2_est, 3)

heading: 16.257 Output & Results
text: \(\tau^2\) estimates five methods. DL typically gives smallest value; REML, PM, SJ, EB often close.

heading: 16.258 Interpretation
text: “\(\tau^2\) estimates ranged 0.041 (DL) 0.061 (SJ); report REML (\(\tau^2 = 0.055\), 95 % CI 0.012-0.142) primary estimator following modern meta-analysis recommendations.”

heading: 16.259 Practical Tips
text: Use REML default; less biased DL conditions.Hartung-Knapp adjustment (test = "knha" metafor) improves CI coverage, especially studies.Compare multiple estimators sensitivity; DL REML disagree substantially, heterogeneity likely real.Report 95 % CI \(\tau^2\) (profile likelihood Q-profile methods) alongside point estimate.studies (\(k < 5\)), \(\tau^2\) estimate unreliable; use Bayesian methods informative priors.

heading: 16.262 Introduction
text: Trim--fill, introduced Duval Tweedie 2000, widely used algorithmic approach adjusting pooled meta-analytic estimate funnel plot looks asymmetric. idea intuitive: smaller studies side opposite dominant tail funnel appear missing — possibly never published, never indexed, never reached systematic-review screening stage — procedure imputes mirror-image counterparts re-pools augmented set. difference unadjusted adjusted estimates quantifies sensitive conclusion presence hypothetical missing studies. Crucially, trim--fill sensitivity analysis, method bias correction strict statistical sense; recover true unbiased effect.

heading: 16.263 Prerequisites
text: Familiarity funnel plots, small-study-effect concept, inverse-variance random-effects pool, distinction sensitivity analysis bias correction.

heading: 16.264 Theory
text: algorithm proceeds iteratively:Estimate number asymmetric studies dominant tail using one three rank-based estimators (\(L_0\), \(R_0\), \(Q_0\)).Trim studies dominant side re-estimate pooled effect; estimate becomes new symmetry centre.Fill analysis reflecting trimmed studies across new centre opposite side.Re-pool augmented dataset obtain adjusted estimate.Iterating convergence yields final imputed studies adjusted pooled estimate. number imputed studies interpretable summary: zero imputations indicate detectable asymmetry, large number indicates substantial sensitivity.

heading: 16.265 Assumptions
text: observed funnel asymmetry attributable selection publication bias rather true -study heterogeneity correlated study size; underlying effect distribution mirror-symmetric around true effect; pooled model otherwise correctly specified.

heading: 16.266 R Implementation
code:
library(metafor)

set.seed(2026)
k <- 18
yi <- rnorm(k, 0.35, 0.25)
vi <- 1 / sample(15:300, k, replace = TRUE)

keep <- !(sqrt(vi) > 0.1 & abs(yi) < 0.15)
yi_b <- yi[keep]; vi_b <- vi[keep]

res <- rma(yi_b, vi_b, method = "REML")
tf  <- trimfill(res)

data.frame(method = c("Original", "Trim-and-fill"),
           estimate = round(c(res$b, tf$b), 3),
           ci = c(sprintf("[%.2f, %.2f]", res$ci.lb, res$ci.ub),
                  sprintf("[%.2f, %.2f]", tf$ci.lb, tf$ci.ub)))

funnel(tf, main = "Trim-and-fill adjusted funnel")

heading: 16.267 Output & Results
text: trimfill() returns number imputed studies, adjusted pooled estimate confidence interval, augmented dataset suitable plotting funnel diagram. Reporting original pool adjusted pool side side — together count imputations — lets readers see exactly much conclusion shifts missing studies introduced.

heading: 16.268 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Trim--fill (Duval Tweedie, \(L_0\) estimator) imputed four potentially missing studies left side funnel; adjusted standardised mean difference 0.18 (95 % CI 0.03 0.33), notably attenuated unadjusted estimate 0.32 (0.21 0.43). result suggests pooled conclusion sensitive plausible publication bias, Egger’s regression PET-PEESE reported triangulating sensitivity analyses.” Always report unadjusted estimate primary adjusted estimate sensitivity.

heading: 16.269 Practical Tips
text: Trim--fill sensitivity analysis, proof publication bias; report , never use adjusted estimate primary effect.method performs poorly funnel asymmetry driven genuine -study heterogeneity correlated study size; settings can spuriously imply bias.Apply alongside Egger’s regression test PET-PEESE triangulation; consistent signals strengthen bias interpretation, divergent signals warn -interpretation.Report original adjusted estimates CIs count imputed studies; imputation count zero indicates funnel already symmetric adjustment needed.\(L_0\) estimator (default metafor) widely used; \(R_0\) conservative; \(Q_0\) rarely used practice. Sensitivity estimator choice noted results borderline.Selection-model approaches (Vevea–Hedges weight-function models) PET-PEESE increasingly preferred trim--fill methodological literature; consider small-study effect consequential conclusion.

heading: 16.270 R Packages Used
text: metafor::trimfill() canonical implementation three estimators funnel() overlay; meta::trimfill() alternative interface within meta ecosystem; metasens trim--fill alongside copas limit-meta-analysis bias-adjustment methods; weightr puniform selection-model alternatives handle complex bias mechanisms.

heading: 16.275 Background
text:
Meta-analysis pools effect estimates across studies sharpen 
overall answer. Fixed-effect models assume studies estimate one true
effect; random-effects models assume true effects vary around common
mean. biomedical research, heterogeneity rule rather 
exception, random effects default — -study
variance (τ²) proportion total variance due heterogeneity
(²) become part primary reporting, afterthought.Forest plots display study’s estimate confidence interval
next pooled estimate. Funnel plots display study precision
effect size; asymmetry hints small-study effects 
potential publication bias. Neither plot settles question — Egger’s
test, trim--fill, careful inspection small studies 
missing part routine.

heading: 16.288 Background
text:
Network meta-analysis extends pairwise meta-analysis two
treatments combining direct comparisons (head--head trials)
indirect comparisons (inferred shared comparator). 
central assumption transitivity: studies compared indirectly
exchangeable — moderator distinguishes way 
systematically shifts effect. statistical analogue,
consistency, checks whether direct indirect estimates agree.SUCRA (surface cumulative ranking curve) summarises 
treatment’s rank distribution 0-1 scale, 1 best. SUCRA
values compress lot uncertainty single number; 
useful signalling reported interval estimates
every pairwise comparison.

library(tidyverse)
library(netmeta)

differ.

within-design heterogeneity -design inconsistency
variances key diagnostics.

treatments across length(unique(Senn2013$studlab)) studies
(nrow(Senn2013) pairwise comparisons), treatment 
highest SUCRA HbA1c lowering top$treatment
(SUCRA = round(top$sucra, 2)). SUCRA rankings 
interpreted alongside pairwise effect estimates 
intervals, standalone conclusion.

heading: 17.3 Introduction
text: Balanced incomplete block designs (BIBDs) allocate treatments blocks block can hold fewer experimental units total number treatments compared. constraint arises constantly practice — litter animals contains fewer pups treatments, panel taste-testers limited capacity per session, microscopy slide accommodates specimens, oven processes limited number samples per run. “balanced” property requires every pair treatments appears together number \(\lambda\) blocks, ensuring every pairwise treatment contrast estimated equal precision despite block-level incompleteness. BIBDs classical constructions agricultural experimentation, sensory evaluation, setting complete randomised blocks physically impossible.

heading: 17.4 Prerequisites
text: working understanding randomised complete block designs, combinatorial design parameters, basic mixed-effects modelling recovery inter-block information.

heading: 17.5 Theory
text: BIBD specified parameters \((v, b, r, k, \lambda)\):\(v\) = number treatments,\(b\) = number blocks,\(r\) = replicates per treatment,\(k\) = block size (\(k < v\)),\(\lambda\) = number blocks treatment pair co-occurs.parameters satisfy two necessary identities: \(bk = vr\) \(\lambda(v - 1) = r(k - 1)\). every parameter combination corresponds existing BIBD; Fisher’s inequality requires \(b \geq v\), Bruck–Ryser–Chowla theorem provides necessary conditions. classical analysis fits blocks either fixed random effects; treating blocks random recovers inter-block information generally efficient.

heading: 17.6 Assumptions
text: Treatment pairs exchangeable via balance property, within-block residuals exchangeable approximately Normal constant variance, block effects additive (block × treatment interaction). design implemented planned — running fewer units specified destroys balance requires reanalysis partially balanced unbalanced incomplete block design.

heading: 17.7 R Implementation
code:
library(crossdes)

bibd <- find.BIB(7, 7, 3)
bibd

set.seed(2026)
df <- data.frame(
  block = rep(1:7, each = 3),
  treatment = factor(as.vector(t(bibd)))
)
df$y <- rnorm(nrow(df), mean = c(10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16)[as.integer(df$treatment)],
              sd = 0.8)

fit <- aov(y ~ factor(block) + treatment, data = df)
summary(fit)

heading: 17.8 Output & Results
text: find.BIB() constructs BIBD specified parameters one exists. fitted ANOVA partitions variability block, treatment, residual components, treatment \(F\)-test computed using intra-block error. Reporting proportion variance absorbed blocks alongside treatment effect characterises gain incomplete blocking.

heading: 17.9 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “\((v, b, r, k, \lambda) = (7, 7, 3, 3, 1)\) BIBD — equivalent Fano plane projective geometry — tested seven treatments seven blocks size three. ANOVA showed significant treatment effect (\(F_{6, 8} = 8.2\), \(p = 0.002\)) block-level variance absorbed block factor (block sum squares 12.4, residual sum squares 4.7). 21 pairwise treatment contrasts estimated equal precision balance property.” Always report BIBD parameters explicitly.

heading: 17.10 Practical Tips
text: Use BIBD whenever block size smaller treatment count balanced pairwise comparisons required; balance property ensures every contrast standard error, simplifies interpretation multiple-comparison adjustment.Analyse blocks random effects ((1 | block) lme4) recover inter-block information; random-effects analysis generally efficient fixed-effect intra-block analysis, especially number blocks large.parameter combinations \((v, k, \lambda)\) correspond existing BIBD; consult catalogue tables use crossdes::find.BIB() check existence finalising design. Fisher’s inequality Bruck–Ryser–Chowla theorem give necessary conditions.large treatment counts BIBD impractical, partially balanced incomplete block designs (PBIBDs) relax equal-pairwise-co-occurrence requirement cost unequal pairwise precision; far easier find adequate applied work.Pre-specify block allocation protocol; changing mid-experiment — adding unit, dropping block — destroys balance property forces reanalysis general unbalanced design.Visualise incidence matrix (treatment × block) running experiment; inadvertent imbalance (treatment missing block, duplicated treatment within block) far easier spot design stage data collection.

heading: 17.14 Introduction
text: Blocking experimental-design technique groups experimental units known source nuisance variation — batch raw material, day measurement, operator, animal litter, plot location — randomises treatment assignments within block. result design systematically removes block--block variation error term, thereby shrinking residual variance substantially improving power detect genuine treatment effects. Fisher’s classical mantra — “block can, randomise ” — captures principle: nuisance source variation known identifiable, controlling design far efficient relying randomisation alone average .

heading: 17.15 Prerequisites
text: working understanding randomisation basis comparative inference, partition total variability systematic residual components ANOVA, distinction fixed random effects mixed-effects models.

heading: 17.16 Theory
text: simplest form — randomised complete block design (RCBD) — model \[y_{ij} = \mu + \rho_i + \tau_j + \varepsilon_{ij}, \qquad \varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2),\]block effect \(\rho_i\) treatment effect \(\tau_j\). Within-block variance typically much smaller -block variance, absorbing latter design removes large component residual error. relative efficiency RCBD versus CRD roughly ratio residual variances; gains order magnitude unusual blocking well chosen.

heading: 17.17 Assumptions
text: Blocks represent exchangeable groupings units, block--treatment interaction present (, , interaction modelled — typically random effect), within-block residual variance homogeneous. Violations additivity invalidate standard analysis can detected Tukey one-degree--freedom test non-additivity.

heading: 17.18 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
block <- factor(rep(1:4, each = 3))
trt <- factor(rep(c("A", "B", "C"), 4))

block_re <- rnorm(4, 0, 2)[as.integer(block)]
trt_eff  <- c(0, 1.5, 2.5)[as.integer(trt)]
y <- 10 + block_re + trt_eff + rnorm(12, 0, 0.5)

df <- data.frame(block, trt, y)

fit_b <- aov(y ~ block + trt, data = df)
fit_nb <- aov(y ~ trt, data = df)

c(with_block_MSE = summary(fit_b)[[1]]["Residuals", "Mean Sq"],
  without_block_MSE = summary(fit_nb)[[1]]["Residuals", "Mean Sq"])

heading: 17.25 Introduction
text: Box-Behnken designs (BBD), introduced Box Behnken 1960, rotatable three-level response-surface designs whose distinguishing feature never run extreme-corner combinations factorial cube. factor set one three coded levels (\(-1\), \(0\), \(+1\)), every experimental run leaves least one factor centre level — run combines factors extreme settings simultaneously. makes design particularly useful extreme corner conditions infeasible (safety limits, equipment constraints, prohibitive cost, unstable chemistry extremes), still allowing estimation full second-order response-surface model main effects, quadratic terms, two-factor interactions.

heading: 17.27 Theory

Replicated centre runs provide pure-error estimation lack--fit test. \(k = 3\) BBD requires 12 factorial-style runs plus typically 3 centre points (15 runs total), comparable size central composite design (CCD) 14–20 runs.BBDs exactly rotatable \(k = 4\) \(k = 7\); values \(k\) nearly rotatable. absence axial (star) points means prediction near cube’s corners extrapolation elevated variance.

library(rsm)

bbd <- bbd(~ x1 + x2 + x3, n0 = 3, randomize = FALSE)
bbd

set.seed(2026)
bbd$y <- 10 + 2 * bbd$x1 + 1.5 * bbd$x2 - 1 * bbd$x3 -
         0.9 * bbd$x1^2 + 0.5 * bbd$x1 * bbd$x2 +
         rnorm(nrow(bbd), 0, 0.4)

fit <- rsm(y ~ SO(x1, x2, x3), data = bbd)
summary(fit)

heading: 17.36 Introduction
text: central composite design (CCD), introduced Box Wilson 1951, workhorse response-surface methodology arguably influential design construction industrial statistics. augments \(2^k\) factorial axial (star) points placed along factor axis distance \(\alpha\) design centre number replicated centre points. Together three components — factorial, axial, centre — provide enough information fit full second-order polynomial response surface \(k\) factors substantially fewer runs \(3^k\) full factorial require, still supporting estimation curvature, lack--fit testing, prediction-variance characterisation across design region.

heading: 17.37 Prerequisites
text: working understanding two-level factorial designs, polynomial regression response-surface modelling, concept design rotatability — prediction variance depends radial distance centre.

heading: 17.38 Theory
text: CCD three components:Factorial: \(2^k\) runs corners unit cube (\(\pm 1\) factor).Axial: \(2k\) runs \((\pm\alpha, 0, \dots, 0), (0, \pm\alpha, 0, \dots, 0), \ldots, (0, \ldots, 0, \pm\alpha)\).Centre: replicated \((0, 0, \ldots, 0)\) runs supplying pure-error estimation.choice \(\alpha\) determines design’s properties: rotatable CCDs use \(\alpha = \sqrt[4]{2^k}\) (giving constant prediction variance fixed distance centre), face-centred CCDs use \(\alpha = 1\) (staying within original \(\pm 1\) cube, useful factor extremes constrained), orthogonal CCDs choose \(\alpha\) make columns second-order design matrix orthogonal.

heading: 17.39 Assumptions
text: response well modelled second-order polynomial within design region, residual error approximately Normal constant variance, centre-point replicates provide valid pure-error estimate lack--fit can tested.

heading: 17.40 R Implementation
code:
library(rsm)

ccd <- ccd(~ x1 + x2, n0 = c(3, 3), alpha = "rotatable",
           randomize = FALSE)
ccd

set.seed(2026)
ccd$y <- 10 + 2 * ccd$x1 + 1.5 * ccd$x2 - 1.2 * ccd$x1^2 -
         0.8 * ccd$x2^2 + 0.6 * ccd$x1 * ccd$x2 +
         rnorm(nrow(ccd), 0, 0.5)

fit <- rsm(y ~ SO(x1, x2), data = ccd)
summary(fit)

steepest(fit, dist = 1.0)

heading: 17.47 Introduction
text: completely randomised design (CRD) simplest flexible comparative experimental designs: available experimental units assigned one several treatment groups unrestricted random mechanism, blocking, stratification, restriction randomisation. heading: 17.48 Prerequisites

heading: 17.49 Theory
text: \(\) treatment levels \(r\) replicates per treatment, \(n = \cdot r\) experimental units randomly allocated treatments without restriction. standard analysis model \[y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}, \qquad \varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2),\]\(\tau_i\) fixed effect \(\)-th treatment \(\varepsilon_{ij}\) independent Normal error. omnibus null hypothesis \(\tau_1 = \cdots = \tau_a\), tested one-way ANOVA \(F\)-statistic. Post-hoc pairwise comparisons (Tukey HSD, Dunnett control, Scheffé arbitrary contrasts) follow omnibus test significant pairwise inference needed.

heading: 17.50 Assumptions
text: experimental units exchangeable prior randomisation (homogeneous), errors independent within across treatment groups, within-group residuals approximately Normal, residual variance group. Departures variance homogeneity can usually addressed transformation, weighted ANOVA, Welch’s \(F\)-test.

heading: 17.51 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)
a <- 4; r <- 6
treatment <- factor(rep(LETTERS[1:a], each = r))
y <- rnorm(a * r, mean = rep(c(10, 12, 11, 14), each = r), sd = 2)

df <- data.frame(treatment, y)
fit <- aov(y ~ treatment, data = df)
summary(fit)

TukeyHSD(fit)

Residual diagnostics (Q-Q plot, Levene’s test \(p = 0.41\)) supported Normal-equal-variance assumptions.” Always pair omnibus test adjusted pairwise comparisons brief assumption-check statement.

library(mixexp)

lb <- c(0.2, 0.1, 0.3)
ub <- c(0.5, 0.6, 0.7)

xv

        6 * xv$x1 * xv$x2 +
        rnorm(nrow(xv), 0, 0.3)

fit <- MixModel(xv, "y",
                mixcomps = c("x1", "x2", "x3"), model = 2)
summary(fit)

heading: 17.69 Introduction
text: Crossover designs apply two treatments subject sequence, separated washout periods, every subject acts control. within-subject comparison gains substantial efficiency parallel-group designs -subject variability — typically largest component variance clinical-pharmacology studies — removed treatment contrast. simplest widely used crossover two-period two-treatment AB/BA design, half subjects receive treatment period 1 B period 2, half receive reverse sequence. Crossovers standard bioequivalence studies, early-phase clinical pharmacology, many sensory ergonomic experiments conditions can evaluated repeatedly subject.

heading: 17.70 Prerequisites
text: working understanding repeated-measures analysis, mixed-effects models subject random effect, concept carryover (residual treatment effect one period influencing next).

heading: 17.71 Theory
text: AB/BA crossover, standard mixed-effects analysis model \[y_{ijk} = \mu + \pi_j + \tau_k + s_i + \varepsilon_{ijk},\]period effect \(\pi_j\), treatment effect \(\tau_k\), subject random effect \(s_i \sim N(0, \sigma_s^2)\), residual error \(\varepsilon_{ijk}\). treatment effect estimated within-subject difference, eliminating -subject variance. Carryover — residual treatment effect period 1 affecting period 2 — manifests sequence--period interaction; present, biases simple within-subject treatment estimate requires either adequate washout, complex Williams design, model includes carryover terms.

heading: 17.72 Assumptions
text: Carryover absent controlled adequate washout period (pharmacologically, least five half-lives active compound), underlying condition stable across periods (progressive disease natural recovery), treatment effects independent order. Subjects exchangeable sequences.

heading: 17.73 R Implementation
code:
library(nlme)

set.seed(2026)
n <- 20
sequence <- sample(c("AB", "BA"), n, replace = TRUE)
subj_re <- rnorm(n, 0, 1)

y_A <- subj_re + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)
y_B <- subj_re + 0.6 + rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)

df <- data.frame(
  subject = rep(1:n, each = 2),
  period  = rep(1:2, n),
  sequence = rep(sequence, each = 2),
  treatment = unlist(lapply(sequence, function(s) strsplit(s, "")[[1]])),
  y = unlist(lapply(1:n, function(i)
    if (sequence[i] == "AB") c(y_A[i], y_B[i]) else c(y_B[i], y_A[i])))
)

fit <- lme(y ~ treatment + period, random = ~ 1 | subject, data = df)
summary(fit)$tTable

heading: 17.74 Output & Results
text: mixed-effects fit returns treatment effect within-subject standard error period effect separate fixed term. random-intercept structure absorbs -subject variance, dramatically tightening treatment estimate compared parallel-group design total sample size.

heading: 17.75 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “two-period AB/BA crossover analysis estimated B–treatment difference 0.56 (95 % CI 0.32 0.80, \(p < 0.001\)), achieving roughly three-fold precision relative parallel-group design number subjects. period effect significant (\(p = 0.41\)), sequence × period interaction (marker carryover) also non-significant (\(p = 0.62\)), supporting -carryover assumption. mandated washout seven half-lives sufficient.” Always report period carryover diagnostics.

heading: 17.76 Practical Tips
text: Adequate washout — least five half-lives active compound, often compounds long-lived metabolites — best defence carryover; designing conservative washouts far cheaper dealing biased results post hoc.Avoid crossover designs conditions evolve naturally study (progressive disease, healing wounds, post-surgical recovery) interventions whose effects short-lived wash rapidly without dosing; violate stability assumption.3 × 3 Williams design balances first-order carryover effects across treatment pairs carryover plausible washout made arbitrarily long; standard alternative AB/BA pharmacology.Always analyse mixed-effects model includes subject random effect; pooled two-sample \(t\)-tests ignore within-subject structure overstate true precision dramatically.Report period effect separately treatment effect; strong period effect (e.g., drift measurement instrument) can bias treatment estimate design imbalanced across sequences.Pre-specify carryover diagnostic done carryover detected; reactive analysis decisions seeing data inflate type-error increasingly flagged regulators.

heading: 17.80 Introduction
text: Many experiments produce one response must optimised jointly: maximise yield minimising impurity, hit target dissolution time keeping particle size within spec, maximise tensile strength minimising weight. Desirability functions, introduced Harrington 1965 refined Derringer Suich 1980, standard tool combining multiple responses single overall optimisation criterion. response converted \([0, 1]\) desirability scale via transformation specific response’s goal (maximise, minimise, hit target), individual desirabilities combined overall desirability via geometric mean. compound score maximised factor space, often using response surfaces fit second-order RSM phase.

heading: 17.81 Prerequisites
