heading: 17.82 Theory
text: response \(y_i\), define desirability function \(d_i(y_i) \[0, 1]\) — larger values desirable, irrespective direction “good” original scale. standard forms (Derringer–Suich) :Maximise: \(d_i = 0\) lower limit, \(d_i = 1\) target value, linearly increasing .Minimise: mirror maximise form.Target: \(d_i = 0\) lower upper limits, \(d_i = 1\) target, linear power transitions.overall desirability \(D = \left(\prod_i d_i\right)^{1/n}\), geometric mean. Crucially, single \(d_i = 0\) \(D = 0\) — geometric mean “non-compensatory”, meaning amount excellence one response can rescue unacceptable value another.

heading: 17.83 Assumptions
text: \(d_i\) appropriately parameterised realistic limits target values, geometric-mean aggregation acceptable decision-maker (alternative weighted-power means exist differential weighting), underlying response surfaces reasonably well estimated.

heading: 17.84 R Implementation
code:
library(desirability)

set.seed(2026)
df <- data.frame(x1 = runif(100, -1, 1), x2 = runif(100, -1, 1))
df$yield  <- 70 + 10 * df$x1 + 5 * df$x2 + rnorm(100)
df$cost   <- 20 - 2 * df$x1 + 4 * df$x2 + rnorm(100)

d_yield <- dMax(low = 60, high = 85)
d_cost  <- dMin(low = 10, high = 25)
dOverall <- dOverall(d_yield, d_cost)

df$d_overall <- predict(dOverall, with(df,
                                       cbind(yield = yield, cost = cost)))

head(df[order(-df$d_overall), ], 5)

heading: 17.95 R Implementation

library(FrF2)

fac <- FrF2(nruns = 8, nfactors = 3, replications = 2,
            randomize = TRUE, seed = 2026)
head(fac)

fac$B <- as.numeric(as.character(fac$B))
fac$C <- as.numeric(as.character(fac$C))

set.seed(2026)
fac$y <- 10 + 2 * fac$A + 1.5 * fac$B + 0.8 * fac$A * fac$B +
         rnorm(nrow(fac), 0, 0.7)

fit <- lm(y ~ A * B * C, data = fac)
summary(fit)

library(agricolae)

heading: 17.146 Introduction
text: Simplex centroid designs mixture experiments include centroids every sub-simplex component simplex: pure component (vertex), binary midpoint (edge centroid), ternary centroid (face centroid), overall centroid full simplex. result set \(2^q - 1\) design points \(q\) components spans blend orders simultaneously, making design well suited detecting higher-order synergies — three-way four-way blend interactions simpler simplex-lattice designs estimate. widely used food science, pharmaceutical formulation, chemical product development whenever response depends combinations multiple ingredients pure components alone capture behaviour.

heading: 17.148 Theory
text: \(q\) components simplex centroid design \(2^q - 1\) points: \(\binom{q}{1} = q\) vertices, \(\binom{q}{2}\) edge midpoints, \(\binom{q}{3}\) face centroids, , overall centroid. Scheffé canonical polynomial including blend orders \[y = \sum_i \beta_i x_i + \sum_{<j} \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \sum_{<j<k} \beta_{ijk} x_i x_j x_k + \cdots,\]intercept (\(\sum_i x_i = 1\)). simplex centroid design supplies exactly enough information estimate every blend term \(q\)-way interaction, contrast simplex-lattice designs estimate chosen order \(m \leq q\).

heading: 17.149 Assumptions

heading: 17.150 R Implementation

library(mixexp)

design <- SCD(3)
design

set.seed(2026)
design$y <- 10 * design$x1 + 12 * design$x2 + 8 * design$x3 +
            6 * design$x1 * design$x2 +
            4 * design$x1 * design$x3 +
            3 * design$x1 * design$x2 * design$x3 +
            rnorm(nrow(design), 0, 0.3)

fit <- MixModel(design, "y",
                mixcomps = c("x1", "x2", "x3"), model = 3)
summary(fit)

heading: 17.157 Introduction
text: Mixture experiments study response depends relative proportions two components blend, proportions constrained sum one. arise routinely food formulation (recipes), paint coating chemistry, pharmaceutical excipient blending, fuel formulation, setting absolute amount fixed relative composition can vary. simplex constraint \(x_1 + \cdots + x_q = 1\) removes one degree freedom, standard polynomial regression models directly applicable, Scheffé’s canonical polynomial forms — omit intercept pure quadratic terms — appropriate functional family. Simplex-lattice designs foundational mixture-design construction natural starting point mixture experiment.

heading: 17.158 Prerequisites
text: working understanding factorial designs, basic simplex geometry, Scheffé canonical polynomial form mixture models.

heading: 17.159 Theory
text: \(\{q, m\}\) simplex-lattice design \(q\) components samples blends component takes proportion set \(\{0, 1/m, 2/m, \dots, 1\}\), restricted proportions sum one. \(q = 3\) \(m = 2\), design six points: three pure components (vertices simplex), three binary midpoints (edge centres \((1/2, 1/2, 0)\), \((1/2, 0, 1/2)\), \((0, 1/2, 1/2)\)). Scheffé canonical polynomial order \(m\) canonical form \[y = \sum_i \beta_i x_i + \sum_{<j} \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \cdots,\]intercept (linear terms already absorb constant) pure-quadratic terms (\(\sum x_i = 1\) implies \(x_i^2 = x_i - \sum_{j \neq } x_i x_j\), redistributing squared terms linear interaction blends).

heading: 17.160 Assumptions
text: components sum one (design lies standard \((q-1)\)-simplex), response smooth function component proportions, residual error approximately Normal constant variance, chosen Scheffé polynomial order \(m\) adequately captures surface curvature.

heading: 17.161 R Implementation
code:
library(mixexp)

design <- SLD(3, 2)
design

set.seed(2026)
design$y <- 10 * design$x1 + 12 * design$x2 + 8 * design$x3 +
            6 * design$x1 * design$x2 +
            rnorm(nrow(design), 0, 0.3)

fit <- MixModel(design, "y", mixcomps = c("x1", "x2", "x3"), model = 2)
summary(fit)

text: reporting sentence: “\(\{3, 2\}\) simplex-lattice mixture design six runs identified component 2 strongest pure component (\(\beta_2 = 12.1\)) positive synergy components 1 2 (\(\beta_{12} = 5.9\)). fitted optimum lies \(x_1\)-\(x_2\) edge near \((0.5, 0.5, 0)\) predicted response 12.5, exceeding pure-component value. follow-centroid-augmented design confirm whether optimum truly lies binary edge shifts toward interior.” Always interpret pure components alongside blend interactions.

heading: 17.168 Introduction
text: Computer-generated optimal designs minimise chosen criterion information matrix. standard design fit constraints (irregular region, mixed-level factors, restricted runs), optimal designs primary recourse.

heading: 17.169 Prerequisites
text: Linear regression; Fisher information matrix.

heading: 17.170 Theory
text:
design matrix \(X\) assumed model, information matrix \(M = X^T X\). Common criteria:
- D-optimal: maximise \(\det(M)\) – minimises volume joint confidence ellipsoid coefficients.
- -optimal: minimise trace(\(M^{-1}\)) – minimises average variance coefficient estimates.
- -optimal: minimise average prediction variance design region.
- G-optimal: minimise maximum prediction variance.Computer algorithms (Fedorov exchange) search optimum.

heading: 17.171 Assumptions
text: Model correctly specified; \(n \geq p\) (parameters); design region explicit.

heading: 17.172 R Implementation
code:
library(AlgDesign)

set.seed(2026)
# Candidate set: 3^2 factorial grid
candidates <- expand.grid(x1 = c(-1, 0, 1), x2 = c(-1, 0, 1))

# D-optimal design for a second-order model, selecting 8 runs
d_opt <- optFederov(~ quad(x1, x2),
                    data = candidates,
                    nTrials = 8,
                    criterion = "D",
                    nRepeats = 20)
d_opt$design

# I-optimal
i_opt <- optFederov(~ quad(x1, x2),
                    data = candidates,
                    nTrials = 8,
                    criterion = "I",
                    nRepeats = 20)
i_opt$design

heading: 17.173 Output & Results
text: Two sets 8 runs 9-point candidate set; D-optimal maximises determinant \(X^TX\); -optimal minimises average prediction variance.

heading: 17.174 Interpretation
text: “D-optimal selected 8 runs tightly constrain polynomial coefficients; -optimal picked different subset minimises prediction variance across design region. Choose D inference coefficients, prediction.”

heading: 17.178 Introduction
text: Orthogonal arrays (OAs) tabulated fractional-factorial designs every pair columns contains every combination levels equal number times. Genichi Taguchi popularised catalogue specific OAs — \(L_4\), \(L_8\), \(L_9\), \(L_{16}\), \(L_{18}\), \(L_{27}\), others — economical screening designs quality-engineering framework, labels now standard shorthand industrial DoE. orthogonality property guarantees main effects distinct factors estimated independently equal footing, OAs deliver cleanest possible main-effect estimates smallest possible run count, given assumed effect-sparsity context.

heading: 17.179 Prerequisites
text: Familiarity fractional factorial designs, Plackett–Burman family, concept orthogonality among columns design matrix.

heading: 17.180 Theory
text: \(L_n(m^k)\) array \(n\) rows (runs) \(k\) columns (factors), column \(m\) equally-replicated levels. Orthogonality means pair columns combination levels appears equal number times, ensuring uncorrelated main-effect estimates.standard catalogue includes:L4: 4 runs, 3 two-level factors.L8: 8 runs, 7 two-level factors.L9: 9 runs, 4 three-level factors.L16: 16 runs, 15 two-level factors.L18: 18 runs, one two-level factor plus 7 three-level factors (mixed-level).L27: 27 runs, 13 three-level factors.

heading: 17.181 Assumptions
text: Main effects dominate response (effect-sparsity principle); two-factor higher-order interactions either negligible assigned specific pre-determined columns reading array’s linear-graph; runs independent constant residual variance.

library(qualityTools)

l9 <- taguchiDesign("L9_3^4")
summary(l9)

set.seed(2026)
l9$y <- rnorm(9, mean = 10 + 2 * as.numeric(l9$A) -
                      1.5 * as.numeric(l9$C), sd = 0.5)

effectPlot(l9, response = "y")

Factors B D showed nearly flat level-means inactive. active subset carried forward full \(3^2\) factorial replicated centre points characterise curvature.” Always justify “active” assignments effect magnitude rather \(p\)-values alone OA screening.

heading: 17.189 Introduction
text: Plackett–Burman (PB) designs, introduced Plackett Burman 1946, two-level orthogonal screening designs \(n\) runs (\(n\) multiple 4) can accommodate \(n - 1\) factors. widely regarded economical screening tool industrial DoE: many candidate factors must sifted identify drive response, 12-run PB design can screen 11 factors, 20-run PB 19, . price economy resolution-III aliasing structure main effects confounded two-factor interactions, PB designs appropriate strictly screening — never confirmatory interaction-aware inference.

library(FrF2)

pb <- pb(nruns = 12, nfactors = 8, randomize = TRUE, seed = 2026)
pb

for (v in paste0("A", 1:8)) {
  pb[[v]] <- as.numeric(as.character(pb[[v]]))
}
set.seed(2026)
pb$y <- 10 + 2.5 * pb$A1 + 1.8 * pb$A2 + rnorm(nrow(pb), 0, 0.6)

fit <- lm(y ~ ., data = pb)
summary(fit)

heading: 17.200 Introduction
text: Power analysis designed experiments determines run count required detect specified main effects interactions adequate probability. bridges experimental-design choice statistical inference: design useful run count gives sensible power smallest effect scientifically actionable. Skimping power means -powered experiments fail detect real effects waste resources inconclusive results; -powering experiment beyond needed wastes resources differently exposes design detecting irrelevantly small effects. pre-specified power calculation therefore now standard DoE protocol — industrial, agricultural, clinical — increasingly required regulators journal reviewers.

heading: 17.201 Prerequisites
text: working understanding effect sizes (Cohen’s \(d\), \(f\), \(f^2\)), structure ANOVA \(F\)-tests, non-central \(F\)-distribution, concepts significance level statistical power.

heading: 17.202 Theory
text: \(2^k\) factorial design, main-effect \(F\)-test single factor non-centrality parameter\[\lambda = \frac{n \Delta^2}{4 \sigma^2},\]\(n\) total number runs, \(\Delta\) effect (difference high- low-level means), \(\sigma^2\) residual variance. Power computed \(P(F > F_{\alpha, 1, \mathrm{df}} \mid \lambda)\) non-central \(F\)-distribution. Interactions typically effect sizes 30–50 % corresponding main effects, designs powered adequately main effects routinely lack power two-factor interactions higher-order terms.

heading: 17.203 Assumptions
text: residual variance known pre-specified, effect size interest fixed advance, design analysis model pre-specified, residuals satisfy standard ANOVA assumptions independence, Normality, constant variance.

heading: 17.204 R Implementation
code:
library(pwr)

pwr.t.test(d = 1, n = 8, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "two.sample", alternative = "two.sided")

set.seed(2026)
n_sim <- 500
reps <- 2
power_sim <- mean(replicate(n_sim, {
  A <- rep(c(-1, 1), each = 4 * reps)
  B <- rep(rep(c(-1, 1), each = 2 * reps), 2)
  C <- rep(c(-1, 1), times = 4 * reps)
  y <- 1 * A + 0.5 * B + rnorm(8 * reps, 0, 1.5)
  pA <- summary(lm(y ~ A + B + C))$coefficients["A", "Pr(>|t|)"]
  pA < 0.05
}))
power_sim

heading: 17.211 Introduction
text: randomised complete block design (RCBD) one useful experimental designs applied research. known source variation – day measurement, animal cage, plate, subject – likely influence outcome, RCBD groups experimental units homogeneous blocks randomises treatments within block. block effect thereby separated treatment effect, residual error within-block variability, typically much smaller pooled unblocked error.

heading: 17.212 Prerequisites
text: reader met one-way ANOVA principle randomisation. Familiarity R’s formula syntax assumed.

heading: 17.213 Theory
text: Let \(y_{ij}\) response experimental unit receiving treatment \(\) block \(j\), \(= 1, \ldots, t\) treatments \(j = 1, \ldots, b\) blocks. additive RCBD model \[y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + \varepsilon_{ij}, \qquad \varepsilon_{ij} \overset{iid}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2),\]\(\tau_i\) treatment effect (summing zero) \(\beta_j\) block effect. treatment appears exactly block – “complete” RCBD.ANOVA decomposes total sum squares treatment, block, residual components. F-test treatments \(t-1\) numerator \((t-1)(b-1)\) denominator degrees freedom. blocking captured real variation, residual mean square smaller completely randomised design, test higher power.design assumes treatment--block interaction. interaction exists (treatment works better blocks others), additive model misspecified. Tukey one-degree--freedom test non-additivity provides partial check.

heading: 17.214 Assumptions
text: Additivity: treatment--block interaction.Independence: residuals mutually independent.Homoscedasticity: residuals constant variance across treatments blocks.Normality: residuals approximately normal.

heading: 17.215 R Implementation
text: design.rcbd() produces randomised plan treatment appearing per block. simulated response includes block effect treatment effect lowest , highest C. aov() fits additive RCBD model; emmeans::pairs() performs pairwise comparisons Tukey adjustment.
code:
library(agricolae)
library(emmeans)

treatments <- LETTERS[1:4]
blocks <- paste0("B", 1:5)

set.seed(2026)
design <- design.rcbd(trt = treatments, r = length(blocks),
                      seed = 2026)
print(design$book)

design$book$y <- with(design$book,
                      5 +
                        as.numeric(factor(block)) * 0.8 +
                        c(0, 1.2, 1.8, 0.4)[as.numeric(factor(treatments))] +
                        rnorm(nrow(design$book), 0, 0.6))

fit <- aov(y ~ block + treatments, data = design$book)
summary(fit)

emm <- emmeans(fit, ~ treatments)
pairs(emm, adjust = "tukey")

Tukey-adjusted pairwise comparisons indicated treatment C produced significantly higher responses treatments B (see Table X).”

heading: 17.221 Introduction
text: Randomisation protective heart experimental design. ensures expectation, unknown sources variation balanced across treatments; validates model’s assumption independent errors; prevents systematic bias lurking variables.

heading: 17.222 Prerequisites
text: Experimental design basics.

heading: 17.223 Theory
text:
Three Fisher principles DoE: replication, randomisation, blocking. Randomisation:
- Ensures expected balance known unknown covariates.
- Justifies random-error model used ANOVA.
- Validates inference (significance tests approximate randomisation tests).Randomisation procedures vary design: CRD = unrestricted, RCBD = within-block, Latin square = row-column, split-plot = nested.

heading: 17.224 Assumptions
text: Randomisation genuinely random (computer-generated sequence, sealed envelopes); allocation concealed experimenter assignment.

heading: 17.225 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Randomise 12 units to 4 treatments for a CRD
treatments <- rep(c("A", "B", "C", "D"), 3)
allocation <- sample(treatments, 12, replace = FALSE)
allocation

# For a RCBD, randomise within each of 3 blocks
blocks <- rep(1:3, each = 4)
alloc_rcbd <- do.call(rbind, lapply(split(1:12, blocks), function(idx) {
  data.frame(unit = idx, treatment = sample(c("A", "B", "C", "D")))
}))
alloc_rcbd

heading: 17.227 Interpretation

heading: 17.231 Introduction
text: Repeated-measures designs measure experimental unit multiple time points multiple conditions. gain statistical power relative -subject designs removing -subject component variance within-subject contrasts interest, introduce within-subject correlation structure must modelled correctly. classical repeated-measures ANOVA requires sphericity restricted balanced complete designs; modern alternative mixed-effects modelling subject random effect, accommodates unbalanced data, missing time points, richer covariance structures remaining valid broader assumptions.

heading: 17.232 Prerequisites
text: working understanding one-way factorial ANOVA, concept correlated data, essentials mixed-effects modelling random intercepts (optionally) random slopes.

heading: 17.233 Theory
text: standard mixed-effects model repeated measures \[y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_j + s_i + \varepsilon_{ij}, \qquad s_i \sim N(0, \sigma_s^2), \quad \varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2),\]within-subject factor effect \(\tau_j\), subject random intercept \(s_i\), residual error \(\varepsilon_{ij}\). Common covariance structures imposed residual vector one subject include:Compound symmetry: equal residual variance equal correlation across time pairs (strict — implied random-intercept model).AR(1): autoregressive order one, correlation decaying lag.Unstructured: fully general \(T \times T\) covariance \(T(T+1)/2\) parameters.classical repeated-measures ANOVA, sphericity relevant assumption, Greenhouse–Geisser Huynh–Feldt corrections applied Mauchly’s test detects violation.

heading: 17.234 Assumptions
text: within-subject correlation structure correctly specified, residuals approximately Normal, missing data missing random (MAR) — standard assumption mixed-effects models can accommodate without listwise deletion.

heading: 17.235 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4); library(lmerTest)

set.seed(2026)
n_sub <- 30; n_time <- 4
sub_re <- rnorm(n_sub, 0, 1)

df <- expand.grid(subject = 1:n_sub, time = 1:n_time)
df$y <- 10 + 0.5 * df$time + sub_re[df$subject] +
        rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 0.5)

fit_ri <- lmer(y ~ factor(time) + (1 | subject), data = df)
anova(fit_ri)

fit_rs <- lmer(y ~ time + (time | subject), data = df)
summary(fit_rs)$coefficients

heading: 17.238 Practical Tips
text: Use mixed-effects models (lmer lme4, lme nlme, generalised-mixed via glmmTMB) rather classical repeated-measures ANOVA whenever possible; handle unbalanced data, missing time points, irregular timing, richer covariance structures naturally.irregular continuous timing, specify time continuous covariate random slopes; far efficient treating time point separate factor level response genuinely smooth function time.must use classical repeated-measures ANOVA (e.g., compatibility standard reporting requirement), check sphericity Mauchly’s test apply Greenhouse–Geisser Huynh–Feldt correction whenever violated.Missing time points handled naturally mixed-effects models MAR assumption; classical repeated-measures ANOVA requires complete cases discards subjects missing time, wasteful can introduce bias.Report fixed-effect estimates (substantive scientific output) random-effect variance components (describe much within-subject correlation present); latter essential judging whether design well chosen.binary count outcomes measured repeatedly, use generalised mixed-effects models (glmer, glmmTMB) generalised estimating equations (GEE via geepack package) — latter trade efficiency robust marginal inference.

heading: 17.253 Introduction
text: Response surface methodology (RSM), introduced Box Wilson 1951, sequential experimental strategy finding characterising optimum process response space multiple controllable factors. RSM combines fractional full factorial screening, steepest-ascent search, second-order polynomial fitting near optimum coherent workflow become standard practice chemical engineering, food science, pharmaceutical formulation, manufacturing process improvement, setting smooth response surface must navigated efficiently. hallmark staged, sequential character: stage experimentation informs next, final stage commits resources dense second-order designs needed full characterisation.

heading: 17.254 Prerequisites
text: working understanding factorial designs, polynomial regression response-surface modelling, gradient-based optimisation concepts, canonical form quadratic surface.

heading: 17.255 Theory
text: RSM operates two main phases:First-order phase: fractional full factorial replicated centre points fits linear model restricted operating region. path steepest ascent (descent) along fitted gradient guides experimentation toward optimum, confirmation runs along path.Second-order phase: experimentation near optimum, central composite Box-Behnken design fits full second-order polynomial. Canonical analysis decomposes quadratic-form matrix eigenvalues, classifying stationary point maximum, minimum, saddle, rising/stationary ridge.canonical form makes RSM just curve-fitting: characterises geometry response surface reveals optimum genuinely peak, lies ridge requiring constrained search, experimentation needed.

heading: 17.256 Assumptions
text: response smooth, well-defined function factors design region; second-order polynomial adequate local approximation neighbourhood optimum; residual error Normal constant variance; runs independent.

heading: 17.257 R Implementation
code:
library(rsm)

set.seed(2026)
ccd <- ccd(~ x1 + x2, n0 = c(3, 3), alpha = "rotatable",
           randomize = FALSE)
ccd$y <- 10 + 2 * ccd$x1 + 1.5 * ccd$x2 -
         1.2 * ccd$x1^2 - 0.9 * ccd$x2^2 +
         0.6 * ccd$x1 * ccd$x2 +
         rnorm(nrow(ccd), 0, 0.4)

fit <- rsm(y ~ SO(x1, x2), data = ccd)
summary(fit)

contour(fit, ~ x1 + x2, at = c(0, 0), image = TRUE,
        main = "RSM response surface")

canonical(fit)

heading: 17.258 Output & Results
text: rsm() summary returns linear, quadratic, interaction coefficients significance tests, plus canonical-analysis classification (maximum, minimum, saddle, ridge) based eigenvalues quadratic-form matrix. contour() persp() render surface visually, canonical() reports stationary point original canonical coordinates.

heading: 17.259 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “Response-surface methodology 14-run rotatable CCD identified concave surface stationary point \((x_1^*, x_2^*) = (0.85, 0.72)\). Canonical analysis confirmed true maximum (eigenvalues negative: \(\lambda_1 = -1.31\), \(\lambda_2 = -0.79\)); predicted peak response 12.1, 95 % prediction interval [11.5, 12.7]. Confirmation runs predicted optimum yielded 12.0 (mean 3 replicates), within interval supporting model.” Always validate predicted optimum confirmation runs.

heading: 17.260 Practical Tips
text: first-order steepest-ascent phase can save many runs; don’t jump straight second-order fit operating region far optimum, quadratic fit far peak yields meaningless coefficients wasted runs.Always inspect contour 3D surface plots addition coefficient table; numerical summaries can hide ridges, saddles, surface features obvious visually.Canonical analysis reveals nature stationary point signs eigenvalues: negative = maximum, positive = minimum, mixed signs = saddle, near-zero = ridge requiring constrained directional search.predicted optimum lies outside experimental design region, use ridge analysis (constrained optimisation along path steepest ascent within cube) rather naive extrapolation, dangerous statistically unsupported.Follow RSM confirmation runs predicted optimum inside prediction interval; falsified predictions reveal model inadequacy motivate higher-order fit re-positioned design region.multiple responses (common situation industrial work — yield, purity, cost), use desirability functions combine surfaces single optimisation criterion rather optimising response independently.

heading: 17.264 Introduction
text: Robust parameter design (Taguchi, 1950s-60s) seeks combinations controllable design factors minimise response variability across noise factors (environmental, batch--batch, use-conditions). goal product process performs consistently regardless noise.

heading: 17.265 Prerequisites
text: Factorial designs; variance decomposition.

heading: 17.266 Theory
text: Inner array control factors crossed outer array noise factors. control setting, compute summary – mean, standard deviation, signal--noise ratio. Choose control setting optimises summary.Modern approach: fit response model control, noise, control-x-noise interactions; find control levels minimise variance meeting target mean.

heading: 17.267 Assumptions
text: Noise factors represent realistic variation use time; interactions control factors primary lever variance reduction.

heading: 17.268 R Implementation
code:
library(FrF2)

set.seed(2026)
# Inner array: 2^3 controllable factors
ctrl <- FrF2(nruns = 8, nfactors = 3, randomize = FALSE)
ctrl$A <- as.numeric(as.character(ctrl$A))
ctrl$B <- as.numeric(as.character(ctrl$B))
ctrl$C <- as.numeric(as.character(ctrl$C))

# Outer array: 2^2 noise factors
noise <- expand.grid(N1 = c(-1, 1), N2 = c(-1, 1))

# Simulate: noise x control interaction
results <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:nrow(ctrl), function(i) {
  vals <- sapply(1:nrow(noise), function(j) {
    10 + 2 * ctrl$A[i] + 1.5 * ctrl$B[i] +
      0.5 * noise$N1[j] + 0.8 * ctrl$A[i] * noise$N1[j] +
      rnorm(1, 0, 0.3)
  })
  data.frame(run = i, mean = mean(vals), sd = sd(vals),
             snr = -10 * log10(var(vals)))
}))
results

# Choose control level minimising variance
results$A <- ctrl$A
cbind(ctrl, results)[order(results$sd), ]

heading: 17.269 Output & Results
text: Per-control-run mean, SD, signal--noise ratio; control combination smallest SD robust-optimal.

heading: 17.270 Interpretation
text: “Robust parameter design identified setting (= -1, B = +1, C = +1) minimising response SD across noise factors. Control factor interacted noise factor N1, explaining variance reduction = -1.”

heading: 17.271 Practical Tips
text: inner-outer array approach can computationally expensive; use combined model control-noise interactions possible.Report mean variance; robust designs often accept small mean-response penalty large variance reduction.Consider dual-response surface methods joint mean-variance optimisation.Pay attention control-noise interactions: robustness lives.Combining Taguchi’s philosophy modern RSM flexible pure Taguchi methods.

heading: 17.274 Introduction
text: Signal--noise (SNR) ratios Taguchi’s framework jointly summarise mean variance response. Three variants handle different quality targets: smaller--better, larger--better, nominal--best. Maximising SNR selects factor combinations produce desirable mean low variance.

heading: 17.275 Prerequisites
text: Taguchi methods; variance mean summaries.

heading: 17.276 Theory
text: Smaller--better (minimisation): \(\text{SNR} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum y_i^2\right)\).Larger--better (maximisation): \(\text{SNR} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n} \sum 1/y_i^2\right)\).Nominal--best (hit target \(\tau\)): \(\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(\bar{y}^2 / s^2)\).Higher SNR always better. Bias: SNRs conflate mean variance; variance reduction can increase SNR even mean drifts.

heading: 17.277 Assumptions
text: Outcomes non-negative (smaller/larger--better); target appropriate; replicates exist estimate variance.

heading: 17.278 R Implementation
code:
set.seed(2026)

# Smaller-is-better: defects per batch
defects <- matrix(rpois(20 * 3, lambda = 4), 20, 3)
snr_sb <- -10 * log10(rowMeans(defects^2))

# Larger-is-better: process yield
yield <- matrix(rnorm(20 * 3, 80, 5), 20, 3)
snr_lb <- -10 * log10(rowMeans(1 / yield^2))

# Nominal-is-best: target 50
values <- matrix(rnorm(20 * 3, 50, 3), 20, 3)
means <- rowMeans(values); sds <- apply(values, 1, sd)
snr_nom <- 10 * log10(means^2 / sds^2)

head(data.frame(snr_sb, snr_lb, snr_nom))

heading: 17.279 Output & Results
text: Per-batch SNR values three variants; batch maximising SNR desirable combination mean variance.

heading: 17.280 Interpretation
text: “Smaller--better SNR -12.5 (batch 3) vs -14.7 (batch 10); batch 3 achieved lower mean lower variance defects. larger--better SNR identified different optimal batch, suggesting SNRs depend target.”

heading: 17.281 Practical Tips
text: SNR combines mean variance – convenient conflates . Modern practice reports mean variance separately.Dual-response surface methods (Myers-Carter, Vining-Myers) extend SNR thinking fitting separate mean variance models.nominal--best, ensure \(\bar{y}\) hits target interpreting SNR.SNR sensitive sample size; fewer replicates inflate deflate metric.Report SNR dB (decibels, .e. using \(10 \log_{10}\)), follow Taguchi conventions.

heading: 17.284 Introduction
text: Split-plot designs arise whenever experimental factors hard change runs others easy change within run. Oven temperature, pasture treatment, baking time, batch-level chemical additive, instrument calibration typically hard change — can set per batch freely samples. contrast, additive level within batch, fertiliser sub-plot within paddock, sample-level annotation can randomised within larger group. Restricted randomisation hard--change factor whole-plot level unrestricted randomisation easy--change factor within whole plots produces design two error strata, requiring appropriate \(F\)-test denominator. Recognising split-plot structure rather ignoring essential valid inference.

heading: 17.285 Prerequisites
text: working understanding factorial designs, mixed-effects models crossed nested random effects, concept multiple error strata restricted-randomisation experiments.

heading: 17.286 Theory
text: standard split-plot model \[y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \delta_{ij} + \beta_k + (\alpha\beta)_{ik} + \varepsilon_{ijk},\]whole-plot effect \(\alpha_i\), whole-plot error \(\delta_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma_W^2)\), sub-plot effect \(\beta_k\), interaction \((\alpha\beta)_{ik}\), sub-plot error \(\varepsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma_S^2)\). whole-plot factor \(\alpha\) tested whole-plot error, sub-plot factor \(\beta\) interaction \(\alpha\beta\) tested (typically smaller) sub-plot error. result, sub-plot factor interaction usually power whole-plot factor — structural asymmetry inform factor assigned stratum.

heading: 17.287 Assumptions
text: Whole-plot errors independent across whole plots; sub-plot errors independent within across whole plots; error components Normal constant variance; random-effects structure correctly specified analysis.

heading: 17.288 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4); library(lmerTest)

set.seed(2026)
n_rep <- 2; n_wp <- 3; n_sp <- 4
df <- expand.grid(rep = 1:n_rep, temp = factor(1:n_wp),
                  form = factor(1:n_sp))
df$batch <- interaction(df$rep, df$temp)
batch_re <- rnorm(nlevels(df$batch), 0, 0.6)[df$batch]

df$y <- 10 + 0.5 * as.numeric(df$temp) + 0.7 * as.numeric(df$form) +
        batch_re + rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 0.5)

fit <- lmer(y ~ temp * form + (1 | batch), data = df)
anova(fit)

heading: 17.289 Output & Results
text: lmer() (1 | batch) random effect captures whole-plot stratum (batch corresponds one whole-plot setting temperature), residual captures sub-plot stratum. anova() lmerTest returns \(F\)-tests Satterthwaite Kenward-Roger denominator df, automatically routing effect appropriate stratum.

heading: 17.290 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “split-plot mixed model showed significant whole-plot effect oven temperature (\(F_{2, 4} = 5.2\), \(p = 0.04\), batch-level error) highly significant sub-plot effect formulation (\(F_{3, 18} = 14.8\), \(p < 0.001\), residual error). temperature × formulation interaction non-significant (\(F_{6, 18} = 1.1\), \(p = 0.40\)). Sub-plot effects tested substantially power sub-plot error much smaller whole-plot error (\(\sigma_S^2 = 0.25\) vs \(\sigma_W^2 = 0.36\)).” Always identify stratum \(F\)-test uses.

heading: 17.291 Practical Tips
text: Always match error term effect’s level design; analysing split-plot CRD treats whole-plot sub-plot effects wrong denominator inflates deflates significance wrong factor.Mixed-effects models (lmer lme4) give correct standard errors \(F\)-tests random-effect structure specified correctly; explicit attention random-effect formula single common source split-plot analysis errors.Replicate whole plots whenever possible — whole-plot \(F\)-tests df otherwise. three whole-plot levels single replicate, whole-plot test essentially power; second replicate minimum recommended.Split-plot structure arises naturally agricultural field experiments (treatments applied whole strips, sub-treatments within), manufacturing (batch-level process settings, within-batch additives), instrumentation studies (calibration per session, sample-level treatments within session).Strip-plot designs extend split-plot idea two hard--change factors applied perpendicular directions; three error strata require correspondingly careful random-effects specification.Assigning factor whole-plot stratum reduces detection power; factor scientifically critical, assign sub-plot stratum possible — even cost operational inconvenience.

heading: 17.295 Introduction
text: method steepest ascent workhorse first phase response-surface methodology. fitting linear (first-order) model small factorial experiment current operating region, gradient vector points direction fastest predicted improvement; subsequent confirmation runs taken sequentially along gradient, moving experimental region toward neighbourhood optimum. strategy efficient replaces exhaustive grid search factor space directed walk informed local linear approximation, dramatically reducing number runs required locate region near process optimum.

heading: 17.296 Prerequisites
text: working understanding first-order linear regression, concept gradient vector, sequential staging experimentation response-surface methodology.

heading: 17.297 Theory
text: Fit first-order linear model\[\hat y = \beta_0 + \sum_i \beta_i x_i\]current operating region using small two-level factorial replicated centre points. steepest-ascent direction gradient \(\nabla \hat y = (\beta_1, \dots, \beta_k)\), normalised unit length. Subsequent runs taken successive multiples chosen step size along direction; experimentally observed responses guide whether continue, slow , stop. response stops improving along path, new first-order design fit new operating region procedure iterates.

heading: 17.298 Assumptions
text: local first-order linear approximation adequate current region (strong curvature invalidate gradient direction), response surface smooth, step size along path appropriate — small search wastes runs, large overshoots optimum. Centre-point replicates allow detection curvature via lack--fit, signalling switch second-order fit.

heading: 17.299 R Implementation
code:
library(rsm)

set.seed(2026)
region1 <- data.frame(x1 = c(-1, 1, -1, 1, 0),
                      x2 = c(-1, -1, 1, 1, 0))
region1$y <- 10 + 2 * region1$x1 + 1.5 * region1$x2 +
             rnorm(nrow(region1), 0, 0.3)

fit1 <- rsm(y ~ FO(x1, x2), data = region1)
summary(fit1)

direction <- steepest(fit1, dist = c(0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, 7))
direction

Confirmation runs distances 1, 3, 5, 7 centre showed monotone increase plateau distance 5; new first-order design fit centred , revealing significant curvature (\(p = 0.004\) centre-point lack--fit), second-order CCD used final characterisation.” Always describe path stopping criterion.

heading: 17.306 Introduction
text: Strip-plot designs — also known split-block designs — apply two hard--change factors perpendicular strips across block, resulting factor combinations realised cells cross. arise naturally agricultural field experiments one factor (e.g., irrigation level) applied along rows another (e.g., fertiliser type) along columns, industrial manufacturing two stages processing cover entire pallet, food pharmaceutical settings two restrictions randomisation operate different directions. defining feature three independent error strata — one row factor, one column factor, one interaction — requiring appropriate \(F\)-test denominator.

heading: 17.307 Prerequisites
text: working understanding split-plot designs, concept multiple error strata, mixed-effects model specification nested random effects.

heading: 17.308 Theory
text: strip-plot model three error strata corresponding three sources restricted randomisation:Row factor: applied entire rows; tested row-stratum error (variation among rows within blocks).Column factor: applied entire columns; tested column-stratum error.Row × column interaction: tested cell-level (smallest) error.main effect therefore lower power completely randomised design, denominator built coarser stratum, design operationally feasible full randomisation two hard--change factors impractical prohibitively expensive.

heading: 17.309 Assumptions
text: three error strata independent, errors Normal homoscedastic stratum, random-effects structure correctly specified — typically random effects row-within-block column-within-block, cell-level residual capturing interaction-level error.

heading: 17.310 R Implementation
code:
library(lme4); library(lmerTest)

set.seed(2026)
n_rep <- 3
rows <- factor(rep(1:3, each = 4, times = n_rep))
cols <- factor(rep(rep(1:4, 3), n_rep))
rep_f <- factor(rep(1:n_rep, each = 12))
df <- data.frame(rep = rep_f, rows, cols)

df$row_batch <- interaction(df$rep, df$rows)
df$col_batch <- interaction(df$rep, df$cols)

row_re <- rnorm(nlevels(df$row_batch), 0, 0.5)[df$row_batch]
col_re <- rnorm(nlevels(df$col_batch), 0, 0.4)[df$col_batch]

df$y <- 10 + 0.6 * as.numeric(df$rows) + 0.4 * as.numeric(df$cols) +
        row_re + col_re + rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 0.3)

fit <- lmer(y ~ rows * cols + (1 | row_batch) + (1 | col_batch),
            data = df)
anova(fit)

heading: 17.311 Output & Results
text: lmer() two crossed random-effect terms (row-within-replicate column-within-replicate) cell-level residual recovers three error strata. anova() returns appropriate \(F\)-tests row column main effects respective stratum errors interaction cell-level error. heading: 17.312 Interpretation
text: reporting sentence: “strip-plot ANOVA showed row factor marginally significant (\(F_{2, 4} = 3.8\), \(p = 0.05\) row-stratum error), column factor significant (\(F_{3, 6} = 6.4\), \(p = 0.01\) column-stratum error), row × column interaction non-significant tested cell-level error (\(F_{6, 24} = 1.2\), \(p = 0.34\)). Random-effect variance components row-within-replicate column-within-replicate modest, indicating strip structure absorbed limited non-trivial nuisance variation.” Always report effect appropriate stratum.

heading: 17.313 Practical Tips
text: Strip-plot designs require replication across blocks (multiple replicates cross structure) valid inference; single block leaves error degrees freedom row column main effects.Strip-plot structure natural agricultural field experiments two perpendicular operations (e.g., ploughing direction spraying direction) applied independently, industrial manufacturing two upstream processes cover whole batches different ways.Correct random-effects specification critical; incorrect model treats restricted randomisation complete, yielding \(F\)-tests wrong denominator inflated false-positive rates.Use lmerTest::ranova() test significance random-effect variance components ; also helps diagnose whether strip structure substantively different CRD analysis implied.industrial settings, strip-plot structure arises whenever two manufacturing stages apply whole pallet, batch, run disaggregating; recognising structure rather ignoring essential valid inference.analysis software-checks, fit dataset wrong (CRD-like) model right (strip-plot) model; comparing two highlights inferential cost ignoring design useful teaching diagnostic.

heading: 17.317 Introduction
text: Taguchi methods (1950s-60s) apply orthogonal-array experiments optimise product process design minimum variability. Central ideas: loss functions defining quality deviation target, signal--noise (SNR) ratios summarising mean variance jointly, compact orthogonal arrays efficient screening.

heading: 17.318 Prerequisites
text: Fractional factorial designs; signal--noise.

heading: 17.319 Theory
text:
Quality loss \(L(y) = k(y - \tau)^2\) target \(\tau\) – quality decreases quadratically deviation.SNR:
- Smaller--better: \(\text{SNR} = -10 \log_{10}(\sum y_i^2 / n)\).
- Larger--better: \(\text{SNR} = -10 \log_{10}(\sum 1/y_i^2 / n)\).
- Nominal--best: \(\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(\bar{y}^2 / s^2)\).Taguchi arrays (L4, L8, L9, L16) Plackett-Burman-style orthogonal arrays Japanese quality-engineering conventions.

heading: 17.320 Assumptions
text: Main effects dominate (sparsity); variance reduction via control factor choice (robust design).

heading: 17.321 R Implementation
code:
library(qualityTools)

set.seed(2026)
# L8 array (2-level, 7 factors)
ta <- taguchiDesign("L8_2^7")
summary(ta)

# Simulate responses
ta$y <- rnorm(8, mean = 10 + 2 * as.numeric(ta$A) +
                          1.5 * as.numeric(ta$B), sd = 0.5)
effectPlot(ta, response = "y",
           main = "Taguchi main-effects plot (L8)")

heading: 17.322 Output & Results
text: L8 orthogonal array main-effects plot showing average response factor’s two levels.

heading: 17.323 Interpretation
text: “L8 design screened 7 factors 8 runs; main-effects plot identified factors B dominant, optimal settings maximising (minimising) target response.”

heading: 17.324 Practical Tips
text: Taguchi arrays essentially Plackett-Burman designs; modern DoE uses statistical fractional factorials aliasing awareness.SNR metrics conflate mean variance; modern practice separates via dual-response surface methods.Taguchi’s three-stage design (system, parameter, tolerance) useful framework; specific SNR tools debated.Orthogonal arrays efficient screening; interactions must explicitly assigned specific columns.manufacturing quality engineering, Taguchi remains widely-used vocabulary; modern methods supersede specifics.

library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 17.331 1. Hypothesis
text:
H0: new treatment worse comparator Δ
= 3 units continuous outcome.
H1: new treatment worse Δ.

heading: 17.332 2. Visualise
code:
trial <- tibble(
  arm = rep(c("comparator", "new"), each = n),
  y   = c(rnorm(n, 70, 10), rnorm(n, 69, 10))
)

trial |>
  ggplot(aes(arm, y, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, colour = "grey30") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Outcome") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

heading: 17.333 3. Assumptions
text:
Independent observations, roughly normal within-arm residuals, 
pre-specified margin declared looking data. 
direction test matters: test whether lower bound 
mean difference (new − comparator) −Δ.

heading: 17.334 4. Conduct
code:
fit
ci <- fit$conf.int
est <- diff(rev(fit$estimate))  # new - comparator
ni_pass <- ci[1] > -delta
tibble(estimate = est, low = ci[1], high = ci[2], margin = -delta, ni_pass)
tibble(estimate = est, low = ci[1], high = ci[2]) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = estimate, y = 1)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = low, xmax = high), height = 0.1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -delta, colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(x = "Mean difference (new - comparator)", y = NULL) +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_blank())

heading: 17.335 5. Concluding statement
text:
new treatment mean difference round(est, 2) versus
comparator (95% CI: round(ci[1], 2) round(ci[2], 2)).
pre-specified non-inferiority margin Δ = 3, new
treatment (ni_pass) "met" else "meet" 
non-inferiority criterion lower bound
(ni_pass) "exceeded" else "fell " −Δ.

heading: 17.343 Setup

library(tidyverse)

heading: 17.344 1. Hypothesis

drug diet affect continuous biomarker, 
combination may non-additive effect (interaction).

heading: 17.345 2. Visualise
code:
fact <- expand_grid(
  drug = c("no", "yes"),
  diet = c("standard", "modified"),
  rep  = seq_len(n_per_cell)
) |>
  mutate(
    y = 10 +
      (drug == "yes")  * 1.5 +
      (diet == "modified") * 1.0 +

  )

fact |>
  ggplot(aes(diet, y, fill = drug)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, colour = "grey30") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Biomarker", fill = "Drug")

Independent observations within cell (every replicate 
separate biological unit), approximately normal residuals, equal
variance across cells. pseudoreplication demonstration 
deliberately break independence assumption.
code:

  summarise(mean = mean(y), sd = sd(y), n = n(), .groups = "drop")

text:
Now pseudoreplication error. Suppose biological replicate 
measured three times (technical replicates) naively treated
3 × rows independent.point estimate barely moves; standard error bad

  slice(rep(seq_len(n()), each = 3)) |>
  mutate(tech_noise = rnorm(n(), 0, 0.3),
         y = y + tech_noise)

fit_bad  <- lm(y ~ drug * diet, data = pseudo)
fit_good <- lm(y ~ drug * diet, data = fact)  # aggregated (original)

bind_rows(
  bad  = broom::tidy(fit_bad)  |> filter(term == "drugyes:dietmodified"),
  good = broom::tidy(fit_good) |> filter(term == "drugyes:dietmodified"),
  .id = "analysis"
)

heading: 17.348 5. Concluding statement

simulated 2 × 2 factorial experiment (10 biological
replicates per cell), evidence interaction 
drug diet biomarker (F p ANOVA table
). Naive analysis technical replicates independent
observations shrinks standard error interaction
estimate without changing point estimate — textbook
pseudoreplication artefact.

heading: 17.368 Background
text:
Traditional regression adjustment fails time-varying confounders

respects temporal order. IV methods exploit variable

heading: 17.369 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 17.370 1. Goal
text:
Simulate time-varying-confounding scenario, estimate causal effect
naively IPTW (marginal structural model), finish
simple HTE estimate.

heading: 17.371 2. Approach
text:
true effect treatment positive additive. l1 
time-varying confounder affected a0 predicts
a1 y.
code:
df <- tibble(
  l0 = rnorm(n),
  a0 = rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.2 * l0)),
  l1 = rnorm(n, mean = 0.5 * a0 + 0.3 * l0),
  a1 = rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.3 * l1 + 0.2 * a0)),
  y  = 1.0 * a0 + 0.8 * a1 + 0.5 * l0 + 0.5 * l1 + rnorm(n)
)

heading: 17.372 3. Execution
text: Naive regression adjusting l1 blocks part a0 effect.IPTW stabilised weights:
code:
tidy(naive, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term %in% c("a0", "a1"))
den0 <- glm(a0 ~ l0, data = df, family = binomial)
num1 <- glm(a1 ~ a0, data = df, family = binomial)
den1 <- glm(a1 ~ a0 + l0 + l1, data = df, family = binomial)

w0 <- ifelse(df$a0 == 1, fitted(num0), 1 - fitted(num0)) /
      ifelse(df$a0 == 1, fitted(den0), 1 - fitted(den0))
w1 <- ifelse(df$a1 == 1, fitted(num1), 1 - fitted(num1)) /
      ifelse(df$a1 == 1, fitted(den1), 1 - fitted(den1))
df$sw <- w0 * w1

msm <- lm(y ~ a0 + a1, data = df, weights = sw)
tidy(msm, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term %in% c("a0", "a1"))

heading: 17.373 4. Check
text:
Balance weight sanity:Stabilised weights centre 1 extreme tail. 
, term treatment model likely misspecified.

heading: 17.374 5. Report
text:
simulated cohort time-varying confounding (n = 2000),
naive regression underestimated effect initial treatment. 
inverse-probability-weighted marginal structural model recovered
effects close true data-generating values. Instrumental
variable, difference--differences, regression-discontinuity
designs exploit alternative identification strategies, 
assumptions. Heterogeneity treatment effect can 
explored causal forests meta-learners.

handles larger complex random structures including

heading: 17.382 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(lme4)
library(glmmTMB)
library(geepack)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 17.383 1. Hypothesis
text:
Treatment reduces probability repeated binary event 
clustered study (clusters = clinics). true log-odds fixed
effect −0.5.

heading: 17.384 2. Visualise
code:
nj <- 20
dat <- tibble(
  clinic = factor(rep(seq_len(J), each = nj)),
  trt    = rbinom(J * nj, 1, 0.5),
  u      = rep(rnorm(J, 0, 0.8), each = nj)
) |>
  mutate(lp = -0.5 * trt + u,
         y  = rbinom(n(), 1, plogis(lp)))

dat |>
  group_by(clinic, trt) |>
  summarise(p = mean(y), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(factor(trt), p)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(x = "Treatment", y = "Event probability per clinic")

heading: 17.385 3. Assumptions
text:
GLMM: normal random intercepts clinic, correct link.
GEE: correct mean structure; standard errors robust working
correlation.

heading: 17.386 4. Conduct
code:
data = dat, family = binomial)
fit_tmb   <- glmmTMB(y ~ trt + (1 | clinic),
                     data = dat, family = binomial)
fit_gee   <- geeglm(y ~ trt, id = clinic, data = dat,
                    family = binomial, corstr = "exchangeable")

summary(fit_glmer)$coefficients
summary(fit_tmb)$coefficients$cond
summary(fit_gee)$coefficients  glmer   = broom.mixed::tidy(fit_glmer, effects = "fixed"),
  glmmTMB = broom.mixed::tidy(fit_tmb,   effects = "fixed"),
  gee     = broom::tidy(fit_gee),
  .id = "model"
) |>
  filter(term == "trt") |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error)

heading: 17.387 5. Concluding statement
text:
GLMM gave subject-specific treatment log-odds 
round(fixef(fit_glmer)["trt"], 2); GEE gave population-
averaged log-odds 
round(coef(fit_gee)["trt"], 2). GEE estimate typically
attenuated toward zero relative GLMM; correct
answers different questions.

text:
Comparing GLMM GEE coefficients estimate 
thing.Using working independence structure GEE clusters 
large treatment heterogeneous within .Ignoring overdispersion count GLMM assuming Poisson 
negative binomial fits better.Fitting GLMM clusters (< 10) trusting 
variance component.

heading: 17.395 Setup

library(tidyverse)

library(lmerTest)
set.seed(42)

heading: 17.396 1. Hypothesis
text:
sleepstudy data, reaction time increases days 
sleep deprivation, rate increase varies subject.

heading: 17.397 2. Visualise
code:
sleepstudy |>
  ggplot(aes(Days, Reaction, group = Subject)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_smooth(aes(group = 1), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(x = "Days of deprivation", y = "Reaction time (ms)")

heading: 17.398 3. Assumptions
17.399 4. Conduct
code:
data = sleepstudy, REML = TRUE)
rs <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject),
           data = sleepstudy, REML = TRUE)
summary(rs)
# but we use REFIT = TRUE in anova() to get correct comparison.
anova(ri, rs, refit = FALSE)
fm_ml <- lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject),
              data = sleepstudy, REML = FALSE)
fm_ml
sleepstudy |>
  mutate(fit_rs = predict(rs)) |>
  ggplot(aes(Days, Reaction, group = Subject)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_line(aes(y = fit_rs), colour = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~ Subject, ncol = 6) +
  theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 7))

heading: 17.410 2. Visualise
code:
df <- tibble(
  x = rnorm(n, 50, 10),
  y = 2 + 0.5 * x + rnorm(n, 0, 5)
)

mcar <- df |> mutate(y_obs = if_else(runif(n) < 0.30, NA_real_, y),
                     mech  = "MCAR")
mar  <- df |> mutate(p = plogis(-2 + 0.05 * (x - 50)),
                     y_obs = if_else(runif(n) < p, NA_real_, y),
                     mech  = "MAR")
mnar <- df |> mutate(p = plogis(-2 + 0.05 * (y - mean(y))),
                     y_obs = if_else(runif(n) < p, NA_real_, y),
                     mech  = "MNAR")

all <- bind_rows(
  mcar |> select(mech, x, y, y_obs),
  mar  |> select(mech, x, y, y_obs),
  mnar |> select(mech, x, y, y_obs)
)

all |>
  mutate(missing = is.na(y_obs)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x, y, colour = missing)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +

heading: 17.434 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 17.435 1. Goal
text:
Simulate source population, carve cohort study nested
case-control study , compare exposure–outcome
association recovered .

heading: 17.436 2. Approach
text:
generate population 5000 people binary exposure 
binary outcome whose probability depends exposure confounder
(age). () analyse full cohort (b) draw 
case-control sample four controls per case.
code:
pop <- tibble(
  id       = seq_len(n_pop),
  age      = rnorm(n_pop, 55, 10),
  exposure = rbinom(n_pop, 1, 0.30)
)
# outcome depends on exposure (log OR ~ 0.8) and age
lp <- -4 + 0.8 * pop$exposure + 0.04 * (pop$age - 55)
pop <- pop |>
  mutate(prob = plogis(lp),
         outcome = rbinom(n_pop, 1, prob))
pop |>
  count(exposure, outcome) |>
  mutate(exposure = factor(exposure, labels = c("unexposed", "exposed")),
         outcome  = factor(outcome,  labels = c("no", "yes"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(exposure, n, fill = outcome)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Count", fill = "Outcome")

heading: 17.437 3. Execution
code:
fit_cohort <- glm(outcome ~ exposure + age, data = pop, family = binomial)
coef_cohort <- broom::tidy(fit_cohort, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE)
coef_cohort
cases    <- pop |> filter(outcome == 1)
controls <- pop |>
  filter(outcome == 0) |>
  slice_sample(n = 4 * nrow(cases))
ncc <- bind_rows(cases, controls)
fit_ncc <- glm(outcome ~ exposure + age, data = ncc, family = binomial)
coef_ncc <- broom::tidy(fit_ncc, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE)
coef_ncc

cohort, outcome uncommon. Precision
worse case-control sample point estimate

associated outcome adjusted odds ratio 
round(coef_cohort$estimate[2], 2) (95% CI:
round(coef_cohort$conf.low[2], 2) 
round(coef_cohort$conf.high[2], 2)) full-cohort analysis,
round(coef_ncc$estimate[2], 2) (95% CI:
round(coef_ncc$conf.low[2], 2)

pre-registration timestamped public record study’s

heading: 17.447 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 17.448 1. Goal
text:
Produce minimal--complete pre-registration template 
fictional two-arm randomised trial, plus SAP skeleton.

heading: 17.449 2. Approach
text:
pre-registration four mandatory elements:Question hypotheses — exact form H₀ H₁ 
primary outcome.Design — eligibility, allocation, blinding, intervention,
follow-.Primary analysis — single specification whose p-value
quoted headline result.Sample size justification — calculation behind target N.SAP adds:Secondary analyses — ordered pre-specified.Handling missing data — mechanism assumed, method used.Subgroup analyses — pre-specified, limited number.Sensitivity analyses — especially primary outcomes.

heading: 17.450 3. Execution — template
code:
# Pre-registration — Trial X

Protocol version 1.0 | Date 2026-04-18 | PI [NAME]

## 1. Research question
Primary: Does [intervention] reduce [outcome] relative to [control]
in [population] over [time frame]?

## 2. Hypotheses
H0: difference in [outcome] = 0.
H1: difference in [outcome] != 0. Two-sided, alpha = 0.05.

## 3. Design
Two-arm, parallel-group, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial.
1:1 allocation, block randomisation (block size 4), stratified by site.

## 4. Primary analysis
ITT. Linear regression of outcome at follow-up on arm, adjusted for
baseline value and stratification factors. Primary estimand:
adjusted mean difference with 95% CI.

## 5. Sample size
Target n = 200 per arm; power = 0.80 for d = 0.3, alpha = 0.05.

## 6. Data handling
- Missing data: multiple imputation under MAR (m = 20).
- Outliers: retained in primary analysis.
- Adherence: per-protocol analysis as sensitivity.

heading: 17.451 4. Check
text:
Three questions ask pre-registration posting:reader reconstruct primary analysis text
without contacting ?exploratory analyses labelled ?target N justified calculation reader can redo?

heading: 17.452 5. Report
text:
pre-registration Trial X deposited OSF 
[date] (DOI [doi]). primary analysis, statistical model, 
sample-size justification specified therein. deviation 
pre-registered plan reported Deviations section 
manuscript rationale.

heading: 17.453 Common pitfalls
text:
Treating pre-registration checkbox without concrete analysis
plan.Pre-registering two primary outcomes quoting winner.Failing report deviations plan.

heading: 17.460 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(MatchIt)
library(cobalt)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 17.461 1. Hypothesis
text:
Treatment reduces outcome known amount. estimate
effect naive regression, matching, IPTW, compare
simulated truth.

heading: 17.462 2. Visualise
code:
dat <- tibble(
  age  = rnorm(n, 60, 10),
  sev  = rnorm(n, 0,   1),
  sex  = rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)
) |>
  mutate(ps = plogis(-1 + 0.04 * (age - 60) + 0.8 * sev + 0.3 * sex),
         trt = rbinom(n, 1, ps),
         y   = 2 - 1.5 * trt + 0.05 * (age - 60) +
                 0.8 * sev + 0.3 * sex + rnorm(n, 0, 1))

ggplot(dat, aes(ps, fill = factor(trt))) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(x = "Propensity score", y = "Density", fill = "Treated?")

heading: 17.463 3. Assumptions
text:
unmeasured confounding (conditional exchangeability); positivity
(everyone non-zero chance treatment); correct
specification propensity model.

heading: 17.464 4. Conduct
code:
fit_naive <- lm(y ~ trt + age + sev + sex, data = dat)
coef(fit_naive)["trt"]
m <- matchit(trt ~ age + sev + sex, data = dat,
             method = "nearest", ratio = 1)
m
matched <- match.data(m)

fit_match <- lm(y ~ trt, data = matched,
                weights = matched$weights)
coef(fit_match)["trt"]
dat <- dat |>
  mutate(w = if_else(trt == 1, 1 / ps, 1 / (1 - ps)))
fit_iptw <- lm(y ~ trt, data = dat, weights = w)
coef(fit_iptw)["trt"]
love.plot(m, thresholds = c(m = 0.1), abs = TRUE)

heading: 17.465 5. Concluding statement
text:
simulated treatment effect −1.5. naive regression
gave round(coef(fit_naive)["trt"], 2); nearest-neighbour
matching gave round(coef(fit_match)["trt"], 2); IPTW gave
round(coef(fit_iptw)["trt"], 2). Balance plots showed 
matching reduced SMDs 0.1.

library(tidyverse)

heading: 17.475 2. Visualise

trial <- tibble(
  id    = seq_len(n),
  arm   = sample(rep(c("placebo", "treatment"), each = n / 2)),
  # 15% of treatment arm never take the drug (will be placebo in reality)
  crossed = if_else(arm == "treatment" & runif(n) < 0.15, TRUE, FALSE),
  received = if_else(crossed, "placebo", arm),
  y = rnorm(n, mean = 50, sd = 8) +
      if_else(received == "treatment", -5, 0)
)

trial |>
  ggplot(aes(arm, y, fill = arm)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, colour = "grey30") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Symptom score") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

itt
pp

heading: 17.486 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(deSolve)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 17.487 1. Goal
text:
Simulate SIR outbreak population 1 million, R₀ values
1.5, 2.5, 4.

heading: 17.488 2. Approach
17.489 3. Execution
code:
params <- c(beta = R0 * gamma, gamma = gamma)
  state  <- c(S = N - I0, I = I0, R = 0)
  times  <- seq(0, days, by = 1)
  out <- as.data.frame(ode(state, times, sir, params))
  out$R0 <- R0
  out
}

trajectories <- bind_rows(
  run_sir(1.5), run_sir(2.5), run_sir(4.0)
) |>
  pivot_longer(c(S, I, R), names_to = "compartment", values_to = "n")
ggplot(trajectories, aes(time, n, colour = compartment)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ R0, labeller = labeller(R0 = \(x) paste0("R0 = ", x))) +
  labs(x = "Day", y = "People")

heading: 17.490 4. Check
text: Peak prevalence attack rate rise R₀.
code:
filter(compartment == "I") |>
  group_by(R0) |>
  summarise(peak_I = max(n), peak_day = time[which.max(n)]) |>
  arrange(R0)

heading: 17.491 5. Report
text:
SIR compartmental model simulated population one
million 7-day mean infectious period across three basic
reproduction numbers (R₀ = 1.5, 2.5, 4). Peak prevalence 
final attack rate increased sharply R₀, illustrating small
changes transmission translate large changes epidemic
size. Calibrated analogues model underpin much real-world
outbreak forecasting.

heading: 17.492 Common pitfalls
text:
Interpreting SIR trajectories forecasts rather scenarios.Forgetting constant β across time ignores interventions.Comparing peak prevalence across parameter settings without holding
N ₀ constant.

heading: 17.493 Further reading
text:
Keeling MJ, Rohani P. Modeling Infectious Diseases Humans 
Animals.Soetaert K et al. Solving Differential Equations R.

heading: 18 Reporting Templates
text: Copy-paste Methods + Results paragraphs, organised reporting guideline. Pages deliberately formulaic: fill bracketed italics numbers, replace placeholder package versions actually used, check Reviewer checklist link method confirm reported every diagnostic reviewer expects see.Templates organised guideline:STROBE — observational studies — 186 method templatesCONSORT — randomised trials — 141 method templatesTRIPOD-AI — prediction models — 163 method templatesPRISMA — systematic reviews & meta-analyses — 25 method templatesARRIVE — animal research — 50 method templatesEvery page currently skeleton: placeholders, narrative text. prose written hand slots marked <!-- TODO:reporting-template -->.

heading: 23.1 Learning objectives
text:
Distinguish explanatory predictive modelling, choose 
correct evaluation metric .Map STROBE, TRIPOD, STARD, CONSORT onto study designs 
meant .Produce publication-ready regression table gtsummary whose
numbers trace directly fitted model.

heading: 23.2 Prerequisites
text: regression tools covered earlier book.

heading: 23.3 Background
text:
Breiman’s 1998 essay Two Cultures Shmueli’s 2010 essay 
Explain Predict? draw line opposite sides. 
explanatory modelling goal inference — estimated
effect X Y, adjusted confounders? — right evaluation
metric bias-unbiasedness, interval coverage, interpretability.
predictive modelling goal accuracy unseen data, 
right evaluation metric --sample loss, calibration, 
decision utility. statistical machinery overlaps habits
around ; regression makes excellent explanation often
makes lacklustre prediction, vice versa.Reporting guidelines mechanism field enforces
discipline around two cultures. STROBE observational studies,
CONSORT randomised trials, STARD diagnostic accuracy, 
TRIPOD (now TRIPOD-AI) prediction models specify items 
reader needs evaluate claim. checklist filled write
much easier one filled submission.

heading: 23.4 Setup
code:
library(tidyverse)
library(gtsummary)
library(broom)
library(palmerpenguins)
set.seed(42)
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

heading: 23.5 1. Goal
text:
Fit single linear model penguins data present two ways:
explanatory analysis (drives body mass?) 
predictive model (can predict body mass held-birds?).

heading: 23.6 2. Approach
code: fit_expl <- lm(body_mass_g ~ flipper_length_mm + sex + species, data = peng)

heading: 23.7 3. Execution
code:
peng |>
  ggplot(aes(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, colour = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(x = "Flipper length (mm)", y = "Body mass (g)")

heading: 23.8 4. Check
text:
Explanatory evaluation: effect sizes intervals, residual
diagnostics, adjusted R².Predictive evaluation: 5-fold split, hand-coded keep example
transparent.
code:
folds <- sample(rep(seq_len(k), length.out = nrow(peng)))
rmse_k <- sapply(seq_len(k), function(i) {
  tr <- peng[folds != i, ]
  te <- peng[folds == i, ]
  f  <- lm(body_mass_g ~ flipper_length_mm + sex + species, data = tr)
  sqrt(mean((predict(f, te) - te$body_mass_g)^2))
})
mean_rmse <- mean(rmse_k)
mean_rmse

heading: 23.9 5. Report
text:
Palmer penguins dataset (n = nrow(peng)), body mass 
associated flipper length, sex, species (adjusted R² =
round(summary(fit_expl)$adj.r.squared, 2)). --sample
performance, estimated 5-fold cross-validation, RMSE =
round(mean_rmse, 0) g.
code:
tbl_regression(fit_expl, intercept = TRUE) |>
  modify_caption("**Table 1. Linear-regression estimates for body mass (g).**")

heading: 23.10 Common pitfalls
text:
Choosing predictor set R² reporting predictive claim, 
tuning predictive model reporting causal-sounding coefficients.Filling reporting checklist submission rather 
drafting.Assuming -sample R² generalises.

heading: 23.11 Further reading
text: Breiman L. (2001). Statistical Modeling: Two Cultures.Shmueli G. (2010). Explain Predict? Statistical Science.Writing report.

heading: 24 The research workflow
text:
Nine stages research question knowledge survives
replication.

heading: 24.1 1. Question
text:
well-formed research question population, exposure 
intervention, comparator, outcome, time frame. PICO
framework evidence-based medicine useful checklist.
vague question leads vague design vague answer; 
well-formed question survives peer review single datum 
collected.
Covered : Scientific process research workflow,
Systematic reviews prisma.

heading: 24.2 2. Measurements
text:
Every question depends measurement scale choose answer .
Accuracy, precision, reliability, difference measurement
proxy vocabulary.
Covered : Data types tidy data accuracy precision,
Kappa icc bland altman.

heading: 24.3 3. Design
text:
Design choose data can tell . 
well-designed observational study answers questions better 
badly designed RCT; good RCT answers .
Covered : Observational designs strobe.

heading: 24.4 4. Acquisition
text:
Getting data computer faithfully statistical
problem: missingness, measurement error, batch effects start .
Covered : Import joins missingness dplyr,
Mcar mar mnar.

heading: 24.5 5. Description
text:
inference, look data. well-designed plot often
whole answer.
Covered : Ggplot2 grammar multi panel layouts,
Descriptive statistics table 1.

heading: 24.6 6. Analysis
text:
formal model takes data returns estimate, 
interval, decision.
Covered : Courses 1–4, throughout.

heading: 24.7 7. Interpretation / prediction
text:
estimate mean scale science? Effect size,
clinical significance, distinction average 
individual prediction live .
Covered : Calibration discrimination roc auc brier score,
Time dependent brier ipa external validation.

heading: 24.8 8. Validation
text:
finding hold data used find ? External validation,
nested CV, replication pool .
Covered : Cross validation nested cv bootstrap 632,
Time dependent brier ipa external validation.

heading: 24.9 9. Knowledge / decisions
text:
act writing others can trust, cite, build 
finding.
Covered : Writing report,
Explanation vs prediction reporting.APPENDIX · GLOSSARY24 research workflow24.1 1. Question24.2 2. Measurements24.3 3. Design24.4 4. Acquisition24.5 5. Description24.6 6. Analysis24.7 7. Interpretation / prediction24.8 8. Validation24.9 9. Knowledge / decisionsView source Edit page

heading: 25 Glossary
text: Plain-English definitions terms curriculum uses .

heading: 25.1 A
text: α (alpha). probability type error — rejecting true null hypothesis. Conventional values 0.05 0.01. Hypothesis testing p values type ii errors.ANCOVA. Analysis covariance: linear model combining categorical predictors continuous covariates, commonly used adjust RCT analyses baseline. Ancova rcts baseline adjustment.ANOVA. Analysis variance: linear model categorical predictors. One way anova contrasts emmeans.Assumption. condition chosen test needs interpretable — normality, equal variance, independence, linearity. Two sample paired t tests.

heading: 25.2 B
text: Bias. Systematic deviation estimator quantity estimated. Populations samples central limit theorem.Bootstrap. Resampling replacement approximate sampling distribution statistic. Bootstrap permutation tests.Brier score. Mean squared error predicted probabilities observed outcomes. Calibration discrimination roc auc brier score.

heading: 25.3 C
text: CI (confidence interval). range plausible values parameter; repeated sampling, 95% CIs cover true value 95% time. Bootstrap permutation tests.Cohen’s d. Standardised difference means; common effect size two-group comparisons. Two sample paired t tests.Competing risk. event whose occurrence precludes alters probability event interest. Competing risks multistate models.Confounding. third variable distorting association exposure outcome. Multiple regression confounding interaction centring.Cox model. proportional-hazards regression time--event outcomes. Survival primer km log rank cox ph.CV (cross-validation). Splitting data folds estimate generalisation error. Cross validation nested cv bootstrap 632.

heading: 25.4 D
text: DAG (directed acyclic graph). graphical representation causal assumptions. Dags dagitty ggdag.

heading: 25.5 E
text: Effect size. standardised measure magnitude effect, independent sample size. Two sample paired t tests.

heading: 25.6 F

heading: 26 Common errors
text:
field guide R, Quarto, renv errors bite 
first.

heading: 26.1 R
text:
Error:
package called 'xyz'Cause: package installed library R looking .Fix: install.packages("xyz"). using renv, run
renv::restore() instead keep lock file authoritative.Error:
find function "ggplot"Cause: package installed loaded.Fix: library(ggplot2) (library(tidyverse)).Error:
non-finite value supplied optimCause: model failed converge, often extreme starting
values, perfect separation (logistic regression), misspecified
formula.Fix: rescale predictors, use sensible starting values, logistic
regression check zero cells outcome--predictor table.Error:
contrasts can applied factors 2 levelsCause: factor predictor filtered single level.Fix: check table(your_data$predictor) restore levels, drop
predictor model.Error:
Error UseMethod("predict") : applicable method 'predict' applied object class "c('list')"Cause: passed wrong object predict(). Usually list
instead fitted model.Fix: check object class() pass model object ,
e.g. predict(fit, newdata = nd).Error:
Loading required package: foo — package called 'foo'Cause: installed package dependency missing.Fix: install.packages("foo", dependencies = TRUE).

heading: 26.2 Quarto
text:
Error:
quarto: command foundCause: Quarto PATH.Fix: install https://quarto.org restart terminal.
macOS, check /usr/local/bin (Homebrew prefix) 
PATH.Error:
Failed render: system missing pandocCause: older versions R Quarto missed bundled pandoc.Fix: upgrade Quarto latest release.Error:
Error running filter shortcodesCause: Quarto extension missing cloning.Fix: run quarto add extension, ignore extension 
optional.Error:
output file: docs/index.html existCause: quarto render interrupted output-dir changed.Fix: re-run quarto render clean state; interruption
wedged .quarto/, delete cache directory try .Error:
GitHub Pages deployment failed: 404Cause: Pages pointing wrong branch path.Fix: repo Settings → Pages, select gh-pages branch 
/ (root) folder. workflow deploys gh-pages default.

heading: 26.3 renv
text:
Error:
renv::restore() failed : install package 'xyz' sourceCause: system library missing. error line usually
names header file.Fix: install matching -dev package Linux, corresponding
Homebrew package macOS, Rtools Windows. Common culprits:libcurl4-openssl-dev curllibxml2-dev XML / xml2libssl-dev openssllibfontconfig1-dev, libfreetype6-dev, libharfbuzz-dev, libfribidi-dev systemfonts, ragg, textshapinglibgit2-dev gertError:
renv sync lockfileCause: project library differs renv.lock.Fix: renv::status() see , renv::restore() synchronise.

heading: 26.4 knitr / rmarkdown
text:
Error:
pandoc document conversion failed error 2Cause: YAML front matter malformed — often unquoted colon 
title.Fix: quote offending string. title: "Two:peas pod".Error:
Error chunkname: object 'xyz' foundCause: earlier chunk skipped cached stale.Fix: clear knitr cache (knitr::clean_cache()) re-render;
check chunk evaluation eval = TRUE.

initialisation.Fix: run git pull --allow-unrelated-histories sure
want merge.Error:
Permission denied (publickey)Cause: SSH key registered GitHub.Fix: add key via Settings → SSH GPG keys, switch
