heading: 27 Writing a report
text:
turn analysis document reviewer, PI, future
version trust.

heading: 27.1 IMRaD
text:
Biomedical writing follows durable convention: Introduction, Methods,
Results, Discussion. structure aesthetic choice — 
mirrors order reader can evaluate work. 
motivate question, describe exactly , present 
findings, interpret . Pre-registration statistical
analysis plans make Methods section possible write 
Results exist, five-step template taught throughout site
feeds directly Methods paragraph every inferential test.

heading: 27.2 Effect sizes and intervals
text:
Every result worth reporting reported point estimate 
interval. p-value alone rarely useful: tells whether 
data surprising null, large effect 
much uncertainty magnitude. concluding
statement embarrass five years reads like :Mean arterial pressure lower treatment group 
placebo group 6.4 mmHg (95% CI: 2.1 10.7; p = 0.004;
Cohen’s d = 0.42).Notice three things: direction magnitude effect, 
interval, — optionally — standardised effect size. Nothing else
needed reader think .

heading: 27.3 Figures
text:
Two rules: one figure, one idea, figure without caption
stand alone. caption tells reader figure
shows (“Forest plot odds ratios arm”), units
, many observations contribute, — anything inferential —
error bars bands represent (95% CI? SD? SE mean?).
Use theme_minimal() site accent palette colour 
discretionary, avoid colour-encoding: pair colour shape 
linetype colourblind readers can follow along.

heading: 27.4 Tables
text:
Tables rarely best way communicate result plot can
show, results genuinely need — baseline characteristics,
multi-row regression tables, multiple-imputation pooling summaries. Use
gtsummary three. point table contrast 
rows, put thing want reader compare next 
round numbers sensible precision (three significant figures 
usually plenty; two proportions).

heading: 27.5 Citing R and packages
text:
R packages freely maintained volunteers deserve
citations. foot every report, include something like:Analysis performed R 4.4.1 (R Core Team 2024) using 
tidyverse (Wickham et al. 2019), lme4 (Bates et al. 2015), 
rstanarm (Goodrich et al. 2024) packages.can get correct citation package 
citation("pkgname"). bibliography returns already BibTeX
form.

heading: 27.6 Reporting guidelines
text:
Every substantive biomedical study reporting guideline. Use ;
reviewers use whether .Randomised controlled trial: CONSORT.Observational study: STROBE.Diagnostic-accuracy study: STARD.Prediction-model study: TRIPOD TRIPOD-AI.Systematic review: PRISMA.Animal research: ARRIVE.checklist filled write much easier one filled 
end. Many journals now require signed checklist supplementary
file; bookmark now.

heading: 27.7 Reproducibility
text:
document submit come single render single
source. numbers text numbers 
sessionInfo() block foot file, analysis 
reproducible. Quarto, renv, version-controlled repository
together enough get right; targets package 
next step anything large enough need dependency graph.

heading: 28 Cheatsheets
text: One-page topical summaries, aggregated printable reference. self-contained reminder syntax, assumptions, diagnostics go methods covers.

heading: 28.1.1 Toolchain, tidy data, and ggplot2## The research workflow
text: Question → Measurements → Design → Acquisition → Description → Analysis → Interpretation → Validation → KnowledgeBiological vs statistical significance: “p < 0.05” ≠ “effect matters”.

heading: 28.3 Data types and scales
text: Accuracy ≠ precision. Bias shifts target; variance spreads shots.

heading: 28.4 Tidy data
text: One variable per column.One observation per row.One observational unit per table.

heading: 28.5 dplyr verbs
text: Joins: left_join, inner_join, anti_join key.Missingness: .na(), drop_na(), tidyr::complete().
code:
df |>
  filter(age >= 18) |>
  select(id, age, sbp) |>
  mutate(sbp_z = (sbp - mean(sbp)) / sd(sbp)) |>
  group_by(arm) |>
  summarise(mean_sbp = mean(sbp, na.rm = TRUE), n = n())

heading: 28.6 ggplot2 grammar
text: Layer order: data → aes → geom → facet → scales → labels → theme.
code:
ggplot(data, aes(x, y, colour = group)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ factor) +
  labs(x = "x label", y = "y label") +
  theme_minimal()

heading: 28.7 Plot families
text: One variable continuous → histogram, density.Two continuous → scatter, sometimes binned / smoothed.One categorical + one continuous → boxplot, violin, strip.Two categorical → bar fill, mosaic.

heading: 28.8 Decision rule
text: read data tibble → fix data entry first.Tibble reads missing mixed types → clean modelling.Plot ugly cluttered → trust plot, summary statistic.

heading: 28.9 Common pitfalls
text: Running statistics untyped / partially-missing data.Adjusting colour scales instead simplifying chart.Reporting mean + SD skewed variable.Forgetting set.seed() simulation chunk.

heading: 28.10 Further reading
text: Wickham, R Data Science (2e).Posit ggplot2 dplyr cheatsheets.

heading: 28.10.1 Descriptive statistics, Bayes, and distributions## Descriptive statistics
28.11 Contingency tables
code:
table(df$x, df$y)
prop.table(table(df$x, df$y), margin = 1)

heading: 28.12 Bayes’ theorem
text: \(P(\mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid ) P()}{P(B)}\)odds form: posterior odds = prior odds × likelihood ratio.

heading: 28.13 Diagnostic testing
text: PPV collapses low prevalence — even 95%-accurate test mostly false positives.

heading: 28.14 Discrete distributions
text: Binomial → Poisson n grows p shrinks np = λ.

heading: 28.15 Continuous distributions
text: R uses d / p / q / r prefix: density, CDF, quantile, random.

heading: 28.16 Q-Q plot
text: S-shape → heavy tails. U-shape → skew. Plot test.
code: ggplot(df, aes(sample = x)) + stat_qq() + stat_qq_line()

heading: 28.17 Decision rule
text: Reporting mean? First check histogram.Testing proportion? First sketch 2×2 table.Choosing distribution? Simulate believing.

heading: 28.18 Common pitfalls
text: Quoting PPV without disclosing prevalence.Assuming normality n > 30.Using mean ± SD skewed data.

heading: 28.19 Further reading
text: Altman, Practical Statistics Medical Research.Gelman et al., Bayesian Data Analysis, ch. 1.

heading: 28.19.1 Sampling, estimation, and one-sample tests## Populations vs samples
text: Parameter = truth population (\(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(\pi\)).Estimator = statistic computed sample (\(\bar x\), \(s\), \(\hat p\)).Bias = \(E[\hat\theta] - \theta\). MSE = bias² + variance.

heading: 28.20 Central limit theorem
text:
iid samples finite variance, \(n \\infty\):
\(\bar X \sim N\!\left(\mu, \sigma / \sqrt n\right)\)Holds roughly \(n \approx 30\) non-pathological distributions.

heading: 28.21 Standard error
text: \(\mathrm{SE}(\bar X) = \sigma / \sqrt n\), estimated \(s / \sqrt n\).

heading: 28.22 Bootstrap
code:
B <- 2000
boot_mean <- replicate(B, mean(sample(x, replace = TRUE)))
quantile(boot_mean, c(0.025, 0.975))   # 95% percentile CI
sd(boot_mean)                           # bootstrap SE

heading: 28.23 Permutation test
code:
obs  <- mean(a) - mean(b)
pool <- c(a, b)
null <- replicate(5000, {
  idx <- sample(seq_along(pool), length(a))
  mean(pool[idx]) - mean(pool[-idx])
})
mean(abs(null) >= abs(obs))   # two-sided p

heading: 28.24 Maximum likelihood
text: Given data \(x\) model \(f(x; \theta)\):\(\ell(\theta) = \sum \log f(x_i; \theta)\).\(\hat\theta_{\text{MLE}} = \arg\max_\theta \ell(\theta)\).Fisher information \((\theta)\); asymptotic SE = \(1/\sqrt{(\hat\theta)}\).

heading: 28.25 One-sample tests (five-step template)
text: Hypothesis → Visualise → Assumptions → Conduct → Conclude.

heading: 28.26 Hypothesis-testing vocabulary
text: Type : reject true H₀ (probability \(\alpha\)).Type II: accept false H₀ (probability \(\beta\)).Power = \(1 - \beta\).p-value probability H₀ true.

heading: 28.27 Decision rule
text: Want CI? Bootstrap first, ask whether formula applies.Want test? State H₀ α writing running .Want know n big enough? Simulate CLT outcome.

heading: 28.28 Common pitfalls
text: Reporting SE meant SD (vice versa).Using 95% CI “prove” effect.Running many tests quoting smallest p-value.

heading: 28.29 Further reading
text: Harrell, Biostatistics Biomedical Research, ch. 3–5.Efron & Tibshirani, Introduction Bootstrap.

heading: 28.29.1 Two-group comparisons, effect sizes, and power## Choosing a comparison
28.30 Effect sizes
code: effectsize::cohens_d(y ~ g)

heading: 28.31 Two proportions
text: Report RR () 95% CI, just p-value.
code:
prop.test(c(tA, tB), c(nA, nB))         # asymptotic
fisher.test(matrix(c(tA, nA - tA,
                     tB, nB - tB), 2))  # exact, small cells

heading: 28.32 Correlation
code: cor.test(x, y, method = "spearman")

heading: 28.34 Power and sample size
text: Simulation-based power anything textbooks skip: simr::powerSim.
code:
pwr::pwr.t.test(d = 0.5, power = 0.80, sig.level = 0.05,
                type = "two.sample")

heading: 28.35 Reporting with gtsummary
code:
library(gtsummary)
trial |>
  tbl_summary(by = arm, statistic = list(all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})")) |>
  add_p() |>
  add_overall()

heading: 28.36 Decision rule
text: Ask: one-group, two-group, paired, many-group?Check: normal-ish, use rank-based test?Report: point estimate + 95% CI + effect size; p-value last, first.

heading: 28.37 Common pitfalls
text: Equal-variance t-test (default languages) variances differ.Reporting audience expects RR (vice versa).Forgetting p-value paired test depends pairing.Chaining correlations one “significant”.

heading: 28.38 Further reading
text: Altman, Machin et al., Statistics Confidence.gtsummary docs.

heading: 28.39.1 Linear regression and diagnostics## Correlation vs regression
text: Correlation: symmetric; variables random (Model II).Regression: asymmetric; predictor fixed, outcome random (Model ).Use Model II (e.g. smatr::sma) variables error.

heading: 28.40 Simple linear regression
text: \(y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\)
code:
fit <- lm(y ~ x, data = df)
broom::tidy(fit, conf.int = TRUE)
broom::glance(fit)

heading: 28.41 Multiple regression
code: fit <- lm(y ~ x1 + x2 + x1:x2, data = df)

heading: 28.42 Diagnostics
text: VIF > 5 → investigate collinearity; > 10 → fix .
code:
plot(fit)                   # base: 4 diagnostic plots
performance::check_model(fit)   # tidy overview
car::vif(fit)               # multicollinearity

heading: 28.43 Robust / weighted
code:
MASS::rlm(y ~ x, data = df)          # robust to outliers
lm(y ~ x, weights = 1 / var_i)        # weighted LS

# Heteroscedasticity-consistent SEs
library(sandwich); library(lmtest)
coeftest(fit, vcov = vcovHC(fit, type = "HC3"))

heading: 28.44 Decision rule
text: Residual plot ugly? Transform, add terms, switch link.VIF explodes? Drop one collinear predictors combine .Outlier high Cook’s distance? Investigate deleting.Variance depends X? HC3 SEs weighted LS.

heading: 28.45 Common pitfalls
text: Interpreting \(\beta_1\) “effect X” confounding.Reporting \(R^2\) -sample data generalisable.Including interaction reporting main effects.Centring categorical predictors (don’t — use reference coding).

heading: 28.46 Further reading
text: Harrell, Regression Modeling Strategies, ch. 4–5.Fox, Applied Regression Analysis.

heading: 28.46.1 ANOVA, mixed models, and non-linear regression## One-way ANOVA
text:
ANOVA linear model categorical predictor. F-test
compares -group within-group variance.
code: aov(y ~ group, data = df) |> summary()

heading: 28.47 Contrasts with emmeans
text:
Pre-specify contrasts looking data; correct 
multiplicity.
code:
library(emmeans)
emm <- emmeans(fit, ~ group)
pairs(emm, adjust = "tukey")   # all pairwise
contrast(emm, list(TrtVsCtrl = c(-1, 1, 1, 1) / 3))

heading: 28.48 Two-way / factorial ANOVA
text:
Interaction means “effect differs level B”. Report 
interaction first; main effects conditional.
code:
aov(y ~ A * B, data = df) |> summary()
emmip(fit, A ~ B)   # interaction plot

heading: 28.49 Repeated measures / blocking
text: RCBD: aov(y ~ treatment + Error(block)).Repeated measures: move mixed model.
code:
library(lme4); library(lmerTest)
lmer(y ~ treatment + time + (1 | subject), data = df)

heading: 28.50 GAMs — smooth non-linear terms
text: edf ≈ 1 → nearly linear; > 4 → clearly non-linear.
code:
library(mgcv); library(gratia)
fit <- gam(y ~ s(x, k = 10) + z, data = df)
summary(fit)      # edf tells you how "wiggly"
draw(fit)         # smooth + CI

heading: 28.51 Non-linear regression (nls)
text: Start values matter. fails, plot first guess reasonable starts.
code:
# Michaelis-Menten: y = Vmax * x / (K + x)
fit <- nls(y ~ Vmax * x / (K + x),
           data = df, start = list(Vmax = 1, K = 1))

heading: 28.52 Decision rule
text: Categorical predictor, > 2 levels → ANOVA + contrasts.Factorial design → include interaction, report first.Effect obviously curved → GAM spline; else try nls.Repeated measures → mixed model, repeated-measures ANOVA.

heading: 28.53 Common pitfalls
text: Tukey HSD without pre-specified contrasts interest.ANOVA p < 0.05 reported alone — without naming groups differ.Forcing GAM onto monotonic data nls fits cleanly.Ignoring random effect clustered designs (pseudoreplication).

heading: 28.54 Further reading
text: Wood, Generalized Additive Models, 2e.emmeans vignette.

heading: 28.54.1 Generalised linear models and prediction evaluation## GLM link functions
28.55 Logistic regression
text: Check perfect separation (huge SEs).Interpret ORs cautiously; RR intuitive audience.
code:
fit <- glm(y ~ x1 + x2, data = df, family = binomial)
exp(coef(fit))                        # odds ratios
exp(confint(fit))                     # 95% CI on OR
predict(fit, newdata = nd, type = "response")

heading: 28.56 ANCOVA in an RCT
text:
Adjust baseline; analyse change score.efficient simple t-test change scores baseline 
follow-correlated.
code: lm(y_followup ~ arm + y_baseline, data = trial)

heading: 28.57 Poisson / negative binomial
text:
Check dispersion: sum(residuals(fit, type = "pearson")^2) / df.residual.
> 1.5, switch NB.
code:
glm(cases ~ x + offset(log(person_years)),
    family = poisson, data = df)
MASS::glm.nb(cases ~ x + offset(log(person_years)), data = df)

heading: 28.58 Evaluation — calibration + discrimination
code:
library(pROC)
roc_obj <- roc(y, phat)
auc(roc_obj); ci.auc(roc_obj)
plot(roc_obj)

heading: 28.59 Decision rule
text: Binary outcome → logistic; report + 95% CI.Count outcome → Poisson; check overdispersion; NB needed.Trial analysis → ANCOVA, change score.Prediction model → calibration curve first, ROC second, decision curves third.

heading: 28.60 Common pitfalls
text:
Quoting AUC without calibration (discriminating miscalibrated
model dangerous).Ignoring offsets count data.Using ordinal logit proportional-odds assumption fails.Presenting logistic regression coefficients log-odds scale without .

heading: 28.61 Further reading
text: Harrell, RMS, ch. 10–12.Steyerberg, Clinical Prediction Models.

heading: 28.61.1 Agreement, survival primer, and reporting## Don’t dichotomise
28.62 Change scores vs ANCOVA
text:
Change scores suffer regression mean.ANCOVA (adjust baseline) efficient analysis RCT.observational studies unequal baselines, approaches
answer subtly different questions — state .

heading: 28.63 Agreement & reliability
code:
mean_diff <- mean(y - x)
loa <- mean_diff + c(-1.96, 1.96) * sd(y - x)
ggplot(df, aes((x + y)/2, y - x)) + geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(mean_diff, loa), linetype = 2)

heading: 28.64 Survival primer
text: Report hazard ratio 95% CI.PH violated? → time-varying coefficient stratify.Interpret HR checking non-informative censoring.

heading: 28.65 Decision curves, NRI, IDI
text:
Decision curve: net benefit vs threshold probability — dominates
“treat ” “treat none” useful.NRI: many events move / non-events move risk.IDI: mean change predicted probability class.Report decision curve first; NRI/IDI secondary.

heading: 28.68 Decision rule
text: Continuous predictor + continuous outcome → median splits.Two-group repeated measurement → ANCOVA.New rater / device → Bland-Altman + ICC.Time--event → KM + Cox, always check PH.Prediction model → TRIPOD checklist submission.

heading: 28.69 Common pitfalls
text: Reporting κ near-constant outcomes (high % agreement, low κ).Quoting median survival never reached.Forgetting mention PH assumption checking.Claiming “prediction model” built data used evaluation.

heading: 28.70 Further reading
text: Harrell, BBR, ch. 17–18.Royston & Altman, Prognosis research.

heading: 28.71.1 Study designs and power## Observational designs
text: STROBE checklist: https://www.strobe-statement.org/

heading: 28.72 Trial designs
text: CONSORT RCTs: https://www.consort-statement.org/

heading: 28.73 Bench / translational
text: Blocking reduces nuisance variation (plate, day, operator).Factorial tests interactions efficiently.Split-plot handles two levels randomisation.Pseudoreplication: technical replicates ≠ biological replicates.

heading: 28.74 Power — closed form
text: Effect-size conventions (Cohen): small \(d\) = 0.2, medium 0.5, large 0.8.
code:
library(pwr)
pwr.t.test(d = 0.5, power = 0.80, sig.level = 0.05,
           type = "two.sample")
pwr.2p.test(h = ES.h(0.3, 0.2), power = 0.80)
pwr.r.test(r = 0.3, power = 0.80)
pwr.anova.test(k = 4, f = 0.25, power = 0.80)

heading: 28.75 Power — simulation
text:
Simulation wins design textbook skips: mixed models,
adaptive rules, non-standard outcomes.
code:
library(simr)
# Build a pilot model, increase N, or tweak fixef, then:
ps <- powerSim(model, nsim = 500, test = fixed("arm"))
ps

heading: 28.76 Decision rule
text: Randomise can. , draw DAG name biases.Power calculation protocol freeze, data collection.Cluster randomisation → design effect \(1 + (\bar m - 1)\rho\); inflate N.Bench experiments → treat batch, plate, operator random effects.

heading: 28.77 Common pitfalls
text: Planning cluster RCT without inflating N design effect.Borrowing pilot effect size without acknowledging noise.Running non-inferiority trial superiority.Treating technical replicates biological.

heading: 28.78 Further reading
text: Matthews, Introduction Randomized Controlled Clinical Trials.Chow & Liu, Design Analysis Clinical Trials.

heading: 28.78.1 Missing data, mixed models, and longitudinal## Missingness mechanisms
text: Test impossible without assumptions — reason mechanism.

heading: 28.79 Multiple imputation with mice
text:
Rule thumb: m ≥ 100 × fraction missing information, 20 
fine starting point.
code:
library(mice)
imp <- mice(df, m = 20, seed = 42, printFlag = FALSE)
fit <- with(imp, lm(y ~ x1 + x2))
pooled <- pool(fit)
summary(pooled, conf.int = TRUE)

heading: 28.80 Linear mixed models
text:
Random intercept: cluster baseline.Random slope: cluster trajectory.ICC \(\rho = \sigma_u^2 / (\sigma_u^2 + \sigma_e^2)\).REML variance components; ML likelihood-ratio tests 
fixed effects.
code:
library(lme4); library(lmerTest)
lmer(y ~ treatment * time + (1 | subject), data = df)
lmer(y ~ treatment * time + (time | subject), data = df)  # random slope

heading: 28.81 GLMMs & GEE
text:
GLMM: subject-specific effects, good prediction.GEE: population-average effects, robust working-correlation
misspecification (combined robust SEs).
code:
library(lme4)
glmer(y ~ x + (1 | cluster), data = df, family = binomial)

library(geepack)
geeglm(y ~ x, id = cluster, data = df,
       family = binomial, corstr = "exchangeable")

heading: 28.82 Time series basics
code:
library(forecast)
ts_obj <- ts(x, frequency = 12)
decompose(ts_obj) |> plot()
auto.arima(ts_obj)

library(changepoint)
cpt.mean(x, method = "PELT") |> plot()

heading: 28.83 Decision rule
text:
Missing data > 5% → least sensitivity analysis; prefer MI.Repeated measurements → mixed model, repeated-measures ANOVA.Binary outcome clusters → GLMM (subject effects matter) GEE
(marginal effect estimand).Time series trend / seasonality → decompose modelling.

heading: 28.84 Common pitfalls
text:
Last-observation-carried-forward; usually biased.Imputing transformation rather right scale.Ignoring random effects clusters small number (degrees
freedom correction).Quoting GEE coefficients subject-specific.

heading: 28.85 Further reading
text: van Buuren, Flexible Imputation Missing Data.Fitzmaurice, Laird, Ware, Applied Longitudinal Analysis.

heading: 28.85.1 Causal inference methods## Time-varying covariates / landmarking
text:
Counting-process data: split follow-intervals.Landmark analysis: define landmark time \(t^*\), condition 
surviving \(t^*\), classify status \(t^*\).Immortal-time bias: exposed group can exposed live
long enough exposed → spurious survival benefit.
code:
library(survival)
coxph(Surv(tstart, tstop, event) ~ x, data = long)

heading: 28.86 Competing risks
text:
Cause-specific HR answers “given event-free, 
hazard event k?”Subdistribution HR answers “hazard event k 
whole cohort, treating competing events obstacles?”
code:
library(tidycmprsk); library(ggsurvfit)
cif <- cuminc(Surv(time, event_f) ~ x, data = df)
ggsurvfit::ggcuminc(cif, outcome = "event1")

# Fine-Gray subdistribution hazard for event1
crr(Surv(time, event_f) ~ x, data = df, failcode = "event1")

heading: 28.87 DAGs
text: Adjust confounders, colliders.Adjusting mediator estimates direct effect .
code:
library(dagitty); library(ggdag)
dag <- dagitty("dag { X -> Y ; C -> X ; C -> Y }")
adjustmentSets(dag, exposure = "X", outcome = "Y")
impliedConditionalIndependencies(dag)

heading: 28.88 Propensity scores / IPTW
text:
IPTW: weight \(1/\hat{e}_i\) treated, \(1/(1-\hat{e}_i)\) untreated.
Stabilised weights stay closer 1; trim extremes avoid outliers.
code:
library(MatchIt); library(cobalt)
m <- matchit(treat ~ x1 + x2, data = df, method = "nearest")
love.plot(m)   # check balance: SMD < 0.1 is the goal
fit <- lm(y ~ treat, data = match.data(m))

heading: 28.90 Heterogeneous treatment effects
text:
Causal forest (grf::causal_forest) meta-learners (S-, T-, X-).Report conditional ATEs adjusted intervals; avoid spurious
subgroup p-values.

heading: 28.91 Decision rule
text:
Treatment assigned time → time-varying Cox.Multiple competing events → cumulative incidence, Fine-Gray.Observational causal question → DAG first, choose identification
strategy second.Want whole distribution treatment effect? → causal forest.

heading: 28.92 Common pitfalls
text: Adjusting post-treatment variable (induces collider bias).Quoting HR Cox model violates PH.Matching never checking balance.Calling IV estimates “generalisable” local compliers.

heading: 28.93 Further reading
text: Hernán & Robins, Causal Inference: .Therneau & Grambsch, Modeling Survival Data.

heading: 28.93.1 Synthesis and pre-registration## Systematic review workflow
text: PICO: Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcome.Register protocol PROSPERO.Build search across ≥ 2 databases; peer-review librarian.Two-screener title/abstract full-text.Extract, appraise (RoB 2 / ROBINS-), synthesise.Report PRISMA 2020 checklist flow diagram.

heading: 28.94 Meta-analysis
text:
Fixed-effect heterogeneity effectively zero. Default 
random effects.
code:
library(metafor)
dat <- escalc(measure = "RR",
              ai = tpos, bi = tneg, ci = cpos, di = cneg,
              data = studies)
fit <- rma(yi, vi, data = dat, method = "REML")
fit
forest(fit, header = TRUE)
funnel(fit); regtest(fit)

heading: 28.95 Network meta-analysis
text: Transitivity + consistency core assumptions.
code:
library(netmeta)
net <- netmeta(TE, seTE, treat1, treat2, studlab,
               data = dat, sm = "MD", random = TRUE)
netgraph(net)
netrank(net, small.values = "good")    # SUCRA ranking
decomp.design(net)                     # inconsistency

heading: 28.96 Infectious disease — SIR / SEIR
text: \(R_0 = \beta / \gamma\). Herd-immunity threshold \(\approx 1 - 1/R_0\).
code:
library(deSolve)
sir <- function(t, y, p) with(as.list(c(y, p)), {
  N <- S + I + R
  list(c(-beta * S * I / N,
          beta * S * I / N - gamma * I,
          gamma * I))
})
out <- ode(c(S = 999, I = 1, R = 0),
           times = 0:120,
           func = sir,
           parms = c(beta = 0.3, gamma = 0.1))

heading: 28.97 Pre-registration and SAP
text: pre-registration specifies, data analysis:Primary outcome + exact estimand.Analysis model (fixed terms, adjustments, interactions).Handling missing data.Multiplicity control.Sample-size justification.OSF ClinicalTrials.gov two common registries.

heading: 28.98 Decision rule
text:
handful studies question → plan systematic review.Multiple comparators interest → network meta-analysis.Outbreak modelling → start SIR, extend data justify.Confirmatory analysis → pre-registered; exploratory analyses 
labelled manuscript.

heading: 28.99 Common pitfalls
text: Declaring review “systematic” without pre-registered protocol.Pooling studies fundamentally different populations / outcomes.Interpreting SUCRA rankings certain.Calling compartmental model “forecast” without uncertainty.

heading: 28.100 Further reading
text: Higgins et al., Cochrane Handbook Systematic Reviews.Keeling & Rohani, Modeling Infectious Diseases Humans Animals.Nosek et al. (2018), preregistration revolution.

heading: 28.101.1 Cross-validation, regularisation, and unsupervised## Cross-validation
text:
Nested CV defensible way estimate generalisation error
also tuning hyperparameters.

heading: 28.102 Regularisation
text: lambda.min: empirical minimum CV error.lambda.1se: regularised, simpler, within 1 SE min.Elastic net: alpha 0 1 blends ridge lasso.
code:
library(glmnet)
x <- model.matrix(y ~ . - 1, df); y <- df$y
fit <- cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)     # alpha = 1 = lasso; 0 = ridge
coef(fit, s = "lambda.1se")           # sparse solution
plot(fit)                              # CV curve

heading: 28.103 Multivariate workhorses
text: Scale PCA variables different units.
code:
pc <- prcomp(x, scale. = TRUE)
summary(pc)   # proportion of variance per PC

heading: 28.104 Clustering
text: Choose k silhouette, gap statistic, BIC.Scale first. K-means assumes round clusters similar size.
code:
km <- kmeans(scale(x), centers = 4, nstart = 25)
hc <- hclust(dist(scale(x)), method = "ward.D2")
library(mclust); bic <- mclustBIC(x)      # model-based, BIC-selected

heading: 28.105 Non-linear embeddings
text:
Embeddings visualisations, distances input 
downstream stats. Global geometry often distorted.
code:
library(uwot)
umap_xy <- umap(scale(x), n_neighbors = 15, min_dist = 0.1)

library(Rtsne)
ts <- Rtsne(scale(x), perplexity = 30)$Y

heading: 28.106 Decision rule
text: Lots predictors, small n → lasso; report lambda.1se.Exploring structure → PCA + UMAP scaled data.Clustering biology → model-based + sensitivity k.Tuning anything → nested CV.

heading: 28.107 Common pitfalls
text: Selecting features full data, estimating error k-fold.PCA scaling features different units.Interpreting UMAP distances real distances.Using AUC rank-ordered hold-error CV error.

heading: 28.108 Further reading
text: Hastie, Tibshirani, Friedman, Elements Statistical Learning.James et al., ISLR2.

heading: 28.108.1 Trees, boosting, and interpretability## Tree-based models
code:
library(ranger)
fit <- ranger(y ~ ., data = df, importance = "permutation",
              num.trees = 500, mtry = floor(sqrt(ncol(df) - 1)))
fit$prediction.error   # OOB error

heading: 28.109 Interpretability
text:
PDP assumes feature independence; features correlated, use ALE
plots.
code:
library(DALEX); library(iml)

ex  <- explain(fit, data = df |> select(-y), y = df$y)
ip  <- model_parts(ex)           # permutation importance
pd  <- model_profile(ex, variables = "x1")   # partial dependence
sh  <- Shapley$new(Predictor$new(fit, data = df, y = df$y),
                   x.interest = df[1, ])     # SHAP

heading: 28.110 Tabular neural networks with torch
text:
small biomedical tabular data, boosted trees usually beat NN. Use
NN need end--end training images, sequences, text.
code:
# sketch only — typically in a loop on GPU
library(torch)
nn <- nn_sequential(
  nn_linear(p, 64), nn_relu(),
  nn_linear(64, 1)
)
opt <- optim_adam(nn$parameters)

heading: 28.111 tidymodels pipeline
code:
library(tidymodels)
rec  <- recipe(y ~ ., data = df) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors())

mod  <- rand_forest(mtry = tune(), trees = 500) |>
  set_engine("ranger") |> set_mode("classification")

wf   <- workflow() |> add_recipe(rec) |> add_model(mod)
cv   <- vfold_cv(df, v = 5, strata = y)
tuned <- tune_grid(wf, cv, grid = 20)
collect_metrics(tuned)

heading: 28.112 Decision rule
text: Tabular, < 100k rows → gradient-boosted trees.Need explanations → SHAP + PDP, validate held-.Images / sequences → CNN / transformer, use torch.Reproducibility → tidymodels pipeline + fixed seed + locked recipe.

heading: 28.113 Common pitfalls
text: Reporting feature importance model trained full data.Interpreting PDPs features strongly correlated.Claiming NN superiority without CV split tree model.Forgetting set seed tune / split operation.

heading: 28.114 Further reading
text: Biecek & Burzykowski, Explanatory Model Analysis.Molnar, Interpretable Machine Learning.

heading: 28.114.1 Bayesian and survival ML## Bayesian thinking
text:
\(\underbrace{p(\theta \mid y)}_{\text{posterior}} \propto
\underbrace{p(y \mid \theta)}_{\text{likelihood}}
\underbrace{p(\theta)}_{\text{prior}}\)Priors part model — specify defend .Posterior summaries: mean, median, 95% credible interval.p-values; use posterior probability direction,
Bayes factors, LOO.

heading: 28.115 brms / Stan
text:
Prior predictive check fitting: simulate \(y\) prior 
confirm implications plausible.
code:
library(brms)
fit <- brm(y ~ x + (1 | group), data = df,
           prior = c(prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b"),
                     prior(exponential(1), class = "sd")),
           chains = 4, iter = 2000, seed = 42)

summary(fit)
loo(fit)               # leave-one-out cross-validation
pp_check(fit)           # posterior predictive checks

heading: 28.116 Biomarker statistics
code:
pROC::roc(y, biomarker) |>
  pROC::coords("best", best.method = "youden")

heading: 28.117 Survival ML
text:
Evaluate time-dependent AUC, integrated Brier score, 
IPA (index prediction accuracy = 1 − Brier(model) / Brier(null)).
code:
library(timeROC)
r <- timeROC(T = df$time, delta = df$event,
             marker = pred, cause = 1, times = c(365, 730))

heading: 28.118 External validation
text:
Never trust apparent performance training data.Minimum: split-sample CV internal data.Better: external validation second cohort.Report calibration slope / intercept, discrimination, decision
curves.

heading: 28.119 Decision rule
text: Rare outcome need uncertainty → Bayesian, bootstrap MLE.New biomarker → NRI + decision curve, just ΔAUC.Survival prediction → time-dependent Brier / IPA, global AUC.clinical claim → external validation publication.

heading: 28.120 Common pitfalls
text:
Reporting posterior default flat priors unscaled predictors.Selecting Youden cut-full data using data
evaluate sensitivity / specificity.Quoting C-statistic single time point calling survival ML.Publishing prediction model without TRIPOD-compliant reporting.

heading: 28.121 Further reading
text: Gelman et al., Bayesian Data Analysis, 3e.Royston & Altman, External validation Cox prognostic model.

heading: 28.121.1 Omics, FDR, and TRIPOD-AI## Bulk RNA-seq
code:
library(DESeq2)
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(counts, coldata, ~ condition)
dds <- DESeq(dds)
res <- results(dds, contrast = c("condition", "B", "A"))
library(edgeR)
dge <- DGEList(counts, group = coldata$condition)
dge <- calcNormFactors(dge)
design <- model.matrix(~ condition, coldata)
fit <- glmQLFit(estimateDisp(dge, design), design)
qlf <- glmQLFTest(fit, coef = 2)
topTags(qlf)

heading: 28.122 Enrichment
text:
Always pre-rank signed statistic (log-FC × -log10 p), 
raw p alone.
code:
library(fgsea)
fgsea(pathways, stats = stat_vector, nperm = 10000) |>
  arrange(padj)

# Over-representation with a hypergeometric test
phyper(k - 1, K, N - K, n, lower.tail = FALSE)

heading: 28.123 Single-cell RNA-seq (Seurat pattern)
code:
library(Seurat)
so <- CreateSeuratObject(counts) |>
  NormalizeData() |>
  FindVariableFeatures() |>
  ScaleData() |>
  RunPCA() |>
  FindNeighbors() |>
  FindClusters(resolution = 0.4) |>
  RunUMAP(dims = 1:20)
DimPlot(so)

heading: 28.124 FDR, knockoffs, replication
text:
Replication crisis shorthand: pre-register + report effect sizes +
share data. Power, p-values.
code: p.adjust(pvals, method = "BH")

heading: 28.125 TRIPOD-AI + fairness
text:
TRIPOD-AI extends TRIPOD prediction models built ML.Report: data provenance, training/evaluation splits, subgroup
performance, calibration, external validation.Fairness: stratify AUC / calibration sex, ancestry, age bands.
Disparities calibration often larger AUC.

heading: 28.126 Reproducibility at scale with targets
code:
# _targets.R
library(targets)
tar_option_set(packages = c("tidyverse"))
list(
  tar_target(raw, read_csv("data/raw.csv")),
  tar_target(clean, clean_data(raw)),
  tar_target(fit, fit_model(clean)),
  tar_target(report, quarto::quarto_render("report.qmd"))
)Rscript -e 'targets::tar_make()'

heading: 28.127 Decision rule
text:
Bulk DE → DESeq2 small samples, edgeR/limma large.Many hypotheses → BH default; knockoffs variable selection.scRNA-seq → Seurat pipeline batch correction + cluster
annotation.ML prediction model → TRIPOD-AI + subgroup analysis.multi-step analysis → targets pipeline.

heading: 28.128 Common pitfalls
text: Running DE pathway-level aggregate rather gene-level.Cherry-picking pathway database post-hoc.-clustering scRNA-seq data “known” groups appear.Calling ML model “fair” checking overall AUC.Submitting paper without sessionInfo() lock file.

heading: 28.129 Further reading
text: Love, Huber, Anders (2014), Moderated estimation fold change…Collins et al. (2024), TRIPOD-AI.Peng, Reproducible Research R.
